Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The distinction between guardians who 'walk by intelligence' and those who act by 'true opinion' echoes the Republic's distinction between knowledge and true belief (Republic 5.476d-477a) and anticipates the Allegory of the Cave. -- 'Mind will bind together all his ordinances' -- nous as the unifying principle of law is a central theme of the Laws. -- The scope of legislation described (economic regulation, contract law, funerary law) corresponds closely to the actual legislative program in Laws Books 5-12. -- The phrase 'laws of Zeus' and 'Pythian Apollo' refers back to the opening claim of divine authorship (1.1). -- Cleinias's question 'How shall we proceed?' marks the transition from the theoretical framework to the detailed examination of specific virtues.

D: Original: I think that we must begin again as before, and first consider the habit of courage; and then we will go on and discuss another and then another form of virtue, if you please. In this way we shall have a model of the whole; and with these and similar discourses we will beguile the way. And when we have gone through all the virtues, we will show, by the grace of God, that the institutions of which I was speaking look to virtue.
D: Narrative: "I think that we must begin again as before, and first consider the habit of courage," said the Athenian Stranger, "and then we will go on and discuss another and then another form of virtue, if you please. In this way we shall have a model of the whole; and with these and similar discourses we will beguile the way. And when we have gone through all the virtues, we will show, by the grace of God, that the institutions of which I was speaking look to virtue."
D: Refashioned: "I think we should start again where we began," said the Stranger, "first examining courage, and then moving on to discuss one form of virtue after another. This way we'll build a model of the whole. We'll pass the journey pleasantly with this kind of discussion. And once we've worked through all the virtues, we will show, by the grace of God, that the institutions I described truly aim at virtue."
D: Elegant: The Stranger proposes a methodical plan: examine each virtue in turn -- beginning again with courage -- to build a complete model. By the journey's end, they will demonstrate that Cretan and Spartan institutions genuinely aim at virtue as a whole.
D: Summary: The Stranger outlines their method: they will examine each virtue in turn, starting with courage, to construct a complete model. By the end of the journey, they aim to show that the institutions in question truly serve all of virtue.
D: Distilled: The methodical plan: examine each virtue in turn, beginning with courage, to prove that true legislation serves complete virtue.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Begin again as before' signals a fresh start after the preliminary argument (1.1-1.18) has established the framework. -- The method of proceeding virtue by virtue parallels the Republic's examination of the four cardinal virtues in Book 4. -- 'Beguile the way' echoes the walking metaphor from 1.2, keeping the physical journey and intellectual journey intertwined. -- 'By the grace of God' reflects the pious tone that distinguishes the Laws from Plato's earlier, more rationalistic dialogues. -- The promise to show that institutions 'look to virtue' is the thesis the rest of the Laws aims to demonstrate.

D: Original:
Very good; and suppose that you first criticize this praiser of Zeus and the laws of Crete.

I will try to criticize you and myself, as well as him, for the argument is a common concern. Tell me-were not first the syssitia, and secondly the gymnasia, invented by your legislator with a view to war?

Yes.

And what comes third, and what fourth? For that, I think, is the sort of enumeration which ought to be made of the remaining parts of virtue, no matter whether you call them parts or what their name is, provided the meaning is clear.

Then I, or any other Lacedaemonian, would reply that hunting is third in order.

Let us see if we can discover what comes fourth and fifth.
D: Narrative:
"Very good; and suppose that you first criticize this praiser of Zeus and the laws of Crete," said Megillus.

"I will try to criticize you and myself, as well as him, for the argument is a common concern," replied the Athenian Stranger. "Tell me -- were not first the syssitia, and secondly the gymnasia, invented by your legislator with a view to war?"

"Yes."

"And what comes third, and what fourth? For that, I think, is the sort of enumeration which ought to be made of the remaining parts of virtue, no matter whether you call them parts or what their name is, provided the meaning is clear."

"Then I, or any other Lacedaemonian, would reply that hunting is third in order," said Megillus.

"Let us see if we can discover what comes fourth and fifth."
D: Refashioned:
"Very well -- suppose you start by critiquing our friend the praiser of Zeus and the Cretan laws," Megillus suggested.

"I'll try to criticize all of us -- you, myself, and him -- since the argument is our shared concern," the Stranger replied. "Tell me: weren't the common meals first, and the gymnasia second, among your legislator's institutions designed for war?"

"Yes."

"And what comes third and fourth? That's the kind of enumeration we should make for the remaining parts of virtue -- whether you call them 'parts' or something else, as long as the meaning is clear."

"I, or any other Spartan, would say hunting comes third," said Megillus.

"Let's see if we can figure out what comes fourth and fifth."
D: Elegant: The Stranger proposes to criticize all three of them equally, then enumerates Cretan and Spartan institutions designed for war: common meals first, gymnasia second, hunting third. He asks what comes fourth and fifth in the training of courage.
D: Summary: Megillus invites the Stranger to critique Cretan laws. The Stranger agrees to criticize all three of them, then enumerates war-related institutions: common meals, gymnasia, and hunting. He asks what further institutions exist.
D: Distilled: The enumeration of military institutions -- meals, gymnastics, hunting -- begins the specific examination of how Cretan and Spartan laws cultivate courage.
Notes: Speakers: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- Syssitia (common meals) and gymnasia were the foundational institutions of both Spartan and Cretan civic life. -- Hunting was a central part of Spartan education (the agoge). Xenophon's Cynegeticus praises hunting as military training. -- The insistence that naming conventions don't matter ('whether you call them parts or what') reflects Plato's characteristic concern with substance over terminology (cf. 1.8, Sophist 218c). -- This passage begins the detailed examination of courage-training that leads to the discussion of pleasure and drinking.

D: Original: I think that I can get as far as the fouth head, which is the frequent endurance of pain, exhibited among us Spartans in certain hand-to-hand fights; also in stealing with the prospect of getting a good beating; there is, too, the so-called Crypteia, or secret service, in which wonderful endurance is shown-our people wander over the whole country by day and by night, and even in winter have not a shoe to their foot, and are without beds to lie upon, and have to attend upon themselves. Marvellous, too, is the endurance which our citizens show in their naked exercises, contending against the violent summer heat; and there are many similar practices, to speak of which in detail would be endless.
D: Narrative:
"I think that I can get as far as the fourth head, which is the frequent endurance of pain, exhibited among us Spartans in certain hand-to-hand fights," said Megillus, "also in stealing with the prospect of getting a good beating; there is, too, the so-called Crypteia, or secret service, in which wonderful endurance is shown -- our people wander over the whole country by day and by night, and even in winter have not a shoe to their foot, and are without beds to lie upon, and have to attend upon themselves.

"Marvellous, too, is the endurance which our citizens show in their naked exercises, contending against the violent summer heat; and there are many similar practices, to speak of which in detail would be endless."
D: Refashioned:
"I can get to the fourth category," said Megillus. "It is the frequent endurance of pain. Among us Spartans this appears in certain hand-to-hand combats, and in ritual theft where you're beaten if caught. Then there's the Crypteia -- the secret service -- which demands extraordinary endurance: our young men roam the entire countryside day and night, even in winter, barefoot and without beds, entirely self-reliant.

"There's also the remarkable endurance shown in our naked exercises, contending against brutal summer heat. And there are countless similar practices -- to describe them all would take forever."
D: Elegant: Megillus identifies the fourth element of Spartan training: the systematic endurance of pain -- through ritual combat, ritual theft with beatings, the Crypteia (barefoot wilderness survival in all seasons), and naked exercises in extreme heat.
D: Summary: Megillus describes the fourth component of Spartan training: endurance of pain. This includes ritual fighting, theft with the risk of beating, the Crypteia (secret service requiring barefoot wandering in all weather), and naked exercises in extreme heat.
D: Distilled: Spartan courage-training centers on systematic endurance of pain through ritual combat, the Crypteia, and exposure to physical extremes.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus. -- The Crypteia was a Spartan institution where young men were sent into the countryside with minimal provisions, reportedly to prove their endurance and to terrorize the helot population. Plutarch (Life of Lycurgus 28) describes it in detail. -- The ritual stealing was part of the Spartan agoge (education system): boys were encouraged to steal food but beaten if caught, training both cunning and pain tolerance. Xenophon describes this in Constitution of the Lacedaemonians 2.6-8. -- The naked exercises (gymnos = naked, hence 'gymnasium') in extreme heat reflect the Spartan principle that comfort breeds weakness. -- The Stranger will use this comprehensive picture of pain-training to ask the critical question: where is the corresponding training against pleasure?

D: Original:
Excellent, O Lacedaemonian Stranger. But how ought we to define courage? Is it to be regarded only as a combat against fears and pains, or also against desires and pleasures, and against flatteries; which exercise such a tremendous power, that they make the hearts even of respectable citizens to melt like wax?

I should say the latter.

In what preceded, as you will remember, our Cnosian friend was speaking of a man or a city being inferior to themselves:-Were you not, Cleinias?

I was.

Now, which is in the truest sense inferior, the man who is overcome by pleasure or by pain?

I should say the man who is overcome by pleasure; for all men deem him to be inferior in a more disgraceful sense, than the other who is overcome by pain.

But surely the lawgivers of Crete and Lacedaemon have not legislated for a courage which is lame of one leg, able only to meet attacks which come from the left, but impotent against the insidious flatteries which come from the right?

Able to meet both, I should say.
D: Narrative:
"Excellent, O Lacedaemonian Stranger," said the Athenian. "But how ought we to define courage? Is it to be regarded only as a combat against fears and pains, or also against desires and pleasures, and against flatteries; which exercise such a tremendous power, that they make the hearts even of respectable citizens to melt like wax?"

"I should say the latter," replied Megillus.

"In what preceded, as you will remember, our Cnosian friend was speaking of a man or a city being inferior to themselves. Were you not, Cleinias?"

"I was."

"Now, which is in the truest sense inferior, the man who is overcome by pleasure or by pain?"

"I should say the man who is overcome by pleasure," replied Cleinias, "for all men deem him to be inferior in a more disgraceful sense, than the other who is overcome by pain."

"But surely the lawgivers of Crete and Lacedaemon have not legislated for a courage which is lame of one leg, able only to meet attacks which come from the left, but impotent against the insidious flatteries which come from the right?"

"Able to meet both, I should say," replied Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Excellent," said the Stranger. "But how should we define courage? Is it only a fight against fears and pains? Or does it also include the battle against desires, pleasures, and flatteries -- which exert such tremendous power that they make even respectable citizens' hearts melt like wax?"

"The latter," Megillus answered.

"You'll recall that our Cretan friend was speaking earlier about a man or a city being inferior to itself. Weren't you, Cleinias?"

"I was."

"Now tell me: who is truly more inferior -- the man overcome by pleasure or the man overcome by pain?"

"The man overcome by pleasure," Cleinias replied. "Everyone considers his condition more disgraceful than that of the man overcome by pain."

"Then surely the Cretan and Spartan lawgivers didn't design a courage that is lame in one leg -- able to resist attacks from the left but powerless against the insidious flatteries coming from the right?"

"Able to meet both, I should say."
D: Elegant: The Stranger redefines courage: it is not merely resistance to pain and fear, but also resistance to pleasure, desire, and flattery -- forces that melt even respectable hearts like wax. Being overcome by pleasure is more disgraceful than being overcome by pain. True courage must not be 'lame of one leg,' able to fight pain but helpless against pleasure.
D: Summary: The Stranger expands the definition of courage beyond resistance to pain to include resistance to pleasure and desire. Cleinias agrees that being overcome by pleasure is more disgraceful than being overcome by pain, and that true legislation must train against both.
D: Distilled: Courage is not just resistance to pain but equally resistance to pleasure -- and yielding to pleasure is the more disgraceful defeat.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Megillus, Cleinias. -- The image of courage 'lame of one leg' is vivid and memorable -- a characteristically Platonic metaphor. -- The idea that pleasure is more dangerous than pain because it corrupts more insidiously parallels the Republic's treatment of the 'democratic' soul seduced by desires (Republic 8.559d-561a). -- 'Hearts melt like wax' echoes Homer (Iliad 22.501; Odyssey 19.205-208). -- This passage is the pivot toward the drinking discussion: if courage must be trained against pleasure, what institution trains this? The answer will be the properly conducted symposium. -- Cf. Protagoras 353c-354e, where Socrates argues that being overcome by pleasure is really a failure of knowledge.

D: Original: Then let me once more ask, what institutions have you in either of your states which give a taste of pleasures, and do not avoid them any more than they avoid pains; but which set a person in the midst of them, and compel or induce him by the prospect of reward to get the better of them? Where is an ordinance about pleasure similar to that about pain to be found in your laws? Tell me what there is of this nature among you:-What is there which makes your citizen equally brave against pleasure and pain, conquering what they ought to conquer, and superior to the enemies who are most dangerous and nearest home?
D: Narrative: "Then let me once more ask," said the Athenian Stranger, "what institutions have you in either of your states which give a taste of pleasures, and do not avoid them any more than they avoid pains; but which set a person in the midst of them, and compel or induce him by the prospect of reward to get the better of them? Where is an ordinance about pleasure similar to that about pain to be found in your laws? Tell me what there is of this nature among you -- what is there which makes your citizen equally brave against pleasure and pain, conquering what they ought to conquer, and superior to the enemies who are most dangerous and nearest home?"
D: Refashioned: "Let me ask again," said the Stranger. "What institutions exist in either of your states that expose citizens to pleasures -- not avoiding them any more than they avoid pains -- placing a person in the middle of temptation and compelling or motivating him to overcome them? Where in your laws is there an ordinance about pleasure corresponding to the ones about pain? Tell me: what makes your citizens equally brave against pleasure and pain, conquering what they should conquer, and proving superior to the enemies that are most dangerous and closest to home?"
D: Elegant: The Stranger poses the decisive challenge: your laws train people to endure pain, but where are the institutions that train them to resist pleasure? What makes a citizen equally courageous against the more dangerous, closer enemies of desire and temptation?
D: Summary: The Stranger asks the crucial question: where are the institutions that train citizens to resist pleasure as they are trained to resist pain? What in Cretan or Spartan law creates equal courage against desire and temptation?
D: Distilled: The critical gap in Cretan and Spartan law: extensive training against pain, but nothing comparable against the more dangerous enemy of pleasure.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Enemies who are most dangerous and nearest home' -- pleasure is the internal enemy, more threatening than external foes. This echoes the earlier argument (1.6) that the greatest war is the war within oneself. -- The phrase 'give a taste of pleasures' without avoiding them anticipates the argument for controlled exposure to wine as a training ground for temperance. -- This question has no good answer within the Spartan system, which relied on avoidance and prohibition of pleasure rather than controlled engagement with it. -- The Stranger's approach (training through exposure rather than avoidance) has parallels in cognitive-behavioral therapy and modern resilience training.

D: Original:
I was able to tell you, Stranger, many laws which were directed against pain; but I do not know that I can point out any great or obvious examples of similar institutions which are concerned with pleasure; there are some lesser provisions, however, which I might mention.

Neither can I show anything of that sort which is at all equally prominent in the Cretan laws.

No wonder, my dear friends; and if, as is very likely, in our search after the true and good, one of us may have to censure the laws of the others, we must not be offended, but take kindly what another says.

You are quite right, Athenian Stranger, and we will do as you say.

At our time of life, Cleinias, there should be no feeling of irritation.

Certainly not.
D: Narrative:
"I was able to tell you, Stranger, many laws which were directed against pain; but I do not know that I can point out any great or obvious examples of similar institutions which are concerned with pleasure; there are some lesser provisions, however, which I might mention," said Megillus.

"Neither can I show anything of that sort which is at all equally prominent in the Cretan laws," added Cleinias.

"No wonder, my dear friends," said the Athenian Stranger, "and if, as is very likely, in our search after the true and good, one of us may have to censure the laws of the others, we must not be offended, but take kindly what another says."

"You are quite right, Athenian Stranger, and we will do as you say," replied Cleinias.

"At our time of life, Cleinias, there should be no feeling of irritation."

"Certainly not."
D: Refashioned:
"I was able to list many laws directed against pain," Megillus admitted, "but I can't point to any great or obvious institutions concerned with pleasure. There are some minor provisions I could mention."

"Nor can I show anything equally prominent in Cretan law," Cleinias added.

"No wonder, my friends," said the Stranger. "And if, as is likely in our search for truth and goodness, one of us has to criticize another's laws, we must not take offense but receive it graciously."

"You're quite right, Stranger, and we'll do as you say," Cleinias replied.

"At our age, Cleinias, there should be no room for irritation."

"Certainly not."
D: Elegant: Both Megillus and Cleinias concede they cannot identify major institutions for training against pleasure. The Stranger gently prepares them for criticism, asking that they receive it without offense -- befitting men of their age.
D: Summary: Both Megillus and Cleinias admit they cannot point to significant institutions that train citizens against pleasure, only against pain. The Stranger diplomatically asks that they accept mutual criticism without offense as they pursue the truth together.
D: Distilled: Neither Sparta nor Crete has significant institutions for training against pleasure -- a critical gap both interlocutors acknowledge.
Notes: Speakers: Megillus, Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. -- Megillus's admission is significant: the most militaristic Greek state has no systematic pleasure-training. -- The Stranger's request for open-minded reception of criticism models the ideal conditions for philosophical inquiry. -- 'At our time of life' -- the speakers' old age licenses frank discussion (cf. 1.25-1.26 on the law permitting only elders to critique legislation). -- The gap identified here -- training against pain but not against pleasure -- is the argumentative opening through which the Stranger will introduce the value of regulated drinking.

D: Original: I will not at present determine whether he who censures the Cretan or Lacedaemonian polities is right or wrong. But I believe that I can tell better than either of you what the many say about them. For assuming that you have reasonably good laws, one of the best of them will be the law forbidding any young men to enquire which of them are right or wrong; but with one mouth and one voice they must all agree that the laws are all good, for they came from God; and any one who says the contrary is not to be listened to. But an old man who remarks any defect in your laws may communicate his observation to a ruler or to an equal in years when no young man is present.
D: Narrative: "I will not at present determine whether he who censures the Cretan or Lacedaemonian polities is right or wrong," said the Athenian Stranger. "But I believe that I can tell better than either of you what the many say about them. For assuming that you have reasonably good laws, one of the best of them will be the law forbidding any young men to enquire which of them are right or wrong; but with one mouth and one voice they must all agree that the laws are all good, for they came from God; and any one who says the contrary is not to be listened to. But an old man who remarks any defect in your laws may communicate his observation to a ruler or to an equal in years when no young man is present."
D: Refashioned: "I won't determine right now whether anyone who criticizes Cretan or Spartan institutions is correct or not," said the Stranger. "But I think I understand better than either of you what most people say about them. Assuming your laws are reasonably good, one of the best among them must be the law forbidding young men from questioning whether any law is right or wrong. With one voice they must all declare that the laws are good, because they came from God, and anyone who says otherwise must not be heard. But an old man who notices a defect in your laws may share his observation with a ruler or an age-mate, provided no young person is present."
D: Elegant: The Stranger identifies a wise provision: young men are forbidden from questioning laws (which must be accepted as divinely given), but old men may criticize them privately to rulers or peers when no youth is present.
D: Summary: The Stranger describes a notable provision in Cretan and Spartan law: young men must accept all laws as divinely good without question, but old men may privately share criticisms with rulers or peers when no young person is present.
D: Distilled: The young must accept laws without question, but the old may privately critique them -- protecting both civic order and the possibility of reform.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage reflects a sophisticated understanding of social stability: public questioning of laws by the young breeds instability, but the inability to critique laws at all prevents reform. The solution is age-restricted private criticism. -- The 'came from God' justification echoes the opening (1.1) and raises the question of whether divine authorship precludes improvement. -- Cf. Republic 3.389b-c on the permissibility of noble lies for social cohesion. -- The provision anticipates the Laws's own structure: three old men walking privately, with no young audience, conducting exactly the kind of elder critique described here.

D: Original:
Exactly so, Stranger; and like a diviner, although not there at the time, you seem to me quite to have hit the meaning of the legislator, and to say what is most true.

As there are no young men present, and the legislator has given old men free licence, there will be no impropriety in our discussing these very matters now that we are alone.

True. And therefore you may be as free as you like in your censure of our laws, for there is no discredit in knowing what is wrong; he who receives what is said in a generous and friendly spirit will be all the better for it.
D: Narrative:
"Exactly so, Stranger; and like a diviner, although not there at the time, you seem to me quite to have hit the meaning of the legislator, and to say what is most true," said Cleinias.

"As there are no young men present, and the legislator has given old men free licence, there will be no impropriety in our discussing these very matters now that we are alone," replied the Athenian Stranger.

"True," said Cleinias. "And therefore you may be as free as you like in your censure of our laws, for there is no discredit in knowing what is wrong; he who receives what is said in a generous and friendly spirit will be all the better for it."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly right, Stranger. Like a prophet, even though you weren't there, you seem to have grasped the legislator's intention perfectly and spoken the truth," said Cleinias.

"Since no young men are present, and the legislator has given old men this freedom, there will be nothing improper in our discussing these matters privately," the Stranger replied.

"True," Cleinias agreed. "So feel free to criticize our laws as openly as you wish. There is no shame in recognizing what is wrong. Anyone who receives such observations in a generous and friendly spirit will only benefit from them."
D: Elegant: Cleinias praises the Stranger's insight into Cretan law. Since they are three old men walking alone -- exactly the circumstance the law permits for critique -- they may freely examine their laws. There is no shame in learning what is wrong.
D: Summary: Cleinias praises the Stranger's insight and confirms they are in the exact circumstance the law permits for critique: old men alone, without young audiences. He invites full freedom of criticism, noting there is no shame in recognizing errors.
D: Distilled: Three old men alone on a road -- the perfect circumstance for the kind of frank legal critique that their own laws permit.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's comparison of the Stranger to a 'diviner' (mantis) is significant: it attributes a quasi-prophetic quality to philosophical insight. -- The dramatic situation perfectly fulfills the conditions just described in 1.25: three elderly men in private conversation with no young men present. Plato has constructed the dramatic setting to license exactly the critique he wants to conduct. -- 'There is no discredit in knowing what is wrong' expresses a philosophical openness that contrasts with the typical Greek competitive defensiveness about one's city's institutions. -- This exchange establishes the ground rules for the rest of the dialogue: frank, friendly, truth-seeking inquiry.

D: Original: Very good; however, I am not going to say anything against your laws until to the best of my ability I have examined them, but I am going to raise doubts about them. For you are the only people known to us, whether Greek or barbarian, whom the legislator commanded to eschew all great pleasures and amusements and never to touch them; whereas in the matter of pains or fears which we have just been discussing, he thought that they who from infancy had always avoided pains and fears and sorrows, when they were compelled to face them would run away from those who were hardened in them, and would become their subjects. Now the legislator ought to have considered that this was equally true of pleasure; he should have said to himself, that if our citizens are from their youth upward unacquainted with the greatest pleasures, and unused to endure amid the temptations of pleasure, and are not disciplined to refrain from all things evil, the sweet feeling of pleasure will overcome them just as fear would overcome the former class; and in another, and even a worse manner, they will be the slaves of those who are able to endure amid pleasures, and have had the opportunity of enjoying them, they being often the worst of mankind. One half of their souls will be a slave, the other half free; and they will not be worthy to be called in the true sense men and freemen. Tell me whether you assent to my words?
D: Narrative:
"Very good; however, I am not going to say anything against your laws until to the best of my ability I have examined them, but I am going to raise doubts about them," said the Athenian Stranger. "For you are the only people known to us, whether Greek or barbarian, whom the legislator commanded to eschew all great pleasures and amusements and never to touch them; whereas in the matter of pains or fears which we have just been discussing, he thought that they who from infancy had always avoided pains and fears and sorrows, when they were compelled to face them would run away from those who were hardened in them, and would become their subjects.

"Now the legislator ought to have considered that this was equally true of pleasure; he should have said to himself, that if our citizens are from their youth upward unacquainted with the greatest pleasures, and unused to endure amid the temptations of pleasure, and are not disciplined to refrain from all things evil, the sweet feeling of pleasure will overcome them just as fear would overcome the former class; and in another, and even a worse manner, they will be the slaves of those who are able to endure amid pleasures, and have had the opportunity of enjoying them, they being often the worst of mankind.

"One half of their souls will be a slave, the other half free; and they will not be worthy to be called in the true sense men and freemen. Tell me whether you assent to my words?"
D: Refashioned:
"Very well -- but I won't speak against your laws until I've examined them as thoroughly as I can. I will, however, raise doubts," said the Stranger.

"You are the only people known to us, Greek or barbarian, whose legislator commanded the complete avoidance of great pleasures and amusements. Yet in the matter of pains and fears we just discussed, your legislator understood that those who have always avoided pain from infancy, when forced to face it, will flee from those hardened to it and become their subjects.

"The legislator should have recognized that the same principle applies to pleasure. He should have told himself: if our citizens grow up unacquainted with the greatest pleasures, unaccustomed to enduring temptation, and undisciplined in refusing evil, then the sweet pull of pleasure will overcome them -- just as fear overcomes the untrained. And in an even worse way, they will become slaves to those who can endure amid pleasures and have had the chance to enjoy them, even though such people are often the worst of mankind.

"Half their souls will be enslaved, the other half free -- and they will not deserve to be called truly free men. Do you agree?"
D: Elegant: The Stranger identifies a fatal inconsistency: the Cretan and Spartan lawgivers understood that avoiding pain creates vulnerability to pain, yet they commanded total avoidance of pleasure. By the same logic, citizens raised without exposure to temptation become slaves to pleasure when they encounter it -- and slaves specifically to those experienced in pleasure, who are often the worst people. Half their soul is free, half enslaved: they cannot be called truly free.
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that Cretan and Spartan laws contain a critical inconsistency: they train against pain through exposure but command total avoidance of pleasure. Citizens raised without experiencing temptation become enslaved by pleasure when they encounter it, leaving half their souls unfree.
D: Distilled: Avoiding pleasure entirely creates the same vulnerability as avoiding pain -- citizens untrained against temptation become enslaved by it.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Half slave, half free' echoes the Republic's description of the internally divided soul (Republic 4.440a-441c). -- The argument that avoidance creates vulnerability rather than strength is a sophisticated psychological insight. It anticipates modern exposure therapy and the concept of 'antifragility.' -- 'Whether Greek or barbarian' universalizes the argument beyond Greek parochialism. -- The Stranger distinguishes between 'saying something against' laws and 'raising doubts' -- a careful methodological distinction between criticism and inquiry. -- The claim that those experienced in pleasure are 'often the worst of mankind' refers to the corrupting effect of unregulated luxury (cf. Republic 8-9 on tyrannical personalities).

D: Original:
On first hearing, what you say appears to be the truth; but to be hasty in coming to a conclusion about such important matters would be very childish and simple.

Suppose, Cleinias and Megillus, that we consider the virtue which follows next of those which we intended to discuss (for after courage comes temperance), what institutions shall we find relating to temperance, either in Crete or Lacedaemon, which, like your military institutions, differ from those of any ordinary state.

That is not an easy question to answer; still I should say that the common meals and gymnastic exercises have been excellently devised for the promotion both of temperance and courage.
D: Narrative:
"On first hearing, what you say appears to be the truth; but to be hasty in coming to a conclusion about such important matters would be very childish and simple," said Cleinias.

"Suppose, Cleinias and Megillus, that we consider the virtue which follows next of those which we intended to discuss -- for after courage comes temperance," said the Athenian Stranger. "What institutions shall we find relating to temperance, either in Crete or Lacedaemon, which, like your military institutions, differ from those of any ordinary state?"

"That is not an easy question to answer," said Megillus, "still I should say that the common meals and gymnastic exercises have been excellently devised for the promotion both of temperance and courage."
D: Refashioned:
"At first hearing, what you say seems true," Cleinias replied, "but it would be childish and naive to rush to conclusions on such important matters."

"Then let us consider the next virtue in our sequence," said the Stranger. "After courage comes temperance. What institutions in Crete or Sparta relate to temperance, just as your military institutions relate to courage -- something that distinguishes you from ordinary states?"

"That's not an easy question to answer," Megillus said, "but I would say that the common meals and gymnastic exercises were excellently designed to promote both temperance and courage."
D: Elegant: Cleinias accepts the argument provisionally but urges caution. The Stranger turns to the next virtue: temperance. Megillus can only offer the same institutions already discussed -- common meals and gymnastics -- as serving both courage and temperance.
D: Summary: Cleinias cautiously accepts the argument about pleasure. The Stranger shifts to temperance, asking what distinctive institutions promote it. Megillus can only cite the common meals and gymnastics already discussed for courage.
D: Distilled: When asked about institutions for temperance, Megillus can only name the same ones designed for courage -- revealing the gap in Spartan legislation.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger, Megillus. -- Cleinias's caution ('childish and simple to be hasty') shows him as a more careful thinker than typical Socratic interlocutors who agree too quickly. -- The transition from courage to temperance follows the order established in 1.17 (wisdom, temperance, justice, courage -- working backward from courage). -- Megillus's inability to name distinct temperance-institutions confirms the Stranger's critique: Spartan law is comprehensive on courage but thin on other virtues. -- The common meals (syssitia) did regulate food consumption and thus had a temperance dimension, but Megillus cannot articulate this distinctly.

D: Original: There seems to be a difficulty, Stranger, with regard to states, in making words and facts coincide so that there can be no dispute about them. As in the human body, the regimen which does good in one way does harm in another; and we can hardly say that any one course of treatment is adapted to a particular constitution. Now the gymnasia and common meals do a great deal of good, and yet they are a source of evil in civil troubles; as is shown in the case of the Milesian, and Boeotian, and Thurian youth, among whom these institutions seem always to have had a tendency to degrade the ancient and natural custom of love below the level, not only of man, but of the beasts. The charge may be fairly brought against your cities above all others, and is true also of most other states which especially cultivate gymnastics. Whether such matters are to be regarded jestingly or seriously, I think that the pleasure is to be deemed natural which arises out of the intercourse between men and women; but that the intercourse of men with men, or of women with women, is contrary to nature, and that the bold attempt was originally due to unbridled lust. The Cretans are always accused of having invented the story of Ganymede and Zeus because they wanted to justify themselves in the enjoyment of unnatural pleasures by the practice of the god whom they believe to have been their lawgiver. Leaving the story, we may observe that any speculation about laws turns almost entirely on pleasure and pain, both in states and in individuals: these are two fountains which nature lets flow, and he who draws from them where and when, and as much as he ought, is happy; and this holds of men and animals-of individuals as well as states; and he who indulges in them ignorantly and at the wrong time, is the reverse of happy.
D: Narrative:
"There seems to be a difficulty, Stranger, with regard to states, in making words and facts coincide so that there can be no dispute about them," said the Athenian Stranger. "As in the human body, the regimen which does good in one way does harm in another; and we can hardly say that any one course of treatment is adapted to a particular constitution.

"Now the gymnasia and common meals do a great deal of good, and yet they are a source of evil in civil troubles; as is shown in the case of the Milesian, and Boeotian, and Thurian youth, among whom these institutions seem always to have had a tendency to degrade the ancient and natural custom of love below the level, not only of man, but of the beasts. The charge may be fairly brought against your cities above all others, and is true also of most other states which especially cultivate gymnastics.

"Whether such matters are to be regarded jestingly or seriously, I think that the pleasure is to be deemed natural which arises out of the intercourse between men and women; but that the intercourse of men with men, or of women with women, is contrary to nature, and that the bold attempt was originally due to unbridled lust.

"The Cretans are always accused of having invented the story of Ganymede and Zeus because they wanted to justify themselves in the enjoyment of unnatural pleasures by the practice of the god whom they believe to have been their lawgiver.

"Leaving the story, we may observe that any speculation about laws turns almost entirely on pleasure and pain, both in states and in individuals: these are two fountains which nature lets flow, and he who draws from them where and when, and as much as he ought, is happy; and this holds of men and animals -- of individuals as well as states; and he who indulges in them ignorantly and at the wrong time, is the reverse of happy."
D: Refashioned:
"There is a difficulty with states," the Stranger observed, "in making words and facts align without dispute. Like the human body, where a treatment that helps in one way can harm in another, we can hardly say that any single course of action is perfectly suited to every constitution.

"Gymnasia and common meals produce great benefits, yet they also breed evil during civil unrest -- as seen among the youth of Miletus, Boeotia, and Thurii, where these institutions have tended to degrade natural love below the level not just of humans, but of animals. This charge applies especially to your cities, and also to most other states that emphasize gymnastics.

"Whether we treat this lightly or seriously, I hold that the pleasure arising from the union of men and women is natural, while sexual relations between men and men, or women and women, are contrary to nature -- the result of unbridled desire.

"The Cretans are always accused of inventing the story of Ganymede and Zeus to justify their practices by attributing them to their divine lawgiver.

"Setting that story aside, we should note that all reflection about laws comes down to pleasure and pain in both states and individuals. These are two fountains that nature lets flow, and whoever draws from them at the right time, in the right place, and in the right measure, is happy. This holds for people and animals, individuals and states alike. Whoever indulges ignorantly or at the wrong time lives the opposite of a happy life."
D: Elegant: Institutions have unintended consequences: gymnasia and common meals promote virtue but also create conditions for vice, particularly unnatural sexual practices. The Stranger condemns same-sex relations as contrary to nature. Setting aside this controversy, the deeper point emerges: all legislation ultimately concerns pleasure and pain. These are nature's two fountains -- and happiness depends on drawing from them in the right measure, at the right time.
D: Summary: The Stranger acknowledges that institutions like gymnasia and common meals have both good and bad effects, citing their association with unnatural sexual practices. He then articulates the fundamental insight: all law ultimately concerns the regulation of pleasure and pain, nature's two fountains, and happiness depends on drawing from them correctly.
D: Distilled: All legislation ultimately comes down to the regulation of pleasure and pain -- nature's two fountains from which we must draw in the right measure.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The discussion of homosexuality is among the most controversial passages in Plato. The Stranger calls it 'contrary to nature' (para phusin), contrasting with the more nuanced treatment in the Symposium and Phaedrus. The Laws represents Plato's strictest position on this topic. -- The Ganymede myth (Zeus abducting a beautiful boy) was widely used in Greek culture to justify pederasty. The Stranger's claim that the Cretans invented it is polemical. -- Miletus, Boeotia, and Thurii were proverbially associated with sexual license. -- The 'two fountains' metaphor is powerful and original: pleasure and pain as natural resources that must be regulated. -- The idea that happiness depends on drawing pleasure and pain 'where and when, and as much as one ought' is the Aristotelian doctrine of the mean avant la lettre (cf. Nicomachean Ethics 2.6).

D: Original: I admit, Stranger, that your words are well spoken, and I hardly know what to say in answer to you; but still I think that the Spartan lawgiver was quite right in forbidding pleasure. Of the Cretan laws, I shall leave the defence to my Cnosian friend. But the laws of Sparta, in as far as they relate to pleasure, appear to me to be the best in the world; for that which leads mankind in general into the wildest pleasure and licence, and every other folly, the law has clean driven out; and neither in the country nor in towns which are under the control of Sparta, will you find revelries and the many incitements of every kind of pleasure which accompany them; and any one who meets a drunken and disorderly person, will immediately have him most severely punished, and will not let him off on any pretence, not even at the time of a Dionysiac festival; although I have remarked that this may happen at your performances "on the cart," as they are called; and among our Tarentine colonists I have seen the whole city drunk at a Dionysiac festival; but nothing of the sort happens among us.
D: Narrative:
"I admit, Stranger, that your words are well spoken, and I hardly know what to say in answer to you," said Megillus, "but still I think that the Spartan lawgiver was quite right in forbidding pleasure. Of the Cretan laws, I shall leave the defence to my Cnosian friend.

"But the laws of Sparta, in as far as they relate to pleasure, appear to me to be the best in the world; for that which leads mankind in general into the wildest pleasure and licence, and every other folly, the law has clean driven out; and neither in the country nor in towns which are under the control of Sparta, will you find revelries and the many incitements of every kind of pleasure which accompany them; and any one who meets a drunken and disorderly person, will immediately have him most severely punished, and will not let him off on any pretence, not even at the time of a Dionysiac festival; although I have remarked that this may happen at your performances 'on the cart,' as they are called; and among our Tarentine colonists I have seen the whole city drunk at a Dionysiac festival; but nothing of the sort happens among us."
D: Refashioned:
"I admit, Stranger, that your words are well spoken, and I hardly know how to answer," said Megillus. "But I still think the Spartan lawgiver was absolutely right to forbid pleasure. I'll leave the defense of Cretan laws to my friend from Knossos.

"Spartan laws concerning pleasure seem to me the best in the world. Everything that leads people into wild pleasure, license, and folly has been completely driven out. In no town or countryside under Spartan control will you find revelries or the countless incitements to pleasure that accompany them. Anyone who encounters a drunken, disorderly person will have them severely punished immediately, with no excuses accepted -- not even during a Dionysiac festival.

"I've noticed this does happen at your Athenian performances 'on the cart,' as they're called. And among our Tarentine colonists, I've seen an entire city drunk at a Dionysiac festival. But nothing of the sort happens among us."
D: Elegant: Megillus defends Sparta's total prohibition of pleasure: no revelries, no drunkenness, no exceptions even during Dionysiac festivals. He contrasts Spartan discipline with Athenian festival excesses and the spectacle of their Tarentine colonists getting drunk city-wide. The Spartan approach is simple: drive pleasure out completely.
D: Summary: Megillus defends Sparta's strict prohibition of pleasure, citing the complete absence of revelry, drunkenness, and disorder in Spartan territory. He contrasts this with Athenian festival excesses and the public drunkenness he witnessed among Sparta's Tarentine colonists.
D: Distilled: Megillus defends Sparta's solution to pleasure: total prohibition, with severe punishment for any drunkenness or revelry, no exceptions.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus. -- 'Performances on the cart' refers to the Athenian Anthesteria festival, where revelers rode on carts and engaged in ribald humor. -- Tarentum (modern Taranto in Italy) was a Spartan colony founded c. 706 BCE. That even a Spartan colony abandoned Spartan discipline regarding drink is telling. -- Megillus's defense represents the prohibitionist position: the best way to handle pleasure is to eliminate it entirely. The Stranger will argue against this in what follows. -- The Dionysiac festival connection is thematically important: Dionysus is the god of wine, and the Laws will develop an argument for the educational value of properly regulated drinking -- essentially a philosophical rehabilitation of Dionysian practice.

D: Original:
O Lacedaemonian Stranger, these festivities are praiseworthy where there is a spirit of endurance, but are very senseless when they are under no regulations. In order to retaliate, an Athenian has only to point out the licence which exists among your women. To all such accusations, whether they are brought against the Tarentines, or us, or you, there is one answer which exonerates the practice in question from impropriety. When a stranger expresses wonder at the singularity of what he sees, any inhabitant will naturally answer him:-Wonder not, O stranger; this is our custom, and you may very likely have some other custom about the same things. Now we are speaking, my friends, not about men in general, but about the merits and defects of the lawgivers themselves. Let us then discourse a little more at length about intoxication, which is a very important subject, and will seriously task the discrimination of the legislator. I am not speaking of drinking, or not drinking, wine at all, but of intoxication. Are we to follow the custom of the Scythians, and Persians, and Carthaginians, and Celts, and Iberians, and Thracians, who are all warlike nations, or that of your countrymen, for they, as you say, altogether abstain? But the Scythians and Thracians, both men and women, drink unmixed wine, which they pour on their garments, and this they think a happy and glorious institution. The Persians, again, are much given to other practices of luxury which you reject, but they have more moderation in them than the Thracians and Scythians.

O best of men, we have only to take arms into our hands, and we send all these nations flying before us.
D: Narrative:
"O Lacedaemonian Stranger, these festivities are praiseworthy where there is a spirit of endurance, but are very senseless when they are under no regulations," said the Athenian Stranger. "In order to retaliate, an Athenian has only to point out the licence which exists among your women. To all such accusations, whether they are brought against the Tarentines, or us, or you, there is one answer which exonerates the practice in question from impropriety. When a stranger expresses wonder at the singularity of what he sees, any inhabitant will naturally answer him: 'Wonder not, O stranger; this is our custom, and you may very likely have some other custom about the same things.'

"Now we are speaking, my friends, not about men in general, but about the merits and defects of the lawgivers themselves. Let us then discourse a little more at length about intoxication, which is a very important subject, and will seriously task the discrimination of the legislator. I am not speaking of drinking, or not drinking, wine at all, but of intoxication. Are we to follow the custom of the Scythians, and Persians, and Carthaginians, and Celts, and Iberians, and Thracians, who are all warlike nations, or that of your countrymen, for they, as you say, altogether abstain? But the Scythians and Thracians, both men and women, drink unmixed wine, which they pour on their garments, and this they think a happy and glorious institution. The Persians, again, are much given to other practices of luxury which you reject, but they have more moderation in them than the Thracians and Scythians."

"O best of men, we have only to take arms into our hands, and we send all these nations flying before us," replied Megillus.
D: Refashioned:
"These festivities are praiseworthy where there is endurance involved, but senseless when unregulated," the Stranger replied. "And to retaliate, an Athenian need only point out the license existing among your women. But to all such accusations -- against Tarentines, Athenians, or Spartans -- there is one answer: 'Don't be surprised, stranger; this is our custom, and you likely have different ones.'

"But we are not speaking about customs in general. We are evaluating the merits and defects of lawgivers themselves. So let us discuss intoxication at greater length -- it is a genuinely important subject that will seriously test a legislator's judgment.

"I am not asking whether to drink wine or not. I am asking about intoxication. Should we follow the Scythians, Persians, Carthaginians, Celts, Iberians, and Thracians -- all warlike peoples? Or your Spartan custom of total abstinence? The Scythians and Thracians, men and women alike, drink unmixed wine and pour it on their clothing, considering this a happy and glorious practice. The Persians indulge heavily in other luxuries you reject, but drink with more moderation than the Scythians and Thracians."

"Best of men," Megillus retorted, "we simply take up arms and put all those nations to flight."
D: Elegant: The Stranger reframes the debate: the question is not whether to drink, but how to handle intoxication. Different warlike nations take different approaches -- Scythians drink wildly, Persians indulge moderately, Spartans abstain entirely. Cultural customs prove nothing by themselves; what matters is the legislator's judgment. When Megillus boasts of Spartan military superiority, the Stranger will show this is irrelevant to the argument.
D: Summary: The Stranger distinguishes between drinking and intoxication, surveying different cultures' approaches: Scythian excess, Persian moderation, Spartan abstinence. He insists the question is about legislative judgment, not cultural custom. Megillus responds with a boast about Spartan military supremacy.
D: Distilled: The question is not whether to drink but how a legislator should handle intoxication -- and military success does not settle the matter.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Megillus. -- 'Licence among your women' refers to the relative freedom of Spartan women, which scandalized other Greeks. Aristotle criticizes it in Politics 2.9 (1269b-1270a). -- The ethnographic survey (Scythians, Persians, Carthaginians, Celts, Iberians, Thracians) shows the Stranger's cosmopolitan knowledge and suggests that the question of drink is universal. -- 'Unmixed wine' (akratos) was considered barbaric by Greeks, who normally diluted wine with water. -- Megillus's militaristic retort ('we send them flying') exemplifies the very reductionism the Stranger is challenging: judging institutions solely by military outcomes. -- The Stranger's point about cultural relativism ('this is our custom') echoes Herodotus 3.38 on the relativity of burial customs.

D: Original:
Nay, my good friend, do not say that; there have been, as there always will be, flights and pursuits of which no account can be given, and therefore we cannot say that victory or defeat in battle affords more than a doubtful proof of the goodness or badness of institutions. For when the greater states conquer and enslave the lesser, as the Syracusans have done the Locrians, who appear to be the best-governed people in their part of the world, or as the Athenians have done the Ceans (and there are ten thousand other instances of the same sort of thing), all this is not to the point; let us endeavour rather to form a conclusion about each institution in itself and say nothing, at present, of victories and defeats. Let us only say that such and such a custom is honourable, and another not. And first permit me to tell you how good and bad are to be estimated in reference to these very matters.

How do you mean?
D: Narrative:
"Nay, my good friend, do not say that; there have been, as there always will be, flights and pursuits of which no account can be given," replied the Athenian Stranger, "and therefore we cannot say that victory or defeat in battle affords more than a doubtful proof of the goodness or badness of institutions.

"For when the greater states conquer and enslave the lesser, as the Syracusans have done the Locrians, who appear to be the best-governed people in their part of the world, or as the Athenians have done the Ceans -- and there are ten thousand other instances of the same sort of thing -- all this is not to the point; let us endeavour rather to form a conclusion about each institution in itself and say nothing, at present, of victories and defeats. Let us only say that such and such a custom is honourable, and another not. And first permit me to tell you how good and bad are to be estimated in reference to these very matters."

"How do you mean?" asked Megillus.
D: Refashioned:
"Don't say that, my friend," the Stranger replied. "There have always been, and always will be, victories and defeats that prove nothing. We cannot say that success or failure in battle provides more than doubtful evidence of whether institutions are good or bad.

"When larger states conquer and enslave smaller ones -- as Syracuse did to Locri, which appears to be the best-governed state in its region, or as Athens did to Ceos -- and there are ten thousand similar examples -- none of this is relevant. Let us instead try to evaluate each institution on its own merits, setting victories and defeats aside. Let us simply determine which customs are honorable and which are not.

"But first, let me explain how good and bad should be assessed in matters like these."

"What do you mean?" Megillus asked.
D: Elegant: Military victory proves nothing about the quality of institutions: well-governed states are conquered by larger ones all the time. Syracuse enslaved Locri; Athens conquered Ceos. Instead, each institution must be evaluated on its own merits, independent of military outcomes.
D: Summary: The Stranger dismisses military victory as evidence of institutional quality, citing examples of well-governed states conquered by larger powers. He insists institutions must be evaluated on their own merits, not by battlefield results.
D: Distilled: Military victory is unreliable evidence of good institutions -- might does not make right in legislation.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Megillus. -- Locri Epizephyrii in southern Italy was famous for its early written law code (attributed to Zaleucus, c. 660 BCE) and was widely regarded as well-governed. Syracuse, the largest Greek city in Sicily, repeatedly dominated smaller neighbors. -- Ceos was an Aegean island subjugated by Athens during the Delian League period. -- The Stranger's self-criticism is notable: he includes Athens among the unjust conquerors. -- The principle that institutions must be evaluated intrinsically rather than by their military outcomes challenges the entire Cretan-Spartan framework established in 1.4 and anticipates Aristotle's argument in Politics 7.2 that military prowess is not the goal of the state.

D: Original: All those who are ready at a moment's notice to praise or censure any practice which is matter of discussion, seem to me to proceed in a wrong way. Let me give you an illustration of what I mean:-You may suppose a person to be praising wheat as a good kind of food, whereupon another person instantly blames wheat, without ever enquiring into its effect or use, or in what way, or to whom, or with what, or in what state and how, wheat is to be given. And that is just what we are doing in this discussion. At the very mention of the word intoxication, one side is ready with their praises and the other with their censures; which is absurd. For either side adduce their witnesses and approvers, and some of us think that we speak with authority because we have many witnesses; and others because they see those who abstain conquering in battle, and this again is disputed by us. Now I cannot say that I shall be satisfied, if we go on discussing each of the remaining laws in the same way. And about this very point of intoxication I should like to speak in another way, which I hold to be the right one; for if number is to be the criterion, are there not myriads upon myriads of nations ready to dispute the point with you, who are only two cities?
D: Narrative:
"All those who are ready at a moment's notice to praise or censure any practice which is matter of discussion, seem to me to proceed in a wrong way," said the Athenian Stranger. "Let me give you an illustration of what I mean. You may suppose a person to be praising wheat as a good kind of food, whereupon another person instantly blames wheat, without ever enquiring into its effect or use, or in what way, or to whom, or with what, or in what state and how, wheat is to be given.

"And that is just what we are doing in this discussion. At the very mention of the word intoxication, one side is ready with their praises and the other with their censures; which is absurd. For either side adduces their witnesses and approvers, and some of us think that we speak with authority because we have many witnesses; and others because they see those who abstain conquering in battle, and this again is disputed by us.

"Now I cannot say that I shall be satisfied, if we go on discussing each of the remaining laws in the same way. And about this very point of intoxication I should like to speak in another way, which I hold to be the right one; for if number is to be the criterion, are there not myriads upon myriads of nations ready to dispute the point with you, who are only two cities?"
D: Refashioned:
"People who instantly praise or condemn any practice the moment it comes up for discussion are going about it the wrong way," said the Stranger. "Here is an illustration. Suppose someone praises wheat as good food, and another person immediately condemns wheat -- without asking about its effects, its uses, who should eat it, how it should be prepared, what it should be combined with, or in what condition it should be consumed.

"That is exactly what we are doing. The moment the word 'intoxication' is mentioned, one side rushes to praise it and the other to condemn it. This is absurd. Each side cites its witnesses and supporters: some of us think we speak with authority because we have many supporters; others because they point to abstainers winning battles -- and even that is disputed.

"I won't be satisfied if we keep discussing laws this way. On this specific matter of intoxication, I want to take a different approach -- the right one, as I see it. If sheer numbers are the standard, there are myriads of nations ready to challenge you, and you are only two cities."
D: Elegant: The Stranger attacks the method of the debate: rushing to praise or condemn a practice without examining its effects, context, and proper use is like praising or blaming wheat as food without asking how, when, and for whom it should be eaten. Each side cites witnesses and military outcomes, but numbers prove nothing -- and two cities cannot outweigh myriads of nations.
D: Summary: The Stranger criticizes the method of discussion: praising or condemning intoxication without examining its proper use is like judging wheat without asking how it should be consumed. He rejects both popular consensus and military outcomes as criteria, proposing a more rigorous approach.
D: Distilled: Judging any practice requires examining its effects, context, and proper use -- not collecting witnesses or counting battlefield victories.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The wheat analogy is simple but effective: it reframes the question from 'is drinking good or bad?' to 'under what conditions, for whom, and in what manner is drinking beneficial or harmful?' -- This methodological point anticipates Aristotle's doctrine of the mean and his emphasis on circumstance (Nicomachean Ethics 2.6-7). -- 'Myriads of nations' drinking vs. two cities abstaining -- the Stranger dismantles Megillus's claim to authority by numbers. -- The insistence on proper method over quick judgment is quintessentially Socratic/Platonic. -- This passage prepares for the analogy of the goatherd (1.34) and the symposiarch (1.37): the question is not whether an institution exists but whether it is properly supervised.

D: Original:
I shall gladly welcome any method of enquiry which is right.

Let me put the matter thus:-Suppose a person to praise the keeping of goats, and the creatures themselves as capital things to have, and then some one who had seen goats feeding without a goatherd in cultivated spots, and doing mischief, were to censure a goat or any other animal who has no keeper, or a bad keeper, would there be any sense or justice in such censure?

Certainly not.

Does a captain require only to have nautical knowledge in order to be a good captain, whether he is sea-sick or not? What do you say?

I say that he is not a good captain if, although he have nautical skill, he is liable to sea-sickness.

And what would you say of the commander of an army? Will he be able to command merely because he has military skill if he be a coward, who, when danger comes, is sick and drunk with fear?

Impossible.

And what if besides being a coward he has no skill?

He is a miserable fellow, not fit to be a commander of men, but only of old women.
D: Narrative:
"I shall gladly welcome any method of enquiry which is right," said Megillus.

"Let me put the matter thus," said the Athenian Stranger. "Suppose a person to praise the keeping of goats, and the creatures themselves as capital things to have, and then some one who had seen goats feeding without a goatherd in cultivated spots, and doing mischief, were to censure a goat or any other animal who has no keeper, or a bad keeper, would there be any sense or justice in such censure?"

"Certainly not."

"Does a captain require only to have nautical knowledge in order to be a good captain, whether he is sea-sick or not? What do you say?"

"I say that he is not a good captain if, although he have nautical skill, he is liable to sea-sickness."

"And what would you say of the commander of an army? Will he be able to command merely because he has military skill if he be a coward, who, when danger comes, is sick and drunk with fear?"

"Impossible."

"And what if besides being a coward he has no skill?"

"He is a miserable fellow, not fit to be a commander of men, but only of old women."
D: Refashioned:
"I gladly welcome any sound method of inquiry," said Megillus.

"Then consider this," said the Stranger. "Suppose someone praises goat-keeping and goats as wonderful to have. Then someone else, having seen goats wandering unsupervised through cultivated fields and causing damage, condemns goats -- or any other animal without a keeper, or with a bad keeper. Would such criticism make sense?"

"Certainly not."

"Does a captain only need nautical knowledge to be a good captain? What about if he gets seasick?"

"He's not a good captain if he's skilled in seamanship but prone to seasickness."

"And what about an army commander? Can he command effectively with military skill alone if he's a coward who becomes sick and paralyzed with fear at the approach of danger?"

"Impossible."

"And what if he's both a coward and unskilled?"

"Then he's a wretched fellow -- not fit to command men, only old women."
D: Elegant: The Stranger deploys three analogies. Goats are not to blame when they destroy crops without a goatherd -- the fault lies in absent supervision. A captain with knowledge but seasickness is no good captain. A general who is both cowardly and unskilled is worthless. The point: do not blame a practice for its unsupervised effects; blame the absence of proper leadership.
D: Summary: The Stranger uses three analogies -- unsupervised goats, a seasick captain, and a cowardly general -- to establish that practices should not be blamed for their mismanaged effects. The fault lies not in the practice itself but in the absence of competent leadership.
D: Distilled: Do not blame goats for destroying crops when there is no goatherd -- blame the absence of proper supervision.
Notes: Speakers: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The goat analogy is charming and characteristic of Plato's use of everyday images to illuminate philosophical points (cf. the allegory of the cave, the allegory of the charioteer). -- The progression from goatherd to captain to general builds from the mundane to the civic, preparing for the argument that drinking parties need a competent 'commander' -- the symposiarch. -- Megillus's dismissive remark about commanding 'old women' reflects typical Greek gender attitudes. -- The underlying principle -- that leadership determines whether an institution functions well -- is applied throughout the Laws to every area of legislation.

D: Original:
And what would you say of some one who blames or praises any sort of meeting which is intended by nature to have a ruler, and is well enough when under his presidency? The critic, however, has never seen the society meeting together at an orderly feast under the control of a president, but always without a ruler or with a bad one:-when observers of this class praise or blame such meetings, are we to suppose that what they say is of any value?

Certainly not, if they have never seen or been present at such a meeting when rightly ordered.

Reflect; may not banqueters and banquets be said to constitute a kind of meeting?

Of course.
D: Narrative:
"And what would you say of some one who blames or praises any sort of meeting which is intended by nature to have a ruler, and is well enough when under his presidency?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "The critic, however, has never seen the society meeting together at an orderly feast under the control of a president, but always without a ruler or with a bad one -- when observers of this class praise or blame such meetings, are we to suppose that what they say is of any value?"

"Certainly not, if they have never seen or been present at such a meeting when rightly ordered," replied Megillus.

"Reflect; may not banqueters and banquets be said to constitute a kind of meeting?"

"Of course."
D: Refashioned:
"What about someone who praises or blames a type of gathering that is meant to have a leader and works perfectly well under proper leadership?" the Stranger asked. "But this critic has never seen such a gathering conducted as an orderly feast under a competent president -- only without a leader, or under a bad one. Should we take their praise or blame seriously?"

"Certainly not, if they've never seen such a meeting rightly conducted," Megillus agreed.

"Now consider: can we not say that banqueters and banquets constitute a kind of meeting?"

"Of course."
D: Elegant: Anyone who criticizes a type of gathering -- having only seen it badly managed -- cannot be taken seriously. The question then becomes: can a banquet be considered a form of meeting that needs proper leadership? Obviously yes.
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that critics who have only seen gatherings mismanaged have no authority to judge them. He then establishes that banquets are a form of meeting, preparing the argument that drinking parties require competent leadership.
D: Distilled: Those who have only seen drinking parties mismanaged cannot legitimately judge the practice -- the question is whether they have ever seen one properly led.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Megillus. -- This argument applies the goatherd principle directly to symposia (drinking parties). -- The Greek symposion was a formally structured event with a symposiarch (master of the drinking) who regulated the mixing of wine and the pace of consumption. -- Megillus, coming from a culture that banned drinking, has by definition never seen a properly managed symposium -- which the Stranger will use against him. -- The argument structure is: (1) a practice needs leadership, (2) you've only seen it without leadership, (3) therefore your criticism is uninformed.

D: Original: And did any one ever see this sort of convivial meeting rightly ordered? Of course you two will answer that you have never seen them at all, because they are not customary or lawful in your country; but I have come across many of them in many different places, and moreover I have made enquiries about them wherever I went, as I may say, and never did I see or hear of anything of the kind which was carried on altogether rightly; in some few particulars they might be right, but in general they were utterly wrong.
D: Narrative: "And did any one ever see this sort of convivial meeting rightly ordered?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "Of course you two will answer that you have never seen them at all, because they are not customary or lawful in your country; but I have come across many of them in many different places, and moreover I have made enquiries about them wherever I went, as I may say, and never did I see or hear of anything of the kind which was carried on altogether rightly; in some few particulars they might be right, but in general they were utterly wrong."
D: Refashioned: "Has anyone ever actually seen a drinking party rightly conducted?" the Stranger asked. "You two will of course say you've never seen one at all, since they're neither customary nor legal in your countries. I, however, have encountered many of them in many different places, and I've investigated them wherever I traveled. I can say that I never saw or heard of one that was conducted altogether rightly. A few details might be right, but on the whole they were completely wrong."
D: Elegant: Neither Megillus nor Cleinias has ever seen a drinking party -- they are illegal in their countries. The Stranger has seen many across the Greek world, yet even he has never seen one conducted entirely rightly. The ideal drinking party remains unrealized.
D: Summary: The Stranger acknowledges that Megillus and Cleinias have never witnessed a drinking party, and admits that even he, having attended many across the Greek world, has never seen one conducted properly. The ideal remains unrealized.
D: Distilled: No one -- not the abstaining Spartans and Cretans, nor the Stranger himself -- has ever witnessed a properly conducted drinking party.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The Stranger's admission that he has traveled widely and investigated symposia everywhere is autobiographical detail rare in Platonic dialogues. -- That the ideal drinking party has never been realized parallels the Republic's admission that the ideal city may never exist in practice (Republic 9.592a-b). -- The Stranger is building toward a constructive proposal: if drinking parties have never been done right, this is an argument for designing them properly, not for abolishing them. -- The universal failure of existing practice doesn't condemn the practice itself -- it condemns the absence of proper regulation.

D: Original:
What do you mean, Stranger, by this remark? Explain; For we, as you say, from our inexperience in such matters, might very likely not know, even if they came in our way, what was right or wrong in such societies.

Likely enough; then let me try to be your instructor: You would acknowledge, would you not, that in all gatherings of man, kind, of whatever sort, there ought to be a leader?

Certainly I should.

And we were saying just now, that when men are at war the leader ought to be a brave man?

We were.

The brave man is less likely than the coward to be disturbed by fears?

That again is true.

And if there were a possibility of having a general of an army who was absolutely fearless and imperturbable, should we not by all means appoint him?

Assuredly.

Now, however, we are speaking not of a general who is to command an army, when foe meets foe in time of war, but of one who is to regulate meetings of another sort, when friend meets friend in time of peace.

True.

And that sort of meeting, if attended with drunkenness, is apt to be unquiet.

Certainly; the reverse of quiet.

In the first place, then, the revellers as well as the soldiers will require a ruler?

To be sure; no men more so.

And we ought, if possible, to provide them with a quiet ruler?

Of course.

And he should be a man who understands society; for his duty is to preserve the friendly feelings which exist among the company at the time, and to increase them for the future by his use of the occasion.

Very true.

Must we not appoint a sober man and a wise to be our master of the revels? For if the ruler of drinkers be himself young and drunken, and not over-wise, only by some special good fortune will he be saved from doing some great evil.

It will be by a singular good fortune that he is saved.
D: Narrative:
"What do you mean, Stranger, by this remark? Explain; for we, as you say, from our inexperience in such matters, might very likely not know, even if they came in our way, what was right or wrong in such societies," said Cleinias.

"Likely enough; then let me try to be your instructor. You would acknowledge, would you not, that in all gatherings of mankind, of whatever sort, there ought to be a leader?"

"Certainly I should."

"And we were saying just now, that when men are at war the leader ought to be a brave man?"

"We were."

"The brave man is less likely than the coward to be disturbed by fears?"

"That again is true."

"And if there were a possibility of having a general of an army who was absolutely fearless and imperturbable, should we not by all means appoint him?"

"Assuredly."

"Now, however, we are speaking not of a general who is to command an army, when foe meets foe in time of war, but of one who is to regulate meetings of another sort, when friend meets friend in time of peace."

"True."

"And that sort of meeting, if attended with drunkenness, is apt to be unquiet."

"Certainly; the reverse of quiet."

"In the first place, then, the revellers as well as the soldiers will require a ruler?"

"To be sure; no men more so."

"And we ought, if possible, to provide them with a quiet ruler?"

"Of course."

"And he should be a man who understands society; for his duty is to preserve the friendly feelings which exist among the company at the time, and to increase them for the future by his use of the occasion."

"Very true."

"Must we not appoint a sober man and a wise to be our master of the revels? For if the ruler of drinkers be himself young and drunken, and not over-wise, only by some special good fortune will he be saved from doing some great evil."

"It will be by a singular good fortune that he is saved."
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean, Stranger? Explain -- since we have no experience with such things, we probably wouldn't recognize what's right or wrong about them even if we encountered them," Cleinias said.
