"Fair enough; let me instruct you. You'd agree that in every gathering of people, whatever the kind, there should be a leader?"

"Certainly."

"And we said that in wartime the leader should be a brave man?"

"We did."

"And the brave man is less likely than the coward to be unnerved by fear?"

"True."

"And if we could find a general who was absolutely fearless and unshakable, we'd appoint him without hesitation?"

"Of course."

"But now we're not talking about a general commanding an army where foe meets foe in war. We're talking about someone who regulates a different kind of gathering, where friend meets friend in peace."

"True."

"And such a gathering, when drunkenness is involved, tends to be disorderly."

"Certainly -- the opposite of orderly."

"So revelers, like soldiers, need a ruler?"

"Absolutely -- no one more so."

"And we should provide them, if possible, with a calm ruler?"

"Of course."

"Someone who understands social dynamics, whose duty is to preserve the friendly feelings that already exist among the company and to deepen them through skillful management of the occasion."

"Very true."

"Then shouldn't we appoint a sober and wise man as master of the revels? If the ruler of drinkers is himself young, drunk, and not particularly wise, only extraordinary luck will save him from causing great harm."

"Only extraordinary luck indeed."
D: Elegant: Every gathering needs a leader. In war, the leader must be brave and fearless. At a drinking party -- where friend meets friend in peace -- the leader must be calm, socially skilled, sober, and wise. His task is to preserve and deepen the friendly feelings among the company. A drunken leader of drinkers will inevitably cause harm, just as a cowardly general will lose battles.
D: Summary: Through rapid Socratic questioning, the Stranger establishes that drinking parties need leadership just as armies do. The ideal master of revels must be sober, wise, and socially skilled, tasked with preserving and deepening friendship among the company.
D: Distilled: A drinking party needs a sober, wise leader just as an army needs a brave general -- and for the same reason: unled gatherings produce chaos.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger in Socratic question-and-answer. -- The 'master of the revels' (symposiarch) was a real Greek institution: a designated person who controlled the ratio of wine to water and the pace of drinking. -- 'Where friend meets friend in time of peace' vs. 'where foe meets foe in time of war' -- this elegant parallel makes the structural analogy between military and social leadership. -- The qualities required of the symposiarch (sobriety, wisdom, understanding of social dynamics) make him a figure of the philosopher-ruler in miniature. -- The argument's conclusion -- that a sober person must rule the drinking -- is paradoxical: the abstinent person is the ideal leader of the non-abstinent. This parallels the Republic's argument that the philosopher, who least wants power, should rule.

D: Original: Now suppose such associations to be framed in the best way possible in states, and that some one blames the very fact of their existence-he may very likely be right. But if he blames a practice which he only sees very much mismanaged, he shows in the first place that he is not aware of the mismanagement, and also not aware that everything done in this way will turn out to be wrong, because done without the superintendence of a sober ruler. Do you not see that a drunken pilot or a drunken ruler of any sort will ruin ship, chariot, army-anything, in short, of which he has the direction?
D: Narrative: "Now suppose such associations to be framed in the best way possible in states, and that some one blames the very fact of their existence -- he may very likely be right," said the Athenian Stranger. "But if he blames a practice which he only sees very much mismanaged, he shows in the first place that he is not aware of the mismanagement, and also not aware that everything done in this way will turn out to be wrong, because done without the superintendence of a sober ruler. Do you not see that a drunken pilot or a drunken ruler of any sort will ruin ship, chariot, army -- anything, in short, of which he has the direction?"
D: Refashioned: "Now suppose such gatherings were organized in the best possible way within a state," the Stranger continued. "Someone who criticizes their very existence might well be right. But someone who criticizes the practice only because he's seen it badly managed reveals two kinds of ignorance: first, he doesn't recognize the mismanagement; second, he doesn't realize that anything done without the oversight of a sober ruler will turn out badly. Surely you can see that a drunken pilot, or a drunken ruler of any kind, will ruin a ship, a chariot, an army -- anything he directs?"
D: Elegant: Criticizing a practice because you've only seen it mismanaged reveals ignorance, not insight. A drunken pilot ruins a ship; a drunken ruler ruins anything he directs. The fault lies in absent supervision, not in the practice itself.
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that criticizing drinking because one has only seen it mismanaged shows ignorance of proper management. A drunken ruler ruins anything he directs -- ship, chariot, army. The problem is never the practice itself but the absence of sober supervision.
D: Distilled: Blaming a practice for its unsupervised failures is like blaming ships for sinking when the pilot is drunk.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The drunken pilot is a memorable image that distills the argument: the practice (sailing, drinking) is not the problem; the leadership is. -- Cf. Republic 6.488a-489a, where Socrates uses the 'ship of state' analogy with a different emphasis (the philosopher as the true navigator). -- The Stranger concedes that someone might legitimately object to drinking parties entirely ('he may very likely be right') -- a careful qualification that keeps the argument honest. -- The argument has the structure: if X fails without good leadership, the conclusion is not 'abolish X' but 'provide good leadership.'

D: Original: The last remark is very true, Stranger; and I see quite clearly the advantage of an army having a good leader-he will give victory in war to his followers, which is a very great advantage; and so of other things. But I do not see any similar advantage which either individuals or states gain from the good management of a feast; and I want you to tell me what great good will be effected, supposing that this drinking ordinance is duly established.
D: Narrative: "The last remark is very true, Stranger; and I see quite clearly the advantage of an army having a good leader -- he will give victory in war to his followers, which is a very great advantage; and so of other things," said Cleinias. "But I do not see any similar advantage which either individuals or states gain from the good management of a feast; and I want you to tell me what great good will be effected, supposing that this drinking ordinance is duly established."
D: Refashioned: "Your last point is certainly true, Stranger," said Cleinias. "I can clearly see the advantage of a good military leader -- he brings victory in war, which is an enormous benefit. The same goes for other fields. But I cannot see any similar advantage that individuals or states gain from a well-managed feast. I want you to tell me: what great good would come from a properly established drinking ordinance?"
D: Elegant: Cleinias sees the value of good leadership in war but challenges the Stranger: what is the great benefit of a well-managed drinking party? What good does it actually produce for individuals or states?
D: Summary: Cleinias accepts that good leadership produces victory in war but demands to know what corresponding benefit a well-managed drinking party would bring to individuals and states.
D: Distilled: What great good does a properly managed drinking party actually produce -- what is its equivalent of military victory?
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- This is a strong and fair challenge: the Stranger has shown that drinking parties need leadership, but has not yet demonstrated their positive value. -- Cleinias's demand for concrete benefits reflects the practical Cretan-Spartan temperament. -- The answer, developed in 1.40-1.60, is that properly managed drinking serves as an educational tool: it tests and trains character, particularly the virtue of temperance. -- This passage sets up the final major argument of Book 1.

D: Original: If you mean to ask what great good accrues to the state from the right training of a single youth, or of a single chorus-when the question is put in that form, we cannot deny that the good is not very great in any particular instance. But if you ask what is the good of education in general, the answer is easy-that education makes good men, and that good men act nobly, and conquer their enemies in battle, because they are good. Education certainly gives victory, although victory sometimes produces forgetfulness of education; for many have grown insolent from victory in war, and this insolence has engendered in them innumerable evils; and many a victory has been and will be suicidal to the victors; but education is never suicidal.
D: Narrative:
"If you mean to ask what great good accrues to the state from the right training of a single youth, or of a single chorus -- when the question is put in that form, we cannot deny that the good is not very great in any particular instance," said the Athenian Stranger. "But if you ask what is the good of education in general, the answer is easy -- that education makes good men, and that good men act nobly, and conquer their enemies in battle, because they are good.

"Education certainly gives victory, although victory sometimes produces forgetfulness of education; for many have grown insolent from victory in war, and this insolence has engendered in them innumerable evils; and many a victory has been and will be suicidal to the victors; but education is never suicidal."
D: Refashioned:
"If you're asking what great good comes from properly training a single young person, or a single chorus -- when the question is framed that way, we must admit the benefit in any single case isn't very large," the Stranger replied. "But if you ask about the good of education in general, the answer is straightforward: education produces good people, and good people act nobly -- and they do conquer their enemies in battle, precisely because they are good.

"Education certainly leads to victory. But victory sometimes produces forgetfulness of education: many have become arrogant from military success, and that arrogance has bred innumerable evils. Many victories have been, and will be, suicidal for the victors. But education is never suicidal."
D: Elegant: The benefit of properly managed drinking is educational. A single instance may seem small, but education in general produces good people, who act nobly and even prevail in battle. Victory can be self-destructive -- many victors have been ruined by the arrogance success breeds. But education is never suicidal.
D: Summary: The Stranger answers that the benefit of well-managed drinking is educational. While each instance may seem small, education as a whole produces good people who act nobly. Crucially, victory can become suicidal through the arrogance it breeds, but education never destroys those who possess it.
D: Distilled: Education makes good people and never destroys them -- unlike military victory, which often breeds the arrogance that destroys the victors.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Education is never suicidal' is one of the most memorable aphorisms in the Laws. -- 'Victory suicidal to the victors' alludes to numerous Greek historical examples: Sparta's victory over Athens in the Peloponnesian War led to Spartan overreach and decline; Athens's own imperial overconfidence led to the Sicilian disaster (415-413 BCE). -- The connection between drinking and education is now explicit: the symposium is an educational institution. -- The argument structure is powerful: (1) you value victory, (2) education produces victory more reliably than military training alone, (3) education is superior because it cannot self-destruct, (4) therefore the educational value of symposia exceeds what you initially suppose.

D: Original:
You seem to imply, my friend, that convivial meetings, when rightly ordered, are an important element of education.

Certainly I do.

And can you show that what you have been saying is true?

To be absolutely sure of the truth of matters concerning which there are many opinions, is an attribute of the Gods not given to man, Stranger; but I shall be very happy to tell you what I think, especially as we are now proposing to enter on a discussion concerning laws and constitutions.

Your opinion, Stranger, about the questions which are now being raised, is precisely what we want to hear.
D: Narrative:
"You seem to imply, my friend, that convivial meetings, when rightly ordered, are an important element of education," said Cleinias.

"Certainly I do," replied the Athenian Stranger.

"And can you show that what you have been saying is true?"

"To be absolutely sure of the truth of matters concerning which there are many opinions, is an attribute of the Gods not given to man, Stranger; but I shall be very happy to tell you what I think, especially as we are now proposing to enter on a discussion concerning laws and constitutions."

"Your opinion, Stranger, about the questions which are now being raised, is precisely what we want to hear."
D: Refashioned:
"You seem to be saying that properly organized drinking parties are an important part of education," Cleinias observed.

"That is exactly what I'm saying," the Stranger confirmed.

"And can you prove it?"

"Absolute certainty about matters where many opinions exist belongs to the gods, not to men. But I'm happy to share my view, especially since we're embarking on a discussion about laws and constitutions."

"Your view on the questions we've raised is precisely what we want to hear."
D: Elegant: Cleinias identifies the Stranger's thesis: properly organized drinking is educational. The Stranger claims certainty belongs to the gods but offers his considered opinion -- which is exactly what they need for their discussion of laws.
D: Summary: Cleinias summarizes the Stranger's claim that well-ordered drinking parties serve education. The Stranger acknowledges that absolute certainty belongs to the gods but offers his opinion as sufficient for their legislative inquiry.
D: Distilled: Well-ordered drinking parties serve education -- and while certainty belongs to the gods, informed opinion suffices for lawmaking.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- 'Absolute certainty is an attribute of the Gods not given to man' is a remarkable epistemological statement, reflecting the humbler tone of the Laws compared to the Republic's bolder claims about philosophical knowledge. -- The distinction between divine certainty and human opinion (doxa) echoes the Timaeus 29b-d, where Timaeus says his cosmological account can only be a 'likely story' (eikos mythos). -- The Stranger's willingness to proceed on the basis of opinion rather than certainty is philosophically significant: legislation requires practical wisdom, not metaphysical knowledge. -- This passage transitions from the preliminary argument to the detailed case for drinking as education.

D: Original: Very good; I will try to find a way of explaining my meaning, and you shall try to have the gift of understanding me. But first let me make an apology. The Athenian citizen is reputed among all the Hellenes to be a great talker, whereas Sparta is renowned for brevity, and the Cretans have more wit than words. Now I am afraid of appearing to elicit a very long discourse out of very small materials. For drinking indeed may appear to be a slight matter, and yet is one which cannot be rightly ordered according to nature, without correct principles of music; these are necessary to any clear or satisfactory treatment of the subject, and music again runs up into education generally, and there is much to be said about all this. What would you say then to leaving these matters for the present, and passing on to some other question of law?
D: Narrative:
"Very good; I will try to find a way of explaining my meaning, and you shall try to have the gift of understanding me," said the Athenian Stranger. "But first let me make an apology. The Athenian citizen is reputed among all the Hellenes to be a great talker, whereas Sparta is renowned for brevity, and the Cretans have more wit than words.

"Now I am afraid of appearing to elicit a very long discourse out of very small materials. For drinking indeed may appear to be a slight matter, and yet is one which cannot be rightly ordered according to nature, without correct principles of music; these are necessary to any clear or satisfactory treatment of the subject, and music again runs up into education generally, and there is much to be said about all this.

"What would you say then to leaving these matters for the present, and passing on to some other question of law?"
D: Refashioned:
"Good -- I'll try to explain my meaning, and you try to understand me," the Stranger said. "But first, an apology. The Athenian citizen has a reputation among all Greeks for talking at length, while Sparta is famous for brevity, and the Cretans have more wit than words.

"I'm afraid it will seem like I'm spinning an enormous discourse from very small material. Drinking may appear to be a trivial subject, yet it cannot be properly understood according to nature without correct principles of music. These principles are necessary for any clear treatment of the topic, and music in turn leads into education generally -- and there is a great deal to say about all of that.

"Would you prefer to set these matters aside and move on to another question of law?"
D: Elegant: The Stranger apologizes for his Athenian loquacity before his laconic Spartan and witty Cretan companions. He warns that drinking, though seemingly trivial, cannot be properly understood without principles of music, which lead to education generally -- a vast subject. He offers to drop it, testing whether his audience is truly committed.
D: Summary: The Stranger warns that the subject of drinking, though seemingly trivial, connects to music and education in ways that require extensive discussion. He apologizes for his Athenian verbosity and offers to change the subject, testing his companions' commitment.
D: Distilled: Drinking properly understood requires principles of music and education -- the subject is far deeper than it appears.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The stereotypes -- Athenians as talkative, Spartans as laconic, Cretans as witty -- were well-established Greek cultural commonplaces. -- The connection between drinking, music, and education is the intellectual core of Laws Books 1-2. Music (mousike) in Greek meant all the arts of the Muses, including poetry, song, and dance -- not just instrumental music. -- The Stranger's offer to change the subject is a classic rhetorical device: it ensures his listeners actively choose to continue, increasing their engagement. -- The claim that drinking requires music connects to the role of the Dionysiac chorus in Athenian education (developed in Book 2).

D: Original: O Athenian Stranger, let me tell you what perhaps you do not know, that our family is the proxenus of your state. I imagine that from their earliest youth all boys, when they are told that they are the proxeni of a particular state, feel kindly towards their second and this has certainly been my own feeling. I can well remember from the days of my boyhood, how, when any Lacedaemonians praised or blamed the Athenians, they used to say to me-"See, Megillus, how ill or how well," as the case might be, "has your state treated us"; and having always had to fight your battles against detractors when I heard you assailed, I became warmly attached to you. And I always like to hear the Athenian tongue spoken; the common saying is quite true, that a good Athenian is more than ordinarily good, for he is the only man who is freely and genuinely good by the divine inspiration of his own nature, and is not manufactured. Therefore be assured that I shall like to hear you say whatever you have to say.
D: Narrative:
"O Athenian Stranger, let me tell you what perhaps you do not know, that our family is the proxenus of your state," said Megillus. "I imagine that from their earliest youth all boys, when they are told that they are the proxeni of a particular state, feel kindly towards their second country; and this has certainly been my own feeling.

"I can well remember from the days of my boyhood, how, when any Lacedaemonians praised or blamed the Athenians, they used to say to me -- 'See, Megillus, how ill or how well,' as the case might be, 'has your state treated us'; and having always had to fight your battles against detractors when I heard you assailed, I became warmly attached to you. And I always like to hear the Athenian tongue spoken; the common saying is quite true, that a good Athenian is more than ordinarily good, for he is the only man who is freely and genuinely good by the divine inspiration of his own nature, and is not manufactured.

"Therefore be assured that I shall like to hear you say whatever you have to say."
D: Refashioned:
"Athenian Stranger, let me tell you something you may not know," said Megillus. "My family holds the position of proxenus -- diplomatic representative -- for your state. I imagine that all boys, when they learn from childhood that they are proxeni for a particular state, develop warm feelings toward that second country. That has certainly been my experience.

"I can remember from my boyhood how, whenever Spartans praised or criticized the Athenians, they would say to me: 'Look, Megillus, how well or how badly your state has treated us.' Having always had to defend you against critics, I became genuinely fond of Athens. I enjoy hearing the Athenian way of speaking. And the common saying is quite true: a good Athenian is exceptionally good, because he is the only person who is freely and genuinely virtuous by the divine inspiration of his own nature, not by manufactured training.

"So please be assured -- I want to hear whatever you have to say."
D: Elegant: Megillus reveals he is Athens's proxenus (diplomatic representative) in Sparta and has defended Athens since boyhood. He affirms the saying that a good Athenian is uniquely good -- virtuous by nature and divine inspiration rather than by training. He urges the Stranger to continue without restraint.
D: Summary: Megillus reveals his family serves as Athens's diplomatic representative in Sparta, giving him lifelong affection for Athens. He endorses the saying that good Athenians are uniquely virtuous -- naturally good rather than manufactured -- and enthusiastically invites the Stranger to continue.
D: Distilled: Megillus's personal connection to Athens as proxenus gives him deep appreciation for Athenian discourse and natural virtue.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus. -- A proxenus was a citizen of one state who served as the official host and representative of another state's interests -- similar to a modern honorary consul. The position was hereditary. -- 'A good Athenian is more than ordinarily good... freely and genuinely good by divine inspiration' is a remarkable compliment from a Spartan, whose culture emphasized manufactured virtue through the agoge. -- The contrast between 'natural' Athenian goodness and 'manufactured' Spartan virtue echoes a major theme: is virtue produced by nature, training, or divine gift? Cf. Meno 70a on whether virtue is teachable. -- Megillus's warm personal narrative humanizes the dialogue and establishes genuine cross-cultural friendship among the speakers.

D: Original: Yes, Stranger; and when you have heard me speak, say boldly what is in your thoughts. Let me remind you of a tie which unites you to Crete. You must have heard here the story of the prophet Epimenides, who was of my family, and came to Athens ten years before the Persian war, in accordance with the response of the Oracle, and offered certain sacrifices which the God commanded. The Athenians were at that time in dread of the Persian invasion; and he said that for ten years they would not come, and that when they came, they would go away again without accomplishing any of their objects, and would suffer more evil than they inflicted. At that time my forefathers formed ties of hospitality with you; thus ancient is the friendship which I and my parents have had for you.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, Stranger; and when you have heard me speak, say boldly what is in your thoughts," said Cleinias. "Let me remind you of a tie which unites you to Crete. You must have heard here the story of the prophet Epimenides, who was of my family, and came to Athens ten years before the Persian war, in accordance with the response of the Oracle, and offered certain sacrifices which the God commanded.

"The Athenians were at that time in dread of the Persian invasion; and he said that for ten years they would not come, and that when they came, they would go away again without accomplishing any of their objects, and would suffer more evil than they inflicted. At that time my forefathers formed ties of hospitality with you; thus ancient is the friendship which I and my parents have had for you."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, Stranger -- and once you've heard me, speak your mind freely," Cleinias added. "Let me remind you of a bond connecting you to Crete. You've surely heard the story of the prophet Epimenides, who was of my family. He came to Athens ten years before the Persian War, following an oracle's instruction, and performed certain sacrifices the god commanded.

"The Athenians were then terrified of the Persian invasion. Epimenides told them the Persians would not come for ten years, and when they did come, they would leave without achieving their objectives and suffer more harm than they caused. It was then that my ancestors established ties of hospitality with your people. So the friendship between my family and Athens is ancient indeed."
D: Elegant: Cleinias reveals his own connection to Athens: the prophet Epimenides, his ancestor, came to Athens before the Persian Wars, performed sacred rites, and prophesied that the Persians would fail. This created bonds of hospitality between Cleinias's family and Athens -- an ancient friendship.
D: Summary: Cleinias reveals that the prophet Epimenides was his ancestor and visited Athens before the Persian Wars to perform sacred rites and deliver prophecy. This established ancient ties of hospitality between his family and Athens, paralleling Megillus's connection.
D: Distilled: Cleinias's family bond with Athens through the prophet Epimenides creates a second tie of friendship, complementing Megillus's proxeny.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- Epimenides of Crete was a semi-legendary sage, purifier, and prophet (c. 600 BCE). He was said to have purified Athens after the Cylonian affair. Aristotle, Diogenes Laertius, and Plutarch all discuss him. -- The claim that Epimenides came 'ten years before the Persian War' places the visit c. 500-490 BCE, later than most ancient accounts suggest (c. 596 BCE). The chronology is debated. -- The ties of hospitality (xenia) between prominent families of different cities were a fundamental institution of Greek interstate relations. -- Both Megillus and Cleinias now have personal bonds with Athens, ensuring the Stranger's critiques are received in a spirit of friendship rather than hostility. -- Together, 1.43 and 1.44 establish that all three speakers have pre-existing relationships of trust -- a crucial dramatic foundation for the frank critique that follows.

D: Original:
You seem to be quite ready to listen; and I am also ready to perform as much as I can of an almost impossible task, which I will nevertheless attempt. At the outset of the discussion, let me define the nature and power of education; for this is the way by which our argument must travel onwards to the God Dionysus.

Let us proceed, if you please.

Well, then, if I tell you what are my notions of education, will you consider whether they satisfy you?

Let us hear.
D: Narrative:
"You seem to be quite ready to listen; and I am also ready to perform as much as I can of an almost impossible task, which I will nevertheless attempt," said the Athenian Stranger. "At the outset of the discussion, let me define the nature and power of education; for this is the way by which our argument must travel onwards to the God Dionysus."

"Let us proceed, if you please," said Cleinias.

"Well, then, if I tell you what are my notions of education, will you consider whether they satisfy you?"

"Let us hear."
D: Refashioned:
"You seem fully ready to listen, and I am ready to attempt what is nearly an impossible task," said the Stranger. "At the start of this discussion, let me define the nature and power of education -- for this is the path our argument must take on its way to the god Dionysus."

"Let us proceed," said Cleinias.

"If I tell you my views on education, will you judge whether they satisfy you?"

"Let us hear them."
D: Elegant: The Stranger announces he will define education -- the path that leads their argument to Dionysus. The task is nearly impossible, but he will attempt it.
D: Summary: The Stranger announces he will define education as the necessary foundation for their argument about drinking, which leads to the god Dionysus. Both companions are eager to hear his account.
D: Distilled: The argument must pass through a definition of education on its way to understanding Dionysus and the value of wine.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- 'Our argument must travel onwards to the God Dionysus' -- the connection between education and Dionysus (god of wine, theater, and ecstasy) will be developed in Book 2. -- The Stranger's modesty ('an almost impossible task') is characteristic of the Laws' more tentative epistemology compared to the Republic's confident assertions. -- The definition of education that follows (1.46-1.47) is one of the most important passages in all of Plato's works.

D: Original:
According to my view, any one who would be good at anything must practise that thing from his youth upwards, both in sport and earnest, in its several branches: for example, he who is to be a good builder, should play at building children's houses; he who is to be a good husbandman, at tilling the ground; and those who have the care of their education should provide them when young with mimic tools. They should learn beforehand the knowledge which they will afterwards require for their art. For example, the future carpenter should learn to measure or apply the line in play; and the future warrior should learn riding, or some other exercise, for amusement, and the teacher should endeavour to direct the children's inclinations and pleasures, by the help of amusements, to their final aim in life. The most important part of education is right training in the nursery. The soul of the child in his play should be guided to the love of that sort of excellence in which when he grows up to manhood he will have to be perfected. Do you agree with me thus far?

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"According to my view, any one who would be good at anything must practise that thing from his youth upwards, both in sport and earnest, in its several branches," said the Athenian Stranger. "For example, he who is to be a good builder, should play at building children's houses; he who is to be a good husbandman, at tilling the ground; and those who have the care of their education should provide them when young with mimic tools. They should learn beforehand the knowledge which they will afterwards require for their art.

"For example, the future carpenter should learn to measure or apply the line in play; and the future warrior should learn riding, or some other exercise, for amusement, and the teacher should endeavour to direct the children's inclinations and pleasures, by the help of amusements, to their final aim in life. The most important part of education is right training in the nursery. The soul of the child in his play should be guided to the love of that sort of excellence in which when he grows up to manhood he will have to be perfected. Do you agree with me thus far?"

"Certainly," said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"In my view, anyone who wants to excel at anything must practice it from youth onward, both in play and in earnest, across all its branches," the Stranger said. "For example, the future builder should play at constructing toy houses; the future farmer should play at tilling soil. Those responsible for children's education should provide them with miniature tools. They should absorb the knowledge they will later need for their craft.

"The future carpenter should learn measuring and using a line through play. The future soldier should learn riding or similar exercises as amusement. The teacher's task is to direct children's natural inclinations and pleasures, through play, toward their ultimate purpose in life.

"The most important part of education is right training in the nursery. In play, the child's soul should be guided toward loving the kind of excellence in which he will need to be perfected as an adult. Do you agree so far?"

"Certainly," Cleinias replied.
D: Elegant: Education begins in play. The future builder plays at building; the future farmer plays at farming. Teachers must direct children's natural pleasures toward the excellence they will need as adults. The most critical stage is early childhood: guide the child's soul through play to love what is excellent.
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that education must begin in early childhood through play that anticipates adult vocations. Teachers should direct children's natural pleasures toward future excellence. The most important part of education is right training in the nursery.
D: Distilled: Education begins in play: the child's soul must be guided through amusement to love the excellence it will need as an adult.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- 'Right training in the nursery' (orthotrophia) is a key concept. -- The idea that play shapes character anticipates modern developmental psychology and Montessori education. Friedrich Froebel's kindergarten concept echoes these ideas. -- The principle that education should work with children's natural inclinations rather than against them distinguishes Plato's approach from pure Spartan discipline. -- Cf. Republic 7.536d-537a: 'Do not use compulsion, but let early education be a sort of amusement.' -- The 'mimic tools' (toy versions of real implements) anticipate modern educational toys and simulation-based learning. -- The emphasis on early childhood education recurs throughout the Laws (especially Books 7-8).

D: Original: Then let us not leave the meaning of education ambiguous or ill-defined. At present, when we speak in terms of praise or blame about the bringing-up of each person, we call one man educated and another uneducated, although the uneducated man may be sometimes very well educated for the calling of a retail trader, or of a captain of a ship, and the like. For we are not speaking of education in this narrower sense, but of that other education in virtue from youth upwards, which makes a man eagerly pursue the ideal perfection of citizenship, and teaches him how rightly to rule and how to obey. This is the only education which, upon our view, deserves the name; that other sort of training, which aims at the acquisition of wealth or bodily strength, or mere cleverness apart from intelligence and justice, is mean and illiberal, and is not worthy to be called education at all. But let us not quarrel with one another about a word, provided that the proposition which has just been granted hold good: to wit, that those who are rightly educated generally become good men. Neither must we cast a slight upon education, which is the first and fairest thing that the best of men can ever have, and which, though liable to take a wrong direction, is capable of reformation. And this work of reformation is the great business of every man while he lives.
D: Narrative:
"Then let us not leave the meaning of education ambiguous or ill-defined," said the Athenian Stranger. "At present, when we speak in terms of praise or blame about the bringing-up of each person, we call one man educated and another uneducated, although the uneducated man may be sometimes very well educated for the calling of a retail trader, or of a captain of a ship, and the like.

"For we are not speaking of education in this narrower sense, but of that other education in virtue from youth upwards, which makes a man eagerly pursue the ideal perfection of citizenship, and teaches him how rightly to rule and how to obey. This is the only education which, upon our view, deserves the name; that other sort of training, which aims at the acquisition of wealth or bodily strength, or mere cleverness apart from intelligence and justice, is mean and illiberal, and is not worthy to be called education at all.

"But let us not quarrel with one another about a word, provided that the proposition which has just been granted hold good: to wit, that those who are rightly educated generally become good men. Neither must we cast a slight upon education, which is the first and fairest thing that the best of men can ever have, and which, though liable to take a wrong direction, is capable of reformation. And this work of reformation is the great business of every man while he lives."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us not leave education undefined or ambiguous," the Stranger continued. "People currently praise or blame someone's upbringing, calling one person 'educated' and another 'uneducated' -- even though the 'uneducated' person may be perfectly well trained as a retail trader, a ship captain, or something similar.

"That is not the education we mean. We mean education in virtue from youth onward -- the kind that makes a person eagerly pursue the ideal of citizenship and teaches them both how to rule rightly and how to obey rightly. This alone deserves the name 'education.' Training aimed merely at acquiring wealth, bodily strength, or cleverness without intelligence and justice is base and illiberal, and does not deserve to be called education at all.

"But let's not argue over a word, as long as this principle holds: those who are rightly educated generally become good people. We must not belittle education itself. It is the first and finest thing the best of people can possess. Though it can go in a wrong direction, it is always capable of reform. And this work of reformation is every person's great task throughout life."
D: Elegant: True education is not vocational training for traders or captains. It is education in virtue -- pursuing the perfection of citizenship, learning to rule and to obey rightly. Training aimed only at wealth, strength, or cleverness without justice is not education at all. Education is the finest possession of the best people, always capable of reform -- and reformation is the great business of every person's life.
D: Summary: The Stranger defines true education: not vocational training but education in virtue that makes a person pursue ideal citizenship and learn both to rule and to obey. Training for mere wealth, strength, or cleverness without justice is unworthy of the name. Education is always reformable, and reformation is every person's lifelong task.
D: Distilled: True education is education in virtue -- teaching people to rule and obey rightly -- and its reformation is every person's lifelong work.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is one of the most important definitions of education in Western philosophy. -- 'Education in virtue from youth upwards' (paideia pros areten) -- the Greek concept of paideia encompasses culture, cultivation, and character formation, not just instruction. -- The distinction between true education and vocational training anticipates the liberal arts tradition. -- 'Teaches him how rightly to rule and how to obey' -- this bidirectional definition anticipates Aristotle's famous statement that the good citizen must know how to rule and be ruled (Politics 3.4, 1277a26-27). -- 'Training which aims at the acquisition of wealth or bodily strength, or mere cleverness apart from intelligence and justice, is mean and illiberal' -- 'illiberal' (aneleutheros) literally means 'unfree,' i.e., unworthy of a free person. -- 'Education is the first and fairest thing' -- education as the highest human good is the culmination of the hierarchy of goods established in 1.17. -- 'Capable of reformation... the great business of every man while he lives' -- education is not a one-time event but a lifelong process of self-correction.

D: Original:
Very true; and we entirely agree with you.

And we agreed before that they are good men who are able to rule themselves, and bad men who are not.

You are quite right.

Let me now proceed, if I can, to clear up the subject a little further by an illustration which I will offer you.

Proceed.

Do we not consider each of ourselves to be one?

We do.

And each one of us has in his bosom two counsellors, both foolish and also antagonistic; of which we call the one pleasure, and the other pain.

Exactly.

Also there are opinions about the future, which have the general name of expectations; and the specific name of fear, when the expectation is of pain; and of hope, when of pleasure; and further, there is reflection about the good or evil of them, and this, when embodied in a decree by the State, is called Law.

I am hardly able to follow you; proceed, however, as if I were.

I am in the like case.
D: Narrative:
"Very true; and we entirely agree with you," said Cleinias.

"And we agreed before that they are good men who are able to rule themselves, and bad men who are not," added the Athenian Stranger.

"You are quite right," said Cleinias.

"Let me now proceed, if I can, to clear up the subject a little further by an illustration which I will offer you."

"Proceed."

"Do we not consider each of ourselves to be one?" asked the Stranger.

"We do."

"And each one of us has in his bosom two counsellors, both foolish and also antagonistic; of which we call the one pleasure, and the other pain."

"Exactly."

"Also there are opinions about the future, which have the general name of expectations; and the specific name of fear, when the expectation is of pain; and of hope, when of pleasure; and further, there is reflection about the good or evil of them, and this, when embodied in a decree by the State, is called Law."

"I am hardly able to follow you; proceed, however, as if I were," said Cleinias.

"I am in the like case," added Megillus.
D: Refashioned:
"Very true -- we entirely agree," said Cleinias.

"And we agreed earlier that good people are those who can rule themselves, and bad people are those who cannot," the Stranger added.

"Correct."

"Let me try to clarify this further with an illustration."

"Go on."

"Don't we each consider ourselves to be a single being?"

"We do."

"Yet each of us carries within two counselors -- both foolish and opposed to each other. One we call pleasure, the other pain."

"Exactly."

"We also have expectations about the future. The general term is 'expectation'; the specific term when we expect pain is 'fear'; when we expect pleasure, 'hope.' Beyond these, there is our rational reflection on whether these expectations are good or bad -- and when this reflection is formalized as a public decree, it is called 'Law.'"

"I can barely follow you," Cleinias admitted, "but continue as though I could."

"I'm in the same position," Megillus added.
D: Elegant: Each person is one being, yet contains two opposing inner counselors: pleasure and pain. We also carry expectations -- fear (expecting pain) and hope (expecting pleasure). Our rational judgment about whether these expectations are good or bad, when formalized by the state, becomes Law. Both Cleinias and Megillus confess difficulty following this abstract argument.
D: Summary: The Stranger analyzes the inner life: each person contains two opposing counselors (pleasure and pain), plus expectations about the future (fear and hope), plus rational reflection on their value. When this reflection is embodied in a state decree, it becomes Law. Both interlocutors find this hard to follow.
D: Distilled: Law is the state's embodiment of rational reflection on pleasure, pain, fear, and hope -- the inner forces that govern human behavior.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, the Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. -- The two 'counselors' (pleasure and pain) prefigure the puppet analogy in 1.49. -- The definition of law as rational reflection embodied in a state decree is highly original. It makes law an externalization of reason's governance over passion. -- The progression from internal psychology (pleasure/pain/fear/hope) to public law is characteristic of Plato's method of deriving political structures from the soul's structure (cf. Republic 2.368d). -- Cleinias's and Megillus's honest admission of difficulty is refreshing: even intelligent, experienced men find this level of abstraction challenging. -- The identification of fear with expectation of pain and hope with expectation of pleasure anticipates Stoic definitions of the passions (cf. Chrysippus in Diogenes Laertius 7.110-116).

D: Original: Let us look at the matter thus: May we not conceive each of us living beings to be a puppet of the Gods, either their plaything only, or created with a purpose-which of the two we cannot certainly know? But we do know, that these affections in us are like cords and strings, which pull us different and opposite ways, and to opposite actions; and herein lies the difference between virtue and vice. According to the argument there is one among these cords which every man ought to grasp and never let go, but to pull with it against all the rest; and this is the sacred and golden cord of reason, called by us the common law of the State; there are others which are hard and of iron, but this one is soft because golden; and there are several other kinds. Now we ought always to cooperate with the lead of the best, which is law. For inasmuch as reason is beautiful and gentle, and not violent, her rule must needs have ministers in order to help the golden principle in vanquishing the other principles. And thus the moral of the tale about our being puppets will not have been lost, and the meaning of the expression "superior or inferior to a man's self" will become clearer; and the individual, attaining to right reason in this matter of pulling the strings of the puppet, should live according to its rule; while the city, receiving the same from some god or from one who has knowledge of these things, should embody it in a law, to be her guide in her dealings with herself and with other states. In this way virtue and vice will be more clearly distinguished by us. And when they have become clearer, education and other institutions will in like manner become clearer; and in particular that question of convivial entertainment, which may seem, perhaps, to have been a very trifling matter, and to have taken a great many more words than were necessary.
D: Narrative:
"Let us look at the matter thus," said the Athenian Stranger. "May we not conceive each of us living beings to be a puppet of the Gods, either their plaything only, or created with a purpose -- which of the two we cannot certainly know? But we do know, that these affections in us are like cords and strings, which pull us different and opposite ways, and to opposite actions; and herein lies the difference between virtue and vice.

"According to the argument there is one among these cords which every man ought to grasp and never let go, but to pull with it against all the rest; and this is the sacred and golden cord of reason, called by us the common law of the State; there are others which are hard and of iron, but this one is soft because golden; and there are several other kinds.

"Now we ought always to cooperate with the lead of the best, which is law. For inasmuch as reason is beautiful and gentle, and not violent, her rule must needs have ministers in order to help the golden principle in vanquishing the other principles. And thus the moral of the tale about our being puppets will not have been lost, and the meaning of the expression 'superior or inferior to a man's self' will become clearer; and the individual, attaining to right reason in this matter of pulling the strings of the puppet, should live according to its rule; while the city, receiving the same from some god or from one who has knowledge of these things, should embody it in a law, to be her guide in her dealings with herself and with other states.

"In this way virtue and vice will be more clearly distinguished by us. And when they have become clearer, education and other institutions will in like manner become clearer; and in particular that question of convivial entertainment, which may seem, perhaps, to have been a very trifling matter, and to have taken a great many more words than were necessary."
D: Refashioned:
"Consider it this way," said the Stranger. "We can think of each living being as a puppet of the gods -- either merely their plaything or created with a purpose; we cannot know which for certain. But we do know that the feelings within us are like cords and strings pulling us in different, opposite directions toward opposing actions. This is where the difference between virtue and vice lies.

"The argument tells us there is one cord among these that every person should grasp and never release, pulling with it against all the rest: the sacred and golden cord of reason, which we call the common law of the state. The other cords are hard and iron; this one is soft because it is golden.

"We must always cooperate with the guidance of the best, which is law. Since reason is beautiful and gentle, not violent, her rule needs servants to help the golden principle overcome the others.

"This is the moral of the puppet metaphor. It also clarifies what it means to be 'superior or inferior to oneself.' The individual who grasps right reason and lives by its guidance does well. The city that receives this guidance -- from some god or from someone who understands these things -- should embody it in law to guide its dealings with itself and other states.

"Once virtue and vice become clearer, education and other institutions will become clearer too -- including the question of convivial entertainment, which may seem trivial and to have consumed far more words than necessary."
D: Elegant: We are puppets of the gods, pulled by opposing cords -- pleasure, pain, fear, hope. Among these is one sacred golden cord: reason, identical with law. Though golden, it is soft and gentle, not violent, so it needs assistance to overcome the hard iron cords of passion. The individual who holds fast to reason is 'superior to himself'; the city that embodies reason in law governs well. This puppet metaphor illuminates virtue, vice, education, and even the seemingly trivial question of drinking parties.
D: Summary: The Stranger presents the famous puppet metaphor: humans are pulled by opposing cords of passion, but the golden cord of reason -- identified with law -- must be held fast against the rest. Reason is gentle and needs support to prevail. This clarifies self-mastery, the nature of law, education, and even the question of drinking.
D: Distilled: We are puppets pulled by iron cords of passion, but must hold fast to the soft golden cord of reason -- which is law.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The puppet (thauma) metaphor is one of Plato's most original images, unique to the Laws. -- The golden cord of reason vs. iron cords of passion creates a powerful material metaphor for the soul's inner conflict. Gold is precious but soft; iron is common but hard. Reason requires institutional support because it cannot overpower passion through force alone. -- 'Whether their plaything only, or created with a purpose' -- this theological uncertainty is remarkable and distinctive of the Laws' more cautious theology. -- The identification of individual reason with 'the common law of the State' makes law the social expression of individual rationality -- a profound philosophical claim. -- 'Reason is beautiful and gentle, and not violent' -- this characterization of reason as non-coercive anticipates Habermas's discourse ethics. -- The passage ties together all the threads of Book 1: the war within the self (1.6), self-mastery (1.6-1.7), the hierarchy of goods (1.17), and education (1.46-1.47).

D: Original:
Perhaps, however, the theme may turn out not to be unworthy of the length of discourse.

Very good; let us proceed with any enquiry which really bears on our present object.

Proceed.

Suppose that we give this puppet of ours drink-what will be the effect on him?

Having what in view do you ask that question?

Nothing as yet; but I ask generally, when the puppet is brought to the drink, what sort of result is likely to follow. I will endeavour to explain my meaning more clearly: what I am now asking is this-Does the drinking of wine heighten and increase pleasures and pains, and passions and loves?

Very greatly.

And are perception and memory, and opinion and prudence, heightened and increased? Do not these qualities entirely desert a man if he becomes saturated with drink?

Yes, they entirely desert him.

Does he not return to the state of soul in which he was when a young child?

He does.

Then at that time he will have the least control over himself?

The least.

And will he not be in a most wretched plight?

Most wretched.

Then not only an old man but also a drunkard becomes a second time a child?

Well said, Stranger.

Is there any argument which will prove to us that we ought to encourage the taste for drinking instead of doing all we can to avoid it?

I suppose that there is; you at any rate, were just now saying that you were ready to maintain such a doctrine.

True, I was; and I am ready still, seeing that you have both declared that you are anxious to hear me.

To sure we are, if only for the strangeness of the paradox, which asserts that a man ought of his own accord to plunge into utter degradation.

Are you speaking of the soul?

Yes.

And what would you say about the body, my friend? Are you not surprised at any one of his own accord bringing upon himself deformity, leanness, ugliness, decrepitude?

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"Perhaps, however, the theme may turn out not to be unworthy of the length of discourse," said Cleinias.

"Very good; let us proceed with any enquiry which really bears on our present object," replied the Athenian Stranger.

"Proceed."

"Suppose that we give this puppet of ours drink -- what will be the effect on him?"

"Having what in view do you ask that question?"

"Nothing as yet; but I ask generally, when the puppet is brought to the drink, what sort of result is likely to follow. I will endeavour to explain my meaning more clearly: what I am now asking is this -- Does the drinking of wine heighten and increase pleasures and pains, and passions and loves?"

"Very greatly."

"And are perception and memory, and opinion and prudence, heightened and increased? Do not these qualities entirely desert a man if he becomes saturated with drink?"

"Yes, they entirely desert him."

"Does he not return to the state of soul in which he was when a young child?"

"He does."

"Then at that time he will have the least control over himself?"

"The least."

"And will he not be in a most wretched plight?"

"Most wretched."

"Then not only an old man but also a drunkard becomes a second time a child?"

"Well said, Stranger."

"Is there any argument which will prove to us that we ought to encourage the taste for drinking instead of doing all we can to avoid it?"

"I suppose that there is; you at any rate, were just now saying that you were ready to maintain such a doctrine."

"True, I was; and I am ready still, seeing that you have both declared that you are anxious to hear me."

"To be sure we are, if only for the strangeness of the paradox, which asserts that a man ought of his own accord to plunge into utter degradation."

"Are you speaking of the soul?"

"Yes."

"And what would you say about the body, my friend? Are you not surprised at any one of his own accord bringing upon himself deformity, leanness, ugliness, decrepitude?"

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"Perhaps the topic will prove worthy of all these words," Cleinias observed.

"Good -- let us continue with whatever truly bears on our subject," the Stranger replied.

"Go on."

"What happens if we give our puppet drink?"

"What are you getting at?"

"Nothing specific yet. I'm asking generally: when the puppet is brought to wine, what results? Let me put it more clearly: does drinking wine intensify pleasures, pains, passions, and desires?"

"Enormously."

"And what about perception, memory, judgment, and prudence? Don't these abandon a person entirely when he's saturated with drink?"

"They desert him completely."

"Doesn't he return to the state of soul he was in as a young child?"

"He does."

"And at that point he has the least control over himself?"

"The very least."

"And isn't he in the most wretched condition?"

"The most wretched."

"So not only an old man, but also a drunkard, becomes a child for the second time?"

"Well said, Stranger."

"Is there any argument that could convince us to encourage drinking rather than avoid it entirely?"

"There must be -- you were just claiming you were ready to make that argument."

"I was, and I still am, since you've both said you want to hear it."

"We certainly do -- if only because of the sheer strangeness of the paradox: that a person should voluntarily plunge into utter degradation."

"Are you speaking of degradation of the soul?"

"Yes."

"And what about the body? Aren't you equally surprised when someone voluntarily brings upon himself deformity, weakness, ugliness, and decrepitude?"

"Certainly."
D: Elegant: Wine intensifies pleasures, pains, and passions while driving out perception, memory, and prudence. The drunkard reverts to the soul of a child -- minimal self-control, maximum wretchedness. Yet the Stranger maintains there is an argument for encouraging this voluntary degradation. The paradox seems as strange for the soul as it would for someone who voluntarily brought physical deformity upon himself.
D: Summary: The Stranger applies the puppet metaphor to drinking: wine amplifies passions and desires while expelling perception, memory, and prudence, reducing the drinker to a childlike state with minimal self-control. He then poses the paradox: why should anyone voluntarily undergo this degradation?
D: Distilled: Wine intensifies passions and strips away reason, reducing the drinker to a child -- yet there is an argument for this voluntary degradation.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger in rapid dialogue. -- 'A drunkard becomes a second time a child' is a vivid formulation. The regression to childhood echoes the puppet metaphor: when the golden cord of reason is loosened, only the iron cords of passion remain. -- The analogy between voluntary soul-degradation and voluntary physical degradation sets up the medical analogy in 1.51: just as one might voluntarily submit to pain for the sake of health, one might submit to the degradation of drink for the sake of self-knowledge. -- The 'paradox' (paradoxos = contrary to expectation) that the Stranger will argue for -- that controlled exposure to degradation serves education -- is genuinely counterintuitive and philosophically original. -- The interlocutors' eagerness to hear the paradox resolved shows Plato's dramatic skill in building intellectual suspense.

D: Original: Yet when a man goes of his own accord to a doctor's shop, and takes medicine, is he not aware that soon, and for many days afterwards, he will be in a state of body which he would die rather than accept as the permanent condition of his life? Are not those who train in gymnasia, at first beginning reduced to a state of weakness?
D: Narrative: "Yet when a man goes of his own accord to a doctor's shop, and takes medicine, is he not aware that soon, and for many days afterwards, he will be in a state of body which he would die rather than accept as the permanent condition of his life?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "Are not those who train in gymnasia, at first beginning reduced to a state of weakness?"
D: Refashioned: "Yet when a person voluntarily goes to a doctor and takes medicine," the Stranger asked, "isn't he fully aware that soon, and for many days afterward, he'll be in a physical state he would rather die than accept permanently? And don't those who begin training in gymnasia start out in a state of weakness?"
D: Elegant: People voluntarily submit to medicine that makes them miserable and to training that weakens them -- because the temporary degradation serves a greater purpose. The same logic applies to drinking.
D: Summary: The Stranger draws an analogy: people voluntarily take medicine that makes them miserable and begin exercise regimens that initially weaken them. Temporary physical degradation is accepted for the sake of long-term improvement.
D: Distilled: We voluntarily submit to medicine and training that temporarily degrade us -- why not to drinking that serves a similar educational purpose?
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The medical analogy is a favorite Platonic device (cf. Gorgias 478a-b, 521e-522a; Republic 3.405c-408e). -- The gymnasium analogy is especially apt given the context: Sparta's gymnasia cause initial weakness for the sake of later strength, just as the Stranger's proposed drinking regime causes initial loss of reason for the sake of later temperance. -- The argument structure is: (1) you accept temporary physical degradation for long-term physical health, (2) you should therefore accept temporary psychological degradation for long-term moral health. -- This is the key move that resolves the paradox posed in 1.50.

D: Original:
Yes, all that is well known.

Also that they go of their own accord for the sake of the subsequent benefit?

Very good.

And we may conceive this to be true in the same way of other practices?

Certainly.

And the same view may be taken of the pastime of drinking wine, if we are right in supposing that the same good effect follows?

To be sure.

If such convivialities should turn out to have any advantage equal in importance to that of gymnastic, they are in their very nature to be preferred to mere bodily exercise, inasmuch as they have no accompaniment of pain.

True; but I hardly think that we shall be able to discover any such benefits to be derived from them.

That is just what we must endeavour to show. And let me ask you a question:-Do we not distinguish two kinds of fear, which are very different?

What are they?

There is the fear of expected evil.

Yes.

And there is the fear of an evil reputation; we are afraid of being thought evil, because we do or say some dishonourable thing, which fear we and all men term shame.

Certainly.

These are the two fears, as I called them; one of which is the opposite of pain and other fears, and the opposite also of the greatest and most numerous sort of pleasures.

Very true.

And does not the legislator and every one who is good for anything, hold this fear in the greatest honour? This is what he terms reverence, and the confidence which is the reverse of this he terms insolence; and the latter he always deems to be a very great evil both to individuals and to states.

True.

Does not this kind of fear preserve us in many important ways? What is there which so surely gives victory and safety in war? For there are two things which give victory-confidence before enemies, and fear of disgrace before friends.

There are.

Then each of us should be fearless and also fearful; and why we should be either has now been determined.

Certainly.

And when we want to make any one fearless, we and the law bring him face to face with many fears.

Clearly.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, all that is well known," said Cleinias.

"Also that they go of their own accord for the sake of the subsequent benefit?" asked the Athenian Stranger.

"Very good."

"And we may conceive this to be true in the same way of other practices?"

"Certainly."

"And the same view may be taken of the pastime of drinking wine, if we are right in supposing that the same good effect follows?"

"To be sure."

"If such convivialities should turn out to have any advantage equal in importance to that of gymnastic, they are in their very nature to be preferred to mere bodily exercise, inasmuch as they have no accompaniment of pain."

"True; but I hardly think that we shall be able to discover any such benefits to be derived from them."

"That is just what we must endeavour to show. And let me ask you a question: Do we not distinguish two kinds of fear, which are very different?"

"What are they?"

"There is the fear of expected evil."

"Yes."

"And there is the fear of an evil reputation; we are afraid of being thought evil, because we do or say some dishonourable thing, which fear we and all men term shame."

"Certainly."

"These are the two fears, as I called them; one of which is the opposite of pain and other fears, and the opposite also of the greatest and most numerous sort of pleasures."

"Very true."

"And does not the legislator and every one who is good for anything, hold this fear in the greatest honour? This is what he terms reverence, and the confidence which is the reverse of this he terms insolence; and the latter he always deems to be a very great evil both to individuals and to states."

"True."

"Does not this kind of fear preserve us in many important ways? What is there which so surely gives victory and safety in war? For there are two things which give victory -- confidence before enemies, and fear of disgrace before friends."

"There are."

"Then each of us should be fearless and also fearful; and why we should be either has now been determined."

"Certainly."

"And when we want to make any one fearless, we and the law bring him face to face with many fears."

"Clearly."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, that's well known," said Cleinias.

"And people undergo these voluntarily for the sake of the benefit that follows?"

"Exactly."

"Can't the same principle apply to other practices?"

"Certainly."

"Then the same view could be taken of drinking wine, if the same kind of benefit follows?"

"Of course."

"If drinking parties prove to have advantages comparable to gymnastics, they would actually be preferable to physical exercise, since they involve no pain."

"True -- but I doubt we can find such benefits in drinking."

"That's precisely what we must try to show. Let me ask you: don't we distinguish two very different kinds of fear?"

"What kinds?"

"First, the fear of expected harm."

"Yes."

"Second, the fear of a bad reputation -- the dread of being thought base because we do or say something dishonorable. Everyone calls this shame."

"Certainly."

"These are the two fears. One is the opposite of pain and ordinary fears; it is also the opposite of the greatest and most common pleasures."

"Very true."

"Doesn't the legislator, and everyone of any worth, hold this second fear -- shame -- in the highest honor? He calls it reverence. And the confidence that is its opposite he calls insolence -- which he considers a great evil for both individuals and states."

"True."

"Doesn't this kind of fear -- reverence -- protect us in many crucial ways? What gives victory and safety in war more reliably? Two things: confidence before enemies and fear of disgrace before friends."

"Exactly."

"So each of us should be both fearless and fearful -- and we've now established why."

"Yes."

"And when we want to make someone fearless, we and the law expose them to many fears."

"Clearly."
D: Elegant: If drinking provides benefits comparable to gymnastics -- and without pain -- it is actually preferable. Two kinds of fear exist: fear of harm, and shame (fear of disgrace). The legislator honors shame as 'reverence' and condemns its absence as 'insolence.' Reverence preserves us: victory in war requires both confidence before enemies and fear of disgrace before friends. We must be both fearless and fearful. To make someone fearless, the law exposes them to fears.
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that drinking could be preferable to gymnastics if it provides comparable benefits without pain. He then distinguishes two kinds of fear: fear of harm and shame (fear of disgrace). Shame, which the legislator calls reverence, is essential for both individual virtue and military victory. To train fearlessness, the law exposes people to fears.
D: Distilled: Shame -- the fear of disgrace -- is the legislator's most honored tool, called reverence; and just as fearlessness is trained through exposure to fear, temperance must be trained through exposure to temptation.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- The distinction between fear of harm and fear of disgrace (shame/aidos) is philosophically important. Aidos was a fundamental Greek ethical concept -- the internalized sense of honor that restrains behavior. -- 'Reverence' (aidos/aischune) vs. 'insolence' (thrasos/hybris) maps onto the Greek honor/shame culture. -- 'Confidence before enemies and fear of disgrace before friends' captures the dual psychology of military courage: you fight because you fear shame more than death. -- The logical structure is building toward the conclusion: if we train fearlessness by exposure to fear, we should train temperance by exposure to pleasure. -- The claim that drinking is 'painless gymnastics' is both clever and substantive: it suggests that moral training need not be painful to be effective.

D: Original: And when we want to make him rightly fearful, must we not introduce him to shameless pleasures, and train him to take up arms against them, and to overcome them? Or does this principle apply to courage only, and must he who would be perfect in valour fight against and overcome his own natural character-since if he be unpractised and inexperienced in such conflicts, he will not be half the man which he might have been-and are we to suppose, that with temperance it is otherwise, and that he who has never fought with the shameless and unrighteous temptations of his pleasures and lusts, and conquered them, in earnest and in play, by word, deed, and act, will still be perfectly temperate?
D: Narrative: "And when we want to make him rightly fearful, must we not introduce him to shameless pleasures, and train him to take up arms against them, and to overcome them?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "Or does this principle apply to courage only, and must he who would be perfect in valour fight against and overcome his own natural character -- since if he be unpractised and inexperienced in such conflicts, he will not be half the man which he might have been -- and are we to suppose, that with temperance it is otherwise, and that he who has never fought with the shameless and unrighteous temptations of his pleasures and lusts, and conquered them, in earnest and in play, by word, deed, and act, will still be perfectly temperate?"
D: Refashioned: "And when we want to make someone rightly fearful -- meaning reverent -- must we not expose them to shameless pleasures and train them to fight and overcome those pleasures?" the Stranger asked. "Or does this principle of training through exposure apply only to courage? Must the person who wants to be perfectly brave fight against and master his own nature -- since without practice in such struggles he'll be only half the person he could be -- while we somehow assume that temperance works differently? That someone who has never battled the shameless temptations of pleasure and desire, and conquered them in earnest and in play, through word, deed, and action, will still be perfectly temperate?"
D: Elegant: The parallel is complete: just as fearlessness is trained by facing fears, temperance must be trained by facing pleasures. A person who has never fought against shameless temptations -- in earnest and in play, by word, deed, and act -- will never be truly temperate, just as the untested soldier will never be truly brave.
D: Summary: The Stranger completes the parallel: if courage requires training through exposure to fear, temperance requires training through exposure to pleasure. No one who has never struggled against temptation in earnest and in play can be perfectly temperate.
D: Distilled: Temperance, like courage, requires practice against the very thing it must overcome -- one cannot be temperate without having fought temptation.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is the culminating argument of Book 1's case for regulated drinking. The logical structure is: (a) courage is trained by exposure to fear, (b) therefore temperance should be trained by exposure to pleasure, (c) regulated drinking provides such exposure. -- 'In earnest and in play, by word, deed, and act' -- this comprehensive formulation echoes 1.46 on education through play and earnest. -- The phrase 'he will not be half the man which he might have been' emphasizes that avoidance stunts moral development rather than protecting it. -- Cf. Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics 2.2 (1104a-b): 'We become temperate by abstaining from pleasures' but also 'we become just by doing just acts, brave by doing brave acts' -- the Aristotelian doctrine of habituation through practice. -- The argument directly refutes the Spartan prohibitionist approach (1.30): mere avoidance produces incomplete virtue.

D: Original:
A most unlikely supposition.

Suppose that some God had given a fear-potion to men, and that the more a man drank of this the more he regarded himself at every draught as a child of misfortune, and that he feared everything happening or about to happen to him; and that at last the most courageous of men utterly lost his presence of mind for a time, and only came to himself again when he had slept off the influence of the draught.

But has such a draught, Stranger, ever really been known among men?

No; but, if there had been, might not such a draught have been of use to the legislator as a test of courage? Might we not go and say to him, "O legislator, whether you are legislating for the Cretan, or for any other state, would you not like to have a touchstone of the courage and cowardice of your citizens?"

"I should," will be the answer of every one.

"And you would rather have a touchstone in which there is no risk and no great danger than the reverse?"

In that proposition every one may safely agree.
D: Narrative:
"A most unlikely supposition," agreed Cleinias.

"Suppose that some God had given a fear-potion to men," said the Athenian Stranger, "and that the more a man drank of this the more he regarded himself at every draught as a child of misfortune, and that he feared everything happening or about to happen to him; and that at last the most courageous of men utterly lost his presence of mind for a time, and only came to himself again when he had slept off the influence of the draught."

"But has such a draught, Stranger, ever really been known among men?" asked Cleinias.

"No; but, if there had been, might not such a draught have been of use to the legislator as a test of courage? Might we not go and say to him, 'O legislator, whether you are legislating for the Cretan, or for any other state, would you not like to have a touchstone of the courage and cowardice of your citizens?'"

"'I should,' will be the answer of every one."

"'And you would rather have a touchstone in which there is no risk and no great danger than the reverse?'"

"In that proposition every one may safely agree."
D: Refashioned:
"That's a most unlikely assumption," Cleinias agreed.

"Now suppose," said the Stranger, "that some god had given humanity a fear-potion. The more you drank, the more miserable and terrified you became. You feared everything present and future. Eventually even the bravest person would completely lose his nerve -- recovering only after sleeping off the potion's effects."

"Has such a potion ever actually existed?" Cleinias asked.

"No. But if it had, wouldn't it be useful to the legislator as a test of courage? We could say to him: 'Legislator, whether you're making laws for Crete or any other state, wouldn't you like a touchstone for testing your citizens' courage and cowardice?'

"Every legislator would answer: 'Yes.'

"'And you'd prefer a touchstone that involves no real risk or danger?'

"Everyone would agree to that."
D: Elegant: Imagine a fear-potion that makes even the bravest person lose all courage temporarily. Such a potion would be invaluable to a legislator as a risk-free test of character -- a touchstone for measuring courage and cowardice without anyone getting hurt.
D: Summary: The Stranger proposes a thought experiment: a hypothetical fear-potion that temporarily destroys courage would be invaluable to any legislator as a safe, risk-free test of character. This sets up the argument that wine serves as the real-world equivalent for testing temperance.
D: Distilled: A hypothetical fear-potion would be invaluable as a risk-free test of courage -- and wine is the real equivalent for testing temperance.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- The thought experiment of the fear-potion is a brilliant philosophical device. It establishes the principle that controlled exposure to a negative state can be valuable for assessment and training, without real-world risk. -- 'Touchstone' (basanos) was literally a stone used to test the purity of gold by rubbing it against the stone. The metaphor was common in Greek for any test of character. -- The argument's structure: (1) a hypothetical fear-potion would be useful for testing courage, (2) wine is a real substance that has the opposite effect (inducing overconfidence rather than fear), (3) therefore wine can serve as a real touchstone for testing temperance. -- The fear-potion is imaginary; wine is real. This is the Stranger's rhetorical coup: he will argue that wine is the actual, available tool that his hypothetical illustrates.

D: Original:
"And in order to make use of the draught, you would lead them amid these imaginary terrors, and prove them, when the affection of fear was working upon them, and compel them to be fearless, exhorting and admonishing them; and also honouring them, but dishonouring any one who will not be persuaded by you to be in all respects such as you command him; and if he underwent the trial well and manfully, you would let him go unscathed; but if ill, you would inflict a punishment upon him? Or would you abstain from using the potion altogether, although you have no reason for abstaining?"

He would be certain, Stranger, to use the potion.
D: Narrative:
