"'And in order to make use of the draught, you would lead them amid these imaginary terrors, and prove them, when the affection of fear was working upon them, and compel them to be fearless, exhorting and admonishing them; and also honouring them, but dishonouring any one who will not be persuaded by you to be in all respects such as you command him; and if he underwent the trial well and manfully, you would let him go unscathed; but if ill, you would inflict a punishment upon him? Or would you abstain from using the potion altogether, although you have no reason for abstaining?'" continued the Athenian Stranger.

"He would be certain, Stranger, to use the potion," said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"'To use the potion,' the Stranger continued, 'you would lead your citizens through these imaginary terrors, testing them while fear gripped them. You would compel them to be fearless, exhorting and coaching them. You would honor those who responded well, and dishonor anyone who refused to meet your standards. Those who passed the trial manfully, you would release unscathed. Those who failed, you would punish. Or would you refuse to use the potion at all, even though there is no reason to abstain?'"

"The legislator would certainly use the potion," Cleinias confirmed.
D: Elegant: The fear-potion would be used to test citizens under controlled conditions: those who mastered their fear would be honored; those who failed would be punished. No legislator would refuse such a tool without reason. The same logic applies to wine as a test of temperance.
D: Summary: The Stranger describes how the fear-potion would be used: citizens would be tested under its influence, honored for courage, and punished for cowardice. No rational legislator would refuse such a tool. Cleinias agrees.
D: Distilled: Any rational legislator would use a risk-free test of character rather than abstain without reason -- and wine provides exactly such a test for temperance.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The testing protocol described (controlled exposure, exhortation, honor/dishonor) mirrors Spartan training methods -- but applied to temperance rather than courage. -- 'You have no reason for abstaining' is pointed at the Spartan/Cretan prohibition of drinking: they abstain from using this testing tool without adequate justification. -- The argument is now almost complete: the fear-potion analogy has established that controlled testing of character is valuable; the next step identifies wine as the real-world equivalent.

D: Original: This would be a mode of testing and training which would be wonderfully easy in comparison with those now in use, and might be applied to a single person, or to a few, or indeed to any number; and he would do well who provided himself with the potion only, rather than with any number of other things, whether he preferred to be by himself in solitude, and there contend with his fears, because he was ashamed to be seen by the eye of man until he was perfect; or trusting to the force of his own nature and habits, and believing that he had been already disciplined sufficiently, he did not hesitate to train himself in company with any number of others, and display his power in conquering the irresistible change effected by the draught-his virtue being such, that he never in any instance fell into any great unseemliness, but was always himself, and left off before he arrived at the last cup, fearing that he, like all other men, might be overcome by the potion.
D: Narrative: "This would be a mode of testing and training which would be wonderfully easy in comparison with those now in use, and might be applied to a single person, or to a few, or indeed to any number," said the Athenian Stranger, "and he would do well who provided himself with the potion only, rather than with any number of other things, whether he preferred to be by himself in solitude, and there contend with his fears, because he was ashamed to be seen by the eye of man until he was perfect; or trusting to the force of his own nature and habits, and believing that he had been already disciplined sufficiently, he did not hesitate to train himself in company with any number of others, and display his power in conquering the irresistible change effected by the draught -- his virtue being such, that he never in any instance fell into any great unseemliness, but was always himself, and left off before he arrived at the last cup, fearing that he, like all other men, might be overcome by the potion."
D: Refashioned:
"This method of testing and training would be wonderfully easy compared to current methods," the Stranger said. "It could be applied to one person, a few, or any number. A person would do well to use the potion alone rather than rely on many other tools -- whether he preferred solitary practice, contending privately with his fears out of shame at being seen until he perfected himself, or whether, trusting his nature and training and believing himself already sufficiently disciplined, he trained openly among others, demonstrating his ability to overcome the potion's irresistible effects.

"The truly virtuous person would never fall into great unseemliness, always remaining himself, and would stop before reaching the last cup -- knowing that, like all people, he might ultimately be overcome."
D: Elegant: This testing method is simpler and more scalable than current approaches. It can be practiced alone (for the modest) or in groups (for the confident). The truly virtuous person maintains control under the potion's influence, never falls into great disgrace, and knows when to stop -- recognizing that even the best can be overcome.
D: Summary: The Stranger praises this testing method as easy, scalable, and adaptable to any circumstance. The virtuous person maintains self-control under the potion's influence and knows when to stop, recognizing that everyone has limits.
D: Distilled: The wise person trains against temptation, maintains composure under its influence, and knows when to stop -- recognizing that even the best have limits.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Left off before he arrived at the last cup' is practical advice embedded in philosophical argument: the wise drinker knows his limits. -- The two modes of training -- private (out of shame) and public (out of confidence) -- map onto different stages of moral development. -- 'He never in any instance fell into any great unseemliness, but was always himself' -- the goal is not to be unaffected by the potion/wine but to maintain one's essential character under its influence. This is a realistic standard of self-mastery: not invulnerability but resilience. -- 'Fearing that he, like all other men, might be overcome' -- the recognition of universal human fallibility is a mark of true wisdom. Even the Stranger does not claim exemption from human weakness.

D: Original:
Yes, Stranger, in that last case, too, he might equally show his self-control.

Let us return to the lawgiver, and say to him:-"Well, lawgiver, there is certainly no such fear-potion which man has either received from the Gods or himself discovered; for witchcraft has no place at our board. But is there any potion which might serve as a test of overboldness and excessive and indiscreet boasting?

I suppose that he will say, Yes-meaning that wine is such a potion.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, Stranger, in that last case, too, he might equally show his self-control," agreed Cleinias.

"Let us return to the lawgiver, and say to him," said the Athenian Stranger, "'Well, lawgiver, there is certainly no such fear-potion which man has either received from the Gods or himself discovered; for witchcraft has no place at our board. But is there any potion which might serve as a test of overboldness and excessive and indiscreet boasting?'"

"I suppose that he will say, Yes -- meaning that wine is such a potion," said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, in that case too he would demonstrate self-control," Cleinias agreed.

"Let us return to the lawgiver," the Stranger said. "We would say to him: 'Lawgiver, no fear-potion has ever been received from the gods or discovered by men -- there is no place for witchcraft at our table. But is there a potion that could serve as a test of overboldness, excessive confidence, and indiscreet boasting?'"

"I suppose the lawgiver would answer: 'Yes' -- meaning wine," said Cleinias.
D: Elegant: The fear-potion is hypothetical -- it does not exist. But wine does exist, and it is the real-world potion that tests overboldness, excessive confidence, and indiscreet boasting. Cleinias himself draws the conclusion.
D: Summary: The Stranger reveals the analogy's payoff: while no fear-potion exists, wine is the real substance that tests overboldness and excessive confidence. Cleinias himself identifies wine as the answer.
D: Distilled: No fear-potion exists, but wine does -- and it is the real test of overboldness, overconfidence, and shamelessness.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- This is the argumentative climax: the long hypothetical about the fear-potion resolves into the claim that wine is its real-world counterpart. -- The fear-potion tested courage (resistance to fear); wine tests temperance (resistance to shameless pleasure and overconfidence). They are complementary. -- 'Witchcraft has no place at our board' -- a pragmatic disclaimer separating philosophy from magic. -- That Cleinias draws the conclusion himself ('I suppose he will say Yes -- meaning wine') is a mark of successful Socratic education: the learner arrives at the insight independently. -- The word translated 'overboldness' (thrasos) was identified earlier (1.52) as the opposite of reverence -- the vice that the legislator most deplores.

D: Original: Is not the effect of this quite the opposite of the effect of the other? When a man drinks wine he begins to be better pleased with himself, and the more he drinks the more he is filled full of brave hopes, and conceit of his power, and at last the string of his tongue is loosened, and fancying himself wise, he is brimming over with lawlessness, and has no more fear or respect, and is ready to do or say anything.
D: Narrative: "Is not the effect of this quite the opposite of the effect of the other?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "When a man drinks wine he begins to be better pleased with himself, and the more he drinks the more he is filled full of brave hopes, and conceit of his power, and at last the string of his tongue is loosened, and fancying himself wise, he is brimming over with lawlessness, and has no more fear or respect, and is ready to do or say anything."
D: Refashioned: "Isn't wine's effect exactly the opposite of the fear-potion's?" the Stranger asked. "When a person drinks wine, they begin to feel more pleased with themselves. The more they drink, the more they fill up with brave hopes and inflated confidence. Eventually their tongue is loosened, and imagining themselves wise, they overflow with lawlessness -- losing all fear and respect, ready to do or say anything."
D: Elegant: Wine produces the opposite of fear: increasing self-satisfaction, inflated confidence, loosened tongues, and the illusion of wisdom. At its peak, it produces total lawlessness -- the loss of all shame and restraint.
D: Summary: The Stranger describes wine's progressive effects: growing self-satisfaction, inflated confidence, loosened speech, false wisdom, and ultimately complete lawlessness -- the loss of all fear and respect. This is the exact opposite of the fear-potion.
D: Distilled: Wine progressively inflates confidence, loosens the tongue, and dissolves all shame and restraint -- the perfect opposite of fear.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The progression described (self-satisfaction > confidence > loosened tongue > false wisdom > lawlessness) is a remarkably accurate phenomenology of intoxication. -- 'Fancying himself wise' connects to the Socratic insight that false wisdom is the greatest ignorance (Apology 21d-22e). -- 'Brimming over with lawlessness' (anomia) is the key term: wine dissolves nomos (law/custom), which is why it serves as a test of how deeply law has been internalized. -- The fear-potion strips away courage; wine strips away restraint. Together they would test the full spectrum of virtue. Since only wine exists, the legislator must use it. -- 'Ready to do or say anything' -- this is the condition of maximum vulnerability to vice, and therefore the condition in which character is most fully revealed.

D: Original:
I think that every one will admit the truth of your description.

Certainly.

Now, let us remember, as we were saying, that there are two things which should be cultivated in the soul: first, the greatest courage; secondly, the greatest fear-

Which you said to be characteristic of reverence, if I am not mistaken.

Thank you for reminding me. But now, as the habit of courage and fearlessness is to be trained amid fears, let us consider whether the opposite quality is not also to be trained among opposites.

That is probably the case.

There are times and seasons at which we are by nature more than commonly valiant and bold; now we ought to train ourselves on these occasions to be as free from impudence and shamelessness as possible, and to be afraid to say or suffer or do anything that is base.

True.
D: Narrative:
"I think that every one will admit the truth of your description," said Cleinias.

"Certainly," added Megillus.

"Now, let us remember, as we were saying, that there are two things which should be cultivated in the soul: first, the greatest courage; secondly, the greatest fear --" said the Athenian Stranger.

"Which you said to be characteristic of reverence, if I am not mistaken," interjected Cleinias.

"Thank you for reminding me. But now, as the habit of courage and fearlessness is to be trained amid fears, let us consider whether the opposite quality is not also to be trained among opposites."

"That is probably the case," agreed Megillus.

"There are times and seasons at which we are by nature more than commonly valiant and bold; now we ought to train ourselves on these occasions to be as free from impudence and shamelessness as possible, and to be afraid to say or suffer or do anything that is base."

"True," said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Everyone will recognize the truth of that description," said Cleinias.

"Certainly," Megillus agreed.

"Now let us recall what we established: two things must be cultivated in the soul -- first, the greatest courage; second, the greatest fear," the Stranger continued.

"Which you said was characteristic of reverence, if I remember correctly," Cleinias interjected.

"Thank you for the reminder. Now, if courage and fearlessness are trained through exposure to fears, shouldn't the opposite quality be trained through exposure to its opposite?"

"That seems likely," Megillus agreed.

"There are natural moments when we feel bolder and more daring than usual. On these occasions, we should train ourselves to remain as free from impudence and shamelessness as possible -- to fear saying, suffering, or doing anything base."

"True," said Cleinias.
D: Elegant: Two things must be cultivated: courage (trained through exposure to fear) and reverence (trained through exposure to boldness-inducing situations). When we naturally feel most daring, we should practice maintaining shame and avoiding baseness. Wine creates exactly such conditions.
D: Summary: The Stranger recalls the twin requirements -- courage and reverence -- and completes the parallel: if courage is trained through fears, reverence should be trained through situations that induce boldness. Those naturally bold moments are when we must practice maintaining restraint and avoiding baseness.
D: Distilled: If courage is trained through exposure to fear, then reverence must be trained through exposure to boldness -- and wine provides that exposure.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Megillus, and the Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's reminder that the 'greatest fear' is reverence shows he has been following the argument carefully and internalized the key distinction from 1.52. -- The principle of 'training among opposites' is now fully symmetrical: fear trains courage, boldness-inducing situations train reverence/temperance. -- 'Times and seasons at which we are by nature more than commonly valiant and bold' -- this describes not only drunkenness but also youth, victory, wealth, and any condition that inflates confidence. -- The argument has circled back to its starting point with full justification: regulated drinking is an institution for training temperance, just as gymnasia and the Crypteia are institutions for training courage.

D: Original:
Are not the moments in which we are apt to be bold and shameless such as these?-when we are under the influence of anger, love, pride, ignorance, avarice, cowardice? or when wealth, beauty, strength, and all the intoxicating workings of pleasure madden us? What is better adapted than the festive use of wine, in the first place to test, and in the second place to train the character of a man, if care be taken in the use of it? What is there cheaper, or more innocent? For do but consider which is the greater risk:-Would you rather test a man of a morose and savage nature, which is the source of ten thousand acts of injustice, by making bargains with him at a risk to yourself, or by having him as a companion at the festival of Dionysus? Or would you, if you wanted to apply a touchstone to a man who is prone to love, entrust your wife, or your sons, or daughters to him, perilling your dearest interests in order to have a view of the condition of his soul? I might mention numberless cases, in which the advantage would be manifest of getting to know a character in sport, and without paying dearly for experience. And I do not believe that either a Cretan, or any other man, will doubt that such a test is a fair test, and safer, cheaper, and speedier than any other.

That is certainly true.

And this knowledge of the natures and habits of men's souls will be of the greatest use in that art which has the management of them; and that art, if I am not mistaken, is politics.

Exactly so.
D: Narrative:
"Are not the moments in which we are apt to be bold and shameless such as these?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "When we are under the influence of anger, love, pride, ignorance, avarice, cowardice? or when wealth, beauty, strength, and all the intoxicating workings of pleasure madden us?

"What is better adapted than the festive use of wine, in the first place to test, and in the second place to train the character of a man, if care be taken in the use of it? What is there cheaper, or more innocent? For do but consider which is the greater risk: Would you rather test a man of a morose and savage nature, which is the source of ten thousand acts of injustice, by making bargains with him at a risk to yourself, or by having him as a companion at the festival of Dionysus?

"Or would you, if you wanted to apply a touchstone to a man who is prone to love, entrust your wife, or your sons, or daughters to him, perilling your dearest interests in order to have a view of the condition of his soul?

"I might mention numberless cases, in which the advantage would be manifest of getting to know a character in sport, and without paying dearly for experience. And I do not believe that either a Cretan, or any other man, will doubt that such a test is a fair test, and safer, cheaper, and speedier than any other."

"That is certainly true," agreed Cleinias.

"And this knowledge of the natures and habits of men's souls will be of the greatest use in that art which has the management of them; and that art, if I am not mistaken, is politics."

"Exactly so."
D: Refashioned:
"Aren't these the moments when we tend to be bold and shameless?" the Stranger asked. "When we're under the influence of anger, love, pride, ignorance, greed, or cowardice? Or when wealth, beauty, strength, and all the intoxicating effects of pleasure drive us to excess?

"What is better suited than the festive use of wine to first test and then train a person's character, provided it is used with care? What method is cheaper or more harmless?

"Consider the alternatives. Would you rather evaluate a person with a savage, morose nature -- the source of countless injustices -- by entering into business dealings with him at your own risk? Or by having him as a companion at a festival of Dionysus?

"If you wanted to test a man prone to sexual passion, would you entrust your wife, sons, or daughters to him, risking your most precious relationships just to examine the state of his soul?

"I could name countless cases where it would obviously be better to discover someone's character through a harmless social occasion rather than paying dearly for the experience. I don't believe any Cretan, or any other person, would doubt that this is a fair test -- and a safer, cheaper, and faster one than any alternative."

"That is certainly true," Cleinias agreed.

"And this knowledge of people's natures and character will be of the greatest use in the art that manages souls. That art, if I'm not mistaken, is politics."

"Exactly so."
D: Elegant: Many situations make us bold and shameless: anger, love, pride, wealth, beauty, and every intoxication of pleasure. Regulated wine-drinking is the safest, cheapest, and most effective way to test and train character under these conditions. Would you rather discover a savage person's nature through risky business dealings, or over a cup of wine? Would you test a man's self-control by entrusting him with your family, or by observing him at a Dionysiac festival? Knowledge gained harmlessly at the drinking table serves politics -- the art of managing human souls.
D: Summary: The Stranger delivers the final argument: wine is the safest, cheapest, and most efficient method for testing and training character. It reveals a person's nature under the influence of boldness-inducing conditions without the real-world risks of business dealings or entrusted relationships. This knowledge of souls is the foundation of politics.
D: Distilled: Regulated wine-drinking is the safest, cheapest, and most revealing test of character -- and knowledge of character is the foundation of politics.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This is the culminating passage of Book 1. The entire argument -- from the opening question about divine lawgivers through the analysis of virtue, the puppet metaphor, and the fear-potion thought experiment -- arrives at this practical conclusion: properly supervised drinking parties serve as testing grounds for character. -- The list of boldness-inducing conditions (anger, love, pride, ignorance, avarice, cowardice, wealth, beauty, strength, pleasure) is comprehensive. -- 'The art which has the management of souls... is politics' -- this definition of politics as psychagogy (soul-management) is distinctively Platonic (cf. Gorgias 464b-465d, Phaedrus 261a-262c, Statesman 304e-305e). -- The practical comparisons (business dealings vs. drinking companions; entrusting family vs. Dionysiac observation) are vivid and persuasive. -- 'Safer, cheaper, and speedier' -- the Stranger appeals to pragmatic values his Cretan and Spartan interlocutors respect. -- Book 1 ends with the theoretical foundation laid; Book 2 will develop the connection between drinking, music, and education in detail.

D: Original:
And now we have to consider whether the insight into human nature is the only benefit derived from well ordered potations, or whether there are not other advantages great and much to be desired. The argument seems to imply that there are. But how and in what way these are to be attained, will have to be considered attentively, or we may be entangled in error.

Proceed.

Let me once more recall our doctrine of right education; which, if I am not mistaken, depends on the due regulation of convivial intercourse.

You talk rather grandly.
D: Narrative:
"And now we have to consider whether the insight into human nature is the only benefit derived from well-ordered potations, or whether there are not other advantages great and much to be desired. The argument seems to imply that there are. But how and in what way these are to be attained, will have to be considered attentively, or we may be entangled in error."

"Proceed."

"Let me once more recall our doctrine of right education; which, if I am not mistaken, depends on the due regulation of convivial intercourse."

"You talk rather grandly."
D: Refashioned:
"We need to consider whether understanding human nature is the only benefit we gain from well-regulated drinking, or whether there are other significant advantages worth pursuing. The argument suggests there are. But we must examine carefully how these benefits are to be achieved, or we risk falling into error."

"Go on."

"Let me return to our theory of proper education, which, if I'm not mistaken, depends on the proper regulation of social gatherings."

"That's quite a grand claim."
D: Elegant:
"We must ask whether regulated drinking yields benefits beyond insight into human nature. The argument implies it does, but we must proceed carefully to avoid error."

"Continue."

"Let me revisit our doctrine of proper education, which depends on regulating social gatherings."

"A grand claim."
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger proposes that well-regulated drinking parties offer benefits beyond mere insight into human nature, and connects this to their earlier theory of education. Cleinias is skeptical of the grand scope of the claim.
D: Distilled: Regulated drinking may serve education in ways beyond simply revealing human character.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage opens Book 2 by connecting the drinking-party discussion from Book 1 to the theme of education. The phrase 'convivial intercourse' refers to the symposium tradition -- the regulated drinking party central to Greek social life. The Stranger's approach of deriving educational theory from social practice anticipates his later arguments about choral performance. Cross-reference: Republic Books 2-3 on the education of the guardians through music and poetry.

D: Original:
Pleasure and pain I maintain to be the first perceptions of children, and I say that they are the forms under which virtue and vice are originally present to them. As to wisdom and true and fixed opinions, happy is the man who acquires them, even when declining in years; and we may say that he who possesses them, and the blessings which are contained in them, is a perfect man. Now I mean by education that training which is given by suitable habits to the first instincts of virtue in children;-when pleasure, and friendship, and pain, and hatred, are rightly implanted in souls not yet capable of understanding the nature of them, and who find them, after they have attained reason, to be in harmony with her. This harmony of the soul, taken as a whole, is virtue; but the particular training in respect of pleasure and pain, which leads you always to hate what you ought to hate, and love what you ought to love from the beginning of life to the end, may be separated off; and, in my view, will be rightly called education.

I think, Stranger, that you are quite right in all that you have said and are saying about education.
D: Narrative:
"Pleasure and pain I maintain to be the first perceptions of children, and I say that they are the forms under which virtue and vice are originally present to them. As to wisdom and true and fixed opinions, happy is the man who acquires them, even when declining in years; and we may say that he who possesses them, and the blessings which are contained in them, is a perfect man. Now I mean by education that training which is given by suitable habits to the first instincts of virtue in children -- when pleasure, and friendship, and pain, and hatred, are rightly implanted in souls not yet capable of understanding the nature of them, and who find them, after they have attained reason, to be in harmony with her. This harmony of the soul, taken as a whole, is virtue; but the particular training in respect of pleasure and pain, which leads you always to hate what you ought to hate, and love what you ought to love from the beginning of life to the end, may be separated off; and, in my view, will be rightly called education."

"I think, Stranger, that you are quite right in all that you have said and are saying about education."
D: Refashioned:
"I hold that pleasure and pain are the first things children perceive, and these are the forms through which virtue and vice first appear to them. As for wisdom and firm convictions, anyone who acquires these even in old age is fortunate; we may call someone who possesses them and their blessings a complete person. By education, I mean the training that shapes a child's earliest instincts toward virtue through proper habits -- when feelings of pleasure, affection, pain, and aversion are rightly planted in souls too young to understand their nature, and which, once reason develops, are found to be in harmony with it. This harmony of the soul, taken as a whole, is virtue. But the specific training of pleasure and pain -- which teaches you to hate what deserves hatred and love what deserves love, from birth to death -- can be distinguished as a separate element. In my view, this is rightly called education."

"I think, Stranger, that you are entirely correct in everything you have said about education."
D: Elegant:
"Children first encounter virtue and vice through pleasure and pain. Education is the training that rightly shapes these early instincts -- implanting proper loves and hatreds in souls too young for reason, so that when reason arrives, it finds the emotions already aligned with it. This harmony of soul is virtue itself. The specific discipline of pleasure and pain, teaching us to love and hate rightly throughout life, is what I call education."

"You are entirely correct."
D: Summary: The Athenian defines education as the proper habituation of pleasure and pain in children before they can reason, so that when reason develops, it finds the emotions already aligned with virtue. Cleinias agrees with this definition.
D: Distilled: Education is the early training of pleasure and pain so that our emotions align with reason when it arrives.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, with Cleinias responding. This is one of Plato's most important definitions of education in the Laws, emphasizing habituation over intellectual instruction. The idea that virtue is a harmony between emotion and reason echoes Republic 4.430d-432a, where justice is defined as the harmony of the soul's parts. The emphasis on pre-rational habituation also connects to Aristotle's later treatment in Nicomachean Ethics 2.1. The distinction between education as emotional training and wisdom as intellectual achievement is central to the Laws' educational program.

D: Original: I am glad to hear that you agree with me; for, indeed, the discipline of pleasure and pain which, when rightly ordered, is a principle of education, has been often relaxed and corrupted in human life. And the Gods, pitying the toils which our race is born to undergo, have appointed holy festivals, wherein men alternate rest with labour; and have given them the Muses and Apollo, the leader of the Muses, and Dionysus, to be companions in their revels, that they may improve their education by taking part in the festivals of the Gods, and with their help. I should like to know whether a common saying is in our opinion true to nature or not. For men say that the young of all creatures cannot be quiet in their bodies or in their voices; they are always wanting to move and cry out; some leaping and skipping, and overflowing with sportiveness and delight at something, others uttering all sorts of cries. But, whereas the animals have no perception of order or disorder in their movements, that is, of rhythm or harmony, as they are called, to us, the Gods, who, as we say, have been appointed to be our companions in the dance, have given the pleasurable sense of harmony and rhythm; and so they stir us into life, and we follow them, joining hands together in dances and songs; and these they call choruses, which is a term naturally expressive of cheerfulness. Shall we begin, then, with the acknowledgment that education is first given through Apollo and the Muses? What do you say?
D: Narrative:
"I am glad to hear that you agree with me; for, indeed, the discipline of pleasure and pain which, when rightly ordered, is a principle of education, has been often relaxed and corrupted in human life. And the Gods, pitying the toils which our race is born to undergo, have appointed holy festivals, wherein men alternate rest with labour; and have given them the Muses and Apollo, the leader of the Muses, and Dionysus, to be companions in their revels, that they may improve their education by taking part in the festivals of the Gods, and with their help."

"I should like to know whether a common saying is in our opinion true to nature or not. For men say that the young of all creatures cannot be quiet in their bodies or in their voices; they are always wanting to move and cry out; some leaping and skipping, and overflowing with sportiveness and delight at something, others uttering all sorts of cries. But, whereas the animals have no perception of order or disorder in their movements, that is, of rhythm or harmony, as they are called, to us, the Gods, who, as we say, have been appointed to be our companions in the dance, have given the pleasurable sense of harmony and rhythm; and so they stir us into life, and we follow them, joining hands together in dances and songs; and these they call choruses, which is a term naturally expressive of cheerfulness. Shall we begin, then, with the acknowledgment that education is first given through Apollo and the Muses? What do you say?"
D: Refashioned:
"I'm glad you agree, because the discipline of pleasure and pain -- which, when properly ordered, is a foundation of education -- has often been weakened and corrupted throughout human history. The gods, taking pity on the hardships our race is born to endure, established sacred festivals where people alternate between rest and labor. They gave us the Muses, Apollo as leader of the Muses, and Dionysus as companions in our celebrations, so that we might improve our education by participating in divine festivals with their guidance."

"Consider whether a common observation is true to nature. People say that the young of all creatures cannot keep their bodies or voices still -- they are always moving and crying out, some leaping and skipping with overflowing energy, others making every kind of noise. But while animals have no sense of order in their movements -- no perception of rhythm or harmony -- the gods who were appointed as our companions in the dance have given us humans the pleasurable sense of harmony and rhythm. They stir us to life, and we follow them, joining hands in dances and songs. These gatherings are called choruses -- a word that naturally expresses cheerfulness. Shall we begin, then, by acknowledging that education is first delivered through Apollo and the Muses?"
D: Elegant:
"The discipline of pleasure and pain, foundation of education, has often been corrupted. So the gods, pitying human hardship, gave us festivals alternating rest with labor, and the Muses, Apollo, and Dionysus as companions to restore our education."

"All young creatures move and cry restlessly. But unlike animals, humans perceive order in movement -- rhythm and harmony. The gods gave us this sense, stirring us to join hands in dance and song. These choruses are education's first instrument, delivered through Apollo and the Muses."
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that the gods gave humanity festivals, the Muses, Apollo, and Dionysus to restore the natural discipline of pleasure and pain that constitutes education. He distinguishes humans from animals by our unique capacity to perceive rhythm and harmony, making choral performance the foundation of education.
D: Distilled: The gods gave humans rhythm, harmony, and choral festivals as the primary instruments of education.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage introduces the divine origin of music and dance as educational tools -- a theological foundation for arts education. The connection of Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus to education through festivals is distinctive to the Laws. The observation that humans alone perceive rhythm and harmony (unlike animals who also move and vocalize) is a key anthropological claim. Cross-reference: Republic 2.376e-3.403c on musical education; Symposium 187a-e on harmony; Ion 533d-534e on divine inspiration through the Muses. The etymology connecting 'chorus' (choros) to 'cheerfulness' (chara) reflects ancient Greek linguistic analysis.

D: Original:
I assent.

And the uneducated is he who has not been trained in the chorus, and the educated is he who has been well trained?

Certainly.

And the chorus is made up of two parts, dance and song?

True.

Then he who is well educated will be able to sing and dance well?

I suppose that he will.

Let us see; what are we saying?

What?

He sings well and dances well; now must we add that he sings what is good and dances what is good?

Let us make the addition.
D: Narrative:
"I assent."

"And the uneducated is he who has not been trained in the chorus, and the educated is he who has been well trained?"

"Certainly."

"And the chorus is made up of two parts, dance and song?"

"True."

"Then he who is well educated will be able to sing and dance well?"

"I suppose that he will."

"Let us see; what are we saying?"

"What?"

"He sings well and dances well; now must we add that he sings what is good and dances what is good?"

"Let us make the addition."
D: Refashioned:
"I agree."

"So the uneducated person is one who has not been trained in choral performance, and the educated person is one who has been thoroughly trained?"

"Certainly."

"And the chorus consists of two elements -- dance and song?"

"True."

"Then a well-educated person will be able to sing and dance well?"

"I would think so."

"But let's pause and examine what we're really saying."

"What do you mean?"

"We say someone sings well and dances well. But shouldn't we add that they sing what is good and dance what is good?"

"Yes, let us add that."
D: Elegant: "The uneducated lack choral training; the educated have it. The chorus combines dance and song, so the educated person sings and dances well. But we must add a crucial distinction: they must sing and dance what is good, not merely perform with technical skill."
D: Summary: Through rapid dialogue, the Athenian establishes that education equals choral training, and that true choral excellence requires not just technical skill in singing and dancing, but performing content that is genuinely good.
D: Distilled: True education in music means not just performing skillfully but performing what is morally good.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger, in rapid dialectical exchange. The famous equation of education with choral training (achoreutos = apaideutos, 'unchorused' = 'uneducated') is a defining claim of the Laws. The crucial pivot is the distinction between technical excellence (singing/dancing well) and moral excellence (singing/dancing what is good) -- anticipating the fuller argument in 2.5. Cross-reference: Republic 3.401d-402a on the moral content of musical education; Protagoras 326a-b on choral education in Athens.

D: Original: We will suppose that he knows the good to be good, and the bad to be bad, and makes use of them accordingly: which now is the better trained in dancing and music-he who is able to move his body and to use his voice in what is understood to be the right manner, but has no delight in good or hatred of evil; or he who is incorrect in gesture and voice, but is right in his sense of pleasure and pain, and welcomes what is good, and is offended at what is evil?
D: Narrative: "We will suppose that he knows the good to be good, and the bad to be bad, and makes use of them accordingly: which now is the better trained in dancing and music -- he who is able to move his body and to use his voice in what is understood to be the right manner, but has no delight in good or hatred of evil; or he who is incorrect in gesture and voice, but is right in his sense of pleasure and pain, and welcomes what is good, and is offended at what is evil?"
D: Refashioned: "Let us assume a person can recognize good as good and bad as bad, and acts accordingly. Now, who is better educated in music and dance -- the person who moves and sings with technical correctness but takes no delight in goodness and feels no aversion to evil? Or the person whose technique is flawed, but whose sense of pleasure and pain is rightly ordered -- who genuinely welcomes what is good and is repelled by what is evil?"
D: Elegant: "Who is truly better educated in music: the technically perfect performer who feels nothing toward good or evil, or the technically flawed performer whose pleasures and pains are rightly aligned -- who delights in goodness and recoils from evil?"
D: Summary: The Athenian poses a fundamental question about education: whether technical skill or moral sensibility is the true mark of musical training, arguing implicitly that right feeling matters more than correct performance.
D: Distilled: Moral sensibility in art matters more than technical perfection without right feeling.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage presents the central dilemma of Plato's aesthetics in the Laws: the priority of moral response over technical virtuosity. The person with 'right pleasure and pain' but poor technique is implicitly the better educated -- education being defined (in 2.2) as the training of pleasure and pain. Cross-reference: Republic 3.401d-e, where correct musical education produces people who 'praise beautiful things and receive them into their soul'; Aristotle, Politics 8.5 (1340a-b) on music's power to shape character. This argument also implicitly critiques the professional musicians (technites) who possessed skill without moral formation.

D: Original:
There is a great difference, Stranger, in the two kinds of education.

If we three know what is good in song and dance, then we truly know also who is educated and who is uneducated; but if not, then we certainly shall not know wherein lies the safeguard of education, and whether there is any or not.

True.

Let us follow the scent like hounds, and go in pursuit of beauty of figure, and melody, and song, and dance; if these escape us, there will be no use in talking about true education, whether Hellenic or barbarian.

Yes.

And what is beauty of figure, or beautiful melody? When a manly soul is in trouble, and when a cowardly soul is in similar case, are they likely to use the same figures and gestures, or to give utterance to the same sounds?

How can they, when the very colours of their faces differ?
D: Narrative:
"There is a great difference, Stranger, in the two kinds of education."

"If we three know what is good in song and dance, then we truly know also who is educated and who is uneducated; but if not, then we certainly shall not know wherein lies the safeguard of education, and whether there is any or not."

"True."

"Let us follow the scent like hounds, and go in pursuit of beauty of figure, and melody, and song, and dance; if these escape us, there will be no use in talking about true education, whether Hellenic or barbarian."

"Yes."

"And what is beauty of figure, or beautiful melody? When a manly soul is in trouble, and when a cowardly soul is in similar case, are they likely to use the same figures and gestures, or to give utterance to the same sounds?"

"How can they, when the very colours of their faces differ?"
D: Refashioned:
"There is a vast difference between those two kinds of education, Stranger."

"If the three of us can identify what is genuinely good in song and dance, then we also know who is truly educated and who is not. But if we cannot, then we have no way of knowing where the safeguard of education lies, or whether one exists at all."

"True."

"Then let us track this down like hounds following a scent -- pursuing beauty of figure, melody, song, and dance. If these elude us, there is no point discussing true education, whether Greek or foreign."

"Agreed."

"So what is beauty of figure, or beautiful melody? When a courageous soul faces hardship, and when a cowardly soul faces the same situation, will they use the same postures and gestures, or produce the same sounds?"

"How could they, when even the color of their faces differs?"
D: Elegant: "If we can identify what is genuinely good in song and dance, we know who is educated. If not, we cannot even locate education's safeguard. Let us pursue beauty of figure, melody, and dance like hounds on a scent. Consider: do the brave and the cowardly express themselves with the same gestures and sounds when in distress? Obviously not -- even their complexions differ."
D: Summary: Cleinias acknowledges the distinction between technical and moral education. The Athenian argues they must identify genuine beauty in the arts to understand education, and begins by observing that different character types naturally express themselves differently in movement and sound.
D: Distilled: To understand education, we must identify genuine beauty in art, which differs because character shapes expression.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The hunting metaphor ('follow the scent like hounds') is characteristic of Plato's dialectical method -- cf. Republic 4.432d. The argument that inner character determines outward artistic expression is foundational: art is not arbitrary but reflects the soul's condition. The question about brave vs. cowardly expression anticipates the theory of artistic imitation (mimesis) developed further in 2.7-2.9. Cross-reference: Republic 3.399a-c on musical modes expressing courage vs. softness; Symposium 215c-216a on how Socrates' words affect the soul. The mention of 'Hellenic or barbarian' education shows the universalist scope of the inquiry.

D: Original:
Good, my friend; I may observe, however, in passing, that in music there certainly are figures and there are melodies: and music is concerned with harmony and rhythm, so that you may speak of a melody or figure having good rhythm or good harmony-the term is correct enough; but to speak metaphorically of a melody or figure having a "good colour," as the masters of choruses do, is not allowable, although you can speak of the melodies or figures of the brave and the coward, praising the one and censuring the other. And not to be tedious, let us say that the figures and melodies which are expressive of virtue of soul or body, or of images of virtue, are without exception good, and those which are expressive of vice are the reverse of good.

Your suggestion is excellent; and let us answer that these things are so.

Once more, are all of us equally delighted with every sort of dance?

Far otherwise.
D: Narrative:
"Good, my friend; I may observe, however, in passing, that in music there certainly are figures and there are melodies: and music is concerned with harmony and rhythm, so that you may speak of a melody or figure having good rhythm or good harmony -- the term is correct enough; but to speak metaphorically of a melody or figure having a 'good colour,' as the masters of choruses do, is not allowable, although you can speak of the melodies or figures of the brave and the coward, praising the one and censuring the other. And not to be tedious, let us say that the figures and melodies which are expressive of virtue of soul or body, or of images of virtue, are without exception good, and those which are expressive of vice are the reverse of good."

"Your suggestion is excellent; and let us answer that these things are so."

"Once more, are all of us equally delighted with every sort of dance?"

"Far otherwise."
D: Refashioned:
"Good point, my friend. I should note, however, that music contains both figures and melodies, and is concerned with harmony and rhythm. It is perfectly correct to say a melody or figure has good rhythm or good harmony. But when chorus directors speak metaphorically of a melody having 'good color,' that is imprecise -- though we can properly speak of the melodies and figures characteristic of the brave or the cowardly, praising one and condemning the other. To avoid being tedious, let us establish this principle: figures and melodies that express virtue of soul or body, or images of virtue, are without exception good; those that express vice are the opposite."

"An excellent proposal. Let us agree on that."

"One more question: are all of us equally delighted by every kind of dance?"

"Not at all."
D: Elegant:
"Music has figures and melodies governed by harmony and rhythm. We can properly speak of brave or cowardly melodies, but not of melodies having 'good color' -- that is imprecise metaphor. The principle is simple: all figures and melodies expressing virtue are good; those expressing vice are not."

"Agreed."

"But are we all equally delighted by every kind of dance?"

"Far from it."
D: Summary: The Athenian clarifies the proper vocabulary of musical criticism, rejecting vague metaphors like 'good color' while affirming that music can be evaluated by whether it expresses virtue or vice. He then asks why different people enjoy different dances.
D: Distilled: Art that expresses virtue is good and art that expresses vice is bad, regardless of subjective preference.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The correction about 'good colour' versus proper musical terms (harmony, rhythm) shows Plato's concern with precision in aesthetic vocabulary -- a theme also in Cratylus. The principle that virtue-expressing art is good and vice-expressing art is bad is the moral foundation of Plato's aesthetics throughout the dialogues. Cross-reference: Republic 3.400d-401a on rhythm and harmony following the character of a good soul; Republic 10.601a-b on the incompetence of artists who use impressive but empty language. The question about unequal delight in dance sets up the problem of subjective taste vs. objective artistic value.

D: Original:
What, then, leads us astray? Are beautiful things not the same to us all, or are they the same in themselves, but not in our opinion of them? For no one will admit that forms of vice in the dance are more beautiful than forms of virtue, or that he himself delights in the forms of vice, and others in a muse of another character. And yet most persons say, that the excellence of music is to give pleasure to our souls. But this is intolerable and blasphemous; there is, however, a much more plausible account of the delusion.

What?
D: Narrative:
"What, then, leads us astray? Are beautiful things not the same to us all, or are they the same in themselves, but not in our opinion of them? For no one will admit that forms of vice in the dance are more beautiful than forms of virtue, or that he himself delights in the forms of vice, and others in a muse of another character. And yet most persons say, that the excellence of music is to give pleasure to our souls. But this is intolerable and blasphemous; there is, however, a much more plausible account of the delusion."

"What?"
D: Refashioned:
"What leads us astray, then? Are beautiful things not the same for everyone? Or are they the same in themselves, but our opinions about them differ? No one would openly admit that dances expressing vice are more beautiful than those expressing virtue, or that they personally delight in vice while others enjoy a different kind of art. Yet most people claim that the excellence of music lies in giving pleasure to the soul. This view is intolerable -- even blasphemous. There is, however, a much more plausible explanation for this confusion."

"What is it?"
D: Elegant:
"What leads us astray? No one openly claims vice in dance is more beautiful than virtue. Yet most people say music's excellence lies in giving pleasure. This is intolerable and blasphemous. There is a better explanation for the confusion."

"What?"
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies a contradiction: no one explicitly defends vice in art as beautiful, yet most people judge music by the pleasure it gives. He calls this view blasphemous and promises a better account of why people are confused.
D: Distilled: Judging music solely by the pleasure it produces is a dangerous error that confuses subjective enjoyment with objective beauty.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The claim that judging music by pleasure alone is 'blasphemous' (literally impious) elevates aesthetic standards to a religious/moral level -- characteristic of the Laws' theistic framework. This directly challenges the pleasure-criterion that dominates popular and sophistic aesthetics. Cross-reference: Republic 10.607a on the 'ancient quarrel between philosophy and poetry'; Gorgias 501d-502d where Socrates argues that rhetoric and music aim at pleasure rather than the good; Philebus 51b-d on true vs. mixed pleasures. The argument anticipates the important distinction in 2.9 between what people enjoy and what is genuinely good.

D: Original:
The adaptation of art to the characters of men. Choric movements are imitations of manners occurring in various actions, fortunes, dispositions-each particular is imitated, and those to whom the words, or songs, or dances are suited, either by nature or habit or both, cannot help feeling pleasure in them and applauding them, and calling them beautiful. But those whose natures, or ways, or habits are unsuited to them, cannot delight in them or applaud them, and they call them base. There are others, again, whose natures are right and their habits wrong, or whose habits are right and their natures wrong, and they praise one thing, but are pleased at another. For they say that all these imitations are pleasant, but not good. And in the presence of those whom they think wise, they are ashamed of dancing and singing in the baser manner, or of deliberately lending any countenance to such proceedings; and yet, they have a secret pleasure in them.

Very true.

And is any harm done to the lover of vicious dances or songs, or any good done to the approver of the opposite sort of pleasure?

I think that there is.
D: Narrative:
"The adaptation of art to the characters of men. Choric movements are imitations of manners occurring in various actions, fortunes, dispositions -- each particular is imitated, and those to whom the words, or songs, or dances are suited, either by nature or habit or both, cannot help feeling pleasure in them and applauding them, and calling them beautiful. But those whose natures, or ways, or habits are unsuited to them, cannot delight in them or applaud them, and they call them base. There are others, again, whose natures are right and their habits wrong, or whose habits are right and their natures wrong, and they praise one thing, but are pleased at another. For they say that all these imitations are pleasant, but not good. And in the presence of those whom they think wise, they are ashamed of dancing and singing in the baser manner, or of deliberately lending any countenance to such proceedings; and yet, they have a secret pleasure in them."

"Very true."

"And is any harm done to the lover of vicious dances or songs, or any good done to the approver of the opposite sort of pleasure?"

"I think that there is."
D: Refashioned:
"The explanation lies in art's adaptation to individual character. Choral performances are imitations of human conduct across various situations, fortunes, and dispositions. Each detail is imitated, and those whose nature or habits -- or both -- align with what is portrayed inevitably feel pleasure, applaud, and call it beautiful. But those whose natures or habits are misaligned with the performance cannot enjoy or praise it, and they call it base."

"Then there is a third group: people whose natures are right but whose habits are wrong, or whose habits are right but whose natures are wrong. These people praise one thing publicly but are privately pleased by something else. They acknowledge that certain performances are pleasant but not good. In the company of those they consider wise, they are ashamed to dance or sing in the baser manner, or to openly support such performances. Yet they take a secret pleasure in them."

"Very true."

"Does any harm come to the person who loves vicious dances and songs, or any benefit to the one who approves the opposite kind of pleasure?"

"I believe so."
D: Elegant:
"Art adapts to character. People whose nature or habits match a performance inevitably enjoy it and call it beautiful; those who don't match it call it base. A third group -- whose natures and habits conflict -- publicly praise virtue while secretly enjoying vice. They are ashamed before the wise but take hidden pleasure in base art."

"Does loving vicious art cause harm, or loving virtuous art bring benefit?"

"I believe it does."
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that people enjoy art that mirrors their own character. Those with conflicting natures and habits experience a split -- praising good art publicly while secretly enjoying bad art. He raises the question of whether loving bad art causes real harm.
D: Distilled: We enjoy art that reflects our own character, which means bad art reinforces bad character and good art strengthens good character.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage contains Plato's psychology of aesthetic response: art functions as a mirror, and we are drawn to what resembles our own disposition. The distinction between those whose 'natures are right and habits wrong' (or vice versa) creates a nuanced four-part typology of aesthetic response. The concept of 'secret pleasure' in base art is psychologically acute -- anticipating the analysis of guilty pleasures. Cross-reference: Republic 10.605c-606d on the dangerous pleasure of tragic poetry; Republic 3.395c-d on how imitation shapes character; Aristotle, Politics 8.5 (1340a) on music's power to produce likenesses of character states. The question about harm from bad art sets up the crucial argument in 2.10.

D: Original:
"I think" is not the word, but I would say, rather, "I am certain." For must they not have the same effect as when a man associates with bad characters, whom he likes and approves rather than dislikes, and only censures playfully because he has a suspicion of his own badness? In that case, he who takes pleasure in them will surely become like those in whom he takes pleasure, even though he be ashamed to praise them. And what greater good or evil can any destiny ever make us undergo?

I know of none.
D: Narrative:
"'I think' is not the word, but I would say, rather, 'I am certain.' For must they not have the same effect as when a man associates with bad characters, whom he likes and approves rather than dislikes, and only censures playfully because he has a suspicion of his own badness? In that case, he who takes pleasure in them will surely become like those in whom he takes pleasure, even though he be ashamed to praise them. And what greater good or evil can any destiny ever make us undergo?"

"I know of none."
D: Refashioned:
"'I think' isn't strong enough -- I would say 'I am certain.' Consider: isn't the effect the same as when someone keeps company with people of bad character, whom he likes and approves of rather than rejects, only offering playful criticism out of a vague suspicion of his own faults? In such a case, the person who takes pleasure in bad company will inevitably become like those he enjoys, even if he is too ashamed to openly praise them. And what greater good or evil could any destiny possibly inflict on us?"

"I can think of none."
D: Elegant:
"Not 'I think' -- 'I am certain.' Enjoying bad art is like keeping bad company: you become what you take pleasure in, even if you are ashamed to praise it openly. What greater good or evil could destiny inflict than the shaping of our character through what we love?"

"None."
D: Summary: The Athenian emphatically corrects Cleinias: enjoying vicious art is not merely harmful but certain to corrupt, just as associating with bad people makes one bad. What we take pleasure in shapes who we become -- the most consequential force destiny can impose.
D: Distilled: We inevitably become like what we take pleasure in, making our aesthetic choices the most consequential force shaping our character.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The correction from 'I think' to 'I am certain' signals the Stranger's conviction about art's moral power. The analogy between bad art and bad companions makes the social-psychological mechanism explicit: pleasure in imitation produces assimilation. This is among Plato's strongest statements about art's formative power. Cross-reference: Republic 3.395d ('he who imitates, from youth up, eventually becomes that which he imitates'); Republic 10.606a-b on tragedy's corruption of even good men; Protagoras 325c-326a on the pervasive influence of songs and stories on character. The rhetorical question about destiny elevates aesthetic education to the highest importance.

D: Original:
Then in a city which has good laws, or in future ages is to have them, bearing in mind the instruction and amusement which are given by music, can we suppose that the poets are to be allowed to teach in the dance anything which they themselves like, in the way of rhythm, or melody, or words, to the young children of any well-conditioned parents? Is the poet to train his choruses as he pleases, without reference to virtue or vice?

That is surely quite unreasonable, and is not to be thought of.

And yet he may do this in almost any state with the exception of Egypt.

And what are the laws about music and dancing in Egypt?
D: Narrative:
"Then in a city which has good laws, or in future ages is to have them, bearing in mind the instruction and amusement which are given by music, can we suppose that the poets are to be allowed to teach in the dance anything which they themselves like, in the way of rhythm, or melody, or words, to the young children of any well-conditioned parents? Is the poet to train his choruses as he pleases, without reference to virtue or vice?"

"That is surely quite unreasonable, and is not to be thought of."

"And yet he may do this in almost any state with the exception of Egypt."

"And what are the laws about music and dancing in Egypt?"
D: Refashioned:
"In a city with good laws -- or one that aims to have them in the future -- given what we understand about music's power to instruct and entertain, can we allow poets to teach children whatever they like in dance, rhythm, melody, or lyrics, regardless of the parents' character? Should poets train their choruses however they please, with no regard for virtue or vice?"

"That would be completely unreasonable."

"And yet this is permitted in almost every state except Egypt."

"What are Egypt's laws concerning music and dance?"
D: Elegant:
"Should a well-governed city allow poets to teach children whatever rhythms, melodies, and words they choose, without regard for virtue or vice? Surely not. Yet this is permitted everywhere except Egypt."

"What are Egypt's laws on music?"
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that in a well-governed city, poets should not be free to teach children whatever they like without moral oversight. He notes that almost every Greek state fails in this regard, with Egypt being the notable exception.
D: Distilled: A well-governed city must not allow poets unrestricted freedom to shape children's education through music.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The argument for state regulation of poetic content is central to Plato's political philosophy. The reference to Egypt as a model of artistic regulation is historically significant -- Plato admired Egyptian cultural conservatism as a counterexample to Athenian artistic license. Cross-reference: Republic 2.377b-378e on censoring poetry for the guardians' education; Republic 3.398a-b on regulating musical modes; Republic 10.595a-608b on the general critique of imitative poetry. The contrast between Egyptian regulation and Greek freedom sets up the detailed discussion in 2.12-2.13.

D: Original:
You will wonder when I tell you: Long ago they appear to have recognized the very principle of which we are now speaking-that their young citizens must be habituated to forms and strains of virtue. These they fixed, and exhibited the patterns of them in their temples; and no painter or artist is allowed to innovate upon them, or to leave the traditional forms and invent new ones. To this day, no alteration is allowed either in these arts, or in music at all. And you will find that their works of art are painted or moulded in the same forms which they had ten thousand years ago;-this is literally true and no exaggeration-their ancient paintings and sculptures are not a whit better or worse than the work of to-day, but are made with just the same skill.

How extraordinary!
D: Narrative:
"You will wonder when I tell you: Long ago they appear to have recognized the very principle of which we are now speaking -- that their young citizens must be habituated to forms and strains of virtue. These they fixed, and exhibited the patterns of them in their temples; and no painter or artist is allowed to innovate upon them, or to leave the traditional forms and invent new ones. To this day, no alteration is allowed either in these arts, or in music at all. And you will find that their works of art are painted or moulded in the same forms which they had ten thousand years ago; -- this is literally true and no exaggeration -- their ancient paintings and sculptures are not a whit better or worse than the work of to-day, but are made with just the same skill."

"How extraordinary!"
D: Refashioned:
"You will be amazed to hear this: long ago the Egyptians appear to have recognized the very principle we are discussing -- that young citizens must be habituated to artistic forms that embody virtue. They established these forms and displayed the patterns in their temples. No painter or artist is permitted to innovate on them or depart from tradition to invent new forms. To this day, no alteration is allowed in these arts or in music whatsoever. You will find that their works of art are painted and sculpted in the same forms they used ten thousand years ago -- this is literally true, not an exaggeration. Their ancient paintings and sculptures are neither better nor worse than contemporary work, but are made with exactly the same skill."

"How extraordinary!"
D: Elegant:
"The Egyptians long ago grasped our principle: young citizens must be trained in fixed artistic forms embodying virtue. These forms are displayed in temples, and no artist may innovate upon them. Their art has remained identical for ten thousand years -- literally, not metaphorically -- with ancient works showing the same skill as modern ones."

"Extraordinary!"
D: Summary: The Athenian describes Egypt's artistic tradition, where virtuous forms were fixed in temples and no artistic innovation has been permitted for ten thousand years, resulting in art of unchanging quality across millennia.
D: Distilled: Egypt preserved artistic forms embodying virtue for ten thousand years by prohibiting artistic innovation.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. Plato's admiration for Egyptian artistic conservatism is a major theme in his late work. The claim of ten thousand years of unchanging art is exaggerated but reflects genuine Greek perceptions of Egyptian cultural stability. The principle that virtue requires fixed artistic forms stands in tension with Greek culture's celebration of artistic innovation. Cross-reference: Timaeus 21e-23c, where Egyptian priests tell Solon about the antiquity of their civilization; Statesman 299d-e on the dangers of innovation in the arts; Republic 4.424b-c where Socrates warns that 'when modes of music change, the fundamental laws of the state always change with them.' Cleinias's amazement reflects the Greek perspective that values novelty.

D: Original: I should rather say, How statesmanlike, how worthy of a legislator! I know that other things in Egypt are nat so well. But what I am telling you about music is true and deserving of consideration, because showing that a lawgiver may institute melodies which have a natural truth and correctness without any fear of failure. To do this, however, must be the work of God, or of a divine person; in Egypt they have a tradition that their ancient chants which have been preserved for so many ages are the composition of the Goddess Isis. And therefore, as I was saying, if a person can only find in any way the natural melodies, he may confidently embody them in a fixed and legal form. For the love of novelty which arises out of pleasure in the new and weariness of the old, has not strength enough to corrupt the consecrated song and dance, under the plea that they have become antiquated. At any rate, they are far from being corrupted in Egypt.
D: Narrative: "I should rather say, How statesmanlike, how worthy of a legislator! I know that other things in Egypt are not so well. But what I am telling you about music is true and deserving of consideration, because showing that a lawgiver may institute melodies which have a natural truth and correctness without any fear of failure. To do this, however, must be the work of God, or of a divine person; in Egypt they have a tradition that their ancient chants which have been preserved for so many ages are the composition of the Goddess Isis. And therefore, as I was saying, if a person can only find in any way the natural melodies, he may confidently embody them in a fixed and legal form. For the love of novelty which arises out of pleasure in the new and weariness of the old, has not strength enough to corrupt the consecrated song and dance, under the plea that they have become antiquated. At any rate, they are far from being corrupted in Egypt."
D: Refashioned:
"I would call it statesmanlike rather than extraordinary -- entirely worthy of a legislator! I know that Egypt is not perfect in other respects. But what I am telling you about their music is true and deserves serious consideration, because it demonstrates that a lawgiver can establish melodies with natural truth and correctness without any risk of failure. Accomplishing this, however, requires the work of a god or a divinely inspired person. The Egyptians have a tradition that their ancient chants, preserved across so many ages, were composed by the goddess Isis."

"The key point is this: if someone can discover the naturally correct melodies by any means, he can confidently establish them in fixed legal form. The love of novelty -- which springs from pleasure in the new and boredom with the old -- is not powerful enough to corrupt consecrated songs and dances by claiming they have become outdated. At any rate, they remain uncorrupted in Egypt."
D: Elegant: "Not extraordinary -- statesmanlike. A lawgiver can establish naturally correct melodies in permanent legal form. This requires divine guidance, and the Egyptians attribute their ancient chants to the goddess Isis. Once the naturally correct forms are discovered and consecrated, the mere love of novelty cannot corrupt them. Egypt proves this."
D: Summary: The Athenian reframes Egypt's artistic conservatism as wise statesmanship rather than mere curiosity. He argues that naturally correct melodies, once discovered (perhaps through divine inspiration), can be fixed in law, and that the love of novelty is too weak to corrupt what has been consecrated.
D: Distilled: Naturally correct artistic forms, once discovered and consecrated by law, can resist the corrupting pressure of novelty.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The claim that discovering natural melodies requires divine aid connects to Plato's broader theology in the Laws, where the gods are the source of all genuine goods. The attribution to Isis reflects the Greek practice of interpreting foreign deities through their own theological framework. The argument against novelty for its own sake is a recurring theme. Cross-reference: Republic 4.424b-c on musical innovation destabilizing the state; Epinomis 991e-992a on Egyptian and Near Eastern contributions to knowledge; Timaeus 22b on Egypt as preserver of ancient wisdom. The concession that 'other things in Egypt are not so well' shows balanced judgment -- Plato admires specific Egyptian practices without idealizing the whole civilization.

D: Original:
Your arguments seem to prove your point.

May we not confidently say that the true use of music and of choral festivities is as follows: We rejoice when we think that we prosper, and again we think that we prosper when we rejoice?

Exactly.

And when rejoicing in our good fortune, we are unable to be still?

True.

Our young men break forth into dancing and singing, and we who are their elders deem that we are fulfilling our part in life when we look on at them. Having lost our agility, we delight in their sports and merry-making, because we love to think of our former selves; and gladly institute contests for those who are able to awaken in us the memory of our youth.

Very true.
D: Narrative:
"Your arguments seem to prove your point."

"May we not confidently say that the true use of music and of choral festivities is as follows: We rejoice when we think that we prosper, and again we think that we prosper when we rejoice?"

"Exactly."

"And when rejoicing in our good fortune, we are unable to be still?"

"True."

"Our young men break forth into dancing and singing, and we who are their elders deem that we are fulfilling our part in life when we look on at them. Having lost our agility, we delight in their sports and merry-making, because we love to think of our former selves; and gladly institute contests for those who are able to awaken in us the memory of our youth."

"Very true."
D: Refashioned:
"Your arguments seem to prove your point."

"Can we not say with confidence that the true purpose of music and choral festivals is this: we rejoice when we believe we are prospering, and we believe we are prospering when we rejoice?"

"Exactly."

"And when we rejoice in our good fortune, we cannot keep still?"

"True."

"Young people burst into dancing and singing, while we elders feel we are fulfilling our role in life by watching them. Having lost our physical agility, we take delight in their energy and celebration because we enjoy remembering our younger selves. We gladly establish contests for those who can reawaken in us the memory of our youth."

"Very true."
D: Elegant: "The true purpose of music and festivals: we rejoice when we think we prosper, and cannot keep still when joyful. The young express this through dancing and singing. Elders, having lost their agility, fulfill their role by watching and remembering their youth, establishing contests to reawaken those memories."
D: Summary: The Athenian explains the natural psychology behind music and festivals: joy at prosperity produces irrepressible movement in the young, while elders find satisfaction in watching and remembering their own youth through the performances of others.
D: Distilled: Music and festivals arise from the natural human impulse to express joy through movement, with elders finding vicarious pleasure in the performances of the young.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. This passage provides a psychological and social account of why festivals exist: they channel the natural connection between prosperity, joy, and physical expression. The distinction between youthful active participation and elder spectatorship introduces the problem of the third chorus (elders who must perform, not merely watch) that becomes central later in Book 2. Cross-reference: Republic 1.328d-329a where Cephalus discusses the pleasures of old age; Laws 7.790c-791b on the natural restlessness of children; Aristotle, Politics 8.6 (1341a) on why older citizens should be spectators rather than performers.

D: Original:
Is it altogether unmeaning to say, as the common people do about festivals, that he should be adjudged the wisest of men, and the winner of the palm, who gives us the greatest amount of pleasure and mirth? For on such occasions, and when mirth is the order of the day, ought not he to be honoured most, and, as I was saying, bear the palm, who gives most mirth to the greatest number? Now is this a true way of speaking or of acting?

Possibly.
D: Narrative:
"Is it altogether unmeaning to say, as the common people do about festivals, that he should be adjudged the wisest of men, and the winner of the palm, who gives us the greatest amount of pleasure and mirth? For on such occasions, and when mirth is the order of the day, ought not he to be honoured most, and, as I was saying, bear the palm, who gives most mirth to the greatest number? Now is this a true way of speaking or of acting?"

"Possibly."
D: Refashioned:
"Is there any truth in the common saying about festivals -- that the wisest person, the one who deserves the prize, is whoever gives us the most pleasure and entertainment? On festive occasions, when merriment is the whole point, shouldn't the highest honor go to whoever provides the most entertainment to the greatest number? Is this a valid way of thinking and acting?"

"Perhaps."
D: Elegant:
"Is the common view correct that at festivals, the winner should be whoever gives the greatest pleasure to the most people? Should entertainment value be the sole criterion for judging artistic merit?"

"Perhaps."
D: Summary: The Athenian raises the popular view that at festivals, the best performer is simply whoever gives the most pleasure to the most people, and asks whether this pleasure-based criterion is valid.
D: Distilled: The popular view equates artistic excellence at festivals with maximizing audience pleasure.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This sets up the thought experiment that follows in 2.16-2.17, where the pleasure criterion is tested against different audience demographics. Cleinias's hesitant 'Possibly' suggests he senses a trap. The principle of 'most pleasure to the greatest number' anticipates utilitarian aesthetics, which Plato is about to critique. Cross-reference: Gorgias 501d-502d where Socrates challenges the view that rhetoric and music should aim at pleasure; Republic 10.602b on the 'mob' as unqualified aesthetic judges; Hippias Major 298a on pleasure as a criterion of beauty.
