D: Original: But, my dear friend, let us distinguish between different cases, and not be hasty in forming a judgment: One way of considering the question will be to imagine a festival at which there are entertainments of all sorts, including gymnastic, musical, and equestrian contests: the citizens are assembled; prizes are offered, and proclamation is made that any one who likes may enter the lists, and that he is to bear the palm who gives the most pleasure to the spectators-there is to be no regulation about the manner how; but he who is most successful in giving pleasure is to be crowned victor, and deemed to be the pleasantest of the candidates: What is likely to be the result of such a proclamation?
D: Narrative: "But, my dear friend, let us distinguish between different cases, and not be hasty in forming a judgment: One way of considering the question will be to imagine a festival at which there are entertainments of all sorts, including gymnastic, musical, and equestrian contests: the citizens are assembled; prizes are offered, and proclamation is made that any one who likes may enter the lists, and that he is to bear the palm who gives the most pleasure to the spectators -- there is to be no regulation about the manner how; but he who is most successful in giving pleasure is to be crowned victor, and deemed to be the pleasantest of the candidates: What is likely to be the result of such a proclamation?"
D: Refashioned: "But, my friend, let us distinguish between different situations and not rush to judgment. Consider this thought experiment: imagine a festival with entertainments of every kind -- gymnastic, musical, and equestrian competitions. All the citizens are gathered, prizes are offered, and a proclamation is made that anyone may compete. The sole criterion is this: whoever gives the most pleasure to the spectators wins. There are no rules about the type of performance -- the person who most successfully pleases the crowd will be crowned victor and declared the most pleasing of all competitors. What do you think would happen?"
D: Elegant: "Let us not judge hastily. Imagine a festival with every kind of entertainment -- athletic, musical, equestrian -- where the only rule is that whoever gives the most pleasure to the spectators wins. No other criterion. What would result?"
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes a thought experiment: a festival where the sole judging criterion is audience pleasure, with no restrictions on the type of performance. He asks what the consequences would be.
D: Distilled: A thought experiment about judging art purely by audience pleasure reveals the absurdity of the pleasure criterion.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The thought experiment is a classic Platonic method -- creating a hypothetical scenario to test a principle. The festival imagined here combines multiple art forms under a single pleasure criterion, which will be shown to produce absurd results based on audience demographics. Cross-reference: Republic 10.605a-606d on the diverse and unreliable pleasures of theatrical audiences; Gorgias 502b-d on poets as demagogues of the theatre; Philebus 63d-64a on the need to test pleasure claims against reason.

D: Original:
In what respect?

There would be various exhibitions: one man, like Homer, will exhibit a rhapsody, another a performance on the lute; one will have a tragedy, and another a comedy. Nor would there be anything astonishing in some one imagining that he could gain the prize by exhibiting a puppet-show. Suppose these competitors to meet, and not these only, but innumerable others as well can you tell me who ought to be the victor?

I do not see how any one can answer you, or pretend to know, unless he has heard with his own ears the several competitors; the question is absurd.

Well, then, if neither of you can answer, shall I answer this question which you deem so absurd?

By all means.

If very small children are to determine the question, they will decide for the puppet show.

Of course.

The older children will be advocates of comedy; educated women, and young men, and people in general, will favour tragedy.

Very likely.

And I believe that we old men would have the greatest pleasure in hearing a rhapsodist recite well the Iliad and Odyssey, or one of the Hesiodic poems, and would award the victory to him. But, who would really be the victor?-that is the question.

Yes.

Clearly you and I will have to declare that those whom we old men adjudge victors ought to win; for our ways are far and away better than any which at present exist anywhere in the world.

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"In what respect?"

"There would be various exhibitions: one man, like Homer, will exhibit a rhapsody, another a performance on the lute; one will have a tragedy, and another a comedy. Nor would there be anything astonishing in some one imagining that he could gain the prize by exhibiting a puppet-show. Suppose these competitors to meet, and not these only, but innumerable others as well -- can you tell me who ought to be the victor?"

"I do not see how any one can answer you, or pretend to know, unless he has heard with his own ears the several competitors; the question is absurd."

"Well, then, if neither of you can answer, shall I answer this question which you deem so absurd?"

"By all means."

"If very small children are to determine the question, they will decide for the puppet show."

"Of course."

"The older children will be advocates of comedy; educated women, and young men, and people in general, will favour tragedy."

"Very likely."

"And I believe that we old men would have the greatest pleasure in hearing a rhapsodist recite well the Iliad and Odyssey, or one of the Hesiodic poems, and would award the victory to him. But, who would really be the victor? -- that is the question."

"Yes."

"Clearly you and I will have to declare that those whom we old men adjudge victors ought to win; for our ways are far and away better than any which at present exist anywhere in the world."

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"There would be all kinds of performances: one person reciting Homeric epic, another playing the lute, one presenting a tragedy, another a comedy. It would not even be surprising if someone thought they could win with a puppet show. Now suppose all these competitors -- and countless others -- are gathered together. Can you tell me who should win?"

"I don't see how anyone could answer that, or claim to know, without actually hearing each competitor. The question seems absurd."

"Well, if neither of you can answer, shall I take on this question you find so absurd?"

"Please do."

"If very small children are the judges, they will choose the puppet show."

"Obviously."

"Older children will prefer comedy. Educated women, young men, and the general public will favor tragedy."

"Very likely."

"And I believe that we old men would take the greatest pleasure in hearing a skilled rhapsodist recite the Iliad, the Odyssey, or the works of Hesiod, and we would award the victory to him. But who would truly deserve to win? That is the real question."

"Yes, it is."

"Clearly you and I must declare that the winners judged by us old men ought to prevail, since our ways are far superior to anything that currently exists anywhere in the world."

"Certainly."
D: Elegant: "Competitors would include epic recitation, lute performance, tragedy, comedy, even puppet shows. Who should win? Small children would choose puppets; older children, comedy; the general public, tragedy; wise old men, Homeric epic. The real question is: who truly deserves victory? Those judged best by the wisest and most experienced -- the old men."
D: Summary: The Athenian demonstrates that different audiences prefer different art forms based on their age and education: children choose puppet shows, older children comedy, the public tragedy, and wise elders Homeric epic. He concludes that the judgment of the wisest and most experienced should prevail.
D: Distilled: Different audiences prefer different art forms based on maturity and education, so only the judgment of the wisest should determine artistic excellence.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. This passage contains Plato's famous hierarchy of audiences and their preferred entertainments -- a sociology of aesthetic taste organized by age and education. The progression from puppet shows to Homer mirrors moral and intellectual development. The Stranger's self-serving conclusion (old men like us should judge) is presented with a touch of humor but makes a serious point about qualified judgment. Cross-reference: Republic 10.602c-d on the 'inferior part of the soul' that enjoys imitation; Gorgias 502b on poets as 'rhetoricians of the theatre'; Aristotle, Poetics 1453a on the audience's role in determining dramatic quality. The reference to Hesiod alongside Homer is notable -- both represent the highest traditional poetry.

D: Original:
Thus far I too should agree with the many, that the excellence of music is to be measured by pleasure. But the pleasure must not be that of chance persons; the fairest music is that which delights the best and best educated, and especially that which delights the one man who is pre-eminent in virtue and education. And therefore the judges must be men of character, for they will require both wisdom and courage; the true judge must not draw his inspiration from the theatre, nor ought he to be unnerved by the clamour of the many and his own incapacity; nor again, knowing the truth, ought he through cowardice and unmanliness carelessly to deliver a lying judgment, with the very same lips which have just appealed to the Gods before he judged. He is sitting not as the disciple of the theatre, but, in his proper place, as their instructor, and he ought to be the enemy of all pandering to the pleasure of the spectators. The ancient and common custom of Hellas, which still prevails in Italy and Sicily, did certainly leave the judgment to the body of spectators, who determined the victor by show of hands. But this custom has been the destruction of the poets; for they are now in the habit of composing with a view to please the bad taste of their judges, and the result is that the spectators instruct themselves;-and also it has been the ruin of the theatre; they ought to be having characters put before them better than their own, and so receiving a higher pleasure, but now by their own act the opposite result follows. What inference is to be drawn from all this? Shall I tell you?

What?
D: Narrative:
"Thus far I too should agree with the many, that the excellence of music is to be measured by pleasure. But the pleasure must not be that of chance persons; the fairest music is that which delights the best and best educated, and especially that which delights the one man who is pre-eminent in virtue and education. And therefore the judges must be men of character, for they will require both wisdom and courage; the true judge must not draw his inspiration from the theatre, nor ought he to be unnerved by the clamour of the many and his own incapacity; nor again, knowing the truth, ought he through cowardice and unmanliness carelessly to deliver a lying judgment, with the very same lips which have just appealed to the Gods before he judged. He is sitting not as the disciple of the theatre, but, in his proper place, as their instructor, and he ought to be the enemy of all pandering to the pleasure of the spectators."

"The ancient and common custom of Hellas, which still prevails in Italy and Sicily, did certainly leave the judgment to the body of spectators, who determined the victor by show of hands. But this custom has been the destruction of the poets; for they are now in the habit of composing with a view to please the bad taste of their judges, and the result is that the spectators instruct themselves; -- and also it has been the ruin of the theatre; they ought to be having characters put before them better than their own, and so receiving a higher pleasure, but now by their own act the opposite result follows. What inference is to be drawn from all this? Shall I tell you?"

"What?"
D: Refashioned:
"To this extent I agree with popular opinion: music's excellence can be measured by pleasure. But not the pleasure of random people. The finest music is that which delights the best and best-educated people, and above all, that which delights the one person who is pre-eminent in virtue and education. Judges must therefore be people of character, requiring both wisdom and courage. A true judge must not take his cues from the theatre audience, nor be intimidated by the crowd's noise and his own self-doubt. Nor should he, knowing the truth, deliver a false verdict through cowardice -- using the very same lips that just invoked the gods before passing judgment. He sits not as the theatre's student but as its teacher, and should be the enemy of all pandering to spectators."

"The ancient Greek custom, still practiced in Italy and Sicily, handed judgment to the audience, who chose the winner by show of hands. But this practice has destroyed the poets: they now compose to please their judges' bad taste, so the spectators end up educating themselves. It has also ruined the theatre itself. Audiences ought to encounter characters better than themselves and receive a higher pleasure from the experience. Instead, through their own doing, the opposite occurs. What conclusion should we draw?"

"Tell me."
D: Elegant:
"Music's excellence is measured by pleasure -- but not any pleasure. The finest music delights the wisest and most virtuous person. Judges need wisdom and courage; they sit as the theatre's teachers, not its students, and must resist pandering to crowds."

"The Greek custom of audience voting destroyed poetry: poets compose for bad taste, spectators educate only themselves, and theatre presents characters worse rather than better than the audience. The result is cultural degradation."
D: Summary: The Athenian concedes that pleasure is relevant to judging music, but only the pleasure of the wisest and most virtuous. He argues that audience-driven judging has corrupted both poets and theatre, since artists pander to popular taste instead of elevating it.
D: Distilled: Music should be judged by the pleasure of the wisest, not the crowd; popular judging corrupts both artists and audiences.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This is Plato's most developed critique of democratic aesthetic judgment, directly addressing the theatrical culture of Athens and the Greek West. The concept of 'theatrocracy' -- rule by theatre audiences -- appears later in Laws 3.701a. The argument that judges should be teachers, not students, of the audience inverts the democratic assumption. The observation that poets compose to please bad taste, creating a feedback loop of degradation, remains remarkably relevant to discussions of popular culture. Cross-reference: Republic 10.605a-606d on the corrupting effects of theatrical pleasure; Gorgias 502b-d on poets as popular rhetoricians; Aristotle, Poetics 1453a on the 'weaker' audience that prefers happy endings.

D: Original: The inference at which we arrive for the third or fourth time is, that education is the constraining and directing of youth towards that right reason, which the law affirms, and which the experience of the eldest and best has agreed to be truly right. In order, then, that the soul of the child may not be habituated to feel joy and sorrow in a manner at variance with the law, and those who obey the law, but may rather follow the law and rejoice and sorrow at the same things as the aged-in order, I say, to produce this effect, chants appear to have been invented, which really enchant, and are designed to implant that harmony of which we speak. And, because the mind of the child is incapable of enduring serious training, they are called plays and songs, and are performed in play; just as when men are sick and ailing in their bodies, their attendants give them wholesome diet in pleasant meats and drinks, but unwholesome diet in disagreeable things, in order that they may learn, as they ought, to like the one, and to dislike the other. And similarly the true legislator will persuade, and, if he cannot persuade, will compel the poet to express, as he ought, by fair and noble words, in his rhythms, the figures, and in his melodies, the music of temperate and brave and in every way good men.
D: Narrative: "The inference at which we arrive for the third or fourth time is, that education is the constraining and directing of youth towards that right reason, which the law affirms, and which the experience of the eldest and best has agreed to be truly right. In order, then, that the soul of the child may not be habituated to feel joy and sorrow in a manner at variance with the law, and those who obey the law, but may rather follow the law and rejoice and sorrow at the same things as the aged -- in order, I say, to produce this effect, chants appear to have been invented, which really enchant, and are designed to implant that harmony of which we speak. And, because the mind of the child is incapable of enduring serious training, they are called plays and songs, and are performed in play; just as when men are sick and ailing in their bodies, their attendants give them wholesome diet in pleasant meats and drinks, but unwholesome diet in disagreeable things, in order that they may learn, as they ought, to like the one, and to dislike the other. And similarly the true legislator will persuade, and, if he cannot persuade, will compel the poet to express, as he ought, by fair and noble words, in his rhythms, the figures, and in his melodies, the music of temperate and brave and in every way good men."
D: Refashioned:
"We arrive, for the third or fourth time, at the same conclusion: education is the constraining and directing of youth toward right reason -- the standard affirmed by law and confirmed by the experience of the wisest elders. The child's soul must not be habituated to feel joy and sorrow in ways that conflict with the law and its adherents. Instead, children should follow the law's guidance and rejoice and grieve at the same things as the aged."

"To achieve this effect, chants were invented that truly enchant -- designed to implant the harmony we have been describing. Because children's minds cannot endure rigorous training, these are called plays and songs and are performed as entertainment. The principle is the same as in medicine: when people are sick, their caregivers present wholesome food in pleasant forms and unwholesome food in unpleasant forms, so that patients learn to prefer what is healthy. Similarly, the true legislator will persuade -- and if persuasion fails, compel -- the poet to express through noble words, proper rhythms, fitting gestures, and beautiful melodies the character of temperate, courageous, and thoroughly good people."
D: Elegant: "Education directs youth toward right reason, confirmed by law and the experience of the wisest. To align children's joys and sorrows with the law, enchanting chants were invented -- presented as play because children cannot endure serious training, just as medicine is made palatable for the sick. The legislator will persuade or compel poets to embody the character of good people in their words, rhythms, and melodies."
D: Summary: The Athenian restates that education directs youth toward right reason through chants disguised as play, just as medicine is made palatable for the sick. The legislator must ensure poets express the character of virtuous people in their art, by persuasion or compulsion.
D: Distilled: Education uses the enchantment of music to implant right reason in children, and the legislator must ensure poets serve this purpose.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The wordplay on 'chants' (epodai) that 'enchant' (epaidein) is deliberate -- songs literally cast spells on the soul. The medical analogy (wholesome diet in pleasant form) is characteristic of Plato's view of education as therapy for the soul. The escalation from persuasion to compulsion regarding poets echoes Republic 3.398a-b, where poets who refuse to conform are garlanded and sent away. Cross-reference: Republic 2.377b-378e on compelling poets; Laws 7.811c-812a on legislating poetic content; Protagoras 325d-326a on how the whole city educates children through music; Phaedrus 261a on rhetoric as 'psychagogia' (soul-leading).

D: Original: But do you really imagine, Stranger, that this is the way in which poets generally compose in States at the present day? As far as I can observe, except among us and among the Lacedaemonians, there are no regulations like those of which you speak; in other places novelties are always being introduced in dancing and in music, generally not under the authority of any law, but at the instigation of lawless pleasures; and these pleasures are so far from being the same, as you describe the Egyptian to be, or having the same principles, that they are never the same.
D: Narrative: "But do you really imagine, Stranger, that this is the way in which poets generally compose in States at the present day? As far as I can observe, except among us and among the Lacedaemonians, there are no regulations like those of which you speak; in other places novelties are always being introduced in dancing and in music, generally not under the authority of any law, but at the instigation of lawless pleasures; and these pleasures are so far from being the same, as you describe the Egyptian to be, or having the same principles, that they are never the same."
D: Refashioned: "But do you really believe, Stranger, that this is how poets actually compose in cities today? As far as I can tell, except among us Cretans and the Spartans, there are no regulations of the kind you describe. Everywhere else, novelties are constantly being introduced in dance and music -- not under any legal authority, but driven by lawless pleasures. And these pleasures are so far from being stable or principled, like the Egyptian tradition you described, that they are never the same from one moment to the next."
D: Elegant: "Do poets actually compose this way anywhere today? Except in Crete and Sparta, there are no such regulations. Everywhere else, musical innovation is driven by lawless pleasures that are constantly changing -- nothing like the stable Egyptian tradition you described."
D: Summary: Cleinias challenges the Athenian's idealism by pointing out that except in Crete and Sparta, no states regulate poetry as described. Elsewhere, music is driven by ever-changing, lawless pleasures rather than stable principles.
D: Distilled: Cleinias observes that outside Crete and Sparta, musical composition is driven by unregulated, constantly shifting pleasures.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias. This is one of Cleinias's most substantive contributions to the dialogue, offering a realistic assessment of Greek musical culture. His pride in Cretan and Spartan conservatism reflects the historical reality that these Dorian states maintained more traditional musical practices than Athens and Ionia. The phrase 'lawless pleasures' (anomoi hedonai) connects musical disorder to political disorder -- a key theme of the Laws. Cross-reference: Republic 4.424b-c on the political danger of musical innovation; Laws 3.700a-701b where the Stranger describes the progressive corruption of Athenian musical standards; Aristophanes, Frogs 1491-1499 for a comic treatment of the same cultural anxiety about musical novelty.

D: Original:
Most true, Cleinias; and I daresay that I may have expressed myself obscurely, and so led you to imagine that I was speaking of some really existing state of things, whereas I was only saying what regulations I would like to have about music; and hence there occurred a misapprehension on your part. For when evils are far gone and irremediable, the task of censuring them is never pleasant, although at times necessary. But as we do not really differ, will you let me ask you whether you consider such institutions to be more prevalent among the Cretans and Lacedaemonians than among the other Hellenes?

Certainly they are.

And if they were extended to the other Hellenes, would it be an improvement on the present state of things?

A very great improvement, if the customs which prevail among them were such as prevail among us and the Lacedaemonians, and such as you were just now saying ought to prevail.
D: Narrative:
"Most true, Cleinias; and I daresay that I may have expressed myself obscurely, and so led you to imagine that I was speaking of some really existing state of things, whereas I was only saying what regulations I would like to have about music; and hence there occurred a misapprehension on your part. For when evils are far gone and irremediable, the task of censuring them is never pleasant, although at times necessary. But as we do not really differ, will you let me ask you whether you consider such institutions to be more prevalent among the Cretans and Lacedaemonians than among the other Hellenes?"

"Certainly they are."

"And if they were extended to the other Hellenes, would it be an improvement on the present state of things?"

"A very great improvement, if the customs which prevail among them were such as prevail among us and the Lacedaemonians, and such as you were just now saying ought to prevail."
D: Refashioned:
"Quite right, Cleinias. I may have expressed myself unclearly, leading you to think I was describing how things actually are, when I was only describing what I would like the regulations to be. Hence the misunderstanding. When evils have gone too far and become incurable, criticizing them is never pleasant -- though sometimes necessary. But since we do not fundamentally disagree, let me ask: do you consider such musical institutions to be more common among the Cretans and Spartans than among other Greeks?"

"They certainly are."

"And if these practices were extended to the rest of Greece, would that be an improvement?"

"A very great improvement, if the customs adopted were like those of our people and the Spartans, and like those you were just now recommending."
D: Elegant:
"I was describing ideal regulations, not existing reality -- I may have been unclear. Since we agree in principle, are such musical institutions more prevalent among Cretans and Spartans than other Greeks?"

"Certainly."

"Would extending them to all Greeks be an improvement?"

"A great improvement, if they followed Cretan and Spartan customs as you recommend."
D: Summary: The Athenian clarifies he was describing ideal regulations, not current practice, and both agree that extending Cretan and Spartan musical institutions to all of Greece would be a great improvement.
D: Distilled: The Athenian's proposals are ideals, not descriptions of current practice, but Cretan and Spartan musical conservatism comes closest to the ideal.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The Stranger's clarification -- that he speaks prescriptively, not descriptively -- is an important methodological moment. The Laws oscillates between describing existing institutions (Cretan, Spartan, Egyptian) and proposing ideal ones. Cleinias's qualified agreement ('if the customs were such as prevail among us') shows Cretan civic pride but also receptivity to reform. Cross-reference: Republic 5.472c-473b on the relationship between ideal theory and practical reality; Laws 1.625a-626b on the initial discussion of Cretan and Spartan laws; Laws 3.702a-d on the founding of the new colony that will implement these ideals.

D: Original: Let us see whether we understand one another:-Are not the principles of education and music which prevail among you as follows: you compel your poets to say that the good man, if he be temperate and just, is fortunate and happy; and this whether he be great and strong or small and weak, and whether he be rich or poor; and, on the other hand, if he have a wealth passing that of Cinyras or Midas, and be unjust, he is wretched and lives in misery? As the poet says, and with truth: I sing not, I care not about him who accomplishes all noble things, not having justice; let him who "draws near and stretches out his hand against his enemies be a just man." But if he be unjust, I would not have him "look calmly upon bloody death," nor "surpass in swiftness the Thracian Boreas"; and let no other thing that is called good ever be his. For the goods of which the many speak are not really good: first in the catalogue is placed health, beauty next, wealth third; and then innumerable others, as for example to have a keen eye or a quick ear, and in general to have all the senses perfect; or, again, to be a tyrant and do as you like; and the final consummation of happiness is to have acquired all these things, and when you have acquired them to become at once immortal.
D: Narrative: "Let us see whether we understand one another: -- Are not the principles of education and music which prevail among you as follows: you compel your poets to say that the good man, if he be temperate and just, is fortunate and happy; and this whether he be great and strong or small and weak, and whether he be rich or poor; and, on the other hand, if he have a wealth passing that of Cinyras or Midas, and be unjust, he is wretched and lives in misery? As the poet says, and with truth: 'I sing not, I care not about him who accomplishes all noble things, not having justice; let him who draws near and stretches out his hand against his enemies be a just man.' But if he be unjust, I would not have him 'look calmly upon bloody death,' nor 'surpass in swiftness the Thracian Boreas'; and let no other thing that is called good ever be his. For the goods of which the many speak are not really good: first in the catalogue is placed health, beauty next, wealth third; and then innumerable others, as for example to have a keen eye or a quick ear, and in general to have all the senses perfect; or, again, to be a tyrant and do as you like; and the final consummation of happiness is to have acquired all these things, and when you have acquired them to become at once immortal."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us make sure we understand each other. Are not the principles of education and music among you as follows: you require your poets to teach that the good person -- if temperate and just -- is fortunate and happy, whether great or small, strong or weak, rich or poor? And conversely, that someone wealthier than Cinyras or Midas is wretched and miserable if unjust?"

"As the poet rightly says: 'I do not sing of, I do not care about the man who accomplishes noble deeds without justice. Let the warrior who advances against his enemies be a just man.' But if he is unjust, I would not have him face death calmly, nor outrun the Thracian north wind; let nothing commonly called good ever be his."

"For the goods the majority prizes are not truly good. Their catalogue begins with health, then beauty, then wealth, followed by countless others -- sharp eyesight, keen hearing, perfect senses generally, tyrannical power to do whatever one wishes. And the ultimate consummation of happiness, in this popular view, would be to acquire all these things and then become immortal."
D: Elegant: "Your poets must teach that the just person is happy regardless of circumstances, and the unjust person is miserable regardless of wealth. The popular catalogue of goods -- health, beauty, wealth, sharp senses, tyrannical power, even immortality -- these are not truly good. Only justice determines whether any possession is a blessing or a curse."
D: Summary: The Athenian outlines the poetic teaching required in Crete and Sparta: that justice alone determines happiness, regardless of external goods. He catalogs the popular but false goods -- health, beauty, wealth, power, and immortality -- arguing that none of these constitute true happiness without justice.
D: Distilled: Poets must teach that only justice determines happiness, and that the popular goods of health, wealth, and power are worthless without it.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The references to Cinyras (legendary wealthy king of Cyprus) and Midas (whose touch turned things to gold) exemplify proverbial wealth. The poetic quotations are from Tyrtaeus, the Spartan martial poet -- fitting since the Stranger is addressing Dorian interlocutors. The catalogue of false goods (health, beauty, wealth, senses, tyranny, immortality) systematically dismantles the conventional Greek hierarchy of values. Cross-reference: Republic 1.352d-354a on justice and happiness; Gorgias 470d-471d on the tyrant Archelaus and whether the unjust can be happy; Euthydemus 278e-282d on the hierarchy of goods; Laws 1.631b-d where the same hierarchy is first introduced. The final ironic touch -- immortality as the consummation -- shows the absurdity of the popular view.

D: Original: But you and I say, that while to the just and holy all these things are the best of possessions, to the unjust they are all, including even health, the greatest of evils. For in truth, to have sight, and hearing, and the use of the senses, or to live at all without justice and virtue, even though a man be rich in all the so-called goods of fortune, is the greatest of evils, if life be immortal; but not so great, if the bad man lives only a very short time. These are the truths which, if I am not mistaken, you will persuade or compel your poets to utter with suitable accompaniments of harmony and rhythm, and in these they must train up your youth. Am I not right? For I plainly declare that evils as they are termed are goods to the unjust, and only evils to the just, and that goods are truly good to the good, but evil to the evil. Let me ask again, Are you and I agreed about this?
D: Narrative: "But you and I say, that while to the just and holy all these things are the best of possessions, to the unjust they are all, including even health, the greatest of evils. For in truth, to have sight, and hearing, and the use of the senses, or to live at all without justice and virtue, even though a man be rich in all the so-called goods of fortune, is the greatest of evils, if life be immortal; but not so great, if the bad man lives only a very short time. These are the truths which, if I am not mistaken, you will persuade or compel your poets to utter with suitable accompaniments of harmony and rhythm, and in these they must train up your youth. Am I not right? For I plainly declare that evils as they are termed are goods to the unjust, and only evils to the just, and that goods are truly good to the good, but evil to the evil. Let me ask again, Are you and I agreed about this?"
D: Refashioned:
"But you and I maintain that while to the just and holy person all these things are the greatest blessings, to the unjust person they are all -- including even health -- the greatest evils. In truth, to have sight, hearing, and the use of all the senses, or indeed to live at all without justice and virtue, even if one possesses every so-called good of fortune, is the greatest of evils -- especially if life were to last forever. The evil is somewhat less if the bad person lives only a short time."

"These are the truths that, if I am not mistaken, you will persuade or compel your poets to express with suitable accompaniments of harmony and rhythm. These are the principles in which they must train your youth. Am I right? For I declare plainly: what people call evils are actually goods to the unjust, and only evils to the just; and what people call goods are truly good only to the good, but evil to the evil. Are we agreed on this?"
D: Elegant: "To the just, all possessions are blessings; to the unjust, even health is the greatest evil. To live without justice and virtue, possessing every worldly good, is the worst fate imaginable -- especially if life were eternal. Poets must teach this through harmony and rhythm. The paradox is clear: so-called goods are truly good only for the good, and evil for the evil."
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that every conventional good -- including health and the senses -- becomes an evil when possessed by an unjust person, especially if that person were immortal. Poets must be required to teach this paradox: goods are only truly good for the good.
D: Distilled: Every worldly good becomes an evil in the hands of the unjust, and poets must teach this truth to the young.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The striking claim that health is evil for the unjust person inverts conventional values entirely. The thought experiment about immortality intensifies the argument: an immortal unjust person would suffer infinite evil from infinite life. The paradox that 'goods are good to the good but evil to the evil' is one of Plato's most distinctive ethical claims. Cross-reference: Republic 1.331c-332a on whether returning weapons to a madman is just; Gorgias 477a-479e on punishment as good for the unjust soul; Euthydemus 281d-e on wisdom making goods truly beneficial; Laws 5.743e-744a on the danger of excessive wealth. The formula 'persuade or compel' regarding poets recurs throughout the Laws.

D: Original:
I think that we partly agree and partly do not.

When a man has health and wealth and a tyranny which lasts, and when he is preeminent in strength and courage, and has the gift of immortality, and none of the so-called evils which counter-balance these goods, but only the injustice and insolence of his own nature-of such an one you are, I suspect, unwilling to believe that he is miserable rather than happy.

That is quite true.

Once more: Suppose that he be valiant and strong, and handsome and rich, and does throughout his whole life whatever he likes, still, if he be unrighteous and insolent, would not both of you agree that he will of necessity live basely? You will surely grant so much?

Certainly.

And an evil life too?

I am not equally disposed to grant that.

Will he not live painfully and to his own disadvantage?

How can I possibly say so?
D: Narrative:
"I think that we partly agree and partly do not."

"When a man has health and wealth and a tyranny which lasts, and when he is preeminent in strength and courage, and has the gift of immortality, and none of the so-called evils which counter-balance these goods, but only the injustice and insolence of his own nature -- of such an one you are, I suspect, unwilling to believe that he is miserable rather than happy."

"That is quite true."

"Once more: Suppose that he be valiant and strong, and handsome and rich, and does throughout his whole life whatever he likes, still, if he be unrighteous and insolent, would not both of you agree that he will of necessity live basely? You will surely grant so much?"

"Certainly."

"And an evil life too?"

"I am not equally disposed to grant that."

"Will he not live painfully and to his own disadvantage?"

"How can I possibly say so?"
D: Refashioned:
"I think we partly agree and partly do not."

"Consider a man who has health, wealth, lasting political power, outstanding strength and courage, even the gift of immortality, and none of the so-called evils to offset these advantages -- only the injustice and arrogance of his own nature. You are reluctant, I suspect, to admit that such a person is miserable rather than happy."

"That is true -- I am reluctant."

"Then consider once more: suppose a man is brave, strong, handsome, and wealthy, and does whatever he pleases his entire life, yet he is unrighteous and arrogant. Would you both at least agree that he must necessarily live a base life?"

"Certainly."

"And an evil life as well?"

"I am not as ready to concede that."

"Will he not at least live painfully and to his own disadvantage?"

"How could I possibly agree to that?"
D: Elegant:
"Imagine a man with health, wealth, power, courage, and immortality, but who is deeply unjust. You resist calling him miserable. But surely he lives basely?"

"Yes."

"And lives an evil life?"

"That I'm less sure of."

"A painful and disadvantageous life?"

"I cannot agree to that."
D: Summary: Cleinias resists the Athenian's argument that an all-powerful but unjust person is miserable. He concedes such a person lives basely but refuses to admit the unjust life is evil, painful, or disadvantageous -- revealing deep disagreement about whether justice and happiness are truly identical.
D: Distilled: Cleinias concedes the unjust powerful person lives basely but resists the stronger claim that such a life is painful and disadvantageous.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. This is a crucial moment of genuine philosophical disagreement in the Laws -- Cleinias does not simply agree with the Stranger. The scenario of the powerful, immortal, unjust person is the ultimate test case for the identity of justice and happiness. Cleinias's graduated resistance (accepts 'base,' hesitates on 'evil,' rejects 'painful') shows the common-sense intuition that the powerful unjust person is envied rather than pitied. Cross-reference: Gorgias 471a-472d where Polus refuses to admit the tyrant Archelaus is miserable; Republic 1.343b-344c where Thrasymachus argues that injustice is profitable; Republic 2.360d-362c on the Ring of Gyges thought experiment. The dialogue here is more honest than in the Republic, where Glaucon and Adeimantus immediately concede the point.

D: Original: How! Then may Heaven make us to be of one mind, for now we are of two. To me, dear Cleinias, the truth of what I am saying is as plain as the fact that Crete is an island. And, if I were a lawgiver, I would try to make the poets and all the citizens speak in this strain, and I would inflict the heaviest penalties on any one in all the land who should dare to say that there are bad men who lead pleasant lives, or that the profitable and gainful is one thing, and the just another; and there are many other matters about which I should make my citizens speak in a manner different from the Cretans and Lacedaemonians of this age, and I may say, indeed, from the world in general. For tell me, my good friends, by Zeus and Apollo tell me, if I were to ask these same Gods who were your legislators-Is not the most just life also the pleasantest? or are there two lives, one of which is the justest and the other the pleasantest?-and they were to reply that there are two; and thereupon I proceeded to ask, (that would be the right way of pursuing the enquiry), Which are the happier-those who lead the justest, or those who lead the pleasantest life? and they replied, Those who lead the pleasantest-that would be a very strange answer, which I should not like to put into the mouth of the Gods.
D: Narrative:
"How! Then may Heaven make us to be of one mind, for now we are of two. To me, dear Cleinias, the truth of what I am saying is as plain as the fact that Crete is an island. And, if I were a lawgiver, I would try to make the poets and all the citizens speak in this strain, and I would inflict the heaviest penalties on any one in all the land who should dare to say that there are bad men who lead pleasant lives, or that the profitable and gainful is one thing, and the just another; and there are many other matters about which I should make my citizens speak in a manner different from the Cretans and Lacedaemonians of this age, and I may say, indeed, from the world in general."

"For tell me, my good friends, by Zeus and Apollo tell me, if I were to ask these same Gods who were your legislators -- Is not the most just life also the pleasantest? or are there two lives, one of which is the justest and the other the pleasantest? -- and they were to reply that there are two; and thereupon I proceeded to ask, (that would be the right way of pursuing the enquiry), Which are the happier -- those who lead the justest, or those who lead the pleasantest life? and they replied, Those who lead the pleasantest -- that would be a very strange answer, which I should not like to put into the mouth of the Gods."
D: Refashioned:
"Well then! May heaven bring us into agreement, for at present we are divided. To me, dear Cleinias, the truth of what I am saying is as obvious as the fact that Crete is an island. If I were a lawgiver, I would make both the poets and all citizens speak accordingly, and I would impose the harshest penalties on anyone who dared claim that bad people lead pleasant lives, or that what is profitable differs from what is just. There are many matters on which I would make my citizens think differently from the Cretans and Spartans of today -- indeed, from the world in general."

"Consider this, my friends: by Zeus and Apollo, suppose I asked the very gods who were your lawmakers -- is the most just life also the most pleasant, or are these two different lives? If they answered that there are two separate lives, and I then asked which people are happier -- those who lead the most just life or those who lead the most pleasant -- and they replied 'those who lead the most pleasant,' that would be an extraordinary answer. I would not want to attribute such a view to the gods."
D: Elegant: "May heaven unite us, for we disagree. To me this truth is as plain as Crete being an island. As a lawgiver, I would penalize anyone who claims bad people live pleasantly or that profit and justice differ. If the gods themselves said the most pleasant life and the most just life were different, and that the pleasant were happier -- that would be an answer unworthy of gods."
D: Summary: The Athenian declares his conviction as obvious as geography: the just life is the pleasant life. As a lawgiver, he would penalize claims to the contrary. He argues that even the gods could not coherently say the most pleasant life differs from the most just.
D: Distilled: The identity of the just life and the pleasant life is so fundamental that even the gods could not coherently deny it.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The comparison to 'Crete is an island' is a vivid expression of subjective certainty. The invocation of Zeus and Apollo as the traditional lawgivers of Crete and Sparta is not merely rhetorical -- it connects the argument to the dialogue's theological foundations (Laws 1.624a-b). The hypothetical question to the gods is a distinctive argumentative strategy: if even gods cannot coherently separate justice from happiness, no legislator should. Cross-reference: Republic 2.357a-358d where Glaucon challenges Socrates to prove justice is intrinsically good; Gorgias 507c where Socrates concludes the just person is happy; Laws 5.742d-743b on penalizing false doctrines about goods.

D: Original:
The words will come with more propriety from the lips of fathers and legislators, and therefore I will repeat my former questions to one of them, and suppose him to say again that he who leads the pleasantest life is the happiest. And to that I rejoin:-O my father, did you not wish me to live as happily as possible? And yet you also never ceased telling me that I should live as justly as possible. Now, here the giver of the rule, whether he be legislator or father, will be in a dilemma, and will in vain endeavour to be consistent with himself. But if he were to declare that the justest life is also the happiest, every one hearing him would enquire, if I am not mistaken, what is that good and noble principle in life which the law approves, and which is superior to pleasure. For what good can the just man have which is separated from pleasure? Shall we say that glory and fame, coming from Gods and men, though good and noble, are nevertheless unpleasant, and infamy pleasant? Certainly not, sweet legislator. Or shall we say that the not-doing of wrong and there being no wrong done is good and honourable, although there is no pleasure in it, and that the doing wrong is pleasant, but evil and base?

Impossible.
D: Narrative:
"The words will come with more propriety from the lips of fathers and legislators, and therefore I will repeat my former questions to one of them, and suppose him to say again that he who leads the pleasantest life is the happiest. And to that I rejoin: -- O my father, did you not wish me to live as happily as possible? And yet you also never ceased telling me that I should live as justly as possible. Now, here the giver of the rule, whether he be legislator or father, will be in a dilemma, and will in vain endeavour to be consistent with himself."

"But if he were to declare that the justest life is also the happiest, every one hearing him would enquire, if I am not mistaken, what is that good and noble principle in life which the law approves, and which is superior to pleasure. For what good can the just man have which is separated from pleasure? Shall we say that glory and fame, coming from Gods and men, though good and noble, are nevertheless unpleasant, and infamy pleasant? Certainly not, sweet legislator. Or shall we say that the not-doing of wrong and there being no wrong done is good and honourable, although there is no pleasure in it, and that the doing wrong is pleasant, but evil and base?"

"Impossible."
D: Refashioned:
"These words are better suited to fathers and legislators, so let me pose my questions to one of them. Suppose a father tells his child that the pleasantest life is the happiest. The child would naturally reply: 'Father, didn't you want me to live as happily as possible? Yet you always told me to live as justly as possible.' The father -- or legislator -- would find himself in a dilemma, unable to be consistent."

"But suppose instead he declares that the most just life is also the happiest. Everyone would then ask: what is the good and noble principle the law approves that is supposedly superior to pleasure? What good does the just person possess apart from pleasure? Should we say that glory from gods and humans, though good and noble, is actually unpleasant, while infamy is pleasant? Certainly not. Should we say that refraining from wrongdoing is good and honorable but brings no pleasure, while committing wrong is pleasant but evil?"

"Impossible."
D: Elegant: "A father who separates justice from pleasure faces a dilemma: he tells his child to be happy and to be just, but these pull in different directions. A father who identifies them faces a different challenge: what good does the law approve that surpasses pleasure? Is glory unpleasant? Is innocence joyless? Is wrongdoing pleasant? None of these can be maintained."
D: Summary: The Athenian shows that separating justice from pleasure creates an impossible dilemma for fathers and legislators: they cannot consistently urge both happiness and justice. But identifying them raises the question of what good justice offers beyond pleasure -- which leads to the conclusion that justice must itself be pleasant.
D: Distilled: Any father or legislator who separates justice from pleasure will be unable to give consistent advice about how to live well.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The father-child dialogue is a rhetorical device that personalizes the abstract philosophical argument. The dilemma is genuinely powerful: if justice and pleasure diverge, moral education collapses into contradiction. The series of rhetorical questions (is glory unpleasant? is innocence joyless?) systematically eliminates ways to separate justice from pleasure. Cross-reference: Republic 2.362e-367e where Adeimantus describes how fathers give contradictory advice about justice; Protagoras 351b-360e on the identity of the good and the pleasant; Philebus 20d-22e on whether pleasure or wisdom constitutes the good life.

D: Original: The view which identifies the pleasant and the pleasant and the just and the good and the noble has an excellent moral and religious tendency. And the opposite view is most at variance with the designs of the legislator, and is, in his opinion, infamous; for no one, if he can help, will be persuaded to do that which gives him more pain than pleasure. But as distant prospects are apt to make us dizzy, especially in childhood, the legislator will try to purge away the darkness and exhibit the truth; he will persuade the citizens, in some way or other, by customs and praises and words, that just and unjust are shadows only, and that injustice, which seems opposed to justice, when contemplated by the unjust and evil man appears pleasant and the just most unpleasant; but that from the just man's point of view, the very opposite is the appearance of both of them.
D: Narrative: "The view which identifies the pleasant and the just and the good and the noble has an excellent moral and religious tendency. And the opposite view is most at variance with the designs of the legislator, and is, in his opinion, infamous; for no one, if he can help, will be persuaded to do that which gives him more pain than pleasure. But as distant prospects are apt to make us dizzy, especially in childhood, the legislator will try to purge away the darkness and exhibit the truth; he will persuade the citizens, in some way or other, by customs and praises and words, that just and unjust are shadows only, and that injustice, which seems opposed to justice, when contemplated by the unjust and evil man appears pleasant and the just most unpleasant; but that from the just man's point of view, the very opposite is the appearance of both of them."
D: Refashioned:
"The view that identifies the pleasant with the just, the good, and the noble has excellent moral and religious consequences. The opposite view -- separating pleasure from justice -- directly opposes the legislator's purpose and is, in his judgment, disgraceful. No one will willingly do what brings more pain than pleasure."

"But distant outcomes tend to distort our vision, especially in childhood. So the legislator will try to dispel the darkness and reveal the truth. Through customs, praise, and teaching, he will persuade citizens that our impressions of justice and injustice are mere shadows: to the unjust person, injustice appears pleasant and justice most unpleasant, but from the just person's perspective, exactly the opposite is true."
D: Elegant: "Identifying the pleasant with the just serves both morality and religion. The opposite view undermines legislation, since no one willingly chooses pain over pleasure. But perspective distorts judgment, especially in youth. The legislator must reveal that our views of justice and injustice are shadows: to the unjust, injustice seems pleasant; to the just, the opposite is true."
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that identifying pleasure with justice is essential for legislation, since no one willingly chooses pain. The legislator must show that our judgments about justice are like shadows -- the unjust person perceives injustice as pleasant only because of a distorted perspective.
D: Distilled: The legislator must reveal that the apparent pleasantness of injustice is a shadow cast by moral distortion, not a true perception.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The imagery of shadows and distorted perspective directly evokes the Cave allegory of Republic 7.514a-517a, though here applied to moral rather than metaphysical perception. The claim that 'just and unjust are shadows only' means our unreflective impressions of them are shadowy, not that justice itself is unreal. The legislator's role as one who 'purges darkness' parallels the philosopher's role in the Republic. Cross-reference: Republic 7.514a-517a (Cave allegory); Republic 9.583b-585a on the illusory pleasures of the unjust; Protagoras 356c-357b on the art of measurement that overcomes the distortion of distance; Philebus 41e-42c on false pleasures.

D: Original:
True.

And which may be supposed to be the truer judgment-that of the inferior or of the better soul?

Surely, that of the better soul.

Then the unjust life must not only be more base and depraved, but also more unpleasant than the just and holy life?

That seems to be implied in the present argument.

And even supposing this were otherwise, and not as the argument has proven, still the lawgiver, who is worth anything, if he ever ventures to tell a lie to the young for their good, could not invent a more useful lie than this, or one which will have a better effect in making them do what is right, not on compulsion but voluntarily.

Truth, Stranger, is a noble thing and a lasting, but a thing of which men are hard to be persuaded.

And yet the story of the Sidonian Cadmus, which is so improbable, has been readily believed, and also innumerable other tales.

What is that story?
D: Narrative:
"True."

"And which may be supposed to be the truer judgment -- that of the inferior or of the better soul?"

"Surely, that of the better soul."

"Then the unjust life must not only be more base and depraved, but also more unpleasant than the just and holy life?"

"That seems to be implied in the present argument."

"And even supposing this were otherwise, and not as the argument has proven, still the lawgiver, who is worth anything, if he ever ventures to tell a lie to the young for their good, could not invent a more useful lie than this, or one which will have a better effect in making them do what is right, not on compulsion but voluntarily."

"Truth, Stranger, is a noble thing and a lasting, but a thing of which men are hard to be persuaded."

"And yet the story of the Sidonian Cadmus, which is so improbable, has been readily believed, and also innumerable other tales."

"What is that story?"
D: Refashioned:
"True."

"And whose judgment should we trust more -- that of the inferior soul or the better soul?"

"The better soul's, surely."

"Then the unjust life must be not only more base and depraved, but also more unpleasant than the just and holy life?"

"That does seem to follow from our argument."

"And even if this were not the case -- even if the argument had not proven it -- a legislator worth anything, if he ever dared tell a lie to the young for their benefit, could not invent a more useful lie than this one. No falsehood would be more effective at making people do what is right voluntarily rather than under compulsion."

"Truth, Stranger, is a noble and enduring thing -- but people are very hard to persuade of it."

"And yet the story of the Sidonian Cadmus, which is wildly improbable, has been readily believed, along with countless other tales."

"What story is that?"
D: Elegant:
"Whose judgment is truer -- the inferior soul's or the better soul's? The better soul's. Therefore the unjust life is both more base and more unpleasant than the just life. Even if this weren't true, no legislator could invent a more useful belief for making people do right voluntarily."

"Truth is noble but hard to believe."

"Yet people readily believe the improbable story of Cadmus and his sown teeth."
D: Summary: The argument concludes that since the better soul judges more truly, the unjust life is genuinely more unpleasant. The Athenian adds that even if this were merely a useful fiction, no better one exists for promoting voluntary virtue. Cleinias objects that truth is hard to accept, and the Athenian counters that people readily believe far more improbable myths.
D: Distilled: The better soul judges more truly, so the unjust life truly is more unpleasant; and even as a 'noble lie,' no more useful belief exists for promoting virtue.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The passage raises the profound question of the 'noble lie' (cf. Republic 3.414b-415d) -- the Stranger suggests that even if the identity of justice and happiness were false, it would still be the most useful belief for a legislator to promote. This pragmatic approach to truth is distinctive to the Laws. Cleinias's remark about truth being 'noble but hard to believe' is one of his most memorable lines. The reference to Cadmus, who founded Thebes by sowing dragon's teeth from which armed men sprang, illustrates the power of myth over reason. Cross-reference: Republic 3.414b-415d on the 'noble lie'; Republic 2.382c-d on beneficial falsehoods; Critias 110a on ancient myths as vehicles of truth.

D: Original:
The story of armed men springing up after the sowing of teeth, which the legislator may take as a proof that he can persuade the minds of the young of anything; so that he has only to reflect and find out what belief will be of the greatest public advantage, and then use all his efforts to make the whole community utter one and the same word in their songs and tales and discourses all their life long. But if you do not agree with me, there is no reason why you should not argue on the other side.

I do not see that any argument can fairly be raised by either of us against what you are now saying.
D: Narrative:
"The story of armed men springing up after the sowing of teeth, which the legislator may take as a proof that he can persuade the minds of the young of anything; so that he has only to reflect and find out what belief will be of the greatest public advantage, and then use all his efforts to make the whole community utter one and the same word in their songs and tales and discourses all their life long. But if you do not agree with me, there is no reason why you should not argue on the other side."

"I do not see that any argument can fairly be raised by either of us against what you are now saying."
D: Refashioned:
"The story of armed warriors springing from sown teeth. The legislator can take this as evidence that the minds of the young can be persuaded of anything. He need only determine which belief will bring the greatest public benefit, and then devote all his efforts to making the entire community express one and the same message in their songs, stories, and conversations throughout their lives. But if you disagree with me, you are free to argue the other side."

"I do not see how either of us could fairly raise an argument against what you are now saying."
D: Elegant:
"If people believe that armed warriors sprang from sown teeth, a legislator can persuade the young of anything. He need only discover the most beneficial belief and ensure the whole community repeats it in songs, stories, and discourse throughout life."

"I cannot argue against that."
D: Summary: The Athenian draws a practical lesson from the Cadmus myth: if people will believe something so improbable, a legislator can surely persuade the young of the truth that justice equals happiness. The entire community should reinforce this message consistently through all forms of expression.
D: Distilled: If people believe the myth of Cadmus, the legislator can persuade them of anything, and should use this power to promote the belief that justice is happiness.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The argument from the Cadmus myth to legislative persuasion is both pragmatic and unsettling -- it acknowledges the manipulability of human belief while directing it toward what the Stranger considers truth. The vision of an entire community 'uttering one and the same word' in all their cultural expressions represents total cultural coordination under legislative authority. Cross-reference: Republic 3.414b-415d on the 'noble lie' of the metals myth; Republic 2.377b-378e on controlling stories told to children; Laws 7.811c-e on selecting poems for civic education. The Stranger's invitation to disagree ('argue on the other side') shows a dialogical openness that softens the authoritarian implications.

D: Original:
The next suggestion which I have to offer is, that all our three choruses shall sing to the young and tender souls of children, reciting in their strains all the noble thoughts of which we have already spoken, or are about to speak; and the sum of them shall be, that the life which is by the Gods deemed to be the happiest is also the best;-we shall affirm this to be a most certain truth; and the minds of our young disciples will be more likely to receive these words of ours than any others which we might address to them.

I assent to what you say.
D: Narrative:
"The next suggestion which I have to offer is, that all our three choruses shall sing to the young and tender souls of children, reciting in their strains all the noble thoughts of which we have already spoken, or are about to speak; and the sum of them shall be, that the life which is by the Gods deemed to be the happiest is also the best; -- we shall affirm this to be a most certain truth; and the minds of our young disciples will be more likely to receive these words of ours than any others which we might address to them."

"I assent to what you say."
D: Refashioned:
"My next proposal is this: all three of our choruses should sing to the young and impressionable souls of children, conveying in their songs all the noble ideas we have discussed or will discuss. The central message shall be that the life the gods consider happiest is also the best. We will declare this to be absolute truth. And the minds of young people will be more receptive to this message than to any other we could give them."

"I agree."
D: Elegant: "All three choruses should sing to children one central truth: the life the gods deem happiest is also the best. Young minds will receive this message more readily than any other."
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes that all three choruses should convey a single core message to children: the life the gods consider happiest is also the most virtuous. He argues young minds are especially receptive to this teaching.
D: Distilled: The three choruses must unite in teaching children that the divinely approved happiest life is also the best and most virtuous life.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The 'three choruses' are formally introduced here and elaborated in 2.31 -- children, young adults, and elders. The theological grounding (what the gods deem happiest) gives the educational message divine authority. The emphasis on children's receptivity connects to the earlier argument (2.2) about education as pre-rational habituation. Cross-reference: Laws 7.811c-812a on selecting appropriate songs for children; Republic 2.377a-b on the importance of the first stories children hear; Laws 2.31 for the detailed structure of the three choruses.

D: Original:
First will enter in their natural order the sacred choir composed of children, which is to sing lustily the heaven-taught lay to the whole city. Next will follow the choir of young men under the age of thirty, who will call upon the God Paean to testify to the truth of their words, and will pray him to be gracious to the youth and to turn their hearts. Thirdly, the choir of elder men, who are from thirty to sixty years of age, will also sing. There remain those who are too old to sing, and they will tell stories, illustrating the same virtues, as with the voice of an oracle.

Who are those who compose the third choir, Stranger? for I do not clearly understand what you mean to say about them.

And yet almost all that I have been saying has said with a view to them.

Will you try to be a little plainer?
D: Narrative:
"First will enter in their natural order the sacred choir composed of children, which is to sing lustily the heaven-taught lay to the whole city. Next will follow the choir of young men under the age of thirty, who will call upon the God Paean to testify to the truth of their words, and will pray him to be gracious to the youth and to turn their hearts. Thirdly, the choir of elder men, who are from thirty to sixty years of age, will also sing. There remain those who are too old to sing, and they will tell stories, illustrating the same virtues, as with the voice of an oracle."

"Who are those who compose the third choir, Stranger? for I do not clearly understand what you mean to say about them."

"And yet almost all that I have been saying has been said with a view to them."

"Will you try to be a little plainer?"
D: Refashioned:
"First, in their natural order, comes the sacred children's choir, which will sing the divinely inspired hymn vigorously to the whole city. Next comes the choir of young men under thirty, who will invoke the god Paean to witness the truth of their words and pray for his grace to turn the hearts of the youth. Third comes the choir of elder men, aged thirty to sixty, who will also sing. Those too old to sing will remain and tell stories illustrating the same virtues, speaking with the authority of an oracle."

"Who exactly makes up this third choir, Stranger? I do not fully understand what you are saying about them."

"And yet almost everything I have been discussing has been said with them specifically in mind."

"Could you try to be a bit clearer?"
D: Elegant:
"Three choruses will perform in order: first, children singing the sacred hymn to the city; second, young men under thirty invoking Paean as witness; third, elders aged thirty to sixty. Those too old to sing will tell stories with oracular authority, illustrating the same virtues."

"Who is the third chorus? I don't fully understand."

"Nearly everything I've said has been directed at them."
D: Summary: The Athenian describes three choruses organized by age: children, young men under thirty (invoking Paean), and elders aged thirty to sixty, with the very oldest serving as oracular storytellers. Cleinias is confused about the third chorus, prompting the Athenian to note that much of the preceding discussion was aimed at explaining their role.
D: Distilled: Three age-graded choruses -- children, young adults, and elders -- will perform the city's sacred educational songs, with the very oldest serving as storytellers.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, with Cleinias and an implied question. The three-chorus structure is the institutional backbone of the Laws' educational system. Paean (or Paian) is Apollo in his capacity as healer -- the young men's invocation connects music to healing. The elders who are 'too old to sing' but tell stories 'with the voice of an oracle' represent the highest authority -- their wisdom transcends the performative requirements of the younger choruses. Cleinias's confusion is dramatically important: it prompts the extended discussion of the Dionysian chorus (the third chorus of elders) that follows. Cross-reference: Laws 7.799a-800a on choral regulations; Republic 3.398c-399e on appropriate musical modes for different ages; Laws 6.765a on the role of elder citizens.

D: Original: I was speaking at the commencement of our discourse, as you will remember, of the fiery nature of young creatures: I said that they were unable to keep quiet either in limb or voice, and that they called out and jumped about in a disorderly manner; and that no other animal attained to any perception of order, but man only. Now the order of motion is called rhythm, and the order of the voice, in which high and low are duly mingled, is called harmony; and both together are termed choric song. And I said that the Gods had pity on us, and gave us Apollo and the Muses to be our playfellows and leaders in the dance; and Dionysus, as I dare say that you will remember, was the third.
D: Narrative: "I was speaking at the commencement of our discourse, as you will remember, of the fiery nature of young creatures: I said that they were unable to keep quiet either in limb or voice, and that they called out and jumped about in a disorderly manner; and that no other animal attained to any perception of order, but man only. Now the order of motion is called rhythm, and the order of the voice, in which high and low are duly mingled, is called harmony; and both together are termed choric song. And I said that the Gods had pity on us, and gave us Apollo and the Muses to be our playfellows and leaders in the dance; and Dionysus, as I dare say that you will remember, was the third."
D: Refashioned: "As you will recall, at the beginning of our discussion I spoke of the fiery nature of young creatures -- how they cannot keep their bodies or voices still, but cry out and jump about in a disorderly way. No other animal achieves any sense of order in its movements; only humans do. The order of bodily motion is called rhythm; the order of the voice, in which high and low are properly blended, is called harmony; and the two combined are called choral song. I also said that the gods, pitying our condition, gave us Apollo and the Muses as companions and leaders in the dance -- and Dionysus, as you will surely remember, was the third."
D: Elegant: "Recall our starting point: young creatures move and cry without order. Only humans perceive rhythm -- order in motion -- and harmony -- order in voice. Together these form choral song. The gods gave us Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus as companions in this education through dance."
D: Summary: The Athenian recapitulates the earlier argument: young creatures move chaotically, but humans uniquely perceive rhythm and harmony, which together constitute choral song. He reminds his listeners that the gods gave Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus as guides for this musical education.
D: Distilled: Humans alone perceive rhythm and harmony, which together form choral song -- a divine gift through Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is a deliberate recapitulation of the argument from 2.3, linking back before moving forward to the Dionysian chorus. The definitions of rhythm (order of motion) and harmony (order of voice) are foundational to Greek musical theory. The triad of Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus maps onto the three choruses: Apollo for children, the Muses for young adults, and Dionysus for elders. Cross-reference: Laws 2.3 (the original statement); Republic 3.399e-400d on rhythm and harmony; Aristoxenus, Elements of Rhythm for the technical development of these concepts.

D: Original:
I quite remember.

Thus far I have spoken of the chorus of Apollo and the Muses, and I have still to speak of the remaining chorus, which is that of Dionysus.

How is that arranged? There is something strange, at any rate on first hearing, in a Dionysiac chorus of old men, if you really mean that those who are above thirty, and may be fifty, or from fifty to sixty years of age, are to dance in his honour.

Very true; and therefore it must be shown that there is good reason for the proposal.

Certainly.

Are we agreed thus far?

About what?

That every man and boy, slave and free, both sexes, and the whole city, should never cease charming themselves with the strains of which we have spoken; and that there should be every sort of change and variation of them in order to take away the effect of sameness, so that the singers may always receive pleasure from their hymns, and may never weary of them?

Every one will agree.

Where, then, will that best part of our city which, by reason of age and intelligence, has the greatest influence, sing these fairest of strains, which are to do so much good? Shall we be so foolish as to let them off who would give us the most beautiful and also the most useful of songs?

But, says the argument, we cannot let them off.

Then how can we carry out our purpose with decorum? Will this be the way?

What?

When a man is advancing in years, he is afraid and reluctant to sing;-he has no pleasure in his own performances; and if compulsion is used, he will be more and more ashamed, the older and more discreet he grows;-is not this true?

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"I quite remember."

"Thus far I have spoken of the chorus of Apollo and the Muses, and I have still to speak of the remaining chorus, which is that of Dionysus."

"How is that arranged? There is something strange, at any rate on first hearing, in a Dionysiac chorus of old men, if you really mean that those who are above thirty, and may be fifty, or from fifty to sixty years of age, are to dance in his honour."

"Very true; and therefore it must be shown that there is good reason for the proposal."

"Certainly."

"Are we agreed thus far?"

"About what?"

"That every man and boy, slave and free, both sexes, and the whole city, should never cease charming themselves with the strains of which we have spoken; and that there should be every sort of change and variation of them in order to take away the effect of sameness, so that the singers may always receive pleasure from their hymns, and may never weary of them?"

"Every one will agree."

"Where, then, will that best part of our city which, by reason of age and intelligence, has the greatest influence, sing these fairest of strains, which are to do so much good? Shall we be so foolish as to let them off who would give us the most beautiful and also the most useful of songs?"

"But, says the argument, we cannot let them off."

"Then how can we carry out our purpose with decorum? Will this be the way?"

"What?"

"When a man is advancing in years, he is afraid and reluctant to sing; -- he has no pleasure in his own performances; and if compulsion is used, he will be more and more ashamed, the older and more discreet he grows; -- is not this true?"

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"I remember."

"So far I have discussed the choruses of Apollo and the Muses. I still need to address the remaining chorus -- that of Dionysus."

"How is it organized? It sounds strange, at least initially, to have a Dionysiac chorus of old men -- people over thirty, perhaps fifty, or even fifty to sixty years old, dancing in his honor."

"Quite true, which is why we must demonstrate good reason for the proposal."

"Of course."

"Do we agree on this much: that everyone in the city -- men and boys, slaves and free, both sexes -- should continually immerse themselves in the musical strains we have described, with enough variety and change to prevent monotony, so that the singers always find fresh pleasure in their hymns?"

"Everyone would agree with that."

"Then where will the best citizens -- those with the greatest influence due to age and wisdom -- sing these finest and most beneficial strains? Shall we foolishly excuse the very people who could give us the most beautiful and useful songs?"

"The logic demands we cannot excuse them."

"But how do we accomplish this with propriety? Consider the problem: as a person grows older, he becomes afraid and reluctant to sing. He takes no pleasure in his own performances, and if forced, he feels increasingly ashamed the older and more dignified he becomes. Is this not true?"

"It certainly is."
D: Elegant: "The chorus of Dionysus remains to be discussed. A chorus of old men dancing for Dionysus sounds strange, so we need good reason. We agree the whole city should sing unceasingly with enough variety to prevent boredom. The wisest elders should sing the finest songs. But the problem: older men are ashamed and reluctant to perform, growing more so with age and dignity."
D: Summary: The Athenian introduces the chorus of Dionysus -- a chorus of elders -- acknowledging it sounds strange. He establishes that the wisest citizens should sing the most important songs, but identifies the practical problem: older people are ashamed and reluctant to perform publicly.
D: Distilled: The wisest elders should sing the most important songs, but their shame and reluctance to perform must be overcome.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The Dionysian chorus of elders is the Laws' most innovative institutional proposal. The association of Dionysus with older performers inverts the usual connection of Dionysus with youthful revelry. The practical problem of elders' performance anxiety is treated with genuine psychological insight. The principle that variety prevents boredom shows attention to the sustainability of cultural institutions.
