I would consider even a day as long as this one -- and we are now approaching the summer solstice -- too short for the conversation."

"Then we must press on with the subject?"

"We must."

"Let us place ourselves mentally at the moment when Sparta, Argos, Messene, and the rest of the Peloponnese were all under the dominion of your ancestors, Megillus. According to tradition, they then divided their forces into three parts and established three cities: Argos, Messene, and Sparta."

"That is correct."

"Temenus became king of Argos, Cresphontes of Messene, and the twin brothers Procles and Eurysthenes of Sparta."

"Yes."

"And all the people of that time swore oaths to these kings, pledging to defend them if anyone attempted to overthrow their rule."

"Correct."

"But can a kingdom be destroyed -- can any form of government be destroyed -- by anyone other than its own rulers? By Zeus, it cannot. Have we already forgotten what we said just recently about this?"

"We have not."
D: Elegant:
Megillus enthusiastically endorses continuing the inquiry, saying even the longest day of the year would be too short for such a discussion.

The Athenian asks them to consider the moment when the Dorian conquerors divided the Peloponnese into three kingdoms: Argos under Temenus, Messene under Cresphontes, and Sparta under Procles and Eurysthenes. All the people swore mutual oaths of protection. Yet the critical principle remains: no government is ever destroyed except by its own rulers.
D: Summary: After Megillus eagerly endorses continuing the discussion, the Athenian turns to the Dorian settlement of the Peloponnese: the three kingdoms of Argos, Messene, and Sparta were established with mutual oaths of protection. He reintroduces the key principle that governments are only ever destroyed from within, by their own rulers.
D: Distilled: The three Dorian kingdoms of Argos, Messene, and Sparta were founded with mutual defense oaths, yet governments are only destroyed by their own rulers.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The mention of the 'longest day of the year' provides a dramatic frame -- the conversation occurs near the summer solstice, echoing the temporal setting of the Republic (set during a festival). The historical detail about the Heraclid division of the Peloponnese follows standard Greek tradition (cf. Apollodorus, Bibliotheca 2.8). The three royal houses: Temenus (Argos), Cresphontes (Messene), and the dual kingship of Procles and Eurysthenes (Sparta) represent the Dorian lineages. The principle that governments are destroyed by their own rulers is central to the Laws' political theory and echoes Republic 545d-546a on how the ideal state would decline. The oath of mutual assistance anticipates modern concepts of collective security.

D: Original: And may we not now further confirm what was then mentioned? For we have come upon facts which have brought us back again to the same principle; so that, in resuming the discussion, we shall not be enquiring about an empty theory, but about events which actually happened. The case was as follows:-Three royal heroes made oath to three cities which were under a kingly government, and the cities to the kings, that both rulers and subjects should govern and be governed according to the laws which were common to all of them: the rulers promised that as time and the race went forward they would not make their rule more arbitrary; and the subjects said that, if the rulers observed these conditions, they would never subvert or permit others to subvert those kingdoms; the kings were to assist kings and peoples when injured, and the peoples were to assist peoples and kings in like manner. Is not this the fact?
D: Narrative: "And may we not now further confirm what was then mentioned? For we have come upon facts which have brought us back again to the same principle; so that, in resuming the discussion, we shall not be enquiring about an empty theory, but about events which actually happened. The case was as follows: Three royal heroes made oath to three cities which were under a kingly government, and the cities to the kings, that both rulers and subjects should govern and be governed according to the laws which were common to all of them: the rulers promised that as time and the race went forward they would not make their rule more arbitrary; and the subjects said that, if the rulers observed these conditions, they would never subvert or permit others to subvert those kingdoms; the kings were to assist kings and peoples when injured, and the peoples were to assist peoples and kings in like manner. Is not this the fact?"
D: Refashioned: "Can we not now confirm what we said earlier with concrete evidence? We have arrived at historical events that illustrate the very same principle, so our discussion will no longer concern mere theory but actual occurrences. Here is what happened: Three royal leaders swore oaths to the three cities under their rule, and the cities swore oaths in return. Both sides pledged that rulers and subjects alike would govern and be governed according to laws common to all three states. The rulers promised that over time they would not make their power more despotic. The subjects pledged that as long as the rulers kept their word, they would never overthrow -- or allow anyone else to overthrow -- these kingdoms. Kings were to assist other kings and peoples when they suffered injustice, and peoples were to assist other peoples and kings in the same way. Is this not what happened?"
D: Elegant: Theory is now confirmed by history. Three kings swore mutual oaths with three cities: rulers and subjects would abide by common laws. The kings promised never to increase their power toward despotism. The subjects promised never to overthrow -- or permit the overthrow of -- the kingdoms, so long as the kings kept their word. Both kings and peoples pledged mutual assistance against injustice.
D: Summary: The Athenian details the mutual constitutional compact between the three Dorian kingdoms: kings swore not to become more despotic over time, subjects swore loyalty conditional on this restraint, and all parties pledged mutual military assistance. This historical arrangement, he argues, confirms the theoretical principles discussed earlier.
D: Distilled: The Dorian compact established a constitutional bargain: kings would not become despotic, subjects would remain loyal, and all three kingdoms would defend each other.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage describes what amounts to a constitutional contract or social compact -- remarkably anticipating Enlightenment contractarian theory (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau). The key elements are: (1) mutual obligation between rulers and ruled, (2) a promise against increasing arbitrary power, (3) conditional loyalty, and (4) collective security among allied states. The emphasis on the rulers' promise not to make their rule 'more arbitrary' over time addresses the central problem of constitutional decay. Compare Republic 562a-563e on how democracy degenerates into tyranny. The phrase 'not an empty theory but events which actually happened' reflects the Laws' distinctive method of grounding political philosophy in historical analysis rather than purely theoretical construction.

D: Original:
Yes.

And the three states to whom these laws were given, whether their kings or any others were the authors of them, had therefore the greatest security for the maintenance of their constitutions?

What security?

That the other two states were always to come to the rescue against a rebellious third.

True.

Many persons say that legislators ought to impose such laws as the mass of the people will be ready to receive; but this is just as if one were to command gymnastic masters or physicians to treat or cure their pupils or patients in an agreeable manner.

Exactly.

Whereas the physician may often be too happy if he can restore health, and make the body whole, without any very great infliction of pain.

Certainly.

There was also another advantage possessed by the men of that day, which greatly lightened the task of passing laws.

What advantage?
D: Narrative:
"Yes."

"And the three states to whom these laws were given, whether their kings or any others were the authors of them, had therefore the greatest security for the maintenance of their constitutions?"

"What security?"

"That the other two states were always to come to the rescue against a rebellious third."

"True."

"Many persons say that legislators ought to impose such laws as the mass of the people will be ready to receive; but this is just as if one were to command gymnastic masters or physicians to treat or cure their pupils or patients in an agreeable manner."

"Exactly."

"Whereas the physician may often be too happy if he can restore health, and make the body whole, without any very great infliction of pain."

"Certainly."

"There was also another advantage possessed by the men of that day, which greatly lightened the task of passing laws."

"What advantage?"
D: Refashioned:
"Indeed."

"So these three states, regardless of whether their kings or others authored the laws, possessed the greatest possible security for maintaining their constitutions?"

"What kind of security?"

"The assurance that if any one of the three states rebelled against the compact, the other two would come to its defense."

"That is right."

"Now, many people argue that legislators should only impose laws that the general population is willing to accept. But that is like telling a gym trainer or doctor to treat their students or patients only in pleasant ways."

"Precisely."

"In reality, a physician is often fortunate if he can restore health and make the body whole without inflicting too much pain."

"Of course."

"The people of that era also had another advantage that made the task of lawmaking much easier."

"What advantage was that?"
D: Elegant:
The three-state alliance provided the ultimate constitutional guarantee: two states would always defend against a rebellious third.

The common demand that legislators only pass popular laws is like telling doctors to cure patients only through pleasant treatments. A good physician is fortunate to restore health without excessive pain.

The Dorian settlers also had a special advantage that made lawmaking easier.
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies the three-state mutual defense pact as the strongest possible constitutional safeguard. He then attacks the populist notion that laws should only be what people want to hear, comparing it to telling doctors to only use pleasant treatments. He also notes that the Dorian settlers had a unique advantage for legislation.
D: Distilled: Constitutional security came from mutual defense among three allied states, and good legislation -- like good medicine -- cannot always be pleasant.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The physician analogy for the legislator is a recurring Platonic motif: see Statesman 293a-c, Gorgias 464b-465d, and Republic 389b. The argument that law should not be constrained by popular acceptability directly challenges democratic principles and reflects the Laws' consistent preference for expert governance. The mutual defense arrangement anticipates the logic of NATO-style collective security alliances. The 'advantage' the Athenian is about to reveal (equal property distribution, no debts) connects to one of the most contentious issues in Greek politics -- land reform and debt cancellation, which drove revolutions in many city-states.

D: Original: The legislators of that day, when they equalized property, escaped the great accusation which generally arises in legislation, if a person attempts to disturb the possession of land, or to abolish debts, because he sees that without this reform there can never be any real equality. Now, in general, when the legislator attempts to make a new settlement of such matters, every one meets him with the cry, that "he is not to disturb vested interests"-declaring with imprecations that he is introducing agrarian laws and cancelling of debts, until a man is at his wits end; whereas no one could quarrel with the Dorians for distributing the land-there was nothing to hinder them; and as for debts, they had none which were considerable or of old standing.
D: Narrative: "The legislators of that day, when they equalized property, escaped the great accusation which generally arises in legislation, if a person attempts to disturb the possession of land, or to abolish debts, because he sees that without this reform there can never be any real equality. Now, in general, when the legislator attempts to make a new settlement of such matters, every one meets him with the cry, that 'he is not to disturb vested interests' -- declaring with imprecations that he is introducing agrarian laws and cancelling of debts, until a man is at his wits end; whereas no one could quarrel with the Dorians for distributing the land -- there was nothing to hinder them; and as for debts, they had none which were considerable or of old standing."
D: Refashioned: "The lawmakers of that era, by equalizing property from the start, avoided the greatest obstacle that legislators typically face: the fierce opposition that arises whenever anyone tries to redistribute land or cancel debts, even when they can see that without such reform, genuine equality is impossible. Normally, when a legislator attempts to restructure these matters, everyone protests that he must not tamper with established property rights. They hurl curses at him for proposing land redistribution and debt forgiveness, until the poor man is driven to desperation. But no one could object to the Dorians distributing land equally -- there were no prior claims to dispute. And as for debts, they had none of any significance or long standing."
D: Elegant: By equalizing property at the outset, the Dorian lawmakers avoided the greatest obstacle in legislation: the furious opposition to land redistribution and debt cancellation. Normally, any attempt to reform these matters meets with outrage over 'vested interests,' even though true equality requires it. But the Dorians faced no such resistance -- there were no established property claims to disturb and no significant debts to cancel.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that the Dorian legislators had the rare advantage of being able to equalize property without opposition, since they were starting fresh with no established land claims or debts. In contrast, any normal legislator who attempts redistribution faces furious resistance from entrenched interests, even when equality is impossible without reform.
D: Distilled: The Dorians could equalize property without opposition because they started fresh, unlike later legislators who face furious resistance from vested interests.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage addresses one of the most explosive issues in Greek politics. Land redistribution (ge anadasmos) and debt cancellation (chreon apokope) were the rallying cries of revolutionary movements throughout the Greek world, from Solon's seisachtheia in Athens (594 BCE) to the reforms of Agis IV and Cleomenes III in Sparta (3rd century BCE). The observation that vested interests block necessary reform is remarkably modern. Compare Laws 684d-e and 736c-e where Plato further discusses the difficulty of property reform. The Dorians' advantage -- founding a new settlement with no prior claims -- is precisely the condition Plato seeks to replicate with his new colony in Magnesia (Books 5-6). See also Republic 462b-c on communal property.

D: Original:
Very true.

But then, my good friends, why did the settlement and legislation of their country turn out so badly?

How do you mean; and why do you blame them?

There were three kingdoms, and of these, two quickly corrupted their original constitution and laws, and the only one which remained was the Spartan.

The question which you ask is not easily answered.

And yet must be answered when we are enquiring about laws, this being our old man's sober game of play, whereby we beguile the way, as I was saying when we first set out on our journey.

Certainly; and we must find out why this was.

What laws are more worthy of our attention than those which have regulated such cities? or what settlements of states are greater or more famous?

I know of none.
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"But then, my good friends, why did the settlement and legislation of their country turn out so badly?"

"How do you mean; and why do you blame them?"

"There were three kingdoms, and of these, two quickly corrupted their original constitution and laws, and the only one which remained was the Spartan."

"The question which you ask is not easily answered."

"And yet must be answered when we are enquiring about laws, this being our old man's sober game of play, whereby we beguile the way, as I was saying when we first set out on our journey."

"Certainly; and we must find out why this was."

"What laws are more worthy of our attention than those which have regulated such cities? or what settlements of states are greater or more famous?"

"I know of none."
D: Refashioned:
"Quite true."

"But then, my friends, why did these settlements and their legislation turn out so badly?"

"What do you mean? Why do you fault them?"

"Of the three kingdoms, two quickly corrupted their original constitutions and laws. Only Sparta endured."

"That is not an easy question to answer."

"Yet it must be answered, since we are investigating laws -- this being, as I said when we started our journey, the sober game that old men play to pass the time."

"Indeed, and we must discover why this happened."

"What laws deserve our study more than those that governed such important states? What political settlements in history have been greater or more famous?"

"I cannot think of any."
D: Elegant:
Despite every advantage, why did the Dorian settlement fail? Two of the three kingdoms quickly corrupted their constitutions; only Sparta endured.

This question demands an answer from anyone investigating laws. No greater or more famous political settlements exist to study. The inquiry itself is the dignified pastime of old men -- a sober game to pass the journey.
D: Summary: The Athenian poses the central puzzle: despite ideal conditions, two of the three Dorian kingdoms rapidly corrupted their constitutions while only Sparta survived. This demands explanation from anyone seriously studying legislation. He characterizes the entire inquiry as the 'sober game' of elderly men beguiling a long journey.
D: Distilled: The central puzzle of Dorian history is why two of three kingdoms with every advantage failed, while only Sparta endured.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The 'old man's sober game of play' (paidia) recalls the Laws' opening framework: three elderly men walking from Cnossos to the cave of Zeus on a long summer day. The characterization of philosophical inquiry as a kind of dignified play connects to the Platonic theme of serious playfulness (spoudaiogeloion). The failure of Argos and Messene despite ideal starting conditions is the historical test case for Plato's political theory. Messene was conquered by Sparta in the Messenian Wars (8th-7th centuries BCE) and its population reduced to helot status. Argos declined through internal faction. Only Sparta maintained its constitution -- attributed to Lycurgus -- for centuries. This pattern of constitutional decay echoes Republic 546a-547a.

D: Original: Can we doubt that your ancestors intended these institutions not only for the protection of Peloponnesus, but of all the Hellenes. in case they were attacked by the barbarian? For the inhabitants of the region about Ilium, when they provoked by their insolence the Trojan war, relied upon the power of the Assyrians and the Empire of Ninus, which still existed and had a great prestige; the people of those days fearing the united Assyrian Empire just as we now fear the Great King. And the second capture of Troy was a serious offence against them, because Troy was a portion of the Assyrian Empire. To meet the danger the single army was distributed between three cities by the royal brothers, sons of Heracles-a fair device, as it seemed, and a far better arrangement than the expedition against Troy. For, firstly, the people of that day had, as they thought, in the Heraclidae better leaders than the Pelopidae; in the next place, they considered that their army was superior in valour to that which went against Troy; for, although the latter conquered the Trojans, they were themselves conquered by the Heraclidae-Achaeans by Dorians. May we not suppose that this was the intention with which the men of those days framed the constitutions of their states?
D: Narrative: "Can we doubt that your ancestors intended these institutions not only for the protection of Peloponnesus, but of all the Hellenes, in case they were attacked by the barbarian? For the inhabitants of the region about Ilium, when they provoked by their insolence the Trojan war, relied upon the power of the Assyrians and the Empire of Ninus, which still existed and had a great prestige; the people of those days fearing the united Assyrian Empire just as we now fear the Great King. And the second capture of Troy was a serious offence against them, because Troy was a portion of the Assyrian Empire. To meet the danger the single army was distributed between three cities by the royal brothers, sons of Heracles -- a fair device, as it seemed, and a far better arrangement than the expedition against Troy. For, firstly, the people of that day had, as they thought, in the Heraclidae better leaders than the Pelopidae; in the next place, they considered that their army was superior in valour to that which went against Troy; for, although the latter conquered the Trojans, they were themselves conquered by the Heraclidae -- Achaeans by Dorians. May we not suppose that this was the intention with which the men of those days framed the constitutions of their states?"
D: Refashioned: "Can we doubt that your ancestors designed these institutions to protect not just the Peloponnese but all of Greece against barbarian invasion? Consider the context: the peoples around Troy, when they provoked the Trojan War through their arrogance, were relying on the power of the Assyrian Empire under Ninus, which still commanded enormous prestige. People feared that unified Assyrian power just as we today fear the Great King of Persia. The destruction of Troy was a serious provocation against Assyria, since Troy was part of their empire. To counter this threat, the single Dorian army was divided among three cities by the royal brothers, the sons of Heracles -- a strategy that seemed excellent and far superior to the Trojan War expedition. First, the people believed the Heraclidae were better leaders than the Pelopidae had been. Second, they considered their army more valiant than the one that fought at Troy -- after all, though the Achaeans conquered Troy, the Dorians had in turn conquered the Achaeans. We can reasonably suppose this was the strategic thinking behind the constitutions they established."
D: Elegant: The Dorian settlement was designed to protect all of Greece against barbarian invasion. Troy had relied on the Assyrian Empire, which the Greeks feared as they now fear Persia. Destroying Troy provoked Assyria, so the Dorian army was strategically divided among three cities -- a better arrangement than the Trojan expedition. The Heraclidae were considered superior leaders to the Pelopidae, and the Dorian army more valiant: the Achaeans conquered Troy, but the Dorians conquered the Achaeans.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that the three-kingdom Dorian settlement was strategically designed to defend all of Greece against barbarian invasion, particularly the Assyrian Empire that had backed Troy. The division of forces among three cities was considered superior to the earlier Trojan War expedition, with the Heraclidae deemed better leaders and the Dorian army more valiant than the Achaeans.
D: Distilled: The Dorian three-kingdom system was a strategic defense arrangement for all Greece, considered superior to the Trojan War expedition in both leadership and military power.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The identification of Troy with the Assyrian Empire is historically dubious but reflects Greek awareness of Near Eastern power structures. The 'Empire of Ninus' refers to the legendary Assyrian king Ninus, associated with Nineveh. The parallel between ancient Assyria and contemporary Persia ('the Great King') grounds the historical analysis in present political concerns -- the Persian threat remained real in Plato's lifetime. The comparison of Pelopidae (Agamemnon's line) and Heraclidae (the Dorian royal houses) uses the mythological genealogies that structured Greek political identity. The logic that the Dorians must be superior because they conquered the Achaeans who conquered Troy is a chain-of-conquest argument. Compare Thucydides 1.12 on the Dorian migration.

D: Original:
Quite true.

And would not men who had shared with one another many dangers, and were governed by a single race of royal brothers, and had taken the advice of oracles, and in particular of the Delphian Apollo, be likely to think that such states would be firmly and lastingly established?

Of course they would.

Yet these institutions, of which such great expectations were entertained, seem to have all rapidly vanished away; with the exception, as I was saying, of that small part of them which existed in yourland.And this third part has never to this day ceased warring against the two others; whereas, if the original idea had been carried out, and they had agreed to be one, their power would have been invincible in war.

No doubt.

But what was the ruin of this glorious confederacy? Here is a subject well worthy of consideration.

Certainly, no one will ever find more striking instances of laws or governments being the salvation or destruction of great and noble interests, than are here presented to his view.

Then now we seem to have happily arrived at a real and important question.

Very true.

Did you never remark, sage friend, that all men, and we ourselves at this moment, often fancy that they see some beautiful thing which might have effected wonders if any one had only known how to make a right use of it in some way; and yet this mode of looking at things may turn out after all to be a mistake, and not according to nature, either in our own case or in any other?

To what are you referring, and what do you mean?

I was thinking of my own admiration of the aforesaid Heracleid expedition, which was so noble, and might have had such wonderful results for the Hellenes, if only rightly used; and I was just laughing at myself.

But were you not right and wise in speaking as you did, and we in assenting to you?

Perhaps; and yet I cannot help observing that any one who sees anything great or powerful, immediately has the feeling that-"If the owner only knew how to use his great and noble possession, how happy would he be, and what great results would he achieve!"

And would he not be justified?
D: Narrative:
"Quite true."

"And would not men who had shared with one another many dangers, and were governed by a single race of royal brothers, and had taken the advice of oracles, and in particular of the Delphian Apollo, be likely to think that such states would be firmly and lastingly established?"

"Of course they would."

"Yet these institutions, of which such great expectations were entertained, seem to have all rapidly vanished away; with the exception, as I was saying, of that small part of them which existed in your land. And this third part has never to this day ceased warring against the two others; whereas, if the original idea had been carried out, and they had agreed to be one, their power would have been invincible in war."

"No doubt."

"But what was the ruin of this glorious confederacy? Here is a subject well worthy of consideration."

"Certainly, no one will ever find more striking instances of laws or governments being the salvation or destruction of great and noble interests, than are here presented to his view."

"Then now we seem to have happily arrived at a real and important question."

"Very true."

"Did you never remark, sage friend, that all men, and we ourselves at this moment, often fancy that they see some beautiful thing which might have effected wonders if any one had only known how to make a right use of it in some way; and yet this mode of looking at things may turn out after all to be a mistake, and not according to nature, either in our own case or in any other?"

"To what are you referring, and what do you mean?"

"I was thinking of my own admiration of the aforesaid Heracleid expedition, which was so noble, and might have had such wonderful results for the Hellenes, if only rightly used; and I was just laughing at myself."

"But were you not right and wise in speaking as you did, and we in assenting to you?"

"Perhaps; and yet I cannot help observing that any one who sees anything great or powerful, immediately has the feeling that -- 'If the owner only knew how to use his great and noble possession, how happy would he be, and what great results would he achieve!'"

"And would he not be justified?"
D: Refashioned:
"Quite so."

"And would not people who had faced many dangers together, who were ruled by a single royal family of brothers, and who had followed the guidance of oracles -- especially the oracle of Apollo at Delphi -- naturally assume that their states would be firmly and permanently established?"

"They certainly would."

"Yet these institutions, in which such great hopes were invested, all rapidly disintegrated -- except for the small portion that survived in your homeland, Sparta. And even that surviving part has never stopped warring against the other two. Had the original vision been carried out and they had remained united, their combined power would have been unbeatable."

"Undoubtedly."

"So what destroyed this magnificent alliance? That question is well worth investigating."

"Indeed -- one could hardly find more vivid examples of laws and governments either saving or ruining great and noble enterprises."

"We seem to have arrived at a genuinely important question."

"We have."

"But have you ever noticed, my wise friend, that people -- including ourselves right now -- often look at something impressive and think: 'If only someone knew how to use this properly, what wonders it could achieve!' And yet that way of thinking may itself be a mistake, not in accord with the nature of things?"

"What exactly do you mean?"

"I was reflecting on my own admiration for the Dorian expedition -- so noble, so full of potential for all of Greece, if only it had been used wisely. And I found myself laughing at my own reaction."

"But surely you were right to speak as you did, and we were right to agree?"

"Perhaps. Yet I keep noticing that whenever anyone sees something great and powerful, the immediate reaction is: 'If only the owner knew how to use this magnificent possession, how happy he would be and what extraordinary things he could accomplish!'"

"And is that reaction not justified?"
D: Elegant:
People who had shared dangers, followed oracles, and were governed by a royal brotherhood naturally expected their alliance to last forever. Yet it disintegrated rapidly -- only Sparta survived, and it never stopped fighting the other two. United, they would have been invincible.

What ruined this confederacy? The question is critical. But the Athenian catches himself: our instinct to see great power and think 'if only it were used wisely!' may itself be a mistake. Admiring potential is not the same as understanding what nature actually allows.
D: Summary: Despite shared dangers, oracular guidance, and royal kinship, the Dorian confederacy rapidly collapsed -- only Sparta survived, perpetually at war with the others. The Athenian raises a deeper philosophical point: our tendency to admire great power and imagine what it could achieve 'if only used wisely' may itself be a fundamental error about human nature.
D: Distilled: The Dorian confederacy's collapse despite every advantage reveals that admiring power's potential is not the same as understanding why power inevitably corrupts.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger alternate. -- The reference to the Delphian Apollo reflects the central role of the Delphic oracle in legitimizing Dorian claims to the Peloponnese (cf. Herodotus 6.52). The Athenian's self-correcting moment -- laughing at his own admiration -- is characteristically Socratic: the examined life requires questioning one's own assumptions. The insight that seeing great power and wishing it were 'used well' may be a mistake 'not according to nature' anticipates the argument that power itself tends to corrupt. Compare Republic 344a-345e (Thrasymachus on power) and the famous Acton dictum. The 'if only' fallacy -- assuming good outcomes from great power given proper use -- is precisely what the Laws seeks to overcome through institutional design rather than reliance on individual virtue.

D: Original: Reflect; in what point of view does this sort of praise appear just: First, in reference to the question in hand:-If the then commanders had known how to arrange their army properly, how would they have attained success? Would not this have been the way? They would have bound them all firmly together and preserved them for ever, giving them freedom and dominion at pleasure, combined with the power of doing in the whole world, Hellenic and barbarian, whatever they and their descendants desired. What other aim would they have had?
D: Narrative: "Reflect; in what point of view does this sort of praise appear just: First, in reference to the question in hand: If the then commanders had known how to arrange their army properly, how would they have attained success? Would not this have been the way? They would have bound them all firmly together and preserved them for ever, giving them freedom and dominion at pleasure, combined with the power of doing in the whole world, Hellenic and barbarian, whatever they and their descendants desired. What other aim would they have had?"
D: Refashioned: "Consider from what perspective this kind of admiration seems justified. Take the case at hand: if the Dorian commanders had known how to properly organize their forces, how would they have achieved success? Presumably they would have bound all three kingdoms firmly together and maintained that unity forever, granting their peoples freedom and dominion as they wished, along with the power to accomplish whatever they and their descendants desired throughout the entire Greek and barbarian world. What other goal could they possibly have had?"
D: Elegant: If the Dorian commanders had known how to properly organize their alliance, they would have bound the three kingdoms together permanently -- achieving freedom, dominion, and the power to do whatever they wished throughout the entire world. What other goal could there be?
D: Summary: The Athenian frames the hypothetical: had the Dorian commanders known how to properly organize their alliance, they would have achieved permanent unity, freedom, and dominion over the entire Greek and barbarian world. This sets up his argument about what actually prevents such ideal outcomes.
D: Distilled: The Dorian dream was permanent unity yielding absolute freedom and universal dominion -- but knowing how to achieve it was the real challenge.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage articulates the universal political fantasy: permanent power, complete freedom, and the ability to do whatever one desires. The Athenian states it so starkly precisely to expose its impossibility. The phrase 'whatever they and their descendants desired' echoes the critique of unconstrained desire that runs through the Laws. Compare Gorgias 466b-468e, where Socrates argues that tyrants who do whatever they wish actually have the least real power. The mention of 'Hellenic and barbarian' together as the scope of potential dominion reflects the pan-Hellenic ambitions that Alexander would later attempt to realize.

D: Original:
Very good.

Suppose any one were in the same way to express his admiration at the sight of great wealth or family honour, or the like, he would praise them under the idea that through them he would attain either all or the greater and chief part of what he desires.

He would.

Well, now, and does not the argument show that there is one common desire of all mankind?

What is it?

The desire which a man has, that all things, if possible-at any rate, things human-may come to pass in accordance with his soul's desire.

Certainly.

And having this desire always, and at every time of life, in youth, in manhood, in age, he cannot help always praying for the fulfilment of it.

No doubt.

And we join in the prayers of our friends, and ask for them what they ask for themselves.

We do.

Dear is the son to the father-the younger to the elder.

Of course.

And yet the son often prays to obtain things which the father prays that he may not obtain.

When the son is young and foolish, you mean?

Yes; or when the father, in the dotage of age or the heat of youth, having no sense of right and justice, prays with fervour, under the influence of feelings akin to those of Theseus when he cursed the unfortunate Hippolytus, do you imagine that the son, having a sense of right and justice, will join in his father's prayers?

I understand you to mean that a man should not desire or be in a hurry to have all things according to his wish, for his wish may be at variance with his reason. But every state and every individual ought to pray and strive for wisdom.
D: Narrative:
"Very good."

"Suppose any one were in the same way to express his admiration at the sight of great wealth or family honour, or the like, he would praise them under the idea that through them he would attain either all or the greater and chief part of what he desires."

"He would."

"Well, now, and does not the argument show that there is one common desire of all mankind?"

"What is it?"

"The desire which a man has, that all things, if possible -- at any rate, things human -- may come to pass in accordance with his soul's desire."

"Certainly."

"And having this desire always, and at every time of life, in youth, in manhood, in age, he cannot help always praying for the fulfilment of it."

"No doubt."

"And we join in the prayers of our friends, and ask for them what they ask for themselves."

"We do."

"Dear is the son to the father -- the younger to the elder."

"Of course."

"And yet the son often prays to obtain things which the father prays that he may not obtain."

"When the son is young and foolish, you mean?"

"Yes; or when the father, in the dotage of age or the heat of youth, having no sense of right and justice, prays with fervour, under the influence of feelings akin to those of Theseus when he cursed the unfortunate Hippolytus, do you imagine that the son, having a sense of right and justice, will join in his father's prayers?"

"I understand you to mean that a man should not desire or be in a hurry to have all things according to his wish, for his wish may be at variance with his reason. But every state and every individual ought to pray and strive for wisdom."
D: Refashioned:
"Very well."

"Similarly, when anyone admires great wealth, or noble lineage, or similar advantages, they praise these things because they imagine that through them they would obtain everything -- or at least the greater part of everything -- they desire."

"They would."

"But doesn't this reveal a single universal desire shared by all humanity?"

"What desire is that?"

"The desire that everything -- or at least all human affairs -- should unfold according to one's own wishes."

"Certainly."

"And since we carry this desire at every stage of life -- in youth, in maturity, in old age -- we cannot help perpetually praying for its fulfillment."

"Of course."

"We even join in our friends' prayers, asking for what they themselves ask."

"We do."

"A father holds his son dear, and the elder naturally cares for the younger."

"Naturally."

"Yet a son often prays for things the father prays he will not get."

"You mean when the son is young and foolish?"

"Yes -- or consider the reverse: when a father, whether in the recklessness of youth or the senility of old age, prays passionately for something unjust, driven by feelings like those of Theseus when he cursed his innocent son Hippolytus. Would the son, if he possesses a sense of justice, join in his father's prayer?"

"I see your point: a person should not rush to have everything go according to their wishes, because those wishes may conflict with reason. What every individual and every state should truly pray and strive for is wisdom."
D: Elegant:
All humans share one universal desire: that everything should unfold according to their wishes. We carry this desire at every age, pray for it constantly, and even join our friends' prayers for it.

But wishes can conflict with reason. A son prays for things his father opposes; a father like Theseus, driven by passion, curses his own innocent son. The lesson: one should not desire that all wishes be fulfilled, because wishes may contradict justice. What individuals and states should truly pursue is wisdom.
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies the universal human desire to have everything accord with one's wishes. But he demonstrates through the example of Theseus cursing Hippolytus that passionate desires can be at war with reason and justice. The conclusion is that neither individuals nor states should pray for the fulfillment of every wish, but rather for wisdom.
D: Distilled: The universal desire to have everything accord with one's wishes is dangerous because wishes often conflict with reason -- the true object of prayer should be wisdom.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The Theseus-Hippolytus story was famously dramatized in Euripides' Hippolytus (428 BCE): Theseus, believing false accusations against his son, used one of three wishes granted by Poseidon to curse Hippolytus to death. It exemplifies how passionate conviction can produce catastrophic injustice. The argument that universal desire must be governed by wisdom recapitulates the Republic's central thesis (441d-442d) about reason ruling the soul. The political analogy is direct: just as a father's unjust prayer should not bind his son, a ruler's unjust wishes should not bind the state. Megillus's concluding formulation -- 'every state and every individual ought to pray and strive for wisdom' -- demonstrates that he has grasped the philosophical point. Compare Alcibiades I 133c on praying for what is truly good.

D: Original: Yes; and I remember, and you will remember, what I said at first, that a statesman and legislator ought to ordain laws with a view to wisdom; while you were arguing that the good lawgiver ought to order all with a view to war. And to this I replied that there were four virtues, but that upon your view one of them only was the aim of legislation; whereas you ought to regard all virtue, and especially that which comes first, and is the leader of all the rest-I mean wisdom and mind and opinion, having affection and desire in their train. And now the argument returns to the same point, and I say once more, in jest if you like, or in earnest if you like, that the prayer of a fool is full of danger, being likely to end in the opposite of what he desires. And if you would rather receive my words in earnest, I am willing that you should; and you will find, I suspect, as I have said already, that not cowardice was the cause of the ruin of the Dorian kings and of their whole design, nor ignorance of military matters, either on the part of the rulers or of their subjects; but their misfortunes were due to their general degeneracy, and especially to their ignorance of the most important human affairs. That was then, and is still, and always will be the case, as I will endeavour, if you will allow me, to make out and demonstrate as well as I am able to you who are my friends, in the course of the argument.
D: Narrative: "Yes; and I remember, and you will remember, what I said at first, that a statesman and legislator ought to ordain laws with a view to wisdom; while you were arguing that the good lawgiver ought to order all with a view to war. And to this I replied that there were four virtues, but that upon your view one of them only was the aim of legislation; whereas you ought to regard all virtue, and especially that which comes first, and is the leader of all the rest -- I mean wisdom and mind and opinion, having affection and desire in their train. And now the argument returns to the same point, and I say once more, in jest if you like, or in earnest if you like, that the prayer of a fool is full of danger, being likely to end in the opposite of what he desires. And if you would rather receive my words in earnest, I am willing that you should; and you will find, I suspect, as I have said already, that not cowardice was the cause of the ruin of the Dorian kings and of their whole design, nor ignorance of military matters, either on the part of the rulers or of their subjects; but their misfortunes were due to their general degeneracy, and especially to their ignorance of the most important human affairs. That was then, and is still, and always will be the case, as I will endeavour, if you will allow me, to make out and demonstrate as well as I am able to you who are my friends, in the course of the argument."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, and you will recall what I said at the beginning: a statesman and legislator should frame laws with a view to wisdom. You were arguing that the good lawgiver should orient everything toward war. I responded that there are four virtues, but on your view, legislation aimed at only one -- courage. The legislator should regard all the virtues, and especially the one that leads the rest: wisdom, which encompasses intellect and sound judgment, with desire and emotion following in its train.

Now the argument has come full circle, and I repeat -- whether you take it as jest or as earnest -- that the prayer of a fool is dangerous, likely to produce the opposite of what he desires. If you prefer to take my words seriously, I believe you will find, as I have said, that the ruin of the Dorian kings and their entire project was caused not by cowardice or by military ignorance on the part of either rulers or subjects, but by general moral decline -- above all, by ignorance of the most important human concerns. This was true then, is true now, and always will be true. I will try, with your permission, to demonstrate this as clearly as I can in the course of our discussion."
D: Elegant:
The argument has come full circle. Legislation should aim at wisdom, not merely at war -- at all four virtues, led by wisdom, with desire and emotion following reason.

The prayer of a fool is dangerous, likely producing the opposite of what is desired. The Dorian kings were ruined not by cowardice or military ignorance but by moral decline and ignorance of the most important human affairs. This was true then, is true now, and always will be.
D: Summary: The Athenian recalls his opening argument that legislation should aim at wisdom and all four virtues, not just military courage. He now applies this to the Dorian kingdoms: their ruin was caused not by cowardice or military incompetence but by general moral degeneracy and ignorance of the most important human concerns -- a universal principle that holds across all times.
D: Distilled: The Dorian kingdoms fell not through military failure but through moral ignorance -- proving that legislation must aim at wisdom, not merely at war.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage explicitly connects Book 3's historical analysis to the argument from Book 1 (625c-632d) about whether legislation should aim at war or at complete virtue. The four virtues are the Platonic cardinal virtues: wisdom (sophia/phronesis), courage (andreia), temperance (sophrosyne), and justice (dikaiosyne) -- see Republic 427e-434d. The claim that wisdom leads with 'affection and desire in their train' articulates the psychological hierarchy of Republic 441d-442d (reason governing spirit and appetite). The phrase 'in jest if you like, or in earnest if you like' recalls Plato's concept of serious play (spoudaiogeloion), central to the Laws. The universal claim -- 'was then, is still, and always will be' -- elevates the historical lesson into a permanent truth about political life.

D: Original:
Pray go on, Stranger;-compliments are troublesome, but we will show, not in word but in deed, how greatly we prize your words, for we will give them our best attention; and that is the way in which a freeman best shows his approval or disapproval.

Excellent, Cleinias; let us do as you say.

By all means, if Heaven wills. Go on.

Well, then, proceeding in the same train of thought, I say that the greatest ignorance was the ruin of the Dorian power, and that now, as then, ignorance is ruin. And if this be true, the legislator must endeavour to implant wisdom in states, and banish ignorance to the utmost of his power.

That is evident.

Then now consider what is really the greatest ignorance. I should like to know whether you and Megillus would agree with me in what I am about to say; for my opinion is-

What?
D: Narrative:
"Pray go on, Stranger; compliments are troublesome, but we will show, not in word but in deed, how greatly we prize your words, for we will give them our best attention; and that is the way in which a freeman best shows his approval or disapproval."

"Excellent, Cleinias; let us do as you say."

"By all means, if Heaven wills. Go on."

"Well, then, proceeding in the same train of thought, I say that the greatest ignorance was the ruin of the Dorian power, and that now, as then, ignorance is ruin. And if this be true, the legislator must endeavour to implant wisdom in states, and banish ignorance to the utmost of his power."

"That is evident."

"Then now consider what is really the greatest ignorance. I should like to know whether you and Megillus would agree with me in what I am about to say; for my opinion is --"

"What?"
D: Refashioned:
"Please continue, Stranger. Compliments are a burden, but we will demonstrate our esteem for your words not through flattery but through attention -- for that is how a free person best shows approval or disapproval."

"Well said, Cleinias. Let us proceed exactly that way."

"Yes, with heaven's blessing. Please go on."

"Very well. Following the same line of reasoning: the greatest ignorance destroyed the Dorian power, and ignorance remains ruinous now as it was then. If this is true, the legislator's duty is to cultivate wisdom in the state and drive out ignorance as far as humanly possible."

"That much is clear."

"Then consider: what is truly the greatest form of ignorance? I would like to know whether you and Megillus agree with what I am about to propose. My view is --"

"What is it?"
D: Elegant:
Cleinias urges the Athenian to continue, noting that free men show esteem through attention, not flattery.

The Athenian restates the thesis: the greatest ignorance destroyed the Dorian power. Ignorance always destroys. Therefore the legislator must cultivate wisdom and banish ignorance as far as possible. But what is the greatest form of ignorance?
D: Summary: Cleinias encourages the Athenian to continue, declaring that attentive listening is the freest form of approval. The Athenian reaffirms that ignorance destroyed the Dorian confederacy and remains the perennial cause of political ruin. The legislator's chief task is therefore to implant wisdom and banish ignorance. He prepares to define the greatest form of ignorance.
D: Distilled: Ignorance destroyed the Dorian power and always destroys; the legislator's supreme duty is implanting wisdom and banishing ignorance.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias, Megillus, and the Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's remark about free men showing approval through attention rather than flattery is significant: it reflects the Laws' concern with what constitutes genuine civic freedom (as opposed to servile flattery or democratic noise). Compare Republic 563a-b on democratic flattery. Megillus's 'if Heaven wills' adds a pious Spartan note. The Athenian's thesis that ignorance is the perennial cause of political ruin sets up the forthcoming definition of 'the greatest ignorance' -- the internal conflict between pleasure and reason -- which is one of the Laws' most important philosophical contributions. The legislator-as-educator theme connects to Republic 376e-412b and the entire educational program of the Laws.

D: Original:
That the greatest ignorance is when a man hates that which he nevertheless thinks to be good and noble, and loves and embraces that which he knows to be unrighteous and evil. This disagreement between the sense of pleasure and the judgment of reason in the soul is, in my opinion, the worst ignorance; and also the greatest, because affecting the great mass of the human soul; for the principle which feels pleasure and pain in the individual is like the mass or populace in a state. And when the soul is opposed to knowledge, or opinion, or reason, which are her natural lords, that I call folly, just as in the state, when the multitude refuses to obey their rulers and the laws; or, again, in the individual, when fair reasonings have their habitation in the soul and yet do no good, but rather the reverse of good. All these cases I term the worst ignorance, whether in individuals or in states. You will understand, Stranger, that I am speaking of something which is very different from the ignorance of handicraftsmen.

Yes, my friend, we understand and agree.
D: Narrative:
"That the greatest ignorance is when a man hates that which he nevertheless thinks to be good and noble, and loves and embraces that which he knows to be unrighteous and evil. This disagreement between the sense of pleasure and the judgment of reason in the soul is, in my opinion, the worst ignorance; and also the greatest, because affecting the great mass of the human soul; for the principle which feels pleasure and pain in the individual is like the mass or populace in a state. And when the soul is opposed to knowledge, or opinion, or reason, which are her natural lords, that I call folly, just as in the state, when the multitude refuses to obey their rulers and the laws; or, again, in the individual, when fair reasonings have their habitation in the soul and yet do no good, but rather the reverse of good. All these cases I term the worst ignorance, whether in individuals or in states. You will understand, Stranger, that I am speaking of something which is very different from the ignorance of handicraftsmen."

"Yes, my friend, we understand and agree."
D: Refashioned:
"The greatest ignorance is when a person hates what they know to be good and noble, yet loves and embraces what they know to be wrong and evil. This conflict between the pull of pleasure and the judgment of reason within the soul is, in my view, the worst form of ignorance -- and also the most widespread, because it affects the largest part of the human soul. The part of us that responds to pleasure and pain is like the common populace in a state. When this appetitive element rebels against knowledge, opinion, or reason -- which are its natural rulers -- I call that folly. It is exactly analogous to a state where the multitude refuses to obey their rulers and the laws. Or again, when sound arguments occupy a person's mind but produce no beneficial effect -- or even produce harm -- that too is the worst ignorance. This applies equally to individuals and to states. You understand, I trust, that I am speaking of something entirely different from mere ignorance of a trade or craft."

"Yes, my friend, we understand and agree completely."
D: Elegant:
The greatest ignorance is the internal civil war of the soul: hating what one knows to be good while loving what one knows to be evil. This conflict between pleasure and reason is the worst and most widespread form of ignorance, because it affects the largest part of the soul -- the appetitive element, which is like the common populace in a state.

When this element rebels against reason, its natural ruler, that is folly -- just as when the masses refuse to obey rulers and laws. When sound reasoning dwells in the soul yet produces no good, that is the worst ignorance. This is entirely different from mere ignorance of a craft.
D: Summary: The Athenian defines the greatest ignorance as the internal conflict where a person's desires rebel against their own rational judgment -- loving what they know to be evil and hating what they know to be good. He draws an explicit analogy between the soul's structure and the state: the appetitive element is like the populace, and when it refuses to obey reason (its natural ruler), the result is folly -- whether in an individual or a city.
D: Distilled: The greatest ignorance is the soul's civil war: when pleasure rebels against reason's judgment, loving evil and hating good -- mirroring a state where the populace defies its laws.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Cleinias assents. -- This is one of the Laws' most important philosophical passages. The definition of ignorance as internal conflict between pleasure and reason directly parallels Republic 439a-441c (the tripartite soul) and the concept of akrasia (weakness of will) discussed in Protagoras 352a-358d. The soul-state analogy is the governing metaphor of the Republic (368d-369a, 441c-442d). The distinction between this 'greatest ignorance' and mere technical ignorance (ignorance of handicrafts) echoes Socrates' distinction in the Apology (22d-e) between genuine wisdom and craft-knowledge. Note that here Cleinias speaks the definition -- a dramatic touch showing that the interlocutors have internalized the Athenian's teaching method. The claim that the pleasure-seeking element is 'the great mass of the human soul' is psychologically astute and grounds the political argument: just as appetite dominates the individual soul, the masses dominate the state.

D: Original: Let us, then, in the first place declare and affirm that the citizen who does not know these things ought never to have any kind of authority entrusted to him: he must be stigmatized as ignorant, even though he be versed in calculation and skilled in all sorts of accomplishments, and feats of mental dexterity; and the opposite are to be called wise, even although, in the words of the proverb, they know neither how to read nor how to swim; and to them, as to men of sense, authority is to be committed. For, O my friends, how can there be the least shadow of wisdom when there is no harmony? There is none; but the noblest and greatest of harmonies may be truly said to be the greatest wisdom; and of this he is a partaker who lives according to reason; whereas he who is devoid of reason is the destroyer of his house and the very opposite of a saviour of the state: he is utterly ignorant of political wisdom. Let this, then, as I was saying, be laid down by us.
D: Narrative: "Let us, then, in the first place declare and affirm that the citizen who does not know these things ought never to have any kind of authority entrusted to him: he must be stigmatized as ignorant, even though he be versed in calculation and skilled in all sorts of accomplishments, and feats of mental dexterity; and the opposite are to be called wise, even although, in the words of the proverb, they know neither how to read nor how to swim; and to them, as to men of sense, authority is to be committed. For, O my friends, how can there be the least shadow of wisdom when there is no harmony? There is none; but the noblest and greatest of harmonies may be truly said to be the greatest wisdom; and of this he is a partaker who lives according to reason; whereas he who is devoid of reason is the destroyer of his house and the very opposite of a saviour of the state: he is utterly ignorant of political wisdom. Let this, then, as I was saying, be laid down by us."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us therefore establish this principle: a citizen who lacks this understanding should never be entrusted with any form of authority. He must be classified as ignorant, even if he is a mathematical genius, skilled in every accomplishment, and capable of extraordinary intellectual feats. Conversely, those who possess inner harmony between desire and reason should be called wise, even if -- as the old saying goes -- they can neither read nor swim. Authority should be given to them, as people of genuine understanding.

For, my friends, how can there be even a trace of wisdom without inner harmony? There cannot. The noblest and greatest of all harmonies is rightly called the greatest wisdom. The person who lives according to reason participates in this harmony. The person who lacks reason is the destroyer of his household and the very opposite of a savior of the state -- utterly devoid of political wisdom. Let us establish this as our foundational principle."
D: Elegant:
No citizen without inner harmony between desire and reason should ever hold authority -- regardless of intellectual accomplishments. Conversely, those with such harmony deserve to be called wise, even if they lack formal education.

True wisdom is the greatest harmony: living according to reason. Without it, a person is the destroyer of his household and the opposite of a statesman. Let this be our foundational principle.
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes the principle that authority should be entrusted only to those who possess inner harmony between desire and reason -- true political wisdom. Intellectual accomplishments without this harmony are worthless for governance, while genuine alignment of desire with reason constitutes wisdom even without formal learning. He defines true wisdom as the greatest harmony: living according to reason.
D: Distilled: True political wisdom is inner harmony between desire and reason, and only those who possess it should hold authority -- regardless of other intellectual accomplishments.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The proverb 'neither how to read nor how to swim' was a Greek expression for complete ignorance, used here paradoxically: those with moral wisdom outrank those with mere technical knowledge. This radically redefines political competence. The identification of wisdom with harmony (harmonia) draws on Pythagorean and musical traditions -- see Republic 430e-432a on temperance as harmony. The phrase 'destroyer of his house' versus 'saviour of the state' creates a stark binary that echoes the Homeric epithet soter (savior) applied to Zeus and political leaders. Compare Statesman 293d-e on the true statesman possessing knowledge. The principle that intellectual brilliance without moral wisdom disqualifies one from office challenges meritocratic assumptions based on technical competence.

D: Original:
Let it be so laid down.

I suppose that there must be rulers and subjects in states?

Certainly.

And what are the principles on which men rule and obey in cities, whether great or small; and similarly in families? What are they, and how many in number? Is there not one claim of authority which is always just-that of fathers and mothers and in general of progenitors to rule over their offspring?

There is.

Next follows the principle that the noble should rule over the ignoble; and, thirdly, that the elder should rule and the younger obey?

To be sure.

And, fourthly, that slaves should be ruled, and their masters rule?

Of course.

Fifthly, if I am not mistaken, comes the principle that the stronger shall rule, and the weaker be ruled?

That is a rule not to be disobeyed.
D: Narrative:
"Let it be so laid down."

"I suppose that there must be rulers and subjects in states?"

"Certainly."

"And what are the principles on which men rule and obey in cities, whether great or small; and similarly in families? What are they, and how many in number? Is there not one claim of authority which is always just -- that of fathers and mothers and in general of progenitors to rule over their offspring?"

"There is."

"Next follows the principle that the noble should rule over the ignoble; and, thirdly, that the elder should rule and the younger obey?"

"To be sure."

"And, fourthly, that slaves should be ruled, and their masters rule?"

"Of course."

"Fifthly, if I am not mistaken, comes the principle that the stronger shall rule, and the weaker be ruled?"

"That is a rule not to be disobeyed."
D: Refashioned:
"Let that be established."

"Now, there must necessarily be rulers and subjects in every state?"

"Of course."

"What principles determine who rules and who obeys, in cities large and small, and in families as well? How many such principles are there? First, there is one claim to authority that is always just: the right of parents -- fathers and mothers and ancestors generally -- to rule over their children."

"That is so."

"Second comes the principle that the well-born should rule over the common. Third, that the elder should rule and the younger obey."

"Certainly."

"Fourth, the principle that slaves should be ruled and their masters should rule."

"Of course."

"And fifth, if I am not mistaken, the principle that the stronger shall rule and the weaker be ruled."

"That is a principle not easily defied."
D: Elegant: Every state requires rulers and subjects. The principles of authority are: first, the always-just right of parents over children; second, the noble over the common; third, the elder over the younger; fourth, the master over the slave; fifth, the stronger over the weaker.
D: Summary: The Athenian begins cataloging the principles that determine who rules and who obeys. He identifies five claims to authority: parental right over children (always just), nobility over commonness, age over youth, master over slave, and strength over weakness.
D: Distilled: Five principles of authority govern political life: parentage, nobility, seniority, mastery, and strength.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- This enumeration of claims to political authority is one of Plato's most systematic treatments of the question 'who should rule?' It parallels and extends Aristotle's analysis in Politics 1284a-b. The five principles listed so far represent different traditional justifications: (1) natural/familial, (2) aristocratic, (3) gerontocratic, (4) institutional/servile, (5) might-makes-right. The Athenian notably calls only parental authority 'always just,' leaving the others implicitly qualified. The principle of the stronger ruling is attributed to nature and acknowledged as practically inescapable, but the Athenian will soon subordinate it to the sixth principle (wisdom). Compare Gorgias 483d-484a (Callicles' argument that the stronger should rule by nature) and Republic 338c (Thrasymachus's 'justice is the interest of the stronger').

D: Original:
Yes, and a rule which prevails very widely among all creatures, and is according to nature, as the Theban poet Pindar once said; and the sixth principle, and the greatest of all, is, that the wise should lead and command, and the ignorant follow and obey; and yet, O thou most wise Pindar, as I should reply him, this surely is not contrary to nature, but according to nature, being the rule of law over willing subjects, and not a rule of compulsion.

Most true.

There is a seventh kind of rule which is awarded by lot, and is dear to the Gods and a token of good fortune: he on whom the lot falls is a ruler, and he who fails in obtaining the lot goes away and is the subject; and this we affirm to be quite just.

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"Yes, and a rule which prevails very widely among all creatures, and is according to nature, as the Theban poet Pindar once said; and the sixth principle, and the greatest of all, is, that the wise should lead and command, and the ignorant follow and obey; and yet, O thou most wise Pindar, as I should reply him, this surely is not contrary to nature, but according to nature, being the rule of law over willing subjects, and not a rule of compulsion."

"Most true."

"There is a seventh kind of rule which is awarded by lot, and is dear to the Gods and a token of good fortune: he on whom the lot falls is a ruler, and he who fails in obtaining the lot goes away and is the subject; and this we affirm to be quite just."

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes -- and this principle of strength prevails widely among all living creatures and is 'according to nature,' as the Theban poet Pindar once declared. But the sixth principle, and the greatest of all, is that the wise should lead and command while the ignorant follow and obey. And yet, O most wise Pindar, I would reply to you that this rule of wisdom is not contrary to nature but is the truest expression of nature -- it is the rule of law over willing subjects, not rule by force."

"That is absolutely right."

"There is also a seventh principle of rule: selection by lot, which is dear to the gods and considered a mark of divine favor. Whoever the lot selects becomes the ruler; whoever it passes over becomes the subject. We declare this too to be just."

"Indeed."
D: Elegant:
The rule of the stronger prevails widely in nature, as Pindar declared. But the sixth and greatest principle is that the wise should rule the ignorant. This, contrary to what Pindar may imply, is not opposed to nature but is its truest expression -- law ruling willing subjects, not compulsion.

The seventh principle is selection by lot: dear to the gods, a token of fortune, and considered just.
D: Summary: The Athenian completes his catalog of authority: the fifth principle (rule of the strong) is widespread in nature, as Pindar noted. But the sixth and greatest is that the wise should rule the ignorant -- not through force but through law over willing subjects. The seventh is selection by lot, considered divinely sanctioned and just.
D: Distilled: The greatest principle of authority is that the wise should rule the ignorant through law and consent -- surpassing even the natural rule of the strong.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The reference is to Pindar's famous fragment 169a ('Law [Nomos] is the king of all, mortals and immortals alike... making just the most violent deed with a sovereign hand'), one of the most debated passages in Greek literature. Plato's clever response redefines 'according to nature': the rule of wisdom over willing subjects is more natural than brute force. This directly counters Callicles in Gorgias 484b, who used the same Pindar fragment to justify the strong dominating the weak. The seven principles of authority form a hierarchy culminating in wisdom -- the same hierarchy as the Republic's philosophical kingship (473c-d). The seventh principle (lot) reflects Athenian democratic practice where magistrates were often chosen by lot (sortition). By calling it 'dear to the Gods,' Plato gives qualified approval while keeping it subordinate to wisdom. Compare Statesman 291d-292a on the classification of constitutions.

D: Original: "Then now," as we say playfully to any of those who lightly undertake the making of laws, "you see, legislator, the principles of government, how many they are, and that they are naturally opposed to each other. There we have discovered a fountain-head of seditions, to which you must attend. And, first, we will ask you to consider with us, how and in what respect the kings of Argos and Messene violated these our maxims, and ruined themselves and the great and famous Hellenic power of the olden time. Was it because they did not know how wisely Hesiod spoke when he said that the half is often more than the whole? His meaning was, that when to take the whole would be dangerous, and to take the half would be the safe and moderate course, then the moderate or better was more than the immoderate or worse."
D: Narrative: "Then now," as we say playfully to any of those who lightly undertake the making of laws, "you see, legislator, the principles of government, how many they are, and that they are naturally opposed to each other. There we have discovered a fountain-head of seditions, to which you must attend. And, first, we will ask you to consider with us, how and in what respect the kings of Argos and Messene violated these our maxims, and ruined themselves and the great and famous Hellenic power of the olden time. Was it because they did not know how wisely Hesiod spoke when he said that the half is often more than the whole? His meaning was, that when to take the whole would be dangerous, and to take the half would be the safe and moderate course, then the moderate or better was more than the immoderate or worse."
D: Refashioned: "Now then," we might say half-playfully to anyone who casually takes up the work of legislation, "you see, legislator, how many principles of government there are, and how they naturally conflict with one another. Here we have found the very source of political strife, which demands your attention. First, let us consider how and in what way the kings of Argos and Messene violated these principles and destroyed both themselves and the great Hellenic power of ancient times. Was it because they failed to heed Hesiod's wise saying that 'the half is often more than the whole'? What Hesiod meant was this: when grasping for everything is dangerous and taking half would be the safe and moderate path, then moderation is worth more than excess -- the better course outweighs the worse."
D: Elegant:
The seven principles of government naturally conflict with each other -- and here is the very source of political sedition. The legislator must understand this.

The kings of Argos and Messene failed because they ignored Hesiod's maxim: 'the half is often more than the whole.' When taking everything is dangerous and moderation is safe, the moderate path is worth more than the excessive one.
D: Summary: The Athenian warns that the seven competing principles of authority naturally conflict, creating the perpetual source of political sedition. He traces the ruin of Argos and Messene to their kings' failure to understand Hesiod's principle of moderation: 'the half is often more than the whole' -- meaning that when grasping for total power is dangerous, taking less is actually gaining more.
D: Distilled: Competing principles of authority are the source of all political sedition, and kings who grasp for total power rather than practicing Hesiod's moderation destroy themselves.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- Hesiod's maxim 'the half is more than the whole' comes from Works and Days 40 and was one of the most famous lines in Greek wisdom literature. The Athenian reinterprets it as a principle of political moderation: rulers who limit their own power actually gain more than those who seize everything. This is the core lesson of the Dorian failure. The metaphor of a 'fountain-head of seditions' (stasis) identifies the fundamental problem of constitutional design: the competing claims to authority must be balanced, not resolved in favor of any single principle. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1301a-1302b on the causes of revolution (stasis). The playful address to the hypothetical legislator maintains the Laws' characteristic tone of serious play (spoudaiogeloion). The principle of moderation connects to Aristotle's later concept of the 'mean' (mesotes) and to the entire Greek tradition of meden agan ('nothing in excess').

D: Original:
Very true.

And may we suppose this immoderate spirit to be more fatal when found among kings than when among peoples?

The probability is that ignorance will be a disorder especially prevalent among kings, because they lead a proud and luxurious life.

Is it not palpable that the chief aim of the kings of that time was to get the better of the established laws, and that they were not in harmony with the principles which they had agreed to observe by word and oath? This want of harmony may have had the appearance of wisdom, but was really, as we assert, the greatest ignorance, and utterly overthrew the whole empire by dissonance and harsh discord.

Very likely.

Good; and what measures ought the legislator to have then taken in order to avert this calamity? Truly there is no great wisdom in knowing, and no great difficulty in telling, after the evil has happened; but to have foreseen the remedy at the time would have taken a much wiser head than ours.

What do you mean?

Any one who looks at what has occurred with you Lacedaemonians, Megillus, may easily know and may easily say what ought to have been done at that time.

Speak a little more clearly.

Nothing can be clearer than the observation which I am about to make.

What is it?
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"And may we suppose this immoderate spirit to be more fatal when found among kings than when among peoples?"

"The probability is that ignorance will be a disorder especially prevalent among kings, because they lead a proud and luxurious life."

"Is it not palpable that the chief aim of the kings of that time was to get the better of the established laws, and that they were not in harmony with the principles which they had agreed to observe by word and oath? This want of harmony may have had the appearance of wisdom, but was really, as we assert, the greatest ignorance, and utterly overthrew the whole empire by dissonance and harsh discord."

"Very likely."

"Good; and what measures ought the legislator to have then taken in order to avert this calamity? Truly there is no great wisdom in knowing, and no great difficulty in telling, after the evil has happened; but to have foreseen the remedy at the time would have taken a much wiser head than ours."

"What do you mean?"

"Any one who looks at what has occurred with you Lacedaemonians, Megillus, may easily know and may easily say what ought to have been done at that time."

"Speak a little more clearly."

"Nothing can be clearer than the observation which I am about to make."

"What is it?"
D: Refashioned:
"Indeed."

"And would not this immoderate spirit be even more dangerous in kings than in ordinary people?"

"It is likely that this kind of ignorance is especially common among kings, since they lead lives of pride and luxury."

"Is it not obvious that the kings of that era aimed above all to override the established laws -- that they were not in harmony with the principles they had sworn oaths to uphold? This disharmony may have looked like cunning wisdom, but it was actually the greatest ignorance, and it destroyed the entire empire through its discord."

"That seems very probable."

"Now, what should the legislator have done to prevent this catastrophe? Of course, it takes no great wisdom to diagnose a problem after it has happened, and no great difficulty to prescribe a remedy in hindsight. Foreseeing the solution at the time would have required far greater wisdom than ours."

"What are you suggesting?"

"Simply this: anyone who examines what happened in Sparta, Megillus, can easily determine what should have been done then."

"Be more specific."

"Nothing could be clearer than what I am about to say."

"Go on."
D: Elegant:
