Immoderate ambition is most dangerous in kings, whose proud and luxurious lives breed the greatest ignorance. The Dorian kings aimed to override the very laws they had sworn to uphold -- a disharmony that looked like cleverness but was in fact the worst ignorance, destroying the empire through discord.

Hindsight requires no wisdom. The real question is what the legislator should have done to prevent this -- and Sparta's example provides the answer.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that the immoderate spirit of the Dorian kings -- their drive to override established laws despite sworn oaths -- was especially dangerous because luxury and pride made royalty uniquely prone to this form of ignorance. While diagnosing the problem in hindsight is easy, he suggests that Sparta's constitutional history reveals the preventive measures that should have been taken.
D: Distilled: Kings are most susceptible to the greatest ignorance -- mistaking lawlessness for wisdom -- and Sparta's constitutional evolution reveals the remedy hindsight makes obvious.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. -- The observation that kings are especially prone to ignorance because of luxury connects to the Persian narrative that follows (3.37-3.41). The distinction between hindsight wisdom and foresight reflects a genuine philosophical problem about political knowledge -- see Statesman 294a-b on the difficulty of legislating for unforeseen circumstances. The Spartan constitutional example the Athenian is about to describe (dual kingship, the Gerousia, the Ephorate) represents Plato's most sustained praise of an actual existing constitution. The phrase 'dissonance and harsh discord' continues the musical metaphor for political harmony that runs through the Laws (cf. Book 2's discussion of music and education).

D: Original:
That if any one gives too great a power to anything, too large a sail to a vessel, too much food to the body, too much authority to the mind, and does not observe the mean, everything is overthrown, and, in the wantonness of excess runs in the one case to disorders, and in the other to injustice, which is the child of excess. I mean to say, my dear friends, that there is no soul of man, young and irresponsible, who will be able to sustain the temptation of arbitrary power-no one who will not, under such circumstances, become filled with folly, that worst of diseases, and be hated by his nearest and dearest friends: when this happens, his kingdom is undermined, and all his power vanishes from him. And great legislators who know the mean should take heed of the danger. As far as we can guess at this distance of time, what happened was as follows:-

What?
D: Narrative:
"That if any one gives too great a power to anything, too large a sail to a vessel, too much food to the body, too much authority to the mind, and does not observe the mean, everything is overthrown, and, in the wantonness of excess runs in the one case to disorders, and in the other to injustice, which is the child of excess. I mean to say, my dear friends, that there is no soul of man, young and irresponsible, who will be able to sustain the temptation of arbitrary power -- no one who will not, under such circumstances, become filled with folly, that worst of diseases, and be hated by his nearest and dearest friends: when this happens, his kingdom is undermined, and all his power vanishes from him. And great legislators who know the mean should take heed of the danger. As far as we can guess at this distance of time, what happened was as follows --"

"What?"
D: Refashioned:
"If you give too much power to anything -- too large a sail to a ship, too much food to the body, too much authority to the mind -- and fail to observe the mean, everything is thrown into chaos. Excess produces physical disorders in one case and injustice in the other, for injustice is the offspring of excess. Let me state this plainly, my friends: no human soul, especially a young and untested one, can withstand the temptation of absolute power. Without exception, such power fills a person with folly -- the worst of all diseases -- until they are hated by their closest friends and allies. When that happens, the kingdom collapses and all power evaporates. Legislators who understand the principle of the mean must guard against this danger. Based on what we can reconstruct from this distance in time, here is what happened --"

"Tell us."
D: Elegant:
Excess destroys everything: too large a sail capsizes a ship, too much food sickens the body, too much authority corrupts the mind. Injustice is the child of excess.

No human soul -- especially a young one -- can withstand unlimited power without being filled with folly. The result is universal hatred, collapsed authority, and vanished power. Wise legislators must observe the mean.
D: Summary: The Athenian articulates his central principle: excess in any domain leads to destruction, whether it is a sail too large for a ship or authority too great for a mind. No human soul can resist the corruption of unlimited power, which inevitably produces folly, alienation, and the collapse of authority. Great legislators must therefore design constitutions around the principle of the mean.
D: Distilled: No human soul can withstand unlimited power without becoming corrupt; excess always produces its own destruction, and wise constitutions must observe the mean.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus responds. -- The sailing metaphor is apt: Greek political vocabulary frequently drew on nautical imagery (kubernetes = pilot/governor). The claim that 'no soul of man' can withstand arbitrary power is one of Plato's most famous assertions about human nature -- it is the philosophical foundation for constitutional government over personal rule. Compare Republic 360b-c (the Ring of Gyges: would anyone resist corruption with absolute power?) and Lord Acton's 'power tends to corrupt.' The identification of folly as 'the worst of diseases' echoes 3.26's definition of the greatest ignorance. The principle of the mean (to meson/to metrion) here anticipates Aristotle's doctrine of the mean in Nicomachean Ethics 1106b-1107a, though Plato applies it institutionally rather than individually.

D: Original:
A God, who watched over Sparta, seeing into the future, gave you two families of kings instead of one; and thus brought you more within the limits of moderation. In the next place, some human wisdom mingled with divine power, observing that the constitution of your government was still feverish and excited, tempered your inborn strength and pride of birth with the moderation which comes of age, making the power of your twenty-eight elders equal with that of the kings in the most important matters. But your third saviour, perceiving that your government was still swelling and foaming, and desirous to impose a curb upon it, instituted the Ephors, whose power he made to resemble that of magistrates elected by lot; and by this arrangement the kingly office, being compounded of the right elements and duly moderated, was preserved, and was the means of preserving all the rest. Since, if there had been only the original legislators, Temenus, Cresphontes, and their contemporaries, as far as they were concerned not even the portion of Aristodemus would have been preserved; for they had no proper experience in legislation, or they would surely not have imagined that oaths would moderate a youthful spirit invested with a power which might be converted into a tyranny. Now that God has instructed us what sort of government would have been or will be lasting, there is no wisdom, as I have already said, in judging after the event; there is no difficulty in learning from an example which has already occurred. But if any one could have foreseen all this at the time, and had been able to moderate the government of the three kingdoms and unite them into one, he might have saved all the excellent institutions which were then conceived; and no Persian or any other armament would have dared to attack us, or would have regarded Hellas as a power to be despised.

True.
D: Narrative:
"A God, who watched over Sparta, seeing into the future, gave you two families of kings instead of one; and thus brought you more within the limits of moderation. In the next place, some human wisdom mingled with divine power, observing that the constitution of your government was still feverish and excited, tempered your inborn strength and pride of birth with the moderation which comes of age, making the power of your twenty-eight elders equal with that of the kings in the most important matters. But your third saviour, perceiving that your government was still swelling and foaming, and desirous to impose a curb upon it, instituted the Ephors, whose power he made to resemble that of magistrates elected by lot; and by this arrangement the kingly office, being compounded of the right elements and duly moderated, was preserved, and was the means of preserving all the rest.

Since, if there had been only the original legislators, Temenus, Cresphontes, and their contemporaries, as far as they were concerned not even the portion of Aristodemus would have been preserved; for they had no proper experience in legislation, or they would surely not have imagined that oaths would moderate a youthful spirit invested with a power which might be converted into a tyranny. Now that God has instructed us what sort of government would have been or will be lasting, there is no wisdom, as I have already said, in judging after the event; there is no difficulty in learning from an example which has already occurred. But if any one could have foreseen all this at the time, and had been able to moderate the government of the three kingdoms and unite them into one, he might have saved all the excellent institutions which were then conceived; and no Persian or any other armament would have dared to attack us, or would have regarded Hellas as a power to be despised."

"True."
D: Refashioned:
"A god watching over Sparta, foreseeing the future, gave you two royal families instead of one -- thus bringing the monarchy closer to moderation. Then a combination of human wisdom and divine guidance, recognizing that the constitution was still dangerously overheated, tempered the kings' inborn strength and aristocratic pride with the restraining influence of age: the council of twenty-eight elders was given power equal to the kings' on the most critical matters. But a third preserver, seeing that the government was still surging and overflowing, imposed yet another check -- the institution of the Ephors, whose power resembled that of magistrates chosen by lot. Through this arrangement, the kingship was composed of properly balanced elements and duly moderated, and its preservation in turn preserved everything else.

Had there been only the original legislators -- Temenus, Cresphontes, and their contemporaries -- not even Sparta's own share would have survived. They lacked real legislative experience, or they would never have imagined that mere oaths could restrain a young spirit armed with power that could easily become tyrannical. Now that events have shown us what kind of government endures, there is -- as I have said -- no special wisdom in judging after the fact. It is easy to learn from what has already happened. But if someone at the time had possessed the foresight to moderate and unite all three kingdoms, he might have preserved every excellent institution they had conceived. No Persian or any other military power would have dared to attack Greece or would have treated it as a power to be despised."

"That is true."
D: Elegant:
Sparta was saved by three successive checks on royal power. First, a god gave two royal families instead of one. Second, human wisdom created the council of twenty-eight elders with power equal to the kings'. Third, the Ephors were instituted as a further restraint, resembling magistrates chosen by lot. Each check moderated the constitution further, and the balanced kingship preserved everything.

The original Dorian legislators naively trusted oaths to restrain young men armed with potentially tyrannical power. Had someone foreseen the need for institutional moderation and united all three kingdoms, Greece would have been an invincible power that no Persian army would have dared to challenge.
D: Summary: The Athenian describes Sparta's three-stage constitutional moderation: dual kingship by divine providence, the Gerousia (council of twenty-eight elders) by human-divine wisdom, and the Ephorate as a final democratic check. Each institution further restrained royal power. He contrasts this with the original Dorian legislators' naive reliance on oaths, arguing that had all three kingdoms adopted such institutional checks and remained united, Greece would have been invincible against Persia.
D: Distilled: Sparta survived because divine and human wisdom progressively checked royal power through dual kingship, the Gerousia, and the Ephorate -- institutional moderation that oaths alone could never achieve.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Cleinias assents. -- This is the Laws' most detailed analysis of the Spartan constitution. The three 'saviours' correspond to: (1) the mythical division into two royal houses (Agiads and Eurypontids), (2) Lycurgus's institution of the Gerousia (council of 28 elders plus 2 kings = 30), and (3) the later institution of the Ephorate (traditionally attributed to Theopompus, 8th century BCE, or Chilon, 6th century). The metaphor of 'feverish,' 'swelling and foaming' for unchecked government is medical-political, connecting to the physician analogy from 3.17. The claim that oaths cannot restrain 'a youthful spirit invested with power' restates 3.32's principle that no soul withstands unlimited power. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1265b and 1313a on the Spartan constitution. The counterfactual about united Greek power against Persia connects to the actual Persian Wars narrative in 3.45-3.46.

D: Original: There was small credit to us, Cleinias, in defeating them; and the discredit was, not that the conquerors did not win glorious victories both by land and sea, but what, in my opinion, brought discredit was, first of all, the circumstance that of the three cities one only fought on behalf of Hellas, and the two others were so utterly good for nothing that the one was waging a mighty war against Lacedaemon, and was thus preventing her from rendering assistance, while the city of Argos, which had the precedence at the time of the distribution, when asked to aid in repelling the barbarian, would not answer to the call, or give aid. Many things might be told about Hellas in connection with that war which are far from honourable; nor, indeed, can we rightly say that Hellas repelled the invader; for the truth is, that unless the Athenians and Lacedaemonians, acting in concert, had warded off the impending yoke, all the tribes of Hellas would have been fused in a chaos of Hellenes mingling with one another, of barbarians mingling with Hellenes, and Hellenes with barbarians; just as nations who are now subject to the Persian power, owing to unnatural separations and combinations of them, are dispersed and scattered, and live miserably. These, Cleinias and Megillus, are the reproaches which we have to make against statesmen and legislators, as they are called, past and present, if we would analyse the causes of their failure, and find out what else might have been done. We said, for instance, just now, that there ought to be no great and unmixed powers; and this was under the idea that a state ought to be free and wise and harmonious, and that a legislator ought to legislate with a view to this end. Nor is there any reason to be surprised at our continually proposing aims for the legislator which appear not to be always the same; but we should consider when we say that temperance is to be the aim, or wisdom is to be the aim, or friendship is to be the aim, that all these aims are really the same; and if so, a variety in the modes of expression ought not to disturb us.
D: Narrative:
"There was small credit to us, Cleinias, in defeating them; and the discredit was, not that the conquerors did not win glorious victories both by land and sea, but what, in my opinion, brought discredit was, first of all, the circumstance that of the three cities one only fought on behalf of Hellas, and the two others were so utterly good for nothing that the one was waging a mighty war against Lacedaemon, and was thus preventing her from rendering assistance, while the city of Argos, which had the precedence at the time of the distribution, when asked to aid in repelling the barbarian, would not answer to the call, or give aid.

Many things might be told about Hellas in connection with that war which are far from honourable; nor, indeed, can we rightly say that Hellas repelled the invader; for the truth is, that unless the Athenians and Lacedaemonians, acting in concert, had warded off the impending yoke, all the tribes of Hellas would have been fused in a chaos of Hellenes mingling with one another, of barbarians mingling with Hellenes, and Hellenes with barbarians; just as nations who are now subject to the Persian power, owing to unnatural separations and combinations of them, are dispersed and scattered, and live miserably.

These, Cleinias and Megillus, are the reproaches which we have to make against statesmen and legislators, as they are called, past and present, if we would analyse the causes of their failure, and find out what else might have been done. We said, for instance, just now, that there ought to be no great and unmixed powers; and this was under the idea that a state ought to be free and wise and harmonious, and that a legislator ought to legislate with a view to this end. Nor is there any reason to be surprised at our continually proposing aims for the legislator which appear not to be always the same; but we should consider when we say that temperance is to be the aim, or wisdom is to be the aim, or friendship is to be the aim, that all these aims are really the same; and if so, a variety in the modes of expression ought not to disturb us."
D: Refashioned:
"There is little credit to Greece, Cleinias, in defeating the Persians. The disgrace was not that the victors failed to win glorious battles by land and sea -- they certainly did. The real disgrace was that of the three Dorian kingdoms, only one fought for Greece. Messene was waging a major war against Sparta, preventing it from offering full assistance. And Argos -- which held the position of precedence from the original settlement -- simply refused to answer the call when asked to help repel the barbarian.

Much could be said about Greece's conduct in that war that is far from honorable. In truth, we cannot properly say that 'Greece' repelled the invader at all. Had Athens and Sparta not acted together, every Greek people would have been thrown into a chaotic mixture -- Greeks jumbled together with Greeks, barbarians with Greeks, Greeks with barbarians -- just as the nations now under Persian control have been unnaturally divided and combined, scattered and dispersed, living in misery.

These are the charges we must bring against statesmen and legislators, past and present, if we want to understand why they failed and what should have been done differently. Our key principle has been that there should be no great, unchecked powers -- because a state should be free, wise, and harmonious, and legislation should aim at producing this condition. We should not be confused by the fact that we keep proposing different goals for the legislator -- temperance, wisdom, friendship. These are all expressions of the same fundamental aim, and the variety of language should not trouble us."
D: Elegant:
Greece deserves little credit for defeating Persia. Only one of three Dorian kingdoms fought; Messene was at war with Sparta, and Argos refused to help. Had Athens and Sparta not allied, all Greeks would have been scrambled into a chaotic fusion with barbarians -- as happened to the nations now under Persian rule.

The lesson for legislators: there must be no great unchecked powers. A state must be free, wise, and harmonious. When we variously name the aim as temperance, wisdom, or friendship, these are all the same fundamental goal.
D: Summary: The Athenian delivers a sobering assessment of the Persian Wars: Greece barely survived because only Sparta and Athens fought, while Messene and Argos failed to contribute. Without that alliance, Greek civilization would have been destroyed. He draws the legislative lesson that unchecked power must be avoided, and that temperance, wisdom, and friendship are merely different expressions of the same political goal: freedom, wisdom, and harmony.
D: Distilled: Greece's narrow survival against Persia -- saved only by the Athens-Sparta alliance -- proves that unchecked power destroys states, and that temperance, wisdom, and friendship are one legislative aim.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is a remarkable passage for an Athenian character to deliver: it credits Athens alongside Sparta while sharply criticizing the broader Greek failure. The mention of Argos's refusal echoes Herodotus 7.148-152. The Messenian War that distracted Sparta is the Third Messenian War (revolt of helots, c. 464 BCE), though the chronology is somewhat compressed. The nightmarish image of Greeks and barbarians 'fused in chaos' expresses a deep Greek cultural anxiety about loss of identity. The identification of temperance, wisdom, and friendship as different names for the same aim is a key philosophical move: it asserts the unity of the virtues (a Socratic thesis from Protagoras 329c-333b) at the political level. The principle against 'great and unmixed powers' is the Laws' central constitutional doctrine, anticipating Montesquieu's separation of powers and Madison's checks and balances in Federalist No. 51.

D: Original:
Let us resume the argument in that spirit. And now, speaking of friendship and wisdom and freedom, I wish that you would tell me at what, in your opinion, the legislator should aim.

Hear me, then: there are two mother forms of states from which the rest may be truly said to be derived; and one of them may be called monarchy and the other democracy: the Persians have the highest form of the one, and we of the other; almost all the rest, as I was saying, are variations of these. Now, if you are to have liberty and the combination of friendship with wisdom, you must have both these forms of government in a measure; the argument emphatically declares that no city can be well governed which is not made up of both.
D: Narrative:
"Let us resume the argument in that spirit. And now, speaking of friendship and wisdom and freedom, I wish that you would tell me at what, in your opinion, the legislator should aim."

"Hear me, then: there are two mother forms of states from which the rest may be truly said to be derived; and one of them may be called monarchy and the other democracy: the Persians have the highest form of the one, and we of the other; almost all the rest, as I was saying, are variations of these. Now, if you are to have liberty and the combination of friendship with wisdom, you must have both these forms of government in a measure; the argument emphatically declares that no city can be well governed which is not made up of both."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us continue in that spirit. Now, regarding friendship, wisdom, and freedom -- I would like you to tell me what you think the legislator's ultimate aim should be."

"Listen carefully: there are two fundamental forms of government from which all others derive. One may be called monarchy, the other democracy. Persia represents the fullest expression of monarchy; Athens represents the fullest expression of democracy. Nearly all other constitutions are variations of these two. Now, if you want to achieve liberty combined with friendship and wisdom, you must incorporate elements of both forms in measured proportions. Our argument makes one thing absolutely clear: no city can be well governed that does not blend both monarchy and democracy."
D: Elegant:
All constitutions derive from two mother forms: monarchy (exemplified by Persia) and democracy (exemplified by Athens). Most other systems are variations of these two.

To achieve liberty, friendship, and wisdom, a state must blend both forms. No city that relies exclusively on one can be well governed.
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies monarchy and democracy as the two fundamental forms of government from which all others derive, with Persia and Athens as their respective exemplars. He argues that a well-governed city must combine elements of both, since neither pure monarchy nor pure democracy alone can produce the combination of liberty, friendship, and wisdom that good government requires.
D: Distilled: All constitutions derive from two mother forms -- monarchy and democracy -- and good government requires a measured blend of both.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias asks; the Athenian Stranger responds. -- This is one of the most influential passages in the history of political theory. The 'mixed constitution' thesis -- that good government blends monarchical and democratic elements -- was adopted by Aristotle (Politics 1293b-1294a), Polybius (Histories 6.3-18, applied to the Roman Republic), Cicero (De Re Publica 1.45), and Montesquieu. The choice of Persia and Athens as polar examples is deliberate: the entire subsequent analysis (3.37-3.49) will show how each failed by excess. The phrase 'mother forms' (meteres politeion) suggests these are generative principles rather than merely descriptive categories. Compare Republic 544c-545a, which classifies five regime types in a different framework. The Laws' mixed constitution model represents Plato's mature departure from the Republic's philosopher-king ideal.

D: Original:
Impossible.

Neither the one, if it be exclusively and excessively attached to monarchy, nor the other, if it be similarly attached to freedom, observes moderation; but your states, the Laconian and Cretan, have more of it; and the same was the case with the Athenians and Persians of old time, but now they have less. Shall I tell you why?

By all means, if it will tend to elucidate our subject.
D: Narrative:
"Impossible."

"Neither the one, if it be exclusively and excessively attached to monarchy, nor the other, if it be similarly attached to freedom, observes moderation; but your states, the Laconian and Cretan, have more of it; and the same was the case with the Athenians and Persians of old time, but now they have less. Shall I tell you why?"

"By all means, if it will tend to elucidate our subject."
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly -- it cannot be done otherwise."

"A state exclusively and excessively committed to monarchy cannot achieve moderation, nor can one similarly devoted to unlimited freedom. Your states, however -- Sparta and Crete -- possess more of this balance. Athens and Persia once had it too, but both have lost ground. Shall I explain why?"

"Please do, if it will help clarify our subject."
D: Elegant: Neither pure monarchy nor pure democracy achieves moderation. Sparta and Crete possess more balance. Athens and Persia once had it but have since declined. The question is why.
D: Summary: The Athenian observes that neither exclusive monarchy nor exclusive democracy achieves moderation. Sparta and Crete currently possess the best balance, while Athens and Persia -- which once had it -- have since declined. He prepares to explain the causes of this deterioration.
D: Distilled: Sparta and Crete achieve the constitutional balance that Athens and Persia have lost through their respective excesses of democracy and monarchy.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- This passage sets up the extended parallel analysis of Persian and Athenian decline (3.37-3.49). The compliment to Spartan and Cretan moderation is diplomatic, flattering both interlocutors, but also substantive: Plato genuinely admired aspects of both constitutions. The claim that Athens once had moderation but has lost it is historically specific -- the Athenian is about to describe the pre-Persian War constitution as more balanced (3.45-3.46). The decline of both Persia and Athens serves as a double cautionary tale about the dangers of excess in either direction. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1296a on the middle constitution as the best, and Polybius 6.10 on the Roman mixed constitution as superior to purely monarchical or democratic forms.

D: Original: Hear, then:-There was a time when the Persians had more of the state which is a mean between slavery and freedom. In the reign of Cyrus they were freemen and also lords of many others: the rulers gave a share of freedom to the subjects, and being treated as equals, the soldiers were on better terms with their generals, and showed themselves more ready in the hour of danger. And if there was any wise man among them, who was able to give good counsel, he imparted his wisdom to the public; for the king was not jealous, but allowed him full liberty of speech, and gave honour to those who could advise him in any matter. And the nation waxed in all respects, because there was freedom and friendship and communion of mind among them.
D: Narrative: "Hear, then: There was a time when the Persians had more of the state which is a mean between slavery and freedom. In the reign of Cyrus they were freemen and also lords of many others: the rulers gave a share of freedom to the subjects, and being treated as equals, the soldiers were on better terms with their generals, and showed themselves more ready in the hour of danger. And if there was any wise man among them, who was able to give good counsel, he imparted his wisdom to the public; for the king was not jealous, but allowed him full liberty of speech, and gave honour to those who could advise him in any matter. And the nation waxed in all respects, because there was freedom and friendship and communion of mind among them."
D: Refashioned: "Listen, then. There was a time when the Persian state achieved a genuine balance between servitude and freedom. Under Cyrus, the Persians were free men who also ruled over many others. The rulers shared a degree of freedom with their subjects, treating soldiers as equals, which produced better relations between troops and commanders and greater willingness to face danger. If any wise person among them had good advice to offer, they could share it publicly, because the king was not jealous -- he permitted full freedom of speech and honored those who could counsel him well. The entire nation flourished because freedom, friendship, and shared purpose prevailed among them."
D: Elegant: Under Cyrus, Persia achieved the ideal balance between authority and freedom. Rulers shared freedom with subjects, soldiers were treated as equals, and the king welcomed wise counsel without jealousy. The nation flourished because freedom, friendship, and communion of mind prevailed.
D: Summary: The Athenian describes Cyrus's Persia as a model of balanced government: rulers shared freedom with subjects, military discipline was built on equality rather than fear, and the king freely welcomed wise counsel without jealousy. This combination of freedom, friendship, and shared purpose produced national flourishing.
D: Distilled: Cyrus's Persia flourished because it balanced authority with freedom, treating subjects as equals and welcoming wise counsel without royal jealousy.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This idealized portrait of Cyrus's Persia draws on Xenophon's Cyropaedia and Herodotus 1.125-130. The three qualities identified -- freedom (eleutheria), friendship (philia), and 'communion of mind' (koinonia nou) -- are precisely the goals the Athenian set for legislation in 3.34-3.35. The emphasis on parrhesia (freedom of speech) as essential to good governance is remarkable given Plato's reservations about democratic free speech elsewhere. The portrait implicitly contrasts with contemporary Persia under Artaxerxes, which had become despotic. The claim that treating soldiers as equals produced better military performance reflects actual military sociology -- contrast the Persian army's later reliance on mercenaries (3.44). Compare Xenophon's Cyropaedia 1.2.1-8 on Persian education and equality.

D: Original:
That certainly appears to have been the case.

How, then, was this advantage lost under Cambyses, and again recovered under Darius? Shall I try to divine?

The enquiry, no doubt, has a bearing upon our subject.

I imagine that Cyrus, though a great and patriotic general, had never given his mind to education, and never attended to the order of his household.

What makes you say so?
D: Narrative:
"That certainly appears to have been the case."

"How, then, was this advantage lost under Cambyses, and again recovered under Darius? Shall I try to divine?"

"The enquiry, no doubt, has a bearing upon our subject."

"I imagine that Cyrus, though a great and patriotic general, had never given his mind to education, and never attended to the order of his household."

"What makes you say so?"
D: Refashioned:
"That certainly seems to have been the case."

"Then how was this advantage lost under Cambyses, and how was it later recovered under Darius? Shall I attempt an explanation?"

"The question clearly bears on our subject."

"My guess is that Cyrus, for all his greatness as a patriotic military leader, never turned his attention to education, and never properly managed his own household."

"What leads you to that conclusion?"
D: Elegant: How was Persia's balanced constitution lost under Cambyses and recovered under Darius? The answer lies in Cyrus's great blind spot: though a brilliant general, he never attended to education or the proper ordering of his household.
D: Summary: The Athenian asks how Persia lost its constitutional balance under Cambyses and recovered it under Darius. He proposes that Cyrus, despite being a great military leader, fatally neglected education and the management of his own household -- setting the stage for his sons' corruption.
D: Distilled: Cyrus's fatal flaw was neglecting education and household order despite his military greatness, leading to Persia's constitutional decline under his successor.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- The sequence of Persian kings -- Cyrus, Cambyses, Darius -- follows Herodotus's narrative (Books 1-3). The critique of Cyrus as militarily great but educationally negligent is a deliberate provocation, given that Xenophon's Cyropaedia presents Cyrus as an ideal of education. The emphasis on household management (oikonomia) as a prerequisite for political leadership connects to the Greek analogy between household and state governance -- see Xenophon's Oeconomicus and Aristotle's Politics 1253b. The Athenian's claim to 'divine' rather than know echoes Socratic language about likely stories (eikos mythos) from the Timaeus. The question of how good constitutions degenerate is central to Republic 545d-576b.

D: Original:
I think that from his youth upwards he was a soldier, and entrusted the education of his children to the women; and they brought them up from their childhood as the favourites of fortune, who were blessed already, and needed no more blessings. They thought that they were happy enough, and that no one should be allowed to oppose them in any way, and they compelled every one to praise all that they said or did. This was how they brought them up.

A splendid education truly!

Such an one as women were likely to give them, and especially princesses who had recently grown rich, and in the absence of the men, too, who were occupied in wars and dangers, and had no time to look after them.

What would you expect?
D: Narrative:
"I think that from his youth upwards he was a soldier, and entrusted the education of his children to the women; and they brought them up from their childhood as the favourites of fortune, who were blessed already, and needed no more blessings. They thought that they were happy enough, and that no one should be allowed to oppose them in any way, and they compelled every one to praise all that they said or did. This was how they brought them up."

"A splendid education truly!"

"Such an one as women were likely to give them, and especially princesses who had recently grown rich, and in the absence of the men, too, who were occupied in wars and dangers, and had no time to look after them."

"What would you expect?"
D: Refashioned:
"I believe that Cyrus was a soldier from youth onwards and left his children's education entirely to the women. They raised the royal children as fortune's favorites -- already blessed, needing nothing more. They were taught that they were supremely happy, that no one should ever oppose them, and that everyone must praise whatever they said or did. That was their upbringing."

"What a magnificent education indeed!"

"Exactly the kind of education you would expect from women -- especially princesses who had recently become wealthy -- raising children in the absence of the men, who were away at war and had no time to supervise."

"What else could you expect?"
D: Elegant: Cyrus the soldier left his children's education to the palace women, who raised them as fortune's favorites: already blessed, needing nothing, never to be opposed, always to be praised. This was the education of princesses who had recently grown wealthy, raising children without fathers who were perpetually at war.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that Cyrus, being a lifelong soldier, delegated his children's education to the palace women. They raised the princes as spoiled favorites of fortune -- never opposed, always flattered -- the predictable result of wealthy princesses raising children in the absence of fathers consumed by war.
D: Distilled: Cyrus's sons were ruined by an upbringing of universal flattery and zero opposition, entrusted to wealthy palace women while their father was perpetually at war.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, with ironic interjections. -- The sarcastic 'splendid education truly!' is rare in Plato's dialogues and gives the passage a sharp, conversational quality. The critique of women's education is characteristically Platonic in its complexity: while seemingly misogynistic by modern standards, the actual blame falls on the structural conditions (absent fathers, sudden wealth, court flattery) rather than on women per se. Compare Republic 451c-457b, where Plato argues for equal education of women -- a radical position. The portrait of royal children raised in flattery directly anticipates the Persian analysis in 3.40-3.41 and connects to the Laws' broader argument that unearned privilege corrupts. The theme of education as the foundation of political stability is central to both the Republic and the Laws.

D: Original:
Their father had possessions of cattle and sheep, and many herds of men and other animals, but he did not consider that those to whom he was about to make them over were not trained in his own calling, which was Persian; for the Persians are shepherds-sons of a rugged land, which is a stern mother, and well fitted to produce sturdy race able to live in the open air and go without sleep, and also to fight, if fighting is required. He did not observe that his sons were trained differently; through the so-called blessing of being royal they were educated in the Median fashion by women and eunuchs, which led to their becoming such as people do become when they are brought up unreproved. And so, after the death of Cyrus, his sons, in the fulness of luxury and licence, took the kingdom, and first one slew the other because he could not endure a rival; and, afterwards, the slayer himself, mad with wine and brutality, lost his kingdom through the Medes and the Eunuch, as they called him, who despised the folly of Cambyses.

So runs the tale, and such probably were the facts.

Yes; and the tradition says, that the empire came back to the Persians, through Darius and the seven chiefs.

True.
D: Narrative:
"Their father had possessions of cattle and sheep, and many herds of men and other animals, but he did not consider that those to whom he was about to make them over were not trained in his own calling, which was Persian; for the Persians are shepherds -- sons of a rugged land, which is a stern mother, and well fitted to produce sturdy race able to live in the open air and go without sleep, and also to fight, if fighting is required. He did not observe that his sons were trained differently; through the so-called blessing of being royal they were educated in the Median fashion by women and eunuchs, which led to their becoming such as people do become when they are brought up unreproved.

And so, after the death of Cyrus, his sons, in the fulness of luxury and licence, took the kingdom, and first one slew the other because he could not endure a rival; and, afterwards, the slayer himself, mad with wine and brutality, lost his kingdom through the Medes and the Eunuch, as they called him, who despised the folly of Cambyses."

"So runs the tale, and such probably were the facts."

"Yes; and the tradition says, that the empire came back to the Persians, through Darius and the seven chiefs."

"True."
D: Refashioned:
"Cyrus possessed vast flocks of cattle and sheep, and governed great multitudes of people and other resources. But he failed to consider that his heirs were not trained in his own way of life -- the Persian way. The Persians are a shepherd people, sons of a harsh and unforgiving land, a stern mother that produces a hardy race capable of living outdoors, going without sleep, and fighting when necessary. Cyrus did not notice that his sons were being raised in a completely different manner. Under the supposed privilege of royalty, they received a Median education from women and eunuchs, which made them exactly what people always become when they grow up without discipline or correction.

After Cyrus died, his sons inherited the kingdom amid luxury and license. One brother killed the other, unable to tolerate a rival. Then the killer himself -- Cambyses, driven mad by drink and brutality -- lost the kingdom to the Medes and the man they called 'the Eunuch,' who held Cambyses' folly in contempt."

"So the story goes, and it was probably true."

"Yes, and tradition tells us the empire was restored to the Persians through Darius and the seven conspirators."

"Correct."
D: Elegant:
Cyrus failed to see that his heirs were not raised as Persians -- a shepherd people from harsh land, bred for endurance and combat. Instead, his sons received a Median court education from women and eunuchs, becoming what people always become when raised without correction.

The result: after Cyrus's death, one son killed the other out of jealousy, and the murderer Cambyses, mad with drink and violence, lost the empire to those who despised his folly. The empire was later restored through Darius and his seven co-conspirators.
D: Summary: The Athenian contrasts the hardy Persian shepherd culture that produced Cyrus with the luxurious Median court education his sons actually received. Raised without discipline by women and eunuchs, the sons proved catastrophic rulers: one committed fratricide, and Cambyses lost the empire through his own drunken brutality. Only Darius and his seven fellow conspirators later restored Persian power.
D: Distilled: Cyrus's sons, raised in Median luxury instead of Persian hardiness, destroyed through fratricide and madness what their father built -- until Darius restored the empire.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Cleinias confirms the tradition. -- The contrast between Persian ruggedness and Median luxury is a standard Greek trope (cf. Herodotus 1.71, 1.126). The narrative follows Herodotus 3.27-38 (Cambyses' madness), 3.61-79 (the conspiracy of the seven, including the 'false Smerdis' episode -- the 'Eunuch' or magus). The seven chiefs who restored Darius include figures like Otanes, who proposed democracy (Herodotus 3.80-82) -- the famous 'Constitutional Debate.' The phrase 'what people always become when they are brought up unreproved' states a universal law of moral psychology: character requires correction. This is the foundation of the Laws' educational program. Compare Republic 548a-550b on how oligarchic character develops through household corruption.

D: Original: Let us note the rest of the story. Observe, that Darius was not the son of a king, and had not received a luxurious education. When he came to the throne, being one of the seven, he divided the country into seven portions, and of this arrangement there are some shadowy traces still remaining; he made laws upon the principle of introducing universal equality in the order of the state, and he embodied in his laws the settlement of the tribute which Cyrus promised-thus creating a feeling of friendship and community among all the Persians, and attaching the people to him with money and gifts. Hence his armies cheerfully acquired for him countries as large as those which Cyrus had left behind him. Darius was succeeded by his son Xerxes; and he again was brought up in the royal and luxurious fashion. Might we not most justly say: "O Darius, how came you to bring up Xerxes in the same way in which Cyrus brought up Cambyses, and not to see his fatal mistake?" For Xerxes, being the creation of the same education, met with much the same fortune as Cambyses; and from that time until now there has never been a really great king among the Persians, although they are all called Great. And their degeneracy is not to be attributed to chance, as I maintain; the reason is rather the evil life which is generally led by the sons of very rich and royal persons; for never will boy or man, young or old, excel in virtue, who has been thus educated. And this, I say, is what the legislator has to consider, and what at the present moment has to be considered by us. Justly may you, O Lacedaemonians, be praised, in that you do not give special honour or a special education to wealth rather than to poverty, or to a royal rather than to a private station, where the divine and inspired lawgiver has not originally commanded them to be given. For no man ought to have pre-eminent honour in a state because he surpasses others in wealth, any more than because he is swift of foot or fair or strong, unless he have some virtue in him; nor even if he have virtue, unless he have this particular virtue of temperance.
D: Narrative:
"Let us note the rest of the story. Observe, that Darius was not the son of a king, and had not received a luxurious education. When he came to the throne, being one of the seven, he divided the country into seven portions, and of this arrangement there are some shadowy traces still remaining; he made laws upon the principle of introducing universal equality in the order of the state, and he embodied in his laws the settlement of the tribute which Cyrus promised -- thus creating a feeling of friendship and community among all the Persians, and attaching the people to him with money and gifts. Hence his armies cheerfully acquired for him countries as large as those which Cyrus had left behind him.

Darius was succeeded by his son Xerxes; and he again was brought up in the royal and luxurious fashion. Might we not most justly say: 'O Darius, how came you to bring up Xerxes in the same way in which Cyrus brought up Cambyses, and not to see his fatal mistake?' For Xerxes, being the creation of the same education, met with much the same fortune as Cambyses; and from that time until now there has never been a really great king among the Persians, although they are all called Great.

And their degeneracy is not to be attributed to chance, as I maintain; the reason is rather the evil life which is generally led by the sons of very rich and royal persons; for never will boy or man, young or old, excel in virtue, who has been thus educated. And this, I say, is what the legislator has to consider, and what at the present moment has to be considered by us. Justly may you, O Lacedaemonians, be praised, in that you do not give special honour or a special education to wealth rather than to poverty, or to a royal rather than to a private station, where the divine and inspired lawgiver has not originally commanded them to be given. For no man ought to have pre-eminent honour in a state because he surpasses others in wealth, any more than because he is swift of foot or fair or strong, unless he have some virtue in him; nor even if he have virtue, unless he have this particular virtue of temperance."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us follow the rest of the story. Note that Darius was not a king's son and had not been raised in luxury. When he took the throne as one of the seven conspirators, he divided the country into seven provinces -- an arrangement whose traces still faintly survive. He legislated on the principle of universal equality within the state and formalized the tribute system that Cyrus had promised, thereby generating friendship and community among the Persians and winning the people's loyalty through fair distribution. As a result, his armies willingly conquered territories as vast as those Cyrus had left behind.

But Darius was succeeded by his son Xerxes, who was raised in the same royal luxury as Cambyses. We might justly exclaim: 'Darius, how could you raise Xerxes the same way Cyrus raised Cambyses, without learning from that fatal error?' Xerxes, product of the same corrupting education, met essentially the same fate. And from that day to this, there has never been a truly great king among the Persians, despite the fact that they all bear the title 'Great.'

This decline is not a matter of chance. The cause is the corrupting life that the sons of the very wealthy and powerful typically lead. No person -- young or old, boy or man -- will ever excel in virtue who has been raised this way. This is what the legislator must consider, and what we must consider now. The Spartans deserve praise precisely because they do not award special honors or special education based on wealth rather than poverty, or royal birth rather than common status, except where their divinely inspired lawgiver commanded it. No one should receive pre-eminent honor in a state simply for being wealthier than others -- any more than for being a faster runner, more handsome, or physically stronger -- unless they also possess virtue. And even virtue alone is insufficient without the specific virtue of temperance."
D: Elegant:
Darius, not a king's son, ruled wisely: he divided the empire equally, established fair tribute, and won loyalty through justice. His armies willingly conquered as much as Cyrus had. But his son Xerxes, raised in luxury like Cambyses, met the same fate -- and since then Persia has produced no truly great king despite the title.

This is not chance but causation: wealth and royal privilege corrupt character. No one raised in unearned luxury achieves virtue. The Spartans rightly honor virtue rather than wealth or birth. No one deserves pre-eminent honor for riches, speed, beauty, or strength alone -- only for virtue, and specifically the virtue of temperance.
D: Summary: The Athenian contrasts Darius -- a non-royal who governed wisely through equality and fair distribution -- with his son Xerxes, whose luxurious upbringing replicated Cyrus's mistake with Cambyses. Since Xerxes, no Persian king has been truly great. The Athenian draws the universal lesson: wealth and privilege corrupt character, and honor should be awarded only for virtue, especially temperance. He praises Sparta for following this principle.
D: Distilled: Darius governed wisely because he was not raised in luxury, but his son Xerxes repeated Cambyses' downfall -- proving that unearned privilege inevitably corrupts, and only temperance deserves public honor.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is one of the Laws' most important passages on the relationship between education, wealth, and political virtue. Darius I's administrative reforms (the satrapy system, tribute regularization) are historically attested (Herodotus 3.89-97). The apostrophe to Darius ('how came you to raise Xerxes...') is a powerful rhetorical device. The claim that no Persian king since Xerxes has been 'really great' reflects Greek contempt for later Achaemenid rulers, though Artaxerxes I and II were competent administrators. The praise of Spartan egalitarianism is somewhat idealized -- Sparta had significant wealth inequality -- but the principle that honor should follow virtue rather than wealth is foundational to the Laws' political program. Compare Laws 631b-d and 697b on the hierarchy of goods. The final stipulation that even virtue requires temperance echoes 3.26-3.27's argument about the greatest ignorance.

D: Original:
What do you mean, Stranger?

I suppose that courage is a part of virtue?

To be sure.

Then, now hear and judge for yourself:-Would you like to have for a fellow-lodger or neighbour a very courageous man, who had no control over himself?

Heaven forbid!

Or an artist, who was clever in his profession, but a rogue?

Certainly not.

And surely justice does not grow apart from temperance?

Impossible.

Any more than our pattern wise man, whom we exhibited as having his pleasures and pains in accordance with and corresponding to true reason, can be intemperate?

No.

There is a further consideration relating to the due and undue award of honours in states.

What is it?

I should like to know whether temperance without the other virtues, existing alone in the soul of man, is rightly to be praised or blamed?

I cannot tell.

And that is the best answer; for whichever alternative you had chosen, I think that you would have gone wrong.

I am fortunate.

Very good; a quality, which is a mere appendage of things which can be praised or blamed, does not deserve an expression of opinion, but is best passed over in silence.

You are speaking of temperance?

Yes; but of the other virtues, that which having this appendage is also most beneficial, will be most deserving of honour, and next that which is beneficial in the next degree; and so each of them will be rightly honoured according to a regular order.

True.

And ought not the legislator to determine these classes?

Certainly he should.

Suppose that we leave to him the arrangement of details. But the general division of laws according to their importance into a first and second and third class, we who are lovers of law may make ourselves.

Very; good.
D: Narrative:
"What do you mean, Stranger?"

"I suppose that courage is a part of virtue?"

"To be sure."

"Then, now hear and judge for yourself: Would you like to have for a fellow-lodger or neighbour a very courageous man, who had no control over himself?"

"Heaven forbid!"

"Or an artist, who was clever in his profession, but a rogue?"

"Certainly not."

"And surely justice does not grow apart from temperance?"

"Impossible."

"Any more than our pattern wise man, whom we exhibited as having his pleasures and pains in accordance with and corresponding to true reason, can be intemperate?"

"No."

"There is a further consideration relating to the due and undue award of honours in states."

"What is it?"

"I should like to know whether temperance without the other virtues, existing alone in the soul of man, is rightly to be praised or blamed?"

"I cannot tell."

"And that is the best answer; for whichever alternative you had chosen, I think that you would have gone wrong."

"I am fortunate."

"Very good; a quality, which is a mere appendage of things which can be praised or blamed, does not deserve an expression of opinion, but is best passed over in silence."

"You are speaking of temperance?"

"Yes; but of the other virtues, that which having this appendage is also most beneficial, will be most deserving of honour, and next that which is beneficial in the next degree; and so each of them will be rightly honoured according to a regular order."

"True."

"And ought not the legislator to determine these classes?"

"Certainly he should."

"Suppose that we leave to him the arrangement of details. But the general division of laws according to their importance into a first and second and third class, we who are lovers of law may make ourselves."

"Very good."
D: Refashioned:
"What exactly do you mean, Stranger?"

"Well, courage is a part of virtue, is it not?"

"Certainly."

"Then consider: would you want a neighbor who was extremely courageous but had no self-control?"

"Absolutely not!"

"Or a professional artist who was brilliant at his craft but morally corrupt?"

"Of course not."

"And justice cannot develop apart from temperance, can it?"

"It cannot."

"No more than the truly wise person -- whose pleasures and pains align with reason -- could be intemperate."

"Correct."

"There is a further question about how honors should be distributed in states."

"What question?"

"Is temperance alone -- existing in the soul without the other virtues -- something to be praised or blamed?"

"I honestly cannot say."

"And that is the best possible answer. Either alternative would have been wrong."

"Lucky me."

"Indeed. A quality that merely accompanies things worthy of praise or blame -- without being independently praiseworthy or blameworthy -- should be passed over in silence rather than judged."

"You mean temperance?"

"Exactly. But among the other virtues, the one that combines this quality of temperance with the greatest practical benefit deserves the highest honor, the next most beneficial the second, and so on in order."

"That makes sense."

"And should the legislator not determine this ranking?"

"He should."

"Let us leave the details to the legislator. But the general classification of laws into a first, second, and third rank of importance -- that we lovers of law can work out ourselves."

"Agreed."
D: Elegant:
Courage without self-control makes a terrible neighbor. Skill without honesty makes a dangerous professional. Justice cannot exist apart from temperance, nor can wisdom coexist with intemperance.

Temperance alone -- without the other virtues -- is neither praiseworthy nor blameworthy; it is a necessary condition rather than an independent good. Among the virtues, the one combining temperance with the greatest benefit deserves the highest honor, and so on in order. The legislator should determine this ranking, though the broad classification of laws we can establish ourselves.
D: Summary: Through a series of examples, the Athenian demonstrates that courage, skill, and justice are all worthless or dangerous without temperance. But temperance alone, without the other virtues, is neither praiseworthy nor blameworthy -- it is a necessary condition. The virtues should be ranked for public honor by combining temperance with practical benefit, and the legislator should determine the specifics.
D: Distilled: Every virtue requires temperance as its necessary condition, and honors should be distributed according to virtues ranked by their combination of temperance with practical benefit.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The argument that temperance is a necessary condition for all other virtues but not independently sufficient is a subtle philosophical point. It qualifies the Republic's account of temperance as one of four independent cardinal virtues (430d-432b) by making it more like a meta-virtue or enabling condition. The neighbor examples are characteristically Socratic in their concreteness (compare Gorgias 474c-475e). Megillus's inability to say whether temperance alone is praiseworthy or blameworthy, and the Athenian's approval of his uncertainty, echoes Socratic aporia (acknowledging one's ignorance). The phrase 'lovers of law' (philonomoi) as a self-description for the three interlocutors parallels 'lovers of wisdom' (philosophoi) and gives the Laws its distinctive identity as a work of political philosophy grounded in practical legislation.

D: Original:
We maintain, then, that a State which would be safe and happy, as far as the nature of man allows, must and ought to distribute honour and dishonour in the right way. And the right way is to place the goods of the soul first and highest in the scale, always assuming temperance to be the condition of them; and to assign the second place to the goods of the body; and the third place to money and property. And it any legislator or state departs from this rule by giving money the place of honour, or in any way preferring that which is really last, may we not say, that he or the state is doing an unholy and unpatriotic thing?

Yes; let that be plainly declared.
D: Narrative:
"We maintain, then, that a State which would be safe and happy, as far as the nature of man allows, must and ought to distribute honour and dishonour in the right way. And the right way is to place the goods of the soul first and highest in the scale, always assuming temperance to be the condition of them; and to assign the second place to the goods of the body; and the third place to money and property. And if any legislator or state departs from this rule by giving money the place of honour, or in any way preferring that which is really last, may we not say, that he or the state is doing an unholy and unpatriotic thing?"

"Yes; let that be plainly declared."
D: Refashioned:
"We conclude, then, that any state which aims to be safe and happy -- to the extent human nature permits -- must distribute honor and dishonor correctly. The correct ordering places the goods of the soul first and highest, always on the condition that temperance underlies them. The goods of the body come second. Money and property come last. Any legislator or state that reverses this order -- giving money the place of highest honor, or in any way elevating what is truly last -- is committing an act that is both impious and unpatriotic."

"Yes -- let that be stated plainly and unequivocally."
D: Elegant: A safe and happy state must rank goods correctly: goods of the soul first (with temperance as their foundation), goods of the body second, money and property last. Any state or legislator that reverses this order -- honoring wealth above all -- commits an impious and unpatriotic act.
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes the fundamental hierarchy for legislation: goods of the soul (conditioned by temperance) rank first, bodily goods second, and material wealth last. Any reversal of this order -- especially elevating money to the highest position -- is declared both impious and unpatriotic.
D: Distilled: The just state ranks soul first, body second, and wealth last -- and any reversal of this hierarchy is both impious and unpatriotic.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus assents emphatically. -- This tripartite hierarchy of goods (soul, body, property) is a cornerstone of the Laws' political philosophy, appearing also at 631b-d, 697b, and 728d-729a. It draws on the Republic's analogy between the soul's three parts and the state's three classes (434d-435c) but simplifies it into a practical principle for legislation. The characterization of wealth-worship as 'unholy and unpatriotic' (anhosiou and apatriou) elevates the hierarchy from philosophical principle to sacred duty. This directly anticipates Adam Smith's later distinction between wealth of nations and moral sentiments, and modern debates about GDP as a measure of national success. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1323a-b on the hierarchy of goods and the good life.

D: Original:
The consideration of the Persian governments led us thus far to enlarge. We remarked that the Persians grew worse and worse. And we affirm the reason of this to have been, that they too much diminished the freedom of the people, and introduced too much of despotism, and so destroyed friendship and community of feeling. And when there is an end of these, no longer do the governors govern on behalf of their subjects or of the people, but on behalf of themselves; and if they think that they can gain ever so small an advantage for themselves, they devastate cities, and send fire and desolation among friendly races. And as they hate ruthlessly and horribly, so are they hated; and when they want the people to fight for them, they find no community of feeling or willingness to risk their lives on their behalf; their untold myriads are useless to them on the field of battle, and they think that their salvation depends on the employment of mercenaries and strangers whom they hire, as if they were in want of more men. And they cannot help being stupid, since they proclaim by actions that the ordinary distinctions of right and wrong which are made in a state are a trifle, when compared with gold and silver.

Quite true.

And now enough of the Persians, and their present maladministration of their government, which is owing to the excess of slavery and despotism among them.

Good.
D: Narrative:
"The consideration of the Persian governments led us thus far to enlarge. We remarked that the Persians grew worse and worse. And we affirm the reason of this to have been, that they too much diminished the freedom of the people, and introduced too much of despotism, and so destroyed friendship and community of feeling. And when there is an end of these, no longer do the governors govern on behalf of their subjects or of the people, but on behalf of themselves; and if they think that they can gain ever so small an advantage for themselves, they devastate cities, and send fire and desolation among friendly races. And as they hate ruthlessly and horribly, so are they hated; and when they want the people to fight for them, they find no community of feeling or willingness to risk their lives on their behalf; their untold myriads are useless to them on the field of battle, and they think that their salvation depends on the employment of mercenaries and strangers whom they hire, as if they were in want of more men. And they cannot help being stupid, since they proclaim by actions that the ordinary distinctions of right and wrong which are made in a state are a trifle, when compared with gold and silver."

"Quite true."

"And now enough of the Persians, and their present maladministration of their government, which is owing to the excess of slavery and despotism among them."

"Good."
D: Refashioned:
"Our examination of the Persian government has brought us this far. We observed that the Persians steadily deteriorated. The reason, we maintain, is that they excessively curtailed the people's freedom and imposed too much despotism, thereby destroying friendship and community spirit. Once these are gone, rulers no longer govern for the benefit of their subjects but only for themselves. If they see even the smallest advantage to be gained, they burn cities and bring devastation upon allied peoples. The hatred they inflict is matched by the hatred they receive. When they need the people to fight for them, they find no shared feeling, no willingness to risk lives on their behalf. Their vast armies are useless on the battlefield. They come to believe their only salvation lies in hiring foreign mercenaries, as though their problem were a shortage of soldiers rather than a shortage of loyalty. And they reveal their stupidity by demonstrating through their actions that the basic distinctions between right and wrong count for nothing compared to gold and silver."

"Exactly right."

"Let us say enough about the Persians and their current governmental failure, which stems from their excess of slavery and despotism."

"Agreed."
D: Elegant:
Persia's decline resulted from excessive despotism and the destruction of freedom, friendship, and community. Once these vanish, rulers govern for themselves alone, devastating cities and allied peoples for the smallest advantage. Hated as they hate, they find their vast armies useless -- no one will fight for them. They resort to mercenaries, revealing by their actions that gold and silver matter more than right and wrong.

Persia's failure is the failure of excessive slavery and despotism.
D: Summary: The Athenian summarizes Persia's decline: excessive despotism destroyed freedom and community, causing rulers to govern selfishly, devastate allies, and inspire universal hatred. Despite enormous armies, Persians could not get their subjects to fight willingly, resorting instead to mercenaries -- revealing that they valued gold over justice. This is the result of excessive slavery and despotism.
D: Distilled: Excessive despotism destroyed Persian community and loyalty, leaving rulers with vast useless armies, dependent on mercenaries, and morally bankrupt.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus assents. -- This passage completes the Persian case study. The observation that despotic rulers resort to mercenaries because their own subjects will not fight for them reflects actual 4th-century Persian military practice -- the reliance on Greek mercenaries (see Xenophon's Anabasis). The critique that despotism makes rulers govern 'on behalf of themselves' rather than their subjects anticipates the distinction between legitimate and corrupt regimes in Aristotle's Politics 1279a-b. The phrase 'proclaim by actions that right and wrong are a trifle compared with gold and silver' could describe many empires. The parallel between Persia's excess of despotism and Athens's coming excess of freedom (3.45-3.49) creates the dialogue's central structural symmetry. Compare Republic 562c-563e on the parallel corruptions of tyranny and extreme democracy.

D: Original: Next, we must pass in review the government of Attica in like manner, and from this show that entire freedom and the absence of all superior authority is not by any means so good as government by others when properly limited, which was our ancient Athenian constitution at the time when the Persians made their attack on Hellas, or, speaking more correctly, on the whole continent of Europe. There were four classes, arranged according to a property census, and reverence was our queen and mistress, and made us willing to live in obedience to the laws which then prevailed. Also the vastness of the Persian armament, both by sea and on land, caused a helpless terror, which made us more and more the servants of our rulers and of the laws; and for all these reasons an exceeding harmony prevailed among us. About ten years before the naval engagement at Salamis, Datis came, leading a Persian host by command of Darius, which was expressly directed against the Athenians and Eretrians, having orders to carry them away captive; and these orders he was to execute under pain of death. Now Datis and his myriads soon became complete masters of Eretria, and he sent a fearful report to Athens that no Eretrian had escaped him; for the soldiers of Datis had joined hands and netted the whole of Eretria. And this report, whether well or ill founded, was terrible to all the Hellenes, and above all to the Athenians, and they dispatched embassies in all directions, but no one was willing to come to their relief, with the exception of the Lacedaemonians; and they, either because they were detained by the Messenian war, which was then going on, or for some other reason of which we are not told, came a day too late for the battle of Marathon. After a while, the news arrived of mighty preparations being made, and innumerable threats came from the king. Then, as time went on, a rumour reached us that Darius had died, and that his son, who was young and hot-headed, had come to the throne and was persisting in his design.
D: Narrative:
"Next, we must pass in review the government of Attica in like manner, and from this show that entire freedom and the absence of all superior authority is not by any means so good as government by others when properly limited, which was our ancient Athenian constitution at the time when the Persians made their attack on Hellas, or, speaking more correctly, on the whole continent of Europe.

There were four classes, arranged according to a property census, and reverence was our queen and mistress, and made us willing to live in obedience to the laws which then prevailed. Also the vastness of the Persian armament, both by sea and on land, caused a helpless terror, which made us more and more the servants of our rulers and of the laws; and for all these reasons an exceeding harmony prevailed among us.

About ten years before the naval engagement at Salamis, Datis came, leading a Persian host by command of Darius, which was expressly directed against the Athenians and Eretrians, having orders to carry them away captive; and these orders he was to execute under pain of death. Now Datis and his myriads soon became complete masters of Eretria, and he sent a fearful report to Athens that no Eretrian had escaped him; for the soldiers of Datis had joined hands and netted the whole of Eretria. And this report, whether well or ill founded, was terrible to all the Hellenes, and above all to the Athenians, and they dispatched embassies in all directions, but no one was willing to come to their relief, with the exception of the Lacedaemonians; and they, either because they were detained by the Messenian war, which was then going on, or for some other reason of which we are not told, came a day too late for the battle of Marathon.

After a while, the news arrived of mighty preparations being made, and innumerable threats came from the king. Then, as time went on, a rumour reached us that Darius had died, and that his son, who was young and hot-headed, had come to the throne and was persisting in his design."
D: Refashioned:
"Now we must examine the government of Athens in the same way, to demonstrate that total freedom and the complete absence of authority are by no means as good as government that is properly limited -- which was the Athenian constitution at the time Persia attacked Greece, or more precisely, the entire continent of Europe.

In those days, Athens had four classes arranged by property census. Reverence was our sovereign and guide, making us willing to obey the laws in force. The sheer scale of the Persian military threat, by both land and sea, produced a deep fear that bound us even more closely to our rulers and our laws. For all these reasons, an extraordinary harmony prevailed among us.

About ten years before the naval battle of Salamis, the Persian general Datis arrived with an army under Darius's orders, specifically targeting the Athenians and Eretrians with instructions to enslave them -- on pain of death if he failed. Datis and his vast forces quickly conquered Eretria completely. He sent a terrifying message to Athens claiming that not a single Eretrian had escaped -- that his soldiers had literally joined hands and swept the entire island. Whether accurate or exaggerated, this report was horrifying to all Greeks, especially the Athenians. We sent ambassadors in every direction, but no one would come to our aid except the Spartans -- and they, whether delayed by the ongoing Messenian War or for some other unknown reason, arrived a day after the battle of Marathon.

Subsequently, reports came of massive new preparations and endless threats from the Persian king. Eventually word reached us that Darius had died and been succeeded by his son -- a young, hot-tempered prince who was determined to continue his father's campaign."
D: Elegant:
Athens must now be examined: total freedom without authority is no better than Persia's total despotism. The old Athenian constitution -- four property classes, rule of law, reverence as sovereign -- produced exceptional harmony, strengthened by fear of the Persian threat.

Datis conquered Eretria completely and sent terrifying reports to Athens. No Greek ally came to help except Sparta, which arrived a day too late for Marathon. Then came news of even greater Persian preparations, Darius's death, and the accession of his hot-headed son Xerxes.
D: Summary: The Athenian turns to Athens's own history, arguing that its ancient constitution -- with four property classes and reverence for law -- was far superior to the unlimited freedom that followed. He recounts the Persian War crisis: the terror of Datis's conquest of Eretria, Athens's isolation before Marathon (with Sparta arriving too late), and the escalating threat culminating in Xerxes' accession.
D: Distilled: Ancient Athens thrived under a constitution of limited authority and reverence for law, tested and strengthened by the crisis of the Persian Wars from Marathon to the accession of Xerxes.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This begins the Athenian case study, paralleling the Persian one. The Solonic four-class property system (pentakosiomedimnoi, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes) is presented as the model of balanced government. The concept of aidos (reverence) as 'queen and mistress' is crucial -- it is not fear of punishment but an internalized respect for law and custom. The Marathon narrative follows Herodotus 6.94-120. The detail about Datis's soldiers 'joining hands' to sweep Eretria is from Herodotus 6.101. Sparta's late arrival at Marathon (Herodotus 6.106, 6.120) -- delayed by the Karneia festival or the Messenian revolt -- is diplomatically noted without blame. The characterization of Xerxes as 'young and hot-headed' echoes Herodotus 7.5-11. The entire passage demonstrates how external threat can produce internal harmony -- a theme with obvious contemporary resonance.

D: Original:
The Athenians were under the impression that the whole expedition was directed against them, in consequence of the battle of Marathon; and hearing of the bridge over the Hellespont, and the canal of Athos, and the host of ships, considering that there was no salvation for them either by land or by sea, for there was no one to help them, and remembering that in the first expedition, when the Persians destroyed Eretria, no one came to their help, or would risk the danger of an alliance with them, they thought that this would happen again, at least on land; nor, when they looked to the sea, could they descry any hope of salvation; for they were attacked by a thousand vessels and more. One chance of safety remained, slight indeed and desperate, but their only one. They saw that on the former occasion they had gained a seemingly impossible victory, and borne up by this hope, they found that their only refuge was in themselves and in the Gods. All these things created in them the spirit of friendship; there was the fear of the moment, and there was that higher fear, which they had acquired by obedience to their ancient laws, and which I have several times in the preceding discourse called reverence, of which the good man ought to be a willing servant, and of which the coward is independent and fearless. If this fear had not possessed them, they would never have met the enemy, or defended their temples and sepulchres and their country, and everything that was near and dear to them, as they did; but little by little they would have been all scattered and dispersed.

Your words, Athenian, are quite true, and worthy of yourself and of your country.
D: Narrative:
