"The Athenians were under the impression that the whole expedition was directed against them, in consequence of the battle of Marathon; and hearing of the bridge over the Hellespont, and the canal of Athos, and the host of ships, considering that there was no salvation for them either by land or by sea, for there was no one to help them, and remembering that in the first expedition, when the Persians destroyed Eretria, no one came to their help, or would risk the danger of an alliance with them, they thought that this would happen again, at least on land; nor, when they looked to the sea, could they descry any hope of salvation; for they were attacked by a thousand vessels and more.

One chance of safety remained, slight indeed and desperate, but their only one. They saw that on the former occasion they had gained a seemingly impossible victory, and borne up by this hope, they found that their only refuge was in themselves and in the Gods. All these things created in them the spirit of friendship; there was the fear of the moment, and there was that higher fear, which they had acquired by obedience to their ancient laws, and which I have several times in the preceding discourse called reverence, of which the good man ought to be a willing servant, and of which the coward is independent and fearless. If this fear had not possessed them, they would never have met the enemy, or defended their temples and sepulchres and their country, and everything that was near and dear to them, as they did; but little by little they would have been all scattered and dispersed."

"Your words, Athenian, are quite true, and worthy of yourself and of your country."
D: Refashioned:
"The Athenians believed Xerxes' entire expedition was directed at them as punishment for Marathon. When they heard about the bridge across the Hellespont, the canal cut through Mount Athos, and the fleet of over a thousand ships, they saw no hope of salvation by land or sea. No one would help them. They remembered that during the first invasion, when Eretria was destroyed, not a single ally had come forward or risked an alliance. They assumed the same abandonment would happen again, at least on land. And at sea, facing more than a thousand warships, they could see no way out.

One chance of survival remained -- slim and desperate, but the only one they had. They recalled that at Marathon they had won what seemed an impossible victory. Sustained by that memory, they recognized that their only refuge was in themselves and in the gods. All of this generated an intense spirit of fellowship among them. There was the immediate fear of the Persian threat, and there was a deeper fear -- that reverence acquired through long obedience to their ancient laws, which I have repeatedly called the foundation of civic virtue. The good person willingly submits to this reverence; the coward neither feels it nor is governed by it. Had this reverence not possessed the Athenians, they would never have confronted the enemy, never defended their temples, ancestral tombs, their homeland, and everything they held dear. Instead, they would have gradually scattered and disappeared."

"Your words, Athenian, are entirely true and worthy both of you and of your country."
D: Elegant:
Athens faced Xerxes' overwhelming force with no allies, no escape by land or sea, and only the desperate hope that Marathon's impossible victory might be repeated. Their only refuge was in themselves and the gods.

What saved them was the spirit of friendship born from two kinds of fear: the immediate terror of the Persian threat, and the deeper reverence acquired through long obedience to ancient laws. This reverence -- which the good person willingly serves and the coward ignores -- was what drove them to defend their temples, tombs, and homeland. Without it, they would have scattered and vanished.
D: Summary: Facing Xerxes' overwhelming invasion with no allies, the Athenians found salvation in the spirit of fellowship created by two forms of fear: the immediate terror of the Persian threat and the deeper reverence cultivated by long obedience to their ancient laws. This reverence -- not mere panic -- enabled them to stand and fight rather than scatter. Megillus praises the Athenian's words as true and worthy of his country.
D: Distilled: Athens survived the Persian invasion because civic reverence -- cultivated by long obedience to law, not mere fear -- created the fellowship and courage needed to face overwhelming odds.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus responds with generous praise. -- The bridge over the Hellespont, canal of Athos, and thousand-ship fleet are from Herodotus 7.33-37, 7.22-24, 7.89. The distinction between immediate fear (phobos) and reverence (aidos) is philosophically significant: aidos is the internalized respect for law and the opinions of the virtuous, while phobos is merely reactive. See Laws 671d and 699c for further development. The claim that Athens's strength came from civic fellowship (philia) created by shared reverence -- not from military advantage -- makes this a political-philosophical argument, not merely patriotic history. Megillus's generous tribute ('worthy of yourself and of your country') is dramatic: a Spartan praising an Athenian's patriotism represents the kind of cross-state friendship the dialogue itself embodies. Compare Thucydides 1.73-78 (the Athenian speech at Sparta defending their Persian War record).

D: Original:
They are true, Megillus; and to you, who have inherited the virtues of your ancestors, I may properly speak of the actions of that day. And I would wish you and Cleinias to consider whether my words have not also a bearing on legislation; for I am not discoursing only for the pleasure of talking, but for the argument's sake. Please to remark that the experience both of ourselves and the Persians was, in a certain sense, the same; for as they led their people into utter servitude, so we too led ours into all freedom. And now, how shall we proceed? for I would like you to observe that our previous arguments have good deal to say for themselves.

True; but I wish that you would give us a fuller explanation.

I will. Under the ancient laws, my friends, the people was not as now the master, but rather the willing servant of the laws.

What laws do you mean?
D: Narrative:
"They are true, Megillus; and to you, who have inherited the virtues of your ancestors, I may properly speak of the actions of that day. And I would wish you and Cleinias to consider whether my words have not also a bearing on legislation; for I am not discoursing only for the pleasure of talking, but for the argument's sake. Please to remark that the experience both of ourselves and the Persians was, in a certain sense, the same; for as they led their people into utter servitude, so we too led ours into all freedom. And now, how shall we proceed? for I would like you to observe that our previous arguments have good deal to say for themselves."

"True; but I wish that you would give us a fuller explanation."

"I will. Under the ancient laws, my friends, the people was not as now the master, but rather the willing servant of the laws."

"What laws do you mean?"
D: Refashioned:
"They are true, Megillus, and to you who have inherited your ancestors' virtues, I may fittingly speak of those events. But I want both you and Cleinias to consider whether my words also bear directly on legislation -- for I am not speaking for the pleasure of conversation but for the sake of the argument. Notice that the experience of Athens and Persia was, in a crucial sense, the same: just as the Persians led their people into total servitude, we Athenians led ours into unlimited freedom. Now, how should we proceed? Our earlier arguments have considerable force here."

"True, but I would like a fuller explanation."

"Very well. Under the ancient laws, my friends, the people were not the masters, as they are now. They were rather the willing servants of the laws."

"Which laws do you mean?"
D: Elegant:
The Athenian and Persian experiences mirror each other: Persia led its people into total servitude, Athens into unlimited freedom. Both extremes are destructive.

Under the ancient laws, the Athenian people were not -- as they are now -- the masters. They were the willing servants of the laws.
D: Summary: The Athenian draws the structural parallel between Persian and Athenian decline: Persia fell into total servitude, Athens into total freedom. Both extremes are equally destructive. He asserts that under Athens's ancient constitution, the people willingly served the laws rather than mastering them -- unlike the current democratic arrangement.
D: Distilled: Athens and Persia represent mirror-image failures: total servitude and total freedom are equally destructive, and the ancient Athenians wisely served the laws rather than mastering them.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus responds. -- The symmetry between Persian despotism and Athenian democracy as mirror-image extremes is the structural core of Book 3's political analysis. The phrase 'willing servant of the laws' (hekousios doulos ton nomon) is a paradox that encapsulates the Laws' political philosophy: true freedom consists in voluntary obedience to good law. Compare Republic 563d-e on democracy where 'the father fears his son' and 'the schoolmaster flatters his pupils.' The contrast between ancient and contemporary Athens reflects nostalgia for the 'ancestral constitution' (patrios politeia) -- a powerful political concept in 4th-century Athenian debates (see Aristotle, Constitution of Athens 29.3). The transition to discussing music and cultural law (3.48-3.49) as the mechanism of Athens's decline is original and influential.

D: Original: In the first place, let us speak of the laws about music-that is to say, such music as then existed-in order that we may trace the growth of the excess of freedom from the beginning. Now music was early divided among us into certain kinds and manners. One sort consisted of prayers to the Gods, which were called hymns; and there was another and opposite sort called lamentations, and another termed paeans, and another, celebrating the birth of Dionysus, called, I believe, "dithyrambs." And they used the actual word "laws," or nomoi, for another kind of song; and to this they added the term "citharoedic." All these and others were duly distinguished, nor were the performers allowed to confuse one style of music with another. And the authority which determined and gave judgment, and punished the disobedient, was not expressed in a hiss, nor in the most unmusical shouts of the multitude, as in our days, nor in applause and clapping of hands. But the directors of public instruction insisted that the spectators should listen in silence to the end; and boys and their tutors, and the multitude in general, were kept quiet by a hint from a stick.
D: Narrative:
"In the first place, let us speak of the laws about music -- that is to say, such music as then existed -- in order that we may trace the growth of the excess of freedom from the beginning. Now music was early divided among us into certain kinds and manners. One sort consisted of prayers to the Gods, which were called hymns; and there was another and opposite sort called lamentations, and another termed paeans, and another, celebrating the birth of Dionysus, called, I believe, 'dithyrambs.' And they used the actual word 'laws,' or nomoi, for another kind of song; and to this they added the term 'citharoedic.' All these and others were duly distinguished, nor were the performers allowed to confuse one style of music with another.

And the authority which determined and gave judgment, and punished the disobedient, was not expressed in a hiss, nor in the most unmusical shouts of the multitude, as in our days, nor in applause and clapping of hands. But the directors of public instruction insisted that the spectators should listen in silence to the end; and boys and their tutors, and the multitude in general, were kept quiet by a hint from a stick."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us begin with the laws about music -- as it existed in the early days -- so that we can trace the growth of excessive freedom from its origins. In those times, music was carefully divided into distinct genres. One type consisted of prayers to the gods, called hymns. There was a contrasting type called lamentations. Another was the paean; another, celebrating the birth of Dionysus, was called the dithyramb. Significantly, they used the word nomoi -- the same word for 'laws' -- for yet another category of song, specifically the citharoedic form. All these genres and others were strictly distinguished, and performers were not permitted to mix one style with another.

The authority that judged performances and punished violators was not the hissing or unmusical shouting of the crowd, as it is today, nor applause and clapping. Instead, the directors of public education insisted that audiences listen in silence until the end. Boys, their tutors, and the general public were kept quiet -- enforced, if necessary, by the threat of a stick."
D: Elegant:
Ancient Athenian music was divided into strict, legally distinct genres: hymns (prayers), lamentations, paeans, dithyrambs (for Dionysus), and nomoi (citharoedic songs -- sharing their name with 'laws'). Performers could not mix genres.

Judgment came not from crowd noise -- hissing, shouting, or applause -- but from educated directors of instruction who enforced silent, attentive listening. Order was maintained by authority, not popular reaction.
D: Summary: The Athenian begins tracing Athens's decline through its musical culture. In the early period, music was strictly categorized into distinct genres -- hymns, lamentations, paeans, dithyrambs, and nomoi (laws/songs) -- and performers could not mix them. Judgment was rendered by educated authorities, not by crowd reaction, and audiences were required to listen in respectful silence.
D: Distilled: Ancient Athens maintained strict musical genres judged by educated authorities in silence, embodying the ordered cultural discipline that preceded democratic excess.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is one of Plato's most original and influential passages: the theory that cultural decline causes political decline. The double meaning of nomos (law/musical mode) is deliberately exploited -- lawful music literally embodies legal order. The strict genre distinctions reflect actual early Greek musical practice. The hymn, lamentation, paean, and dithyramb were real genres associated with different gods and occasions. The critique of audience noise anticipates modern debates about 'dumbing down' culture. Compare Republic 398c-401d on the regulation of musical modes and rhythms. The 'directors of public instruction' correspond to historical Athenian officials. The transition from musical to political lawlessness (3.49-3.50) is the Laws' most famous cultural argument. The 'stick' as enforcer of order is characteristically blunt.

D: Original:
Such was the good order which the multitude were willing to observe; they would never have dared to give judgment by noisy cries. And then, as time went on, the poets themselves introduced the reign of vulgar and lawless innovation. They were men of genius, but they had no perception of what is just and lawful in music; raging like Bacchanals and possessed with inordinate delights-mingling lamentations with hymns, and paeans with dithyrambs; imitating the sounds of the flute on the lyre, and making one general confusion; ignorantly affirming that music has no truth, and, whether good or bad, can only be judged of rightly by the pleasure of the hearer. And by composing such licentious works, and adding to them words as licentious, they have inspired the multitude with lawlessness and boldness, and made them fancy that they can judge for themselves about melody and song. And in this way the theatres from being mute have become vocal, as though they had understanding of good and bad in music and poetry; and instead of an aristocracy, an evil sort of theatrocracy has grown up. For if the democracy which judged had only consisted of educated persons, no fatal harm would have been done; but in music there first arose the universal conceit of omniscience and general lawlessness;-freedom came following afterwards, and men, fancying that they knew what they did not know, had no longer any fear, and the absence of fear begets shamelessness. For what is this shamelessness, which is so evil a thing, but the insolent refusal to regard the opinion of the better by reason of an over-daring sort of liberty?

Very true.
D: Narrative:
"Such was the good order which the multitude were willing to observe; they would never have dared to give judgment by noisy cries. And then, as time went on, the poets themselves introduced the reign of vulgar and lawless innovation. They were men of genius, but they had no perception of what is just and lawful in music; raging like Bacchanals and possessed with inordinate delights -- mingling lamentations with hymns, and paeans with dithyrambs; imitating the sounds of the flute on the lyre, and making one general confusion; ignorantly affirming that music has no truth, and, whether good or bad, can only be judged of rightly by the pleasure of the hearer.

And by composing such licentious works, and adding to them words as licentious, they have inspired the multitude with lawlessness and boldness, and made them fancy that they can judge for themselves about melody and song. And in this way the theatres from being mute have become vocal, as though they had understanding of good and bad in music and poetry; and instead of an aristocracy, an evil sort of theatrocracy has grown up.

For if the democracy which judged had only consisted of educated persons, no fatal harm would have been done; but in music there first arose the universal conceit of omniscience and general lawlessness; freedom came following afterwards, and men, fancying that they knew what they did not know, had no longer any fear, and the absence of fear begets shamelessness. For what is this shamelessness, which is so evil a thing, but the insolent refusal to regard the opinion of the better by reason of an over-daring sort of liberty?"

"Very true."
D: Refashioned:
"The people willingly maintained this discipline; no one dared give judgment through shouting or noise. But as time passed, the poets themselves introduced a reign of vulgar, lawless innovation. They were talented, certainly, but they had no understanding of what was just or proper in music. Possessed like Bacchanals, driven by unrestrained appetite for novelty, they mixed lamentations with hymns and paeans with dithyrambs, imitated flutes on lyres, and created general confusion. They ignorantly declared that music contains no objective truth and can only be judged by the pleasure it gives the listener.

By composing such undisciplined works -- with equally undisciplined lyrics -- they inspired the masses with lawlessness and presumption, convincing them that they were qualified to judge music and poetry for themselves. The theaters, once silent and attentive, became noisy and opinionated, as if the crowd genuinely understood artistic quality. In place of an aristocracy of educated judgment, an evil 'theatrocracy' arose.

Had this democratic judgment consisted only of educated people, no serious harm would have been done. But what began in music as universal pretension to expertise and general lawlessness soon spread everywhere. Freedom of the wrong kind followed, and people -- imagining they knew what they did not know -- lost all sense of reverence. The absence of reverence produces shamelessness. And what is shamelessness but the arrogant refusal to defer to the judgment of one's betters, born from an excessive, reckless sort of liberty?"

"That is absolutely right."
D: Elegant:
Innovative but undisciplined poets destroyed musical order: mixing genres, denying objective standards, and declaring pleasure the only judge. This inspired the masses with the presumption that they could judge art for themselves. Silent theaters became noisy, and rule by educated taste gave way to 'theatrocracy' -- mob aesthetic judgment.

The pretension to universal expertise that began in music spread to everything. False confidence replaced reverence, and the absence of reverence bred shamelessness -- the arrogant refusal to defer to the better-informed, born of reckless liberty.
D: Summary: The Athenian traces Athens's political decline to a cultural revolution: genius poets without moral understanding mixed genres, denied objective musical truth, and declared pleasure the only criterion. This empowered the masses to believe they could judge art, transforming theater from orderly aristocratic judgment into chaotic 'theatrocracy.' The resulting pretension to universal expertise spread from music to all of life, destroying reverence and breeding the shamelessness that is the essence of excessive liberty.
D: Distilled: Athens's decline began when poets destroyed musical standards, empowering the masses to judge by pleasure alone -- creating a 'theatrocracy' whose pretension to universal expertise destroyed reverence and bred shamelessness.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus assents. -- The term 'theatrocracy' (theatrokratia) is Plato's coinage and one of the most memorable terms in political philosophy -- rule by the theater audience, meaning cultural populism as political force. The argument traces a causal chain: artistic innovation without standards leads to cultural relativism ('music has no truth'), which empowers popular judgment, which breeds presumption in all areas, which destroys reverence, which produces shamelessness and political lawlessness. This is arguably the first systematic 'culture wars' argument in Western thought. Compare Republic 607b-608b (the ancient quarrel between philosophy and poetry). The critique of pleasure as the sole criterion of art echoes Gorgias 501d-502d. The innovation-to-lawlessness sequence parallels Thucydides 3.82-83 on the corruption of language during the Corcyrean revolution. The Bacchanal imagery connects to Laws Book 2's discussion of Dionysian festivals.

D: Original: Consequent upon this freedom comes the other freedom, of disobedience to rulers; and then the attempt to escape the control and exhortation of father, mother, elders, and when near the end, the control of the laws also; and at the very end there is the contempt of oaths and pledges, and no regard at all for the Gods-herein they exhibit and imitate the old so called Titanic nature, and come to the same point as the Titans when they rebelled against God, leading a life of endless evils. But why have I said all this? I ask, because the argument ought to be pulled up from time to time, and not be allowed to run away, but held with bit and bridle, and then we shall not, as the proverb says, fall off our ass. Let us then once more ask the question, To what end has all this been said?
D: Narrative:
"Consequent upon this freedom comes the other freedom, of disobedience to rulers; and then the attempt to escape the control and exhortation of father, mother, elders, and when near the end, the control of the laws also; and at the very end there is the contempt of oaths and pledges, and no regard at all for the Gods -- herein they exhibit and imitate the old so called Titanic nature, and come to the same point as the Titans when they rebelled against God, leading a life of endless evils.

But why have I said all this? I ask, because the argument ought to be pulled up from time to time, and not be allowed to run away, but held with bit and bridle, and then we shall not, as the proverb says, fall off our ass. Let us then once more ask the question, To what end has all this been said?"
D: Refashioned:
"From this cultural freedom comes the next: disobedience to political rulers. Then comes the attempt to escape the guidance of fathers, mothers, and elders. Near the end, they reject the authority of the laws themselves. And at the very end comes contempt for oaths and pledges, and complete disregard for the gods. At this point they exhibit and imitate what the ancients called the Titanic nature -- reaching the same state as the Titans when they rebelled against the divine order, leading a life of unending evils.

But why have I gone through all this? Because an argument needs to be reined in from time to time -- held with bit and bridle -- rather than allowed to run away from us. Otherwise, as the proverb says, we will fall off our donkey. So let us ask once more: to what end has all this been said?"
D: Elegant:
Cultural freedom cascades into political disobedience, then rejection of parental authority, then defiance of law, and finally contempt for oaths and the gods themselves. At this terminal point, humans imitate the Titans who rebelled against divine order -- leading a life of endless evils.

But the argument must be held with bit and bridle. To what end has all this been said?
D: Summary: The Athenian traces the cascade of declining authority: from cultural freedom to political disobedience, to rejection of parental control, to defiance of law, to contempt for oaths and gods -- ultimately reproducing the Titanic rebellion against divine order. He then pulls back, noting that the argument needs reining in, and asks what purpose the entire historical survey has served.
D: Distilled: Excessive freedom cascades from cultural lawlessness through political disobedience to contempt for gods and oaths -- reproducing the Titans' rebellion against divine order.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The cascade from cultural to political to moral to religious lawlessness is one of the Laws' most powerful arguments. The Titanic nature (titanike physis) refers to the myth of the Titans' rebellion against the Olympian gods -- here used as a metaphor for the ultimate human hubris of rejecting all authority, both human and divine. In Orphic tradition, humans were born from the ashes of the Titans who had consumed Dionysus, giving humanity a dual nature: Titanic (rebellious) and Dionysian (divine). Compare Republic 571c-572b on the 'lawless' desires released in dreams. The horse-and-rider metaphor ('bit and bridle') for controlling argument echoes Phaedrus 253c-254e (the chariot allegory). The self-reflexive moment -- stepping back to ask 'why have I said all this?' -- is characteristic of the Laws' conversational method, maintaining the fiction of a real-time dialogue while ensuring the philosophical argument remains on track.

D: Original:
Very good.

This, then, has been said for the sake-

Of what?

We were maintaining that the lawgiver ought to have three things in view: first, that the city for which he legislates should be free; and secondly, be at unity with herself; and thirdly, should have understanding;-these were our principles, were they not?

Certainly.

With a view to this we selected two kinds of government, the despotic, and the other the most free; and now we are considering which of them is the right form: we took a mean in both cases, of despotism in the one, and of liberty in the other, and we saw that in a mean they attained their perfection; but that when they were carried to the extreme of either, slavery or licence, neither party were the gainers.

Very true.
D: Narrative:
"Very good."

"This, then, has been said for the sake --"

"Of what?"

"We were maintaining that the lawgiver ought to have three things in view: first, that the city for which he legislates should be free; and secondly, be at unity with herself; and thirdly, should have understanding; these were our principles, were they not?"

"Certainly."

"With a view to this we selected two kinds of government, the despotic, and the other the most free; and now we are considering which of them is the right form: we took a mean in both cases, of despotism in the one, and of liberty in the other, and we saw that in a mean they attained their perfection; but that when they were carried to the extreme of either, slavery or licence, neither party were the gainers."

"Very true."
D: Refashioned:
"Good."

"Everything we have said has been directed toward --"

"Toward what?"

"We established that the lawgiver must keep three goals in view: first, that the city he legislates for should be free; second, that it should be internally unified; and third, that it should possess wisdom. Those were our principles, correct?"

"Correct."

"To achieve this, we examined two types of government: despotism and the most extreme freedom. We have now seen that each reaches its best form in moderation -- a measured amount of authority in one case, a measured amount of liberty in the other. When either is pushed to the extreme -- total slavery or total license -- neither ruler nor ruled benefits."

"That is exactly right."
D: Elegant:
The entire discussion serves three legislative principles: a city must be free, unified, and wise.

Two forms of government were examined: despotism and extreme democracy. Both reach perfection in moderation. Pushed to extremes -- total slavery or total license -- neither benefits anyone.
D: Summary: The Athenian restates the three legislative goals -- freedom, unity, and wisdom -- that have guided the entire discussion. He summarizes the conclusion drawn from the parallel analysis of Persia and Athens: both despotism and democracy achieve their best form through moderation, while extremes of either slavery or license benefit no one.
D: Distilled: The lawgiver must ensure three things -- freedom, unity, and wisdom -- achieved only through the moderation of both authority and liberty.
Notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- This passage performs a crucial recapitulatory function, tying together the entire Book 3 argument. The three legislative goals (freedom, unity, wisdom) correspond to the three virtues that the Athenian earlier identified as different names for the same aim (3.34: temperance, wisdom, friendship). The 'mean' between despotism and democracy is the Laws' version of the mixed constitution, more explicitly formulated than Aristotle's later 'polity' (Politics 1293b) but conceptually identical. The structural parallel between Persia (excess despotism) and Athens (excess freedom) now receives its formal conclusion. This principle -- moderation between extremes -- is arguably the single most important political teaching of the Laws. Compare Aristotle's doctrine of the mean (Nicomachean Ethics 1106b-1107a) applied to political constitutions.

D: Original: And that was our reason for considering the settlement of the Dorian army, and of the city built by Dardanus at the foot of the mountains, and the removal of cities to the seashore, and of our mention of the first men, who were the survivors of the deluge. And all that was previously said about music and drinking, and what preceded, was said with the view of seeing how a state might be best administered, and how an individual might best order his own life. And now, Megillus and Cleinias, how can we put to the proof the value of our words?
D: Narrative: "And that was our reason for considering the settlement of the Dorian army, and of the city built by Dardanus at the foot of the mountains, and the removal of cities to the seashore, and of our mention of the first men, who were the survivors of the deluge. And all that was previously said about music and drinking, and what preceded, was said with the view of seeing how a state might be best administered, and how an individual might best order his own life. And now, Megillus and Cleinias, how can we put to the proof the value of our words?"
D: Refashioned: "This is why we examined the Dorian military settlement, and the city Dardanus built at the mountain's foot, and the migration of cities to the coast, and the earliest survivors of the deluge. And everything we discussed earlier about music and drinking customs was directed toward the same question: how might a state be best governed, and how might an individual best order their own life? Now, Megillus and Cleinias, how can we test whether our conclusions have real value?"
D: Elegant: Every topic we have examined -- the deluge survivors, the Dorian settlement, Dardanus's city, the coastal migrations, the discussions of music and drinking -- all served one purpose: determining how a state should be governed and how an individual should live. The question now is: how do we test these conclusions?
D: Summary: The Athenian reviews the entire scope of the discussion -- from the deluge survivors through the Dorian settlement to music and drinking customs -- showing that every topic served the dual question of how to govern a state and how to live well individually. He then poses the practical challenge: how can they test whether their theoretical conclusions have real value?
D: Distilled: Every topic in the discussion -- from primeval catastrophe to cultural customs -- served the dual question of how to govern well and live well, and the task now is to test these conclusions in practice.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage connects Book 3 back to Books 1-2 (music and drinking customs) and forward to the practical legislative program of Books 4-12. The dual aim -- 'how a state might be best administered' and 'how an individual might best order his own life' -- echoes the Republic's famous parallel between justice in the soul and justice in the state (368d-369a). The question 'how can we put to the proof the value of our words?' is the Socratic demand for practical verification rather than mere theoretical satisfaction. It directly sets up Cleinias's revelation in 3.53 about the Cretan colony, which transforms the entire dialogue from theoretical inquiry to practical legislation.

D: Original:
Stranger, I think that I see how a proof of their value may be obtained. This discussion of ours appears to me to have been singularly fortunate, and just what I at this moment want; most auspiciously have you and my friend Megillus come in my way. For I will tell you what has happened to me; and I regard the coincidence as a sort of omen. The greater part of Crete is going to send out a colony, and they have entrusted the management of the affair to the Cnosians; and the Cnosian government to me and nine others. And they desire us to give them any laws which we please, whether taken from the Cretan model or from any other; and they do not mind about their being foreign if they are better. Grant me then this favour, which will also be a gain to yourselves:-Let us make a selection from what has been said, and then let us imagine a State of which we will suppose ourselves to be the original founders. Thus we shall proceed with our enquiry, and, at the same time, I may have the use of the framework which you are constructing, for the city which is in contemplation.

Good news, Cleinias; if Megillus has no objection, you may be sure that I will do all in my power to please you.

Thank you.

And so will I.

Excellent; and now let us begin to frame the State.
D: Narrative:
"Stranger, I think that I see how a proof of their value may be obtained. This discussion of ours appears to me to have been singularly fortunate, and just what I at this moment want; most auspiciously have you and my friend Megillus come in my way. For I will tell you what has happened to me; and I regard the coincidence as a sort of omen. The greater part of Crete is going to send out a colony, and they have entrusted the management of the affair to the Cnosians; and the Cnosian government to me and nine others. And they desire us to give them any laws which we please, whether taken from the Cretan model or from any other; and they do not mind about their being foreign if they are better. Grant me then this favour, which will also be a gain to yourselves: Let us make a selection from what has been said, and then let us imagine a State of which we will suppose ourselves to be the original founders. Thus we shall proceed with our enquiry, and, at the same time, I may have the use of the framework which you are constructing, for the city which is in contemplation."

"Good news, Cleinias; if Megillus has no objection, you may be sure that I will do all in my power to please you."

"Thank you."

"And so will I."

"Excellent; and now let us begin to frame the State."
D: Refashioned:
"Stranger, I think I see how we can test the value of our conclusions. This whole conversation seems to have been remarkably fortunate -- exactly what I need right now. Your arrival and Megillus's could not have been more auspicious. Let me explain: most of Crete is about to establish a colony, and they have entrusted the project to the government of Cnossos, which in turn has appointed me and nine others to oversee it. We have been given complete freedom to provide whatever laws we see fit -- whether based on the Cretan model or any other. They do not care if the laws are foreign, as long as they are better. So grant me this favor, which will benefit you as well: let us draw on everything we have discussed and imagine that we are the original founders of a new state. In this way, we can continue our philosophical inquiry while I gain a practical framework for the real city we are planning."

"Wonderful news, Cleinias! If Megillus has no objection, I will do everything in my power to help."

"Thank you."

"And I will do the same."

"Excellent. Now let us begin to design the state."
D: Elegant:
Cleinias reveals the purpose this conversation can serve: Crete is founding a colony, and he has been appointed one of ten commissioners with full authority to give it whatever laws they choose -- Cretan or foreign, as long as they are superior.

He proposes that the three companions draw on their discussion to imagine themselves as founders of a new state -- combining philosophical inquiry with practical legislation. The Athenian and Megillus eagerly agree. Let us begin to frame the state.
D: Summary: In the dialogue's pivotal dramatic moment, Cleinias reveals that Crete is actually founding a colony and that he has been appointed to a commission with full legislative authority. He proposes that their philosophical discussion become the foundation for actual legislation, asking the three companions to imagine themselves as the colony's founders. The Athenian and Megillus enthusiastically agree, and the work of framing the new state begins.
D: Distilled: Cleinias reveals that Crete is founding a real colony for which he has legislative authority, transforming the philosophical discussion into practical constitutional design.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias, the Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. -- This is the pivotal dramatic moment of the entire Laws: the revelation that transforms a theoretical conversation into a practical legislative project. The colony will become Magnesia, the city whose laws occupy Books 4-12. Cleinias's characterization of the coincidence as an 'omen' (oionos) adds a religious dimension -- suggesting divine providence has arranged the meeting. The commission of ten echoes historical Greek colonial practice (oikistai). The openness to 'foreign' laws that are 'better' reflects the cosmopolitan spirit of the dialogue and justifies the Athenian Stranger's role as an outsider advising on legislation. Compare the Republic's construction of Kallipolis 'in speech' (369a), which remains purely theoretical -- here the Laws claims practical applicability. The closing line -- 'let us begin to frame the State' -- marks the transition from Book 3's historical-philosophical groundwork to the positive legislative program that constitutes the remainder of the dialogue.

D: Original: And now, what will this city be? I do not mean to ask what is or will hereafter be the name of the place; that may be determined by the accident of locality or of the original settlement-a river or fountain, or some local deity may give the sanction of a name to the newly-founded city; but I do want to know what the situation is, whether maritime or inland.
D: Narrative: "And now, what will this city be? I do not mean to ask what is or will hereafter be the name of the place; that may be determined by the accident of locality or of the original settlement -- a river or fountain, or some local deity may give the sanction of a name to the newly-founded city; but I do want to know what the situation is, whether maritime or inland."
D: Refashioned: "Now then, what kind of city will this be? I am not asking about its name -- that can be decided by geography or the circumstances of its founding. A river, a spring, or some local god may lend the new city its name. What I want to know is its physical situation: is it on the coast, or inland?"
D: Elegant: "I am not asking what the city will be called -- names come from geography or local gods. What I need to know is the situation: is it coastal or inland?"
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger shifts the conversation from naming the proposed colony to its physical location, asking whether it will be situated on the coast or inland.
D: Distilled: The Athenian opens the discussion of the new colony by asking the crucial question of whether it will be coastal or inland.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This marks the beginning of Book 4, transitioning from the historical survey of Book 3 to the practical planning of the new Cretan colony. The question of maritime vs. inland situation is not merely geographical but deeply political for Plato: proximity to the sea brings commerce, naval power, and moral corruption, themes developed extensively in what follows. Compare Republic II (370e-371a) on trade and the city's relationship to external goods. The colony is being founded by Cleinias's commission from the city of Cnosus.

D: Original:
I should imagine, Stranger, that the city of which we are speaking is about eighty stadia distant from the sea.

And are there harbours on the seaboard?

Excellent harbours, Stranger; there could not be better.

Alas! what a prospect! And is the surrounding country productive, or in need of importations?

Hardly in need of anything.

And is there any neighbouring State?

None whatever, and that is the reason for selecting the place; in days of old, there was a migration of the inhabitants, and the region has been deserted from time immemorial.

And has the place a fair proportion of hill, and plain, and wood?

Like the rest of Crete in that.

You mean to say that there is more rock than plain?

Exactly.
D: Narrative:
"I should imagine, Stranger, that the city of which we are speaking is about eighty stadia distant from the sea."

"And are there harbours on the seaboard?"

"Excellent harbours, Stranger; there could not be better."

"Alas! what a prospect! And is the surrounding country productive, or in need of importations?"

"Hardly in need of anything."

"And is there any neighbouring State?"

"None whatever, and that is the reason for selecting the place; in days of old, there was a migration of the inhabitants, and the region has been deserted from time immemorial."

"And has the place a fair proportion of hill, and plain, and wood?"

"Like the rest of Crete in that."

"You mean to say that there is more rock than plain?"

"Exactly."
D: Refashioned:
"I would estimate, Stranger, that the city we are discussing is about eighty stadia from the sea."

"And are there harbors along the coast?"

"Excellent ones, Stranger -- they could not be better."

"That is a troubling prospect! And is the surrounding land productive, or does it need imports?"

"It is hardly in need of anything."

"Is there any neighboring state?"

"None at all. That is precisely why the place was chosen. Long ago the inhabitants migrated away, and the region has been deserted since time out of memory."

"Does the area have a good balance of hills, plains, and forests?"

"It is much like the rest of Crete in that regard."

"You mean there is more rock than flat land?"

"Exactly."
D: Elegant: The proposed site is about eighty stadia from the sea, with excellent harbors, self-sufficient land, no neighboring states, and terrain typical of Crete -- more rocky than flat. The region has been deserted since an ancient migration.
D: Summary: Cleinias describes the proposed colony site: roughly ten miles from the sea with fine harbors, self-sufficient agriculture, no neighboring states, and rocky Cretan terrain. The area has been uninhabited since an ancient migration.
D: Distilled: The colony site is ten miles inland with excellent harbors, self-sufficient land, no neighbors, and the characteristically rocky terrain of Crete.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- Eighty stadia is approximately 9-10 miles (14-15 km). The Stranger's exclamation "Alas!" at the excellent harbors reveals his concern about maritime influence before he has even stated it -- a dramatic device showing how deeply he holds this conviction. The absence of neighboring states and the deserted condition of the region create an ideal blank canvas for legislation, echoing the Republic's thought experiment of building a city in speech (Republic II, 369a). The rocky terrain of Crete is geographically accurate and will prove philosophically convenient.

D: Original: Then there is some hope that your citizens may be virtuous: had you been on the sea, and well provided with harbours, and an importing rather than a producing country, some mighty saviour would have been needed, and lawgivers more than mortal, if you were ever to have a chance of preserving your state from degeneracy and discordance of manners. But there is comfort in the eighty stadia; although the sea is too near, especially if, as you say, the harbours are so good. Still we may be content. The sea is pleasant enough as a daily companion, but has indeed also a bitter and brackish quality; filling the streets with merchants and shopkeepers, and begetting in the souls of men uncertain and unfaithful ways-making the state unfriendly and unfaithful both to her own citizens, and also to other nations. There is a consolation, therefore, in the country producing all things at home; and yet, owing to the ruggedness of the soil, not providing anything in great abundance. Had there been abundance, there might have been a great export trade, and a great return of gold and silver; which, as we may safely affirm, has the most fatal results on a State whose aim is the attainment of just and noble sentiments: this was said by us, if you remember, in the previous discussion.
D: Narrative: "Then there is some hope that your citizens may be virtuous: had you been on the sea, and well provided with harbours, and an importing rather than a producing country, some mighty saviour would have been needed, and lawgivers more than mortal, if you were ever to have a chance of preserving your state from degeneracy and discordance of manners. But there is comfort in the eighty stadia; although the sea is too near, especially if, as you say, the harbours are so good. Still we may be content. The sea is pleasant enough as a daily companion, but has indeed also a bitter and brackish quality; filling the streets with merchants and shopkeepers, and begetting in the souls of men uncertain and unfaithful ways -- making the state unfriendly and unfaithful both to her own citizens, and also to other nations. There is a consolation, therefore, in the country producing all things at home; and yet, owing to the ruggedness of the soil, not providing anything in great abundance. Had there been abundance, there might have been a great export trade, and a great return of gold and silver; which, as we may safely affirm, has the most fatal results on a State whose aim is the attainment of just and noble sentiments: this was said by us, if you remember, in the previous discussion."
D: Refashioned:
"Then there is some hope that your citizens may turn out virtuous. If the city were right on the coast, with fine harbors, importing rather than producing -- you would have needed a superhuman savior, lawgivers beyond mortal capacity, just to have any chance of keeping the state from moral degeneracy and social discord. The eighty stadia give us comfort, though the sea is still too close, especially with harbors as good as you describe. Still, we can be content.

The sea is pleasant enough as a daily companion, but it carries a bitter, brackish quality as well. It fills the streets with merchants and shopkeepers and breeds in people's souls a disposition toward unreliability and bad faith -- making the state disloyal both to its own citizens and to other nations.

It is therefore a consolation that the land produces everything the colony needs at home, and that the rugged soil prevents anything from growing in great abundance. If there were surplus, a booming export trade would follow, flooding the country with gold and silver -- which, as we agreed in our earlier discussion, produces the most destructive effects on any state that aims at justice and nobility of character."
D: Elegant: There is hope for virtue because the city is not directly on the coast. The sea is a pleasant but corrupting companion: it fills streets with merchants and breeds unreliability in people's souls, making the state faithless to its own citizens and to foreigners alike. Self-sufficiency is a consolation, and the rugged soil that prevents surplus is actually a blessing -- abundance would create export trade and an influx of gold and silver, which are fatal to any state that aims at justice and noble character.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that distance from the sea offers hope for virtue, since maritime commerce fills cities with merchants and breeds moral unreliability. The colony's self-sufficient but modest agriculture is an advantage, since surplus would generate trade and wealth that corrupt states pursuing justice.
D: Distilled: The sea breeds commercial mentality and moral unreliability, so the colony's inland position and modest self-sufficiency are advantages for cultivating virtue.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage contains one of Plato's most sustained critiques of maritime commercial culture. The metaphor of the sea as pleasant but "bitter and brackish" encapsulates the tension between material prosperity and moral health. Compare Republic II (370e-373d) where the growth of trade and luxury transforms the "healthy city" into the "feverish city." The reference to the "previous discussion" points back to Laws Book 3 (696a-b) on the corrupting effects of wealth. Plato's anti-commercial stance likely reflects Athenian anxieties about their own naval empire and its moral consequences -- a theme central to Thucydides' account of Athenian decline.

D: Original:
I remember, and am of opinion that we both were and are in the right.

Well, but let me ask, how is the country supplied with timber for ship-building?

There is no fir of any consequence, nor pine, and not much cypress; and you will find very little stone-pine or plane-wood, which shipwrights always require for the interior of ships.

These are also natural advantages.

Why so?

Because no city ought to be easily able to imitate its enemies in what is mischievous.

How does that bear upon any of the matters of which we have been speaking?
D: Narrative:
"I remember, and am of opinion that we both were and are in the right."

"Well, but let me ask, how is the country supplied with timber for ship-building?"

"There is no fir of any consequence, nor pine, and not much cypress; and you will find very little stone-pine or plane-wood, which shipwrights always require for the interior of ships."

"These are also natural advantages."

"Why so?"

"Because no city ought to be easily able to imitate its enemies in what is mischievous."

"How does that bear upon any of the matters of which we have been speaking?"
D: Refashioned:
"I remember, and I believe we were right then and remain right now."

"Good. But let me ask: how well is the region supplied with timber for building ships?"

"There is no significant fir or pine, and very little cypress. You will find almost no stone-pine or plane-wood either -- the kinds of timber that shipwrights always need for the interior parts of ships."

"These are also natural advantages."

"How so?"

"Because no city should find it easy to imitate its enemies in what is harmful."

"How does that connect to anything we have been discussing?"
D: Elegant: The region lacks fir, pine, and other shipbuilding timber. The Athenian counts this as a natural advantage: no city should find it easy to imitate its enemies in what is harmful. Cleinias asks how this connects to their discussion.
D: Summary: The lack of shipbuilding timber in the colony's region is identified as a natural advantage. The Athenian argues that a city should not be able to easily imitate the harmful practices of its enemies, setting up his argument against naval warfare.
D: Distilled: The colony's lack of shipbuilding timber is a blessing because it prevents the city from imitating its enemies' harmful naval practices.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- The principle that a city should not easily imitate its enemies' vices is a distinctive contribution of the Laws not found in the Republic. It reflects a practical wisdom about how military necessity can reshape a society's values -- a concern that pervades Thucydides' History, particularly his account of how the Peloponnesian War corrupted Greek civic life. The specific timbers mentioned (fir, pine, cypress, stone-pine, plane) are accurate to ancient Mediterranean shipbuilding. Cleinias's puzzlement sets up the Athenian's extended critique of naval power.

D: Original: Remember, my good friend, what I said at first about the Cretan laws, that they look to one thing only, and this, as you both agreed, was war; and I replied that such laws, in so far as they tended to promote virtue, were good; but in that they regarded a part only, and not the whole of virtue, I disapproved of them. And now I hope that you in your turn will follow and watch me if I legislate with a view to anything but virtue, or with a view to a part of virtue only. For I consider that the true lawgiver, like an archer, aims only at that on which some eternal beauty is always attending, and dismisses everything else, whether wealth or any other benefit, when separated from virtue. I was saying that the imitation of enemies was a bad thing; and I was thinking of a case in which a maritime people are harassed by enemies, as the Athenians were by Minos (I do not speak from any desire to recall past grievances); but he, as we know, was a great naval potentate, who compelled the inhabitants of Attica to pay him a cruel tribute; and in those days they had no ships of war as they now have, nor was the country filled with ship-timber, and therefore they could not readily build them.
D: Narrative: "Remember, my good friend, what I said at first about the Cretan laws, that they look to one thing only, and this, as you both agreed, was war; and I replied that such laws, in so far as they tended to promote virtue, were good; but in that they regarded a part only, and not the whole of virtue, I disapproved of them. And now I hope that you in your turn will follow and watch me if I legislate with a view to anything but virtue, or with a view to a part of virtue only. For I consider that the true lawgiver, like an archer, aims only at that on which some eternal beauty is always attending, and dismisses everything else, whether wealth or any other benefit, when separated from virtue. I was saying that the imitation of enemies was a bad thing; and I was thinking of a case in which a maritime people are harassed by enemies, as the Athenians were by Minos (I do not speak from any desire to recall past grievances); but he, as we know, was a great naval potentate, who compelled the inhabitants of Attica to pay him a cruel tribute; and in those days they had no ships of war as they now have, nor was the country filled with ship-timber, and therefore they could not readily build them."
D: Refashioned:
"Remember, my good friend, what I said at the start about the Cretan laws -- that they aim at one thing only, which you both agreed was war. I replied that such laws were good insofar as they promoted virtue, but I disapproved of them insofar as they addressed only one part of virtue rather than the whole.

Now I ask you to hold me to the same standard. Watch carefully whether I legislate with anything other than virtue in view, or with only a part of virtue in mind. The true lawgiver, I believe, is like an archer who aims only at what is attended by an eternal beauty, dismissing everything else -- wealth, or any other advantage -- when it is separated from virtue.

When I said that imitating one's enemies is a bad thing, I was thinking of a specific case. Consider a maritime people harassed by enemies -- as the Athenians were by Minos. I do not bring this up to revive old grievances, but Minos was, as we know, a great naval power who forced the people of Attica to pay a cruel tribute. In those days the Athenians had no warships as they do now, nor was their country full of ship-timber, so they could not easily build them."
D: Elegant: The true lawgiver, like an archer, aims only at what eternal beauty attends and dismisses everything else when separated from virtue. Cretan laws that aim only at war address a part of virtue, not the whole. The danger of imitating enemies is illustrated by the Athenians and Minos: harassed by a naval power, the Athenians lacked warships and shipbuilding timber, which initially prevented them from adopting their enemy's naval ways.
D: Summary: The Athenian recalls his earlier critique that Cretan laws aim only at military virtue, not virtue as a whole. He argues the true lawgiver aims only at eternal beauty and virtue, and introduces the example of Athens and Minos to illustrate the danger of imitating an enemy's naval power.
D: Distilled: The true lawgiver aims at complete virtue like an archer at an eternal mark, dismissing wealth and all advantages separated from virtue.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The archer metaphor for the lawgiver recurs in Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics (I.2), possibly borrowed from this passage. The reference to Cretan laws and war recalls Laws Book 1 (625d-630d), where the entire dialogue began with the question of whether laws should aim at military virtue alone. Minos was the legendary king of Crete who exacted tribute from Athens, including the seven youths and seven maidens sent to the Minotaur -- an ironic example to raise before Cleinias the Cretan. The distinction between partial and complete virtue is central to the Laws and parallels the Republic's argument that justice encompasses all virtues (Republic IV, 427d-434d).

D: Original: Hence they could not learn how to imitate their enemy at sea, and in this way, becoming sailors themselves, directly repel their enemies. Better for them to have lost many times over the seven youths, than that heavy-armed and stationary troops should have been turned into sailors, and accustomed to be often leaping on shore, and again to come running back to their ships; or should have fancied that there was no disgrace in not awaiting the attack of an enemy and dying boldly; and that there were good reasons, and plenty of them, for a man throwing away his arms, and betaking himself to flight-which is not dishonourable, as people say, at certain times. This is the language of naval warfare, and is anything but worthy of extraordinary praise. For we should not teach bad habits, least of all to the best part of the citizens. You may learn the evil of such a practice from Homer, by whom Odysseus is introduced, rebuking Agamemnon because he desires to draw down the ships to the sea at a time when the Achaeans are hard pressed by the Trojans-he gets angry with him, and says:
D: Narrative: "Hence they could not learn how to imitate their enemy at sea, and in this way, becoming sailors themselves, directly repel their enemies. Better for them to have lost many times over the seven youths, than that heavy-armed and stationary troops should have been turned into sailors, and accustomed to be often leaping on shore, and again to come running back to their ships; or should have fancied that there was no disgrace in not awaiting the attack of an enemy and dying boldly; and that there were good reasons, and plenty of them, for a man throwing away his arms, and betaking himself to flight -- which is not dishonourable, as people say, at certain times. This is the language of naval warfare, and is anything but worthy of extraordinary praise. For we should not teach bad habits, least of all to the best part of the citizens. You may learn the evil of such a practice from Homer, by whom Odysseus is introduced, rebuking Agamemnon because he desires to draw down the ships to the sea at a time when the Achaeans are hard pressed by the Trojans -- he gets angry with him, and says:"
D: Refashioned:
"Because they lacked ships and timber, they could not learn to imitate their enemy at sea and repel him by becoming sailors themselves. And this was actually better for them. It would have been preferable to lose those seven youths many times over than to have their heavy-armed infantry transformed into sailors -- troops accustomed to leaping ashore, then running back to their ships, who came to believe there was no disgrace in refusing to stand and fight to the death, and who found plenty of good reasons for throwing away their weapons and fleeing. 'At certain times,' people say, 'retreat is not dishonorable.' That is the language of naval warfare, and it deserves anything but extraordinary praise.

We should not teach bad habits, especially not to the best citizens. You can learn the evil of such practices from Homer, where Odysseus rebukes Agamemnon for wanting to drag the ships down to the sea while the Achaeans are under heavy pressure from the Trojans. Odysseus becomes angry and says:"
D: Elegant: Athens was better off unable to imitate Minos at sea. It would have been preferable to lose those seven youths many times over than to transform heavy infantry into sailors who learned to retreat and throw away their weapons -- accepting the naval maxim that flight is sometimes honorable. We should never teach bad habits, especially to the best citizens. Homer shows the evil of this when Odysseus rebukes Agamemnon for wanting to launch the ships during battle.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues it would have been better for Athens to lose the tribute victims repeatedly than to transform its infantry into sailors, which teaches retreat and the abandonment of arms. He cites Homer's Odysseus rebuking Agamemnon to illustrate the corrupting effect of naval warfare on martial virtue.
D: Distilled: Naval warfare corrupts martial virtue by teaching soldiers that retreat and abandoning weapons are sometimes acceptable, destroying the courage that infantry warfare demands.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The "seven youths" refers to the seven young men (and seven young women) sent as tribute to Minos to be devoured by the Minotaur. The argument that naval warfare degrades infantry courage echoes a widespread aristocratic Greek prejudice against rowers and sailors as socially inferior. Compare Aristotle's Politics (VII.6, 1327a) for a similar anti-maritime view. The Homeric reference is to Iliad XIV.96-102, where Agamemnon proposes launching the ships in retreat. The distinction between hoplite (heavy-armed, stationary) warfare and naval warfare maps onto class distinctions: hoplites were propertied citizens, while rowers were typically the urban poor.

D: Original:
Who, at a time when the battle is in full cry, biddest to drag the well-benched ships into the sea, that the prayers of the Trojans may be accomplished yet more, and high ruin falls upon us. For the Achaeans will not maintain the battle, when the ships are drawn into the sea, but they will look behind and will cease from strife; in that the counsel which you give will prove injurious. You see that he quite knew triremes on the sea, in the neighbourhood of fighting men, to be an evil;-lions might be trained in that way to fly from a herd of deer. Moreover, naval powers which owe their safety to ships, do not give honour to that sort of warlike excellence which is most deserving of it. For he who owes his safety to the pilot and the captain, and the oarsman, and all sorts of rather inferior persons cannot rightly give honour to whom honour is due. But how can a state be in a right condition which cannot justly award honour?

It is hardly possible, I admit; and yet, Stranger, we Cretans are in the habit of saying that the battle of Salamis was the salvation of Hellas.
D: Narrative:
"Who, at a time when the battle is in full cry, biddest to drag the well-benched ships into the sea, that the prayers of the Trojans may be accomplished yet more, and high ruin falls upon us. For the Achaeans will not maintain the battle, when the ships are drawn into the sea, but they will look behind and will cease from strife; in that the counsel which you give will prove injurious."

"You see that he quite knew triremes on the sea, in the neighbourhood of fighting men, to be an evil; -- lions might be trained in that way to fly from a herd of deer. Moreover, naval powers which owe their safety to ships, do not give honour to that sort of warlike excellence which is most deserving of it. For he who owes his safety to the pilot and the captain, and the oarsman, and all sorts of rather inferior persons cannot rightly give honour to whom honour is due. But how can a state be in a right condition which cannot justly award honour?"

"It is hardly possible, I admit; and yet, Stranger, we Cretans are in the habit of saying that the battle of Salamis was the salvation of Hellas."
D: Refashioned:
"'You who, at the height of battle, command us to drag the well-benched ships to the sea -- so that the Trojans' prayers may be fulfilled even further and utter ruin fall upon us! The Achaeans will not hold their ground once the ships are drawn to the water. They will look behind them and abandon the fight. Your counsel will prove our destruction.'

You see that Homer understood perfectly: having ships at sea near fighting men is an evil. By such means, lions could be trained to run from a herd of deer. Furthermore, naval powers that owe their safety to ships cannot properly honor the kind of warlike excellence that most deserves it. When a man owes his survival to the pilot, the captain, the oarsman, and all sorts of rather inferior persons, how can he rightly give honor where it is due? And how can a state be in good order if it cannot justly distribute honor?"

"That is hardly possible, I admit. And yet, Stranger, we Cretans are in the habit of saying that the battle of Salamis was the salvation of Greece."
D: Elegant: Homer understood that ships near fighting men are an evil -- by such means, lions could be trained to flee from deer. Naval powers cannot properly honor true martial excellence because survival depends on pilots, captains, and oarsmen rather than individual courage. A state that cannot justly award honor cannot be in good order. Cleinias concedes the point but notes that Cretans traditionally credit the naval battle of Salamis with saving Greece.
D: Summary: The Athenian quotes Homer to show that ships undermine infantry courage, then argues that naval powers cannot properly honor true martial excellence since survival depends on collective seamanship rather than individual valor. Cleinias concedes but raises the counterexample of Salamis.
D: Distilled: Naval powers cannot justly award honor because survival at sea depends on collective seamanship rather than individual martial courage, yet Cleinias raises Salamis as a counterpoint.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The Homeric quotation is from Iliad XIV.96-102 (Odysseus to Agamemnon). The argument about honor distribution echoes the Statesman's concern with proper classification and ranking of different forms of knowledge and skill. The lions-and-deer metaphor vividly captures how naval retreat demoralizes. Cleinias's invocation of Salamis (480 BC) -- where the Greek fleet decisively defeated Xerxes' Persian navy -- is the strongest possible counterargument. The Athenian's response will provocatively downgrade Salamis in favor of the land battles at Marathon and Plataea, reflecting a Spartan-sympathetic and oligarchic perspective on the Persian Wars.

D: Original:
Why, yes; and that is an opinion which is widely spread both among Hellenes and barbarians. But Megillus and I say rather, that the battle of Marathon was the beginning, and the battle of Plataea the completion, of the great deliverance, and that these battles by land made the Hellenes better; whereas the sea-fights of Salamis and Artemisium-for I may as well put them both together-made them no better, if I may say so without offence about the battles which helped to save us. And in estimating the goodness of a state, we regard both the situation of the country and the order of the laws, considering that the mere preservation and continuance of life is not the most honourable thing for men, as the vulgar think, but the continuance of the best life, while we live; and that again, if I am jot mistaken, is remark which has been made already.

Yes.

Then we have only to ask whether we are taking the course which we acknowledge to be the best for the settlement and legislation of states.

The best by far.
D: Narrative:
"Why, yes; and that is an opinion which is widely spread both among Hellenes and barbarians. But Megillus and I say rather, that the battle of Marathon was the beginning, and the battle of Plataea the completion, of the great deliverance, and that these battles by land made the Hellenes better; whereas the sea-fights of Salamis and Artemisium -- for I may as well put them both together -- made them no better, if I may say so without offence about the battles which helped to save us. And in estimating the goodness of a state, we regard both the situation of the country and the order of the laws, considering that the mere preservation and continuance of life is not the most honourable thing for men, as the vulgar think, but the continuance of the best life, while we live; and that again, if I am not mistaken, is a remark which has been made already."

"Yes."

"Then we have only to ask whether we are taking the course which we acknowledge to be the best for the settlement and legislation of states."

"The best by far."
D: Refashioned:
"Yes, and that opinion is widespread among both Greeks and non-Greeks. But Megillus and I maintain that the battle of Marathon was the beginning, and the battle of Plataea the completion, of the great deliverance -- and that these land battles made the Greeks better. The sea battles of Salamis and Artemisium -- I may as well group them together -- made the Greeks no better, if I may say so without giving offense about engagements that helped save us.

In judging the goodness of a state, we must consider both its geographical situation and the quality of its laws. The mere preservation and continuation of life is not the most honorable thing for human beings, as ordinary people suppose. What matters is the continuation of the best possible life while we are alive. And that, if I am not mistaken, is a point we have already established."

"Yes."

"Then we need only ask whether we are pursuing the course we have acknowledged to be best for founding and legislating states."

"The best course by far."
D: Elegant: Marathon began and Plataea completed the great deliverance, and these land battles made the Greeks better. The naval victories at Salamis and Artemisium saved Greece but improved no one's character. In judging a state's goodness, we consider both geography and laws, recognizing that mere survival is not the highest aim -- what matters is living the best possible life.
D: Summary: The Athenian provocatively argues that the land battles of Marathon and Plataea, not the naval victory at Salamis, truly saved Greece because they improved Greek character. He establishes the principle that a state's goodness depends on geography and laws, and that the goal is the best life, not mere survival.
D: Distilled: The land battles of Marathon and Plataea improved Greek character, while the naval victory at Salamis merely preserved Greek life -- and the best life, not bare survival, is what matters.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- Marathon (490 BC) was fought by Athenian hoplites and Plataean allies against Persian invaders. Plataea (479 BC) was the decisive land battle that ended the Persian invasion. Artemisium was the indecisive naval engagement fought simultaneously with Thermopylae. The claim that Marathon and Plataea "made the Hellenes better" while Salamis did not is a moral rather than military judgment: land warfare cultivates individual courage, while naval warfare does not. This view aligns with Spartan rather than Athenian self-understanding. The principle that the best life rather than mere survival is the aim of legislation connects to Republic I (352d-354a) and anticipates Aristotle's Politics (I.2, III.9).

D: Original:
And now let me proceed to another question: Who are to be the colonists? May any one come out of all Crete; and is the idea that the population in the several states is too numerous for the means of subsistence? For I suppose that you are not going to send out a general invitation to any Hellene who likes to come. And yet I observe that to your country settlers have come from Argos and Aegina and other parts of Hellas. Tell me, then, whence do you draw your recruits in the present enterprise?

They will come from all Crete; and of other Hellenes, Peloponnesians will be most acceptable. For, as you truly observe, there are Cretans of Argive descent; and the race of Cretans which has the highest character at the present day is the Gortynian, and this has come from Gortys in the Peloponnesus.
D: Narrative:
"And now let me proceed to another question: Who are to be the colonists? May any one come out of all Crete; and is the idea that the population in the several states is too numerous for the means of subsistence? For I suppose that you are not going to send out a general invitation to any Hellene who likes to come. And yet I observe that to your country settlers have come from Argos and Aegina and other parts of Hellas. Tell me, then, whence do you draw your recruits in the present enterprise?"

"They will come from all Crete; and of other Hellenes, Peloponnesians will be most acceptable. For, as you truly observe, there are Cretans of Argive descent; and the race of Cretans which has the highest character at the present day is the Gortynian, and this has come from Gortys in the Peloponnesus."
D: Refashioned:
"Now let me turn to another question: who will the colonists be? Will anyone from anywhere in Crete be eligible, on the assumption that the various cities are overpopulated relative to their resources? I assume you are not planning an open invitation to any Greek who wants to come. And yet I notice that settlers have come to Crete in the past from Argos, Aegina, and other parts of Greece. So tell me: where will you draw your colonists from for this venture?"

"They will come from all over Crete. Among other Greeks, Peloponnesians will be the most welcome. As you rightly observe, there are Cretans of Argive descent. The Cretan community with the highest reputation today is the Gortynian, and it traces its origins to Gortys in the Peloponnese."
D: Elegant: The colonists will be drawn from all of Crete, with Peloponnesians most welcome among other Greeks. Some Cretans are already of Argive descent, and the most reputable Cretan community -- the Gortynians -- traces its origins to Gortys in the Peloponnese.
D: Summary: The Athenian asks about the composition of the colonial population. Cleinias explains that settlers will come from across Crete, with Peloponnesians preferred among non-Cretan Greeks, noting that many Cretans already have Argive and Peloponnesian ancestry.
D: Distilled: The colony will draw settlers from all of Crete with a preference for Peloponnesians, reflecting existing kinship ties between Crete and the Peloponnese.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The question of colonial composition is practically significant: the mix of settlers will affect political stability and receptiveness to new laws, a theme developed in 4.10. Gortyn was indeed one of the most important Cretan cities, famous for its law code (the Great Code of Gortyn, c. 450 BC) -- the longest surviving Greek legal inscription. The Argive connection reflects mythological and historical traditions of Dorian migration from the Peloponnese to Crete. The preference for Peloponnesians aligns with the Dorian cultural affinity shared by Crete, Sparta, and Argos.

D: Original: Cities find colonization in some respects easier if the colonists are one race, which like a swarm of bees is sent out from a single country, either when friends leave friends, owing to some pressure of population or other similar necessity, or when a portion of a state is driven by factions to emigrate. And there have been whole cities which have taken flight when utterly conquered by a superior power in war. This, however, which is in one way an advantage to the colonist or legislator, in another point of view creates a difficulty. There is an element of friendship in the community of race, and language, and language, and laws, and in common temples and rites of worship; but colonies which are of this homogeneous sort are apt to kick against any laws or any form of constitution differing from that which they had at home; and although the badness of their own laws may have been the cause of the factions which prevailed among them, yet from the force of habit they would fain preserve the very customs which were their ruin, and the leader of the colony, who is their legislator, finds them troublesome and rebellious. On the other hand, the conflux of several populations might be more disposed to listen to new laws; but then, to make them combine and pull together, as they say of horses, is a most difficult task, and the work of years. And yet there is nothing which tends more to the improvement of mankind than legislation and colonization.
D: Narrative: "Cities find colonization in some respects easier if the colonists are one race, which like a swarm of bees is sent out from a single country, either when friends leave friends, owing to some pressure of population or other similar necessity, or when a portion of a state is driven by factions to emigrate. And there have been whole cities which have taken flight when utterly conquered by a superior power in war. This, however, which is in one way an advantage to the colonist or legislator, in another point of view creates a difficulty.
