There is an element of friendship in the community of race, and language, and laws, and in common temples and rites of worship; but colonies which are of this homogeneous sort are apt to kick against any laws or any form of constitution differing from that which they had at home; and although the badness of their own laws may have been the cause of the factions which prevailed among them, yet from the force of habit they would fain preserve the very customs which were their ruin, and the leader of the colony, who is their legislator, finds them troublesome and rebellious. On the other hand, the conflux of several populations might be more disposed to listen to new laws; but then, to make them combine and pull together, as they say of horses, is a most difficult task, and the work of years. And yet there is nothing which tends more to the improvement of mankind than legislation and colonization."
D: Refashioned:
"Colonization is easier in some ways when the colonists are of a single people -- sent out like a swarm of bees from one country, whether because friends part from friends under population pressure or some similar necessity, or because a faction within a state is driven into exile. There have even been entire cities that fled when utterly defeated in war.

But this homogeneity, which is an advantage in one respect, creates a difficulty in another. A shared race, language, set of laws, and common temples and religious rites all foster friendship. Yet homogeneous colonies tend to resist any laws or constitutional arrangements that differ from what they had at home. Even though the defects in their own laws may have caused the very factions that drove them out, the force of habit makes them cling to the customs that ruined them. The colonial leader, who is their lawgiver, finds them troublesome and rebellious.

A mixed population from several sources might be more willing to accept new laws, but getting them to cooperate and work together -- to 'pull together,' as they say of horses -- is an enormously difficult task that takes years. Still, nothing tends more toward the improvement of humanity than legislation and colonization."
D: Elegant: Homogeneous colonies share the bonds of race, language, and religion, but they resist new laws and cling to the very customs that caused their problems. Mixed populations are more open to new legislation but far harder to unify. Both paths are difficult, yet nothing contributes more to human improvement than legislation and colonization.
D: Summary: The Athenian analyzes the tradeoffs in colonial composition: homogeneous groups share strong bonds but resist legal reform, while diverse populations accept new laws more readily but are difficult to unite. Despite both challenges, legislation and colonization are humanity's greatest instruments of improvement.
D: Distilled: Homogeneous colonies resist new laws while mixed populations resist unity, yet legislation and colonization remain the greatest instruments of human improvement.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The bee-swarm metaphor for colonization was conventional in Greek thought; compare Aristotle's Politics (I.2) on the natural development of political communities. The insight that people cling to harmful customs through force of habit anticipates modern concepts of path dependence and institutional inertia. The horse-team metaphor ("pull together") was a common Greek image for political cooperation. The final claim that legislation and colonization are humanity's greatest instruments of improvement reflects the ambitious scope of the Laws project. Compare Republic VI (497a-b) on the difficulty of implementing philosophical reform, and Statesman (293d-e) on the statesman's task of weaving together diverse populations.

D: Original:
No doubt; but I should like to know why you say so.

My good friend, I am afraid that the course of my speculations is leading me to say something depreciatory of legislators; but if the word be to the purpose, there can be no harm. And yet, why am I disquieted, for I believe that the same principle applies equally to all human things?

To what are you referring?
D: Narrative:
"No doubt; but I should like to know why you say so."

"My good friend, I am afraid that the course of my speculations is leading me to say something depreciatory of legislators; but if the word be to the purpose, there can be no harm. And yet, why am I disquieted, for I believe that the same principle applies equally to all human things?"

"To what are you referring?"
D: Refashioned:
"I do not doubt it, but I would like to know why you say so."

"My good friend, I am afraid that my line of thinking is leading me to say something rather unflattering about legislators. But if the point is relevant, there can be no harm in it. And really, why should I be troubled? I believe the same principle applies to all human affairs."

"What are you referring to?"
D: Elegant: Cleinias asks the Athenian to explain his claim. The Athenian hesitates, sensing his argument will be unflattering to legislators, but resolves to proceed since the principle applies to all human affairs.
D: Summary: Cleinias presses the Athenian to explain his claim about legislation and colonization. The Athenian hesitates, aware his next argument will seem to diminish the role of legislators, but decides to proceed since the principle he has in mind applies universally.
D: Distilled: The Athenian hesitates before an argument that seems to diminish legislators, then resolves to proceed because the principle applies to all human endeavors.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- This transitional passage sets up the crucial discussion of chance (tyche) versus art (techne) in legislation (4.12-4.13). The Athenian's momentary hesitation is a characteristic dramatic device in the Laws, where controversial claims are introduced with qualifications and apologies. The universal principle he is about to articulate -- that chance governs most human affairs -- will be balanced by the counterclaim that divine providence and human skill also play essential roles.

D: Original: I was going to say that man never legislates, but accidents of all sorts, which legislate for us in all sorts of ways. The violence of war and the hard necessity of poverty are constantly overturning governments and changing laws. And the power of discase has often caused innovations in the state, when there have been pestilences, or when there has been a succession of bad seasons continuing during many years. Any one who sees all this, naturally rushes to the conclusion of which I was speaking, that no mortal legislates in anything, but that in human affairs chance is almost everything. And this may be said of the arts of the sailor, and the pilot, and the physician, and the general, and may seem to be well said; and yet there is another thing which may be said with equal truth of all of them.
D: Narrative: "I was going to say that man never legislates, but accidents of all sorts, which legislate for us in all sorts of ways. The violence of war and the hard necessity of poverty are constantly overturning governments and changing laws. And the power of disease has often caused innovations in the state, when there have been pestilences, or when there has been a succession of bad seasons continuing during many years. Any one who sees all this, naturally rushes to the conclusion of which I was speaking, that no mortal legislates in anything, but that in human affairs chance is almost everything. And this may be said of the arts of the sailor, and the pilot, and the physician, and the general, and may seem to be well said; and yet there is another thing which may be said with equal truth of all of them."
D: Refashioned:
"I was going to say that human beings never truly legislate -- rather, accidents of all kinds legislate for us in all kinds of ways. The violence of war and the hard pressure of poverty are constantly overturning governments and changing laws. Disease, too, has often forced political innovation, whether through plagues or long successions of bad harvests.

Anyone who observes all this naturally rushes to the conclusion I was hinting at: that no mortal truly legislates about anything, and that in human affairs chance is almost everything. The same could be said of the arts of sailing, piloting, medicine, and generalship -- and it might seem well said. Yet there is something else that can be said with equal truth about all of them."
D: Elegant: Human beings do not truly legislate -- accidents do. War, poverty, disease, and bad harvests constantly overturn governments and change laws, leading to the natural conclusion that chance governs almost everything in human affairs. The same appears true of sailing, medicine, and generalship. Yet something equally true can be said from another angle.
D: Summary: The Athenian observes that wars, famines, and plagues -- not deliberate human planning -- are the real forces that shape laws and governments, suggesting that chance dominates human affairs. He extends this observation to all practical arts before hinting at a counterargument.
D: Distilled: Chance appears to govern human affairs almost entirely, since wars, plagues, and famines -- not deliberate planning -- are what actually reshape laws and governments.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage presents the strongest possible case for the dominance of chance (tyche) before the Athenian introduces the countervailing roles of divine providence and human art. The argument resembles Thucydides' analysis of how the plague at Athens (430 BC) destroyed social norms and legal order (History II.47-54). The comparison to sailing, medicine, and generalship -- all arts that operate under conditions of uncertainty -- prepares for the synthesis in 4.13 where God, chance, and art are woven together. Compare Statesman (291d-292a) on the statesman's art operating under conditions of uncertainty, and Timaeus (47e-48a) on the interplay of reason and necessity.

D: Original:
What is it?

That God governs all things, and that chance and opportunity co-operate with him in the government of human affairs. There is, however, a third and less extreme view, that art should be there also; for I should say that in a storm there must surely be a great advantage in having the aid of the pilot's art. You would agree?

Yes.

And does not a like principle apply to legislation as well as to other things: even supposing all the conditions to be favourable which are needed for the happiness of the state, yet the true legislator must from time to time appear on the scene?

Most true.

In each case the artist would be able to pray rightly for certain conditions, and if these were granted by fortune, he would then only require to exercise his art?

Certainly.

And all the other artists just now mentioned, if they were bidden to offer up each their special prayer, would do so?

Of course.

And the legislator would do likewise?

I believe that he would.

"Come, legislator," we will say to him; "what are the conditions which you require in a state before you can organize it?" How ought he to answer this question? Shall I give his answer?

Yes.

He will say-"Give me a state which is governed by a tyrant, and let the tyrant be young and have a good memory; let him be quick at learning, and of a courageous and noble nature; let him have that quality which, as I said before, is the inseparable companion of all the other parts of virtue, if there is to be any good in them."

I suppose, Megillus, that this companion virtue of which the Stranger speaks, must be temperance?
D: Narrative:
"What is it?"

"That God governs all things, and that chance and opportunity co-operate with him in the government of human affairs. There is, however, a third and less extreme view, that art should be there also; for I should say that in a storm there must surely be a great advantage in having the aid of the pilot's art. You would agree?"

"Yes."

"And does not a like principle apply to legislation as well as to other things: even supposing all the conditions to be favourable which are needed for the happiness of the state, yet the true legislator must from time to time appear on the scene?"

"Most true."

"In each case the artist would be able to pray rightly for certain conditions, and if these were granted by fortune, he would then only require to exercise his art?"

"Certainly."

"And all the other artists just now mentioned, if they were bidden to offer up each their special prayer, would do so?"

"Of course."

"And the legislator would do likewise?"

"I believe that he would."

"'Come, legislator,' we will say to him; 'what are the conditions which you require in a state before you can organize it?' How ought he to answer this question? Shall I give his answer?"

"Yes."

"He will say -- 'Give me a state which is governed by a tyrant, and let the tyrant be young and have a good memory; let him be quick at learning, and of a courageous and noble nature; let him have that quality which, as I said before, is the inseparable companion of all the other parts of virtue, if there is to be any good in them.'"

"I suppose, Megillus, that this companion virtue of which the Stranger speaks, must be temperance?"
D: Refashioned:
"What is it?"

"That God governs all things, and that chance and opportunity cooperate with him in governing human affairs. But there is a third, less extreme position: that human skill must also play its part. In a storm, surely there is a great advantage in having the aid of a skilled pilot. Would you agree?"

"Yes."

"And does the same principle not apply to legislation? Even if all conditions needed for a state's happiness are favorable, a true legislator must still appear on the scene from time to time."

"That is certainly true."

"In every art, the practitioner could rightly pray for certain conditions, and if fortune granted them, he would then only need to exercise his skill."

"Certainly."

"Every artist we just mentioned would offer their own specific prayer if asked to do so?"

"Of course."

"And the legislator would do the same?"

"I believe so."

"Then let us say to him: 'Come, legislator -- what conditions do you require in a state before you can organize it?' How should he answer? Shall I speak for him?"

"Please do."

"He will say: 'Give me a state governed by a tyrant. Let the tyrant be young and have an excellent memory. Let him be quick to learn, courageous, and noble in nature. And let him possess that quality which, as I said before, is the inseparable companion of all other parts of virtue -- without which none of them has any real value.'"

"I suppose, Megillus, that the companion virtue the Stranger means is temperance?"
D: Elegant: God governs all things, with chance and opportunity cooperating, but human art must also play its part -- just as a pilot's skill is invaluable in a storm. The same applies to legislation: favorable conditions alone are not enough; a true legislator must appear. If asked what conditions he requires, the ideal legislator would say: give me a state ruled by a tyrant who is young, quick to learn, courageous, noble, and possessed of temperance -- the virtue that makes all other virtues effective.
D: Summary: The Athenian synthesizes three forces governing human affairs: God, chance, and human art. Applied to legislation, this means the true lawgiver needs both favorable circumstances and personal skill. Surprisingly, the ideal starting condition is a state ruled by a young, virtuous tyrant -- one who above all possesses temperance.
D: Distilled: God, chance, and human skill together govern affairs, and the ideal legislator would pray for a state ruled by a young tyrant possessing temperance, the companion virtue that makes all others effective.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- The tripartite framework of God, chance, and art (techne) is a major philosophical contribution of the Laws, balancing theological, contingent, and rationalist accounts of political order. The shocking claim that the ideal legislator wants a tyranny to work with is developed in 4.15-4.20. The insistence on temperance (sophrosyne) as the companion virtue echoes Laws Book 1 (631c-d) and Republic IV (430d-432a), where temperance is the virtue that harmonizes all parts of the soul. Cleinias addresses Megillus rather than the Stranger, showing the Spartan is also actively engaged in the discussion.

D: Original: Yes, Cleinias, temperance in the vulgar sense; not that which in the forced and exaggerated language of some philosophers is called prudence, but that which is the natural gift of children and animals, of whom some live continently and others incontinently, but when isolated, was as we said, hardly worth reckoning in the catalogue of goods. I think that you must understand my meaning.
D: Narrative: "Yes, Cleinias, temperance in the vulgar sense; not that which in the forced and exaggerated language of some philosophers is called prudence, but that which is the natural gift of children and animals, of whom some live continently and others incontinently, but when isolated, was as we said, hardly worth reckoning in the catalogue of goods. I think that you must understand my meaning."
D: Refashioned: "Yes, Cleinias -- temperance in the ordinary sense. Not what some philosophers, in their strained and exaggerated language, call prudence, but the kind that is a natural gift found even in children and animals, some of whom live with self-restraint and others without it. By itself, as we said earlier, this natural temperance is hardly worth counting among the goods. I trust you understand what I mean."
D: Elegant: The temperance required is the ordinary, natural kind found in children and animals -- not the elaborate 'prudence' of certain philosophers. By itself it counts for little, but as a companion to other virtues, it is indispensable.
D: Summary: The Athenian clarifies that the temperance he means is the natural, everyday sort -- the instinctive self-control found even in children and animals -- not the elevated 'prudence' theorized by some philosophers. Alone it has little value, but combined with other virtues it is essential.
D: Distilled: The required temperance is the natural self-restraint found even in children and animals, not philosophical prudence -- modest on its own but essential as a companion to other virtues.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The distinction between vulgar temperance (natural self-control) and philosophical prudence (phronesis) targets specific philosophical positions, possibly those of the Academy itself or Socratic intellectualism which identified virtue with knowledge. The claim that some children and animals are naturally continent echoes the discussion in Republic IV (430e-431a) where temperance is described as a kind of order and self-mastery. The idea that temperance alone is "hardly worth reckoning" recurs in Laws Book 1 (631c), where virtues are ranked and temperance without wisdom is deemed incomplete. Compare Aristotle's distinction between natural virtue and full virtue in Nicomachean Ethics VI.13.

D: Original:
Certainly.

Then our tyrant must have this as well as the other qualities, if the state is to acquire in the best manner and in the shortest time the form of government which is most conducive to happiness; for there neither is nor ever will be a better or speedier way of establishing a polity than by a tyranny.

By what possible arguments, Stranger, can any man persuade himself of such a monstrous doctrine?

There is surely no difficulty in seeing, Cleinias, what is in accordance with the order of nature?

You would assume, as you say, a tyrant who was young, temperate, quick at learning, having a good memory, courageous, of a noble nature?
D: Narrative:
"Certainly."

"Then our tyrant must have this as well as the other qualities, if the state is to acquire in the best manner and in the shortest time the form of government which is most conducive to happiness; for there neither is nor ever will be a better or speedier way of establishing a polity than by a tyranny."

"By what possible arguments, Stranger, can any man persuade himself of such a monstrous doctrine?"

"There is surely no difficulty in seeing, Cleinias, what is in accordance with the order of nature?"

"You would assume, as you say, a tyrant who was young, temperate, quick at learning, having a good memory, courageous, of a noble nature?"
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly."

"Then our tyrant must possess this natural temperance along with all his other qualities, if the state is to acquire the form of government most conducive to happiness in the best and quickest way possible. For there never has been, nor ever will be, a better or faster method of establishing a good constitution than through a tyranny."

"By what conceivable arguments, Stranger, could anyone persuade himself of such a monstrous doctrine?"

"Surely there is no difficulty in seeing what accords with the order of nature, Cleinias?"

"You are assuming, as you say, a tyrant who is young, temperate, quick to learn, with a good memory, courageous, and noble by nature?"
D: Elegant: The temperate tyrant offers the fastest and best path to establishing a good constitution -- there never has been and never will be a better method. Cleinias finds this doctrine monstrous, but the Athenian insists it follows from the order of nature. The requirements are clear: the tyrant must be young, temperate, intelligent, courageous, and noble.
D: Summary: The Athenian makes the provocative claim that tyranny offers the fastest route to an ideal constitution, provided the tyrant is virtuous. Cleinias is shocked by this 'monstrous doctrine,' but the Athenian maintains it follows naturally from the logic of political transformation.
D: Distilled: A temperate tyranny provides the fastest possible path to ideal government -- a claim Cleinias finds monstrous but the Athenian considers a natural truth.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's shock at the "monstrous doctrine" mirrors the likely reaction of Plato's audience. The argument is not that tyranny is good but that concentrated power, when combined with virtue and a great legislator, enables the swiftest transformation. This revisits themes from the Republic's philosopher-king argument (V, 473c-d), but in a more pragmatic register. The Statesman (293c-e) similarly argues that the ideal ruler's knowledge matters more than the form of government. The phrase "order of nature" (kata physin) signals that this is presented as a structural truth about power, not a moral endorsement of tyranny.

D: Original: Yes; and you must add fortunate; and his good fortune must be that he is the contemporary of a great legislator, and that some happy chance brings them together. When this has been accomplished, God has done all that he ever does for a state which he desires to be eminently prosperous; He has done second best for a state in which there are two such rulers, and third best for a state in which there are three. The difficulty increases with the increase, and diminishes with the diminution of the number.
D: Narrative: "Yes; and you must add fortunate; and his good fortune must be that he is the contemporary of a great legislator, and that some happy chance brings them together. When this has been accomplished, God has done all that he ever does for a state which he desires to be eminently prosperous; He has done second best for a state in which there are two such rulers, and third best for a state in which there are three. The difficulty increases with the increase, and diminishes with the diminution of the number."
D: Refashioned: "Yes -- and you must add one more quality: good fortune. His good fortune must be that he lives at the same time as a great legislator, and that some happy chance brings them together. When that occurs, God has done everything he ever does for a state he wishes to make supremely prosperous. The second-best divine dispensation is a state with two such rulers, the third-best a state with three. The difficulty increases as the number of rulers grows, and diminishes as it shrinks."
D: Elegant: The virtuous tyrant must also be fortunate -- specifically, fortunate enough to be a contemporary of a great legislator and to be brought together with him by happy chance. This is the most God ever does for a state. With two rulers it is second best, with three third best -- difficulty increases with the number of those who must be persuaded.
D: Summary: The ideal tyrant must be fortunate enough to encounter a great legislator. This convergence of virtuous power and legislative wisdom is the greatest gift God gives a state. The more rulers involved, the harder the transformation becomes.
D: Distilled: The convergence of a virtuous tyrant and a great legislator is the greatest gift God bestows on a state, and difficulty increases with each additional ruler.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The principle that fewer decision-makers enable faster reform is a political observation with lasting relevance. The idea that God's role is to create favorable conditions (the meeting of tyrant and legislator) while leaving the work to human art connects back to the tripartite framework of 4.13 (God, chance, art). Compare the Republic's requirement that philosophers and political power coincide (V, 473c-d) -- the same structural insight expressed differently. The inverse relationship between number of rulers and ease of reform implicitly ranks regime types by their capacity for transformation, a ranking made explicit in 4.18.

D: Original: You mean to say, I suppose, that the best government is produced from a tyranny, and originates in a good lawgiver and an orderly tyrant, and that the change from such a tyranny into a perfect form of government takes place most easily; less easily when from an oligarchy; and, in the third degree, from a democracy: is not that your meaning?
D: Narrative: "You mean to say, I suppose, that the best government is produced from a tyranny, and originates in a good lawgiver and an orderly tyrant, and that the change from such a tyranny into a perfect form of government takes place most easily; less easily when from an oligarchy; and, in the third degree, from a democracy: is not that your meaning?"
D: Refashioned: "You mean to say, I take it, that the best government originates from a tyranny -- specifically, from the combination of a good lawgiver and a well-ordered tyrant -- and that the transformation from such a tyranny into an ideal constitution is the easiest; less easy from an oligarchy; and third easiest from a democracy. Is that your meaning?"
D: Elegant: Cleinias summarizes: the best government originates from a tyranny paired with a good legislator, less easily from an oligarchy, and third from a democracy.
D: Summary: Cleinias attempts to summarize the Athenian's argument: the ideal constitution emerges most easily from a virtuous tyranny, less easily from an oligarchy, and with the most difficulty from a democracy.
D: Distilled: Cleinias restates the argument as a ranking: tyranny transforms most easily into ideal government, followed by oligarchy, then democracy.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- Cleinias's summary is close but not quite right, as the Athenian will correct in 4.18. He places democracy third in difficulty, whereas the Athenian will rank it above oligarchy. This misunderstanding is dramatically effective: it shows Cleinias is engaged but thinking conventionally, and gives the Athenian an opportunity to refine the point. The ranking of regime types by transformative potential contrasts with the Republic's sequence of constitutional degeneration (aristocracy, timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny -- Republic VIII).

D: Original: Not so; I mean rather to say that the change is best made out of a tyranny; and secondly, out of a monarchy; and thirdly, out of some sort of democracy: fourth, in the capacity for improvement, comes oligarchy, which has the greatest difficulty in admitting of such a change, because the government is in the hands of a number of potentates. I am supposing that the legislator is by nature of the true sort, and that his strength is united with that of the chief men of the state; and when the ruling element is numerically small, and at the same time very strong, as in a tyranny, there the change is likely to be easiest and most rapid.
D: Narrative: "Not so; I mean rather to say that the change is best made out of a tyranny; and secondly, out of a monarchy; and thirdly, out of some sort of democracy: fourth, in the capacity for improvement, comes oligarchy, which has the greatest difficulty in admitting of such a change, because the government is in the hands of a number of potentates. I am supposing that the legislator is by nature of the true sort, and that his strength is united with that of the chief men of the state; and when the ruling element is numerically small, and at the same time very strong, as in a tyranny, there the change is likely to be easiest and most rapid."
D: Refashioned:
"Not quite. What I mean is that transformation is most easily achieved from a tyranny; second, from a monarchy; third, from some form of democracy; and fourth -- with the greatest difficulty -- from an oligarchy, because power is in the hands of multiple potentates.

I am assuming that the legislator is genuinely capable and that his strength is united with that of the state's leading men. When the ruling element is numerically small yet very powerful, as in a tyranny, the change is likely to be easiest and most rapid."
D: Elegant: The correct ranking for ease of political transformation is: tyranny first, monarchy second, democracy third, and oligarchy last with the greatest difficulty. Power concentrated in few hands enables rapid change; oligarchy resists because multiple potentates must be persuaded.
D: Summary: The Athenian corrects Cleinias's summary: the ranking for ease of constitutional transformation is tyranny, monarchy, democracy, then oligarchy. Oligarchy is hardest to reform because power is dispersed among multiple powerful individuals, while tyranny concentrates power and enables the fastest change.
D: Distilled: Political transformation is easiest from tyranny and hardest from oligarchy, because concentrated power enables rapid reform while dispersed power among potentates creates maximum resistance.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The correction placing democracy above oligarchy in transformative capacity is significant: democracies, despite their diffusion of power among many, are more receptive to persuasion than oligarchies where a few powerful individuals block change. This insight anticipates Tocqueville's observations about democratic receptiveness to reform. The ranking differs fundamentally from the Republic's degeneration sequence (VIII, 545c-569c), where tyranny is the worst outcome. Here, the Laws treats tyranny instrumentally -- as a starting point for transformation, not an end state. Compare Statesman (302e-303b) for a different ranking of regime types.

D: Original:
How? I do not understand.

And yet I have repeated what I am saying a good many times; but I suppose that you have never seen a city which is under a tyranny?

No, and I cannot say that I have any great desire to see one.

And yet, where there is a tyranny, you might certainly see that of which I am now speaking.

What do you mean?

I mean that you might see how, without trouble and in no very long period of time, the tyrant, if he wishes, can change the manners of a state: he has only to go in the direction of virtue or of vice, whichever he prefers, he himself indicating by his example the lines of conduct, praising and rewarding some actions and reproving others, and degrading those who disobey.

But how can we imagine that the citizens in general will at once follow the example set to them; and how can he have this power both of persuading and of compelling them?

Let no one, my friends, persuade us that there is any quicker and easier way in which states change their laws than when the rulers lead: such changes never have, nor ever will, come to pass in any other way. The real impossibility or difficulty is of another sort, and is rarely surmounted in the course of ages; but when once it is surmounted, ten thousand or rather all blessings follow.

Of what are you speaking?
D: Narrative:
"How? I do not understand."

"And yet I have repeated what I am saying a good many times; but I suppose that you have never seen a city which is under a tyranny?"

"No, and I cannot say that I have any great desire to see one."

"And yet, where there is a tyranny, you might certainly see that of which I am now speaking."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean that you might see how, without trouble and in no very long period of time, the tyrant, if he wishes, can change the manners of a state: he has only to go in the direction of virtue or of vice, whichever he prefers, he himself indicating by his example the lines of conduct, praising and rewarding some actions and reproving others, and degrading those who disobey."

"But how can we imagine that the citizens in general will at once follow the example set to them; and how can he have this power both of persuading and of compelling them?"

"Let no one, my friends, persuade us that there is any quicker and easier way in which states change their laws than when the rulers lead: such changes never have, nor ever will, come to pass in any other way. The real impossibility or difficulty is of another sort, and is rarely surmounted in the course of ages; but when once it is surmounted, ten thousand or rather all blessings follow."

"Of what are you speaking?"
D: Refashioned:
"How? I do not understand."

"I have said this many times now, but I suppose you have never seen a city living under tyranny?"

"No, and I cannot say I have any great desire to see one."

"Yet in a tyranny you could certainly observe what I am describing."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean that you could see how easily and quickly a tyrant, if he chooses, can transform a state's character. He simply moves in the direction of virtue or vice -- whichever he prefers -- setting the example himself, praising and rewarding certain behaviors, reproving others, and punishing those who disobey."

"But how can we imagine that citizens will immediately follow his example? How can he have the power both to persuade and to compel them?"

"Let no one persuade us, my friends, that there is any quicker or easier way for states to change their laws than when their rulers lead the way. Such changes have never occurred, and never will occur, by any other means. The real difficulty -- the impossibility, almost -- is of an entirely different kind, and it is rarely overcome even across the span of ages. But when it is overcome, ten thousand blessings follow -- or rather, all blessings."

"What are you speaking of?"
D: Elegant: A tyrant who chooses virtue can transform a state's character quickly by personal example, praise, reward, and punishment. No faster method of political change exists or ever will. The real difficulty is not the mechanism of change but finding a ruler who will use this power virtuously -- a challenge rarely met across the ages, but one whose solution brings all blessings.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that a tyrant can reshape a state's character rapidly through personal example and a system of rewards and punishments. The true difficulty is not how political change happens -- it always flows from leadership -- but finding rulers who will lead toward virtue rather than vice.
D: Distilled: Political change always flows from leadership, and a tyrant can transform a state's character faster than any other ruler -- the real difficulty is finding one who chooses virtue.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's lack of experience with tyranny is consistent with Crete's mixed constitution. The argument that the ruler's personal example is the primary mechanism of moral transformation echoes Republic VI (500d) where the philosopher-king models his own soul and the state on the Forms. The distinction between the ease of the mechanism (leadership by example) and the difficulty of the condition (finding a virtuous ruler) is the crux of the argument. The phrase "ten thousand or rather all blessings" suggests that virtuous leadership is not merely beneficial but the foundational condition for all other goods -- a claim that parallels Republic V's assertion that philosophical rule is the one change that would transform everything (473c-d).

D: Original: The difficulty is to find the divine love of temperate and just institutions existing in any powerful forms of government, whether in a monarchy or oligarchy of wealth or of birth. You might as well hope to reproduce the character of Nestor, who is said to have excelled all men in the power of speech, and yet more in his temperance. This, however, according to the tradition, was in the times of Troy; in our own days there is nothing of the sort; but if such an one either has or ever shall come into being, or is now among us, blessed is he and blessed are they who hear the wise words that flow from his lips. And this may be said of power in general: When the supreme power in man coincides with the greatest wisdom and temperance, then the best laws and the best constitution come into being; but in no other way. And let what I have been saying be regarded as a kind of sacred legend or oracle, and let this be our proof that, in one point of view, there may be a difficulty for a city to have good laws, but that there is another point of view in which nothing can be easier or sooner effected, granting our supposition.
D: Narrative: "The difficulty is to find the divine love of temperate and just institutions existing in any powerful forms of government, whether in a monarchy or oligarchy of wealth or of birth. You might as well hope to reproduce the character of Nestor, who is said to have excelled all men in the power of speech, and yet more in his temperance. This, however, according to the tradition, was in the times of Troy; in our own days there is nothing of the sort; but if such an one either has or ever shall come into being, or is now among us, blessed is he and blessed are they who hear the wise words that flow from his lips. And this may be said of power in general: When the supreme power in man coincides with the greatest wisdom and temperance, then the best laws and the best constitution come into being; but in no other way. And let what I have been saying be regarded as a kind of sacred legend or oracle, and let this be our proof that, in one point of view, there may be a difficulty for a city to have good laws, but that there is another point of view in which nothing can be easier or sooner effected, granting our supposition."
D: Refashioned:
"The difficulty is finding a genuine love of temperate and just institutions in any powerful form of government -- whether a monarchy, an oligarchy of wealth, or an aristocracy of birth. You might as well hope to reproduce the character of Nestor, who is said to have surpassed all men in eloquence, and even more in his temperance. But that, according to tradition, was in the age of Troy. In our own time there is nothing like it.

Yet if such a person has ever existed, or will exist, or exists among us now -- blessed is he, and blessed are those who hear the wise words that flow from his lips. The same may be said of power in general: when supreme power coincides with the greatest wisdom and temperance in one person, then the best laws and the best constitution come into being. But never in any other way.

Let what I have said be treated as a kind of sacred legend or oracle. Let it serve as proof that from one perspective it is enormously difficult for a city to have good laws, but from another perspective -- given the right conditions -- nothing could be easier or more quickly accomplished."
D: Elegant: The true difficulty is finding a love of justice and temperance in any powerful ruler. When supreme power coincides with supreme wisdom and temperance, the best laws come into being -- but never otherwise. This is as rare as reproducing the character of Nestor. Let this be received as an oracle: good laws are nearly impossible from one perspective, yet from another -- given the right person in power -- nothing is easier.
D: Summary: The real obstacle to ideal legislation is the extreme rarity of rulers who love justice and temperance. When supreme power and supreme wisdom coincide, the best constitution arises effortlessly -- but this convergence is as rare as the legendary Nestor. The Athenian offers this as an oracle: good laws are both nearly impossible and potentially very easy, depending on who holds power.
D: Distilled: The best constitution arises only when supreme power coincides with supreme wisdom and temperance -- a convergence so rare it must be treated as an oracle rather than a practical expectation.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage is the Laws' equivalent of the Republic's philosopher-king thesis (V, 473c-d): the best political order requires the coincidence of power and wisdom. Nestor, king of Pylos, appears in the Iliad as the wisest and most temperate of the Greek leaders at Troy. The framing as "sacred legend or oracle" (hieros logos) signals that this is being presented as a foundational truth beyond empirical demonstration. The paradox that good laws are simultaneously nearly impossible and potentially effortless captures the central tension of Platonic political philosophy: the conditions for the best regime are simple in theory but vanishingly rare in practice. Compare Statesman (293e-294a) on the true statesman whose knowledge makes constitutional form secondary.

D: Original:
How do you mean?

Let us try to amuse ourselves, old boys as we are, by moulding in words the laws which are suitable to your state.

Let us proceed without delay.

Then let us invoke God at the settlement of our state; may he hear and be propitious to us, and come and set in order the State and the laws!

May he come!

But what form of polity are we going to give the city?

Tell us what you mean a little more clearly. Do you mean some form of democracy, or oligarchy, or aristocracy, or monarchy? For we cannot suppose that you would include tyranny.

Which of you will first tell me to which of these classes his own government is to be referred?

Ought I to answer first, since I am the elder?

Perhaps you should.
D: Narrative:
"How do you mean?"

"Let us try to amuse ourselves, old boys as we are, by moulding in words the laws which are suitable to your state."

"Let us proceed without delay."

"Then let us invoke God at the settlement of our state; may he hear and be propitious to us, and come and set in order the State and the laws!"

"May he come!"

"But what form of polity are we going to give the city?"

"Tell us what you mean a little more clearly. Do you mean some form of democracy, or oligarchy, or aristocracy, or monarchy? For we cannot suppose that you would include tyranny."

"Which of you will first tell me to which of these classes his own government is to be referred?"

"Ought I to answer first, since I am the elder?"

"Perhaps you should."
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"Let us amuse ourselves, old as we are, by shaping in words the laws that would suit your state."

"Let us begin without delay."

"Then let us invoke God at the founding of our state. May he hear us and look upon us with favor, and come to set in order both the state and its laws!"

"May he come!"

"But what form of government shall we give the city?"

"Explain more clearly what you mean. Do you mean some form of democracy, or oligarchy, or aristocracy, or monarchy? We cannot suppose you would include tyranny."

"Which of you two will first tell me what category his own government falls into?"

"Should I answer first, since I am the elder?"

"Perhaps you should."
D: Elegant: The three elders turn to the practical task of shaping laws for the new colony, invoking God's favor. When the Athenian asks what form of government the city should have, Cleinias lists the conventional options -- democracy, oligarchy, aristocracy, monarchy -- and excludes tyranny. The Athenian deflects by asking them to classify their own governments first.
D: Summary: The conversation shifts from theory to practice as the three companions begin the actual work of legislation, invoking divine aid. When asked about the form of government, the Athenian first asks Megillus and Cleinias to classify their own existing governments.
D: Distilled: The three elders invoke God and begin the practical work of legislation, but before choosing a form of government the Athenian asks them to first classify their own existing constitutions.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. -- The phrase "old boys" (literally "old men playing") captures the unique tone of the Laws: serious philosophical legislation undertaken as a form of play (paidia) during a festival walk. This ludic framing recurs throughout the dialogue. The invocation of God at the founding echoes actual Greek colonial practice, where new settlements were established with religious ceremony. The exclusion of tyranny from Cleinias's list is ironic given the preceding discussion about tyranny's advantages for reform. The Athenian's tactic of asking about existing governments before proposing a new one is characteristically Socratic -- examining assumptions before building on them.

D: Original:
And yet, Stranger, I perceive that I cannot say, without more thought, what I should call the government of Lacedaemon, for it seems to me to be like a tyranny-the power of our Ephors is marvellously tyrannical; and sometimes it appears to me to be of all cities the most democratical; and who can reasonably deny that it is an aristocracy? We have also a monarchy which is held for life, and is said by all mankind, and not by ourselves only, to be the most ancient of all monarchies; and, therefore, when asked on a sudden, I cannot precisely say which form of government the Spartan is.

I am in the same difficulty, Megillus; for I do not feel confident that the polity of Cnosus is any of these.
D: Narrative:
"And yet, Stranger, I perceive that I cannot say, without more thought, what I should call the government of Lacedaemon, for it seems to me to be like a tyranny -- the power of our Ephors is marvellously tyrannical; and sometimes it appears to me to be of all cities the most democratical; and who can reasonably deny that it is an aristocracy? We have also a monarchy which is held for life, and is said by all mankind, and not by ourselves only, to be the most ancient of all monarchies; and, therefore, when asked on a sudden, I cannot precisely say which form of government the Spartan is."

"I am in the same difficulty, Megillus; for I do not feel confident that the polity of Cnosus is any of these."
D: Refashioned:
"And yet, Stranger, on reflection I find I cannot easily say what to call the government of Sparta. It seems in some ways like a tyranny -- the power of our Ephors is remarkably tyrannical. At other times it appears to be the most democratic of all cities. And who could reasonably deny that it is an aristocracy? We also have a dual monarchy held for life, said by everyone -- not just by us Spartans -- to be the most ancient monarchy in the world. So when asked without warning, I simply cannot say which category Spartan government falls into."

"I am in the same difficulty, Megillus. I cannot confidently say that the government of Cnosus fits any of these categories either."
D: Elegant: Megillus cannot classify Sparta's government: it seems tyrannical in its Ephors' power, democratic in some respects, aristocratic in others, and monarchical in its ancient dual kingship. Cleinias faces the same difficulty with Cnosus -- it fits no single category.
D: Summary: Megillus admits he cannot classify Sparta's mixed constitution, which combines elements of tyranny, democracy, aristocracy, and monarchy. Cleinias faces the same difficulty with Cretan government, setting up the Athenian's argument that true polities transcend conventional categories.
D: Distilled: Both Megillus and Cleinias find their own constitutions unclassifiable under conventional categories, since Sparta and Crete blend tyrannical, democratic, aristocratic, and monarchical elements.
Notes: Speakers: Megillus and Cleinias. -- Megillus's analysis of the Spartan constitution as a mixture of all forms anticipates Aristotle's discussion of mixed constitutions in Politics (II.6, IV.9) and Polybius's later theory of the mixed constitution as the key to Roman stability (Histories VI). The five Ephors were annually elected magistrates with extraordinary powers, including the ability to put kings on trial. The dual Spartan kingship (two hereditary kings from the Agiad and Eurypontid families) was indeed regarded as the oldest continuous monarchy in Greece. Cleinias's parallel difficulty with Cnosus reflects the similarly mixed character of Cretan government, which ancient sources compared to Sparta's (Aristotle, Politics II.10).

D: Original: The reason is, my excellent friends, that you really have polities, but the states of which we were just now speaking are merely aggregations of men dwelling in cities who are the subjects and servants of a part of their own state, and each of them is named after the dominant power; they are not polities at all. But if states are to be named after their rulers, the true state ought to be called by the name of the God who rules over wise men.
D: Narrative: "The reason is, my excellent friends, that you really have polities, but the states of which we were just now speaking are merely aggregations of men dwelling in cities who are the subjects and servants of a part of their own state, and each of them is named after the dominant power; they are not polities at all. But if states are to be named after their rulers, the true state ought to be called by the name of the God who rules over wise men."
D: Refashioned: "The reason, my excellent friends, is that you actually have genuine constitutions. The other states we were just discussing are merely collections of people living in cities who are subjects and servants of one faction within their own state. Each is named after whichever power dominates -- but they are not true constitutions at all. If states must be named after their rulers, then the true state should be called by the name of the God who rules over wise men."
D: Elegant: Sparta and Crete are unclassifiable because they are genuine constitutions -- unlike so-called 'democracies' and 'oligarchies,' which are merely cities where one faction dominates and the rest are its servants. If a state must be named after its ruler, the true state should bear the name of God, who rules over the wise.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that Sparta and Crete resist classification because they are genuine mixed constitutions, unlike ordinary states that are merely one faction dominating the rest. He proposes that the true state should be named after God, who rules over wise men.
D: Distilled: Ordinary states named 'democracy' or 'oligarchy' are merely factional dominations, not true constitutions -- the genuine state should be named after the God who rules over the wise.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is one of the most radical claims in the Laws: that conventional regime classifications (democracy, oligarchy, etc.) describe pathologies rather than genuine political orders. A true polity is one where no faction dominates. The suggestion that the state should be named after God introduces the theological foundation of the Laws' political theory: legitimate rule derives from divine reason (nous), not human power. This anticipates the myth of Cronos in 4.25 and connects to the etymology of "law" (nomos) from "mind" (nous) that Plato develops there. Compare Republic V (473c-d) on philosopher-kings and Statesman (271d-272b) on the age of Cronos.

D: Original:
And who is this God?

May I still make use of fable to some extent, in the hope that I may be better able to answer your question: shall I?

By all means.

In the primeval world, and a long while before the cities came into being whose settlements we have described, there is said to have been in the time of Cronos a blessed rule and life, of which the best-ordered of existing states is a copy.

It will be very necessary to hear about that.

I quite agree with you; and therefore I have introduced the subject.

Most appropriately; and since the tale is to the point, you will do well in giving us the whole story.
D: Narrative:
"And who is this God?"

"May I still make use of fable to some extent, in the hope that I may be better able to answer your question: shall I?"

"By all means."

"In the primeval world, and a long while before the cities came into being whose settlements we have described, there is said to have been in the time of Cronos a blessed rule and life, of which the best-ordered of existing states is a copy."

"It will be very necessary to hear about that."

"I quite agree with you; and therefore I have introduced the subject."

"Most appropriately; and since the tale is to the point, you will do well in giving us the whole story."
D: Refashioned:
"And who is this God?"

"May I use a myth to help answer your question?"

"By all means."

"In the primeval world, long before the cities we have been discussing came into existence, there is said to have been, in the age of Cronos, a blessed rule and a blessed life -- of which the best-ordered states that exist today are merely copies."

"It will be essential to hear about this."

"I quite agree, which is why I have raised the subject."

"Most fitting. Since the tale bears directly on our discussion, please give us the whole story."
D: Elegant: The Athenian introduces a myth to answer who should rule: in the age of Cronos, before any existing cities, there was a blessed form of rule and life that the best current states can only copy. Cleinias insists on hearing the full story.
D: Summary: The Athenian introduces the myth of the age of Cronos to explain what genuine divine rule looks like. In that primeval era, there was a blessed form of governance of which even the best existing states are imperfect copies.
D: Distilled: The Athenian introduces the myth of Cronos's golden age as the model of divine governance that all existing states imperfectly copy.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- The age of Cronos (Roman: Saturn) was the Greek version of the Golden Age, a time of peace, abundance, and divine governance before the present age of Zeus. Plato uses this myth differently in different dialogues: in the Statesman (271c-272d), the age of Cronos is a time when God directly steers the cosmos, while the present age is one of divine withdrawal. Here in the Laws, it serves as the model for law-governed human communities. The use of myth (mythos) to express philosophical truths is a characteristic Platonic method; see the Allegory of the Cave (Republic VII), the Allegory of Er (Republic X), and the creation myth of the Timaeus.

D: Original:
I will do as you suggest. There is a tradition of the happy life of mankind in days when all things were spontaneous and abundant. And of this the reason is said to have been as follows:-Cronos knew what we ourselves were declaring, that no human nature invested with supreme power is able to order human affairs and not overflow with insolence and wrong. Which reflection led him to appoint not men but demigods, who are of a higher and more divine race, to be the kings and rulers of our cities; he did as we do with flocks of sheep and other tame animals. For we do not appoint oxen to be the lords of oxen, or goats of goats; but we ourselves are a superior race, and rule over them. In like manner God, in his love of mankind, placed over us the demons, who are a superior race, and they with great case and pleasure to themselves, and no less to us, taking care us and giving us peace and reverence and order and justice never failing, made the tribes of men happy and united. And this tradition, which is true, declares that cities of which some mortal man and not God is the ruler, have no escape from evils and toils. Still we must do all that we can to imitate the life which is said to have existed in the days of Cronos, and, as far as the principle of immortality dwells in us, to that we must hearken, both in private and public life, and regulate our cities and houses according to law, meaning by the very term "law," the distribution of mind. But if either a single person or an oligarchy or a democracy has a soul eager after pleasures and desires-wanting to be filled with them, yet retaining none of them, and perpetually afflicted with an endless and insatiable disorder; and this evil spirit, having first trampled the laws under foot, becomes the master either of a state or of an individual-then, as I was saying, salvation is hopeless. And now, Cleinias, we have to consider whether you will or will not accept this tale of mine.

Certainly we will.
D: Narrative:
"I will do as you suggest. There is a tradition of the happy life of mankind in days when all things were spontaneous and abundant. And of this the reason is said to have been as follows: -- Cronos knew what we ourselves were declaring, that no human nature invested with supreme power is able to order human affairs and not overflow with insolence and wrong. Which reflection led him to appoint not men but demigods, who are of a higher and more divine race, to be the kings and rulers of our cities; he did as we do with flocks of sheep and other tame animals. For we do not appoint oxen to be the lords of oxen, or goats of goats; but we ourselves are a superior race, and rule over them. In like manner God, in his love of mankind, placed over us the demons, who are a superior race, and they with great ease and pleasure to themselves, and no less to us, taking care of us and giving us peace and reverence and order and justice never failing, made the tribes of men happy and united. And this tradition, which is true, declares that cities of which some mortal man and not God is the ruler, have no escape from evils and toils. Still we must do all that we can to imitate the life which is said to have existed in the days of Cronos, and, as far as the principle of immortality dwells in us, to that we must hearken, both in private and public life, and regulate our cities and houses according to law, meaning by the very term 'law,' the distribution of mind. But if either a single person or an oligarchy or a democracy has a soul eager after pleasures and desires -- wanting to be filled with them, yet retaining none of them, and perpetually afflicted with an endless and insatiable disorder; and this evil spirit, having first trampled the laws under foot, becomes the master either of a state or of an individual -- then, as I was saying, salvation is hopeless."

"And now, Cleinias, we have to consider whether you will or will not accept this tale of mine."

"Certainly we will."
D: Refashioned:
"I will do as you ask. There is a tradition of a happy era for mankind when everything was spontaneous and abundant. The reason is said to be this: Cronos understood what we ourselves have been arguing -- that no human being invested with supreme power can govern human affairs without becoming swollen with arrogance and injustice. This insight led him to appoint not men but demigods, beings of a higher and more divine nature, as kings and rulers of our cities. He did what we do with our flocks of sheep and domesticated animals: we do not set oxen over oxen or goats over goats, but we ourselves, being a superior species, rule over them. In the same way, God, out of love for humanity, placed over us the daemons -- a superior race -- who with great ease and pleasure to themselves, and no less to us, cared for us and provided peace, reverence, order, and unfailing justice, making the human tribes happy and united.

This tradition, which is true, tells us that cities ruled by mortal men rather than by God have no escape from evils and hardships. Still, we must do everything we can to imitate the life said to have existed under Cronos. Insofar as the principle of immortality dwells in us, we must listen to it in both private and public life, and govern our cities and households according to law -- understanding the very word 'law' to mean the distribution of reason.

But if a single person, an oligarchy, or a democracy has a soul hungry for pleasures and desires -- craving to be filled yet never satisfied, perpetually afflicted by an insatiable disorder -- and this corrupt spirit, having trampled the laws underfoot, becomes master of either a state or an individual, then salvation is hopeless.

Now, Cleinias, you must decide whether to accept this account of mine."

"We certainly accept it."
D: Elegant: Cronos understood that no human with supreme power can avoid becoming arrogant and unjust. So he appointed demigods -- superior beings -- to rule over humanity, just as we set ourselves over our flocks rather than letting oxen govern oxen. Under their care, humanity enjoyed peace, order, and unfailing justice. The lesson: cities ruled by mortals cannot escape evil. We must imitate the age of Cronos by governing according to law, understanding 'law' as the distribution of reason. When any regime -- tyranny, oligarchy, or democracy -- is driven by insatiable desire and tramples law underfoot, salvation is impossible.
D: Summary: The myth of Cronos teaches that human nature cannot be trusted with absolute power. God appointed superior beings to rule because mortals inevitably become corrupt. The practical lesson is that cities must govern by law -- understood as the distribution of reason -- and that any regime driven by insatiable desire rather than law is beyond saving.
D: Distilled: No human nature can wield supreme power without corruption, so law -- the distribution of reason -- must rule in place of any individual or faction, imitating the divine governance of the age of Cronos.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This is one of the most important passages in the Laws, establishing its central political theology. The shepherd analogy (superior beings ruling inferior ones) appears in the Statesman (271d-275a) but is used there more critically; the Statesman ultimately rejects the shepherd model for human politics, while the Laws uses it to argue for the rule of law as a substitute for divine governance. The etymology connecting nomos (law) to nous (mind/reason) is philosophically significant: law is not mere convention but the embodiment of rational order. The description of the insatiable soul echoes Republic IX (571b-572b) on the tyrannical soul driven by lawless desires. Compare Timaeus (90a-d) on the divine element in human nature that must be cultivated.

D: Original: You are aware-are you not?-that there are of said to be as many forms of laws as there are of governments, and of the latter we have already mentioned all those which are commonly recognized. Now you must regard this as a matter of first-rate importance. For what is to be the standard of just and unjust, is once more the point at issue. Men say that the law ought not to regard either military virtue, or virtue in general, but only the interests and power and preservation of the established form of government; this is thought by them to be the best way of expressing the natural definition of justice.
D: Narrative: "You are aware -- are you not? -- that there are said to be as many forms of laws as there are of governments, and of the latter we have already mentioned all those which are commonly recognized. Now you must regard this as a matter of first-rate importance. For what is to be the standard of just and unjust, is once more the point at issue. Men say that the law ought not to regard either military virtue, or virtue in general, but only the interests and power and preservation of the established form of government; this is thought by them to be the best way of expressing the natural definition of justice."
D: Refashioned:
"You are aware, I trust, that there are said to be as many kinds of law as there are forms of government -- and we have already listed all the commonly recognized forms. Now, you must treat this as a matter of the highest importance. The real question is: what will serve as the standard of just and unjust?

Some people say that law should concern itself not with military virtue, or with virtue of any kind, but solely with the interests, power, and preservation of whichever form of government happens to be in place. They consider this the best expression of the natural definition of justice."
D: Elegant: There are as many kinds of law as forms of government, and the critical question is the standard of justice. Some hold that law should serve only the interests and preservation of whatever regime is in power -- not virtue of any kind -- and call this the natural definition of justice.
D: Summary: The Athenian raises the fundamental question of what standard should define justice in law. He identifies a common view that law should serve only the preservation of the ruling power, regardless of virtue -- effectively defining justice as the interest of the established regime.
D: Distilled: The central question of legislation is the standard of justice, and the common but false view is that justice means whatever preserves the ruling power.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage introduces the Thrasymachean thesis -- that justice is the interest of the stronger -- which Plato had already engaged at length in Republic I (338c-339a). Thrasymachus argued that justice is nothing more than what benefits the ruling class, and the Athenian here presents the same position as a common political assumption. The phrase "natural definition of justice" is ironic: what is presented as natural is actually the corruption of true justice. The Laws' answer will be that justice is obedience to law that embodies reason (nous), not service to any particular faction or regime.

D: Original:
How?

Justice is said by them to be the interest of the stronger.

Speak plainer.

I will:-"Surely," they say, "the governing power makes whatever laws have authority in any state?"

True.

"Well," they would add, "and do you suppose that tyranny or democracy, or any other conquering power, does not make the continuance of the power which is possessed by them the first or principal object of their laws?"

How can they have any other?

"And whoever transgresses these laws is punished as an evil-doer by the legislator, who calls the laws just?"

Naturally.

"This, then, is always the mode and fashion in which justice exists."

Certainly, if they are correct in their view.

Why, yes, this is one of those false principles of government to which we were referring.

Which do you mean?

Those which we were examining when we spoke of who ought to govern whom. Did we not arrive at the conclusion that parents ought to govern their children, and the elder the younger, and the noble the ignoble? And there were many other principles, if you remember, and they were not always consistent. One principle was this very principle of might, and we said that Pindar considered violence natural and justified it.

Yes; I remember.

Consider, then, to whom our state is to be entrusted. For there is a thing which has occurred times without number in states-

What thing?
D: Narrative:
"How?"

"Justice is said by them to be the interest of the stronger."

"Speak plainer."

"I will: -- 'Surely,' they say, 'the governing power makes whatever laws have authority in any state?'"

"True."

"'Well,' they would add, 'and do you suppose that tyranny or democracy, or any other conquering power, does not make the continuance of the power which is possessed by them the first or principal object of their laws?'"

"How can they have any other?"

"'And whoever transgresses these laws is punished as an evil-doer by the legislator, who calls the laws just?'"

"Naturally."

"'This, then, is always the mode and fashion in which justice exists.'"

"Certainly, if they are correct in their view."

"Why, yes, this is one of those false principles of government to which we were referring."

"Which do you mean?"

"Those which we were examining when we spoke of who ought to govern whom. Did we not arrive at the conclusion that parents ought to govern their children, and the elder the younger, and the noble the ignoble? And there were many other principles, if you remember, and they were not always consistent. One principle was this very principle of might, and we said that Pindar considered violence natural and justified it."

"Yes; I remember."

"Consider, then, to whom our state is to be entrusted. For there is a thing which has occurred times without number in states --"

"What thing?"
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"Justice, they say, is the interest of the stronger."

"Explain more clearly."

"I will. 'Surely,' they argue, 'the governing power in any state makes whatever laws have authority there.'"

"True."

"'And do you suppose,' they continue, 'that any conquering power -- whether tyranny or democracy -- does not make the continuation of its own power the first and principal object of its laws?'"

"What else could they aim at?"

"'And whoever breaks these laws is punished as a criminal by the legislator, who calls the laws just?'"

"Naturally."

"'This, then, is always the way justice works.'"

"Certainly, if their view is correct."

"But this is precisely one of those false principles of government we discussed earlier."

"Which do you mean?"

"The ones we examined when we asked who ought to govern whom. We concluded that parents should govern children, elders should govern the young, and the noble should govern the ignoble. There were many such principles, and they were not always consistent. One of them was this very principle of might, and we noted that Pindar considered violence natural and justified it."

"Yes, I remember."

"Consider, then, to whom our state should be entrusted. For there is something that has happened countless times in states --"

"What is it?"
D: Elegant: The argument that justice is the interest of the stronger runs as follows: the ruling power makes the laws; every regime makes its own preservation the chief aim of its laws; whoever breaks those laws is punished as a criminal; therefore justice simply means obedience to the ruling power. The Athenian identifies this as a false principle, recalling their earlier discussion of competing claims to rule -- including Pindar's view that violence is natural and justified -- and turns to the question of who should govern the new colony.
D: Summary: The Athenian presents and then rejects the argument that justice is the interest of the stronger: every regime legislates to preserve its own power and punishes those who resist. He links this to earlier discussions of competing claims to authority, including Pindar's justification of natural violence, and pivots to who should govern the new state.
D: Distilled: The thesis that justice is the interest of the stronger -- that every regime simply legislates to maintain its own power -- is identified as a false principle that must be replaced by a truer standard of political authority.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- The argument presented here closely mirrors Thrasymachus's position in Republic I (338c-341a). Pindar's fragment about violence (nomos) being "king of all" and making "the most violent appear just" was discussed in Laws III (690b-c). The reference to competing principles of rule (parents over children, elder over younger, noble over ignoble) recalls Laws III (689e-690d), where seven distinct claims to political authority were enumerated. The Athenian's identification of this position as "false" sets up the alternative: that legitimate authority must be grounded in law and virtue rather than mere power.

D: Original:
That when there has been a contest for power, those who gain the upper hand so entirely monopolize the government, as to refuse all share to the defeated party and their descendants-they live watching one another, the ruling class being in perpetual fear that some one who has a recollection of former wrongs will come into power and rise up against them. Now, according to our view, such governments are not polities at all, nor are laws right which are passed for the good of particular classes and not for the good of the whole state. States which have such laws are not polities but parties, and their notions of justice are simply unmeaning. I say this, because I am going to assert that we must not entrust the government in your state to any one because he is rich, or because he possesses any other advantage, such as strength, or stature, or again birth: but he who is most obedient to the laws of the state, he shall win the palm; and to him who is victorious in the first degree shall be given the highest office and chief ministry of the gods; and the second to him who bears the second palm; and on a similar principle shall all the other be assigned to those who come next in order. And when I call the rulers servants or ministers of the law, I give them this name not for the sake of novelty, but because I certainly believe that upon such service or ministry depends the well- or ill-being of the state. For that state in which the law is subject and has no authority, I perceive to be on the highway to ruin; but I see that the state in which the law is above the rulers, and the rulers are the inferiors of the law, has salvation, and every blessing which the Gods can confer.

Truly, Stranger, you see with the keen vision of age.

Why, yes; every man when he is young has that sort of vision dullest, and when he is old keenest.

Very true.

And now, what is to be the next step? May we not suppose the colonists to have arrived, and proceed to make our speech to them?

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"That when there has been a contest for power, those who gain the upper hand so entirely monopolize the government, as to refuse all share to the defeated party and their descendants -- they live watching one another, the ruling class being in perpetual fear that some one who has a recollection of former wrongs will come into power and rise up against them. Now, according to our view, such governments are not polities at all, nor are laws right which are passed for the good of particular classes and not for the good of the whole state. States which have such laws are not polities but parties, and their notions of justice are simply unmeaning. I say this, because I am going to assert that we must not entrust the government in your state to any one because he is rich, or because he possesses any other advantage, such as strength, or stature, or again birth: but he who is most obedient to the laws of the state, he shall win the palm; and to him who is victorious in the first degree shall be given the highest office and chief ministry of the gods; and the second to him who bears the second palm; and on a similar principle shall all the others be assigned to those who come next in order. And when I call the rulers servants or ministers of the law, I give them this name not for the sake of novelty, but because I certainly believe that upon such service or ministry depends the well- or ill-being of the state. For that state in which the law is subject and has no authority, I perceive to be on the highway to ruin; but I see that the state in which the law is above the rulers, and the rulers are the inferiors of the law, has salvation, and every blessing which the Gods can confer."

"Truly, Stranger, you see with the keen vision of age."

"Why, yes; every man when he is young has that sort of vision dullest, and when he is old keenest."

"Very true."

"And now, what is to be the next step? May we not suppose the colonists to have arrived, and proceed to make our speech to them?"

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"When there has been a struggle for power, the victors monopolize the government so completely that they refuse any share to the defeated party or their descendants. Both sides live in constant suspicion -- the ruling class perpetually afraid that someone with a memory of past wrongs will rise up against them.

In our view, such governments are not true constitutions at all, and laws passed for the benefit of one class rather than the whole state are not legitimate laws. States with such laws are not polities but factions, and their conceptions of justice are meaningless.

I say this because I intend to assert that in your state, no one should be entrusted with government because he is rich, or strong, or tall, or well-born. Instead, the person most obedient to the state's laws shall win the highest place. The most law-abiding citizen shall receive the highest office and the chief service of the gods. The second most obedient shall receive the second-highest office, and so on in order.

When I call rulers 'servants' or 'ministers' of the law, I do not use this term for novelty's sake. I genuinely believe that the well-being or ruin of the state depends on this distinction. Where the law is subordinate and has no real authority, I see a state on the road to destruction. But where the law stands above the rulers, and the rulers are subordinate to the law -- there I see salvation and every blessing the gods can bestow."

"Truly, Stranger, you see with the keen vision of age."

"Indeed -- when a man is young, his vision for such things is at its dullest; when he is old, at its sharpest."

"Very true."

"Now, what is the next step? Shall we suppose that the colonists have arrived, and proceed to address them?"

"Certainly."
D: Elegant: When victors in political struggle monopolize power and exclude the defeated, the result is faction, not a true constitution, and their concept of justice is meaningless. Government should be entrusted not to the rich, strong, or wellborn, but to whoever is most obedient to the laws. Rulers must be servants and ministers of law -- not a mere phrase but a foundational principle. Where law is subordinate to rulers, the state is on the road to ruin; where law stands above the rulers, there lies salvation and every divine blessing.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that factional governments that exclude the defeated are not true constitutions, and their justice is meaningless. In the new colony, authority must go to whoever is most obedient to law, with rulers serving as ministers of law. The key principle: where law rules over rulers, the state is saved; where rulers rule over law, the state is doomed.
D: Distilled: Where the law is sovereign over rulers, there is salvation; where rulers are sovereign over law, there is ruin -- so authority must go to whoever is most obedient to the laws, not to the rich or wellborn.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (with Cleinias responding). -- The declaration that rulers are "servants of the law" (hyperetai tou nomou) is one of the most influential formulations in Western political philosophy, anticipating the modern concept of the rule of law.
