All these states differ in number, magnitude, intensity, and proportion, and their desirability depends on these variables. Given this natural ordering, we desire a life containing many great and intense experiences of both pleasure and pain -- provided the pleasures predominate. We reject any life where pains predominate. Nor do we desire a life where both pleasures and pains are few, small, and weak, yet the pains still outweigh the pleasures.

When pleasure and pain are in equal balance, we may call this the balanced life. We prefer lives that exceed in what we enjoy and reject those that exceed in what we find painful. All human lives fall within these categories, and we must consider which ones we naturally desire. If we seem to desire any life outside these categories, it is only through ignorance and inexperience of the lives that actually exist.
D: Elegant: What is a genuine experience of different lives? We must use argument to determine what accords with nature. We desire pleasure, not pain; we accept neutrality as a substitute for pain but not for pleasure. The desirability of any life depends on the number, magnitude, intensity, and balance of its pleasures and pains. We want the life where pleasures predominate, reject the one where pains predominate, and are uncertain about perfect balance. All human lives fall within this framework. If we seem to want something outside it, that desire stems from ignorance of the lives that actually exist.
D: Summary: The Athenian develops a systematic framework for evaluating lives based on the balance of pleasure and pain. We naturally desire the life where pleasures predominate in number, intensity, and magnitude. Any desire for a life outside this framework stems from ignorance.
D: Distilled: All human lives are evaluated by the balance of their pleasures and pains -- we naturally choose the life where pleasures predominate, and any contrary desire reflects ignorance.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage develops a proto-utilitarian calculus of pleasure and pain that anticipates Epicurus and Bentham. It extends the hedonistic argument begun in the Protagoras (356a-357e), where Socrates proposes an 'art of measurement' for pleasures and pains. The emphasis on nature as the standard connects to Philebus 31a-55c, Plato's most sustained analysis of pleasure. The claim that mistaken life-choices arise from 'ignorance and inexperience' reinforces the Socratic intellectualism of 5.9-5.10 (wrongdoing from ignorance). The analytical categories (number, magnitude, intensity, equality) prefigure Aristotle's treatment of pleasure in NE VII.11-14 and X.1-5.

D: Original: Now, what lives are they, and how many in which, having searched out and beheld the objects of will and desire and their opposites, and making of them a law, choosing, I say, the dear and the pleasant and the best and noblest, a man may live in the happiest way possible? Let us say that the temperate life is one kind of life, and the rational another, and the courageous another, and the healthful another; and to these four let us oppose four other lives-the foolish, the cowardly, the intemperate, the diseased. He who knows the temperate life will describe it as in all things gentle, having gentle pains and gentle pleasures, and placid desires and loves not insane; whereas the intemperate life is impetuous in all things, and has violent pains and pleasures, and vehement and stinging desires, and loves utterly insane; and in the temperate life the pleasures exceed the pains, but in the intemperate life the pains exceed the pleasures in greatness and number and frequency. Hence one of the two lives is naturally and necessarily more pleasant and the other more painful, and he who would live pleasantly cannot possibly choose to live intemperately.
D: Narrative:
"Now, what lives are they, and how many in which, having searched out and beheld the objects of will and desire and their opposites, and making of them a law, choosing, I say, the dear and the pleasant and the best and noblest, a man may live in the happiest way possible? Let us say that the temperate life is one kind of life, and the rational another, and the courageous another, and the healthful another; and to these four let us oppose four other lives -- the foolish, the cowardly, the intemperate, the diseased.

"He who knows the temperate life will describe it as in all things gentle, having gentle pains and gentle pleasures, and placid desires and loves not insane; whereas the intemperate life is impetuous in all things, and has violent pains and pleasures, and vehement and stinging desires, and loves utterly insane; and in the temperate life the pleasures exceed the pains, but in the intemperate life the pains exceed the pleasures in greatness and number and frequency. Hence one of the two lives is naturally and necessarily more pleasant and the other more painful, and he who would live pleasantly cannot possibly choose to live intemperately."
D: Refashioned:
What kinds of lives exist in which a person, having examined the objects of will and desire alongside their opposites and established a principle of choice -- selecting what is dear, pleasant, best, and noblest -- can live as happily as possible?

Let us identify four types of virtuous life: the temperate, the rational, the courageous, and the healthy. Against these we set four opposing lives: the foolish, the cowardly, the intemperate, and the diseased.

Anyone who understands the temperate life will describe it as gentle in all respects -- having mild pains and mild pleasures, calm desires, and loves that are sane. The intemperate life, by contrast, is violent in every way: its pains and pleasures are extreme, its desires are fierce and stinging, its loves are utterly deranged. In the temperate life, pleasures exceed pains; in the intemperate life, pains surpass pleasures in magnitude, frequency, and number.

Consequently, one of these two lives is naturally and necessarily more pleasant, the other more painful. Whoever truly wishes to live pleasantly cannot possibly choose the intemperate life.
D: Elegant: Four virtuous lives exist -- temperate, rational, courageous, and healthy -- opposed by four vicious ones: foolish, cowardly, intemperate, and diseased. The temperate life is gentle throughout: mild pleasures, mild pains, calm desires, sane loves. The intemperate life is violent: extreme pleasures and pains, fierce desires, deranged loves. Crucially, in the temperate life pleasures exceed pains, while in the intemperate life the reverse is true. Therefore, whoever genuinely wants to live pleasantly cannot choose intemperance.
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies four virtuous lives (temperate, rational, courageous, healthy) and their vicious opposites. The temperate life, with its gentle pleasures and calm desires, naturally produces more pleasure than pain, while the intemperate life -- despite its violent pleasures -- produces more pain overall.
D: Distilled: The temperate life, with its gentle pleasures and calm desires, is naturally more pleasant than the intemperate life, whose violent pleasures are outweighed by even greater pains.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The four virtuous lives correspond roughly to the four cardinal virtues (temperance, wisdom, courage, and bodily health as a stand-in for justice's social function). This schema simplifies the Republic's more complex treatment. The characterization of intemperate desires as 'insane loves' connects to Republic IX.571b-576b on the tyrannical soul driven by lawless eros. The argument that temperance yields more net pleasure than intemperance is central to the Laws' strategy of making virtue attractive on hedonistic grounds -- cf. Philebus 63d-66d, where the 'mixed life' of pleasure and intelligence is ranked. The pairing of opposites (temperate/intemperate, wise/foolish, etc.) anticipates the method of division in the Sophist and Statesman.

D: Original: And if this is true, the inference clearly is that no man is voluntarily intemperate; but that the whole multitude of men lack temperance in their lives, either from ignorance, or from want of self-control, or both. And the same holds of the diseased and healthy life; they both have pleasures and pains, but in health the pleasure exceeds the pain, and in sickness the pain exceeds the pleasure. Now our intention in choosing the lives is not that the painful should exceed, but the life in which pain is exceeded by pleasure we have determined to be the more pleasant life. And we should say that the temperate life has the elements both of pleasure and pain fewer and smaller and less frequent than the intemperate, and the wise life than the foolish life, and the life of courage than the life of cowardice; one of each pair exceeding in pleasure and the other in pain, the courageous surpassing the cowardly, and the wise exceeding the foolish. And so the one dass of lives exceeds the other class in pleasure; the temperate and courageous and wise and healthy exceed the cowardly and foolish and intemperate and diseased lives; and generally speaking, that which has any virtue, whether of body or soul, is pleasanter than the vicious life, and far superior in beauty and rectitude and excellence and reputation, and causes him who lives accordingly to be infinitely happier than the opposite.
D: Narrative:
"And if this is true, the inference clearly is that no man is voluntarily intemperate; but that the whole multitude of men lack temperance in their lives, either from ignorance, or from want of self-control, or both. And the same holds of the diseased and healthy life; they both have pleasures and pains, but in health the pleasure exceeds the pain, and in sickness the pain exceeds the pleasure.

"Now our intention in choosing the lives is not that the painful should exceed, but the life in which pain is exceeded by pleasure we have determined to be the more pleasant life. And we should say that the temperate life has the elements both of pleasure and pain fewer and smaller and less frequent than the intemperate, and the wise life than the foolish life, and the life of courage than the life of cowardice; one of each pair exceeding in pleasure and the other in pain, the courageous surpassing the cowardly, and the wise exceeding the foolish.

"And so the one class of lives exceeds the other class in pleasure; the temperate and courageous and wise and healthy exceed the cowardly and foolish and intemperate and diseased lives; and generally speaking, that which has any virtue, whether of body or soul, is pleasanter than the vicious life, and far superior in beauty and rectitude and excellence and reputation, and causes him who lives accordingly to be infinitely happier than the opposite."
D: Refashioned:
If this argument holds, the clear conclusion is that no one is voluntarily intemperate. The masses who lack temperance in their lives do so either from ignorance, lack of self-control, or both. The same reasoning applies to the diseased and healthy life: both involve pleasures and pains, but in health pleasure predominates, while in sickness pain does.

Our purpose in evaluating these lives has not been to seek out the life with the greatest pain, but to identify the one where pleasure most exceeds pain. We can now observe that the temperate life has fewer, smaller, and less frequent experiences of both pleasure and pain than the intemperate life; the wise life fewer than the foolish; the courageous life fewer than the cowardly. In each pair, the virtuous life exceeds in pleasure and the vicious one in pain.

Thus the virtuous class of lives -- temperate, courageous, wise, and healthy -- surpasses the vicious class in pleasure. Speaking broadly, any life possessing virtue, whether of body or soul, is more pleasant than its vicious counterpart, and far superior in beauty, moral correctness, excellence, and reputation. The person who lives virtuously is infinitely happier than the one who lives the opposite way.
D: Elegant: No one is voluntarily intemperate -- those who lack temperance do so from ignorance, weak self-control, or both. The temperate life has fewer and milder pleasures and pains than the intemperate, yet its pleasures predominate; the same holds for the wise over the foolish, the courageous over the cowardly, the healthy over the diseased. In every pair, the virtuous life exceeds in net pleasure. Any life possessing virtue is more pleasant, more beautiful, more correct, more excellent, and more reputable than its opposite -- making the virtuous person infinitely happier.
D: Summary: No one chooses intemperance voluntarily -- it results from ignorance or weak self-control. In every comparison between virtuous and vicious lives, the virtuous life contains more net pleasure. Virtue surpasses vice not only in pleasure but also in beauty, moral correctness, excellence, and reputation.
D: Distilled: Virtue wins on every measure -- the temperate, wise, courageous, and healthy lives all produce more net pleasure and greater happiness than their vicious counterparts.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage completes the hedonistic proof of virtue's superiority. The claim that no one is voluntarily intemperate extends the Socratic paradox from 5.9 and 5.14 -- intemperance is reframed as a cognitive and volitional failure rather than a free choice. The explanation via 'ignorance or want of self-control (akrasia)' is notable because Socrates in the Protagoras (352b-358d) famously denies that akrasia exists, reducing all vice to ignorance. Here the Laws acknowledges akrasia as a separate cause alongside ignorance -- a significant concession to common experience that Aristotle would later develop (NE VII.1-10). The conclusion that virtue wins on every dimension (pleasure, beauty, rectitude, reputation) mirrors Republic IX.580b-583a, where the philosophical life is shown to be most pleasant.

D: Original: Enough of the preamble; and now the laws should follow; or, to speak more correctly, outline of them. As, then, in the case of a web or any other tissue, the warp and the woof cannot be made of the same materials, but the warp is necessarily superior as being stronger, and having a certain character of firmness, whereas the woof is softer and has a proper degree of elasticity;-in a similar manner those who are to hold great offices in states, should be distinguished truly in each case from those who have been but slenderly proven by education. Let us suppose that there are two parts in the constitution of a state-one the creation of offices, the other the laws which are assigned to them to administer.
D: Narrative:
"Enough of the preamble; and now the laws should follow; or, to speak more correctly, an outline of them. As, then, in the case of a web or any other tissue, the warp and the woof cannot be made of the same materials, but the warp is necessarily superior as being stronger, and having a certain character of firmness, whereas the woof is softer and has a proper degree of elasticity -- in a similar manner those who are to hold great offices in states, should be distinguished truly in each case from those who have been but slenderly proven by education.

"Let us suppose that there are two parts in the constitution of a state -- one the creation of offices, the other the laws which are assigned to them to administer."
D: Refashioned:
The preamble has gone on long enough; now the actual laws should follow -- or, to be more precise, an outline of them.

Consider the analogy of weaving. In any web or fabric, the warp and the woof cannot be made of the same material. The warp must be the stronger thread, possessing firmness and resilience, while the woof is softer and has the proper degree of flexibility. In the same way, those who are to hold high offices in the state must be clearly distinguished from those whose education and testing have been only superficial.

Let us therefore recognize two fundamental components in the constitution of a state: the establishment of public offices, and the laws that those officeholders are charged with administering.
D: Elegant: The preamble is complete; now for the laws themselves -- or rather their outline. Like weaving, where the firm warp threads must differ from the flexible woof, those who hold high office must be distinguished from those only lightly tested by education. A state's constitution has two essential parts: the creation of offices and the laws those offices administer.
D: Summary: The Athenian concludes the ethical preamble and transitions to actual legislation. Using a weaving analogy, he argues that officeholders must be rigorously distinguished from ordinary citizens, and identifies two components of a constitution: the creation of offices and the laws they administer.
D: Distilled: A constitution requires two things -- the creation of offices filled by rigorously tested leaders, and the laws those offices administer -- just as weaving requires both firm warp and flexible woof.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The weaving metaphor is central to the Statesman (279a-283b, 305e-311c), where the art of the statesman is literally compared to the art of weaving -- intertwining the bold/firm (warp) with the gentle/flexible (woof) natures in the citizenry. The transition from preamble to legislation proper marks a structural turning point in Book V. The distinction between offices and laws as the two parts of a constitution anticipates Aristotle's Politics III.6-8 (classification of constitutions by who rules and by what laws). The emphasis on educational testing for officeholders connects to Republic VII.535a-540c on the selection and education of philosopher-rulers.

D: Original: But, before all this, comes the following consideration:-The shepherd or herdsman, or breeder of horses or the like, when he has received his animals will not begin to train them until he has first purified them in a manner which befits a community of animals; he will divide the healthy and unhealthy, and the good breed and the bad breed, and will send away the unhealthy and badly bred to other herds, and tend the rest, reflecting that his labours will be vain and have no effect, either on the souls or bodies of those whom nature and ill nurture have corrupted, and that they will involve in destruction the pure and healthy nature and being of every other animal, if he should neglect to purify them. Now the case of other animals is not so important-they are only worth introducing for the sake of illustration; but what relates to man is of the highest importance; and the legislator should make enquiries, and indicate what is proper for each one in the way of purification and of any other procedure. Take, for example, the purification of a city-there are many kinds of purification, some easier and others more difficult; and some of them, and the best and most difficult of them, the legislator, if he be also a despot, may be able to effect; but the legislator, who, not being a despot, sets up a new government and laws, even if he attempt the mildest of purgations, may think himself happy if he can complete his work. The best kind of purification is painful, like similar cures in medicine, involving righteous punishment and inflicting death or exile in the last resort.
D: Narrative:
"But, before all this, comes the following consideration: The shepherd or herdsman, or breeder of horses or the like, when he has received his animals will not begin to train them until he has first purified them in a manner which befits a community of animals; he will divide the healthy and unhealthy, and the good breed and the bad breed, and will send away the unhealthy and badly bred to other herds, and tend the rest, reflecting that his labours will be vain and have no effect, either on the souls or bodies of those whom nature and ill nurture have corrupted, and that they will involve in destruction the pure and healthy nature and being of every other animal, if he should neglect to purify them.

"Now the case of other animals is not so important -- they are only worth introducing for the sake of illustration; but what relates to man is of the highest importance; and the legislator should make enquiries, and indicate what is proper for each one in the way of purification and of any other procedure. Take, for example, the purification of a city -- there are many kinds of purification, some easier and others more difficult; and some of them, and the best and most difficult of them, the legislator, if he be also a despot, may be able to effect; but the legislator, who, not being a despot, sets up a new government and laws, even if he attempt the mildest of purgations, may think himself happy if he can complete his work. The best kind of purification is painful, like similar cures in medicine, involving righteous punishment and inflicting death or exile in the last resort."
D: Refashioned:
But before all of this, there is a prior consideration. A shepherd, herdsman, or horse breeder, upon receiving their animals, does not begin training them without first sorting them in a way appropriate to managing a herd. They separate the healthy from the unhealthy, the well-bred from the poorly bred, sending the inferior ones away to other herds while tending the rest. The breeder recognizes that efforts spent on animals corrupted by nature or poor upbringing will be wasted -- producing no improvement in either body or soul -- and that if left unaddressed, these animals will drag down and corrupt the healthy ones as well.

The case of animals is introduced only as an illustration; what applies to human beings matters far more. The lawgiver must investigate what kind of purification and procedure is appropriate for each situation. Consider the purification of a city: there are many methods, some easy and some difficult. The best and most difficult purifications can only be carried out by a legislator who also holds absolute power. A legislator who lacks despotic authority and is establishing a new government and legal system should count themselves fortunate if they can accomplish even the mildest form of purification. The best purification is painful, like the harsh remedies of medicine -- involving righteous punishment and, in extreme cases, death or exile.
D: Elegant: A herdsman sorts animals before training them -- separating healthy from unhealthy, good breed from bad -- knowing that effort spent on the corrupted is wasted and that they will drag down the rest. The same applies to cities, only more urgently. The legislator must purify the population before building. Many methods exist, but the best and hardest require absolute power. A non-despotic legislator attempting even mild purification should count themselves fortunate to succeed. The strongest purification, like harsh medicine, involves righteous punishment -- and in the last resort, death or exile.
D: Summary: Just as a herdsman purifies a flock before training it, a legislator must purify the citizenry before establishing laws. The most effective purification -- involving punishment, exile, or death -- requires absolute power; a non-despotic legislator can only attempt milder forms.
D: Distilled: Before building a state, the legislator must purify the population as a herdsman culls a herd -- though the most effective purification requires absolute power most legislators lack.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The shepherd/herdsman analogy for the statesman appears in Republic I.343a-345e (Thrasymachus' challenge), Statesman 261d-268d (the extended shepherd analogy), and Gorgias 516a-b. The concept of civic 'purification' (katharsis) is troubling to modern readers but was a standard Greek political concept -- historical examples include Solon's reforms and colonial foundations. The distinction between the despotic legislator (who can effect full purification) and the non-despotic one (who must settle for milder measures) reflects the Laws' awareness of its own practical limitations compared to the Republic's ideal state, where philosopher-kings have absolute authority. The medical analogy for political reform is common in Plato -- cf. Gorgias 521e-522a, Statesman 293a-c.

D: Original: For in this way we commonly dispose of great sinners who are incurable, and are the greatest injury of the whole state. But the milder form of purification is as follows:-when men who have nothing, and are in want of food, show a disposition to follow their leaders in an attack on the property of the rich-these, who are the natural plague of the state, are sent away by the legislator in a friendly spirit as far as he is able; and this dismissal of them is euphemistically termed a colony. And every legislator should contrive to do this at once. Our present case, however, is peculiar. For there is no need to devise any colony or purifying separation under the circumstances in which we are placed. But as, when many streams flow together from many sources, whether springs or mountain torrents, into a single lake, we ought to attend and take care that the confluent waters should be perfectly clear, and in order to effect this, should pump and draw off and divert impurities, so in every political arrangement there may be trouble and danger. But, seeing that we are now only discoursing and not acting, let our selection be supposed to be completed, and the desired purity attained. Touching evil men, who want to join and be citizens of our state, after we have tested them by every sort of persuasion and for a sufficient time, we will prevent them from coming; but the good we will to the utmost of our ability receive as friends with open arms.
D: Narrative:
"For in this way we commonly dispose of great sinners who are incurable, and are the greatest injury of the whole state. But the milder form of purification is as follows: when men who have nothing, and are in want of food, show a disposition to follow their leaders in an attack on the property of the rich -- these, who are the natural plague of the state, are sent away by the legislator in a friendly spirit as far as he is able; and this dismissal of them is euphemistically termed a colony.

"And every legislator should contrive to do this at once. Our present case, however, is peculiar. For there is no need to devise any colony or purifying separation under the circumstances in which we are placed. But as, when many streams flow together from many sources, whether springs or mountain torrents, into a single lake, we ought to attend and take care that the confluent waters should be perfectly clear, and in order to effect this, should pump and draw off and divert impurities, so in every political arrangement there may be trouble and danger.

"But, seeing that we are now only discoursing and not acting, let our selection be supposed to be completed, and the desired purity attained. Touching evil men, who want to join and be citizens of our state, after we have tested them by every sort of persuasion and for a sufficient time, we will prevent them from coming; but the good we will to the utmost of our ability receive as friends with open arms."
D: Refashioned:
Through severe purification we typically deal with great criminals who are beyond reform and who pose the gravest threat to the state as a whole. But there is also a milder form. When people who own nothing and lack food begin following agitators who would lead them to seize the property of the wealthy -- these people being the natural plague of any state -- the legislator sends them away as amicably as possible. This removal is politely called 'founding a colony.'

Every legislator should arrange for this immediately. Our situation, however, is unique. We have no need to organize a colony or purifying separation given our present circumstances. But consider the analogy of many streams flowing from various sources -- springs and mountain torrents -- into a single lake. We must ensure that the confluent waters are perfectly clean, pumping out, drawing off, and diverting any impurities. The same principle applies to every political arrangement, though trouble and danger may attend the process.

Since we are only theorizing and not yet acting, let us suppose the selection is complete and the desired purity has been achieved. As for wicked people who wish to join and become citizens of our state, we will test them through every kind of persuasion over a sufficient period of time and bar them from entry. But the good we will welcome as friends with open arms, to the fullest extent of our ability.
D: Elegant: Severe purification removes incurable criminals. A milder form sends away the landless poor who threaten to seize the rich's property -- politely called 'founding a colony.' Our unique situation requires neither measure. But like streams flowing into a lake that must be kept clean by diverting impurities, every political arrangement must be purified of corrupting elements. Since we are theorizing, let us assume the purification complete. We will test would-be citizens thoroughly and bar the wicked, while warmly welcoming the good.
D: Summary: Severe purification removes incurable criminals through punishment or death. Milder purification sends away the dangerous poor as 'colonists.' The Athenian's theoretical city requires neither method, but the principle remains: impurities must be diverted, the wicked excluded, and the good warmly welcomed.
D: Distilled: The state must purify itself of corrupting elements -- removing incurable criminals, redirecting the dangerous poor as colonists, barring the wicked from citizenship, and welcoming the good.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The euphemistic description of colonization as a form of social 'purification' reflects actual Greek practice -- colonies (apoikiai) were often founded partly to relieve social pressure from landless poor (cf. the founding of Thurii, in which Plato's circle was involved). The threat of the poor attacking the property of the rich echoes Republic VIII.555c-557a on the oligarchic-democratic revolution. The water/streams metaphor for civic purity is original to the Laws. The theoretical idealization -- 'let us suppose the purification complete' -- acknowledges the gap between political theory and practice that the Laws repeatedly confronts. The openness to receiving good immigrants contrasts with many Greek cities' exclusionary policies and recalls the cosmopolitan tendencies in Plato's thought.

D: Original: Another piece of good fortune must not be forgotten, which, as we were saying, the Heraclid colony had, and which is also ours-that we have escaped division of land and the abolition of debts; for these are always a source of dangerous contention, and a city which is driven by necessity to legislate upon such matters can neither allow the old ways to continue, nor yet venture to alter them. We must have recourse to prayers, so to speak, and hope that a slight change may be cautiously effected in a length of time. And such a change can be accomplished by those who have abundance of land, and having also many debtors, are willing, in a kindly spirit, to share with those who are in want, sometimes remitting and sometimes giving, holding fast in a path of moderation, and deeming poverty to be the increase of a man's desires and not the diminution of his property. For this is the great beginning of salvation to a state, and upon this lasting basis may be erected afterwards whatever political order is suitable under the circumstances; but if the change be based upon an unsound principle, the future administration of the country will be full of difficulties. That is a danger which, as I am saying, is escaped by us, and yet we had better say how, if we had not escaped, we might have escaped; and we may venture now to assert that no other way of escape, whether narrow or broad, can be devised but freedom from avarice and a sense of justice-upon this rock our city shall be built; for there ought to be no disputes among citizens about property. If there are quarrels of long standing among them, no legislator of any degree of sense will proceed a step in the arrangement of the state until they are settled. But that they to whom God has given, as he has to us, to be the founders of a new state as yet free from enmity-that they should create themselves enmities by their mode of distributing lands and houses, would be superhuman folly and wickedness.
D: Narrative:
"Another piece of good fortune must not be forgotten, which, as we were saying, the Heraclid colony had, and which is also ours -- that we have escaped division of land and the abolition of debts; for these are always a source of dangerous contention, and a city which is driven by necessity to legislate upon such matters can neither allow the old ways to continue, nor yet venture to alter them. We must have recourse to prayers, so to speak, and hope that a slight change may be cautiously effected in a length of time.

"And such a change can be accomplished by those who have abundance of land, and having also many debtors, are willing, in a kindly spirit, to share with those who are in want, sometimes remitting and sometimes giving, holding fast in a path of moderation, and deeming poverty to be the increase of a man's desires and not the diminution of his property. For this is the great beginning of salvation to a state, and upon this lasting basis may be erected afterwards whatever political order is suitable under the circumstances; but if the change be based upon an unsound principle, the future administration of the country will be full of difficulties.

"That is a danger which, as I am saying, is escaped by us, and yet we had better say how, if we had not escaped, we might have escaped; and we may venture now to assert that no other way of escape, whether narrow or broad, can be devised but freedom from avarice and a sense of justice -- upon this rock our city shall be built; for there ought to be no disputes among citizens about property. If there are quarrels of long standing among them, no legislator of any degree of sense will proceed a step in the arrangement of the state until they are settled. But that they to whom God has given, as he has to us, to be the founders of a new state as yet free from enmity -- that they should create themselves enmities by their mode of distributing lands and houses, would be superhuman folly and wickedness."
D: Refashioned:
There is another stroke of good fortune we must not overlook -- one shared by the original Heraclid colony and by ourselves: we have been spared the crisis of land redistribution and debt cancellation. These issues are perpetual sources of dangerous conflict. A city forced to legislate on such matters can neither preserve the existing arrangements nor safely change them. In such situations, one can only resort to what amounts to prayer, hoping that gradual and cautious reform might be achieved over a long period.

Such change can be accomplished when those who possess abundant land and hold many debts are willing, in a generous spirit, to share with those in need -- sometimes forgiving debts, sometimes making outright gifts -- while maintaining a path of moderation. The key insight is that true poverty consists in the increase of a person's desires, not in the decrease of their property. This understanding is the foundation of a state's salvation, and upon it any suitable political order can later be erected. But if reform rests on an unsound principle, the future governance of the country will be plagued with difficulties.

We have escaped this danger, but let us still consider how we might have escaped it had circumstances been different. The answer is that no other path to safety exists -- whether wide or narrow -- except freedom from greed and a commitment to justice. On this rock our city shall be built, for there should be no disputes among citizens over property. Where longstanding quarrels about property exist, no sensible legislator will take a single step toward organizing the state until those quarrels are resolved. For founders whom God has blessed with a new state free from existing enmities to create enmities through their own method of distributing land and houses would be folly and wickedness beyond what is humanly imaginable.
D: Elegant: We share the Heraclid colony's good fortune: we need not face the explosive issues of land redistribution and debt cancellation. Cities forced to legislate on such matters are trapped -- unable to maintain the status quo or safely change it. Gradual reform is possible only when the wealthy willingly share with those in need, understanding that poverty is really the growth of desire, not the shrinking of property. This insight is the foundation of political salvation. Our city must be built on freedom from greed and a sense of justice -- the only rock that holds. No wise legislator proceeds while property disputes remain unsettled, and founders blessed with a fresh start who create enmity through their own land distribution would be guilty of superhuman folly.
D: Summary: The founders are fortunate to avoid the explosive issues of land redistribution and debt cancellation. True poverty is the increase of desire, not the decrease of property. The city must be built on freedom from greed and justice, since unresolved property disputes make political progress impossible.
D: Distilled: The city must be built on freedom from avarice and a sense of justice, for true poverty lies in the growth of desire, not the shrinking of possessions.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The Heraclid colony refers to the Dorian settlement of the Peloponnese, discussed in Laws III (682d-693e). Land redistribution (gēs anadasmos) and debt cancellation (chreōn apokopē) were the two most revolutionary demands in Greek politics -- cf. Solon's seisachtheia in Athens (594 BC). The definition of poverty as 'increase of desires rather than diminution of property' is a profound reorientation that anticipates Epicurean and Stoic economics. The phrase 'upon this rock our city shall be built' uses architectural metaphor for justice as foundation, paralleling Republic IV.433a-434c (justice as the foundation of the ideal state). The warning against creating enmities through distribution connects to Republic V.462a-e on unity as the greatest good for a state.

D: Original: How then can we rightly order the distribution of the land? In the first place, the number of the citizens has to be determined, and also the number and size of the divisions into which they will have to be formed; and the land and the houses will then have to be apportioned by us as fairly as we can. The number of citizens can only be estimated satisfactorily in relation to the territory and the neighbouring states. The territory must be sufficient to maintain a certain number of inhabitants in a moderate way of life-more than this is not required; and the number of citizens should be sufficient to defend themselves against the injustice of their neighbours, and also to give them the power of rendering efficient aid to their neighbours when they are wronged. After having taken a survey of theirs and their neighbours' territory, we will determine the limits of them in fact as well as in theory. And now, let us proceed to legislate with a view to perfecting the form and outline of our state. The number of our citizens shall be 5040-this will be a convenient number; and these shall be owners of the land and protectors of the allotment. The houses and the land will be divided in the same way, so that every man may correspond to a lot. Let the whole number be first divided into two parts, and then into three; and the number is further capable of being divided into four or five parts, or any number of parts up to ten. Every legislator ought to know so much arithmetic as to be able to tell what number is most likely to be useful to all cities; and we are going to take that number which contains the greatest and most regular and unbroken series of divisions.
D: Narrative:
"How then can we rightly order the distribution of the land? In the first place, the number of the citizens has to be determined, and also the number and size of the divisions into which they will have to be formed; and the land and the houses will then have to be apportioned by us as fairly as we can. The number of citizens can only be estimated satisfactorily in relation to the territory and the neighbouring states. The territory must be sufficient to maintain a certain number of inhabitants in a moderate way of life -- more than this is not required; and the number of citizens should be sufficient to defend themselves against the injustice of their neighbours, and also to give them the power of rendering efficient aid to their neighbours when they are wronged.

"After having taken a survey of theirs and their neighbours' territory, we will determine the limits of them in fact as well as in theory. And now, let us proceed to legislate with a view to perfecting the form and outline of our state. The number of our citizens shall be 5040 -- this will be a convenient number; and these shall be owners of the land and protectors of the allotment. The houses and the land will be divided in the same way, so that every man may correspond to a lot.

"Let the whole number be first divided into two parts, and then into three; and the number is further capable of being divided into four or five parts, or any number of parts up to ten. Every legislator ought to know so much arithmetic as to be able to tell what number is most likely to be useful to all cities; and we are going to take that number which contains the greatest and most regular and unbroken series of divisions."
D: Refashioned:
How, then, should we properly organize the distribution of land? First, the number of citizens must be determined, along with the number and size of the divisions they will be organized into. Then the land and houses must be apportioned as equitably as possible.

The appropriate number of citizens can only be calculated in relation to the available territory and the neighboring states. The territory must sustain a certain number of inhabitants living moderately -- nothing more is needed. The number of citizens must be large enough for self-defense against unjust neighbors and also sufficient to provide effective aid to neighbors who are wronged.

After surveying both our territory and our neighbors', we will set these boundaries in practice as well as in theory. Now let us proceed to legislate toward perfecting our state's design. The number of citizens shall be 5,040 -- a highly convenient number. These citizens shall be the owners of the land and guardians of their respective allotments. Houses and land will be divided correspondingly, so that each citizen has a designated lot.

The total can first be divided into two parts, then three, and further into four, five, or any number of parts up to ten. Every legislator should understand enough arithmetic to identify the number most useful for organizing a city. We have chosen the number that allows the greatest, most regular, and most unbroken series of divisions.
D: Elegant: Land distribution requires first fixing the number of citizens relative to the territory and neighboring states. The territory must sustain a moderate way of life; the population must be large enough for defense and mutual aid. The number shall be 5,040 -- chosen because it admits the greatest and most regular series of divisions, being divisible into two, three, four, five, and every number up to ten. Each citizen receives one lot; houses and land are divided correspondingly. Every legislator needs sufficient arithmetic to select the most useful number for organizing a city.
D: Summary: The number of citizens is set at 5,040, chosen for its exceptional mathematical divisibility. The territory must sustain moderate living and provide sufficient population for defense. Each citizen receives a corresponding lot of land and housing.
D: Distilled: The state shall have 5,040 citizen-landholders -- a number chosen for its unmatched mathematical divisibility, enabling flexible organization of all civic, military, and economic arrangements.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The number 5,040 equals 7! (7 factorial = 7x6x5x4x3x2x1) and has 59 divisors, making it exceptionally useful for administrative divisions. This reflects Plato's Pythagorean convictions about the governance-mathematics connection. The moderate self-sufficiency of the territory echoes Aristotle's later principle in Politics VII.4-5 (1325b-1327a). The dual military purpose -- defense and aiding neighbors -- reflects the principle of reciprocal justice among states. The insistence on arithmetic knowledge for legislators connects to Republic VII.522c-526c on mathematics as essential preparation for rulers. The fixed number of lots with corresponding citizen-owners establishes the property system that the remainder of Book V will elaborate.

D: Original: The whole of number has every possible division, and the number 5040 can be divided by exactly fifty-nine divisors, and ten of these proceed without interval from one to ten: this will furnish numbers for war and peace, and for all contracts and dealings, including taxes and divisions of the land. These properties of number should be ascertained at leisure by those who are bound by law to know them; for they are true, and should be proclaimed at the foundation of the city, with a view to use. Whether the legislator is establishing a new state or restoring an old and decayed one, in respect of Gods and temples-the temples which are to be built in each city, and the Gods or demi-gods after whom they are to be called-if he be a man of sense, he will make no change in anything which the oracle of Delphi, or Dodona, or the God Ammon, or any ancient tradition has sanctioned in whatever manner, whether by apparitions or reputed inspiration of Heaven, in obedience to which mankind have established sacrifices in connection with mystic rites, either originating on the spot, or derived from Tyrrhenia or Cyprus or some other place, and on the strength of which traditions they have consecrated oracles and images, and altars and temples, and portioned out a sacred domain for each of them. The least part of all these ought not to be disturbed by the legislator; but he should assign to the several districts some God, or demi-god, or hero, and, in the distribution of the soil, should give to these first their chosen domain and all things fitting, that the inhabitants of the several districts may meet at fixed times, and that they may readily supply their various wants, and entertain one another with sacrifices, and become friends and acquaintances; for there is no greater good in a state than that the citizens should be known to one another. When not light but darkness and ignorance of each other's characters prevails among them, no one will receive the honour of which he is deserving, or the power or the justice to which he is fairly entitled: wherefore, in every state, above all things, every man should take heed that he have no deceit in him, but that he be always true and simple; and that no deceitful person take any advantage of him.
D: Narrative:
"The whole of number has every possible division, and the number 5040 can be divided by exactly fifty-nine divisors, and ten of these proceed without interval from one to ten: this will furnish numbers for war and peace, and for all contracts and dealings, including taxes and divisions of the land. These properties of number should be ascertained at leisure by those who are bound by law to know them; for they are true, and should be proclaimed at the foundation of the city, with a view to use.

"Whether the legislator is establishing a new state or restoring an old and decayed one, in respect of Gods and temples -- the temples which are to be built in each city, and the Gods or demi-gods after whom they are to be called -- if he be a man of sense, he will make no change in anything which the oracle of Delphi, or Dodona, or the God Ammon, or any ancient tradition has sanctioned in whatever manner, whether by apparitions or reputed inspiration of Heaven, in obedience to which mankind have established sacrifices in connection with mystic rites, either originating on the spot, or derived from Tyrrhenia or Cyprus or some other place, and on the strength of which traditions they have consecrated oracles and images, and altars and temples, and portioned out a sacred domain for each of them.

"The least part of all these ought not to be disturbed by the legislator; but he should assign to the several districts some God, or demi-god, or hero, and, in the distribution of the soil, should give to these first their chosen domain and all things fitting, that the inhabitants of the several districts may meet at fixed times, and that they may readily supply their various wants, and entertain one another with sacrifices, and become friends and acquaintances; for there is no greater good in a state than that the citizens should be known to one another. When not light but darkness and ignorance of each other's characters prevails among them, no one will receive the honour of which he is deserving, or the power or the justice to which he is fairly entitled: wherefore, in every state, above all things, every man should take heed that he have no deceit in him, but that he be always true and simple; and that no deceitful person take any advantage of him."
D: Refashioned:
The number 5,040 can be divided by exactly fifty-nine divisors, and ten of these form an unbroken sequence from one to ten. This provides ready-made numbers for military organization, peacetime administration, contracts, commercial dealings, taxation, and land division. These mathematical properties should be studied carefully by those whom the law requires to understand them, since they are factual truths that should be publicly declared at the city's founding for practical application.

Whether establishing a new state or restoring a decayed one, when it comes to gods and temples -- which temples to build in each district and which gods or demigods they should be named after -- a sensible legislator will alter nothing that has been sanctioned by the oracle at Delphi, Dodona, the god Ammon, or any ancient tradition. Whether these traditions arose through divine visions, reported heavenly inspiration, or mystic rites originating locally or derived from Etruria, Cyprus, or elsewhere -- and whether they gave rise to consecrated oracles, sacred images, altars, temples, or sacred domains -- the legislator must not disturb even the smallest part of them.

Instead, each district should be assigned a patron god, demigod, or hero, and in distributing the land, sacred domains should be allotted first with all proper provisions. This ensures that the inhabitants of each district will gather at regular intervals, supply one another's needs, share in sacrificial festivals, and become friends and acquaintances. There is no greater good for a state than that its citizens know one another. When darkness and mutual ignorance of character prevail, no one receives the honor, power, or justice they deserve. Therefore, above all else, every person should ensure they harbor no deceit but remain always truthful and straightforward, and should guard against being taken advantage of by the deceitful.
D: Elegant: The number 5,040 has fifty-nine divisors, including every number from one to ten consecutively -- providing ready-made divisions for war, peace, taxes, contracts, and land. These properties should be publicly declared at the city's founding. Regarding temples and gods, a wise legislator changes nothing sanctioned by Delphi, Dodona, Ammon, or any ancient tradition. Each district should receive a patron deity and sacred domain first, so inhabitants gather regularly for festivals, supply mutual needs, and form friendships. No greater good exists for a state than citizens knowing one another's character. Where mutual ignorance reigns, no one receives deserved honor or justice. Therefore, cultivate truthfulness and guard against deceit.
D: Summary: The number 5,040 provides exceptional divisibility for all civic purposes. Religious traditions should be preserved unchanged. Each district needs a patron deity and regular festivals to foster the mutual acquaintance among citizens that is the greatest good of any state.
D: Distilled: Sacred traditions must be preserved, each district given a patron deity and regular festivals, because the greatest good of any state is that citizens truly know one another's character.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The mathematical discussion reflects the deep Pythagorean influence on late Plato -- cf. Timaeus 35b-36d on mathematical structure in the cosmos. The deference to Delphi, Dodona, and Ammon for religious matters echoes Republic IV.427b-c, where Socrates delegates all religious legislation to Apollo at Delphi. Tyrrhenia (Etruria) and Cyprus were known sources of mystery religions. The principle that citizens must know one another is fundamental to Plato's political theory and explains the small scale of the ideal city -- cf. Republic V.462a-e on unity through mutual knowledge. Aristotle develops this further in Politics VII.4 (1326b), arguing the state must be 'surveyable' (eusynoptos). The emphasis on transparency and against deceit returns to the truth-theme of 5.8.

D: Original: The next move in our pastime of legislation, like the withdrawal of the stone from the holy line in the game of draughts, being an unusual one, will probably excite wonder when mentioned for the first time. And yet, if a man will only reflect and weigh the matter with care, he will see that our city is ordered in a manner which, if not the best, is the second best. Perhaps also some one may not approve this form, because he thinks that such a constitution is ill adapted to a legislator who has not despotic power. The truth is, that there are three forms of government, the best, the second and the third best, which we may just mention, and then leave the selection to the ruler of the settlement. Following this method in the present instance, let us speak of the states which are respectively first, second, and third in excellence, and then we will leave the choice to Cleinias now, or to any one else who may hereafter have to make a similar choice among constitutions, and may desire to give to his state some feature which is congenial to him and which he approves in his own country.
D: Narrative:
"The next move in our pastime of legislation, like the withdrawal of the stone from the holy line in the game of draughts, being an unusual one, will probably excite wonder when mentioned for the first time. And yet, if a man will only reflect and weigh the matter with care, he will see that our city is ordered in a manner which, if not the best, is the second best.

"Perhaps also some one may not approve this form, because he thinks that such a constitution is ill adapted to a legislator who has not despotic power. The truth is, that there are three forms of government, the best, the second and the third best, which we may just mention, and then leave the selection to the ruler of the settlement. Following this method in the present instance, let us speak of the states which are respectively first, second, and third in excellence, and then we will leave the choice to Cleinias now, or to any one else who may hereafter have to make a similar choice among constitutions, and may desire to give to his state some feature which is congenial to him and which he approves in his own country."
D: Refashioned:
The next step in our legislative exercise -- like making an unusual move in a game of draughts by withdrawing a piece from the sacred line -- will likely provoke surprise when first mentioned. Yet anyone who reflects on it carefully will recognize that our city is organized in a manner that is, if not the absolute best, then the second best.

Some may object to this form of constitution, believing it unsuited to a legislator who lacks absolute power. But the truth is that there are three grades of government -- the best, the second best, and the third best. Let us briefly describe each and then leave the selection to the ruler of the settlement. In this spirit, let us outline the states that rank first, second, and third in excellence, and leave the final choice to Cleinias -- or to anyone else who may someday face a similar constitutional decision and wish to incorporate features that suit their own temperament and the traditions of their homeland.
D: Elegant: The next legislative move may seem startling, but careful reflection shows our city's organization to be the second best, if not the best. Three grades of government exist -- best, second best, and third best. Let us describe each and leave the selection to the founder. Cleinias, or any future constitution-maker, can then choose what suits their circumstances and the traditions of their homeland.
D: Summary: The Athenian prepares to introduce an unconventional feature of the constitution, noting that there are three grades of government. He will describe each and leave the choice to Cleinias or any future founder.
D: Distilled: Three grades of government exist -- best, second best, and third best -- and the Athenian will describe each before leaving the constitutional choice to the settlement's founder.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 'game of draughts' (pesseia) metaphor -- specifically moving a piece from the 'sacred line' (hiera gramme) -- was a proverbial expression for a bold or irreversible move. The ranking of constitutions as first, second, and third best is unique to the Laws and represents Plato's late political realism. The 'first best' state (described in 5.23) is the communistic Republic; the 'second best' is the Laws' own project. The deference to Cleinias as the actual settler marks the Laws' distinctive respect for practical political agency, unlike the Republic's more purely theoretical approach. The acknowledgment that a legislator without despotic power faces constraints echoes 5.17 and reflects the dialogue's consistent theme of political realism.

D: Original: The first and highest form of the state and of the government and of the law is that in which there prevails most widely the ancient saying, that "Friends have all things in common." Whether there is anywhere now, or will ever be, this communion of women and children and of property, in which the private and individual is altogether banished from life, and things which are by nature private, such as eyes and ears and hands, have become common, and in some way see and hear and act in common, and all men express praise and blame and feel joy and sorrow on the same occasions, and whatever laws there are unite the city to the utmost-whether all this is possible or not, I say that no man, acting upon any other principle, will ever constitute a state which will be truer or better or more exalted in virtue. Whether such a state is governed by Gods or sons of Gods, one, or more than one, happy are the men who, living after this manner, dwell there; and therefore to this we are to look for the pattern of the state, and to cling to this, and to seek with all our might for one which is like this. The state which we have now in hand, when created, will be nearest to immortality and the only one which takes the second place; and after that, by the grace of God, we will complete the third one. And we will begin by speaking of the nature and origin of the second.
D: Narrative:
"The first and highest form of the state and of the government and of the law is that in which there prevails most widely the ancient saying, that 'Friends have all things in common.' Whether there is anywhere now, or will ever be, this communion of women and children and of property, in which the private and individual is altogether banished from life, and things which are by nature private, such as eyes and ears and hands, have become common, and in some way see and hear and act in common, and all men express praise and blame and feel joy and sorrow on the same occasions, and whatever laws there are unite the city to the utmost -- whether all this is possible or not, I say that no man, acting upon any other principle, will ever constitute a state which will be truer or better or more exalted in virtue.

"Whether such a state is governed by Gods or sons of Gods, one, or more than one, happy are the men who, living after this manner, dwell there; and therefore to this we are to look for the pattern of the state, and to cling to this, and to seek with all our might for one which is like this. The state which we have now in hand, when created, will be nearest to immortality and the only one which takes the second place; and after that, by the grace of God, we will complete the third one. And we will begin by speaking of the nature and origin of the second."
D: Refashioned:
The first and highest form of state, government, and law is the one that most fully realizes the ancient principle: 'Friends have all things in common.' Whether such a community of women, children, and property exists anywhere now or ever will -- one in which the private and individual is entirely abolished from life, where even things naturally private, like eyes and ears and hands, somehow see and hear and act in common, where all people express the same praise and blame and feel the same joy and sorrow on the same occasions, and where laws unite the city to the greatest possible degree -- whether all this is achievable or not, no one operating on any other principle will ever build a state that is truer, better, or more exalted in virtue.

Whether such a state is governed by gods or the children of gods, by one ruler or many, those who live in it are blessed. This is the pattern we must look to, the ideal we must cling to, the model we must strive with all our strength to approximate. The state we are now designing will come nearest to this immortal ideal and will hold second place -- and afterward, with God's help, we will describe the third. Let us begin by discussing the nature and origin of this second-best state.
D: Elegant: The highest form of state fully realizes the principle that friends share all things in common -- property, women, children, even perception and emotion unified so the city acts as one. Whether achievable or not, no other principle can produce a truer, better, or more virtuous state. This ideal is the pattern we must approximate. Our current project -- the second-best state -- will come nearest to this immortal model. We will describe the third form afterward.
D: Summary: The ideal state is one of total communism where friends share everything -- property, family, even perception and feeling. Whether achievable or not, it remains the standard against which all constitutions must be measured. The state being designed in the Laws is the second-best approximation of this ideal.
D: Distilled: The highest political ideal is total community of property, family, and even feeling -- and though it may be unachievable, the state we are designing must approximate it as closely as possible.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is Plato's most explicit cross-reference to the Republic's communism of property, women, and children (Republic V.449a-471c). The saying 'friends have all things in common' (koina ta philōn) was attributed to Pythagoras and appears in Republic IV.424a and Phaedrus 279c. The vision of unified perception -- eyes, ears, hands acting in common -- pushes the Republic's ideal toward an almost mystical organicism that goes beyond anything in the earlier dialogue. The ranking of the Republic's state as 'first best' and the Laws' state as 'second best' is the Laws' definitive statement of its relationship to the Republic. The acknowledgment that the ideal may not be 'possible' echoes Republic V.472c-473b, where Socrates admits the ideal state may never exist. The phrase 'nearest to immortality' for the second-best state suggests that approximating the divine pattern is the most mortals can achieve.

D: Original: Let the citizens at once distribute their land and houses, and not till the land in common, since a community of goods goes beyond their proposed origin, and nurture, and education. But in making the distribution, let the several possessors feel that their particular lots also belong to the whole city; and seeing that the earth is their parent, let them tend her more carefully than children do their mother. For she is a goddess and their queen, and they are her mortal subjects. Such also are the feelings which they ought to entertain to the Gods and demi-gods of the country. And in order that the distribution may always remain, they ought to consider further that the present number of families should be always retained, and neither increased nor diminished. This may be secured for the whole city in the following manner:-Let the possessor of a lot leave the one of his children who is his best beloved, and one only, to be the heir of his dwelling, and his successor in the duty of ministering to the Gods, the state and the family, as well the living members of it as those who are departed when he comes into the inheritance; but of his other children, if he have more than one, he shall give the females in marriage according to the law to be hereafter enacted, and the males he shall distribute as sons to those citizens who have no children and are disposed to receive them; or if there should be none such, and particular individuals have too many children, male or female, or too few, as in the case of barrenness-in all these cases let the highest and most honourable magistracy created by us judge and determine what is to be done with the redundant or deficient, and devise a means that the number of 5040 houses shall always remain the same. There are many ways of regulating numbers; for they in whom generation is affluent may be made to refrain, and, on the other hand, special care may be taken to increase the number of births by rewards and stigmas, or we may meet the evil by the elder men giving advice and administering rebuke to the younger-in this way the object may be attained.
D: Narrative:
"Let the citizens at once distribute their land and houses, and not till the land in common, since a community of goods goes beyond their proposed origin, and nurture, and education. But in making the distribution, let the several possessors feel that their particular lots also belong to the whole city; and seeing that the earth is their parent, let them tend her more carefully than children do their mother. For she is a goddess and their queen, and they are her mortal subjects.

"Such also are the feelings which they ought to entertain to the Gods and demi-gods of the country. And in order that the distribution may always remain, they ought to consider further that the present number of families should be always retained, and neither increased nor diminished. This may be secured for the whole city in the following manner: Let the possessor of a lot leave the one of his children who is his best beloved, and one only, to be the heir of his dwelling, and his successor in the duty of ministering to the Gods, the state and the family, as well the living members of it as those who are departed when he comes into the inheritance; but of his other children, if he have more than one, he shall give the females in marriage according to the law to be hereafter enacted, and the males he shall distribute as sons to those citizens who have no children and are disposed to receive them; or if there should be none such, and particular individuals have too many children, male or female, or too few, as in the case of barrenness -- in all these cases let the highest and most honourable magistracy created by us judge and determine what is to be done with the redundant or deficient, and devise a means that the number of 5040 houses shall always remain the same.

"There are many ways of regulating numbers; for they in whom generation is affluent may be made to refrain, and, on the other hand, special care may be taken to increase the number of births by rewards and stigmas, or we may meet the evil by the elder men giving advice and administering rebuke to the younger -- in this way the object may be attained."
D: Refashioned:
Citizens should immediately distribute the land and houses among themselves rather than farming in common, since complete communal ownership exceeds what their particular origins, upbringing, and education have prepared them for. However, in making this distribution, each possessor should feel that their individual lot also belongs to the city as a whole. The earth is their parent, and they should tend her more carefully than children care for their mother -- for she is a goddess and their sovereign, and they are her mortal subjects. The same reverence should extend to the gods and demigods of the land.

To ensure the distribution remains permanent, the number of households must be kept constant -- neither increased nor decreased. This can be achieved as follows: each landholder should designate one child -- their most beloved -- as sole heir to the dwelling and successor in the duties of serving the gods, the state, and the family, including both the living and the departed. Other daughters should be given in marriage according to laws yet to be enacted. Other sons should be adopted out to childless citizens willing to receive them.

Where adoption is not possible and some families have too many children or too few -- whether from fertility or barrenness -- the highest and most honorable magistracy we establish shall judge each case, devising means to ensure that the number of 5,040 households always remains the same. There are many methods for regulating population: those with abundant fertility can be encouraged to restrain themselves, while births can be stimulated through incentives and social pressure, or elders can counsel and admonish the young. Through such measures, the objective can be achieved.
D: Elegant: Citizens should divide land and houses individually rather than hold them in common -- full communism exceeds their preparation. Yet each possessor must feel their lot belongs to the whole city, tending the earth as their divine mother, goddess, and sovereign. The same reverence extends to the local gods and demigods. To preserve the distribution permanently, the number of households must remain fixed at exactly 5,040. One beloved child inherits each lot as sole heir, succeeding to the duties of serving gods, state, and family -- both living and departed. Surplus daughters are given in marriage; surplus sons are adopted out to childless families. Where imbalances in family size persist, the highest magistracy intervenes to judge each case, regulating population through restraint of the fertile, incentives and social pressure to increase births, or counsel from elders to the young.
D: Summary: Land should be distributed individually rather than held in common, but possessors must view their lots as belonging to the whole city. The number of households must remain fixed at 5,040 through single-heir inheritance, adoption of surplus children, and population regulation by magistrates.
D: Distilled: Each of the 5,040 lots passes to a single heir, with surplus children adopted out and population regulated by magistrates -- ensuring the fixed number of households endures permanently.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The retreat from communal property to individual lots with communal consciousness represents the Laws' 'second-best' compromise with the Republic's communism (Republic V.449a-466d). The reverence for earth as divine mother reflects Hesiod's Theogony (Gaia) and the autochthony myth used in Republic III.414d-415d (the 'noble lie' that citizens are born from the earth). The single-heir inheritance system, with adoption as a population-balancing mechanism, was influenced by actual Spartan practices (the Spartan kleros system). Population regulation through magistrates anticipates Aristotle's discussion in Politics VII.16 (1335a-1335b). The concern for maintaining exactly 5,040 households reflects the mathematical rigor established in 5.20-5.21.

D: Original: And if after all there be very great difficulty about the equal preservation of the 5040 houses, and there be an excess of citizens, owing to the too great love of those who live together, and we are at our wits' end, there is still the old device often mentioned by us of sending out a colony, which will part friends with us, and be composed of suitable persons. If, on the other hand, there come a wave bearing a deluge of disease, or a plague of war, and the inhabitants become much fewer than the appointed number by reason of bereavement, we ought not to introduce citizens of spurious birth and education, if this can be avoided; but even God is said not to be able to fight against necessity.
D: Narrative:
"And if after all there be very great difficulty about the equal preservation of the 5040 houses, and there be an excess of citizens, owing to the too great love of those who live together, and we are at our wits' end, there is still the old device often mentioned by us of sending out a colony, which will part friends with us, and be composed of suitable persons.

"If, on the other hand, there come a wave bearing a deluge of disease, or a plague of war, and the inhabitants become much fewer than the appointed number by reason of bereavement, we ought not to introduce citizens of spurious birth and education, if this can be avoided; but even God is said not to be able to fight against necessity."
D: Refashioned:
If, despite all these measures, maintaining exactly 5,040 households proves extremely difficult and an excess of citizens accumulates -- due to the excessive fertility that comes from the affection of those who live together -- and we find ourselves at a loss, there remains the old expedient we have mentioned before: founding a colony. This colony will depart on friendly terms and consist of suitable individuals.

Conversely, if a devastating wave of disease or the ravages of war reduce the inhabitants far below the appointed number through mass bereavement, we should avoid introducing citizens of inferior birth and education if at all possible. But even God, it is said, cannot fight against necessity.
D: Elegant: If the 5,040 households cannot be maintained and population grows excessive, the remedy is colonization -- sending out settlers on friendly terms. If plague or war reduces the population drastically, citizens of inferior birth and education should not be admitted if avoidable. But even God, it is said, cannot fight against necessity.
D: Summary: If population exceeds 5,040 households, surplus citizens should be sent out as colonists on friendly terms. If plague or war drastically reduces the population, admitting citizens of inferior quality should be a last resort -- but even God cannot fight necessity.
D: Distilled: Colonization handles population surplus and necessity may force accepting lesser citizens after catastrophe, for even God cannot fight against necessity.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The colonization solution echoes the 'milder purification' of 5.18 and reflects actual Greek practice -- overpopulation was a primary driver of colonization from the 8th century BC onward. The proverb 'even God cannot fight against necessity' (ananke) is attributed to various sources including Simonides and Pittacus (one of the Seven Sages). Necessity (ananke) is a fundamental concept in Greek thought -- cf. Timaeus 47e-48a, where necessity is the co-cause alongside intelligence in the creation of the cosmos. Republic III.415a-c similarly deals with the problem of maintaining class quality while acknowledging that nature sometimes produces different types. The reluctance to admit 'spurious' citizens reflects the Greek concept of eugeneia (good birth) -- cf. Republic V.459a-461e on eugenic breeding among guardians.

D: Original: Wherefore let us suppose this "high argument" of ours to address us in the following terms:-Best of men, cease not to honour according to nature similarity and equality and sameness and agreement, as regards number and every good and noble quality. And, above all, observe the aforesaid number 5040 throughout life; in the second place, do not disparage the small and modest proportions of the inheritances which you received in the distribution, by buying and selling them to one another. For then neither will the God who gave you the lot be your friend, nor will the legislator; and indeed the law declares to the disobedient that these are the terms upon which he may or may not take the lot. In the first place, the earth as he is informed is sacred to the Gods; and in the next place, priests and priestesses will offer up prayers over a first, and second, and even a third sacrifice, that he who buys or sells the houses or lands which he has received, may suffer the punishment which he deserves; and these their prayers they shall write down in the temples, on tablets of cypress-wood, for the instruction of posterity. Moreover they will set a watch over all these things, that they may be observed;-the magistracy which has the sharpest eyes shall keep watch that any infringement of these commands may be discovered and punished as offences both against the law and the God. How great is the benefit of such an ordinance to all those cities, which obey and are administered accordingly, no bad man can ever know, as the old proverb says; but only a man of experience and good habits. For in such an order of things there will not be much opportunity for making money; no man either ought, or indeed will be allowed, to exercise any ignoble occupation, of which the vulgarity is a matter of reproach to a freeman, and should never want to acquire riches by any such means.
D: Narrative:
"Wherefore let us suppose this 'high argument' of ours to address us in the following terms: Best of men, cease not to honour according to nature similarity and equality and sameness and agreement, as regards number and every good and noble quality. And, above all, observe the aforesaid number 5040 throughout life; in the second place, do not disparage the small and modest proportions of the inheritances which you received in the distribution, by buying and selling them to one another. For then neither will the God who gave you the lot be your friend, nor will the legislator; and indeed the law declares to the disobedient that these are the terms upon which he may or may not take the lot.

"In the first place, the earth as he is informed is sacred to the Gods; and in the next place, priests and priestesses will offer up prayers over a first, and second, and even a third sacrifice, that he who buys or sells the houses or lands which he has received, may suffer the punishment which he deserves; and these their prayers they shall write down in the temples, on tablets of cypress-wood, for the instruction of posterity.

"Moreover they will set a watch over all these things, that they may be observed; the magistracy which has the sharpest eyes shall keep watch that any infringement of these commands may be discovered and punished as offences both against the law and the God.
