How great is the benefit of such an ordinance to all those cities, which obey and are administered accordingly, no bad man can ever know, as the old proverb says; but only a man of experience and good habits.

"For in such an order of things there will not be much opportunity for making money; no man either ought, or indeed will be allowed, to exercise any ignoble occupation, of which the vulgarity is a matter of reproach to a freeman, and should never want to acquire riches by any such means."
D: Refashioned:
Let us imagine our 'high argument' addressing us directly: 'Best of men, never cease honoring -- in accordance with nature -- similarity, equality, sameness, and harmony, both in number and in every good and noble quality. Above all, maintain the number 5,040 throughout your lives. And do not devalue the modest proportions of the inheritances you received in the original distribution by buying and selling them among yourselves. If you do, neither the God who assigned you the lot nor the legislator will be your friend. The law declares to the disobedient that these are the conditions under which the lot is granted or withheld.'

First, the citizen must understand that the earth is sacred to the gods. Second, priests and priestesses will perform prayers over a first, second, and even third sacrifice, invoking due punishment on anyone who buys or sells the houses or lands they received. These prayers shall be inscribed in the temples on tablets of cypress-wood as a warning for future generations.

Furthermore, a watch will be set over all these matters to ensure compliance. The magistracy with the sharpest oversight shall monitor for any violation, and transgressions shall be punished as offenses against both the law and the divine order. How great the benefit of such an ordinance is to cities that obey it, no wicked person can ever understand, as the old proverb says -- only a person of experience and good character.

Under such an arrangement, there will be little opportunity for accumulating wealth. No citizen should -- or will be permitted to -- pursue any degrading occupation whose vulgarity would be a disgrace to a free person, nor should anyone seek to grow rich by such means.
D: Elegant: The argument itself speaks to the citizens: honor natural equality, sameness, and harmony, and maintain the number 5,040 throughout life. Never buy or sell the lots you received in the distribution, for the earth is sacred to the gods. Priests and priestesses will invoke curses on any who traffic in houses or land, inscribing their prayers on cypress-wood tablets in the temples as a permanent warning for posterity. The sharpest-eyed magistracy will enforce this, punishing all violations as offenses against both the law and divine order. How great the benefit of such an ordinance, no wicked person can ever comprehend -- only the experienced and virtuous understand. Under this system, little opportunity exists for accumulating money, and no citizen should pursue any degrading occupation whose vulgarity would disgrace a free person.
D: Summary: Lots of land must never be bought or sold -- the earth is sacred, and priests will invoke curses on violators inscribed on temple tablets. Magistrates will enforce this prohibition. Under such a system, there is little opportunity for wealth accumulation, and no free citizen should pursue degrading occupations.
D: Distilled: Land allotments are sacred and inalienable -- priests will curse violators, magistrates will punish them, and the system deliberately limits opportunities for wealth accumulation.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The personification of the argument addressing the citizens is a rhetorical device used throughout the Laws (cf. the Laws speaking in the Crito 50a-54d). The inalienability of land lots was a feature of some historical Greek constitutions, notably Sparta's legendary Lycurgan system (Plutarch, Lycurgus 8). The inscribing of curses on cypress-wood tablets in temples combines religious and legal enforcement -- a distinctively Laws approach that integrates theology into governance. The prohibition against 'ignoble occupations' (banausic trades) for free citizens reflects widespread Greek elite attitudes (cf. Aristotle, Politics III.5, 1278a) and connects to the Republic's guardian class restrictions (III.415a-417b). The proverb about bad men not understanding good governance echoes the philosopher-knowledge theme of Republic VII.

D: Original: Further, the law enjoins that no private man shall be allowed to possess gold and silver, but only coin for daily use, which is almost necessary in dealing with artisans, and for payment of hirelings, whether slaves or immigrants, by all those persons who require the use of them. Wherefore our citizens, as we say, should have a coin passing current among themselves, but not accepted among the rest of mankind; with a view, however, to expeditions and journeys to other lands-for embassies, or for any other occasion which may arise of sending out a herald, the state must also possess a common Hellenic currency. If a private person is ever obliged to go abroad, let him have the consent of the magistrates and go; and if when he returns he has any foreign money remaining, let him give the surplus back to the treasury, and receive a corresponding sum in the local currency. And if he is discovered to appropriate it, let it be confiscated, and let him who knows and does not inform be subject to curse and dishonour equally him who brought the money, and also to a fine not less in amount than the foreign money which has been brought back. In marrying and giving in marriage, no one shall give or receive any dowry at all; and no one shall deposit money with another whom he does not trust as a friend, nor shall he lend money upon interest; and the borrower should be under no obligation to repay either capital or interest. That these principles are best, any one may see who compares them with the first principle and intention of a state.
D: Narrative:
"Further, the law enjoins that no private man shall be allowed to possess gold and silver, but only coin for daily use, which is almost necessary in dealing with artisans, and for payment of hirelings, whether slaves or immigrants, by all those persons who require the use of them. Wherefore our citizens, as we say, should have a coin passing current among themselves, but not accepted among the rest of mankind; with a view, however, to expeditions and journeys to other lands -- for embassies, or for any other occasion which may arise of sending out a herald, the state must also possess a common Hellenic currency.

"If a private person is ever obliged to go abroad, let him have the consent of the magistrates and go; and if when he returns he has any foreign money remaining, let him give the surplus back to the treasury, and receive a corresponding sum in the local currency. And if he is discovered to appropriate it, let it be confiscated, and let him who knows and does not inform be subject to curse and dishonour equally with him who brought the money, and also to a fine not less in amount than the foreign money which has been brought back.

"In marrying and giving in marriage, no one shall give or receive any dowry at all; and no one shall deposit money with another whom he does not trust as a friend, nor shall he lend money upon interest; and the borrower should be under no obligation to repay either capital or interest. That these principles are best, any one may see who compares them with the first principle and intention of a state."
D: Refashioned:
The law further stipulates that no private citizen may possess gold or silver -- only local coinage for everyday transactions, which is virtually essential for dealings with craftsmen and for paying wages to hired workers, whether slaves or immigrants. Citizens shall therefore use a currency that circulates among themselves but has no value abroad.

However, for military expeditions, diplomatic missions, embassies, and any other occasion requiring travel to foreign lands, the state itself must maintain a reserve of standard Greek currency. If a private citizen must travel abroad, they shall first obtain permission from the magistrates. Upon returning, any remaining foreign currency must be surrendered to the treasury in exchange for an equivalent sum in local coin. Anyone caught keeping foreign money shall have it confiscated. Furthermore, anyone who knows of such an offense and fails to report it shall face the same curse and dishonor as the offender, plus a fine at least equal to the amount of foreign currency involved.

In matters of marriage, no one shall give or receive any dowry whatsoever. No one shall deposit money with another person they do not trust as a genuine friend. No one shall lend money at interest. And borrowers shall be under no obligation to repay either principal or interest. Anyone can see that these principles are sound by comparing them with the fundamental purpose and intention of a state.
D: Elegant: No private citizen may possess gold or silver -- only local coin for daily commerce with artisans and hired workers. This currency has no value abroad. The state alone maintains foreign currency for diplomatic and military purposes. Citizens traveling abroad need magistrate approval and must surrender remaining foreign money upon return or face confiscation; those who fail to report violations share the same penalties. No dowries in marriage. No lending at interest. No obligation to repay loans of capital or interest. These principles align with the fundamental purpose of the state.
D: Summary: Private citizens may only possess local currency of no foreign value. The state alone holds Greek currency for official purposes. Foreign money must be surrendered upon return from travel. Marriage dowries are prohibited, as is lending at interest -- borrowers bear no obligation to repay.
D: Distilled: Private gold and silver are banned, only local currency is permitted, dowries are prohibited, and lending at interest is abolished -- all to prevent wealth accumulation from undermining the state's purpose.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The dual currency system -- local coin without foreign value, plus state-held Greek currency -- is one of the Laws' most distinctive economic proposals. It parallels Sparta's iron currency (Plutarch, Lycurgus 9), which similarly aimed to discourage commerce and wealth accumulation. The prohibition on gold and silver for private citizens echoes Republic III.416d-417b (guardians may not touch gold/silver). The ban on interest-bearing loans reflects widespread ancient criticism of usury -- cf. Aristotle, Politics I.10 (1258b), where money-lending is called the most unnatural form of acquisition. The abolition of dowries aims to prevent marriage from becoming a financial transaction. The remarkable provision that borrowers need not repay capital or interest makes lending essentially a gift -- radically discouraging the credit economy. The information requirement (reporting violations) creates a system of civic mutual surveillance that recalls Sparta's institutions.

D: Original: The intention, as we affirm, of a reasonable statesman, is not what the many declare to be the object of a good legislator, namely, that the state for the true interests of which he is advising should be as great and as rich as possible, and should possess gold and silver, and have the greatest empire by sea and land;-this they imagine to be the real object of legislation, at the same time adding, inconsistently, that the true legislator desires to have the city the best and happiest possible. But they do not see that some of these things are possible, and some of them are impossible; and he who orders the state will desire what is possible, and will not indulge in vain wishes or attempts to accomplish that which is impossible. The citizen must indeed be happy and good, and the legislator will seek to make him so; but very rich and very good at the same time he cannot be, not, at least, in the sense in which the many speak of riches. For they mean by "the rich" the few who have the most valuable possessions, although the owner of them may quite well be a rogue.
D: Narrative:
"The intention, as we affirm, of a reasonable statesman, is not what the many declare to be the object of a good legislator, namely, that the state for the true interests of which he is advising should be as great and as rich as possible, and should possess gold and silver, and have the greatest empire by sea and land; this they imagine to be the real object of legislation, at the same time adding, inconsistently, that the true legislator desires to have the city the best and happiest possible.

"But they do not see that some of these things are possible, and some of them are impossible; and he who orders the state will desire what is possible, and will not indulge in vain wishes or attempts to accomplish that which is impossible. The citizen must indeed be happy and good, and the legislator will seek to make him so; but very rich and very good at the same time he cannot be, not, at least, in the sense in which the many speak of riches. For they mean by 'the rich' the few who have the most valuable possessions, although the owner of them may quite well be a rogue."
D: Refashioned:
The true aim of a reasonable statesman is not what the masses declare to be the purpose of good legislation -- namely, that the state should be made as large and wealthy as possible, overflowing with gold and silver, commanding the greatest empire on land and sea. This is what people imagine legislation exists to achieve, and they add, quite inconsistently, that the true lawgiver should also make the city as virtuous and happy as possible.

What they fail to see is that some of these goals are achievable while others are not, and a sensible organizer of the state will pursue only what is possible, refusing to waste effort on impossibilities. Citizens must indeed be happy and good -- this the legislator will work to ensure. But a person cannot be both extremely wealthy and extremely good at the same time, at least not in the way most people understand wealth. When the masses speak of 'the rich,' they mean the few who possess the most valuable property -- though the owner of such property may very well be a scoundrel.
D: Elegant: A reasonable statesman does not aim for what the masses demand -- maximum size, wealth, gold, silver, and empire. People inconsistently add that the state should also be virtuous and happy. But some goals are compatible and others are not. A wise legislator pursues only what is possible. Citizens must be happy and good, but they cannot be both extremely wealthy and extremely good. The masses define 'rich' as those with the most valuable possessions -- though such owners may well be scoundrels.
D: Summary: The true legislator does not aim for maximum wealth and empire but for citizens who are happy and good. Extreme wealth and true goodness are incompatible, since the masses define 'rich' as possessing the most -- regardless of the owner's moral character.
D: Distilled: A wise legislator pursues goodness and happiness for citizens rather than maximum wealth and empire, because extreme riches and genuine virtue are incompatible.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage directly challenges the imperialist ideology of Athenian democracy -- the pursuit of wealth and naval empire that Thucydides chronicles in the History of the Peloponnesian War. The argument that a person cannot be both very rich and very good echoes Republic I.330d-331b (Cephalus' moderate view of wealth) and anticipates the detailed proof in 5.29. The critique of popular opinion about legislation's purpose parallels the philosopher-king argument in Republic V-VII -- the masses have fundamentally wrong ideas about what constitutes political success. The term 'rogue' for the wealthy owner connects to Republic VIII.553a-555b on the oligarchic character, driven by acquisitiveness regardless of justice.

D: Original: And if this is true, I can never assent to the doctrine that the rich man will be happy-he must be good as well as rich. And good in a high degree, and rich in a high degree at the same time, he cannot be. Some one will ask, why not? And we shall answer-Because acquisitions which come from sources which are just and unjust indifferently, are more than double those which come from just sources only; and the sums which are expended neither honourably nor disgracefully, are only half as great as those which are expended honourably and on honourable purposes. Thus, if the one acquires double and spends half, the other who is in the opposite case and is a good man cannot possibly be wealthier than he. The first-I am speaking of the saver and not of the spender-is not always bad; he may indeed in some cases be utterly bad, but, as I was saying, a good man he never is. For he who receives money unjustly as well as justly, and spends neither nor unjustly, will be a rich man if he be also thrifty. On the other hand, the utterly bad is in general profligate, and therefore very poor; while he who spends on noble objects, and acquires wealth by just means only, can hardly be remarkable for riches, any more than he can be very poor. Our statement, then, is true, that the very rich are not good, and, if they are not good, they are not happy.
D: Narrative:
"And if this is true, I can never assent to the doctrine that the rich man will be happy -- he must be good as well as rich. And good in a high degree, and rich in a high degree at the same time, he cannot be. Some one will ask, why not? And we shall answer: Because acquisitions which come from sources which are just and unjust indifferently, are more than double those which come from just sources only; and the sums which are expended neither honourably nor disgracefully, are only half as great as those which are expended honourably and on honourable purposes.

"Thus, if the one acquires double and spends half, the other who is in the opposite case and is a good man cannot possibly be wealthier than he. The first -- I am speaking of the saver and not of the spender -- is not always bad; he may indeed in some cases be utterly bad, but, as I was saying, a good man he never is. For he who receives money unjustly as well as justly, and spends neither honourably nor dishonourably, will be a rich man if he be also thrifty. On the other hand, the utterly bad is in general profligate, and therefore very poor; while he who spends on noble objects, and acquires wealth by just means only, can hardly be remarkable for riches, any more than he can be very poor.

"Our statement, then, is true, that the very rich are not good, and, if they are not good, they are not happy."
D: Refashioned:
If this is so, I cannot accept the view that the rich person will necessarily be happy -- they must also be good. But a person cannot be both highly virtuous and highly wealthy at the same time. Someone might ask: why not? Here is the answer.

A person who acquires wealth from both just and unjust sources indiscriminately accumulates more than double what someone who earns only through just means can gather. Meanwhile, a person who spends without concern for honor or dishonor saves roughly half as much as one who directs spending only toward honorable purposes. So if the indiscriminate earner acquires double and saves half, the honest person in the opposite position can never match their wealth.

The indiscriminate earner and saver is not always thoroughly wicked -- they may be in some cases -- but they are never truly good. Someone who takes money from both just and unjust sources and spends it in morally neutral ways will be rich if they are also frugal. The thoroughly bad person, on the other hand, tends to be extravagant and therefore quite poor. And the genuinely good person, who earns only through just means and spends on noble causes, can hardly accumulate remarkable wealth -- though they will not be destitute either.

Our claim therefore stands: the very rich are not good, and if they are not good, they are not happy.
D: Elegant: The rich person is not necessarily happy -- they must be good as well. But extreme wealth and high virtue are incompatible. Why? Those who acquire from both just and unjust sources gain more than double what the just earner gains. Those who spend without moral concern save far more than those who spend honorably. The morally indifferent accumulator grows wealthy; the thoroughly bad person is usually extravagant and poor; the genuinely good person, earning justly and spending nobly, can never match the wealth of the unscrupulous. Therefore: the very rich are not good, and if not good, not happy.
D: Summary: The Athenian proves mathematically that extreme wealth and virtue are incompatible. The unscrupulous person earns from all sources and spends cheaply, outpacing the virtuous person who earns only justly and spends nobly. Therefore the very rich cannot be good, and without goodness, they cannot be happy.
D: Distilled: The very rich cannot be good because moral constraints on earning and spending inevitably limit accumulation -- and without goodness, there is no happiness.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is one of Plato's most remarkable economic arguments -- a quasi-mathematical proof that structural features of just vs. unjust acquisition make extreme wealth and virtue incompatible. The logic anticipates modern arguments about competitive disadvantage for ethical actors in unregulated markets. The conclusion that 'the very rich are not good' echoes Jesus' later teaching about the difficulty of the rich entering the kingdom of heaven (Mark 10:25), though from different premises. In Plato, cf. Republic I.330d (Cephalus: moderate wealth and a clear conscience), Republic VIII.550d-555a (oligarchic wealth-obsession as moral corruption), and Gorgias 470d-471d (Polus' claim that the tyrant is happy, which Socrates refutes). The three-way typology -- morally neutral accumulator, profligate bad person, and moderate good person -- is original to the Laws.

D: Original:
But the intention of our laws was that the citizens should be as happy as may be, and as friendly as possible to one another. And men who are always at law with one another, and amongst whom there are many wrongs done, can never be friends to one another, but only those among whom crimes and lawsuits are few and slight. Therefore we say that gold and silver ought not to be allowed in the city, nor much of the vulgar sort of trade which is carried on by lending money, or rearing the meaner kinds of live stock; but only the produce of agriculture, and only so much of this as will not compel us in pursuing it to neglect that for the sake of which riches exist-I mean, soul and body, which without gymnastics, and without education, will never be worth anything; and therefore, as we have said not once but many times, the care of riches should have the last place in our thoughts. For there are in all three things about which every man has an interest; and the interest about money, when rightly regarded, is the third and lowest of them: midway comes the interest of the body; and, first of all, that of the soul; and the state which we are describing will have been rightly constituted if it ordains honours according to this scale. But if, in any of the laws which have been ordained, health has been preferred to temperance, or wealth to health and temperate habits, that law must clearly be wrong. Wherefore, also, the legislator ought often to impress upon himself the question-"What do I want?" and "Do I attain my aim, or do I miss the mark?" In this way, and in this way only, he ma acquit himself and free others from the work of legislation.

Let the allottee then hold his lot upon the conditions which we have mentioned.
D: Narrative:
"But the intention of our laws was that the citizens should be as happy as may be, and as friendly as possible to one another. And men who are always at law with one another, and amongst whom there are many wrongs done, can never be friends to one another, but only those among whom crimes and lawsuits are few and slight.

"Therefore we say that gold and silver ought not to be allowed in the city, nor much of the vulgar sort of trade which is carried on by lending money, or rearing the meaner kinds of live stock; but only the produce of agriculture, and only so much of this as will not compel us in pursuing it to neglect that for the sake of which riches exist -- I mean, soul and body, which without gymnastics, and without education, will never be worth anything; and therefore, as we have said not once but many times, the care of riches should have the last place in our thoughts.

"For there are in all three things about which every man has an interest; and the interest about money, when rightly regarded, is the third and lowest of them: midway comes the interest of the body; and, first of all, that of the soul; and the state which we are describing will have been rightly constituted if it ordains honours according to this scale. But if, in any of the laws which have been ordained, health has been preferred to temperance, or wealth to health and temperate habits, that law must clearly be wrong. Wherefore, also, the legislator ought often to impress upon himself the question -- 'What do I want?' and 'Do I attain my aim, or do I miss the mark?' In this way, and in this way only, he may acquit himself and free others from the work of legislation.

"Let the allottee then hold his lot upon the conditions which we have mentioned."
D: Refashioned:
The purpose of our laws is to make citizens as happy as possible and as friendly toward one another as they can be. People who are constantly in court with each other, among whom many wrongs are committed, can never be friends -- only those among whom offenses and lawsuits are few and minor.

For this reason, gold and silver should not be permitted in the city, nor should the vulgar forms of commerce carried on through money-lending or the raising of inferior livestock. Only agricultural production should be allowed, and only so much of it as will not force us to neglect the very things that wealth is supposed to serve -- namely, the soul and body. Without physical training and education, neither soul nor body will ever be worth anything. Therefore, as we have said repeatedly, concern for wealth should occupy the lowest place in our priorities.

Every person has three fundamental interests. Properly understood, the interest in money ranks third and lowest. The interest in the body comes second. And first and highest is the interest in the soul. A state will be rightly constituted only if it distributes honors according to this scale. If any law has been enacted that places health above moral temperance, or wealth above health and temperate habits, that law is clearly wrong.

The legislator should therefore constantly ask himself: 'What is my aim?' and 'Am I achieving it, or am I missing the mark?' Only by this discipline can the legislator fulfill his duty and relieve others of the burden of further legislation.

Let each allottee hold their lot on the conditions we have described.
D: Elegant: Our laws aim to make citizens happy and friendly. Constant litigation destroys friendship; therefore gold, silver, money-lending, and vulgar commerce must be excluded. Only agriculture is permitted, and only enough to avoid neglecting what wealth serves -- soul and body, which require education and training. Three interests govern every life: soul (highest), body (middle), wealth (lowest). Any law preferring health to temperance or wealth to health is wrong. The legislator must constantly ask: 'What is my aim? Am I hitting the mark?' Let each allottee hold their lot on these conditions.
D: Summary: Laws should promote happiness and friendship, not litigation. Gold, silver, and vulgar commerce are excluded. The three human interests rank soul first, body second, wealth third -- and all legislation must honor this order. The legislator's constant question must be: Am I achieving my aim?
D: Distilled: Every law must honor the hierarchy of soul over body over wealth -- any legislation that inverts this order is wrong, and the legislator must constantly check whether they are hitting their mark.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage synthesizes the entire Book V preamble: the soul-body-wealth hierarchy from 5.1-5.4, the economic restrictions from 5.26-5.27, and the pleasure-virtue argument from 5.12-5.15. The connection between litigation and enmity echoes Republic V.462a-e on unity. The restriction to agriculture reflects the physiocratic economics common in ancient thought -- Aristotle similarly privileges agriculture in Politics I.8 (1256a-b). The self-questioning legislator -- 'What do I want? Am I hitting the mark?' -- recalls the Socratic method of self-examination (Apology 38a) applied to political craft. The Greek word for 'missing the mark' (hamartanein) is also the word for 'sinning,' connecting legislative error to moral failure. The final sentence transitions from preamble to specific constitutional provisions.

D: Original: It would be well that every man should come to the colony having all things equal; but seeing that this is not possible, and one man will have greater possessions than another, for many reasons and in particular in order to preserve equality in special crises of the state, qualifications of property must be unequal, in order that offices and contributions and distributions may be proportioned to the value of each person's wealth, and not solely to the virtue of his ancestors or himself, nor yet to the strength and beauty of his person, but also to the measure of his wealth or poverty; and so by a law of inequality, which will be in proportion to his wealth, he will receive honours and offices as equally as possible, and there will be no quarrels and disputes. To which end there should be four different standards appointed according to the amount of property: there should be a first and a second and a third and a fourth class, in which the citizens will be placed, and they will be called by these or similar names: they may continue in the same rank, or pass into another in any individual case, on becoming richer from being, poorer, or poorer from being richer. The form of law which I should propose as the natural sequel would be as follows:-In a state which is desirous of being saved from the greatest of all plagues-not faction, but rather distraction;-here should exist among the citizens neither extreme poverty, nor, again, excess of wealth, for both are productive of both these evils.
D: Narrative:
"It would be well that every man should come to the colony having all things equal; but seeing that this is not possible, and one man will have greater possessions than another, for many reasons and in particular in order to preserve equality in special crises of the state, qualifications of property must be unequal, in order that offices and contributions and distributions may be proportioned to the value of each person's wealth, and not solely to the virtue of his ancestors or himself, nor yet to the strength and beauty of his person, but also to the measure of his wealth or poverty; and so by a law of inequality, which will be in proportion to his wealth, he will receive honours and offices as equally as possible, and there will be no quarrels and disputes.

"To which end there should be four different standards appointed according to the amount of property: there should be a first and a second and a third and a fourth class, in which the citizens will be placed, and they will be called by these or similar names: they may continue in the same rank, or pass into another in any individual case, on becoming richer from being poorer, or poorer from being richer.

"The form of law which I should propose as the natural sequel would be as follows: In a state which is desirous of being saved from the greatest of all plagues -- not faction, but rather distraction -- there should exist among the citizens neither extreme poverty, nor, again, excess of wealth, for both are productive of both these evils."
D: Refashioned:
Ideally, every settler would arrive at the colony with equal possessions. Since this is impossible -- some will inevitably have more than others -- and particularly since preserving equality during state crises requires it, property qualifications must be unequal. Offices, tax contributions, and public distributions should be apportioned not solely according to ancestral virtue, personal merit, physical strength, or beauty, but also according to the measure of each person's wealth or poverty. Through this law of proportional inequality -- calibrated to wealth -- each citizen will receive honors and offices as equitably as possible, minimizing quarrels and disputes.

To implement this, four property classes should be established -- first, second, third, and fourth -- into which citizens are sorted. Citizens may remain in their class or move to another as their fortunes rise or fall.

The natural law to follow from this arrangement is: any state that wishes to be saved from the greatest plague -- not mere faction, but complete social disintegration -- must ensure that neither extreme poverty nor excessive wealth exists among its citizens, since both conditions produce these catastrophic evils.
D: Elegant: Perfect equality among settlers is impossible, so proportional inequality is necessary. Offices, taxes, and distributions should be apportioned by wealth as well as virtue and merit, creating four property classes through which citizens may rise or fall. This proportional system minimizes disputes. The essential law: a state seeking to avoid the greatest plague -- not mere faction but total social disintegration -- must prevent both extreme poverty and excessive wealth, since both produce catastrophe.
D: Summary: Since perfect equality is impossible, four property classes should proportion offices and contributions to wealth. Citizens may move between classes as fortunes change. The essential law: neither extreme poverty nor extreme wealth should exist, as both produce social disintegration.
D: Distilled: Four property classes distribute offices and obligations proportionally, and the state must prevent both extreme poverty and excessive wealth to avoid social disintegration.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The four property classes recall Solon's Athenian census classes (pentakosiomedimnoi, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes) -- a real historical precedent for the Laws' proposal. The principle of proportional equality (geometric rather than arithmetic) echoes Gorgias 508a and connects to Aristotle's later distinction in Politics III.9 (1280a) and NE V.3 (1131a-1132b). The greatest plague being 'distraction' (diaspasis) rather than mere faction (stasis) is a striking distinction -- civil war is not the worst outcome; complete social fragmentation is. The prohibition of extremes of wealth and poverty echoes Republic IV.421d-422a (neither wealth nor poverty for guardians) and anticipates the specific wealth limits in 5.32. The mobility between classes represents a more flexible system than the Republic's fixed class structure.

D: Original: Now the legislator should determine what is to be the limit of poverty or wealth. Let the limit of poverty be the value of the lot; this ought to be preserved, and no ruler, nor any one else who aspires after a reputation for virtue, will allow the lot to be impaired in any case. This the legislator gives as a measure, and he will permit a man to acquire double or triple, or as much as four times the amount of this. But if a person have yet greater riches, whether he has found them, or they have been given to him, or he has made them in business, or has acquired by any stroke of fortune that which is in excess of the measure, if he give back the surplus to the state, and to the Gods who are the patrons of the state, he shall suffer no penalty or loss of reputation; but if he disobeys this our law any one who likes may inform against him and receive half the value of the excess, and the delinquent shall pay a sum equal to the excess out of his own property, and the other half of the excess shall belong to the Gods. And let every possession of every man, with the exception of the lot, be publicly registered before the magistrates whom the law appoints, so that all suits about money may be easy and quite simple.
D: Narrative:
"Now the legislator should determine what is to be the limit of poverty or wealth. Let the limit of poverty be the value of the lot; this ought to be preserved, and no ruler, nor any one else who aspires after a reputation for virtue, will allow the lot to be impaired in any case. This the legislator gives as a measure, and he will permit a man to acquire double or triple, or as much as four times the amount of this.

"But if a person have yet greater riches, whether he has found them, or they have been given to him, or he has made them in business, or has acquired by any stroke of fortune that which is in excess of the measure, if he give back the surplus to the state, and to the Gods who are the patrons of the state, he shall suffer no penalty or loss of reputation; but if he disobeys this our law any one who likes may inform against him and receive half the value of the excess, and the delinquent shall pay a sum equal to the excess out of his own property, and the other half of the excess shall belong to the Gods.

"And let every possession of every man, with the exception of the lot, be publicly registered before the magistrates whom the law appoints, so that all suits about money may be easy and quite simple."
D: Refashioned:
The legislator must establish the boundaries of poverty and wealth. The minimum -- the floor below which no one should fall -- is the value of the original land allotment. This must be preserved in every case; no ruler or person aspiring to virtue should ever allow an allotment to be diminished. This serves as the baseline measure. A citizen may acquire up to double, triple, or as much as four times this amount.

If someone comes to possess wealth exceeding four times the lot's value -- whether through discovery, gifts, business success, or any stroke of fortune -- they must return the surplus to the state and to the gods who are the state's patrons. If they do so willingly, they suffer no penalty or loss of reputation. But if they violate this law, any citizen may bring a charge against them and receive half the value of the excess. The offender must pay from their own property a sum equal to the excess, and the other half goes to the gods.

Furthermore, every citizen's possessions, except the lot itself, must be publicly registered with the legally appointed magistrates, so that all financial disputes may be resolved easily and straightforwardly.
D: Elegant: The minimum wealth is the value of the lot -- this must never be diminished. The maximum is four times the lot's value. Any surplus must be returned to the state and the gods without penalty. Violators face prosecution by any citizen, who receives half the excess as reward; the offender pays the full excess from personal funds, with the other half going to the gods. All possessions except the lot must be publicly registered with magistrates, ensuring simple resolution of financial disputes.
D: Summary: The lot's value sets the poverty floor; four times the lot sets the wealth ceiling. Surplus must be returned to the state and gods. Violators face citizen prosecution, with the informer receiving half the excess. All property except the lot must be publicly registered.
D: Distilled: Wealth is capped at four times the value of the original lot -- surplus must be returned to the state, and public property registration ensures transparency and simple dispute resolution.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 4:1 wealth ratio is remarkably specific and relatively modest by ancient standards -- Aristotle in Politics II.7 (1266b) discusses and critiques Plato's property limits. The whistleblower incentive (informer receives half the excess) adapts the Athenian legal institution of phasis, where private prosecutors could receive a portion of fines. The public property registry anticipates modern wealth transparency requirements. The preservation of the lot as an inviolable minimum creates a de facto social safety net. Cf. Republic III.416d-417b (absolute prohibition on private wealth for guardians) -- the Laws' four-tier system is a pragmatic compromise that allows inequality within defined bounds. The dedication of surplus to the gods sacralizes the wealth limit, making violation both a legal and religious offense.

D: Original: The next thing to be noted is, that the city should be placed as nearly as possible in the centre of the country; we should choose a place which possesses what is suitable for a city, and this may easily be imagined and described. Then we will divide the city into twelve portions, first founding temples to Hestia, to Zeus and to Athene, in a spot which we will call the Acropolis, and surround with a circular wall, making the division of the entire city and country radiate from this point. The twelve portions shall be equalized by the provision that those which are of good land shall be smaller. while those of inferior quality shall be larger. The number of the lots shall be 5040, and each of them shall be divided into two, and every allotment shall be composed of two such sections; one of land near the city, the other of land which is at a distance. This arrangement shall be carried out in the following manner: The section which is near the city shall be added to that which is on borders, and form one lot, and the portion which is next nearest shall be added to the portion which is next farthest; and so of the rest. Moreover, in the two sections of the lots the same principle of equalization of the soil ought to be maintained; the badness and goodness shall be compensated by more and less. And the legislator shall divide the citizens into twelve parts, and arrange the rest of their property, as far as possible, so as to form twelve equal parts; and there shall be a registration of all. After this they shall assign twelve lots to twelve Gods, and call them by their names, and dedicate to each God their several portions, and call the tribes after them. And they shall distribute the twelve divisions of the city in the same way in which they divided the country; and every man shall have two habitations, one in the centre of the country, and the other at the extremity. Enough of the manner of settlement.
D: Narrative:
"The next thing to be noted is, that the city should be placed as nearly as possible in the centre of the country; we should choose a place which possesses what is suitable for a city, and this may easily be imagined and described. Then we will divide the city into twelve portions, first founding temples to Hestia, to Zeus and to Athene, in a spot which we will call the Acropolis, and surround with a circular wall, making the division of the entire city and country radiate from this point.

"The twelve portions shall be equalized by the provision that those which are of good land shall be smaller, while those of inferior quality shall be larger. The number of the lots shall be 5040, and each of them shall be divided into two, and every allotment shall be composed of two such sections; one of land near the city, the other of land which is at a distance. This arrangement shall be carried out in the following manner: The section which is near the city shall be added to that which is on the borders, and form one lot, and the portion which is next nearest shall be added to the portion which is next farthest; and so of the rest.

"Moreover, in the two sections of the lots the same principle of equalization of the soil ought to be maintained; the badness and goodness shall be compensated by more and less. And the legislator shall divide the citizens into twelve parts, and arrange the rest of their property, as far as possible, so as to form twelve equal parts; and there shall be a registration of all.

"After this they shall assign twelve lots to twelve Gods, and call them by their names, and dedicate to each God their several portions, and call the tribes after them. And they shall distribute the twelve divisions of the city in the same way in which they divided the country; and every man shall have two habitations, one in the centre of the country, and the other at the extremity. Enough of the manner of settlement."
D: Refashioned:
The city should be situated as close to the center of the territory as possible, in a location possessing the qualities suitable for an urban center. The city is then divided into twelve portions. First, temples to Hestia, Zeus, and Athena are founded on a site we shall call the Acropolis, enclosed within a circular wall. The entire division of city and countryside radiates from this central point.

The twelve portions are equalized by making those with superior soil smaller and those with inferior soil larger. The 5,040 lots are each split into two halves: one section near the city and one at the territory's edge. The nearest urban section is paired with the farthest border section to form one lot; the next-nearest pairs with the next-farthest, and so on throughout.

The same equalizing principle applies within each lot's two sections -- inferior soil is compensated by greater area. The legislator shall also divide the citizens into twelve groups and distribute the rest of their property as equally as possible, registering everything.

Twelve lots are then assigned to twelve patron gods, named after them, with each portion dedicated to its respective deity. The tribes take their names from these gods. The twelve urban divisions mirror the twelve country divisions, and every citizen receives two residences -- one in the center and one at the borders. That concludes the plan of settlement.
D: Elegant: The city sits at the territory's center, radiating outward from an Acropolis enclosed by a circular wall, with temples to Hestia, Zeus, and Athena at its heart. The entire city and countryside are divided into twelve portions, equalized so that better land receives smaller allotments and worse land larger ones. Each of the 5,040 lots is split into two halves: one section near the city paired with one at the territorial border, with soil quality compensated by adjusting area. The nearest section pairs with the farthest, the next-nearest with the next-farthest, and so on throughout. Citizens are divided into twelve tribes, each named after one of twelve patron gods to whom their respective territorial portions are dedicated. The twelve urban divisions mirror the twelve country divisions. All property is registered. Each citizen maintains two residences -- one in the center of the country, the other at its extremity.
D: Summary: The city is centered in the territory with an Acropolis dedicated to Hestia, Zeus, and Athena. Twelve portions are equalized by compensating soil quality with size. Each of 5,040 lots has two sections paired by proximity and distance. Twelve tribes are named after patron gods, and each citizen has two residences.
D: Distilled: The city radiates from a central Acropolis, divided into twelve god-named tribal portions with equalized lots that each span both the city center and the territory's borders.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The centrally planned city radiating from an Acropolis reflects idealized Greek city planning -- cf. Hippodamus of Miletus, whom Aristotle discusses in Politics II.8 (1267b) as the first to theorize city planning. The three Acropolis deities -- Hestia (hearth/home), Zeus (sovereign authority), Athena (wisdom/craft) -- represent the foundations of civic life. The twelve-tribe system echoes the Cleisthenic reforms in Athens (508 BC), which created ten tribes, and the twelve tribes of Israel. The pairing of near and far sections to equalize lots is an ingenious solution to the problem of geographic inequality. The dual residence requirement -- center and periphery -- ensures that citizens have stakes in both urban and border areas, distributing defensive responsibility. The number twelve connects to the twelve Olympian gods and the twelve divisions established in 5.20-5.21.

D: Original: Now we ought by all means to consider that there can never be such a happy concurrence of circumstances as we have described; neither can all things coincide as they are wanted. Men who will not take offence at such a mode of living together, and will endure all their life long to have their property fixed at a moderate limit, and to beget children in accordance with our ordinances, and will allow themselves to be deprived of gold and other things which the legislator, as is evident from these enactments, will certainly forbid them; and will endure, further, the situation of the land with the city in the middle and dwellings round about;-all this is as if the legislator were telling his dreams, or making a city and citizens of wax. There is truth in these objections, and therefore every one should take to heart what I am going to say. Once more, then, the legislator shall appear and address us:-"O my friends," he will say to us, "do not suppose me ignorant that there is a certain degree of truth in your words; but I am of opinion that, in matters which are not present but future, he who exhibits a pattern of that at which he aims, should in nothing fall short of the fairest and truest; and that if he finds any part of this work impossible of execution he should avoid and not execute it, but he should contrive to carry out that which is nearest and most akin to it; you must allow the legislator to perfect his design, and when it is perfected, you should join with him in considering what part of his legislation is expedient and what will arouse opposition; for surely the artist who is to be deemed worthy of any regard at all, ought always to make his work self-consistent."
D: Narrative:
"Now we ought by all means to consider that there can never be such a happy concurrence of circumstances as we have described; neither can all things coincide as they are wanted. Men who will not take offence at such a mode of living together, and will endure all their life long to have their property fixed at a moderate limit, and to beget children in accordance with our ordinances, and will allow themselves to be deprived of gold and other things which the legislator, as is evident from these enactments, will certainly forbid them; and will endure, further, the situation of the land with the city in the middle and dwellings round about -- all this is as if the legislator were telling his dreams, or making a city and citizens of wax.

"There is truth in these objections, and therefore every one should take to heart what I am going to say. Once more, then, the legislator shall appear and address us: 'O my friends,' he will say to us, 'do not suppose me ignorant that there is a certain degree of truth in your words; but I am of opinion that, in matters which are not present but future, he who exhibits a pattern of that at which he aims, should in nothing fall short of the fairest and truest; and that if he finds any part of this work impossible of execution he should avoid and not execute it, but he should contrive to carry out that which is nearest and most akin to it; you must allow the legislator to perfect his design, and when it is perfected, you should join with him in considering what part of his legislation is expedient and what will arouse opposition; for surely the artist who is to be deemed worthy of any regard at all, ought always to make his work self-consistent.'"
D: Refashioned:
We must frankly acknowledge that such a perfect alignment of circumstances as we have described can never actually occur. Not everything will fall into place as desired. Finding people who will accept without complaint this mode of communal life -- who will endure fixed property limits throughout their lives, bear children according to regulation, surrender gold and other things the legislator will obviously prohibit, and tolerate the prescribed arrangement of land with the city at center and dwellings radiating outward -- all of this sounds as though the legislator were recounting a dream, or modeling a city and its citizens out of wax.

These objections contain real truth, and everyone should take seriously what follows. The legislator speaks again: 'My friends, do not imagine I am unaware of the truth in your words. But I hold that when designing something for the future rather than the present, the person presenting a pattern of what they aim at should make that pattern as fair and true as possible, without shortfall. Where any part of the design proves impossible to execute, it should be set aside. But the nearest practicable approximation should be pursued instead. Allow the legislator to complete the full design first. Once it is finished, join in evaluating which parts are expedient and which will provoke resistance. An artist who deserves any regard at all must always keep their work internally consistent.'
D: Elegant: We must honestly admit that such perfect circumstances can never be realized -- the scheme sounds like a legislator's dream or a city modeled in wax. These objections are fair. But the legislator responds: in designing the future, present the fullest and truest pattern possible. Where execution proves impossible, set that part aside and pursue the nearest approximation. Complete the design first, then evaluate what is practical and what will face resistance. Any artist worthy of respect must keep their work self-consistent.
D: Summary: The Athenian acknowledges that the proposed arrangements are idealistic -- like a legislator's dream. But the legislator defends the method: design the fullest possible pattern first, then identify what is achievable and approximate the rest. The design must be internally consistent.
D: Distilled: Present the ideal design in full first -- then set aside what proves impossible and pursue the nearest achievable approximation, always maintaining internal consistency.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, voicing a self-aware objection and the legislator's response. The 'city of wax' objection echoes the challenge in Republic V.472c-473b (is the ideal state possible?) and Republic IX.592a-b (the ideal state as a 'pattern laid up in heaven'). The method described -- design the ideal, then approximate -- is Plato's mature political methodology, representing a significant evolution from the Republic's more uncompromising idealism. The artist/craftsman analogy for the legislator connects to the Demiurge in the Timaeus (28a-29b), who looks to an eternal pattern and copies it as closely as the material allows. The principle of self-consistency (to homologoumenon) is central to Platonic dialectic -- cf. Gorgias 482b-c on the importance of not contradicting oneself.

D: Original: Having determined that there is to be a distribution into twelve parts, let us now see in what way this may be accomplished. There is no difficulty in perceiving that the twelve parts admit of the greatest number of divisions of that which they include, or in seeing the other numbers which are consequent upon them, and are produced out of them up to 5040; wherefore the law ought to order phratries and demes and villages, and also military ranks and movements, as well as coins and measures, dry and liquid, and weights, so as to be commensurable and agreeable to one another. Nor should we fear the appearance of minuteness, if the law commands that all the vessels which a man possesses should have a common measure, when we consider generally that the divisions and variations of numbers have a use in respect of all the variations of which they are susceptible, both in themselves and as measures of height and depth, and in all sounds, and in motions, as well those which proceed in a straight direction, upwards or downwards, as in those which go round and round. The legislator is to consider all these things and to bid the citizens, as far as possible, not to lose sight of numerical order; for no single instrument of youthful education has such mighty power, both as regards domestic economy and politics, and in the arts, as the study of arithmetic. Above all, arithmetic stirs up him who is by nature sleepy and dull, and makes him quick to learn, retentive, shrewd, and aided by art divine he makes progress quite beyond his natural powers. All such things, if only the legislator, by other laws and institutions, can banish meanness and covetousness from the souls of men, so that they can use them properly and to their own good, will be excellent and suitable instruments of education.
D: Narrative:
"Having determined that there is to be a distribution into twelve parts, let us now see in what way this may be accomplished. There is no difficulty in perceiving that the twelve parts admit of the greatest number of divisions of that which they include, or in seeing the other numbers which are consequent upon them, and are produced out of them up to 5040; wherefore the law ought to order phratries and demes and villages, and also military ranks and movements, as well as coins and measures, dry and liquid, and weights, so as to be commensurable and agreeable to one another.

"Nor should we fear the appearance of minuteness, if the law commands that all the vessels which a man possesses should have a common measure, when we consider generally that the divisions and variations of numbers have a use in respect of all the variations of which they are susceptible, both in themselves and as measures of height and depth, and in all sounds, and in motions, as well those which proceed in a straight direction, upwards or downwards, as in those which go round and round.

"The legislator is to consider all these things and to bid the citizens, as far as possible, not to lose sight of numerical order; for no single instrument of youthful education has such mighty power, both as regards domestic economy and politics, and in the arts, as the study of arithmetic. Above all, arithmetic stirs up him who is by nature sleepy and dull, and makes him quick to learn, retentive, shrewd, and aided by art divine he makes progress quite beyond his natural powers.

"All such things, if only the legislator, by other laws and institutions, can banish meanness and covetousness from the souls of men, so that they can use them properly and to their own good, will be excellent and suitable instruments of education."
D: Refashioned:
Having established a twelvefold division, let us consider how to implement it. It is easy to see that twelve admits the greatest number of subdivisions, and that the numbers generated from it up to 5,040 offer extraordinary flexibility. Therefore the law should organize clans, districts, and villages, as well as military formations and maneuvers, together with coinage, measures for dry and liquid goods, and weights, so that all these systems are mutually commensurable and harmonious.

We should not worry about seeming overly detailed if the law requires all household vessels to conform to a common standard of measurement. The divisions and variations of numbers have applications across every domain -- dimensions of height and depth, musical intervals, every kind of motion whether linear or rotational.

The legislator must attend to all of this and instruct citizens never to lose sight of numerical order. No single instrument of education holds as much power as the study of arithmetic -- in household management, in politics, and in the arts alike. Above all else, arithmetic awakens those who are naturally sluggish and slow, making them quick learners, retentive, and sharp-witted. Aided by this divine art, they progress far beyond what their natural abilities alone would allow.

All of these mathematical tools will serve as excellent instruments of education -- but only if the legislator can, through other laws and institutions, banish greed and pettiness from people's souls, enabling them to use these tools properly and for genuine benefit.
D: Elegant: The twelvefold division and the number 5,040 provide extraordinary flexibility for organizing clans, districts, villages, military units, coinage, weights, and measures into a single commensurable system. We should not fear being too detailed: numbers govern every domain of experience -- dimensions of height and depth, musical sounds, all motions whether linear or circular. The law should therefore require even household vessels to share a common standard. No single educational instrument matches the power of arithmetic, which awakens the naturally dull, makes them quick learners and sharp-witted, and through this divine art enables progress far beyond natural ability -- in domestic economy, in politics, and in all the arts. But mathematical tools serve education well only if the legislator can first banish greed and pettiness from citizens' souls through other laws and institutions, enabling proper use of these instruments.
D: Summary: The twelvefold division should be applied universally -- to social organization, military ranks, coinage, weights, and measures. Arithmetic is the most powerful educational instrument, awakening dull minds and sharpening intelligence. But mathematics serves good only if greed and pettiness are first banished.
D: Distilled: Arithmetic is the most powerful instrument of education -- sharpening minds, organizing society, and harmonizing all measures -- but only if greed is first banished from the soul.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The praise of arithmetic as the supreme educational instrument echoes Republic VII.522c-526c, where mathematics is the first subject in the philosopher-ruler's curriculum because it draws the soul from becoming to being. The application of numerical order to weights, measures, and coinage reflects Pythagorean ideals of universal mathematical harmony -- cf. Philolaus fragments on the cosmos as number. The condition that mathematics becomes dangerous without moral reform anticipates the problem described in 5.36 (Egyptian and Phoenician craftiness). The notion that numbers govern sounds connects to Pythagorean musical theory (cf. Timaeus 35b-36d on the mathematical intervals of the world-soul). Plato's claim that arithmetic can elevate beyond natural powers is a remarkable statement of educational optimism.

D: Original:
But if he cannot, he will unintentionally create in them, instead of wisdom, the habit of craft, which evil tendency may be observed in the Egyptians and Phoenicians, and many other races, through the general vulgarity of their pursuits and acquisitions, whether some unworthy legislator theirs has been the cause, or some impediment of chance or nature. For we must not fail to observe, O Megillus and Cleinias, that there is a difference in places, and that some beget better men and others worse; and we must legislate accordingly. Some places are subject to strange and fatal influences by reason of diverse winds and violent heats, some by reason of waters; or, again, from the character of the food given by the earth, which not only affects the bodies of men for good or evil, but produces similar results in their souls. And in all such qualities those spots excel in which there is a divine inspiration, and in which the demi-gods have their appointed lots, and are propitious, not adverse, to the settlers in them. To all these matters the legislator, if he have any sense in him, will attend as far as man can, and frame his laws accordingly. And this is what you, Cleinias, must do, and to matters of this kind you must turn your mind since you are going to colonize a new country.

Your words, Athenian Stranger, are excellent, and I will do as you say.
D: Narrative:
"But if he cannot, he will unintentionally create in them, instead of wisdom, the habit of craft, which evil tendency may be observed in the Egyptians and Phoenicians, and many other races, through the general vulgarity of their pursuits and acquisitions, whether some unworthy legislator theirs has been the cause, or some impediment of chance or nature.

"For we must not fail to observe, O Megillus and Cleinias, that there is a difference in places, and that some beget better men and others worse; and we must legislate accordingly. Some places are subject to strange and fatal influences by reason of diverse winds and violent heats, some by reason of waters; or, again, from the character of the food given by the earth, which not only affects the bodies of men for good or evil, but produces similar results in their souls.

"And in all such qualities those spots excel in which there is a divine inspiration, and in which the demi-gods have their appointed lots, and are propitious, not adverse, to the settlers in them. To all these matters the legislator, if he have any sense in him, will attend as far as man can, and frame his laws accordingly. And this is what you, Cleinias, must do, and to matters of this kind you must turn your mind since you are going to colonize a new country."

Cleinias responds: "Your words, Athenian Stranger, are excellent, and I will do as you say."
D: Refashioned:
But if the legislator fails to banish greed, mathematical education will unintentionally produce cunning rather than wisdom. This corrupting tendency can be observed among the Egyptians, Phoenicians, and many other peoples, whose pursuits and acquisitions have taken on a general vulgarity -- whether because of an unworthy legislator, or because of some accident of circumstance or natural environment.

We must recognize, Megillus and Cleinias, that different places produce different kinds of people -- some locations beget better citizens and others worse, and legislation must take this into account. Certain places are subject to strange and harmful influences from diverse winds, extreme heat, or the quality of their water. Others are affected by the character of the food the earth yields, which influences not only the body for good or ill but produces corresponding effects on the soul.

The places that excel in all these respects are those blessed with divine inspiration, where demigods have their appointed domains and look favorably rather than adversely upon the settlers. Any legislator with sense will attend to all these factors as far as humanly possible and frame the laws accordingly. This is precisely what you, Cleinias, must do. Since you are going to found a new colony, you must devote your attention to matters of this kind.

Cleinias replies: "Your words, Athenian Stranger, are excellent, and I will do as you say."
D: Elegant: Without moral reform, mathematical education breeds cunning instead of wisdom -- as seen among the Egyptians, Phoenicians, and others whose vulgar pursuits corrupted their natural talents. Geography matters: different places produce different people through winds, heat, water, and food, affecting both body and soul. The best locations enjoy divine favor and benevolent demigods. A wise legislator accounts for all these environmental factors. Cleinias, as the founder of a new colony, must attend to such matters carefully. Cleinias agrees.
D: Summary: Without moral foundation, mathematical skill degenerates into mere cunning. Geography and environment -- winds, water, food -- shape both bodies and souls, producing better or worse citizens. The wise legislator accounts for these factors. Cleinias commits to doing so as he prepares to found his colony.
D: Distilled: Mathematics without moral reform breeds cunning rather than wisdom, and the legislator must account for how geography and environment shape both bodies and souls.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The warning about Egyptian and Phoenician 'craft' (panourgia) reflects Greek cultural attitudes -- both peoples were admired for technical skill but criticized for commercial shrewdness. The environmental determinism (climate, water, food affecting character) echoes Hippocrates' Airs, Waters, Places and anticipates Aristotle's Politics VII.7 (1327b). Plato is more cautious, adding divine favor as a factor alongside natural ones. The address to both Megillus and Cleinias by name marks this as a significant moment -- the theoretical discussion is being applied to Cleinias' practical task of founding a Cretan colony. Cleinias' brief, respectful reply closes Book V and sets the stage for the detailed legislative provisions of Books VI-XII. The closing exchange returns the dialogue from extended monologue to its dramatic framework of three elderly men walking the road to the cave of Zeus on Crete.

D: Original:
And now having made an end of the preliminaries we will proceed to the appointment of magistracies.

Very good.

In the ordering of a state there are two parts: first, the number of the magistracies, and the mode of establishing them; and, secondly, when they have been established, laws again will have to be provided for each of them, suitable in nature and number. But before electing the magistrates let us stop a little and say a word in season about the election of them.

What have you got to say?

This is what I have to say; every one can see, that although the work of legislation is a most important matter, yet if a well-ordered city superadd to good laws unsuitable offices, not only will there be no use in having the good laws-not only will they be ridiculous and useless, but the greatest political injury and evil will accrue from them.

Of course.
D: Narrative:
"And now, having made an end of the preliminaries, we will proceed to the appointment of magistracies."

"Very good."

"In the ordering of a state there are two parts: first, the number of the magistracies, and the mode of establishing them; and, secondly, when they have been established, laws again will have to be provided for each of them, suitable in nature and number. But before electing the magistrates let us stop a little and say a word in season about the election of them."

"What have you got to say?"

"This is what I have to say: every one can see that although the work of legislation is a most important matter, yet if a well-ordered city superadd to good laws unsuitable offices, not only will there be no use in having the good laws -- not only will they be ridiculous and useless, but the greatest political injury and evil will accrue from them."

"Of course."
D: Refashioned:
"Now that we have finished with the preliminaries, let us move on to establishing the offices of government."

"Let's do that."

"When organizing a state, there are two stages: first, deciding how many offices there should be and how they will be filled; and second, once the offices exist, providing each with appropriate laws suited to its function. But before we begin electing officials, let us pause to make an important observation about the election process itself."

"What observation is that?"

"Here is what I want to say: anyone can see that legislation is a matter of the highest importance. But if a well-governed city pairs good laws with poorly chosen officials, those good laws will not merely be wasted -- they will become laughable and pointless, and the greatest political harm will result from them."

"Obviously."
D: Elegant: With preliminaries complete, we turn to establishing magistracies. Two tasks face the state-builder: determining how many offices to create and how to fill them, then equipping each office with proper laws. But before elections begin, one crucial point deserves attention: even the finest laws become useless -- indeed harmful -- when administered by unsuitable officials. Good governance requires the union of good laws and good officers.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger transitions from preliminary matters to the appointment of magistrates. He establishes that founding a state requires both creating offices and equipping them with laws, but warns that good laws paired with unsuitable officials will produce not just failure but active political harm.
D: Distilled: Good laws become harmful when administered by unsuitable officials, making the proper selection of magistrates as important as the laws themselves.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). This passage introduces Book 6's central concern: institutional design. The warning that good laws require good administrators parallels Republic 412b-414a on guardian selection -- laws alone are insufficient without virtuous enforcers. Cf. Statesman 293e-294a on the relationship between law and wise rulers. The Athenian's insistence on pausing before elections to discuss method reflects the Platonic commitment to examining foundations before building -- a structural parallel to the Republic's careful construction of the ideal city.

D: Original:
Then now, my friend, let us observe what will happen in the constitution of out intended state. In the first place, you will acknowledge that those who are duly appointed to magisterial power, and their families, should severally have given satisfactory proof of what they are, from youth upward until the time of election; in the next place, those who are to elect should have been trained in habits of law, and be well educated, that they may have a right judgment, and may be able to select or reject men whom they approve or disapprove, as they are worthy of either. But how can we imagine that those who are brought together for the first time, and are strangers to one another, and also uneducated, will avoid making mistakes in the choice of magistrates?

Impossible.
D: Narrative:
"Then now, my friend, let us observe what will happen in the constitution of our intended state. In the first place, you will acknowledge that those who are duly appointed to magisterial power, and their families, should severally have given satisfactory proof of what they are, from youth upward until the time of election; in the next place, those who are to elect should have been trained in habits of law, and be well educated, that they may have a right judgment, and may be able to select or reject men whom they approve or disapprove, as they are worthy of either. But how can we imagine that those who are brought together for the first time, and are strangers to one another, and also uneducated, will avoid making mistakes in the choice of magistrates?"

"Impossible."
D: Refashioned:
"Now then, my friend, let us consider the practical challenge facing our new state. First, you would agree that anyone appointed to hold magisterial power, along with their families, should have demonstrated their character and capability throughout their lives, from youth up to the point of election. Second, the voters themselves must have been raised under good laws and properly educated, so that they can exercise sound judgment in approving or rejecting candidates based on genuine merit. But given these requirements, how can we possibly expect people who have just been brought together for the first time -- strangers to one another and without the benefit of this civic education -- to make wise choices in selecting magistrates?"

"We cannot."
D: Elegant: Two prerequisites exist for sound elections: candidates must have demonstrated their character from youth through a lifetime of public observation, and voters must be educated under good laws to judge merit accurately. A newly assembled colony of strangers, lacking both mutual knowledge and shared civic education, faces an inherent paradox -- the very conditions that make good elections possible do not yet exist.
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies a bootstrapping problem in founding a new state: proper elections require both well-known candidates and educated voters, neither of which exist when colonists are strangers assembled for the first time.
D: Distilled: Good elections require voters who know candidates well and are educated in law, but a new colony has neither, creating a foundational paradox.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). This passage identifies what might be called the 'founding paradox' -- the institutions needed to create good governance presuppose the existence of good governance. This parallels Republic 376c-412a on the education of guardians: virtue in leaders requires prior virtuous formation. The emphasis on family reputation and lifelong scrutiny anticipates the scrutiny (dokimasia) procedures detailed later. Cf. Statesman 308d-311c on the need for political knowledge in selecting rulers.

D: Original: The matter is serious, and excuses will not serve the turn. I will tell you, then, what you and I will have to do, since you, as you tell me, with nine others, have offered to settle the new state on behalf of the people of Crete, and I am to help you by the invention of the present romance. I certainly should not like to leave the tale wandering all over the world without a head;-a headless monster is such a hideous thing.
D: Narrative: "The matter is serious, and excuses will not serve the turn. I will tell you, then, what you and I will have to do, since you, as you tell me, with nine others, have offered to settle the new state on behalf of the people of Crete, and I am to help you by the invention of the present romance. I certainly should not like to leave the tale wandering all over the world without a head -- a headless monster is such a hideous thing."
D: Refashioned: "This is a serious matter, and we cannot simply brush it aside. So let me tell you what you and I must do. You, as you have explained, along with nine others, have volunteered to establish a new colony on behalf of the Cretan people, and my role is to assist you by devising this legislative blueprint. I certainly would not want to leave our plan wandering without proper leadership -- a headless creation is a monstrous thing."
D: Elegant: The founding problem is too serious for excuses. Since Cleinias and his nine colleagues will settle the colony while the Athenian provides its legislative design, their theoretical state must not be left leaderless -- for a headless creation is monstrous.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger insists the founding problem cannot be ignored. He acknowledges the division of labor: Cleinias and nine others will physically establish the colony, while the Athenian provides the legislative framework, and this framework must not lack proper leadership.
D: Distilled: The colony's legislative design must include provisions for leadership from the start, since a state without a head is a monstrous thing.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The phrase 'present romance' (mythos) is significant -- the Athenian characterizes the Laws itself as a kind of philosophical fiction, a thought experiment with practical aims. This self-awareness about the dialogic project parallels Republic 376d (the 'noble lie') and Timaeus 26c-e. The 'headless monster' metaphor recalls the organic imagery of Republic 462c-d, where the state is compared to a unified body. The mention of ten founders (Cleinias plus nine) provides concrete grounding for what has been an abstract discussion.

D: Original:
Excellent, Stranger.

Yes; and I will be as good as my word.

Let us by all means do as you propose.
