As for the interpreters, they shall be appointed thus: Let the twelve tribes be distributed into groups of four, and let each group select four, one out of each tribe within the group, three times; and let the three who have the greatest number of votes out of the twelve appointed by each group, after undergoing a scrutiny, nine in all, be sent to Delphi, in order that the God may return one out of each triad; their age shall be the same as that of the priests, and the scrutiny of them shall be conducted in the same manner; let them be interpreters for life, and when any one dies let the four tribes select another from the tribe of the deceased.

Moreover, besides priests and interpreters, there must be treasurers, who will take charge of the property of the several temples, and of the sacred domains, and shall have authority over the produce and the letting of them; and three of them shall be chosen from the highest classes for the greater temples, and two for the lesser, and one for the least of all; the manner of their election and the scrutiny of them shall be the same as that of the generals. This shall be the order of the temples."
D: Refashioned:
"All laws regarding divine matters should be obtained from Delphi, and interpreters should be appointed to direct their application. Priesthoods should be held for one year only, and no priest may be under sixty years of age. The same requirements apply to priestesses.

The interpreters shall be appointed as follows: the twelve tribes shall be arranged into three groups of four. Each group shall elect four interpreters, one from each tribe in the group, selecting three times. From the twelve thus chosen by each group, the three who receive the most votes, after passing scrutiny -- nine in total -- shall be sent to Delphi so that the God may select one from each set of three. These interpreters must meet the same age and scrutiny requirements as priests, and they shall serve for life. When one dies, the four tribes from his group shall select a replacement from the deceased's own tribe.

Beyond priests and interpreters, temple treasurers are also needed. They shall manage temple property and sacred lands, with authority over their produce and leasing arrangements. Three treasurers from the highest property classes shall serve the major temples, two for medium temples, and one for the smallest. Their election and scrutiny shall follow the same procedure as that of the generals. This shall be the complete order for temple administration."
D: Elegant: Religious law comes from Delphi through appointed interpreters. Priests serve one-year terms and must be sixty or older. Nine lifetime interpreters are selected through tribal elections and Delphic oracle confirmation. Temple treasurers manage sacred property, with their number proportional to temple importance and their selection following the same rigorous process as military generals.
D: Summary: Religious laws are sourced from Delphi through lifetime interpreters selected by tribal election and divine confirmation. Priests serve one-year terms from age sixty. Temple treasurers manage sacred property, elected with the same rigor as generals.
D: Distilled: Religious authority flows from Delphi through lifetime interpreters, while priests serve annual terms and treasurers manage temple property, all subject to rigorous scrutiny.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. Delphi's role as the source of religious law reflects historical Greek practice and the Laws' deference to divine authority in sacred matters. The interpreters (exegetai) are a uniquely important office: selected through the most elaborate process in the Laws -- tribal election, scrutiny, then final selection by the Delphic oracle itself. Their lifetime tenure and divine confirmation make them the most authoritative religious officials. This contrasts sharply with the Republic, which has no formal religious apparatus. The age requirement of sixty for priests emphasizes reverence and experience. The proportional assignment of treasurers (3, 2, 1 by temple importance) is characteristic of the Laws' detailed institutional design.

D: Original: Let everything have a guard as far as possible. Let the defence of the city be commited to the generals, and taxiarchs, and hipparchs, and phylarchs, and prytanes, and the wardens of the city, and of the agora, when the election of them has been completed. The defence of the country shall be provided for as follows:-The entire land has been already distributed into twelve as nearly as possible equal parts, and let the tribe allotted to a division provide annually for it five wardens of the country and commanders of the watch; and let each body of five have the power of selecting twelve others out of the youth of their own tribe-these shall be not less than twenty-five years of age, and not more than thirty. And let there be allotted to them severally every month the various districts, in order that they may all acquire knowledge and experience of the whole country. The term of service for commanders and for watchers shall continue during two years. After having had their stations allotted to them, they will go from place to place in regular order, making their round from left to right as their commanders direct them; (when I speak of going to the right, I mean that they are to go to the east). And at the commencement of the second year, in order that as many as possible of the guards may not only get a knowledge of the country at any one season of the year, but may also have experience of the manner in which different places are affected at different seasons of the year, their then commanders shall lead them again towards the left, from place to place in succession, until they have completed the second year.
D: Narrative:
"Let everything have a guard as far as possible. Let the defence of the city be committed to the generals, and taxiarchs, and hipparchs, and phylarchs, and prytanes, and the wardens of the city, and of the agora, when the election of them has been completed.

The defence of the country shall be provided for as follows: The entire land has been already distributed into twelve as nearly as possible equal parts, and let the tribe allotted to a division provide annually for it five wardens of the country and commanders of the watch; and let each body of five have the power of selecting twelve others out of the youth of their own tribe -- these shall be not less than twenty-five years of age, and not more than thirty. And let there be allotted to them severally every month the various districts, in order that they may all acquire knowledge and experience of the whole country.

The term of service for commanders and for watchers shall continue during two years. After having had their stations allotted to them, they will go from place to place in regular order, making their round from left to right as their commanders direct them; (when I speak of going to the right, I mean that they are to go to the east). And at the commencement of the second year, in order that as many as possible of the guards may not only get a knowledge of the country at any one season of the year, but may also have experience of the manner in which different places are affected at different seasons of the year, their then commanders shall lead them again towards the left, from place to place in succession, until they have completed the second year."
D: Refashioned:
"Let everything be guarded as thoroughly as possible. City defense shall be the responsibility of generals, taxiarchs, hipparchs, phylarchs, prytanes, and the city and market wardens, once all have been duly elected.

The defense of the countryside shall be organized as follows: since the land has already been divided into twelve roughly equal parts, each tribe assigned to a division shall annually provide five country wardens and watch commanders. Each group of five shall select twelve additional young men from their own tribe, between twenty-five and thirty years of age. These groups shall be rotated monthly through different districts so that every guard acquires thorough knowledge of the entire territory.

Service for both commanders and watchmen shall last two years. After receiving their station assignments, they shall patrol in regular rotation from west to east, as directed by their commanders. In the second year, the direction reverses -- the commanders lead them from east to west through successive stations -- ensuring that as many guards as possible experience not just different locations but how those locations change across different seasons of the year."
D: Elegant: Every part of the state must have guardians. City defense falls to elected military and civic officials. The countryside is protected by tribal watch groups: five commanders select twelve young men (ages 25-30) per tribal district, rotating monthly through all territories over a two-year term. In the first year they patrol west to east; in the second, east to west -- ensuring complete knowledge of every region in every season.
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes a comprehensive defense system. The city is guarded by elected officials, while the countryside is protected by rotating tribal watch groups of young men who patrol all twelve districts over two years, reversing direction each year to learn the terrain in all seasons.
D: Distilled: Country defense is entrusted to rotating tribal watch groups of young men who patrol all districts over two years, gaining complete territorial knowledge across every season.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The country wardens (agroikoi phylakes) system is remarkably similar to the Athenian ephebeia -- the two-year military service required of young Athenian citizens. The rotational patrol system serves a dual purpose: military defense and civic education, as young men learn every part of their territory. The seasonal rotation (reversing direction in year two) is a sophisticated detail ensuring comprehensive knowledge. Cf. Republic 412d-414a on testing guardians through various experiences. This institution also recalls the 'secret police' (krypteia) of Sparta, mentioned later in 6.25, though the Laws' version is more educational than punitive.

D: Original: In the third year other wardens of the country shall be chosen and commanders of the watch, five for each division, who are to be the superintendents of the bands of twelve. While on service at each station, their attention shall be directed to the following points:-In the first place, they shall see that the country is well protected against enemies; they shall trench and dig wherever this is required, and, as far as they can, they shall by fortifications keep off the evil-disposed, in order to prevent them from doing any harm to the country or the property; they shall use the beasts of burden and the labourers whom they find on the spot: these will be their instruments whom they will superintend, taking them, as far as possible, at the times when they are not engaged in their regular business. They shall make every part of the country inaccessible to enemies, and as accessible as possible to friends; there shall be ways for man and beasts of burden and for cattle, and they shall take care to have them always as smooth as they can; and shall provide against the rains doing harm instead of good to the land, when they come down from the mountains into the hollow dells; and shall keep in the overflow by the help of works and ditches, in order that the valleys, receiving and drinking up the rain from heaven, and providing fountains and streams in the fields and regions which lie underneath, may furnish even to the dry places plenty of good water. The fountains of water, whether of rivers or of springs, shall be ornamented with plantations and buildings for beauty; and let them bring together the streams in subterraneous channels, and make all things plenteous; and if there be a sacred grove or dedicated precinct in the neighbourhood, they shall conduct the water to the actual temples of the Gods, and so beautify them at all seasons of the year. Everywhere in such places the youth shall make gymnasia for themselves, and warm baths for the aged, placing by them abundance of dry wood, for the benefit of those labouring under disease-there the weary frame of the rustic, worn with toil, will receive a kindly welcome, far better than he would at the hands of a not over-wise doctor.
D: Narrative:
"In the third year other wardens of the country shall be chosen and commanders of the watch, five for each division, who are to be the superintendents of the bands of twelve. While on service at each station, their attention shall be directed to the following points:

In the first place, they shall see that the country is well protected against enemies; they shall trench and dig wherever this is required, and, as far as they can, they shall by fortifications keep off the evil-disposed, in order to prevent them from doing any harm to the country or the property; they shall use the beasts of burden and the labourers whom they find on the spot: these will be their instruments whom they will superintend, taking them, as far as possible, at the times when they are not engaged in their regular business.

They shall make every part of the country inaccessible to enemies, and as accessible as possible to friends; there shall be ways for man and beasts of burden and for cattle, and they shall take care to have them always as smooth as they can; and shall provide against the rains doing harm instead of good to the land, when they come down from the mountains into the hollow dells; and shall keep in the overflow by the help of works and ditches, in order that the valleys, receiving and drinking up the rain from heaven, and providing fountains and streams in the fields and regions which lie underneath, may furnish even to the dry places plenty of good water.

The fountains of water, whether of rivers or of springs, shall be ornamented with plantations and buildings for beauty; and let them bring together the streams in subterraneous channels, and make all things plenteous; and if there be a sacred grove or dedicated precinct in the neighbourhood, they shall conduct the water to the actual temples of the Gods, and so beautify them at all seasons of the year.

Everywhere in such places the youth shall make gymnasia for themselves, and warm baths for the aged, placing by them abundance of dry wood, for the benefit of those labouring under disease -- there the weary frame of the rustic, worn with toil, will receive a kindly welcome, far better than he would at the hands of a not over-wise doctor."
D: Refashioned:
"In the third year, new country wardens and watch commanders shall be chosen -- five per division -- to supervise the bands of twelve. While serving at each station, they shall attend to the following duties:

First, they must ensure the country is well defended against enemies. They shall dig trenches and construct fortifications wherever needed to prevent the ill-intentioned from harming the land or property. They shall make use of the draft animals and laborers available locally, employing them as assistants and scheduling their service during times when they are free from their regular work.

They shall make the countryside inaccessible to enemies yet as accessible as possible to friends. Roads for people, pack animals, and livestock shall be maintained in the smoothest condition. They shall manage rainfall to prevent erosion, catching mountain runoff with embankments and channels so that valleys can absorb the rain, feeding fountains and streams to irrigate the fields below and bringing plentiful water even to the driest areas.

Water sources -- whether rivers or springs -- shall be beautified with plantings and structures. Streams shall be channeled underground where useful to maximize their benefit. If sacred groves or dedicated precincts stand nearby, water shall be directed to the temples to beautify them throughout the year.

In all such places, the young men shall build gymnasia for themselves and warm baths for the elderly, stocked with ample firewood. These shall especially serve those suffering from illness -- for a weary farmer, worn out by labor, will find better healing in such a place than from any mediocre physician."
D: Elegant: Country wardens must fortify the land against enemies while keeping it accessible to friends. Roads must be maintained, rainfall managed through earthworks to prevent erosion and channel water to dry areas. Springs and rivers are beautified with plantings and buildings, with water directed to temples. The young men build gymnasia and warm baths for the elderly and sick -- rest and warm water being better medicine for a worn-out farmer than an unskilled doctor.
D: Summary: Country wardens fortify the land, maintain roads, manage water resources through embankments and channels, beautify springs and temples with plantings, and build gymnasia and baths. These facilities serve both the young and the elderly sick, offering better healing than a mediocre physician.
D: Distilled: Country wardens combine military fortification with infrastructure development -- managing roads, water, and public facilities that serve defense, beauty, and the health of both young and old.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage reveals the Laws' holistic vision of governance: military defense, agriculture, public works, aesthetics, and public health are integrated into a single administrative framework. The water management system -- catching mountain runoff, channeling it to dry areas, beautifying springs -- reflects practical Greek engineering wisdom. The preference for warm baths over physicians echoes Plato's skepticism about doctors in Republic 405c-408b and anticipates the Greek emphasis on preventive health through exercise and environment. The integration of sacred groves and temples into the water system unifies civic religion with practical infrastructure.

D: Original: The building of these and the like works will be useful and ornamental; they will provide a pleasing amusement, but they will be a serious employment too; for the sixty wardens will have to guard their several divisions, not only with a view to enemies, but also with an eye to professing friends. When a quarrel arises among neighbours or citizens, and any one, whether slave or freeman wrongs another, let the five wardens decide small matters on their own authority; but where the charge against another relates to greater matters, the seventeen composed of the fives and twelves, shall determine any charges which one man brings against another, not involving more than three minae. Every judge and magistrate shall be liable to give an account of his conduct in office, except those who, like kings, have the final decision. Moreover, as regards the aforesaid wardens of the country, if they do any wrong to those of whom they have the care, whether by imposing upon them unequal tasks, or by taking the produce of the soil or implements of husbandry without their consent; also if they receive anything in the way of a bribe, or decide suits unjustly, or if they yield to the influences of flattery, let them be publicly dishonoured; and in regard to any other wrong which they do to the inhabitants of the country, if the question be of a mina, let them submit to the decision of the villagers in the neighbourhood; but in suits of greater amount, or in case of lesser, if they refuse to submit, trusting that their monthly removal into another part of the country will enable them to escape-in such cases the injured party may bring his suit in the common court, and if he obtain a verdict he may exact from the defendant, who refused to submit, a double penalty.
D: Narrative:
"The building of these and the like works will be useful and ornamental; they will provide a pleasing amusement, but they will be a serious employment too; for the sixty wardens will have to guard their several divisions, not only with a view to enemies, but also with an eye to professing friends.

When a quarrel arises among neighbours or citizens, and any one, whether slave or freeman, wrongs another, let the five wardens decide small matters on their own authority; but where the charge against another relates to greater matters, the seventeen composed of the fives and twelves, shall determine any charges which one man brings against another, not involving more than three minae.

Every judge and magistrate shall be liable to give an account of his conduct in office, except those who, like kings, have the final decision.

Moreover, as regards the aforesaid wardens of the country, if they do any wrong to those of whom they have the care, whether by imposing upon them unequal tasks, or by taking the produce of the soil or implements of husbandry without their consent; also if they receive anything in the way of a bribe, or decide suits unjustly, or if they yield to the influences of flattery, let them be publicly dishonoured; and in regard to any other wrong which they do to the inhabitants of the country, if the question be of a mina, let them submit to the decision of the villagers in the neighbourhood; but in suits of greater amount, or in case of lesser, if they refuse to submit, trusting that their monthly removal into another part of the country will enable them to escape -- in such cases the injured party may bring his suit in the common court, and if he obtain a verdict he may exact from the defendant, who refused to submit, a double penalty."
D: Refashioned:
"Building these works will be both useful and beautiful -- a pleasant occupation but also a serious duty. The sixty wardens must guard their divisions not only against enemies but also against those who claim to be friends.

When disputes arise between neighbors or citizens, and someone -- whether slave or free -- wrongs another, the five wardens shall decide minor cases on their own authority. For more serious matters involving claims up to three minae, the combined body of seventeen -- the five wardens plus their twelve assistants -- shall adjudicate.

Every judge and magistrate must be held accountable for their conduct in office, except those who hold supreme judicial authority.

As for the country wardens themselves, if they wrong those under their care -- by assigning unequal burdens, seizing farm produce or tools without consent, accepting bribes, judging cases unjustly, or yielding to flattery -- they shall be publicly disgraced. For other offenses against country inhabitants: cases involving up to a mina shall be judged by the local villagers. For larger amounts, or for smaller cases where the warden refuses to submit to local judgment, hoping that his monthly transfer to another district will let him escape accountability -- in such cases, the injured party may bring suit in the common court, and upon winning may exact double the penalty from the warden who refused to face judgment locally."
D: Elegant: The sixty wardens serve as both builders and judges. Minor disputes are decided by the five wardens; larger cases by the combined seventeen. All magistrates must account for their conduct. Wardens who abuse their power through unequal burdens, theft, bribery, unjust decisions, or susceptibility to flattery face public disgrace. Those who evade local judgment by exploiting their monthly rotation face double penalties in the common court. Accountability is inescapable.
D: Summary: Country wardens both develop infrastructure and adjudicate disputes, with minor cases decided by five and larger ones by seventeen. All officials must account for their conduct. Wardens who abuse their authority face public disgrace, and those who try to evade justice through their monthly rotation face double penalties.
D: Distilled: Wardens serve as both developers and judges with strict accountability -- abuses bring public disgrace, and attempts to evade judgment through rotation result in double penalties.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The accountability provisions are remarkably detailed: the Laws anticipates specific forms of corruption (unequal tasks, seizure of property, bribes, unjust verdicts, flattery) and designs countermeasures. The double penalty for evading local jurisdiction by exploiting rotation is a clever anti-corruption mechanism. The principle that all magistrates must give account (euthyna) reflects actual Athenian practice. The exception for those with 'final decision' parallels the Republic's philosopher-kings who are accountable only to reason itself. Cf. Republic 412d-e on testing guardians against corruption.

D: Original: The wardens and the overseers of the country, while on their two years service, shall have common meals at their several stations, and shall all live together; and he who is absent from the common meal, or sleeps out, if only for one day or night, unless by order of his commanders, or by reason of absolute necessity, if the five denounce him and inscribe his name the agora as not having kept his guard, let him be deemed to have betrayed the city, as far as lay in his power, and let him be disgraced and beaten with impunity by any one who meets him and is willing to punish him. If any of the commanders is guilty of such an irregularity, the whole company of sixty shall see to it, and he who is cognizant of the offence, and does not bring the offender to trial, shall be amenable to the same laws as the younger offender himself, and shall pay a heavier fine, and be incapable of ever commanding the young. The guardians of the law are to be careful inspectors of these matters, and shall either prevent or punish offenders. Every man should remember the universal rule, that he who is not a good servant will not be a good master; a man should pride himself more upon serving well than upon commanding well: first upon serving the laws, which is also the service of the Gods; in the second place, upon having. served ancient and honourable men in the days of his youth.
D: Narrative:
"The wardens and the overseers of the country, while on their two years' service, shall have common meals at their several stations, and shall all live together; and he who is absent from the common meal, or sleeps out, if only for one day or night, unless by order of his commanders, or by reason of absolute necessity, if the five denounce him and inscribe his name in the agora as not having kept his guard, let him be deemed to have betrayed the city, as far as lay in his power, and let him be disgraced and beaten with impunity by any one who meets him and is willing to punish him.

If any of the commanders is guilty of such an irregularity, the whole company of sixty shall see to it, and he who is cognizant of the offence, and does not bring the offender to trial, shall be amenable to the same laws as the younger offender himself, and shall pay a heavier fine, and be incapable of ever commanding the young.

The guardians of the law are to be careful inspectors of these matters, and shall either prevent or punish offenders. Every man should remember the universal rule, that he who is not a good servant will not be a good master; a man should pride himself more upon serving well than upon commanding well: first upon serving the laws, which is also the service of the Gods; in the second place, upon having served ancient and honourable men in the days of his youth."
D: Refashioned:
"During their two years of service, the wardens and overseers of the country shall eat communal meals at their stations and live together. Anyone absent from the common meal or sleeping elsewhere, even for a single day or night, without the commander's order or absolute necessity, shall -- if the five denounce him and post his name in the agora as having deserted his guard -- be considered to have betrayed the city to the extent of his power. He shall be disgraced and may be beaten without consequence by anyone who encounters him and chooses to administer the punishment.

If a commander commits such an offense, the entire company of sixty is responsible for addressing it. Any member who knows of the offense and fails to bring the offender to trial shall be subject to the same penalties as the younger offender, shall pay a heavier fine, and shall be permanently barred from ever commanding young men again.

The guardians of the law shall carefully inspect these matters, preventing or punishing violations. Everyone should remember the universal principle: he who has not learned to serve well will never command well. A person should take greater pride in serving excellently than in commanding -- first in serving the laws, which is also service to the Gods, and second in having served honorable elders during one's youth."
D: Elegant: Country wardens must live and eat communally during their two-year service. Unauthorized absence, even for one day, constitutes betrayal of the city, punishable by public disgrace and physical correction. Commanders who violate this rule face harsher penalties, and those who cover for them share their guilt and are permanently barred from command. The deeper principle: good leadership requires first learning to serve -- serving the laws (which is serving the Gods) and serving one's elders.
D: Summary: Country wardens must maintain strict communal discipline during service, with unauthorized absence treated as betrayal. Commanders face even harsher penalties, and cover-ups are punished equally. The underlying principle is that good command requires first learning good service -- to the laws, to the gods, and to one's elders.
D: Distilled: Good leadership requires first learning to serve -- the laws, the gods, and one's elders -- and communal military discipline enforces this principle through strict accountability.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The maxim 'he who is not a good servant will not be a good master' is one of the Laws' most quoted principles. It directly inverts the typical Greek aristocratic attitude and anticipates the servant-leadership concept. The communal meals (syssitia) echo Spartan practice, which Plato admired. The severe punishment for absence -- public disgrace and beating -- reflects military discipline. The chain of accountability (wardens -> commanders -> company of sixty -> guardians of the law) creates a nested enforcement system. Cf. Republic 412e-413d on testing guardians through hardship, and the Spartan krypteia for comparison with this rural patrol system.

D: Original: Furthermore, during the two years in which any one is a warden of the country, his daily food ought to be of a simple and humble kind. When the twelve have been chosen, let them and the five meet together, and determine that they will be their own servants, and, like servants, will not have other slaves and servants for their own use, neither will they use those of the villagers and husbandmen for their private advantage, but for the public service only; and in general they should make up their minds to live independently by themselves, servants of each other and of themselves. Further, at all seasons of the year, summer and winter alike, let them be under arms and survey minutely the whole country; thus they will at once keep guard, and at the same time acquire a perfect knowledge of every locality. There can be no more important kind of information than the exact knowledge of a man's own country; and for this as well as for more general reasons of pleasure and advantage, hunting with dogs and other kinds of sports should be pursued by the young. The service to whom this is committed may be called the secret police, or wardens of the country; the name does not much signify, but every one who has the safety of the state at heart will use his utmost diligence in this service.
D: Narrative:
"Furthermore, during the two years in which any one is a warden of the country, his daily food ought to be of a simple and humble kind. When the twelve have been chosen, let them and the five meet together, and determine that they will be their own servants, and, like servants, will not have other slaves and servants for their own use, neither will they use those of the villagers and husbandmen for their private advantage, but for the public service only; and in general they should make up their minds to live independently by themselves, servants of each other and of themselves.

Further, at all seasons of the year, summer and winter alike, let them be under arms and survey minutely the whole country; thus they will at once keep guard, and at the same time acquire a perfect knowledge of every locality. There can be no more important kind of information than the exact knowledge of a man's own country; and for this as well as for more general reasons of pleasure and advantage, hunting with dogs and other kinds of sports should be pursued by the young.

The service to whom this is committed may be called the secret police, or wardens of the country; the name does not much signify, but every one who has the safety of the state at heart will use his utmost diligence in this service."
D: Refashioned:
"Furthermore, during their two years as country wardens, their daily food should be simple and modest. When the twelve assistants are chosen, they and the five commanders should agree to serve themselves: they will not use slaves or servants for their personal needs, nor will they commandeer the villagers' or farmers' workers for private purposes -- only for public service. In general, they should resolve to live independently, serving one another and themselves.

In every season -- summer and winter alike -- they shall remain under arms and survey the entire countryside in detail. This serves a dual purpose: maintaining guard while acquiring thorough knowledge of every locality. There is no more valuable information than intimate familiarity with one's own territory. For this reason, as well as for the general benefits of pleasure and fitness, the young men should pursue hunting with dogs and other outdoor activities.

This service might be called the secret police, or simply the country wardens -- the name matters little. What matters is that everyone who cares about the state's safety devotes their fullest energy to this duty."
D: Elegant: Country wardens live simply, serve themselves without slaves, and use local labor only for public purposes. Year-round and under arms, they survey every part of the territory -- for no knowledge is more valuable than intimate familiarity with one's own land. Hunting and outdoor sport complement this military-geographic education. Call them secret police or country wardens; the name matters less than the devotion every patriot owes this service.
D: Summary: Country wardens live simply, serve themselves, and patrol the territory year-round under arms, building intimate knowledge of the land through both military survey and hunting. The Athenian calls this service the most important form of civic knowledge, regardless of its formal name.
D: Distilled: Country wardens live austerely, serve themselves, and patrol year-round to acquire the most valuable civic knowledge: intimate familiarity with every part of their own territory.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The self-service requirement -- no slaves for personal use -- is a striking egalitarian measure within an institution that otherwise maintains slavery. The emphasis on geographic knowledge recalls the Republic's requirement that guardians know their city thoroughly (Republic 415d-416a). The hunting recommendation connects to Laws Book 7's extensive treatment of proper and improper hunting. The term 'secret police' (krypteia) directly references the Spartan institution where young men were sent into the countryside to surveil and sometimes kill helots. The Laws transforms this brutal Spartan practice into something more educational and constructive, characteristic of its approach to Spartan institutions throughout.

D: Original: After the wardens of the country, we have to speak of the election of wardens of the agora and of the city. The wardens of the country were sixty in number, and the wardens of the city will be three, and will divide the twelve parts of the city into three; like the former, they shall have care of the ways, and of the different high roads which lead out of the country into the city, and of the buildings, that they may be all made according to law;-also of the waters, which the guardians of the supply preserve and convey to them, care being taken that they may reach the fountains pure and abundant, and be both an ornament and a benefit to the city. These also should be men of influence, and at leisure to take care of the public interest. Let every man propose as warden of the city any one whom he likes out of the highest class, and when the vote has been given on them, and the number is reduced to the six who have the greatest number of votes, let the electing officers choose by lot three out of the six, and when they have undergone a scrutiny let them hold office according to the laws laid down for them. Next, let the wardens of the agora be elected in like manner, out of the first and second class, five in number: ten are to be first elected, and out of the ten five are to be chosen by lot, as in the election of the wardens of the city:-these when they have undergone a scrutiny are to be declared magistrates. Every one shall vote for every one, and he who will not vote, if he be informed against before the magistrates, shall be fined fifty drachmae, and shall also be deemed a bad citizen. Let any one who likes go to the assembly and to the general council; it shall be compulsory to go on citizens of the first and second class, and they shall pay a fine of ten drachmae if they be found not answering to their names at the assembly. the third and fourth class shall be under no compulsion, and shall be let off without a fine, unless the magistrates have commanded all to be present, in consequence of some urgent necessity.
D: Narrative:
"After the wardens of the country, we have to speak of the election of wardens of the agora and of the city. The wardens of the country were sixty in number, and the wardens of the city will be three, and will divide the twelve parts of the city into three; like the former, they shall have care of the ways, and of the different high roads which lead out of the country into the city, and of the buildings, that they may be all made according to law; also of the waters, which the guardians of the supply preserve and convey to them, care being taken that they may reach the fountains pure and abundant, and be both an ornament and a benefit to the city.

These also should be men of influence, and at leisure to take care of the public interest. Let every man propose as warden of the city any one whom he likes out of the highest class, and when the vote has been given on them, and the number is reduced to the six who have the greatest number of votes, let the electing officers choose by lot three out of the six, and when they have undergone a scrutiny let them hold office according to the laws laid down for them.

Next, let the wardens of the agora be elected in like manner, out of the first and second class, five in number: ten are to be first elected, and out of the ten five are to be chosen by lot, as in the election of the wardens of the city -- these when they have undergone a scrutiny are to be declared magistrates.

Every one shall vote for every one, and he who will not vote, if he be informed against before the magistrates, shall be fined fifty drachmae, and shall also be deemed a bad citizen. Let any one who likes go to the assembly and to the general council; it shall be compulsory to go on citizens of the first and second class, and they shall pay a fine of ten drachmae if they be found not answering to their names at the assembly. The third and fourth class shall be under no compulsion, and shall be let off without a fine, unless the magistrates have commanded all to be present, in consequence of some urgent necessity."
D: Refashioned:
"After the country wardens, we must discuss electing wardens for the agora and the city. There were sixty country wardens; the city wardens shall be three, dividing the twelve city districts into three zones. Like their rural counterparts, they shall oversee roads, highways connecting city and countryside, and buildings to ensure legal compliance. They shall also manage the water supply, ensuring that what the supply guardians deliver reaches the fountains pure and plentiful, serving as both a civic ornament and public benefit.

These should be influential men with sufficient leisure for public service. Any citizen may nominate a candidate from the highest class. After voting narrows the field to six, the electing officers choose three by lot, who then undergo scrutiny and hold office according to the prescribed laws.

Five market wardens shall be elected similarly from the first and second classes: ten are initially elected, then five chosen by lot, as with the city wardens. After scrutiny, they are declared magistrates.

Every citizen must vote for every office. Anyone who refuses and is reported to the magistrates shall be fined fifty drachmae and labeled a bad citizen. Attendance at the assembly and general council is voluntary for lower classes but compulsory for the first and second classes, who face a ten-drachmae fine for absence. The third and fourth classes attend without compulsion and face no penalty, unless the magistrates declare a special session due to urgent necessity."
D: Elegant: Three city wardens oversee roads, buildings, and water supply across the twelve districts. Five market wardens manage the agora. Both are selected through nomination, voting, lottery, and scrutiny -- combining democratic participation with meritocratic filtering. Universal voting is compulsory under penalty. Assembly attendance is mandatory for the wealthiest two classes but voluntary for the lower two, unless an emergency is declared. Wealth brings not just privilege but heightened civic obligation.
D: Summary: Three city wardens and five market wardens are elected through nomination, voting, lottery, and scrutiny. Universal voting is compulsory with fines for abstention. Assembly attendance is mandatory for wealthy classes but optional for lower classes, reflecting the principle that greater wealth demands greater civic participation.
D: Distilled: City and market wardens are elected through a combined process of vote and lot, with compulsory voting for all citizens and compulsory assembly attendance increasing with wealth class.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The graduated civic obligations -- compulsory assembly for wealthy classes, voluntary for poorer ones -- inverts modern assumptions about democratic participation. The Laws treats civic engagement as a duty that increases with means, reflecting the proportional equality principle from 6.15-16. The four-stage selection process (nomination, voting, lottery, scrutiny) is the Laws' signature mechanism, blending democratic, oligarchic, and meritocratic elements. The infrastructure focus -- roads, buildings, water supply -- shows the Laws' attention to material governance. Cf. Republic 420b-421c on whether guardians should be 'happy' or serve the whole.

D: Original: The wardens of the agora shall observe the order appointed by law for the agora, and shall have the charge of the temples and fountains which are in the agora; and they shall see that no one injures anything, and punish him who does, with stripes and bonds, if he be a slave or stranger; but if he be a citizen who misbehaves in this way, they shall have the power themselves of inflicting a fine upon him to the amount of a hundred drachmae, or with the consent of the wardens of the city up to double that amount. And let the wardens of the city have a similar power of imposing punishments and fines in their own department; and let them impose fines by their own department; and let them impose fines by their own authority, up to a mina, or up to two minae with the consent of the wardens of the agora.
D: Narrative: "The wardens of the agora shall observe the order appointed by law for the agora, and shall have the charge of the temples and fountains which are in the agora; and they shall see that no one injures anything, and punish him who does, with stripes and bonds, if he be a slave or stranger; but if he be a citizen who misbehaves in this way, they shall have the power themselves of inflicting a fine upon him to the amount of a hundred drachmae, or with the consent of the wardens of the city up to double that amount. And let the wardens of the city have a similar power of imposing punishments and fines in their own department; and let them impose fines by their own authority, up to a mina, or up to two minae with the consent of the wardens of the agora."
D: Refashioned:
"The market wardens shall enforce the legal order of the agora and take charge of the temples and fountains within it. They must ensure that nothing is damaged, punishing offenders appropriately: slaves and foreigners with physical punishment and detention, but citizens with fines. Market wardens may fine a misbehaving citizen up to one hundred drachmae on their own authority, or up to double that amount with the consent of the city wardens.

City wardens shall have corresponding powers in their own jurisdiction: they may impose fines up to one mina on their own authority, or up to two minae with the consent of the market wardens."
D: Elegant: Market wardens enforce order in the agora and protect its temples and fountains. Punishment varies by status: slaves and foreigners face physical correction; citizens face fines. Both market and city wardens may impose fines within limits, with mutual consent required for larger penalties -- creating cooperative oversight between jurisdictions.
D: Summary: Market wardens enforce agora order and protect its sacred and public spaces. Punishments differ by status: physical for slaves and foreigners, fines for citizens. Both market and city wardens can impose fines within set limits, with mutual consent needed for larger amounts.
D: Distilled: Market and city wardens enforce order through graduated punishments -- physical for non-citizens, monetary for citizens -- with cooperative authority over larger fines.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The differential punishment by status (stripes for slaves/foreigners, fines for citizens) reflects standard Greek legal practice. The mutual consent requirement for larger fines -- market wardens need city wardens' agreement and vice versa -- creates a system of checks and balances at the local level, preventing any single magistrate from wielding excessive punitive power. This cooperative model is characteristic of the Laws' approach to institutional design: overlapping jurisdictions with built-in checks. Cf. Republic 433a-b on justice as each doing their proper function.

D: Original: In the next place, it will be proper to appoint directors of music and gymnastic, two kinds of each-of the one kind the business will be education, of the other, the superintendence of contests. In speaking of education, the law means to speak of those who have the care of order and instruction in gymnasia and schools, and of the going to school, and of school buildings for boys and girls; and in speaking of contests, the law refers to the judges of gymnastics and of music; these again are divided into two classes, the one having to do with music, the other with gymnastics; and the same who judge of the gymnastic contests of men, shall judge of horses; but in music there shall be one set of judges of solo singing, and of imitation-I mean of rhapsodists, players on the harp, the flute and the like, and another who shall judge of choral song. First of all, we must choose directors for the choruses of boys, and men, and maidens, whom they shall follow in the amusement of the dance, and for our other musical arrangements; -one director will be enough for the choruses, and he should be not less than forty years of age. One director will also be enough to introduce the solo singers, and to give judgment on the competitors, and he ought not to be less than thirty years of age.
D: Narrative:
"In the next place, it will be proper to appoint directors of music and gymnastic, two kinds of each -- of the one kind the business will be education, of the other, the superintendence of contests. In speaking of education, the law means to speak of those who have the care of order and instruction in gymnasia and schools, and of the going to school, and of school buildings for boys and girls; and in speaking of contests, the law refers to the judges of gymnastics and of music; these again are divided into two classes, the one having to do with music, the other with gymnastics; and the same who judge of the gymnastic contests of men, shall judge of horses; but in music there shall be one set of judges of solo singing, and of imitation -- I mean of rhapsodists, players on the harp, the flute and the like, and another who shall judge of choral song.

First of all, we must choose directors for the choruses of boys, and men, and maidens, whom they shall follow in the amusement of the dance, and for our other musical arrangements; one director will be enough for the choruses, and he should be not less than forty years of age. One director will also be enough to introduce the solo singers, and to give judgment on the competitors, and he ought not to be less than thirty years of age."
D: Refashioned:
"Next, we should appoint directors of music and gymnastics, two types of each. One type handles education, the other oversees competitive contests.

By education, the law means those responsible for order and instruction in gymnasia and schools -- overseeing attendance, school facilities, and the education of both boys and girls. By contests, the law means judges who evaluate athletic and musical competitions. The contest judges are further divided into two groups: one for gymnastics and one for music. Those who judge men's athletic competitions shall also judge equestrian events. For music, there shall be separate judges for solo performance -- rhapsodists, harp players, flute players, and similar artists -- and for choral singing.

First, we must select directors for the choruses of boys, men, and young women, who shall lead them in dance and other musical activities. One director suffices for all choruses, and he must be at least forty years old. One director also suffices for solo performances, responsible for presenting competitors and rendering judgment; he must be at least thirty years old."
D: Elegant: Directors of music and gymnastics are needed for two purposes: education and competitive contests. Educational directors oversee schools and gymnasia for boys and girls. Contest judges are divided between athletics (including equestrian events) and music (solo performance versus choral song). One chorus director aged forty or older leads all choral activities; one solo performance director aged thirty or older manages competitions.
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes directors for music and gymnastics, divided between education and contests. Contest judges handle athletics (including horses) or music (solo versus choral). A chorus director must be at least forty; a solo performance director at least thirty.
D: Distilled: Directors of music and gymnastics serve dual functions -- education and contest supervision -- with age requirements ensuring maturity in leadership of the arts.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The equal treatment of boys and girls in education is notable for ancient Greece, though the Laws is less radical than the Republic's full gender equality for guardians (Republic 451c-457b). The distinction between educational and competitive roles for musical/athletic directors reflects the Laws' understanding that these arts serve two purposes: character formation and public performance. The age requirements (40 for choral, 30 for solo) suggest that choral leadership requires greater maturity than solo performance direction. Cf. Republic 376e-403c on the role of music and gymnastics in guardian education.

D: Original: The director and manager of the choruses shall be elected after the following manner:-Let any persons who commonly take an interest in such matters go to the meeting, and be fined if they do not go (the guardians of the law shall judge of their fault), but those who have no interest shall not be compelled. The elector shall propose as director some one who understands music, and he in the scrutiny may be challenged on the one part by those who say he has no skill, and defended on the other hand by those who say that he has. Ten are to be elected by vote, and he of the ten who is chosen by lot shall undergo a scrutiny, and lead the choruses for a year according to law. And in like manner the competitor who wins the lot shall be leader of the solo and concert music for that year; and he who is thus elected shall deliver the award to the judges. In the next place, we have to choose judges in the contests of horses and of men; these shall be selected from the third and also from the second class of citizens, and three first classes shall be compelled to go to the election, but the lowest may stay away with impunity; and let there be three elected by lot out of the twenty who have been chosen previously, and they must also have the vote and approval of the examiners. But if any one is rejected in the scrutiny at any ballot or decision, others shall be chosen in the same manner, and undergo a similar scrutiny.
D: Narrative:
"The director and manager of the choruses shall be elected after the following manner: Let any persons who commonly take an interest in such matters go to the meeting, and be fined if they do not go (the guardians of the law shall judge of their fault), but those who have no interest shall not be compelled. The elector shall propose as director some one who understands music, and he in the scrutiny may be challenged on the one part by those who say he has no skill, and defended on the other hand by those who say that he has.

Ten are to be elected by vote, and he of the ten who is chosen by lot shall undergo a scrutiny, and lead the choruses for a year according to law. And in like manner the competitor who wins the lot shall be leader of the solo and concert music for that year; and he who is thus elected shall deliver the award to the judges.

In the next place, we have to choose judges in the contests of horses and of men; these shall be selected from the third and also from the second class of citizens, and three first classes shall be compelled to go to the election, but the lowest may stay away with impunity; and let there be three elected by lot out of the twenty who have been chosen previously, and they must also have the vote and approval of the examiners. But if any one is rejected in the scrutiny at any ballot or decision, others shall be chosen in the same manner, and undergo a similar scrutiny."
D: Refashioned:
"The chorus director shall be elected as follows: anyone who regularly takes an interest in such matters must attend the meeting, facing a fine if absent -- the guardians of the law shall judge any delinquency. Those without interest in music are not compelled to attend. Electors shall nominate candidates who understand music; during scrutiny, opponents may challenge the nominee's competence while supporters defend it.

Ten candidates are elected by vote, and one of the ten is chosen by lot, undergoes scrutiny, and leads the choruses for one year according to law. The solo and concert music director is selected the same way: the lot-winner leads for one year and delivers awards to the judges.

Next, judges for athletic and equestrian contests shall be selected from the second and third property classes. The top three classes must attend the election; the lowest class may abstain without penalty. Twenty candidates are first elected, then three are chosen by lot from those twenty, and these must also receive the approval of the examiners. If anyone fails scrutiny at any stage, replacements are chosen and examined by the same method."
D: Elegant: Musical directors are elected by those with genuine interest in the arts -- compelled to attend, while the indifferent are excused. Ten nominees are voted on, one chosen by lot and scrutinized for the annual post. Athletic judges come from the middle property classes, chosen by vote then lot then scrutiny. Replacements follow the same rigorous process. The system ensures that only the genuinely competent and approved lead cultural and athletic life.
D: Summary: Musical directors are elected by interested citizens through a process of nomination, voting, lottery, and scrutiny, serving for one year. Athletic judges are selected from middle classes through the same multi-stage process. Any candidate failing scrutiny is replaced through identical procedures.
D: Distilled: Directors of music and athletic judges are selected through vote, lot, and scrutiny, with compulsory participation by those with relevant interest and expertise.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The requirement that those 'who commonly take an interest' must attend, while the uninterested may abstain, is a pragmatic compromise: it ensures informed voters without forcing the ignorant to make blind choices. This principle -- expertise-weighted participation -- distinguishes the Laws' approach from pure democracy. The multi-stage process (vote -> lot -> scrutiny) remains consistent across all offices. The middle-class selection base for athletic judges prevents both elite monopoly and unqualified selection. Cf. Republic 401b-402a on the importance of proper artistic judgment for the state.

D: Original: There remains the minister of the education of youth, male and female; he too will rule according to law; one such minister will be sufficient, and he must be fifty years old, and have children lawfully begotten, both boys and girls by preference, at any rate, one or the other. He who is elected, and he who is the elector, should consider that of all the great offices of state, this is the greatest; for the first shoot of any plant, if it makes a good start towards the attainment of its natural excellence, has the greatest effect on its maturity; and this is not only true of plants, but of animals wild and tame, and also of men. Man, as we say, is a tame or civilized animal; nevertheless, he requires proper instruction and a fortunate nature, and then of all animals he becomes the most divine and most civilized; but if he be insufficiently or ill educated he is the most savage of earthly creatures. Wherefore the legislator ought not to allow the education of children to become a secondary or accidental matter. In the first place, he who would be rightly provident about them, should begin by taking care that he is elected, who of all the citizens is in every way best; him the legislator shall do his utmost to appoint guardian and superintendent. To this end all the magistrates, with the exception of the council and prytanes, shall go to the temple of Apollo, and elect by ballot him of the guardians of the law whom they severally think will be the best superintendent of education. And he who has the greatest number of votes, after he has undergone a scrutiny at the hands of all the magistrates who have been his electors, with the exception of the guardians of the law-shall hold office for five years; and in the sixth year let another be chosen in like manner to fill his office.
D: Narrative:
"There remains the minister of the education of youth, male and female; he too will rule according to law; one such minister will be sufficient, and he must be fifty years old, and have children lawfully begotten, both boys and girls by preference, at any rate, one or the other.

He who is elected, and he who is the elector, should consider that of all the great offices of state, this is the greatest; for the first shoot of any plant, if it makes a good start towards the attainment of its natural excellence, has the greatest effect on its maturity; and this is not only true of plants, but of animals wild and tame, and also of men. Man, as we say, is a tame or civilized animal; nevertheless, he requires proper instruction and a fortunate nature, and then of all animals he becomes the most divine and most civilized; but if he be insufficiently or ill educated he is the most savage of earthly creatures.

Wherefore the legislator ought not to allow the education of children to become a secondary or accidental matter. In the first place, he who would be rightly provident about them, should begin by taking care that he is elected, who of all the citizens is in every way best; him the legislator shall do his utmost to appoint guardian and superintendent.

To this end all the magistrates, with the exception of the council and prytanes, shall go to the temple of Apollo, and elect by ballot him of the guardians of the law whom they severally think will be the best superintendent of education. And he who has the greatest number of votes, after he has undergone a scrutiny at the hands of all the magistrates who have been his electors, with the exception of the guardians of the law -- shall hold office for five years; and in the sixth year let another be chosen in like manner to fill his office."
D: Refashioned:
"There remains the minister of education for young people, both male and female. One minister is sufficient, ruling according to law. He must be at least fifty years old and must have legitimate children -- preferably both sons and daughters, though at minimum one or the other.

Both the electors and the elected should understand that of all the great offices of state, this is the greatest. The first growth of any plant, if it starts well toward its natural potential, has the greatest impact on its eventual maturity. This applies not only to plants but to animals both wild and domesticated, and to human beings as well. Humans are, as we say, the civilized animal. Yet with proper instruction and a fortunate nature, humans become the most divine and civilized of all creatures; insufficiently or poorly educated, they become the most savage things on earth.

Therefore, the legislator must never treat children's education as secondary or incidental. The first step toward proper provision for children is ensuring that the very best citizen is chosen as their guardian and superintendent. The legislator must do everything in his power to make this appointment correctly.

To this end, all magistrates except the council and prytanes shall go to the temple of Apollo and vote by ballot for whichever guardian of the law they believe would be the best superintendent of education. The candidate with the most votes, after undergoing scrutiny by all the magistrates who elected him except the guardians of the law, shall serve for five years. In the sixth year, a successor shall be chosen by the same process."
D: Elegant: The minister of education is the greatest office in the state. Just as the first growth of a plant determines its maturity, so the early formation of human character determines everything that follows. Humans are uniquely capable of becoming either the most divine of creatures through proper education or the most savage through its absence. Therefore the very best citizen must be chosen as superintendent of education -- elected by all magistrates at the temple of Apollo, scrutinized, and appointed for five years.
D: Summary: The minister of education is declared the most important office in the state, since early formation determines character just as a plant's first growth determines its maturity. Properly educated humans become the most divine of creatures; poorly educated, the most savage. The best citizen must be chosen, elected by magistrates and serving five years.
D: Distilled: Education is the greatest office of state because a human being's first formation, like a plant's first growth, determines whether they become the most divine or most savage of creatures.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage contains one of Plato's most powerful statements on education. The declaration that the superintendent of education holds the greatest office surpasses even the guardians of the law in importance, reflecting the Laws' conviction that character formation is the foundation of political life. The plant metaphor for early education appears also at Republic 377a-b. The paradox of human nature -- potentially the most divine or most savage -- echoes Republic 589a-b and Protagoras 321c-322d. The requirement for the minister to have children reflects practical wisdom: personal experience of parenthood informs educational judgment. The five-year term (longest in the Laws) reflects the office's gravity. Cf. Republic 376c-412a on the education of guardians as the Republic's central concern.

D: Original: If any one dies while he is holding a public office, and more than thirty days before his term of office expires, let those whose business it is elect another to the office in the same manner as before. And if any one who is entrusted with orphans dies, let the relations both on the father's and mother's side, who are residing at home, including cousins, appoint another guardian within ten days, or be fined a drachma a day for neglect to do so.
D: Narrative: "If any one dies while he is holding a public office, and more than thirty days before his term of office expires, let those whose business it is elect another to the office in the same manner as before. And if any one who is entrusted with orphans dies, let the relations both on the father's and mother's side, who are residing at home, including cousins, appoint another guardian within ten days, or be fined a drachma a day for neglect to do so."
D: Refashioned: "If any officeholder dies more than thirty days before his term expires, those responsible for elections shall fill the vacancy using the same procedures as the original appointment. If a guardian of orphans dies, relatives on both the father's and mother's side -- including cousins -- who reside locally must appoint a replacement guardian within ten days or face a daily fine of one drachma for each day of neglect."
D: Elegant: When an officeholder dies more than thirty days before his term ends, a replacement is elected by the same process. When a guardian of orphans dies, relatives from both sides must appoint a successor within ten days or pay a daily fine. Continuity of care and governance must never lapse.
D: Summary: Vacancies in public office are filled by the standard election process if more than thirty days remain in the term. Orphans' guardians who die must be replaced by relatives within ten days, with daily fines for delay.
D: Distilled: Public office vacancies are filled by standard procedures, and guardians of orphans must be replaced by relatives within ten days or face daily fines.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This brief but important provision addresses institutional continuity. The thirty-day threshold for replacement is practical -- shorter vacancies are not worth the disruption of a full election. The orphan guardianship provision reflects the Laws' deep concern for children's welfare, consistent with the emphasis on education as the greatest office (6.30). The daily fine creates an escalating incentive for prompt action. Orphan protection was a serious matter in Greek law -- cf. Athenian practices where the archon had special responsibility for orphans and widows. The requirement that relatives from both paternal and maternal sides participate broadens the support network.

D: Original: A city which has no regular courts of law ceases to be a city; and again, if a judge is silent and says no more in preliminary proceedings than the litigants, as is the case in arbitrations, he will never be able to decide justly; wherefore a multitude of judges will not easily judge well, nor a few if they are bad. The point in dispute between the parties should be made clear; and time, and deliberation, and repeated examination, greatly tend to clear up doubts. For this reason, he who goes to law with another should go first of all to his neighbours and friends who know best the questions at issue. And if he be unable to obtain from them a satisfactory decision, let him have recourse to another court; and if the two courts cannot settle the matter, let a third put an end to the suit.
D: Narrative:
"A city which has no regular courts of law ceases to be a city; and again, if a judge is silent and says no more in preliminary proceedings than the litigants, as is the case in arbitrations, he will never be able to decide justly; wherefore a multitude of judges will not easily judge well, nor a few if they are bad. The point in dispute between the parties should be made clear; and time, and deliberation, and repeated examination, greatly tend to clear up doubts.

For this reason, he who goes to law with another should go first of all to his neighbours and friends who know best the questions at issue. And if he be unable to obtain from them a satisfactory decision, let him have recourse to another court; and if the two courts cannot settle the matter, let a third put an end to the suit."
D: Refashioned:
"A city without regular courts of law is no city at all. At the same time, if a judge stays silent during preliminary proceedings and contributes no more than the litigants themselves -- as happens in arbitrations -- he will never reach just decisions. Therefore neither a large number of judges nor a small number of poor ones will produce good justice. The essential thing is to make the disputed point clear between the parties, since time, careful deliberation, and repeated examination are the greatest aids to resolving doubts.

For this reason, anyone bringing a legal dispute should first go to neighbors and friends who best understand the matter at issue. If they cannot provide a satisfactory resolution, let him proceed to a second court. And if that court also cannot settle the dispute, let a third and final court bring the matter to a close."
D: Elegant: Without courts, a city is no city. But courts must be properly structured: passive judges who merely listen, as in arbitration, cannot decide justly. Clarity, time, deliberation, and repeated examination are essential for just outcomes. Disputes should first go to neighbors who know the situation best, then escalate through two higher courts if needed -- three levels ensuring that every dispute finds resolution.
D: Summary: Courts are essential to any city, but justice requires active judges, clear presentation of issues, and sufficient time for deliberation. Disputes should be resolved first by knowledgeable neighbors, then escalate through two additional courts if needed.
D: Distilled: Justice requires active judges with time for deliberation, and disputes should escalate through three court levels -- neighbors first, then progressively higher tribunals.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The three-tiered court system (neighbors -> second court -> final court) creates a practical dispute-resolution ladder that begins with informal, local knowledge and escalates to formal authority. The critique of passive judges and arbitration is significant: the Laws wants judges who actively investigate and question, not mere arbiters. This reflects Plato's broader epistemological commitment -- truth emerges through active dialectical inquiry, not passive listening. Cf. Republic 405a-b on the proliferation of lawsuits as a sign of civic illness, and Statesman 305b-c on the relationship between judgment and political authority.

D: Original: Now the establishment of courts of justice may be regarded as a choice of magistrates, for every magistrate must also be a judge of some things; and the judge, though he be not a magistrate, yet in certain respects is a very important magistrate on the day on which he is determining a suit. Regarding then the judges also as magistrates, let us say who are fit to be judges, and of what they are to be judges, and how many of them are to judge in each suit. Let that be the supreme tribunal which the litigants appoint in common for themselves, choosing certain persons by agreement. And let there be two other tribunals: one for private causes, when a citizen accuses another of wronging him and wishes to get a decision; the other for public causes, in which some citizen is of opinion that the public has been wronged by an individual, and is willing to vindicate the common interests. And we must not forget to mention how the judges are to be qualified, and who they are to be.
D: Narrative:
"Now the establishment of courts of justice may be regarded as a choice of magistrates, for every magistrate must also be a judge of some things; and the judge, though he be not a magistrate, yet in certain respects is a very important magistrate on the day on which he is determining a suit. Regarding then the judges also as magistrates, let us say who are fit to be judges, and of what they are to be judges, and how many of them are to judge in each suit.

Let that be the supreme tribunal which the litigants appoint in common for themselves, choosing certain persons by agreement. And let there be two other tribunals: one for private causes, when a citizen accuses another of wronging him and wishes to get a decision; the other for public causes, in which some citizen is of opinion that the public has been wronged by an individual, and is willing to vindicate the common interests. And we must not forget to mention how the judges are to be qualified, and who they are to be."
D: Refashioned:
"Establishing courts of justice is essentially a form of choosing magistrates, since every magistrate must act as a judge in some matters. And while a judge may not formally be a magistrate, on the day he determines a case he functions as a very important one. So let us treat judges as magistrates and consider who is qualified to judge, what cases they should hear, and how many judges should sit on each case.

The supreme tribunal shall be one that both litigants agree to form, choosing judges by mutual consent. Beyond this, there shall be two additional courts: one for private disputes, where a citizen accuses another of personal wrong and seeks a ruling; and one for public offenses, where a citizen believes an individual has wronged the community and is willing to champion the public interest. We must also specify the qualifications judges must meet and who they shall be."
D: Elegant: Courts are a form of magistracy, since judges exercise magistrate-like authority when deciding cases. Three tribunals are needed: a supreme tribunal formed by mutual agreement of the parties; a court for private disputes between citizens; and a court for public offenses where individuals have wronged the community. The qualifications and number of judges for each must be carefully specified.
D: Summary: The Athenian treats courts as a species of magistracy, since judges exercise real authority. He establishes three tribunals: a supreme court chosen by the parties, a private court for personal disputes, and a public court for offenses against the community.
D: Distilled: Three tribunals are needed: a mutually agreed supreme court, a private court for personal disputes, and a public court for offenses against the community.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The insight that judges are effectively magistrates on the day they decide cases is characteristic of Plato's functional analysis of political roles. The three-court system separates consensual dispute resolution (supreme tribunal), private litigation, and public prosecution -- a sophisticated jurisdictional division. The public cause provision, where a citizen 'vindicates the common interests,' anticipates the modern concept of standing in public interest litigation. Cf. Republic 433e on justice as each person doing their proper function, applied here to judicial specialization.

D: Original: In the first place, let there be a tribunal open to all private persons who are trying causes one against another for the third time, and let this be composed as follows:-All the officers of state, as well annual as those holding office for a longer period, when the new year is about to commence, in the month following after the summer solstice, on the last day but one of the year, shall meet in some temple, and calling God to witness, shall dedicate one judge from every magistracy to be their first-fruits, choosing in each office him who seems to them to be the best, and whom they deem likely to decide the causes of his fellow-citizens during the ensuing year in the best and holiest manner. And when the election is completed, a scrutiny shall be held in the presence of the electors themselves, and if any one be rejected another shall be chosen in the same manner. Those who have undergone the scrutiny shall judge the causes of those who have declined the inferior courts, and shall give their vote openly. The councillors and other magistrates who have elected them shall be required to be hearers and spectators of the causes; and any one else may be present who pleases. If one man charges another with having intentionally decided wrong, let him go to the guardians of the law and lay his accusation before them, and he who is found guilty in such a case shall pay damages to the injured party equal to half the injury; but if he shall appear to deserve a greater penalty, the judges shall determine what additional punishment he shall suffer, and how much more he ought to pay to the public treasury, and to the party who brought the suit.
D: Narrative:
"In the first place, let there be a tribunal open to all private persons who are trying causes one against another for the third time, and let this be composed as follows: All the officers of state, as well annual as those holding office for a longer period, when the new year is about to commence, in the month following after the summer solstice, on the last day but one of the year, shall meet in some temple, and calling God to witness, shall dedicate one judge from every magistracy to be their first-fruits, choosing in each office him who seems to them to be the best, and whom they deem likely to decide the causes of his fellow-citizens during the ensuing year in the best and holiest manner.

And when the election is completed, a scrutiny shall be held in the presence of the electors themselves, and if any one be rejected another shall be chosen in the same manner. Those who have undergone the scrutiny shall judge the causes of those who have declined the inferior courts, and shall give their vote openly.

The councillors and other magistrates who have elected them shall be required to be hearers and spectators of the causes; and any one else may be present who pleases.
