If one man charges another with having intentionally decided wrong, let him go to the guardians of the law and lay his accusation before them, and he who is found guilty in such a case shall pay damages to the injured party equal to half the injury; but if he shall appear to deserve a greater penalty, the judges shall determine what additional punishment he shall suffer, and how much more he ought to pay to the public treasury, and to the party who brought the suit."
D: Refashioned:
"First, there shall be a tribunal for all private persons whose cases have reached a third hearing. It shall be constituted as follows: all officeholders -- whether serving annual or longer terms -- shall gather in a temple on the second-to-last day of the year, in the month following the summer solstice. There, calling God as witness, they shall dedicate one judge from each magistracy as their 'first-fruits,' selecting from each office the person they consider best qualified and most likely to judge their fellow citizens' cases in the holiest and most just manner during the coming year.

After election, scrutiny shall be conducted in the electors' presence. Any rejected candidate shall be replaced through the same process. The approved judges shall hear cases that lower courts could not resolve, and they shall cast their votes openly.

The councillors and magistrates who elected them must attend as observers, and any other citizen may attend as well. If anyone accuses a judge of having intentionally rendered a wrong decision, he shall bring the charge before the guardians of the law. A judge found guilty shall pay the injured party damages equal to half the harm. If the offense warrants greater punishment, the judges shall determine the additional penalty, specifying payments both to the public treasury and to the accuser."
D: Elegant: The highest private tribunal is formed annually through a sacred ceremony: on the year's penultimate day, all magistrates gather in a temple to select the best judge from each office as a 'first-fruit' offering to justice. These judges hear cases that lower courts failed to resolve, voting openly while magistrates observe. Judges found guilty of intentional wrongdoing pay half the damages to the injured party, with additional penalties at the court's discretion.
D: Summary: The supreme private tribunal is constituted annually through a sacred ceremony where magistrates select their best members as judges. These judges hear third-instance cases with open voting and mandatory public observation. Corrupt judges face damages and additional penalties.
D: Distilled: The highest court is formed annually when magistrates dedicate their best members as judges in a sacred ceremony, with open voting and penalties for judicial corruption.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The 'first-fruits' (aparchai) language is remarkable -- dedicating judges as sacred offerings transforms judicial selection into a religious act, reflecting the Laws' pervasive integration of politics and theology. Open voting ensures transparency, while mandatory observation by electing magistrates creates ongoing accountability. The penalties for intentional judicial wrongdoing (half-damages plus discretionary additional punishment) address a perennial problem in legal systems. The summer solstice timing anchors the civic calendar to the cosmic order. Cf. Republic 360b-362a (Ring of Gyges) on whether judges and rulers would be just without oversight -- the Laws' answer is institutional accountability rather than philosophical virtue alone.

D: Original: In the judgment of offences against the state, the people ought to participate, for when any one wrongs the state all are wronged, and may reasonably complain if they are not allowed to share in the decision. Such causes ought to originate with the people, and the ought also to have the final decision of them, but the trial of them shall take place before three of the highest magistrates, upon whom the plaintiff and the defendant shall agree; and if they are not able to come to an agreement themselves, the council shall choose one of the two proposed. And in private suits, too, as far as is possible, all should have a share; for he who has no share in the administration of justice, is apt to imagine that he has no share in the state at all. And for this reason there shall be a court of law in every tribe, and the judges shall be chosen by lot;-they shall give their decisions at once, and shall be inaccessible to entreaties. The final judgment shall rest with that court which, as we maintain, has been established in the most incorruptible form of which human things admit: this shall be the court established for those who are unable to get rid of their suits either in the courts of neighbours or of the tribes.
D: Narrative:
"In the judgment of offences against the state, the people ought to participate, for when any one wrongs the state all are wronged, and may reasonably complain if they are not allowed to share in the decision. Such causes ought to originate with the people, and they ought also to have the final decision of them, but the trial of them shall take place before three of the highest magistrates, upon whom the plaintiff and the defendant shall agree; and if they are not able to come to an agreement themselves, the council shall choose one of the two proposed.

And in private suits, too, as far as is possible, all should have a share; for he who has no share in the administration of justice, is apt to imagine that he has no share in the state at all. And for this reason there shall be a court of law in every tribe, and the judges shall be chosen by lot; they shall give their decisions at once, and shall be inaccessible to entreaties.

The final judgment shall rest with that court which, as we maintain, has been established in the most incorruptible form of which human things admit: this shall be the court established for those who are unable to get rid of their suits either in the courts of neighbours or of the tribes."
D: Refashioned:
"When it comes to offenses against the state, the people must participate in judgment. When someone wrongs the state, every citizen is wronged, and they have a legitimate grievance if excluded from the decision. Such cases should originate with the people and receive their final verdict. However, the actual trial shall be conducted before three of the highest magistrates agreed upon by both plaintiff and defendant. If the parties cannot agree, the council shall select from between the two proposed candidates.

In private suits as well, everyone should participate to the greatest extent possible. A person who has no role in administering justice will naturally feel that he has no stake in the state at all. For this reason, every tribe shall have its own court of law, with judges chosen by lot. These judges shall render immediate decisions and remain immune to outside pressure.

The final court of appeal shall be the one we have established in the most incorruptible form humanly achievable. This court shall serve those whose disputes could not be resolved in either the neighborhood courts or the tribal courts."
D: Elegant: Offenses against the state demand popular participation in judgment, since wronging the state wrongs every citizen. Such cases originate with and are finally decided by the people, though tried before agreed-upon magistrates. Private suits also require broad participation: exclusion from justice breeds alienation from the state. Every tribe has its own lottery-selected court for immediate decisions, with a final incorruptible court for unresolved disputes.
D: Summary: Public offenses require popular participation in judgment since all citizens are wronged. Private suits also need broad civic involvement to prevent alienation. Each tribe has its own court with lottery-selected judges, and an incorruptible supreme court serves as final resort.
D: Distilled: Participation in justice is participation in the state itself -- public offenses require popular judgment, every tribe needs its own court, and a supreme incorruptible court provides final resolution.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The principle that 'he who has no share in the administration of justice is apt to imagine that he has no share in the state at all' is a profound democratic insight, remarkable coming from Plato. It anticipates Aristotle's definition of citizenship as participation in judicial and deliberative functions (Politics III.1, 1275a22). The combination of popular participation (origination and final decision), elite trial (three magistrates), and lottery selection (tribal courts) reflects the Laws' characteristic blending of democratic, oligarchic, and meritocratic elements. Cf. Republic 557a on democratic participation and Crito 50a-51d on the citizen's relationship to law.

D: Original: Thus much of the courts of law, which, as I was saying, cannot be precisely defined either as being or not being offices; a superficial sketch has been given of them, in which some things have been told and others omitted. For the right place of an exact statement of the laws respecting suits, under their several heads, will be at the end of the body of legislation;-let us then expect them at the end. Hitherto our legislation has been chiefly occupied with the appointment of offices. Perfect unity and exactness, extending to the whole and every particular of political administration, cannot be attained to the full, until the discussion shall have a beginning, middle, and end, and is complete in every part. At present we have reached the election of magistrates, and this may be regarded as a sufficient termination of what preceded. And now there need no longer be any delay or hesitation in beginning the work of legislation.
D: Narrative:
"Thus much of the courts of law, which, as I was saying, cannot be precisely defined either as being or not being offices; a superficial sketch has been given of them, in which some things have been told and others omitted. For the right place of an exact statement of the laws respecting suits, under their several heads, will be at the end of the body of legislation; let us then expect them at the end.

Perfect unity and exactness, extending to the whole and every particular of political administration, cannot be attained to the full, until the discussion shall have a beginning, middle, and end, and is complete in every part. At present we have reached the election of magistrates, and this may be regarded as a sufficient termination of what preceded. And now there need no longer be any delay or hesitation in beginning the work of legislation."
D: Refashioned:
"This is enough about courts of law for now -- which, as I noted, cannot be neatly classified as offices or non-offices. What I have given is a preliminary sketch, with some matters explained and others left out. The proper place for detailed laws governing lawsuits, organized by category, will be at the end of our legislative body; we should expect them there.

Perfect coherence and precision in political administration, covering both the whole and every particular, cannot be achieved until the entire discussion has a proper beginning, middle, and end, complete in all its parts. At this point we have finished with the election of magistrates, which serves as a satisfactory conclusion to everything that preceded it. Now we may begin the actual work of legislation without further delay or hesitation."
D: Elegant: The treatment of courts remains a preliminary sketch -- detailed laws on litigation belong at the end of the legislative code. Perfect systematic coherence requires completing the whole before perfecting the parts. With magistrate elections established, the constitutional groundwork is laid. Now the substantive work of legislation can begin.
D: Summary: The Athenian acknowledges that his treatment of courts is preliminary, with detailed litigation laws to come later. Having completed the constitutional groundwork of magistrate elections, he declares the state ready for substantive legislation.
D: Distilled: With magistrate elections complete, the constitutional foundation is laid, and the substantive work of legislation can begin without further delay.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The methodological self-awareness here is characteristic of late Plato: the discussion must have 'beginning, middle, and end' before achieving full coherence -- echoing Aristotle's later prescription for dramatic unity (Poetics 1450b). The acknowledgment that courts 'cannot be precisely defined either as being or not being offices' shows philosophical honesty about classificatory difficulties. The transition from constitutional framework (offices, elections) to substantive legislation marks a major structural turning point in Book 6. Cf. Statesman 283c-285c on the importance of proper measure and completeness in political science.

D: Original:
I like what you have said, Stranger-and I particularly like your manner of tacking on the beginning of your new discourse to the end of the former one.

Thus far, then, the old men's rational pastime has gone off well.

You mean, I suppose, their serious and noble pursuit?

Perhaps; but I should like to know whether you and I are agreed about a certain thing.

About what thing?

You know. the endless labour which painters expend upon their pictures-they are always putting in or taking out colours, or whatever be the term which artists employ; they seem as if they would never cease touching up their works, which are always being made brighter and more beautiful.

I know something of these matters from report, although I have never had any great acquaintance with the art.
D: Narrative:
"I like what you have said, Stranger -- and I particularly like your manner of tacking on the beginning of your new discourse to the end of the former one."

"Thus far, then, the old men's rational pastime has gone off well."

"You mean, I suppose, their serious and noble pursuit?"

"Perhaps; but I should like to know whether you and I are agreed about a certain thing."

"About what thing?"

"You know the endless labour which painters expend upon their pictures -- they are always putting in or taking out colours, or whatever be the term which artists employ; they seem as if they would never cease touching up their works, which are always being made brighter and more beautiful."

"I know something of these matters from report, although I have never had any great acquaintance with the art."
D: Refashioned:
"I like what you have said, Stranger, and I especially admire how you connected the beginning of your new topic to the conclusion of the previous one."

"So far, then, this rational pastime of ours -- old men reasoning together -- has gone rather well."

"You mean their serious and noble pursuit, I take it?"

"Perhaps. But I want to make sure you and I agree on a certain point."

"What point is that?"

"Consider the endless labor that painters devote to their pictures. They are constantly adding or removing colors -- or whatever term artists use for it. They seem never to stop refining their work, always making it brighter and more beautiful."

"I know something about that from what I have heard, though I have never been closely acquainted with the art myself."
D: Elegant: Cleinias praises the Athenian's seamless transition between topics. The Athenian characterizes their dialogue as old men's rational pastime -- or, Cleinias corrects, a serious and noble pursuit. The Athenian then introduces an analogy: painters endlessly refine their work, always adding and adjusting to make it more beautiful.
D: Summary: Cleinias compliments the Athenian's rhetorical transitions. The Athenian modestly calls their work a rational pastime, and Cleinias elevates it to a noble pursuit. The Athenian then introduces the analogy of painters who endlessly refine their work.
D: Distilled: The dialogue is both rational pastime and noble pursuit, and legislation, like painting, requires endless refinement.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias (initiating), Athenian Stranger (responding). The painter analogy introduces a major theme: legislation as an ongoing work of art that requires perpetual refinement. The self-deprecating 'old men's rational pastime' (presbyton logike paidia) versus Cleinias's 'serious and noble pursuit' captures the Laws' characteristic tone -- philosophical seriousness leavened with ironic self-awareness about the limits of theoretical legislation. The painting metaphor will be extended in 6.38-39 to argue that laws need successors who can correct and improve them. Cf. Republic 472c-473b on the relationship between the theoretical 'painting' of the ideal state and its practical realization.

D: Original:
No matter; we may make use of the illustration notwithstanding:-Suppose that some one had a mind to paint a figure in the most beautiful manner, in the hope that his work instead of losing would always improve as time went on-do you not see that being a mortal, unless he leaves some one to succeed him who will correct the flaws which time may introduce, and be able to add what is left imperfect through the defect of the artist, and who will further brighten up and improve the picture, all his great labour will last but a short time?

True.
D: Narrative:
"No matter; we may make use of the illustration notwithstanding: Suppose that some one had a mind to paint a figure in the most beautiful manner, in the hope that his work instead of losing would always improve as time went on -- do you not see that being a mortal, unless he leaves some one to succeed him who will correct the flaws which time may introduce, and be able to add what is left imperfect through the defect of the artist, and who will further brighten up and improve the picture, all his great labour will last but a short time?"

"True."
D: Refashioned:
"No matter -- we can use the illustration regardless. Imagine a painter who creates the most beautiful figure he can, hoping that his work will not deteriorate but actually improve over time. Do you not see that, being mortal, unless he leaves behind a successor who can repair the flaws that time introduces, complete what the artist left imperfect, and continue to brighten and improve the painting, all his great labor will endure only briefly?"

"That is true."
D: Elegant: Even the most beautiful painting will not endure unless the mortal artist leaves behind successors who can repair time's damage, complete imperfections, and continue improving the work. No human creation sustains itself without ongoing care.
D: Summary: A painter's masterpiece will not last unless he leaves behind successors who can repair time's damage and continue improving the work. No mortal creation is self-sustaining.
D: Distilled: Every mortal creation requires successors to maintain, repair, and improve it -- without them, even the finest work deteriorates.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The painting metaphor serves a practical political purpose: legislation, like art, requires ongoing stewardship. This passage prepares the argument (6.39-40) that guardians of the law must be trained not just to preserve but to improve the legal code. The mortality theme -- the artist is mortal, so the work needs successors -- adds urgency to institutional design. Cf. Republic 472d on the painter of the ideal state, and Statesman 293e-294a on the limitations of written law that cannot adapt to changing circumstances.

D: Original: And is not the aim of the legislator similar? First, he desires that his laws should be written down with all possible exactness; in the second place, as time goes on and he has made an actual trial of his decrees, will he not find omissions? Do you imagine that there ever was a legislator so foolish as not to know that many things are necessarily omitted, which some one coming after him must correct, if the constitution and the order of government is not to deteriorate, but to improve in the state which he has established?
D: Narrative: "And is not the aim of the legislator similar? First, he desires that his laws should be written down with all possible exactness; in the second place, as time goes on and he has made an actual trial of his decrees, will he not find omissions? Do you imagine that there ever was a legislator so foolish as not to know that many things are necessarily omitted, which some one coming after him must correct, if the constitution and the order of government is not to deteriorate, but to improve in the state which he has established?"
D: Refashioned: "Is not the legislator's aim similar to the painter's? First, he wants his laws written down with the greatest possible precision. But second, as time passes and his decrees are tested in practice, will he not inevitably discover omissions? Can you imagine a legislator so foolish as to believe his code is complete -- not knowing that many things are necessarily left out, and that someone after him must correct these gaps if the constitution and political order are to improve rather than deteriorate?"
D: Elegant: The legislator, like the painter, first aims for precision but inevitably discovers omissions through practical experience. No legislator can foresee everything; successors must correct gaps to prevent constitutional deterioration. Laws, like paintings, require ongoing revision.
D: Summary: The legislator's situation parallels the painter's: laws are written with maximum precision but inevitably prove incomplete when tested in practice. Successors must correct these omissions to prevent constitutional decline.
D: Distilled: No legislator's code is ever complete -- practical experience reveals inevitable omissions that successors must correct to prevent constitutional deterioration.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage marks a significant departure from the Republic's more static conception of the ideal state. The Laws acknowledges that legislation is inherently incomplete and requires ongoing revision -- a position closer to modern constitutional theory. The emphasis on 'actual trial of his decrees' reflects an empirical, pragmatic epistemology absent from the Republic's more rationalist approach. Cf. Statesman 294a-295b on the inherent limitations of written law, and Republic 425d-427a where Socrates dismisses the need for detailed legislation in the ideal state.

D: Original:
Assuredly, that is the sort of thing which every one would desire.

And if any one possesses any means of accomplishing this by word or deed, or has any way great or small by which he can teach a person to understand how he can maintain and amend the laws, he should finish what he has to say, and not leave the work incomplete.

By all means.

And is not this what you and I have to do at the present moment?

What have we to do?

As we are about to legislate and have chosen our guardians of the law, and are ourselves in the evening of life, and they as compared with us are young men, we ought not only to legislate for them, but to endeavour to make them not only guardians of the law but legislators themselves, as far as this is possible.

Certainly; if we can.

At any rate, we must do our best.

Of course.
D: Narrative:
"Assuredly, that is the sort of thing which every one would desire."

"And if any one possesses any means of accomplishing this by word or deed, or has any way great or small by which he can teach a person to understand how he can maintain and amend the laws, he should finish what he has to say, and not leave the work incomplete."

"By all means."

"And is not this what you and I have to do at the present moment?"

"What have we to do?"

"As we are about to legislate and have chosen our guardians of the law, and are ourselves in the evening of life, and they as compared with us are young men, we ought not only to legislate for them, but to endeavour to make them not only guardians of the law but legislators themselves, as far as this is possible."

"Certainly; if we can."

"At any rate, we must do our best."

"Of course."
D: Refashioned:
"Certainly, every legislator would want that."

"And if anyone has any means -- through word or deed, great or small -- of teaching someone how to maintain and improve the laws, he must complete that teaching and not leave the work unfinished."

"Absolutely."

"And is that not precisely what you and I must do right now?"

"What do you mean?"

"We are about to legislate, and we have established guardians of the law. But we are in the evening of our lives, while the guardians are young by comparison. Therefore we must not merely legislate for them but strive to make them not just guardians of the law but legislators in their own right, to the greatest extent possible."

"Certainly, if we are able."

"At the very least, we must try our best."

"Of course."
D: Elegant: The task is not merely to write laws but to train the guardians of the law to become legislators themselves. Since the founders are old and the guardians young, the transmission of legislative wisdom -- the capacity not just to enforce but to maintain and improve the legal code -- is essential. Teaching successors to be co-creators of the law is the highest duty of the departing generation.
D: Summary: The interlocutors agree that their task extends beyond writing laws: they must train the young guardians of the law to become legislators themselves, transmitting the capacity to maintain and improve the legal code across generations.
D: Distilled: The founders' highest duty is training guardians of the law to become legislators in their own right, ensuring the code can be maintained and improved after the founders are gone.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias (responding), Athenian Stranger (primary). The 'evening of life' metaphor poignantly underscores the intergenerational nature of political founding. The transformation of guardians from mere enforcers into legislators represents the Laws' highest ambition for institutional continuity. This parallels Republic 502c-503a on the philosopher-king's role in training successors, but the Laws' approach is more practical: training within existing legal frameworks rather than through philosophical dialectic. The insistence on completing the teaching -- 'not leave the work incomplete' -- reflects the passage's theme of perpetual institutional refinement.

D: Original: We will say to them-O friends and saviours of our laws, in laying down any law, there are many particulars which we shall omit, and this cannot be helped; at the same time, we will do our utmost to describe what is important, and will give an outline which you shall fill up. And I will explain on what principle you are to act. Megillus and Cleinias and I have often spoken to one another touching these matters, and we are of opinion that we have spoken well. And we hope that you will be of the same mind with us, and become our disciples, and keep in view the things which in our united opinion the legislator and guardian of the law ought to keep in view. There was one main point about which we were agreed-that a man's whole energies throughout life should be devoted to the acquisition of the virtue proper to a man, whether this was to be gained by study, or habit, or some mode of acquisition, or desire, or opinion, or knowledge-and this applies equally to men and women, old and young-the aim of all should always be such as I have described; anything which may be an impediment, the good man ought to show that he utterly disregards. And if at last necessity plainly compels him to be an outlaw from his native land, rather than bow his neck to the yoke of slavery and be ruled by inferiors, and he has to fly, an exile he must be and endure all such trials, rather than accept another form of government, which is likely to make men worse. These are our original principles; and do you now, fixing your eyes upon the standard of what a man and a citizen ought or ought not to be, praise and blame the laws-blame those which have not this power of making the citizen better, but embrace those which have; and with gladness receive and live in them; bidding a long farewell to other institutions which aim at goods, as they are termed, of a different kind.
D: Narrative:
"We will say to them -- O friends and saviours of our laws, in laying down any law, there are many particulars which we shall omit, and this cannot be helped; at the same time, we will do our utmost to describe what is important, and will give an outline which you shall fill up. And I will explain on what principle you are to act.

Megillus and Cleinias and I have often spoken to one another touching these matters, and we are of opinion that we have spoken well. And we hope that you will be of the same mind with us, and become our disciples, and keep in view the things which in our united opinion the legislator and guardian of the law ought to keep in view.

There was one main point about which we were agreed -- that a man's whole energies throughout life should be devoted to the acquisition of the virtue proper to a man, whether this was to be gained by study, or habit, or some mode of acquisition, or desire, or opinion, or knowledge -- and this applies equally to men and women, old and young -- the aim of all should always be such as I have described; anything which may be an impediment, the good man ought to show that he utterly disregards.

And if at last necessity plainly compels him to be an outlaw from his native land, rather than bow his neck to the yoke of slavery and be ruled by inferiors, and he has to fly, an exile he must be and endure all such trials, rather than accept another form of government, which is likely to make men worse.

These are our original principles; and do you now, fixing your eyes upon the standard of what a man and a citizen ought or ought not to be, praise and blame the laws -- blame those which have not this power of making the citizen better, but embrace those which have; and with gladness receive and live in them; bidding a long farewell to other institutions which aim at goods, as they are termed, of a different kind."
D: Refashioned:
"We will address our guardians thus: 'Friends and protectors of our laws, in drafting any law there will be many details we must inevitably omit. Nevertheless, we will do our best to describe what matters most, providing an outline for you to complete. Let me explain the principle by which you should work.

Megillus, Cleinias, and I have discussed these matters extensively, and we believe we have reached sound conclusions. We hope you will share our views and become our students, keeping always in mind what we believe the legislator and guardian of the law should focus on.

Our central agreement was this: a person's entire energy throughout life should be devoted to acquiring virtue -- whether through study, habit, practice, desire, conviction, or knowledge. This applies equally to men and women, old and young. The pursuit of virtue should be everyone's supreme aim, and a good person should show utter contempt for anything that obstructs it.

If necessity ultimately forces a person into exile from his homeland rather than submit to slavery under inferior rulers, then exile he must accept and all its hardships, rather than live under a government that makes people worse.

These are our founding principles. Keep your eyes fixed on the standard of what a person and citizen should be. Praise laws that have the power to make citizens better; condemn those that do not. Embrace the good laws joyfully and live by them, bidding permanent farewell to institutions that pursue so-called goods of a different and lesser kind.'"
D: Elegant: The address to future guardians: the founders' code is inevitably incomplete, requiring the guardians to fill in the outline. The governing principle is singular -- all human energy must be devoted to acquiring virtue, equally for men and women, old and young. Nothing may obstruct this aim; exile is preferable to servitude under a regime that degrades character. Laws should be judged by one standard alone: do they make citizens better?
D: Summary: The Athenian instructs future guardians of the law to fill the gaps in legislation according to one supreme principle: all human energy must pursue virtue, equally for men and women. Exile is preferable to living under a government that corrupts character. Laws should be judged solely by whether they make citizens better.
D: Distilled: The single standard for all legislation is whether it promotes virtue -- exile is preferable to submission to a government that makes people worse.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage articulates the Laws' moral foundation in its most concentrated form. The primacy of virtue echoes Republic 352d-354a (justice as the soul's proper function) and the Apology (38a: the unexamined life is not worth living). The gender equality in the pursuit of virtue ('men and women, old and young') is notable and parallels Republic 451c-457b. The exile-over-degradation principle echoes Socrates' refusal to compromise in the Apology and Crito. The instruction to 'fill up' the legislative outline transforms guardians from passive enforcers into active co-legislators -- the culmination of 6.38-40's painter analogy. Cf. Statesman 309c-311c on the true statesman's commitment to virtue above all.

D: Original: Let us proceed to another class of laws, beginning with their foundation in religion. And we must first return to the number 5040-the entire number had, and has, a great many convenient divisions, and the number of the tribes which was a twelfth part of the whole, being correctly formed by 21 X 20 [5040/(21 X 20), i.e., 5040/420=12], also has them. And not only is the whole number divisible by twelve, but also the number of each tribe is divisible by twelve. Now every portion should be regarded by us as a sacred gift of Heaven, corresponding to the months and to the revolution of the universe. Every city has a guiding and sacred principle given by nature, but in some the division or distribution has been more right than in others, and has been more sacred and fortunate. In our opinion, nothing can be more right than the selection of the number 5040, which may be divided by all numbers from one to twelve with the single exception of eleven, and that admits of a very easy correction; for if, turning to the dividend (5040), we deduct two families, the defect in the division is cured. And the truth of this may be easily proved when we have leisure. But for the present, trusting to the mere assertion of this principle, let us divide the state; and assigning to each portion some God or son of a God, let us give them altars and sacred rites, and at the altars let us hold assemblies for sacrifice twice in the month-twelve assemblies for the tribes, and twelve for the city, according to their divisions; the first in honour of the Gods and divine things, and the second to promote friendship and "better acquaintance," as the phrase is, and every sort of good fellowship with one another. For people must be acquainted with those into whose families and whom they marry and with those to whom they give in marriage; in such matters, as far as possible, a man should deem it all important to avoid a mistake, and with this serious purpose let games be instituted in which youths and maidens shall dance together, seeing one another and being seen naked, at a proper age, and on a suitable occasion, not transgressing the rules of modesty.
D: Narrative:
"Let us proceed to another class of laws, beginning with their foundation in religion. And we must first return to the number 5040 -- the entire number had, and has, a great many convenient divisions, and the number of the tribes which was a twelfth part of the whole, being correctly formed by 21 x 20 (5040/(21 x 20), i.e., 5040/420=12), also has them. And not only is the whole number divisible by twelve, but also the number of each tribe is divisible by twelve.

Now every portion should be regarded by us as a sacred gift of Heaven, corresponding to the months and to the revolution of the universe. Every city has a guiding and sacred principle given by nature, but in some the division or distribution has been more right than in others, and has been more sacred and fortunate. In our opinion, nothing can be more right than the selection of the number 5040, which may be divided by all numbers from one to twelve with the single exception of eleven, and that admits of a very easy correction; for if, turning to the dividend (5040), we deduct two families, the defect in the division is cured. And the truth of this may be easily proved when we have leisure.

But for the present, trusting to the mere assertion of this principle, let us divide the state; and assigning to each portion some God or son of a God, let us give them altars and sacred rites, and at the altars let us hold assemblies for sacrifice twice in the month -- twelve assemblies for the tribes, and twelve for the city, according to their divisions; the first in honour of the Gods and divine things, and the second to promote friendship and 'better acquaintance,' as the phrase is, and every sort of good fellowship with one another.

For people must be acquainted with those into whose families and whom they marry and with those to whom they give in marriage; in such matters, as far as possible, a man should deem it all important to avoid a mistake, and with this serious purpose let games be instituted in which youths and maidens shall dance together, seeing one another and being seen naked, at a proper age, and on a suitable occasion, not transgressing the rules of modesty."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us turn to another category of laws, beginning with their religious foundations. We must first revisit the number 5040, which has an extraordinary number of convenient divisions. The twelve tribes, each being a twelfth of the whole, are correctly formed since 5040 divided by 420 (21 times 20) equals 12. Both the total and each tribal portion are divisible by twelve.

Every numerical portion should be regarded as a sacred gift from Heaven, corresponding to the months and the cosmic cycle. Every city has a guiding sacred principle given by nature, though some cities achieve more fortunate divisions than others. In our view, nothing surpasses the number 5040, which is divisible by every number from one to twelve except eleven -- and that deficiency is easily corrected by subtracting two households. This can be proved at leisure.

For now, accepting this principle, let us divide the state and assign each portion to a god or divine being, establishing altars and sacred rites. At these altars, sacrificial assemblies shall be held twice monthly -- twelve for the tribes and twelve for the city. The first assembly honors the gods and divine things; the second promotes friendship, mutual acquaintance, and fellowship among citizens.

People must know the families into which they marry and to whom they give their children in marriage. Mistakes in such matters must be avoided at all costs. For this serious purpose, festivals with games shall be instituted where young men and women dance together, seeing one another and being seen in natural form, at a proper age and appropriate occasion, without transgressing the rules of modesty."
D: Elegant: The number 5040 is nearly perfect -- divisible by every integer from 1 to 12 except 11, a defect easily corrected. Each numerical portion is a sacred gift mirroring the cosmic order. The state is divided under divine patronage, with twice-monthly assemblies: one for worship, one for social fellowship. Marriage requires mutual knowledge, so festivals with modest but natural displays allow young people to evaluate potential partners. Religion, mathematics, and social life converge in institutional design.
D: Summary: The number 5040, with its extraordinary divisibility, grounds the state's sacred-mathematical order. Each tribal portion has a patron deity with regular assemblies for worship and fellowship. Marriage festivals where youth see each other dancing promote informed mate selection within modesty's bounds.
D: Distilled: The state's sacred order rests on the mathematically perfect number 5040, with twice-monthly assemblies for worship and social fellowship, and festivals for marriage-oriented acquaintance among the young.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The number 5040 (= 7!) is central to the Laws' mathematical-theological framework. Its extraordinary divisibility (59 factors) enables flexible administrative subdivision. The cosmic correspondence -- portions matching months and cosmic revolution -- reflects Pythagorean influence and the Laws' vision of the state as a microcosm. The marriage festivals with modest nudity parallel Republic 452a-e on gymnastic nudity for both sexes. The dual purpose of assemblies (worship + social bonding) reflects the Laws' integration of religion and civic life. The mathematical-theological foundation here contrasts with the Republic's reliance on the Form of the Good (Republic 509b) -- the Laws grounds political order in number and divine patronage rather than metaphysical abstraction.

D: Original: The directors of choruses will be the superintendents and regulators of these games, and they, together with the guardians of the law, will legislate in any matters which we have omitted; for, as we said, where there are numerous and minute details, the legislator must leave out something. And the annual officers who have experience, and know what is wanted, must make arrangements and improvements year by year, until such enactments and provisions are sufficiently determined. A ten years experience of sacrifices and dances, if extending to all particulars, will be quite sufficient; and if the legislator be alive they shall communicate with him, but if he be dead then the several officers shall refer the omissions which come under their notice to the guardians of the law, and correct them, until all is perfect; and from that time there shall be no more change, and they shall establish and use the new laws with the others which the legislator originally gave them, and of which they are never, if they can help, to change aught; or, if some necessity overtakes them, the magistrates must be called into counsel, and the whole people, and they must go to all the oracles of the Gods; and if they are all agreed, in that case they may make the change, but if they are not agreed, by no manner of means, and any one who dissents shall prevail, as the law ordains.
D: Narrative:
"The directors of choruses will be the superintendents and regulators of these games, and they, together with the guardians of the law, will legislate in any matters which we have omitted; for, as we said, where there are numerous and minute details, the legislator must leave out something.

And the annual officers who have experience, and know what is wanted, must make arrangements and improvements year by year, until such enactments and provisions are sufficiently determined. A ten years' experience of sacrifices and dances, if extending to all particulars, will be quite sufficient; and if the legislator be alive they shall communicate with him, but if he be dead then the several officers shall refer the omissions which come under their notice to the guardians of the law, and correct them, until all is perfect; and from that time there shall be no more change, and they shall establish and use the new laws with the others which the legislator originally gave them, and of which they are never, if they can help, to change aught; or, if some necessity overtakes them, the magistrates must be called into counsel, and the whole people, and they must go to all the oracles of the Gods; and if they are all agreed, in that case they may make the change, but if they are not agreed, by no manner of means, and any one who dissents shall prevail, as the law ordains."
D: Refashioned:
"The chorus directors shall supervise and regulate these games. Together with the guardians of the law, they shall legislate on matters we have omitted -- for as we acknowledged, the legislator inevitably leaves out numerous small details.

Annual officers with practical experience shall make adjustments and improvements year by year until these provisions are thoroughly refined. Ten years of experience with sacrifices and dances, attending to every detail, should be quite sufficient. If the original legislator is still alive, they shall consult him. If he has died, the officers shall report any gaps they discover to the guardians of the law, who shall correct them until the code is perfected.

From that point forward, no further changes shall be made. The refined laws shall stand alongside the original legislation, which should never be altered if it can be helped. Should genuine necessity require a change, all magistrates must be consulted, along with the entire populace, and all the oracles of the gods must be sought. Only if everyone agrees may the change be made. If there is any dissent whatsoever, the proposed change shall not proceed -- the dissenting voice prevails, as the law ordains."
D: Elegant: Chorus directors and guardians of the law fill legislative gaps through ten years of practical refinement, consulting the original legislator if alive. After this period, the code is fixed. Future changes require unanimous agreement of magistrates, the people, and the divine oracles. Any single dissent blocks change. The law favors stability over innovation, with an extraordinarily high bar for amendment.
D: Summary: Chorus directors and guardians fill legislative gaps over a ten-year refinement period. After that, the legal code becomes fixed. Any future change requires unanimous consent of magistrates, the people, and divine oracles -- a single dissent blocks amendment.
D: Distilled: After ten years of practical refinement, the legal code becomes fixed, with future amendments requiring unanimous agreement of magistrates, people, and oracles.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The ten-year refinement period reflects a pragmatic approach: laws are tested and adjusted through experience before becoming permanent. The extremely conservative amendment process -- requiring unanimity of magistrates, people, and oracles, with any dissent blocking change -- creates an almost unalterable legal code. This reflects Plato's deep suspicion of innovation (kainotomia) in political matters, expressed also in Republic 424b-c (no changes in music or gymnastics). The requirement to consult 'all the oracles' elevates divine authority above human political will. Cf. Crito 50a-54d on the authority of the laws and the danger of individual citizens undermining them.

D: Original:
Whenever any one over twenty-five years of age, having seen and been seen by others, believes himself to have found a marriage connection which is to his mind, and suitable for the procreation of children, let him marry if he be still under the age of five-and-thirty years; but let him first hear how he ought to seek after what is suitable and appropriate. For, as Cleinias says, every law should have a suitable prelude.

You recollect at the right moment, Stranger, and do not miss the opportunity which the argument affords of saying a word in season.
D: Narrative:
"Whenever any one over twenty-five years of age, having seen and been seen by others, believes himself to have found a marriage connection which is to his mind, and suitable for the procreation of children, let him marry if he be still under the age of five-and-thirty years; but let him first hear how he ought to seek after what is suitable and appropriate. For, as Cleinias says, every law should have a suitable prelude."

"You recollect at the right moment, Stranger, and do not miss the opportunity which the argument affords of saying a word in season."
D: Refashioned:
"When any man over twenty-five, having observed others and been observed by them in return, believes he has found a suitable marriage partner for the procreation of children, let him marry, provided he is still under thirty-five. But first he should hear guidance on how to seek what is truly suitable and appropriate. For as Cleinias reminds us, every law deserves a fitting prelude."

"You recall that at just the right moment, Stranger, and you take the opportunity the argument offers to say exactly what is needed."
D: Elegant: Men between twenty-five and thirty-five who have found a suitable partner through mutual acquaintance should marry. But before the marriage law itself, a prelude is needed -- guidance on what makes a marriage truly suitable. Every law deserves its proper introduction.
D: Summary: Marriage should occur between ages twenty-five and thirty-five, after a period of mutual observation. Before stating the marriage law, the Athenian announces a prelude on what constitutes a suitable match, following the principle that every law deserves a fitting introduction.
D: Distilled: Marriage between twenty-five and thirty-five follows a period of mutual acquaintance, and the law requires a prelude explaining what makes a match truly suitable.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias (responding). The marriage age range (25-35 for men) reflects Greek norms, though the lower bound is later than typical Athenian practice (where men often married around 30, women around 15). The 'prelude' (prooimion) concept is one of the Laws' distinctive innovations -- the idea that laws should persuade as well as command, with philosophical justifications preceding prescriptions. This was introduced in Laws Book 4 (722d-723d). Cleinias's praise of the prelude concept reflects his growing philosophical sophistication. The transition from festivals and acquaintance (6.42) to marriage proper shows careful legislative sequencing.

D: Original: I thank you. We will say to him who is born of good parents-O my son, you ought to make such a marriage as wise men would approve. Now they would advise you neither to avoid a poor marriage, nor specially to desire a rich one; but if other things are equal, always to honour inferiors, and with them to form connections;-this will be for the benefit of the city and of the families which are united; for the equable and symmetrical tends infinitely more to virtue than the unmixed. And he who is conscious of being too headstrong, and carried away more than is fitting in all his actions, ought to desire to become the relation of orderly parents; and he who is of the opposite temper ought to seek the opposite alliance. Let there be one word concerning all marriages:-Every man shall follow, not after the marriage which is most pleasing to himself, but after that which is most beneficial to the state. For somehow every one is by nature prone to that which is likest to himself, and in this way the whole city becomes unequal in property and in disposition; and hence there arise in most states the very results which we least desire to happen. Now, to add to the law an express provision, not only that the rich man shall not marry into the rich family, nor the powerful into the family of the powerful, but that the slower natures shall be compelled to enter into marriage with the quicker, and the quicker with the slower, may awaken anger as well as laughter in the minds of many; for there is a difficulty in perceiving that the city ought to be well mingled like a cup, in which the maddening wine is hot and fiery, but when chastened by a soberer God, receives a fair associate and becomes an excellent and temperate drink. Yet in marriage no one is able to see that the same result occurs. Wherefore also the law must let alone such matters, but we should try to charm the spirits of men into believing the equability of their children's disposition to be of more importance than equality in excessive fortune when they marry; and him who is too desirous of making a rich marriage we should endeavour to turn aside by reproaches, not, however, by any compulsion of written law.
D: Narrative:
"I thank you. We will say to him who is born of good parents -- O my son, you ought to make such a marriage as wise men would approve. Now they would advise you neither to avoid a poor marriage, nor specially to desire a rich one; but if other things are equal, always to honour inferiors, and with them to form connections -- this will be for the benefit of the city and of the families which are united; for the equable and symmetrical tends infinitely more to virtue than the unmixed.

And he who is conscious of being too headstrong, and carried away more than is fitting in all his actions, ought to desire to become the relation of orderly parents; and he who is of the opposite temper ought to seek the opposite alliance. Let there be one word concerning all marriages: Every man shall follow, not after the marriage which is most pleasing to himself, but after that which is most beneficial to the state.

For somehow every one is by nature prone to that which is likest to himself, and in this way the whole city becomes unequal in property and in disposition; and hence there arise in most states the very results which we least desire to happen.

Now, to add to the law an express provision, not only that the rich man shall not marry into the rich family, nor the powerful into the family of the powerful, but that the slower natures shall be compelled to enter into marriage with the quicker, and the quicker with the slower, may awaken anger as well as laughter in the minds of many; for there is a difficulty in perceiving that the city ought to be well mingled like a cup, in which the maddening wine is hot and fiery, but when chastened by a soberer God, receives a fair associate and becomes an excellent and temperate drink.

Yet in marriage no one is able to see that the same result occurs. Wherefore also the law must let alone such matters, but we should try to charm the spirits of men into believing the equability of their children's disposition to be of more importance than equality in excessive fortune when they marry; and him who is too desirous of making a rich marriage we should endeavour to turn aside by reproaches, not, however, by any compulsion of written law."
D: Refashioned:
"Thank you. The prelude to marriage law shall be this: 'Son of good parents, you should make the kind of marriage that wise people would approve. They would advise you neither to shun a match with someone of modest means nor to actively pursue wealth. All else being equal, you should always prefer to marry into a family of lower standing -- this benefits both the city and the united families, since balanced and blended unions tend far more toward virtue than unmixed ones.

If you know yourself to be headstrong and impulsive in your conduct, you should seek marriage with an orderly, temperate family. If you are naturally reserved and slow, seek the opposite. One principle governs all marriages: every man should pursue not the marriage most pleasing to himself but the one most beneficial to the state.

Human nature inclines everyone toward what resembles themselves, and this tendency creates ever-widening inequalities in both wealth and temperament across the city -- producing precisely the outcomes we most wish to avoid.

Now, to legislate explicitly that the rich must not marry the rich, the powerful not the powerful, and that slow temperaments must be legally compelled to marry quick ones and vice versa -- this would provoke both outrage and ridicule in many people. It is difficult to see that a city, like a cup of wine, must be properly blended: the hot, fiery wine, tempered by a soberer element, becomes an excellent and balanced drink.

Yet when it comes to marriage, people cannot perceive this same principle at work. Therefore the law should not compel such mixing directly. Instead, we should use persuasion to charm people into valuing balanced temperament in their children above accumulation of excessive wealth through marriage. Those too eager for rich marriages should be turned aside through social disapproval, not through legislative force.'"
D: Elegant: The marriage prelude: marry for the city's benefit, not personal preference. Prefer matches with families of lower station, since blended unions produce greater virtue than concentrated ones. The headstrong should marry the temperate, and vice versa. Like wine that needs a sobering element to become an excellent drink, the city needs mixed marriages to prevent the concentration of wealth and character extremes. But this mixing should be encouraged through persuasion and social pressure, not compelled by law.
D: Summary: The marriage prelude advises choosing partners for the city's benefit over personal preference, favoring marriages that blend opposite temperaments and different wealth levels. Like wine needing a sobering element, the city needs mixed marriages. However, this should be achieved through persuasion rather than legal compulsion.
D: Distilled: Marriage should blend opposite temperaments and wealth levels for the city's benefit -- like wine needing a tempering element -- but through persuasion rather than legal force.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The wine-mixing metaphor (krasis) is one of Plato's most vivid images for political moderation, recalling Statesman 306a-311c on the weaving together of opposite temperaments. The distinction between persuasion and compulsion in marriage law exemplifies the Laws' prelude doctrine: where direct legislation would provoke resistance, the legislator uses rhetoric and social norms. This contrasts sharply with Republic 458c-461e, where mating is directly controlled by rulers through a 'noble lie.' The Laws' approach is more realistic and less authoritarian. The principle that like marries like, producing civic inequality, anticipates modern concerns about assortative mating.

D: Original: Let this then be our exhortation concerning marriage, and let us remember what was said before-that a man should cling to immortality, and leave behind him children's children to be the servants of God in his place for ever. All this and much more may be truly said by way of prelude about the duty of marriage.
D: Narrative: "Let this then be our exhortation concerning marriage, and let us remember what was said before -- that a man should cling to immortality, and leave behind him children's children to be the servants of God in his place for ever. All this and much more may be truly said by way of prelude about the duty of marriage."
D: Refashioned: "Let this be our exhortation regarding marriage. And let us recall what was said earlier: that a person should grasp at immortality by leaving behind children and grandchildren who will serve God in his place forever. All this, and much more besides, can be truly said as a prelude about the duty of marriage."
D: Elegant: Marriage is a duty through which humans grasp at immortality, leaving behind generations who continue to serve the divine. This is the essence of the marriage prelude.
D: Summary: The marriage prelude concludes with the reminder that marriage is a sacred duty through which humans achieve a kind of immortality, leaving behind descendants who serve God in perpetuity.
D: Distilled: Marriage is the means by which mortals achieve immortality, leaving behind descendants who serve God in their place forever.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The concept of biological immortality through offspring connects to Symposium 207a-208b, where Diotima explains that mortal beings participate in immortality through reproduction. The phrase 'servants of God' elevates procreation from a biological to a theological duty -- children continue their parents' divine service. This theological framing of marriage as duty is characteristic of the Laws' religious orientation, absent from the Republic's more philosophical treatment of mating (Republic 458c-461e). The brevity of this passage signals it as a closing summary of the longer prelude in 6.45.

D: Original: But if a man will not listen and remains unsocial and alien among his fellow-citizens, and is still unmarried at thirty-five years of age, let him pay a yearly fine;-he who of the highest class shall pay a fine of a hundred drachmae, and he who is of the second dass a fine of seventy drachmae; the third class shall pay sixty drachmae, and the fourth thirty drachmae, and let the money be sacred to Here; he who does not pay the fine annually shall owe ten times the sum, which the treasurer of the goddess shall exact; and if he fails in doing so, let him be answerable and give an account of the. money at his audit. He who refuses to marry shall be thus punished in money, and also be deprived of all honour which the younger show to the elder; let no young man voluntarily obey him, and if he attempt to punish any one, let every one come to the rescue and defend the injured person, and he who is present and does not come to the rescue, shall be pronounced by the law to be a coward and a bad citizen. Of the marriage portion I have already spoken; and again I say for the instruction of poor men that he who neither gives nor receives a dowry on account of poverty, has a compensation; for the citizens of our state are provided with the necessaries of life, and wives will be less likely to be insolent, and husbands to be mean and subservient to them on account of property. And he who obeys this law will do a noble action; but he who will not obey, and gives or receives more than fifty drachmae as the price of the marriage garments if he be of the lowest, or more than a mina, or a mina and-a-half, if he be of the third or second classes, or two minae if he be of the highest class, shall owe to the public treasury a similar sum, and that which is given or received shall be sacred to Here and Zeus; and let the treasurers of these Gods exact the money, as was said before about the unmarried-that the treasurers of Here were to exact the money, or pay the fine themselves.
D: Narrative:
"But if a man will not listen and remains unsocial and alien among his fellow-citizens, and is still unmarried at thirty-five years of age, let him pay a yearly fine -- he who of the highest class shall pay a fine of a hundred drachmae, and he who is of the second class a fine of seventy drachmae; the third class shall pay sixty drachmae, and the fourth thirty drachmae, and let the money be sacred to Hera; he who does not pay the fine annually shall owe ten times the sum, which the treasurer of the goddess shall exact; and if he fails in doing so, let him be answerable and give an account of the money at his audit.

He who refuses to marry shall be thus punished in money, and also be deprived of all honour which the younger show to the elder; let no young man voluntarily obey him, and if he attempt to punish any one, let every one come to the rescue and defend the injured person, and he who is present and does not come to the rescue, shall be pronounced by the law to be a coward and a bad citizen.

Of the marriage portion I have already spoken; and again I say for the instruction of poor men that he who neither gives nor receives a dowry on account of poverty, has a compensation; for the citizens of our state are provided with the necessaries of life, and wives will be less likely to be insolent, and husbands to be mean and subservient to them on account of property.

And he who obeys this law will do a noble action; but he who will not obey, and gives or receives more than fifty drachmae as the price of the marriage garments if he be of the lowest, or more than a mina, or a mina and-a-half, if he be of the third or second classes, or two minae if he be of the highest class, shall owe to the public treasury a similar sum, and that which is given or received shall be sacred to Hera and Zeus; and let the treasurers of these Gods exact the money, as was said before about the unmarried -- that the treasurers of Hera were to exact the money, or pay the fine themselves."
D: Refashioned:
"But if a man refuses to listen and remains unsocial and alienated from his fellow citizens, still unmarried at thirty-five, he shall pay an annual fine: one hundred drachmae for the highest property class, seventy for the second, sixty for the third, and thirty for the fourth. This money shall be sacred to Hera. Anyone who fails to pay annually shall owe ten times the amount, to be collected by Hera's treasurer; if the treasurer fails to collect, he must account for the shortfall at his audit.

The man who refuses to marry shall suffer not only monetary penalties but also social consequences: he shall lose all the respect that younger citizens normally show to their elders. No young person shall voluntarily obey him. If such a man tries to discipline anyone, everyone present must come to the other person's defense. Anyone who witnesses this and fails to intervene shall be legally declared a coward and bad citizen.

Regarding dowries, I have spoken before. Let me restate for the benefit of those with modest means: a man who neither gives nor receives a dowry because of poverty suffers no real loss, since our state provides all citizens with life's necessities. Moreover, without large dowries, wives are less likely to become arrogant and husbands less likely to become servile over money matters.

Obeying this dowry law is a noble act. But anyone who disobeys -- giving or receiving more than fifty drachmae for marriage garments if he is of the lowest class, or more than a mina to a mina and a half for the middle classes, or more than two minae for the highest class -- shall owe the public treasury an equal sum. The excess dowry shall be dedicated to Hera and Zeus, and their treasurers shall collect it, or face the consequences themselves."
D: Elegant: Bachelors at thirty-five face graduated fines sacred to Hera, with penalties multiplied tenfold for non-payment. Beyond money, the unmarried man loses all social authority: the young need not respect or obey him, and anyone who defends him against this treatment is declared a bad citizen. Dowries are capped by class to prevent wealth from distorting marriages -- the state provides necessities, making large dowries unnecessary and their social effects (wifely arrogance, husbandly servility) undesirable.
D: Summary: Men unmarried at thirty-five face graduated fines and loss of social authority -- the young need not respect or obey them. Dowries are capped by property class, since the state provides necessities and large dowries distort marital relationships through arrogance and servility.
D: Distilled: Bachelors face escalating fines and complete loss of social authority, while dowry caps prevent wealth from corrupting marriage relationships.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The penalties for bachelorhood are exceptionally severe: not just fines but total loss of social standing, including the right to discipline the young. This reflects the Laws' view of marriage as a civic duty, not a personal choice. The dedication of fines to Hera (goddess of marriage) gives enforcement a sacred dimension. The dowry caps are remarkably egalitarian, explicitly designed to prevent wealth-based power dynamics within marriage. The observation that large dowries make 'wives insolent and husbands mean and subservient' shows psychological insight into how money distorts intimate relationships. Cf. Republic 464b-e on the abolition of private property and family among guardians -- the Laws preserves marriage and property but regulates both carefully.

D: Original: The betrothal by a father shall be valid in the first degree, that by a grandfather in the second degree, and in the third degree, betrothal by brothers who have the same father; but if there are none of these alive, the betrothal by a mother shall be valid in like manner; in cases of unexampled fatality, the next of kin and the guardians shall have authority. What are to be the rites before marriages, or any other sacred acts, relating either to future, present, or past marriages, shall be referred to the interpreters; and he who follows their advice may be satisfied. Touching the marriage festival, they shall assemble not more than five male and five female friends of both families; and a like number of members of the family of either sex, and no man shall spend more than his means will allow; he who is of the richest class may spend a mina-he who is of the second, half a mina, and in the same proportion as the census of each decreases: all men shall praise him who is obedient to the law; but he who is disobedient shall be punished by the guardians of the law as a man wanting in true taste, and uninstructed in the laws of bridal song. Drunkenness is always improper, except at the festivals of the God who gave wine; and peculiarly dangerous, when a man is engaged in the business of marriage; at such a crisis of their lives a bride and bridegroom ought to have all their wits about them-they ought to take care that their offspring may be born of reasonable beings; for on what day or night Heaven will give them increase, who can say? Moreover, they ought not to begetting children when their bodies are dissipated by intoxication, but their offspring should be compact and solid, quiet and compounded properly; whereas the drunkard is all abroad in all his actions, and beside himself both in body and soul.
D: Narrative:
"The betrothal by a father shall be valid in the first degree, that by a grandfather in the second degree, and in the third degree, betrothal by brothers who have the same father; but if there are none of these alive, the betrothal by a mother shall be valid in like manner; in cases of unexampled fatality, the next of kin and the guardians shall have authority.

What are to be the rites before marriages, or any other sacred acts, relating either to future, present, or past marriages, shall be referred to the interpreters; and he who follows their advice may be satisfied.

Touching the marriage festival, they shall assemble not more than five male and five female friends of both families; and a like number of members of the family of either sex, and no man shall spend more than his means will allow; he who is of the richest class may spend a mina -- he who is of the second, half a mina, and in the same proportion as the census of each decreases: all men shall praise him who is obedient to the law; but he who is disobedient shall be punished by the guardians of the law as a man wanting in true taste, and uninstructed in the laws of bridal song.

Drunkenness is always improper, except at the festivals of the God who gave wine; and peculiarly dangerous, when a man is engaged in the business of marriage; at such a crisis of their lives a bride and bridegroom ought to have all their wits about them -- they ought to take care that their offspring may be born of reasonable beings; for on what day or night Heaven will give them increase, who can say? Moreover, they ought not to be begetting children when their bodies are dissipated by intoxication, but their offspring should be compact and solid, quiet and compounded properly; whereas the drunkard is all abroad in all his actions, and beside himself both in body and soul."
D: Refashioned:
"Betrothal authority follows this hierarchy: first the father, then the grandfather, then brothers sharing the same father. If none of these survive, the mother's betrothal is equally valid. In cases of extraordinary misfortune where even she is absent, the next of kin and the guardians shall have authority.

All religious rites pertaining to marriages -- whether future, present, or past -- shall be determined by the interpreters. Anyone who follows their guidance may rest assured.

Regarding the wedding celebration: no more than five male and five female friends from each family shall attend, along with an equal number of family members of each sex. Spending must not exceed one's means: the richest class may spend up to one mina, the second class half a mina, and so on in proportion to each census bracket. Society shall praise those who observe this law, and the guardians of the law shall punish the disobedient as persons of poor taste who are ignorant of proper wedding tradition.

Drunkenness is always improper except at festivals of the god who gave wine, and it is especially dangerous during marriage. At this critical moment in their lives, bride and bridegroom must have their full faculties. They should ensure that their offspring are born of sober, reasonable parents -- for who can predict on which day or night Heaven will grant them a child? Children should not be conceived when the parents' bodies are corrupted by intoxication. Offspring should be sound, stable, and properly formed, whereas the drunkard is unstable in every action, beside himself in both body and soul."
D: Elegant: Betrothal authority descends through father, grandfather, brothers, then mother, with kinsmen and guardians as last resort. Wedding rites follow the interpreters' guidance. Wedding celebrations are limited in size and spending proportional to class. Drunkenness at weddings is especially condemned: children should be conceived by sober, clear-headed parents, since intoxication produces unstable offspring. The drunkard is disordered in body and soul alike.
D: Summary: Betrothal authority follows a family hierarchy from father through kinsmen. Weddings are capped in both attendance and spending by class. Drunkenness at weddings is condemned because children should be conceived by sober parents -- intoxication produces disordered offspring.
D: Distilled: Betrothal follows family hierarchy, weddings are regulated in size and cost, and sobriety at marriage is essential because conception by intoxicated parents produces disordered children.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The betrothal hierarchy reflects standard Greek patriarchal practice, with the notable inclusion of the mother's authority in the absence of male relatives. The spending limits reinforce the anti-luxury ethic pervading the Laws' marriage legislation. The concern with sobriety at conception reflects Greek beliefs about prenatal influence (cf. Aristotle, Politics VII.16, 1335b) and the Laws' broader project of regulating private life for civic benefit. The exception for festivals of Dionysus acknowledges the god of wine's domain while restricting intoxication elsewhere. Cf. Laws Book 1's discussion of the proper use of wine (637a-650b).

D: Original: Wherefore, also, the drunken man is bad and unsteady in sowing the seed of increase, and is likely to beget offspring who will be unstable and untrustworthy, and cannot be expected to walk straight either in body or mind. Hence during the whole year and all his life long, and especially while he is begetting children, ought to take care and not intentionally do what is injurious to health, or what involves insolence and wrong; for he cannot help leaving the impression of himself on the souls and bodies of his offspring, and he begets children in every way inferior. And especially on the day and night of marriage should a man abstain from such things. For the beginning, which is also a God dwelling in man, preserves all things, if it meet with proper respect from each individual. He who marries is further to consider that one of the two houses in the lot is the nest and nursery of his young, and there he is to marry and make a home for himself and bring up his children, going away from his father and mother. For in friendships there must be some degree of desire, in order to cement and bind together diversities of character; but excessive intercourse not having the desire which is created by time, insensibly dissolves friendships from a feeling of satiety; wherefore a man and his wife shall leave to his and her father and mother their own dwelling-places, and themselves go as to a colony and dwell there, and visit and be visited by their parents; and they shall beget and bring up children, handing on the torch of life from one generation to another, and worshipping the Gods according to law for ever.
D: Narrative:
"Wherefore, also, the drunken man is bad and unsteady in sowing the seed of increase, and is likely to beget offspring who will be unstable and untrustworthy, and cannot be expected to walk straight either in body or mind. Hence during the whole year and all his life long, and especially while he is begetting children, he ought to take care and not intentionally do what is injurious to health, or what involves insolence and wrong; for he cannot help leaving the impression of himself on the souls and bodies of his offspring, and he begets children in every way inferior.

And especially on the day and night of marriage should a man abstain from such things. For the beginning, which is also a God dwelling in man, preserves all things, if it meet with proper respect from each individual.

He who marries is further to consider that one of the two houses in the lot is the nest and nursery of his young, and there he is to marry and make a home for himself and bring up his children, going away from his father and mother. For in friendships there must be some degree of desire, in order to cement and bind together diversities of character; but excessive intercourse not having the desire which is created by time, insensibly dissolves friendships from a feeling of satiety; wherefore a man and his wife shall leave to his and her father and mother their own dwelling-places, and themselves go as to a colony and dwell there, and visit and be visited by their parents; and they shall beget and bring up children, handing on the torch of life from one generation to another, and worshipping the Gods according to law for ever."
D: Refashioned:
"A drunkard is unreliable in the act of procreation and will likely produce offspring who are unstable and untrustworthy, unable to go straight in either body or mind. Therefore, throughout the entire year and indeed his whole life -- but especially during the period of begetting children -- a man must take care not to do anything intentionally harmful to health or involving reckless behavior. He inevitably stamps his own image on his children's souls and bodies, producing inferior offspring through his own vice.

Above all, on the wedding day and night itself, a man must abstain from such conduct. The beginning is itself a god dwelling within human beings, and it preserves all things when given proper reverence.

The married man should also understand that one of the two houses in his land allotment is to serve as the nest and nursery for his children. There he shall establish his household and raise his family, having left his father's and mother's home. In friendships, some degree of longing is necessary to bind together different characters; but excessive proximity, lacking the desire that only absence creates, gradually dissolves affection through satiety. Therefore husband and wife shall leave their parents' dwellings and go to their own home as if to a new colony. They shall visit their parents and be visited in return, begetting and raising children, passing the torch of life from generation to generation, and worshipping the gods according to law forever."
D: Elegant: The drunkard stamps his disorder on his children's bodies and souls. The beginning is sacred -- a god dwelling in human beings -- and must be treated with reverence.
