In every joint undertaking, people succeed when they are attentive and focused, but fail when they are careless or mindless. Therefore the bridegroom should devote his full attention to his bride and to the begetting of children, and the bride should devote equal attention to the bridegroom -- especially during the period before their children are born.

The women we have selected as overseers shall supervise these matters. In whatever number and at whatever times the magistrates command, they shall assemble daily in the temple of Eileithyia for a third of the day. There they shall share reports about anyone -- man or woman -- among those who are begetting children who appears to be disregarding the ordinances established during the wedding sacrifices and ceremonies.

The supervised period of procreation shall last ten years and no longer -- the period during which marriage is naturally fertile. If a couple remains childless after this time, they shall consult with their relatives and the women overseers and divorce for their mutual benefit.

If any dispute arises about what is proper or beneficial for either party, they shall select ten of the guardians of the law to decide, and shall abide by their ruling.

The women overseers shall have the authority to enter the homes of young couples and, through a combination of encouragement and warnings, correct any foolish or improper behavior. If the couple persists, the women shall report to the guardians of the law, who shall intervene."
D: Elegant: Marriage is a civic duty: bride and bridegroom must focus on producing the state's best children. Female overseers, meeting daily at Eileithyia's temple, monitor compliance with procreative ordinances for ten years. Childless couples divorce for mutual benefit after this period. The overseers may enter homes to correct behavior through admonition and threat, escalating to the guardians of the law if needed. Reproduction is too important to be left to private discretion.
D: Summary: Couples must focus on producing the best children for the state. Female overseers monitor procreative conduct for ten years, meeting daily at Eileithyia's temple. Childless couples divorce after this period. Overseers may enter homes to correct improper behavior, escalating to guardians of the law if needed.
D: Distilled: Female overseers monitor married couples' procreative conduct for ten years, with authority to enter homes, correct behavior, and mandate divorce for persistent childlessness.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This passage represents the Laws' most intrusive regulation of private life. Female overseers (a remarkable institution) monitor procreation, enter homes, and report violations -- a level of state intervention in intimate life that exceeds even the Republic's breeding program (Republic 459d-461e), which at least operated through deception rather than surveillance. The temple of Eileithyia (goddess of childbirth) sacralizes the oversight. The ten-year fertility window with mandatory divorce for childlessness treats reproduction as a civic obligation with a statute of limitations. The female overseers represent one of the Laws' few female officeholding positions. Cf. Statesman 310b on the statesman's role in directing marriages.

D: Original: But if they too cannot prevent them, they shall bring the matter before the people; and let them write up their names and make oath that they cannot reform such and such an one; and let him who is thus written up, if he cannot in a court of law convict those who have inscribed his name, be deprived of the privileges of a citizen in the following respects:-let him not go to weddings nor to the thanksgivings after the birth of children; and if he go, let any one who pleases strike him with impunity; and let the same regulations hold about women: let not a woman be allowed to appear abroad, or receive honour, or go to nuptial and birthday festivals, if she in like manner be written up as acting disorderly and cannot obtain a verdict. And if, when they themselves have done begetting children according to the law, a man or woman have connection with another man or woman who are still begetting children, let the same penalties be inflicted upon them as upon those who are still having a family; and when the time for procreation has passed let the man or woman who refrains in such matters be held in esteem, and let those who do not refrain be held in the contrary of esteem-that is to say, disesteem. Now, if the greater part of mankind behave modestly, the enactments of law may be left to slumber; but, if they are disorderly, the enactments having been passed, let them be carried into execution. To every man the first year is the beginning of life, and the time of birth ought to be written down in the temples of their fathers as the beginning of existence to every child, whether boy or girl. Let every phratria have inscribed on a whited wall the names of the successive archons by whom the years are reckoned. And near to them let the living members of the phratria be inscribed, and when they depart life let them be erased.
D: Narrative:
"But if they too cannot prevent them, they shall bring the matter before the people; and let them write up their names and make oath that they cannot reform such and such an one; and let him who is thus written up, if he cannot in a court of law convict those who have inscribed his name, be deprived of the privileges of a citizen in the following respects: let him not go to weddings nor to the thanksgivings after the birth of children; and if he go, let any one who pleases strike him with impunity; and let the same regulations hold about women: let not a woman be allowed to appear abroad, or receive honour, or go to nuptial and birthday festivals, if she in like manner be written up as acting disorderly and cannot obtain a verdict.

And if, when they themselves have done begetting children according to the law, a man or woman have connection with another man or woman who are still begetting children, let the same penalties be inflicted upon them as upon those who are still having a family; and when the time for procreation has passed let the man or woman who refrains in such matters be held in esteem, and let those who do not refrain be held in the contrary of esteem -- that is to say, disesteem.

Now, if the greater part of mankind behave modestly, the enactments of law may be left to slumber; but, if they are disorderly, the enactments having been passed, let them be carried into execution.

To every man the first year is the beginning of life, and the time of birth ought to be written down in the temples of their fathers as the beginning of existence to every child, whether boy or girl. Let every phratria have inscribed on a whited wall the names of the successive archons by whom the years are reckoned. And near to them let the living members of the phratria be inscribed, and when they depart life let them be erased."
D: Refashioned:
"But if even the guardians of the law cannot correct them, they shall bring the matter before the public. The guardians shall post the offender's name and swear under oath that they were unable to reform the person. Anyone thus publicly named, unless they can successfully prosecute those who inscribed their name, shall lose certain citizen privileges: they may not attend weddings or celebrations after the birth of children. If they appear at such events, anyone may strike them without legal consequence.

The same rules apply to women: a woman publicly listed as disorderly, who cannot secure a verdict in her favor, shall not be permitted to appear in public, receive honors, or attend wedding or birthday celebrations.

If anyone who has already completed their own childbearing has sexual relations with someone still in their procreative period, they shall face the same penalties as those still actively begetting children who violate the rules. Once the procreative period has passed, those who exercise restraint shall be held in esteem; those who do not shall be held in disesteem.

If the majority of citizens behave with propriety, these laws may remain dormant. But if there is widespread disorder, they must be fully enforced.

Every person's first year is the beginning of their life, and the date of birth should be recorded in the ancestral temple for every child, boy or girl, as the commencement of their existence. Every phratria shall maintain a whitened wall inscribed with the names of the successive archons who mark the years. Beside these, the names of living members shall be recorded, and when they die, their names shall be erased."
D: Elegant: Those who defy procreative regulations face public shaming and loss of civic privileges -- exclusion from weddings and birth celebrations, with physical punishment for trespassing. Post-procreative adults who interfere with others still begetting face the same penalties. Restraint after one's own period earns esteem; lack of restraint earns disgrace. These laws remain dormant if citizens behave, but activate when disorder arises. Every birth is recorded in ancestral temples, and each phratria maintains a living register on whitened walls, adding names at birth and erasing them at death.
D: Summary: Citizens who violate procreative regulations face public naming, loss of civic privileges, and exclusion from celebrations. Post-procreative interference with others brings the same penalties. These laws lie dormant unless disorder requires their enforcement. Births are recorded in temples, and each phratria maintains a living register on whitened walls.
D: Distilled: Procreative violations bring public shaming and civic exclusion, laws remain dormant unless disorder demands enforcement, and births are permanently recorded in ancestral temples.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. The enforcement mechanism is layered: overseers admonish, guardians intervene, and ultimately public shaming strips civic privileges. The 'sleeping law' concept -- enactments that exist but remain dormant unless needed -- is a sophisticated legislative tool, acknowledging that most regulation should be self-enforcing through social norms. The birth registry on whitened walls creates a visible, public record of civic membership, connecting individual existence to the communal phratria. The erasure at death poignantly mirrors the addition at birth. Cf. Republic 460c-461e on the Republic's more covert management of reproduction. The Laws' approach is more transparent but also more invasive in its mechanisms of social control.

D: Original: The limit of marriageable ages for a woman shall be from sixteen to twenty years at the longest-for a man, from thirty to thirty-five years; and let a woman hold office at forty, and a man at thirty years. Let a man go out to war from twenty to sixty years, and for a woman, if there appear any need to make use of her in military service, let the time of service be after she shall have brought forth children up to fifty years of age; and let regard be had to what is possible and suitable to each.
D: Narrative: "The limit of marriageable ages for a woman shall be from sixteen to twenty years at the longest -- for a man, from thirty to thirty-five years; and let a woman hold office at forty, and a man at thirty years. Let a man go out to war from twenty to sixty years, and for a woman, if there appear any need to make use of her in military service, let the time of service be after she shall have brought forth children up to fifty years of age; and let regard be had to what is possible and suitable to each."
D: Refashioned: "The marriageable age for women shall be from sixteen to twenty at the latest. For men, from thirty to thirty-five. Women may hold office beginning at forty, men at thirty. Military service for men extends from age twenty to sixty. If circumstances require women's military service, they shall serve after completing childbearing, up to the age of fifty. In all cases, what is possible and suitable for each individual should be the guiding consideration."
D: Elegant: Marriage ages: women sixteen to twenty, men thirty to thirty-five. Office-holding: women from forty, men from thirty. Military service: men from twenty to sixty, women after childbearing until fifty if needed. All provisions should be adapted to what is possible and suitable for each person.
D: Summary: Book 6 concludes with age specifications: women marry at sixteen to twenty, men at thirty to thirty-five. Women may hold office from forty, men from thirty. Men serve militarily from twenty to sixty, women after childbearing until fifty if needed.
D: Distilled: Women marry at 16-20 and may hold office from 40; men marry at 30-35, hold office from 30, and serve militarily from 20-60 -- with women's military service possible after childbearing.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger. This closing passage provides the concrete age parameters for civic life. The marriage age gap (women 16-20, men 30-35) reflects Greek norms but also the Laws' interest in pairing mature men with younger women for optimal childrearing. Women's eligibility for office at 40 (after childbearing) and potential military service represent significant departures from Greek practice -- the Laws grants women more civic roles than any historical Greek state. The final clause -- 'regard to what is possible and suitable to each' -- introduces a principle of individual accommodation that tempers the rigidity of the age requirements. Cf. Republic 460e-461a on the guardians' reproductive ages, and Republic 451d-452a on women's military training. Book 6 thus ends with the lifecycle fully mapped: from marriage through procreation, office-holding, military service, and implicitly through to old age.

D: Original: And now, assuming children of both sexes to have been born, it will be proper for us to consider, in the next place, their nurture and education; this cannot be left altogether unnoticed, and yet may be thought a subject fitted rather for precept and admonition than for law. In private life there are many little things, not always apparent, arising out of the pleasures and pains and desires of individuals, which run counter to the intention of the legislator, and make the characters of the citizens various and dissimilar:-this is an evil in states; for by reason of their smallness and frequent occurrence, there would be an unseemliness and want of propriety in making them penal by law; and if made penal, they are the destruction of the written law because mankind get the habit of frequently transgressing the law in small matters. The result is that you cannot legislate about them, and still less can you be silent. I speak somewhat darkly, but I shall endeavour also to bring my wares into the light of day, for I acknowledge that at present there is a want of clearness in what I am saying.
D: Narrative: And now, assuming children of both sexes to have been born, it will be proper for us to consider, in the next place, their nurture and education; this cannot be left altogether unnoticed, and yet may be thought a subject fitted rather for precept and admonition than for law. In private life there are many little things, not always apparent, arising out of the pleasures and pains and desires of individuals, which run counter to the intention of the legislator, and make the characters of the citizens various and dissimilar -- this is an evil in states; for by reason of their smallness and frequent occurrence, there would be an unseemliness and want of propriety in making them penal by law; and if made penal, they are the destruction of the written law because mankind get the habit of frequently transgressing the law in small matters. The result is that you cannot legislate about them, and still less can you be silent. I speak somewhat darkly, but I shall endeavour also to bring my wares into the light of day, for I acknowledge that at present there is a want of clearness in what I am saying.
D: Refashioned: Now that we have assumed children of both sexes have been born, we should next consider their upbringing and education. This subject cannot be ignored entirely, yet it may seem better suited to guidance and advice than to formal legislation. In private life, there are countless small matters -- not always visible -- arising from individual pleasures, pains, and desires, that work against the legislator's intentions and produce citizens of widely varying character. This is a real problem for states. Because these matters are so trivial individually yet so frequent, it would be improper and undignified to make them criminal offenses. Yet if you do penalize them, you destroy the authority of written law itself, because people develop the habit of breaking the law in minor ways. The result is a dilemma: you cannot legislate about these things, but neither can you remain silent about them. I realize I am speaking rather obscurely, but I will try to bring my meaning into the open, since I admit that what I am saying lacks clarity at present.
D: Elegant: With children now born, we must address their nurture and education -- a subject better suited to guidance than rigid law. Private life is full of small matters arising from individual pleasures and pains that quietly undermine the legislator's aims, producing inconsistent citizens. Making such trivialities criminal is undignified; yet penalizing them erodes respect for all law, since people learn to transgress habitually in small things. You cannot legislate these matters, but you cannot ignore them either. I speak obscurely now, but will clarify.
D: Summary: The Athenian introduces the topic of children's education, noting the paradox that many private habits shaping character are too trivial to legislate yet too important to ignore. Penalizing small matters erodes respect for law, but silence allows bad habits to flourish.
D: Distilled: Many habits that shape character are too small to legislate but too important to ignore, creating a fundamental dilemma for lawgivers.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage introduces Book 7's central theme of education. The distinction between formal law and informal custom echoes Republic 425a-427a, where Socrates argues against legislating petty matters. The concept of 'unwritten laws' (agrapha nomima) was important in Greek political thought -- compare Thucydides 2.37. The Athenian's admission of speaking 'darkly' is characteristic of the Laws' self-aware methodology.

D: Original:
Very true. Athenian. Stranger. Am I not right in maintaining that a good education is that which tends most, to the improvement of mind and body?

Undoubtedly.

And nothing can be plainer than that the fairest bodies are those which grow up from infancy in the best and straightest manner?

Certainly.

And do we not further observe that the first shoot of every living thing is by far the greatest and fullest? Many will even contend that a man at twenty-five does not reach twice the height which he attained at five.

True.

Well, and is not rapid growth without proper and abundant exercise the source endless evils in the body?

Yes.

And the body should have the most exercise when it receives most nourishment?

But, Stranger, are we to impose this great amount of exercise upon newly-born infants?

Nay, rather on the bodies of infants still unborn.

What do you mean, my good sir? In the process of gestation?

Exactly. I am not at all surprised that you have never heard of this very peculiar sort of gymnastic applied to such little creatures, which, although strange, I will endeavour to explain to you.

By all means.
D: Narrative:
"Very true."

"Am I not right in maintaining that a good education is that which tends most to the improvement of mind and body?"

"Undoubtedly."

"And nothing can be plainer than that the fairest bodies are those which grow up from infancy in the best and straightest manner?"

"Certainly."

"And do we not further observe that the first shoot of every living thing is by far the greatest and fullest? Many will even contend that a man at twenty-five does not reach twice the height which he attained at five."

"True."

"Well, and is not rapid growth without proper and abundant exercise the source of endless evils in the body?"

"Yes."

"And the body should have the most exercise when it receives most nourishment?"

"But, Stranger, are we to impose this great amount of exercise upon newly-born infants?"

"Nay, rather on the bodies of infants still unborn."

"What do you mean, my good sir? In the process of gestation?"

"Exactly. I am not at all surprised that you have never heard of this very peculiar sort of gymnastic applied to such little creatures, which, although strange, I will endeavour to explain to you."

"By all means."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian then asked whether good education is that which best improves both mind and body. Cleinias confirmed this without hesitation. The Athenian continued: surely the finest bodies are those that grow from infancy in the healthiest, most natural way. And the first growth spurt of every living thing is by far the greatest -- many would argue that a person at twenty-five has not even doubled the height they reached at five. Rapid growth without sufficient exercise is the source of countless physical problems. The body needs the most exercise precisely when it receives the most nourishment.

Cleinias objected: are we really going to demand heavy exercise from newborn infants? The Athenian surprised him -- not newborns, but the unborn. Cleinias was astonished: exercise during pregnancy? Exactly, the Athenian replied. He was not surprised Cleinias had never heard of this unusual form of prenatal gymnastics, but he would do his best to explain it. Cleinias urged him to proceed.
D: Elegant: Good education improves both mind and body. The finest bodies develop from proper care beginning in infancy. Early growth is the most dramatic -- a person at twenty-five may not have doubled their height at five. Rapid growth without exercise causes great harm, and the body needs the most movement when it receives the most nourishment. This principle applies not just to infants but to the unborn -- prenatal exercise matters.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that education must develop both mind and body, and that the earliest growth period is the most critical. He shocks Cleinias by extending this principle to prenatal care, arguing that exercise during pregnancy benefits the unborn child.
D: Distilled: The earliest period of growth is the most critical, and the principle that the body needs exercise proportional to its nourishment applies even before birth.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The principle that education encompasses both body and soul echoes Republic 376e, where Socrates proposes gymnastic for the body and music for the soul. The emphasis on early childhood development is remarkably modern. The prenatal exercise idea, while unusual, connects to the Greek medical tradition (Hippocratic corpus) and anticipates the detailed physical regimen of Book 7. Compare also Aristotle's Politics 7.16, which directly draws on this passage for its own prenatal recommendations.

D: Original: The practice is more easy for us to understand than for you, by reason of certain amusements which are carried to excess by us at Athens. Not only boys, but often older persons, are in the habit of keeping quails and cocks, which they train to fight one another. And they are far from thinking that the contests in which they stir them up to fight with one another are sufficient exercise; for, in addition to this, they carry them about tucked beneath their armpits, holding the smaller birds in their hands, the larger under their arms, and go for a walk of a great many miles for the sake of health, that is to say, not their own, health, but the health of the birds; whereby they prove to any intelligent person, that all bodies are benefited by shakings and movements, when they are moved without weariness, whether motion proceeds from themselves, or is caused by a swing, or at sea, or on horseback, or by other bodies in whatever way moving, and that thus gaining the mastery over food and drink, they are able to impart beauty and health and strength. But admitting all this, what follows? Shall we make a ridiculous law that the pregnant woman shall walk about and fashion the embryo within as we fashion wax before it hardens, and after birth swathe the infant for two years? Suppose that we compel nurses, under penalty of a legal fine, to be always carrying the children somewhere or other, either to the temples, or into the country, or to their relations, houses, until they are well able to stand, and to take care that their limbs are not distorted by leaning on them when they are too young-they should continue to carry them until the infant has completed its third year; the nurses should be strong, and there should be more than one of them. Shall these be our rules, and shall we impose a penalty for the neglect of them? No, no; the penalty of which we were speaking will fall upon our own heads more than enough.
D: Narrative:
"The practice is more easy for us to understand than for you, by reason of certain amusements which are carried to excess by us at Athens. Not only boys, but often older persons, are in the habit of keeping quails and cocks, which they train to fight one another. And they are far from thinking that the contests in which they stir them up to fight with one another are sufficient exercise; for, in addition to this, they carry them about tucked beneath their armpits, holding the smaller birds in their hands, the larger under their arms, and go for a walk of a great many miles for the sake of health, that is to say, not their own health, but the health of the birds; whereby they prove to any intelligent person, that all bodies are benefited by shakings and movements, when they are moved without weariness, whether motion proceeds from themselves, or is caused by a swing, or at sea, or on horseback, or by other bodies in whatever way moving, and that thus gaining the mastery over food and drink, they are able to impart beauty and health and strength.

"But admitting all this, what follows? Shall we make a ridiculous law that the pregnant woman shall walk about and fashion the embryo within as we fashion wax before it hardens, and after birth swathe the infant for two years? Suppose that we compel nurses, under penalty of a legal fine, to be always carrying the children somewhere or other, either to the temples, or into the country, or to their relations' houses, until they are well able to stand, and to take care that their limbs are not distorted by leaning on them when they are too young -- they should continue to carry them until the infant has completed its third year; the nurses should be strong, and there should be more than one of them. Shall these be our rules, and shall we impose a penalty for the neglect of them? No, no; the penalty of which we were speaking will fall upon our own heads more than enough."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian explained that this concept was easier for Athenians to grasp because of a common practice taken to extremes in Athens. Not only boys but also older men kept quails and fighting cocks, training them to battle each other. But they did not consider the fights alone to be enough exercise for the birds. In addition, they carried them around -- the smaller birds in their hands, the larger ones tucked under their arms -- walking many miles not for their own health, but for the health of the birds. This proved to any thoughtful observer that all bodies benefit from being shaken and moved, provided the motion does not cause exhaustion. It does not matter whether the movement comes from the creature itself or from an external source -- a swing, the sea, horseback, or any other moving body. Through such motion, bodies master their food and drink and gain beauty, health, and strength.

But what follows from this? Should they pass a ridiculous law requiring pregnant women to walk about, shaping the embryo within like wax before it hardens? And after birth, should infants be swaddled for two years? Should they compel nurses, under threat of fines, to carry children everywhere -- to temples, to the countryside, to relatives' homes -- until the children can stand on their own, ensuring their limbs are not warped from bearing weight too early? The nurses should be strong, and there should be more than one per child. Should they carry them until the child turns three? Should they impose penalties for neglecting these rules? No -- the penalty they spoke of earlier would fall on their own heads quite enough already.
D: Elegant: Athenians understand prenatal motion because they carry fighting birds for miles -- not for their own health but for the birds'. This proves all bodies benefit from movement that does not exhaust them, whether self-generated or external. But should we therefore legislate that pregnant women must walk to shape their unborn children like wax? That nurses must carry infants everywhere until age three, under penalty of fines? No -- attempting to enforce such rules would bring more ridicule upon ourselves than compliance from others.
D: Summary: The Athenian uses the Athenian practice of carrying fighting birds for exercise as proof that all bodies benefit from gentle motion. He then proposes -- and immediately retracts -- the idea of legally mandating prenatal walks and infant-carrying, acknowledging such laws would be ridiculous to enforce.
D: Distilled: While gentle motion benefits all bodies including the unborn, attempting to enforce such care through law would invite ridicule rather than compliance.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The quail and cock-fighting reference reveals everyday Athenian life -- bird-fighting was a popular pastime (see Aristophanes' Birds; Aelian, De Natura Animalium). The analogy between shaping wax and shaping embryos connects to Greek embryological theories. The self-deprecating withdrawal of the proposed law ('the penalty will fall on our own heads') demonstrates the Athenian's awareness that legislation has practical limits -- a theme running through Book 7. The passage anticipates the later distinction between law and custom (7.12).

D: Original:
What penalty?

Ridicule, and the difficulty of getting the feminine and servant-like dispositions of the nurses to comply.

Then why was there any need to speak of the matter at all?

The reason is that masters and freemen in states, when they hear of it, are very likely to arrive at a true conviction that without due regulation of private life in cities, stability in the laying down of laws is hardly to be expected; and he who makes this reflection may himself adopt the laws just now mentioned, and, adopting them, may order his house and state well and be happy.

Likely enough.

And therefore let us proceed with our legislation until we have determined the exercises which are suited to the souls of young children, in the same manner in which we have begun to go through the rules relating to their bodies.

By all means.
D: Narrative:
"What penalty?"

"Ridicule, and the difficulty of getting the feminine and servant-like dispositions of the nurses to comply."

"Then why was there any need to speak of the matter at all?"

"The reason is that masters and freemen in states, when they hear of it, are very likely to arrive at a true conviction that without due regulation of private life in cities, stability in the laying down of laws is hardly to be expected; and he who makes this reflection may himself adopt the laws just now mentioned, and, adopting them, may order his house and state well and be happy."

"Likely enough."

"And therefore let us proceed with our legislation until we have determined the exercises which are suited to the souls of young children, in the same manner in which we have begun to go through the rules relating to their bodies."

"By all means."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias asked what penalty. The Athenian replied: ridicule, and the near impossibility of getting nurses -- with their subservient and resistant dispositions -- to comply. Cleinias then asked why bother raising the subject at all.

The Athenian explained: because when masters and free citizens in states hear such reasoning, they are very likely to reach the conviction that without proper regulation of private life, stability in public law is hardly achievable. A person who grasps this truth may voluntarily adopt the practices just described, and by doing so, order both household and state well, and be happy.

Cleinias conceded this was likely enough. The Athenian proposed they continue their legislation, now turning to exercises suited to the souls of young children, just as they had been addressing rules for their bodies. Cleinias agreed.
D: Elegant: The penalty for such laws would be ridicule and non-compliance from nurses. But the discussion has value: when thoughtful citizens hear the reasoning, they may voluntarily adopt these practices, understanding that stable public law depends on well-ordered private life. Let us now turn from bodily exercises to those suited to children's souls.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that while such rules cannot be enforced as laws, discussing them can persuade thoughtful citizens to adopt good practices voluntarily. He then proposes moving from physical to psychological education of young children.
D: Distilled: Though unenforceable as law, sound principles of child-rearing can be voluntarily adopted by thoughtful citizens who understand that public order depends on private discipline.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The remark about 'feminine and servant-like dispositions' reflects the patriarchal assumptions of ancient Athens -- though the Laws is notably more progressive on women's roles than most Greek texts (see 7.39, 7.42). The key philosophical point -- that voluntary adoption of good practices matters more than legal compulsion -- connects to the concept of the 'prelude' (prooimion) developed in Laws 4.722d-723d. The transition from body to soul mirrors the Republic's dual education in gymnastic and music (Republic 376e).

D: Original:
Let us assume, then, as a first principle in relation both to the body and soul of very young creatures, that nursing and moving about by day and night is good for them all, and that the younger they are, the more they will need it; infants should live, if that were possible, as if they were always rocking at sea. This is the lesson which we may gather from the experience of nurses, and likewise from the use of the remedy of motion in the rites of the Corybantes; for when mothers want their restless children to go to sleep they do not employ rest, but, on the contrary, motion-rocking them in their arms; nor do they give them silence, but they sing to them and lap them in sweet strains; and the Bacchic women are cured of their frenzy in the same manner by the use of the dance and of music.

Well, Stranger, and what is the reason of this?

The reason is obvious.

What?
D: Narrative:
"Let us assume, then, as a first principle in relation both to the body and soul of very young creatures, that nursing and moving about by day and night is good for them all, and that the younger they are, the more they will need it; infants should live, if that were possible, as if they were always rocking at sea. This is the lesson which we may gather from the experience of nurses, and likewise from the use of the remedy of motion in the rites of the Corybantes; for when mothers want their restless children to go to sleep they do not employ rest, but, on the contrary, motion -- rocking them in their arms; nor do they give them silence, but they sing to them and lap them in sweet strains; and the Bacchic women are cured of their frenzy in the same manner by the use of the dance and of music."

"Well, Stranger, and what is the reason of this?"

"The reason is obvious."

"What?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian established a first principle for both the body and soul of very young children: movement and nursing by day and night is beneficial for all of them, and the younger they are, the more they need it. Ideally, infants should live as though constantly rocking at sea.

This lesson comes from the experience of nurses and from the healing use of motion in the rites of the Corybantes. When mothers want their restless children to sleep, they do not use stillness but the opposite -- movement, rocking them in their arms. They do not use silence but song, wrapping their children in gentle melodies. The Bacchic women are cured of their frenzy in exactly the same way, through dance and music.

Cleinias asked what the reason for this could be. The Athenian replied that the reason was obvious. Cleinias pressed him to explain.
D: Elegant: First principle: constant gentle motion is good for the bodies and souls of young children -- the younger, the more they need it. Ideally, infants would live as if always rocking at sea. Nurses know this instinctively: to calm restless children, they use movement, not stillness; song, not silence. The Bacchic women are cured of frenzy through the same remedy -- dance and music.
D: Summary: The Athenian states a first principle: gentle constant motion benefits both body and soul in the very young. He supports this with the evidence of nurses who rock and sing to calm children, and Bacchic rites that cure frenzy through dance and music.
D: Distilled: Constant gentle motion and music calm and benefit infants' bodies and souls, as shown by the universal practice of rocking and singing to children.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The Corybantes were ecstatic priests of Cybele whose rites involved wild dancing and music believed to have healing properties -- referenced also in Symposium 215e and Ion 533e-534a. The Bacchic women (Maenads) experienced divine frenzy in Dionysian rites. The parallel between infant care and religious ecstasy is a striking psychological insight -- both involve using controlled external motion to override internal agitation. This anticipates the explicit therapeutic theory in the next passage (7.6).

D: Original: The affection both of the Bacchantes and of the children is an emotion of fear, which springs out of an evil habit of the soul. And when some one applies external agitation to affections of this sort, the motion coming from without gets the better of the terrible and violent internal one, and produces a peace and calm in the soul, and quiets the restless palpitation of the heart, which is a thing much to be desired, sending the children to sleep, and making the Bacchantes, although they remain awake, to dance to the pipe with the help of the Gods to whom they offer acceptable sacrifices, and producing in them a sound mind, which takes the place of their frenzy. And, to express what I mean in a word, there is a good deal to be said in favour of this treatment.
D: Narrative: "The affection both of the Bacchantes and of the children is an emotion of fear, which springs out of an evil habit of the soul. And when some one applies external agitation to affections of this sort, the motion coming from without gets the better of the terrible and violent internal one, and produces a peace and calm in the soul, and quiets the restless palpitation of the heart, which is a thing much to be desired, sending the children to sleep, and making the Bacchantes, although they remain awake, to dance to the pipe with the help of the Gods to whom they offer acceptable sacrifices, and producing in them a sound mind, which takes the place of their frenzy. And, to express what I mean in a word, there is a good deal to be said in favour of this treatment."
D: Refashioned: The Athenian explained that the condition of both the Bacchantes and restless children is fundamentally an emotion of fear, arising from a disordered state of the soul. When someone applies external movement to this kind of agitation, the external motion overpowers the terrible and violent internal one. It produces peace and calm in the soul and quiets the restless pounding of the heart -- a highly desirable result. In children, this external motion sends them to sleep. In the Bacchantes, who remain awake, it replaces their frenzy with orderly dancing to the pipe, accompanied by the gods to whom they offer pleasing sacrifices, and produces a sound mind in place of madness. In short, there is much to be said in favor of this treatment.
D: Elegant: Both the Bacchantes' frenzy and children's restlessness are forms of fear rooted in a disordered soul. When external motion is applied, it overpowers the violent internal agitation, producing calm. Children fall asleep; Bacchantes replace frenzy with orderly dance. External rhythm masters internal chaos -- there is much to commend this treatment.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that both infant restlessness and Bacchic frenzy stem from fear and inner disorder. External rhythmic motion overpowers the violent internal agitation, producing calm -- sleep in children, sound-minded dance in the Bacchantes.
D: Distilled: External rhythmic motion overpowers internal agitation by replacing disordered fear with calm, whether putting children to sleep or transforming Bacchic frenzy into orderly dance.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage presents a remarkable proto-psychological theory: anxiety and restlessness are forms of fear caused by internal disorder, and external rhythmic stimulus can override and regulate them. This anticipates modern understanding of how rhythmic sensory input (rocking, music) activates the parasympathetic nervous system. The idea that external order can impose internal order connects to the Republic's theory of musical education shaping the soul (Republic 398c-402a). The theological dimension -- that the Bacchantes achieve 'sound mind' through divine help -- maintains the Laws' characteristic integration of psychology and religion.

D: Original:
Certainly.

But if fear has such a power we ought to infer from these facts, that every soul which from youth upward has been familiar with fears, will be made more liable to fear, and every one will allow that this is the way to form a habit of cowardice and not of courage.

No doubt.

And, on the other hand, the habit of overcoming, from our youth upwards, the fears and terrors which beset us, may be said to be an exercise of courage.

True.

And we may say that the use of exercise and motion in the earliest years of life greatly contributes to create a part of virtue in the soul.

Quite true.

Further, a cheerful temper, or the reverse, may be regarded as having much to do with high spirit on the one hand, or with cowardice on the other.

To be sure.

Then now we must endeavour to show how and to what extent we may, if we please, without difficulty implant either character in the young.

Certainly.

There is a common opinion, that luxury makes the disposition of youth discontented and irascible and vehemently excited by trifles; that on the other hand excessive and savage servitude makes men mean and abject, and haters of their kind, and therefore makes them undesirable associates.

But how must the state educate those who do not as yet understand the language of the country, and are therefore incapable of appreciating any sort of instruction?

I will tell you how:-Every animal that is born is wont to utter some cry, and this is especially the case with man, and he is also affected with the inclination to weep more than any other animal.

Quite true.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly."

"But if fear has such a power we ought to infer from these facts, that every soul which from youth upward has been familiar with fears, will be made more liable to fear, and every one will allow that this is the way to form a habit of cowardice and not of courage."

"No doubt."

"And, on the other hand, the habit of overcoming, from our youth upwards, the fears and terrors which beset us, may be said to be an exercise of courage."

"True."

"And we may say that the use of exercise and motion in the earliest years of life greatly contributes to create a part of virtue in the soul."

"Quite true."

"Further, a cheerful temper, or the reverse, may be regarded as having much to do with high spirit on the one hand, or with cowardice on the other."

"To be sure."

"Then now we must endeavour to show how and to what extent we may, if we please, without difficulty implant either character in the young."

"Certainly."

"There is a common opinion, that luxury makes the disposition of youth discontented and irascible and vehemently excited by trifles; that on the other hand excessive and savage servitude makes men mean and abject, and haters of their kind, and therefore makes them undesirable associates."

"But how must the state educate those who do not as yet understand the language of the country, and are therefore incapable of appreciating any sort of instruction?"

"I will tell you how: Every animal that is born is wont to utter some cry, and this is especially the case with man, and he is also affected with the inclination to weep more than any other animal."

"Quite true."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian drew a further inference: if fear has such power, then every soul habituated to fears from youth will become more prone to fear. Everyone would agree this produces cowardice, not courage. Conversely, the habit of overcoming fears and terrors from youth onward is an exercise in courage. Early motion and exercise greatly contribute to developing virtue in the soul. Moreover, a cheerful disposition promotes spiritedness, while its opposite fosters cowardice.

They must therefore show how and to what extent they can implant the right character in the young. There is a common view that luxury makes youth discontented, irritable, and violently upset by trifles, while excessive and cruel servitude makes people mean, degraded, and hostile toward others -- unfit companions either way.

Cleinias posed a practical question: how should the state educate those who do not yet understand language and cannot comprehend instruction? The Athenian replied that every animal makes some cry at birth, but humans above all -- and humans are also more inclined to weeping than any other animal. Cleinias confirmed this.
D: Elegant: If fear shapes the soul, then habitual exposure to fear breeds cowardice, while habitual overcoming of fear builds courage. Early motion and exercise cultivate virtue in the soul, and cheerfulness promotes spiritedness. Luxury makes youth irritable over trifles; excessive servitude makes them mean and misanthropic. But how do you educate those too young for language? Through the body: every animal cries at birth, but humans cry and weep more than any other creature.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that habitual fear breeds cowardice while overcoming fear builds courage, and that early childhood conditions -- neither luxurious nor harsh -- shape character. When Cleinias asks how to educate pre-verbal children, the Athenian turns to the body's natural expressions.
D: Distilled: Early habits of overcoming fear build courage while luxury breeds irritability and harsh treatment breeds meanness, and pre-verbal children must be educated through physical rather than verbal means.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The discussion of courage as a habit formed in youth echoes Republic 429c-430b (courage as preserving right belief about what is fearful) and Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics 2.1-3 (virtue as habit). The middle path between luxury and servitude parallels Aristotle's doctrine of the mean. The question about pre-verbal education is practically important and philosophically significant -- it pushes education theory beyond the merely intellectual. Compare Republic 401d-402a on music's pre-rational influence on the soul.

D: Original: Do not nurses, when they want to know what an infant desires, judge by these signs?-when anything is brought to the infant and he is silent, then he is supposed to be pleased, but, when he weeps and cries out, then he is not pleased. For tears and cries are the inauspicious signs by which children show what they love and hate. Now the time which is thus spent is no less than three years, and is a very considerable portion of life to be passed ill or well.
D: Narrative: "Do not nurses, when they want to know what an infant desires, judge by these signs? -- when anything is brought to the infant and he is silent, then he is supposed to be pleased, but, when he weeps and cries out, then he is not pleased. For tears and cries are the inauspicious signs by which children show what they love and hate. Now the time which is thus spent is no less than three years, and is a very considerable portion of life to be passed ill or well."
D: Refashioned: The Athenian pointed out that nurses determine what an infant wants by reading these very signs: when something is brought to the child and it remains silent, the child is pleased; when it weeps and cries out, it is displeased. Tears and cries are the unfavorable signals by which children reveal what they love and hate. This pre-verbal period lasts no less than three years -- a very considerable portion of life to be spent well or badly.
D: Elegant: Nurses read infants' desires through silence (pleasure) and crying (displeasure). These first three pre-verbal years -- a substantial portion of life -- can be spent well or badly, and their quality matters enormously.
D: Summary: Nurses interpret infant desires through silence (contentment) and crying (displeasure). The Athenian emphasizes that these first three pre-verbal years constitute a significant portion of life that profoundly affects development.
D: Distilled: The first three pre-verbal years, read through an infant's silence and cries, are a crucial and substantial portion of life.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The observation about nurses reading infant signals is empirically grounded and connects to the broader Greek interest in semiotics (reading signs). The emphasis on the first three years as formative anticipates modern developmental psychology. The word 'inauspicious' (dyssema) carries religious overtones -- the child's cries are like ill omens, suggesting that unhappiness in infancy portends future problems. This reinforces the Athenian's argument that pre-verbal education through physical conditions is critically important.

D: Original:
True.

Does not the discontented and ungracious nature appear to you to be full of lamentations and sorrows more than a good man ought to be?

Certainly.

Well, but if during these three years every possible care were taken that our nursling should have as little of sorrow and fear, and in general of pain as was possible, might we not expect in early childhood to make his soul more gentle and cheerful?

To be sure, Stranger-more especially if we could procure him a variety of pleasures.

There I can no longer agree, Cleinias: you amaze me. To bring him up in such a way would be his utter ruin; for the beginning is always the most critical part of education. Let us see whether I am right.

Proceed.
D: Narrative:
"True."

"Does not the discontented and ungracious nature appear to you to be full of lamentations and sorrows more than a good man ought to be?"

"Certainly."

"Well, but if during these three years every possible care were taken that our nursling should have as little of sorrow and fear, and in general of pain as was possible, might we not expect in early childhood to make his soul more gentle and cheerful?"

"To be sure, Stranger -- more especially if we could procure him a variety of pleasures."

"There I can no longer agree, Cleinias: you amaze me. To bring him up in such a way would be his utter ruin; for the beginning is always the most critical part of education. Let us see whether I am right."

"Proceed."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian asked whether the discontented, ungracious person seems to be filled with more lamentation and sorrow than is fitting for a good person. Cleinias confirmed this. The Athenian then asked: if during these first three years, every care were taken to minimize sorrow, fear, and pain, would this not make the child's soul more gentle and cheerful?

Cleinias enthusiastically agreed -- especially, he added, if they could supply the child with a variety of pleasures. At this, the Athenian sharply disagreed: "You amaze me. Raising a child that way would be his utter ruin, for the beginning is always the most critical part of education." He asked Cleinias to hear him out, and Cleinias told him to proceed.
D: Elegant: The discontented nature overflows with lamentation. If we minimize sorrow and fear in an infant's first three years, we might expect a gentler soul. But Cleinias goes too far in suggesting we maximize pleasures -- that would be the child's ruin. The beginning is the most critical part of education, and a surfeit of pleasure corrupts from the start.
D: Summary: When Cleinias suggests maximizing an infant's pleasures to produce a cheerful soul, the Athenian sharply disagrees. While minimizing unnecessary pain is good, flooding a child with pleasures would be ruinous because the beginning of education is its most critical phase.
D: Distilled: Minimizing unnecessary suffering in infancy is wise, but maximizing pleasures would ruin the child because the earliest phase of education is the most decisive.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). This is a pivotal exchange where the Athenian corrects Cleinias' hedonistic instinct -- not all pleasure is beneficial, especially in education. This echoes the Republic's critique of undisciplined pleasure (Republic 559d-561a) and anticipates the Laws' own doctrine of the 'middle state' elaborated in 7.10. The principle that 'the beginning is the most critical part' appears also at Republic 377a-b regarding early exposure to stories. Aristotle quotes this principle approvingly in Politics 7.17.

D: Original: The point about which you and I differ is of great importance, and I hope that you, Megillus, will help to decide between us. For I maintain that the true life should neither seek for pleasures, nor, on the other hand, entirely avoid pains, but should embrace the middle state, which I just spoke of as gentle and benign, and is a state which we by some divine presage and inspiration rightly ascribe to God. Now, I say, he among men, too, who would be divine ought to pursue after this mean habit-he should not rush headlong into pleasures, for he will not be free from pains; nor should we allow any one, young or old, male or female, to be thus given any more than ourselves, and least of all the newly-born infant, for in infancy more than at any other time the character is engrained by habit. Nay, more, if I were not afraid of appearing to be ridiculous, I would say that a woman during her year of pregnancy should of all women be most carefully tended, and kept from violent or excessive pleasures and pains, and should at that time cultivate gentleness and benevolence and kindness.
D: Narrative: "The point about which you and I differ is of great importance, and I hope that you, Megillus, will help to decide between us. For I maintain that the true life should neither seek for pleasures, nor, on the other hand, entirely avoid pains, but should embrace the middle state, which I just spoke of as gentle and benign, and is a state which we by some divine presage and inspiration rightly ascribe to God. Now, I say, he among men, too, who would be divine ought to pursue after this mean habit -- he should not rush headlong into pleasures, for he will not be free from pains; nor should we allow any one, young or old, male or female, to be thus given any more than ourselves, and least of all the newly-born infant, for in infancy more than at any other time the character is engrained by habit. Nay, more, if I were not afraid of appearing to be ridiculous, I would say that a woman during her year of pregnancy should of all women be most carefully tended, and kept from violent or excessive pleasures and pains, and should at that time cultivate gentleness and benevolence and kindness."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian declared that the point of disagreement between himself and Cleinias was of great importance, and he asked Megillus to help adjudicate. His position was this: the true life should neither chase after pleasures nor entirely avoid pains, but embrace the middle state -- gentle and benign -- which humans by divine intuition rightly attribute to God himself.

Anyone who aspires to the divine should pursue this balanced disposition. One should not rush headlong into pleasures, because pains are unavoidable regardless. This principle applies to everyone -- young and old, male and female -- but above all to the newborn infant, for in infancy character is shaped by habit more deeply than at any other time.

Moreover, if he were not afraid of seeming ridiculous, the Athenian would add that a pregnant woman should, of all women, be the most carefully attended to. She should be kept from violent or excessive pleasures and pains alike, and should cultivate gentleness, benevolence, and kindness throughout her pregnancy.
D: Elegant: The true life neither chases pleasure nor flees pain, but embraces the middle state -- gentle and benign -- which we rightly attribute to God. Anyone aspiring to divinity should pursue this balance. It applies to all, but especially to newborns, whose characters are shaped by habit more than at any other age. Even pregnant women should be kept from extremes of pleasure and pain, cultivating gentleness and kindness instead.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues for a middle path between pleasure-seeking and pain-avoidance, a gentle state he attributes to the divine nature. This balance is most critical for infants, whose character is formed by early habit, and should extend even to the care of pregnant women.
D: Distilled: The ideal life pursues neither pleasure nor avoidance of pain but a gentle middle state attributed to God, and this balance is most crucial during infancy when character is formed by habit.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue, addressing both interlocutors). This passage articulates the Laws' distinctive ethical position: the 'middle state' (mese hexis) between pleasure and pain. This differs subtly from Aristotle's 'mean' (mesotes) in the Nicomachean Ethics, which concerns virtue between excess and deficiency. The attribution of this calm state to God connects to Laws 10's theology. The appeal to Megillus as arbiter is a dramatic device reflecting Sparta's cultural emphasis on moderation and discipline. The prenatal care recommendations again anticipate Aristotle's Politics 7.16.

D: Original:
You need not, ask Megillus, Stranger, which of us has most truly spoken; for I myself agree that all men ought to avoid the life of unmingled pain or pleasure, and pursue always a middle course. And having spoken well, may I add that you have been well answered?

Very good, Cleinias; and now let us all three consider a further point.

What is it?
D: Narrative:
"You need not ask Megillus, Stranger, which of us has most truly spoken; for I myself agree that all men ought to avoid the life of unmingled pain or pleasure, and pursue always a middle course. And having spoken well, may I add that you have been well answered?"

"Very good, Cleinias; and now let us all three consider a further point."

"What is it?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias told the Athenian there was no need to consult Megillus -- he himself agreed that everyone should avoid a life of pure pain or pure pleasure and pursue a middle course instead. He added, with some warmth, that having spoken well, the Athenian had also been well answered.

The Athenian accepted this graciously and proposed that all three of them now consider a further point. Cleinias asked what it was.
D: Elegant: Cleinias concedes without needing Megillus as judge: everyone should avoid extremes of pain or pleasure and pursue the middle course. The Athenian proposes they now consider a further point together.
D: Summary: Cleinias agrees with the Athenian's doctrine of the middle path between pleasure and pain without needing Megillus to adjudicate. The Athenian then moves to introduce a new topic.
D: Distilled: Cleinias voluntarily accepts the doctrine of the middle path between pleasure and pain, and the discussion moves forward.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). Cleinias' ready agreement here is dramatically significant -- it shows the Athenian's persuasive power. The phrase 'unmingled pain or pleasure' (akratos) uses wine-mixing vocabulary, a common metaphor in Plato (compare Philebus 61b-c on the 'mixed life'). Cleinias' slightly competitive remark about being 'well answered' adds human texture to the dialogue.

D: Original:
That all the matters which we are now describing are commonly called by the general name of unwritten customs, and what are termed the laws of our ancestors are all of similar nature. And the reflection which lately arose in our minds, that we can neither call these things laws, nor yet leave them unmentioned, is justified; for they are the bonds of the whole state, and come in between the written laws which are or are hereafter to be laid down; they are just ancestral customs of great antiquity, which, if they are rightly ordered and made habitual, shield and preserve the previously existing written law; but if they depart from right and fall into disorder, then they are like the props of builders which slip away out of their Place and cause a universal ruin-one part drags another down, and the fair super-structure falls because the old foundations are undermined. Reflecting upon this, Cleinias, you ought to bind together the new state in every possible way, omitting nothing, whether great or small, of what are called laws or manners or pursuits, for by these means a city is bound together, and all these things are only lasting when they depend upon one another; and, therefore, we must not wonder if we find that many apparently trifling customs or usages come pouring in and lengthening out our laws.

Very true: we are disposed to agree with you.
D: Narrative:
"That all the matters which we are now describing are commonly called by the general name of unwritten customs, and what are termed the laws of our ancestors are all of similar nature. And the reflection which lately arose in our minds, that we can neither call these things laws, nor yet leave them unmentioned, is justified; for they are the bonds of the whole state, and come in between the written laws which are or are hereafter to be laid down; they are just ancestral customs of great antiquity, which, if they are rightly ordered and made habitual, shield and preserve the previously existing written law; but if they depart from right and fall into disorder, then they are like the props of builders which slip away out of their place and cause a universal ruin -- one part drags another down, and the fair superstructure falls because the old foundations are undermined. Reflecting upon this, Cleinias, you ought to bind together the new state in every possible way, omitting nothing, whether great or small, of what are called laws or manners or pursuits, for by these means a city is bound together, and all these things are only lasting when they depend upon one another; and, therefore, we must not wonder if we find that many apparently trifling customs or usages come pouring in and lengthening out our laws."

"Very true: we are disposed to agree with you."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian explained that everything they had been discussing falls under the general heading of "unwritten customs" -- what people call the laws of their ancestors. The earlier reflection -- that these things cannot properly be called laws yet cannot be left unmentioned -- was justified. These unwritten customs are the bonds of the entire state. They occupy the space between written laws already established and those yet to come. They are ancestral practices of great antiquity which, when rightly maintained and habitual, shield and preserve the written law. But when they fall into disorder, they are like a builder's props slipping out of place, causing the whole structure to collapse -- one part dragging another down, the fine superstructure falling because its old foundations have been undermined.

With this in mind, the Athenian urged Cleinias to bind the new state together in every possible way, omitting nothing -- whether great or small -- of what are called laws, customs, or practices. A city is held together by all of these, and they endure only when they depend on one another. They should not be surprised, therefore, if many seemingly trivial customs come pouring in and lengthening their laws.

Cleinias agreed they were disposed to accept this view.
D: Elegant: What they have been discussing are "unwritten customs" -- ancestral practices that cannot be called laws yet cannot be ignored. These customs are the bonds of the state, occupying the space between written laws. When rightly maintained, they shield written law; when disordered, they are like slipping props that bring the whole structure down. A new state must be bound together by every means -- laws, customs, practices great and small -- for all depend on one another.
D: Summary: The Athenian explains that unwritten customs are the bonds holding the state together, bridging the gaps between written laws. When maintained, they protect the legal order; when corrupted, they undermine it like collapsing foundations. The new state must attend to every custom, great and small.
D: Distilled: Unwritten customs are the bonds of the state that either preserve or destroy the written law, and a well-founded city must attend to every practice, great and small.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger with Cleinias responding (dialogue). This is one of the most important theoretical passages in the Laws. The concept of 'unwritten laws' (agraphoi nomoi) was debated throughout Greek political thought -- Pericles invokes them in Thucydides 2.37, and they appear in Sophocles' Antigone (454-455). The building metaphor (props, foundations, superstructure) is vivid and architecturally precise. The insight that formal law depends on informal custom for its stability anticipates modern sociological and legal theory (e.g., Lon Fuller's 'internal morality of law'). Compare Republic 425a-e for a contrasting view where Socrates dismisses legislating minor customs.

D: Original: Up to the age of three years, whether of boy or girl, if a person strictly carries out our previous regulations and makes them a principal aim, he will do much for the advantage of the young creatures. But at three, four, five, and even six years the childish nature will require sports; now is the time to get rid of self-will in him, punishing him, but not so as to disgrace him. We were saying about slaves, that we ought neither to add insult to punishment so as to anger them, nor yet to leave them unpunished lest they become self-willed; and a like rule is to be observed in the case of the free-born. Children at that age have certain natural modes of amusement which they find out for themselves when they meet. And all the children who are between the ages of three and six ought to meet at the temples the villages, the several families of a village uniting on one spot.
D: Narrative: "Up to the age of three years, whether of boy or girl, if a person strictly carries out our previous regulations and makes them a principal aim, he will do much for the advantage of the young creatures. But at three, four, five, and even six years the childish nature will require sports; now is the time to get rid of self-will in him, punishing him, but not so as to disgrace him. We were saying about slaves, that we ought neither to add insult to punishment so as to anger them, nor yet to leave them unpunished lest they become self-willed; and a like rule is to be observed in the case of the free-born. Children at that age have certain natural modes of amusement which they find out for themselves when they meet. And all the children who are between the ages of three and six ought to meet at the temples of the villages, the several families of a village uniting on one spot."
D: Refashioned:
Up to age three, strictly following the previously outlined regulations will do a great deal for the welfare of the child, whether boy or girl. But from three to six years old, children's nature demands play. This is the time to curb self-will through punishment -- but never in a way that humiliates the child. The same principle was established for slaves: punishment should not include insult that provokes anger, nor should offenses go unpunished lest the slave become willful. The same rule applies to freeborn children.

At this age, children naturally invent their own forms of play when they come together. All children between three and six should gather at the village temples, with the families of each village assembling in one place.
D: Elegant: Until age three, follow the earlier regulations carefully. From three to six, children need play, and self-will must be corrected through punishment that does not humiliate -- the same principle applied to slaves. Children naturally invent games when they gather, and all children aged three to six should meet together at village temples.
D: Summary: Children up to three benefit from the earlier physical regimen. From three to six, play becomes essential, and self-will must be corrected without humiliation. Children of this age naturally create their own games and should gather together at village temples.
D: Distilled: From ages three to six, children need communal play and correction of self-will through firm but non-humiliating discipline.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The parallel between disciplining children and disciplining slaves is striking and characteristic of the Laws' practical realism. The principle of punishment without humiliation is notably humane for its era. The communal gathering at temples establishes early socialization as a civic responsibility, not just a family matter. Compare Republic 537a on compulsion in education ('no forced learning can remain in the soul'). The village temple as gathering place shows the Laws' integration of religious and civic life.

D: Original:
The nurses are to see that the children behave properly and orderly-they themselves and all their companies are to be under the control of twelve matrons, one for each company, who are annually selected to inspect them from the women previously mentioned, [i.e., the women who have authority over marriage], whom the guardians of the law appoint. These matrons shall be chosen by the women who have authority over marriage, one out of each tribe; all are to be of the same age; and let each of them, as soon as she is appointed, hold office and go to the temples every day, punishing all offenders, male or female, who are slaves or strangers, by the help of some of the public slaves; but if any citizen disputes the punishment, let her bring him before the wardens of the city; or, if there be no dispute, let her punish him herself. After the age of six years the time has arrived for the separation of the sexes-let boys live with boys, and girls in like manner with girls. Now they must begin to learn-the boys going to teachers of horsemanship and the use of the bow, the javelin, and sling, and the girls too, if they do not object, at any rate until they know how to manage these weapons, and especially how to handle heavy arms; for I may note, that the practice which now prevails is almost universally misunderstood.

In what respect?
D: Narrative:
"The nurses are to see that the children behave properly and orderly -- they themselves and all their companies are to be under the control of twelve matrons, one for each company, who are annually selected to inspect them from the women previously mentioned, [i.e., the women who have authority over marriage], whom the guardians of the law appoint. These matrons shall be chosen by the women who have authority over marriage, one out of each tribe; all are to be of the same age; and let each of them, as soon as she is appointed, hold office and go to the temples every day, punishing all offenders, male or female, who are slaves or strangers, by the help of some of the public slaves; but if any citizen disputes the punishment, let her bring him before the wardens of the city; or, if there be no dispute, let her punish him herself.

"After the age of six years the time has arrived for the separation of the sexes -- let boys live with boys, and girls in like manner with girls. Now they must begin to learn -- the boys going to teachers of horsemanship and the use of the bow, the javelin, and sling, and the girls too, if they do not object, at any rate until they know how to manage these weapons, and especially how to handle heavy arms; for I may note, that the practice which now prevails is almost universally misunderstood."

"In what respect?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian specified the administrative structure: nurses must ensure orderly behavior among the children. Both nurses and their groups shall be supervised by twelve matrons -- one per company -- selected annually from the women who oversee marriage, as appointed by the guardians of the law. One matron is chosen from each tribe, all of the same age. Each matron, upon appointment, must go to the temples daily, punishing offending slaves or foreigners with the help of public slaves. If a citizen disputes the punishment, the matron brings the case before the city wardens; if there is no dispute, she administers the punishment herself.

After age six, the sexes are to be separated -- boys living with boys, girls with girls. Formal education begins: boys learn horsemanship, archery, javelin-throwing, and slinging. Girls should learn the same skills, if they do not object -- at minimum until they can handle these weapons competently, especially heavy arms. The Athenian noted that the prevailing practice in this area is almost universally misunderstood.

Cleinias asked: in what respect?
D: Elegant: Twelve matrons -- one per tribe, annually selected -- supervise the nurses and children, with authority to punish offenders. After age six, sexes separate and formal education begins: boys learn horsemanship, archery, javelin, and sling. Girls should learn the same, at least until they can handle heavy arms competently. Current practice is almost universally misguided on this point.
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes a system of twelve elected matrons to oversee early childhood care. After age six, boys and girls separate and begin formal education in martial skills. Girls should also learn weapons handling, especially heavy arms -- a point the Athenian says is widely misunderstood.
D: Distilled: After communal early childhood supervised by elected matrons, children at six begin formal martial education, with girls included in weapons training alongside boys.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The institutional detail here is characteristic of the Laws' practical legislative ambition. The twelve matrons recall the Republic's female guardians (Republic 540c) but are more administratively specific. The inclusion of girls in martial education is radical for ancient Greece -- even Sparta, which trained women in athletics, did not go this far with weapons. The phrase 'if they do not object' shows the Athenian's awareness of cultural resistance. The 'misunderstanding' he mentions concerns handedness, discussed in the next passage (7.15).

D: Original: In that the right and left hand are supposed to be by nature differently suited for our various uses of them; whereas no difference is found in the use of the feet and the lower limbs; but in the use of the hands we are, as it were, maimed by the folly of nurses and mothers; for although our several limbs are by nature balanced, we create a difference in them by bad habit. In some cases this is of no consequence, as, for example, when we hold the lyre in the left hand, and the plectrum in the right, but it is downright folly to make the same distinction in other cases. The custom of the Scythians proves our error; for they not only hold the bow from them with the left hand and draw the arrow to them with their right, but use either hand for both purposes. And there are many similar examples in charioteering and other things, from which we may learn that those who make the left side weaker than the right act contrary to nature.
D: Narrative: "In that the right and left hand are supposed to be by nature differently suited for our various uses of them; whereas no difference is found in the use of the feet and the lower limbs; but in the use of the hands we are, as it were, maimed by the folly of nurses and mothers; for although our several limbs are by nature balanced, we create a difference in them by bad habit. In some cases this is of no consequence, as, for example, when we hold the lyre in the left hand, and the plectrum in the right, but it is downright folly to make the same distinction in other cases. The custom of the Scythians proves our error; for they not only hold the bow from them with the left hand and draw the arrow to them with their right, but use either hand for both purposes. And there are many similar examples in charioteering and other things, from which we may learn that those who make the left side weaker than the right act contrary to nature."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian explained the misunderstanding: people assume the right and left hands are naturally suited for different tasks. Yet no such distinction exists for the feet and lower limbs. When it comes to the hands, we are effectively crippled by the foolishness of nurses and mothers, because although our limbs are naturally balanced, we create asymmetry through bad habit.

In some cases this does not matter -- holding the lyre in the left hand and the plectrum in the right is fine. But applying this same distinction across all activities is sheer folly. The Scythians prove the point: they not only hold the bow with the left hand while drawing with the right, but use either hand interchangeably for both tasks. Many similar examples from chariot-driving and other skills show that those who make the left side weaker than the right are acting contrary to nature.
D: Elegant: People wrongly assume the hands are naturally suited for different tasks. Our feet show no such bias -- the asymmetry in our hands is created by bad habit, not nature. In some cases the distinction is harmless, but in others it is folly. The Scythians prove this: they use either hand for the bow. Making the left side weaker than the right goes against nature.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that hand dominance is not natural but is created by habit -- nurses and mothers cripple children by training them to favor one hand. The Scythians, who use both hands equally for archery, prove that ambidexterity is the natural state.
