D: Distilled: Hand dominance is not natural but a product of bad habit; our limbs are inherently balanced, as the ambidextrous Scythians demonstrate.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This is a remarkably modern observation about handedness. While contemporary science shows some neurological basis for hand preference, the Athenian's emphasis on the role of training and habit in reinforcing asymmetry has merit. The Scythians, nomadic warriors of the Central Asian steppes, were famous for their mounted archery -- Herodotus describes their customs extensively (Histories 4.1-82). The argument from nature versus habit (physis vs. nomos) is a recurring theme in Greek philosophy, central to the Sophists and addressed in Protagoras 320c-328d.

D: Original: In the case of the plectrum, which is of horn only, and similar instruments, as I was saying, it is of no consequence, but makes a great difference, and may be of very great importance to the warrior who has to use iron weapons, bows and javelins, and the like; above all, when in heavy armour, he has to fight against heavy armour. And there is a very great difference between one who has learnt and one who has not, and between one who has been trained in gymnastic exercises and one who has not been. For as he who is perfectly skilled in the Pancratium or boxing or wrestling, is not unable to fight from his left side, and does not limp and draggle in confusion when his opponent makes him change his position, so in heavy-armed fighting, and in all other things if I am not mistaken, the like holds-he who has these double powers of attack and defence ought not in any case to leave them either unused or untrained, if he can help; and if a person had the nature of Geryon or Briareus he ought to be able with his hundred hands to throw a hundred darts. Now, the magistrates, male and female, should see to all these things, the women superintending the nursing and amusements of the children, and the men superintending their education, that all of them, boys and girls alike, may be sound hand and foot, and may not, if they can help, spoil the gifts of nature by bad habits.
D: Narrative: "In the case of the plectrum, which is of horn only, and similar instruments, as I was saying, it is of no consequence, but makes a great difference, and may be of very great importance to the warrior who has to use iron weapons, bows and javelins, and the like; above all, when in heavy armour, he has to fight against heavy armour. And there is a very great difference between one who has learnt and one who has not, and between one who has been trained in gymnastic exercises and one who has not been. For as he who is perfectly skilled in the Pancratium or boxing or wrestling, is not unable to fight from his left side, and does not limp and draggle in confusion when his opponent makes him change his position, so in heavy-armed fighting, and in all other things if I am not mistaken, the like holds -- he who has these double powers of attack and defence ought not in any case to leave them either unused or untrained, if he can help; and if a person had the nature of Geryon or Briareus he ought to be able with his hundred hands to throw a hundred darts. Now, the magistrates, male and female, should see to all these things, the women superintending the nursing and amusements of the children, and the men superintending their education, that all of them, boys and girls alike, may be sound hand and foot, and may not, if they can help, spoil the gifts of nature by bad habits."
D: Refashioned:
As the Athenian had noted, for the plectrum and similar instruments the distinction between hands is of no consequence. But it makes a great difference -- indeed, it may be critically important -- for a warrior using iron weapons, bows, javelins, and the like. This is especially true in heavy-armed combat against other heavy-armed fighters. The gap between the trained and the untrained is enormous. Just as a master of the Pancratium, boxing, or wrestling can fight equally well from either side and does not stumble in confusion when forced to change position, the same principle applies to heavy-armed combat and everything else. A person with these dual powers of attack and defense should never leave them unused or untrained. If someone had the hundred hands of Geryon or Briareus, he should be able to throw a hundred darts.

Magistrates, both male and female, should oversee all of this -- women superintending the nursing and play of young children, men superintending their education -- so that all children, boys and girls alike, may be sound in hand and foot and not ruin nature's gifts through bad habits.
D: Elegant: For instruments the hand distinction hardly matters, but for warriors with iron weapons and heavy armor, ambidexterity may be decisive. A skilled boxer or wrestler fights equally from either side without confusion -- the same applies to armed combat. Anyone with dual powers of attack should train them fully. Male and female magistrates should ensure all children develop both sides equally, not spoiling nature's gifts through bad habits.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that while hand preference is harmless in music, it is critical in combat -- a trained fighter must be equally capable from either side. Magistrates should ensure all children, boys and girls, are trained ambidextrously to preserve nature's balanced gifts.
D: Distilled: Ambidextrous training is essential for warriors since a skilled fighter must be able to attack and defend equally from either side.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). Geryon was the three-bodied giant killed by Heracles; Briareus (Aegaeon) was one of the Hundred-Handed Ones (Hecatoncheires) in Hesiod's Theogony. The Pancratium was a combined boxing-wrestling event in the ancient Olympics, considered the most demanding athletic contest. The practical military argument for ambidexterity is sound -- warriors who can only fight from one side are vulnerable. The joint oversight by male and female magistrates again reflects the Laws' progressive inclusion of women in civic governance, developing the principles of Republic 451c-457b.

D: Original: Education has two branches-one of gymnastic, which is concerned with the body, and the other of music, which is designed for the improvement of the soul. And gymnastic has also two branches-dancing and wrestling; and one sort of dancing imitates musical recitation, and aims at preserving dignity and freedom, the other aims at producing health, agility, and beauty in the limbs and parts of the body, giving the proper flexion and extension to each of them, a harmonious motion being diffused everywhere, and forming a suitable accompaniment to the dance. As regards wrestling, the tricks which Antaeus and Cercyon devised in their systems out of a vain spirit of competition, or the tricks of boxing which Epeius or Amycus invented, are useless and unsuitable for war, and do not deserve to have much said about them; but the art of wrestling erect and keeping free the neck and hands and sides, working with energy and constancy, with a composed strength, and for the sake of health-these are always useful, and are not to be neglected, but to be enjoined alike on masters and scholars, when we reach that part of legislation; and we will desire the one to give their instructions freely, and the others to receive them thankfully.
D: Narrative:
"Education has two branches -- one of gymnastic, which is concerned with the body, and the other of music, which is designed for the improvement of the soul. And gymnastic has also two branches -- dancing and wrestling; and one sort of dancing imitates musical recitation, and aims at preserving dignity and freedom, the other aims at producing health, agility, and beauty in the limbs and parts of the body, giving the proper flexion and extension to each of them, a harmonious motion being diffused everywhere, and forming a suitable accompaniment to the dance.

"As regards wrestling, the tricks which Antaeus and Cercyon devised in their systems out of a vain spirit of competition, or the tricks of boxing which Epeius or Amycus invented, are useless and unsuitable for war, and do not deserve to have much said about them; but the art of wrestling erect and keeping free the neck and hands and sides, working with energy and constancy, with a composed strength, and for the sake of health -- these are always useful, and are not to be neglected, but to be enjoined alike on masters and scholars, when we reach that part of legislation; and we will desire the one to give their instructions freely, and the others to receive them thankfully."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian outlined the structure of education: it has two branches. Gymnastic is concerned with the body, and music with the improvement of the soul. Gymnastic itself divides into two: dancing and wrestling.

Dancing has two forms. One imitates musical recitation, aiming to preserve dignity and freedom. The other aims at health, agility, and beauty of the body, giving each limb its proper flexion and extension, diffusing harmonious motion throughout, and forming a fitting accompaniment to the dance.

As for wrestling, the Athenian dismissed the tricks devised by Antaeus and Cercyon out of competitive vanity, and the boxing techniques invented by Epeius and Amycus, as useless for war and not worth discussing. But the art of upright wrestling -- keeping the neck, hands, and sides free, working with energy, constancy, and composed strength, for the sake of health -- is always useful. This form should be required of both teachers and students when the time comes for that part of legislation, with teachers giving instruction generously and students receiving it gratefully.
D: Elegant: Education has two branches: gymnastic for the body, music for the soul. Gymnastic divides into dancing and wrestling. One type of dance preserves dignity through imitating song; the other develops health, agility, and bodily beauty. Flashy wrestling tricks invented by Antaeus, Cercyon, Epeius, and Amycus are useless for war. But upright wrestling -- keeping neck, hands, and sides free, with composed strength for health's sake -- is always valuable and should be required of all.
D: Summary: The Athenian divides education into gymnastic (body) and music (soul), and gymnastic into dancing and wrestling. He rejects flashy wrestling tricks as useless for war but endorses upright wrestling for health and military readiness.
D: Distilled: Education divides into gymnastic for the body and music for the soul, with useful wrestling and dignified dancing preferred over flashy competitive tricks.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The gymnastic/music division is the same as Republic 376e. Antaeus was the Libyan giant wrestler defeated by Heracles; Cercyon was the Eleusinian wrestling king killed by Theseus. Epeius was the Greek boxer at Troy who also built the Trojan Horse (Iliad 23.664-699). Amycus was the Bebrycian king who forced travelers to box him, defeated by Polydeuces (Apollonius Rhodius, Argonautica 2.1-97). The preference for upright wrestling over ground-fighting reflects practical military priorities -- a soldier in armor needs to stay on his feet.

D: Original: Nor, again, must we omit suitable imitations of war in our choruses; here in Crete you have the armed dances if the Curetes, and the Lacedaemonians have those of the Dioscuri. And our virgin lady, delighting in the amusement of the dance, thought it not fit to amuse herself with empty hands; she must be clothed in a complete suit of armour, and in this attire go through the dance; and youths and maidens should in every respect imitate her, esteeming highly the favour of the Goddess, both with a view to the necessities of war, and to festive occasions: it will be right also for the boys, until such time as they go out to war, to make processions and supplications to all the Gods in goodly array, armed and on horseback, in dances, and marches, fast or slow, offering up prayers to the Gods and to the sons of Gods; and also engaging in contests and preludes of contests, if at all, with these objects: For these sorts of exercises, and no others, are useful both in peace and war, and are beneficial alike to states and to private houses.
D: Narrative: "Nor, again, must we omit suitable imitations of war in our choruses; here in Crete you have the armed dances of the Curetes, and the Lacedaemonians have those of the Dioscuri. And our virgin lady, delighting in the amusement of the dance, thought it not fit to amuse herself with empty hands; she must be clothed in a complete suit of armour, and in this attire go through the dance; and youths and maidens should in every respect imitate her, esteeming highly the favour of the Goddess, both with a view to the necessities of war, and to festive occasions: it will be right also for the boys, until such time as they go out to war, to make processions and supplications to all the Gods in goodly array, armed and on horseback, in dances, and marches, fast or slow, offering up prayers to the Gods and to the sons of Gods; and also engaging in contests and preludes of contests, if at all, with these objects: For these sorts of exercises, and no others, are useful both in peace and war, and are beneficial alike to states and to private houses."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian continued: they must not neglect suitable imitations of war within their choral performances. Crete already has the armed dances of the Curetes, and the Lacedaemonians have those of the Dioscuri. Athena herself -- their virgin goddess -- delighted in dance but refused to dance with empty hands. She clothed herself in full armor to perform her dance. Youths and maidens should imitate her in every way, honoring the goddess both for the necessities of war and for festive celebrations.

Boys, until they go to war, should make processions and supplications to all the gods in splendid array -- armed and on horseback, in dances and marches both fast and slow -- offering prayers to the gods and sons of gods. They should engage in contests and preliminary contests with these same purposes in mind. These exercises, and only these, are useful in both peace and war, and beneficial alike to states and to private households.
D: Elegant: Choruses should include armed dances imitating war -- as the Curetes dance in Crete and the Dioscuri in Sparta. Athena herself danced in full armor, and youths should imitate her. Boys should process armed and on horseback, praying to the gods through dance, march, and contest. Only exercises serving both war and worship are truly useful to states and households.
D: Summary: Armed dances should be part of civic choruses, following the example of Cretan Curetes, Spartan Dioscuri, and Athena herself who danced in full armor. Boys should regularly practice armed processions, marches, and contests as preparation for both war and worship.
D: Distilled: Armed dances and martial processions, following the divine example of Athena dancing in armor, serve both military preparation and religious worship.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The Curetes were mythical warriors who danced around the infant Zeus to protect him from Kronos -- their armed dance (pyrrhiche) was central to Cretan religious practice. The Dioscuri (Castor and Pollux) were Spartan patron deities. The 'virgin lady' is Athena, whose armed birth and warlike character are central to Athenian identity. The image of Athena dancing in armor may reference the Panathenaic festival. The integration of military training, religious ritual, and aesthetic performance is characteristic of the Laws' holistic approach to education. Compare Republic 399a-b on appropriate musical modes for warriors.

D: Original:
But other labours and sports and exercises of the body are unworthy of freemen, O Megillus and Cleinias.

I have now completely described the kind of gymnastic which I said at first ought to be described; if you know of any better, will you communicate your thoughts?

It is not easy, Stranger, to put aside these principles of gymnastic and wrestling and to enunciate better ones.

Now we must say what has yet to be said about the gifts of the Muses and of Apollo: before, we fancied that we had said all, and that gymnastic alone remained; but now we see clearly what points have been omitted, and should be first proclaimed; of these, then, let us proceed to speak.

By all means.

Let me tell you once more-although you have heard me say the same before that caution must be always exercised, both by the speaker and by the hearer, about anything that is very singular and unusual. For my tale is one, which many a man would be afraid to tell, and yet I have a confidence which makes me go on.

What have you to say, Stranger?
D: Narrative:
"But other labours and sports and exercises of the body are unworthy of freemen, O Megillus and Cleinias."

"I have now completely described the kind of gymnastic which I said at first ought to be described; if you know of any better, will you communicate your thoughts?"

"It is not easy, Stranger, to put aside these principles of gymnastic and wrestling and to enunciate better ones."

"Now we must say what has yet to be said about the gifts of the Muses and of Apollo: before, we fancied that we had said all, and that gymnastic alone remained; but now we see clearly what points have been omitted, and should be first proclaimed; of these, then, let us proceed to speak."

"By all means."

"Let me tell you once more -- although you have heard me say the same before -- that caution must be always exercised, both by the speaker and by the hearer, about anything that is very singular and unusual. For my tale is one which many a man would be afraid to tell, and yet I have a confidence which makes me go on."

"What have you to say, Stranger?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian declared that other forms of physical labor, sport, and exercise are unworthy of free citizens. He stated that he had now fully described the kind of gymnastic he had promised to discuss, and invited Megillus and Cleinias to propose anything better.

Cleinias admitted it was not easy to set aside these principles and articulate superior ones.

The Athenian then shifted focus: they must now address what remains to be said about the gifts of the Muses and Apollo. Earlier, they thought they had covered everything and only gymnastic remained, but now they could see important points had been omitted that should be proclaimed first.

Cleinias urged him to continue. The Athenian cautioned that both speaker and listener must exercise care when the subject is singular and unusual. His argument was one that many would fear to make, yet he had the confidence to press forward.

Cleinias asked what he had to say.
D: Elegant: Other sports and exercises are beneath free citizens. The gymnastic program is now complete. But important matters concerning the Muses and Apollo have been omitted and must be addressed first. Caution is required -- what follows is unusual, and many would be afraid to say it -- but the Athenian presses on with confidence.
D: Summary: Having completed his account of gymnastic, the Athenian turns to the still-unaddressed topic of musical education. He warns that what he is about to say is singular and unusual -- something many would be afraid to assert -- but he has the confidence to proceed.
D: Distilled: With gymnastic fully described, the Athenian turns to musical education with a warning that his next argument will be bold and unusual.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue, Megillus also addressed). The dismissal of certain sports as 'unworthy of freemen' reflects aristocratic Greek values about leisure (schole) and its proper uses. The transition to 'the gifts of the Muses and Apollo' signals the move from physical to cultural education. The Athenian's cautionary preface about 'singular and unusual' claims builds dramatic tension for his argument about children's play (7.20), which he considers one of his most important and counter-intuitive points.

D: Original:
I say that in states generally no one has observed that the plays of childhood have a great deal to do with the permanence or want of permanence in legislation. For when plays are ordered with a view to children having the same plays, and amusing themselves after the same manner, and finding delight in the same playthings, the more solemn institutions of the state are allowed to remain undisturbed. Whereas if sports are disturbed, and innovations are made in them, and they constantly change, and the young never speak of their having the same likings, or the same established notions of good and bad taste, either in the bearing of their bodies or in their dress, but he who devises something new and out of the way in figures and colours and the like is held in special honour, we may truly say that no greater evil can happen in a state; for he who changes the sports is secretly changing the manners of the young, and making the old to be dishonoured among them and the new to be honoured. And I affirm that there is nothing which is a greater injury to all states than saying or thinking thus. Will you hear me tell how great I deem the evil to be?

You mean the evil of blaming antiquity in states?

Exactly.

If you are speaking of that, you will find in us hearers who are disposed to receive what you say not unfavourably but most favourably.

I should expect so.

Proceed.
D: Narrative:
"I say that in states generally no one has observed that the plays of childhood have a great deal to do with the permanence or want of permanence in legislation. For when plays are ordered with a view to children having the same plays, and amusing themselves after the same manner, and finding delight in the same playthings, the more solemn institutions of the state are allowed to remain undisturbed. Whereas if sports are disturbed, and innovations are made in them, and they constantly change, and the young never speak of their having the same likings, or the same established notions of good and bad taste, either in the bearing of their bodies or in their dress, but he who devises something new and out of the way in figures and colours and the like is held in special honour, we may truly say that no greater evil can happen in a state; for he who changes the sports is secretly changing the manners of the young, and making the old to be dishonoured among them and the new to be honoured. And I affirm that there is nothing which is a greater injury to all states than saying or thinking thus. Will you hear me tell how great I deem the evil to be?"

"You mean the evil of blaming antiquity in states?"

"Exactly."

"If you are speaking of that, you will find in us hearers who are disposed to receive what you say not unfavourably but most favourably."

"I should expect so."

"Proceed."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian made his bold claim: in states generally, no one has noticed that children's play has a profound connection to the stability of legislation. When children's games remain constant -- when children play the same way, enjoy the same amusements, and delight in the same toys -- the more serious institutions of the state remain undisturbed. But when games are disrupted, when innovations are constantly introduced, when the young never share the same tastes or the same established sense of good and bad -- whether in how they carry themselves or how they dress -- and when whoever invents something novel in shapes, colors, and the like receives special honor, then no greater evil can befall a state. The person who changes children's games is secretly changing the character of the young, making the old ways despised and the new ones honored. Nothing, the Athenian affirmed, causes greater harm to states than this attitude.

Cleinias guessed he meant the evil of disparaging tradition. Exactly, the Athenian confirmed. Cleinias assured him that on this subject, being Cretans and Spartans, they would be the most receptive audience imaginable. The Athenian expected as much and asked to continue.
D: Elegant: No one has noticed that children's play profoundly affects the stability of legislation. When games remain constant, institutions remain stable. When games constantly change and novelty is celebrated, the character of youth is secretly altered -- old ways become despised, new ways honored. No greater evil can befall a state than this habit of disparaging tradition in favor of innovation.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that constant innovation in children's games secretly undermines the stability of the entire state by reshaping young people's character and teaching them to despise tradition. Stable play produces stable institutions; changing play produces changing values.
D: Distilled: Innovation in children's games secretly undermines civic stability by teaching the young to despise established customs and honor novelty.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger with Cleinias responding (dialogue). This is one of the Laws' most famous and controversial arguments -- that children's play shapes political stability. The conservatism here goes beyond the Republic's censorship of poetry (Republic 376e-398b) to target even changes in children's games. Cleinias and Megillus, as representatives of traditionally conservative Crete and Sparta, are the ideal audience. The argument anticipates modern observations about cultural change beginning with shifts in popular taste. The Egyptian model of cultural fixity, mentioned in 7.23, is offered as the positive example.

D: Original: Well, then, let us give all the greater heed to one another's words. The argument affirms that any change whatever except from evil is the most dangerous of all things; this is true in the case of the seasons and of the winds, in the management of our bodies and the habits of our minds-true of all things except, as I said before, of the bad. He who looks at the constitution of individuals accustomed to eat any sort of meat, or drink any drink, or to do any work which they can get, may see that they are at first disordered by them, but afterwards, as time goes on, their bodies grow adapted to them, and they learn to know and like variety, and have good health and enjoyment of life; and if ever afterwards they are confined again to a superior diet, at first they are troubled with disorders, and with difficulty become habituated to their new food. A similar principle we may imagine to hold good about the minds of men and the natures of their souls. For when they have been brought up in certain laws, which by some Divine Providence have remained unchanged during long ages, so that no one has any memory or tradition of their ever having been otherwise than they are, then every one is afraid and ashamed to change that which is established. The legislator must somehow find a way of implanting this reverence for antiquity, and I would propose the following way:-People are apt to fancy, as I was saying before, that when the plays of children are altered they are merely plays, not seeing that the most serious and detrimental consequences arise out of the change; and they readily comply with the child's wishes instead of deterring him, not considering that these children who make innovations in their games, when they grow up to be men, will be different from the last generation of children, and, being different, will desire a different sort of life, and under the influence of this desire will want other institutions and laws; and no one of them reflects that there will follow what I just now called the greatest of evils to states. Changes in bodily fashions are no such serious evils, but frequent changes in the praise and censure of manners are the greatest of evils, and require the utmost prevision.
D: Narrative:
"Well, then, let us give all the greater heed to one another's words. The argument affirms that any change whatever except from evil is the most dangerous of all things; this is true in the case of the seasons and of the winds, in the management of our bodies and the habits of our minds -- true of all things except, as I said before, of the bad. He who looks at the constitution of individuals accustomed to eat any sort of meat, or drink any drink, or to do any work which they can get, may see that they are at first disordered by them, but afterwards, as time goes on, their bodies grow adapted to them, and they learn to know and like variety, and have good health and enjoyment of life; and if ever afterwards they are confined again to a superior diet, at first they are troubled with disorders, and with difficulty become habituated to their new food.

"A similar principle we may imagine to hold good about the minds of men and the natures of their souls. For when they have been brought up in certain laws, which by some Divine Providence have remained unchanged during long ages, so that no one has any memory or tradition of their ever having been otherwise than they are, then every one is afraid and ashamed to change that which is established. The legislator must somehow find a way of implanting this reverence for antiquity, and I would propose the following way: People are apt to fancy, as I was saying before, that when the plays of children are altered they are merely plays, not seeing that the most serious and detrimental consequences arise out of the change; and they readily comply with the child's wishes instead of deterring him, not considering that these children who make innovations in their games, when they grow up to be men, will be different from the last generation of children, and, being different, will desire a different sort of life, and under the influence of this desire will want other institutions and laws; and no one of them reflects that there will follow what I just now called the greatest of evils to states. Changes in bodily fashions are no such serious evils, but frequent changes in the praise and censure of manners are the greatest of evils, and require the utmost prevision."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian urged close attention: their argument was that any change, except away from evil, is the most dangerous of all things. This holds for the seasons and winds, for bodily health and mental habits -- for everything except what is already bad. Consider people accustomed to eating any food, drinking any drink, doing any available work: at first their bodies are disordered, but over time they adapt, learn to enjoy variety, and maintain good health. If they are later confined to a supposedly superior diet, they initially suffer and struggle to adjust.

The same principle applies to minds and souls. When people have been raised under laws that, by some divine providence, have remained unchanged for ages -- so long that no one remembers them ever being different -- then everyone feels fear and shame at the thought of changing what is established. The legislator must find a way to implant this reverence for tradition.

The Athenian proposed this approach: people tend to think that changes in children's games are trivial -- mere play. They do not see the serious consequences. They yield to children's wishes instead of holding firm, not realizing that children who innovate in their games will grow into adults different from the previous generation. Being different, they will want different lives, different institutions, different laws. No one reflects that this leads to the greatest evil in states. Changes in bodily fashion are not so serious, but frequent changes in what society praises and blames regarding conduct are the gravest of evils, requiring the utmost foresight.
D: Elegant: Any change except away from evil is dangerous -- in seasons, bodily health, and mental habits. Bodies adapt to consistent conditions; disruption causes disorder even when the change seems beneficial. The same applies to souls raised under stable laws: long custom creates reverence that makes change feel shameful. Parents who indulge children's desire for novelty in play do not realize these children will grow into adults demanding new institutions and laws. Changes in fashion are trivial; changes in moral standards are the greatest evil.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that all change except from evil is dangerous, drawing parallels between bodily adaptation and cultural stability. Laws unchanged for ages create natural reverence; children's play innovations, though seemingly trivial, produce adults who demand different institutions. Changes in moral standards are the gravest evil of all.
D: Distilled: All change except from evil is dangerous because stability creates reverence, and children who innovate in play grow into adults who demand new institutions and new moral standards.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage extends the argument from 7.20 with a biological analogy -- the body adapts to consistent conditions and is disturbed by change, even beneficial change. This echoes Hippocratic medical thinking. The claim that 'any change except from evil is dangerous' is the Laws' most explicitly conservative thesis, going well beyond the Republic's more nuanced view. The distinction between changes in bodily fashion (superficial) and changes in moral praise and censure (fundamental) is analytically sharp. The 'Divine Providence' that preserves laws connects to the Laws' theological framework developed in Book 10.

D: Original:
To be sure.

And now do we still hold to our former assertion, that rhythms and music in general are imitations of good and evil characters in men? What say you?

That is the only doctrine which we can admit.

Must we not, then, try in every possible way to prevent our youth from even desiring to imitate new modes either in dance or song? nor must any one be allowed to offer them varieties of pleasures.

Most true.

Can any of us imagine a better mode of effecting this object than that of the Egyptians?

What is their method?
D: Narrative:
"To be sure."

"And now do we still hold to our former assertion, that rhythms and music in general are imitations of good and evil characters in men? What say you?"

"That is the only doctrine which we can admit."

"Must we not, then, try in every possible way to prevent our youth from even desiring to imitate new modes either in dance or song? nor must any one be allowed to offer them varieties of pleasures."

"Most true."

"Can any of us imagine a better mode of effecting this object than that of the Egyptians?"

"What is their method?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian asked whether they still held to their earlier assertion that rhythms and music in general are imitations of good and evil characters in people. Cleinias replied that this was the only doctrine they could accept.

Then, the Athenian pressed, must they not try every possible means to prevent their youth from even wanting to imitate new modes of dance or song? No one should be permitted to offer them novel varieties of pleasure.

Cleinias emphatically agreed. The Athenian then asked: could any of them imagine a better way to achieve this than the method of the Egyptians? Cleinias asked what that method was.
D: Elegant: Music and rhythm imitate good and evil character -- this doctrine stands. Therefore youth must be prevented from even desiring new modes of dance or song, and no one should offer them novel pleasures. The best model for achieving this is Egypt.
D: Summary: Reaffirming that music imitates moral character, the Athenian argues youth must be prevented from desiring new musical modes. He introduces Egypt as the model for achieving cultural fixity in the arts.
D: Distilled: Since music imitates moral character, youth must be kept from desiring novel modes, following the Egyptian model of artistic fixity.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The doctrine that music imitates character comes from Republic 398c-401d and Laws 2.654a-660a. The reference to Egypt as a model of cultural stability is developed in the next passage (7.23). The Athenian's admiration for Egypt's artistic conservatism is historically grounded -- Egyptian art maintained remarkably consistent forms over millennia. This passage connects the abstract argument about change (7.21) to concrete policy about musical regulation.

D: Original:
To consecrate every sort of dance or melody. First we should ordain festivals-calculating for the year what they ought to be, and at what time, and in honour of what Gods, sons of Gods, and heroes they ought to be celebrated; and, in the next place, what hymns ought to be sung at the several sacrifices, and with what dances the particular festival is to be honoured. This has to be arranged at first by certain persons, and, when arranged, the whole assembly of the citizens are to offer sacrifices and libations to the Fates and all the other Gods, and to consecrate the several odes to gods and heroes: and if any one offers any other hymns or dances to any one of the Gods, the priests and priestesses, acting in concert with the guardians of the law, shall, with the sanction of religion and the law, exclude him, and he who is excluded, if he do not submit, shall be liable all his life long to have a suit of impiety brought against him by any one who likes.

Very good.

In the consideration of this subject, let us remember what is due to ourselves.

To what are you referring?
D: Narrative:
"To consecrate every sort of dance or melody. First we should ordain festivals -- calculating for the year what they ought to be, and at what time, and in honour of what Gods, sons of Gods, and heroes they ought to be celebrated; and, in the next place, what hymns ought to be sung at the several sacrifices, and with what dances the particular festival is to be honoured. This has to be arranged at first by certain persons, and, when arranged, the whole assembly of the citizens are to offer sacrifices and libations to the Fates and all the other Gods, and to consecrate the several odes to gods and heroes: and if any one offers any other hymns or dances to any one of the Gods, the priests and priestesses, acting in concert with the guardians of the law, shall, with the sanction of religion and the law, exclude him, and he who is excluded, if he do not submit, shall be liable all his life long to have a suit of impiety brought against him by any one who likes."

"Very good."

"In the consideration of this subject, let us remember what is due to ourselves."

"To what are you referring?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian described the Egyptian method: consecrate every form of dance and melody. First, ordain the year's festivals -- determining what they should be, when they should occur, and which gods, demigods, and heroes they should honor. Next, determine what hymns should be sung at each sacrifice and what dances should accompany each festival. This must initially be arranged by designated authorities. Once established, the entire citizen body offers sacrifices and libations to the Fates and all the other gods, consecrating the official odes to their respective gods and heroes.

If anyone offers unauthorized hymns or dances to any god, the priests and priestesses, acting together with the guardians of the law, shall exclude that person with the sanction of both religion and law. If the excluded person refuses to submit, anyone may bring a suit of impiety against him for the rest of his life.

Cleinias approved. The Athenian added a caveat: in considering this subject, they must remember what is due to themselves. Cleinias asked what he meant.
D: Elegant: The Egyptian method: consecrate every dance and melody. Ordain the year's festivals, specifying which gods are honored, what hymns are sung, what dances performed. Once established by authority and ratified by the citizenry, these forms become fixed. Anyone offering unauthorized hymns or dances is excluded by priests and lawmakers, and faces lifelong liability for impiety. But in adopting such rules, we must remember what is due to ourselves.
D: Summary: The Egyptian method is to consecrate every dance and melody, fixing the year's festivals, hymns, and dances by authority and then treating any innovation as impiety punishable by exclusion and legal action. The Athenian approves but adds a cautionary note about self-awareness.
D: Distilled: The Egyptian model fixes all dances and melodies through religious consecration, making unauthorized artistic innovation punishable as impiety.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The Egyptian model of consecrating artistic forms is historically accurate -- Egyptian temple art and music were regulated by priestly authority for millennia. The legal mechanism proposed -- permanent liability for impiety (asebeia) -- is severe; impiety trials were real in Athens (Socrates himself was condemned on this charge). The Athenian's self-reflexive caveat ('what is due to ourselves') signals awareness that they are proposing something radical. Compare Republic 424b-c on the danger of musical innovation, which is less extreme in its enforcement mechanisms.

D: Original: I mean that any young man, and much more any old one, when he sees or hears anything strange or unaccustomed, does not at once run to embrace the paradox, but he stands considering, like a person who is at a place where three paths meet, and does not very well know his way-he may be alone or he may be walking with others, and he will say to himself and them, "Which is the way?" and will not move forward until he is satisfied that he is going right. And this is what we must do in the present instance:-A strange discussion on the subject of law has arisen, which requires the utmost consideration, and we should not at our age be too ready to speak about such great matters, or be confident that we can say anything certain all in a moment.
D: Narrative: "I mean that any young man, and much more any old one, when he sees or hears anything strange or unaccustomed, does not at once run to embrace the paradox, but he stands considering, like a person who is at a place where three paths meet, and does not very well know his way -- he may be alone or he may be walking with others, and he will say to himself and them, 'Which is the way?' and will not move forward until he is satisfied that he is going right. And this is what we must do in the present instance -- A strange discussion on the subject of law has arisen, which requires the utmost consideration, and we should not at our age be too ready to speak about such great matters, or be confident that we can say anything certain all in a moment."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian explained: when any person -- young or especially old -- encounters something strange and unfamiliar, the proper response is not to rush to embrace the paradox. Instead, one should stand and consider, like a traveler at a fork where three paths meet who does not know the way. Whether alone or with companions, the traveler asks, "Which is the way?" and does not move forward until satisfied of the right direction.

This is what they must do now. A strange discussion about law has arisen that requires the deepest consideration. At their age, they should not be too hasty in speaking about such weighty matters, nor too confident that they can reach certainty in an instant.
D: Elegant: When encountering something strange, the wise person pauses like a traveler at a crossroads, asking "Which is the way?" rather than rushing forward. A strange discussion about law has arisen requiring the deepest consideration. At our age, we should not be hasty or overconfident about such great matters.
D: Summary: The Athenian counsels caution through the metaphor of a traveler at a crossroads: before embracing any paradoxical conclusion about law, one should pause and deliberate carefully, especially on matters of such gravity.
D: Distilled: Like a traveler at a crossroads, the wise person pauses before unfamiliar ideas rather than rushing to embrace them, especially regarding matters of law.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The crossroads metaphor (triodos) has deep resonance in Greek culture -- crossroads were associated with Hecate and with moments of decision. The image of the deliberating traveler recalls the Choice of Heracles (Xenophon, Memorabilia 2.1.21-34) and Parmenides' philosophical journey (fr. 1). The Athenian's self-awareness about the limits of confident assertion is characteristic of the Laws' more modest epistemology compared to the Republic's. This passage also serves as a methodological interlude, slowing the argument before proceeding.

D: Original:
Most true.

Then we will allow time for reflection, and decide when we have given the subject sufficient consideration. But that we may not be hindered from completing the natural arrangement of our laws, let us proceed to the conclusion of them in due order; for very possibly, if God will, the exposition of them, when completed, may throw light on our present perplexity.

Excellent, Stranger; let us do as you propose.
D: Narrative:
"Most true."

"Then we will allow time for reflection, and decide when we have given the subject sufficient consideration. But that we may not be hindered from completing the natural arrangement of our laws, let us proceed to the conclusion of them in due order; for very possibly, if God will, the exposition of them, when completed, may throw light on our present perplexity."

"Excellent, Stranger; let us do as you propose."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed that caution was needed. The Athenian proposed they allow time for reflection and decide only after sufficient consideration. In the meantime, so as not to delay the natural arrangement of their laws, they should proceed to complete them in due order. With God's help, the finished exposition might shed light on their present difficulty.

Cleinias thought this was excellent and agreed to follow the Athenian's proposal.
D: Elegant: They will allow time for reflection while continuing to complete the laws in order. The finished whole may illuminate the difficulty they currently face.
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes continuing with the laws in order while deferring the difficult question, trusting that the completed exposition will illuminate their current perplexity.
D: Distilled: Proceeding with the complete exposition of laws in order may resolve current perplexities that cannot be settled in isolation.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). This is a characteristic Platonic methodological move -- deferring judgment while continuing inquiry, trusting that systematic exposition will clarify particular difficulties. The phrase 'if God will' (ean theos ethele) maintains the Laws' pervasive theological framing. The passage exemplifies the dialogue's self-conscious awareness of its own method, more pronounced in the Laws than in earlier dialogues.

D: Original:
Let us then affirm the paradox that strains of music are our laws (nomoi), and this latter being the name which the ancients gave to lyric songs, they probably would not have very much objected to our proposed application of the word. Some one, either asleep or awake, must have had a dreamy suspicion of their nature. And let our decree be as follows:-No one in singing or dancing shall offend against public and consecrated models, and the general fashion among the youth, any more than he would offend against any other law. And he who observes this law shall be blameless; but he who is disobedient, as I was saying, shall be punished by the guardians of the laws, and by the priests and priestesses. Suppose that we imagine this to be our law.

Very good.
D: Narrative:
"Let us then affirm the paradox that strains of music are our laws (nomoi), and this latter being the name which the ancients gave to lyric songs, they probably would not have very much objected to our proposed application of the word. Some one, either asleep or awake, must have had a dreamy suspicion of their nature. And let our decree be as follows: No one in singing or dancing shall offend against public and consecrated models, and the general fashion among the youth, any more than he would offend against any other law. And he who observes this law shall be blameless; but he who is disobedient, as I was saying, shall be punished by the guardians of the laws, and by the priests and priestesses. Suppose that we imagine this to be our law."

"Very good."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian put forward the paradox: musical compositions are our laws. The Greek word nomoi means both "laws" and "lyric songs," and the ancients who gave this name to their melodies probably would not have objected to the proposed application. Someone, whether sleeping or awake, must have had an intuitive sense of this connection.

The decree should be as follows: no one, in singing or dancing, shall violate public and consecrated models or the established fashion among the youth -- no more than they would violate any other law. Whoever observes this law is blameless; whoever disobeys shall be punished by the guardians of the laws and by the priests and priestesses.

Cleinias accepted this as their law.
D: Elegant: Here is the paradox: musical compositions are laws -- nomoi means both. The ancients who named their songs 'laws' sensed this connection. Therefore: no one shall violate consecrated musical models any more than they would violate any other law. Obedience brings blamelessness; disobedience brings punishment from law-guardians, priests, and priestesses.
D: Summary: The Athenian exploits the double meaning of nomoi (laws/songs) to argue that musical forms literally are laws. He decrees that violating consecrated musical models should be treated as seriously as violating any other law, with punishment from both secular and religious authorities.
D: Distilled: Musical compositions are literally laws -- the word nomoi means both -- and violating consecrated musical forms should be punished like violating any other law.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The pun on nomoi (laws/lyric songs) is one of the most famous wordplays in Plato. The dual meaning was already noted by ancient commentators. The suggestion that some ancient dreamer intuited the connection has a mythic quality characteristic of the Laws' approach to etymology (compare Cratylus). The enforcement mechanism -- joint punishment by law-guardians and priests -- unifies civic and religious authority over artistic expression. This is the culmination of the argument begun in 7.20 about the political significance of cultural stability.

D: Original:
Can any one who makes such laws escape ridicule? Let us see. I think that our only safety will be in first framing certain models for composers. One of these models shall be as follows:-If when a sacrifice is going on, and the victims are being burnt according to law-if, I say, any one who may be a son or brother, standing by another at the altar and over the victims, horribly blasphemes, will not his words inspire despondency and evil omens and forebodings in the mind of his father and of his other kinsmen?

Of course.
D: Narrative:
"Can any one who makes such laws escape ridicule? Let us see. I think that our only safety will be in first framing certain models for composers. One of these models shall be as follows: If when a sacrifice is going on, and the victims are being burnt according to law -- if, I say, any one who may be a son or brother, standing by another at the altar and over the victims, horribly blasphemes, will not his words inspire despondency and evil omens and forebodings in the mind of his father and of his other kinsmen?"

"Of course."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian acknowledged the risk: can anyone who makes such laws escape ridicule? He thought their only safety lay in first establishing models for composers to follow.

He offered one such model through an analogy: imagine a sacrifice is underway, the offerings being burnt according to law. If someone standing at the altar -- a son or brother -- begins to utter horrible blasphemies, would not these words fill his father and kinsmen with despondency, evil omens, and dread?

Cleinias confirmed that they certainly would.
D: Elegant: Can such laws avoid ridicule? Only by first establishing clear models for composers. Consider: if someone blasphemes at a sacred sacrifice, his words fill those present with dread and ill omen. The same logic applies to inappropriate music at public rites.
D: Summary: Acknowledging the risk of ridicule, the Athenian argues that musical laws need clear compositional models. He illustrates with an analogy: just as blasphemy at a sacrifice would horrify those present, inappropriate music at sacred occasions produces similar spiritual harm.
D: Distilled: To avoid ridicule, musical laws need clear models, just as the horror of blasphemy at a sacrifice illustrates why inappropriate music at sacred occasions must be prevented.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The sacrifice analogy is rhetorically effective -- it grounds the abstract argument about musical regulation in the visceral horror of religious blasphemy. The concern about ridicule shows the Athenian's awareness that his proposals are extreme by contemporary standards. The concept of 'models' (tupoi) for composers echoes Republic 379a, where Socrates establishes tupoi for theological poetry. The Laws develops this into a more concrete institutional framework.

D: Original: And this is just what takes place in almost all our cities. A magistrate offers a public sacrifice, and there come in not one but many choruses, who take up a position a little way from the altar, and from time to time pour forth all sorts of horrible blasphemies on the sacred rites, exciting the souls of the audience with words and rhythms and melodies most sorrowful to hear; and he who at the moment when the city is offering sacrifice makes the citizens weep most, carries away the palm of victory. Now, ought we not to forbid such strains as these? And if ever our citizens must hear such lamentations, then on some unblest and inauspicious day let there be choruses of foreign and hired minstrels, like those hirelings who accompany the departed at funerals with barbarous Carian chants. That is the sort of thing which will be appropriate if we have such strains at all; and let the apparel of the singers be, not circlets and ornaments of gold, but the reverse. Enough of all this. I will simply ask once more whether we shall lay down as one of our principles of song-
D: Narrative: "And this is just what takes place in almost all our cities. A magistrate offers a public sacrifice, and there come in not one but many choruses, who take up a position a little way from the altar, and from time to time pour forth all sorts of horrible blasphemies on the sacred rites, exciting the souls of the audience with words and rhythms and melodies most sorrowful to hear; and he who at the moment when the city is offering sacrifice makes the citizens weep most, carries away the palm of victory. Now, ought we not to forbid such strains as these? And if ever our citizens must hear such lamentations, then on some unblest and inauspicious day let there be choruses of foreign and hired minstrels, like those hirelings who accompany the departed at funerals with barbarous Carian chants. That is the sort of thing which will be appropriate if we have such strains at all; and let the apparel of the singers be, not circlets and ornaments of gold, but the reverse. Enough of all this. I will simply ask once more whether we shall lay down as one of our principles of song --"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian pointed out that this is exactly what happens in nearly all Greek cities. A magistrate offers a public sacrifice, and multiple choruses position themselves near the altar. From time to time, they pour forth what amounts to blasphemy against the sacred rites -- stirring the audience's souls with words, rhythms, and melodies of deepest sorrow. The chorus that makes the citizens weep most during the sacrifice wins the prize.

Should such performances not be forbidden? If citizens must ever hear such lamentations, let them be performed on some accursed, inauspicious day by foreign, hired minstrels -- like those professionals who accompany the dead at funerals with barbarous Carian chants. If such music must exist at all, that is its proper setting. The singers should wear not gold circlets and ornaments but their opposite.

The Athenian concluded this topic and asked whether they would establish as a fundamental principle of song --
D: Elegant: In most cities, choruses at public sacrifices compete to produce the most sorrowful music, winning prizes for making citizens weep at the very moment of worship. This must be forbidden. If lamentation is needed at all, let it be performed on inauspicious days by foreign hired musicians in mourning attire -- not by citizens at sacred rites.
D: Summary: The Athenian criticizes the practice of competitive choral lamentations at public sacrifices, where the chorus that produces the most tears wins the prize. Such music should be banned from sacred rites and, if needed at all, confined to inauspicious days performed by foreign hired musicians.
D: Distilled: Competitive sorrowful music at public sacrifices is blasphemous and should be replaced by joyful worship, with lamentation confined to hired foreign performers on inauspicious days.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The passage criticizes the dithyrambic and tragic choral competitions that were central to Athenian religious festivals like the City Dionysia. The Carian chants were a type of professional funeral music associated with hired mourners from Caria (southwestern Asia Minor) -- the practice of hiring professional mourners was widespread in the ancient Mediterranean. The proposal to ban sorrowful music from sacrifices connects to Republic 387d-388e, where Socrates bans lamentation from the ideal city's education. The emphasis on appropriate emotional affect at worship prefigures Christian debates about liturgical music.

D: Original:
What?

That we should avoid every word of evil omen; let that kind of song which is of good omen be heard everywhere and always in our state. I need hardly ask again, but shall assume that you agree with me.

By all means; that law is approved by the suffrages of us all.

But what shall be our next musical law or type? Ought not prayers to be offered up to the Gods when we sacrifice?

Certainly.

And our third law, if I am not mistaken, will be to the effect that our poets, understanding prayers to be requests which we make to the Gods, will take especial heed that they do not by mistake ask for evil instead of good. To make such a prayer would surely be too ridiculous.

Very true.

Were we not a little while ago quite convinced that no silver or golden Plutus should dwell in our state?

To be sure.
D: Narrative:
"What?"

"That we should avoid every word of evil omen; let that kind of song which is of good omen be heard everywhere and always in our state. I need hardly ask again, but shall assume that you agree with me."

"By all means; that law is approved by the suffrages of us all."

"But what shall be our next musical law or type? Ought not prayers to be offered up to the Gods when we sacrifice?"

"Certainly."

"And our third law, if I am not mistaken, will be to the effect that our poets, understanding prayers to be requests which we make to the Gods, will take especial heed that they do not by mistake ask for evil instead of good. To make such a prayer would surely be too ridiculous."

"Very true."

"Were we not a little while ago quite convinced that no silver or golden Plutus should dwell in our state?"

"To be sure."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias asked what principle. The Athenian proposed: every word of evil omen should be avoided, and only songs of good omen should be heard everywhere and always throughout the state. He assumed their agreement.

Cleinias confirmed the law was approved by all of them. The Athenian then asked about the next musical principle: should not prayers be offered to the gods during sacrifice? Cleinias agreed.

The third law, then, would require poets -- understanding that prayers are requests made to the gods -- to take special care not to accidentally ask for evil instead of good. Such a mistaken prayer would be absurd.

Cleinias agreed. The Athenian reminded them that they had already established that no silver or golden Plutus should dwell in their state. Cleinias confirmed this.
D: Elegant: First law: only songs of good omen, always and everywhere. Second: prayers during sacrifice. Third: poets must take care that their prayers do not accidentally request evil instead of good -- especially since the state has already excluded the worship of wealth.
D: Summary: Three musical laws are established: all songs must be of good omen, prayers must accompany sacrifice, and poets must ensure their prayers request good rather than evil. The previous exclusion of wealth-worship from the state is recalled as context.
D: Distilled: Songs must be auspicious, prayers must accompany sacrifice, and poets must ensure they pray for genuine goods rather than mistakenly requesting evils like wealth.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The concept of euphemia (speaking words of good omen) was fundamental to Greek religious practice -- improper speech could pollute a sacrifice. The three laws establish a hierarchy: auspicious language, prayer with sacrifice, and theological accuracy in poetry. The reference to Plutus (the god of wealth) connects to Laws 5.742a-743c, which limits private wealth. The concern about poets accidentally praying for evil echoes Republic 379a-383c, where Socrates insists that god is the cause of good only. This also recalls the Protagoras 345d-e discussion of involuntary error.

D: Original:
And what has it been the object of our argument to show? Did we not imply that the poets are not always quite capable of knowing what is good or evil? And if one of them utters a mistaken prayer in song or words, he will make our citizens pray for the opposite of what is good in matters of the highest import; than which, as I was saying, there can be few greater mistakes. Shall we then propose as one of our laws and models relating to the Muses-

What?-will you explain the law more precisely?
D: Narrative:
"And what has it been the object of our argument to show? Did we not imply that the poets are not always quite capable of knowing what is good or evil? And if one of them utters a mistaken prayer in song or words, he will make our citizens pray for the opposite of what is good in matters of the highest import; than which, as I was saying, there can be few greater mistakes. Shall we then propose as one of our laws and models relating to the Muses --"

"What? -- will you explain the law more precisely?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian asked what their argument had shown. Had they not established that poets are not always capable of distinguishing good from evil? If a poet utters a mistaken prayer in song or words, he leads the entire citizenry to pray for the opposite of what is truly good on matters of the highest importance. There can be few greater errors than this.

Should they then propose this as one of their laws and models relating to the Muses? Cleinias asked him to explain the law more precisely.
D: Elegant: Poets do not always know good from evil. A mistaken poetic prayer leads citizens to pray for the opposite of what is good on the most important matters. This is among the gravest possible errors and must be prevented by law.
D: Summary: The Athenian reiterates that poets cannot always distinguish good from evil, and a mistaken prayer in song could lead citizens to pray for harm on the most important matters. This justifies establishing legal models for poetic composition.
D: Distilled: Because poets cannot always distinguish good from evil, their mistaken prayers could lead entire cities to pray for harm, making poetic regulation essential.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). This passage develops the Republic's critique of poets' epistemological limitations (Republic 598d-602b, the poet as imitator 'three removes from the truth'). But the Laws takes a more practical approach: rather than banishing poets entirely, it proposes regulatory oversight. The concern about mistaken prayers has a real theological dimension in Greek religion, where prayer formulas had to be precise -- an error could offend the gods or invite the wrong outcome. The Athenian's 'models relating to the Muses' (Mousikon tupoi) parallels the 'theological models' (theologias tupoi) of Republic 379a.

D: Original: Shall we make a law that the poet shall compose nothing contrary to the ideas of the lawful, or just, or beautiful, or good, which are allowed in the state? nor shall he be permitted to communicate his compositions to any private individuals, until he shall have shown them to the appointed judges and the guardians of the law, and they are satisfied with them. As to the persons whom we appoint to be our legislators about music and as to the director of education, these have been already indicated. Once more then, as I have asked more than once, shall this be our third law, and type, and model-What do you say?
D: Narrative: "Shall we make a law that the poet shall compose nothing contrary to the ideas of the lawful, or just, or beautiful, or good, which are allowed in the state? nor shall he be permitted to communicate his compositions to any private individuals, until he shall have shown them to the appointed judges and the guardians of the law, and they are satisfied with them. As to the persons whom we appoint to be our legislators about music and as to the director of education, these have been already indicated. Once more then, as I have asked more than once, shall this be our third law, and type, and model -- What do you say?"
D: Refashioned: The Athenian proposed the law: no poet shall compose anything contrary to the state's established ideas of what is lawful, just, beautiful, or good. Furthermore, no poet shall share his compositions with any private individual until he has submitted them to the appointed judges and guardians of the law and they are satisfied. The persons designated as musical legislators and the director of education have already been identified. He asked once more: shall this be their third law, type, and model?
D: Elegant: The law: no poet may compose anything contrary to the state's standards of the lawful, just, beautiful, or good, nor share compositions with anyone until approved by the appointed judges and guardians of the law.
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes that poets must submit all compositions for approval by judges and law-guardians before sharing them, and may compose nothing contrary to the state's established standards of justice, beauty, and goodness.
D: Distilled: Poets must have all compositions approved by state-appointed judges before publication and may create nothing contrary to the city's standards of justice and goodness.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This is the Laws' version of censorship, more institutional than the Republic's. In Republic 401b-d, Socrates envisions craftsmen supervised to produce only images of good character; here the mechanism is formalized into pre-publication review by judges and law-guardians. The four standards (lawful, just, beautiful, good) are Platonic cardinal values. The director of education (mentioned in Laws 6.765d-766b) is the most important magistrate in the state. This passage has been extensively discussed in debates about artistic freedom, state censorship, and Plato's legacy in political philosophy.

D: Original:
Let it be so, by all means.

Then it will be proper to have hymns and praises of the Gods, intermingled with prayers; and after the Gods prayers and praises should be offered in like manner to demigods and heroes, suitable to their several characters.

Certainly.

In the next place there will be no objection to a law, that citizens who are departed and have done good and energetic deeds, either with their souls or with their bodies, and have been obedient to the laws, should receive eulogies; this will be very fitting.

Quite true.
D: Narrative:
"Let it be so, by all means."

"Then it will be proper to have hymns and praises of the Gods, intermingled with prayers; and after the Gods prayers and praises should be offered in like manner to demigods and heroes, suitable to their several characters."

"Certainly."

"In the next place there will be no objection to a law, that citizens who are departed and have done good and energetic deeds, either with their souls or with their bodies, and have been obedient to the laws, should receive eulogies; this will be very fitting."

"Quite true."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed to the law. The Athenian then outlined the proper hierarchy of musical praise: first, hymns and praises of the gods intermingled with prayers; after the gods, similar prayers and praises for demigods and heroes, suited to their individual characters.

Cleinias approved. The Athenian added that there should be no objection to a law allowing eulogies for deceased citizens who performed good and vigorous deeds -- whether through their souls or their bodies -- and who were obedient to the laws. This would be entirely fitting.

Cleinias agreed.
D: Elegant: The proper order of song: first, hymns and prayers to the gods; then praises of demigods and heroes suited to their characters; finally, eulogies for deceased citizens who performed noble deeds and obeyed the laws.
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes a hierarchy for musical praise: gods first with hymns and prayers, then demigods and heroes, then deceased citizens who performed noble deeds and were law-abiding.
D: Distilled: Musical praise follows a hierarchy from gods to demigods to deceased citizens who lived nobly and obeyed the laws.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The hierarchy of hymnic praise -- gods, demigods/heroes, worthy dead -- follows traditional Greek religious practice. The inclusion of the dead who 'have done good and energetic deeds' (whether intellectual or physical) and 'have been obedient to the laws' establishes civic virtue as the criterion for memorialization. This connects to the funeral oration tradition (epitaphios logos) exemplified by Pericles' Funeral Oration in Thucydides 2.35-46. The next passage (7.33) importantly limits this to the dead only.

D: Original:
But to honour with hymns and panegyrics those who are still alive is not safe; a man should run his course, and make a fair ending, and then we will praise him; and let praise be given equally to women as well as men who have been distinguished in virtue. The order of songs and dances shall be as follows:-There are many ancient musical compositions and dances which are excellent, and from these the newly-founded city may freely select what is proper and suitable; and they shall choose judges of not less than fifty years of age, who shall make the selection, and any of the old poems which they deem sufficient they shall include; any that are deficient or altogether unsuitable, they shall either utterly throw aside, or examine and amend, taking into their counsel poets and musicians, and making use of their poetical genius; but explaining to them the wishes of the legislator in order that they may regulate dancing, music, and all choral strains, according to the mind of the judges; and not allowing them to indulge, except in some few matters, their individual pleasures and fancies. Now the irregular strain of music is always made ten thousand times better by attaining to law and order, and rejecting the honeyed Muse-not however that we mean wholly to exclude pleasure, which is the characteristic of all music. And if a man be brought up from childhood to the age of discretion and maturity in the use of the orderly and severe music, when he hears the opposite he detests it, and calls it illiberal; but if trained in the sweet and vulgar music, he deems the severer kind cold and displeasing. So that, as I was saying before, while he who hears them gains no more pleasure from the one than from the other, the one has the advantage of making those who are trained in it better men, whereas the other makes them worse.

Very true.
D: Narrative:
"But to honour with hymns and panegyrics those who are still alive is not safe; a man should run his course, and make a fair ending, and then we will praise him; and let praise be given equally to women as well as men who have been distinguished in virtue. The order of songs and dances shall be as follows: There are many ancient musical compositions and dances which are excellent, and from these the newly-founded city may freely select what is proper and suitable; and they shall choose judges of not less than fifty years of age, who shall make the selection, and any of the old poems which they deem sufficient they shall include; any that are deficient or altogether unsuitable, they shall either utterly throw aside, or examine and amend, taking into their counsel poets and musicians, and making use of their poetical genius; but explaining to them the wishes of the legislator in order that they may regulate dancing, music, and all choral strains, according to the mind of the judges; and not allowing them to indulge, except in some few matters, their individual pleasures and fancies.

"Now the irregular strain of music is always made ten thousand times better by attaining to law and order, and rejecting the honeyed Muse -- not however that we mean wholly to exclude pleasure, which is the characteristic of all music. And if a man be brought up from childhood to the age of discretion and maturity in the use of the orderly and severe music, when he hears the opposite he detests it, and calls it illiberal; but if trained in the sweet and vulgar music, he deems the severer kind cold and displeasing. So that, as I was saying before, while he who hears them gains no more pleasure from the one than from the other, the one has the advantage of making those who are trained in it better men, whereas the other makes them worse."

"Very true."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian added an important restriction: honoring the living with hymns and panegyrics is unsafe. A person should complete his life and make a good ending before receiving praise. And praise should be given equally to women and men who have distinguished themselves in virtue.

The procedure for songs and dances should be as follows: many excellent ancient compositions and dances exist, from which the new city may freely choose what is proper and suitable. Judges of at least fifty years of age shall make the selection. Old poems they deem adequate shall be retained; those that are deficient or unsuitable shall be either discarded entirely or revised. For revisions, they shall consult poets and musicians, using their creative talents -- but explaining the legislator's intentions so that all dancing, music, and choral work conforms to the judges' vision. Poets shall not be allowed, except in minor matters, to follow their individual pleasures and fancies.

Irregular music is always immeasurably improved by attaining law and order, by rejecting the seductive Muse -- though pleasure, being the characteristic of all music, should not be entirely excluded. A person raised from childhood on orderly, severe music will detest the opposite as vulgar; one raised on sweet, indulgent music will find the severe kind cold and unpleasant. Yet the listener derives equal pleasure from whichever style he was trained in. The difference is that orderly music makes people better while indulgent music makes them worse.

Cleinias agreed.
D: Elegant: Do not praise the living -- let a person finish their course before receiving hymns. Praise women and men equally for virtue. The city should select from excellent ancient compositions, with judges over fifty making choices and revising deficient works with help from poets -- who must follow the legislator's intent, not their own fancies. Orderly music is vastly superior to the indulgent kind: each produces equal pleasure in those trained on it, but one makes people better while the other makes them worse.
D: Summary: The Athenian forbids praising the living, requires equal praise for virtuous women and men, and establishes a system where elder judges select and revise ancient musical works with poets' help. Orderly music and indulgent music produce equal pleasure in their respective audiences, but orderly music builds better character.
D: Distilled: Praise only the dead equally regardless of sex, select music through elder judges who direct poets, and prefer orderly music that builds character over indulgent music that degrades it.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger with Cleinias responding (monologue). The prohibition on praising the living echoes Solon's famous warning to Croesus: 'count no man happy until he is dead' (Herodotus 1.32). Equal praise for women is progressive for the era. The system of elder judges reviewing and amending poetry is the Laws' institutional mechanism for the kind of poetic supervision outlined abstractly in the Republic. The argument about musical habituation -- that people enjoy whatever they are trained on, so the relevant criterion is moral effect, not pleasure -- is psychologically sophisticated and appears also at Laws 2.655d-656a. The 'honeyed Muse' recalls Republic 607a's 'sweet lyric muse.'

D: Original: Again, we must distinguish and determine on some general principle what songs are suitable to women, and what to men, and must assign to them their proper melodies and rhythms. It is shocking for a whole harmony to be inharmonical, or for a rhythm to be unrhythmical, and this will happen when the melody is inappropriate to them. And therefore the legislator must assign to these also their forms. Now both sexes have melodies and rhythms which of necessity belong to them; and those of women are clearly enough indicated by their natural difference. The grand, and that which tends to courage, may be fairly called manly; but that which inclines to moderation and temperance, may be declared both in law and in ordinary speech to be the more womanly quality. This, then, will be the general order of them.
D: Narrative: "Again, we must distinguish and determine on some general principle what songs are suitable to women, and what to men, and must assign to them their proper melodies and rhythms. It is shocking for a whole harmony to be inharmonical, or for a rhythm to be unrhythmical, and this will happen when the melody is inappropriate to them. And therefore the legislator must assign to these also their forms. Now both sexes have melodies and rhythms which of necessity belong to them; and those of women are clearly enough indicated by their natural difference. The grand, and that which tends to courage, may be fairly called manly; but that which inclines to moderation and temperance, may be declared both in law and in ordinary speech to be the more womanly quality. This, then, will be the general order of them."
D: Refashioned:
