The Athenian continued: they must also determine on some general principle which songs suit women and which suit men, assigning each sex its proper melodies and rhythms. It is intolerable for a harmony to be discordant or a rhythm to be irregular -- which is what happens when melody is mismatched to its subject. The legislator must therefore prescribe these forms.

Both sexes have melodies and rhythms that naturally belong to them, indicated by their inherent differences. The grand style tending toward courage may fairly be called masculine; the style inclining toward moderation and temperance may be called, in both law and common speech, more feminine. This shall be the general order.
D: Elegant: Songs must be distinguished by sex, with proper melodies and rhythms assigned to each. Mismatched melody produces disharmony. The grand and courageous style is masculine; the moderate and temperate style is feminine. The legislator must prescribe these forms.
D: Summary: The Athenian requires that different musical modes be assigned to men and women according to their natures -- grand and courageous music for men, moderate and temperate music for women -- to prevent disharmony between melody and character.
D: Distilled: Musical modes must match the natural characters of men and women: grand and courageous for men, moderate and temperate for women.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The assignment of musical modes by gender reflects the Greek theory of ethos -- that specific musical modes embody specific character traits. Compare Republic 398c-399c, where Socrates retains only the Dorian (manly/courageous) and Phrygian (moderate/persuasive) modes. The gender essentialism here is typical of ancient thought but the Laws is notably more inclusive of women in education than most Greek practice. The technical musical terms -- harmony, rhythm, melody -- had precise meanings in Greek music theory (see Aristoxenus, Harmonics).

D: Original:
Let us now speak of the manner of teaching and imparting them, and the persons to whom, and the time when, they are severally to be imparted. As the shipwright first lays down the lines of the keel, and thus, as it were, draws the ship in outline, so do I seek to distinguish the patterns of life, and lay down their keels according to the nature of different men's souls; seeking truly to consider by what means, and in what ways, we may go through the voyage of life best. Now human affairs are hardly worth considering in earnest, and yet we must be in earnest about them-a sad necessity constrains us. And having got thus far, there will be a fitness in our completing the matter, if we can only find some suitable method of doing so. But what do I mean? Some one may ask this very question, and quite rightly, too.

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"Let us now speak of the manner of teaching and imparting them, and the persons to whom, and the time when, they are severally to be imparted. As the shipwright first lays down the lines of the keel, and thus, as it were, draws the ship in outline, so do I seek to distinguish the patterns of life, and lay down their keels according to the nature of different men's souls; seeking truly to consider by what means, and in what ways, we may go through the voyage of life best.

"Now human affairs are hardly worth considering in earnest, and yet we must be in earnest about them -- a sad necessity constrains us. And having got thus far, there will be a fitness in our completing the matter, if we can only find some suitable method of doing so. But what do I mean? Some one may ask this very question, and quite rightly, too."

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian turned to the practical question of how, to whom, and when these teachings should be imparted. He used an analogy: just as a shipwright first lays down the keel and thereby draws the ship in outline, so the Athenian was seeking to distinguish the patterns of life and lay down their foundations according to the different natures of human souls -- truly considering by what means and in what ways they might navigate the voyage of life best.

He then offered a remarkable aside: human affairs are hardly worth taking seriously, and yet we must take them seriously -- a melancholy necessity compels us. Having come this far, there would be a fitness in completing the task, if they could find a suitable method. But what exactly did he mean? Someone might rightly ask that question.

Cleinias agreed.
D: Elegant: As a shipwright lays the keel to outline the ship, so must we lay down the patterns of life according to different souls' natures, seeking the best way to navigate life's voyage. Human affairs are hardly worth taking seriously -- yet sad necessity compels us to be earnest about them anyway.
D: Summary: Using the metaphor of a shipwright laying a keel, the Athenian describes his method of establishing life patterns suited to different souls. He then offers the famous aside that human affairs hardly deserve earnest attention, yet necessity compels seriousness.
D: Distilled: Like a shipwright laying a keel, the legislator must establish life patterns suited to different souls, even though human affairs are hardly worth the earnestness that necessity forces upon us.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger with Cleinias (dialogue). The ship-building metaphor is characteristically Greek -- ships were central to Mediterranean life. The remarkable statement that 'human affairs are hardly worth considering in earnest' is one of the most quoted passages in the Laws and connects to the puppet metaphor in Laws 1.644d-645b and the fuller development in 7.36-38. This 'pessimistic' note is not nihilistic but rather a call to proportion -- human seriousness should be directed toward the divine. Compare Republic 486a, where the philosopher views human life from a cosmic perspective.

D: Original:
I say that about serious matters a man should be serious, and about a matter which is not serious he should not be, serious; and that God is the natural and worthy object of our most serious and blessed endeavours, for man, as I said before, is made to be the plaything of God, and this, truly considered, is the best of him; wherefore also every man and woman should walk seriously, and pass life in the noblest of pastimes, and be of another mind from what they are at present.

In what respect?
D: Narrative:
"I say that about serious matters a man should be serious, and about a matter which is not serious he should not be serious; and that God is the natural and worthy object of our most serious and blessed endeavours, for man, as I said before, is made to be the plaything of God, and this, truly considered, is the best of him; wherefore also every man and woman should walk seriously, and pass life in the noblest of pastimes, and be of another mind from what they are at present."

"In what respect?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian elaborated: about serious matters, one should be serious; about what is not serious, one should not be. God is the natural and worthy object of our most serious and blessed efforts. For humankind, as he had said before, was made to be the plaything of God -- and this, properly understood, is the best thing about us. Therefore every man and woman should conduct themselves with gravity, passing life in the noblest of pastimes, and thinking quite differently from how they think now.

Cleinias asked in what respect they should think differently.
D: Elegant: Be serious about what is truly serious. God is the proper object of our deepest efforts. Humankind was made to be God's plaything -- and this is the best thing about us. Therefore live in the noblest pastimes and think differently from how you think now.
D: Summary: The Athenian declares that God alone deserves our deepest seriousness. Humanity was made to be God's plaything, which is paradoxically the highest thing about us. People should therefore rethink their values and devote life to the noblest pastimes.
D: Distilled: God alone deserves ultimate seriousness; humanity's highest dignity is being God's plaything, which calls us to devote life to the noblest pursuits.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger with Cleinias (dialogue). The 'plaything of God' (theou paignion) is one of the Laws' most provocative metaphors, first introduced at 1.644d-645b with the puppet analogy. Rather than degrading humanity, it reorients human seriousness away from worldly affairs toward divine play -- a theology of sacred playfulness. This connects to Heraclitus fr. 52: 'Time is a child playing, moving pieces in a game.' The idea that the best human life consists in 'the noblest pastimes' (playing seriously) is developed further in 7.37 as a life of sacrifice, song, and dance.

D: Original: At present they think that their serious suits should be for the sake of their sports, for they deem war a serious. pursuit, which must be managed well for the sake of peace; but the truth is, that there neither is, nor has been, nor ever will be, either amusement or instruction in any degree worth, speaking of in war, which is nevertheless deemed by us to be the most serious of our pursuits. And therefore, as we say, every one of us should live the life of peace as long and as well as he can. And what is the right way of living? Are we to live in sports always? If so, in what kind of sports? We ought to live sacrificing, and singing, and dancing, and then a man will be able to propitiate the Gods, and to defend himself against his enemies and conquer them in battle. The type of song or dance by which he will propitiate them has been described, and the paths along which he is to proceed have been cut for him. He will go forward in the spirit of the poet:
D: Narrative: "At present they think that their serious pursuits should be for the sake of their sports, for they deem war a serious pursuit, which must be managed well for the sake of peace; but the truth is, that there neither is, nor has been, nor ever will be, either amusement or instruction in any degree worth speaking of in war, which is nevertheless deemed by us to be the most serious of our pursuits. And therefore, as we say, every one of us should live the life of peace as long and as well as he can. And what is the right way of living? Are we to live in sports always? If so, in what kind of sports? We ought to live sacrificing, and singing, and dancing, and then a man will be able to propitiate the Gods, and to defend himself against his enemies and conquer them in battle. The type of song or dance by which he will propitiate them has been described, and the paths along which he is to proceed have been cut for him. He will go forward in the spirit of the poet:"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian explained: people currently believe their serious pursuits exist for the sake of their leisure -- they consider war a serious business that must be managed well so they can enjoy peace. But the truth is that war contains nothing of real amusement or instruction worth mentioning, despite being considered the most serious of all pursuits.

Therefore, everyone should live the life of peace as long and as well as possible. But what is the right way to live? Should we live in play always? If so, what kind of play? We ought to live sacrificing, singing, and dancing -- and through this a person will be able to propitiate the gods, defend against enemies, and conquer in battle. The types of song and dance that propitiate the gods have been described, and the paths have been laid out. The citizen will proceed in the spirit of the poet --
D: Elegant: People wrongly think war is serious and peace is for play. The truth is reversed: war contains nothing of real value. Live in peace as long and well as possible. The right life consists of sacrificing, singing, and dancing -- this propitiates the gods and prepares for defense against enemies. The path has been laid out.
D: Summary: The Athenian inverts conventional values: war, though deemed the most serious pursuit, contains nothing of real worth, while the life of peace -- spent in sacrifice, song, and dance -- is both the highest good and the best preparation for defense.
D: Distilled: War is not truly serious; the right life consists of peaceful sacrifice, song, and dance, which both honors the gods and best prepares citizens for defense.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This is one of the Laws' most radical claims -- the inversion of the Greek warrior ethic. The idea that war serves peace (not vice versa) challenges the militaristic values of Sparta and Crete, the very cultures of his interlocutors. Compare Laws 1.628c-e, where the Athenian first argues that the true aim of law is peace, not victory. The vision of life as sacred play (sacrifice, song, dance) develops the 'plaything of God' theme from 7.36. The unnamed poet quoted next (7.38) is Homer.

D: Original:
Telemachus, some things thou wilt thyself find in thy heart, but other things God will suggest; for I deem that thou wast not brought up without the will of the Gods. And this ought to be the view of our alumni; they ought to think that what has been said is enough for them, and that any other things their Genius and God will suggest to them-he will tell them to whom, and when, and to what Gods severally they are to sacrifice and perform dances, and how they may propitiate the deities, and live according to the appointment of nature; being for the most part puppets, but having some little share of reality.

You have a low opinion of mankind, Stranger.

Nay, Megillus, be not amazed, but forgive me:-I was comparing them with the Gods; and under that feeling I spoke. Let us grant, if you wish, that the human race is not to be despised, but is worthy of some consideration.
D: Narrative:
"Telemachus, some things thou wilt thyself find in thy heart, but other things God will suggest; for I deem that thou wast not brought up without the will of the Gods."

"And this ought to be the view of our alumni; they ought to think that what has been said is enough for them, and that any other things their Genius and God will suggest to them -- he will tell them to whom, and when, and to what Gods severally they are to sacrifice and perform dances, and how they may propitiate the deities, and live according to the appointment of nature; being for the most part puppets, but having some little share of reality."

"You have a low opinion of mankind, Stranger."

"Nay, Megillus, be not amazed, but forgive me -- I was comparing them with the Gods; and under that feeling I spoke. Let us grant, if you wish, that the human race is not to be despised, but is worthy of some consideration."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian quoted Homer, addressing Telemachus: "Some things you will find in your own heart, but other things God will suggest; for I believe you were not raised without the will of the Gods."

This, he said, should be the view of their graduates: what has been taught is sufficient, and whatever else they need, their divine guardian and God will suggest to them -- telling them to whom and when and to which gods they should sacrifice and dance, and how to propitiate the deities and live according to the appointment of nature. Humans are for the most part puppets, though they possess some small share of reality.

Megillus objected: "You have a low opinion of mankind, Stranger." The Athenian asked forgiveness -- he was comparing humans with the gods, and it was in that light he spoke. He conceded: if they wish, let them grant that the human race is not to be despised but is worthy of some consideration.
D: Elegant: As Homer told Telemachus: some things you will find in your heart, others God will suggest. Our graduates should trust their education and their divine guidance. Humans are mostly puppets, with a small share of reality. This is not contempt -- it is what we are when measured against the gods. But the human race is still worthy of consideration.
D: Summary: Quoting Homer on Telemachus, the Athenian says graduates should trust both their education and divine guidance. He reaffirms that humans are mostly puppets with a small share of reality -- not from contempt, but measured against the divine standard. Megillus objects to this, and the Athenian softens his position.
D: Distilled: Graduates should trust their education and divine guidance, understanding that humans are mostly puppets compared to the gods -- not despicable, but measured against a higher reality.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Megillus (dialogue). The Homer quotation is from Odyssey 3.26-28, spoken by Athena (disguised as Mentor) to Telemachus. The puppet metaphor returns from Laws 1.644d-645b, now explicitly connecting 'having some share of reality' with the puppet's divine strings. Megillus' objection ('You have a low opinion of mankind') shows the provocative effect of this theology. The Athenian's graceful retreat ('I was comparing them with the gods') reveals that his apparent pessimism is actually a statement about proportional perspective -- humans matter, but not in the way they think.

D: Original: Next follow the buildings for gymnasia and schools open to all; these are to be in three places in the midst of the city; and outside the city and in the surrounding country, also in three places, there shall be schools for horse exercise, and large grounds arranged with a view to archery and the throwing of missiles, at which young men may learn and practise. Of these mention has already been made, and if the mention be not sufficiently explicit, let us speak, further of them and embody them in laws. In these several schools let there be dwellings for teachers, who shall be brought from foreign parts by pay, and let them teach those who attend the schools the art of war and the art of music, and the children shall come not only if their parents please, but if they do not please; there shall be compulsory education, as the saying is, of all and sundry, as far this is possible; and the pupils shall be regarded as belonging to the state rather than to their parents. My law would apply to females as well as males; they shall both go through the same exercises. I assert without fear of contradiction that gymnastic and horsemanship are as suitable to women as to men. Of the truth of this I am persuaded from ancient tradition, and at the present day there are said to be countless myriads of women in the neighbourhood of the Black Sea, called Sauromatides, who not only ride on horseback like men, but have enjoined upon them the use of bows and other weapons equally with the men. And I further affirm, that if these things are possible, nothing can be more absurd than the practice which prevails in our own country, of men and women not following the same pursuits with all their strength and with one mind, for thus the state, instead of being a whole, is reduced to a half, but has the same imposts to pay and the same toils to undergo; and what can be a greater mistake for any legislator to make than this?
D: Narrative:
"Next follow the buildings for gymnasia and schools open to all; these are to be in three places in the midst of the city; and outside the city and in the surrounding country, also in three places, there shall be schools for horse exercise, and large grounds arranged with a view to archery and the throwing of missiles, at which young men may learn and practise. Of these mention has already been made, and if the mention be not sufficiently explicit, let us speak further of them and embody them in laws. In these several schools let there be dwellings for teachers, who shall be brought from foreign parts by pay, and let them teach those who attend the schools the art of war and the art of music, and the children shall come not only if their parents please, but if they do not please; there shall be compulsory education, as the saying is, of all and sundry, as far as this is possible; and the pupils shall be regarded as belonging to the state rather than to their parents.

"My law would apply to females as well as males; they shall both go through the same exercises. I assert without fear of contradiction that gymnastic and horsemanship are as suitable to women as to men. Of the truth of this I am persuaded from ancient tradition, and at the present day there are said to be countless myriads of women in the neighbourhood of the Black Sea, called Sauromatides, who not only ride on horseback like men, but have enjoined upon them the use of bows and other weapons equally with the men. And I further affirm, that if these things are possible, nothing can be more absurd than the practice which prevails in our own country, of men and women not following the same pursuits with all their strength and with one mind, for thus the state, instead of being a whole, is reduced to a half, but has the same imposts to pay and the same toils to undergo; and what can be a greater mistake for any legislator to make than this?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian turned to infrastructure: gymnasia and schools open to all should be built in three locations within the city. Outside the city and in the surrounding countryside, three more schools should be established for horse training and large grounds for archery and missile practice. Teachers, hired from abroad, should live on-site and teach both the arts of war and music. Children shall attend not only when their parents agree but even when they do not -- education shall be compulsory for everyone, as far as possible, and pupils shall be regarded as belonging to the state rather than to their parents.

His law would apply equally to females and males; both shall undergo the same exercises. He asserted without fear of contradiction that gymnastic and horsemanship are as suitable to women as to men. Ancient tradition and present evidence proved this -- the Sauromatides near the Black Sea, countless in number, not only rode horses like men but were required to use bows and other weapons equally. If such things are possible, nothing is more absurd than the current practice of men and women not pursuing the same activities with all their strength and one mind. The state is thereby reduced from a whole to a half, yet must bear the same taxes and endure the same hardships. No greater legislative error is conceivable.
D: Elegant: Three gymnasia in the city, three riding and archery schools outside it, with foreign-hired teachers living on-site. Education is compulsory for all -- children belong to the state, not their parents. Women and men shall undergo the same exercises. The Sauromatides prove women can ride and fight equally. A state that educates only its men reduces itself to half its potential while bearing full costs -- the greatest legislative blunder possible.
D: Summary: The Athenian mandates compulsory co-educational schooling with hired foreign teachers, three urban gymnasia and three rural training grounds. He argues forcefully for equal education of women, citing the Sauromatides as evidence, declaring that excluding women halves the state's capacity while maintaining full costs.
D: Distilled: Education must be compulsory and equal for both sexes, with children belonging to the state rather than parents, because excluding women halves the state's strength while bearing full burdens.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This is the Laws' most radical passage on women's education. The Sauromatides (Sarmatians) were a real people near the Black Sea -- Herodotus (4.110-117) tells their origin story from Amazons intermarrying with Scythians. Archaeological evidence confirms Sarmatian women were buried with weapons. The principle of compulsory education and children 'belonging to the state' echoes Republic 457c-461e but goes further in its institutional specificity. The economic argument -- a half-educated state bearing full costs -- is characteristic of the Laws' practical reasoning. Compare Republic 451c-457b on women guardians.

D: Original: Very true; yet much of what has been asserted by us, Stranger is contrary to the custom of states; still, in saying that the discourse should be allowed to proceed, and that when the discussion is completed, we should choose what seems best, you spoke very properly, and I now feel compunction for what I have said. Tell me, then, what you would next wish to say.
D: Narrative: "Very true; yet much of what has been asserted by us, Stranger, is contrary to the custom of states; still, in saying that the discourse should be allowed to proceed, and that when the discussion is completed, we should choose what seems best, you spoke very properly, and I now feel compunction for what I have said. Tell me, then, what you would next wish to say."
D: Refashioned: Cleinias acknowledged that much of what had been proposed was contrary to the customs of existing states. Nevertheless, the Athenian had been right to say that the discussion should proceed and that they would choose the best conclusions at the end. Cleinias felt some remorse for any resistance he had shown and invited the Athenian to continue with whatever he wished to say next.
D: Elegant: Cleinias admits their proposals contradict existing customs but agrees the discussion should proceed to completion before judgment. He feels remorse for earlier resistance and invites the Athenian to continue.
D: Summary: Cleinias acknowledges that their proposals are radical and contrary to existing practice, but agrees with the method of letting the discussion proceed to completion before choosing what seems best. He expresses some regret for earlier objections.
D: Distilled: Cleinias accepts that radical proposals should be fully explored before judgment, expressing regret for earlier resistance to unconventional ideas.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias (monologue). This is a significant dramatic moment -- Cleinias, the practical Cretan legislator, acknowledges being persuaded against his initial instincts. His 'compunction' (metameleia) for earlier resistance shows the dialogue's persuasive process at work. The methodological principle -- proceed with the full discussion before choosing -- is important for the Laws' approach: theoretical completeness before practical selection. This willingness to entertain radical ideas from a conservative Cretan is one of the Laws' most effective dramatic techniques.

D: Original:
I should wish to say, Cleinias, as I said before, that if the possibility of these things were not sufficiently proven in fact, then there might be an objection to the argument, but the fact being as I have said, he who rejects the law must find some other ground of objection; and, failing this, our exhortation will still hold good, nor will any one deny that women ought to share as far as possible in education and in other ways with men. For consider;-if women do not share in their whole life with men, then they must have some other order of life.

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"I should wish to say, Cleinias, as I said before, that if the possibility of these things were not sufficiently proven in fact, then there might be an objection to the argument, but the fact being as I have said, he who rejects the law must find some other ground of objection; and, failing this, our exhortation will still hold good, nor will any one deny that women ought to share as far as possible in education and in other ways with men. For consider -- if women do not share in their whole life with men, then they must have some other order of life."

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian told Cleinias that if the possibility of women's equal participation were not proven by actual evidence, there might be grounds for objection. But since the facts are as stated, anyone rejecting the law must find some other basis for objection. Failing that, the exhortation stands: women ought to share in education and other activities with men as far as possible.

Consider the logic: if women do not share in the whole of life with men, then they must have some entirely different order of life. Cleinias agreed that this followed necessarily.
D: Elegant: If the facts did not support women's capability, there would be grounds for objection. But since they do, anyone rejecting equal education must find other arguments. The logic is simple: if women do not share in life with men, they must have a separate order of life entirely.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that since real-world evidence proves women can participate in martial and physical training, objectors must find other grounds to reject equal education. The logical alternative -- a completely separate order of life for women -- is the only other option.
D: Distilled: Since evidence proves women's capability, objectors to equal education must either find other grounds or accept that women need an entirely separate social order.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The Athenian employs an empirical argument -- appealing to the Sauromatides and other evidence -- rather than pure philosophical reasoning. This is characteristic of the Laws' more grounded approach compared to the Republic's abstract arguments for women guardians (Republic 455c-456a). The dilemma posed (share in men's life or have a separate order) anticipates modern debates about gender equality: integration versus separate spheres. The Athenian clearly favors integration.

D: Original: And what arrangement of life to be found anywhere is preferable to this community which we are now assigning to them? Shall we prefer that which is adopted by the Thracians and many other races who use their women to till the ground and to be shepherds of their herds and flocks, and to minister to them like slaves?-Or shall we do as we and people in our part of the world do-getting together, as the phrase is, all our goods and chattels into one dwelling, we entrust them to our women, who are the stewards of them, and who also preside over the shuttles and the whole art of spinning? Or shall we take a middle course, in Lacedaemon, Megillus-letting the girls share in gymnastic and music, while the grown-up women, no longer employed in spinning wool, are hard at work weaving the web of life, which will be no cheap or mean employment, and in the duty of serving and taking care of the household and bringing up children, in which they will observe a sort of mean, not participating in the toils of war; and if there were any necessity that they should fight for their city and families, unlike the Amazons, they would be unable to take part in archery or any other skilled use of missiles, nor could they, after the example of the Goddess, carry shield or spear, or stand up nobly for their country when it was being destroyed, and strike terror into their enemies, if only because they were seen in regular order? Living as they do, they would never dare at all to imitate the Sauromatides, who, when compared with ordinary women, would appear to be like men. Let him who will, praise your legislators, but I must say what I think. The legislator ought to be whole and perfect, and not half a man only; he ought not to let the female sex live softly and waste money and have no order of life, while he takes the utmost care of the male sex, and leaves half of life only blest with happiness, when he might have made the whole state happy.
D: Narrative: "And what arrangement of life to be found anywhere is preferable to this community which we are now assigning to them? Shall we prefer that which is adopted by the Thracians and many other races who use their women to till the ground and to be shepherds of their herds and flocks, and to minister to them like slaves? -- Or shall we do as we and people in our part of the world do -- getting together, as the phrase is, all our goods and chattels into one dwelling, we entrust them to our women, who are the stewards of them, and who also preside over the shuttles and the whole art of spinning? Or shall we take a middle course, in Lacedaemon, Megillus -- letting the girls share in gymnastic and music, while the grown-up women, no longer employed in spinning wool, are hard at work weaving the web of life, which will be no cheap or mean employment, and in the duty of serving and taking care of the household and bringing up children, in which they will observe a sort of mean, not participating in the toils of war; and if there were any necessity that they should fight for their city and families, unlike the Amazons, they would be unable to take part in archery or any other skilled use of missiles, nor could they, after the example of the Goddess, carry shield or spear, or stand up nobly for their country when it was being destroyed, and strike terror into their enemies, if only because they were seen in regular order? Living as they do, they would never dare at all to imitate the Sauromatides, who, when compared with ordinary women, would appear to be like men. Let him who will, praise your legislators, but I must say what I think. The legislator ought to be whole and perfect, and not half a man only; he ought not to let the female sex live softly and waste money and have no order of life, while he takes the utmost care of the male sex, and leaves half of life only blest with happiness, when he might have made the whole state happy."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian surveyed the alternatives. What arrangement of life found anywhere is better than the community they are now establishing? Should they follow the Thracians and other peoples who use their women to till the soil, tend herds and flocks, and serve like slaves? Or should they follow the common Greek practice -- gathering all possessions into one household, entrusting them to women as stewards who preside over spinning and weaving?

Or should they take the middle course of Sparta -- where girls share in gymnastic and music, but grown women, no longer spinning wool, are occupied with the demanding work of household management and child-rearing? In Sparta women observe a kind of moderate position, not participating in the labor of war. But should necessity arise for them to fight for their city and families, unlike the Amazons they would be unable to use bows or missiles skillfully, nor could they carry shield and spear like Athena, standing in regular formation to defend their country and strike terror into enemies. Living as they do, they could never imitate the Sauromatides, who compared to ordinary women appear to be men.

Let whoever wishes praise the Spartan legislators, the Athenian concluded, but he must speak his mind. A legislator should be whole and complete, not half a man. He should not let the female sex live softly, waste money, and have no order of life while lavishing every care on males alone -- leaving only half of life blessed with happiness when he might have made the whole state happy.
D: Elegant: The alternatives are all worse. The Thracian model uses women as farm slaves. The common Greek model confines them to household stewardship and spinning. Even the Spartan middle way -- girls in athletics but women excluded from military training -- leaves them unable to defend the city in crisis. A legislator who neglects half the population is half a legislator. He should not let women live without order while lavishing care on men alone, when he could make the whole state happy.
D: Summary: The Athenian compares three models for women's roles -- Thracian servitude, Greek domesticity, and Spartan partial athletics -- finding all inadequate. Even Sparta leaves women unable to defend the city. A complete legislator must care equally for both sexes rather than making only half the state happy.
D: Distilled: Every existing model for women's roles -- slave labor, household confinement, or partial Spartan training -- leaves the state half-developed; a true legislator makes the whole state happy by educating both sexes fully.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This is the Laws' most comprehensive comparative analysis of women's social roles. The three models -- Thracian (barbaric subordination), Athenian (domestic confinement), Spartan (partial inclusion) -- represent the actual range of Greek experience. The Athenian boldly criticizes even Sparta, directly challenging Megillus' civic pride. The reference to Athena (the Goddess) carrying shield and spear invokes divine precedent for women warriors. The metaphor of the 'half legislator' who makes 'half the state happy' is economically precise. Compare Republic 451c-457b, which makes similar arguments but in a more abstract philosophical framework.

D: Original:
What shall we do, Cleinias? Shall we allow a stranger to run down Sparta in this fashion?

Yes; for as we have given him liberty of speech we must let him go on until we have perfected the work of legislation.

Very true.

Then now I may proceed?

By all means.
D: Narrative:
"What shall we do, Cleinias? Shall we allow a stranger to run down Sparta in this fashion?"

"Yes; for as we have given him liberty of speech we must let him go on until we have perfected the work of legislation."

"Very true."

"Then now I may proceed?"

"By all means."
D: Refashioned:
Megillus turned to Cleinias: should they allow a stranger to criticize Sparta so freely? Cleinias replied that since they had granted him liberty of speech, they must let him continue until the work of legislation was complete.

The Athenian confirmed this was fair and asked if he might proceed. They urged him onward.
D: Elegant: Megillus asks Cleinias whether they should tolerate a stranger criticizing Sparta. Cleinias replies that having granted liberty of speech, they must see the legislative work through. The Athenian proceeds.
D: Summary: Megillus bristles at the criticism of Sparta, but Cleinias insists they must allow the Athenian to continue since they have granted him freedom of speech and the legislative project must be completed.
D: Distilled: Megillus objects to the criticism of Sparta, but Cleinias insists intellectual liberty must be maintained for the sake of completing the legislative project.
Notes: Speakers: Megillus, Cleinias, and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). This brief exchange is dramatically rich. Megillus' Spartan patriotic pride surfaces as defensive indignation. Cleinias' response is philosophically mature -- he prioritizes the integrity of the inquiry over national sensitivities. This models the Laws' ideal of open discussion in the service of legislation. The dynamic recalls Gorgias 461d-462a where Socrates insists on frank speech (parrhesia) as necessary for philosophical inquiry.

D: Original: What will be the manner of life among men who may be supposed to have their food and clothing provided for them in moderation, and who have entrusted the practice of the arts to others, and whose husbandry, committed to slaves paying a part of the produce, brings them a return sufficient for men living temperately; who, moreover, have common tables in which the men are placed apart, and near them are the common tables of their families, of their daughters and mothers, which day by day, the officers, male and female, are to inspect-they shall see to the behaviour of the company, and so dismiss them; after which the presiding magistrate and his attendants shall honour with libations those Gods to whom that day and night are dedicated, and then go home? To men whose lives are thus ordered, is there no work remaining to be done which is necessary and fitting, but shall each one of them live fattening like a beast? Such a life is neither just nor honourable, nor can he who lives it fail of meeting his due; and the due reward of the idle fatted beast is that he should be torn in pieces by some other valiant beast whose fatness is worn down by brave deeds and toil. These regulations, if we duly consider them, will never be exactly carried into execution under present circumstances, nor as long as women and children and houses and all other things are the private property of individuals; but if we can attain the second-best form of polity, we shall be very well off. And to men living under this second polity there remains a work to be accomplished which is far from being small or insignificant, but is the greatest of all works, and ordained by the appointment of righteous law. For the life which may be truly said to be concerned with the virtue of body and soul is twice, or more than twice, as full of toil and trouble as the pursuit after Pythian and Olympic victories, which debars a man from every employment of life.
D: Narrative:
"What will be the manner of life among men who may be supposed to have their food and clothing provided for them in moderation, and who have entrusted the practice of the arts to others, and whose husbandry, committed to slaves paying a part of the produce, brings them a return sufficient for men living temperately; who, moreover, have common tables in which the men are placed apart, and near them are the common tables of their families, of their daughters and mothers, which day by day, the officers, male and female, are to inspect -- they shall see to the behaviour of the company, and so dismiss them; after which the presiding magistrate and his attendants shall honour with libations those Gods to whom that day and night are dedicated, and then go home?

"To men whose lives are thus ordered, is there no work remaining to be done which is necessary and fitting, but shall each one of them live fattening like a beast? Such a life is neither just nor honourable, nor can he who lives it fail of meeting his due; and the due reward of the idle fatted beast is that he should be torn in pieces by some other valiant beast whose fatness is worn down by brave deeds and toil. These regulations, if we duly consider them, will never be exactly carried into execution under present circumstances, nor as long as women and children and houses and all other things are the private property of individuals; but if we can attain the second-best form of polity, we shall be very well off. And to men living under this second polity there remains a work to be accomplished which is far from being small or insignificant, but is the greatest of all works, and ordained by the appointment of righteous law. For the life which may be truly said to be concerned with the virtue of body and soul is twice, or more than twice, as full of toil and trouble as the pursuit after Pythian and Olympic victories, which debars a man from every employment of life."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian described the situation: their citizens would have food and clothing provided in moderation, the practice of arts delegated to others, and farming committed to slaves who pay a portion of their produce -- a return sufficient for temperate living. They would eat at common tables, men seated separately with their families' tables nearby for daughters and mothers. Officers, both male and female, would inspect behavior daily and dismiss the company, after which the presiding magistrate would honor the day's patron gods with libations before going home.

For men whose lives are so ordered, is there no necessary and fitting work remaining? Should they live fattening themselves like beasts? Such a life is neither just nor honorable, and the person who lives it will meet his due: the idle, fattened beast is inevitably torn apart by some lean, valiant beast hardened by brave deeds and toil.

These ideal regulations will never be fully implemented under current conditions, where women, children, houses, and property remain private. But if they can achieve the second-best form of government, they will do very well. Under this second-best polity, there remains a task of the greatest magnitude, ordained by righteous law. The life truly concerned with virtue of body and soul demands twice or more than twice the toil and dedication of training for Pythian and Olympic victories -- which already require a person to abandon every other occupation in life.
D: Elegant: Citizens with basic needs met and arts delegated must not live like fattened beasts -- such idleness invites destruction by leaner, harder rivals. The ideal state with full communal property cannot be achieved now, but the second-best polity demands a work greater than any other: pursuing virtue of body and soul, which requires more than twice the dedication of Olympic training.
D: Summary: The Athenian warns that citizens with material security must not become idle -- like fattened beasts destined to be destroyed by hardened rivals. The ideal communal state is unachievable, but the second-best polity demands a greater commitment to virtue than even Olympic training requires.
D: Distilled: Material security without purposeful virtue-training produces idle citizens destined for destruction, and the second-best state demands more dedication than Olympic athletics.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The 'second-best polity' (deuteros plous, literally 'second sailing') is the Laws' own constitutional proposal, explicitly distinguished from the ideal state of the Republic where property, women, and children are held in common (Republic 449c-466d). The Pythian games were held at Delphi, the Olympic games at Olympia -- both required full-time training. The vivid metaphor of the fattened beast torn by the lean one echoes Heraclitus on war (fr. 53) and the Laws' earlier insistence that peace, not war, is the true goal but that preparation must be constant.

D: Original: For there ought to be no bye-work interfering with the greater work of providing the necessary exercise and nourishment for the body, and instruction and education for the soul. Night and day are not long enough for the accomplishment of their perfection and consummation; and therefore to this end all freemen ought to arrange the way in which they will spend their time during the whole course of the day, from morning till evening and from evening till the morning of the next sunrise. There may seem to be some impropriety in the legislator determining minutely the numberless details of the management of the house, including such particulars as the duty of wakefulness in those who are to be perpetual watchmen of the whole city; for that any citizen should continue during the whole of any night in sleep, instead of being seen by all his servants, always the first to awake and get up-this, whether the regulation is to be called a law or only a practice, should be deemed base and unworthy of a freeman; also that the mistress of the house should be awakened by her handmaidens instead of herself first awakening them, is what the slaves, male and female, and the serving-boys, and, if that were possible, everybody and everything in the house should regard as base. If they rise early, they may all of them do much of their public and of their household business, as magistrates in the city, and masters and mistresses in their private houses, before the sun is up. Much sleep is not required by nature, either for our souls or bodies, or for the actions which they perform. For no one who is asleep is good for anything, any more than if he were dead; but he of us who has the most regard for life and reason keeps awake as long he can, reserving only so much time for sleep as is expedient for health; and much sleep is not required, if the habit of moderation be once rightly formed. Magistrates in states who keep awake at night are terrible to the bad, whether enemies or citizens, and are honoured and reverenced by the just and temperate, and are useful to themselves and to the whole state.
D: Narrative:
"For there ought to be no bye-work interfering with the greater work of providing the necessary exercise and nourishment for the body, and instruction and education for the soul. Night and day are not long enough for the accomplishment of their perfection and consummation; and therefore to this end all freemen ought to arrange the way in which they will spend their time during the whole course of the day, from morning till evening and from evening till the morning of the next sunrise.

"There may seem to be some impropriety in the legislator determining minutely the numberless details of the management of the house, including such particulars as the duty of wakefulness in those who are to be perpetual watchmen of the whole city; for that any citizen should continue during the whole of any night in sleep, instead of being seen by all his servants, always the first to awake and get up -- this, whether the regulation is to be called a law or only a practice, should be deemed base and unworthy of a freeman; also that the mistress of the house should be awakened by her handmaidens instead of herself first awakening them, is what the slaves, male and female, and the serving-boys, and, if that were possible, everybody and everything in the house should regard as base.

"If they rise early, they may all of them do much of their public and of their household business, as magistrates in the city, and masters and mistresses in their private houses, before the sun is up. Much sleep is not required by nature, either for our souls or bodies, or for the actions which they perform. For no one who is asleep is good for anything, any more than if he were dead; but he of us who has the most regard for life and reason keeps awake as long as he can, reserving only so much time for sleep as is expedient for health; and much sleep is not required, if the habit of moderation be once rightly formed. Magistrates in states who keep awake at night are terrible to the bad, whether enemies or citizens, and are honoured and reverenced by the just and temperate, and are useful to themselves and to the whole state."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian insisted there should be no secondary occupation interfering with the primary work of providing proper exercise and nourishment for the body and instruction and education for the soul. Night and day together are not long enough to achieve perfection in these matters. Therefore all free citizens must plan how they spend every hour, from morning to evening and from evening to the next sunrise.

It may seem improper for a legislator to determine minute household details, but certain practices are essential. Any citizen who sleeps through an entire night, instead of being the first in the household to wake -- whether this counts as law or custom -- should be considered base and unworthy of a free person. Likewise, any mistress who is awakened by her servants instead of waking them first should be viewed with contempt by the entire household.

By rising early, citizens can accomplish much public and private business before sunrise. Nature does not require much sleep for either soul or body. A sleeping person is good for nothing, no more than if dead. Whoever most values life and reason stays awake as long as possible, sleeping only as much as health requires -- and properly established habits of moderation reduce even that requirement. Magistrates who stay wakeful at night inspire terror in wrongdoers, whether foreign enemies or domestic criminals, and are honored by the just and temperate. They serve both themselves and the entire state.
D: Elegant: No secondary work should interfere with exercising the body and educating the soul -- the full day is barely sufficient. Free citizens must plan every hour. Sleeping through the night is base; the master and mistress should wake before their servants. Nature requires little sleep. A sleeping person is as useless as a dead one. Wakeful magistrates terrify wrongdoers and inspire the just.
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that virtue-training requires every waking hour, making excessive sleep shameful. Free citizens should always rise before their servants. Nature requires minimal sleep, and wakeful magistrates both deter crime and inspire respect.
D: Distilled: The pursuit of bodily and spiritual excellence requires every waking hour, making excessive sleep base and early rising a mark of the free citizen.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The regulation of sleep and waking hours is one of the Laws' most detailed prescriptions for daily life. The principle that 'no one who is asleep is good for anything, any more than if he were dead' appears also in Heraclitus (fr. 21, 26). The emphasis on magistrates' vigilance connects to the Laws' concern with city security and the night-watches described in Laws 6.758d-e. The requirement that masters and mistresses wake before servants reinforces the aristocratic ethic of noblesse oblige -- leadership through example rather than mere command.

D: Original: A night which is passed in such a manner, in addition to all the above-mentioned advantages, infuses a sort of courage into the minds of the citizens. When the day breaks, the time has arrived for youth to go to their schoolmasters. Now neither sheep nor any other animals can live without a shepherd, nor can children be left without tutors, or slaves without masters. And of all animals the boy is the most unmanageable, inasmuch as he has the fountain of reason in him not yet regulated; he is the most insidious, sharp-witted, and insubordinate of animals. Wherefore he must be bound with many bridles; in the first place, when he gets away from mothers and nurses, he must be under the management of tutors on account of his childishness and foolishness; then, again, being a freeman, he must be controlled by teachers, no matter what they teach, and by studies; but he is also a slave, and in that regard any freeman who comes in his way may punish him and his tutor and his instructor, if any of them does anything wrong; and he who comes across him and does not inflict upon him the punishment which he deserves, shall incur the greatest disgrace; and let the guardian of the law, who is the director of education, see to him who coming in the way of the offences which we have mentioned, does not chastise them when he ought, or chastises them in a way which he ought not; let him keep a sharp look-out, and take especial care of the training of our children, directing their natures, and always turning them to good according to the law.
D: Narrative: "A night which is passed in such a manner, in addition to all the above-mentioned advantages, infuses a sort of courage into the minds of the citizens. When the day breaks, the time has arrived for youth to go to their schoolmasters. Now neither sheep nor any other animals can live without a shepherd, nor can children be left without tutors, or slaves without masters. And of all animals the boy is the most unmanageable, inasmuch as he has the fountain of reason in him not yet regulated; he is the most insidious, sharp-witted, and insubordinate of animals. Wherefore he must be bound with many bridles; in the first place, when he gets away from mothers and nurses, he must be under the management of tutors on account of his childishness and foolishness; then, again, being a freeman, he must be controlled by teachers, no matter what they teach, and by studies; but he is also a slave, and in that regard any freeman who comes in his way may punish him and his tutor and his instructor, if any of them does anything wrong; and he who comes across him and does not inflict upon him the punishment which he deserves, shall incur the greatest disgrace; and let the guardian of the law, who is the director of education, see to him who coming in the way of the offences which we have mentioned, does not chastise them when he ought, or chastises them in a way which he ought not; let him keep a sharp look-out, and take especial care of the training of our children, directing their natures, and always turning them to good according to the law."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian added that a night spent in vigilant wakefulness, beyond its other advantages, infuses a kind of courage into citizens' minds. When day breaks, it is time for youth to go to their teachers.

Neither sheep nor any other animals can live without a shepherd; children cannot be left without tutors, nor slaves without masters. Of all animals, the boy is the most unmanageable: he possesses the fountain of reason, but it is not yet regulated. This makes him the most cunning, sharp-witted, and insubordinate of all creatures. He must therefore be bound by many restraints.

First, when he leaves his mother and nurses, tutors must manage him because of his childishness and folly. Then, as a free citizen, he must be controlled by teachers and their studies. But he is also, in a sense, a slave, and any free citizen who encounters him doing wrong may punish both the boy and his tutor or instructor. Anyone who witnesses such wrongdoing and fails to administer the punishment deserved shall incur the greatest disgrace.

The guardian of the law -- the director of education -- must watch carefully for anyone who fails to discipline when required or who disciplines improperly. He must take special care of the training of children, directing their natures and always turning them toward good in accordance with the law.
D: Elegant: Vigilant nights breed courage. When day breaks, children go to school. The boy is the most unmanageable animal -- possessing unregulated reason, he is cunning, sharp, and insubordinate. He needs multiple restraints: tutors after leaving his mother, teachers for his studies, and any citizen may punish him for wrongdoing. The director of education oversees all, ensuring proper discipline that turns children's natures toward good.
D: Summary: After noting that vigilant nights build courage, the Athenian describes boys as the most unmanageable animals due to their unregulated reason. Multiple layers of discipline are needed -- tutors, teachers, and any citizen -- all overseen by the director of education.
D: Distilled: Boys are the most unmanageable creatures because their reason is powerful but unregulated, requiring multiple layers of supervision from tutors, teachers, citizens, and the director of education.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The famous characterization of the boy as 'the most unmanageable of animals' is one of the Laws' most quoted lines. The phrase 'fountain of reason not yet regulated' (pege tou nou) captures the developmental insight that intelligence without discipline is dangerous. The system of universal citizen authority over children reflects the Greek concept of collective child-rearing -- any adult could discipline any child. The director of education (epistates paideias) is established in Laws 6.765d-766b as the most important magistrate in the state. Compare Protagoras 325c-326e on the multiple layers of moral education in Athens.

D: Original: But how can our law sufficiently train the director of education. himself; for as yet all has been imperfect, and nothing has been said either clear or satisfactory? Now, as far as possible, the law ought to leave nothing to him, but to explain everything, that he may be an interpreter and tutor to others. About dances and music and choral strains, I have already spoken both to the character of the selection of them, and the manner in which they are to be amended and consecrated. But we have not as yet spoken, O illustrious guardian of education, of the manner in which your pupils are to use those strains which are written in prose, although you have been informed what martial strains they are to learn and practise; what relates in the first place to the learning of letters, and secondly, to the lyre, and also to calculation, which, as we were saying, is needful for them all to learn, and any other things which are required with a view to war and the management of house and city, and, looking to the same object, what is useful in the revolutions of the heavenly bodies-the stars and sun and moon, and the various regulations about these matters which are necessary for the whole state-I am speaking of the arrangements of; days in periods of months, and of months in years, which are to be observed, in order that seasons and sacrifices and festivals may have their regular and natural order, and keep the city alive and awake, the Gods receiving the honours due to them, and men having a better understanding about them: all these things, O my friend, have not yet been sufficiently declared to you by the legislator. Attend, then, to what I am now going to say:-We were telling you, in the first place, that you were not sufficiently informed about letters, and the objection was to this effect-that you were never told whether he who was meant to be a respectable citizen should apply himself in detail to that sort of learning, or not apply himself at all; and the same remark holds good of the study of the lyre.
D: Narrative:
"But how can our law sufficiently train the director of education himself; for as yet all has been imperfect, and nothing has been said either clear or satisfactory? Now, as far as possible, the law ought to leave nothing to him, but to explain everything, that he may be an interpreter and tutor to others. About dances and music and choral strains, I have already spoken both to the character of the selection of them, and the manner in which they are to be amended and consecrated. But we have not as yet spoken, O illustrious guardian of education, of the manner in which your pupils are to use those strains which are written in prose, although you have been informed what martial strains they are to learn and practise; what relates in the first place to the learning of letters, and secondly, to the lyre, and also to calculation, which, as we were saying, is needful for them all to learn, and any other things which are required with a view to war and the management of house and city, and, looking to the same object, what is useful in the revolutions of the heavenly bodies -- the stars and sun and moon, and the various regulations about these matters which are necessary for the whole state -- I am speaking of the arrangements of days in periods of months, and of months in years, which are to be observed, in order that seasons and sacrifices and festivals may have their regular and natural order, and keep the city alive and awake, the Gods receiving the honours due to them, and men having a better understanding about them: all these things, O my friend, have not yet been sufficiently declared to you by the legislator.

"Attend, then, to what I am now going to say: We were telling you, in the first place, that you were not sufficiently informed about letters, and the objection was to this effect -- that you were never told whether he who was meant to be a respectable citizen should apply himself in detail to that sort of learning, or not apply himself at all; and the same remark holds good of the study of the lyre."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian raised a deeper question: how can law sufficiently prepare the director of education himself? Everything said so far has been imperfect -- nothing clear or satisfactory enough. As far as possible, the law should leave nothing to the director's discretion but explain everything, so he can serve as interpreter and tutor to others.

Dances, music, and choral compositions have been addressed -- their selection, amendment, and consecration. But nothing has been said about how students should use prose writings. Nor have they adequately covered the learning of letters, the lyre, and calculation -- all of which every citizen must learn -- nor other subjects required for war, household management, and city governance. The useful knowledge of celestial revolutions -- stars, sun, moon -- and the regulation of calendars (days arranged in months, months in years) so that seasons, sacrifices, and festivals maintain their proper order, keeping the city alive and awake with the gods receiving due honors and citizens gaining better understanding -- none of this has been sufficiently explained to the director.

The Athenian drew attention to the first gap: they had never specified whether a citizen destined for respectability should study letters in depth or not at all. The same omission applied to the study of the lyre.
D: Elegant: How can law prepare the director of education when so much remains unspecified? The law should explain everything, leaving nothing to discretion. Dance and music have been covered, but not prose, letters, lyre, calculation, calendar science, or astronomy -- all essential for war, governance, and religious observance. The first gap: should a respectable citizen study letters deeply or not at all?
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies major gaps in their educational legislation: the director of education has not been told how to handle prose writings, letters, lyre, calculation, astronomy, or calendar science. The law must be comprehensive enough to guide the director, who in turn guides everyone else.
D: Distilled: The educational legislation remains incomplete on prose, letters, lyre, calculation, astronomy, and calendar science -- gaps that must be filled so the director of education can properly guide all instruction.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This transitional passage inventories the remaining educational topics. The curriculum outlined -- letters, lyre, calculation, astronomy, calendar science -- is broader than the Republic's but shares its mathematical emphasis (Republic 521c-531d). The calendar regulation connecting astronomical knowledge to religious festivals shows the Laws' integration of science and religion. The self-critical acknowledgment of incompleteness is characteristic of the Laws' honest methodology. The director of education's role as 'interpreter' (exegetes) borrows religious vocabulary -- the exegetes traditionally interpreted sacred law.

D: Original: But now we say that he ought to attend to them. A fair time for a boy of ten years old to spend in letters is three years; the age of thirteen is the proper time for him to begin to handle the lyre, and he may continue at this for another three years, neither more nor less, and whether his father or himself like or dislike the study, he is not to be allowed to spend more or less time in learning music than the law allows. And let him who disobeys the law be deprived of those youthful honours of which we shall hereafter speak. Hear, however, first of all, what the young ought to learn in the early years of life, and what their instructors ought to teach them. They ought to be occupied with their letters until they are to read and write; but the acquisition of perfect beauty or quickness in writinig, if nature has not stimulated them to acquire these accomplishments in the given number of years, they should let alone. And as to the learning of compositions committed to writing which are not set to the lyre, whether metrical or without rhythmical divisions, compositions in prose, as they are termed, having no rhythm or harmony-seeing how dangerous are the writings handed down to us by many writers of this class-what will you do with them, O most excellent guardians of the law? or how can the lawgiver rightly direct you about them? I believe that he will be in great difficulty.
D: Narrative:
"But now we say that he ought to attend to them. A fair time for a boy of ten years old to spend in letters is three years; the age of thirteen is the proper time for him to begin to handle the lyre, and he may continue at this for another three years, neither more nor less, and whether his father or himself like or dislike the study, he is not to be allowed to spend more or less time in learning music than the law allows. And let him who disobeys the law be deprived of those youthful honours of which we shall hereafter speak.

"Hear, however, first of all, what the young ought to learn in the early years of life, and what their instructors ought to teach them. They ought to be occupied with their letters until they are able to read and write; but the acquisition of perfect beauty or quickness in writing, if nature has not stimulated them to acquire these accomplishments in the given number of years, they should let alone. And as to the learning of compositions committed to writing which are not set to the lyre, whether metrical or without rhythmical divisions, compositions in prose, as they are termed, having no rhythm or harmony -- seeing how dangerous are the writings handed down to us by many writers of this class -- what will you do with them, O most excellent guardians of the law? or how can the lawgiver rightly direct you about them? I believe that he will be in great difficulty."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian now specified the timeline. A boy of ten should spend three years on letters. At thirteen, he should begin the lyre and continue for exactly three years -- neither more nor less, regardless of whether the boy or his father likes or dislikes the study. The law's timeframe is fixed, and disobedience will result in losing certain youthful honors to be described later.

As for what should be learned: children should study letters until they can read and write. But if nature has not given them the gift of beautiful or rapid handwriting within the allotted time, they should not pursue it further. The more difficult question concerns prose writings -- compositions not set to music, whether metrical or without rhythmic structure, that lack rhythm and harmony. Many inherited writings of this kind are dangerous. What should the guardians of the law do with them? How can the lawgiver properly guide them on this matter? The Athenian believed the lawgiver would face great difficulty here.
D: Elegant: The curriculum timeline: letters from age ten for three years, lyre from thirteen for three years -- fixed by law regardless of preference. Children must learn to read and write, but need not perfect handwriting beyond natural ability. The harder question: what to do with dangerous prose writings that lack music's ordering harmony? The lawgiver faces great difficulty here.
D: Summary: The Athenian sets fixed educational periods -- three years for letters starting at ten, three years for lyre starting at thirteen. Reading and writing are essential but perfect handwriting is not required. The deeper problem is regulating prose literature, which lacks the ordering structure of music and may be dangerous.
D: Distilled: Education should follow fixed periods for letters and lyre, with basic literacy required but prose literature posing a regulatory challenge because it lacks music's ordering harmony.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The specific ages and durations are unusually precise for Plato. The fixed three-year periods regardless of aptitude or preference reflect the Laws' authoritarian approach to education. The distinction between basic literacy and calligraphic excellence is practical. The concern about 'dangerous' prose writings introduces a problem that the Republic solved by banning most literature (Republic 398a); the Laws seeks a more nuanced solution. The observation that prose lacks the structural discipline of music connects to the broader argument that music (mousike) is the foundation of education because it orders the soul.

D: Original:
What troubles you, Stranger? and why are you so perplexed in your mind?

You naturally ask, Cleinias, and to you and Megillus, who are my partners in the work of legislation, I must state the more difficult as well as the easier parts of the task.

To what do you refer in this instance?

I will tell you. There is a difficulty in opposing many myriads of mouths.

Well, and have we not already opposed the popular voice in many important enactments?
D: Narrative:
"What troubles you, Stranger? and why are you so perplexed in your mind?"

"You naturally ask, Cleinias, and to you and Megillus, who are my partners in the work of legislation, I must state the more difficult as well as the easier parts of the task."

"To what do you refer in this instance?"

"I will tell you. There is a difficulty in opposing many myriads of mouths."

"Well, and have we not already opposed the popular voice in many important enactments?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias asked what was troubling the Athenian and why he seemed so perplexed. The Athenian replied that Cleinias naturally asked this, and as partners in the work of legislation, both Cleinias and Megillus must hear the difficult parts of the task alongside the easy ones.

Cleinias asked what he was referring to specifically. The Athenian explained: there is a difficulty in opposing countless voices. Cleinias pointed out that they had already opposed popular opinion in many important enactments.
D: Elegant: Cleinias asks why the Athenian is troubled. The difficulty is opposing popular opinion -- though they have already done so many times in this legislation.
D: Summary: The Athenian expresses perplexity about opposing popular opinion on literature, but Cleinias reminds him they have already defied convention repeatedly in their legislative project.
D: Distilled: The difficulty of opposing popular opinion on literature is real but not new -- they have already defied convention throughout their legislation.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The 'many myriads of mouths' represents the weight of literary tradition -- Homer, Hesiod, the tragedians, and countless other poets and prose writers whose works constituted Greek education. Cleinias' response shows growing confidence and independence -- he is becoming a true legislative partner rather than a passive student. The exchange reveals the Laws' awareness of operating against cultural consensus, a position it shares with the Republic but handles more diplomatically.

D: Original:
That is quite true; and you mean to imply, that the road which we are taking may be disagreeable to some but is agreeable to as many others, or if not to as many, at any rate to persons not inferior to the others, and in company with them you bid me, at whatever risk, to proceed along the path of legislation which has opened out of our present discourse, and to be of good cheer, and not to faint.

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"That is quite true; and you mean to imply, that the road which we are taking may be disagreeable to some but is agreeable to as many others, or if not to as many, at any rate to persons not inferior to the others, and in company with them you bid me, at whatever risk, to proceed along the path of legislation which has opened out of our present discourse, and to be of good cheer, and not to faint."

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian acknowledged Cleinias' point: the path they are taking may displease some people but will please as many others, or at least persons no less worthy than those who object. With such company, Cleinias was bidding him to proceed along the legislative path that had opened before them, whatever the risk, and to take courage without faltering.

Cleinias confirmed this was exactly what he meant.
D: Elegant: Their path may displease some but will please others equally worthy. Cleinias bids the Athenian to proceed with courage despite the risks.
D: Summary: The Athenian takes heart from Cleinias' encouragement: their legislative path, though controversial, has the support of people no less worthy than its opponents, and he should proceed with confidence.
D: Distilled: Controversial legislation may displease some but will find support among equally worthy people, and should be pursued with courage.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). This passage illustrates the Laws' political realism -- good legislation need not please everyone, only a sufficient number of worthy supporters. The language of courage ('be of good cheer, and not to faint') applies the earlier discussion of courage (7.7) to intellectual and legislative bravery. The partnership between the Athenian and Cleinias has deepened -- Cleinias now encourages the Athenian rather than merely questioning him.

D: Original: And I do not faint; I say, indeed, that we have a great many poets writing in hexameter, trimeter, and all sorts of measures-some who are serious, others who aim only at raising a laugh-and all mankind declare that the youth who are rightly educated should be brought up in them and saturated with them; some insist that they should be constantly hearing them read aloud, and always learning them, so as to get by heart entire poets; while others select choice passages and long speeches, and make compendiums of them, saying that these ought to be committed to memory, if a man is to be made good and wise by experience and learning of many things. And you want me now to tell them plainly in what they are right and in what they are wrong.
D: Narrative: "And I do not faint; I say, indeed, that we have a great many poets writing in hexameter, trimeter, and all sorts of measures -- some who are serious, others who aim only at raising a laugh -- and all mankind declare that the youth who are rightly educated should be brought up in them and saturated with them; some insist that they should be constantly hearing them read aloud, and always learning them, so as to get by heart entire poets; while others select choice passages and long speeches, and make compendiums of them, saying that these ought to be committed to memory, if a man is to be made good and wise by experience and learning of many things. And you want me now to tell them plainly in what they are right and in what they are wrong."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian declared he would not falter. He observed that there are a great many poets writing in hexameter, trimeter, and every other meter -- some serious, some comic. The universal opinion is that properly educated youth should be raised on these works and saturated with them. Some insist children should constantly hear poetry read aloud and memorize entire poets; others prefer selecting choice passages and long speeches to compile anthologies for memorization, claiming this produces goodness and wisdom through broad learning.

Cleinias and Megillus wanted the Athenian to tell them plainly where conventional opinion is right and where it is wrong.
D: Elegant: Poets abound in every meter, serious and comic. Everyone agrees youth should be saturated with their works -- whether by memorizing entire poets or selected anthologies. The question is where this consensus is right and where wrong.
D: Summary: The Athenian surveys the conventional view that youth should be immersed in poetry of all kinds, memorizing either entire works or selected passages. He prepares to evaluate where this widespread practice is right and where it goes astray.
D: Distilled: The universal practice of saturating youth in poetry through memorization must be evaluated for where it serves education well and where it fails.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The passage describes actual Greek educational practice -- poetry memorization was the foundation of Greek paideia (education). Homer's works were widely memorized in full. Hexameter was epic meter (Homer, Hesiod); trimeter was the standard meter of tragic dialogue (Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides). The distinction between memorizing whole poets versus anthologies reflects real pedagogical debates. Compare Protagoras 325d-326a on the role of poetry in education, and Republic 606e-607a on the traditional claim that Homer 'educated Greece.'

D: Original:
Yes, I do. Ath. But how can I in one word rightly comprehend all of them?
