I am of opinion, and, if I am not mistaken, there is a general agreement, that every one of these poets has said many things well and many things the reverse of well; and if this be true, then I do affirm that much learning is dangerous to youth.

How would you advise the guardian of the law to act?

In what respect?

I mean to what pattern should he look as his guide in permitting the young to learn some things and forbidding them to learn others. Do not shrink from answering.

My good Cleinias, I rather think that I am fortunate.

How so?
D: Narrative:
"Yes, I do."

"But how can I in one word rightly comprehend all of them? I am of opinion, and, if I am not mistaken, there is a general agreement, that every one of these poets has said many things well and many things the reverse of well; and if this be true, then I do affirm that much learning is dangerous to youth."

"How would you advise the guardian of the law to act?"

"In what respect?"

"I mean to what pattern should he look as his guide in permitting the young to learn some things and forbidding them to learn others. Do not shrink from answering."

"My good Cleinias, I rather think that I am fortunate."

"How so?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias confirmed he wanted the Athenian's judgment. The Athenian replied that it was difficult to summarize his view of all poets in a single statement. His opinion -- and he believed there was general agreement -- was that every one of these poets has said many things well and many things badly. If this is true, then extensive literary learning is dangerous to youth.

Cleinias asked how the guardian of the law should act. The Athenian asked him to be more specific. Cleinias clarified: what pattern should guide the guardian in deciding what to allow and what to forbid youth from learning?

The Athenian replied with apparent surprise: he considered himself rather fortunate in this regard. Cleinias asked how so.
D: Elegant: Every poet has said many things well and many things badly -- on this there is general agreement. Therefore much literary learning is dangerous to youth. What pattern should the guardian of the law use in choosing what to permit and forbid? The Athenian finds himself fortunate in having an answer.
D: Summary: The Athenian observes that all poets mix good and bad content, making extensive literary study dangerous for youth. When Cleinias asks what pattern should guide the guardian of the law in selecting appropriate material, the Athenian hints that he has a fortunate answer.
D: Distilled: Since every poet mixes good and bad, extensive literary learning is dangerous for youth, and the guardian needs a reliable pattern for selecting what to teach.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The judgment that 'every poet has said many things well and many things the reverse of well' is characteristically Platonic moderation -- neither total acceptance nor total rejection of the poetic tradition. The phrase 'much learning is dangerous' (polumathia) echoes Heraclitus fr. 40: 'Much learning does not teach intelligence.' The Athenian's claim to be 'fortunate' sets up the self-referential argument in 7.53, where he will suggest their own dialogue as the educational model.

D: Original: I think that I am not wholly in want of a pattern, for when I consider the words which we have spoken from early dawn until now, and which, as I believe, have been inspired by Heaven, they appear to me to be quite like a poem. When I reflected upon all these words of ours. I naturally felt pleasure, for of all the discourses which I have ever learnt or heard, either in poetry or prose, this seemed to me to be the justest, and most suitable for young men to hear; I cannot imagine any better pattern than this which the guardian of the law who is also the director of education can have. He cannot do better than advise the teachers to teach the young these words and any which are of a like nature, if he should happen to find them, either in poetry or prose, or if he come across unwritten discourses akin to ours, he should certainly preserve them, and commit them to writing. And, first of all, he shall constrain the teachers themselves to learn and approve them, and any of them who will not, shall not be employed by him, but those whom he finds agreeing in his judgment, he shall make use of and shall commit to them the instruction and education of youth. And here and on this wise let my fanciful tale about letters and teachers of letters come to an end.
D: Narrative:
"I think that I am not wholly in want of a pattern, for when I consider the words which we have spoken from early dawn until now, and which, as I believe, have been inspired by Heaven, they appear to me to be quite like a poem. When I reflected upon all these words of ours, I naturally felt pleasure, for of all the discourses which I have ever learnt or heard, either in poetry or prose, this seemed to me to be the justest, and most suitable for young men to hear; I cannot imagine any better pattern than this which the guardian of the law who is also the director of education can have. He cannot do better than advise the teachers to teach the young these words and any which are of a like nature, if he should happen to find them, either in poetry or prose, or if he come across unwritten discourses akin to ours, he should certainly preserve them, and commit them to writing.

"And, first of all, he shall constrain the teachers themselves to learn and approve them, and any of them who will not, shall not be employed by him, but those whom he finds agreeing in his judgment, he shall make use of and shall commit to them the instruction and education of youth. And here and on this wise let my fanciful tale about letters and teachers of letters come to an end."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian explained his good fortune: he was not entirely without a model. When he considered the conversation they had shared from early dawn until now -- which he believed had been divinely inspired -- it appeared to him quite like a poem. Reflecting on their words, he naturally felt pleasure, for of all the discourses he had ever learned or heard, whether in poetry or prose, this one seemed the most just and most suitable for young men to hear.

He could imagine no better model for the guardian of the law and director of education. The director should advise teachers to teach these very words and anything similar they might find in poetry, prose, or unwritten discourse. Such similar works should be preserved and committed to writing.

First and foremost, teachers themselves must be required to learn and approve of these texts. Any teacher who refuses should not be employed; those who agree with the director's judgment should be entrusted with the instruction and education of youth.

With this, the Athenian concluded his account of letters and the teaching of letters.
D: Elegant: The model already exists: their own divinely inspired conversation. Of all discourses the Athenian has ever encountered, this seems the most just and suitable for youth. The director of education should make it and similar works the standard, requiring teachers to learn and approve them. Teachers who refuse are not employed; those who agree receive the trust of educating youth.
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes their own dialogue as the educational model -- the most just and suitable discourse for youth he has ever encountered. The director of education should use it as the standard, requiring teachers to learn and endorse it, and dismissing those who refuse.
D: Distilled: The conversation itself -- divinely inspired and supremely just -- serves as the ideal educational model, and teachers must learn, approve, and teach it or be dismissed.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This remarkable self-referential moment has the Laws propose itself as the model text for education. The claim of divine inspiration connects to the Laws' theology and to Plato's earlier accounts of philosophical inspiration (Phaedrus 244a-245c, Ion 533d-534e). The practical mechanism -- teachers must approve the curriculum or be dismissed -- is the Laws' version of the Republic's more dramatic expulsion of poets (Republic 398a). The phrase 'fanciful tale' (muthos) is modestly self-deprecating but also connects to the Laws' own genre as a mythically structured legislative dialogue.

D: Original:
I do not think, Stranger, that we have wandered out of the proposed limits of the argument; but whether we are right or not in our whole conception, I cannot be very certain.

The truth, Cleinias, may be expected to become clearer when, as we have often said, we arrive at the end of the whole discussion about laws.

Yes.

And now that we have done with the teacher of letters, the teacher of the lyre has to receive orders from us.

Certainly.

I think that we have only to recollect our previous discussions, and we shall be able to give suitable regulations touching all this part of instruction and education to the teachers of the lyre.

To what do you refer?
D: Narrative:
"I do not think, Stranger, that we have wandered out of the proposed limits of the argument; but whether we are right or not in our whole conception, I cannot be very certain."

"The truth, Cleinias, may be expected to become clearer when, as we have often said, we arrive at the end of the whole discussion about laws."

"Yes."

"And now that we have done with the teacher of letters, the teacher of the lyre has to receive orders from us."

"Certainly."

"I think that we have only to recollect our previous discussions, and we shall be able to give suitable regulations touching all this part of instruction and education to the teachers of the lyre."

"To what do you refer?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias said he did not think they had strayed beyond their proposed limits, though he could not be entirely certain whether their whole conception was correct. The Athenian replied that the truth, as they had often said, could be expected to become clearer when they reached the end of their entire discussion about laws.

Cleinias agreed. Having finished with the teacher of letters, the Athenian said the teacher of the lyre now needed their directions. He believed that by recalling their earlier discussions, they could give suitable regulations for this part of instruction.

Cleinias asked what specifically he was referring to.
D: Elegant: Cleinias is not certain of their conclusions but trusts that clarity will come at the end of the full discussion. With letters completed, they turn to the lyre, for which earlier discussions provide sufficient guidance.
D: Summary: Cleinias expresses measured confidence in their progress. The Athenian reassures him that the complete discussion will bring clarity, then transitions from letters to lyre instruction, drawing on their earlier discussions for guidance.
D: Distilled: Clarity will emerge from the completed discussion, and earlier principles about music provide sufficient guidance for regulating lyre instruction.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). Cleinias' measured uncertainty ('I cannot be very certain') is philosophically appropriate -- the Laws does not claim certainty, unlike the more confident Republic. The methodological principle that truth emerges from the completed whole echoes the hermeneutic circle. The transition from letters to lyre follows the standard Greek educational sequence: grammata (letters) preceded mousike (music/lyre) in a boy's education. The 'previous discussions' referred to include Laws 2 on music and choral education.

D: Original:
We were saying, if I remember rightly, that the sixty-year-old choristers of Dionysus were to be specially quick in their perceptions of rhythm and musical composition, that they might be able to distinguish good and bad imitation, that is to say, the imitation of the good or bad soul when under the influence of passion, rejecting the one and displaying the other in hymns and songs, charming the souls of youth, and inviting them to follow and attain virtue by the way of imitation.

Very true.
D: Narrative:
"We were saying, if I remember rightly, that the sixty-year-old choristers of Dionysus were to be specially quick in their perceptions of rhythm and musical composition, that they might be able to distinguish good and bad imitation, that is to say, the imitation of the good or bad soul when under the influence of passion, rejecting the one and displaying the other in hymns and songs, charming the souls of youth, and inviting them to follow and attain virtue by the way of imitation."

"Very true."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian recalled their earlier discussion: the sixty-year-old choristers of Dionysus were to be specially perceptive about rhythm and musical composition, able to distinguish between good and bad imitation -- that is, between music that imitates a good soul under the influence of passion and music that imitates a bad one. They should reject the latter and promote the former in hymns and songs, charming the souls of youth and inviting them to pursue virtue through imitation.

Cleinias confirmed this was what they had said.
D: Elegant: The sixty-year-old Dionysian choristers must distinguish between music that imitates good souls and bad ones, rejecting the latter and using the former to charm youth toward virtue through imitation.
D: Summary: The Athenian recalls that elderly Dionysian choristers were designated to evaluate musical compositions, distinguishing between imitations of good and bad character and using approved music to guide youth toward virtue.
D: Distilled: Elderly Dionysian choristers serve as musical judges, distinguishing good from bad character imitation and using approved music to guide youth toward virtue.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The sixty-year-old Dionysian chorus was established in Laws 2.665d-666d as the most important of three age-graded choruses. Their special qualification -- being old enough to have wisdom about character yet still capable of musical judgment -- reflects the Laws' faith in elder authority. The concept of music as 'imitation of the soul under passion' (mimesis) comes from Laws 2.655d and connects to Republic 395c-398b on imitation. 'Charming the souls of youth' uses the vocabulary of enchantment (epode), suggesting music's pre-rational persuasive power.

D: Original:
And with this view, the teacher and the learner ought to use the sounds of the lyre, because its notes are pure, the player who teaches and his pupil rendering note for note in unison; but complexity, and variation of notes, when the strings give one sound and the poet or composer of the melody gives another-also when they make concords and harmonies in which lesser and greater intervals, slow and quick, or high and low notes, are combined-or, again, when they make complex variations of rhythms, which they adapt to the notes of the lyre-all that sort of thing is not suited to those who have to acquire a speedy and useful knowledge of music in three years; for opposite principles are confusing, and create a difficulty in learning, and our young men should learn quickly, and their mere necessary acquirements are not few or trifling, as will be shown in due course. Let the director of education attend to the principles concerning music which we are laying down. As to the songs and words themselves which the masters of choruses are to teach and the character of them, they have been already described by us, and are the same which, when consecrated and adapted to the different festivals, we said were to benefit cities by affording them an innocent amusement.

That, again, is true.
D: Narrative:
"And with this view, the teacher and the learner ought to use the sounds of the lyre, because its notes are pure, the player who teaches and his pupil rendering note for note in unison; but complexity, and variation of notes, when the strings give one sound and the poet or composer of the melody gives another -- also when they make concords and harmonies in which lesser and greater intervals, slow and quick, or high and low notes, are combined -- or, again, when they make complex variations of rhythms, which they adapt to the notes of the lyre -- all that sort of thing is not suited to those who have to acquire a speedy and useful knowledge of music in three years; for opposite principles are confusing, and create a difficulty in learning, and our young men should learn quickly, and their mere necessary acquirements are not few or trifling, as will be shown in due course.

"Let the director of education attend to the principles concerning music which we are laying down. As to the songs and words themselves which the masters of choruses are to teach and the character of them, they have been already described by us, and are the same which, when consecrated and adapted to the different festivals, we said were to benefit cities by affording them an innocent amusement."

"That, again, is true."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian specified the method of lyre instruction: teacher and student should use the lyre's pure notes, playing note for note in unison. Complexity and variation of notes -- where the strings produce one sound while the melody line produces another; where concords combine lesser and greater intervals, slow and quick, high and low notes; where elaborate rhythmic variations are adapted to the lyre's accompaniment -- all such complexity is unsuitable for those who must acquire a practical knowledge of music in three years. Conflicting principles confuse and hinder learning, and the young have many other essential subjects to master, as would be shown later.

The director of education should attend to these musical principles. As for the songs and words that chorus masters should teach -- their content and character -- these have already been described: the consecrated forms adapted to different festivals that benefit cities through innocent entertainment.

Cleinias confirmed this was so.
D: Elegant: Lyre instruction should be simple: teacher and student playing note for note in unison. Complex harmonies, varied intervals, and elaborate rhythmic adaptations are unsuitable for a three-year course. Conflicting principles confuse learners who have many other essential subjects ahead. The director of education oversees these principles; the songs themselves have already been consecrated for specific festivals.
D: Summary: Lyre instruction must be simple and unisonal -- no complex harmonies or rhythmic variations within the three-year course, as conflicting principles confuse young learners who have many other subjects to master. Song content has already been prescribed for consecrated festivals.
D: Distilled: Lyre instruction should be simple unison playing, avoiding complex harmonies that confuse young learners who must master many subjects in limited time.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The preference for unison over polyphony reflects both musical conservatism and pedagogical pragmatism. Greek music was primarily monophonic (melody-based), though heterophony (slight variation between voice and instrument) was common. The Athenian's prohibition of 'complexity' targets the New Music of the late 5th century -- composers like Timotheus and Philoxenus who introduced complex modulations criticized by conservative thinkers. Compare Republic 399c-d on the simplification of musical instruments. The three-year limit (ages 13-16) creates practical constraints that justify the simplicity.

D: Original: Then let him who has been elected a director of music receive these rules from us as containing the very truth; and may he prosper in his office! Let us now proceed to lay down other rules in addition to the preceding about dancing and gymnastic exercise in general. Having said what remained to be said about the teaching of music, let us speak in like manner about gymnastic. For boys and girls ought to learn to dance and practise gymnastic exercises-ought they not?
D: Narrative: "Then let him who has been elected a director of music receive these rules from us as containing the very truth; and may he prosper in his office! Let us now proceed to lay down other rules in addition to the preceding about dancing and gymnastic exercise in general. Having said what remained to be said about the teaching of music, let us speak in like manner about gymnastic. For boys and girls ought to learn to dance and practise gymnastic exercises -- ought they not?"
D: Refashioned: The Athenian proposed that the elected director of music receive these rules as containing the essential truth, and wished him well in his office. They should now proceed to lay down additional rules about dancing and gymnastic exercise. Having completed their discussion of music teaching, they would address gymnastic in the same manner. Boys and girls ought to learn dancing and practice gymnastic exercises -- the Athenian assumed agreement on this point.
D: Elegant: Let the director of music receive these rules as truth. Now they must address dancing and gymnastic exercise. Both boys and girls should learn to dance and practice gymnastics.
D: Summary: The Athenian concludes music instruction, wishing the director of music well, and transitions to dancing and gymnastic exercise, affirming that both boys and girls must participate.
D: Distilled: With music instruction complete, the discussion turns to dancing and gymnastics, which both boys and girls must learn.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This transitional passage moves from music (mousike) back to gymnastic (gymnastike), completing the circuit of the two main educational branches established at 7.17. The reaffirmation that girls participate equally in gymnastics continues the progressive gender policy established at 7.39. The 'director of music' is a distinct office from the 'director of education' -- the Laws' bureaucratic specificity contrasts with the Republic's more abstract treatment.

D: Original:
Yes.

Then the boys ought to have dancing masters, and the girls dancing mistresses to exercise them.

Very good.

Then once more let us summon him who has the chief concern in the business, the superintendent of youth [i.e., the director of education]; he will have plenty to do, if he is to have the charge of music and gymnastic.

But how will old man be able to attend to such great charges?
D: Narrative:
"Yes."

"Then the boys ought to have dancing masters, and the girls dancing mistresses to exercise them."

"Very good."

"Then once more let us summon him who has the chief concern in the business, the superintendent of youth [i.e., the director of education]; he will have plenty to do, if he is to have the charge of music and gymnastic."

"But how will an old man be able to attend to such great charges?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed that both should learn. The Athenian specified that boys should have male dancing masters and girls female dancing mistresses.

Cleinias approved. The Athenian proposed summoning the superintendent of youth -- that is, the director of education -- who would have plenty to do if charged with overseeing both music and gymnastic.

Cleinias raised a practical concern: how would an old man manage such enormous responsibilities?
D: Elegant: Boys need male dancing masters, girls female dancing mistresses. The director of education will oversee both music and gymnastic -- a heavy charge. Cleinias wonders how an old man can handle such responsibility.
D: Summary: Dancing instruction requires separate male and female instructors. The director of education, responsible for both music and gymnastic, faces an enormous workload, prompting Cleinias to ask how an elderly person could manage it.
D: Distilled: Dancing instruction requires gender-specific teachers, and the director of education's dual responsibility for music and gymnastic demands practical support.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The gender-specific dancing instructors reflect Greek norms of propriety while maintaining equal access. The director of education's heavy burden is a realistic administrative concern that Cleinias raises -- showing his practical Cretan sensibility. The next passage (7.59) addresses this by allowing the director to select assistants, illustrating the Laws' systematic approach to institutional design.

D: Original: O my friend, there will be no difficulty, for the law has already given and will give him permission to select as his assistants in this charge any citizens, male or female, whom he desires; and he will know whom he ought to choose, and will be anxious not to make a mistake, from a due sense of responsibility, and from a consciousness of the importance of his office, and also because he will consider that if young men have been and are well brought up, then all things go swimmingly, but if not, it is not meet to say, nor do we say, what will follow, lest the regarders of omens should take alarm about our infant state. Many things have been said by us about dancing and about gymnastic movements in general; for we include under gymnastics all military exercises, such as archery, and all hurling of weapons, and the use of the light shield, and all fighting with heavy arms, and military evolutions, and movements of armies, and encampings, and all that relates to horsemanship. Of all these things there ought to be public teachers, receiving pay from the state, and their pupils should be the men and boys in the state, and also the girls and women, who are to know all these things.
D: Narrative:
"O my friend, there will be no difficulty, for the law has already given and will give him permission to select as his assistants in this charge any citizens, male or female, whom he desires; and he will know whom he ought to choose, and will be anxious not to make a mistake, from a due sense of responsibility, and from a consciousness of the importance of his office, and also because he will consider that if young men have been and are well brought up, then all things go swimmingly, but if not, it is not meet to say, nor do we say, what will follow, lest the regarders of omens should take alarm about our infant state.

"Many things have been said by us about dancing and about gymnastic movements in general; for we include under gymnastics all military exercises, such as archery, and all hurling of weapons, and the use of the light shield, and all fighting with heavy arms, and military evolutions, and movements of armies, and encampings, and all that relates to horsemanship. Of all these things there ought to be public teachers, receiving pay from the state, and their pupils should be the men and boys in the state, and also the girls and women, who are to know all these things."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian reassured Cleinias: there would be no difficulty. The law already grants the director permission to select any citizens, male or female, as assistants. The director will know who to choose and will be anxious to choose well, both from a sense of responsibility and from understanding the importance of his office. He will recognize that if young people are well brought up, everything goes smoothly -- but if not, it would be inauspicious even to describe the consequences, lest those who watch for omens take alarm about the new state.

Much has been said about dancing and gymnastic movement. Under gymnastics they include all military exercises: archery, weapon-throwing, use of the light shield, heavy-armed fighting, military formations, army movements, encampment, and everything related to horsemanship. All of these should have publicly funded teachers, and their students should include all men and boys as well as all girls and women.
D: Elegant: The director may select male or female citizen assistants -- his responsibility and understanding will guide good choices. Well-educated youth means everything goes smoothly; the alternative is too ominous to describe. Gymnastics encompasses all military training: archery, weapons, shield-work, heavy arms, formations, and horsemanship. All should be publicly taught to everyone -- men, women, boys, and girls.
D: Summary: The director of education may select any citizens as assistants and will choose wisely from a sense of responsibility. Gymnastics broadly encompasses all military training, and publicly funded teachers must instruct men, women, boys, and girls alike in every martial discipline.
D: Distilled: The director of education selects citizen assistants for his enormous task, and publicly funded military training in all martial arts must include women and girls alongside men and boys.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The superstitious aside about 'regarders of omens' (oionoskopoi) is unusual for Plato and may reflect the Laws' engagement with popular religion. The comprehensive definition of gymnastics as including all military training (archery, weapons, shield, heavy arms, formations, horsemanship) reveals how thoroughly militarized Greek physical education was. The co-educational principle, applied now to military training, goes further than any Greek state actually practiced -- even Sparta did not train women in heavy arms. The public funding of teachers (misthon para tes poleos) is an important institutional detail.

D: Original:
While they are yet girls they should have practised dancing in arms and the whole art of fighting-when grown-up women, they should apply themselves to evolutions and tactics, and the mode of grounding and taking up arms; if for no other reason, yet in case the whole military force should have to leave the city and carry on operations of war outside, that those who will have to guard the young and the rest of the city may be equal to the task; and, on the other hand, when enemies, whether barbarian or Hellenic, come from without with mighty force and make a violent assault upon them, and thus compel them to fight for the possession of the city, which is far from being an impossibility, great would be the disgrace to the state, if the women had been so miserably trained that they could not fight for their young, as birds will, against any creature however strong, and die or undergo any danger, but must instantly rush to the temples and crowd at the altars and shrines, and bring upon human nature the reproach, that of all animals man is the most cowardly!

Such a want of education, Stranger, is certainly an unseemly thing to happen in a state, as well as a great misfortune.

Suppose that we carry our law to the extent of saying that women ought not to neglect military matters, but that all citizens, male and female alike, shall attend to them?

I quite agree.
D: Narrative:
"While they are yet girls they should have practised dancing in arms and the whole art of fighting -- when grown-up women, they should apply themselves to evolutions and tactics, and the mode of grounding and taking up arms; if for no other reason, yet in case the whole military force should have to leave the city and carry on operations of war outside, that those who will have to guard the young and the rest of the city may be equal to the task; and, on the other hand, when enemies, whether barbarian or Hellenic, come from without with mighty force and make a violent assault upon them, and thus compel them to fight for the possession of the city, which is far from being an impossibility, great would be the disgrace to the state, if the women had been so miserably trained that they could not fight for their young, as birds will, against any creature however strong, and die or undergo any danger, but must instantly rush to the temples and crowd at the altars and shrines, and bring upon human nature the reproach, that of all animals man is the most cowardly!"

"Such a want of education, Stranger, is certainly an unseemly thing to happen in a state, as well as a great misfortune."

"Suppose that we carry our law to the extent of saying that women ought not to neglect military matters, but that all citizens, male and female alike, shall attend to them?"

"I quite agree."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian elaborated: while still girls, they should practice armed dancing and the full art of fighting. As grown women, they should learn military formations, tactics, and how to ground and take up arms. At minimum, this is necessary because when the entire military force must leave the city for external operations, those guarding the young and the city must be capable. Furthermore, when enemies -- whether barbarian or Greek -- attack with overwhelming force, compelling defense of the city itself (which is entirely possible), it would be a profound disgrace if women were so badly trained that they could not fight for their children. Even birds fight against any creature, however strong, to defend their young and are willing to die. But if women must instead rush to temples and crowd at altars and shrines, they bring upon the entire human race the reproach that of all animals, man is the most cowardly.

Cleinias agreed that such a failure of education would be both shameful and disastrous for a state. The Athenian proposed extending the law: women must not neglect military matters, and all citizens, male and female, shall attend to them.

Cleinias fully agreed.
D: Elegant: Girls should practice armed dance and fighting; grown women should learn formations, tactics, and arms handling. When the army marches out, someone must guard the city. When enemies attack, women too poorly trained to fight for their children -- unlike birds who defend their young against any threat -- bring the reproach that humans are the most cowardly of all animals. Military training must be required of all citizens regardless of sex.
D: Summary: Women must train in armed dance as girls and military tactics as adults, both for city defense when the army is away and for resisting invasion. The Athenian argues that women unable to fight for their children would be a greater disgrace than any other animal's behavior, since even birds defend their young.
D: Distilled: Women must train for war because cities need defenders when armies march out, and women unable to fight for their children would prove humans the most cowardly of animals.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The bird comparison is rhetorically powerful -- even small birds mobbing predators to defend their nests exemplify the courage the Athenian expects. The scenario of women cowering at altars during an attack is a vivid picture drawn from real events -- during the Persian Wars and Peloponnesian War, non-combatant civilians did shelter at temples. The Laws' position on women's military training surpasses even the Republic's female guardians (Republic 457a) in its practical specificity. Compare also Aristotle's Politics 2.9.1269b-1270a, which criticizes Spartan women's behavior during the Theban invasion.

D: Original:
Of wrestling we have spoken in part, but of what I should call the most important part we have not spoken, and cannot easily speak without showing at the same time by gesture as well as in word what we mean; when word and action combine, and not till then, we shall explain clearly what has been said, pointing out that of all movements wrestling is most akin to the military art, and is to be pursued for the sake of this, and not this for the sake of wrestling.

Excellent.
D: Narrative:
"Of wrestling we have spoken in part, but of what I should call the most important part we have not spoken, and cannot easily speak without showing at the same time by gesture as well as in word what we mean; when word and action combine, and not till then, we shall explain clearly what has been said, pointing out that of all movements wrestling is most akin to the military art, and is to be pursued for the sake of this, and not this for the sake of wrestling."

"Excellent."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian noted that they had discussed wrestling partially, but the most important aspect remained -- and it could not easily be conveyed in words alone without physical demonstration. Only when word and gesture combine could they fully explain that of all movements, wrestling is most akin to the military art. Wrestling should be pursued for the sake of military readiness, not the reverse.

Cleinias approved.
D: Elegant: The most important part of wrestling cannot be explained in words alone -- it requires physical demonstration. The key principle: wrestling is most akin to military art and should serve it, not the reverse.
D: Summary: The Athenian acknowledges that the most important aspects of wrestling require physical demonstration alongside verbal explanation. The governing principle is that wrestling serves military training, not the other way around.
D: Distilled: Wrestling's most important aspects require physical demonstration, and wrestling must serve military readiness rather than being an end in itself.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The acknowledgment that some knowledge requires embodied demonstration -- 'word and action combine' -- is a significant epistemological point. It recognizes the limits of purely verbal instruction for physical practices, anticipating Gilbert Ryle's distinction between 'knowing that' and 'knowing how.' The subordination of wrestling to military art continues the Laws' consistently practical orientation -- athletic excellence matters only insofar as it serves civic and military purposes. Compare Republic 403c-404e on appropriate gymnastics for guardians.

D: Original: Enough of wrestling; we will now proceed to speak of other movements of the body. Such motion may be in general called dancing, and is of two kinds: one of nobler figures, imitating the honourable, the other of the more ignoble figures, imitating the mean; and of both these there are two further subdivisions. Of the serious, one kind is of those engaged in war and vehement action, and is the exercise of a noble person and a manly heart; the other exhibits a temperate soul in the enjoyment of prosperity and modest pleasures, and may be truly called and is the dance of peace. The warrior dance is different from the peaceful one, and may be rightly termed Pyrrhic; this imitates the modes of avoiding blows and missiles by dropping or giving way, or springing aside, or rising up or falling down; also the opposite postures which are those of action, as, for example, the imitation of archery and the hurling of javelins, and of all sorts of blows. And when the imitation is of brave bodies and souls, and the action is direct and muscular, giving for the most part a straight movement to the limbs of the body-that, I say, is the true sort; but the opposite is not right. In the dance of peace what we have to consider is whether a man bears himself naturally and gracefully, and after the manner of men who duly conform to the law. But before proceeding I must distinguish the dancing about which there is any doubt, from that about which there is no doubt. Which is the doubtful kind, and how are the two to be distinguished? There are dances of the Bacchic sort, both those in which, as they say, they imitate drunken men, and which are named after the Nymphs, and Pan, and Silenuses, and Satyrs; and also those in which purifications are made or mysteries celebrated-all this sort of dancing cannot be rightly defined as having either a peaceful or a warlike character, or indeed as having any meaning whatever and may, I think, be most truly described as distinct from the warlike dance, and distinct from the peaceful, and not suited for a city at all. There let it lie; and so leaving it to lie, we will proceed to the dances of war and peace, for with these we are undoubtedly concerned.
D: Narrative:
"Enough of wrestling; we will now proceed to speak of other movements of the body. Such motion may be in general called dancing, and is of two kinds: one of nobler figures, imitating the honourable, the other of the more ignoble figures, imitating the mean; and of both these there are two further subdivisions. Of the serious, one kind is of those engaged in war and vehement action, and is the exercise of a noble person and a manly heart; the other exhibits a temperate soul in the enjoyment of prosperity and modest pleasures, and may be truly called and is the dance of peace. The warrior dance is different from the peaceful one, and may be rightly termed Pyrrhic; this imitates the modes of avoiding blows and missiles by dropping or giving way, or springing aside, or rising up or falling down; also the opposite postures which are those of action, as, for example, the imitation of archery and the hurling of javelins, and of all sorts of blows. And when the imitation is of brave bodies and souls, and the action is direct and muscular, giving for the most part a straight movement to the limbs of the body -- that, I say, is the true sort; but the opposite is not right.

"In the dance of peace what we have to consider is whether a man bears himself naturally and gracefully, and after the manner of men who duly conform to the law. But before proceeding I must distinguish the dancing about which there is any doubt, from that about which there is no doubt. Which is the doubtful kind, and how are the two to be distinguished? There are dances of the Bacchic sort, both those in which, as they say, they imitate drunken men, and which are named after the Nymphs, and Pan, and Silenuses, and Satyrs; and also those in which purifications are made or mysteries celebrated -- all this sort of dancing cannot be rightly defined as having either a peaceful or a warlike character, or indeed as having any meaning whatever and may, I think, be most truly described as distinct from the warlike dance, and distinct from the peaceful, and not suited for a city at all. There let it lie; and so leaving it to lie, we will proceed to the dances of war and peace, for with these we are undoubtedly concerned."
D: Refashioned:
Having finished with wrestling, the Athenian turned to other bodily movements, which may be generally called dancing. Dance has two kinds: noble dance imitating the honorable, and ignoble dance imitating the base. Each subdivides further.

Of the serious dances, one belongs to war and vigorous action -- the exercise of a noble person and courageous heart. The other expresses a temperate soul enjoying prosperity and moderate pleasures -- this is the dance of peace. The war dance, rightly termed the Pyrrhic, imitates evasive maneuvers: dropping, giving way, springing aside, rising, and falling -- and also offensive actions like archery, javelin throwing, and striking. When the imitation depicts brave bodies and souls with direct, muscular, straight-limbed movement, it is genuine; its opposite is not.

The dance of peace concerns whether a person carries himself naturally, gracefully, and in conformity with the law. But a distinction must first be drawn between dances that are doubtful and those that are not. The Bacchic dances -- those imitating drunken men and named after Nymphs, Pan, Silenuses, and Satyrs, including purification rites and mystery celebrations -- cannot properly be classified as either warlike or peaceful. They have no clear meaning and are not suited for a city at all. Setting these aside, they would deal only with the dances of war and peace.
D: Elegant: Dance divides into noble (imitating the honorable) and ignoble (imitating the base). Noble dance subdivides further: the Pyrrhic war dance imitates combat evasion and attack with direct, muscular movement; the peaceful dance expresses temperance and graceful lawfulness. Bacchic dances -- imitating drunkenness, named after Nymphs, Pan, Satyrs -- have no clear character and are unfit for the city. Only war and peace dances concern us.
D: Summary: The Athenian classifies dance into noble and ignoble, subdividing noble dance into the Pyrrhic war dance (imitating combat with direct, muscular movement) and the peaceful dance (expressing temperance and grace). Bacchic dances are excluded as having no clear meaning and being unsuitable for the city.
D: Distilled: Dance divides into the Pyrrhic war dance of direct muscular combat-imitation, the peaceful dance of temperate grace, and the excluded Bacchic dances that serve no civic purpose.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The Pyrrhic dance (pyrrhiche) was a famous armed dance performed at the Panathenaea and other festivals -- tradition attributed its invention to Pyrrhus (Neoptolemus) or Pyrrhichus of Crete. The classification system (noble/ignoble, war/peace, plus excluded Bacchic) is systematic and characteristic of the Laws' taxonomic approach. The exclusion of Bacchic/Dionysian dance is notable given Dionysus' importance to Athenian culture and the earlier establishment of a Dionysian chorus (Laws 2.665d). Compare Republic 399e-400a on appropriate and inappropriate musical modes. The emphasis on 'straight movement' in the Pyrrhic connects to Greek aesthetic preference for directness (euthytes) as a moral quality.

D: Original: Now the unwarlike muse, which honours in dance the Gods and the sons of the Gods, is entirely associated with the consciousness of prosperity; this class may be subdivided into two lesser classes, of which one is expressive of an escape from some labour or danger into good, and has greater pleasures, the other expressive of preservation and increase of former good, in which the pleasure is less exciting;-in all these cases, every man when the pleasure is greater, moves his body more, and less when the pleasure is less; and, again, if he be more orderly and has learned courage from discipline he waves less, but if he be a coward, and has no training or self-control, he makes greater and more violent movements, and in general when he is speaking or singing he is not altogether able to keep his body still; and so out of the imitation of words in gestures the whole art of dancing has arisen. And in these various kinds of imitation one man moves in an orderly, another in a disorderly manner; and as the ancients may be observed to have given many names which are according to nature and deserving of praise, so there is an excellent one which they have given to the dances of men who in their times of prosperity are moderate in their pleasures-the giver of names, whoever he was, assigned to them a very true, and poetical, and rational name, when he called them Emmeleiai, or dances of order, thus establishing two kinds of dances of the nobler sort, the dance of war which he called the Pyrrhic, and the dance of peace which he called Emmeleia, or the dance of order; giving to each their appropriate and becoming name. These things the legislator should indicate in general outline, and the guardian of the law should enquire into them and search them out, combining dancing with music, and assigning to the several sacrificial feasts that which is suitable to them; and when he has consecrated all of them in due order, he shall for the future change nothing, whether of dance or song. Thenceforward the city and the citizens shall continue to have the same pleasures, themselves being as far as possible alike, and shall live well and happily.
D: Narrative:
"Now the unwarlike muse, which honours in dance the Gods and the sons of the Gods, is entirely associated with the consciousness of prosperity; this class may be subdivided into two lesser classes, of which one is expressive of an escape from some labour or danger into good, and has greater pleasures, the other expressive of preservation and increase of former good, in which the pleasure is less exciting; -- in all these cases, every man when the pleasure is greater, moves his body more, and less when the pleasure is less; and, again, if he be more orderly and has learned courage from discipline he waves less, but if he be a coward, and has no training or self-control, he makes greater and more violent movements, and in general when he is speaking or singing he is not altogether able to keep his body still; and so out of the imitation of words in gestures the whole art of dancing has arisen.

"And in these various kinds of imitation one man moves in an orderly, another in a disorderly manner; and as the ancients may be observed to have given many names which are according to nature and deserving of praise, so there is an excellent one which they have given to the dances of men who in their times of prosperity are moderate in their pleasures -- the giver of names, whoever he was, assigned to them a very true, and poetical, and rational name, when he called them Emmeleiai, or dances of order, thus establishing two kinds of dances of the nobler sort, the dance of war which he called the Pyrrhic, and the dance of peace which he called Emmeleia, or the dance of order; giving to each their appropriate and becoming name. These things the legislator should indicate in general outline, and the guardian of the law should enquire into them and search them out, combining dancing with music, and assigning to the several sacrificial feasts that which is suitable to them; and when he has consecrated all of them in due order, he shall for the future change nothing, whether of dance or song. Thenceforward the city and the citizens shall continue to have the same pleasures, themselves being as far as possible alike, and shall live well and happily."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian continued: the peaceful muse, which honors the gods and their children in dance, is entirely connected with the sense of prosperity. It subdivides into two: one expressing relief at escape from labor or danger into good fortune, which brings more intense pleasure; the other expressing the preservation and increase of existing good, which brings calmer pleasure.

In both cases, greater pleasure produces greater bodily movement, lesser pleasure less. The orderly person, disciplined in courage, moves less; the cowardly and undisciplined person makes larger, more violent movements. In general, when speaking or singing, people cannot keep their bodies entirely still -- and it is from this imitation of words through gestures that the entire art of dancing arose.

Some imitate in an orderly fashion, others in a disorderly one. The ancients gave an excellent name to the dances of people who are moderate in their pleasures during prosperity: they called them Emmeleiai -- dances of order. This established two noble dance types: the Pyrrhic for war and the Emmeleia for peace. The legislator should indicate these in general outline; the guardian of the law should investigate them further, combining dance with music and assigning each to its proper sacrificial feast. Once consecrated, nothing should be changed -- neither dance nor song. The city and its citizens will thenceforth share the same pleasures, being as alike as possible, and will live well and happily.
D: Elegant: The peaceful muse divides into dances of relief from danger (more intense pleasure) and dances preserving existing good (calmer pleasure). Greater pleasure produces greater movement; discipline restrains it. Dancing itself arose from the body's inability to stay still during speech and song. The ancients named the orderly peace dance Emmeleia, matching the Pyrrhic war dance. Once consecrated and assigned to festivals, neither dance nor song should ever change -- ensuring shared pleasures and civic happiness.
D: Summary: The peaceful dance subdivides into exuberant celebration of escape from danger and calmer celebration of ongoing prosperity. Dancing arose from the body's natural response to speech and song. The Emmeleia (dance of order) and Pyrrhic form the two noble dance types. Once established and consecrated, they must never change.
D: Distilled: Dancing arose from the body's natural movement during speech and song, and the two noble forms -- Pyrrhic for war and Emmeleia for peace -- once consecrated, must remain forever unchanged.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The etymology of Emmeleia (from en melos, 'in tune/in order') was already discussed in antiquity. The observation that dancing arises from the body's inability to remain still during speech is a naturalistic origin theory for the art. The psychological observation about discipline and self-control in movement connects dance quality to character -- the orderly person dances moderately, the undisciplined person flails. The final injunction against change recapitulates the conservative principle from 7.20-21. The vision of unchanging shared pleasures as the foundation of civic happiness is the Laws' distinctive social ideal.

D: Original: I have described the dances which are appropriate to noble bodies and generous souls. But it is necessary also to consider and know uncomely persons and thoughts, and those which are intended to produce laughter in comedy, and have a comic character in respect of style, song, and dance, and of the imitations which these afford. For serious things cannot be understood without laughable things, nor opposites at all without opposites, if a man is really to have intelligence of either; but he can not carry out both in action, if he is to have any degree of virtue. And for this very reason he should learn them both, in order that he may not in ignorance do or say anything which is ridiculous and out of place-he should command slaves and hired strangers to imitate such things, but he should never take any serious interest in them himself, nor should any freeman or freewoman be discovered taking pains to learn them; and there should always be some element of novelty in the imitation. Let these then be laid down, both in law and in our discourse, as the regulations of laughable amusements which are generally called comedy. And, if any of the serious poets, as they are termed, who write tragedy, come to us and say-"O strangers, may we go to your city and country or may we not, and shall we bring with us our poetry-what is your will about these matters?"-how shall we answer the divine men? I think that our answer should be as follows:-Best of strangers, we will say to them, we also according to our ability are tragic poets, and our tragedy is the best and noblest; for our whole state is an imitation of the best and noblest life, which we affirm to be indeed the very truth of tragedy. You are poets and we are poets, both makers of the same strains, rivals and antagonists in the noblest of dramas, which true law can alone perfect, as our hope is. Do not then suppose that we shall all in a moment allow you to erect your stage in the agora, or introduce the fair voices of your actors, speaking above our own, and permit you to harangue our women and children, and the common people, about our institutions, in language other than our own, and very often the opposite of our own.
D: Narrative:
"I have described the dances which are appropriate to noble bodies and generous souls. But it is necessary also to consider and know uncomely persons and thoughts, and those which are intended to produce laughter in comedy, and have a comic character in respect of style, song, and dance, and of the imitations which these afford. For serious things cannot be understood without laughable things, nor opposites at all without opposites, if a man is really to have intelligence of either; but he can not carry out both in action, if he is to have any degree of virtue. And for this very reason he should learn them both, in order that he may not in ignorance do or say anything which is ridiculous and out of place -- he should command slaves and hired strangers to imitate such things, but he should never take any serious interest in them himself, nor should any freeman or freewoman be discovered taking pains to learn them; and there should always be some element of novelty in the imitation.

"Let these then be laid down, both in law and in our discourse, as the regulations of laughable amusements which are generally called comedy. And, if any of the serious poets, as they are termed, who write tragedy, come to us and say -- 'O strangers, may we go to your city and country or may we not, and shall we bring with us our poetry -- what is your will about these matters?' -- how shall we answer the divine men? I think that our answer should be as follows: 'Best of strangers,' we will say to them, 'we also according to our ability are tragic poets, and our tragedy is the best and noblest; for our whole state is an imitation of the best and noblest life, which we affirm to be indeed the very truth of tragedy. You are poets and we are poets, both makers of the same strains, rivals and antagonists in the noblest of dramas, which true law can alone perfect, as our hope is. Do not then suppose that we shall all in a moment allow you to erect your stage in the agora, or introduce the fair voices of your actors, speaking above our own, and permit you to harangue our women and children, and the common people, about our institutions, in language other than our own, and very often the opposite of our own.'"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian had described dances appropriate to noble bodies and generous souls. But it was also necessary to consider and understand uncomely persons and thoughts -- the comic characters in style, song, dance, and imitation meant to produce laughter. Serious things cannot be understood without ridiculous things, nor any opposites without their counterparts, if a person is to have genuine understanding of either. However, one cannot practice both serious and comic arts and maintain virtue. For this reason, citizens should learn about comic imitation so they do not unknowingly behave ridiculously, but they should command slaves and hired foreigners to perform such imitations. No freeman or freewoman should be found seriously studying them. Comic imitations should always contain some element of novelty.

These regulations of comic amusement -- comedy, as it is called -- should be established in both law and discourse.

As for the serious poets who write tragedy: if they came asking permission to perform in the city, what should be the answer? The Athenian proposed this response: "Best of strangers, we too are tragic poets, to the best of our ability, and our tragedy is the finest and most noble -- for our entire state is an imitation of the best and noblest life, which we hold to be the very truth of tragedy. You are poets and we are poets, both creators of the same art, rivals and competitors in the noblest of dramas, which only true law can perfect. Do not expect that we will readily allow you to set up your stage in our marketplace, introduce your actors' fine voices to speak above our own, and permit you to address our women, children, and common people about our institutions in language different from ours -- and often the opposite of ours."
D: Elegant: Comedy must be understood -- serious things require knowledge of their opposites -- but free citizens should not practice it themselves; slaves and hired foreigners should perform comic imitations. As for tragic poets seeking admission to the city: 'We too are tragic poets. Our whole state is an imitation of the best and noblest life -- the very truth of tragedy. You are our rivals in the noblest drama. We will not readily let you harangue our people in language opposite to our own.'
D: Summary: Citizens must understand comedy to avoid ridiculousness but should never perform it -- that is for slaves and foreigners. When tragic poets seek admission, the city responds as rival poets: their entire state is the truest tragedy, an imitation of the best life, and they will not easily allow competing voices to address their people.
D: Distilled: Citizens should understand comedy without practicing it, and the city itself is the truest tragedy -- an imitation of the noblest life that rivals any poet's drama.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The claim that 'the state is the truest tragedy' is one of the most celebrated passages in the Laws and one of the most philosophically rich in all of Plato. It redefines tragedy from a theatrical genre to a mode of life -- the well-ordered state 'performs' the best human existence. This transforms the Republic's banishment of tragic poets (Republic 398a, 607a) into something subtler: not rejection but competition. The epistemological argument that 'opposites require opposites' for understanding echoes Phaedo 70d-71a and Heraclitus. The distinction between knowing comedy and practicing it is pragmatic -- understand vice but do not embody it.

D: Original:
For a state would be mad which gave you this licence, until the magistrates had determined whether your poetry might be recited, and was fit for publication or not. Wherefore, O ye sons and scions of the softer Muses, first of all show your songs to the magistrates, and let them compare them with our own, and if they are the same or better we will give you a chorus; but if not, then, my friends, we cannot. Let these, then, be the customs ordained by law about all dances and the teaching of them, and let matters relating to slaves be separated from those relating to masters, if you do not object.

We can have no hesitation in assenting when you put the matter thus.
D: Narrative:
"For a state would be mad which gave you this licence, until the magistrates had determined whether your poetry might be recited, and was fit for publication or not. Wherefore, O ye sons and scions of the softer Muses, first of all show your songs to the magistrates, and let them compare them with our own, and if they are the same or better we will give you a chorus; but if not, then, my friends, we cannot.

"Let these, then, be the customs ordained by law about all dances and the teaching of them, and let matters relating to slaves be separated from those relating to masters, if you do not object."

"We can have no hesitation in assenting when you put the matter thus."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian continued: a state would be insane to grant poets a license to perform without magistrates first determining whether their poetry was fit for recitation and publication. Therefore, sons and heirs of the gentler Muses should first submit their songs to the magistrates, who will compare them with the city's own compositions. If the poets' works are equal to or better than these, they will receive a chorus; if not, they cannot be permitted.

These customs should be ordained by law for all dances and their teaching. Matters relating to slaves should be separated from those relating to free citizens.

Cleinias assented without hesitation.
D: Elegant: No state should let poets perform without magistrates first judging whether their work is fit for publication. Poets must submit their songs for comparison with the city's own -- equal or better earns a chorus, otherwise they are refused. These customs become law for all dance and teaching, with slave matters separated from free citizens' concerns.
D: Summary: Poets must submit compositions to magistrates for comparison with the city's official works before receiving permission to perform. Only equal or superior works earn a chorus. These regulations for dance and teaching are established as law, with slave and citizen matters kept separate.
D: Distilled: Visiting poets must pass magistrate review by matching or surpassing the city's own compositions before being granted permission to perform.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The phrase 'softer Muses' (malthakoteron Mouson) is gently condescending -- implying that the poets' art is pleasant but lacks the rigor of true legislation. The review mechanism -- comparison with official compositions -- creates a competitive standard rather than arbitrary censorship. This is more sophisticated than the Republic's outright ban. The separation of slave and free citizen regulations in dance and education reflects the pervasive Greek distinction between free and unfree, which the Laws accepts even while being progressive in other ways. The 'chorus' granted to approved poets refers to the state funding of dramatic performances.

D: Original:
There still remain three studies suitable for freemen. Arithmetic is one of them; the measurement of length, surface, and depth is the second; and the third has to do with the revolutions of the stars in relation to one another. Not every one has need to toil through all these things in a strictly scientific manner, but only a few, and who they are to be we will hereafter indicate at the end, which will be the proper place; not to know what is necessary for mankind in general, and what is the truth, is disgraceful to every one: and yet to enter into these matters minutely is neither easy, nor at all possible for every one; but there is something in them which is necessary and cannot be set aside, and probably he who made the proverb about God originally had this in view when he said, that "not even God himself can fight against necessity";-he meant, if I am not mistaken, divine necessity; for as to the human necessities of which the many speak, when they talk in this manner, nothing can be more ridiculous than such an application of the words.

And what necessities of knowledge are there, Stranger, which are divine and not human?
D: Narrative:
"There still remain three studies suitable for freemen. Arithmetic is one of them; the measurement of length, surface, and depth is the second; and the third has to do with the revolutions of the stars in relation to one another. Not every one has need to toil through all these things in a strictly scientific manner, but only a few, and who they are to be we will hereafter indicate at the end, which will be the proper place; not to know what is necessary for mankind in general, and what is the truth, is disgraceful to every one: and yet to enter into these matters minutely is neither easy, nor at all possible for every one; but there is something in them which is necessary and cannot be set aside, and probably he who made the proverb about God originally had this in view when he said, that 'not even God himself can fight against necessity'; -- he meant, if I am not mistaken, divine necessity; for as to the human necessities of which the many speak, when they talk in this manner, nothing can be more ridiculous than such an application of the words."

"And what necessities of knowledge are there, Stranger, which are divine and not human?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian identified three remaining studies suitable for free citizens: arithmetic; the measurement of length, surface, and depth (geometry); and the study of celestial revolutions -- astronomy. Not everyone needs to master these subjects with full scientific rigor; only a few will, and who they are will be specified later at the appropriate point.

However, basic ignorance of what is necessary for humanity in general, and of what is true, is disgraceful for everyone. Yet to pursue these matters in minute detail is neither easy nor possible for all. There is something in them that is necessary and cannot be set aside. Probably the originator of the proverb about God had precisely this in mind when he said, "Not even God himself can fight against necessity" -- meaning divine necessity. As for the human necessities that ordinary people invoke when quoting this saying, nothing could be more ridiculous than such a trivial application.

Cleinias asked what necessities of knowledge are divine rather than human.
D: Elegant: Three studies remain for free citizens: arithmetic, geometry, and astronomy. Not everyone must master them scientifically, but basic ignorance of necessary truths is disgraceful. Something in these studies is divinely necessary -- as the proverb says, 'not even God can fight against necessity.' This means divine necessity, not the trivial human necessities people usually cite.
D: Summary: The Athenian introduces arithmetic, geometry, and astronomy as the three remaining studies for free citizens. Full scientific mastery is needed by only a few, but basic knowledge is universally required. He reinterprets the proverb about divine necessity to mean that certain mathematical and astronomical truths are inescapable.
D: Distilled: Arithmetic, geometry, and astronomy are necessary studies: everyone must grasp their basics, though only a few need full mastery, because these truths constitute divine necessity itself.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The three mathematical disciplines correspond roughly to the Republic's educational program (Republic 521c-531d: arithmetic, plane geometry, solid geometry, astronomy), though simplified. The proverb 'not even God fights against necessity' (ananke) was attributed to various sources -- Simonides, Pittacus, and others. The Athenian's reinterpretation -- applying it to mathematical/astronomical necessity rather than human circumstances -- is philosophically sophisticated, connecting mathematical truth to divine order. This passage transitions from cultural education to the mathematical curriculum that culminates in astronomy (7.72-76).

D: Original: I conceive them to be those of which he who has no use nor any knowledge at all cannot be a God, or demi-god, or hero to mankind, or able to take any serious thought or charge of them. And very unlike a divine man would he be, who is unable to count one, two, three, or to distinguish odd and even numbers, or is unable to count at all, or reckon night and day, and who is totally unacquainted with the revolution of the sun and moon, and the other stars. There would be great folly in supposing that all these are not necessary parts of knowledge to him who intends to know anything about the highest kinds of knowledge; but which these are, and how many there are of them, and when they are to be learned, and what is to be learned together and what apart, and the whole correlation of them, must be rightly apprehended first; and these leading the way we may proceed to the other parts of knowledge. For so necessity grounded in nature constrains us, against which we say that no God contends, or ever will contend.
D: Narrative: "I conceive them to be those of which he who has no use nor any knowledge at all cannot be a God, or demi-god, or hero to mankind, or able to take any serious thought or charge of them. And very unlike a divine man would he be, who is unable to count one, two, three, or to distinguish odd and even numbers, or is unable to count at all, or reckon night and day, and who is totally unacquainted with the revolution of the sun and moon, and the other stars. There would be great folly in supposing that all these are not necessary parts of knowledge to him who intends to know anything about the highest kinds of knowledge; but which these are, and how many there are of them, and when they are to be learned, and what is to be learned together and what apart, and the whole correlation of them, must be rightly apprehended first; and these leading the way we may proceed to the other parts of knowledge. For so necessity grounded in nature constrains us, against which we say that no God contends, or ever will contend."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian defined divine necessities as follows: no god, demigod, or hero could take serious thought or charge of humanity without knowledge of them. Anyone unable to count one, two, three -- or to distinguish odd from even numbers -- or unable to count at all, reckon day from night, or understand the revolutions of the sun, moon, and stars would be utterly unlike a divine being.

It would be foolish to suppose these are not necessary components of knowledge for anyone who aspires to the highest learning. Which subjects these are, how many, when they should be learned, what should be learned together and what separately, and their entire interrelation must be properly understood first. These foundational studies lead the way to all other knowledge. Natural necessity compels this order -- and against this necessity, no god contends or ever will contend.
D: Elegant: Divine necessities: no god, demigod, or hero could govern humanity without counting, distinguishing odd from even, reckoning day from night, and understanding celestial motions. These are foundational to all higher knowledge. Their number, sequence, and interrelation must be grasped first. Natural necessity demands this order, and no god fights against it.
D: Summary: The divine necessities are mathematical and astronomical knowledge -- counting, number theory, and understanding celestial motions. Without these, no god or hero could govern humanity. These foundational studies must be properly ordered before any higher knowledge is possible.
D: Distilled: Mathematical and astronomical knowledge constitutes divine necessity -- the foundation without which no higher understanding or governance of human affairs is possible.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The argument that even gods need mathematical knowledge is remarkable -- it elevates mathematics to a divine and ontological status. This connects to the Timaeus' mathematical cosmology and the Republic's placing of mathematics as the pathway to the Forms (Republic 525a-526e). The sequence -- counting, number theory, calendar reckoning, astronomy -- mirrors the Republic's mathematical curriculum but is presented as divinely necessary rather than merely philosophically useful. The final statement ('no god contends against this necessity') frames mathematical truth as more fundamental even than divine will, anticipating Leibniz's rationalism.

D: Original:
I think, Stranger, that what you have now said is very true and agreeable to nature.

Yes, Cleinias, that is so. But it is difficult for the legislator to begin with these studies; at a more convenient time we will make regulations for them.

You seem, Stranger, to be afraid of our habitual ignorance of the subject: there is no reason why that should prevent you from speaking out.

I certainly am afraid of the difficulties to which you allude, but I am still more afraid of those who apply themselves to this sort of knowledge, and apply themselves badly. For entire ignorance is not so terrible or extreme an evil, and is far from being the greatest of all; too much cleverness and too much learning, accompanied with an ill bringing up, are far more fatal.

True.
D: Narrative:
"I think, Stranger, that what you have now said is very true and agreeable to nature."

"Yes, Cleinias, that is so. But it is difficult for the legislator to begin with these studies; at a more convenient time we will make regulations for them."

"You seem, Stranger, to be afraid of our habitual ignorance of the subject: there is no reason why that should prevent you from speaking out."

"I certainly am afraid of the difficulties to which you allude, but I am still more afraid of those who apply themselves to this sort of knowledge, and apply themselves badly. For entire ignorance is not so terrible or extreme an evil, and is far from being the greatest of all; too much cleverness and too much learning, accompanied with an ill bringing up, are far more fatal."

"True."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias found the Athenian's argument both true and in accordance with nature. The Athenian agreed but noted the difficulty of legislating these studies from the outset -- he proposed making regulations at a more convenient time.

Cleinias accused him of being afraid of their habitual ignorance. The Athenian replied that while he was concerned about their ignorance, he feared something far worse: people who study these subjects and study them badly. Complete ignorance is not the most terrible evil -- it is far from the greatest. Excessive cleverness and learning combined with poor moral education are far more dangerous.

Cleinias agreed this was true.
D: Elegant: The difficulty is not ignorance itself but poorly directed learning. Complete ignorance is far from the greatest evil -- excessive cleverness with bad moral education is far more dangerous. The legislator must be cautious about mathematical studies.
D: Summary: The Athenian acknowledges the difficulty of legislating mathematical studies but warns that the greater danger is not ignorance but poorly directed learning. Excessive cleverness without proper moral formation is far more destructive than complete ignorance.
D: Distilled: Complete ignorance is less dangerous than excessive learning combined with poor moral education, making the proper ordering of mathematical studies critical.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The warning that 'too much cleverness and too much learning with ill bringing up are far more fatal' than ignorance is one of the Laws' most important educational principles. It echoes the Republic's concern about philosophers corrupted by sophistical education (Republic 490d-495b) and resonates with Socrates' distinction in the Apology between wisdom and mere cleverness. The passage anticipates the danger of technical expertise divorced from ethical formation -- a perennial concern in education theory. Compare Protagoras 312b-313c on the danger of education from the wrong teachers.

D: Original: All freemen, I conceive, should learn as much of these branches of knowledge as every child in Egypt is taught when he learns the alphabet. In that country arithmetical games have been invented for the use of mere children, which they learn as a pleasure and amusement. They have to distribute apples and garlands, using the same number sometimes for a larger and sometimes for a lesser number of persons; and they arrange pugilists, and wrestlers as they pair together by lot or remain over, and show how their turns come in natural order. Another mode of amusing them is to distribute vessels, sometimes of gold, brass, silver, and the like, intermixed with one another, sometimes of one metal only; as I was saying they adapt to their amusement the numbers in common use, and in this way make more intelligible to their pupils the arrangements and movements of armies and expeditions, in the management of a household they make people more useful to themselves, and more wide awake; and again in measurements of things which have length, and breadth, and depth, they free us from that natural ignorance of all these things which is so ludicrous and disgraceful.
D: Narrative: "All freemen, I conceive, should learn as much of these branches of knowledge as every child in Egypt is taught when he learns the alphabet. In that country arithmetical games have been invented for the use of mere children, which they learn as a pleasure and amusement. They have to distribute apples and garlands, using the same number sometimes for a larger and sometimes for a lesser number of persons; and they arrange pugilists, and wrestlers as they pair together by lot or remain over, and show how their turns come in natural order. Another mode of amusing them is to distribute vessels, sometimes of gold, brass, silver, and the like, intermixed with one another, sometimes of one metal only; as I was saying they adapt to their amusement the numbers in common use, and in this way make more intelligible to their pupils the arrangements and movements of armies and expeditions, in the management of a household they make people more useful to themselves, and more wide awake; and again in measurements of things which have length, and breadth, and depth, they free us from that natural ignorance of all these things which is so ludicrous and disgraceful."
D: Refashioned:
