The Athenian proposed that all free citizens should learn as much of these subjects as every child in Egypt learns alongside the alphabet. In Egypt, arithmetical games have been invented for very young children, who learn them as pleasure and amusement. Children practice distributing apples and garlands, using the same number for larger or smaller groups of people. They arrange boxers and wrestlers in pairs by lot, showing how turns proceed in natural order. They also distribute vessels -- sometimes of gold, brass, and silver mixed together, sometimes of a single metal -- adapting everyday numbers to these games.

This approach makes military arrangements and troop movements more intelligible. In household management, it makes people more capable and alert. In measurement of length, breadth, and depth, it frees people from that natural ignorance that is both ludicrous and disgraceful.
D: Elegant: Every free citizen should learn as much mathematics as Egyptian children do through play. Egyptians teach arithmetic through games: distributing apples and garlands among varying groups, pairing wrestlers by lot, sorting mixed vessels of gold, brass, and silver. This makes military logistics, household management, and spatial measurement intuitive -- freeing citizens from the ludicrous ignorance of basic quantitative reasoning.
D: Summary: The Athenian cites Egypt's model of teaching mathematics through games and play, making arithmetic intuitive through everyday activities like distributing objects and pairing athletes. This approach develops practical skills in military logistics, household management, and spatial measurement.
D: Distilled: Mathematics should be taught through playful games as in Egypt, making arithmetic, logistics, and measurement intuitive rather than abstract.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). Egypt again serves as a positive model (see also 7.23 on music). The mathematical games described are remarkably concrete and pedagogically sound -- distributing objects among groups teaches division, pairing by lot teaches combinatorics, and sorting mixed vessels teaches classification. The practical applications listed (military logistics, household management, measurement) show the Laws' commitment to useful knowledge. Compare Republic 525c-526b, where Socrates also advocates teaching arithmetic through practical problems but ultimately aims at pure mathematical understanding. The Laws is more firmly grounded in practical utility.

D: Original:
What kind of ignorance do you mean?

O my dear Cleinias, I, like yourself, have late in life heard with amazement of our ignorance in these matters; to me we appear to be more like pigs than men, and I am quite ashamed, not only of myself, but of all Hellenes.

About what? Say, Stranger, what you mean.

I will; or rather I will show you my meaning by a question, and do you please to answer me: You know, I suppose, what length is?

Certainly.

And what breadth is?

To be sure.

And you know that these are two distinct things, and that there is a third thing called depth?

Of course.

And do not all these seem to you to be commensurable with themselves?

Yes.

That is to say, length is naturally commensurable with length, and breadth with breadth, and depth in like manner with depth?

Undoubtedly.

But if some things are commensurable and others wholly incommensurable, and you think that all things are commensurable, what is your position in regard to them?

Clearly, far from good.

Concerning length and breadth when compared with depth, or breadth when and length when compared with one another, are not all the Hellenes agreed that these are commensurable with one in some way?

Quite true.

But if they are absolutely incommensurable, and yet all of us regard them as commensurable, have we not reason to be ashamed of our compatriots; and might we not say to them:-O ye best of Hellenes, is not this one of the things of which we were saying that not to know them is disgraceful, and of which to have a bare knowledge only is no great distinction?

Certainly.

And there are other things akin to these, in which there spring up other errors of the same family.

What are they?
D: Narrative:
"What kind of ignorance do you mean?"

"O my dear Cleinias, I, like yourself, have late in life heard with amazement of our ignorance in these matters; to me we appear to be more like pigs than men, and I am quite ashamed, not only of myself, but of all Hellenes."

"About what? Say, Stranger, what you mean."

"I will; or rather I will show you my meaning by a question, and do you please to answer me: You know, I suppose, what length is?"

"Certainly."

"And what breadth is?"

"To be sure."

"And you know that these are two distinct things, and that there is a third thing called depth?"

"Of course."

"And do not all these seem to you to be commensurable with themselves?"

"Yes."

"That is to say, length is naturally commensurable with length, and breadth with breadth, and depth in like manner with depth?"

"Undoubtedly."

"But if some things are commensurable and others wholly incommensurable, and you think that all things are commensurable, what is your position in regard to them?"

"Clearly, far from good."

"Concerning length and breadth when compared with depth, or breadth and length when compared with one another, are not all the Hellenes agreed that these are commensurable with one another in some way?"

"Quite true."

"But if they are absolutely incommensurable, and yet all of us regard them as commensurable, have we not reason to be ashamed of our compatriots; and might we not say to them: 'O ye best of Hellenes, is not this one of the things of which we were saying that not to know them is disgraceful, and of which to have a bare knowledge only is no great distinction?'"

"Certainly."

"And there are other things akin to these, in which there spring up other errors of the same family."

"What are they?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias asked what kind of ignorance was meant. The Athenian confessed that he himself had only late in life been amazed by this particular ignorance -- it made Greeks seem more like pigs than men, and he was ashamed not only for himself but for all Hellenes.

Cleinias pressed him to explain. The Athenian proceeded through questions: Do you know what length is? Yes. Breadth? Yes. These are two distinct things, and there is a third called depth? Of course. Are these commensurable with themselves -- length with length, breadth with breadth, depth with depth? Undoubtedly.

But, the Athenian asked, if some quantities are commensurable and others wholly incommensurable, and you mistakenly think all are commensurable, what is your position? Clearly very poor, Cleinias admitted.

Do not all Greeks agree that length, breadth, and depth are commensurable with one another in some way? Yes. But if they are actually incommensurable -- and yet everyone treats them as commensurable -- should they not be ashamed? Could they not say to their compatriots: "O best of Hellenes, is this not one of those things where ignorance is disgraceful and mere basic knowledge is no great distinction?"

Cleinias agreed, and asked what other related errors existed.
D: Elegant: The Athenian is ashamed of Greek ignorance about incommensurability -- it makes them seem like pigs. Through Socratic questioning he reveals: everyone assumes length, breadth, and depth are always commensurable with each other, but some are absolutely incommensurable. This widespread mathematical error is disgraceful, and merely knowing the basics is no great distinction.
D: Summary: Through Socratic questioning, the Athenian reveals the widespread Greek ignorance of incommensurability -- the fact that some magnitudes (like the diagonal and side of a square) cannot be expressed as ratios. This mathematical illiteracy shames all Greeks and represents the kind of ignorance that is truly disgraceful.
D: Distilled: Greek ignorance of mathematical incommensurability -- assuming all magnitudes are rationally comparable -- is shameful and represents a fundamental failure in understanding reality.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The discovery of incommensurability (that the diagonal of a square is irrational relative to its side, proved by the Pythagoreans) was one of the great crises in Greek mathematics. The Athenian's shame about Greek ignorance echoes Theaetetus 147c-148b, where the young Theaetetus works on the theory of irrationals. The 'pigs' comparison is characteristically Platonic bluntness. The Socratic method of questioning used here recalls the Meno's geometry lesson (Meno 82a-86b). The passage argues that mathematical truths about incommensurability are not esoteric but fundamental to any educated person's understanding of reality.

D: Original: The natures of commensurable and incommensurable quantities in their relation to one another. A man who is good for a thing ought to be able, when he thinks, to distinguish them; and different persons should compete with one another in asking questions, which will be a fair, better and more graceful way of passing their time than the old man's game of draughts.
D: Narrative: "The natures of commensurable and incommensurable quantities in their relation to one another. A man who is good for anything ought to be able, when he thinks, to distinguish them; and different persons should compete with one another in asking questions, which will be a far better and more graceful way of passing their time than the old man's game of draughts."
D: Refashioned: The Athenian identified the related errors: the relationship between commensurable and incommensurable quantities. Anyone worth anything ought to be able to distinguish between them through careful thought. People should compete with one another in posing and solving such mathematical questions -- a far better and more elegant way to pass the time than the old man's game of draughts.
D: Elegant: Understanding the relationship between commensurable and incommensurable quantities is essential for anyone of worth. Competing in mathematical questions is a far more elegant pastime than playing draughts.
D: Summary: The Athenian states that distinguishing commensurable from incommensurable quantities is a mark of intellectual competence, and that competitive mathematical questioning is a nobler pastime than board games.
D: Distilled: Distinguishing commensurable from incommensurable quantities is essential intellectual competence, and mathematical challenges surpass board games as worthy pastimes.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The game of draughts (petteia or pessoi) was a popular Greek board game. The comparison between mathematical inquiry and board games echoes the Republic's argument that mathematical pleasures surpass bodily ones (Republic 583b-587a). The competitive format -- 'asking questions' -- recalls the Greek tradition of mathematical challenges between scholars, as well as the Socratic elenchus itself. The passage makes a cultural argument: an intellectually serious society should value mathematical conversation over casual entertainment.

D: Original:
I dare say; and these pastimes are not so very unlike a game of draughts.

And these, as I maintain, Cleinias, are the studies which our youth ought to learn, for they are innocent and not difficult; the learning of them will be an amusement, and they will benefit the state. If anyone is of another mind, let him say what he has to say.

Certainly.

Then if these studies are such as we maintain we will include them; if not, they shall be excluded.

Assuredly: but may we not now, Stranger, prescribe these studies as necessary, and so fill up the lacunae of our laws?

They shall be regarded as pledges which may be hereafter redeemed and removed from our state, if they do not please either us who give them, or you who accept them.

A fair condition.

Next let us see whether we are or are not willing that the study of astronomy shall be proposed for our youth.

Proceed.

Here occurs a strange phenomenon, which certainly cannot in any point of view be tolerated.

To what are you referring?

Men say that we ought not to enquire into the supreme God and the nature of the universe, nor busy ourselves in searching out the causes of things, and that such enquiries are impious; whereas the very opposite is the truth.

What do you mean?

Perhaps what I am saying may seem paradoxical, and at variance with the usual language of age. But when any one has any good and true notion which is for the advantage of the state and in every way acceptable to God, he cannot abstain from expressing it.

Your words are reasonable enough; but shall we find any good or true notion about the stars?

My good friends, at this hour all of us Hellenes tell lies, if I may use such an expression, about those great Gods, the Sun and the Moon.

Lies of what nature?

We say that they and divers other stars do not keep the same path, and we call them planets or wanderers.
D: Narrative:
"I dare say; and these pastimes are not so very unlike a game of draughts."

"And these, as I maintain, Cleinias, are the studies which our youth ought to learn, for they are innocent and not difficult; the learning of them will be an amusement, and they will benefit the state. If anyone is of another mind, let him say what he has to say."

"Certainly."

"Then if these studies are such as we maintain we will include them; if not, they shall be excluded."

"Assuredly: but may we not now, Stranger, prescribe these studies as necessary, and so fill up the lacunae of our laws?"

"They shall be regarded as pledges which may be hereafter redeemed and removed from our state, if they do not please either us who give them, or you who accept them."

"A fair condition."

"Next let us see whether we are or are not willing that the study of astronomy shall be proposed for our youth."

"Proceed."

"Here occurs a strange phenomenon, which certainly cannot in any point of view be tolerated."

"To what are you referring?"

"Men say that we ought not to enquire into the supreme God and the nature of the universe, nor busy ourselves in searching out the causes of things, and that such enquiries are impious; whereas the very opposite is the truth."

"What do you mean?"

"Perhaps what I am saying may seem paradoxical, and at variance with the usual language of age. But when any one has any good and true notion which is for the advantage of the state and in every way acceptable to God, he cannot abstain from expressing it."

"Your words are reasonable enough; but shall we find any good or true notion about the stars?"

"My good friends, at this hour all of us Hellenes tell lies, if I may use such an expression, about those great Gods, the Sun and the Moon."

"Lies of what nature?"

"We say that they and divers other stars do not keep the same path, and we call them planets or wanderers."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed that mathematical pastimes were not so different from draughts after all. The Athenian maintained these studies should be included in youth education: they are harmless, not overly difficult, and will be both amusing and beneficial to the state. If anyone disagreed, they should speak. Cleinias agreed.

The studies would be included if they proved to be as described; otherwise, they would be excluded. Cleinias asked whether they could now prescribe them as necessary to fill the gaps in their laws. The Athenian suggested treating them as provisional pledges that could be withdrawn later if unsatisfactory. Cleinias accepted this as fair.

The Athenian then turned to astronomy, where a strange and intolerable phenomenon occurred. Cleinias asked what he meant. People say, the Athenian explained, that we ought not to inquire into the supreme God and the nature of the universe, nor investigate the causes of things, and that such inquiries are impious. The very opposite is true.

Cleinias was puzzled. The Athenian acknowledged his view might seem paradoxical and contrary to conventional elderly wisdom, but when someone has a true notion that benefits the state and is acceptable to God, he cannot refrain from expressing it.

Cleinias asked whether any good truth could be found about the stars. The Athenian declared that at this very moment, all Greeks are telling lies about the great gods -- the Sun and the Moon. Lies of what nature? They say that the sun, moon, and certain other stars do not follow the same path, and call them planets -- meaning wanderers.
D: Elegant: Mathematical studies are harmless, amusing, and beneficial -- they should be included provisionally. On astronomy: people call scientific inquiry impious, but the opposite is true. All Greeks currently blaspheme the Sun and Moon by calling them 'wanderers' (planets), implying they do not follow fixed paths. This is false.
D: Summary: After provisionally including mathematical studies, the Athenian makes a radical claim: scientific astronomical inquiry is not impious but necessary. All Greeks blaspheme the Sun and Moon by calling them 'planets' (wanderers), when in fact they follow fixed circular paths.
D: Distilled: Scientific inquiry into the heavens is not impious but essential, and the common belief that the Sun and Moon 'wander' is a blasphemous falsehood against the divine celestial order.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). This is one of the Laws' most scientifically important passages. The claim that the planets do not wander but follow fixed circular paths reflects the astronomical theory that Plato reportedly encouraged Eudoxus of Cnidus to develop -- the theory of concentric spheres that 'saved the phenomena' by explaining apparent planetary wandering through combinations of uniform circular motions. The declaration that calling stars 'wanderers' is blasphemous connects astronomy to theology -- the heavenly bodies are divine, and their motions must be orderly. Compare Timaeus 38c-39e on the perfect circular motions of celestial bodies. The defense of scientific inquiry against charges of impiety recalls Socrates' defense in the Apology against accusations of 'investigating things beneath the earth and in the heavens' (Apology 18b).

D: Original:
Very true, Stranger; and in the course of my life I have often myself seen the morning star and the evening star and divers others not moving in their accustomed course, but wandering out of their path in all manner of ways, and I have seen the sun and moon doing what we all know that they do.

Just so, Megillus and Cleinias; and I maintain that our citizens and our youth ought to learn about the nature of the Gods in heaven, so far as to be able to offer sacrifices and pray to them in pious language, and not to blaspheme about them.

There you are right if such a knowledge be only attainable; and if we are wrong in our mode of speaking now, and can be better instructed and learn to use better language, then I quite agree with you that such a degree of knowledge as will enable us to speak rightly should be acquired by us. And now do you try to explain to us your whole meaning, and we, on our part, will endeavour to understand you.
D: Narrative:
"Very true, Stranger; and in the course of my life I have often myself seen the morning star and the evening star and divers others not moving in their accustomed course, but wandering out of their path in all manner of ways, and I have seen the sun and moon doing what we all know that they do."

"Just so, Megillus and Cleinias; and I maintain that our citizens and our youth ought to learn about the nature of the Gods in heaven, so far as to be able to offer sacrifices and pray to them in pious language, and not to blaspheme about them."

"There you are right if such a knowledge be only attainable; and if we are wrong in our mode of speaking now, and can be better instructed and learn to use better language, then I quite agree with you that such a degree of knowledge as will enable us to speak rightly should be acquired by us. And now do you try to explain to us your whole meaning, and we, on our part, will endeavour to understand you."
D: Refashioned:
Megillus confirmed the point from personal observation: he had often seen the morning star, the evening star, and other celestial bodies appearing to stray from their usual courses in various ways, and the sun and moon doing what everyone knows they seem to do.

The Athenian maintained that citizens and youth should learn enough about the nature of the heavenly gods to offer sacrifices and pray in pious language, without blasphemy.

Cleinias agreed: if such knowledge is attainable, and if their current way of speaking is wrong, they should learn to speak rightly. He asked the Athenian to explain his full meaning, promising they would try their best to understand.
D: Elegant: Megillus confirms from observation that celestial bodies appear to wander. The Athenian insists citizens should learn enough astronomy to speak of the heavenly gods without blasphemy. Cleinias agrees: if better understanding is possible, they should seek it. He asks the Athenian to explain fully.
D: Summary: Megillus confirms the apparent wandering of celestial bodies from personal observation. The Athenian argues that astronomical knowledge is needed for proper religious language. Cleinias agrees they should learn to speak accurately about the heavenly gods and asks for full explanation.
D: Distilled: Citizens need enough astronomical knowledge to speak of the heavenly bodies without the blasphemy of calling their orderly motions 'wandering.'
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger, with Megillus participating (dialogue). Megillus' personal observations of apparent planetary retrograde motion are empirically accurate -- planets do appear to reverse direction periodically. The 'morning star' and 'evening star' are both Venus, whose identity was recognized by the Greeks (attributed to Parmenides or Pythagoras). The connection between astronomical accuracy and pious speech -- avoiding 'blasphemy' against divine celestial bodies -- makes theology dependent on science, a remarkable inversion of the usual relationship. This passage shows all three interlocutors contributing, with Megillus providing empirical evidence and Cleinias philosophical openness.

D: Original:
There is some difficulty in understanding my meaning, but not a very great one, nor will any great length of time be required. And of this I am myself a proof; for I did not know these things long ago, nor in the days of my youth, and yet I can explain them to you in a brief space of time; whereas if they had been difficult I could certainly never have explained them all, old as I am, to old men like yourselves.

True; but what is this study which you describe as wonderful and fitting for youth to learn, but of which we are ignorant? Try and explain the nature of it to us as clearly as you can.
D: Narrative:
"There is some difficulty in understanding my meaning, but not a very great one, nor will any great length of time be required. And of this I am myself a proof; for I did not know these things long ago, nor in the days of my youth, and yet I can explain them to you in a brief space of time; whereas if they had been difficult I could certainly never have explained them all, old as I am, to old men like yourselves."

"True; but what is this study which you describe as wonderful and fitting for youth to learn, but of which we are ignorant? Try and explain the nature of it to us as clearly as you can."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian assured them that while there was some difficulty in understanding his meaning, it was not very great, nor would much time be needed. He himself was proof: he had not known these things in his youth and had only learned them recently, yet he could explain them briefly. If they were truly difficult, he -- an old man -- could never have conveyed them to other old men.

Cleinias accepted this but pressed him: what exactly is this wonderful study, suitable for youth yet unknown to them? He asked the Athenian to explain as clearly as possible.
D: Elegant: The astronomy he means is not extremely difficult nor time-consuming -- the Athenian himself learned it late in life and can explain it briefly to fellow elders. Cleinias asks him to make this wonderful subject clear.
D: Summary: The Athenian reassures his companions that the astronomical knowledge he is advocating is neither extremely difficult nor time-consuming -- he himself learned it late in life. He promises a brief explanation to satisfy their curiosity.
D: Distilled: The astronomical truth about celestial motions is accessible even to those who learn it late in life, proving it is neither too difficult nor too lengthy for inclusion in education.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The Athenian's claim that he learned this astronomy 'not long ago' may reflect Plato's own late encounter with Eudoxus' astronomical theory. The argument from personal accessibility is rhetorically effective -- if an old man learned it recently, young people can certainly master it. The modest self-deprecation ('old as I am, to old men like yourselves') is characteristic of the Laws' conversational style, more relaxed than the Republic's. This passage sets up the astronomical revelation in 7.75.

D: Original: I will. For, O my good friends, that other doctrine about the wandering of the sun and the moon and the other stars is not the truth, but the very reverse of the truth. Each of them moves in the same path-not in many paths, but in one only, which is circular, and the varieties are only apparent. Nor are we right in supposing that the swiftest of them is the slowest, nor conversely, that the slowest is the quickest. And if what I say is true, only just imagine that we had a similar notion about horses running at Olympia, or about men who ran in the long course, and that we addressed the swiftest as the slowest and the slowest as the swiftest, and sang the praises of the vanquished as though he were the victor,-in that case our praises would not be true, nor very agreeable to the runners, though they be but men; and now, to commit the same error about the Gods which would have been ludicrous and erroneous in the case of men-is not that ludicrous and erroneous?
D: Narrative: "I will. For, O my good friends, that other doctrine about the wandering of the sun and the moon and the other stars is not the truth, but the very reverse of the truth. Each of them moves in the same path -- not in many paths, but in one only, which is circular, and the varieties are only apparent. Nor are we right in supposing that the swiftest of them is the slowest, nor conversely, that the slowest is the quickest. And if what I say is true, only just imagine that we had a similar notion about horses running at Olympia, or about men who ran in the long course, and that we addressed the swiftest as the slowest and the slowest as the swiftest, and sang the praises of the vanquished as though he were the victor, -- in that case our praises would not be true, nor very agreeable to the runners, though they be but men; and now, to commit the same error about the Gods which would have been ludicrous and erroneous in the case of men -- is not that ludicrous and erroneous?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian delivered his astronomical revelation: the popular doctrine about the wandering of the sun, moon, and stars is not the truth but its very opposite. Each celestial body moves in a single path -- not many paths but one, which is circular. The apparent variations in their courses are just that -- appearances. Furthermore, the conventional wisdom that the fastest celestial body is actually the slowest, and the slowest actually the fastest, is also wrong.

To illustrate: imagine applying the same confusion to horses racing at Olympia or runners in the long course, praising the slowest as the swiftest and the defeated as the victor. Such praise would be both false and offensive to the athletes, though they are merely human. To commit the same error about the gods -- who are the celestial bodies -- is that not equally ludicrous and wrong?
D: Elegant: The truth is the opposite of common belief: each celestial body moves in one circular path, not many. The apparent wandering is just appearance. The supposed fastest are not the slowest, nor vice versa. Imagine praising the loser of an Olympic race as the winner -- that is the error we commit when we misidentify the motions of the celestial gods.
D: Summary: The Athenian reveals that each celestial body follows a single circular path -- apparent wandering is mere appearance. He illustrates the absurdity of misidentifying celestial motions by comparing it to praising the slowest Olympic runner as the fastest -- an error about gods that is even more shameful than about men.
D: Distilled: Each celestial body moves in one circular path, and their apparent wandering is mere illusion -- misidentifying divine motions is as absurd as praising the loser of an Olympic race as the winner.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage presents the core astronomical doctrine that Plato championed: planetary motions are fundamentally circular and uniform, with apparent irregularities explained as combinations of circular motions. This view, developed mathematically by Eudoxus and later refined by Aristotle and Ptolemy, dominated Western astronomy until Kepler. The Olympic analogy is characteristically vivid. The identification of celestial bodies with gods (common in Greek thought) gives astronomical error a theological dimension. Compare Timaeus 38c-39e on the 'perfect number of time' and circular celestial motions. The passage represents one of the most direct statements of Plato's astronomical cosmology.

D: Original:
Worse than ludicrous, I should say.

At all events, the Gods cannot like us to be spreading a false report of them.

Most true, if such is the fact.

And if we can show that such is really the fact, then all these matters ought to be learned so far as is necessary for the avoidance of impiety; but if we cannot, they may be let alone, and let this be our decision.

Very good.
D: Narrative:
"Worse than ludicrous, I should say."

"At all events, the Gods cannot like us to be spreading a false report of them."

"Most true, if such is the fact."

"And if we can show that such is really the fact, then all these matters ought to be learned so far as is necessary for the avoidance of impiety; but if we cannot, they may be let alone, and let this be our decision."

"Very good."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias declared it worse than ludicrous. The Athenian pressed the point: at the very least, the gods cannot approve of humans spreading false reports about them. Cleinias agreed -- if the facts are as stated.

The Athenian concluded: if they can demonstrate that this is indeed the truth, then these astronomical matters must be learned to the extent necessary for avoiding impiety. If they cannot demonstrate it, the subject may be left alone. This would be their decision.

Cleinias agreed to these terms.
D: Elegant: The gods cannot approve false reports about them. If the circular-path doctrine can be demonstrated, astronomy must be studied to avoid impiety. If not, it may be set aside.
D: Summary: Cleinias agrees the error is worse than ridiculous. The Athenian establishes a conditional: if they can prove celestial bodies follow circular paths, astronomy becomes necessary to avoid impiety; if not, the subject may be dropped.
D: Distilled: If the truth of uniform circular celestial motion can be demonstrated, then learning astronomy becomes a religious duty to avoid impiety toward the gods.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The conditional structure -- 'if we can show' -- is characteristically careful. The Athenian does not demand blind acceptance but makes the requirement for astronomical education contingent on demonstration. This reflects the Laws' respect for reasoned persuasion over dogmatic assertion. The connection between astronomical accuracy and piety elevates science to a religious obligation -- a position with significant implications for the history of the relationship between science and religion. The passage is recalled in Laws 10.886a-887c, where astronomical knowledge supports the proof of the gods' existence.

D: Original: Enough of laws relating to education and learning. But hunting and similar pursuits in like manner claim our attention. For the legislator appears to have a duty imposed upon him which goes beyond mere legislation. There is something over and above law which lies in a region between admonition and law, and has several times occurred to us in the course of discussion; for example, in the education of very young children there were things, as we maintain, which are not to be defined, and to regard them as matters of positive law is a great absurdity. Now, our laws and the whole constitution of our state having been thus delineated, the praise of the virtuous citizen is not complete when he is described as the person who serves the laws best and obeys them most, but the higher form of praise is that which describes him as the good citizen who passes through life undefiled and is obedient to the words of the legislator, both when he is giving laws and when he assigns praise and blame. This is the truest word that can be spoken in praise of a citizen; and the true legislator ought not only to write his laws, but also to interweave with them all such things as seem to him honourable and dishonourable. And the perfect citizen ought to seek to strengthen these no less than the principles of law which are sanctioned by punishments.
D: Narrative: "Enough of laws relating to education and learning. But hunting and similar pursuits in like manner claim our attention. For the legislator appears to have a duty imposed upon him which goes beyond mere legislation. There is something over and above law which lies in a region between admonition and law, and has several times occurred to us in the course of discussion; for example, in the education of very young children there were things, as we maintain, which are not to be defined, and to regard them as matters of positive law is a great absurdity. Now, our laws and the whole constitution of our state having been thus delineated, the praise of the virtuous citizen is not complete when he is described as the person who serves the laws best and obeys them most, but the higher form of praise is that which describes him as the good citizen who passes through life undefiled and is obedient to the words of the legislator, both when he is giving laws and when he assigns praise and blame. This is the truest word that can be spoken in praise of a citizen; and the true legislator ought not only to write his laws, but also to interweave with them all such things as seem to him honourable and dishonourable. And the perfect citizen ought to seek to strengthen these no less than the principles of law which are sanctioned by punishments."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian declared that they had said enough about laws relating to education and learning. But hunting and similar pursuits also demanded their attention. The legislator has a duty that goes beyond mere legislation. There is a domain between admonition and formal law that had arisen repeatedly in their discussion -- for instance, the education of very young children involved matters that should not be codified as positive law.

Now that their laws and constitution had been delineated, the Athenian noted that praising a citizen merely for serving and obeying the laws is incomplete. The higher praise belongs to the citizen who lives undefiled and obeys the legislator's words in their entirety -- not only the formal laws but also the legislator's guidance about what is honorable and dishonorable. This is the truest praise of citizenship.

The true legislator must not only write laws but weave into them everything he considers honorable or dishonorable. The perfect citizen should strengthen these moral norms no less than the formal principles of law backed by punishment.
D: Elegant: Education laws are complete, but hunting and related pursuits need attention. The legislator's duty goes beyond formal law into the space between admonition and legislation. The highest citizen is not one who merely obeys laws but one who also follows the legislator's guidance on honor and shame. The true legislator interweaves moral norms with legal requirements, and the perfect citizen upholds both equally.
D: Summary: Transitioning to hunting, the Athenian reaffirms that the legislator's duty extends beyond formal law to moral guidance. The highest citizen obeys not just legal penalties but the legislator's broader vision of honor and shame, and the true legislator interweaves both into a unified code.
D: Distilled: The highest citizenship means obeying not only punishable laws but the legislator's complete moral vision of what is honorable and dishonorable.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage returns to the theme introduced at 7.1 and developed at 7.12 -- the space between formal law and informal custom. The distinction between 'the person who serves the laws best' and the one who follows the legislator's complete moral vision anticipates the modern distinction between legality and morality. The 'true legislator' who interweaves honor and shame with legal requirements recalls the concept of the prelude (prooimion) from Laws 4.722d-723d -- persuasive discourse that accompanies and enriches the bare legal command. The transition to hunting signals the final section of Book 7.

D: Original: I will adduce an example which will clear up my meaning, and will be a sort of witness to my words. Hunting is of wide extent, and has a name under which many things are included, for there is a hunting of creatures in the water, and of creatures in the air, and there is a great deal of hunting of land animals of all kinds, and not of wild beasts only. The hunting after man is also worthy of consideration; there is the hunting after him in war, and there is often a hunting after him in the way of friendship, which is praised and also blamed; and there is thieving, and the hunting which is practised by robbers, and that of armies against armies. Now the legislator, in laying down laws about hunting, can neither abstain from noting these things, nor can he make threatening ordinances which will assign rules and penalties about all of them. What is he to do? He will have to praise and blame hunting with a view to the exercise and pursuits of youth. And, on the other hand, the young man must listen obediently; neither pleasure nor pain should hinder him, and he should regard as his standard of action the praises and injunctions of the legislator rather than the punishments which he imposes by law. This being premised, there will follow next in order moderate praise and censure of hunting; the praise being assigned to that kind which will make the souls of young men better, and the censure to that which has the opposite effect.
D: Narrative:
"I will adduce an example which will clear up my meaning, and will be a sort of witness to my words. Hunting is of wide extent, and has a name under which many things are included, for there is a hunting of creatures in the water, and of creatures in the air, and there is a great deal of hunting of land animals of all kinds, and not of wild beasts only. The hunting after man is also worthy of consideration; there is the hunting after him in war, and there is often a hunting after him in the way of friendship, which is praised and also blamed; and there is thieving, and the hunting which is practised by robbers, and that of armies against armies.

"Now the legislator, in laying down laws about hunting, can neither abstain from noting these things, nor can he make threatening ordinances which will assign rules and penalties about all of them. What is he to do? He will have to praise and blame hunting with a view to the exercise and pursuits of youth. And, on the other hand, the young man must listen obediently; neither pleasure nor pain should hinder him, and he should regard as his standard of action the praises and injunctions of the legislator rather than the punishments which he imposes by law. This being premised, there will follow next in order moderate praise and censure of hunting; the praise being assigned to that kind which will make the souls of young men better, and the censure to that which has the opposite effect."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian offered hunting as an example to clarify his meaning. Hunting is a broad concept encompassing many activities: hunting creatures in water, in the air, and on land -- not only wild beasts. The hunting of human beings also deserves consideration: hunting in war, hunting through friendship (which is both praised and blamed), thieving, robbery, and armies pursuing armies.

The legislator, in making laws about hunting, can neither ignore all these varieties nor create threatening ordinances with penalties for every type. What should he do? He must praise and blame different kinds of hunting with a view to youth education. The young person must listen obediently -- neither pleasure nor pain should interfere -- and should take the legislator's praise and moral injunctions as his guide to action, rather than merely the punishments attached to formal law.

With this principle established, what follows is moderate praise and censure of hunting: praise for the kinds that improve the souls of young men, and censure for those that have the opposite effect.
D: Elegant: Hunting illustrates the point. It encompasses water, air, and land hunting, hunting men in war and friendship, thievery, and robbery. The legislator cannot penalize every type but must praise what improves youth and censure what degrades them. The young should be guided by the legislator's moral praise and blame, not merely by fear of punishment.
D: Summary: Using hunting as his example, the Athenian shows that not all activities can be regulated by punitive law. The legislator must use praise and blame to guide youth toward forms of hunting that improve the soul and away from those that degrade it. Moral guidance, not just punishment, should be the standard.
D: Distilled: Hunting illustrates that moral praise and blame, not just punitive law, must guide youth toward soul-improving activities and away from degrading ones.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The expansive definition of 'hunting' (thera) -- encompassing fishing, bird-catching, war, friendship-seeking, and thievery -- recalls the Sophist 218d-221c, where the Stranger uses a similar taxonomic approach to define the sophist as a kind of hunter. The principle that moral praise and blame operate alongside legal penalties is central to the Laws' educational philosophy and connects to 7.77. The next two passages (7.79-80) will specify which hunts deserve praise and which censure. Compare Xenophon's Cynegeticus (On Hunting), which also argues for hunting's educational value.

D: Original: And now let us address young men in the form of a prayer for their welfare: O friends, we will say to them, may no desire or love of hunting in the sea, or of angling or of catching the creatures in the waters, ever take possession of you, either when you are awake or when you are asleep, by hook or with weels, which latter is a very lazy contrivance; and let not any desire of catching men and of piracy by sea enter into your souls and make you cruel and lawless hunters. And as to the desire of thieving in town or country, may it never enter into your most passing thoughts; nor let the insidious fancy of catching birds, which is hardly worthy of freemen, come into the head of any youth. There remains therefore for our athletes only the hunting and catching of land animals, of which the one sort is called hunting by night, in which the hunters sleep in turn and are lazy; this is not to be commended any more than that which has intervals of rest, in which the will strength of beasts is subdued by nets and snares, and not by the victory of a laborious spirit. Thus, only the best kind of hunting is allowed at all-that of quadrupeds, which is carried on with horses and dogs and men's own persons, and they get the victory over the animals by running them down and striking them and hurling at them, those who have a care of godlike manhood taking them with their own hands.
D: Narrative:
"And now let us address young men in the form of a prayer for their welfare: 'O friends,' we will say to them, 'may no desire or love of hunting in the sea, or of angling or of catching the creatures in the waters, ever take possession of you, either when you are awake or when you are asleep, by hook or with weels, which latter is a very lazy contrivance; and let not any desire of catching men and of piracy by sea enter into your souls and make you cruel and lawless hunters. And as to the desire of thieving in town or country, may it never enter into your most passing thoughts; nor let the insidious fancy of catching birds, which is hardly worthy of freemen, come into the head of any youth.

"'There remains therefore for our athletes only the hunting and catching of land animals, of which the one sort is called hunting by night, in which the hunters sleep in turn and are lazy; this is not to be commended any more than that which has intervals of rest, in which the wild strength of beasts is subdued by nets and snares, and not by the victory of a laborious spirit. Thus, only the best kind of hunting is allowed at all -- that of quadrupeds, which is carried on with horses and dogs and men's own persons, and they get the victory over the animals by running them down and striking them and hurling at them, those who have a care of godlike manhood taking them with their own hands.'"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian addressed the young in the form of a prayer: "O friends, may no desire for sea-hunting, angling, or catching water creatures ever possess you, whether waking or sleeping -- not by hook or by fish-traps, those lazy devices. May no desire for piracy or human hunting at sea enter your souls and make you cruel, lawless hunters. May thieving in town or country never cross even your passing thoughts. And may the sly pursuit of bird-catching, which is hardly worthy of free people, never enter any young person's mind.

"What remains for our athletes is only the hunting of land animals. Of these, one kind -- night-hunting, where hunters sleep in turns -- is lazy and not commendable, just as any method using nets and snares to subdue wild beasts through cleverness rather than the victory of a laborious spirit deserves no praise. Only the best hunting is permitted: hunting quadrupeds with horses, dogs, and one's own body, running animals down, striking them, and hurling weapons at them -- and those who truly care about godlike manhood take their prey with their own hands."
D: Elegant: A prayer for youth: may they never desire sea-fishing, piracy, thieving, or bird-catching -- all unworthy of free people. Even night-hunting and trapping with nets are lazy. Only one form of hunting is truly permitted: pursuing quadrupeds on foot with horses, dogs, and personal strength, subduing them through labor and courage, taking them by hand in pursuit of godlike manhood.
D: Summary: In a prayer to youth, the Athenian censures fishing, piracy, thieving, bird-catching, night-hunting, and trapping as unworthy of free citizens. Only active hunting of land animals with horses, dogs, and personal physical effort is approved as developing godlike manhood.
D: Distilled: Only active hunting of land animals with horses, dogs, and personal courage is worthy of free citizens -- fishing, trapping, and all passive or cruel forms of hunting are condemned.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The hierarchy of hunting -- from most despicable (piracy, thieving) through merely lazy (fishing, trapping) to genuinely noble (active pursuit with personal exertion) -- reflects aristocratic Greek values about worthy labor. The emphasis on personal physical effort ('with their own hands') connects to the virtue of courage (andreia, literally 'manliness'). The prayer form (euche) fits the Laws' practice of interweaving religious and legislative language. Compare Xenophon's Cynegeticus 12-13, which similarly praises active hunting while disparaging trapping. The fish-trap (kurtos) and 'weels' are types of wicker fishing devices -- the Athenian's contempt for them reflects the aristocratic disdain for commercial fishing.

D: Original:
The praise and blame which is assigned to all these things has now been declared; and let the law be as follows:-Let no one hinder these who verily are sacred hunters from following the chase wherever and whither soever they will; but the hunter by night, who trusts to his nets and gins, shall not be allowed to hunt anywhere. The fowler in the mountains and waste places shall be permitted, but on cultivated ground and on consecrated wilds he shall not be permitted; and any one who meets him may stop him. As to the hunter in waters, he may hunt anywhere except in harbours or sacred streams or marshes or pools, provided only that he do not pollute the water with poisonous juices. And now we may say that all our enactments about education are complete.

Very good.
D: Narrative:
"The praise and blame which is assigned to all these things has now been declared; and let the law be as follows: Let no one hinder these who verily are sacred hunters from following the chase wherever and whither soever they will; but the hunter by night, who trusts to his nets and gins, shall not be allowed to hunt anywhere. The fowler in the mountains and waste places shall be permitted, but on cultivated ground and on consecrated wilds he shall not be permitted; and any one who meets him may stop him. As to the hunter in waters, he may hunt anywhere except in harbours or sacred streams or marshes or pools, provided only that he do not pollute the water with poisonous juices. And now we may say that all our enactments about education are complete."

"Very good."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian declared that the praise and blame for all forms of hunting had now been established. The law should be as follows: no one shall hinder the sacred hunters -- those who hunt quadrupeds with horses, dogs, and their own persons -- from following the chase wherever they wish. But the hunter by night, who relies on nets and traps, shall not be allowed to hunt anywhere.

The fowler may hunt in mountains and wastelands, but not on cultivated ground or consecrated wilderness -- anyone who encounters him there may stop him. The hunter in waters may fish anywhere except in harbors, sacred streams, marshes, or pools, and must not pollute the water with poisonous substances.

With this, the Athenian declared all their enactments about education complete.

Cleinias approved.
D: Elegant: The law: sacred hunters pursuing game with horses, dogs, and personal effort may hunt anywhere without hindrance. Night-hunters with nets are banned entirely. Fowlers may hunt only in mountains and wastelands. Water-hunters are restricted from harbors, sacred waters, and pools, and must not poison the water. With this, all education enactments are complete.
D: Summary: The formal hunting law gives full freedom to noble hunters using horses, dogs, and personal effort, while restricting night-hunting, bird-catching, and fishing to specific areas and conditions. Water-poisoning is banned. With these regulations, the entire educational program of Book 7 is declared complete.
D: Distilled: Noble hunters have full freedom while passive methods face restrictions or bans, and with these hunting laws the comprehensive educational program of Book 7 reaches its conclusion.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The term 'sacred hunters' (hieroi thereutai) elevates noble hunting to a quasi-religious status, consistent with the Laws' integration of civic, moral, and religious life. The specific environmental regulations -- no hunting on cultivated land, no poisoning water -- show practical ecological awareness. The ban on water-poisoning may reference the practice of using toxic plants to stun fish, which was considered unsporting and environmentally destructive. The declaration that 'all our enactments about education are complete' closes Book 7, which has covered the entire educational program from prenatal care through early childhood, physical training, music, dance, letters, mathematics, astronomy, and hunting. It is the most comprehensive treatment of education in all of Plato's works.

D: Original:
Next, with the help of the Delphian oracle, we have to institute festivals and make laws about them, and to determine what sacrifices will be for the good of the city, and to what Gods they shall be offered; but when they shall be offered, and how often, may be partly regulated by us.

The number-yes.
D: Narrative:
"Next, with the help of the Delphian oracle, we have to institute festivals and make laws about them, and to determine what sacrifices will be for the good of the city, and to what Gods they shall be offered; but when they shall be offered, and how often, may be partly regulated by us."

"The number -- yes."
D: Refashioned:
"Our next task is to establish festivals with the guidance of the Delphic oracle and to create laws governing them. We must determine which sacrifices will benefit the city and to which gods they should be offered. However, the timing and frequency of these sacrifices -- that much we can decide ourselves."

"The number of them -- yes, that we can settle."
D: Elegant:
"We must next establish festivals and sacrificial laws with the oracle's guidance, deciding which gods receive offerings. The timing and frequency, however, we may determine ourselves."

"The number -- yes."
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes that while the Delphic oracle should guide which gods receive sacrifices and what form festivals take, the practical scheduling details can be determined by the legislators themselves. Cleinias agrees regarding the number.
D: Distilled: The oracle determines which gods receive sacrifices, but the legislators themselves may regulate the timing and frequency of festivals.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The deference to the Delphic oracle reflects Plato's consistent respect for traditional religious authority in legislative matters -- cf. Republic 427b-c, where Socrates similarly defers to Apollo at Delphi for decisions about temples, sacrifices, and worship. The distinction between divine authority (which gods) and human regulation (when and how often) establishes the cooperative framework between religion and legislation that structures Book 8.

D: Original: Then we will first determine the number; and let the whole number be 365-one for every day-so that one magistrate at least will sacrifice daily to some God or demi-god on behalf of the city, and the citizens, and their possessions. And the interpreters, and priests, and priestesses, and prophets shall meet, and, in company with the guardians of the law, ordain those things which the legislator of necessity omits; and I may remark that they are the very persons who ought to take note of what is omitted. The law will say that there are twelve feasts dedicated to the twelve Gods, after whom the several tribes are named; and that to each of them they shall sacrifice every month, and appoint choruses, and musical and gymnastic contests, assigning them so as to suit the Gods and seasons of the year. And they shall have festivals for women, distinguishing those which ought to be separated from the men's festivals, and those which ought not. Further, they shall not confuse the infernal deities and their rites with the Gods who are termed heavenly and their rites, but shall separate them, giving to Pluto his own in the twelfth month, which is sacred to him, according to the law. To such a deity warlike men should entertain no aversion, but they should honour him as being always the best friend of man. For the connection of soul and body is no way better than the dissolution of them, as I am ready to maintain quite seriously. Moreover, those who would regulate these matters rightly should consider, that our city among existing cities has fellow, either in respect of leisure or comin and of the necessaries of life, and that like an individual she ought to live happily. And those who would live happily should in the first place do no wrong to one another, and ought not themselves to be wronged by others; to attain the first is not difficult, but there is great difficulty, in acquiring the power of not being wronged. No man can be perfectly secure against wrong, unless he has become perfectly good; and cities are like individuals in this, for a city if good has a life of peace, but if evil, a life of war within and without.
D: Narrative:
"Then we will first determine the number; and let the whole number be 365 -- one for every day -- so that one magistrate at least will sacrifice daily to some God or demi-god on behalf of the city, and the citizens, and their possessions. And the interpreters, and priests, and priestesses, and prophets shall meet, and, in company with the guardians of the law, ordain those things which the legislator of necessity omits; and I may remark that they are the very persons who ought to take note of what is omitted.

The law will say that there are twelve feasts dedicated to the twelve Gods, after whom the several tribes are named; and that to each of them they shall sacrifice every month, and appoint choruses, and musical and gymnastic contests, assigning them so as to suit the Gods and seasons of the year. And they shall have festivals for women, distinguishing those which ought to be separated from the men's festivals, and those which ought not.

Further, they shall not confuse the infernal deities and their rites with the Gods who are termed heavenly and their rites, but shall separate them, giving to Pluto his own in the twelfth month, which is sacred to him, according to the law. To such a deity warlike men should entertain no aversion, but they should honour him as being always the best friend of man. For the connection of soul and body is no way better than the dissolution of them, as I am ready to maintain quite seriously.

Moreover, those who would regulate these matters rightly should consider, that our city among existing cities has fellow, either in respect of leisure or command of the necessaries of life, and that like an individual she ought to live happily. And those who would live happily should in the first place do no wrong to one another, and ought not themselves to be wronged by others; to attain the first is not difficult, but there is great difficulty in acquiring the power of not being wronged. No man can be perfectly secure against wrong, unless he has become perfectly good; and cities are like individuals in this, for a city if good has a life of peace, but if evil, a life of war within and without."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us begin by fixing the total number of festivals at 365 -- one for each day of the year -- ensuring that at least one magistrate performs a daily sacrifice to some god or demigod on behalf of the city, its citizens, and their property. The interpreters, priests, priestesses, and prophets shall convene together with the guardians of the law to establish whatever the legislator necessarily leaves unaddressed. And indeed, these are precisely the people who should be attentive to such gaps.

The law shall prescribe twelve major feasts dedicated to the twelve gods for whom the tribes are named. Each month shall include sacrifices to the appropriate god, along with choruses and contests in music and athletics, arranged to suit both the gods honored and the seasons of the year. There shall also be festivals specifically for women, with clear distinctions between those that should be held separately from men's festivals and those that need not be.

Furthermore, the rites of the underworld deities must not be confused with those of the heavenly gods. They must be kept apart. Pluto shall have his own observances in the twelfth month, which is sacred to him by law. Warriors should feel no hostility toward such a deity; rather, they should honor him as humanity's truest friend. For I am prepared to argue in all seriousness that the union of soul and body is in no way superior to their separation.

Those who would order these matters properly must also recognize that our city, among all existing cities, is unrivaled in both leisure and command of life's necessities, and that, like an individual, it ought to live happily. Those who wish to live happily must first refrain from wronging one another and must also not be wronged by others. The first condition is not hard to achieve, but the second -- acquiring the power to avoid being wronged -- is profoundly difficult. No one can be perfectly secure against injustice unless he has become perfectly good. Cities mirror individuals in this respect: a good city enjoys peace, while an evil one endures war both within and without."
D: Elegant:
"Let there be 365 festivals -- one daily sacrifice by a magistrate on behalf of the city. Priests, interpreters, and law-guardians shall jointly fill whatever gaps the legislator leaves. Twelve major feasts shall honor the twelve tribal gods monthly, with choruses and contests suited to the seasons. Women shall have their own festivals. The rites of the underworld gods must be kept separate from those of the heavenly gods, with Pluto honored in the twelfth month. Warriors should not despise him; death is the soul's truest friend, for the union of body and soul is no better than their dissolution.

Our city, blessed with leisure and abundance, ought to live happily. Happiness requires both doing no wrong and suffering none. The first is easy; the second is nearly impossible without perfect goodness. A good city lives in peace; an evil city lives in perpetual war."
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes a comprehensive festival calendar of 365 daily sacrifices, twelve major feasts for the tribal gods, and separate observances for women and for the underworld deities. He argues that Pluto deserves honor rather than aversion, since the separation of soul from body is no worse than their union. He concludes that a city blessed with leisure must pursue goodness to achieve peace, since only perfect virtue can protect against being wronged.
D: Distilled: A city should hold daily sacrifices, honor both heavenly and underworld gods in proper separation, and recognize that only perfect goodness can secure happiness and peace.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The claim that the dissolution of soul and body is no worse than their union echoes the Phaedo's argument that death is the philosopher's liberation (Phaedo 64a-67b). The twelve tribal gods correspond to the twelve Olympians -- cf. Laws 745d-e and 771c for the tribal organization. The principle that security from injustice requires perfect goodness parallels Republic 335b-e on justice. The emphasis on Pluto as 'the best friend of man' is a striking revaluation that connects to the Cratylus 403a-404a, where Plato etymologizes Pluto's name as 'wealth-giver.' The distinction between heavenly and chthonic rites reflects standard Greek religious practice.

D: Original: Wherefore the citizens ought to practise war-not in time of war, but rather while they are at peace. And every city which has any sense, should take the field at least for one day in every month; and for more if the magistrates think fit, having no regard to winter cold or summer heat; and they should go out en masse, including their wives and their children, when the magistrates determine to lead forth the whole people, or in separate portions when summoned by them; and they should always provide that there should be games and sacrificial feasts, and they should have tournaments, imitating in as lively a manner as they can real battles. And they should distribute prizes of victory and valour to the competitors, passing censures and encomiums on one another according to the characters which they bear in the contests and their whole life, honouring him who seems to be the best, and blaming him who is the opposite. And let poets celebrate the victors-not however every poet, but only one who in the first place is not less than fifty years of age; nor should he be one who, although he may have musical and poetical gifts, has never in his life done any noble or illustrious action; but those who are themselves good and also honourable in the state, creators of noble actions-let their poems be sung, even though they be not very musical. And let the judgment of them rest with the instructor of youth and the other guardians of the laws, who shall give them this privilege, and they alone shall be free to sing; but the rest of the world shall not have this liberty. Nor shall any one dare to sing a song which has not been approved by the judgment of the guardians of the laws, not even if his strain be sweeter than the songs of Thamyras and Orpheus; but only and Orpheus; but only such poems as have been judged sacred and dedicated to the Gods, and such as are the works of good men, which praise of blame has been awarded and which have been deemed to fulfil their design fairly.
D: Narrative:
"Wherefore the citizens ought to practise war -- not in time of war, but rather while they are at peace. And every city which has any sense, should take the field at least for one day in every month; and for more if the magistrates think fit, having no regard to winter cold or summer heat; and they should go out en masse, including their wives and their children, when the magistrates determine to lead forth the whole people, or in separate portions when summoned by them; and they should always provide that there should be games and sacrificial feasts, and they should have tournaments, imitating in as lively a manner as they can real battles.

And they should distribute prizes of victory and valour to the competitors, passing censures and encomiums on one another according to the characters which they bear in the contests and their whole life, honouring him who seems to be the best, and blaming him who is the opposite.

And let poets celebrate the victors -- not however every poet, but only one who in the first place is not less than fifty years of age; nor should he be one who, although he may have musical and poetical gifts, has never in his life done any noble or illustrious action; but those who are themselves good and also honourable in the state, creators of noble actions -- let their poems be sung, even though they be not very musical.

And let the judgment of them rest with the instructor of youth and the other guardians of the laws, who shall give them this privilege, and they alone shall be free to sing; but the rest of the world shall not have this liberty. Nor shall any one dare to sing a song which has not been approved by the judgment of the guardians of the laws, not even if his strain be sweeter than the songs of Thamyras and Orpheus; but only such poems as have been judged sacred and dedicated to the Gods, and such as are the works of good men, which praise or blame has been awarded and which have been deemed to fulfil their design fairly."
D: Refashioned:
"Therefore, citizens should train for war not during wartime but during peace. Any city with good sense should conduct field exercises at least one day each month -- more if the magistrates see fit -- regardless of winter cold or summer heat. The entire population, including wives and children, should march out when the magistrates call for a general mobilization, or in smaller groups when summoned. These outings should always include games and sacrificial feasts, with tournaments that simulate actual combat as realistically as possible.

Prizes for victory and valor should be awarded to the competitors. Citizens should publicly praise and censure one another based on their conduct both in these contests and in their lives generally, honoring those who prove best and criticizing those who fall short.

As for poetry celebrating the victors: not just any poet should compose it. The poet must be at least fifty years old, and he must not be someone who possesses literary talent but has never performed a noble deed in his own life. Rather, let those who are themselves virtuous and respected in the state -- people who have accomplished great things -- have their poems sung, even if their verse is not particularly polished.

The director of education and the guardians of the law shall judge which poems qualify, and only approved poets shall have the freedom to perform publicly. No one else may sing publicly, even if his music surpasses the songs of Thamyras and Orpheus. Only poems judged sacred and dedicated to the gods, or works by good citizens that have been evaluated for their proper use of praise and blame, shall be permitted."
D: Elegant:
"Citizens should train for war during peacetime. Every sensible city should hold military exercises monthly, in all weather, with the whole population -- wives and children included. These exercises should feature realistic combat simulations, games, feasts, and prizes for valor, with public praise for the best and censure for the worst.

Only poets over fifty who have themselves performed noble deeds may celebrate the victors. The guardians of the law shall approve all public poetry. No song may be performed without their sanction, however beautiful -- only works that are sacred, composed by good citizens, and properly employ praise and blame."
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that citizens should practice warfare during peacetime through monthly military exercises involving the entire population, with realistic combat simulations, prizes, and public evaluation of character. He restricts the poetry celebrating victors to elder poets of proven virtue, subject to state approval, even if less skilled poets produce more beautiful verse.
D: Distilled: Citizens must train for war during peace through monthly exercises with the whole population, and only virtuous elder poets approved by the state may celebrate the victors.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The insistence on peacetime military training anticipates the Roman concept of training as preparation (cf. Vegetius). The age requirement for poets (50+) and the demand for personal virtue echo Republic 378-403 on censorship of poetry, though here the mechanism is institutional approval rather than outright prohibition. Thamyras was a mythical Thracian singer who challenged the Muses and was blinded -- mentioned also at Iliad 2.594-600. The integration of wives and children into military exercises reflects the Laws' more inclusive social vision compared to earlier dialogues.

D: Original:
The regulations about and about liberty of speech in poitry, ought to apply equally to men and women. The legislator may be supposed to argue the question in his own mind:-Who are my citizens for whom I have set in order the city? Are they not competitors in the greatest of all contests, and have they not innumerable rivals? To be sure, will be the natural, reply. Well, but if we were training boxers, or pancratiasts, or any other sort of athletes, would they never meet until the hour of contest arrived; and should we do nothing to prepare ourselves previously by daily practice? Surely, if we were boxers we should have been learning to fight for many days before, and exercising ourselves in imitating all those blows and wards which we were intending to use in the hour of conflict; and in order that we might come as near to reality as possible, instead of cestuses we should put on boxing gloves, that the blows and the wards might be practised by us to the utmost of our power. And if there were a lack of competitors, the ridicule of fools would ryot deter us from hanging up a lifeless image and practising at that. Or if we had no adversary at all, animate or inanimate, should we not venture in the dearth of antagonists to spar by ourselves? In what other manner could we ever study the art of self-defence?

The way which you mention Stranger, would be the only way.
D: Narrative:
"The regulations about military training and about liberty of speech in poetry ought to apply equally to men and women. The legislator may be supposed to argue the question in his own mind: 'Who are my citizens for whom I have set in order the city? Are they not competitors in the greatest of all contests, and have they not innumerable rivals?'

To be sure, will be the natural reply.

'Well, but if we were training boxers, or pancratiasts, or any other sort of athletes, would they never meet until the hour of contest arrived; and should we do nothing to prepare ourselves previously by daily practice? Surely, if we were boxers we should have been learning to fight for many days before, and exercising ourselves in imitating all those blows and wards which we were intending to use in the hour of conflict; and in order that we might come as near to reality as possible, instead of cestuses we should put on boxing gloves, that the blows and the wards might be practised by us to the utmost of our power. And if there were a lack of competitors, the ridicule of fools would not deter us from hanging up a lifeless image and practising at that. Or if we had no adversary at all, animate or inanimate, should we not venture in the dearth of antagonists to spar by ourselves? In what other manner could we ever study the art of self-defence?'"

"The way which you mention, Stranger, would be the only way."
D: Refashioned:
"These regulations regarding military training and freedom of expression in poetry should apply equally to men and women. The legislator should reason with himself as follows: 'Who are the citizens for whom I am building this city? Are they not contestants in the greatest of all competitions, facing countless rivals?'

The natural answer is: of course they are.

'Then consider: if we were training boxers or mixed martial artists or athletes of any kind, would we let them sit idle until the day of competition arrived, doing nothing to prepare through daily practice? Of course not. If we were boxers, we would spend many days beforehand learning to fight, rehearsing every punch and defensive move we planned to use in actual combat. To make our practice as realistic as possible, we would train with padded gloves instead of bare fists so we could drill at full intensity. If we lacked sparring partners, we would not let mockery stop us from setting up a punching bag and training against that. And if we had no target at all -- living or otherwise -- we would shadowbox alone rather than neglect our training. How else could we ever learn self-defense?'"

"The method you describe, Stranger, would be the only way."
D: Elegant:
"Military and poetic regulations should apply equally to men and women. The legislator should ask: 'My citizens face the greatest contest of all -- life itself. Would any athlete skip daily training and wait for competition day? A boxer drills punches on gloves, bags, or even thin air. Why should citizens preparing for the ultimate contest do any less?'"

"That would be the only way, Stranger."
D: Summary: The Athenian argues that military and poetic regulations should apply equally to both sexes, then uses the analogy of athletic training to show that citizens must practice constantly for the contest of life itself. Just as boxers would never wait until competition day to begin training -- even practicing on dummies or shadowboxing if no partners were available -- so citizens must drill for their civic duties.
D: Distilled: Citizens of both sexes must train constantly for the great contest of life, just as athletes practice daily with whatever means are available.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The equal application of regulations to women is a distinctive feature of the Laws, paralleling Republic 451c-457b on women's education. The pancration (pankration) was an ancient Greek combat sport combining wrestling and boxing. Cestuses were leather hand-wraps reinforced with metal, used in ancient boxing. The boxing analogy illustrates the broader Platonic principle that virtue requires habitual practice -- cf. Aristotle's later development of this idea in Nicomachean Ethics 1103a-b, which may have been influenced by this passage.

D: Original: And shall the warriors of our city, who are destined when occasion calli to enter the greatest of all contests, and to fight for their lives, and their children, and their property, and the whole city, be worse prepared than boxers? And will the legislator, because he is afraid that their practising with one another may appear to some ridiculous, abstain from commanding them to go out and fight; will he not ordain that soldiers shall perform lesser exercises without arms every day, making dancing and all gymnastic tend to this end; and also will he not require that they shall practise some gymnastic exercises, greater as well as lesser, as often as every month; and that they shall have contests one with another in every part of the country, seizing upon posts and lying in ambush, and imitating in every respect the reality of war; fighting with boxing-gloves and hurling javelins, and using weapons somewhat dangerous, and as nearly as possible like the true ones, in order that the sport may not be altogether without fear, but may have terrors and to a certain degree show the man who has and who has not courage; and that the honour and dishonour which are assigned to them respectively, may prepare the whole city for the true conflict of life? If any one dies in these mimic contests, the homicide is involuntary, and we will make the slayer, when he has been purified according to law, to be pure of blood, considering that if a few men should die, others as good as they will be born; but that if fear is dead then the citizens will never find a test of superior and inferior natures, which is a far greater evil to the state than the loss of a few.
D: Narrative:
"And shall the warriors of our city, who are destined when occasion calls to enter the greatest of all contests, and to fight for their lives, and their children, and their property, and the whole city, be worse prepared than boxers?
