The age distinction (under/over thirty) reflects Greek assumptions about the different social expectations appropriate to different life stages -- cf. the age requirements for various offices throughout the Laws. The toleration of secret fruit-taking (no 'disgrace') creates an interesting legal gray zone where the act is not criminalized but getting caught has consequences. The differentiation between storable and fresh fruit reflects the economic reality that dried figs, raisins, and wine were valuable trade commodities, while fresh fruit was perishable and less economically significant.

D: Original: Water is the greatest element of nutrition in gardens, but is easily polluted. You cannot poison the soil, or the soil, or the sun, or the air, which are other elements of nutrition in plants, or divert them, or steal them; but all these things may very likely happen in regard to water, which must therefore be protected by law. And let this be the law:-If any one intentionally pollutes the water of another, whether the water of a spring, or collected in reservoirs, either by poisonous substances, or by digging or by theft, let the injured party bring the cause before the wardens of the city, and claim in writing the value of the loss; if the accused be found guilty of injuring the water by deleterious substances, let him not only pay damages, but purify the stream or the cistern which contains the water, in such manner as the laws of the interpreters order the purification to be made by the offender in each case.
D: Narrative:
"Water is the greatest element of nutrition in gardens, but is easily polluted. You cannot poison the soil, or the sun, or the air, which are other elements of nutrition in plants, or divert them, or steal them; but all these things may very likely happen in regard to water, which must therefore be protected by law.

And let this be the law: If any one intentionally pollutes the water of another, whether the water of a spring, or collected in reservoirs, either by poisonous substances, or by digging or by theft, let the injured party bring the cause before the wardens of the city, and claim in writing the value of the loss; if the accused be found guilty of injuring the water by deleterious substances, let him not only pay damages, but purify the stream or the cistern which contains the water, in such manner as the laws of the interpreters order the purification to be made by the offender in each case."
D: Refashioned:
"Water is the most vital element for growing gardens, but it is also the most easily polluted. You cannot poison the soil, the sun, or the air -- the other essential elements for plant growth -- nor can you divert or steal them. But all of these things can and do happen with water. Therefore, water must be protected by law.

The law shall be this: if anyone intentionally pollutes another person's water supply -- whether a spring or a reservoir -- by introducing poisonous substances, by digging to divert it, or by stealing it, the injured party shall bring the case before the wardens of the city and submit a written claim for the value of the loss. If the accused is found guilty of contaminating the water with harmful substances, he must not only pay damages but must also personally purify the stream or cistern, following the purification procedures prescribed by the religious interpreters for each specific case."
D: Elegant:
"Water is the most vital and most vulnerable element in agriculture. Unlike soil, sun, and air, it can be poisoned, diverted, or stolen, and therefore requires legal protection.

Anyone who intentionally pollutes another's water -- by poison, diversion, or theft -- must pay damages and personally purify the contaminated source according to the religious interpreters' prescribed methods."
D: Summary: The Athenian identifies water as uniquely vulnerable among agricultural necessities because it alone can be poisoned, diverted, or stolen. He establishes that intentional water pollution shall be prosecuted before the city wardens, with the guilty party required both to pay damages and to personally purify the contaminated water source according to religious ritual.
D: Distilled: Water, uniquely vulnerable to pollution, diversion, and theft among agricultural necessities, requires legal protection with penalties including both damages and mandatory religious purification.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The ecological insight that water is uniquely susceptible to human interference among the four elements of plant nutrition (soil, sun, air, water) is remarkably perceptive. The combination of financial penalties (damages) and religious obligation (purification) reflects the Laws' fusion of secular and sacred authority. The role of the 'interpreters' (exegetai) in prescribing purification rituals connects to the established Athenian institution of religious interpreters who advised on matters of ritual pollution -- cf. Laws 759c-d. Water pollution laws have parallels in other ancient legal codes, including the Code of Hammurabi. The requirement for written claims shows the Laws' reliance on documented legal proceedings.

D: Original: With respect to the gathering in of the fruits of the soil, let a man, if he pleases, carry his own fruits through any place in which he either does no harm to any one, or himself gains three times as much as his neighbour loses. Now of these things the magistrates should be cognisant, as of all other things in which a man intentionally does injury to another or to the property of another, by fraud or force, in the use which he makes of his own property. All these matters a man should lay before the magistrates, and receive damages, supposing the injury to be not more than three minae; or if he have a charge against another which involves a larger amount, let him bring his suit into the public courts and have the evil-doer punished. But if any of the magistrates appear to adjudge the penalties which he imposes in an unjust spirit, let him be liable to pay double to the injured party. Any one may bring the offences of magistrates, in any particular case, before the public courts. There are innumerable little matters relating to the modes of punishment, and applications for suits, and summonses and the witnesses to summonses-for example, whether two witnesses should be required for a summons, or how many-and all such details, which cannot be omitted in legislation, but are beneath the wisdom of an aged legislator. These lesser matters, as they indeed are in comparison with the greater ones, let a younger generation regulate by law, after the patterns which have preceded, and according to their own experience of the usefulness and necessity of such laws; and when they are duly regulated let there be no alteration, but let the citizens live in the observance of them.
D: Narrative:
"With respect to the gathering in of the fruits of the soil, let a man, if he pleases, carry his own fruits through any place in which he either does no harm to any one, or himself gains three times as much as his neighbour loses. Now of these things the magistrates should be cognisant, as of all other things in which a man intentionally does injury to another or to the property of another, by fraud or force, in the use which he makes of his own property.

All these matters a man should lay before the magistrates, and receive damages, supposing the injury to be not more than three minae; or if he have a charge against another which involves a larger amount, let him bring his suit into the public courts and have the evil-doer punished.

But if any of the magistrates appear to adjudge the penalties which he imposes in an unjust spirit, let him be liable to pay double to the injured party. Any one may bring the offences of magistrates, in any particular case, before the public courts.

There are innumerable little matters relating to the modes of punishment, and applications for suits, and summonses and the witnesses to summonses -- for example, whether two witnesses should be required for a summons, or how many -- and all such details, which cannot be omitted in legislation, but are beneath the wisdom of an aged legislator. These lesser matters, as they indeed are in comparison with the greater ones, let a younger generation regulate by law, after the patterns which have preceded, and according to their own experience of the usefulness and necessity of such laws; and when they are duly regulated let there be no alteration, but let the citizens live in the observance of them."
D: Refashioned:
"Regarding the harvest: a person may transport his own produce through any route, provided he either causes no harm to anyone or gains three times more than his neighbor loses from the passage. Magistrates should oversee these matters, as well as all cases where someone intentionally harms another person or their property -- whether by fraud or force -- through the use of his own property.

Claims involving three minae or less shall be brought before the magistrates for resolution. Claims exceeding that amount shall be taken to the public courts for prosecution and punishment of the offender.

If any magistrate is found to have rendered judgment in an unjust spirit, he shall be liable to pay double the amount to the injured party. Any citizen may bring charges of judicial misconduct against a magistrate before the public courts.

There are countless procedural details -- methods of punishment, procedures for filing suits, rules for summonses, the number of witnesses required for a summons, and similar matters -- that legislation cannot omit but that are beneath the concern of an elderly legislator. Let a younger generation establish these procedural laws, following the patterns already laid down and drawing on their own practical experience of what is useful and necessary. Once these regulations are properly established, they should remain unchanged, and citizens should live by them."
D: Elegant:
"A person may transport his produce through any route where the benefit outweighs the harm threefold. Magistrates handle disputes up to three minae; larger claims go to public courts. Magistrates who judge unjustly pay double to the injured party and may be prosecuted.

Countless procedural details -- summonses, witness requirements, filing procedures -- are necessary but beneath an aged legislator. Let the younger generation codify these, following established principles, and once settled, keep them fixed."
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes rules for transporting produce, a tiered judicial system for agricultural disputes (magistrates for small claims, public courts for larger ones), and accountability for unjust magistrates who face double penalties. He then delegates procedural details to future younger legislators, acknowledging that such minutiae are necessary but unworthy of an elderly lawgiver's attention.
D: Distilled: Agricultural disputes are handled by magistrates for small claims and public courts for larger ones, with unjust magistrates facing double penalties, while procedural details are delegated to younger legislators.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The three-to-one benefit-to-harm ratio for produce transport is a notable early instance of cost-benefit analysis in law. The double penalty for unjust magistrates demonstrates the Laws' emphasis on judicial accountability -- a theme developed extensively in Laws 12.945b-948b. The principle that citizens may bring charges against magistrates reflects a democratic element in the otherwise mixed constitution. The delegation of procedural details to younger legislators is both practical and philosophically interesting, as it acknowledges the limits of top-down legislative design and the need for experiential wisdom. The instruction to keep these procedural laws fixed once established reflects the Laws' general conservatism about legal change -- cf. Laws 7.797a-798d on the danger of innovation.

D: Original: Now of artisans, let the regulations be as follows:-In the first place, let no citizen or servant of a citizen be occupied in handicraft arts; for he who is to secure and preserve the public order of the state, has an art which requires much study and many kinds of knowledge, and does not admit of being made a secondary occupation; and hardly any human being is capable of pursuing two professions or two arts rightly, or of practising one art himself, and superintending some one else who is practising another. Let this, then, be our first principle in the state:-No one who is a smith shall also be a carpenter, and if he be a carpenter, he shall not superintend the smith's art rather than his own, under the pretext that in superintending many servants who are working for him, he is likely to superintend them better, because more revenue will accrue to him from them than from his own art; but let every man in the state have one art, and get his living by that. Let the wardens of the city labour to maintain this law, and if any citizen incline to any other art than the study of virtue, let them punish him with disgrace and infamy, until they bring him back into his own right course; and if any stranger profess two arts, let them chastise him with bonds and money penalties, and expulsion from the state, until they compel him to be one only and not many.
D: Narrative:
"Now of artisans, let the regulations be as follows: In the first place, let no citizen or servant of a citizen be occupied in handicraft arts; for he who is to secure and preserve the public order of the state, has an art which requires much study and many kinds of knowledge, and does not admit of being made a secondary occupation; and hardly any human being is capable of pursuing two professions or two arts rightly, or of practising one art himself, and superintending some one else who is practising another.

Let this, then, be our first principle in the state: No one who is a smith shall also be a carpenter, and if he be a carpenter, he shall not superintend the smith's art rather than his own, under the pretext that in superintending many servants who are working for him, he is likely to superintend them better, because more revenue will accrue to him from them than from his own art; but let every man in the state have one art, and get his living by that.

Let the wardens of the city labour to maintain this law, and if any citizen incline to any other art than the study of virtue, let them punish him with disgrace and infamy, until they bring him back into his own right course; and if any stranger profess two arts, let them chastise him with bonds and money penalties, and expulsion from the state, until they compel him to be one only and not many."
D: Refashioned:
"Regarding artisans, the regulations shall be as follows. First, no citizen or citizen's servant shall practice a manual trade. The art of maintaining public order requires extensive study and diverse knowledge, and cannot be treated as a side occupation. Almost no human being can properly pursue two professions, or practice one trade while supervising someone else practicing another.

Our fundamental principle: no smith shall also be a carpenter, and no carpenter shall supervise a smithy instead of his own workshop, even under the excuse that managing many workers in a different trade generates more revenue than his own craft. Every person in the state shall have one trade and earn his living from that alone.

The wardens of the city shall enforce this law. If any citizen turns to any occupation other than the pursuit of virtue, they shall punish him with public disgrace and dishonor until he returns to his proper course. If any foreigner practices two trades, he shall be punished with imprisonment, fines, and expulsion from the state until he is compelled to practice one trade only, not several."
D: Elegant:
"No citizen shall practice a manual trade -- civic duty is itself a full-time art requiring extensive study. Even among artisans, no one shall practice two trades: a smith must not also be a carpenter, nor supervise another's trade for profit.

Citizens who stray from the pursuit of virtue face public disgrace. Foreigners practicing two trades face imprisonment, fines, and expulsion. Every person shall have one art and live by it alone."
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes that citizens are barred from manual trades because civic duty is itself a full-time profession. Among artisans, strict specialization is required: each person must practice a single trade and earn their living from it alone. Citizens who neglect virtue for other pursuits face public disgrace, while foreigners who practice multiple trades face imprisonment, fines, and banishment.
D: Distilled: Citizens must devote themselves entirely to virtue and civic duty rather than manual trades, and every artisan must practice one craft only, with severe penalties for those who diversify.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The prohibition on citizens practicing trades reflects the widespread Greek aristocratic disdain for banausic (manual) labor -- cf. Xenophon, Oeconomicus 4.2-3 and Aristotle, Politics 1278a. The principle of one person, one trade echoes Republic 370b-374d, where specialization is the founding principle of the just city. The argument that supervising others' work for profit should not override one's own craft anticipates concerns about managerial capitalism. The distinction between citizen penalties (disgrace) and foreigner penalties (physical, financial, and expulsion) reflects the different legal statuses in Greek city-states. The characterization of the citizen's proper art as 'the study of virtue' is the passage's most significant philosophical claim -- it redefines citizenship itself as a vocation.

D: Original: But as touching payments for hire, and contracts of work, or in case any one does wrong to any of the citizens or they do wrong to any other, up to fifty drachmae, let the wardens of the city decide the case; but if greater amount be involved, then let the public courts decide according to law. Let no one pay any duty either on the importation or exportation of goods; and as to frankincense and similar perfumes, used in the service of the Gods, which come from abroad, and purple and other dyes which are not produced in the country, or the materials of any art which have to be imported, and which are not necessary-no one should import them; nor again, should any one export anything which is wanted in the country. Of all these things let there be inspectors and superintendents, taken from the guardians of the law; and they shall be the twelve next in order to the five seniors. Concerning arms, and all implements which are for military purposes, if there be need of introducing any art, or plant, or metal, or chains of any kind, or animals for use in war, let the commanders of the horse and the generals have authority over their importation and exportation; the city shall send them out and also receive them, and the guardians of the law shall make fit and proper laws about them. But let there be no retail trade for the sake of money-making, either in these or any other articles, in the city or country at all.
D: Narrative:
"But as touching payments for hire, and contracts of work, or in case any one does wrong to any of the citizens or they do wrong to any other, up to fifty drachmae, let the wardens of the city decide the case; but if a greater amount be involved, then let the public courts decide according to law.

Let no one pay any duty either on the importation or exportation of goods; and as to frankincense and similar perfumes, used in the service of the Gods, which come from abroad, and purple and other dyes which are not produced in the country, or the materials of any art which have to be imported, and which are not necessary -- no one should import them; nor again, should any one export anything which is wanted in the country.

Of all these things let there be inspectors and superintendents, taken from the guardians of the law; and they shall be the twelve next in order to the five seniors.

Concerning arms, and all implements which are for military purposes, if there be need of introducing any art, or plant, or metal, or chains of any kind, or animals for use in war, let the commanders of the horse and the generals have authority over their importation and exportation; the city shall send them out and also receive them, and the guardians of the law shall make fit and proper laws about them. But let there be no retail trade for the sake of money-making, either in these or any other articles, in the city or country at all."
D: Refashioned:
"Regarding wages, work contracts, and civil wrongs involving amounts up to fifty drachmae: the wardens of the city shall decide these cases. For amounts exceeding fifty drachmae, the public courts shall decide according to law.

No customs duties shall be imposed on either imports or exports. However, certain goods shall not be imported at all: frankincense and similar religious perfumes from abroad, purple and other dyes not produced domestically, and the materials for any art that must be imported but are not essential. Likewise, nothing that is needed within the country shall be exported.

Inspectors and superintendents for these trade regulations shall be drawn from the guardians of the law -- specifically, the twelve next in seniority after the five senior guardians.

For military materiel -- including any craft, plant, metal, chains, or animals needed for warfare -- the cavalry commanders and generals shall have authority over importation and exportation. The city itself shall conduct these transactions, and the guardians of the law shall establish appropriate regulations.

Above all: there shall be no retail trade conducted for the purpose of profit, in any goods whatsoever, anywhere in the city or the countryside."
D: Elegant:
"Civil disputes up to fifty drachmae go to city wardens; larger cases to public courts. No customs duties, but unnecessary luxury imports are banned outright, and essential goods may not be exported.

Twelve guardians of the law shall oversee trade. Military imports and exports fall under generals and cavalry commanders. Most importantly: no retail trade for profit shall exist anywhere in the state."
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes a tiered judicial system for commercial disputes, eliminates customs duties while banning unnecessary luxury imports and the export of essential goods, places trade oversight under twelve guardians of the law, gives military commanders authority over weapons imports, and categorically prohibits all retail trade for profit throughout the state.
D: Distilled: The state bans luxury imports, prohibits essential exports, places trade under guardian oversight, and categorically forbids all retail trade for profit anywhere in the city or countryside.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The prohibition of retail trade (kapeleia) for profit is one of the Laws' most radical economic provisions, reflecting Plato's deep hostility to commerce as morally corrupting -- cf. Republic 371c-d and Laws 918d-920c. The ban on luxury imports (frankincense, purple dye) targets precisely the goods that symbolized aristocratic consumption in the ancient Mediterranean. Purple dye, derived from murex snails, was one of the most expensive commodities in the ancient world. The military exemption for imports shows pragmatic realism within the anti-commercial framework. The fifty-drachmae threshold for judicial jurisdiction creates a practical small-claims system. The assignment of trade oversight to the 'twelve next after the five seniors' reflects the Laws' detailed hierarchical bureaucratic structure.

D: Original:
With respect to food and the distribution of the produce of the country, the right and proper way seems to be nearly that which is the custom of Crete; for all should be required to distribute the fruits of the soil into twelve parts, and in this way consume them. Let the twelfth portion of each (as for instance of wheat and barley, to which the rest of the fruits of the earth shall be added, as well as the animals which are for sale in each of the twelve divisions) be divided in due proportion into three parts; one part for freemen, another for their servants, and a third for craftsmen and in general for strangers, whether sojourners who may be dwelling in the city, and like other men must live, or those who come on some business which they have with the state, or with some individual. Let only this third part of all necessaries be required to be sold; out of the other two-thirds no one shall be compelled to sell. And how will they be best distributed? In the first place, we see clearly that the distribution will be of equals in one point of view, and in another point of view of unequals.

What do you mean?

I mean that the earth of necessity produces and nourishes the various articles of food, sometimes better and sometimes worse.

Of course.
D: Narrative:
"With respect to food and the distribution of the produce of the country, the right and proper way seems to be nearly that which is the custom of Crete; for all should be required to distribute the fruits of the soil into twelve parts, and in this way consume them.

Let the twelfth portion of each (as for instance of wheat and barley, to which the rest of the fruits of the earth shall be added, as well as the animals which are for sale in each of the twelve divisions) be divided in due proportion into three parts; one part for freemen, another for their servants, and a third for craftsmen and in general for strangers, whether sojourners who may be dwelling in the city, and like other men must live, or those who come on some business which they have with the state, or with some individual. Let only this third part of all necessaries be required to be sold; out of the other two-thirds no one shall be compelled to sell.

And how will they be best distributed? In the first place, we see clearly that the distribution will be of equals in one point of view, and in another point of view of unequals."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean that the earth of necessity produces and nourishes the various articles of food, sometimes better and sometimes worse."

"Of course."
D: Refashioned:
"The proper method for distributing the country's food production follows closely the Cretan custom. All agricultural produce should be divided into twelve parts and consumed accordingly.

Each twelfth portion -- of wheat, barley, and all other produce, together with the livestock available for sale in each of the twelve districts -- shall be further divided into three equal shares. One share goes to the free citizens, another to their servants, and the third to craftsmen and foreigners generally -- including both permanent residents who need to make a living and visitors who come on business with the state or with individuals. Only this third portion shall be offered for sale; no one shall be compelled to sell any of the other two-thirds.

How should these portions be most fairly distributed? The distribution will be equal in one sense and unequal in another."

"What do you mean?"

"Simply that the earth inevitably produces food of varying quality -- sometimes better, sometimes worse."

"Of course."
D: Elegant:
"Food production shall be divided into twelve parts following Cretan custom. Each twelfth is further split into thirds: one for free citizens, one for servants, one for craftsmen and foreigners. Only the third portion may be sold.

Distribution is equal in shares but unequal in quality, since the earth necessarily produces food of varying grades."

"Of course."
D: Summary: The Athenian prescribes a Cretan-style food distribution system: all produce is divided into twelve territorial portions, each further divided into three equal shares for citizens, servants, and foreigners/craftsmen. Only the foreigners' third may be sold commercially. He raises the complication that equal shares may be unequal in quality, since agricultural output naturally varies.
D: Distilled: Food production is divided into twelve territorial portions, each split into thirds for citizens, servants, and foreigners, with only the foreigners' share available for sale.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The tripartite division of produce reflects the three social classes in the proposed city -- free citizens, servants/slaves, and foreign residents (metics) and craftsmen. The restriction of commercial sale to only one-third of production is designed to prevent a market economy from developing. The twelve divisions correspond to the twelve tribes. The acknowledgment that equal shares may be unequal in quality shows the Athenian's awareness of the practical complications of distributive justice -- cf. Aristotle's distinction between numerical and proportional equality in Nicomachean Ethics 5.3-4 (1131a-1131b). The Cretan custom of common meals (syssitia) involved communal food distribution, which this system extends to the entire agricultural economy.

D: Original: Such being the case, let no one of the three portions be greater than either of the other two-neither that which is assigned to masters or to slaves, nor again that of the stranger; but let the distribution to all be equal and alike, and let every citizen take his two portions and distribute them among slaves and freemen, he having power to determine the quantity and quality. And what remains he shall distribute by measure and numb among the animals who have to be sustained from the earth, taking the whole number of them.
D: Narrative: "Such being the case, let no one of the three portions be greater than either of the other two -- neither that which is assigned to masters or to slaves, nor again that of the stranger; but let the distribution to all be equal and alike, and let every citizen take his two portions and distribute them among slaves and freemen, he having power to determine the quantity and quality. And what remains he shall distribute by measure and number among the animals who have to be sustained from the earth, taking the whole number of them."
D: Refashioned: "Given this reality, none of the three shares shall be larger than the others -- neither the masters' portion nor the slaves' portion nor the foreigners'. The distribution to all three groups shall be equal and alike. Each citizen shall take his two portions -- for himself and for his household -- and distribute them between free members and servants as he sees fit, with discretion over both quantity and quality. Whatever remains shall be distributed proportionally by measure and number among the livestock that must be fed from the land, based on their total count."
D: Elegant: "All three shares -- for citizens, servants, and foreigners -- shall be equal. Each citizen receives two portions and distributes them within his household as he chooses, determining quantity and quality. The surplus feeds livestock in proportion to their numbers."
D: Summary: The Athenian mandates strict equality among the three shares of produce -- for citizens, servants, and foreigners. Each citizen takes responsibility for two portions (citizens' and servants'), distributing them within his household at his discretion, while the remainder is allocated to livestock by measure and number.
D: Distilled: The three shares of produce for citizens, servants, and foreigners must be strictly equal, with citizens managing their household allocation and the surplus feeding livestock proportionally.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The equal distribution among the three social classes is notable -- servants and foreigners receive the same share as citizens, reflecting a degree of egalitarianism unusual for ancient Greek political thought. However, the citizen retains control over the internal distribution within his household, maintaining the patriarchal household structure. The provision for livestock reflects the mixed farming economy (agriculture and animal husbandry) of the proposed state. The combination of equal macro-distribution with discretionary micro-distribution (citizen determines quality and quantity within his share) represents a compromise between egalitarian principles and practical household management.

D: Original: In the second place, our citizens should have separate houses duly ordered, and this will be the order proper for men like them. There shall be twelve hamlets, one in the middle of each twelfth portion, and in each hamlet they shall first set apart a market-place, and the temples of the Gods, and of their attendant demigods; and if there be any local deities of the Magnetes, or holy seats of other ancient deities, whose memory has been preserved, to these let them pay their ancient honours. But Hestia, and Zeus, and Athene will have temples everywhere together with the God who presides in each of the twelve districts. And the first erection of houses shall be around these temples, where the ground is highest, in order to provide the safest and most defensible place of retreat for the guards. All the rest of the country they shall settle in the following manner:-They shall make thirteen divisions of the craftsmen; one of them they shall establish in the city, and this, again, they shall subdivide into twelve lesser divisions, among the twelve districts of the city, and the remainder shall be distributed in the country round about; and in each village they shall settle various classes of craftsmen, with a view to the convenience of the husbandmen. And the chief officers of the wardens of the country shall superintend all these matters, and see how many of them, and which class of them, each place requires; and fix them where they are likely to be least troublesome, and most useful to the husbandman. And the wardens of the city shall see to similar matters in the city.
D: Narrative:
"In the second place, our citizens should have separate houses duly ordered, and this will be the order proper for men like them. There shall be twelve hamlets, one in the middle of each twelfth portion, and in each hamlet they shall first set apart a market-place, and the temples of the Gods, and of their attendant demigods; and if there be any local deities of the Magnetes, or holy seats of other ancient deities, whose memory has been preserved, to these let them pay their ancient honours.

But Hestia, and Zeus, and Athene will have temples everywhere together with the God who presides in each of the twelve districts. And the first erection of houses shall be around these temples, where the ground is highest, in order to provide the safest and most defensible place of retreat for the guards.

All the rest of the country they shall settle in the following manner: They shall make thirteen divisions of the craftsmen; one of them they shall establish in the city, and this, again, they shall subdivide into twelve lesser divisions, among the twelve districts of the city, and the remainder shall be distributed in the country round about; and in each village they shall settle various classes of craftsmen, with a view to the convenience of the husbandmen. And the chief officers of the wardens of the country shall superintend all these matters, and see how many of them, and which class of them, each place requires; and fix them where they are likely to be least troublesome, and most useful to the husbandman. And the wardens of the city shall see to similar matters in the city."
D: Refashioned:
"Furthermore, citizens shall have properly ordered separate dwellings, arranged as follows. There shall be twelve hamlets, one at the center of each territorial twelfth. In each hamlet, they shall first designate a marketplace and temples for the gods and their attendant demigods. If there are local deities of the Magnetes or sacred sites of other ancient divinities whose memory has been preserved, they shall continue to receive their traditional honors.

However, Hestia, Zeus, and Athena shall have temples in every hamlet, along with whichever god presides over each of the twelve districts. The first houses shall be built around these temples on the highest ground, providing the guards with the safest and most defensible position for retreat.

The rest of the territory shall be settled as follows. The craftsmen shall be organized into thirteen groups. One group shall be established in the city proper, further subdivided into twelve sections distributed among the city's twelve districts. The remaining twelve groups shall be spread throughout the countryside. In each village, various types of craftsmen shall be settled according to the needs of the local farmers. The senior officers among the country wardens shall oversee this entire arrangement, determining how many craftsmen of each type each location requires and placing them where they will cause the least disruption and provide the most service to the farmers. The city wardens shall manage similar matters within the city."
D: Elegant:
"Twelve hamlets, one per territorial division, each with a marketplace and temples. Hestia, Zeus, and Athena have temples everywhere; local deities retain their honors. Houses are built on high ground around the temples for defensibility.

Thirteen groups of craftsmen: one in the city (subdivided among twelve districts), twelve distributed across the countryside to serve farmers. Country wardens determine placement for maximum utility and minimum disruption; city wardens do the same in town."
D: Summary: The Athenian designs the physical settlement of the state: twelve hamlets centered in each territorial division, each with a marketplace and temples to Hestia, Zeus, Athena, and the district's patron god, with housing on defensible high ground. Craftsmen are divided into thirteen groups -- one for the city and twelve for the countryside -- placed by wardens for maximum service to farmers with minimum disruption.
D: Distilled: The territory is organized into twelve hamlets with temples, marketplaces, and defensible housing, while thirteen groups of craftsmen are distributed between city and countryside to serve agricultural needs.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The triad of Hestia (hearth/home), Zeus (supreme deity), and Athena (wisdom/civic order) represents the core values of the proposed state -- domesticity, divine authority, and rational governance. The Magnetes are the historical inhabitants of the Magnesia region in Thessaly, which is the proposed location for the new city. The defensible placement of houses on high ground around temples reflects both military pragmatism and the symbolic centrality of religion. The thirteen-fold division of craftsmen (12 + 1 for the city) mirrors the twelve-tribe territorial system with the addition of the urban center. The concern for placing craftsmen where they are 'least troublesome and most useful' reflects the Laws' characteristic balancing of social order with practical utility. This passage provides the most detailed urban planning in any Platonic dialogue.

D: Original: Now the wardens of the agora ought to see to the details of the agora. Their first care, after the temples which are in the agora have been seen to, should be to prevent any one from doing any in dealings between man and man; in the second; place, as being inspectors of temperance and violence, they should chastise him who requires chastisement. Touching articles of gale, they should first see whether the articles which the citizens are under regulations to sell to strangers are sold to them, as the law ordains. And let the law be as follows:-on the first day of the month, the persons in charge, whoever they are, whether strangers or slaves, who have the charge on behalf of the citizens, shall produce to the strangers the portion which falls to them, in the first place, a twelfth portion of the corn;-the stranger shall purchase corn for the whole month, and other cereals, on the first market day; and on the tenth day of the month the one party shall sell, and the other buy, liquids sufficient to last during the whole month; and on the twenty-third day there shall be a sale of animals by those who are willing to sell to the people who want to buy, and of implements and other things which husbandmen sell (such as skins and all kinds of clothing, either woven or made of felt and other goods of the same sort), and which strangers are compelled to buy and purchase of others.
D: Narrative:
"Now the wardens of the agora ought to see to the details of the agora. Their first care, after the temples which are in the agora have been seen to, should be to prevent any one from doing any wrong in dealings between man and man; in the second place, as being inspectors of temperance and violence, they should chastise him who requires chastisement.

Touching articles of sale, they should first see whether the articles which the citizens are under regulations to sell to strangers are sold to them, as the law ordains. And let the law be as follows: on the first day of the month, the persons in charge, whoever they are, whether strangers or slaves, who have the charge on behalf of the citizens, shall produce to the strangers the portion which falls to them, in the first place, a twelfth portion of the corn; -- the stranger shall purchase corn for the whole month, and other cereals, on the first market day; and on the tenth day of the month the one party shall sell, and the other buy, liquids sufficient to last during the whole month; and on the twenty-third day there shall be a sale of animals by those who are willing to sell to the people who want to buy, and of implements and other things which husbandmen sell (such as skins and all kinds of clothing, either woven or made of felt and other goods of the same sort), and which strangers are compelled to buy and purchase of others."
D: Refashioned:
"The wardens of the marketplace must attend to its operations. Their first responsibility, after ensuring that the temples within the marketplace are properly maintained, is to prevent dishonest dealings between individuals. Their second duty, as inspectors of both temperance and violence, is to discipline anyone who requires correction.

Regarding goods for sale: the wardens must ensure that the articles citizens are required by regulation to sell to foreigners are in fact made available as the law prescribes. The law shall be as follows.

On the first day of each month, the designated agents -- whether foreigners or slaves acting on behalf of the citizens -- shall present the foreigners' allotted portion, beginning with a twelfth share of the grain supply. Foreigners shall purchase their entire month's supply of grain and other cereals on this first market day.

On the tenth day of the month, liquids sufficient for the whole month shall be bought and sold.

On the twenty-third day, there shall be a market for livestock, sold by those willing to sell to those wishing to buy, along with farm implements and other goods typically sold by farmers -- such as hides, woven cloth, felt, and similar products. These are goods that foreigners must purchase from others."
D: Elegant:
"Marketplace wardens maintain temple order, prevent commercial fraud, and discipline offenders. They ensure that citizens' regulated sales to foreigners proceed as required.

Three market days per month: grain on the first, liquids on the tenth, and livestock, implements, and textiles on the twenty-third. Foreigners purchase their monthly supplies on these fixed dates."
D: Summary: The Athenian defines the duties of the marketplace wardens: maintaining temples, preventing fraud, and enforcing civil conduct. He establishes a regulated monthly market schedule with three designated trading days -- grain on the first, liquids on the tenth, and livestock, tools, and textiles on the twenty-third -- ensuring that foreigners receive their mandated portion of goods.
D: Distilled: Marketplace wardens oversee fairness and order, while a three-day monthly market schedule regulates all commercial transactions between citizens and foreigners.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The agoranomoi (marketplace wardens) were actual officials in many Greek cities, especially Athens, responsible for commercial regulation. The three-day monthly market schedule creates a controlled, periodic exchange system fundamentally different from the continuous commercial activity of contemporary Athens. The dual role of the wardens as inspectors of both 'temperance and violence' -- combining commercial regulation with moral policing -- reflects the Laws' characteristic fusion of economic and ethical governance. The requirement that foreigners 'must' purchase certain goods indicates a compulsory participation in the regulated economy. The specified goods (grain, liquids, livestock, hides, textiles) represent the basic necessities of an agrarian economy.

D: Original: As to the retail trade in these things, whether of barley or wheat set apart for meal and flour, or any other kind of food, no one shall sell them to citizens or their slaves, nor shall any one buy of a citizen; but let the stranger sell them in the market of strangers, to artisans and their slaves, making an exchange of wine and food, which is commonly called retail trade. And butchers shall offer for sale parts of dismembered animals to the strangers, and artisans, and their servants. Let any stranger who likes buy fuel from day to day wholesale, from those who have the care of it in the country, and let him sell to the strangers as much he pleases and when he pleases. As to other goods and implements which are likely to be wanted, they shall sell them in common market, at any place which the guardians of the law and the wardens of the market and city, choosing according to their judgment, shall determine; at such places they shall exchange money for goods, and goods for money, neither party giving credit to the other; and he who gives credit must be satisfied, whether he obtain his money not, for in such exchanges he will not be protected by law.
D: Narrative:
"As to the retail trade in these things, whether of barley or wheat set apart for meal and flour, or any other kind of food, no one shall sell them to citizens or their slaves, nor shall any one buy of a citizen; but let the stranger sell them in the market of strangers, to artisans and their slaves, making an exchange of wine and food, which is commonly called retail trade.

And butchers shall offer for sale parts of dismembered animals to the strangers, and artisans, and their servants. Let any stranger who likes buy fuel from day to day wholesale, from those who have the care of it in the country, and let him sell to the strangers as much as he pleases and when he pleases.

As to other goods and implements which are likely to be wanted, they shall sell them in the common market, at any place which the guardians of the law and the wardens of the market and city, choosing according to their judgment, shall determine; at such places they shall exchange money for goods, and goods for money, neither party giving credit to the other; and he who gives credit must be satisfied, whether he obtain his money or not, for in such exchanges he will not be protected by law."
D: Refashioned:
"As for retail trade in processed foods -- whether barley or wheat ground into meal and flour, or any other kind of food -- no one shall sell these to citizens or their slaves, and no citizen shall buy them. Instead, foreigners shall sell such goods in the foreigners' market, to artisans and their slaves, exchanging wine and food in what is commonly known as retail trade.

Butchers shall sell dismembered animal parts to foreigners, artisans, and their servants. Any foreigner may purchase firewood in bulk on a daily basis from the rural suppliers and resell it to other foreigners in whatever quantity and at whatever time he chooses.

For other goods and equipment likely to be needed, they shall be sold in the common market at locations determined by the judgment of the guardians of the law and the wardens of the marketplace and city. At these designated locations, goods shall be exchanged for money and money for goods -- but no credit shall be extended by either party. Anyone who gives credit does so at his own risk: if he fails to collect, the law will not help him recover the debt."
D: Elegant:
"No retail food sales between citizens -- only foreigners sell processed grain, meat, and provisions to artisans and other foreigners in the foreigners' market. Firewood may be freely traded among foreigners.

Other goods trade at designated common-market locations. All transactions are cash only -- no credit. Anyone who extends credit has no legal recourse for collection."
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes strict rules separating citizen and foreigner commerce: no retail food may be sold to or by citizens. Foreigners conduct retail trade in processed grains, meat, and provisions among themselves and with artisans. A common market for other goods operates at designated locations on a cash-only basis, with no legal protection for credit transactions.
D: Distilled: Citizens are entirely excluded from retail food trade, which is confined to foreigners and artisans in designated markets, and all commercial transactions must be cash-only with no legal recourse for credit.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The complete exclusion of citizens from retail trade reflects the Laws' determination to prevent commercial activity from corrupting civic virtue -- cf. 8.7 on the love of wealth. The restriction of retail to the foreigners' market creates a two-tier economy: a non-commercial citizen economy based on distribution, and a regulated commercial economy for foreigners and artisans. The prohibition of credit is striking and reflects Plato's suspicion of financial instruments -- cf. the ban on moneylending and compound interest in 8.30. The phrase 'commonly called retail trade' (kapeleia) carries a pejorative connotation in Greek, suggesting something base or unseemly. The provision that whoever gives credit 'must be satisfied whether he obtains his money or not' is a powerful market-disciplining mechanism that discourages speculative lending.

D: Original: But whenever property has been bought or sold, greater in quantity or value than is allowed by the law, which has determined within what limited a man may increase and diminish his possessions, let the excess be registered in the books of the guardians of the law; in case of diminution, let there be an erasure made. And let the same rule be observed about the registration of the property of the metics. Any one who likes may come and be a metic on certain conditions; a foreigner, if he likes, and is able to settle, may dwell in the land, but he must practise an art, and not abide more than twenty years from the time at which he has registered himself; and he shall pay no sojourner's tax, however small, except good conduct, nor any other tax for buying and selling. But when the twenty years have expired, he shall take his property with him and depart. And if in the course of these years he should chance to distinguish himself by any considerable benefit which he confers on the state, and he thinks that he can persuade the council and assembly, either to grant him delay in leaving the country, or to allow him to remain for the whole of his life, let him go and persuade the city, and whatever they assent to at his instance shall take effect. For the children of the metics, being artisans, and of fifteen years of age, let the time of their sojourn commence after their fifteenth year; and let them remain for twenty years, and then go where they like; but any of them who wishes to remain, may do so, if he can persuade the council and assembly. And if he depart, let him erase all the entries which have been made by him in the register kept by the magistrates.
D: Narrative:
"But whenever property has been bought or sold, greater in quantity or value than is allowed by the law, which has determined within what limits a man may increase and diminish his possessions, let the excess be registered in the books of the guardians of the law; in case of diminution, let there be an erasure made. And let the same rule be observed about the registration of the property of the metics.

Any one who likes may come and be a metic on certain conditions; a foreigner, if he likes, and is able to settle, may dwell in the land, but he must practise an art, and not abide more than twenty years from the time at which he has registered himself; and he shall pay no sojourner's tax, however small, except good conduct, nor any other tax for buying and selling.

But when the twenty years have expired, he shall take his property with him and depart. And if in the course of these years he should chance to distinguish himself by any considerable benefit which he confers on the state, and he thinks that he can persuade the council and assembly, either to grant him delay in leaving the country, or to allow him to remain for the whole of his life, let him go and persuade the city, and whatever they assent to at his instance shall take effect.

For the children of the metics, being artisans, and of fifteen years of age, let the time of their sojourn commence after their fifteenth year; and let them remain for twenty years, and then go where they like; but any of them who wishes to remain, may do so, if he can persuade the council and assembly. And if he depart, let him erase all the entries which have been made by him in the register kept by the magistrates."
D: Refashioned:
"Whenever property is bought or sold in quantities or values exceeding what the law permits -- the law having set limits on how much a person may increase or decrease his possessions -- the excess shall be recorded in the guardians of the law's registry. In cases of diminution, the corresponding entry shall be erased. The same registration rules shall apply to the property of resident foreigners.

Anyone who wishes may become a resident foreigner under certain conditions. A foreigner who is willing and able to settle may dwell in the land, provided he practices a trade. He may remain for no more than twenty years from the date of his registration. He shall pay no residence tax of any kind, however small -- his only obligation is good conduct -- nor any sales tax.

When the twenty years expire, he must take his property and leave. However, if during his stay he has distinguished himself through some significant service to the state, and if he believes he can persuade the council and assembly to grant him an extension or permanent residency, he may petition the city. Whatever the council and assembly agree to at his request shall take effect.

The children of resident foreigners, being artisans, shall have their twenty-year residency period begin at age fifteen. After twenty years, they may go wherever they wish. Any who desire to remain permanently may do so if they can persuade the council and assembly. If a metic departs, he must erase all entries he has made in the magistrates' registry."
D: Elegant:
"Property transactions exceeding legal limits must be registered with the guardians of the law. The same rules apply to metics.

Anyone may become a resident foreigner if he practices a trade. He pays no taxes beyond good conduct, but must leave after twenty years with all his property. Exceptional service to the state may earn permanent residency by persuading the council and assembly.

Metics' children begin their twenty-year clock at fifteen. All may petition for permanent residence; departing metics must clear their registry entries."
D: Summary: The Athenian concludes Book 8 with regulations on property transactions and resident foreigners. All transactions beyond legal property limits must be registered. Foreigners may settle as metics for up to twenty years, tax-free, provided they practice a trade. Those who perform distinguished service may petition for permanent residency through the council and assembly. Children of metics begin their own twenty-year period at age fifteen and may also petition to stay permanently.
D: Distilled: Resident foreigners may settle for twenty years tax-free if they practice a trade, with the possibility of permanent residency for those who perform distinguished service and persuade the assembly.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The metic (metoikos) system was a well-established institution in Athens and other Greek cities, where resident foreigners had defined legal status but limited political rights. The Laws' version is notably generous in some respects (no residence tax, no sales tax) but strict in others (twenty-year limit, mandatory departure). The provision for earning permanent residency through meritorious service introduces a performance-based immigration pathway unusual in ancient law. The property registration system reflects the Laws' overall concern with controlling wealth accumulation -- cf. Laws 5.744b-745a on property limits. The erasure of registry entries upon departure shows the administrative thoroughness of the proposed state. This passage concludes Book 8, which has traversed an extraordinary range: from festivals and military training through sexual regulation to agricultural law, trade policy, and immigration -- a scope reflecting the Laws' ambition to legislate comprehensively for every aspect of civic life.

D: Original:
Next to all the matters which have preceded in the natural order of legislation will come suits of law. Of suits those which relate to agriculture have been already described, but the more important have not been described. Having mentioned them severally under their usual names, we will proceed to say what punishments are to be inflicted for each offence, and who are to be the judges of them.

Very good.
D: Narrative:
"Next to all the matters which have preceded in the natural order of legislation will come suits of law. Of suits those which relate to agriculture have been already described, but the more important have not been described. Having mentioned them severally under their usual names, we will proceed to say what punishments are to be inflicted for each offence, and who are to be the judges of them."

"Very good," Cleinias replied.
D: Refashioned:
"Following the natural order of our legislation, the next topic is lawsuits. We have already dealt with suits relating to agriculture, but the more significant categories remain undiscussed. After identifying each type of offense by its standard name, we will specify the punishments appropriate to each crime and designate the judges who will preside over them."

"Very good," Cleinias agreed.
D: Elegant:
"Next in order come lawsuits. Agricultural disputes have been addressed; the serious offenses have not. We will now name each crime, assign its punishment, and appoint its judges."

"Very good," said Cleinias.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger announces the transition to criminal law. Agricultural suits have been covered; now the discussion turns to naming serious offenses, specifying punishments, and assigning judges.
D: Distilled: The Stranger introduces criminal legislation as the next topic, moving from agricultural suits to serious offenses and their punishments.
Notes: Speakers: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The Stranger opens Book 9 by transitioning from the agricultural regulations of Book 8 to criminal law proper. This follows the structural pattern of the Laws, which proceeds through topics in the order a real legislator would encounter them. The brief exchange with Cleinias ('Very good') signals assent and maintains the dialogic form, though Cleinias and Megillus play increasingly passive roles in the later books. The concern with 'natural order' in legislation reflects Plato's conviction that law must follow the structure of reality itself -- a theme developed in Republic 6-7 regarding the philosopher's grasp of the Good as prerequisite for governance.

D: Original: There is a sense of disgrace in legislating, as we are about to do, for all the details of crime in a state which, as we say, is to be well regulated and will be perfectly adapted to the practice of virtue. To assume that in such a state there will arise some one who will be guilty of crimes as heinous as any which are ever perpetrated in other states, and that we must legislate for him by anticipation, and threaten and make laws against him if he should arise, in order to deter him, and punish his acts, under the idea that he will arise-this, as I was saying, is in a manner disgraceful.
D: Narrative: "There is a sense of disgrace in legislating, as we are about to do, for all the details of crime in a state which, as we say, is to be well regulated and will be perfectly adapted to the practice of virtue. To assume that in such a state there will arise some one who will be guilty of crimes as heinous as any which are ever perpetrated in other states, and that we must legislate for him by anticipation, and threaten and make laws against him if he should arise, in order to deter him, and punish his acts, under the idea that he will arise -- this, as I was saying, is in a manner disgraceful."
D: Refashioned: "There is something embarrassing about legislating in detail for crime in a state that we claim will be well governed and perfectly oriented toward virtue. To assume that even in such a state someone will commit crimes as terrible as those found anywhere else -- and that we must preemptively draft laws to threaten and deter this hypothetical criminal, as though his appearance were inevitable -- this, as I was saying, is somewhat disgraceful."
D: Elegant: "It is embarrassing to legislate for serious crime in a state designed for virtue. To assume such crimes will occur even here -- and to draft deterrent laws in anticipation -- feels disgraceful."
D: Summary: The Stranger expresses discomfort at having to write criminal laws for an ideal state. If the state is truly virtuous, it seems contradictory to assume heinous crimes will occur and to legislate preventively against them.
D: Distilled: The Stranger finds it paradoxical and somewhat shameful to draft criminal laws for a state supposedly designed for perfect virtue.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage captures a tension central to Plato's political philosophy: the gap between the ideal and the practically necessary. In the Republic, Socrates can design a city governed by philosopher-kings who need minimal criminal law because virtue is cultivated directly. The Laws, Plato's last and longest work, represents his mature acknowledgment that real legislation must account for human imperfection. The word 'disgraceful' (aischron) carries strong moral weight in Greek ethics -- it is not mere inconvenience but a genuine ethical failing to assume the worst of one's citizens. Compare Republic 405a-c, where Socrates criticizes elaborate legal systems as symptoms of a sick society.

D: Original: Yet seeing that we are not like the ancient legislators, who gave laws to heroes and sons of gods, being, according to the popular belief, themselves the offspring of the gods, and legislating for others, who were also the children of divine parents, but that we are only men who are legislating for the sons of men, there is no uncharitableness in apprehending that some one of our citizens may be like a seed which has touched the ox's horn, having a heart so hard that it cannot be softened any more than those seeds can be softened by fire. Among our citizens there may be those who cannot be subdued by all the strength of the laws; and for their sake, though an ungracious task, I will proclaim my first law about the robbing of temples, in case any one should dare to commit such a crime. I do not expect or imagine that any well-brought-up citizen will ever take the infection, but their servants, and strangers, and strangers' servants may be guilty of many impieties. And with a view to them especially, and yet not without a provident eye to the weakness of human nature generally, I will proclaim the law about robbers of temples and similar incurable, or almost incurable, criminals.
D: Narrative:
"Yet seeing that we are not like the ancient legislators, who gave laws to heroes and sons of gods, being, according to the popular belief, themselves the offspring of the gods, and legislating for others, who were also the children of divine parents, but that we are only men who are legislating for the sons of men, there is no uncharitableness in apprehending that some one of our citizens may be like a seed which has touched the ox's horn, having a heart so hard that it cannot be softened any more than those seeds can be softened by fire.

"Among our citizens there may be those who cannot be subdued by all the strength of the laws; and for their sake, though an ungracious task, I will proclaim my first law about the robbing of temples, in case any one should dare to commit such a crime. I do not expect or imagine that any well-brought-up citizen will ever take the infection, but their servants, and strangers, and strangers' servants may be guilty of many impieties. And with a view to them especially, and yet not without a provident eye to the weakness of human nature generally, I will proclaim the law about robbers of temples and similar incurable, or almost incurable, criminals."
D: Refashioned:
"But we are not like the ancient lawgivers, who -- according to popular tradition -- were themselves children of gods, giving laws to heroes and the offspring of divine parents. We are merely human beings legislating for other human beings. So there is nothing uncharitable in expecting that some of our citizens may prove to be like seeds that have hardened against the ox's horn -- hearts so obstinate that no fire can soften them.

"There may be people among us whom the full force of our laws cannot tame. For their sake, unpleasant as the task is, I will lay down the first law concerning temple robbery, in case anyone dares to commit such a crime. I do not expect that any properly educated citizen will succumb to this impulse, but servants, foreigners, and foreigners' servants may commit many impious acts. It is primarily with them in mind -- though also with a prudent awareness of human nature's general weakness -- that I will proclaim the law against temple robbers and similarly incurable, or nearly incurable, criminals."
D: Elegant: "Unlike the ancient lawgivers -- who were reputedly gods' children legislating for heroes -- we are merely humans legislating for humans. Some citizens may have hearts hardened beyond all correction. For their sake, I reluctantly introduce the first criminal law: against temple robbery. I doubt any well-educated citizen will commit such crimes, but servants and foreigners may. This law addresses them specifically, while acknowledging universal human weakness."
D: Summary: The Stranger justifies criminal legislation by contrasting their situation with mythic lawgivers who governed divine beings. Since they legislate for fallible humans, some citizens may be incorrigible. He introduces temple robbery as the first offense, expecting it mainly from servants and foreigners but acknowledging universal human weakness.
D: Distilled: Since we are humans legislating for humans rather than gods for heroes, we must realistically prepare criminal laws for incorrigible offenders, starting with temple robbery.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 'seed which has touched the ox's horn' is an ancient Greek agricultural metaphor -- seeds placed inside a hollowed ox horn were believed to become extremely hard. This image of moral incorrigibility is central to the Laws' theory of punishment. The distinction between 'curable' and 'incurable' criminals recurs throughout Book 9 and connects directly to the Gorgias (525b-c), where Socrates argues that some souls are punished in Hades for their improvement while incurable souls serve only as deterrent examples to others. The reference to 'ancient legislators' likely alludes to figures like Minos (associated with Crete and thus relevant to Cleinias) and Lycurgus of Sparta (relevant to Megillus). The qualification that servants and strangers are more likely offenders reflects the realities of Greek social hierarchy while the final clause -- 'not without a provident eye to the weakness of human nature generally' -- prevents this from becoming mere class prejudice.

D: Original: Having already agreed that such enactments ought always to have a short prelude, we may speak to the criminal, whom some tormenting desire by night and by day tempts to go and rob a temple, the fewest possible words of admonition and exhortation:-O sir, we will say to him, the impulse which moves you to rob temples is not an ordinary human malady, nor yet a visitation of heaven, but a madness which is begotten in a man from ancient and unexpiated crimes of his race, an ever-recurring curse;-against this you must guard with all your might, and how you are to guard we will explain to you. When any such thought comes into your mind, go and perform expiations, go as a suppliant to the temples of the Gods who avert evils, go to the society of those who are called good men among you; hear them tell and yourself try to repeat after them, that every man should honour the noble and the just. Fly from the company of the wicked-fly and turn not back; and if your disorder is lightened by these remedies, well and good, but if not, then acknowledge death to be nobler than life, and depart hence.
D: Narrative:
"Having already agreed that such enactments ought always to have a short prelude, we may speak to the criminal, whom some tormenting desire by night and by day tempts to go and rob a temple, the fewest possible words of admonition and exhortation:

"'O sir, the impulse which moves you to rob temples is not an ordinary human malady, nor yet a visitation of heaven, but a madness which is begotten in a man from ancient and unexpiated crimes of his race, an ever-recurring curse -- against this you must guard with all your might, and how you are to guard we will explain to you.

"'When any such thought comes into your mind, go and perform expiations, go as a suppliant to the temples of the Gods who avert evils, go to the society of those who are called good men among you; hear them tell and yourself try to repeat after them, that every man should honour the noble and the just. Fly from the company of the wicked -- fly and turn not back; and if your disorder is lightened by these remedies, well and good, but if not, then acknowledge death to be nobler than life, and depart hence.'"
D: Refashioned:
"Since we have already agreed that every law should open with a brief prelude, let us address the potential criminal -- the person tormented day and night by the desire to rob a temple -- with as few words of warning and encouragement as possible:

"'The impulse driving you to rob temples is not an ordinary sickness, nor a divine visitation. It is a madness born from ancient, unatoned crimes in your family line -- a recurring curse. You must fight it with everything you have, and we will tell you how.

"'When such a thought enters your mind, go perform rites of purification. Go as a humble petitioner to the temples of the gods who ward off evil. Seek the company of those recognized as good people among you. Listen to them affirm -- and repeat their words yourself -- that every person should honor what is noble and just. Flee the company of the wicked -- flee without looking back. If these remedies ease your affliction, good. But if they do not, accept that death is more honorable than a life so corrupted, and leave this world.'"
D: Elegant: "Every law needs a prelude. To the one tempted to rob a temple, we say: Your impulse is not ordinary illness or divine punishment but inherited madness from unatoned ancestral crimes. Fight it. Seek purification, pray to the gods, keep company with good people, repeat their teachings about justice. Flee the wicked. If these remedies fail, accept that death is nobler than such a life."
D: Summary: The Stranger models the 'prelude' to criminal law -- a direct address to the potential temple robber. It diagnoses the criminal impulse as inherited ancestral madness, prescribes purification rituals and association with good people, and warns that if all remedies fail, death is preferable to a criminal life.
D: Distilled: The legal prelude addresses the would-be temple robber directly, diagnosing criminal impulse as inherited madness and prescribing purification, good company, and -- if all else fails -- acceptance of death over corruption.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The concept of legal 'preludes' (prooemia) is one of the Laws' most distinctive innovations. Introduced in Book 4 (719e-724b), the prelude is the persuasive, educational component that precedes the coercive letter of the law -- analogous to the difference between the free doctor who explains treatment to free patients and the slave doctor who simply prescribes (720a-e). This passage is also notable for its theory of inherited guilt (ancestral miasma), a concept deeply rooted in Greek religion and tragedy (cf. the house of Atreus in Aeschylus' Oresteia). The recommendation to 'fly from the company of the wicked' echoes themes from Protagoras 325c-326e on moral education through association. The startling final counsel -- that death is preferable to incurable criminality -- connects to the Gorgias (512a-b), where Socrates argues that a wicked life is not worth living, and to Apology 38a: 'the unexamined life is not worth living.'

D: Original: Such are the preludes which we sing to all who have thoughts of unholy and treasonable actions, and to him who hearkens to them the law has nothing to say. But to him who is disobedient when the prelude is over, cry with a loud voice,-He who is taken in the act of robbing temples, if he be a slave or stranger, shall have his evil deed engraven on his face and hands, and shall be beaten with as many stripes as may seem good to the judges, and be cast naked beyond the borders of the land. And if he suffers this punishment he will probably return to his right mind and be improved; for no penalty which the law inflicts is designed for evil, but always makes him who suffers either better or not so much worse as he would have been. But if any citizen be found guilty of any great or unmentionable wrong, either in relation to the gods, or his parents, or the state, let the judge deem him to be incurable, remembering that after receiving such an excellent education and training from youth upward, he has not abstained from the greatest of crimes. His punishment shall be death, which to him will be the least of evils; and his example will benefit others, if he perish ingloriously, and be cast beyond the borders of the land. But let his children and family, if they avoid the ways of their father, have glory, and let honourable mention be made of them, as having nobly and manfully escaped out of evil into good. None of them should have their goods confiscated to the state, for the lots of the citizens ought always to continue the same and equal.
D: Narrative:
"Such are the preludes which we sing to all who have thoughts of unholy and treasonable actions, and to him who hearkens to them the law has nothing to say. But to him who is disobedient when the prelude is over, cry with a loud voice:

"'He who is taken in the act of robbing temples, if he be a slave or stranger, shall have his evil deed engraven on his face and hands, and shall be beaten with as many stripes as may seem good to the judges, and be cast naked beyond the borders of the land.'

"And if he suffers this punishment he will probably return to his right mind and be improved; for no penalty which the law inflicts is designed for evil, but always makes him who suffers either better or not so much worse as he would have been.

"But if any citizen be found guilty of any great or unmentionable wrong, either in relation to the gods, or his parents, or the state, let the judge deem him to be incurable, remembering that after receiving such an excellent education and training from youth upward, he has not abstained from the greatest of crimes. His punishment shall be death, which to him will be the least of evils; and his example will benefit others, if he perish ingloriously, and be cast beyond the borders of the land.

"But let his children and family, if they avoid the ways of their father, have glory, and let honourable mention be made of them, as having nobly and manfully escaped out of evil into good. None of them should have their goods confiscated to the state, for the lots of the citizens ought always to continue the same and equal."
D: Refashioned:
"These are the preludes we address to everyone contemplating unholy or treasonable acts. For those who listen, the law has nothing further to say. But for those who remain disobedient after the prelude, the law declares loudly:

"A slave or foreigner caught robbing a temple shall have his crime branded on his face and hands, shall receive as many lashes as the judges see fit, and shall be stripped naked and expelled beyond the borders.

"If he endures this punishment, he will likely come to his senses and improve. No legal penalty is designed to cause harm for its own sake -- it always makes the offender either better or at least less depraved than he would otherwise have become.

"But if a citizen is convicted of a grave offense against the gods, his parents, or the state, the judge should consider him incurable. After receiving an excellent education and moral training from childhood, he still could not refrain from the worst crimes. His punishment shall be death -- which for him is actually the least of evils. His inglorious execution and expulsion from the land will serve as a deterrent to others.

"His children and family, however, if they reject their father's ways, shall receive public honor and praise for having courageously escaped from evil into good. Their property shall not be confiscated by the state, for the citizens' allotments of land must always remain equal and unchanged."
D: Elegant:
"The prelude appeals to those who will listen; the law speaks to those who will not. A slave or foreigner caught robbing temples shall be branded, flogged, and expelled. Legal punishment is never designed to cause harm -- it aims to improve the offender or at least limit his deterioration.

A citizen guilty of grave crimes against gods, parents, or state is deemed incurable -- someone who had every educational advantage yet still committed the worst offenses. Death is his sentence, both his least evil and a deterrent to others. But his children, if they choose a different path, shall be honored, and their property shall remain intact."
D: Summary: The Stranger distinguishes the prelude's audience (who may be persuaded) from the law's subjects (who cannot). Slaves or foreigners caught in temple robbery face branding, flogging, and exile. Citizens guilty of grave crimes are deemed incurable and sentenced to death. Punishment always aims at improvement or deterrence, never mere retribution. Criminals' families keep their property and honor if they live virtuously.
D: Distilled: After the persuasive prelude, the law itself punishes temple robbers with branding and exile (for non-citizens) or death (for citizens deemed incurable), while sparing their families' honor and property.
