Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage contains one of the Laws' most important penological principles: 'no penalty which the law inflicts is designed for evil, but always makes him who suffers either better or not so much worse as he would have been.' This rehabilitative theory of punishment is the legal application of the Gorgias' central argument (477a-479e) that punishment is medicine for the soul -- the unjust person who escapes punishment is worse off than the one who is punished, just as the sick person who avoids the doctor is worse off than the one who submits to treatment. The distinction between curable offenders (punished for reform) and incurable ones (executed as deterrent examples) maps directly onto the Gorgias' afterlife myth (525b-d). The rule against confiscating families' property reflects the Laws' commitment to maintaining the 5,040 equal lots that form the economic foundation of the state (Book 5, 737c-738b). The principle that children should not suffer for their parents' crimes was progressive for its time, contrasting with the inherited guilt that pervaded Greek legal and religious tradition.

D: Original: Touching the exaction of penalties, when a man appears to have done anything which deserves a fine, he shall pay the fine, if he have anything in excess of the lot which is assigned to him; but more than that he shall not pay. And to secure exactness, let the guardians of the law refer to the registers, and inform the judges of the precise truth, in order that none of the lots may go uncultivated for want of money. But if any one seems to deserve a greater penalty, let him undergo a long and public imprisonment and be dishonoured, unless some of his friends are willing to be surety for him, and liberate him by assisting him to pay the fine. No criminal shall go unpunished, not even for a single offence, nor if he have fled the country; but let the penalty be according to his deserts-death, or bonds, or blows, or degrading places of sitting or standing, or removal to some temple on the borders of the land; or let him pay fines, as we said before. In cases of death, let the judges be the guardians of the law, and a court selected by merit from the last year's magistrates. But how the causes are to be brought into to court, how the summonses are to be served, the like, these things may be left to the younger generation of legislators to determine; the manner of voting we must determine ourselves.
D: Narrative:
"Touching the exaction of penalties, when a man appears to have done anything which deserves a fine, he shall pay the fine, if he have anything in excess of the lot which is assigned to him; but more than that he shall not pay. And to secure exactness, let the guardians of the law refer to the registers, and inform the judges of the precise truth, in order that none of the lots may go uncultivated for want of money.

"But if any one seems to deserve a greater penalty, let him undergo a long and public imprisonment and be dishonoured, unless some of his friends are willing to be surety for him, and liberate him by assisting him to pay the fine. No criminal shall go unpunished, not even for a single offence, nor if he have fled the country; but let the penalty be according to his deserts -- death, or bonds, or blows, or degrading places of sitting or standing, or removal to some temple on the borders of the land; or let him pay fines, as we said before.

"In cases of death, let the judges be the guardians of the law, and a court selected by merit from the last year's magistrates. But how the causes are to be brought into court, how the summonses are to be served, and the like -- these things may be left to the younger generation of legislators to determine; the manner of voting we must determine ourselves."
D: Refashioned:
"Regarding the collection of penalties: when someone is convicted of an offense deserving a fine, he shall pay it from whatever property he possesses beyond his assigned land allotment -- but no more than that. To ensure accuracy, the guardians of the law shall consult the property registers and report the exact figures to the judges, so that no citizen's land goes uncultivated because he has been financially ruined by fines.

"If someone deserves a punishment greater than a fine, he shall be publicly imprisoned for an extended period and stripped of his civic honors -- unless friends are willing to post bond for him and help pay the fine to secure his release. No criminal shall escape punishment for even a single offense, not even by fleeing the country. The penalty shall fit his crime: death, imprisonment, flogging, public humiliation through degrading assigned positions, banishment to a temple on the borders, or monetary fines as described.

"In capital cases, the judges shall be the guardians of the law together with a special court of distinguished magistrates selected from the previous year's officeholders. Procedural details -- how cases are brought to trial, how summonses are issued, and similar matters -- may be left for the next generation of legislators to work out. But the method of voting we must settle ourselves."
D: Elegant: "Fines shall be paid only from property exceeding the citizen's assigned lot, to prevent land from going uncultivated. The guardians of the law shall verify exact amounts from the registers. For crimes deserving more than fines: public imprisonment and civic disgrace, unless friends post bond. No criminal escapes punishment, even by fleeing. Penalties range from death to imprisonment, flogging, public humiliation, exile, or fines. Capital cases require the guardians of the law and a merit-selected court. Procedural details can be left to future legislators, but voting methods must be decided now."
D: Summary: The Stranger establishes rules for collecting penalties. Fines cannot exceed a citizen's surplus property beyond his land allotment. Greater offenses merit imprisonment and public dishonor. No criminal escapes punishment. Capital cases are judged by the guardians of the law and a merit-based court.
D: Distilled: Fines are limited to surplus property to protect land allotments, no criminal escapes punishment regardless of flight, and capital cases require specially qualified judges.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The protection of land allotments (kleroi) from excessive fines is critical to the Laws' entire social architecture. The 5,040 lots are meant to be inalienable and indivisible (Book 5, 740b-741e), forming the permanent economic basis of citizenship. By limiting fines to surplus property, the Stranger prevents criminal penalties from undermining the state's economic foundation. The range of punishments -- death, bonds, blows, degrading positions, temple exile, fines -- reflects historical Greek penal practice. 'Degrading places of sitting or standing' (atimia) refers to the loss of civic rights and the public shaming practices common in Greek cities. The delegation of procedural matters to 'the younger generation of legislators' is a rare acknowledgment within the Laws that the founding generation cannot anticipate every administrative detail -- a pragmatic concession unusual for Plato.

D: Original: Let the vote be given openly; but before they come to the vote let the judges sit in order of seniority over against plaintiff and defendant, and let all the citizens who can spare time hear and take a serious interest in listening to such causes. First of all the plaintiff shall make one speech, and then the defendant shall make another; and after the speeches have been made the eldest judge shall begin to examine the parties, and proceed to make an adequate enquiry into what has been said; and after the oldest has spoken, the rest shall proceed in order to examine either party as to what he finds defective in the evidence, whether of statement or omission; and he who has nothing to ask shall hand over the examination to another. And on so much of what has been said as is to the purpose all the judges shall set their seals, and place the writings on the altar of Hestia. On the next day they shall meet again, and in like manner put their questions and go through the cause, and again set their seals upon the evidence; and when they have three times done this, and have had witnesses and evidence enough, they shall each of them give a holy vote, after promising by Hestia that they will decide justly and truly to the utmost of their power; and so they shall put an end to the suit.
D: Narrative:
"Let the vote be given openly; but before they come to the vote let the judges sit in order of seniority over against plaintiff and defendant, and let all the citizens who can spare time hear and take a serious interest in listening to such causes.

"First of all the plaintiff shall make one speech, and then the defendant shall make another; and after the speeches have been made the eldest judge shall begin to examine the parties, and proceed to make an adequate enquiry into what has been said; and after the oldest has spoken, the rest shall proceed in order to examine either party as to what he finds defective in the evidence, whether of statement or omission; and he who has nothing to ask shall hand over the examination to another.

"And on so much of what has been said as is to the purpose all the judges shall set their seals, and place the writings on the altar of Hestia. On the next day they shall meet again, and in like manner put their questions and go through the cause, and again set their seals upon the evidence; and when they have three times done this, and have had witnesses and evidence enough, they shall each of them give a holy vote, after promising by Hestia that they will decide justly and truly to the utmost of their power; and so they shall put an end to the suit."
D: Refashioned:
"Votes shall be cast openly. Before voting, the judges shall sit in order of seniority facing the plaintiff and defendant, and all citizens who have the time should attend and listen attentively to these proceedings.

"The plaintiff shall speak first, then the defendant. After both speeches, the senior judge shall begin examining the parties, conducting a thorough inquiry into what has been said. The remaining judges shall then examine each party in order of seniority, questioning whatever they find deficient in the evidence -- whether something was inadequately stated or omitted entirely. Any judge with no questions shall pass his turn to the next.

"The judges shall then seal their signatures on all relevant portions of the testimony and place these written records on the altar of Hestia. They shall reconvene the next day, repeat the process of questioning and reviewing the case, and seal the evidence again. After doing this three times -- and having gathered sufficient witnesses and evidence -- each judge shall cast a sacred vote, having sworn by Hestia to decide justly and truthfully to the best of his ability. In this way the suit shall be concluded."
D: Elegant: "Votes shall be open. Judges sit by seniority facing both parties. Citizens are encouraged to attend. After plaintiff and defendant each speak, judges examine the parties in order of seniority, probing gaps in evidence. They seal relevant testimony and place it on the altar of Hestia. This process repeats over three days. Then each judge swears by Hestia to decide justly and casts a sacred vote, ending the suit."
D: Summary: The Stranger describes trial procedure: open voting, judges seated by seniority, public attendance encouraged. After speeches from both sides, judges sequentially examine the evidence over three days, sealing testimony on the altar of Hestia each time. Final votes are cast under sacred oath.
D: Distilled: Trials proceed with open voting, sequential judicial examination over three days, sealed testimony placed on Hestia's altar, and sacred oaths before final judgment.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This detailed trial procedure is among the most specific institutional designs in the Laws. The three-day structure with sealed testimony anticipates concerns about judicial corruption and hasty judgment -- themes that echo the Apology's implicit critique of Socrates' one-day trial and the Athenian system's vulnerability to rhetorical manipulation. The invocation of Hestia (goddess of the hearth and civic center) as guarantor of judicial oaths connects law to religion and civic identity. Open voting contrasts with the secret ballot used in some Athenian courts; the Stranger apparently believes public accountability produces better outcomes than anonymous voting. The sequential examination by seniority ensures that less experienced judges can learn from their elders' questioning before contributing their own -- an educational dimension to the judicial process itself. Compare Republic 409a-c on the ideal judge who knows injustice through learning rather than personal experience.

D: Original:
Next, after what relates to the Gods, follows what relates to the dissolution of the state:-Whoever by promoting a man to power enslaves the laws, and subjects the city to factions, using violence and stirring up sedition contrary to law, him we will deem the greatest enemy of the whole state. But he who takes no part in such proceedings, and, being one of the chief magistrates of the state, has no knowledge of the treason, or, having knowledge of it, by reason of cowardice does not interfere on behalf of his country, such an one we must consider nearly as bad. Every man who is worth anything will inform the magistrates, and bring the conspirator to trial for making a violent and illegal attempt to change the government. The judges of such cases shall be the same as of the robbers of temples; and let the whole proceeding be carried on in the same way, and the vote of the majority condemn to death. But let there be a general rule, that the disgrace and punishment of the father is not to be visited on the children, except in the case of some one whose father, grandfather, and great-grandfather have successively undergone the penalty of death. Such persons the city shall send away with all their possessions to the city and country of their ancestors, retaining only and wholly their appointed lot. And out of the citizens who have more than one son of not less than ten years of age, they shall select ten whom their father or grandfather by the mother's or father's side shall appoint, and let them send to Delphi the names of those who are selected, and him whom the God chooses they shall establish as heir of the house which has failed; and may he have better fortune than his predecessors!

Very good.
D: Narrative:
"Next, after what relates to the Gods, follows what relates to the dissolution of the state: Whoever by promoting a man to power enslaves the laws, and subjects the city to factions, using violence and stirring up sedition contrary to law, him we will deem the greatest enemy of the whole state. But he who takes no part in such proceedings, and, being one of the chief magistrates of the state, has no knowledge of the treason, or, having knowledge of it, by reason of cowardice does not interfere on behalf of his country -- such an one we must consider nearly as bad.

"Every man who is worth anything will inform the magistrates, and bring the conspirator to trial for making a violent and illegal attempt to change the government. The judges of such cases shall be the same as of the robbers of temples; and let the whole proceeding be carried on in the same way, and the vote of the majority condemn to death.

"But let there be a general rule, that the disgrace and punishment of the father is not to be visited on the children, except in the case of some one whose father, grandfather, and great-grandfather have successively undergone the penalty of death. Such persons the city shall send away with all their possessions to the city and country of their ancestors, retaining only and wholly their appointed lot. And out of the citizens who have more than one son of not less than ten years of age, they shall select ten whom their father or grandfather by the mother's or father's side shall appoint, and let them send to Delphi the names of those who are selected, and him whom the God chooses they shall establish as heir of the house which has failed; and may he have better fortune than his predecessors!"

"Very good," Cleinias replied.
D: Refashioned:
"After offenses against the gods comes the next gravest category: crimes that threaten to destroy the state itself. Whoever elevates a man to power in a way that enslaves the laws, divides the city into hostile factions, and stirs up violent sedition in defiance of law -- this person we declare the greatest enemy of the entire state. But nearly as culpable is the senior magistrate who either remains ignorant of such a conspiracy through negligence or, knowing about it, fails to act against it out of cowardice.

"Every citizen of any worth will report conspirators to the magistrates and bring them to trial for their violent and illegal attempt to overthrow the government. These cases shall be tried by the same judges and procedures used for temple robbers, and a majority vote shall condemn the offender to death.

"As a general principle, the disgrace and punishment of the father shall not fall upon his children -- except when father, grandfather, and great-grandfather have all successively been sentenced to death. In that case, the family shall be expelled to their ancestral homeland with all their movable possessions, while the state retains their assigned land allotment. To fill the vacated household, the citizens shall choose from families with more than one son over the age of ten. The father or grandfather on either side shall nominate ten candidates, whose names shall be sent to the oracle at Delphi. Whomever the god selects shall be established as heir to the failed house -- and may he have better fortune than his predecessors!"

"Very good," said Cleinias.
D: Elegant:
"After crimes against the gods come crimes against the state. Whoever uses violence to elevate a tyrant, enslave the laws, and foment sedition is the state's worst enemy. A magistrate who ignores or cowardly tolerates such treason is nearly as guilty. Good citizens will report conspirators, who shall be tried like temple robbers and condemned to death by majority vote.

Children shall not bear their father's punishment -- unless three consecutive generations have been executed, in which case the family is exiled and their lot reassigned. A new heir is chosen by the Delphic oracle from among nominated candidates."

"Very good," said Cleinias.
D: Summary: The Stranger addresses sedition and treason as crimes second only to offenses against the gods. Conspirators who enslave the laws through violence face death. Cowardly magistrates who fail to act are nearly as guilty. Children are generally exempt from parental punishment, but families with three consecutive generations of capital offenders are exiled and replaced through Delphic oracle selection.
D: Distilled: Those who violently subvert the state's laws are its greatest enemies and face death; their children are spared unless three generations have been executed, in which case the family is exiled and replaced.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage addresses stasis -- civil faction and sedition -- which ancient Greeks considered the gravest political disease. Thucydides' account of the Corcyrean civil war (3.82-84) is the classic description of stasis destroying a city. The Stranger's equation of cowardly inaction with active treason is remarkable and reflects Republic 496c-e, where Socrates describes the philosopher's temptation to withdraw from corrupt politics. The three-generation rule for exiling families is a compromise between individual responsibility and the Greek belief in hereditary moral tendencies. The Delphic oracle's role in selecting replacement heirs gives divine sanction to the transfer and reflects the Laws' consistent reliance on traditional religious institutions alongside rational legislation. Historically, Greek tyrants often rose to power exactly as described here -- through faction-building and constitutional subversion -- making these provisions practically relevant. Compare Aristotle's Politics 5, which systematically analyzes the causes of revolution.

D: Original:
Once more let there be a third general law respecting the judges who are to give judgment, and the manner of conducting suits against those who are tried on an accusation of treason; and as concerning the remaining or departure of their descendants-there shall be one law for all three, for the traitor, and the robber of temples, and the subverter by violence of the laws of the state. For a thief, whether he steal much or little, let there be one law, and one punishment for all alike: in the first place, let him pay double the amount of the theft if he be convicted, and if he have so much over and above the allotment;-if he have not, he shall be bound until he pay the penalty, or persuade him has obtained the sentence against him to forgive him. But if a person be convicted of a theft against the state, then if he can persuade the city, or if he will pay back twice the amount of the theft, he shall be set free from his bonds.

What makes you say, Stranger, that a theft is all one, whether the thief may have taken much or little, and either from sacred or secular places-and these are not the only differences in thefts:-seeing, then, that they are of many kinds, ought not the legislator to adapt himself to them, and impose upon them entirely different penalties?
D: Narrative:
"Once more let there be a third general law respecting the judges who are to give judgment, and the manner of conducting suits against those who are tried on an accusation of treason; and as concerning the remaining or departure of their descendants -- there shall be one law for all three, for the traitor, and the robber of temples, and the subverter by violence of the laws of the state.

"For a thief, whether he steal much or little, let there be one law, and one punishment for all alike: in the first place, let him pay double the amount of the theft if he be convicted, and if he have so much over and above the allotment; if he have not, he shall be bound until he pay the penalty, or persuade him who has obtained the sentence against him to forgive him. But if a person be convicted of a theft against the state, then if he can persuade the city, or if he will pay back twice the amount of the theft, he shall be set free from his bonds."

"What makes you say, Stranger," Cleinias asked, "that a theft is all one, whether the thief may have taken much or little, and either from sacred or secular places -- and these are not the only differences in thefts -- seeing, then, that they are of many kinds, ought not the legislator to adapt himself to them, and impose upon them entirely different penalties?"
D: Refashioned:
"Let a single, unified law govern the judges and trial procedures for all three categories of grave crime -- treason, temple robbery, and violent subversion of the state's laws -- including the rules about whether descendants may remain in the city or must depart.

"As for theft, whether a thief steals a great deal or very little, one law and one punishment shall apply equally: the convicted thief shall pay double the value of what was stolen, provided he has property beyond his land allotment. If he does not, he shall remain imprisoned until he pays or persuades the person who won the judgment against him to grant forgiveness. If someone is convicted of stealing from the state, he shall be freed from imprisonment if he can either persuade the city to forgive him or repay twice the stolen amount."

"Why do you say, Stranger," Cleinias objected, "that all theft is the same, whether the thief took much or little, and whether from sacred or secular places? These are not even the only distinctions among thefts. Since there are so many varieties, shouldn't the legislator recognize these differences and impose entirely different penalties for each?"
D: Elegant:
"One unified law shall govern trials for treason, temple robbery, and violent subversion of the state, including rules about descendants' exile.

For theft, one law applies regardless of amount: the thief pays double, funded only from property beyond his allotment. If unable to pay, he is imprisoned until he pays or gains forgiveness. State theft follows similar rules."

Cleinias objected: "Why treat all theft identically? Thefts differ in amount, in whether they target sacred or secular property, and in many other ways. Shouldn't different penalties match different kinds of theft?"
D: Summary: The Stranger proposes unified procedures for the three gravest crimes and a single theft law requiring double repayment. Cleinias challenges this, arguing that theft varies greatly in kind -- sacred vs. secular, large vs. small -- and different penalties should apply to different types.
D: Distilled: The Stranger proposes one uniform theft penalty (double repayment), prompting Cleinias to object that different kinds of theft deserve different punishments.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This is one of the more dramatically interesting passages in Book 9, as Cleinias interrupts with a genuinely probing objection. His challenge -- that different thefts deserve different penalties -- reflects common-sense legal thinking and anticipates the Stranger's famous digression on the two kinds of doctors. The uniform theft penalty of double repayment has precedent in Greek law; Athenian law also imposed double penalties for certain theft categories, though it distinguished between types more finely than the Stranger initially proposes here. The protection of the land allotment (payment only from surplus property) again reflects the Laws' fundamental economic architecture. Cleinias' intervention also serves a philosophical purpose: it prompts the Stranger to explore the deeper question of whether law should aim primarily at precise calibration of penalties or at the moral education of citizens -- a question that leads to the central philosophical discussion of Book 9.

D: Original: Excellent. I was running on too fast, Cleinias, and you impinged upon me, and brought me to my senses, reminding me of what, indeed, had occurred to mind already, that legislation was never yet rightly worked out, as I may say in passing.-Do you remember the image in which I likened the men for whom laws are now made to slaves who are doctored by slaves? For of this you may be very sure, that if one of those empirical physicians, who practise medicine without science, were to come upon the gentleman physician talking to his gentleman patient, and using the language almost of philosophy, beginning at the beginning of the disease and discoursing about the whole nature of the body, he would burst into a hearty laugh-he would say what most of those who are called doctors always have at their tongue's end:-Foolish fellow, he would say, you are not healing the sick man, but you are educating him; and he does not want to be made a doctor, but to get well.
D: Narrative:
"Excellent. I was running on too fast, Cleinias, and you impinged upon me, and brought me to my senses, reminding me of what, indeed, had occurred to my mind already, that legislation was never yet rightly worked out, as I may say in passing.

"Do you remember the image in which I likened the men for whom laws are now made to slaves who are doctored by slaves? For of this you may be very sure, that if one of those empirical physicians, who practise medicine without science, were to come upon the gentleman physician talking to his gentleman patient, and using the language almost of philosophy, beginning at the beginning of the disease and discoursing about the whole nature of the body, he would burst into a hearty laugh -- he would say what most of those who are called doctors always have at their tongue's end: 'Foolish fellow,' he would say, 'you are not healing the sick man, but you are educating him; and he does not want to be made a doctor, but to get well.'"
D: Refashioned:
"Excellent point. I was going too fast, Cleinias, and you pulled me back to my senses -- reminding me of something I had already been thinking: that legislation has never yet been properly done. Let me explain what I mean.

"Do you remember the analogy I used earlier, comparing the people for whom we now make laws to slaves being treated by slave doctors? Consider this: if one of those empirical physicians -- the kind who practices medicine by rote, without scientific understanding -- happened to overhear a trained physician speaking with his educated patient, discussing the disease from its origins and reasoning about the body's entire nature in almost philosophical language, the empirical doctor would burst out laughing. He would say what most so-called doctors always have ready on their lips: 'You fool, you are not curing the patient -- you are giving him a medical education! He does not want to become a doctor; he wants to get well.'"
D: Elegant: "You are right to slow me down, Cleinias -- legislation has never been done properly. Remember the analogy of the two doctors? If an empirical physician overheard a scientific doctor philosophically explaining a disease's origins and the body's nature to his patient, he would laugh and say: 'You are not healing the man -- you are educating him. He does not want to become a doctor; he wants to get well.'"
D: Summary: The Stranger thanks Cleinias for the correction and revisits his analogy of the two kinds of doctors. The empirical physician who works without theory would mock the philosophical doctor for educating rather than simply curing the patient -- just as practical legislators might mock the approach of explaining laws rather than simply imposing them.
D: Distilled: The Stranger recalls the analogy of two doctors: the empirical practitioner mocks the philosophical physician for educating the patient rather than just curing him, paralleling the tension between simply imposing laws and explaining their rationale.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 'two doctors' analogy is one of the Laws' most celebrated images, first introduced in Book 4 (720a-e). The slave doctor treats slave patients with quick prescriptions and no explanation; the free doctor treats free patients with philosophical discussion of the disease and the body. This maps directly onto two styles of legislation: authoritarian law that merely commands and punishes versus the Laws' innovative approach of combining persuasive preludes with coercive statutes. The passage reflects a key Platonic conviction from the Gorgias (464b-465d): that genuine expertise (techne) requires understanding of causes and principles, while mere empirical practice (empeiria) operates by habit and guesswork. The Stranger's self-deprecating acknowledgment that 'legislation was never yet rightly worked out' reflects the Laws' characteristic intellectual humility -- a marked contrast with the Republic's more confident philosophical architecture. Cleinias' interruption functions dramatically as the kind of patient-physician dialogue the Stranger himself advocates.

D: Original:
And would he not be right?

Perhaps he would; and he might remark upon us that he who discourses about laws, as we are now doing, is giving the citizens education and not laws; that would be rather a telling observation.

Very true.

But we are fortunate.

In what way?

Inasmuch as we are not compelled to give laws, but we may take into consideration every form of government, and ascertain what is best and what is most needful, and how they may both be carried into execution; and we may also, if we please, at this very moment choose what is best, or, if we prefer, what is most necessary-which shall we do?
D: Narrative:
"And would he not be right?" asked Cleinias.

"Perhaps he would," the Stranger replied, "and he might remark upon us that he who discourses about laws, as we are now doing, is giving the citizens education and not laws; that would be rather a telling observation."

"Very true."

"But we are fortunate."

"In what way?"

"Inasmuch as we are not compelled to give laws, but we may take into consideration every form of government, and ascertain what is best and what is most needful, and how they may both be carried into execution; and we may also, if we please, at this very moment choose what is best, or, if we prefer, what is most necessary -- which shall we do?"
D: Refashioned:
"Would the empirical doctor be wrong?" Cleinias asked.

"Perhaps not," the Stranger conceded. "And he could level the same criticism at us: that in discussing laws the way we are doing, we are giving citizens an education rather than actual laws. That would be a fairly devastating observation."

"Very true."

"But we have an advantage."

"What advantage?"

"We are not under pressure to produce laws immediately. We are free to examine every form of government, determine what is ideal and what is practically necessary, and figure out how to achieve both. We can choose right now: shall we pursue what is best, or what is most urgently needed?"
D: Elegant: "Would the empirical doctor be wrong?" asked Cleinias. "Perhaps not," the Stranger conceded -- "someone could say we are educating citizens rather than giving them laws. But we have an advantage: we are not compelled to legislate immediately. We can examine every form of government, determine both what is best and what is most needed, and choose which to pursue."
D: Summary: Cleinias agrees the criticism of the philosophical approach has force. The Stranger acknowledges this but notes their advantage: they are not under pressure to legislate immediately and can freely explore what is ideal versus what is practically necessary.
D: Distilled: The Stranger concedes that their philosophical approach to law might seem impractical, but their freedom from immediate legislative pressure allows them to pursue both the ideal and the necessary.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The distinction between 'what is best' (to ariston) and 'what is most needful' (to anankainotaton) encapsulates the Laws' central methodological tension. The Republic pursues the best without compromise; the Laws recognizes that practical necessity often requires something less than the ideal. The Stranger's framing of their discussion as exploratory rather than immediately binding reflects Plato's awareness that the dialogue format itself -- philosophical conversation among friends on a long walk to the cave of Zeus -- provides a freedom that real legislation does not. This meta-awareness about the conditions of philosophical inquiry echoes Theaetetus 172c-173b, where Socrates contrasts the philosopher's leisure with the orator's urgency in court.

D: Original: There is something ridiculous, Stranger, in our proposing such an alternative as if we were legislators, simply bound under some great necessity which cannot be deferred to the morrow. But we, as I may by grace of Heaven affirm, like, gatherers of stones or beginners of some composite work, may gather a heap of materials, and out of this, at our leisure, select what is suitable for our projected construction. Let us then suppose ourselves to be at leisure, not of necessity building, but rather like men who are partly providing materials, and partly putting them together. And we may truly say that some of our laws, like stones, are already fixed in their places, and others lie at hand.
D: Narrative: "There is something ridiculous, Stranger," Cleinias said, "in our proposing such an alternative as if we were legislators, simply bound under some great necessity which cannot be deferred to the morrow. But we, as I may by grace of Heaven affirm, like gatherers of stones or beginners of some composite work, may gather a heap of materials, and out of this, at our leisure, select what is suitable for our projected construction. Let us then suppose ourselves to be at leisure, not of necessity building, but rather like men who are partly providing materials, and partly putting them together. And we may truly say that some of our laws, like stones, are already fixed in their places, and others lie at hand."
D: Refashioned:
"It would be rather absurd, Stranger," said Cleinias, "to present this choice as though we were actual legislators facing some urgent deadline that cannot wait until tomorrow. We are more like people gathering stones for a construction project, or artisans at the beginning of a composite work. We can collect a large supply of materials and then, at our leisure, select whatever suits the building we have in mind.

"So let us think of ourselves as working without urgency -- not builders compelled to finish, but craftsmen who are partly collecting materials and partly assembling them. Some of our laws, like stones, are already set in place; others are lying ready at hand."
D: Elegant: Cleinias found the forced choice absurd: "We are not legislators under deadline. We are like builders gathering stones -- collecting materials at leisure, selecting what suits our design. Some laws are already in place; others lie ready at hand."
D: Summary: Cleinias rejects the forced choice between ideal and necessary as artificially urgent. He compares their legislative project to builders gathering stones at leisure -- some laws are already established, others await selection and placement.
D: Distilled: Cleinias proposes they work like leisurely builders gathering and selecting stones rather than legislators under urgent pressure.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias. The builder/stone-gatherer metaphor is one of Cleinias' most substantial philosophical contributions to the dialogue. It captures the Laws' self-understanding as a work-in-progress rather than a finished system -- unusual for Plato, whose earlier dialogues tend toward more definitive architectures. The image also resonates with the Greek concept of techne (craft knowledge): just as a master builder selects and fits stones according to a plan, the legislator selects and fits laws according to a vision of the good city. The notion that 'some of our laws are already fixed in their places' acknowledges the cumulative nature of their discussion across nine books. Compare Statesman 278b-279b, where Plato uses weaving as an analogous craft metaphor for statesmanship.

D: Original:
Certainly, in that case, Cleinias, our view of law will be more in accordance with nature. For there is another matter affecting legislators, which I must earnestly entreat you to consider.

What is it?

There are many writings to be found in cities, and among them there, are composed by legislators as well as by other persons.

To be sure.

Shall we give heed rather to the writings of those others-poets and the like, who either in metre or out of metre have recorded their advice about the conduct of life, and not to the writings of legislators? or shall we give heed to them above all?

Yes; to them far above all others.

And ought the legislator alone among writers to withhold his opinion about the beautiful, the good, and the just, and not to teach what they are, and how they are to be pursued by those who intend to be happy?

Certainly not.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly, in that case, Cleinias, our view of law will be more in accordance with nature," the Stranger said. "For there is another matter affecting legislators, which I must earnestly entreat you to consider."

"What is it?"

"There are many writings to be found in cities, and among them there are composed by legislators as well as by other persons."

"To be sure."

"Shall we give heed rather to the writings of those others -- poets and the like, who either in metre or out of metre have recorded their advice about the conduct of life -- and not to the writings of legislators? Or shall we give heed to them above all?"

"Yes; to them far above all others."

"And ought the legislator alone among writers to withhold his opinion about the beautiful, the good, and the just, and not to teach what they are, and how they are to be pursued by those who intend to be happy?"

"Certainly not," Cleinias answered.
D: Refashioned:
"In that case, Cleinias, our approach to law will be more in harmony with nature," the Stranger agreed. "But there is another important point about legislation that I want you to consider carefully."

"What is it?"

"Every city contains a wealth of written works, some by legislators and some by other writers."

"Of course."

"Should we pay more attention to the writings of poets and similar authors who have offered guidance on how to live -- whether in verse or prose -- than to the writings of legislators? Or should legal writings command our highest respect?"

"Legal writings, far above all others."

"Then should the legislator be the only writer who refrains from expressing his views about beauty, goodness, and justice? Should he alone decline to teach what these things are and how those who seek happiness should pursue them?"

"Certainly not," Cleinias replied.
D: Elegant: The Stranger raised a further point: among all the writings found in cities -- by poets, philosophers, and legislators alike -- which deserve the highest regard? Cleinias answered: the writings of legislators, above all. Then, the Stranger pressed, shouldn't the legislator teach about beauty, goodness, and justice, rather than being the only writer who withholds his moral views?
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that if legal writings deserve the highest respect among all a city's literature, the legislator should not be the only writer who refrains from teaching about beauty, goodness, and justice. The legislator has a duty to educate, not merely to command.
D: Distilled: Since legal writings should be the most authoritative texts in a city, the legislator has a duty to teach about beauty, goodness, and justice rather than merely issuing commands.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage articulates a radical claim about the legislator's role: the lawgiver is not merely a technician who establishes rules and penalties but a moral teacher whose writings should be the most authoritative in the city. This directly challenges the status of poets (especially Homer) as the primary moral educators of Greece -- a theme developed extensively in Republic 2-3 and 10. The argument also implicitly justifies the Laws' own methodology of including philosophical preludes alongside legal statutes. By arguing that legislative writing should surpass poetic writing in moral authority, the Stranger positions the Laws itself as the model of what legal literature should be. Compare Ion 531c-532a on poets' claims to moral knowledge, and Protagoras 338e-339a on the interpretation of poetry as moral education.

D: Original: And is it disgraceful for Homer and Tyrtaeus and other poets to lay down evil precepts in their writings respecting life and the pursuits of men, but not so disgraceful for Lycurgus and Solon and others who were legislators as well as writers? Is it not true that of all the writings to be found in cities, those which relate to laws, when you unfold and read them, ought to be by far the noblest and the best? and should not other writings either agree with them, or if they disagree, be deemed ridiculous? We should consider whether the laws of states ought not to have the character of loving and wise parents, rather than of tyrants and masters, who command and threaten, and, after writing their decrees on walls, go their ways; and whether, in discoursing of laws, we should not take the gentler view of them which may or may not be attainable-at any rate, we will show our readiness to entertain such a view, and be prepared to undergo whatever may be the result. And may the result be good, and if God be gracious, it will be good!
D: Narrative:
"And is it disgraceful for Homer and Tyrtaeus and other poets to lay down evil precepts in their writings respecting life and the pursuits of men, but not so disgraceful for Lycurgus and Solon and others who were legislators as well as writers? Is it not true that of all the writings to be found in cities, those which relate to laws, when you unfold and read them, ought to be by far the noblest and the best? And should not other writings either agree with them, or if they disagree, be deemed ridiculous?

"We should consider whether the laws of states ought not to have the character of loving and wise parents, rather than of tyrants and masters, who command and threaten, and, after writing their decrees on walls, go their ways; and whether, in discoursing of laws, we should not take the gentler view of them which may or may not be attainable -- at any rate, we will show our readiness to entertain such a view, and be prepared to undergo whatever may be the result. And may the result be good, and if God be gracious, it will be good!"
D: Refashioned:
"Is it shameful for Homer, Tyrtaeus, and other poets to promote harmful values in their writings about human life, yet somehow not shameful for Lycurgus, Solon, and other legislator-writers to do the same? Surely, of all the writings found in any city, those concerning laws should be by far the noblest and finest when opened and read. All other writings should either harmonize with the laws or, if they contradict them, be considered absurd.

"We should ask whether laws ought to have the character of loving and wise parents rather than tyrants and masters who issue threats, write their decrees on walls, and walk away. Perhaps, in our discussion of laws, we should adopt this gentler vision -- even if it proves difficult or impossible to achieve. At the very least, we will demonstrate our willingness to pursue it and accept whatever consequences follow. May the result be good -- and if God is gracious, it will be."
D: Elegant: "If it is shameful for poets to promote harmful values, it is equally shameful for legislators. Legal writings should be the noblest in any city; all other literature should harmonize with them. Laws should speak like loving, wise parents -- not like tyrants who write decrees on walls and depart. Even if this gentler vision of law proves unattainable, we must try. May God grant a good result."
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that legal texts should be the noblest writings in any city, surpassing poetry in moral authority. Laws should resemble loving parents, not threatening tyrants. He commits to pursuing this gentler vision of legislation even if success is uncertain.
D: Distilled: Laws should be the noblest writings in a city, speaking as wise parents rather than commanding tyrants, and the Stranger commits to pursuing this ideal.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The contrast between laws as 'loving and wise parents' versus 'tyrants and masters' is one of the Laws' most distinctive contributions to political philosophy. It anticipates modern debates about paternalistic versus authoritarian governance. The specific mention of Homer and Tyrtaeus connects to the Republic's famous critique and partial banishment of poets (Republic 595a-608b). Tyrtaeus was a martial poet especially associated with Sparta, making this reference pointed in front of Megillus. Lycurgus (the legendary Spartan lawgiver) and Solon (the Athenian reformer) represent the historical tradition the Stranger both draws on and seeks to surpass. The image of decrees written on walls refers to the actual practice of inscribing laws on stone stelae in Greek cities -- a practice the Stranger finds insufficient without the accompanying education that preludes provide. The prayer closing the passage -- 'if God be gracious, it will be good' -- strikes a characteristic note of pious humility in the Laws.

D: Original:
Excellent; let us do as you say.

Then we will now consider accurately, as we proposed, what relates to robbers of temples, and all kinds of thefts, and offences in general; and we must not be annoyed if, in the course of legislation, we have enacted some things, and have not made up our minds about some others; for as yet we are not legislators, but we may soon be. Let us, if you please, consider these matters.

By all means.

Concerning all things honourable and just, let us then endeavour to ascertain how far we are consistent with ourselves, and how far we are inconsistent, and how far the many, from whom at any rate we should profess a desire to differ, agree and disagree among themselves.

What are the inconsistencies which you observe in us?

I will endeavour to explain. If I am not mistaken, we are all agreed that justice, and just men and things and actions, are all fair, and, if a person were to maintain that just men, even when they are deformed in body, are still perfectly beautiful in respect of the excellent justice of their minds, no one would say that there was any inconsistency in this.

They would be quite right.

Perhaps; but let us consider further, that if all things which are just are fair and honourable, in the term "all" we must include just sufferings which are the correlatives of just actions.

And what is the inference?

The inference is, that a just action in partaking of the just partakes also in the same degree of the fair and honourable.

Certainly.

And must not a suffering which partakes of the just principle be admitted to be in the same degree fair and honourable, if the argument is consistently carried out?

True.

But then if we admit suffering to be just and yet dishonourable, and the term "dishonourable" is applied to justice, will not the just and the honourable disagree?

What do you mean?

A thing not difficult to understand; the laws which have been already enacted would seem to announce principles directly opposed to what we are saying.

To what?
D: Narrative:
"Excellent; let us do as you say," Cleinias agreed.

"Then we will now consider accurately, as we proposed, what relates to robbers of temples, and all kinds of thefts, and offences in general; and we must not be annoyed if, in the course of legislation, we have enacted some things, and have not made up our minds about some others; for as yet we are not legislators, but we may soon be. Let us, if you please, consider these matters."

"By all means."

"Concerning all things honourable and just, let us then endeavour to ascertain how far we are consistent with ourselves, and how far we are inconsistent, and how far the many, from whom at any rate we should profess a desire to differ, agree and disagree among themselves."

"What are the inconsistencies which you observe in us?"

"I will endeavour to explain. If I am not mistaken, we are all agreed that justice, and just men and things and actions, are all fair, and, if a person were to maintain that just men, even when they are deformed in body, are still perfectly beautiful in respect of the excellent justice of their minds, no one would say that there was any inconsistency in this."

"They would be quite right."

"Perhaps; but let us consider further, that if all things which are just are fair and honourable, in the term 'all' we must include just sufferings which are the correlatives of just actions."

"And what is the inference?"

"The inference is, that a just action in partaking of the just partakes also in the same degree of the fair and honourable."

"Certainly."

"And must not a suffering which partakes of the just principle be admitted to be in the same degree fair and honourable, if the argument is consistently carried out?"

"True."

"But then if we admit suffering to be just and yet dishonourable, and the term 'dishonourable' is applied to justice, will not the just and the honourable disagree?"

"What do you mean?"

"A thing not difficult to understand; the laws which have been already enacted would seem to announce principles directly opposed to what we are saying."

"To what?"
D: Refashioned:
"Excellent -- let us proceed as you suggest," Cleinias said.

"Then we will now examine carefully, as planned, the topics of temple robbery, all varieties of theft, and criminal offenses in general. We should not be troubled if we have already legislated on some matters while remaining undecided about others -- after all, we are not yet actual legislators, though we may soon become so. Shall we take up these questions?"

"By all means."

"Regarding everything we call honorable and just, let us try to determine how far we are internally consistent, how far we contradict ourselves, and how far ordinary opinion -- from which we should want to distinguish ourselves -- is itself divided."

"What inconsistencies do you see in our position?"

"Let me explain. We all agree, I think, that justice and just people and just actions are all fine and admirable. If someone claimed that a just person remains perfectly beautiful in the excellence of his soul even when physically deformed, no one would call that inconsistent."

"Correct."

"But consider: if everything just is also fine and honorable, then 'everything' must include just punishments -- the sufferings that correspond to just actions."

"What follows from that?"

"That a just action, in partaking of justice, also partakes equally of the fine and honorable."

"Certainly."

"And must not a suffering that partakes of justice be equally fine and honorable, if we are being logically consistent?"

"True."

"But what if we admit that certain punishments are just yet also shameful? If we apply the label 'shameful' to something just, then justice and honor come apart -- they contradict each other."

"What do you mean?"

"It is not hard to understand. The laws we have already enacted seem to announce principles directly opposed to what we are now saying."

"Opposed to what?"
D: Elegant: Returning to criminal law, the Stranger raises a philosophical puzzle. We agree that all just things are honorable. But just punishments are also just things. If punishment partakes of justice, it should also be honorable. Yet we commonly regard punishments as shameful -- even when they are deserved. If just suffering is shameful, then justice and honor contradict each other. The Stranger claims their own earlier laws embody this very contradiction.
D: Summary: The Stranger identifies a deep inconsistency in their position. If all just things are honorable, then just punishments should also be honorable. But punishments are commonly regarded as shameful, even when deserved. This creates a contradiction between justice and honor that their own earlier legislation embodies.
D: Distilled: If everything just is also honorable, then just punishments should be honorable too -- yet punishments are commonly regarded as shameful, revealing a contradiction between justice and honor.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. This passage initiates one of Book 9's most important philosophical arguments -- the paradox of just punishment. The reasoning is characteristically Socratic in structure: accept a general principle (all just things are fine/honorable), trace its implications (just punishments must also be fine), and reveal the conflict with common belief (punishments seem shameful). This method echoes the Protagoras (349d-351b) and Gorgias (474c-475e), where Socrates forces interlocutors to confront contradictions in their moral intuitions. The specific puzzle -- whether deserved punishment can be both just and dishonorable -- bears directly on the Gorgias' argument that being justly punished is actually beneficial (477a), and on Republic 1's Thrasymachean challenge that justice harms the just person. The Stranger's remark that they should distinguish their views from 'the many' (hoi polloi) echoes the Republic's consistent distrust of popular moral opinion. This passage also prepares the ground for the Laws' central claim about criminal law: that all injustice is involuntary.

D: Original: We had enacted, if I am not mistaken, that the robber of temples, and he who was the enemy of law and order, might justly be put to death, and we were proceeding to make divers other enactments of a similar nature. But we stopped short, because we saw that these sufferings are infinite in number and degree, and that they are, at once, the most just and also the most dishonourable of all sufferings. And if this be true, are not the just and the honourable at one time all the same, and at another time in the most diametrical opposition?
D: Narrative: "We had enacted, if I am not mistaken, that the robber of temples, and he who was the enemy of law and order, might justly be put to death, and we were proceeding to make divers other enactments of a similar nature. But we stopped short, because we saw that these sufferings are infinite in number and degree, and that they are, at once, the most just and also the most dishonourable of all sufferings. And if this be true, are not the just and the honourable at one time all the same, and at another time in the most diametrical opposition?"
D: Refashioned: "If I recall correctly, we enacted that temple robbers and enemies of legal order may justly be put to death, and we were about to make various similar provisions. But we paused, because we realized that legal punishments are infinite in variety and severity, and that they represent simultaneously the most just and the most disgraceful of all human sufferings. If this is true, then justice and honor are sometimes identical and sometimes diametrically opposed."
D: Elegant: "We enacted that temple robbers and subverters of law may justly die. But punishments are both supremely just and supremely shameful. If so, justice and honor sometimes align and sometimes stand in direct opposition."
D: Summary: The Stranger illustrates the paradox with their own legislation: they enacted death for temple robbers and enemies of order, but noticed that punishments are both maximally just and maximally shameful, making justice and honor sometimes identical and sometimes opposed.
D: Distilled: Their own laws illustrate the paradox: legal punishments are simultaneously the most just and the most dishonorable sufferings, putting justice and honor in direct conflict.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage sharpens the philosophical difficulty introduced in 9.15. The paradox is not merely theoretical but embedded in their own concrete legislation. The phrase 'infinite in number and degree' regarding punishments reflects the complexity of matching penalties to crimes -- a practical legislative challenge that the Stranger transforms into a philosophical problem. The tension between justice and honor in punishment anticipates Hegel's later analysis of punishment as the criminal's 'right' -- a concept that tries to dissolve the paradox by making just punishment honorable precisely because it respects the criminal's rational nature. In the Greek context, the problem is acute because both dikaion (just) and kalon (fine/honorable) are fundamental value terms that should, on Socratic principles, converge. Their divergence in the case of punishment threatens the unity of virtue that Plato generally defends (Protagoras 329c-333b).

D: Original:
Such appears to be the case.

In this discordant and inconsistent fashion does the language of the many rend asunder the honourable and just.

Very true, Stranger.

Then now, Cleinias, let us see how far we ourselves are consistent about these matters.

Consistent in what?

I think that I have clearly stated in the former part of the discussion, but if I did not, let me now state-

What?

That all bad men are always involuntarily bad; and from this must proceed to draw a further inference.

What is it?
D: Narrative:
"Such appears to be the case," Cleinias said.

"In this discordant and inconsistent fashion does the language of the many rend asunder the honourable and just."

"Very true, Stranger."

"Then now, Cleinias, let us see how far we ourselves are consistent about these matters."

"Consistent in what?"

"I think that I have clearly stated in the former part of the discussion, but if I did not, let me now state --"

"What?"

"That all bad men are always involuntarily bad; and from this must proceed to draw a further inference."

"What is it?"
D: Refashioned:
"So it appears," Cleinias agreed.

"This is how popular opinion tears apart honor and justice -- in a contradictory and inconsistent way."

"Very true, Stranger."

"So let us examine, Cleinias, how consistent our own position is on these matters."

"Consistent in what respect?"

"I believe I made this clear earlier in our discussion, but if not, let me state it now --"

"State what?"

"That all bad people are bad involuntarily -- always. And from this principle, we must now draw a further conclusion."

"What conclusion?"
D: Elegant: Popular opinion inconsistently separates justice from honor. The Stranger turns to their own consistency and introduces a key premise: all bad people are involuntarily bad. A further inference must now be drawn from this principle.
D: Summary: The Stranger attributes the justice-honor paradox to popular confusion and pivots to examining their own consistency. He introduces the Socratic thesis that all wrongdoers are involuntarily bad, signaling that a further inference will follow.
D: Distilled: Having traced the justice-honor paradox to popular confusion, the Stranger introduces the foundational principle that all wrongdoing is involuntary.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The thesis that 'all bad men are always involuntarily bad' is one of the most characteristically Socratic doctrines in all of Plato. It appears in the Protagoras (345d-e), Meno (77b-78b), and Gorgias (468b-c, 509e), and is grounded in the intellectualist principle that no one willingly does what he believes to be bad for himself -- all wrongdoing stems from ignorance of the true good. The Stranger's phrase 'the language of the many rend asunder the honourable and just' echoes the Republic's consistent theme that popular moral language is confused and unreliable (Republic 479d on the many's confused beliefs about justice and beauty). By introducing involuntary wrongdoing here, the Stranger prepares for a fundamental challenge to standard Greek legal categories, which distinguished voluntary from involuntary offenses as the basis for differential punishment.

D: Original:
That the unjust man may be bad, but that he is bad against his will. Now that an action which is voluntary should be done involuntarily is a contradiction; wherefore he who maintains that injustice is involuntary will deem that the unjust does injustice involuntarily. I too admit that all men do injustice involuntarily, and if any contentious or disputatious person says that men are unjust against their will, and yet that many do injustice willingly, I do not agree with him. But, then, how can I avoid being inconsistent with myself, if you, Cleinias, and you, Megillus, say to me-Well, Stranger, if all this be as you say, how about legislating for the city of the Magnetes-shall we legislate or not-what do you advise? Certainly we will, I should reply. Then will you determine for them what are voluntary and what are involuntary crimes, and shall we make the punishments greater of voluntary errors and crimes and less for the involuntary? or shall we make the punishment of all to be alike, under the idea that there is no such thing as voluntary crime?

Very good, Stranger; and what shall we say in answer to these objections?

That is a very fair question. In the first place, let us-

Do what?
D: Narrative:
"That the unjust man may be bad, but that he is bad against his will. Now that an action which is voluntary should be done involuntarily is a contradiction; wherefore he who maintains that injustice is involuntary will deem that the unjust does injustice involuntarily. I too admit that all men do injustice involuntarily, and if any contentious or disputatious person says that men are unjust against their will, and yet that many do injustice willingly, I do not agree with him.

"But, then, how can I avoid being inconsistent with myself, if you, Cleinias, and you, Megillus, say to me: 'Well, Stranger, if all this be as you say, how about legislating for the city of the Magnetes -- shall we legislate or not -- what do you advise?' Certainly we will, I should reply. 'Then will you determine for them what are voluntary and what are involuntary crimes, and shall we make the punishments greater of voluntary errors and crimes and less for the involuntary? Or shall we make the punishment of all to be alike, under the idea that there is no such thing as voluntary crime?'"

"Very good, Stranger," Cleinias said, "and what shall we say in answer to these objections?"

"That is a very fair question. In the first place, let us --"

"Do what?"
D: Refashioned:
"The unjust person may be wicked, but he is wicked against his own will. Now, saying that a voluntary action is performed involuntarily is a contradiction. Therefore, anyone who holds that injustice is involuntary must conclude that the unjust person commits injustice involuntarily. I myself accept that all people commit injustice involuntarily. If some argumentative person insists that people are unjust against their will yet many commit injustice willingly, I disagree -- that is self-contradictory.

"But this creates a practical dilemma. Suppose you ask me: 'Stranger, if all wrongdoing is involuntary, how should we legislate for Magnesia? Should we still distinguish voluntary from involuntary crimes, punishing the former more severely? Or should we punish all crimes equally, since voluntary crime does not truly exist?' I would answer that we must certainly legislate."

"Very well, Stranger," said Cleinias. "But how do we respond to these objections?"

"An entirely fair question. First, let us --"

"Let us do what?"
D: Elegant: The unjust person is bad involuntarily -- since no one willingly does what he believes harmful to himself. All injustice is involuntary. But this creates a legislative dilemma: if there is no voluntary crime, should all offenses carry equal punishment? Or can we still distinguish degrees of culpability? The Stranger insists legislation remains necessary but must find a new basis for these distinctions.
D: Summary: The Stranger develops the Socratic paradox: if all wrongdoing is involuntary, the traditional legal distinction between voluntary and involuntary crime collapses. This creates a dilemma for their legislative project -- should all crimes be punished equally, or must they find a new basis for distinguishing degrees of culpability?
D: Distilled: If all injustice is involuntary, the standard legal distinction between voluntary and involuntary crime collapses, forcing a search for alternative grounds for differential punishment.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This is one of Book 9's pivotal passages. The Socratic thesis that all wrongdoing is involuntary directly challenges the foundation of Greek criminal law, which (like most legal systems) distinguished voluntary from involuntary offenses. Athenian law distinguished phonos hekousios (voluntary homicide) from phonos akousios (involuntary homicide), with different courts (Areopagus vs. Palladion) and different penalties. The Stranger's challenge is not merely philosophical -- it threatens to undermine an entire legal tradition. The mention of 'the city of the Magnetes' refers to the colony they are designing laws for, named after the historical Magnesia, a city in Crete. The Stranger's self-aware presentation of the difficulty -- anticipating his interlocutors' practical objections -- shows the Laws' characteristic method of taking practical concerns as seriously as philosophical principles. The resolution will come through a distinction between 'harm' and 'injustice' that preserves differential treatment while maintaining the involuntary nature of all wrongdoing.

D: Original: Let us remember what has been well said by us already, that our ideas of justice are in the highest degree confused and contradictory. Bearing this in mind, let us proceed to ask ourselves once more whether we have discovered a way out of the difficulty. Have we ever determined in what respect these two classes of actions differ from one another? For in all states and by all legislators whatsoever, two kinds of actions have been distinguished-the one, voluntary, the other, involuntary; and they have legislated about them accordingly. But shall this new word of ours, like an oracle of God, be only spoken, and get away without giving any explanation or verification of itself? How can a word not understood be the basis of legislation? Impossible. Before proceeding to legislate, then, we must prove that they are two, and what is the difference between them, that when we impose the penalty upon either, every one may understand our proposal, and be able in some way to judge whether the penalty is fitly or unfitly inflicted.
D: Narrative:
"Let us remember what has been well said by us already, that our ideas of justice are in the highest degree confused and contradictory. Bearing this in mind, let us proceed to ask ourselves once more whether we have discovered a way out of the difficulty. Have we ever determined in what respect these two classes of actions differ from one another?

"For in all states and by all legislators whatsoever, two kinds of actions have been distinguished -- the one, voluntary, the other, involuntary; and they have legislated about them accordingly. But shall this new word of ours, like an oracle of God, be only spoken, and get away without giving any explanation or verification of itself? How can a word not understood be the basis of legislation? Impossible.

"Before proceeding to legislate, then, we must prove that they are two, and what is the difference between them, that when we impose the penalty upon either, every one may understand our proposal, and be able in some way to judge whether the penalty is fitly or unfitly inflicted."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us recall our own conclusion: that our ideas about justice are deeply confused and contradictory. With that in mind, let us ask again whether we have found any escape from the difficulty. Have we ever properly defined how these two categories of action -- voluntary and involuntary -- actually differ from each other?

"Every state and every legislator in history has distinguished between voluntary and involuntary acts and legislated accordingly. But should our new principle -- that all wrongdoing is involuntary -- simply be proclaimed like a divine oracle and left unexplained and unverified? A principle that no one understands cannot serve as the foundation for legislation. That is impossible.

"Therefore, before we legislate further, we must demonstrate that there really are two distinct categories and clarify what differentiates them. Only then can citizens understand our laws and evaluate whether the penalties we impose are appropriate or not."
D: Elegant: "Our ideas of justice are confused. Every legal system distinguishes voluntary from involuntary actions, but we must not proclaim our principle like an unexplained oracle. Before legislating, we must define the real difference between these categories so that citizens can understand and evaluate the penalties we impose."
D: Summary: The Stranger insists that the distinction between voluntary and involuntary action, universally recognized in legislation, must be rigorously defined rather than simply asserted. A principle that citizens cannot understand cannot serve as the foundation for law.
D: Distilled: Before legislating, they must define the real difference between voluntary and involuntary action rather than treating their principle as an unexplained oracle.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The comparison of an unexamined legal principle to 'an oracle of God' that is 'only spoken' without explanation encapsulates the Laws' entire philosophy of legislation. Oracles deliver pronouncements without reasons; good laws must provide both commands and justifications. This distinction maps onto the prelude/statute structure that is the Laws' signature innovation. The passage also reflects the Socratic commitment to logos (reasoned account) over mere doxa (opinion) -- the same commitment that drives the method of the earlier dialogues, particularly the demand in the Meno (98a) that true belief be 'tied down' by reasoning to become knowledge. The observation that all legislators have distinguished voluntary from involuntary acts is historically accurate: Draco's homicide law (ca. 621 BCE), the oldest surviving Athenian legislation, made this distinction, and it was fundamental to both Athenian and Spartan legal practice.

D: Original:
I agree with you, Stranger; for one of two things is certain: either we must not say that all unjust acts are involuntary, or we must show the meaning and truth of this statement.

Of these two alternatives, the one is quite intolerable-not to speak what I believe to be the truth would be to me unlawful and unholy. But if acts of injustice cannot be divided into voluntary and involuntary, I must endeavour to find some other distinction between them.

Very true, Stranger; there cannot be two opinions among us upon that point.

Reflect, then; there are hurts of various kinds done by the citizens to one another in the intercourse of life, affording plentiful examples both of the voluntary and involuntary.

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"I agree with you, Stranger," Cleinias said, "for one of two things is certain: either we must not say that all unjust acts are involuntary, or we must show the meaning and truth of this statement."

"Of these two alternatives, the one is quite intolerable," the Stranger replied. "Not to speak what I believe to be the truth would be to me unlawful and unholy. But if acts of injustice cannot be divided into voluntary and involuntary, I must endeavour to find some other distinction between them."

"Very true, Stranger; there cannot be two opinions among us upon that point."

"Reflect, then; there are hurts of various kinds done by the citizens to one another in the intercourse of life, affording plentiful examples both of the voluntary and involuntary."

"Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"I agree, Stranger," said Cleinias. "We face a clear choice: either we stop claiming that all injustice is involuntary, or we must explain what that claim truly means."

"Of those two options, the first is unacceptable to me," the Stranger replied. "Failing to speak what I believe to be true would be both impious and wrong. But since I cannot divide injustice into voluntary and involuntary categories, I must find some other way to distinguish between different kinds of wrongdoing."

"There can be no disagreement among us on that, Stranger."

"Then consider this: citizens inflict many kinds of harm on one another in the course of daily life. Among these harms, there are clearly both voluntary and involuntary examples."

"Certainly."
D: Elegant: Cleinias poses the dilemma: either abandon the claim that all injustice is involuntary, or explain it. The Stranger refuses to abandon what he believes true but acknowledges he must find a new distinction among wrongs if the voluntary-involuntary division fails. He redirects attention to 'harms' -- where the voluntary-involuntary distinction clearly does apply.
D: Summary: Cleinias sharpens the dilemma, and the Stranger refuses to abandon his conviction that all injustice is involuntary. Instead, he pivots to a new distinction: while injustice may all be involuntary, harms can be either voluntary or involuntary, and this becomes the new basis for legislation.
D: Distilled: Rather than abandon the thesis that all injustice is involuntary, the Stranger pivots to distinguishing voluntary from involuntary harms as the new foundation for criminal law.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The Stranger's declaration that 'not to speak what I believe to be the truth would be to me unlawful and unholy' is a striking echo of Socrates in the Apology (29d): 'I shall obey God rather than you.' It reveals the Stranger as sharing Socrates' fundamental commitment to intellectual honesty, even at the cost of philosophical difficulty. The crucial move in this passage is the shift from 'injustice' (adikia) to 'harms' (blabai). While all injustice may be involuntary (because no one knowingly chooses what is truly bad for himself), harms can be clearly voluntary or involuntary. This distinction will become the basis for the Laws' innovative penal theory: the law should compensate for harms (where voluntariness matters) and cure injustice (which is always a disease of the soul, hence involuntary). This parallels the Gorgias' treatment of punishment as therapy (477a-479e) and represents one of Plato's most sophisticated integrations of philosophical ethics with practical jurisprudence.

D: Original:
I would not have any one suppose that all these hurts are injuries, and that these injuries are of two kinds-one, voluntary, and the other, involuntary; for the involuntary hurts of all men are quite as many and as great as the voluntary? And please to consider whether I am right or quite wrong in what I am going to say; for I deny, Cleinias and Megillus, that he who harms another involuntarily does him an injury involuntarily, nor should I legislate about such an act under the idea that I am legislating for an involuntary injury. But I should rather say that such a hurt, whether great or small, is not an injury at all; and, on the other hand, if I am right, when a benefit is wrongly conferred, the author of the benefit may often be said to injure. For I maintain, O my friends, that the mere giving or taking away of anything is not to be described either as just or unjust; but the legislator has to consider whether mankind do good or harm to one another out of a just principle and intention. On the distinction between injustice and hurt he must fix his eye; and when there is hurt, he must, as far as he can, make the hurt good by law, and save that which is ruined, and raise up that which is fallen, and make that which is dead or wounded whole. And when compensation has been given for injustice, the law must always seek to win over the doers and sufferers of the several hurts from feelings of enmity to those of friendship.

Very good.

Then as to unjust hurts (and gains also, supposing the injustice to bring gain), of these we may heal as many as are capable of being healed, regarding them as diseases of the soul; and the cure of injustice will take the following direction.

What direction?
D: Narrative:
"I would not have any one suppose that all these hurts are injuries, and that these injuries are of two kinds -- one, voluntary, and the other, involuntary; for the involuntary hurts of all men are quite as many and as great as the voluntary.

"And please to consider whether I am right or quite wrong in what I am going to say; for I deny, Cleinias and Megillus, that he who harms another involuntarily does him an injury involuntarily, nor should I legislate about such an act under the idea that I am legislating for an involuntary injury. But I should rather say that such a hurt, whether great or small, is not an injury at all; and, on the other hand, if I am right, when a benefit is wrongly conferred, the author of the benefit may often be said to injure.
