Compare Euripides' Medea, which dramatizes the horror of a parent killing children, and the Oresteia, which centers on the cycle of family murder and its resolution. The provision for spousal killing is gender-neutral -- both husband killing wife and wife killing husband receive identical treatment, which is noteworthy for the period.

D: Original: And he who is disobedient shall be justly liable to the law concerning impiety, which relates to these matters. If any one is so violent in his passion against his parents, that in the madness of his anger he dares to kill one of them, if the murdered person before dying freely forgives the murderer, let him undergo the purification which is assigned to those who have been guilty of involuntary homicide, and do as they do, and he shall be pure. But if he be not acquitted, the perpetrator of such a deed shall be amenable to many laws;-he shall be amenable to the extreme punishments for assault, and impiety, and robbing of temples, for he has robbed his parent of life; and if a man could be slain more than once, most justly would he who in a fit of passion has slain father or mother, undergo many deaths. How can he, whom, alone of all men, even in defence of his life, and when about to suffer death at the hands of his parents, no law will allow to kill his father or his mother who are the authors of his being, and whom the legislator will command to endure any extremity rather than do this-how can he, I say, lawfully receive any other punishment? Let death then be the appointed punishment of him who in a fit of passion slays his father or his mother.
D: Narrative:
"And he who is disobedient shall be justly liable to the law concerning impiety, which relates to these matters.

"If any one is so violent in his passion against his parents, that in the madness of his anger he dares to kill one of them, if the murdered person before dying freely forgives the murderer, let him undergo the purification which is assigned to those who have been guilty of involuntary homicide, and do as they do, and he shall be pure.

"But if he be not acquitted, the perpetrator of such a deed shall be amenable to many laws -- he shall be amenable to the extreme punishments for assault, and impiety, and robbing of temples, for he has robbed his parent of life; and if a man could be slain more than once, most justly would he who in a fit of passion has slain father or mother undergo many deaths.

"How can he, whom, alone of all men, even in defence of his life, and when about to suffer death at the hands of his parents, no law will allow to kill his father or his mother who are the authors of his being, and whom the legislator will command to endure any extremity rather than do this -- how can he, I say, lawfully receive any other punishment? Let death then be the appointed punishment of him who in a fit of passion slays his father or his mother."
D: Refashioned:
"Anyone who violates these family restrictions shall rightly face prosecution under the impiety laws.

"If someone is so overcome by rage against his parents that in the madness of his anger he dares to kill one of them -- and if the dying parent, before death, freely forgives the murderer -- then the killer shall undergo the purification prescribed for involuntary homicide and shall follow those procedures. He will then be considered pure.

"But if the dying parent does not forgive, the killer is subject to multiple laws simultaneously -- the harshest penalties for assault, impiety, and temple robbery, since he has robbed his parent of the most sacred possession: life itself. If it were possible to kill a man more than once, the parricide would justly deserve multiple deaths.

"Consider: this is the one person in all of humanity whom no law will permit to kill his parents even in self-defense, even when facing death at their hands. The legislator commands him to endure any extremity rather than raise his hand against the authors of his existence. How, then, can he lawfully receive any punishment less than death? Death must be the sentence for anyone who kills a father or mother in a fit of passion."
D: Elegant: "If a dying parent forgives their killer, the parricide undergoes involuntary homicide purification and is clean. If not, he faces the combined penalties for assault, impiety, and temple robbery -- and would deserve multiple deaths if that were possible. Since no law permits killing one's parents even in self-defense, no punishment short of death can be appropriate for parricide."
D: Summary: The Stranger addresses parricide. If the dying parent forgives, the killer undergoes involuntary homicide purification. Without forgiveness, the parricide faces the combined weight of laws against assault, impiety, and temple robbery. Since no one may kill a parent even in self-defense, death is the only adequate punishment.
D: Distilled: Parricide is punishable by death unless the dying parent forgives the killer, since no law permits killing one's parents even in self-defense.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The treatment of parricide as the most extreme crime reflects a near-universal moral conviction in ancient cultures. The striking provision for deathbed forgiveness by the dying parent introduces a remarkable element of victim autonomy into criminal law -- the most severe crime can be mitigated by the victim's own choice to forgive. The argument from the prohibition of self-defense against parents is logically elegant: if you may not kill your parent even to save your own life, then killing a parent under any lesser provocation must be punishable by death. The comparison to 'robbing of temples' (since the parent has been 'robbed' of life) treats life as sacred in a quasi-religious sense. The wish that the parricide 'could be slain more than once' expresses the perceived inadequacy of any finite punishment for this crime. Compare Sophocles' Oedipus at Colonus, where Oedipus argues that his parricide was involuntary and therefore not truly guilty -- exactly the kind of case the Stranger's forgiveness provision might address.

D: Original:
But if brother kills brother in a civil broil, or under other like circumstances, if the other has begun, and he only defends himself, let him be free from guilt, as he would be if he had slain an enemy; and the same rule will apply if a citizen kill a citizen, or a stranger a stranger. Or if a stranger kill a citizen or a citizen a stranger in self-defence, let him be free from guilt in like manner; and so in the case of a slave who has killed a slave; but if a slave have killed a freeman in self-defence, let him be subject to the same law as he who has killed a father; and let the law about the remission of penalties in the case of parricide apply equally to every other remission. Whenever any sufferer of his own accord remits the guilt of homicide to another, under the idea that his act was involuntary, let the perpetrator of the deed undergo a purification and remain in exile for a year, according to law.

Enough has been said of murders violent and involuntary and committed in passion: we have now to speak of voluntary crimes done with injustice of every kind and with premeditation, through the influence of pleasures, and desires, and jealousies.

Very good.
D: Narrative:
"But if brother kills brother in a civil broil, or under other like circumstances, if the other has begun, and he only defends himself, let him be free from guilt, as he would be if he had slain an enemy; and the same rule will apply if a citizen kill a citizen, or a stranger a stranger. Or if a stranger kill a citizen or a citizen a stranger in self-defence, let him be free from guilt in like manner; and so in the case of a slave who has killed a slave.

"But if a slave have killed a freeman in self-defence, let him be subject to the same law as he who has killed a father; and let the law about the remission of penalties in the case of parricide apply equally to every other remission. Whenever any sufferer of his own accord remits the guilt of homicide to another, under the idea that his act was involuntary, let the perpetrator of the deed undergo a purification and remain in exile for a year, according to law."

"Enough has been said of murders violent and involuntary and committed in passion: we have now to speak of voluntary crimes done with injustice of every kind and with premeditation, through the influence of pleasures, and desires, and jealousies."

"Very good."
D: Refashioned:
"If a brother kills a brother during civil unrest or similar circumstances, and the victim started the conflict while the killer acted only in self-defense, the killer shall be free from guilt -- just as if he had killed an enemy in battle. The same rule applies when a citizen kills a citizen, or a stranger kills a stranger, in self-defense. Similarly, if a stranger kills a citizen, or a citizen kills a stranger, in self-defense, both shall be guiltless. And the same holds for a slave who kills another slave.

"However, if a slave kills a free person in self-defense, he shall be subject to the same law that governs parricide. The provision for forgiveness that applies to parricide shall likewise apply to all other cases where remission is possible. Whenever a dying victim voluntarily forgives his killer, believing the act to have been involuntary, the killer must still undergo purification and remain in exile for one year as required by law."

"We have now said enough about violent, involuntary, and passionate homicides. It is time to discuss voluntary crimes committed with full injustice and premeditation, driven by pleasures, desires, and jealousies."

"Very good."
D: Elegant: "Self-defense killing in civil conflict, between citizens, between strangers, or across status lines, is guiltless -- except when a slave kills a free person in self-defense, which is treated as parricide. Forgiveness by the dying victim reduces any homicide to purification and one year of exile. This concludes violent, involuntary, and passionate homicide. We now turn to voluntary, premeditated crimes driven by pleasure, desire, and jealousy."
D: Summary: The Stranger establishes self-defense as a complete justification for homicide across most status categories, except for slaves killing free persons. Deathbed forgiveness by the victim reduces any homicide to purification and one year of exile. He then transitions to voluntary, premeditated crimes.
D: Distilled: Self-defense justifies killing in most cases except slaves killing free persons; deathbed forgiveness reduces any homicide to purification plus one year of exile; the discussion now shifts to premeditated voluntary crime.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The self-defense provisions are notably egalitarian for their time -- citizens, strangers, and even slaves may kill in self-defense without guilt, with the significant exception that a slave may never justifiably kill a free person. This exception reflects the deep status inequality of the slave system: the slave's life is valued less than the free person's under any circumstances. The parallel between a slave killing a free person and parricide suggests that the slave-master relationship is conceived as analogous to the parent-child relationship -- a hierarchical bond so fundamental that it cannot be overridden even by self-preservation. The transition to voluntary, premeditated crime marks a major structural pivot in Book 9: from homicides that are involuntary or committed in passion (which the harm-injustice distinction and rehabilitation can address) to homicides driven by the soul's deliberate submission to pleasure, desire, and jealousy -- the domain of genuine, if still in some sense involuntary, injustice.

D: Original: Let us first speak, as far as we are able, of their various kinds. The greatest cause of them is lust, which gets the mastery of the soul maddened by desire; and this is most commonly found to exist where the passion reigns which is strongest and most prevalent among mass of mankind: I mean where the power of wealth breeds endless desires of never-to-be-satisfied acquisition, originating in natural disposition, and a miserable want of education. Of this want of education, the false praise of wealth which is bruited about both among Hellenes and barbarians is the cause; they deem that to be the first of goods which in reality is only the third. And in this way they wrong both posterity and themselves, for nothing can be nobler and better than that the truth about wealth should be spoken in all states-namely, that riches are for the sake of the body, as the body is for the sake of the soul. They are good, and wealth is intended by nature to be for the sake of them, and is therefore inferior to them both, and third in order of excellence. This argument teaches us that he who would be happy ought not to seek to be rich, or rather he should seek to be rich justly and temperately, and then there would be no murders in states requiring to be purged away by other murders.
D: Narrative:
"Let us first speak, as far as we are able, of their various kinds. The greatest cause of them is lust, which gets the mastery of the soul maddened by desire; and this is most commonly found to exist where the passion reigns which is strongest and most prevalent among the mass of mankind: I mean where the power of wealth breeds endless desires of never-to-be-satisfied acquisition, originating in natural disposition, and a miserable want of education.

"Of this want of education, the false praise of wealth which is bruited about both among Hellenes and barbarians is the cause; they deem that to be the first of goods which in reality is only the third. And in this way they wrong both posterity and themselves, for nothing can be nobler and better than that the truth about wealth should be spoken in all states -- namely, that riches are for the sake of the body, as the body is for the sake of the soul. They are good, and wealth is intended by nature to be for the sake of them, and is therefore inferior to them both, and third in order of excellence.

"This argument teaches us that he who would be happy ought not to seek to be rich, or rather he should seek to be rich justly and temperately, and then there would be no murders in states requiring to be purged away by other murders."
D: Refashioned:
"Let us try to identify the different types of voluntary crime. The greatest cause is greed -- the lust for acquisition that takes command of a soul maddened by desire. This is most prevalent where the strongest and most widespread human passion holds sway: the power of wealth, which breeds insatiable desires for endless accumulation. These desires originate partly from natural temperament but mainly from a deplorable lack of education.

"The root of this educational failure is the false glorification of wealth that prevails among Greeks and non-Greeks alike. People regard wealth as the highest good when in reality it is only the third. This error harms both the present generation and their descendants. The truth that every state should teach is this: wealth exists for the sake of the body, and the body exists for the sake of the soul. Both body and soul are genuine goods, and since wealth exists to serve them, it is naturally inferior to both and ranks third in the order of human goods.

"The lesson is clear: the person who seeks happiness should not pursue wealth as his primary goal. Rather, he should pursue wealth only through just and temperate means. If this principle prevailed, states would not suffer murders that must be purged by yet more murders."
D: Elegant: "The chief cause of voluntary crime is greed -- insatiable desire for wealth, born from poor education and the universal false belief that wealth is the highest good. In truth, wealth ranks third: the soul is first, the body second, and wealth serves both. If people pursued wealth only justly and temperately, states would not require murders to purge earlier murders."
D: Summary: The Stranger identifies greed as the primary cause of voluntary crime. The root problem is educational: both Greeks and non-Greeks falsely rank wealth as the highest good when it is actually third, behind the soul and the body. If people pursued wealth justly and temperately, violent crime would largely cease.
D: Distilled: Greed is the chief cause of voluntary crime, rooted in the universal educational failure of treating wealth as the highest good rather than third after soul and body.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The hierarchy of goods -- soul first, body second, wealth third -- is a foundational Platonic doctrine, stated explicitly in Laws 1 (631b-d) and implicit throughout the Republic (especially 580d-583a, where the three classes of pleasure correspond to soul, honor, and money). The identification of poor education (apaideusia) as the root cause of crime connects directly to the Republic's insistence that justice begins with education (376e-412b). The phrase 'bruited about both among Hellenes and barbarians' universalizes the critique -- this is not a Greek cultural failing but a human one. The closing observation about murders 'requiring to be purged away by other murders' is a powerful image of violence as self-perpetuating cycle, recalling the curse on the house of Atreus and anticipating modern observations about cycles of violence. Compare Gorgias 466c-468e, where Socrates argues that tyrants and the wealthy have the least genuine power because they do what seems best rather than what truly is best.

D: Original: But now, as I said at first, avarice is the chiefest cause and source of the worst trials for voluntary homicide. A second cause is ambition: this creates jealousies, which are troublesome companions, above all to the jealous man himself, and in a less degree to the chiefs of the state. And a third cause is cowardly and unjust fear, which has been the occasion of many murders. When a man is doing or has done something which he desires that no one should know him to be doing or to have done, he will take the life of those who are likely to inform of such things, if he have no other means of getting rid of them. Let this be said as a prelude concerning crimes of violence in general; and I must not omit to mention a tradition which is firmly believed by many, and has been received by them from those who are learned in the mysteries: they say that such deeds will be punished in the world below, and also that when the perpetrators return to this world they will pay the natural penalty which is due to the sufferer, and end their lives in like manner by the hand of another. If he who is about to commit murder believes this, and is made by the mere prelude to dread such a penalty, there is no need to proceed with the proclamation of the law. But if he will not listen, let the following law be declared and registered against him:
D: Narrative:
"But now, as I said at first, avarice is the chiefest cause and source of the worst trials for voluntary homicide. A second cause is ambition: this creates jealousies, which are troublesome companions, above all to the jealous man himself, and in a less degree to the chiefs of the state. And a third cause is cowardly and unjust fear, which has been the occasion of many murders. When a man is doing or has done something which he desires that no one should know him to be doing or to have done, he will take the life of those who are likely to inform of such things, if he have no other means of getting rid of them.

"Let this be said as a prelude concerning crimes of violence in general; and I must not omit to mention a tradition which is firmly believed by many, and has been received by them from those who are learned in the mysteries: they say that such deeds will be punished in the world below, and also that when the perpetrators return to this world they will pay the natural penalty which is due to the sufferer, and end their lives in like manner by the hand of another.

"If he who is about to commit murder believes this, and is made by the mere prelude to dread such a penalty, there is no need to proceed with the proclamation of the law. But if he will not listen, let the following law be declared and registered against him:"
D: Refashioned:
"As I said initially, avarice is the primary cause and origin of the most terrible voluntary homicide cases. The second cause is political ambition, which breeds jealousies -- painful primarily to the jealous person himself, but also damaging to the state's leaders. The third cause is cowardly and unjust fear: when someone is engaged in wrongdoing or has already committed it and wants no one to find out, he will kill potential witnesses if he sees no other way to silence them.

"Let this serve as the prelude to our laws about crimes of violence in general. I should also mention a tradition widely and firmly believed, received from those learned in the religious mysteries: that murderers will be punished in the afterlife, and that when they are reborn into this world, they will suffer the same fate they inflicted -- dying by another's hand in the same manner they killed.

"If the would-be murderer believes this tradition and is deterred by the prelude alone, there is no need to proceed to the formal statement of the law. But if he will not listen, let the following law be proclaimed and inscribed against him:"
D: Elegant: "The three causes of voluntary homicide: first, avarice; second, political ambition breeding destructive jealousy; third, cowardly fear that drives people to kill witnesses. As a prelude, the tradition from the mysteries should also be invoked: murderers are punished in the afterlife and, upon rebirth, die by the same means they used to kill. If this deters, the law need not speak. If not, the formal statute follows."
D: Summary: The Stranger identifies three causes of voluntary homicide: avarice, political ambition breeding jealousy, and the cowardly fear that leads to killing witnesses. He invokes the mystery tradition of afterlife punishment and karmic retribution as a prelude. If this religious deterrent fails, the formal law must be declared.
D: Distilled: Three causes drive voluntary homicide -- avarice, ambitious jealousy, and cowardly fear of exposure -- and the prelude invokes afterlife retribution before stating the law itself.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The three causes of voluntary homicide -- greed, ambition, and fear -- form a concise taxonomy of criminal motivation that remains influential. The third cause (killing to conceal other crimes) is particularly acute and anticipates what modern criminology calls 'instrumental violence.' The appeal to mystery traditions about afterlife punishment and reincarnation reflects the Laws' consistent strategy of incorporating religious belief into legal persuasion. The specific doctrine mentioned -- that murderers are reborn to die in the same manner -- appears to draw on Orphic and Pythagorean teachings about metempsychosis (transmigration of souls), which also influenced the afterlife myths in the Gorgias (523a-527a), Phaedo (113d-114c), and Republic (614b-621d). The pragmatic structure -- prelude first, law only if needed -- enacts the Laws' central principle that persuasion should precede compulsion. The Stranger is not merely asserting the truth of these traditions but using them instrumentally as part of the legislative prelude's persuasive apparatus.

D: Original: Whoever shall wrongfully and of design slay with his own hand any of his kinsmen, shall in the first place be deprived of legal privileges; and he shall not pollute the temples, or the agora, or the harbours, or any other place of meeting, whether he is forbidden of men or not; for the law, which represents the whole state, forbids him, and always is and will be in the attitude of forbidding him. And if a cousin or nearer relative of the deceased, whether on the male or female side, does not prosecute the homicide when he ought, and have him proclaimed an outlaw, he shall in the first place be involved in the pollution, and incur the hatred of the Gods, even as the curse of the law stirs up the voices of men against him; and in the second place he shall be liable to be prosecuted by any one who is willing to inflict retribution on behalf of the dead. And he who would avenge a murder shall observe all the precautionary ceremonies of lavation, and any others which the God commands in cases of this kind. Let him have proclamation made, and then go forth and compel the perpetrator to suffer the execution of justice according to the law. Now the legislator may easily show that these things must be accomplished by prayers and sacrifices to certain Gods, who are concerned with the prevention of murders in states.
D: Narrative:
"Whoever shall wrongfully and of design slay with his own hand any of his kinsmen, shall in the first place be deprived of legal privileges; and he shall not pollute the temples, or the agora, or the harbours, or any other place of meeting, whether he is forbidden of men or not; for the law, which represents the whole state, forbids him, and always is and will be in the attitude of forbidding him.

"And if a cousin or nearer relative of the deceased, whether on the male or female side, does not prosecute the homicide when he ought, and have him proclaimed an outlaw, he shall in the first place be involved in the pollution, and incur the hatred of the Gods, even as the curse of the law stirs up the voices of men against him; and in the second place he shall be liable to be prosecuted by any one who is willing to inflict retribution on behalf of the dead.

"And he who would avenge a murder shall observe all the precautionary ceremonies of lavation, and any others which the God commands in cases of this kind. Let him have proclamation made, and then go forth and compel the perpetrator to suffer the execution of justice according to the law.

"Now the legislator may easily show that these things must be accomplished by prayers and sacrifices to certain Gods, who are concerned with the prevention of murders in states."
D: Refashioned:
"Whoever deliberately and wrongfully kills a kinsman with his own hand shall first be stripped of all legal rights. He shall not pollute the temples, the marketplace, the harbors, or any other public gathering place -- regardless of whether any person specifically forbids him entry. The law itself, representing the entire state, forbids him and will always forbid him.

"If a cousin or closer relative of the victim, on either the mother's or father's side, fails to prosecute the killer and have him declared an outlaw, that relative shall first incur the pollution himself and attract the hatred of the gods -- the law's curse will turn public opinion against him. Second, he will become liable to prosecution by any citizen willing to seek justice on behalf of the dead.

"Anyone who undertakes to avenge a murder must observe all required purification rituals, including ceremonial washing and whatever else the god prescribes for such cases. He shall have the formal proclamation made, then proceed to compel the murderer to face the law's execution of justice.

"The legislator can readily demonstrate that these proceedings must be accompanied by prayers and sacrifices to those specific gods who are concerned with preventing murder in the state."
D: Elegant: "A deliberate kinsman-killer loses all legal rights and is permanently barred from public spaces by the law itself. Relatives who fail to prosecute absorb the pollution and face prosecution by any citizen. Those who avenge the murder must observe purification rites and formal proclamation before compelling the killer to face justice. The entire process requires prayers and sacrifices to the gods who prevent murder."
D: Summary: The Stranger enacts the law for deliberate kinsman-murder: the killer loses all rights and is barred from public spaces. Relatives who fail to prosecute absorb the pollution. Avengers must observe purification rites before pursuing justice. The proceedings are framed within a religious context of prayer and sacrifice.
D: Distilled: Deliberate kinsman-killers are stripped of legal rights and banned from all public spaces; negligent relatives absorb the pollution; and prosecution must follow prescribed religious purification ceremonies.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The statement that the law 'always is and will be in the attitude of forbidding him' -- even without specific human enforcement -- personifies the law as a moral force independent of any particular enforcer. This reflects the Laws' broader commitment to the rule of law as superior to the rule of individual persons (compare 9.49 on the superiority of law to personal rule). The pollution mechanism that transfers guilt to negligent relatives creates a powerful incentive for prosecution, leveraging family honor and religious fear to ensure enforcement. The requirement for purification before prosecution protects the avenger from contamination by the violence he is about to oversee -- a recognition that even justified involvement with murder carries spiritual risk. The gods 'concerned with the prevention of murders' likely include Apollo (the god of purification, whose Delphic oracle prescribes purification rites) and Zeus Meilichios (Zeus of propitiation, worshipped in connection with blood guilt). These provisions closely parallel historical Athenian homicide law, which required formal proclamation (prorrhesis) by the victim's family.

D: Original: But who these Gods are, and what should be the true manner of instituting such trials with due regard to religion, the guardians of the law, aided by the interpreters, and the prophets, and the God, shall determine, and when they have determined let them carry on the prosecution at law. The cause shall have the same judges who are appointed to decide in the case of those who plunder temples. Let him who is convicted be punished with death, and let him not be buried in the country of the murdered man, for this would be shameless as well as impious. But if he fly and will not stand his trial, let him fly for ever; or, if he set foot anywhere on any part of the murdered man's country, let any relation of the deceased, or any other citizen who may first happen to meet with him, kill him with impunity, or bind and deliver him to those among the judges of the case who are magistrates, that they may put him to death. And let the prosecutor demand surety of him whom he prosecutes; three sureties sufficient in the opinion of the magistrates who try the cause shall be provided by him, and they shall undertake to produce him at the trial. But if he be unwilling or unable to provide sureties, then the magistrates shall take him and keep him in bonds, and produce him at the day of trial.
D: Narrative:
"But who these Gods are, and what should be the true manner of instituting such trials with due regard to religion, the guardians of the law, aided by the interpreters, and the prophets, and the God, shall determine, and when they have determined let them carry on the prosecution at law.

"The cause shall have the same judges who are appointed to decide in the case of those who plunder temples. Let him who is convicted be punished with death, and let him not be buried in the country of the murdered man, for this would be shameless as well as impious.

"But if he fly and will not stand his trial, let him fly for ever; or, if he set foot anywhere on any part of the murdered man's country, let any relation of the deceased, or any other citizen who may first happen to meet with him, kill him with impunity, or bind and deliver him to those among the judges of the case who are magistrates, that they may put him to death.

"And let the prosecutor demand surety of him whom he prosecutes; three sureties sufficient in the opinion of the magistrates who try the cause shall be provided by him, and they shall undertake to produce him at the trial. But if he be unwilling or unable to provide sureties, then the magistrates shall take him and keep him in bonds, and produce him at the day of trial."
D: Refashioned:
"Which gods are relevant and how such trials should be properly conducted with due religious observance shall be determined by the guardians of the law, working together with the religious interpreters, the prophets, and the god himself. Once these procedures are established, prosecution shall proceed accordingly.

"These cases shall be tried by the same judges who handle temple robbery. Anyone convicted shall be executed, and his body shall not be buried anywhere in the murdered person's country -- that would be both shameless and impious.

"If the accused flees and refuses to stand trial, he shall remain a fugitive forever. Should he ever set foot on any part of the victim's country, any relative of the deceased -- or any citizen who happens to encounter him first -- may kill him without penalty, or apprehend him and deliver him to the judicial magistrates for execution.

"The prosecutor shall require the accused to provide three sureties, deemed sufficient by the presiding magistrates, who will guarantee his appearance at trial. If the accused is unwilling or unable to provide sureties, the magistrates shall take him into custody and hold him in bonds until the day of trial."
D: Elegant: "Religious authorities and the guardians of the law shall determine proper trial procedures. The same judges who try temple robbers shall preside. The convicted killer dies and is denied burial in the victim's country. Fugitives may be killed or captured by any citizen. The accused must provide three sureties for trial appearance or be held in custody."
D: Summary: The Stranger specifies trial procedures for premeditated kinsman-murder: religious authorities establish proper procedures, temple-robbery judges preside, conviction means death and denial of local burial, fugitives may be killed by anyone, and the accused must provide sureties or be detained.
D: Distilled: Premeditated kinsman-killers face trial by temple-robbery judges, death and denial of local burial if convicted, and any citizen may kill a fugitive who returns to the victim's country.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The provision allowing any citizen to kill a returned fugitive on sight reflects the ancient Greek institution of outlawry (atimia in its extreme form), where the community collectively withdrew legal protection from the offender. This is not vigilante justice but formal legal sanction: the outlaw has been placed outside the law's protection by the law itself. The surety system (bail) described here closely parallels Athenian legal practice, where sureties (eggutai) guaranteed the defendant's appearance. The denial of burial in the victim's country serves both practical and symbolic purposes: it prevents the murdered person's spirit from being further disturbed by the murderer's proximity (connecting to the ghost beliefs described in 9.30) and symbolizes the murderer's total exclusion from the community he violated. The collaborative determination of religious procedure by guardians of the law, interpreters, prophets, and the god reflects the Laws' consistent integration of human reason and divine guidance in institutional design.

D: Original: If a man do not commit a murder with his own hand, but contrives the death of another, and is the author of the deed in intention and design, and he continues to dwell in the city, having his soul not pure of the guilt of murder, let him be tried in the same way, except in what relates to the sureties; and also, if he be found guilty, his body after execution may have burial in his native land, but in all other respects his case shall be as the former; and whether a stranger shall kill a citizen, or a citizen a stranger, or a slave a slave, there shall be no difference as touching murder by one's own hand or by contrivance, except in the matter of sureties; and these, as has been said, shall be required of the actual murderer only, and he who brings the accusation shall bind them over at the time. If a slave be convicted of slaying a freeman voluntarily, either by his own hand or by contrivance, let the public executioner take him in the direction of the sepulchre, to a place whence he can see the tomb of the dead man, and inflict upon him as many stripes as the person who caught him orders, and if he survive, let him put him to death.
D: Narrative:
"If a man do not commit a murder with his own hand, but contrives the death of another, and is the author of the deed in intention and design, and he continues to dwell in the city, having his soul not pure of the guilt of murder, let him be tried in the same way, except in what relates to the sureties; and also, if he be found guilty, his body after execution may have burial in his native land, but in all other respects his case shall be as the former.

"And whether a stranger shall kill a citizen, or a citizen a stranger, or a slave a slave, there shall be no difference as touching murder by one's own hand or by contrivance, except in the matter of sureties; and these, as has been said, shall be required of the actual murderer only, and he who brings the accusation shall bind them over at the time.

"If a slave be convicted of slaying a freeman voluntarily, either by his own hand or by contrivance, let the public executioner take him in the direction of the sepulchre, to a place whence he can see the tomb of the dead man, and inflict upon him as many stripes as the person who caught him orders, and if he survive, let him put him to death."
D: Refashioned:
"If someone does not commit murder with his own hands but orchestrates another person's death -- being the author of the killing in intention and design -- and continues living in the city with a soul polluted by the guilt of murder, he shall be tried under the same procedures, with one exception regarding sureties. Additionally, if convicted, his body may be buried in his native land after execution. In all other respects, his case is treated identically to murder by one's own hand.

"Whether a stranger kills a citizen, a citizen kills a stranger, or a slave kills a slave, there shall be no distinction between murder committed personally and murder committed by contrivance, except regarding sureties. As stated, sureties are required only of the person who physically committed the killing; the prosecutor shall secure them at the time charges are brought.

"If a slave is convicted of voluntarily killing a free person, whether by his own hand or through contrivance, the public executioner shall take him near the victim's tomb -- to a place where he can see the burial site -- and shall flog him with as many stripes as the person who captured him orders. If the slave survives the flogging, the executioner shall put him to death."
D: Elegant: "Conspiracy to murder is tried the same as murder by one's own hand, except the conspirator may be buried in his native land if convicted. Sureties are required only of the physical killer. These rules apply regardless of status -- citizen, stranger, or slave. A slave convicted of voluntarily killing a free person is flogged within sight of the victim's tomb and then executed."
D: Summary: The Stranger extends murder law to cover conspiracy and contrivance, treating it nearly identically to direct killing. Rules apply across all status categories. Sureties are required only of the physical killer. Slaves convicted of voluntarily killing free persons are flogged in sight of the victim's tomb and then executed.
D: Distilled: Murder by contrivance is treated the same as murder by one's own hand regardless of the parties' status, with sureties required only of the physical killer.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The extension of murder liability to those who 'contrive' (bouleusas) a death -- the masterminds of murder -- reflects a principle also found in Athenian law, which tried conspirators and those who planned murders alongside the actual killers. The Athenian court at the Palladion handled cases of bouleusis (planning or instigation of murder). The slight distinction in punishment -- the conspirator may be buried in his native land while the direct killer may not -- perhaps reflects a judgment that while the moral guilt is equivalent, the physical act of killing creates a different kind of pollution. The brutal punishment prescribed for slaves who kill free persons -- flogging within sight of the victim's tomb followed by execution -- combines retributive elements (the slave sees the consequence of his act) with the status-based severity that characterizes the Laws' treatment of slave criminality throughout Book 9.

D: Original: And if any one kills a slave who has done no wrong, because he is afraid that he may inform of some base and evil deeds of his own, or for any similar reason, in such a case let him pay the penalty of murder, as he would have done if he had slain a citizen. There are things about which it is terrible and unpleasant to legislate, but impossible not to legislate. If, for example, there should be murders of kinsmen, either perpetrated by the hands of kinsmen, or by their contrivance, voluntary and purely malicious, which most often happen in ill-regulated and ill-educated states, and may perhaps occur even in a country where a man would not expect to find them, we must repeat once more the tale which we narrated a little while ago, in the hope that he who hears us will be the more disposed to abstain voluntarily on these grounds from murders which are utterly abominable. For the myth, or saying, or whatever we ought to call it, has been plainly set forth by priests of old; they have pronounced that the justice which guards and avenges the blood of kindred, follows the law of retaliation, and ordains that he who has done any murderous act should of necessity suffer that which he has done. He who has slain a father shall himself be slain at some time or other by his children-if a mother, he shall of necessity take a woman's nature, and lose his life at the hands of his offspring in after ages; for where the blood of a family has been polluted there is no other purification, nor can the pollution be washed out until the homicidal soul which the deed has given life for life, and has propitiated and laid to sleep the wrath of the whole family. These are the retributions of Heaven, and by such punishments men should be deterred.
D: Narrative:
"And if any one kills a slave who has done no wrong, because he is afraid that he may inform of some base and evil deeds of his own, or for any similar reason, in such a case let him pay the penalty of murder, as he would have done if he had slain a citizen.

"There are things about which it is terrible and unpleasant to legislate, but impossible not to legislate. If, for example, there should be murders of kinsmen, either perpetrated by the hands of kinsmen, or by their contrivance, voluntary and purely malicious, which most often happen in ill-regulated and ill-educated states, and may perhaps occur even in a country where a man would not expect to find them, we must repeat once more the tale which we narrated a little while ago, in the hope that he who hears us will be the more disposed to abstain voluntarily on these grounds from murders which are utterly abominable.

"For the myth, or saying, or whatever we ought to call it, has been plainly set forth by priests of old; they have pronounced that the justice which guards and avenges the blood of kindred follows the law of retaliation, and ordains that he who has done any murderous act should of necessity suffer that which he has done. He who has slain a father shall himself be slain at some time or other by his children -- if a mother, he shall of necessity take a woman's nature, and lose his life at the hands of his offspring in after ages; for where the blood of a family has been polluted there is no other purification, nor can the pollution be washed out until the homicidal soul which did the deed has given life for life, and has propitiated and laid to sleep the wrath of the whole family. These are the retributions of Heaven, and by such punishments men should be deterred."
D: Refashioned:
"If someone kills an innocent slave out of fear that the slave might expose his wrongdoing, or for any similar reason, he shall pay the full penalty for murder, exactly as if he had killed a citizen.

"Some crimes are terrible and unpleasant to legislate about, yet legislation is unavoidable. For example, deliberate and malicious murders among family members -- committed by kinsmen's own hands or through their scheming -- occur most often in poorly governed and poorly educated states, but may arise even in places where one would never expect them. We must therefore repeat the ancient tradition mentioned earlier, hoping that those who hear it will be more inclined to abstain voluntarily from these utterly abominable acts.

"The myth -- or teaching, or whatever we should call it -- was clearly stated by priests long ago: the divine justice that guards and avenges the blood of kinsmen operates by the law of retaliation. Whoever commits murder must inevitably suffer the same fate. He who kills his father will someday be killed by his own children. He who kills his mother must in a future life take on a woman's nature and die at the hands of his offspring in a later age. Once a family's blood has been polluted, there is no purification except life given for life -- the homicidal soul must repay in kind until the wrath of the entire family line is appeased and laid to rest. These are Heaven's retributions, and they should deter anyone considering such acts."
D: Elegant: "Killing an innocent slave to silence him carries the same penalty as killing a citizen. As for deliberate family murders -- terrible to legislate but impossible to ignore -- the ancient priestly tradition teaches that divine retribution follows the law of retaliation: a father-killer will be killed by his children, a mother-killer will be reborn as a woman and killed by offspring in a future life. Family blood pollution can only be purified by life given for life."
D: Summary: The Stranger rules that killing an innocent slave to prevent exposure carries the full murder penalty. He then addresses premeditated family murder, invoking the ancient tradition of divine retribution: killers of parents will be killed by their own children, either in this life or through reincarnation, until the blood pollution is purged life for life.
D: Distilled: Killing a slave to silence him carries the full murder penalty; premeditated family murder is deterred by the priestly teaching that divine justice demands life for life across generations and reincarnations.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The provision treating the murder of an innocent slave as equivalent to the murder of a citizen is remarkably progressive for ancient legislation and directly applies the Stranger's earlier principle (9.22) that murder for fear of exposure is one of the three causes of voluntary homicide. The retaliatory theology draws heavily on Orphic and Pythagorean doctrines of metempsychosis. The specific detail that a mother-killer must 'take a woman's nature' in a future life reflects the belief that karmic justice operates with exact symmetry -- the punishment mirrors the crime. The Stranger's hedging about what to call this teaching -- 'myth, or saying, or whatever we ought to call it' -- is characteristic of Plato's careful handling of religious traditions that he finds useful for moral persuasion without necessarily affirming their literal truth. Compare the Phaedo's treatment of afterlife myths (114d): 'No sensible man would insist that these things are exactly as I have described... but that either this or something like it is true about our souls and their dwelling places... this seems to me fitting and worth risking.'

D: Original: But if they are not deterred, and any one should be incited by some fatality to deprive his father or mother, or brethren, or children, of life voluntarily and of purpose, for him the earthly lawgiver legislates as follows:-There shall be the same proclamations about outlawry, and there shall be the same sureties which have been enacted in the former cases. But in his case, if he be convicted, the servants of the judges and the magistrates shall slay him at an appointed place without the city where three ways meet, and there expose his body naked, and each of the magistrates on behalf of the whole city shall take a stone and cast it upon the head of the dead man, and so deliver the city from pollution; after that, they shall bear him to the borders of the land, and cast him forth unburied, according to law. And what shall he suffer who slays him who of all men, as they say, is his own best friend? I mean the suicide, who deprives himself by violence of his appointed share of life, not because the law of the state requires him, nor yet under the compulsion of some painful and inevitable misfortune which has come upon him, nor because he has had to suffer from irremediable and intolerable shame, but who from sloth or want of manliness imposes upon himself an unjust penalty.
D: Narrative:
"But if they are not deterred, and any one should be incited by some fatality to deprive his father or mother, or brethren, or children, of life voluntarily and of purpose, for him the earthly lawgiver legislates as follows:

"There shall be the same proclamations about outlawry, and there shall be the same sureties which have been enacted in the former cases. But in his case, if he be convicted, the servants of the judges and the magistrates shall slay him at an appointed place without the city where three ways meet, and there expose his body naked, and each of the magistrates on behalf of the whole city shall take a stone and cast it upon the head of the dead man, and so deliver the city from pollution; after that, they shall bear him to the borders of the land, and cast him forth unburied, according to law.

"And what shall he suffer who slays him who of all men, as they say, is his own best friend? I mean the suicide, who deprives himself by violence of his appointed share of life, not because the law of the state requires him, nor yet under the compulsion of some painful and inevitable misfortune which has come upon him, nor because he has had to suffer from irremediable and intolerable shame, but who from sloth or want of manliness imposes upon himself an unjust penalty."
D: Refashioned:
"But if the religious prelude fails to deter, and someone is driven by fate to deliberately and purposefully kill his father, mother, siblings, or children, the earthly legislator prescribes as follows:

"The same proclamations of outlawry and the same surety requirements from previous cases shall apply. If convicted, the servants of the judges and magistrates shall execute him at a designated place outside the city where three roads meet. There they shall expose his body naked, and each magistrate, acting on behalf of the entire city, shall cast a stone upon the dead man's head, thereby delivering the city from pollution. Afterward, they shall carry his body to the borders and cast it forth unburied, as the law requires.

"And what of the person who kills his own greatest friend -- himself? I mean the suicide, who violently deprives himself of his allotted portion of life. I am not speaking of one compelled by state law, or driven by some painful and unavoidable catastrophe, or forced by irremediable and intolerable shame. I mean the person who, out of mere laziness or lack of courage, imposes upon himself an unjust penalty."
D: Elegant: "If religious deterrence fails: the convicted family-killer is executed outside the city at a crossroads, his body exposed naked, and each magistrate casts a stone on the corpse to purge the city's pollution. The body is then carried to the borders and cast out unburied. Next, the question of suicide: one who kills himself not from necessity, catastrophe, or unbearable shame, but from laziness or cowardice, imposes an unjust penalty on himself."
D: Summary: For convicted family murderers, the law prescribes execution at a crossroads, public stoning of the corpse by magistrates to purge pollution, and denial of burial. The Stranger then raises suicide, distinguishing justified cases (state compulsion, unavoidable suffering, irremediable shame) from unjustified ones (laziness and cowardice).
D: Distilled: Convicted family murderers are executed at a crossroads and their bodies are stoned by magistrates and cast out unburied; suicide from laziness or cowardice -- but not from necessity or unbearable suffering -- is an unjust self-penalty.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The execution at the crossroads (triodos) where three roads meet is rich with religious significance. Crossroads were associated with Hecate and with the disposal of pollution in Greek religion. The ritual stoning of the corpse by magistrates is not punishment of the dead but a communal purification -- each official, acting for the whole city, symbolically expels the pollution. This recalls the scapegoat (pharmakos) rituals in which Greek cities ceremonially expelled a polluted figure. The transition to suicide is philosophically significant. Plato's treatment of suicide here is more nuanced than is often noted: he distinguishes three legitimate grounds (state requirement, unavoidable misfortune, intolerable shame) from the illegitimate ground of 'sloth or want of manliness.' This connects to the Phaedo (61c-62c), where Socrates argues that we should not kill ourselves because we are the gods' possessions and must await their summons -- yet Socrates himself faces death willingly when the state requires it. The phrase 'his own best friend' applied to the self echoes the Aristotelian dictum (Nicomachean Ethics 9.4) that a person's relationship to himself is the model for friendship.

D: Original: For him, what ceremonies there are to be of purification and burial God knows, and about these the next of kin should enquire of the interpreters and of the laws thereto relating, and do according to their injunctions. They who meet their death in this way shall be buried alone, and none shall be laid by their side; they shall be buried ingloriously in the borders of the twelve portions the land, in such places as are uncultivated and nameless, and no column or inscription shall mark the place of their interment. And if a beast of burden or other animal cause the death of any one, except in the case of anything of that kind happening to a competitor in the public contests, the kinsmen of the deceased shall prosecute the slayer for murder, and the wardens of the country, such, and so many as the kinsmen appoint, shall try the cause, and let the beast when condemned be slain by them, and let them cast it beyond the borders. And if any lifeless thing deprive a man of life, except in the case of a thunderbolt or other fatal dart sent from the Gods-whether a man is killed by lifeless objects, falling upon him, or by his falling upon them, the nearest of kin shall appoint the nearest neighbour to be a judge, and thereby acquit himself and the whole family of guilt. And he shall cast forth the guilty thing beyond the border, as has been said about the animals.
D: Narrative:
"For him, what ceremonies there are to be of purification and burial God knows, and about these the next of kin should enquire of the interpreters and of the laws thereto relating, and do according to their injunctions.

"They who meet their death in this way shall be buried alone, and none shall be laid by their side; they shall be buried ingloriously in the borders of the twelve portions of the land, in such places as are uncultivated and nameless, and no column or inscription shall mark the place of their interment.

"And if a beast of burden or other animal cause the death of any one, except in the case of anything of that kind happening to a competitor in the public contests, the kinsmen of the deceased shall prosecute the slayer for murder, and the wardens of the country, such and so many as the kinsmen appoint, shall try the cause, and let the beast when condemned be slain by them, and let them cast it beyond the borders.

"And if any lifeless thing deprive a man of life, except in the case of a thunderbolt or other fatal dart sent from the Gods -- whether a man is killed by lifeless objects falling upon him, or by his falling upon them -- the nearest of kin shall appoint the nearest neighbour to be a judge, and thereby acquit himself and the whole family of guilt. And he shall cast forth the guilty thing beyond the border, as has been said about the animals."
D: Refashioned:
"As for the suicide's purification and burial ceremonies -- that is a matter for God to determine. The next of kin should consult the religious interpreters and the relevant laws and follow their instructions.

"Those who die by their own hand shall be buried alone with no one beside them. They shall be interred ingloriously on the boundaries of the twelve land divisions, in uncultivated and unnamed places. No column or inscription shall mark their burial site.

"If a beast of burden or other animal causes someone's death -- except during public competitions -- the victim's kinsmen shall prosecute the animal for murder. The countryside wardens, as many as the kinsmen designate, shall try the case. If the animal is found guilty, they shall kill it and cast it beyond the borders.

"If a lifeless object causes death -- except for a thunderbolt or other divine missile -- whether by falling on the person or by the person falling onto it, the nearest kin shall appoint the nearest neighbor as judge. This procedure acquits the kin and the entire family of guilt. The 'guilty' object shall then be cast beyond the borders, just as with animals."
D: Elegant: "Suicides are buried alone, without markers, in nameless uncultivated land on the borders. Animals that cause death are tried for murder and, if convicted, killed and expelled beyond the borders. Lifeless objects that cause death -- except divine instruments like thunderbolts -- are ceremonially expelled after a neighbor serves as judge, acquitting the family of guilt."
D: Summary: The Stranger prescribes inglorious, unmarked burial for suicides. Animals that cause death are formally tried and expelled. Lifeless objects that cause death (except divine instruments) undergo a similar ritual expulsion, with a neighbor serving as judge to acquit the victim's family of pollution.
D: Distilled: Suicides receive isolated, unmarked burial; death-causing animals are tried and expelled; and death-causing objects are ceremonially cast out to acquit the family of pollution.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The treatment of suicides -- solitary burial, no markers, unnamed locations -- reflects a combination of moral disapproval and practical pollution management. The suicide is neither honored nor publicly shamed; he is simply erased from communal memory. This stands between the honor burial of the good citizen and the unburied exposure of the criminal. The trial and execution of animals and objects that cause death may seem bizarre to modern readers but was genuine Greek legal practice. Athenian law, administered through the court at the Prytaneion, tried animals and inanimate objects for causing death. The procedure served an important function: by formally identifying and expelling the 'guilty' agent, the community purified itself of the pollution associated with violent death. The exception for thunderbolts and other 'darts sent from the Gods' acknowledges divine agency as exempt from human jurisdiction -- an important theological boundary. Compare Aristotle's discussion of the Prytaneion court (Constitution of Athens 57.4) and Aeschines' reference to the trial of inanimate objects (Against Ctesiphon 244).

D: Original: If a man is found dead, and his murderer be unknown, and after a diligent search cannot be detected, there shall be the same proclamation as in the previous cases, and the same interdict on the murderer; and having proceeded against him, they shall proclaim in the agora by a herald, that he who has slain such and such a person, and has been convicted of murder, shall not set his foot in the temples, nor at all in the country of the murdered man, and if he appears and is discovered, he shall die, and be cast forth unburied beyond the border. Let this one law then be laid down by us about murder; and let cases of this sort be so regarded.
D: Narrative:
"If a man is found dead, and his murderer be unknown, and after a diligent search cannot be detected, there shall be the same proclamation as in the previous cases, and the same interdict on the murderer; and having proceeded against him, they shall proclaim in the agora by a herald, that he who has slain such and such a person, and has been convicted of murder, shall not set his foot in the temples, nor at all in the country of the murdered man, and if he appears and is discovered, he shall die, and be cast forth unburied beyond the border.

"Let this one law then be laid down by us about murder; and let cases of this sort be so regarded."
D: Refashioned:
"If a person is found dead and the killer remains unknown despite a thorough investigation, the same proclamation and interdiction shall apply as in previous cases. Having formally proceeded against the unknown killer, the authorities shall announce by herald in the marketplace: 'Whoever killed this person, having been convicted of murder in absentia, shall not set foot in any temple or anywhere in the murdered person's country. If he appears and is identified, he shall be put to death and his body cast out unburied beyond the borders.'

"Let this be our final word on murder law, and let all such cases be handled accordingly."
D: Elegant: "When a killer remains unknown despite investigation, the same proclamation and banishment apply. A herald announces in the marketplace that the unidentified killer is banned from temples and the victim's country; if discovered, he faces death and denial of burial. This concludes the law of murder."
D: Summary: The Stranger addresses unsolved murders: the standard proclamation and banishment apply to the unknown killer, announced publicly by herald. Discovery means death and denial of burial. This passage closes the homicide section of Book 9.
D: Distilled: For unsolved murders, the unknown killer is formally proclaimed an outlaw by herald, banned from temples and the victim's country, and subject to death if later discovered.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This provision for unsolved murders reflects the Greek conviction that blood pollution must be addressed even when the killer is unknown. Athenian law similarly provided for proclamations against unknown murderers, issued at the Prytaneion. The formal proclamation by herald (kerux) in the agora -- the public marketplace and civic center -- ensures maximum publicity. The legal fiction of proceeding against an unknown killer served the important psychological and religious function of demonstrating that the community had not acquiesced in the murder. By formally declaring the unknown killer an outlaw, the city affirms its commitment to justice even when practical enforcement is impossible. This passage also provides a clear structural marker: 'Let this one law then be laid down by us about murder' signals the transition from homicide to the next category of violent crime (wounding and assault).

D: Original: And now let us say in what cases and under what circumstances the murderer is rightly free from guilt:-If a man catch a thief coming, into his house by night to steal, and he take and kill him, or if he slay a footpad in self-defence, he shall be guiltless. And any one who does violence to a free woman or a youth, shall be slain with impunity by the injured person, or by his or her father or brothers or sons. If a man find his wife suffering violence, he may kill the violator, and be guiltless in the eye of the law; or if a person kill another in warding off death from his father or mother or children or brethren or wife who are doing no wrong, he shall assuredly be guiltless.
D: Narrative:
"And now let us say in what cases and under what circumstances the murderer is rightly free from guilt: If a man catch a thief coming into his house by night to steal, and he take and kill him, or if he slay a footpad in self-defence, he shall be guiltless.

"And any one who does violence to a free woman or a youth shall be slain with impunity by the injured person, or by his or her father or brothers or sons. If a man find his wife suffering violence, he may kill the violator, and be guiltless in the eye of the law; or if a person kill another in warding off death from his father or mother or children or brethren or wife who are doing no wrong, he shall assuredly be guiltless."
D: Refashioned:
"Now let us specify the cases where killing is justifiable and the killer is rightly free from guilt.

"If someone catches a thief breaking into his house at night and kills him, or if he kills a highway robber in self-defense, he shall be guiltless.

"Anyone who sexually assaults a free woman or a young person may be killed with impunity by the victim, or by the victim's father, brothers, or sons. If a man discovers someone assaulting his wife, he may kill the attacker and stand guiltless before the law. And if anyone kills another person while defending the life of a parent, child, sibling, or spouse who is innocent of wrongdoing, that person shall be entirely guiltless."
D: Elegant: "Justifiable homicide: killing a nighttime burglar or a highway robber in self-defense. Killing a rapist -- by the victim or the victim's father, brothers, or sons. Killing someone assaulting one's wife. Killing in defense of the life of an innocent parent, child, sibling, or spouse. All guiltless."
D: Summary: The Stranger establishes the categories of justifiable homicide: killing burglars and highway robbers in self-defense, killing sexual assailants, and killing to defend innocent family members from lethal threat.
D: Distilled: Killing in defense of home, person, or innocent family members -- including killing burglars, robbers, rapists, and those threatening the lives of kin -- is entirely guiltless.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. These justifiable homicide provisions closely parallel Athenian law. Draco's homicide law (as preserved in the re-inscription of 409/8 BCE) included provisions for killing in self-defense, and Athenian law specifically permitted killing a man caught in the act of sexual violence against one's wife, mother, sister, daughter, or concubine (as described in Lysias' speech On the Murder of Eratosthenes). The right to kill in defense of family members extends the self-defense principle beyond the individual to the household (oikos), which was the fundamental social unit of Greek life. The specification that the protected family members must be 'doing no wrong' prevents the justification from being used in cases where the 'victim' was actually an aggressor. These provisions represent the law's recognition that some situations require immediate violent action and that the legal system cannot always be invoked in time to protect life and honor.

D: Original: Thus much as to the nurture and education of the living soul of man, having which, he can, and without which, if he unfortunately be without them, he cannot live; and also concerning the punishments:-which are to be inflicted for violent deaths, let thus much be enacted. Of the nurture and education of the body we have spoken before, and next in order we have to speak of deeds of violence, voluntary and involuntary, which men do to one another; these we will now distinguish, as far as we are able, according to their nature and number, and determine what will be the suitable penalties of each, and so assign to them their proper place in the series of our enactments. The poorest legislator will have no difficulty in determining that wounds and mutilations arising out of wounds should follow next in order after deaths. Let wounds be divided as homicides were divided-into those which are involuntary, and which are given in passion or from fear, and those inflicted voluntarily and with premeditation. Concerning all this, we must make some such proclamation as the following:-Mankind must have laws, and conform to them, or their life would be as bad as that of the most savage beast. And the reason of this is that no man's nature is able to know what is best for human society; or knowing, always able and willing to do what is best.
D: Narrative:
"Thus much as to the nurture and education of the living soul of man, having which, he can, and without which, if he unfortunately be without them, he cannot live; and also concerning the punishments which are to be inflicted for violent deaths, let thus much be enacted.

"Of the nurture and education of the body we have spoken before, and next in order we have to speak of deeds of violence, voluntary and involuntary, which men do to one another; these we will now distinguish, as far as we are able, according to their nature and number, and determine what will be the suitable penalties of each, and so assign to them their proper place in the series of our enactments.

"The poorest legislator will have no difficulty in determining that wounds and mutilations arising out of wounds should follow next in order after deaths. Let wounds be divided as homicides were divided -- into those which are involuntary, and which are given in passion or from fear, and those inflicted voluntarily and with premeditation.

"Concerning all this, we must make some such proclamation as the following: Mankind must have laws, and conform to them, or their life would be as bad as that of the most savage beast. And the reason of this is that no man's nature is able to know what is best for human society; or knowing, always able and willing to do what is best."
D: Refashioned:
"We have now covered the nurture and education of the human soul -- without which a person cannot truly live -- as well as the penalties for violent death. Let this much be enacted.

"We have previously discussed the care of the body, and the next topic in order is violent acts that people commit against one another, both voluntary and involuntary. We will classify these as precisely as possible according to their nature and variety, determine appropriate penalties for each, and assign them their proper place in our legal code.

"Even the most basic legislator can see that wounds and mutilations should naturally follow deaths in our sequence. Wounds shall be categorized the same way as homicides: involuntary, passionate or fear-driven, and voluntary with premeditation.

"Before proceeding, a fundamental proclamation is necessary: Human beings must have laws and obey them, or their lives would be no better than the most savage beasts. The reason is that no person's natural capacity is sufficient to know what is truly best for human society -- or, even if he knows, to remain always able and willing to do what is best."
D: Elegant: "Having covered homicide law, we turn to wounding and mutilation, classified like homicide into involuntary, passionate, and premeditated categories. A foundational proclamation: humans must have laws or become beasts. No one naturally knows what is best for society, and even those who know cannot always will themselves to act on that knowledge."
D: Summary: The Stranger transitions from homicide to wounding and assault, using the same classification system. He prefaces this section with a fundamental claim: humans need laws because no one's natural capacity is sufficient to consistently know and do what is best for society.
D: Distilled: Wounding and mutilation are classified like homicide; the foundational principle is that humans need laws because no one can naturally and consistently know and do what is best for society.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The proclamation that 'mankind must have laws, or their life would be as bad as that of the most savage beast' is one of the Laws' most quoted passages and represents a fundamental departure from the Republic's aspiration to governance by perfect knowledge rather than law. The two-part argument -- that humans cannot reliably know the good, and even knowing it, cannot reliably act on it -- combines the epistemological problem (ignorance) with the motivational problem (weakness of will, or akrasia). The Republic famously denies the possibility of akrasia (following Protagoras 352b-358d), but the Laws here implicitly acknowledges it as a practical reality. This admission is central to understanding why the Laws turns from philosopher-kings to law: the 'second-best' regime relies on fixed rules precisely because no human can be trusted to exercise perfect judgment consistently. Compare Aristotle's response in Politics 3.15-16 on whether it is better to be ruled by the best laws or the best man.

D: Original: In the first place, there is a difficulty in apprehending that the true art or politics is concerned, not with private but with public good (for public good binds together states, but private only distracts them); and that both the public and private good as well of individuals as of states is greater when the state and not the individual is first considered. In the second place, although a person knows in the abstract that this is true, yet if he be possessed of absolute and irresponsible power, he will never remain firm in his principles or persist in regarding the public good as primary in the state, and the private good as secondary. Human nature will be always drawing him into avarice and selfishness, avoiding pain and pursuing Pleasure without any reason, and will bring these to the front, obscuring the juster and better; and so working darkness in his soul will at last fill with evils both him and the whole city. For if a man were born so divinely gifted that he could naturally apprehend the truth, he would have no need of laws to rule over him; for there is no law or order which is above knowledge, nor can mind, without impiety, be deemed the subject or slave of any man, but rather the lord of all. I speak of mind, true and free, and in harmony with nature. But then there is no such mind anywhere, or at least not much; and therefore we must choose law and order, which are second best. These look at things as they exist for the most part only, and are unable to survey the whole of them. And therefore I have spoken as I have.
D: Narrative:
"In the first place, there is a difficulty in apprehending that the true art of politics is concerned, not with private but with public good -- for public good binds together states, but private only distracts them -- and that both the public and private good as well of individuals as of states is greater when the state and not the individual is first considered.

"In the second place, although a person knows in the abstract that this is true, yet if he be possessed of absolute and irresponsible power, he will never remain firm in his principles or persist in regarding the public good as primary in the state, and the private good as secondary. Human nature will be always drawing him into avarice and selfishness, avoiding pain and pursuing pleasure without any reason, and will bring these to the front, obscuring the juster and better; and so working darkness in his soul will at last fill with evils both him and the whole city.

"For if a man were born so divinely gifted that he could naturally apprehend the truth, he would have no need of laws to rule over him; for there is no law or order which is above knowledge, nor can mind, without impiety, be deemed the subject or slave of any man, but rather the lord of all. I speak of mind, true and free, and in harmony with nature. But then there is no such mind anywhere, or at least not much; and therefore we must choose law and order, which are second best. These look at things as they exist for the most part only, and are unable to survey the whole of them. And therefore I have spoken as I have."
D: Refashioned:
"The first difficulty is recognizing that the true art of politics concerns public good, not private good -- for public good unites states while private interest tears them apart -- and that both public and private welfare are actually greater when the state's interests are placed above the individual's.

"The second difficulty is this: even when someone understands this truth in the abstract, if given absolute and unchecked power, he will never consistently prioritize public good over private. Human nature will constantly pull him toward greed and selfishness, toward irrational avoidance of pain and pursuit of pleasure. These impulses will push their way to the front of his mind, obscuring what is just and good. The resulting darkness in his soul will eventually fill both him and the entire city with evils.

"Now, if anyone were so divinely gifted as to naturally grasp the truth, he would need no laws to govern him. No law or institution is superior to genuine knowledge, and it would be impious to make true, free mind -- mind in harmony with nature -- the subject or slave of anything. Mind should be lord of all. But such a mind does not exist anywhere, or at least is exceedingly rare. Therefore we must settle for law and order, which are second best. Laws can only address how things usually are; they cannot encompass every particular situation. That is why I have argued as I have."
D: Elegant: "True politics serves public good, not private -- yet even someone who knows this will, given absolute power, inevitably be corrupted by human nature's pull toward selfishness and pleasure. A perfectly wise mind would need no laws and should be lord of all. But such minds are essentially nonexistent. So we settle for law -- second best, covering only the general case, unable to survey every particular. This is why we need legislation."
D: Summary: The Stranger argues that true politics serves public over private good, but absolute power corrupts even those who know this. A mind with perfect knowledge would rightfully rule without law, but since such minds are virtually nonexistent, law is the necessary second-best solution -- general rules that cannot cover every particular case.
D: Distilled: Since no human mind can reliably know and pursue the public good when given unchecked power, law -- though merely a second-best approximation that cannot cover every particular -- is humanity's necessary recourse.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage is one of the most philosophically significant in the entire Laws and marks Plato's mature position on the relationship between knowledge and political authority. In the Republic (473c-e), philosopher-kings rule by knowledge, making law almost superfluous. Here, the Stranger explicitly declares law 'second best' to knowledge but necessary because perfect knowledge does not exist in practice. The statement that 'there is no law or order which is above knowledge, nor can mind, without impiety, be deemed the subject or slave of any man' preserves the theoretical superiority of the Republic's ideal while conceding its practical impossibility.
