The analysis of how absolute power corrupts anticipates Lord Acton by two millennia and echoes the Republic's own analysis of the tyrant's soul (571a-576b). The concept of law as dealing only with 'things as they exist for the most part' connects to Aristotle's later discussion of equity (epieikeia) in Nicomachean Ethics 5.10 -- the recognition that general rules cannot anticipate every particular situation, requiring judicial discretion to fill the gaps. Compare also Statesman 294a-300c on the relationship between true statesmanship and written law.

D: Original:
And now we will determine what penalty he ought to pay or suffer who has hurt or wounded another. Any one may easily imagine the questions which have to be asked in all such cases:-What did he wound, or whom, or how, or when? for there are innumerable particulars of this sort which greatly vary from one another. And to allow courts of law to determine all these things, or not to determine any of them, is alike impossible. There is one particular which they must determine in all cases-the question of fact. And then, again, that the legislator should not permit them to determine what punishment is to be inflicted in any of these cases, but should himself decide about, of them, small or great, is next to impossible.

Then what is to be the inference?

The inference is, that some things should be left to courts of law; others the legislator must decide for himself.

And what ought the legislator to decide, and what ought he to leave to courts of law?
D: Narrative:
"And now we will determine what penalty he ought to pay or suffer who has hurt or wounded another. Any one may easily imagine the questions which have to be asked in all such cases: What did he wound, or whom, or how, or when? For there are innumerable particulars of this sort which greatly vary from one another. And to allow courts of law to determine all these things, or not to determine any of them, is alike impossible. There is one particular which they must determine in all cases -- the question of fact.

"And then, again, that the legislator should not permit them to determine what punishment is to be inflicted in any of these cases, but should himself decide about all of them, small or great, is next to impossible."

"Then what is to be the inference?"

"The inference is, that some things should be left to courts of law; others the legislator must decide for himself."

"And what ought the legislator to decide, and what ought he to leave to courts of law?"
D: Refashioned:
"Now let us determine the penalties for hurting or wounding another person. The questions to be asked in each case are easy to imagine: What was wounded? Who was the victim? How was the wound inflicted? When did it happen? The particulars are innumerable and vary enormously from case to case. It is equally impossible to have courts determine all of these matters and to have them determine none.

"One thing courts must always determine: the factual question of what actually happened. But for the legislator to personally decide the punishment in every single case, large or small, is nearly impossible."

"What follows from this?"

"That some matters must be left to courts, while others the legislator must decide himself."

"Which matters should the legislator decide, and which should he leave to the courts?"
D: Elegant: "Wounding cases involve countless variables -- what, who, how, when. Courts cannot determine everything, nor can they be denied all judgment. They must always decide the question of fact. But the legislator cannot prescribe the exact penalty for every possible case. The solution: divide the work. Some things the legislator decides; others he leaves to the courts."
D: Summary: The Stranger addresses the practical problem of legislating for wounding cases: the infinite variety of circumstances makes it impossible for either the legislator or the courts to handle everything alone. Courts must determine facts; the allocation of sentencing authority between legislator and courts requires careful division.
D: Distilled: The infinite variety of wounding cases requires dividing authority between legislator and courts: courts determine facts, while sentencing is shared between predetermined rules and judicial discretion.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage addresses a perennial problem in legal philosophy: the relationship between legislative prescription and judicial discretion. The Stranger's solution -- that courts must determine facts while the legislator prescribes some penalties and leaves others to judicial judgment -- anticipates the modern distinction between mandatory and discretionary sentencing. The underlying principle from 9.49 informs this: since law deals only with generalities while cases involve infinite particulars, some flexibility must be built into the system. The Stranger's inclusion of a dialogical question ('What is the inference?') within his own speech creates the impression of thinking through the problem in real time, a technique characteristic of the Socratic method even when the Stranger is speaking at length. Compare Aristotle's discussion of the necessity for equity (epieikeia) as a correction to law's generality (Nicomachean Ethics 5.10, 1137b-1138a).

D: Original: I may reply, that in a state in which the courts are bad and mute, because the judges conceal their opinions and decide causes clandestinely; or what is worse, when they are disorderly and noisy, as in a theatre, clapping or hooting in turn this or that orator-I say that then there is a very serious evil, which affects the whole state. Unfortunate is the necessity of having to legislate for such courts, but where the necessity exists, the legislator should only allow them to ordain the penalties for the smallest offences; if the state for which he is legislating be of this character, he must take most matters into his own hands and speak distinctly. But when a state has good courts, and the judges are well trained and scrupulously tested, the determination of the penalties or punishments which shall be inflicted on the guilty may fairly and with advantage be left to them. And we are not to be blamed for not legislating concerning all that large class of matters which judges far worse educated than ours would be able to determine, assigning to each offence what is due both to the perpetrator and to the sufferer. We believe those for whom we are legislating to be best able to judge, and therefore to them the greater part may be left. At the same time, as I have often said, we should exhibit to the judges, as we have done, the outline and form of the punishments to be inflicted, and then they will not transgress the just rule. That was an excellent practice, which we observed before, and which now that we are resuming the work of legislation, may with advantage be repeated by us.
D: Narrative:
"I may reply, that in a state in which the courts are bad and mute, because the judges conceal their opinions and decide causes clandestinely; or what is worse, when they are disorderly and noisy, as in a theatre, clapping or hooting in turn this or that orator -- I say that then there is a very serious evil, which affects the whole state.

"Unfortunate is the necessity of having to legislate for such courts, but where the necessity exists, the legislator should only allow them to ordain the penalties for the smallest offences; if the state for which he is legislating be of this character, he must take most matters into his own hands and speak distinctly.

"But when a state has good courts, and the judges are well trained and scrupulously tested, the determination of the penalties or punishments which shall be inflicted on the guilty may fairly and with advantage be left to them. And we are not to be blamed for not legislating concerning all that large class of matters which judges far worse educated than ours would be able to determine, assigning to each offence what is due both to the perpetrator and to the sufferer.

"We believe those for whom we are legislating to be best able to judge, and therefore to them the greater part may be left. At the same time, as I have often said, we should exhibit to the judges, as we have done, the outline and form of the punishments to be inflicted, and then they will not transgress the just rule. That was an excellent practice, which we observed before, and which now that we are resuming the work of legislation, may with advantage be repeated by us."
D: Refashioned:
"It depends on the quality of the courts. In a state where judges conceal their opinions and decide cases secretly, or worse, where the court is disorderly and noisy like a theater audience, cheering or jeering at each speaker in turn -- that is a grave evil affecting the entire state. A legislator forced to work with such courts should allow them to set penalties only for the most trivial offenses and should prescribe everything else himself.

"But in a state with good courts, where judges are well trained and rigorously tested, it is both fair and advantageous to let them determine punishments for the guilty. We should not be criticized for leaving to judicial discretion the many cases that judges far less educated than ours could handle competently, matching penalties to what is due both the offender and the victim.

"Since we believe our judges will be the best qualified to assess individual cases, we can entrust them with the greater part of sentencing. But as I have said many times, we should provide them with the general outline and framework of appropriate penalties. With these guidelines in hand, they will not depart from just standards. This is the excellent practice we followed before and should continue as we resume our legislation."
D: Elegant: "Bad courts -- secretive or theatrical -- should have minimal sentencing power; the legislator must prescribe most penalties himself. Good courts with well-trained judges can be trusted with broad sentencing discretion. Since our judges will be excellently qualified, we leave them most determinations while providing the outline and framework of just penalties as a guide."
D: Summary: The Stranger's answer depends on court quality: bad courts should have sentencing authority restricted to trivial cases, while good courts with trained judges can be trusted with broad discretion. For their state, well-qualified judges will handle most sentencing, guided by the legislator's general framework.
D: Distilled: Sentencing discretion should be proportional to court quality: bad courts deserve minimal authority, while well-trained judges deserve broad discretion guided by legislative frameworks.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The comparison of bad courts to a noisy theater audience is a vivid critique of democratic legal practice. Athenian courts (dikasteria) were notorious for audience-like behavior -- large juries of hundreds of citizens would react vocally to speakers, and judicial decisions could be influenced by rhetorical skill more than by the merits of the case. This criticism echoes the Gorgias' attack on rhetoric as flattery rather than genuine art (462b-466a) and the Republic's concern about the influence of popular opinion on judgment (492a-493d). The Stranger's solution -- matching judicial discretion to institutional quality -- is a sophisticated institutional design principle. The concept of providing judges with an 'outline and form' (tupon kai schama) of appropriate punishments while leaving specific application to their judgment anticipates modern sentencing guidelines, which provide frameworks while allowing judicial discretion within established parameters.

D: Original: Let the enactment about wounding be in the following terms:-If anyone has a purpose and intention to slay another who is not his enemy, and whom the law does not permit him to slay, and he wounds him, but is unable to kill him, he who had the intent and has wounded him is not to be pitied-he deserves no consideration, but should be regarded as a murderer and be tried for murder. Still having respect to the fortune which has in a manner favoured him, and to the providence which in pity to him and to the wounded man saved the one from a fatal blow, and the other from an accursed fate and calamity-as a thank-offering to this deity, and in order not to oppose his will-in such a case the law will remit the punishment of death, and only compel the offender to emigrate to a neighbouring city for the rest of his life, where he shall remain in the enjoyment of all his possessions. But if he have injured the wounded man, he shall make such compensation for the injury as the court deciding the cause shall assess, and the same judges shall decide who would have decided if the man had died of his wounds. And if a child intentionally wound his parents, or a servant his master, death shall be the penalty. And if a brother ora sister intentionally wound a brother or a sister, and is found guilty, death shall be the penalty. And if a husband wound a wife, or a wife a husband, with intent to kill, let him or her undergo perpetual exile; if they have sons or daughters who are still young, the guardians shall take care of their property, and have charge of the children as orphans. If their sons are grown up, they shall be under no obligation to support the exiled parent, but they shall possess the property themselves.
D: Narrative:
"Let the enactment about wounding be in the following terms: If anyone has a purpose and intention to slay another who is not his enemy, and whom the law does not permit him to slay, and he wounds him, but is unable to kill him, he who had the intent and has wounded him is not to be pitied -- he deserves no consideration, but should be regarded as a murderer and be tried for murder.

"Still having respect to the fortune which has in a manner favoured him, and to the providence which in pity to him and to the wounded man saved the one from a fatal blow, and the other from an accursed fate and calamity -- as a thank-offering to this deity, and in order not to oppose his will -- in such a case the law will remit the punishment of death, and only compel the offender to emigrate to a neighbouring city for the rest of his life, where he shall remain in the enjoyment of all his possessions.

"But if he have injured the wounded man, he shall make such compensation for the injury as the court deciding the cause shall assess, and the same judges shall decide who would have decided if the man had died of his wounds.

"And if a child intentionally wound his parents, or a servant his master, death shall be the penalty. And if a brother or a sister intentionally wound a brother or a sister, and is found guilty, death shall be the penalty. And if a husband wound a wife, or a wife a husband, with intent to kill, let him or her undergo perpetual exile; if they have sons or daughters who are still young, the guardians shall take care of their property, and have charge of the children as orphans. If their sons are grown up, they shall be under no obligation to support the exiled parent, but they shall possess the property themselves."
D: Refashioned:
"The law on wounding shall be as follows: If someone intends and attempts to kill a person who is not an enemy and whom the law does not authorize him to kill, but succeeds only in wounding rather than killing -- that person deserves no pity. He should be treated as a murderer and tried for murder.

"However, out of gratitude to the good fortune -- or divine providence -- that mercifully spared the attacker from delivering a fatal blow and the victim from a cursed death, the law will not impose the death penalty. Instead, the offender shall be permanently exiled to a neighboring city, retaining full enjoyment of his property.

"He must also compensate the wounded person for injuries, as assessed by the court that would have tried the case had the victim died.

"If a child intentionally wounds a parent, or a servant his master, the penalty is death. If a brother or sister intentionally wounds a sibling and is convicted, the penalty is death. If a husband wounds his wife, or a wife her husband, with intent to kill, the penalty is permanent exile. If they have young children, the guardians shall manage the property and care for the children as orphans. If the children are grown, they owe no support to the exiled parent and shall keep the property themselves."
D: Elegant: "Attempted murder (wounding with intent to kill) is tried as murder but, in gratitude for the fortune that prevented a death, the penalty is permanent exile rather than execution. Compensation for injuries is additionally assessed. Children who wound parents, or servants who wound masters: death. Siblings who wound siblings with intent: death. Spouses who wound spouses with intent to kill: permanent exile, with children treated as orphans."
D: Summary: The Stranger treats attempted murder as morally equivalent to murder but reduces the penalty to permanent exile in deference to the fortune that prevented death. Additional compensation for injuries is required. Special cases: wounding parents or masters carries death; spousal assault with intent to kill means permanent exile with children treated as orphans.
D: Distilled: Attempted murder is tried as murder but punished by permanent exile rather than death in gratitude for the fortune that prevented the killing; wounding parents or masters carries the death penalty.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The treatment of attempted murder as morally equivalent to completed murder is philosophically consistent with the Laws' focus on the soul's condition (intention) rather than external outcomes (9.26). The reduction of penalty from death to exile is justified not by the attacker's lesser guilt but by gratitude to divine providence -- a characteristically religious rationale. This creates an elegant compromise: the law acknowledges equal moral culpability while pragmatically recognizing that the actual harm was less. The special severity for children wounding parents and servants wounding masters reflects the hierarchical values that pervade the Laws' social vision. The spousal wounding provisions are again gender-neutral (husband wounding wife and wife wounding husband are treated identically), and the treatment of children as orphans when a parent is exiled for attempted spousal murder is a practical provision that prioritizes the children's welfare over the criminal parent's rights.

D: Original: And if he who meets with such a misfortune has no children, the kindred of the exiled man to the degree of sons of cousins, both on the male and female side, shall meet together, and after taking counsel with the guardians of the and the priests, shall appoint a 5040th citizen to be the heir of the house, considering and reasoning that no house of all the 5040 belongs to the inhabitant or to the whole family, but is the public and private property of the state. Now the state should seek to have its houses as holy and happy as possible. And if any one of the houses be unfortunate, and stained with impiety, and the owner leave no posterity, but dies unmarried, or married and childless, having suffered death as the penalty of murder or some other crime committed against the Gods or against his fellow-citizens, of which death is the penalty distinctly laid down in the law; or if any of the citizens be in perpetual exile, and also childless, that house shall first of all be purified and undergo expiation according to law; and then let the kinsmen of the house, as we were just now saying, and the guardians of the law, meet and consider what family there is in the state which is of the highest repute for virtue and also for good fortune, in which there are a number of sons; from that family let them take one and introduce him to the father and forefathers of the dead man as their son, and, for the sake of the omen, let him be called so, that he may be the continuer of their family, the keeper of their hearth, and the minister of their sacred rites with better fortune than his father had; and when they have made this supplication, they shall make him heir according to law, and the offending person they shall leave nameless and childless and portionless when calamities such as these overtake him.
D: Narrative:
"And if he who meets with such a misfortune has no children, the kindred of the exiled man to the degree of sons of cousins, both on the male and female side, shall meet together, and after taking counsel with the guardians of the law and the priests, shall appoint a 5040th citizen to be the heir of the house, considering and reasoning that no house of all the 5040 belongs to the inhabitant or to the whole family, but is the public and private property of the state.

"Now the state should seek to have its houses as holy and happy as possible. And if any one of the houses be unfortunate, and stained with impiety, and the owner leave no posterity, but dies unmarried, or married and childless, having suffered death as the penalty of murder or some other crime committed against the Gods or against his fellow-citizens, of which death is the penalty distinctly laid down in the law; or if any of the citizens be in perpetual exile, and also childless, that house shall first of all be purified and undergo expiation according to law; and then let the kinsmen of the house, as we were just now saying, and the guardians of the law, meet and consider what family there is in the state which is of the highest repute for virtue and also for good fortune, in which there are a number of sons; from that family let them take one and introduce him to the father and forefathers of the dead man as their son, and, for the sake of the omen, let him be called so, that he may be the continuer of their family, the keeper of their hearth, and the minister of their sacred rites with better fortune than his father had; and when they have made this supplication, they shall make him heir according to law, and the offending person they shall leave nameless and childless and portionless when calamities such as these overtake him."
D: Refashioned:
"If the exiled person has no children, his relatives up to the degree of second cousins, on both the maternal and paternal sides, shall assemble. In consultation with the guardians of the law and the priests, they shall appoint someone to become the 5,040th citizen and heir to the vacant house. The fundamental principle is that no household among the 5,040 belongs exclusively to its inhabitant or family; each is simultaneously public and private property of the state.

"The state should strive to make every household as sacred and prosperous as possible. When a household is unfortunate -- stained by impiety, its owner dead or exiled without leaving heirs, whether through execution for murder or other capital crimes against the gods or fellow citizens, or through childless perpetual exile -- the house must first be purified and undergo legal expiation.

"Then the kinsmen and the guardians of the law shall identify the family in the state most distinguished for both virtue and good fortune, one that has several sons. They shall take one son from that family and formally adopt him as the child of the failed household's ancestors. For the sake of the omen, he shall bear the family name and serve as the continuer of their line, keeper of their hearth, and minister of their sacred rites -- with better fortune than his predecessor. After this formal adoption, he becomes the legal heir, while the offending person is left nameless, childless, and without inheritance."
D: Elegant: "If the exiled person is childless, relatives and authorities select an heir for the vacant household from the state's most virtuous and fortunate families. No house belongs solely to its inhabitant -- all 5,040 are the state's property. Polluted houses must be purified first, then restocked with an adopted heir who continues the family's sacred rites. The criminal is left nameless and portionless."
D: Summary: The Stranger provides for childless criminal households. Since all 5,040 lots are state property, vacated houses are purified and assigned an adopted heir chosen from the most virtuous families. The heir continues the household's sacred functions, while the criminal is erased from family memory.
D: Distilled: Childless criminal households are purified and assigned adopted heirs from virtuous families, since all 5,040 lots belong to the state; the criminal is erased from family record.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage reveals the deeply communal nature of property in the Laws' ideal state. The principle that 'no house of all the 5040 belongs to the inhabitant or to the whole family, but is the public and private property of the state' is one of the Laws' most radical claims about property rights. While less extreme than the Republic's abolition of private property for the guardian class (416d-417b), it establishes that property rights in the Laws are always conditional on the state's needs. The selection of heirs based on both virtue and good fortune (eutychia) is characteristic of the Laws' pragmatic synthesis of moral and practical considerations. The formal adoption ritual -- introduction to the ancestors, naming, and installation as keeper of hearth and rites -- reflects the centrality of the oikos (household) as both an economic unit and a religious institution in Greek life. The ancestral hearth (hestia) was literally the religious center of the home, connecting to the civic hearth of Hestia discussed in 9.7. The erasure of the criminal's name and family line (becoming 'nameless, childless, and portionless') is a form of damnatio memoriae -- total obliteration from social existence.

D: Original: Now the boundaries of some things do not touch one another, but there is a borderland which comes in between, preventing them from touching. And we were saying that actions done from passion are of this nature, and come in between the voluntary and involuntary. If a person be convicted of having inflicted wounds in a passion, in the first place he shall pay twice the amount of the injury, if the wound be curable, or, if incurable, four times the amount of the injury; or if the wound be curable, and at the same time cause great and notable disgrace to the wounded person, he shall pay fourfold. And whenever any one in wounding another injures not only the sufferer, but also the city, and makes him incapable of defending his country against the enemy, he, besides the other penalties, shall pay a penalty for the loss which the state has incurred. And the penalty shall be, that in addition to his own times of service, he shall serve on behalf of the disabled person, and shall take his place in war; or, if he refuse, he shall be liable to be convicted by law of refusal to serve.
D: Narrative:
"Now the boundaries of some things do not touch one another, but there is a borderland which comes in between, preventing them from touching. And we were saying that actions done from passion are of this nature, and come in between the voluntary and involuntary.

"If a person be convicted of having inflicted wounds in a passion, in the first place he shall pay twice the amount of the injury, if the wound be curable, or, if incurable, four times the amount of the injury; or if the wound be curable, and at the same time cause great and notable disgrace to the wounded person, he shall pay fourfold.

"And whenever any one in wounding another injures not only the sufferer, but also the city, and makes him incapable of defending his country against the enemy, he, besides the other penalties, shall pay a penalty for the loss which the state has incurred. And the penalty shall be, that in addition to his own times of service, he shall serve on behalf of the disabled person, and shall take his place in war; or, if he refuse, he shall be liable to be convicted by law of refusal to serve."
D: Refashioned:
"Some categories do not share clear boundaries but have a borderland between them. Actions done from passion are precisely this -- they fall in the borderland between voluntary and involuntary.

"If someone is convicted of wounding another in a fit of passion, the penalties shall be as follows: for a curable wound, double the assessed damage. For an incurable wound, four times the damage. If the wound is curable but causes the victim great and conspicuous disgrace, the penalty is fourfold.

"Furthermore, whenever a wound not only harms the individual victim but also damages the state by rendering the person incapable of military service, the attacker must pay an additional penalty for the state's loss. This penalty takes a specific form: in addition to fulfilling his own military obligations, the attacker must serve in the military on behalf of the person he disabled, taking his place in war. If he refuses, he may be prosecuted for refusal to serve."
D: Elegant: "Passionate wounding falls in the borderland between voluntary and involuntary. Penalties: double compensation for curable wounds, quadruple for incurable or disgraceful ones. If the wound renders the victim unfit for military service, the attacker must serve in the military on the victim's behalf, in addition to his own service obligations. Refusal to serve is a prosecutable offense."
D: Summary: The Stranger addresses wounding in passion, which occupies a borderland between voluntary and involuntary. Penalties are proportional: double for curable wounds, quadruple for incurable or disgraceful ones. If the wound renders the victim unfit for military duty, the attacker must personally serve in the victim's place.
D: Distilled: Passionate wounding is penalized by proportional compensation (double for curable, quadruple for incurable or disgraceful wounds), plus mandatory substitute military service if the wound disabled the victim.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The metaphor of 'borderland' (methorios) between the voluntary and involuntary, applied to passionate action, elegantly recapitulates the theoretical discussion from 9.32. The graduated penalty structure -- doubling for curable wounds, quadrupling for incurable ones, and a separate multiplier for wounds causing public disgrace -- represents a sophisticated approach to damages assessment that accounts for both physical and social harm. The military service provision is particularly innovative: by requiring the attacker to serve in the disabled person's place, the law simultaneously compensates the state for its military loss and imposes a personal burden on the attacker that is directly proportional to the harm he caused. This penalty has no close parallel in known Greek law and anticipates the logic of specific performance in modern civil law. The distinction between harm to the individual and harm to the state recognizes that some injuries have public as well as private dimensions -- a concept reflected in modern categories of crimes against the state versus civil torts.

D: Original: The compensation for the injury, whether to be twofold or threefold or fourfold, shall be fixed by the judges who convict him. And if, in like manner, a brother wounds a brother, the parents and kindred of either sex, including the children of cousins, whether on the male or female side, shall meet, and when they have judged the cause, they shall entrust the assessment of damages to the parents, as is natural; and if the estimate be disputed, then the kinsmen on the male side shall make the estimate, or if they cannot, they shall commit the matter to the guardians of the law. And when similar charges of wounding are brought by children against their parents, those who are more than sixty years of age, having children of their own, not adopted, shall be required to decide; and if any one is convicted, they shall determine whether he or she ought to die, or suffer some other punishment either greater than death, or, at any rate, not much less. A kinsman of the offender shall not be allowed to judge the cause, not even if he be of the age which is prescribed by the law. If a slave in a fit of anger wound a freeman, the owner of the slave shall give him up to the wounded man, who may do as he pleases with him, and if be not give him up he shall himself make good the injury. And if any one says that the slave and the wounded man are conspiring together, let him argue the point, and if he is cast, he shall pay for the wrong three times over, but if he gains his case, the freeman who conspired with the slave shall reliable to an action for kidnapping. And if any one unintentionally wounds another he shall simply pay for the harm, for no legislator is able to control chance. In such a case the judges shall be the same as those who are appointed in the case of children suing their parents; and they shall estimate the amount of the injury.
D: Narrative:
"The compensation for the injury, whether to be twofold or threefold or fourfold, shall be fixed by the judges who convict him.

"And if, in like manner, a brother wounds a brother, the parents and kindred of either sex, including the children of cousins, whether on the male or female side, shall meet, and when they have judged the cause, they shall entrust the assessment of damages to the parents, as is natural; and if the estimate be disputed, then the kinsmen on the male side shall make the estimate, or if they cannot, they shall commit the matter to the guardians of the law.

"And when similar charges of wounding are brought by children against their parents, those who are more than sixty years of age, having children of their own, not adopted, shall be required to decide; and if any one is convicted, they shall determine whether he or she ought to die, or suffer some other punishment either greater than death, or, at any rate, not much less. A kinsman of the offender shall not be allowed to judge the cause, not even if he be of the age which is prescribed by the law.

"If a slave in a fit of anger wound a freeman, the owner of the slave shall give him up to the wounded man, who may do as he pleases with him, and if he not give him up he shall himself make good the injury. And if any one says that the slave and the wounded man are conspiring together, let him argue the point, and if he is cast, he shall pay for the wrong three times over, but if he gains his case, the freeman who conspired with the slave shall be liable to an action for kidnapping.

"And if any one unintentionally wounds another he shall simply pay for the harm, for no legislator is able to control chance. In such a case the judges shall be the same as those who are appointed in the case of children suing their parents; and they shall estimate the amount of the injury."
D: Refashioned:
"The exact compensation -- whether double, triple, or quadruple -- shall be determined by the judges who hear the case.

"When a brother wounds a brother, the parents and relatives of both sides, including second cousins on both the male and female line, shall assemble and judge the matter. They shall naturally delegate the assessment of damages to the parents. If the parents' estimate is disputed, the paternal relatives shall decide; if they cannot resolve it, the matter goes to the guardians of the law.

"When children bring wounding charges against their parents, the judges must be citizens over sixty who have biological children of their own (not adopted). If a parent is convicted, these judges shall determine whether the penalty should be death or some other punishment of comparable severity -- not much less than death. No kinsman of the accused may serve as judge, regardless of his age or qualifications.

"If a slave wounds a free person in anger, the slave's owner must surrender the slave to the wounded person, who may do whatever he wishes with him. If the owner refuses, he must personally compensate for the injury. If someone claims the slave and the wounded person staged the incident in collusion, the claim may be argued in court. If the claim fails, the accuser pays triple damages. If it succeeds, the free person who conspired with the slave faces prosecution for kidnapping.

"If someone wounds another unintentionally, he simply pays for the harm caused, since no legislator can control chance. The judges in such cases shall be the same as those who decide cases of children suing parents, and they shall assess the injury."
D: Elegant: "Judges fix the exact compensation. Family wounding cases (brother on brother) are judged by assembled relatives, with parents assessing damages. When children accuse parents of wounding, judges must be citizens over sixty with their own children; penalties range up to death. No relative of the accused may judge. Slaves who wound free persons are surrendered to the victim. Unintentional wounding requires simple compensation, since no law can control chance."
D: Summary: The Stranger details wounding provisions for specific relationships: family wounds are judged by assembled relatives with parents assessing damages; children suing parents require independent elderly judges with severe penalties possible; slave assaults on free persons result in surrender of the slave; unintentional wounding requires only compensation for the harm.
D: Distilled: Family wounding cases are judged by relatives, children's charges against parents require independent elderly judges with penalties up to death, and unintentional wounding requires only compensation since law cannot control chance.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The family adjudication system for sibling wounding reflects the Greek tradition of handling intra-family disputes within the family unit rather than through public courts -- the oikos as self-governing legal entity. The requirement that judges of parental wounding charges must be over sixty with their own biological children ensures both life experience and personal understanding of the parent-child relationship. The prohibition against kinsmen serving as judges in these cases establishes a conflict-of-interest principle. The slave-and-freeman collusion provision addresses a practical concern: a free person might arrange to be 'wounded' by a slave to obtain the slave as property. The triple penalty for false accusations of collusion deters abuse of this defense. The final principle -- 'no legislator is able to control chance' -- echoes the acknowledgment from 9.49 that law can only address generalities. Compare Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics 3.1 (1110a-1111b) on the distinction between voluntary and involuntary action, where chance (tuche) features as a category beyond moral assessment.

D: Original: All the preceding injuries and every kind of assault are deeds of violence; and every man, woman, or child ought to consider that the elder has the precedence of the younger in honour, both among the Gods and also among men who would live in security and happiness. Wherefore it is a foul thing and hateful to the Gods to see an elder man assaulted by a younger in the city; and it is reasonable that a young man when struck by an elder should lightly endure his anger, laying up in store for himself a like honour when he is old. Let this be the law:-Every one shall reverence his elder in word and deed; he shall respect any one who is twenty years older than himself, whether male or female, regarding him or her as his father or mother; and he shall abstain from laying hands on any one who is of an age to have been his father or his mother, out of reverence to the Gods who preside over birth; similarly he shall keep his hands from a stranger, whether he be an old inhabitant or newly arrived; he shall not venture to correct such an one by blows, either as the aggressor or in self-defence. If he thinks that some stranger has struck him out of wantonness or insolence, and ought to be punished, he shall take him to the wardens of the city, but let him not strike him, that the stranger may be kept far away from the possibility of lifting up his hand against a citizen, and let the wardens of the city take the offender and examine him, not forgetting their duty to the God of Strangers, and in case the stranger appears to have struck the citizen unjustly, let them inflict upon him as many blows with the scourge as he has himself inflicted, and quell his presumption.
D: Narrative:
"All the preceding injuries and every kind of assault are deeds of violence; and every man, woman, or child ought to consider that the elder has the precedence of the younger in honour, both among the Gods and also among men who would live in security and happiness. Wherefore it is a foul thing and hateful to the Gods to see an elder man assaulted by a younger in the city; and it is reasonable that a young man when struck by an elder should lightly endure his anger, laying up in store for himself a like honour when he is old.

"Let this be the law: Every one shall reverence his elder in word and deed; he shall respect any one who is twenty years older than himself, whether male or female, regarding him or her as his father or mother; and he shall abstain from laying hands on any one who is of an age to have been his father or his mother, out of reverence to the Gods who preside over birth; similarly he shall keep his hands from a stranger, whether he be an old inhabitant or newly arrived; he shall not venture to correct such an one by blows, either as the aggressor or in self-defence.

"If he thinks that some stranger has struck him out of wantonness or insolence, and ought to be punished, he shall take him to the wardens of the city, but let him not strike him, that the stranger may be kept far away from the possibility of lifting up his hand against a citizen, and let the wardens of the city take the offender and examine him, not forgetting their duty to the God of Strangers, and in case the stranger appears to have struck the citizen unjustly, let them inflict upon him as many blows with the scourge as he has himself inflicted, and quell his presumption."
D: Refashioned:
"All the injuries discussed so far are acts of violence. Every person -- man, woman, and child -- must understand that elders take precedence over the younger in honor, both among the gods and among people who wish to live securely and happily. It is therefore disgraceful and hateful to the gods when a younger person assaults an elder in the city. When a young person is struck by an elder, it is reasonable for the young person to endure the blow patiently, storing up the same deference for himself when he reaches old age.

"Let the law be this: Everyone shall show reverence to their elders in word and deed. Anyone twenty or more years older shall be treated as a father or mother, regardless of sex. No one shall lay hands on anyone old enough to have been his parent, out of reverence for the gods who preside over birth. The same protection extends to strangers, whether long-established or newly arrived; no one shall presume to correct a stranger with blows, either as aggressor or in self-defense.

"If a citizen believes a stranger has struck him out of wantonness or insolence, he shall bring the stranger before the city wardens rather than striking back -- this prevents any situation where a stranger might raise his hand against a citizen. The wardens shall examine the case, mindful of their obligation to Zeus, the God of Strangers. If the stranger is found to have struck the citizen unjustly, the wardens shall scourge him with exactly as many blows as he inflicted, suppressing his presumption."
D: Elegant: "Elders hold precedence in honor among both gods and humans. Assaulting an elder is foul; a young person struck by an elder should endure it patiently. The law: revere anyone twenty or more years older as a parent. Never strike anyone old enough to be your parent, or any stranger. If a stranger strikes you, bring him to the wardens rather than retaliating. The wardens, honoring the God of Strangers, shall scourge him with equal blows if he struck unjustly."
D: Summary: The Stranger establishes laws protecting elders and strangers from assault. Anyone twenty or more years older must be treated as a parent. Young people must endure blows from elders patiently. Strangers who assault citizens are examined by wardens and scourged; citizens must not retaliate directly.
D: Distilled: Elders and strangers receive special legal protection from assault: anyone twenty years older must be treated as a parent, and disputes with strangers must be referred to city wardens rather than resolved by personal retaliation.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The principle of elder reverence is deeply rooted in Greek culture and appears prominently in both Spartan custom (where young men stood for elders and deferred to their authority) and Athenian social norms. The specific threshold of twenty years -- treating anyone twenty years older as equivalent to a parent -- provides a precise, enforceable rule. The protection of strangers reflects the Greek institution of xenia (guest-friendship) and the religious importance of Zeus Xenios (Zeus as protector of strangers), explicitly mentioned here. The requirement to bring complaints to wardens rather than retaliating directly serves multiple purposes: it prevents escalation, maintains public order, and ensures that the stranger receives fair examination rather than mob justice. The scourging penalty -- exactly matching the number of blows inflicted -- applies the law of retaliation (lex talionis) in its most literal form. Compare Odyssey 14.56-58, where even the swineherd Eumaeus invokes Zeus Xenios to justify hospitality to the disguised Odysseus.

D: Original: But if he be innocent, they shall threaten and rebuke the man who arrested him, and let them both go. If a person strikes another of the same age or somewhat older than himself, who has no children, whether he be an old man who strikes an old man or a young man who strikes a young man, let the person struck defend himself in the natural way without a weapon and with his hands only. He who, being more than forty years of age, dares to fight with another, whether he be the aggressor or in self defence, shall be regarded as rude and ill-mannered and slavish;-this will be a disgraceful punishment, and therefore suitable to him. The obedient nature will readily yield to such exhortations, but the disobedient, who heeds not the prelude, shall have the law ready for him:-If any man smite another who is older than himself, either by twenty or by more years, in the first place, he who is at hand, not being younger than the combatants, nor their equal in age, shall separate them, or be disgraced according to law; but if he be the equal in age of the person who is struck or younger, he shall defend the person injured as he would a brother or father or still older relative.
D: Narrative:
"But if he be innocent, they shall threaten and rebuke the man who arrested him, and let them both go.

"If a person strikes another of the same age or somewhat older than himself, who has no children, whether he be an old man who strikes an old man or a young man who strikes a young man, let the person struck defend himself in the natural way without a weapon and with his hands only.

"He who, being more than forty years of age, dares to fight with another, whether he be the aggressor or in self-defence, shall be regarded as rude and ill-mannered and slavish; this will be a disgraceful punishment, and therefore suitable to him.

"The obedient nature will readily yield to such exhortations, but the disobedient, who heeds not the prelude, shall have the law ready for him: If any man smite another who is older than himself, either by twenty or by more years, in the first place, he who is at hand, not being younger than the combatants, nor their equal in age, shall separate them, or be disgraced according to law; but if he be the equal in age of the person who is struck or younger, he shall defend the person injured as he would a brother or father or still older relative."
D: Refashioned:
"But if the stranger is found innocent, the wardens shall rebuke the citizen who brought the false charge, and both parties shall be released.

"When someone strikes a person of roughly the same age or slightly older, and neither party has children, whether the combatants are both old or both young, the person struck may defend himself naturally -- with his hands only, without weapons.

"Any person over forty who engages in a brawl, whether as aggressor or in self-defense, shall be stigmatized as crude, ill-mannered, and slavish. This social disgrace is itself an appropriate punishment.

"Those with good character will heed these exhortations readily. For the disobedient, the formal law is as follows: If anyone strikes a person twenty or more years his senior, any bystander who is older than both combatants must intervene to separate them or face legal disgrace. If the bystander is the same age as the victim or younger, he must defend the injured person as he would defend a brother, father, or still older relative."
D: Elegant: "If the stranger is innocent, the accuser is rebuked and both go free. Among age-equals, the struck person may defend himself with bare hands only. Fighting after age forty is deemed slavish and disgraceful. The formal law: bystanders must intervene when an elder is struck -- older bystanders must separate the combatants, younger ones must defend the victim as they would a family member."
D: Summary: The Stranger prescribes rules for fighting between age-equals (bare-handed self-defense only), stigmatizes brawling after age forty as slavish, and requires bystanders to intervene when elders are assaulted -- older bystanders must separate combatants while younger ones must actively defend the victim.
D: Distilled: Self-defense between age-equals must be bare-handed, brawling after forty is shameful, and bystanders have a legal duty to intervene when an elder is struck.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The social stigma of being called 'slavish' (andrapododous) for fighting after forty reflects the Greek ideal of sophrosyne (self-control) as a mark of free and cultured citizens. Slaves were associated with lack of self-control; a free citizen who brawls demonstrates that he lacks the very quality that distinguishes him from a slave. The bystander duty to intervene creates a system of collective responsibility for public order -- citizens are not merely prohibited from wrongdoing but obligated to prevent it. This anticipates modern 'duty to rescue' laws, though the Laws' version is more demanding. The age-based differentiation of bystander duties (older must separate, younger must defend) is pragmatically wise: older bystanders have the authority to command respect from both parties, while younger ones have the physical capacity to actively protect the victim. The prelude-law structure is maintained here: social stigma (the prelude) is offered first; formal legal penalties follow for those unmoved by shame.

D: Original: Further, let him who dares to smite an elder be tried for assault, as I have said, and if he be found guilty, let him be imprisoned for a period of not less than a year, or if the judges approve of a longer period, their decision shall be final. But if a stranger or metic smite one who is older by twenty years or more, the same law shall hold about the bystanders assisting, and he who is found guilty in such a suit, if he be a stranger but not resident, shall be imprisoned during a period of two years; and a metic who disobeys the laws shall be imprisoned for three years, unless the court assign him a longer term. And let him who was present in any of these cases and did not assist according to law be punished, if he be of the highest dass, by paying a fine of a mina; or if he be of the second class, of fifty drachmas; or if of the third class, by a fine of thirty drachmas; or if he be of the fourth class, by a fine of twenty drachmas; and the generals and taxiarchs and phylarchs and hipparchs shall form the court in such cases.
D: Narrative:
"Further, let him who dares to smite an elder be tried for assault, as I have said, and if he be found guilty, let him be imprisoned for a period of not less than a year, or if the judges approve of a longer period, their decision shall be final.

"But if a stranger or metic smite one who is older by twenty years or more, the same law shall hold about the bystanders assisting, and he who is found guilty in such a suit, if he be a stranger but not resident, shall be imprisoned during a period of two years; and a metic who disobeys the laws shall be imprisoned for three years, unless the court assign him a longer term.

"And let him who was present in any of these cases and did not assist according to law be punished, if he be of the highest class, by paying a fine of a mina; or if he be of the second class, of fifty drachmas; or if of the third class, by a fine of thirty drachmas; or if he be of the fourth class, by a fine of twenty drachmas; and the generals and taxiarchs and phylarchs and hipparchs shall form the court in such cases."
D: Refashioned:
"Furthermore, anyone who strikes an elder shall be tried for assault and, if convicted, imprisoned for at least one year. If the judges deem a longer sentence appropriate, their judgment is final.

"If a foreigner or resident alien strikes someone twenty or more years his senior, the same bystander obligations apply. A non-resident foreigner convicted of such assault shall be imprisoned for two years. A resident alien shall be imprisoned for three years, unless the court imposes a longer term.

"Bystanders who fail to assist as required by law shall be fined according to their property class: citizens of the highest class pay one mina; those of the second class, fifty drachmas; the third class, thirty drachmas; the fourth class, twenty drachmas. The court for these cases shall consist of the generals, taxiarchs, phylarchs, and hipparchs."
D: Elegant: "Assaulting an elder: minimum one year imprisonment for citizens, two years for non-resident foreigners, three for resident aliens. Bystanders who fail to intervene are fined by property class -- from twenty drachmas to one mina. Military officers form the court for these cases."
D: Summary: The Stranger prescribes graduated imprisonment for assaulting elders: at least one year for citizens, two for non-resident foreigners, three for resident aliens. Bystanders who fail to intervene face fines scaled to their property class. Military officers serve as judges.
D: Distilled: Assaulting an elder brings imprisonment graduated by the offender's status, while bystanders who fail to intervene face fines scaled to their wealth class.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The graduated imprisonment by status -- citizens (one year), non-resident foreigners (two years), resident aliens (three years) -- reverses the usual hierarchy where citizens receive lighter treatment. Here, the non-citizen receives harsher punishment for the same act, reflecting the greater presumption he violated: as a guest or resident, his duty to respect local customs and elders is even more fundamental than a citizen's, since it is grounded in the sacred obligations of xenia rather than mere civic duty. The property-class fines for negligent bystanders (one mina, fifty drachmas, thirty drachmas, twenty drachmas) create progressive penalties where the wealthy pay more -- the highest class pays five times what the lowest pays. The four property classes recall Solon's Athenian census classes (pentakosiomedimnoi, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes). The military court -- generals (strategoi), taxiarchs, phylarchs, and hipparchs -- is appropriate because the duty to defend elders is conceived as analogous to military duty: both require courage, physical intervention, and subordination of personal safety to communal obligation.

D: Original: Laws are partly framed for the sake of good men, in order to instruct them how they thay live on friendly terms with one another, and partly for the sake of those who refuse to be instructed, whose spirit cannot be subdued, or softened, or hindered from plunging into evil. These are the persons who cause the word to be spoken which I am about to utter; for them the legislator legislates of necessity, and in the hope that there may be no need of his laws. He who shall dare to lay violent hands upon his father or mother, or any still older relative, having no fear either of the wrath of the Gods above, or of the punishments that are spoken of in the world below, but transgresses in contempt of ancient and universal traditions as though he were too wise to believe in them, requires some extreme measure of prevention. Now death is not the worst that can happen to men; far worse are the punishments which are said to pursue them in the world below. But although they are most true tales, they work on such souls no prevention; for if they had any effect there would be no slayers of mothers, or impious hands lifted up against parents; and therefore the punishments of this world which are inflicted during life ought not in such cases to fall short, if possible, of the terrors of the world below. Let our enactment then be as follows:-If a man dare to strike his father or his mother, or their fathers or mothers, he being at the time of sound mind, then let any one who is at hand come to the rescue as has been already said, and the metic or stranger who comes to the rescue shall be called to the first place in the games; but if he do not come he shall suffer the punishment of perpetual exile. He who is not a metic, if he comes to the rescue, shall have praise, and if he do not come, blame.
D: Narrative:
"Laws are partly framed for the sake of good men, in order to instruct them how they may live on friendly terms with one another, and partly for the sake of those who refuse to be instructed, whose spirit cannot be subdued, or softened, or hindered from plunging into evil. These are the persons who cause the word to be spoken which I am about to utter; for them the legislator legislates of necessity, and in the hope that there may be no need of his laws.

"He who shall dare to lay violent hands upon his father or mother, or any still older relative, having no fear either of the wrath of the Gods above, or of the punishments that are spoken of in the world below, but transgresses in contempt of ancient and universal traditions as though he were too wise to believe in them, requires some extreme measure of prevention.

"Now death is not the worst that can happen to men; far worse are the punishments which are said to pursue them in the world below. But although they are most true tales, they work on such souls no prevention; for if they had any effect there would be no slayers of mothers, or impious hands lifted up against parents; and therefore the punishments of this world which are inflicted during life ought not in such cases to fall short, if possible, of the terrors of the world below.

"Let our enactment then be as follows: If a man dare to strike his father or his mother, or their fathers or mothers, he being at the time of sound mind, then let any one who is at hand come to the rescue as has been already said, and the metic or stranger who comes to the rescue shall be called to the first place in the games; but if he do not come he shall suffer the punishment of perpetual exile. He who is not a metic, if he comes to the rescue, shall have praise, and if he do not come, blame."
D: Refashioned:
"Laws serve two purposes: to teach good people how to live harmoniously with one another, and to restrain those who refuse instruction -- whose nature cannot be subdued, softened, or prevented from rushing toward evil. It is for these people that the legislator now speaks, reluctantly and in the hope that his laws will never need to be applied.

"Anyone who dares to lay violent hands on a parent or grandparent, fearing neither the wrath of the gods above nor the punishments awaiting him below, who transgresses in contempt of ancient and universal traditions as though he were too clever to believe in them, requires the most extreme preventive measures.

"Death is not the worst thing that can happen to a person -- the punishments said to await such criminals in the afterlife are far worse. But though these accounts are entirely true, they have no deterrent effect on such souls. If they did, no one would kill a mother or raise an impious hand against a parent. Therefore, the punishments imposed in this life must, as far as possible, match the terrors of the world below.

"The law shall be as follows: If anyone of sound mind dares to strike a parent or grandparent, every bystander must come to the rescue as previously stated. A resident alien or foreigner who intervenes shall be honored with the first place at the public games; one who fails to intervene shall be permanently exiled. A citizen who comes to the rescue shall receive public praise; one who does not shall receive public blame."
D: Elegant: "Laws instruct the good and restrain the incorrigible. For those who assault parents without fearing gods or afterlife punishments, earthly penalties must match the terrors of the underworld. The law: anyone of sound mind who strikes a parent or grandparent faces extreme measures. Bystanders must intervene -- resident aliens earn honors at the games for helping or face exile for failing; citizens earn praise or blame."
D: Summary: The Stranger distinguishes the dual purpose of law: instruction for the good and restraint for the incorrigible. Those who assault parents despite all religious deterrence require extreme punishment. Earthly penalties should match underworld terrors. Bystanders must intervene, with rewards and punishments scaled by status.
D: Distilled: Laws serve both to educate the good and to restrain the irredeemably wicked; those who assault parents despite all religious deterrence must face punishments as severe as the terrors of the afterlife.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The opening statement -- that laws serve both education and restraint -- encapsulates the entire penal philosophy of Book 9 in a single sentence. The passage then applies this dual function to the most extreme case: assault on parents, which violates not only human law but divine order and universal tradition. The Stranger's assertion that afterlife punishments are 'most true tales' that nevertheless fail to deter some people represents a remarkable pragmatic theology: religious truth exists but is insufficient as a sole basis for social order, requiring the supplement of earthly enforcement. The incentive structure for bystanders is carefully calibrated by status: resident aliens, who have the most to gain and lose from their standing in the community, face the highest stakes (games honors vs. permanent exile); citizens, whose position is more secure, face the softer consequences of praise or blame. This graduated incentive system reflects the Laws' general principle that obligations and rewards should be proportional to one's stake in the community.

D: Original: And if a slave come to the rescue, let him be made free, but if he do not come the rescue, let him receive 100 strokes of the whip, by order of the wardens of the agora, if the occurrence take place in the agora; or if somewhere in the city beyond the limits of the agora, any warden of the city is in residence shall punish him; or if in the country, then the commanders of the wardens of the country. If those who are near at the time be inhabitants of the same place, whether they be youths, or men, or women, let them come to the rescue and denounce him as the impious one; and he who does not come to the rescue shall fall under the curse of Zeus, the God of kindred and of ancestors, according to law. And if any one is found guilty of assaulting a parent, let him in the first place be for ever banished from the city into the country, and let him abstain from the temples; and if he do not abstain, the wardens of the country shall punish him with blows, or in any way which they please, and if he return he shall be put to death. And if any freeman eat or drink, or have any other sort of intercourse with him, or only meeting him have voluntarily touched him, he shall not enter into any temple, nor into the agora, nor into the city, until he is purified; for he should consider that he has become tainted by a curse. And if he disobeys the law, and pollutes the city and the temples contrary to law, and one of the magistrates sees him and does not indict him, when he gives in his account this omission shall be a most serious charge.
D: Narrative:
"And if a slave come to the rescue, let him be made free, but if he do not come to the rescue, let him receive 100 strokes of the whip, by order of the wardens of the agora, if the occurrence take place in the agora; or if somewhere in the city beyond the limits of the agora, any warden of the city who is in residence shall punish him; or if in the country, then the commanders of the wardens of the country.

"If those who are near at the time be inhabitants of the same place, whether they be youths, or men, or women, let them come to the rescue and denounce him as the impious one; and he who does not come to the rescue shall fall under the curse of Zeus, the God of kindred and of ancestors, according to law.

"And if any one is found guilty of assaulting a parent, let him in the first place be for ever banished from the city into the country, and let him abstain from the temples; and if he do not abstain, the wardens of the country shall punish him with blows, or in any way which they please, and if he return he shall be put to death.

"And if any freeman eat or drink, or have any other sort of intercourse with him, or only meeting him have voluntarily touched him, he shall not enter into any temple, nor into the agora, nor into the city, until he is purified; for he should consider that he has become tainted by a curse. And if he disobeys the law, and pollutes the city and the temples contrary to law, and one of the magistrates sees him and does not indict him, when he gives in his account this omission shall be a most serious charge."
D: Refashioned:
"If a slave comes to the rescue of an assaulted parent, he shall be freed. If he fails to intervene, he shall receive one hundred lashes -- administered by the marketplace wardens if the assault occurs in the marketplace, by the city wardens if elsewhere in the city, or by the country warden commanders if in the countryside.

"Every local inhabitant who is nearby -- whether young or old, male or female -- must come to the rescue and publicly denounce the assailant as impious. Anyone who fails to intervene shall fall under the curse of Zeus, the God of kinship and ancestors, as the law provides.

"If anyone is convicted of assaulting a parent, he shall be permanently banished from the city to the countryside, and he must keep away from all temples. If he enters a temple, the country wardens shall punish him by beating or any other means they choose. If he returns to the city, he shall be executed.

"Any free person who eats, drinks, associates with, or even voluntarily touches the convicted person shall be barred from temples, the marketplace, and the city until he undergoes purification -- for he must understand that he has been contaminated by a curse. If he disobeys this law and pollutes the city and temples, and any magistrate who witnesses this fails to indict him, that omission shall count as a most serious charge at the magistrate's accounting."
D: Elegant: "A slave who rescues an assaulted parent earns freedom; one who fails receives one hundred lashes. All nearby inhabitants -- regardless of age or sex -- must intervene and denounce the assailant or fall under Zeus' curse. The convicted parent-assaulter is permanently exiled to the countryside, banned from temples, and executed if he returns to the city. Anyone who has contact with him is polluted until purified. Magistrates who fail to enforce these rules face serious charges."
D: Summary: The Stranger establishes comprehensive enforcement for parent-assault laws. Slaves earn freedom for intervention. All inhabitants must denounce assailants or face divine curse. Convicted parent-assaulters are permanently exiled and executed if they return. Anyone who has contact with them becomes polluted. Magistrates face charges for failing to enforce.
D: Distilled: Enforcement of parent-assault laws involves rewards for slave rescuers (freedom), universal bystander obligations under divine curse, permanent exile for offenders, contagious pollution from contact, and mandatory indictment by magistrates.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The provision freeing a slave who intervenes to protect a parent from assault is one of the Laws' most striking social provisions. It creates a powerful incentive for slaves to enforce communal norms and acknowledges that moral action can transcend social status. The universal bystander obligation -- extending to 'youths, or men, or women' -- is notably inclusive for an ancient Greek legal code. The curse of Zeus Patroos (Zeus of ancestors) and Zeus Homognios (Zeus of kindred) invokes the most powerful divine sanctions available in the Greek religious framework. The concept of contagious pollution -- that even casual contact with the convicted parent-assaulter contaminates the innocent party -- functions as a powerful social enforcement mechanism: the entire community has a self-interested reason to avoid the criminal and ensure his exile is maintained. The magistrate accountability provision (failure to indict counts as a serious charge at the euthynai, or annual accounting) ensures that officials cannot quietly tolerate violations.

D: Original: If a slave strike a freeman, whether a stranger or a citizen, let any one who is present come to the rescue, or pay the penalty already mentioned; and let the bystanders bind him, and deliver him up to the injured person, and he receiving him shall put him in chains, and inflict on him as many stripes as he pleases; but having punished him he must surrender him to his master according to law, and not deprive him of his property. Let the law be as follows:-The slave who strikes a freeman, not at the command of the magistrates, his owner shall receive bound from the man whom he has stricken, and not release him until the slave has persuaded the man whom he has stricken that he ought to be released. And let there be the same laws about women in relation to women, about men and women in relation to one another.
D: Narrative:
"If a slave strike a freeman, whether a stranger or a citizen, let any one who is present come to the rescue, or pay the penalty already mentioned; and let the bystanders bind him, and deliver him up to the injured person, and he receiving him shall put him in chains, and inflict on him as many stripes as he pleases; but having punished him he must surrender him to his master according to law, and not deprive him of his property.

"Let the law be as follows: The slave who strikes a freeman, not at the command of the magistrates, his owner shall receive bound from the man whom he has stricken, and not release him until the slave has persuaded the man whom he has stricken that he ought to be released.

"And let there be the same laws about women in relation to women, about men and women in relation to one another."
D: Refashioned:
"If a slave strikes a free person, whether a stranger or a citizen, every bystander must come to the rescue or face the penalties already prescribed. The bystanders shall seize and bind the slave and deliver him to the injured person. The injured person may chain the slave and inflict as many lashes as he sees fit. However, after punishing the slave, he must return him to his master according to law and must not keep him as property.

"The formal law is this: When a slave strikes a free person without orders from the magistrates, the slave's owner shall receive the slave back in chains from the person struck and shall not release him until the slave has personally persuaded the person he struck that he deserves to be released.

"These same laws shall apply equally to women in relation to women, and to men and women in relation to one another."
D: Elegant: "When a slave strikes a free person, bystanders must intervene. The slave is bound and delivered to the victim, who may punish him with as many lashes as he wishes but must then return him to his owner. The owner must keep the slave bound until the slave personally persuades the victim to consent to his release. These laws apply equally across all gender combinations."
D: Summary: The Stranger prescribes that slaves who strike free persons are seized by bystanders, delivered to the victim for punishment, and returned to their master in chains. The slave remains bound until he personally persuades his victim to consent to his release. All assault laws apply equally regardless of gender.
D: Distilled: Slaves who strike free persons are delivered to the victim for punishment and remain bound until they personally persuade the victim to consent to their release; all assault laws apply equally regardless of gender.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This final passage of Book 9 addresses the lowest-status form of assault (slave on free person) and includes a remarkable restorative element: the slave must personally persuade the victim that he deserves release. This is not merely a punishment but an interpersonal reconciliation process -- the slave must engage the victim's judgment and mercy, and the victim retains agency over when the reconciliation is complete. This mirrors the Laws' broader commitment to converting enmity into friendship through the legal process (9.21). The requirement that the slave be returned to his master and not kept by the victim prevents the assault from becoming a mechanism for property transfer -- protecting both the slave's owner and the integrity of the penalty. The closing statement that 'the same laws' apply 'about women in relation to women, about men and women in relation to one another' extends gender neutrality across the entire assault code, a notably progressive principle for fourth-century BCE Greek legislation. This egalitarian closing note is fitting for the conclusion of Book 9, which has consistently tried to balance universal moral principles with the practical realities of Greek social hierarchy.

D: Original:
And now having spoken of assaults, let us sum up all acts of violence under a single law, which shall be as follows:-No one shall take or carry away any of his neighbour's goods, neither shall he use anything which is his neighbour's without the consent of the owner; for these are the offences which are and have been, and will ever be, the source of all the aforesaid evils. The greatest of them are excesses and insolences of youth, and are offences against the greatest when they are done against religion; and especially great when in violation of public and holy rites, or of the partly-common rites in which tribes and phratries share; and in the second degree great when they are committed against private rites and sepulchres, and in the third degree (not to repeat the acts formerly mentioned), when insults are offered to parents; the fourth kind of violence is when any one, regardless of the authority of the rulers, takes or carries away or makes use of anything which belongs to them, not having their consent; and the fifth kind is when the violation of the civil rights of an individual demands reparation. There should be a common law embracing all these cases. For we have already said in general terms what shall be the punishment of sacrilege, whether fraudulent or violent, and now we have to determine what is to be the punishment of those who speak or act insolently toward the Gods. But first we must give them an admonition which may be in the following terms:-No one who in obedience to the laws believed that there were Gods, ever intentionally did any unholy act, or uttered any unlawful word; but he who did must have supposed one of three things-either that they did not exist,-which is the first possibility, or secondly, that, if they did, they took no care of man, or thirdly, that they were easily appeased and turned aside from their purpose, by sacrifices and prayers.

What shall we say or do to these persons?

My good friend, let us first hear the jests which I suspect that they in their superiority will utter against us.

What jests?
D: Narrative:
"And now, having spoken of assaults, let us sum up all acts of violence under a single law, which shall be as follows: No one shall take or carry away any of his neighbour's goods, neither shall he use anything which is his neighbour's without the consent of the owner; for these are the offences which are and have been, and will ever be, the source of all the aforesaid evils. The greatest of them are excesses and insolences of youth, and are offences against the greatest when they are done against religion; and especially great when in violation of public and holy rites, or of the partly-common rites in which tribes and phratries share; and in the second degree great when they are committed against private rites and sepulchres, and in the third degree -- not to repeat the acts formerly mentioned -- when insults are offered to parents; the fourth kind of violence is when any one, regardless of the authority of the rulers, takes or carries away or makes use of anything which belongs to them, not having their consent; and the fifth kind is when the violation of the civil rights of an individual demands reparation. There should be a common law embracing all these cases. For we have already said in general terms what shall be the punishment of sacrilege, whether fraudulent or violent, and now we have to determine what is to be the punishment of those who speak or act insolently toward the Gods. But first we must give them an admonition which may be in the following terms: No one who in obedience to the laws believed that there were Gods, ever intentionally did any unholy act, or uttered any unlawful word; but he who did must have supposed one of three things -- either that they did not exist, which is the first possibility, or secondly, that, if they did, they took no care of man, or thirdly, that they were easily appeased and turned aside from their purpose, by sacrifices and prayers."

"What shall we say or do to these persons?" asked Cleinias.

"My good friend, let us first hear the jests which I suspect that they in their superiority will utter against us," replied the Athenian.

"What jests?" asked Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Now that we have dealt with assault, let us bring all acts of violence under a single law: No person shall take, remove, or use any neighbour's property without the owner's consent. These offences have always been -- and always will be -- the root of every evil we have discussed. The worst are the reckless excesses of youth, and they are gravest when committed against religion -- especially against public sacred rites or the shared ceremonies of tribes and brotherhoods. Next in severity come offences against private religious rites and tombs. Third are insults against parents.
