Fourth is when someone defies the rulers' authority by seizing or using what belongs to them without permission. Fifth is when an individual's civil rights are violated and demand redress. A single law should cover all these cases.

We have already laid down the general punishment for sacrilege, whether by fraud or force. Now we must determine the punishment for those who speak or act with contempt toward the gods. But first, an admonition: No person who genuinely believed in the gods -- in obedience to the laws -- ever intentionally committed an unholy act or spoke an unlawful word. Anyone who did so must have held one of three beliefs: first, that the gods do not exist at all; second, that they exist but take no interest in human affairs; or third, that they can be easily bought off and turned from their purpose through sacrifices and prayers."

"What shall we say or do about such people?" asked Cleinias.

"My friend, let us first hear what clever objections they will throw at us from their position of imagined superiority," replied the Athenian.

"What objections?" asked Cleinias.
D: Elegant:
All acts of violence fall under one law: no one shall take or use a neighbour's property without consent. The worst offences are the excesses of youth against religion -- against public rites, then private rites and tombs, then parents, then rulers' authority, then individual rights.

We have already addressed sacrilege. Now we must determine the punishment for contempt toward the gods. But first, an admonition: no true believer ever intentionally committed an impious act. Whoever did must have held one of three views -- that gods do not exist, that they do not care about humanity, or that they can be bribed with sacrifices and prayers.

"What do we say to such people?" asked Cleinias. "Let us first hear their objections," replied the Athenian.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger proposes unifying all acts of violence under a single law, ranking offences by severity from religious violations down to civil wrongs. Before prescribing punishment for impiety, he identifies three root beliefs behind all godlessness: atheism, divine indifference, or the notion that gods can be bribed. He suggests hearing the impious person's objections before proceeding.
D: Distilled: All impiety stems from one of three beliefs: that gods do not exist, that they are indifferent to human affairs, or that they can be appeased through bribes of sacrifice and prayer.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger (primary), Cleinias. This passage serves as the transitional bridge from the criminal law of Book 9 into the theological prelude of Book 10. The threefold classification of impiety -- atheism, divine negligence, and divine corruptibility -- structures the entire argument of Book 10 and represents Plato's systematic taxonomy of irreligion, the first of its kind in Western philosophy. The ranking of offences (sacred before secular, public before private) reflects the Republic's principle that justice in the city mirrors justice in the soul (Republic IV, 427b-434d). The insistence on admonition before punishment anticipates the persuasive preludes Plato advocates throughout the Laws. Cross-reference: the idea that genuine belief prevents wrongdoing echoes the Socratic thesis that no one errs willingly (Protagoras 345d-e).

D: Original: They will make some irreverent speech of this sort:-"O inhabitants of Athens, and Sparta, and Cnosus," they will reply, "in that you speak truly; for some of us deny the very existence of the Gods, while others, as you say, are of opinion that they do not care about us; and others that they are turned from their course by gifts. Now we have a right to claim, as you yourself allowed, in the matter of laws, that before you are hard upon us and threaten us, you should argue with us and convince us-you should first attempt to teach and persuade us that there are Gods by reasonable evidences, and also that they are too good to be unrighteous, or to be propitiated, or turned from their course by gifts. For when we hear such things said of them by those who are esteemed to be the best of poets, and orators, and prophets, and priests, and by innumerable others, the thoughts of most of us are not set upon abstaining from unrighteous acts, but upon doing them and atoning for them. When lawgivers profess that they are gentle and not stern, we think that they should first of all use persuasion to us, and show us the existence of Gods, if not in a better manner than other men, at any rate in a truer; and who knows but that we shall hearken to you? If then our request is a fair one, please to accept our challenge."
D: Narrative: "They will make some irreverent speech of this sort: 'O inhabitants of Athens, and Sparta, and Cnosus,' they will reply, 'in that you speak truly; for some of us deny the very existence of the Gods, while others, as you say, are of opinion that they do not care about us; and others that they are turned from their course by gifts. Now we have a right to claim, as you yourself allowed, in the matter of laws, that before you are hard upon us and threaten us, you should argue with us and convince us -- you should first attempt to teach and persuade us that there are Gods by reasonable evidences, and also that they are too good to be unrighteous, or to be propitiated, or turned from their course by gifts. For when we hear such things said of them by those who are esteemed to be the best of poets, and orators, and prophets, and priests, and by innumerable others, the thoughts of most of us are not set upon abstaining from unrighteous acts, but upon doing them and atoning for them. When lawgivers profess that they are gentle and not stern, we think that they should first of all use persuasion to us, and show us the existence of Gods, if not in a better manner than other men, at any rate in a truer; and who knows but that we shall hearken to you? If then our request is a fair one, please to accept our challenge.'"
D: Refashioned:
"They will make some irreverent speech along these lines: 'O people of Athens, Sparta, and Cnosus -- you speak truly enough. Some of us deny the gods exist at all. Others believe, as you say, that the gods simply do not care about us. Still others hold that gods can be swayed from their course by gifts.

But we have a right to demand -- as you yourselves have allowed in the matter of law -- that before you come down hard on us with threats, you first argue with us and convince us. You should attempt to teach and persuade us, through reasonable evidence, that gods exist, and furthermore that they are too good to act unjustly or to be bought off and diverted from their course by offerings.

After all, when we hear such things said about the gods by the most respected poets, orators, prophets, priests, and countless others, most of us are not inspired to abstain from wrongdoing -- we are encouraged to do wrong and then atone for it afterward. If lawgivers claim to be gentle rather than harsh, they should use persuasion first. Show us the gods exist -- if not more skillfully than others have tried, then at least more truthfully. Who knows? Perhaps we will listen. If our request is fair, accept our challenge.'"
D: Elegant: The impious will demand: "Before you punish us, persuade us. Prove through reason that gods exist, that they are too good to act unjustly, and that they cannot be bribed. When the best poets, prophets, and priests portray gods who accept bribes, we are encouraged not to avoid wrongdoing but to commit it and atone afterward. If you claim to be gentle lawmakers, use persuasion before punishment. Convince us, and perhaps we will listen."
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger ventriloquizes the impious, who demand rational proof of the gods' existence and goodness before accepting punishment. They argue that traditional poets, prophets, and priests actually encourage wrongdoing by depicting gods who can be appeased, and that gentle lawmakers owe their citizens persuasion before coercion.
D: Distilled: The impious fairly demand that lawmakers prove through rational argument -- not mere tradition -- that gods exist, care about justice, and cannot be bribed, before imposing penalties for disbelief.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, ventriloquizing the impious. This passage establishes the methodological principle that law requires rational persuasion (peitho), not just coercion (bia) -- a central theme of the Laws. The impious speaker's challenge is presented sympathetically; Plato grants the atheist a fair hearing, unprecedented in ancient legal thought. The critique of poets who depict corruptible gods echoes Republic II (377d-383c), where Socrates censors myths showing gods behaving immorally. The reference to poets, orators, prophets, and priests as sources of corruption anticipates the critique of traditional theology found in Euthyphro (5e-6c). The demand for "reasonable evidences" (tekmeria) marks the transition from mythological to philosophical theology -- the birth of natural theology in Western thought.

D: Original:
But is there any difficulty in proving the existence of the Gods?

How would you prove it?

How? In the first place, the earth and the sun, and the stars and the universe, and the fair order of the seasons, and the division of them into years and months, furnish proofs of their existence; and also there is the fact that all Hellenes and barbarians believe in them.

I fear, my sweet friend, though I will not say that I much regard, the contempt with which the profane will be likely to assail us. For you do not understand the nature of their complaint, and you fancy that they rush into impiety only from a love of sensual pleasure.

Why, Stranger, what other reason is there?

One which you who live in a different atmosphere would never guess.

What is it?

A very grievous sort of ignorance which is imagined to be the greatest wisdom.

What do you mean?
D: Narrative:
"But is there any difficulty in proving the existence of the Gods?" asked Cleinias.

"How would you prove it?" replied the Athenian.

"How? In the first place, the earth and the sun, and the stars and the universe, and the fair order of the seasons, and the division of them into years and months, furnish proofs of their existence; and also there is the fact that all Hellenes and barbarians believe in them," said Cleinias.

"I fear, my sweet friend, though I will not say that I much regard, the contempt with which the profane will be likely to assail us. For you do not understand the nature of their complaint, and you fancy that they rush into impiety only from a love of sensual pleasure," said the Athenian.

"Why, Stranger, what other reason is there?" asked Cleinias.

"One which you who live in a different atmosphere would never guess," replied the Athenian.

"What is it?" asked Cleinias.

"A very grievous sort of ignorance which is imagined to be the greatest wisdom," said the Athenian.

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Is it really so difficult to prove the gods exist?" asked Cleinias.

"How would you go about it?" the Athenian replied.

"Well -- the earth, the sun, the stars, the entire universe, the beautiful order of the seasons, the division of time into years and months -- all of these furnish proof. And then there is the fact that every Greek and every foreigner believes in the gods."

The Athenian shook his head. "I fear -- though I will not say I greatly care about -- the contempt that the godless will heap upon such arguments. You do not understand the real nature of their complaint. You imagine that people rush into impiety only from a love of sensual pleasure."

"What other reason could there be?" asked Cleinias.

"One that you, living in the wholesome atmosphere of Crete, would never guess."

"What is it?"

"A very dangerous kind of ignorance that masquerades as the highest wisdom."  

"What do you mean?"
D: Elegant: Cleinias suggests proving the gods through nature's order and universal belief. The Athenian warns that the impious will scorn such arguments -- their atheism springs not from sensual indulgence but from a dangerous ignorance that masquerades as supreme wisdom.
D: Summary: Cleinias proposes standard proofs for the gods' existence -- the cosmic order and universal belief -- but the Athenian warns these will not convince the truly impious. Their atheism, he explains, comes not from moral weakness but from a pernicious intellectual error that mistakes itself for the highest wisdom.
D: Distilled: The deepest source of atheism is not moral corruption but a dangerous form of ignorance that disguises itself as the greatest wisdom.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. Cleinias offers the traditional teleological argument (design in nature) and the argument from universal consent (consensus gentium) -- both standard in ancient Greek theology. The Athenian's dismissal of these as insufficient marks a philosophical advance: mere appeals to cosmic order and popular belief will not withstand materialist critique. The "grievous ignorance imagined to be the greatest wisdom" refers to the pre-Socratic natural philosophers (physiologoi) -- thinkers like Anaxagoras, Democritus, and possibly the atomists whose materialist cosmologies explained the universe without divine agency. Cross-reference: Socrates was charged with impiety partly for Anaxagoras's influence (Apology 26d). The distinction between moral and intellectual sources of atheism is original to this passage and anticipates modern debates about the relationship between scientific materialism and religious belief.

D: Original:
At Athens there are tales preserved in writing which the virtue of your state, as I am informed, refuses to admit. They speak of the Gods in prose as well as verse, and the oldest of them tell of the origin of the heavens and of the world, and not far from the beginning of their story they proceed to narrate the birth of the Gods, and how after they were born they behaved to one another. Whether these stories have in other ways a good or a bad influence, I should not like to be severe upon them, because they are ancient; but, looking at them with reference to the duties of children to their parents, I cannot praise them, or think that they are useful, or at all true. Of the words of the ancients I have nothing more to say; and I should wish to say of them only what is pleasing to the Gods. But as to our younger generation and their wisdom, I cannot let them off when they do mischief. For do but mark the effect of their words: when you and I argue for the existence of the Gods, and produce the sun, moon, stars, and earth, claiming for them a divine being, if we would listen to the aforesaid philosophers we should say that they are earth and stones only, which can have no care at all of human affairs, and that all religion is a cooking up of words and a make-believe.

One such teacher, O Stranger, would be bad enough, and you imply that there are many of them, which is worse.
D: Narrative:
"At Athens there are tales preserved in writing which the virtue of your state, as I am informed, refuses to admit. They speak of the Gods in prose as well as verse, and the oldest of them tell of the origin of the heavens and of the world, and not far from the beginning of their story they proceed to narrate the birth of the Gods, and how after they were born they behaved to one another. Whether these stories have in other ways a good or a bad influence, I should not like to be severe upon them, because they are ancient; but, looking at them with reference to the duties of children to their parents, I cannot praise them, or think that they are useful, or at all true. Of the words of the ancients I have nothing more to say; and I should wish to say of them only what is pleasing to the Gods. But as to our younger generation and their wisdom, I cannot let them off when they do mischief. For do but mark the effect of their words: when you and I argue for the existence of the Gods, and produce the sun, moon, stars, and earth, claiming for them a divine being, if we would listen to the aforesaid philosophers we should say that they are earth and stones only, which can have no care at all of human affairs, and that all religion is a cooking up of words and a make-believe," said the Athenian.

"One such teacher, O Stranger, would be bad enough, and you imply that there are many of them, which is worse," replied Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"In Athens," the Athenian explained, "there are tales preserved in writing that the moral discipline of your Cretan state, as I understand it, refuses to admit. These stories speak of the gods in both prose and verse. The oldest accounts tell of the origin of the heavens and the world, and soon after begin narrating the birth of the gods and how they treated one another.

Whether these myths are good or bad in other respects, I would rather not judge harshly -- they are, after all, ancient. But considered in light of the duties children owe their parents, I cannot praise them, call them useful, or think them true. Of these old stories I will say no more, except what would be pleasing to the gods.

But the younger generation and their 'wisdom' -- them I cannot excuse when they cause harm. Consider the effect of their teachings: when you and I argue for the existence of the gods, pointing to the sun, moon, stars, and earth as evidence of divine being, these philosophers would have us say that such things are nothing but earth and stones, incapable of caring about human affairs, and that all religion is merely a fabrication of clever words and make-believe."

"One such teacher would be bad enough, Stranger," Cleinias replied. "You suggest there are many of them, which is far worse."
D: Elegant: At Athens, ancient myths tell of gods behaving badly toward one another -- stories of questionable truth and poor moral example. But the greater danger comes from the younger generation of philosophers, who teach that the sun, moon, and stars are mere earth and stone, incapable of caring about humanity, and that all religion is nothing but fabricated words and make-believe. One such teacher would be dangerous enough; many are far worse.
D: Summary: The Athenian distinguishes two threats to piety: ancient myths depicting gods behaving immorally, and modern materialist philosophers who reduce celestial bodies to inert matter and dismiss all religion as fabrication. While he treats the old myths gently, he insists the young materialists cause real damage by stripping the cosmos of divinity.
D: Distilled: Ancient myths portraying immoral gods are troubling, but the far greater danger comes from modern materialist philosophers who reduce the heavens to dead matter and dismiss all religion as make-believe.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. The ancient tales referenced are Hesiod's Theogony and similar cosmogonic myths depicting divine conflicts -- Kronos castrating Ouranos, Zeus overthrowing Kronos -- which Plato censors in Republic II (377e-378e) for showing gods in violent conflict. The "younger generation" refers to pre-Socratic natural philosophers, especially Anaxagoras (who called the sun a hot stone -- cf. Apology 26d), Democritus (atomic materialism), and possibly Diogenes of Apollonia. The phrase "cooking up of words and a make-believe" anticipates the modern concept of religion as social construction. Crete's exclusion of these writings reflects the conservative Dorian educational tradition. The Athenian's reluctance to condemn ancient myths while firmly opposing materialist philosophy reveals Plato's nuanced position: myth may contain truth symbolically, but systematic materialism is philosophically corrosive.

D: Original: Well, then; what shall we say or do?-Shall we assume that some one is accusing us among unholy men, who are trying to escape from the effect of our legislation; and that they say of us-How dreadful that you should legislate on the supposition that there are Gods! Shall we make a defence of ourselves? or shall we leave them and return to our laws, lest the prelude should become longer than the law? For the discourse will certainly extend to great length, if we are to treat the impiously disposed as they desire, partly demonstrating to them at some length the things of which they demand an explanation, partly making them afraid or dissatisfied, and then proceed to the requisite enactments.
D: Narrative: "Well, then; what shall we say or do? Shall we assume that some one is accusing us among unholy men, who are trying to escape from the effect of our legislation; and that they say of us -- 'How dreadful that you should legislate on the supposition that there are Gods!' Shall we make a defence of ourselves? or shall we leave them and return to our laws, lest the prelude should become longer than the law? For the discourse will certainly extend to great length, if we are to treat the impiously disposed as they desire, partly demonstrating to them at some length the things of which they demand an explanation, partly making them afraid or dissatisfied, and then proceed to the requisite enactments," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"So what shall we do?" the Athenian continued. "Shall we imagine that someone among these ungodly men -- who are trying to escape the force of our legislation -- is accusing us, saying: 'How outrageous that you would legislate on the assumption that gods exist!' Shall we defend ourselves against this charge? Or shall we set the matter aside and return to our laws, before the prelude grows longer than the law itself?

For the discussion will certainly stretch to considerable length if we give the impious what they want -- demonstrating at length the truths they demand explained, while also instilling in them a proper fear and dissatisfaction with their current views -- and only then proceed to the actual legal enactments."
D: Elegant: The Athenian raises a practical dilemma: the impious accuse lawmakers of presupposing the gods' existence. Should they mount a full philosophical defence -- risking a prelude longer than the law itself -- or simply proceed to legislation? Engaging the atheists requires both lengthy demonstration and persuasion before any enactments can follow.
D: Summary: The Athenian poses the practical question of whether to engage with atheist objections at length or proceed directly to legislation, noting that a full philosophical response risks making the theological prelude longer than the laws it introduces.
D: Distilled: The lawgiver faces a dilemma: either engage in a lengthy philosophical defence of the gods' existence or risk legislating on an unproven foundation.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage addresses the structural tension between philosophical argument and practical legislation that runs throughout the Laws. The idea of a "prelude" (prooimion) to law -- a persuasive preamble preceding the coercive statute -- is one of Plato's most original contributions to legal theory (see Laws IV, 722d-723d). The concern that the prelude might outgrow the law itself is both humorous and methodologically significant: it acknowledges that philosophical theology cannot be compressed into a legal formula. The phrase "making them afraid or dissatisfied" reveals the dual strategy of rational demonstration combined with emotional persuasion -- logos and pathos working together, recalling the Phaedrus on rhetoric's proper use (Phaedrus 271c-272b).

D: Original: Yes, Stranger; but then how often have we repeated already that on the present occasion there is no reason why brevity should be preferred to length; who is "at our heels"?-as the saying goes, and it would be paltry and ridiculous to prefer the shorter to the better. It is a matter of no small consequence, in some way or other to prove that there are Gods, and that they are good, and regard justice more than men do. The demonstration of this would be the best and noblest prelude of all our laws. And therefore, without impatience, and without hurry, let us unreservedly consider the whole matter, summoning up all the power of persuasion which we possess.
D: Narrative: "Yes, Stranger; but then how often have we repeated already that on the present occasion there is no reason why brevity should be preferred to length; who is 'at our heels'? -- as the saying goes, and it would be paltry and ridiculous to prefer the shorter to the better. It is a matter of no small consequence, in some way or other to prove that there are Gods, and that they are good, and regard justice more than men do. The demonstration of this would be the best and noblest prelude of all our laws. And therefore, without impatience, and without hurry, let us unreservedly consider the whole matter, summoning up all the power of persuasion which we possess," said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned: "But Stranger," Cleinias replied, "how many times have we said already that on this occasion there is no reason to prefer brevity over thoroughness? Who is chasing us? As the saying goes, it would be petty and absurd to choose the shorter argument over the better one. It is a matter of enormous consequence to prove that gods exist, that they are good, and that they care about justice more than human beings do. Such a demonstration would be the finest and most noble prelude to all our laws. So let us consider the whole matter without impatience or haste, bringing to bear every power of persuasion we possess."
D: Elegant: Cleinias insists there is no reason to rush. Proving that the gods exist, are good, and care about justice more than mortals do would be the noblest possible prelude to their laws. They should take all the time needed, summoning every power of persuasion at their disposal.
D: Summary: Cleinias urges the Athenian not to cut the theological argument short for the sake of brevity. Proving the gods' existence and goodness is too important to rush, and such a demonstration would serve as the finest possible prelude to their entire legal code.
D: Distilled: Proving that gods exist, are good, and love justice more than humans do is too important to abbreviate -- it deserves the fullest and most careful argument possible.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias. Cleinias here serves as the eager student who authorizes the extended theological digression, a dramatic device Plato uses to justify the philosophical depth of what follows. The phrase "who is at our heels" is a proverbial expression suggesting they have no pursuer or deadline -- a contrast with the rushed political deliberations of actual legislative assemblies. The tripartite proof Cleinias outlines (gods exist, gods are good, gods love justice) maps precisely onto the three forms of impiety identified in 10.1. This is the programmatic statement for Book 10's entire theological argument. Cross-reference: the idea that divine beings are more just than humans connects to Republic II (379a-383c), where Socrates argues that gods must be depicted as perfectly good.

D: Original: Seeing you thus in earnest, I would fain offer up a prayer that I may succeed:-but I must proceed at once. Who can be calm when he is called upon to prove the existence of the Gods? Who can avoid hating and abhorring the men who are and have been the cause of this argument; I speak of those who will not believe the tales which they have heard as babes and sucklings from their mothers and nurses, repeated by them both in jest and earnest, like charms, who have also heard them in the sacrificial prayers, and seen sights accompanying them-sights and sounds delightful to children-and their parents during the sacrifices showing an intense earnestness on behalf of their children and of themselves, and with eager interest talking to the Gods, and beseeching them, as though they were firmly convinced of their existence; who likewise see and hear the prostrations and invocations which are made by Hellenes and barbarians at the rising and setting of the sun and moon, in all the vicissitudes of life, not as if they thought that there were no Gods, but as if there could be no doubt of their existence, and no suspicion of their non-existence; when men, knowing all these things, despise them on no real grounds, as would be admitted by all who have any particle of intelligence, and when they force us to say what we are now saying, how can any one in gentle terms remonstrate with the like of them, when he has to begin by proving to them the very existence of the Gods?
D: Narrative: "Seeing you thus in earnest, I would fain offer up a prayer that I may succeed -- but I must proceed at once. Who can be calm when he is called upon to prove the existence of the Gods? Who can avoid hating and abhorring the men who are and have been the cause of this argument; I speak of those who will not believe the tales which they have heard as babes and sucklings from their mothers and nurses, repeated by them both in jest and earnest, like charms, who have also heard them in the sacrificial prayers, and seen sights accompanying them -- sights and sounds delightful to children -- and their parents during the sacrifices showing an intense earnestness on behalf of their children and of themselves, and with eager interest talking to the Gods, and beseeching them, as though they were firmly convinced of their existence; who likewise see and hear the prostrations and invocations which are made by Hellenes and barbarians at the rising and setting of the sun and moon, in all the vicissitudes of life, not as if they thought that there were no Gods, but as if there could be no doubt of their existence, and no suspicion of their non-existence; when men, knowing all these things, despise them on no real grounds, as would be admitted by all who have any particle of intelligence, and when they force us to say what we are now saying, how can any one in gentle terms remonstrate with the like of them, when he has to begin by proving to them the very existence of the Gods?" said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"Seeing how earnest you are," the Athenian began, "I would offer up a prayer for success -- but I must proceed at once. Who can remain calm when called upon to prove the existence of the gods? Who can help but feel indignation toward those who have caused this necessity?

I mean those who refuse to believe what they heard as infants from their mothers and nurses -- stories repeated in both play and earnest, like incantations -- who heard these same truths in sacrificial prayers and saw the rites that accompanied them, sights and sounds that delighted them as children. They watched their parents sacrificing with intense devotion, speaking to the gods and beseeching them with complete conviction. They have seen Greeks and foreigners alike prostrating themselves and calling upon the gods at the rising and setting of the sun and moon, through every turn of fortune -- not as people who doubted the gods' existence, but as those for whom it was beyond all question.

When men who have witnessed all of this cast it aside on no real grounds -- as anyone with a shred of intelligence would admit -- and when they force us into the position of having to prove what should never need proving, how can anyone address them gently? How can one remain calm while being compelled to demonstrate the very existence of the gods?"
D: Elegant: The Athenian expresses indignation at having to prove the gods' existence at all. Those who demand such proof grew up hearing prayers from their mothers, watching their parents sacrifice with total conviction, and witnessing all of humanity -- Greek and foreign alike -- prostrate before the rising and setting of celestial bodies. To dismiss all this on no real grounds and force others to argue for what should be self-evident tests the patience of any reasonable person.
D: Summary: The Athenian expresses frustration at being forced to prove the gods' existence to people who were raised in a world saturated with religious practice and universal belief. He finds it difficult to address gently those who dismiss the cumulative weight of human religious experience on no substantial grounds.
D: Distilled: It strains all patience to prove the gods' existence to those who grew up immersed in universal religious practice and dismiss it all without grounds.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage reveals the emotional tension between philosophical obligation and personal conviction -- the Athenian must argue dispassionately for what he feels passionately. The description of religious experience from infancy (mothers, nurses, sacrificial rites, prostrations at sunrise and sunset) constitutes what might be called an argument from lived religious experience or phenomenological religion. The universal practice of prayer and prostration among "Hellenes and barbarians" is an expanded version of the consensus gentium argument Cleinias offered in 10.3, but here given concrete, experiential detail. Cross-reference: the Athenian's struggle to remain gentle echoes Socrates' composure before hostile interlocutors (cf. Gorgias 482c-486d, Republic I). The comparison of religious stories to "charms" (epodai) connects to Laws II (659d-e) on the enchanting power of correct education.

D: Original: Yet the attempt must be made; for it would be unseemly that one half of mankind should go mad in their lust of pleasure, and the other half in their indignation at such persons. Our address to these lost and perverted natures should not be spoken in passion; let us suppose ourselves to select some one of them, and gently reason with him, smothering our anger:-O my son, we will say to him, you are young, and the advance of time will make you reverse may of the opinions which you now hold. Wait awhile, and do not attempt to judge at present of the highest things; and that is the highest of which you now think nothing-to know the Gods rightly and to live accordingly. And in the first place let me indicate to you one point which is of great importance, and about which I cannot be deceived:-You and your friends are not the first who have held this opinion about the Gods. There have always been persons more or less numerous who have had the same disorder. I have known many of them, and can tell you, that no one who had taken up in youth this opinion, that the Gods do not exist, ever continued in the same until he was old; the two other notions certainly do continue in some cases, but not in many; the notion, I mean, that the Gods exist, but take no heed of human things, and the other notion that they do take heed of them, but are easily propitiated with sacrifices and prayers. As to the opinion about the Gods which may some day become clear to you, I advise you go wait and consider if it be true or not; ask of others, and above all of the legislator. In the meantime take care that you do not offend against the Gods. For the duty of the legislator is and always will be to teach you the truth of these matters.
D: Narrative: "Yet the attempt must be made; for it would be unseemly that one half of mankind should go mad in their lust of pleasure, and the other half in their indignation at such persons. Our address to these lost and perverted natures should not be spoken in passion; let us suppose ourselves to select some one of them, and gently reason with him, smothering our anger: 'O my son, we will say to him, you are young, and the advance of time will make you reverse many of the opinions which you now hold. Wait awhile, and do not attempt to judge at present of the highest things; and that is the highest of which you now think nothing -- to know the Gods rightly and to live accordingly. And in the first place let me indicate to you one point which is of great importance, and about which I cannot be deceived: You and your friends are not the first who have held this opinion about the Gods. There have always been persons more or less numerous who have had the same disorder. I have known many of them, and can tell you, that no one who had taken up in youth this opinion, that the Gods do not exist, ever continued in the same until he was old; the two other notions certainly do continue in some cases, but not in many; the notion, I mean, that the Gods exist, but take no heed of human things, and the other notion that they do take heed of them, but are easily propitiated with sacrifices and prayers. As to the opinion about the Gods which may some day become clear to you, I advise you to wait and consider if it be true or not; ask of others, and above all of the legislator. In the meantime take care that you do not offend against the Gods. For the duty of the legislator is and always will be to teach you the truth of these matters,'" said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"Yet the attempt must be made," the Athenian continued. "It would be unbecoming for one half of mankind to go mad with pleasure-seeking while the other half goes mad with indignation at them. Our address to these misguided natures should not be spoken in anger. Let us imagine selecting one of them and reasoning with him gently, suppressing our irritation.

'My son,' we would say, 'you are young, and the passage of time will reverse many of the opinions you now hold. Be patient. Do not try to pass judgment yet on the highest matters -- and the highest of all, which you currently think nothing of, is to know the gods rightly and to live accordingly.

Let me make one point of great importance, about which I cannot be mistaken: you and your friends are not the first to hold these views about the gods. There have always been people, in greater or lesser numbers, afflicted with the same condition. I have known many of them, and I can assure you that no one who adopted atheism in youth ever held to it into old age. The other two views -- that gods exist but ignore human affairs, or that they can be bribed with sacrifices -- do persist in some cases, though not many.

As for the truth about the gods, which may one day become clear to you, I advise you to wait, reflect, consult others, and above all consult the legislator. In the meantime, take care not to offend the gods. For it is the legislator's duty -- and always will be -- to teach you the truth about these things.'"
D: Elegant: The attempt to persuade must be made without anger. To the young atheist, the Athenian would say: You are not the first to deny the gods -- many have, and none has persisted in that denial into old age. The highest knowledge is to know the gods rightly and live accordingly. Be patient, consult the wise, and in the meantime do not offend the gods. The legislator's permanent duty is to teach you these truths.
D: Summary: The Athenian counsels restraint: neither the pleasure-seekers nor those indignant at them should lose their composure. He models a gentle address to a young atheist, noting that youthful atheism never persists into old age, urging patience and deference to the legislator's guidance, and warning against offending the gods while the question remains open.
D: Distilled: No one who adopted atheism in youth ever maintained it into old age -- the young should wait, consult the wise, and take care not to offend the gods while their understanding matures.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The claim that youthful atheism never persists into old age is an empirical observation (possibly reflecting Plato's own experience with intellectual circles in Athens) rather than a philosophical argument -- it functions as pastoral counsel, not proof. The address "O my son" establishes the paternalistic tone of the legislative prelude. The characterization of atheism as a "disorder" (nosema) medicalizes impiety, treating it as a curable condition rather than criminal intent -- this anticipates the distinction between the "House of Reformation" and harsher punishments in 10.46-48. Cross-reference: the advice to defer judgment on the highest things recalls Socrates' characteristic intellectual humility (Apology 21d). The distinction between youthful and mature belief anticipates Republic VII (539a-d), where Socrates warns against exposing the young too early to dialectic, lest they misuse it for nihilistic purposes.

D: Original:
Our address, Stranger, thus far, is excellent.

Quite true, Megillus and Cleinias, but I am afraid that we have unconsciously lighted on a strange doctrine.

What doctrine do you mean?

The wisest of all doctrines, in the opinion of many.

I wish that you would speak plainer.

The doctrine that all things do become, have become, and will become, some by nature, some by art, and some by chance.

Is not that true?

Well, philosophers are probably right; at any rate we may as well follow in their track, and examine what is the meaning of them and their disciples.

By all means.

They say that the greatest and fairest things are the work of nature and of chance, the lesser of art, which, receiving from nature the greater and primeval creations, moulds and fashions all those lesser works which are generally termed artificial.

How is that?
D: Narrative:
"Our address, Stranger, thus far, is excellent," said Cleinias.

"Quite true, Megillus and Cleinias, but I am afraid that we have unconsciously lighted on a strange doctrine," replied the Athenian.

"What doctrine do you mean?" asked Cleinias.

"The wisest of all doctrines, in the opinion of many," said the Athenian.

"I wish that you would speak plainer," said Cleinias.

"The doctrine that all things do become, have become, and will become, some by nature, some by art, and some by chance," replied the Athenian.

"Is not that true?" asked Cleinias.

"Well, philosophers are probably right; at any rate we may as well follow in their track, and examine what is the meaning of them and their disciples," said the Athenian.

"By all means," agreed Cleinias.

"They say that the greatest and fairest things are the work of nature and of chance, the lesser of art, which, receiving from nature the greater and primeval creations, moulds and fashions all those lesser works which are generally termed artificial," said the Athenian.

"How is that?" asked Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Your address so far is excellent, Stranger," said Cleinias.

"Thank you, Megillus and Cleinias -- but I am afraid we have stumbled, without realizing it, upon a strange doctrine," the Athenian replied.

"What doctrine?"

"The one that many people consider the wisest of all."

"Please speak more plainly."

"The doctrine that everything which exists -- everything that has come into being, is coming into being, or will come into being -- arises from one of three causes: nature, art, or chance."

"Is that not true?"

"The philosophers may well be right. In any case, let us follow their line of reasoning and examine what they and their followers actually mean."

"By all means," Cleinias agreed.

"They claim that the greatest and most beautiful things in existence are the work of nature and chance, while the lesser things are products of art. Art, they say, takes the great primeval creations of nature and merely shapes and moulds them into the smaller works we call artificial."

"How so?"
D: Elegant: The Athenian introduces the materialist doctrine that everything arises from nature, art, or chance. According to this view, the greatest and most beautiful things -- the cosmos itself -- are products of nature and chance, while art merely reshapes nature's raw materials into lesser, artificial works.
D: Summary: The Athenian introduces the philosophical doctrine he intends to refute: the materialist claim that all things arise from nature, art, or chance. In this framework, the grandest features of reality are products of nature and blind chance, while human art merely reshapes nature's raw material into lesser works.
D: Distilled: The materialist doctrine holds that everything arises from nature, art, or chance -- with the greatest things produced by nature and chance alone, and art contributing only lesser works.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. This passage introduces the materialist cosmology that Plato will systematically refute. The nature-art-chance triad represents the pre-Socratic worldview, particularly associated with Democritus (who attributed cosmic order to necessity and chance) and possibly Empedocles (whose cosmic cycles operate through natural forces). The crucial claim -- that the greatest things are products of nature and chance while art is secondary and derivative -- inverts Plato's own position that mind (nous) and art (techne) are prior to and sovereign over nature. Cross-reference: this is the antithesis of the Timaeus (28a-29a), where the cosmos is the deliberate product of a divine craftsman (demiurge) working according to rational design. The Sophist (265c-266d) similarly distinguishes divine from human production. Plato's refutation will argue that the materialists have the causal order exactly backward: soul and mind are prior to matter, not derived from it.

D: Original:
I will explain my meaning still more clearly. They say that fire and water, and earth and air, all exist by nature and chance, and none of them by art, and that as to the bodies which come next in order-earth, and sun, and moon, and stars-they have been created by means of these absolutely inanimate existences. The elements are severally moved by chance and some inherent force according to certain affinities among them-of hot with cold, or of dry with moist, or of soft with hard, and according to all the other accidental admixtures of opposites which have been formed by necessity. After this fashion and in this manner the whole heaven has been created, and all that is in the heaven, as well as animals and all plants, and all the seasons come from these elements, not by the action of mind, as they say, or of any God, or from art, but as I was saying, by nature and chance only. Art sprang up afterwards and out of these, mortal and of mortal birth, and produced in play certain images and very partial imitations of the truth, having an affinity to one another, such as music and painting create and their companion arts. And there are other arts which have a serious purpose, and these co-operate with nature, such, for example, as medicine, and husbandry, and gymnastic. And they say that politics cooperate with nature, but in a less degree, and have more of art; also that legislation is entirely a work of art, and is based on assumptions which are not true.

How do you mean?
D: Narrative:
"I will explain my meaning still more clearly. They say that fire and water, and earth and air, all exist by nature and chance, and none of them by art, and that as to the bodies which come next in order -- earth, and sun, and moon, and stars -- they have been created by means of these absolutely inanimate existences. The elements are severally moved by chance and some inherent force according to certain affinities among them -- of hot with cold, or of dry with moist, or of soft with hard, and according to all the other accidental admixtures of opposites which have been formed by necessity. After this fashion and in this manner the whole heaven has been created, and all that is in the heaven, as well as animals and all plants, and all the seasons come from these elements, not by the action of mind, as they say, or of any God, or from art, but as I was saying, by nature and chance only. Art sprang up afterwards and out of these, mortal and of mortal birth, and produced in play certain images and very partial imitations of the truth, having an affinity to one another, such as music and painting create and their companion arts. And there are other arts which have a serious purpose, and these co-operate with nature, such, for example, as medicine, and husbandry, and gymnastic. And they say that politics cooperate with nature, but in a less degree, and have more of art; also that legislation is entirely a work of art, and is based on assumptions which are not true," said the Athenian.

"How do you mean?" asked Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Let me explain more clearly," the Athenian continued. "These thinkers say that fire, water, earth, and air all exist by nature and chance -- none of them by art or design. The celestial bodies that come next -- the earth itself, the sun, moon, and stars -- were formed by these completely inanimate elements. Each element moves by chance and by some inherent force, according to natural affinities: hot combining with cold, dry with moist, soft with hard, and every other accidental mixture of opposites formed by necessity.

In this way, they claim, the entire heavens were created, along with everything in them -- all animals, all plants, all the seasons -- not through the action of mind, or any god, or any art, but through nature and chance alone.

Art, they say, came afterward, born from these material processes -- mortal and of mortal origin. Art produced merely playful images and partial imitations of truth, like music, painting, and their companion arts. Other arts serve more serious purposes and cooperate with nature -- medicine, agriculture, and physical training. Politics, they claim, cooperates with nature to some extent but relies more heavily on art. And legislation, they conclude, is entirely artificial -- based on assumptions that have no foundation in truth."

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.
D: Elegant: The materialist position, fully stated: The four elements exist by nature and chance alone, not design. From their blind interactions -- hot with cold, dry with moist -- the entire cosmos arose, including all life, without any action of mind or god. Art is a late, mortal invention that merely imitates nature. Serious arts like medicine cooperate with nature; politics less so. Legislation is wholly artificial, based on false assumptions.
D: Summary: The Athenian lays out the full materialist cosmology: the four elements exist by nature and chance, their blind interactions produced the entire cosmos including all life, art is a late mortal invention, and legislation is entirely artificial with no basis in natural truth. This is the position he will systematically refute.
D: Distilled: The materialist worldview claims the cosmos arose from blind elemental interactions without mind or god, making art a late invention and legislation a purely artificial construct with no basis in nature.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This is the fullest statement of the materialist position Plato will refute. The cosmology described closely matches Empedocles (elemental affinities and mixtures) and Democritus (chance and necessity as cosmic causes). The hierarchy from nature to art to legislation inverts Plato's metaphysics entirely. Key claims being targeted: (1) elements are inanimate and self-moving, (2) mind/soul arises from matter rather than preceding it, (3) art is derivative and imitative, (4) law is conventional, not natural. The distinction between playful arts (music, painting) and serious arts (medicine, agriculture) that cooperate with nature reflects categories found in the Sophist (219a-c) and Statesman (258d-e). The claim that legislation is "based on assumptions which are not true" is the sophistic position of Callicles in the Gorgias (482c-484c) and Thrasymachus in Republic I (338c-339a), where law is presented as artificial convention rather than natural order. Cross-reference: Timaeus 46c-e explicitly rejects the idea that physical causes operate without intelligent direction.

D: Original:
In the first place, my dear friend, these people would say that the Gods exist not by nature, but by art, and by the laws of states, which are different in different places, according to the agreement of those who make them; and that the honourable is one thing by nature and another thing by law, and that the principles of justice have no existence at all in nature, but that mankind are always disputing about them and altering them; and that the alterations which are made by art and by law have no basis in nature, but are of authority for the moment and at the time at which they are made.-These, my friends, are the sayings of wise men, poets and prose writers, which find a way into the minds of youth. They are told by them that the highest right is might, and in this way the young fall into impieties, under the idea that the Gods are not such as the law bids them imagine; and hence arise factions, these philosophers inviting them to lead a true life according to nature, that is, to live in real dominion over others, and not in legal subjection to them.

What a dreadful picture, Stranger, have you given, and how great is the injury which is thus inflicted on young men to the ruin both of states and families!
D: Narrative:
"In the first place, my dear friend, these people would say that the Gods exist not by nature, but by art, and by the laws of states, which are different in different places, according to the agreement of those who make them; and that the honourable is one thing by nature and another thing by law, and that the principles of justice have no existence at all in nature, but that mankind are always disputing about them and altering them; and that the alterations which are made by art and by law have no basis in nature, but are of authority for the moment and at the time at which they are made. These, my friends, are the sayings of wise men, poets and prose writers, which find a way into the minds of youth. They are told by them that the highest right is might, and in this way the young fall into impieties, under the idea that the Gods are not such as the law bids them imagine; and hence arise factions, these philosophers inviting them to lead a true life according to nature, that is, to live in real dominion over others, and not in legal subjection to them," said the Athenian.

"What a dreadful picture, Stranger, have you given, and how great is the injury which is thus inflicted on young men to the ruin both of states and families!" exclaimed Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"First of all," the Athenian continued, "these thinkers would say that the gods do not exist by nature but only by art -- by the laws of states, which differ from place to place according to the agreements of those who make them. They claim that what is honourable is one thing by nature and another by law, and that the principles of justice have no existence in nature whatsoever. Humanity is always disputing and revising them, and whatever changes are made through art and law have no natural foundation -- they carry authority only in the moment and the circumstances of their creation.

These, my friends, are the teachings of those considered wise -- poets and prose writers alike -- and they find their way into the minds of the young. The young are told that the highest right is might. And so they fall into impiety, believing the gods are not what the law says they are. From this arise political factions, as these philosophers invite the young to live a 'true life according to nature' -- meaning to live in dominion over others rather than in lawful subjection to them."

"What a terrible picture you have painted, Stranger!" Cleinias exclaimed. "How great is the damage inflicted on young people, to the ruin of both states and families!"
D: Elegant: The materialists' conclusions: gods are human inventions, varying by local convention. Justice has no natural existence -- it is always disputed and revised. Law carries only momentary authority. These teachings lead the young to believe that might is the highest right, that the gods are fictions, and that the "natural" life means dominating others rather than submitting to law. The result: factions and the ruin of states and families.
D: Summary: The Athenian traces the political consequences of materialism: if gods and justice are mere conventions with no natural basis, then might becomes right, the young abandon piety, and philosophers encourage them to seek domination over others rather than lawful coexistence. Cleinias recognizes this as devastating to both states and families.
D: Distilled: When gods and justice are reduced to artificial conventions, the young conclude that might is the highest right and abandon law for the pursuit of domination.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This passage connects materialist cosmology to moral and political nihilism -- the chain runs: material cosmos without mind, therefore gods are human inventions, therefore justice is conventional, therefore might makes right. This is the nomos-physis (convention-nature) debate central to fifth-century Greek thought, associated with Antiphon the Sophist, Callicles (Gorgias 482c-484c), and Thrasymachus (Republic I, 338c). The phrase "the highest right is might" echoes Thrasymachus directly. The invitation to live "according to nature" in dominion over others is Callicles' position verbatim (Gorgias 483c-484a). Plato here shows that materialism is not merely a cosmological error but a source of political catastrophe -- atheism leads to tyranny. Cross-reference: Republic VIII-IX traces the degeneration from democracy to tyranny through precisely this logic of lawless self-assertion.

D: Original: True, Cleinias; but then what should the lawgiver do when this evil is of long standing? should he only rise up in the state and threaten all mankind, proclaiming that if they will not say and think that the Gods are such as the law ordains (and this may be extended generally to the honourable, the just, and to all the highest things, and to all that relates to virtue and vice), and if they will not make their actions conform to the copy which the law gives them, then he who refuses to obey the law shall die, or suffer stripes and bonds, or privation of citizenship, or in some cases be punished by loss of property and exile? Should he not rather, when he is making laws for men, at the same time infuse the spirit of persuasion into his words, and mitigate the severity of them as far as he can?
D: Narrative: "True, Cleinias; but then what should the lawgiver do when this evil is of long standing? Should he only rise up in the state and threaten all mankind, proclaiming that if they will not say and think that the Gods are such as the law ordains -- and this may be extended generally to the honourable, the just, and to all the highest things, and to all that relates to virtue and vice -- and if they will not make their actions conform to the copy which the law gives them, then he who refuses to obey the law shall die, or suffer stripes and bonds, or privation of citizenship, or in some cases be punished by loss of property and exile? Should he not rather, when he is making laws for men, at the same time infuse the spirit of persuasion into his words, and mitigate the severity of them as far as he can?" said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"True enough, Cleinias," the Athenian replied. "But what should the lawgiver do when this evil has taken deep root? Should he simply stand up in the state and threaten everyone, declaring that whoever refuses to believe and say that the gods are as the law prescribes -- and extending this to the honourable, the just, and everything relating to virtue and vice -- and whoever will not conform their actions to the model the law provides, shall be put to death, or suffer flogging, imprisonment, loss of citizenship, confiscation of property, or exile?

Or should the lawgiver, while making laws for human beings, also weave persuasion into his words and soften their severity as far as he can?"
D: Elegant: When impiety is deeply rooted, should the lawgiver simply threaten death, imprisonment, and exile for disbelief? Or should he weave persuasion into his laws and moderate their severity? The choice is between pure coercion and the combination of reason with authority.
D: Summary: The Athenian asks whether a lawgiver confronting entrenched impiety should rely solely on threats and punishment, or should instead combine legal authority with persuasive argument, moderating severity wherever possible.
D: Distilled: The lawgiver's choice is not between punishment and permissiveness, but between bare coercion and law infused with the spirit of persuasion.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is a pivotal moment in the Laws' legal philosophy: the distinction between law as pure command (backed by force) and law as reasoned persuasion (backed by understanding). The Athenian clearly favors the latter, which is why Book 10 exists as a theological prelude rather than a bare criminal statute. The list of punishments -- death, flogging, bonds, loss of citizenship, property, exile -- represents the full coercive toolkit of ancient Greek law. The phrase "the copy which the law gives them" (paradeigma) uses the language of Platonic Forms applied to legislation: law provides a model of right conduct. Cross-reference: Laws IV (718b-723d) develops the theory of legislative preludes at length. The contrast between persuasion and force also appears in the Statesman (276d-e), where the true statesman rules by persuasion rather than compulsion.

D: Original:
Why, Stranger, if such persuasion be at all possible, then a legislator who has anything in him ought never to weary of persuading men; he ought to leave nothing unsaid in support of the ancient opinion that there are Gods, and of all those other truths which you were just now mentioning; he ought to support the law and also art, and acknowledge that both alike exist by nature, and no less than nature, if they are the creations of mind in accordance with right reason, you appear to me to maintain, and I am disposed to agree with you in thinking.

Yes, my enthusiastic Cleinias; but are not these things when spoken to a multitude hard to be understood, not to mention that they take up a dismal length of time?
D: Narrative:
"Why, Stranger, if such persuasion be at all possible, then a legislator who has anything in him ought never to weary of persuading men; he ought to leave nothing unsaid in support of the ancient opinion that there are Gods, and of all those other truths which you were just now mentioning; he ought to support the law and also art, and acknowledge that both alike exist by nature, and no less than nature, if they are the creations of mind in accordance with right reason, you appear to me to maintain, and I am disposed to agree with you in thinking," said Cleinias.

"Yes, my enthusiastic Cleinias; but are not these things when spoken to a multitude hard to be understood, not to mention that they take up a dismal length of time?" replied the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"If such persuasion is at all possible, Stranger," Cleinias said, "then a legislator worth anything should never tire of persuading people. He should leave nothing unsaid in support of the ancient belief that gods exist, and all those other truths you just mentioned. He should defend law and art alike, acknowledging that both exist by nature -- no less than nature itself -- if they are creations of mind working in accordance with right reason. That is what you seem to be arguing, and I am inclined to agree."

"Yes, my enthusiastic Cleinias," the Athenian replied. "But when these arguments are addressed to a general audience, are they not extremely difficult to follow -- not to mention that they take an appalling amount of time?"
D: Elegant: Cleinias grasps the key insight: law and art, when created by mind in accordance with right reason, exist by nature no less than physical nature itself. A worthy legislator should never tire of making this case. The Athenian agrees in principle but worries that such arguments are hard for a general audience to follow and take considerable time to develop.
D: Summary: Cleinias endorses the Athenian's core thesis -- that law and art, as products of rational mind, are as natural as physical nature -- and insists a good legislator must argue this tirelessly. The Athenian accepts but raises practical concerns about the difficulty and length of communicating such philosophy to the public.
D: Distilled: Law and art, as creations of mind working through right reason, are no less natural than physical nature itself -- but convincing a general audience of this is both difficult and time-consuming.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. Cleinias here articulates the central counter-thesis to materialism: mind-produced order (law, art) is natural in the truest sense, because mind is prior to and sovereign over matter. This directly inverts the materialist claim of 10.10 that legislation is "entirely a work of art" with no basis in nature. The phrase "creations of mind in accordance with right reason" (nous kata orthon logon) is the Platonic formula for genuine natural law -- an anticipation of Stoic and later natural law theory. The Athenian's concern about accessibility to the multitude reflects a persistent Platonic tension between philosophical truth and democratic communication (cf. Republic VI, 493a-494a on the philosopher's difficulty communicating with the many). Cross-reference: Timaeus 29a-d similarly holds that the cosmos, as a product of rational design, is more truly "natural" than random material processes.

D: Original: Why, Stranger, shall we, whose patience failed not when drinking or music were the themes of discourse, weary now of discoursing about the Gods, and about divine things? And the greatest help to rational legislation is that the laws when once written down are always at rest; they can be put to the test at any future time, and therefore, if on first hearing they seem difficult, there is no reason for apprehension about them, because any man however dull can go over them and consider them again and again; nor if they are tedious but useful, is there any reason or religion, as it seems to me, in any man refusing to maintain the principles of them to the utmost of his power.
D: Narrative: "Why, Stranger, shall we, whose patience failed not when drinking or music were the themes of discourse, weary now of discoursing about the Gods, and about divine things? And the greatest help to rational legislation is that the laws when once written down are always at rest; they can be put to the test at any future time, and therefore, if on first hearing they seem difficult, there is no reason for apprehension about them, because any man however dull can go over them and consider them again and again; nor if they are tedious but useful, is there any reason or religion, as it seems to me, in any man refusing to maintain the principles of them to the utmost of his power," said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Come now, Stranger," Cleinias replied. "We did not lose patience when drinking and music were our topics of conversation -- shall we grow weary now that the subject is the gods and divine things?

Besides, the greatest advantage of rational legislation is this: once laws are written down, they remain fixed and available. They can be examined and tested at any future time. So if these arguments seem difficult on first hearing, there is no cause for worry -- even the slowest mind can return to them and study them again and again. And if the arguments are tedious but useful, then no person of conscience has any excuse for refusing to uphold their principles with all their strength."
D: Elegant: Cleinias argues they should not lose patience on theology when they endured lengthy discussions of drinking and music. Written laws have a permanent advantage: they can be studied repeatedly. Even difficult arguments become accessible through re-reading, and useful ones deserve full support regardless of their length.
D: Summary: Cleinias makes the case for persistence: if they had patience for discussions of wine and music, they should have far more for theology. He adds that written laws have a unique advantage -- they can be re-read and reconsidered indefinitely, so initial difficulty is no obstacle to eventual understanding.
D: Distilled: Written laws can be studied again and again, so their initial difficulty is no barrier -- what matters is that they are true and useful, not that they are immediately easy.
Notes: Speaker: Cleinias. The reference to drinking and music points back to the extended discussions in Books I-II of the Laws, where the proper regulation of wine-drinking and musical education occupied considerable space. Cleinias's argument about the permanence of written law is philosophically significant: it amounts to a defence of the written philosophical prelude as a form of ongoing education. Unlike oral persuasion, which is fleeting, written law creates a permanent text that allows repeated engagement -- anticipating the hermeneutic tradition of close reading. This contrasts interestingly with Plato's famous critique of writing in the Phaedrus (274b-278b), where he argues that written words cannot defend themselves or adapt to their audience. Here in the Laws, the permanence of writing is presented as an advantage rather than a limitation. Cross-reference: Laws VII (810b-812a) on the educational value of studying legal texts.

D: Original:
Stranger, I like what Cleinias is saying.

Yes, Megillus, and we should do as he proposes; for if impious discourses were not scattered, as I may say, throughout the world, there would have been no need for any vindication of the existence of the Gods-but seeing that they are spread far and wide, such arguments are needed; and who should come to the rescue of the greatest laws, when they are being undermined by bad men, but the legislator himself?

There is no more proper champion of them.

Well, then, tell me, Cleinias-for I must ask you to be my partner-does not he who talks in this way conceive fire and water and earth and air to be the first elements of all things? These he calls nature, and out of these he supposes the soul to be formed afterwards; and this is not a mere conjecture of ours about his meaning, but is what he really means.

Very true.

Then, by Heaven, we have discovered the source of this vain opinion of all those physical investigators; and I would have you examine their arguments with the utmost care, for their impiety is a very serious matter; they not only make a bad and mistaken use of argument, but they lead away the minds of others: that is my opinion of them.

You are right; but I should like to know how this happens.

I fear that the argument may seem singular.

Do not hesitate, Stranger; I see that you are afraid of such a discussion carrying you beyond the limits of legislation. But if there be no other way of showing our agreement in the belief that there are Gods, of whom the law is said now to approve, let us take this way, my good sir.
D: Narrative:
"Stranger, I like what Cleinias is saying," said Megillus.

"Yes, Megillus, and we should do as he proposes; for if impious discourses were not scattered, as I may say, throughout the world, there would have been no need for any vindication of the existence of the Gods -- but seeing that they are spread far and wide, such arguments are needed; and who should come to the rescue of the greatest laws, when they are being undermined by bad men, but the legislator himself?" replied the Athenian.

"There is no more proper champion of them," agreed Megillus.

"Well, then, tell me, Cleinias -- for I must ask you to be my partner -- does not he who talks in this way conceive fire and water and earth and air to be the first elements of all things? These he calls nature, and out of these he supposes the soul to be formed afterwards; and this is not a mere conjecture of ours about his meaning, but is what he really means," said the Athenian.

"Very true," replied Cleinias.

"Then, by Heaven, we have discovered the source of this vain opinion of all those physical investigators; and I would have you examine their arguments with the utmost care, for their impiety is a very serious matter; they not only make a bad and mistaken use of argument, but they lead away the minds of others: that is my opinion of them," said the Athenian.

"You are right; but I should like to know how this happens," said Cleinias.

"I fear that the argument may seem singular," replied the Athenian.

"Do not hesitate, Stranger; I see that you are afraid of such a discussion carrying you beyond the limits of legislation. But if there be no other way of showing our agreement in the belief that there are Gods, of whom the law is said now to approve, let us take this way, my good sir," urged Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"I agree with what Cleinias is saying, Stranger," said Megillus.

"And we should follow his proposal," the Athenian replied. "If impious doctrines were not scattered throughout the world, there would be no need to vindicate the existence of the gods. But since such ideas are widespread, the arguments are necessary. And who should come to the defence of the greatest laws, when they are being undermined by wicked men, if not the legislator himself?"

"No one is more suited to the task," Megillus agreed.

"Very well then, Cleinias -- I must enlist you as my partner in this. Tell me: does not the materialist conceive of fire, water, earth, and air as the first elements of all things? These he calls 'nature.' And he supposes the soul to be formed from these elements afterward. This is not our interpretation of his meaning -- it is precisely what he means."

"That is exactly right," said Cleinias.

"Then we have identified the source of this misguided view held by all the natural philosophers. I urge you to examine their arguments with the utmost care, for their impiety is an extremely serious matter. They not only reason badly and reach false conclusions, but they lead other minds astray. That is what I find most dangerous about them."

"You are right," said Cleinias. "But I would like to understand how this happens."

"I am afraid the argument may seem unusual," the Athenian cautioned.

"Do not hesitate, Stranger," Cleinias urged. "I can see you are worried that this discussion will carry us beyond the proper limits of legislation. But if there is no other way to establish our shared belief in the gods -- the very gods our law is designed to honour -- then let us take this path."
D: Elegant: The core error of materialism identified: natural philosophers treat fire, water, earth, and air as the first elements and suppose the soul is formed from them afterward. This gets the causal order exactly backward. Their impiety is dangerous not only because they reason falsely, but because they lead other minds astray. The legislator must be the one to answer them, even if it requires venturing beyond the normal boundaries of legal discussion.
D: Summary: Megillus endorses the project. The Athenian identifies the central error of materialist philosophy: treating the physical elements as primary and the soul as derivative. This inversion of the true causal order is the source of all impiety among the natural philosophers. Cleinias urges the Athenian to pursue the argument even if it goes beyond conventional legislation.
D: Distilled: The root of materialist impiety is placing physical elements before soul in the order of existence -- getting causation exactly backward by treating mind as a product of matter rather than its source.
Notes: Speakers: Megillus, Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This is Megillus's most significant contribution to Book 10 -- the Spartan's brief endorsement carries dramatic weight, showing unanimous agreement among all three interlocutors (representing Athens, Crete, and Sparta). The Athenian's identification of the materialists' core error -- placing body before soul, matter before mind -- is the philosophical hinge of the entire argument. Everything that follows (the proof that soul is prior to body) flows from diagnosing this inversion. The phrase "physical investigators" (physiologoi) is Plato's technical term for pre-Socratic natural philosophers. Cross-reference: Phaedo 96a-99d recounts Socrates' youthful engagement with and ultimate rejection of natural philosophy, arriving at the same conclusion that mind (nous) must be the ordering cause. Timaeus 46d-e makes the identical point: material causes are "secondary and auxiliary," while intelligent purpose is the true cause.

D: Original:
Then I suppose that I must repeat the singular argument of those who manufacture the soul according to their own impious notions; they affirm that which is the first cause of the generation and destruction of all things, to be not first, but last, and that which is last to be first, and hence they have fallen into error about the true nature of the Gods.

Still I do not understand you.
D: Narrative:
"Then I suppose that I must repeat the singular argument of those who manufacture the soul according to their own impious notions; they affirm that which is the first cause of the generation and destruction of all things, to be not first, but last, and that which is last to be first, and hence they have fallen into error about the true nature of the Gods," said the Athenian.

"Still I do not understand you," replied Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Then I must restate the peculiar argument of those who construct their account of the soul according to their own impious ideas," the Athenian said. "They declare that what is actually the first cause of the generation and destruction of all things is not first but last, and what is truly last they place first. This is why they have fallen into error about the true nature of the gods."

"I still do not follow you," said Cleinias.
D: Elegant: The materialists commit a fundamental inversion: they place last what is actually first (soul, the cause of all generation and destruction) and first what is actually last (matter). This reversal of the causal order is the root of their error about the gods.
D: Summary: The Athenian restates the materialists' core error in its simplest form: they reverse the true order of causation, placing the soul -- which is actually the first cause of all generation and destruction -- last, and placing matter first. This inversion leads directly to their false view of the gods.
D: Distilled: The materialists' fundamental mistake is inverting the order of causation: what is truly first (soul) they place last, and what is truly last (matter) they place first.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This concise formulation captures the entire dialectical strategy of Book 10 in a single principle: the materialists have the order of being inverted. The argument that follows will demonstrate this inversion by proving soul's priority to body. The language of "manufacturing" (demiourgein) the soul ironically applies the craftsman metaphor to the materialists' own theory -- they are constructing an account of soul out of their own imaginings rather than discovering its true nature. Cross-reference: this inversion thesis is structurally identical to the allegory of the Cave in Republic VII (514a-517a), where prisoners mistake shadows (derivative phenomena) for primary reality. Phaedo 99b similarly criticizes Anaxagoras for acknowledging mind as a cause but then explaining everything by material causes instead.

D: Original: Nearly all of them, my friends, seem to be ignorant of the nature and power of the soul, especially in what relates to her origin: they do not know that she is among the first of things, and before all bodies, and is the chief author of their changes and transpositions. And if this is true, and if the soul is older than the body, must not the things which are of the soul's kindred be of necessity prior to those which appertain to the body?
