D: Narrative: "Nearly all of them, my friends, seem to be ignorant of the nature and power of the soul, especially in what relates to her origin: they do not know that she is among the first of things, and before all bodies, and is the chief author of their changes and transpositions. And if this is true, and if the soul is older than the body, must not the things which are of the soul's kindred be of necessity prior to those which appertain to the body?" said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned: "Nearly all of them, my friends, seem ignorant of the soul's true nature and power -- especially regarding her origin," the Athenian continued. "They do not realize that the soul is among the very first of all things, prior to every body, and the principal author of all their changes and transformations. If this is true -- if the soul is older than the body -- then must not everything belonging to the soul's family also be prior to everything belonging to the body?"
D: Elegant: The natural philosophers are ignorant of the soul's true nature: she is among the first of all things, prior to every body, and the chief cause of all physical change. If soul is older than body, then everything akin to soul -- thought, purpose, will -- must be prior to everything physical.
D: Summary: The Athenian states his central thesis: nearly all materialist thinkers are ignorant of the soul's nature, especially its origin. The soul is among the first things in existence, prior to all bodies, and the chief cause of all physical change. If soul precedes body, then all qualities of soul must precede all qualities of body.
D: Distilled: The soul is among the first of all things, prior to every body, and the chief author of all physical change -- if soul is older than body, then thought precedes matter.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is the thesis statement of the entire proof of the gods' existence. The claim that soul (psyche) is "among the first of things" and "before all bodies" is Plato's most direct assertion of psychic priority -- the metaphysical foundation for his theology. The gendered pronoun "she" (Greek psyche is feminine) is preserved from Jowett. The argument that kindred properties follow their source (if soul is prior, then soul-properties like thought are prior to body-properties like weight) is a principle of ontological inheritance. Cross-reference: Phaedo 79b-80b argues for the soul's kinship with the eternal and unchanging Forms. Timaeus 34b-35a describes the World Soul as created before the World Body. The idea that soul is the "chief author" of bodily changes anticipates the proof in 10.22-24 that self-motion (soul's defining characteristic) is the origin of all other motion.

D: Original:
Certainly.

Then thought and attention and mind and art and law will be prior to that which is hard and soft and heavy and light; and the great and primitive works and actions will be works of art; they will be the first, and after them will come nature and works of nature, which however is a wrong term for men to apply to them; these will follow, and will be under the government of art and mind.

But why is the word "nature" wrong?

Because those who use the term mean to say that nature is the first creative power; but if the soul turn out to be the primeval element, and not fire or air, then in the truest sense and beyond other things the soul may be said to exist by nature; and this would be true if you proved that the soul is older than the body, but not otherwise.

You are quite right.

Shall we, then, take this as the next point to which our attention should be directed?

By all means.
D: Narrative:
"Certainly," agreed Cleinias.

"Then thought and attention and mind and art and law will be prior to that which is hard and soft and heavy and light; and the great and primitive works and actions will be works of art; they will be the first, and after them will come nature and works of nature, which however is a wrong term for men to apply to them; these will follow, and will be under the government of art and mind," said the Athenian.

"But why is the word 'nature' wrong?" asked Cleinias.

"Because those who use the term mean to say that nature is the first creative power; but if the soul turn out to be the primeval element, and not fire or air, then in the truest sense and beyond other things the soul may be said to exist by nature; and this would be true if you proved that the soul is older than the body, but not otherwise," replied the Athenian.

"You are quite right," said Cleinias.

"Shall we, then, take this as the next point to which our attention should be directed?" asked the Athenian.

"By all means," agreed Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Of course," Cleinias agreed.

"Then thought, attention, mind, art, and law are all prior to hardness, softness, weight, and lightness," the Athenian continued. "The great primordial works and actions are works of art -- they come first. What people call 'nature' and 'works of nature' come after, and are governed by art and mind. Though 'nature' is actually the wrong term to apply to these secondary things."

"Why is the word 'nature' wrong?" asked Cleinias.

"Because those who use it mean to say that nature is the first creative power. But if soul turns out to be the primordial element -- rather than fire or air -- then in the truest and deepest sense, it is soul that exists 'by nature,' more than anything else. This would be established if we can prove that soul is older than body -- but not otherwise."

"You are quite right."

"Shall we take this, then, as the next point to address?"

"By all means."
D: Elegant: If soul is prior to body, then thought, mind, art, and law precede hardness, softness, and weight. The great primordial works are works of intelligent design, not blind matter. The word "nature" is misapplied by materialists: if soul is the primeval element rather than fire or air, then soul is what truly exists "by nature." Everything depends on proving that soul is older than body.
D: Summary: The Athenian draws out the revolutionary implications: if soul precedes body, then thought, mind, art, and law all precede physical properties. The materialists' use of 'nature' is backwards -- if soul is the primordial element, then soul is the most natural thing of all. The entire argument now hinges on proving soul's priority to body.
D: Distilled: If soul is the primeval element, then mind, art, and law are the truly 'natural' things, and what materialists call 'nature' is secondary -- everything depends on proving soul's priority.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. This passage performs a stunning conceptual reversal: the materialists' own key term -- "nature" (physis) -- is turned against them. If soul is primary, then soul is what most truly exists "by nature," and physical elements are secondary, derivative, and in a sense "artificial." This reclamation of the concept of nature for the side of mind and purpose is one of Plato's most brilliant argumentative moves. The list "thought and attention and mind and art and law" represents the hierarchy of psychic activities that, once proven prior, will ground the entire theological argument. Cross-reference: this argument directly parallels Timaeus 34c-35a, where soul is created before body. The Sophist (265b-266d) similarly argues that divine art (the intelligent production of the natural world) is prior to human art. The idea that genuine "nature" is rational order rather than material process anticipates Aristotle's concept of nature as form and final cause (Physics II.1-2).

D: Original:
Let us be on our guard lest this most deceptive argument with its youthful looks, beguiling us old men, give us the slip and make a laughing-stock of us. Who knows but we may be aiming at the greater, and fail of attaining the lesser? Suppose that we three have to pass a rapid river, and I, being the youngest of the three and experienced in rivers, take upon me the duty of making the attempt first by myself; leaving you in safety on the bank, I am to examine whether the river is passable by older men like yourselves, and if such appears to be the case then I shall invite you to follow, and my experience will help to convey you across; but if the river is impassable by you, then there will have been no danger to anybody but myself-would not that seem to be a very fair proposal? I mean to say that the argument in prospect is likely to be too much for you, out of your depth and beyond your strength, and I should be afraid that the stream of my questions might create in you who are not in the habit of answering, giddiness and confusion of mind, and hence a feeling of unpleasantness and unsuitableness might arise. I think therefore that I had better first ask the questions and then answer them myself while you listen in safety; in that way I can carry on the argument until I have completed the proof that the soul is prior to the body.

Excellent, Stranger, and I hope that you will do as you propose.
D: Narrative:
"Let us be on our guard lest this most deceptive argument with its youthful looks, beguiling us old men, give us the slip and make a laughing-stock of us. Who knows but we may be aiming at the greater, and fail of attaining the lesser? Suppose that we three have to pass a rapid river, and I, being the youngest of the three and experienced in rivers, take upon me the duty of making the attempt first by myself; leaving you in safety on the bank, I am to examine whether the river is passable by older men like yourselves, and if such appears to be the case then I shall invite you to follow, and my experience will help to convey you across; but if the river is impassable by you, then there will have been no danger to anybody but myself -- would not that seem to be a very fair proposal? I mean to say that the argument in prospect is likely to be too much for you, out of your depth and beyond your strength, and I should be afraid that the stream of my questions might create in you who are not in the habit of answering, giddiness and confusion of mind, and hence a feeling of unpleasantness and unsuitableness might arise. I think therefore that I had better first ask the questions and then answer them myself while you listen in safety; in that way I can carry on the argument until I have completed the proof that the soul is prior to the body," said the Athenian.

"Excellent, Stranger, and I hope that you will do as you propose," replied Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Let us be careful," the Athenian cautioned, "lest this deceptive argument, with its attractive and youthful appearance, beguile us old men and slip away, making fools of us. We might aim for the greater prize and fail to secure even the lesser.

Imagine the three of us must cross a swift river. I, being the youngest and experienced with rivers, would go first alone -- leaving you safe on the bank -- to test whether the crossing is manageable for older men. If it proves safe, I would invite you to follow, and my experience would help guide you across. If the river proves impassable for you, then only I would have been at risk. Would that not be a fair proposal?

What I mean is this: the argument ahead is likely to be out of your depth and beyond your strength. I worry that the rapid current of my questions might create dizziness and confusion in minds unaccustomed to dialectical exchange, producing frustration rather than understanding. So I propose to ask the questions and answer them myself, while you listen in safety. In this way I can carry the argument through to its conclusion: the proof that soul is prior to body."

"Excellent, Stranger," said Cleinias. "Please proceed as you propose."
D: Elegant: The Athenian warns against overconfidence and proposes a method: like testing a swift river before inviting others to cross, he will conduct the argument himself -- asking and answering his own questions -- while his companions listen safely. The dialectic ahead is demanding, and confusion would defeat its purpose. His goal: to complete the proof that soul is prior to body.
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes a methodological shift: rather than risk confusing his elderly companions with rapid dialectical questioning, he will conduct both sides of the argument himself, like a young man testing a river crossing before inviting older companions. This allows him to proceed efficiently toward proving soul's priority to body.
D: Distilled: The Athenian will ask and answer his own questions while his companions listen, carrying the argument safely to its conclusion: the proof that soul is prior to body.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. The river-crossing metaphor is a memorable image of philosophical pedagogy: the teacher tests difficult intellectual terrain before exposing students to it. The shift from dialogue to quasi-monologue is a practical adaptation -- the argument about motion that follows (10.20-24) is technically demanding. The Athenian's concern about "giddiness" (ilingos) in those unaccustomed to answering philosophical questions recalls Meno 80a-b, where Meno compares Socrates to a torpedo fish that numbs those it touches. The self-description as the "youngest" is intriguing -- it may reflect the dramatic fiction or indicate that the Athenian Stranger, while elderly, is younger than the very old Cleinias and Megillus. Cross-reference: Republic VII (532b-c) describes dialectic as requiring long training, and Theaetetus (155c-d) characterizes philosophical wonder as a form of productive disorientation.

D: Original: Come, then, and if ever we are to call upon the Gods, let us call upon them now in all seriousness to come to the demonstration of their own existence. And so holding fast to the rope we will venture upon the depths of the argument. When questions of this sort are asked of me, my safest answer would appear to be as follows:-Some one says to me, "O Stranger, are all things at rest and nothing in motion, or is the exact opposite of this true, or are some things in motion and others at rest?-To this I shall reply that some things are in motion and others at rest. "And do not things which move a place, and are not the things which are at rest at rest in a place?" Certainly. "And some move or rest in one place and some in more places than one?" You mean to say, we shall rejoin, that those things which rest at the centre move in one place, just as the circumference goes round of globes which are said to be at rest? "Yes." And we observe that, in the revolution, the motion which carries round the larger and the lesser circle at the same time is proportionally distributed to greater and smaller, and is greater and smaller in a certain proportion. Here is a wonder which might be thought an impossibility, that the same motion should impart swiftness and slowness in due proportion to larger and lesser circles.
D: Narrative: "Come, then, and if ever we are to call upon the Gods, let us call upon them now in all seriousness to come to the demonstration of their own existence. And so holding fast to the rope we will venture upon the depths of the argument. When questions of this sort are asked of me, my safest answer would appear to be as follows: Some one says to me, 'O Stranger, are all things at rest and nothing in motion, or is the exact opposite of this true, or are some things in motion and others at rest?' To this I shall reply that some things are in motion and others at rest. 'And do not things which move move in a place, and are not the things which are at rest at rest in a place?' Certainly. 'And some move or rest in one place and some in more places than one?' You mean to say, we shall rejoin, that those things which rest at the centre move in one place, just as the circumference goes round of globes which are said to be at rest? 'Yes.' And we observe that, in the revolution, the motion which carries round the larger and the lesser circle at the same time is proportionally distributed to greater and smaller, and is greater and smaller in a certain proportion. Here is a wonder which might be thought an impossibility, that the same motion should impart swiftness and slowness in due proportion to larger and lesser circles," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"Come then," the Athenian began. "If ever we should call upon the gods, let us call upon them now in all seriousness to aid in the demonstration of their own existence. Holding fast to our rope, we will venture into deep waters.

When confronted with questions of this sort, the safest starting point seems to be the following. Someone asks me: 'Stranger, are all things at rest with nothing in motion? Or is the opposite true -- everything in motion? Or are some things in motion while others are at rest?' I would answer: some things are in motion, others at rest.

'And things that move, do they not move in a place? And things at rest, are they not at rest in a place?' Certainly.

'And do some things move or rest in one place, while others move through many places?' By moving in one place, we mean something like this: when a globe spins on its axis, its centre remains fixed while its circumference revolves. The same rotational motion carries both the larger and smaller circles simultaneously, distributing proportional speeds to each -- greater speed to the larger circumference, lesser to the smaller. Here is something remarkable, almost paradoxical: a single motion imparting different speeds to different circles in exact proportion."
D: Elegant: The Athenian invokes the gods to aid the proof of their own existence, then begins his analysis of motion. Starting from the basic observation that some things move and others rest, he identifies rotational motion: a spinning globe whose centre is at rest while its circumference revolves. A single rotation distributes proportional speeds to larger and smaller circles -- a remarkable fact that sets up the taxonomy of motion to follow.
D: Summary: The Athenian solemnly invokes the gods to assist in proving their own existence, then begins his systematic analysis of motion. He starts with the basic distinction between rest and motion, then examines rotational motion -- where a spinning object is both at rest (at its centre) and in motion (at its circumference), with proportional speeds distributed to circles of different sizes.
D: Distilled: The proof begins with a taxonomy of motion, starting from the observation that rotational motion unites rest and motion in a single object, distributing proportional speeds to its larger and smaller circles.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The invocation of the gods to assist in proving their own existence is a moment of high philosophical drama -- and deliberate irony. The "rope" metaphor continues the river-crossing imagery from 10.19. The analysis of rotational motion begins the ten-fold classification of types of motion (completed in 10.21-22) that culminates in the identification of self-motion as the primary type. The observation about proportional speeds in concentric circles reflects contemporary Greek astronomical knowledge -- Eudoxus of Cnidus (a contemporary of Plato at the Academy) developed a system of concentric rotating spheres to explain planetary motion. Cross-reference: Timaeus 34a describes the World Soul's circular rotation as the motion most appropriate to intelligence. Republic VII (528a-530c) discusses astronomy as a mathematical science of circular motions. The starting premise -- that some things are in motion, some at rest -- recalls Parmenides' distinction between being and becoming, which Plato synthesizes rather than choosing between.

D: Original: "Very true." And when you speak of bodies moving in many places, you seem to me to mean those which move from one place to another, and sometimes have one centre of motion and sometimes more than one because they turn upon their axis; and whenever they meet anything, if it be stationary, they are divided by it; but if they get in the midst between bodies which are approaching and moving towards the same spot from opposite directions, they unite with them. "I admit the truth of what you are saying." Also when they unite they grow, and when they are divided they waste away-that is, supposing the constitution of each to remain, or if that fails, then there is a second reason of their dissolution. "And when are all things created and how?" Clearly, they are created when the first principle receives increase and attains to the second dimension, and from this arrives at the one which is neighbour to this, and after reaching the third becomes perceptible to sense. Everything which is thus changing and moving is in process of generation; only when at rest has it real existence, but when passing into another state it is destroyed utterly. Have we not mentioned all motions that there are, and comprehended them under their kinds and numbered them with the exception, my friends, of two?

Everything which is thus changing and moving is in process of generation; only when at rest has it real existence, but when passing into another state it is destroyed utterly. Have we not mentioned all motions that there are, and comprehended them under their kinds and numbered them with the exception, my friends, of two?" said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"Bodies that move through many places," the Athenian continued, "travel from one location to another, sometimes turning on a single axis, sometimes on more than one. When they encounter a stationary object, they are divided by it; when they find themselves between bodies approaching from opposite directions, they merge with them.

When things combine, they grow; when divided, they diminish -- assuming their basic constitution holds. If it fails, that is another cause of dissolution.

How are things created? A first principle extends from one dimension to a second, then from the second to the third, and upon reaching three dimensions becomes perceptible to the senses. Everything in this process of change and motion is in a state of becoming; only when at rest does it have stable existence, and when it passes into another state it is destroyed entirely.

Now -- have we not catalogued and classified all the kinds of motion that exist, with the exception of two?"
D: Elegant: Bodies moving through space divide when they hit stationary objects and merge when caught between converging ones. Growth comes from combination, decay from division. Creation occurs as a principle extends through one, two, then three dimensions until it becomes perceptible. All that changes is in the process of becoming; only what is at rest truly exists. All motions have been classified except two -- the most important ones.
D: Summary: The Athenian continues cataloguing types of motion: locomotion, collision, division, combination, growth, decay, and the dimensional process of creation from point to line to surface to solid. He observes that only what is at rest has stable existence, while everything in motion is in a state of becoming. He notes that two crucial types of motion remain unclassified.
D: Distilled: All types of physical motion have been catalogued -- combination, division, growth, decay, dimensional generation -- except for two: the most important kinds that relate directly to the soul.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The taxonomy of motion continues, covering locomotion, collision (division and combination), growth and decay, and dimensional generation (from point to line to plane to solid). The observation that "only when at rest has it real existence" echoes the Parmenidean and Platonic privileging of stable being over flux -- cf. Timaeus 27d-28a on the distinction between what always is and what is always becoming. The dimensional progression from first to second to third dimension recalls the mathematical cosmology of the Timaeus (53c-55c), where the physical elements are constructed from geometric shapes. The dramatic pause before revealing the two remaining motions creates suspense: these two -- motion that moves others but not itself, and self-motion -- are the philosophical climax of the entire argument.

D: Original:
Which are they?

Just the two, with which our present enquiry is concerned.

Speak plainer.

I suppose that our enquiry has reference to the soul?

Very true.

Let us assume that there is a motion able to move other things, but not to move itself;-that is one kind; and there is another kind which can move itself as well as other things, working in composition and decomposition, by increase and diminution and generation and destruction-that is also one of the many kinds of motion.

Granted.

And we will assume that which moves other, and is changed by other, to be the ninth, and that which changes itself and others, and is co-incident with every action and every passion, and is the true principle of change and motion in all that is-that we shall be inclined to call the tenth.

Certainly.

And which of these ten motions ought we to prefer as being the mightiest and most efficient?

I must say that the motion which is able to move itself is ten thousand times superior to all the others.

Very good; but may I make one or two corrections in what I have been saying?

What are they?

When I spoke of the tenth sort of motion, that was not quite correct.

What was the error?

According to the true order, the tenth was really the first in generation and power; then follows the second, which was strangely enough termed the ninth by us.

What do you mean?

I mean this: when one thing changes another, and that another, of such will there be any primary changing element? How can a thing which is moved by another ever be the beginning of change? Impossible. But when the self-moved changes other, and that again other, and thus thousands upon tens of thousands of bodies are set in motion, must not the beginning of all this motion be the change of the self-moving principle?

Very true, and I quite agree.

Or, to put the question in another way, making answer to ourselves:-If, as most of these philosophers have the audacity to affirm, all things were at rest in one mass, which of the above-mentioned principles of motion would first spring up among them?

Clearly the self-moving; for there could be no change in them arising out of any external cause; the change must first take place in themselves.

D: Refashioned:
"Which two are missing?" asked Cleinias.

"The very two our whole inquiry concerns," the Athenian replied.

"Explain more clearly."

"Our inquiry concerns the soul, does it not?"

"It does."

"Then consider: there is a kind of motion that can move other things but cannot move itself -- that is one kind. And there is another that can move both itself and other things, operating through combination and separation, increase and decrease, generation and destruction."

"Granted."

"Let us call the motion that moves others but is moved by others the ninth kind. And the motion that moves itself and others -- that coincides with every action and every experience, and is the true principle of all change and motion in everything that exists -- we shall call the tenth."

"Agreed."

"Now, which of these ten motions should we regard as the mightiest and most effective?"

"Self-motion is ten thousand times superior to all the others," Cleinias declared.

"Excellent. But let me correct something I said. When I called self-motion the tenth, that was not quite right."

"What was the error?"

"In the true order, the 'tenth' is actually the first -- first in generation and power. What we called the ninth is really the second."

"What do you mean?"

"Consider: when one thing moves another, and that moves another, can there be any starting point in such a chain? How can something moved by another ever be the origin of change? Impossible. But when the self-moved sets another in motion, and that sets another, and so on through thousands upon tens of thousands -- the origin of all that motion must be the self-moving principle."

"That is certainly true."

"Or put it another way: if, as these materialist philosophers audaciously claim, all things were once at rest in a single undifferentiated mass -- which of our ten kinds of motion would first arise?"

"Obviously self-motion," Cleinias answered. "No change could arise from any external cause in such a state. The first change must originate from within."
D: Elegant: The two crucial types of motion: that which moves others but not itself (ninth), and that which moves both itself and others (tenth). Self-motion is infinitely superior -- and though numbered last, it is actually first in the order of existence. In any chain of caused motions, the origin must be a self-mover. If everything were once at rest, only self-motion could initiate change. Therefore self-motion is the first principle of all motion.
D: Summary: The Athenian reveals the two remaining types of motion: motion that moves other things but not itself, and self-motion that moves both itself and others. Self-motion, though numbered tenth, is actually first in the true order -- since no chain of externally caused motions can have a beginning unless a self-mover initiates it. Even from a state of universal rest, only self-motion could arise first.
D: Distilled: Self-motion is the first principle of all motion: no chain of externally caused changes can begin without a self-mover, and from universal rest only self-motion could arise.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. This is the philosophical heart of Book 10 -- the proof that self-motion is the first principle. The argument proceeds in two steps: (1) regress argument -- any chain of moved movers requires an unmoved (self-moving) origin; (2) cosmogonic argument -- from a hypothetical state of universal rest, only self-motion could initiate change. The numbering reversal (tenth becomes first) is a deliberate dramatic device emphasizing that what seemed last in enumeration is first in reality -- mirroring the materialists' inversion that the argument corrects. Cleinias's exclamation that self-motion is "ten thousand times superior" uses the Greek idiom murioplasion, expressing vast superiority. Cross-reference: Phaedrus 245c-246a presents the same argument: "All soul is immortal, for that which is ever in motion is immortal. But that which while imparting motion is itself moved by something else can cease to move and therefore cease to live." Aristotle's Unmoved Mover (Metaphysics XII, Physics VIII) develops from and transforms this Platonic argument, replacing self-motion with a motionless final cause.

D: Original:
Then we must say that self-motion being the origin of all motions, and the first which arises among things at rest as well as among things in motion, is the eldest and mightiest principle of change, and that which is changed by another and yet moves other is second.

Quite true.

At this stage of the argument let us put a question.

What question?

If we were to see this power existing in any earthy, watery, or fiery substance, simple or compound-how should we describe it?

You mean to ask whether we should call such a self-moving power life?

I do.

Certainly we should.

And when we see soul in anything, must we not do the same-must we not admit that this is life?

We must.

And now, I beseech you, reflect;-you would admit that we have a threefold knowledge of things?

What do you mean?

I mean that we know the essence, and that we know the definition of the essence, and the name,-these are the three; and there are two questions which may be raised about anything.

How two?

Sometimes a person may give the name and ask the definition; or he may give the definition and ask the name. I may illustrate what I mean in this way.

How?

Number like some other things is capable of being divided into equal parts; when thus divided, number is named "even," and the definition of the name "even" is "number divisible into two equal parts"?

True.

I mean, that when we are asked about the definition and give the name, or when we are asked about the name and give the definition-in either case, whether we give name or definition, we speak of the same thing, calling "even" the number which is divided into two equal parts.

Quite true.

And what is the definition of that which is named "soul"? Can we conceive of any other than that which has been already given-the motion which can move itself?

You mean to say that the essence which is defined as the self-moved is the same with that which has the name soul?

"How?" asked Cleinias.

"Number like some other things is capable of being divided into equal parts; when thus divided, number is named 'even,' and the definition of the name 'even' is 'number divisible into two equal parts,'" said the Athenian.

"True," agreed Cleinias.

"I mean, that when we are asked about the definition and give the name, or when we are asked about the name and give the definition -- in either case, whether we give name or definition, we speak of the same thing, calling 'even' the number which is divided into two equal parts," said the Athenian.

"Quite true," said Cleinias.

"And what is the definition of that which is named 'soul'? Can we conceive of any other than that which has been already given -- the motion which can move itself?" asked the Athenian.

"You mean to say that the essence which is defined as the self-moved is the same with that which has the name soul?" said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Self-motion, then, is the origin of all motions," the Athenian concluded. "It is the first to arise whether among things at rest or things already in motion -- the eldest and mightiest principle of change. Motion that is caused by another while itself causing motion in something else is second."

"Exactly right," said Cleinias.

"Now let us ask a question. If we observed this power of self-motion in any earthy, watery, or fiery substance, whether simple or compound -- what would we call it?"

"You are asking whether we would call that power 'life,'" Cleinias anticipated.

"I am."

"Of course we would."

"And when we recognize soul in anything, must we not acknowledge the same thing -- that where there is soul, there is life?"

"We must."

"Now reflect on this: we have three ways of knowing any given thing -- its essence, its definition, and its name. And two questions can be asked about anything: given the name, what is the definition? Or given the definition, what is the name?"

"Go on."

"Take number as an example. A number divisible into two equal parts is named 'even.' The definition of 'even' is 'a number divisible into two equal parts.' Whether you start from the name or from the definition, you arrive at the same thing."

"True."

"Now -- what is the definition of that which bears the name 'soul'? Can we conceive any definition other than what we have already established: the motion that can move itself?"

"You are saying," Cleinias replied, "that the essence defined as 'self-moved' is identical with what we call 'soul.'"
D: Elegant: Self-motion is the first and mightiest principle of all change. Wherever we see self-moving power in any substance, we call it life. Where there is soul, there is life. Every thing has an essence, a definition, and a name. Just as 'even' is defined as 'number divisible into two equal parts,' so 'soul' is defined as 'self-moving motion.' The essence of soul is self-motion.
D: Summary: The Athenian establishes that self-motion is the origin of all motion, then connects it to life and soul through a careful semantic analysis. Self-moving power in any substance is called life; soul is wherever life is; and the very definition of 'soul' is 'the motion that moves itself.' The essence, definition, and name all converge on the same identity.
D: Distilled: Soul is defined as self-motion -- wherever we find the power to move itself, there we find life, and there we find soul.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. The argument here moves from physics to ontology to semantics: (1) self-motion is the first principle of all motion, (2) self-motion in any substance is called life, (3) the name for this life-principle is 'soul,' (4) therefore soul = self-motion by definition. The semantic analysis using essence/definition/name is an application of the method developed in the Sophist (218b-221c) and Cratylus (383a-390e). The example of 'even' as 'number divisible into two equal parts' demonstrates that name and definition pick out the same essence -- applied to soul, this yields the crucial identity: soul = self-moving motion. Cross-reference: Phaedrus 245e: "Soul, whose definition is self-motion." This definition of soul as self-motion is Plato's most enduring contribution to psychology (literally, the study of psyche). Aristotle will modify it in De Anima II.1, defining soul instead as the "first actuality of a natural body having life potentially."

D: Original:
Yes; and if this is true, do we still maintain that there is anything wanting in the proof that the soul is the first origin and moving power of all that is, or has become, or will be, and their contraries, when she has been clearly shown to be the source of change and motion in all things?

Certainly not; the soul as being the source of motion, has been most satisfactorily shown to be the oldest of all things.

And is not that motion which is produced in another, by reason of another, but never has any self-moving power at all, being in truth the change of an inanimate body, to be reckoned second, or by any lower number which you may prefer?

Exactly.

Then we are right, and speak the most perfect and absolute truth, when we say that the soul is prior to the body, and that the body is second and comes afterwards, and is born to obey the soul, which is the ruler?

Nothing can be more true.

Do you remember our old admission, that if the soul was prior to the body the things of the soul were also prior to those of the body?

Certainly.

Then characters and manners, and wishes and reasonings, and true opinions, and reflections, and recollections are prior to length and breadth and depth and strength of bodies, if the soul is prior to the body.

To be sure.

In the next place, must we not of necessity admit that the soul is the cause of good and evil, base and honourable, just and unjust, and of all other opposites, if we suppose her to be the cause of all things?

We must.

And as the soul orders and inhabits all things that move, however moving, must we not say that she orders also the heavens?

Of course.

One soul or more? More than one-I will answer for you; at any rate, we must not suppose that there are less than two-one the author of good, and the other of evil.

Very true.
