"I will explain: When the king saw that our actions had life, and that there was much virtue in them and much vice, and that the soul and body, although not, like the Gods of popular opinion, eternal, yet having once come into existence, were indestructible -- for if either of them had been destroyed, there would have been no generation of living beings -- and when he observed that the good of the soul was ever by nature designed to profit men, and the evil to harm them -- he, seeing all this, contrived so to place each of the parts that their position might in the easiest and best manner procure the victory of good and the defeat of evil in the whole. And he contrived a general plan by which a thing of a certain nature found a certain seat and room. But the formation of qualities he left to the wills of individuals. For every one of us is made pretty much what he is by the bent of his desires and the nature of his soul," said the Athenian.

"Yes, that is probably true," agreed Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Let me explain," the Athenian continued. "When the divine king observed that our actions are alive -- containing much virtue and much vice -- and that soul and body, though not eternal in the way popular religion supposes, are nonetheless indestructible once they come into existence (for if either were destroyed, the generation of living beings would cease) -- and when he further observed that the soul's goodness naturally benefits human beings while its evil naturally harms them -- seeing all this, he arranged each part so that its placement would most easily and effectively ensure the victory of good over evil in the whole.

He designed a general plan in which each nature finds its appropriate place and station. But the formation of individual character he left to the will of each person. Every one of us becomes what we are largely through the direction of our desires and the nature of our soul."

"That seems likely," Cleinias agreed.
D: Elegant: The divine king observed that soul and body, once created, are indestructible, and that the soul's good naturally benefits while its evil naturally harms. He arranged the cosmic order so that each part occupies the position best suited to ensure good's victory over evil. But he designed a general plan for placement, not individual character -- that he left to individual will. We become what we are through our own desires.
D: Summary: The Athenian describes the divine king's strategy: observing that soul and body are indestructible and that good naturally profits while evil harms, he arranged the cosmic order to give good the advantage over evil. He established the general framework of placement but left the formation of individual character to each person's own will and desires.
D: Distilled: The divine king arranged the cosmos so that each nature finds its proper place and good prevails over evil, but left the formation of individual character to each person's own will and desires.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This passage presents Plato's solution to the problem of evil and free will simultaneously. The divine king (basileus) establishes the framework (the cosmic game board) but does not determine individual character -- that depends on each soul's desires. This is a remarkable articulation of compatibilism: divine providence sets the structure, individual will determines moral quality. The claim that soul and body are "indestructible" (anolethros) once created differs subtly from the Phaedo's argument for the soul's immortality: here both soul and body persist (through transmigration and elemental recycling), but neither is eternal in the divine sense. Cross-reference: Timaeus 41a-d where the Demiurge creates souls and assigns them to stars, but warns that their subsequent lives depend on their own choices. Republic X (617e): "The blame belongs to the chooser; god is blameless" (aitia helomenou; theos anaitios). This is also Plato's most explicit statement of moral responsibility in the context of cosmic determinism.

D: Original: Then all things which have a soul change, and possess in themselves a principle of change, and in changing move according to law and to the order of destiny: natures which have undergone a lesser change move less and on the earth's surface, but those which have suffered more change and have become more criminal sink into the abyss, that is to say, into Hades and other places in the world below, of which the very names terrify men, and which they picture to themselves as in a dream, both while alive and when released from the body. And whenever the soul receives more of good or evil from her own energy and the strong influence of others-when she has communion with divine virtue and becomes divine, she is carried into another and better place, which is perfect in holiness; but when she has communion with evil, then she also changes the Place of her life.
D: Narrative: "Then all things which have a soul change, and possess in themselves a principle of change, and in changing move according to law and to the order of destiny: natures which have undergone a lesser change move less and on the earth's surface, but those which have suffered more change and have become more criminal sink into the abyss, that is to say, into Hades and other places in the world below, of which the very names terrify men, and which they picture to themselves as in a dream, both while alive and when released from the body. And whenever the soul receives more of good or evil from her own energy and the strong influence of others -- when she has communion with divine virtue and becomes divine, she is carried into another and better place, which is perfect in holiness; but when she has communion with evil, then she also changes the place of her life," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"All ensouled things change," the Athenian continued, "for they carry within themselves a principle of change. And in changing, they move according to law and the order of destiny.

Natures that have undergone only small changes move less dramatically and remain on the earth's surface. But those that have changed more profoundly -- those that have become deeply criminal -- sink into the abyss: into Hades and those places below the earth whose very names fill people with terror, places they imagine in dreams both while alive and after death.

Whenever the soul accumulates more good -- through her own energy and the powerful influence of others -- when she enters into communion with divine virtue and becomes divine herself, she is carried to a higher, better place, one perfect in holiness. But when she enters into communion with evil, she likewise changes the place of her life -- downward."
D: Elegant: All ensouled things change according to law and destiny. Those who change little remain on earth's surface. Those who become deeply criminal sink into the abyss -- Hades and the terrifying places below. But when a soul communes with divine virtue and becomes divine, she rises to a place perfect in holiness. When she communes with evil, she sinks. Each soul's trajectory follows from its own moral character.
D: Summary: The Athenian describes the cosmic law of moral movement: all ensouled beings change and move according to destiny. Minor changes keep souls on earth's surface; deep criminality sinks them into Hades. Communion with divine virtue elevates the soul to a holy place; communion with evil drags it down. The soul's destination is determined by its moral character.
D: Distilled: Souls move according to moral destiny: virtue carries them upward toward divine holiness, while vice sinks them into the abyss -- each soul's trajectory determined by its own moral character.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage presents Plato's eschatological vision -- the cosmic consequences of individual moral choice. The vertical moral geography (earth's surface, the heights of holiness, the depths of Hades) maps moral quality onto spatial location, a scheme common in Plato's afterlife myths. The mention of Hades "whose very names terrify men" acknowledges the psychological power of traditional mythology while reinterpreting it philosophically: the underworld represents not arbitrary punishment but the natural destination of corrupted souls. The phrase "communion with divine virtue" (theias aretes koinonesasa) describes a transformative participation that makes the soul itself divine -- a form of deification (theosis) that will become central to Neoplatonic and Christian mystical theology. Cross-reference: Phaedo 107d-114c (the myth of the afterlife), Republic X (614b-621d, the Myth of Er), Phaedrus (248a-249b), and Gorgias (523a-527a) all present eschatological visions of souls ascending or descending based on their moral condition. The Laws' version is notably less mythological and more systematic than these earlier accounts.

D: Original: This is the justice of the Gods who inhabit Olympus. O youth or young man, who fancy that you are neglected by the Gods, know that if you become worse you shall go to the worse souls, or if better to the better, and in every succession of life and death you will do and suffer what like may fitly suffer at the hands of like. This is the justice of heaven, which neither you nor any other unfortunate will ever glory in escaping, and which the ordaining powers have specially ordained; take good heed thereof, for it will be sure to take heed of you. If you say:-I am small and will creep into the depths of the earth, or I am high and will fly up to heaven, you are not so small or so high but that you shall pay the fitting penalty, either here or in the world below or in some still more savage place whither you shall be conveyed. This is also the explanation of the fate of those whom you saw, who had done unholy and evil deeds, and from small beginnings had grown great, and you fancied that from being miserable they had become happy; and in their actions, as in a mirror, you seemed to see the universal neglect of the Gods, not knowing how they make all things work together and contribute to the great whole. And thinkest thou, bold man, that thou needest not to know this?-he who knows it not can never form any true idea of the happiness or unhappiness of life or hold any rational discourse respecting either. If Cleinias and this our reverend company succeed in bringing to you that you know not what you say of the Gods, then will God help you; but should you desire to hear more, listen to what we say to the third opponent, if you have any understanding whatsoever. For I think that we have sufficiently proved the existence of the Gods, and that they care for men:-The other notion that they are appeased by the wicked, and take gifts, is what we must not concede to any one, and what every man should disprove to the utmost of his power.
D: Narrative: "This is the justice of the Gods who inhabit Olympus. O youth or young man, who fancy that you are neglected by the Gods, know that if you become worse you shall go to the worse souls, or if better to the better, and in every succession of life and death you will do and suffer what like may fitly suffer at the hands of like. This is the justice of heaven, which neither you nor any other unfortunate will ever glory in escaping, and which the ordaining powers have specially ordained; take good heed thereof, for it will be sure to take heed of you. If you say: 'I am small and will creep into the depths of the earth, or I am high and will fly up to heaven,' you are not so small or so high but that you shall pay the fitting penalty, either here or in the world below or in some still more savage place whither you shall be conveyed. This is also the explanation of the fate of those whom you saw, who had done unholy and evil deeds, and from small beginnings had grown great, and you fancied that from being miserable they had become happy; and in their actions, as in a mirror, you seemed to see the universal neglect of the Gods, not knowing how they make all things work together and contribute to the great whole. And thinkest thou, bold man, that thou needest not to know this? He who knows it not can never form any true idea of the happiness or unhappiness of life or hold any rational discourse respecting either. If Cleinias and this our reverend company succeed in proving to you that you know not what you say of the Gods, then will God help you; but should you desire to hear more, listen to what we say to the third opponent, if you have any understanding whatsoever. For I think that we have sufficiently proved the existence of the Gods, and that they care for men: The other notion that they are appeased by the wicked, and take gifts, is what we must not concede to any one, and what every man should disprove to the utmost of his power," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"This is the justice of the gods who inhabit Olympus," the Athenian declared. "Young man, you who imagine the gods have neglected you -- know this: if you become worse, you will join worse souls; if better, better ones. In every cycle of life and death, you will both do and suffer what is fitting for your kind, at the hands of those like yourself.

This is the justice of heaven. Neither you nor any other person will ever escape it -- the ordaining powers have made certain of this. Pay close attention, for this justice will certainly pay attention to you.

If you say, 'I am so small I will burrow into the depths of the earth,' or 'I am so exalted I will fly up to heaven' -- you are neither so small nor so great that you can avoid the fitting penalty, whether in this life, in the underworld, or in some still harsher place.

This also explains what troubled you: those wicked people who rose from nothing to great power, whom you mistakenly thought had gone from misery to happiness. In their apparent success, as in a mirror, you thought you saw proof of divine neglect. But you did not understand how the gods make all things work together for the good of the great whole.

Do you suppose you can afford not to know this? Without this knowledge, no one can form a true understanding of happiness or unhappiness, or reason about either one coherently.

If our company succeeds in showing you that you do not understand what you say about the gods, then god will help you. But if you wish to hear more, listen to what we say to the third kind of impiety -- those who think the gods can be bribed. We have sufficiently proved that gods exist and that they care for humanity. The notion that gods can be appeased by the wicked and accept their gifts is something no one should concede, and everyone should refute with all their power."
D: Elegant: This is heaven's justice: the worse you become, the worse your company and fate in the cycle of lives; the better, the better. No one escapes this -- not by hiding in the depths nor fleeing to the heights. Those wicked people who appeared to prosper were not evidence of divine neglect; they were part of a larger pattern you failed to comprehend. Without understanding how all things work together for the whole, you cannot grasp true happiness or misery. The existence of the gods and their care for humanity is proved. Now remains the third impiety: the claim that gods can be bribed.
D: Summary: The Athenian delivers the culminating address to the believer in divine negligence: cosmic justice ensures that moral character determines destiny across the cycle of lives, and no one can escape it. The apparent prosperity of the wicked is not evidence of divine neglect but part of a larger cosmic pattern. He declares the first two proofs complete and transitions to the third form of impiety: divine corruptibility.
D: Distilled: Cosmic justice is inescapable -- moral character determines destiny across all lives, the apparent prosperity of the wicked is no proof of divine neglect, and the gods' existence and care for humanity are now sufficiently proved.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is the rhetorical and emotional climax of the argument against divine negligence. The address shifts from philosophical argument to prophetic proclamation: "This is the justice of heaven." The impossibility of escape ("neither small nor high") recalls Psalm 139:7-12 ("Where can I go from your Spirit?") and reflects a universal religious intuition about divine omniscience. The resolution of the problem of evil (wicked people prospering) is explicitly teleological: their apparent success is part of a larger cosmic pattern working for the whole. The transition to the third impiety (divine corruptibility) is signaled clearly, marking the structural turning point of Book 10. Cross-reference: Republic II (363a-367e) raises the same problem of wicked prosperity; Republic X (612b-613e) resolves it with similar cosmic justice. Gorgias 523a-527a provides the mythological version. The phrase "all things work together" (panta syntetagmena) anticipates Romans 8:28: "All things work together for good."

D: Original:
Very good; let us do as you say.

Well, then, by the Gods themselves I conjure you to tell me-if they are to be propitiated, how are they to be propitiated? Who are they, and what is their nature? Must they not be at least rulers who have to order unceasingly the whole heaven?

True.
D: Narrative:
"Very good; let us do as you say," said Cleinias.

"Well, then, by the Gods themselves I conjure you to tell me -- if they are to be propitiated, how are they to be propitiated? Who are they, and what is their nature? Must they not be at least rulers who have to order unceasingly the whole heaven?" asked the Athenian.

"True," agreed Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Very well -- let us proceed as you say," agreed Cleinias.

"Then I ask you, in the name of the gods themselves: if the gods can be propitiated, how exactly would this work? What kind of beings are they? What is their nature? At the very least, they must be rulers who continuously order the entire heavens."

"That is true," Cleinias agreed.
D: Elegant: If the gods can be propitiated, what are they? At minimum, they are rulers who ceaselessly order the whole heaven. The nature of these beings determines whether bribery is even coherent.
D: Summary: The Athenian begins the argument against divine corruptibility by asking what kind of beings the gods actually are. If they are rulers who ceaselessly order the entire heavens, the question of whether they can be bribed must be examined in light of their nature and responsibilities.
D: Distilled: To determine whether gods can be bribed, we must first consider what they are: rulers who ceaselessly order the entire heavens.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. The invocation "by the gods themselves" to discuss whether gods can be bribed carries deliberate irony. The methodological point is that the nature of the gods -- already established through the prior arguments -- constrains what can coherently be said about them. Since gods are cosmic rulers who order the heavens, the claim that they accept bribes must be tested against this conception. The Athenian is about to deploy a series of analogies comparing gods to human guardians (pilots, generals, physicians, shepherds), all of whom would be disgraced by accepting bribes. Cross-reference: Republic I (342a-e) establishes that rulers in the true sense serve the interest of the ruled, not themselves. The Statesman (267c-268d) classifies different types of rulers to identify the true statesman -- a similar method to what follows here.

D: Original: And to what earthly rulers can they be compared, or who to them? How in the less can we find an image of the greater? Are they charioteers of contending pairs of steeds, or pilots of vessels? Perhaps they might be compared to the generals of armies, or they might be likened to physicians providing against the diseases which make war upon the body, or to husbandmen observing anxiously the effects of the seasons on the growth of plants; or I perhaps, to shepherds of flocks. For as we acknowledge the world to be full of many goods and also of evils, and of more evils than goods, there is, as we affirm, an immortal conflict going on among us, which requires marvellous watchfulness; and in that conflict the Gods and demigods are our allies, and we are their property. Injustice and insolence and folly are the destruction of us, and justice and temperance and wisdom are our salvation; and the place of these latter is in the life of the Gods, although some vestige of them may occasionally be discerned among mankind. But upon this earth we know that there dwell souls possessing an unjust spirit, who may be compared to brute animals, which fawn upon their keepers, whether dogs or shepherds, or the best and most perfect masters; for they in like manner, as the voices of the wicked declare, prevail by flattery and prayers and incantations, and are allowed to make their gains with impunity. And this sin, which is termed dishonesty, is an evil of the same kind as what is termed disease in living bodies or pestilence in years or seasons of the year, and in cities and governments has another name, which is injustice.
D: Narrative: "And to what earthly rulers can they be compared, or who to them? How in the less can we find an image of the greater? Are they charioteers of contending pairs of steeds, or pilots of vessels? Perhaps they might be compared to the generals of armies, or they might be likened to physicians providing against the diseases which make war upon the body, or to husbandmen observing anxiously the effects of the seasons on the growth of plants; or perhaps, to shepherds of flocks. For as we acknowledge the world to be full of many goods and also of evils, and of more evils than goods, there is, as we affirm, an immortal conflict going on among us, which requires marvellous watchfulness; and in that conflict the Gods and demigods are our allies, and we are their property. Injustice and insolence and folly are the destruction of us, and justice and temperance and wisdom are our salvation; and the place of these latter is in the life of the Gods, although some vestige of them may occasionally be discerned among mankind. But upon this earth we know that there dwell souls possessing an unjust spirit, who may be compared to brute animals, which fawn upon their keepers, whether dogs or shepherds, or the best and most perfect masters; for they in like manner, as the voices of the wicked declare, prevail by flattery and prayers and incantations, and are allowed to make their gains with impunity. And this sin, which is termed dishonesty, is an evil of the same kind as what is termed disease in living bodies or pestilence in years or seasons of the year, and in cities and governments has another name, which is injustice," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"To what earthly rulers can the gods be compared?" the Athenian asked. "How can we find, in lesser things, an image of the greater? Are the gods like charioteers driving rival teams? Pilots steering ships? Generals commanding armies? Physicians fighting the diseases that wage war on the body? Farmers anxiously watching the seasons' effects on their crops? Or perhaps shepherds tending their flocks?

For we acknowledge that the world contains many goods and many evils -- more evils than goods, in fact -- and that an immortal battle rages among us, requiring extraordinary vigilance. In this battle, the gods and demigods are our allies, and we are their property.

Injustice, insolence, and folly are our destruction; justice, temperance, and wisdom are our salvation. These virtues have their true home in the life of the gods, though faint traces of them sometimes appear among human beings.

But upon this earth there dwell souls of unjust spirit, comparable to fawning animals that ingratiate themselves with their keepers -- whether dogs, shepherds, or even the best of masters. These wicked souls, as they themselves boast, prevail through flattery, prayers, and incantations, and are permitted to profit from their dishonesty with impunity.

This sin called dishonesty is an evil of the same species as disease in living bodies, pestilence in the seasons, and -- under another name -- injustice in cities and governments."
D: Elegant: The gods may be compared to charioteers, pilots, generals, physicians, farmers, or shepherds -- all engaged in an eternal battle where justice is our salvation and injustice our destruction. Wicked souls resemble fawning animals that bribe their keepers through flattery and offerings. This corruption -- called dishonesty in private life, disease in the body, pestilence in the seasons, and injustice in politics -- is a single evil taking different forms in different domains.
D: Summary: The Athenian searches for the right analogy for the gods' role and settles on multiple guardians engaged in an eternal conflict between good and evil. He identifies wicked people as fawning creatures who try to corrupt their divine guardians through flattery and bribes. The corruption they represent is a universal evil that manifests as dishonesty, disease, pestilence, or injustice depending on the domain.
D: Distilled: The gods are guardians engaged in an immortal battle between justice and injustice, and those who try to bribe them are like fawning animals corrupting their keepers -- spreading a universal evil that takes the form of dishonesty, disease, and political injustice.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The startling claim that there are "more evils than goods" in the world differs from the usual Platonic optimism and may reflect the late Plato's more sober assessment. The identification of dishonesty, disease, pestilence, and injustice as manifestations of a single evil across different domains is a powerful unifying insight -- it treats moral, physical, seasonal, and political corruption as structurally identical phenomena. The fawning-animal analogy is devastating: those who claim to propitiate the gods are compared to brute beasts that manipulate their masters through servile flattery. Cross-reference: Republic II (365d-366b) describes the wicked as thinking they can sacrifice their way out of divine punishment. Gorgias (521d-522a) compares flattery to a counterfeit art. The identification of injustice as a disease of the soul is developed in Republic IV (444c-d) and Sophist (228a-e). The cosmic battle between good and evil has Zoroastrian overtones and anticipates Plotinus's treatment of evil as privation (Enneads I.8).

D: Original:
Quite true.

What else can he say who declares that the Gods are always lenient to the doers of unjust acts, if they divide the spoil with them? As if wolves were to toss a portion of their prey to the dogs, and they, mollified by the gift, suffered them to tear the flocks. Must not he who maintains that the Gods can be propitiated argue thus?

Precisely so.

And to which of the above-mentioned classes of guardians would any man compare the Gods without absurdity? Will he say that they are like pilots, who are themselves turned away from their duty by "libations of wine and the savour of fat," and at last overturn both ship and sailors?

Assuredly not.

And surely they are not like charioteers who are bribed to give up the victory to other chariots?

That would be a fearful image of the Gods.

Nor are they like generals, or physicians, or husbandmen, or shepherds; and no one would compare them to dogs who have silenced by wolves.

A thing not to be spoken of.

And are not all the Gods the chiefest of all guardians, and do they not guard our highest interests?

Yes; the chiefest.

And shall we say that those who guard our noblest interests, and are the best of guardians, are inferior in virtue to dogs, and to men even of moderate excellence, who would never betray justice for the sake of gifts which unjust men impiously offer them?

Certainly not: nor is such a notion to be endured, and he who holds this opinion may be fairly singled out and characterized as of all impious men the wickedest and most impious.

Then are the three assertions-that the Gods exist, and that they take care of men, and that they can never be persuaded to do injustice, now sufficiently demonstrated? May we say that they are?

You have our entire assent to your words.
D: Narrative:
"Quite true," said Cleinias.

"What else can he say who declares that the Gods are always lenient to the doers of unjust acts, if they divide the spoil with them? As if wolves were to toss a portion of their prey to the dogs, and they, mollified by the gift, suffered them to tear the flocks. Must not he who maintains that the Gods can be propitiated argue thus?" asked the Athenian.

"Precisely so," agreed Cleinias.

"And to which of the above-mentioned classes of guardians would any man compare the Gods without absurdity? Will he say that they are like pilots, who are themselves turned away from their duty by 'libations of wine and the savour of fat,' and at last overturn both ship and sailors?" asked the Athenian.

"Assuredly not," said Cleinias.

"And surely they are not like charioteers who are bribed to give up the victory to other chariots?" asked the Athenian.

"That would be a fearful image of the Gods," said Cleinias.

"Nor are they like generals, or physicians, or husbandmen, or shepherds; and no one would compare them to dogs who have been silenced by wolves," said the Athenian.

"A thing not to be spoken of," said Cleinias.

"And are not all the Gods the chiefest of all guardians, and do they not guard our highest interests?" asked the Athenian.

"Yes; the chiefest," agreed Cleinias.

"And shall we say that those who guard our noblest interests, and are the best of guardians, are inferior in virtue to dogs, and to men even of moderate excellence, who would never betray justice for the sake of gifts which unjust men impiously offer them?" asked the Athenian.

"Certainly not: nor is such a notion to be endured, and he who holds this opinion may be fairly singled out and characterized as of all impious men the wickedest and most impious," said Cleinias.

"Then are the three assertions -- that the Gods exist, and that they take care of men, and that they can never be persuaded to do injustice -- now sufficiently demonstrated? May we say that they are?" asked the Athenian.

"You have our entire assent to your words," said Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"Exactly," said Cleinias.

"Consider what it means to claim that gods are always lenient to the unjust when they share the spoils," the Athenian continued. "It is as if wolves tossed a portion of their kill to the guard dogs, who, mollified by the gift, allowed the wolves to tear the flock apart. Is this not what someone must be arguing when they say gods can be propitiated?"

"That is precisely what they are arguing."

"Now, to which of the guardians we named would anyone compare the gods without absurdity? Would anyone say the gods are like pilots who abandon their duty for libations of wine and the aroma of sacrificial fat, and end up capsizing their ship with all its crew?"

"Certainly not."

"Or like charioteers who accept bribes to throw the race?"

"That would be a terrible image of the gods."

"They cannot be compared to generals, physicians, farmers, or shepherds who abandon their posts for gifts. And surely no one would compare them to guard dogs bought off by wolves."

"That must not even be spoken."

"Yet the gods are the supreme guardians, guarding our highest interests. Shall we say that these supreme guardians have less virtue than dogs, or even than moderately decent human beings, who would never betray justice for the bribes wicked people offer?"

"Absolutely not. Such a notion is intolerable. Anyone who holds this view deserves to be singled out as the most wicked and impious of all."

"Then are our three propositions now sufficiently demonstrated: that gods exist, that they care for human beings, and that they can never be persuaded to commit injustice?"

"You have our complete agreement," Cleinias declared.
D: Elegant: Those who say gods can be bribed are saying that wolves can buy off the guard dogs with scraps of their kill. But would anyone claim the gods are like drunken pilots, corrupt charioteers, or dogs silenced by wolves? The gods are the supreme guardians of our highest interests -- to say they have less integrity than ordinary dogs or moderately decent people is the most wicked and impious claim of all. The three-fold demonstration is complete: gods exist, they care for humanity, and they cannot be corrupted.
D: Summary: The Athenian devastates the claim of divine corruptibility through a series of analogies: bribing the gods is like wolves buying off guard dogs, or corrupting pilots, charioteers, and generals. Since the gods are the supreme guardians, attributing to them less virtue than dogs or ordinary people is the worst possible impiety. All three propositions are now established: gods exist, care for humanity, and cannot be corrupted.
D: Distilled: To say gods accept bribes from the wicked is to say the supreme guardians have less integrity than guard dogs -- the most impious claim possible; all three theological proofs are now complete.
Notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. The wolf-and-dog analogy is devastating and memorable -- it reduces the claim of divine corruptibility to absurdity through a vivid image. The systematic rejection of every guardian analogy (pilot, charioteer, general, physician, farmer, shepherd, dog) creates a comprehensive argument by exhaustion. The Homeric quotation "libations of wine and the savour of fat" (adapted from Iliad IX.499-500, where Phoenix describes how the gods may be turned aside by sacrifice) is precisely the traditional view being refuted -- Plato directly engages and rejects Homer's theology. The conclusion that the holder of this view is "the wickedest and most impious" is significant: divine corruptibility is worse even than atheism or divine negligence, because it combines belief in the gods with deliberate moral corruption. Cross-reference: Republic II (364b-365a) quotes the same Homeric tradition of gods turned by sacrifice. Euthyphro (14c-15a) raises similar questions about whether piety is a form of commercial exchange with the gods. The formal conclusion declaring all three propositions established marks the end of Book 10's theological argument proper.

D: Original:
I have spoken with vehemence because I am zealous against evil men; and I will tell dear Cleinias, why I am so. I would not have the wicked think that, having the superiority in argument, they may do as they please and act according to their various imaginations about the Gods; and this zeal has led me to speak too vehemently; but if we have at all succeeded in persuading the men to hate themselves and love their opposites, the prelude of our laws about impiety will not have been spoken in vain.

So let us hope; and even if we have failed, the style of our argument will not discredit the lawgiver.
D: Narrative:
"I have spoken with vehemence because I am zealous against evil men; and I will tell dear Cleinias, why I am so. I would not have the wicked think that, having the superiority in argument, they may do as they please and act according to their various imaginations about the Gods; and this zeal has led me to speak too vehemently; but if we have at all succeeded in persuading the men to hate themselves and love their opposites, the prelude of our laws about impiety will not have been spoken in vain," said the Athenian.

"So let us hope; and even if we have failed, the style of our argument will not discredit the lawgiver," replied Cleinias.
D: Refashioned:
"I have spoken with intensity," the Athenian acknowledged, "because I am passionate in my opposition to evil. Let me explain why, dear Cleinias. I would not have the wicked think that because they possess a certain cleverness in argument, they may do as they please and hold whatever views about the gods suit their purposes. This passion has perhaps led me to speak more forcefully than necessary. But if we have succeeded even partially in persuading such people to despise their own wickedness and love its opposite, then the prelude to our laws against impiety will not have been spoken in vain."

"Let us hope so," Cleinias replied. "And even if we have not fully succeeded, the quality of our argument will reflect well on the lawgiver."
D: Elegant: The Athenian explains his vehemence: he refuses to let the wicked believe that intellectual cleverness gives them license to act as they please regarding the gods. If the theological prelude has persuaded even some to despise their own wickedness and love virtue, it will not have been in vain. And even if it fails, the argument itself does credit to the lawgiver.
D: Summary: The Athenian apologizes for his intensity but explains his motive: preventing the wicked from thinking that argumentative skill gives them moral license. The goal of the entire theological prelude was to make the impious hate their own vice and love its opposite. Cleinias adds that even if the persuasion fails, the quality of the argument honours the legislator.
D: Distilled: The theological prelude succeeds if it persuades the wicked to hate their own vice and love virtue; even if it fails in this, the quality of the argument itself honours the lawgiver.
Notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This reflective moment marks the transition from the theological prelude to the actual laws against impiety. The Athenian's self-awareness about his vehemence is characteristic of the Laws' more human, less idealized tone compared to the Republic. The goal of the prelude -- making the impious "hate themselves and love their opposites" -- is not merely intellectual conversion but moral transformation, recalling the Socratic project of turning the soul from ignorance to knowledge (Republic VII, 518c-d). Cleinias's remark that the argument "will not discredit the lawgiver" even if it fails introduces an important pragmatic note: in legislation, unlike pure philosophy, even imperfect persuasion has value. Cross-reference: Laws IV (718c-723d) on the purpose of legislative preludes as persuasion. The Phaedrus (270b-272b) on the relationship between philosophical truth and effective rhetoric. The apology for excessive zeal recalls Socrates' defense of his mission in the Apology (30e-31a).

D: Original: After the prelude shall follow a discourse, which will be the interpreter of the law; this shall proclaim to all impious persons:-that they must depart from their ways and go over to the pious. And to those who disobey, let the law about impiety be as follows:-If a man is guilty of any impiety in word or deed, any one who happens to present shall give information to the magistrates, in aid of the law; and let the magistrates who. first receive the information bring him before the appointed court according to the law; and if a magistrate, after receiving information, refuses to act, he shall be tried for impiety at the instance of any one who is willing to vindicate the laws; and if any one be cast, the court shall estimate the punishment of each act of impiety; and let all such criminals be imprisoned. There shall be three prisons in the state: the first of them is to be the common prison in the neighbourhood of the agora for the safe-keeping of the generality of offenders; another is to be in the neighbourhood of the nocturnal council, and is to be called the "House of Reformation"; another, to be situated in some wild and desolate region in the centre of the country, shall be called by some name expressive of retribution. Now, men fall into impiety from three causes, which have been already mentioned, and from each of these causes arise two sorts of impiety, in all six, which are worth distinguishing, and should not all have the same punishment. For he who does not believe in Gods, and yet has a righteous nature, hates the wicked and dislikes and refuses to do injustice, and avoids unrighteous men, and loves the righteous. But they who besides believing that the world is devoid of Gods are intemperate, and have at the same time good memories and quick wits, are worse; although both of them are unbelievers, much less injury is done by the one than by the other. The one may talk loosely about the Gods and about sacrifices and oaths, and perhaps by laughing at other men he may make them like himself, if he be not punished.
D: Narrative: "After the prelude shall follow a discourse, which will be the interpreter of the law; this shall proclaim to all impious persons -- that they must depart from their ways and go over to the pious. And to those who disobey, let the law about impiety be as follows: If a man is guilty of any impiety in word or deed, any one who happens to be present shall give information to the magistrates, in aid of the law; and let the magistrates who first receive the information bring him before the appointed court according to the law; and if a magistrate, after receiving information, refuses to act, he shall be tried for impiety at the instance of any one who is willing to vindicate the laws; and if any one be cast, the court shall estimate the punishment of each act of impiety; and let all such criminals be imprisoned. There shall be three prisons in the state: the first of them is to be the common prison in the neighbourhood of the agora for the safe-keeping of the generality of offenders; another is to be in the neighbourhood of the nocturnal council, and is to be called the 'House of Reformation'; another, to be situated in some wild and desolate region in the centre of the country, shall be called by some name expressive of retribution. Now, men fall into impiety from three causes, which have been already mentioned, and from each of these causes arise two sorts of impiety, in all six, which are worth distinguishing, and should not all have the same punishment. For he who does not believe in Gods, and yet has a righteous nature, hates the wicked and dislikes and refuses to do injustice, and avoids unrighteous men, and loves the righteous. But they who besides believing that the world is devoid of Gods are intemperate, and have at the same time good memories and quick wits, are worse; although both of them are unbelievers, much less injury is done by the one than by the other. The one may talk loosely about the Gods and about sacrifices and oaths, and perhaps by laughing at other men he may make them like himself, if he be not punished," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"Following the prelude comes a proclamation that interprets the law, declaring to all impious persons that they must abandon their ways and turn to piety. For those who refuse, the law against impiety shall be as follows:

Anyone who witnesses impiety in word or deed shall report it to the magistrates. The magistrates who first receive the report shall bring the offender before the appointed court. Any magistrate who refuses to act on such a report shall himself be tried for impiety by anyone willing to uphold the law. The court shall determine the appropriate punishment for each act of impiety, and all convicted persons shall be imprisoned.

There shall be three prisons in the state. The first is the common prison near the marketplace, for ordinary offenders. The second, near the nocturnal council, shall be called the 'House of Reformation.' The third, located in a wild and desolate region in the centre of the country, shall bear a name expressive of retribution.

People fall into impiety from three causes -- as we have established -- and each cause produces two varieties, making six types in all, which deserve distinct punishments. Consider: the person who does not believe in gods yet possesses a righteous nature -- who hates wickedness, refuses to commit injustice, avoids unrighteous people, and loves the righteous -- is quite different from the atheist who is also intemperate, though clever and sharp-witted. Both are unbelievers, but the first does far less harm than the second. The righteous atheist may talk carelessly about gods, sacrifices, and oaths, and perhaps by mocking others may spread his disbelief -- if he goes unpunished."
D: Elegant: After the theological prelude come the actual laws. All impiety must be reported; magistrates who refuse to act are themselves liable. Three prisons serve different purposes: a common prison, a House of Reformation, and a place of retribution. The three causes of impiety produce six varieties. Crucially, a righteous atheist who hates injustice is far less dangerous than a clever, intemperate atheist -- though both disbelieve, the harm they do differs enormously.
D: Summary: The Athenian transitions from philosophical argument to actual legislation. He establishes reporting requirements, magistrate accountability, and three specialized prisons. He then classifies impiety into six types based on the three causes (atheism, divine negligence, divine corruptibility), each subdivided by moral character. The righteous atheist is distinguished from the dangerous, clever atheist -- both disbelieve, but their harm to society differs greatly.
D: Distilled: The law establishes three prisons and six categories of impiety, crucially distinguishing between the righteous atheist who does little harm and the clever, intemperate atheist who corrupts others.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage marks the formal transition from persuasion (the prelude) to coercion (the law). The three-prison system is remarkably differentiated: (1) the common prison for ordinary offenders, (2) the House of Reformation (sophronisterion) for those who can be rehabilitated through philosophical conversation with the nocturnal council, and (3) a remote prison of retribution for the incorrigible. This graduated system anticipates modern penological distinctions between detention, rehabilitation, and maximum-security confinement. The six-fold classification (3 causes x 2 moral types) is a sophisticated taxonomy: within each form of impiety, the morally decent but intellectually confused person is treated differently from the morally corrupt and intellectually capable one. The righteous atheist -- who hates injustice despite disbelief -- is a striking figure, suggesting Plato recognizes that moral virtue can exist independently of theological belief. Cross-reference: Laws XII (951d-952d) describes the nocturnal council in detail. Republic VII (519d-520a) on philosophical education as soul-healing. The graduated punishment system contrasts with the undifferentiated penalties in most ancient Greek law.

D: Original: But the other who holds the same opinions and is called a clever man, is full of stratagem and deceit-men of this class deal in prophecy and jugglery of all kinds, and out of their ranks sometimes come tyrants and demagogues and generals and hierophants of private mysteries and the Sophists, as they are termed, with their ingenious devices. There are many kinds of unbelievers, but two only for whom legislation is required; one the hypocritical sort, whose crime is deserving of death many times over, while the other needs only bonds and admonition. In like manner also the notion that the Gods take no thought of men produces two other sorts of crimes, and the notion that they may be propitiated produces two more. Assuming these divisions, let those who have been made what they are only from want of understanding, and not from malice or an evil nature, be placed by the judge in the House of Reformation, and ordered to suffer imprisonment during a period of not less than five years. And in the meantime let them have no intercourse with the other citizens, except with members of the nocturnal council, and with them let them converse with a view to the improvement of their soul's health.
D: Narrative: "But the other who holds the same opinions and is called a clever man, is full of stratagem and deceit -- men of this class deal in prophecy and jugglery of all kinds, and out of their ranks sometimes come tyrants and demagogues and generals and hierophants of private mysteries and the Sophists, as they are termed, with their ingenious devices. There are many kinds of unbelievers, but two only for whom legislation is required; one the hypocritical sort, whose crime is deserving of death many times over, while the other needs only bonds and admonition. In like manner also the notion that the Gods take no thought of men produces two other sorts of crimes, and the notion that they may be propitiated produces two more. Assuming these divisions, let those who have been made what they are only from want of understanding, and not from malice or an evil nature, be placed by the judge in the House of Reformation, and ordered to suffer imprisonment during a period of not less than five years. And in the meantime let them have no intercourse with the other citizens, except with members of the nocturnal council, and with them let them converse with a view to the improvement of their soul's health," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"But the other type of atheist -- the one called 'clever' -- is full of cunning and deceit," the Athenian continued. "From this class come practitioners of fraudulent prophecy and every kind of charlatanry. From their ranks sometimes emerge tyrants, demagogues, generals, leaders of secret religious cults, and those called Sophists with their ingenious schemes.

There are many varieties of unbelief, but only two require legislation: the hypocritical kind, whose crime deserves death many times over, and the sincere kind, which requires only confinement and admonition. Similarly, the belief that gods are indifferent to humanity produces two criminal types, and the belief that gods can be bribed produces two more.

Given these divisions, those whose impiety results from mere lack of understanding -- not from malice or evil character -- shall be placed by the judge in the House of Reformation and imprisoned for no less than five years. During this time, they shall have no contact with other citizens except members of the nocturnal council, with whom they shall converse for the improvement of their soul's health."
D: Elegant: The clever atheist is dangerous: from this type come charlatans, false prophets, tyrants, demagogues, and Sophists. Two types require legislation -- the hypocrite who deserves death and the sincere but confused who need confinement and instruction. Those whose impiety stems from ignorance rather than malice shall be confined in the House of Reformation for at least five years, conversing only with members of the nocturnal council to heal their souls.
D: Summary: The Athenian describes the dangerous atheist: clever, deceitful, and the breeding ground for tyrants, demagogues, and Sophists. He distinguishes the hypocritical atheist (deserving death) from the merely confused (needing rehabilitation). Those whose impiety comes from ignorance, not malice, are sentenced to at least five years in the House of Reformation, where they converse exclusively with the nocturnal council for philosophical therapy.
D: Distilled: The clever, hypocritical atheist who breeds tyranny and demagoguery deserves death; the sincerely confused atheist deserves five years of philosophical rehabilitation in the House of Reformation, conversing with the nocturnal council.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The portrait of the clever atheist is a composite of Plato's most dangerous adversaries: the Sophists (Protagoras, Gorgias, Thrasymachus), the tyrant (Dionysius of Syracuse?), and the religious charlatan. The association of intellectual atheism with political tyranny reprises the Republic's argument that philosophical corruption leads to tyrannical souls (Republic IX, 571a-576b). The House of Reformation (sophronisterion) is one of the most remarkable institutions proposed in the Laws: it is essentially a philosophical prison where inmates undergo forced dialogue with the city's most senior philosophers (the nocturnal council). This treats impiety as a disease of the soul requiring therapy, not merely a crime requiring punishment -- consistent with the Socratic principle that wrongdoing is involuntary ignorance. The five-year minimum sentence is severe but coupled with genuinely rehabilitative intent. Cross-reference: Gorgias (464b-465c) treats sophistry as a counterfeit of true political art. Republic VI (492a-493d) on the corrupting influence of clever speakers on the young. The nocturnal council is described in Laws XII (961a-969d) as the highest philosophical and legislative body of the state.

D: Original:
And when the time of their imprisonment has expired, if any of them be of sound mind let him be restored to sane company, but if not, and if he be condemned a second time, let him be punished with death. As to that class of monstrous natures who not only believe that there are no Gods, or that they are negligent, or to be propitiated, but in contempt of mankind conjure the souls of the living and say that they can conjure the dead and promise to charm the Gods with sacrifices and prayers, and will utterly overthrow individuals and whole houses and states for the sake of money-let him who is guilty of any of these things be condemned by the court to be bound according to law in the prison which is in the centre of the land, and let no freeman ever approach him, but let him receive the rations of food appointed by the guardians of the law from the hands of the public slaves; and when he is dead let him be cast beyond the borders unburied, and if any freeman assist in burying him, let him pay the penalty of impiety to any one who is willing to bring a suit against him.

But if he leaves behind him children who are fit to be citizens, let the guardians of orphans take care of them, just as they would of any other orphans, from the day on which their father is convicted.
D: Narrative:
"And when the time of their imprisonment has expired, if any of them be of sound mind let him be restored to sane company, but if not, and if he be condemned a second time, let him be punished with death. As to that class of monstrous natures who not only believe that there are no Gods, or that they are negligent, or to be propitiated, but in contempt of mankind conjure the souls of the living and say that they can conjure the dead and promise to charm the Gods with sacrifices and prayers, and will utterly overthrow individuals and whole houses and states for the sake of money -- let him who is guilty of any of these things be condemned by the court to be bound according to law in the prison which is in the centre of the land, and let no freeman ever approach him, but let him receive the rations of food appointed by the guardians of the law from the hands of the public slaves; and when he is dead let him be cast beyond the borders unburied, and if any freeman assist in burying him, let him pay the penalty of impiety to any one who is willing to bring a suit against him.

But if he leaves behind him children who are fit to be citizens, let the guardians of orphans take care of them, just as they would of any other orphans, from the day on which their father is convicted," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"When their term of imprisonment expires," the Athenian continued, "if any of them have recovered their sound judgment, let them be restored to society. But if they are convicted a second time, let them be punished with death.

As for those monstrous natures who go beyond mere unbelief -- who not only deny the gods' existence, or their concern for humanity, or their incorruptibility, but who in contempt of their fellow human beings claim to conjure the souls of the living, raise the dead, and charm the gods themselves through sacrifices and prayers, and who would destroy individuals, entire families, and whole states for the sake of money -- such persons shall be condemned by the court and imprisoned in the remote prison at the centre of the country. No free citizen shall ever visit them. They shall receive their food rations from public slaves, as the guardians of the law prescribe.

When they die, their bodies shall be cast beyond the state's borders, unburied. Any free citizen who assists in burying such a person shall be liable to prosecution for impiety by anyone willing to bring the charge.

However, if the condemned person leaves behind children who are fit for citizenship, the guardians of orphans shall care for them as they would any other orphans, from the day of their parent's conviction."
D: Elegant: Those who recover after rehabilitation are released; those convicted twice receive death. But the worst class -- those who exploit impiety for profit by claiming to conjure souls and charm gods, destroying individuals and states for money -- are sentenced to permanent solitary confinement in the remote prison, fed by slaves, with no free person permitted to visit. At death, they are cast out unburied. Their innocent children, however, are protected as orphans of the state.
D: Summary: The Athenian completes the graduated punishment system. Reformed inmates are released; repeat offenders face death. The worst category -- fraudulent practitioners who exploit religious fears for profit and power, claiming to conjure souls and bribe gods -- receive permanent solitary confinement, death without burial, and total exclusion from free society. Yet their innocent children receive state protection as orphans.
D: Distilled: Rehabilitated offenders are released, repeat offenders face death, and the worst -- those who exploit impiety to destroy others for profit -- receive permanent solitary confinement and denial of burial, though their innocent children are protected.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The graduated severity is striking: first offense receives philosophical rehabilitation, second offense death, and the exploitative charlatan receives what amounts to life imprisonment in solitary confinement followed by the supreme post-mortem dishonour of denial of burial. In Greek culture, denial of burial was among the most severe punishments imaginable (cf. Sophocles' Antigone). The distinction between the merely confused and the deliberately exploitative is morally sophisticated: the law reserves its harshest penalties not for intellectual error but for the cynical manipulation of others' religious fears for profit. The protection of convicted persons' children is a humane touch characteristic of the Laws' attention to practical justice -- the sins of the parent do not extend to the child. Cross-reference: Gorgias (524e-525a) describes the worst souls in Hades as those who used power to commit injustice. Republic II (364b-365a) describes itinerant priests who promise to purify crimes through rituals -- precisely the charlatans targeted here. The nocturnal council's rehabilitative function connects to Protagoras 324a-326e on the correctional purpose of punishment.

D: Original: In all these cases there should be one law, which will make men in general less liable to transgress in word or deed, and less foolish, because they will not be allowed to practise religious rites contrary to law. And let this be the simple form of the law:-No man shall have sacred rites in a private house. When he would sacrifice, let him go to the temples and hand over his offerings to the priests and priestesses, who see to the sanctity of such things, and let him pray himself, and let any one who pleases join with him in prayer. The reason of this is as follows:-Gods and temples are not easily instituted, and to establish them rightly is the work of a mighty intellect. And women especially, and men too, when they are sick or in danger, or in any sort of difficulty, or again on their receiving any good fortune, have a way of consecrating the occasion, vowing sacrifices, and promising shrines to Gods, demigods, and sons of Gods; and when they are awakened by terrible apparitions and dreams or remember visions, they find in altars and temples the remedies of them, and will fill every house and village with them, placing them in the open air, or wherever they may have had such visions; and with a view to all these cases we should obey the law.
D: Narrative: "In all these cases there should be one law, which will make men in general less liable to transgress in word or deed, and less foolish, because they will not be allowed to practise religious rites contrary to law. And let this be the simple form of the law: No man shall have sacred rites in a private house. When he would sacrifice, let him go to the temples and hand over his offerings to the priests and priestesses, who see to the sanctity of such things, and let him pray himself, and let any one who pleases join with him in prayer. The reason of this is as follows: Gods and temples are not easily instituted, and to establish them rightly is the work of a mighty intellect. And women especially, and men too, when they are sick or in danger, or in any sort of difficulty, or again on their receiving any good fortune, have a way of consecrating the occasion, vowing sacrifices, and promising shrines to Gods, demigods, and sons of Gods; and when they are awakened by terrible apparitions and dreams or remember visions, they find in altars and temples the remedies of them, and will fill every house and village with them, placing them in the open air, or wherever they may have had such visions; and with a view to all these cases we should obey the law," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"In all these cases," the Athenian said, "there should be a single overarching law that makes people generally less inclined to transgress in word or deed, and less foolish, by prohibiting unauthorized religious practices. The law in its simplest form is this: No person shall conduct sacred rites in a private house. When anyone wishes to sacrifice, they shall go to the temples and hand over their offerings to the priests and priestesses responsible for sanctifying such things. They may pray there themselves, and anyone who wishes may join them.

The reason is as follows: establishing gods and temples properly is no easy matter -- it is the work of a great intellect. But people -- women especially, though men as well -- when they are sick, in danger, in any kind of difficulty, or conversely when they receive good fortune, have a habit of consecrating the moment by vowing sacrifices and promising shrines to gods, demigods, and divine offspring. When they are disturbed by frightening visions, dreams, or remembered apparitions, they seek remedy in altars and temples, and will fill every house and village with them -- placing shrines in the open air or wherever they had their visions.

For all these reasons, the law against private worship must be obeyed."
D: Elegant: A single law governs all cases: no sacred rites in private houses. All sacrifices must go through public temples and authorized priests. The reason: establishing proper worship requires great wisdom, but people in crisis -- illness, danger, fear, or good fortune -- impulsively consecrate private shrines, filling homes and villages with unauthorized altars based on dreams and visions. This unregulated worship must be brought under public control.
D: Summary: The Athenian proposes a fundamental law: no private religious rites. All worship must occur in public temples through authorized priests. The reason is that people, especially when frightened, sick, or grateful, impulsively create private shrines based on dreams and visions, proliferating unauthorized worship. Proper religious establishment requires the wisdom of a great intellect, not individual impulse.
D: Distilled: No sacred rites shall be conducted in private houses -- all worship must go through public temples, because unregulated private religion, driven by fear, crisis, and dreams, proliferates unauthorized cults and undermines proper piety.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The prohibition of private religious rites is one of the most controversial and far-reaching laws in the entire work. It centralizes religious authority in public institutions, eliminating the household cults that were a fundamental feature of Greek religious life. The psychological observation about crisis-driven religious impulse is acute: people create private shrines under emotional pressure (illness, danger, nightmares), not rational reflection. The mention of women as especially prone to this is characteristic of ancient Greek attitudes but also reflects genuine sociological observation about women's religious roles in domestic cult. The principle that "to establish gods and temples rightly is the work of a mighty intellect" is a remarkable claim: it subordinates religious establishment to philosophical competence, making theology a specialized expertise rather than a popular prerogative. Cross-reference: Laws V (738b-d) on the proper establishment of temples and festivals. Republic II (378e-383c) on the regulation of theological claims. The prohibition of private cult anticipates aspects of Roman imperial religious regulation and later state-church models.

D: Original: The law has also regard to the impious, and would not have them fancy that by the secret performance of these actions-by raising temples and by building altars in private houses, they can propitiate the God secretly with sacrifices and prayers, while they are really multiplying their crimes infinitely, bringing guilt from heaven upon themselves, and also upon those who permit them, and who are better men than they are; and the consequence is that the whole state reaps the fruit of their impiety, which, in a certain sense, is deserved. Assuredly God will not blame the legislator, who will enact the following law:-No one shall possess shrines of the Gods in private houses, and he who is found to possess them, and perform any sacred rites not publicly authorized-supposing the offender to be some man or woman who is not guilty of any other great and impious crime-shall be informed against by him who is acquainted with the fact, which shall be announced by him to the guardians of the law; and let them issue orders that he or she shall carry away their private rites to the public temples, and if they do not persuade them, let them inflict a penalty on them until they comply. And if a person be proven guilty of impiety, not merely from childish levity, but such as grown-up men may be guilty of, whether he have sacrificed publicly or privately to any Gods, let him be punished with death, for his sacrifice is impure. Whether the deed has been done in earnest, or only from childish levity, let the guardians of the law determine, before they bring the matter into court and prosecute the offender for impiety.
D: Narrative: "The law has also regard to the impious, and would not have them fancy that by the secret performance of these actions -- by raising temples and by building altars in private houses -- they can propitiate the God secretly with sacrifices and prayers, while they are really multiplying their crimes infinitely, bringing guilt from heaven upon themselves, and also upon those who permit them, and who are better men than they are; and the consequence is that the whole state reaps the fruit of their impiety, which, in a certain sense, is deserved. Assuredly God will not blame the legislator, who will enact the following law: No one shall possess shrines of the Gods in private houses, and he who is found to possess them, and perform any sacred rites not publicly authorized -- supposing the offender to be some man or woman who is not guilty of any other great and impious crime -- shall be informed against by him who is acquainted with the fact, which shall be announced by him to the guardians of the law; and let them issue orders that he or she shall carry away their private rites to the public temples, and if they do not persuade them, let them inflict a penalty on them until they comply. And if a person be proven guilty of impiety, not merely from childish levity, but such as grown-up men may be guilty of, whether he have sacrificed publicly or privately to any Gods, let him be punished with death, for his sacrifice is impure. Whether the deed has been done in earnest, or only from childish levity, let the guardians of the law determine, before they bring the matter into court and prosecute the offender for impiety," said the Athenian.
D: Refashioned:
"The law also addresses the impious directly," the Athenian concluded. "It will not allow them to imagine that through secret worship -- raising temples and building altars in private houses -- they can quietly propitiate the gods with sacrifices and prayers. In reality, they are multiplying their crimes infinitely, bringing divine guilt upon themselves and upon those who permit such practices, even if those persons are better people. The consequence is that the entire state suffers the fruits of their impiety -- and in a sense, deservedly so.

God will certainly not blame the legislator who enacts the following law: No one shall possess shrines of the gods in a private house. Anyone found possessing such shrines and performing unauthorized sacred rites -- assuming they are not guilty of any other great impiety -- shall be reported by anyone aware of the violation to the guardians of the law. The guardians shall order the offender to transfer their private rites to the public temples. If persuasion fails, penalties shall be imposed until compliance is achieved.

But if a person is proven guilty of genuine impiety -- not mere childish thoughtlessness but the serious kind adults commit -- whether the worship was conducted publicly or privately, that person shall be punished with death, for the sacrifice itself is impure. The guardians of the law shall determine whether the offense was committed in earnest or from childish levity before bringing the case to court and prosecuting the offender for impiety."
D: Elegant: Secret private worship does not propitiate the gods -- it multiplies crime and brings guilt upon the worshiper and all who permit it, tainting the whole state. The law: no private shrines. Offenders must transfer their rites to public temples or face penalties. But those guilty of serious, adult impiety -- whether in public or private worship -- face death, for their sacrifice is impure. The guardians of the law must distinguish childish carelessness from genuine criminal impiety before prosecution.
D: Summary: The Athenian concludes Book 10's legislation by prohibiting private shrines and unauthorized worship. Secret worship multiplies guilt rather than propitiating the gods, harming both the worshiper and the state. Minor offenders must transfer rites to public temples or face penalties. Serious adult impiety, whether in public or private, is punishable by death. The guardians of the law must distinguish between childish thoughtlessness and genuine criminal impiety.
D: Distilled: Private worship multiplies guilt rather than propitiating gods; unauthorized shrines must be transferred to public temples, and serious adult impiety -- whether public or private -- is punishable by death.
Notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This final passage of Book 10 completes the legal framework for regulating impiety. The key principle -- that secret worship harms the entire state, not just the individual -- reflects the communal understanding of pollution (miasma) in Greek religion: private sin has public consequences. The distinction between childish and adult impiety introduces judicial discretion, an important feature of the Laws' legal system. The death penalty for serious impiety seems harsh by modern standards but was consistent with Athenian practice (recall Socrates' trial and execution). The phrase "his sacrifice is impure" captures the essence of the prohibition: unauthorized worship is not merely illegal but ritually contaminating. The responsibility placed on "those who permit" unauthorized worship extends liability beyond the offender to enablers -- a principle of complicity. Cross-reference: the pollution of the state through individual impiety recalls Oedipus Tyrannus and the concept of miasma. Laws IX (865a-874d) develops the law of homicide with similar attention to degrees of culpability. The role of the guardians of the law (nomophylakes) as both investigators and prosecutors is defined more fully in Laws VI (752e-755b). This final passage brings the theological prelude full circle: the philosophical arguments of 10.1-45 provide the rational foundation for the positive legislation of 10.46-50.

D: Original: In the next place, dealings between man and man require to be suitably regulated. The principle of them is very simple:-Thou shalt not, if thou canst help, touch that which is mine, or remove the least thing which belongs to me without my consent; and may I be of a sound mind, and do to others as I would that they should do to me. First, let us speak of treasure trove:-May I never pray the Gods to find the hidden treasure, which another has laid up for himself and his family, he not being one of my ancestors, nor lift, if I should find, such a treasure. And may I never have any dealings with those who are called diviners, and who in any way or manner counsel me to take up the deposit entrusted to the earth, for I should not gain so much in the increase of my possessions, if I take up the prize, as I should grow in justice and virtue of soul, if I abstain; and this will be a better possession to me than the other in a better part of myself; for the possession of justice in the soul is preferable to the possession of wealth. And of many things it is well said-"Move not the immovables," and this may be regarded as one of them. And we shall do well to believe the common tradition which says that such deeds prevent a man from having a family.
D: Narrative:
In the next place, dealings between man and man require to be suitably regulated. The principle of them is very simple: Thou shalt not, if thou canst help, touch that which is mine, or remove the least thing which belongs to me without my consent; and may I be of a sound mind, and do to others as I would that they should do to me.

First, let us speak of treasure trove: May I never pray the Gods to find the hidden treasure, which another has laid up for himself and his family, he not being one of my ancestors, nor lift, if I should find, such a treasure. And may I never have any dealings with those who are called diviners, and who in any way or manner counsel me to take up the deposit entrusted to the earth, for I should not gain so much in the increase of my possessions, if I take up the prize, as I should grow in justice and virtue of soul, if I abstain; and this will be a better possession to me than the other in a better part of myself; for the possession of justice in the soul is preferable to the possession of wealth.

And of many things it is well said, "Move not the immovables," and this may be regarded as one of them. And we shall do well to believe the common tradition which says that such deeds prevent a man from having a family.
D: Refashioned:
Next, we need proper regulations for dealings between people. The underlying principle is straightforward: you must not, if you can help it, touch what belongs to me, nor take even the smallest thing of mine without my permission; and I pray I remain of sound mind, treating others as I would wish to be treated.

First, let us address the matter of found treasure. May I never pray to the gods that I discover hidden wealth laid up by someone else for himself and his family -- someone who is not my ancestor -- nor pick up such treasure if I happen to find it. And may I never consult those so-called diviners who advise me to dig up deposits entrusted to the earth. For the gain in material wealth from seizing such a prize would be far less than the growth in justice and virtue I would achieve by leaving it alone. Justice in the soul is a better possession, held in a better part of myself, than any material wealth could be.

As the old saying goes, "Do not disturb what is settled" -- and this situation is a prime example. We would also do well to credit the common tradition that taking what is not yours prevents a man from having descendants.
D: Elegant: Dealings between people rest on a simple principle: do not touch what belongs to another without consent, and treat others as you would wish to be treated. Found treasure should be left alone -- the growth in justice from abstaining far exceeds any material gain, for possessing justice in the soul outweighs possessing wealth. The old saying applies: do not disturb what is settled. Tradition warns that taking what is not yours prevents a man from having descendants.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger introduces the laws governing private dealings with a golden-rule principle: do not take what belongs to another. Found treasure must be left untouched, because the spiritual gain from justice far outweighs any material prize, and tradition warns that such theft prevents one from having descendants.
D: Distilled: The foundation of all commercial law is simple: do not take what is not yours, for justice in the soul is worth more than any material wealth.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, introducing Book 11's treatment of commercial and property law. -- The golden-rule formulation ('do to others as I would that they should do to me') is one of the earliest expressions of this principle in Western philosophy, predating its more famous biblical articulations. -- 'Move not the immovables' was a proverbial saying attributed to Pythagoras and widely circulated in Greek wisdom literature. -- The idea that justice in the soul is preferable to external wealth echoes Republic IV (443c-444a), where Socrates argues that justice is the proper ordering of the soul's parts. -- The belief that theft prevents one from having descendants reflects Greek religious tradition about divine punishment extending across generations, found also in Solon's poetry and Herodotus. -- The 'Goddess of ways' mentioned in the next passage is Hecate, protectress of crossroads and wayside property.

D: Original: Now as to him who is careless about having children and regardless of the legislator, taking up that which neither he deposited, nor any ancestor of his, without the consent of the depositor, violating the simplest and noblest of laws which was the enactment of no mean man:-"Take not up that which was not laid down by thee"-of him, I say, who despises these two legislators, and takes up, not small matter which he has not deposited, but perhaps a great heap of treasure, what he ought to suffer at the hands of the Gods, God only knows; but I would have the first person who sees him go and tell the wardens of the city, if the occurrence has taken place in the city, or if the occurrence has taken place in the agora he shall tell the wardens of the agora, or if in the country he shall tell the wardens of the country and their commanders. When information has been received the city shall send to Delphi, and, whatever the God answers about the money and the remover of the money, that the city shall do in obedience to the oracle; the informer, if he be a freeman, shall have the honour of doing rightly, and he who informs not, the dishonour of doing wrongly; and if he be a slave who gives information, let him be freed, as he ought to be, by the state, which shall give his master the price of him; but if he do not inform he shall be punished with death.
