D: Distilled: Only non-citizens may engage in retail trade, with fair profit margins set and enforced by the guardians of the law in consultation with experienced practitioners.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger lays out three interrelated commercial laws. -- The escalating imprisonment (1, 2, 4, 8 years, etc.) for citizens engaging in trade is exceptionally harsh, reflecting the seriousness with which Plato treats the corruption of citizen virtue. -- The restriction of trade to metics and foreigners mirrors Spartan practice and contrasts with Athens, where citizen participation in commerce was unrestricted though socially stigmatized. -- The provision for regulated profit margins is remarkably modern, resembling medieval just-price theory later developed by Thomas Aquinas (Summa Theologiae II-II, Q.77). -- The requirement to consult experienced traders before setting regulations shows Plato's pragmatic recognition that lawmakers need specialized knowledge, consistent with the expert-consultation principle in passage 11.10. -- The concept of 'moderate gain' (metrios kerdos) connects to the broader theme of moderation (sophrosyne) that pervades the Laws. -- Compare Aristotle, Politics I.9-10, where he distinguishes natural household management (oikonomike) from unnatural unlimited acquisition (chrematistike).

D: Original: When a man makes an agreement which he does not fulfil, unless the agreement be of a nature which the law or a vote of the assembly does not allow, or which he has made under the influence of some unjust compulsion, or which he is prevented from fulfilling against his will by some unexpected chance, the other party may go to law with him in the courts of the tribes, for not having completed his agreement, if the parties are not able previously to come to terms before arbiters or before their neighbours. The class of craftsmen who have furnished human life with the arts is dedicated to Hephaestus and Athene; and there is a class of craftsmen who preserve the works of all craftsmen by arts of defence, the votaries of Ares and Athene, to which divinities they too are rightly dedicated. All these continue through life serving the country and the people; some of them are leaders in battle; others make for hire implements and works, and they ought not to deceive in such matters, out of respect to the Gods who are their ancestors. If any craftsman through indolence omit to execute his work in a given time, not reverencing the God who gives him the means of life, but considering, foolish fellow, that he is his own God and will let him off easily, in the first place, he shall suffer at the hands of the God, and in the second place, the law shall follow in a similar spirit. He shall owe to him who contracted with him the price of the works which he has failed in performing, and he shall begin again and execute them gratis in the given time. When a man undertakes a work, the law gives him the same advice which was given to the seller, that he should not attempt to raise the price, but simply ask the value; this the law enjoins also on the contractor; for the craftsman assuredly knows the value of his work.
D: Narrative:
"When a man makes an agreement which he does not fulfil, unless the agreement be of a nature which the law or a vote of the assembly does not allow, or which he has made under the influence of some unjust compulsion, or which he is prevented from fulfilling against his will by some unexpected chance, the other party may go to law with him in the courts of the tribes, for not having completed his agreement, if the parties are not able previously to come to terms before arbiters or before their neighbours.

"The class of craftsmen who have furnished human life with the arts is dedicated to Hephaestus and Athene; and there is a class of craftsmen who preserve the works of all craftsmen by arts of defence, the votaries of Ares and Athene, to which divinities they too are rightly dedicated. All these continue through life serving the country and the people; some of them are leaders in battle; others make for hire implements and works, and they ought not to deceive in such matters, out of respect to the Gods who are their ancestors.

"If any craftsman through indolence omit to execute his work in a given time, not reverencing the God who gives him the means of life, but considering, foolish fellow, that he is his own God and will let him off easily, in the first place, he shall suffer at the hands of the God, and in the second place, the law shall follow in a similar spirit. He shall owe to him who contracted with him the price of the works which he has failed in performing, and he shall begin again and execute them gratis in the given time.

"When a man undertakes a work, the law gives him the same advice which was given to the seller, that he should not attempt to raise the price, but simply ask the value; this the law enjoins also on the contractor; for the craftsman assuredly knows the value of his work."
D: Refashioned:
"When a person makes an agreement and fails to fulfill it, the other party may sue in the tribal courts -- unless the agreement was prohibited by law or assembly vote, was made under unjust compulsion, or was prevented by unforeseen circumstances beyond the person's control. However, the parties should first attempt to resolve the matter through arbitration or mediation by their neighbors.

"The class of craftsmen who have equipped human life with the arts is dedicated to Hephaestus and Athena. There is also a class of craftsmen who protect the works of all other craftsmen through the arts of defense -- the devotees of Ares and Athena, to whom they are rightly dedicated. All of these serve the country and the people throughout their lives: some as leaders in battle, others making implements and works for hire. They must not deceive in such matters, out of respect for the gods who are their patrons.

"If a craftsman through laziness fails to complete his work on time -- not honoring the god who provides his livelihood, but foolishly thinking he is his own god who will let himself off easily -- he shall first suffer divine consequences, and then the law shall follow in the same spirit. He must refund the contracted price for the work he failed to perform, and he must start over and complete the work for free within the original timeframe.

"When someone undertakes a commission, the law gives the same advice as for sellers: do not try to inflate the price, but charge the fair value. The craftsman, after all, surely knows what his work is worth."
D: Elegant: Broken agreements may be brought to tribal courts, unless the agreement was illegal, coerced, or prevented by unforeseeable events. Parties should first attempt resolution through arbiters or neighbors. The class of craftsmen who furnish human life with the arts is dedicated to Hephaestus and Athena; the class that defends all other crafts through the arts of war is dedicated to Ares and Athena. Neither class should deceive, out of respect for the gods who are their patrons. A lazy craftsman who misses his deadline, foolishly thinking he is his own god who will let himself off easily, must refund the contract price and redo the work for free within the original timeframe. Like sellers, contractors must charge fair value rather than inflated prices, for the craftsman surely knows what his work is worth.
D: Summary: Contract law provides for enforcement of agreements in tribal courts, with exceptions for illegal, coerced, or force majeure situations. Craftsmen, as servants of the gods, must not deceive. Those who miss deadlines through laziness must refund their fee and redo the work without charge, and all must price their services at fair value.
D: Distilled: Broken contracts are enforceable in court unless illegal, coerced, or prevented by chance; lazy craftsmen must refund their fee and complete the work for free.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses contract law and the obligations of craftsmen. -- The three exceptions to contractual enforcement (illegal agreements, duress, and force majeure) closely parallel modern contract defenses and reflect sophisticated legal reasoning. -- The requirement for prior arbitration or mediation before litigation is a feature of actual Athenian legal procedure; public arbitrators (diaitetai) were appointed to hear civil cases before they went to trial. -- The dedication of craftsmen to Hephaestus (god of the forge and metalworking) and Athena (goddess of wisdom and crafts) reflects genuine Greek religious practice; Athens had a major festival, the Hephaestia, honoring artisan culture. -- The striking phrase 'considering, foolish fellow, that he is his own God' recalls Republic II (381c-382a) on human presumption regarding divine matters. -- The penalty of free re-performance plus refund is more severe than simple contract damages, serving a punitive and moralizing function. -- The principle that a craftsman 'knows the value of his work' echoes Socrates' frequent appeals to craft knowledge as a model for ethical expertise.

D: Original: Wherefore, in free states the man of art ought not to attempt to impose upon private individuals by the help of his art, which is by nature a true thing; and he who is wronged in a matter of this sort, shall have a right of action against the party who has wronged him. And if any one lets out work to a craftsman, and does not pay him duly according to the lawful agreement, disregarding Zeus the guardian of the city and Athene, who are the partners of the state, and overthrows the foundations of society for the sake of a little gain, in his case let the law and the Gods maintain the common bonds of the state. And let him who, having already received the work in exchange, does not pay the price in the time agreed, pay double the price; and if a year has elapsed, although interest is not to be taken on loans, yet for every drachma which he owes to the contractor let him pay a monthly interest of an obol. Suits about these matters are to be decided by the courts of the tribes; and by the way, since we have mentioned craftsmen at all, we must not forget the other craft of war, in which generals and tacticians are the craftsmen, who undertake voluntarily the work of our safety, as other craftsmen undertake other public works;-if they execute their work well the law will never tire of praising him who gives them those honours which are the just rewards of the soldier; but if any one, having already received the benefit of any noble service in war, does not make the due return of honour, the law will blame him. Let this then be the law, having an ingredient of praise, not compelling but advising the great body of the citizens to honour the brave men who are the saviours of the whole state, whether by their courage or by their military skill;-they should honour them, I say, in the second place; for the first and highest tribute of respect is to be given to those who are able above other men to honour the words of good legislators.
D: Narrative:
"Wherefore, in free states the man of art ought not to attempt to impose upon private individuals by the help of his art, which is by nature a true thing; and he who is wronged in a matter of this sort, shall have a right of action against the party who has wronged him.

"And if any one lets out work to a craftsman, and does not pay him duly according to the lawful agreement, disregarding Zeus the guardian of the city and Athene, who are the partners of the state, and overthrows the foundations of society for the sake of a little gain, in his case let the law and the Gods maintain the common bonds of the state. And let him who, having already received the work in exchange, does not pay the price in the time agreed, pay double the price; and if a year has elapsed, although interest is not to be taken on loans, yet for every drachma which he owes to the contractor let him pay a monthly interest of an obol.

"Suits about these matters are to be decided by the courts of the tribes; and by the way, since we have mentioned craftsmen at all, we must not forget the other craft of war, in which generals and tacticians are the craftsmen, who undertake voluntarily the work of our safety, as other craftsmen undertake other public works; if they execute their work well the law will never tire of praising him who gives them those honours which are the just rewards of the soldier; but if any one, having already received the benefit of any noble service in war, does not make the due return of honour, the law will blame him.

"Let this then be the law, having an ingredient of praise, not compelling but advising the great body of the citizens to honour the brave men who are the saviours of the whole state, whether by their courage or by their military skill; they should honour them, I say, in the second place; for the first and highest tribute of respect is to be given to those who are able above other men to honour the words of good legislators."
D: Refashioned:
"In a free state, a skilled professional must not exploit private individuals through his expertise, which is by its nature something honest and true. Anyone wronged in this way has the right to bring an action against the offender.

"Equally, anyone who commissions work from a craftsman and then fails to pay according to the lawful agreement -- disregarding Zeus the guardian of the city and Athena who are the state's partners, and undermining the foundations of society for the sake of a small saving -- shall face the enforcement of law and the displeasure of the gods. Whoever receives the completed work but does not pay within the agreed time must pay double the price. If a full year passes, then despite the general prohibition on interest for loans, he must pay a monthly surcharge of one obol for every drachma owed.

"These disputes shall be resolved in the tribal courts. And since we have raised the subject of craftsmen, we should not overlook the craft of war. Generals and tacticians are craftsmen too, who voluntarily undertake the work of keeping us safe, just as other craftsmen undertake public works. When they do their job well, the law will never tire of praising those who give them the honors soldiers justly deserve. But anyone who has benefited from noble military service and fails to return the appropriate honor will be condemned by the law.

"This law carries an element of praise rather than compulsion, advising the citizenry to honor the brave men who save the state through courage or military skill. They should be honored in the second place, however; the first and highest respect belongs to those who, more than all others, are able to honor the words of good legislators."
D: Elegant: In a free state, skilled professionals must not exploit private individuals through their art, which by nature is an honest thing; anyone wronged has a right of action. Equally, clients who fail to pay craftsmen according to lawful agreements disregard Zeus the guardian of the city and Athena, partners of the state, undermining the foundations of society for petty gain. Those who receive completed work but do not pay on time owe double, and after a year, interest accrues at one obol per drachma monthly despite the general prohibition on loan interest. Military leaders are craftsmen of public safety who deserve honor when they execute their work well, and the law shall never tire of praising those who honor them. Yet the highest tribute belongs not to soldiers but to those who are able above all others to honor the words of good legislators.
D: Summary: Craftsmen must not exploit clients, and clients must pay on time or face double penalties and interest. Military leaders deserve honor as craftsmen of public safety, but the highest respect is reserved for those who uphold and honor the words of good legislators.
D: Distilled: Both craftsmen and their clients owe honest dealing, with the highest civic honor going not to soldiers but to those who best honor the laws.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses both craftsman fraud and client non-payment. -- The invocation of Zeus Polieus (guardian of the city) and Athena Polias (city-protector) as 'partners of the state' reflects the deep integration of religion and civic life in Greek political thought. -- The interest rate of one obol per drachma per month (approximately 16.7% annually) is notable because the Laws generally prohibits interest on loans; this exception for contract non-payment shows the severity of the offense. -- The comparison of generals to craftsmen is characteristically Platonic, echoing the Republic's treatment of the guardian class as practitioners of a specialized art (techne). -- The hierarchy placing law-honoring citizens above military heroes is a distinctly Platonic value judgment, reversing the typical Greek prioritization of martial valor. Compare Laws I (630d-631d), where courage is ranked below wisdom and justice. -- The phrase 'the words of good legislators' (logoi ton agathon nomotheton) implicitly includes the Laws itself.

D: Original: The greater part of the dealings between man and man have been now regulated by us with the exception of those that relate to orphans and the supervision of orphans by their guardians. These follow next in order, and must be regulated in some way. But to arrive at them we must begin with the testamentary wishes of the dying and the case of those who may have happened to die intestate. When I said, Cleinias, that we must regulate them, I had in my mind the difficulty and perplexity in which all such matters are involved. You cannot leave them unregulated, for individuals would make regulations at variance with one another, and repugnant to the laws and habits of the living and to their own previous habits, if a person were simply allowed to make any will which he pleased, and this were to take effect in whatever state he may have been at the end of his life; for most of us lose our senses in a manner, and feel crushed when we think that we are about to die.
D: Narrative:
"The greater part of the dealings between man and man have been now regulated by us with the exception of those that relate to orphans and the supervision of orphans by their guardians. These follow next in order, and must be regulated in some way. But to arrive at them we must begin with the testamentary wishes of the dying and the case of those who may have happened to die intestate.

"When I said, Cleinias, that we must regulate them, I had in my mind the difficulty and perplexity in which all such matters are involved. You cannot leave them unregulated, for individuals would make regulations at variance with one another, and repugnant to the laws and habits of the living and to their own previous habits, if a person were simply allowed to make any will which he pleased, and this were to take effect in whatever state he may have been at the end of his life; for most of us lose our senses in a manner, and feel crushed when we think that we are about to die."
D: Refashioned:
"We have now regulated the greater part of dealings between people, with the exception of those concerning orphans and the oversight of guardians. These must come next and require careful regulation. But to address them properly, we must first deal with the testamentary wishes of the dying and the cases of those who die without a will.

"When I said we must regulate these matters, Cleinias, I had in mind the enormous difficulty and perplexity they involve. You simply cannot leave them unregulated. If people were allowed to make whatever will they pleased, to take effect regardless of their mental state at the end of life, their dispositions would contradict one another and conflict with the laws and customs of the living -- and even with their own earlier intentions. For most of us lose our rational faculties to some degree, and feel overwhelmed when we contemplate our approaching death."
D: Elegant: Most interpersonal dealings have now been regulated, but orphan care and guardianship remain. These require first addressing wills and intestate succession -- matters of great difficulty. Testamentary freedom cannot be unlimited, because dying people often lose their rational judgment and make dispositions that contradict the laws, the customs of the living, and even their own prior wishes.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger transitions to inheritance law, arguing that unlimited testamentary freedom is dangerous because dying people often lose their judgment and make wills that conflict with established laws, customs, and their own earlier intentions.
D: Distilled: Unlimited testamentary freedom is dangerous because the dying often lose their rational judgment and make dispositions at odds with law, custom, and their own prior wishes.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses Cleinias, transitioning to inheritance law. -- This passage introduces one of the Laws' most significant legal innovations: the restriction of testamentary freedom in favor of family and state interests. -- The observation that the dying 'lose their senses' reflects Greek medical and philosophical understanding of mental decline near death; similar observations appear in Hippocratic writings on prognosis. -- Athenian law, particularly as reformed by Solon, gave citizens considerable testamentary freedom, which Plato here criticizes as excessive indulgence. Solon's reform allowing childless men to will property outside the family was considered revolutionary. -- The tension between individual testamentary wishes and communal interests remains a live issue in modern inheritance law. -- This passage anticipates John Stuart Mill's observation that people's deathbed wishes deserve less weight than their considered lifetime judgments. -- Compare Republic V (464b-d), where Socrates eliminates private property entirely for the guardian class, making inheritance law irrelevant.

D: Original:
What do you mean, Stranger?

O Cleinias, a man when he is about to die is an intractable creature, and is apt to use language which causes a great deal of anxiety and trouble to the legislator.

In what way?

He wants to have the entire control of all his property, and will use angry words.

Such as what?

O ye Gods, he will say, how monstrous that I am not allowed to give, or not to give my own to whom I will-less to him who has been bad to me, and more to him who has been good to me, and whose badness and goodness have been tested by me in time of sickness or in old age and in every other sort of fortune!

Well Stranger, and may he not very fairly say so?

In my opinion, Cleinias, the ancient legislators were too good-natured, and made laws without sufficient observation or consideration of human things.

What do you mean?

I mean, my friend that they were afraid of the testator's reproaches, and so they passed a law to the effect that a man should be allowed to dispose of his property in all respects as he liked; but you and I, if I am not mistaken, will have something better to say to our departing citizens.

What?
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "What do you mean, Stranger?"

"O Cleinias, a man when he is about to die is an intractable creature, and is apt to use language which causes a great deal of anxiety and trouble to the legislator."

Cleinias: "In what way?"

"He wants to have the entire control of all his property, and will use angry words."

Cleinias: "Such as what?"

"'O ye Gods,' he will say, 'how monstrous that I am not allowed to give, or not to give my own to whom I will -- less to him who has been bad to me, and more to him who has been good to me, and whose badness and goodness have been tested by me in time of sickness or in old age and in every other sort of fortune!'"

Cleinias: "Well Stranger, and may he not very fairly say so?"

"In my opinion, Cleinias, the ancient legislators were too good-natured, and made laws without sufficient observation or consideration of human things."

Cleinias: "What do you mean?"

"I mean, my friend, that they were afraid of the testator's reproaches, and so they passed a law to the effect that a man should be allowed to dispose of his property in all respects as he liked; but you and I, if I am not mistaken, will have something better to say to our departing citizens."

Cleinias: "What?"
D: Refashioned:
"What do you mean, Stranger?" asked Cleinias.

"A man on his deathbed, Cleinias, is an intractable creature. He tends to say things that cause the legislator a great deal of anxiety and trouble."

"In what way?"

"He demands total control over all his property and speaks in angry terms."

"What sort of things does he say?"

"Something like this: 'Good gods, how outrageous that I cannot give or withhold my own property as I please! I want to give less to those who have treated me badly and more to those who have been good to me -- people whose true character I have tested through times of sickness, old age, and every kind of fortune!'"

"Well, Stranger, can he not say that quite fairly?"

"In my view, Cleinias, the ancient legislators were too soft-hearted. They made laws without sufficient observation of human nature."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean that they were intimidated by the dying man's complaints, and so they passed laws allowing people to dispose of their property however they wished. But you and I, if I am not mistaken, will have something better to say to our departing citizens."

"What is that?"
D: Elegant: A dying man is intractable -- he demands absolute control over his property and angrily insists on rewarding those who treated him well and punishing those who treated him badly. Ancient legislators, intimidated by such complaints, granted unlimited testamentary freedom. But this was legislating from weakness, without sufficient understanding of human nature. Better laws are needed.
D: Summary: Through a vivid dialogue, the Athenian Stranger argues that ancient legislators were too intimidated by dying men's complaints to restrict testamentary freedom. The dying demand total control over their property to reward and punish, but this demand should not determine the law.
D: Distilled: Ancient legislators caved to the dying man's angry demand for absolute control over his property, but good lawmaking requires resisting such emotional pressure.
Notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger speak in rapid dialogue. -- The dying man's speech is a masterful piece of dramatic ventriloquism, capturing the emotional intensity of deathbed property disputes. -- The critique of ancient legislators being 'too good-natured' (euethesteroi) is a recurring Platonic theme: well-meaning naivete is not the same as wisdom. Compare Laws III (690a-c) on the dangers of excessive democratic indulgence. -- Solon's testamentary reforms (c. 594 BCE) are likely the target of criticism here; Solon allowed Athenians without legitimate sons to will their property freely, which Plutarch (Life of Solon 21) describes as a revolutionary change. -- The phrase 'tested by me in time of sickness or in old age' reflects the Greek practice of evaluating character through adversity, a theme in Plato's discussion of testing guardians in Republic III (413c-414a). -- Cleinias's sympathetic response ('may he not fairly say so?') represents the common-sense view that Plato must argue against.

D: Original: O my friends, we will say to them, hard is it for you, who are creatures of a day, to know what is yours-hard too, as the Delphic oracle says, to know yourselves at this hour. Now I, as the legislator, regard you and your possessions, not as belonging to yourselves, but as belonging to your whole family, both past and future, and yet more do regard both family and possessions as belonging to the state; wherefore, if some one steals upon you with flattery, when you are tossed on the sea of disease or old age, and persuades you to dispose of your property in a way that is not for the best, I will not, if I can help, allow this; but I will legislate with a view to the whole, considering what is best both for the state and for the family, esteeming as I ought the feelings of an individual at a lower rate; and I hope that you will depart in peace and kindness towards us, as you are going the way of all mankind; and we will impartially take care of all your concerns, not neglecting any of them, if we can possibly help. Let this be our prelude and consolation to the living and dying, Cleinias, and let the law be as follows:
D: Narrative:
"O my friends, we will say to them, hard is it for you, who are creatures of a day, to know what is yours -- hard too, as the Delphic oracle says, to know yourselves at this hour. Now I, as the legislator, regard you and your possessions, not as belonging to yourselves, but as belonging to your whole family, both past and future, and yet more do I regard both family and possessions as belonging to the state; wherefore, if some one steals upon you with flattery, when you are tossed on the sea of disease or old age, and persuades you to dispose of your property in a way that is not for the best, I will not, if I can help, allow this; but I will legislate with a view to the whole, considering what is best both for the state and for the family, esteeming as I ought the feelings of an individual at a lower rate; and I hope that you will depart in peace and kindness towards us, as you are going the way of all mankind; and we will impartially take care of all your concerns, not neglecting any of them, if we can possibly help.

"Let this be our prelude and consolation to the living and dying, Cleinias, and let the law be as follows:"
D: Refashioned:
"We will say to the dying: 'Dear friends, it is hard for you, who are creatures of a day, to know what truly belongs to you -- and hard too, as the Delphic oracle says, to know yourselves at this hour. I, as legislator, do not regard you or your possessions as belonging to yourselves alone. You belong to your whole family, past and future, and even more fundamentally, both family and possessions belong to the state.

"'Therefore, if someone approaches you with flattery when you are tossed on the sea of sickness or old age, and persuades you to dispose of your property unwisely, I will not allow it if I can prevent it. I will legislate with a view to the whole, considering what is best for both the state and the family, and ranking the feelings of any one individual lower, as I should.

"'I hope you will depart in peace and goodwill toward us, as you go the way of all humanity. We will take impartial care of all your affairs, neglecting nothing, as far as we possibly can.'

"Let this serve as our prelude and consolation to the living and the dying, Cleinias, and let the law be as follows:"
D: Elegant: The legislator's address to the dying: you are creatures of a day, and at this hour you cannot truly know what is yours or even know yourselves. Your possessions belong not to you alone but to your whole family -- past and future -- and ultimately to the state. The law will prevent flatterers from exploiting your weakness to produce unwise bequests, legislating for the good of the whole rather than individual sentiment. Depart in peace; we will care for all your concerns impartially.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger composes a consolatory address to the dying: your property belongs to your family and the state, not to you alone. The legislator will protect against deathbed manipulation by prioritizing the good of family and state over individual wishes.
D: Distilled: The dying do not truly own their property -- it belongs to family and state -- and the legislator must protect against deathbed manipulation by flatterers.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, addressing Cleinias and composing a model legislative prelude. -- 'Creatures of a day' (ephemerioi) is a Pindaric phrase (Pythian 8.95) expressing human mortality and limitation. -- 'Know thyself' (gnothi seauton) was inscribed at the temple of Apollo at Delphi and attributed to various sages; its application here to the dying is a devastating rhetorical move -- if you cannot know yourself at the best of times, how much less at the hour of death? -- The doctrine that individual property ultimately belongs to the state is Plato's most radical inheritance principle, going further than Republic V's communism for guardians by applying to all citizens. -- The metaphor of being 'tossed on the sea of disease or old age' is characteristically Platonic maritime imagery; compare Republic VI (488a-489a), the Ship of State analogy. -- The prelude as consolation (paramythia) to the dying recalls the Phaedo's depiction of philosophy as preparation for death. -- This legislative technique of offering persuasive preambles before coercive laws is unique to the Laws among Plato's works and is discussed programmatically in Laws IV (719e-724b).

D: Original: He who makes a disposition in a testament, if he be the father of a family, shall first of all inscribe as his heir any one of his sons whom he may think fit; and if he gives any of his children to be adopted by another citizen, let the adoption be inscribed. And if he has a son remaining over and above who has not been adopted upon any lot, and who may be expected to be sent out to a colony according to law, to him his father may give as much as he pleases of the rest of his property, with the exception of the paternal lot and the fixtures on the lot. And if there are other sons, let him distribute among them what there is more than the lot in such portions as he pleases. And if one of the sons has already a house of his own, he shall not give him of the money, nor shall he give money to a daughter who has been betrothed, but if she is not betrothed he may give her money. And if any of the sons or daughters shall be found to have another lot of land in the country, which has accrued after the testament has been made, they shall leave the lot which they have inherited to the heir of the man who has made the will. If the testator has no sons, but only daughters, let him choose the husband of any one of his daughters whom he pleases, and leave and inscribe him as his son and heir.
D: Narrative:
"He who makes a disposition in a testament, if he be the father of a family, shall first of all inscribe as his heir any one of his sons whom he may think fit; and if he gives any of his children to be adopted by another citizen, let the adoption be inscribed. And if he has a son remaining over and above who has not been adopted upon any lot, and who may be expected to be sent out to a colony according to law, to him his father may give as much as he pleases of the rest of his property, with the exception of the paternal lot and the fixtures on the lot.

"And if there are other sons, let him distribute among them what there is more than the lot in such portions as he pleases. And if one of the sons has already a house of his own, he shall not give him of the money, nor shall he give money to a daughter who has been betrothed, but if she is not betrothed he may give her money. And if any of the sons or daughters shall be found to have another lot of land in the country, which has accrued after the testament has been made, they shall leave the lot which they have inherited to the heir of the man who has made the will.

"If the testator has no sons, but only daughters, let him choose the husband of any one of his daughters whom he pleases, and leave and inscribe him as his son and heir."
D: Refashioned:
"A father making a will shall first designate one of his sons as heir to the family lot. If he gives any of his children in adoption to another citizen, the adoption must be formally recorded. If he has a surplus son who has not been adopted onto any lot and is expected to be sent to a colony under the law, the father may give him as much of his remaining property as he wishes -- but not the paternal lot itself or anything fixed on it.

"If there are additional sons, the father may distribute whatever property exceeds the lot among them in whatever portions he chooses. However, he shall not give money to a son who already has his own household, nor to a daughter who is already betrothed. An unbetrothed daughter may receive money. If any son or daughter subsequently acquires another lot of land, they must surrender the inherited lot to the testator's designated heir.

"If the testator has no sons but only daughters, he shall choose a husband for whichever daughter he prefers and inscribe that husband as his adopted son and heir."
D: Elegant: A testator must designate one son as heir to the family lot, which cannot be divided. Surplus sons bound for colonies may receive other property. Sons with their own households and betrothed daughters receive nothing extra. If children later acquire additional lots, they must return the inherited one. A father with only daughters selects a son-in-law to become his adopted son and heir.
D: Summary: Inheritance law prioritizes preserving the family lot intact: one son inherits it, surplus property may be distributed among other children with restrictions, and fathers with only daughters must adopt a son-in-law as heir.
D: Distilled: The paternal lot must pass intact to one designated heir, with surplus property distributable under restrictions designed to maintain the fixed number of household units.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger details testamentary law for the colony of Magnesia. -- The indivisibility of the paternal lot (kleros) is fundamental to Magnesia's social structure: the 5,040 lots must remain intact and equal across generations, preventing both fragmentation and concentration of land. -- The provision for 'surplus sons' bound for colonies reflects Greek colonization practices; overpopulation was managed by establishing new settlements (apoikiai). -- The rule against giving money to sons with their own households or betrothed daughters prevents double inheritance and maintains household equality. -- The adoption of a son-in-law (epikleros system) has close parallels in Athenian law, where an heiress (epikleros) without brothers would be married to her nearest male relative to keep property within the family. -- Compare Aristotle, Politics II.6 (1265a-1266b), where he critiques the Laws' property system for not adequately preventing inequality over time.

D: Original: And if a man have lost his son, when he was a child, and before he could be reckoned among grown-up men, whether his own or an adopted son, let the testator make mention of the circumstance and inscribe whom he will to be his second son in hope of better fortune. If the testator has no children at all, he may select and give to any one whom he pleases the tenth part of the property which he has acquired; but let him not be blamed if he gives all the rest to his adopted son, and makes a friend of him according to the law. If the sons of a man require guardians, and: the father when he dies leaves a will appointing guardians, those have been named by him, whoever they are and whatever their number be, if they are able and willing to take charge of the children, shall be recognized according to the provisions of the will. But if he dies and has made no will, or a will in which he has appointed no guardians, then the next of kin, two on the father's and two on the mother's side, and one of the friends of the deceased, shall have the authority of guardians, whom the guardians of the law shall appoint when the orphans require guardians. And the fifteen eldest guardians of the law shall have the whole care and charge of the orphans, divided into threes according to seniority-a body of three for one year, and then another body of three for the next year, until the cycle of the five periods is complete; and this, as far as possible, is to continue always. If a man dies, having made no will at all, and leaves sons who require the care of guardians, they shall share in the protection which is afforded by these laws.
D: Narrative:
"And if a man have lost his son, when he was a child, and before he could be reckoned among grown-up men, whether his own or an adopted son, let the testator make mention of the circumstance and inscribe whom he will to be his second son in hope of better fortune. If the testator has no children at all, he may select and give to any one whom he pleases the tenth part of the property which he has acquired; but let him not be blamed if he gives all the rest to his adopted son, and makes a friend of him according to the law.

"If the sons of a man require guardians, and the father when he dies leaves a will appointing guardians, those have been named by him, whoever they are and whatever their number be, if they are able and willing to take charge of the children, shall be recognized according to the provisions of the will. But if he dies and has made no will, or a will in which he has appointed no guardians, then the next of kin, two on the father's and two on the mother's side, and one of the friends of the deceased, shall have the authority of guardians, whom the guardians of the law shall appoint when the orphans require guardians.

"And the fifteen eldest guardians of the law shall have the whole care and charge of the orphans, divided into threes according to seniority -- a body of three for one year, and then another body of three for the next year, until the cycle of the five periods is complete; and this, as far as possible, is to continue always. If a man dies, having made no will at all, and leaves sons who require the care of guardians, they shall share in the protection which is afforded by these laws."
D: Refashioned:
"If a man's son -- whether biological or adopted -- has died in childhood before reaching adulthood, the testator shall note this circumstance and designate a new second son in the hope of better fortune. If the testator has no children at all, he may give anyone he chooses up to one-tenth of his acquired property; he should not be faulted for giving the remainder to an adopted son, thereby making a friend of him under the law.

"When a father's children need guardians and he leaves a will appointing them, whoever he has named -- regardless of their number -- shall serve as guardians, provided they are willing and able. But if the father dies without a will, or without naming guardians, then the next of kin shall serve: two from the father's side, two from the mother's side, and one friend of the deceased. The guardians of the law shall make these appointments when orphans require protection.

"The fifteen eldest guardians of the law shall exercise overall care and supervision of all orphans. They shall serve in groups of three, rotating annually by seniority, completing a five-year cycle that continues perpetually. Anyone who dies intestate and leaves children requiring guardianship shall have his children protected under these same provisions."
D: Elegant: If a testator's son dies in childhood, he shall record the loss and designate a new second son in hope of better fortune. A childless testator may freely give one-tenth of his acquired property to anyone he chooses and leave the rest to an adopted son, making a friend of him under the law. Guardians named in a will serve if willing and able, regardless of who they are or how many; without a will, two paternal kin, two maternal kin, and one friend of the deceased are appointed by the guardians of the law. The fifteen eldest guardians of the law have overall charge of all orphans, serving in rotating groups of three for one-year terms, cycling perpetually through five periods. Those who die intestate leaving children in need of guardians shall receive the same protections.
D: Summary: The law provides for succession when a son has died and for cases of complete childlessness, allowing adoption and limited free disposal of property. Guardianship is either testamentary or assigned from kin, with the fifteen eldest guardians of the law exercising permanent rotating oversight of all orphans.
D: Distilled: Guardianship of orphans is either appointed by will or assigned from kin, with the fifteen eldest guardians of the law maintaining perpetual rotating oversight.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger continues with guardianship and orphan protection. -- The 'second son in hope of better fortune' (eutychesteron) reflects Greek anxieties about child mortality, which was extremely high in the ancient world -- perhaps 30-50% of children did not survive to adulthood. -- The one-tenth limit on free disposal of acquired property balances individual freedom against family claims, a moderate position compared to complete testamentary freedom or complete restriction. -- The five-member default guardian panel (2 paternal + 2 maternal + 1 friend) provides balanced representation of both family lines while including someone chosen for personal relationship. -- The rotating system of fifteen guardians of the law in groups of three ensures continuous institutional oversight of orphan welfare, preventing the neglect that could arise from individual responsibility. -- Athenian law had similar provisions: the archon was specifically charged with orphan protection, and guardians (epitropoi) could be appointed by will, by kinship, or by archontal appointment.

D: Original: And if a man dying by some unexpected fate leaves daughters behind him, let him pardon the legislator if he gives them in marriage, he have a regard only to two out of three conditions-nearness of kin and the preservation of the lot, and omits the third condition, which a father would naturally consider, for he would choose out of all the citizens a son for himself, and a husband for his daughter, with a view to his character and disposition-the father, say, shall forgive the legislator if he disregards this, which to him is an impossible consideration. Let the law about these matters where practicable be as follows:-If a man dies without making a will, and leaves behind him daughters, let his brother, being the son of the same father or of the same mother, having no lot, marry the daughter and have the lot of the dead man. And if he have no brother, but only a brother's son, in like manner let them marry, if they be of a suitable age; and if there be not even a brother's son, but only the son of a sister, let them do likewise, and so in the fourth degree, if there be only the testator's father's brother, or in the fifth degree, his father's brother's son, or in the sixth degree, the child of his father's sister. Let kindred be always reckoned in this way: if a person leaves daughters the relationship shall proceed upwards through brothers and sisters, and brothers' and sisters' children, and first the males shall come, and after them the females in the same family.
D: Narrative:
"And if a man dying by some unexpected fate leaves daughters behind him, let him pardon the legislator if he gives them in marriage, having regard only to two out of three conditions -- nearness of kin and the preservation of the lot -- and omits the third condition, which a father would naturally consider, for he would choose out of all the citizens a son for himself, and a husband for his daughter, with a view to his character and disposition -- the father, say, shall forgive the legislator if he disregards this, which to him is an impossible consideration.

"Let the law about these matters where practicable be as follows: If a man dies without making a will, and leaves behind him daughters, let his brother, being the son of the same father or of the same mother, having no lot, marry the daughter and have the lot of the dead man. And if he have no brother, but only a brother's son, in like manner let them marry, if they be of a suitable age; and if there be not even a brother's son, but only the son of a sister, let them do likewise, and so in the fourth degree, if there be only the testator's father's brother, or in the fifth degree, his father's brother's son, or in the sixth degree, the child of his father's sister.

"Let kindred be always reckoned in this way: if a person leaves daughters the relationship shall proceed upwards through brothers and sisters, and brothers' and sisters' children, and first the males shall come, and after them the females in the same family."
D: Refashioned:
"If a man dies unexpectedly and leaves only daughters, he must forgive the legislator for arranging their marriages based on only two of three desirable conditions: nearness of kinship and preservation of the lot. The third condition -- choosing a husband based on character and disposition, which any father would naturally wish to consider -- is an impossible standard for the law to apply.

"The law shall be as follows. If a man dies intestate leaving daughters, his brother -- whether from the same father or the same mother -- shall marry the daughter and assume the dead man's lot, provided the brother has no lot of his own. If there is no brother, a brother's son shall marry her, provided they are of suitable age. If there is no brother's son, a sister's son shall marry her. Failing that, the testator's father's brother takes fourth priority, the father's brother's son fifth, and the child of the father's sister sixth.

"Kinship shall always be reckoned in this way: when a man leaves daughters, the succession moves upward through brothers and sisters and their children, with males taking priority over females within the same family line."
D: Elegant: The legislator can only consider two of three marriage conditions: nearness of kin and preservation of the lot, not personal character. The inheritance-marriage order for daughters of an intestate father runs: brother, brother's son, sister's son, father's brother, father's brother's son, father's sister's child -- always with males taking priority within each degree of kinship.
D: Summary: When a man dies intestate leaving only daughters, the law matches them with the nearest male kinsman who lacks his own lot, following a strict order of priority through six degrees of kinship. The legislator acknowledges he cannot consider character, only kinship and lot preservation.
D: Distilled: Daughters of intestate fathers must marry the nearest eligible male kinsman in a fixed order of priority, preserving both the family lot and kinship ties.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses the inheritance-marriage of heiresses. -- This system closely resembles the Athenian epikleros (heiress) institution, whereby a daughter with no brothers was 'attached to the lot' (epi + kleros) and married to the nearest male relative to keep property within the family. -- The honest acknowledgment that the legislator cannot assess individual character reflects a pragmatic awareness of law's limitations, unusual in Plato. -- The six-degree kinship ladder follows standard Greek reckoning of anchisteia (degrees of family relationship). -- The priority of males over females within each degree reflects the patrilineal structure of Greek inheritance, though the inclusion of maternal kin (sister's son, father's sister's child) shows some recognition of matrilineal connections. -- Spartan inheritance law was more favorable to women; Aristotle (Politics II.9, 1270a) criticizes Sparta for allowing women to own two-fifths of the land. -- Compare Laws VI (774b-776a) on marriage regulations more broadly.

D: Original: The judge shall consider and determine the suitableness or unsuitableness of age in marriage; he shall make an inspection of the males naked, and of the women naked down to the navel. And if there be a lack of kinsmen in a family extending to grandchildren of a brother, or to the grandchildren of a grandfather's children, the maiden may choose with the consent of her guardians any one of the citizens who is willing and whom she wills, and he shall be the heir of the dead man, and the husband of his daughter. Circumstances vary, and there may sometimes be a still greater lack of relations within the limits of the state; and if any maiden has no kindred living in the city, and there is some one who has been sent out to a colony, and she is disposed to make him the heir of her father's possessions, if he be indeed of her kindred, let him proceed to take the lot according to the regulation of the law; but if he be not of her kindred, she having no kinsmen within the city, and he be chosen by the daughter of the dead man, and empowered to marry by the guardians, let him return home and take the lot of him who died intestate.
D: Narrative:
"The judge shall consider and determine the suitableness or unsuitableness of age in marriage; he shall make an inspection of the males naked, and of the women naked down to the navel. And if there be a lack of kinsmen in a family extending to grandchildren of a brother, or to the grandchildren of a grandfather's children, the maiden may choose with the consent of her guardians any one of the citizens who is willing and whom she wills, and he shall be the heir of the dead man, and the husband of his daughter.

"Circumstances vary, and there may sometimes be a still greater lack of relations within the limits of the state; and if any maiden has no kindred living in the city, and there is some one who has been sent out to a colony, and she is disposed to make him the heir of her father's possessions, if he be indeed of her kindred, let him proceed to take the lot according to the regulation of the law; but if he be not of her kindred, she having no kinsmen within the city, and he be chosen by the daughter of the dead man, and empowered to marry by the guardians, let him return home and take the lot of him who died intestate."
D: Refashioned:
"The judge shall evaluate whether the ages of the proposed couple are suitable for marriage. He shall inspect the males unclothed, and the women unclothed from the waist up, to assess physical fitness.

"If the family lacks kinsmen extending to a brother's grandchildren or a grandfather's great-grandchildren, the maiden may -- with her guardians' consent -- choose any willing citizen she prefers. That man shall become the heir of her deceased father and her husband.

"Circumstances vary, and sometimes the lack of relatives within the state is even more severe. If a maiden has no kindred at all in the city, and there is someone who has been sent to a colony, and she wishes to make him her father's heir: if he is indeed her kinsman, let him take the lot according to law. But if he is not her kinsman, yet she chooses him and her guardians authorize the marriage, let him return home and take the lot of the man who died intestate."
D: Elegant: Judges must assess age suitability and physical fitness for proposed marriages through bodily inspection. When no eligible kinsman exists through the standard degrees, the maiden may choose any willing citizen with her guardians' consent. In extreme cases, even colonists may be recalled: kinsmen by right, non-kinsmen by the maiden's choice and guardian approval.
D: Summary: Judges assess physical and age suitability for inheritance-marriages. When no eligible kinsman can be found through the standard degrees, the heiress may freely choose a citizen husband with her guardians' consent, and colonists may even be recalled to take vacant lots.
D: Distilled: When kinship claims are exhausted, the heiress gains the freedom to choose her own husband with guardian approval, and colonists may be recalled to fill vacant lots.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses edge cases in heiress-marriage. -- The physical inspection provision is striking to modern readers but reflects Greek cultural norms: physical fitness was considered essential for marriage and reproduction, and nudity in athletic contexts was commonplace for men. The partial inspection of women (to the navel) represents a compromise with modesty. -- The provision allowing the maiden to choose freely when kinship claims are exhausted is a notable moment of individual agency within an otherwise rigidly structured system. -- The recall of colonists to fill vacant lots shows the importance of maintaining the fixed 5,040 households; empty lots threaten the entire social structure. -- Athenian law similarly allowed an epikleros to petition the archon if a designated kinsman was unsuitable. -- The progressive expansion of choice as kinship ties thin out (from mandatory kinship marriage to free choice) shows sophisticated legal reasoning about balancing structural imperatives against individual circumstances.

D: Original:
And if a man has no children, either male or female, and dies without making a will, let the previous law in general hold; and let a man and a woman go forth from the family and share the deserted house, and let the lot belong absolutely to them; and let the heiress in the first degree be a sister, and in a second degree a daughter of a brother, and in the third, a daughter of a sister, in the fourth degree the sister of a father, and in the fifth degree the daughter of a father's brother, and in a sixth degree of a father's sister; and these shall dwell with their male kinsmen, according to the degree of relationship and right, as we enacted before. Now we must not conceal from ourselves that such laws are apt to be oppressive and that there may sometimes be a hardship in the lawgiver commanding the kinsman of the dead man to marry his relation; be may be thought not to have considered the innumerable hindrances which may arise among men in the execution of such ordinances; for there may be cases in which the parties refuse to obey, and are ready to do anything rather than marry, when there is some bodily or mental malady or defect among those who are bidden to marry or be married. Persons may fancy that the legislator never thought of this, but they are mistaken; wherefore let us make a common prelude on behalf of the lawgiver and of his subjects, the law begging the latter to forgive the legislator, in that he, having to take care of the common weal, cannot order at the same time the various circumstances of individuals, and begging him to pardon them if naturally they are sometimes unable to fulfil the act which he in his ignorance imposes upon them.

And how, Stranger, can we act most fairly under the circumstances?

There must be arbiters chosen to deal with such laws and the subjects of them.

What do you mean?
D: Narrative:
"And if a man has no children, either male or female, and dies without making a will, let the previous law in general hold; and let a man and a woman go forth from the family and share the deserted house, and let the lot belong absolutely to them; and let the heiress in the first degree be a sister, and in a second degree a daughter of a brother, and in the third, a daughter of a sister, in the fourth degree the sister of a father, and in the fifth degree the daughter of a father's brother, and in a sixth degree of a father's sister; and these shall dwell with their male kinsmen, according to the degree of relationship and right, as we enacted before.

"Now we must not conceal from ourselves that such laws are apt to be oppressive and that there may sometimes be a hardship in the lawgiver commanding the kinsman of the dead man to marry his relation; he may be thought not to have considered the innumerable hindrances which may arise among men in the execution of such ordinances; for there may be cases in which the parties refuse to obey, and are ready to do anything rather than marry, when there is some bodily or mental malady or defect among those who are bidden to marry or be married. Persons may fancy that the legislator never thought of this, but they are mistaken; wherefore let us make a common prelude on behalf of the lawgiver and of his subjects, the law begging the latter to forgive the legislator, in that he, having to take care of the common weal, cannot order at the same time the various circumstances of individuals, and begging him to pardon them if naturally they are sometimes unable to fulfil the act which he in his ignorance imposes upon them."

Cleinias: "And how, Stranger, can we act most fairly under the circumstances?"

"There must be arbiters chosen to deal with such laws and the subjects of them."

Cleinias: "What do you mean?"
D: Refashioned:
"If a man dies childless and intestate, the previous law applies in general. A man and a woman from the family shall come forward to occupy the deserted household, and the lot shall belong absolutely to them. The female order of succession is: first, a sister; second, a brother's daughter; third, a sister's daughter; fourth, a father's sister; fifth, a father's brother's daughter; sixth, a father's sister's daughter. These women shall dwell with their male kinsmen according to the degree of relationship, as previously established.

"We must be honest with ourselves: such laws can be oppressive. There may be genuine hardship when the legislator commands someone to marry a relative. People may think the legislator failed to consider the countless obstacles that arise -- cases where the parties refuse to obey and would do anything rather than marry, because there is some bodily or mental illness or defect in the person they are required to marry. But they are wrong to think the legislator overlooked this. Let us therefore compose a shared prelude on behalf of both the lawgiver and his subjects. The law asks the people to forgive the legislator: he must care for the common good and cannot simultaneously accommodate every individual circumstance. And the law asks the legislator to forgive the people if they sometimes cannot fulfill requirements he imposes without full knowledge of their situation."

"And how, Stranger, can we act most fairly under these circumstances?" asked Cleinias.

"Arbiters must be chosen to mediate between such laws and the people subject to them."

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.
D: Elegant: When a man dies childless and intestate, a female heir is selected in six degrees of kinship to occupy the lot with her male kinsman. Yet such mandatory marriage laws can be oppressive -- parties may refuse when bodily or mental defects make the match unbearable. The legislator acknowledges this: a prelude asks mutual forgiveness between law and people, since general rules cannot accommodate every individual circumstance. Arbiters must be appointed to mediate these cases.
D: Summary: The law establishes a six-degree female succession for childless intestate estates but frankly acknowledges that mandatory kinship marriages can be oppressive. A mutual-forgiveness prelude asks people to understand the legislator's limitations and vice versa, with arbiters appointed to handle individual hardship cases.
D: Distilled: Mandatory kinship marriage laws are acknowledged as sometimes oppressive, requiring arbiters to mediate between the legislator's general rules and individuals' particular circumstances.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias responding. -- The remarkable mutual-forgiveness prelude is one of the Laws' most philosophically sophisticated moments, acknowledging a fundamental tension in all legislation: general rules inevitably create individual injustice. -- The female succession order mirrors the male order in passage 11.23 but applied to women inheriting deserted households. -- The recognition that bodily or mental 'malady or defect' may justify refusal to marry shows sensitivity to what modern law would call hardship exemptions or unconscionability. -- The concept of mutual forgiveness between legislator and citizen anticipates modern theories of the social contract and the idea that law must balance community interests against individual rights. -- Compare Republic IV (420b-421c), where Socrates argues that the legislator aims at the happiness of the whole city, not of any individual class. -- Aristotle, Politics II.6 (1265a), criticizes the Laws for not adequately addressing the practical difficulties of its marriage and property regulations.

D: Original: I mean to say, that a case may occur in which the nephew, having a rich father, will be unwilling to marry the daughter of his uncle; he will have a feeling of pride, and he will wish to look higher. And there are cases in which the legislator will be imposing upon him the greatest calamity, and he will be compelled to disobey the law, if he is required, for example, to take a wife who is mad, or has some other terrible malady of soul or body, such as makes life intolerable to the sufferer. Then let what we are saying concerning these cases be embodied in a law:-If any one finds fault with the established laws respecting testaments, both as to other matters and especially in what relates to marriage, and asserts that the legislator, if he were alive and present, would not compel him to obey-that is to say, would not compel those who are by our law required to marry or be given in marriage, to do either-and some kinsman or guardian dispute this, the reply is that the legislator left fifteen of the guardians of the law to be arbiters and fathers of orphans, male or female, and to them let the disputants have recourse, and by their aid determine any matters of the kind, admitting their decision to be final. But if any one thinks that too great power is thus given to the guardians of the law, let him bring his adversaries into the court of the select judges, and there have the points in dispute determined. And he who loses the cause shall have censure and blame from the legislator, which, by a man of sense, is felt to be a penalty far heavier than a great loss of money.
D: Narrative:
"I mean to say, that a case may occur in which the nephew, having a rich father, will be unwilling to marry the daughter of his uncle; he will have a feeling of pride, and he will wish to look higher. And there are cases in which the legislator will be imposing upon him the greatest calamity, and he will be compelled to disobey the law, if he is required, for example, to take a wife who is mad, or has some other terrible malady of soul or body, such as makes life intolerable to the sufferer.

"Then let what we are saying concerning these cases be embodied in a law: If any one finds fault with the established laws respecting testaments, both as to other matters and especially in what relates to marriage, and asserts that the legislator, if he were alive and present, would not compel him to obey -- that is to say, would not compel those who are by our law required to marry or be given in marriage, to do either -- and some kinsman or guardian dispute this, the reply is that the legislator left fifteen of the guardians of the law to be arbiters and fathers of orphans, male or female, and to them let the disputants have recourse, and by their aid determine any matters of the kind, admitting their decision to be final.

"But if any one thinks that too great power is thus given to the guardians of the law, let him bring his adversaries into the court of the select judges, and there have the points in dispute determined. And he who loses the cause shall have censure and blame from the legislator, which, by a man of sense, is felt to be a penalty far heavier than a great loss of money."
D: Refashioned:
"Here is what I mean. A nephew with a wealthy father may be unwilling to marry his uncle's daughter -- he feels entitled to aim higher. And in other cases, the legislator may be imposing the greatest possible calamity: a man might be required to marry a woman who is insane, or who suffers from some other terrible mental or physical affliction that makes life intolerable.

"So let us embody this in law. If anyone objects to the established inheritance and marriage laws, claiming that the legislator himself, were he alive and present, would not enforce compliance -- that he would not force those required by law to marry or be given in marriage to do so -- and if a kinsman or guardian disputes this claim, the resolution is as follows: the legislator has designated fifteen of the guardians of the law to serve as arbiters and surrogate parents for orphans of both sexes. Disputants shall bring their cases to these fifteen, whose decision shall be final.

"If anyone feels that this gives the guardians of the law too much power, he may take his case to the court of select judges for a final determination. Whoever loses shall receive censure and blame from the legislator -- a penalty that any person of sense will feel more keenly than a large financial loss."
D: Elegant: A wealthy nephew may refuse to marry a poor uncle's daughter out of pride, and the law may demand someone marry a person afflicted with madness or severe illness. For such cases, fifteen guardians of the law serve as arbiters and surrogate parents, with final authority over disputes. Appeals go to the court of select judges. The penalty for losing is the legislator's censure -- which a sensible person feels more heavily than any financial loss.
D: Summary: The law creates an appeal mechanism for mandatory marriage requirements: fifteen guardians of the law serve as arbiters, with recourse to the select judges' court. Cases involving pride, wealth disparity, or serious illness can be resolved through this system, with the losing party receiving the legislator's moral censure.
D: Distilled: Hardship cases in mandatory marriages are resolved by fifteen guardian-arbiters, with the legislator's moral censure serving as a penalty weightier than money.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger provides the arbitration mechanism. -- The fifteen guardians acting as 'fathers of orphans' creates an institutional substitute for the parental authority that death has removed, a sophisticated solution to the problem of who decides for those who cannot decide for themselves. -- The appeal to the 'court of select judges' (eklektoi dikastai) provides a check on guardian power, reflecting the Laws' general concern with preventing the abuse of authority. -- The striking claim that moral censure is 'heavier than a great loss of money' expresses Plato's characteristic valuation of honor and shame over material wealth, connecting to Republic IX (591a-592b) on the proper ordering of values. -- The example of the wealthy nephew's pride is psychologically acute and anticipates modern concerns about class endogamy. -- The provision for cases involving insanity or severe illness shows that Plato recognized what modern law calls 'undue hardship' as a legitimate ground for relief from legal obligations.

D: Original: Thus will orphan children have a second birth. After their first birth we spoke of their nurture and education, and after their second birth, when they have lost their parents, we ought to take measures that the misfortune of orphanhood may be as little sad to them as possible. In the first place, we say that the guardians of the law are lawgivers and fathers to them, not inferior to their natural fathers. Moreover, they shall take charge of them year by year as of their own kindred; and we have given both to them and to the children's own guardians a suitable admonition concerning the nurture of orphans. And we seem to have spoken opportunely in our former discourse, when we said that the souls of the dead have the power after death of taking an interest in human affairs, about which there are many tales and traditions, long indeed, but true; and seeing that they are so many and so ancient, we must believe them, and we must also believe the lawgivers, who tell us that these things are true, if they are not to be regarded as utter fools.
D: Narrative:
"Thus will orphan children have a second birth. After their first birth we spoke of their nurture and education, and after their second birth, when they have lost their parents, we ought to take measures that the misfortune of orphanhood may be as little sad to them as possible.

"In the first place, we say that the guardians of the law are lawgivers and fathers to them, not inferior to their natural fathers. Moreover, they shall take charge of them year by year as of their own kindred; and we have given both to them and to the children's own guardians a suitable admonition concerning the nurture of orphans.

"And we seem to have spoken opportunely in our former discourse, when we said that the souls of the dead have the power after death of taking an interest in human affairs, about which there are many tales and traditions, long indeed, but true; and seeing that they are so many and so ancient, we must believe them, and we must also believe the lawgivers, who tell us that these things are true, if they are not to be regarded as utter fools."
D: Refashioned:
"In this way, orphan children receive a kind of second birth. After their actual birth, we discussed their nurture and education. Now, after this second birth -- the loss of their parents -- we must take measures to make the misfortune of being an orphan as bearable as possible.

"First, the guardians of the law shall serve as both legislators and surrogate fathers to these children, in no way inferior to their natural fathers. They shall take charge of orphans year after year, treating them as their own kin. We have already given both the guardians of the law and the children's personal guardians appropriate guidance on the nurture of orphans.

"Our earlier discussion was well-timed, when we said that the souls of the dead retain the power to take an interest in human affairs after death. There are many tales and traditions about this -- lengthy, but true. Given how numerous and ancient these accounts are, we must credit them. And we must also credit the lawgivers who affirm these truths, unless we are prepared to dismiss them as utter fools."
D: Elegant: Orphanhood is a kind of second birth. The guardians of the law must serve as surrogate fathers, no less devoted than natural ones, caring for orphans as their own kin. The souls of the dead watch over human affairs -- many ancient traditions affirm this, and we must believe them unless we dismiss all lawgivers as fools.
D: Summary: Orphans undergo a 'second birth' at the loss of their parents, requiring the guardians of the law to serve as surrogate fathers equal to natural ones. The belief that the dead watch over their children's welfare lends additional moral weight to the obligation of guardianship.
D: Distilled: The guardians of the law must serve as surrogate fathers to orphans, motivated in part by the belief that the souls of the dead continue to watch over their children.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger introduces the orphan-care provisions. -- The metaphor of 'second birth' (deutera genesis) is powerful: orphanhood represents a new beginning requiring a new set of protectors. -- The guardians of the law as surrogate 'fathers and lawmakers' combines two distinct roles (nurturing and authoritative) in a single institution. -- The appeal to the souls of the dead watching over human affairs connects to Laws IX (865d-e) and the broader Greek belief in ancestor spirits. -- The rhetorical strategy of invoking 'many tales and traditions' to support belief in the afterlife contrasts with the more philosophical arguments for the soul's immortality in the Phaedo and Republic X. Here Plato appeals to tradition rather than pure reason, consistent with the Laws' generally more conservative approach. -- Compare Republic X (614b-621b), the Myth of Er, where the dead observe and are affected by the actions of the living.

D: Original: But if these things are really so, in the first place men should have a fear of the Gods above, who regard the loneliness of the orphans; and in the second place of the souls of the departed, who by nature incline to take an especial care of their own children, and are friendly to those who honour, and unfriendly to those who dishonour them. Men should also fear the souls of the living who are aged and high in honour; wherever a city is well ordered and prosperous, their descendants cherish them, and so live happily; old persons are quick to see and hear all that relates to them, and are propitious to those who are just in the fulfilment of such duties, and they punish those who wrong the orphan and the desolate, considering that they are the greatest and most sacred of trusts. To all which matters the guardian and magistrate ought to apply his mind, if he has any, and take heed of the nurture and education of the orphans, seeking in every possible way to do them good, for he is making a contribution to his own good and that of his children. He who obeys the tale which precedes the law, and does no wrong to an orphan, will never experience the wrath of the legislator.
D: Narrative:
"But if these things are really so, in the first place men should have a fear of the Gods above, who regard the loneliness of the orphans; and in the second place of the souls of the departed, who by nature incline to take an especial care of their own children, and are friendly to those who honour, and unfriendly to those who dishonour them.

"Men should also fear the souls of the living who are aged and high in honour; wherever a city is well ordered and prosperous, their descendants cherish them, and so live happily; old persons are quick to see and hear all that relates to them, and are propitious to those who are just in the fulfilment of such duties, and they punish those who wrong the orphan and the desolate, considering that they are the greatest and most sacred of trusts.

"To all which matters the guardian and magistrate ought to apply his mind, if he has any, and take heed of the nurture and education of the orphans, seeking in every possible way to do them good, for he is making a contribution to his own good and that of his children. He who obeys the tale which precedes the law, and does no wrong to an orphan, will never experience the wrath of the legislator."
D: Refashioned:
"If these things are true, then people should fear, first, the gods above, who watch over the loneliness of orphans. Second, they should fear the souls of the departed, who are naturally inclined to take special care of their own children -- friendly to those who honor their memory, hostile to those who dishonor it.

"People should also respect the souls of living elders who are held in high esteem. In every well-ordered and prosperous city, their descendants cherish them and live happily for it. The elderly are quick to notice everything that concerns them. They reward those who faithfully care for orphans and the vulnerable, and they punish those who wrong them, considering orphan care to be the greatest and most sacred of all trusts.

"Every guardian and magistrate with any sense should apply his mind to these matters and attend carefully to the nurture and education of orphans, doing them good in every possible way. In doing so, he contributes to his own welfare and that of his children. Whoever heeds this prelude and wrongs no orphan will never experience the legislator's wrath."
D: Elegant: Three sources of moral authority compel orphan care: the gods who watch over the lonely, the souls of the dead who protect their children from beyond, and the living elders whose keen perception rewards the just and punishes the negligent. Orphan care is the greatest and most sacred trust. Every guardian who heeds this serves his own good and his children's, and will never face the legislator's punishment.
D: Summary: Three forces compel the proper care of orphans: the gods, the watchful souls of the dead, and the moral authority of living elders. Guardians who care well for orphans serve their own interests and their children's, while those who neglect this sacred trust face divine and legal punishment.
