D: Distilled: The gods, the souls of the dead, and the moral authority of the living elderly all compel the faithful care of orphans as the most sacred of trusts.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger constructs a three-tiered moral framework for orphan protection. -- The three-level structure (gods, dead, living elders) creates overlapping systems of moral enforcement, a characteristic strategy in the Laws. -- The idea that the souls of the dead are 'friendly to those who honour, and unfriendly to those who dishonour them' reflects Greek hero-cult beliefs, where the dead could both protect and harm the living. -- The observation that the elderly 'are quick to see and hear all that relates to them' draws on the Greek cultural role of elders as moral witnesses and community conscience. -- The pragmatic argument that caring for orphans serves one's own interest connects to Republic I (352d-354a), where Socrates argues that justice benefits the just person. -- The phrase 'greatest and most sacred of trusts' (megiste kai hagiōtate parakatatheke) uses the language of sacred deposits, connecting orphan care to the property-deposit laws earlier in Book 11. -- Compare Deuteronomy 10:18 and James 1:27 for parallel religious injunctions to protect orphans.

D: Original: But he who is disobedient, and wrongs any one who is bereft of father or mother, shall pay twice the penalty which he would have paid if he had wronged one whose parents had been alive. As touching other legislation concerning guardians in their relation to orphans, or concerning magistrates and their superintendence of the guardians, if they did not possess examples of the manner in which children of freemen should be brought up in the bringing up of their own children, and of the care of their property in the care of their own, or if they had not just laws fairly stated about these very things-there would have been reason in making laws for them, under the idea that they were a peculiar-class, and we might distinguish and make separate rules for the life of those who are orphans and of those who are not orphans. But as the case stands, the condition of orphans with us not different from the case of those who have father, though in regard to honour and dishonour, and the attention given to them, the two are not usually placed upon a level. Wherefore, touching the legislation about orphans, the law speaks in serious accents, both of persuasion and threatening, and such a threat as the following will be by no means out of place:-He who is the guardian of an orphan of either sex, and he among the guardians of the law to whom the superintendence of this guardian has been assigned, shall love the unfortunate orphan as though he were his own child, and he shall be as careful and diligent in the management of his possessions as he would be if they were his own, or even more careful and dilligent. Let every one who has the care of an orphan observe this law.
D: Narrative:
"But he who is disobedient, and wrongs any one who is bereft of father or mother, shall pay twice the penalty which he would have paid if he had wronged one whose parents had been alive.

"As touching other legislation concerning guardians in their relation to orphans, or concerning magistrates and their superintendence of the guardians, if they did not possess examples of the manner in which children of freemen should be brought up in the bringing up of their own children, and of the care of their property in the care of their own, or if they had not just laws fairly stated about these very things -- there would have been reason in making laws for them, under the idea that they were a peculiar class, and we might distinguish and make separate rules for the life of those who are orphans and of those who are not orphans.

"But as the case stands, the condition of orphans with us is not different from the case of those who have a father, though in regard to honour and dishonour, and the attention given to them, the two are not usually placed upon a level. Wherefore, touching the legislation about orphans, the law speaks in serious accents, both of persuasion and threatening, and such a threat as the following will be by no means out of place: He who is the guardian of an orphan of either sex, and he among the guardians of the law to whom the superintendence of this guardian has been assigned, shall love the unfortunate orphan as though he were his own child, and he shall be as careful and diligent in the management of his possessions as he would be if they were his own, or even more careful and diligent. Let every one who has the care of an orphan observe this law."
D: Refashioned:
"Anyone who wrongs a person who has lost father or mother shall pay double the penalty he would have incurred for the same offense against someone whose parents were alive.

"As for further legislation on guardian-orphan relations and magistrate oversight of guardians: if guardians did not already have the example of raising their own freeborn children and managing their own property, there might be reason to create a separate body of law treating orphans as a special class with distinct rules. But as things stand in our state, the situation of orphans does not fundamentally differ from that of children with living parents -- except that orphans rarely receive the same degree of honor and attention in practice.

"Therefore the law on orphan care speaks with the utmost seriousness, combining persuasion with threat. This threat is entirely appropriate: every guardian of an orphan, whether male or female, and every guardian of the law assigned to supervise that guardian, shall love the orphan as though the child were his own. He shall manage the orphan's possessions with the same care and diligence he would apply to his own -- or even greater care and diligence. Let everyone responsible for an orphan observe this law."
D: Elegant: Wrongs against orphans carry double the normal penalty. Separate laws for orphans are unnecessary, since their condition does not fundamentally differ from that of other children -- though in practice they rarely receive equal honor and attention. What orphans need is not different laws but the same laws enforced with greater vigilance and seriousness. The law therefore commands: every guardian of an orphan, and every guardian of the law assigned to supervise that guardian, shall love the orphan as his own child and manage the orphan's property with equal or greater care and diligence than his own. The law speaks in the most serious accents, combining persuasion with threat, on this point.
D: Summary: Wrongs against orphans incur double penalties. Since orphans' legal situation does not fundamentally differ from other children's, no separate body of law is needed -- only stricter enforcement. Guardians must love orphans as their own children and manage their property with equal or greater diligence.
D: Distilled: Orphans deserve double protection under existing laws: double penalties for those who wrong them, and guardians who must love and care for them as their own children.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger states the enforceable orphan-protection law. -- The double-penalty provision for wrongs against orphans creates a powerful economic deterrent against exploitation of the vulnerable. -- The insight that orphans need not a separate legal code but better enforcement of existing norms is remarkably modern, anticipating contemporary debates about whether vulnerable populations need special legislation or simply better application of universal protections. -- The command to 'love the orphan as though he were his own child' imposes an emotional as well as a legal obligation, characteristic of the Laws' attempt to legislate not just behavior but attitudes. -- The distinction between guardians of the orphan and guardians of the law who supervise them creates a two-tier oversight system preventing abuse. -- Compare Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics VIII.12 (1161b-1162a), on the natural affection parents owe children and the corresponding obligations of care.

D: Original: But any one who acts contrary to the law on these matters, if he be a guardian of the child, may be fined by a magistrate, or, if he be himself a magistrate, the guardian may bring him before the court of select judges, and punish him, if convicted, by exacting a fine of double the amount of that inflicted by the court. And if a guardian appears to the relations of the orphan, or to any other citizen, to act negligently or dishonestly, let them bring him before the same court, and whatever damages are given against him, let him pay fourfold, and let half belong to the orphan and half to him who procured the conviction. If any orphan arrives at years of discretion, and thinks that he has been ill-used by his guardians, let him within five years of the expiration of the guardianship be allowed to bring them to trial; and if any of them be convicted, the court shall determine what he shall pay or suffer. And if magistrate shall appear to have wronged the orphan by neglect, and he be convicted, let the court determine what he shall suffer or pay to the orphan, and if there be dishonesty in addition to neglect, besides paying the fine, let him be deposed from his office of guardian of the law, and let the state appoint another guardian of the law for the city and for the country in his room.
D: Narrative:
"But any one who acts contrary to the law on these matters, if he be a guardian of the child, may be fined by a magistrate, or, if he be himself a magistrate, the guardian may bring him before the court of select judges, and punish him, if convicted, by exacting a fine of double the amount of that inflicted by the court.

"And if a guardian appears to the relations of the orphan, or to any other citizen, to act negligently or dishonestly, let them bring him before the same court, and whatever damages are given against him, let him pay fourfold, and let half belong to the orphan and half to him who procured the conviction.

"If any orphan arrives at years of discretion, and thinks that he has been ill-used by his guardians, let him within five years of the expiration of the guardianship be allowed to bring them to trial; and if any of them be convicted, the court shall determine what he shall pay or suffer.

"And if a magistrate shall appear to have wronged the orphan by neglect, and he be convicted, let the court determine what he shall suffer or pay to the orphan, and if there be dishonesty in addition to neglect, besides paying the fine, let him be deposed from his office of guardian of the law, and let the state appoint another guardian of the law for the city and for the country in his room."
D: Refashioned:
"If a guardian of a child violates this law, a magistrate may fine him. If the offending party is himself a magistrate, the guardian may bring him before the court of select judges; if convicted, the magistrate must pay double the court's assessed fine.

"If a guardian appears to the orphan's relatives or to any other citizen to be acting negligently or dishonestly, they may bring him before the same court. If damages are awarded, the guardian must pay fourfold: half goes to the orphan and half to the person who brought the successful prosecution.

"When an orphan reaches the age of discretion, he shall have five years from the end of his guardianship to bring his former guardians to trial. If any guardian is convicted, the court shall determine the penalty or compensation.

"If a magistrate is found to have wronged an orphan through neglect, the court shall determine his penalty. If dishonesty is proven in addition to neglect, he must pay the fine and be removed from his position as guardian of the law. The state shall then appoint a replacement for both city and country."
D: Elegant: Guardians who violate orphan-protection laws face fines; magistrates face double fines. Negligent or dishonest guardians pay fourfold damages, split between the orphan and the prosecutor. Adult orphans have five years after guardianship ends to sue. Magistrates convicted of dishonesty in orphan oversight are removed from office and replaced.
D: Summary: The law creates an escalating enforcement system for orphan protection: guardians face fines, negligent ones pay fourfold damages, adult orphans can sue within five years, and dishonest magistrates are removed from office.
D: Distilled: Orphan-protection enforcement escalates from fines to fourfold damages to removal from office, with adult orphans retaining the right to sue for five years after guardianship ends.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger details the enforcement mechanisms for orphan protection. -- The fourfold damages provision is among the harshest in the Laws, reflecting the seriousness of orphan exploitation. The split between orphan and prosecutor (half each) creates a bounty-hunter incentive for citizen enforcement. -- The five-year limitation period for orphans to sue after reaching adulthood is an early statute of limitations, balancing the need for accountability with practical concerns about stale evidence. -- The removal of dishonest magistrates from office represents the ultimate institutional sanction, treating corruption in orphan care as an offense against the entire governmental structure. -- The distinction between 'neglect' (ameleia) and 'dishonesty' (kakourgia) creates graduated penalties proportional to the moral gravity of the offense. -- Compare Athenian law, where the archon had specific jurisdiction over orphan affairs and guardians could be prosecuted through a public action (graphe) for mismanagement of orphan estates (Demosthenes, Against Aphobus).

D: Original: Greater differences than there ought to be sometimes arise between fathers and sons, on the part either of fathers who will be of opinion that the legislator should enact that they may, if they wish, lawfully renounce their son by the proclamation of a herald in the face of the world, or of sons who think that they should be allowed to indict their fathers on the charge of imbecility when they are disabled by disease or old age. These things only happen, as a matter of fact, where the natures of men are utterly bad; for where only half is bad, as, for example, if the father be not bad, but the son be bad, or conversely, no great calamity is the result of such an amount of hatred as this. In another state, a son disowned by his father would not of necessity cease to be a citizen, but in our state, of which these are to be the laws, the disinherited must necessarily emigrate into another country, for no addition can be made even of a single family to the 5040 households; and, therefore, he who deserves to suffer these things must be renounced not only by his father, who is a single person, but by the whole family, and what is done in these cases must be regulated by some such law as the following:-He who in the sad disorder of his soul has a mind, justly or unjustly, to expel from his family a son whom he has begotten and brought up, shall not lightly or at once execute his purpose; but first of all he shall collect together his own kinsmen extending to cousins, and in like manner his son's kinsmen by the mother's side, and in their presence he shall accuse his son, setting forth that he deserves at the hands of them all to be dismissed from the family; and the son shall be allowed to address them in a similar manner, and show that he does not deserve to suffer any of these things.
D: Narrative:
"Greater differences than there ought to be sometimes arise between fathers and sons, on the part either of fathers who will be of opinion that the legislator should enact that they may, if they wish, lawfully renounce their son by the proclamation of a herald in the face of the world, or of sons who think that they should be allowed to indict their fathers on the charge of imbecility when they are disabled by disease or old age.

"These things only happen, as a matter of fact, where the natures of men are utterly bad; for where only half is bad, as, for example, if the father be not bad, but the son be bad, or conversely, no great calamity is the result of such an amount of hatred as this. In another state, a son disowned by his father would not of necessity cease to be a citizen, but in our state, of which these are to be the laws, the disinherited must necessarily emigrate into another country, for no addition can be made even of a single family to the 5040 households; and, therefore, he who deserves to suffer these things must be renounced not only by his father, who is a single person, but by the whole family, and what is done in these cases must be regulated by some such law as the following:

"He who in the sad disorder of his soul has a mind, justly or unjustly, to expel from his family a son whom he has begotten and brought up, shall not lightly or at once execute his purpose; but first of all he shall collect together his own kinsmen extending to cousins, and in like manner his son's kinsmen by the mother's side, and in their presence he shall accuse his son, setting forth that he deserves at the hands of them all to be dismissed from the family; and the son shall be allowed to address them in a similar manner, and show that he does not deserve to suffer any of these things."
D: Refashioned:
"Sometimes the conflicts between fathers and sons exceed what they should. Fathers may want the legislator to allow them to publicly renounce their sons by herald's proclamation. Sons may want permission to indict their fathers for incompetence when disease or old age has impaired them.

"These extreme conflicts only arise where people's natures are thoroughly bad. When only one party is at fault -- the father good but the son bad, or vice versa -- the resulting hostility does not lead to catastrophe. But in our state, disownment has uniquely severe consequences. In other cities, a disowned son might still remain a citizen. In ours, the disinherited must emigrate, because not a single family can be added to the fixed 5,040 households. Therefore, a son who deserves disownment must be renounced not merely by his father alone but by the entire family. The process requires a law like this:

"A father who, in the troubled disorder of his soul, intends -- whether justly or unjustly -- to expel from the family a son he has begotten and raised must not act hastily or impulsively. First he must assemble all his own kinsmen out to cousins, and likewise his son's kinsmen on the mother's side. Before this assembly he must formally accuse his son, arguing that the son deserves to be expelled from the family. The son shall have equal opportunity to address the assembly and demonstrate that he does not deserve such treatment."
D: Elegant: Sometimes greater differences than there ought to be arise between fathers and sons -- fathers wanting to renounce sons by public herald's proclamation, sons wanting to indict fathers for incompetence due to age or illness. These extremes arise only where both natures are utterly bad. But in Magnesia, disownment has uniquely severe consequences: since not a single family can be added to the fixed 5,040 households, the disinherited must emigrate entirely. Therefore, disownment requires not just a father's decision but the whole family's judgment. The father must assemble all kinsmen from both sides -- his own out to cousins and his son's maternal kin -- formally accuse his son before them, and give the son equal opportunity to defend himself and show he does not deserve such treatment.
D: Summary: Because disownment in Magnesia means emigration from the state entirely, the process requires a family tribunal rather than a father's unilateral decision. The father must formally accuse his son before an assembly of kinsmen from both sides, and the son has an equal right of defense.
D: Distilled: Disownment requires a family tribunal because in Magnesia's fixed 5,040-household system, disinheritance means exile from the state.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses family conflicts and disownment. -- The practice of public renunciation by herald (apokeryxis) was a recognized, if controversial, procedure in some Greek states. Athenian law allowed fathers to disown sons, though the details are debated among historians. -- The connection between the fixed 5,040 households and the severity of disownment is a powerful example of how Magnesia's institutional design creates cascading consequences. -- The requirement for family consensus rather than paternal unilateral action is a significant check on patriarchal authority, unusual in Greek legal thought. -- The phrase 'sad disorder of his soul' acknowledges that the desire to disown may itself be pathological, not a clear-eyed moral judgment. -- The equal right of defense for the son reflects Plato's commitment to procedural fairness even within family disputes. -- Compare Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics VIII.12 (1162a), where he discusses whether a son can ever rightly disown his father, and concludes that the parent-child bond creates an asymmetric obligation.

D: Original: And if the father persuades them, and obtains the suffrages of more than half of his kindred, exclusive of the father and mother and the offender himself-I say, if he obtains more than half the suffrages of all the other grown-up members of the family, of both sexes, the father shall be permitted to put away his son, but not otherwise. And if any other citizen is willing to adopt the son who is put away, no law shall hinder him; for the characters of young men are subject to many changes in the course of their lives. And if he has been put away, and in a period of ten years no one is willing to adopt him, let those who have the care of the superabundant population which is sent out into colonies, see to him, in order that he may be suitably provided for in the colony. And if disease or age or harshness of temper, or all these together, makes a man to be more out of his mind than the rest of the world are-but this is not observable, except to those who live with him-and he, being master of his property, is the ruin of the house, and his son doubts and hesitates about indicting his father for insanity, let the law in that case or, that he shall first of all go to the eldest guardians of the law and tell them of his father's misfortune, and they shall duly look into the matter, and take counsel as to whether he shall indict him or not.
D: Narrative:
"And if the father persuades them, and obtains the suffrages of more than half of his kindred, exclusive of the father and mother and the offender himself -- I say, if he obtains more than half the suffrages of all the other grown-up members of the family, of both sexes, the father shall be permitted to put away his son, but not otherwise.

"And if any other citizen is willing to adopt the son who is put away, no law shall hinder him; for the characters of young men are subject to many changes in the course of their lives. And if he has been put away, and in a period of ten years no one is willing to adopt him, let those who have the care of the superabundant population which is sent out into colonies, see to him, in order that he may be suitably provided for in the colony.

"And if disease or age or harshness of temper, or all these together, makes a man to be more out of his mind than the rest of the world are -- but this is not observable, except to those who live with him -- and he, being master of his property, is the ruin of the house, and his son doubts and hesitates about indicting his father for insanity, let the law in that case order that he shall first of all go to the eldest guardians of the law and tell them of his father's misfortune, and they shall duly look into the matter, and take counsel as to whether he shall indict him or not."
D: Refashioned:
"If the father convinces the assembly and wins the votes of more than half his kindred -- excluding the father himself, the mother, and the accused son, and counting all other adult family members of both sexes -- then he shall be permitted to disown his son. Not otherwise.

"If another citizen is willing to adopt the disowned son, no law shall prevent it -- for the characters of young people undergo many changes throughout their lives. But if ten years pass and no one is willing to adopt him, those who manage the surplus population sent to colonies shall see to it that he is properly settled in a colony.

"Now consider the reverse situation. If disease, old age, harshness of temper, or all three together make a father more deranged than ordinary people -- though this may only be apparent to those who live with him -- and he is master of his property yet ruining the household, and his son is uncertain about indicting him for insanity: the law directs that the son shall first go to the eldest guardians of the law and describe his father's condition. They shall investigate the matter properly and advise whether or not he should bring the indictment."
D: Elegant: Disownment requires a majority vote of adult family members (excluding parents and the accused). The disowned son may be adopted by any citizen -- young characters change greatly -- but after ten years without adoption, he is settled in a colony. For the reverse case: if a father's mental decline is ruining the household, the son must first consult the eldest guardians of the law, who investigate and advise whether to indict for insanity.
D: Summary: Disownment requires a supermajority family vote; the disowned son can be adopted or eventually settled in a colony. When a father's mental decline threatens the household, the son must first consult the eldest guardians of the law before pursuing an insanity indictment.
D: Distilled: Disownment needs family majority approval with adoption as a safety valve, while parental insanity proceedings require prior consultation with the guardians of the law.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the disownment procedure and introduces parental incapacity. -- The exclusion of parents and the accused from voting ensures that the decision rests with disinterested family members -- an early form of conflict-of-interest recusal. -- The inclusion of women in the family vote is notable for ancient Greek law, which generally excluded women from legal proceedings. -- The optimistic note about young men's characters changing echoes Republic X (617e), where souls choose their next lives -- suggesting that character is not fixed. -- The ten-year waiting period before colonial settlement provides ample time for rehabilitation and adoption. -- The parental insanity provision creates an institutional pathway that protects both the son (who needs guidance on such a drastic step) and the father (who receives investigation rather than summary judgment). -- The observation that insanity 'is not observable, except to those who live with him' is psychologically acute, recognizing that mental decline can be invisible in public while devastating in private. -- Compare Roman law's procedures for interdictio, which similarly placed mentally incapacitated persons under the supervision of relatives.

D: Original: And if they advise him to proceed, they shall be both his witnesses and his advocates; and if the father is cast, he shall henceforth be incapable of ordering the least particular of his life; let him be as a child dwelling in the house for the remainder of his days. And if a man and his wife have an unfortunate incompatibility of temper, ten of the guardians of the law, who are impartial, and ten of the women who regulate marriages, shall look to the matter, and if they are able to reconcile them they shall be formally reconciled; but if their souls are too much tossed with passion, they shall endeavour to find other partners. Now they are not likely to have very gentle tempers; and, therefore, we must endeavour to associate with them deeper and softer natures. Those who have no children, or only a few, at the time of their separation, should choose their new partners with a view to the procreation of children; but those who have a sufficient number of children should separate and marry again in order that they may have some one to grow old with and that the pair may take care of one another in age. If a woman dies, leaving children, male or female, the law will advise rather than compel the husband to bring up the children without introducing into the house a stepmother.
D: Narrative:
"And if they advise him to proceed, they shall be both his witnesses and his advocates; and if the father is cast, he shall henceforth be incapable of ordering the least particular of his life; let him be as a child dwelling in the house for the remainder of his days.

"And if a man and his wife have an unfortunate incompatibility of temper, ten of the guardians of the law, who are impartial, and ten of the women who regulate marriages, shall look to the matter, and if they are able to reconcile them they shall be formally reconciled; but if their souls are too much tossed with passion, they shall endeavour to find other partners. Now they are not likely to have very gentle tempers; and, therefore, we must endeavour to associate with them deeper and softer natures.

"Those who have no children, or only a few, at the time of their separation, should choose their new partners with a view to the procreation of children; but those who have a sufficient number of children should separate and marry again in order that they may have some one to grow old with and that the pair may take care of one another in age.

"If a woman dies, leaving children, male or female, the law will advise rather than compel the husband to bring up the children without introducing into the house a stepmother."
D: Refashioned:
"If the guardians advise the son to proceed with the indictment, they shall serve as both his witnesses and his advocates. If the father is found incapacitated, he shall henceforth be unable to control any aspect of his own life. He shall live as a child in the household for the rest of his days.

"When a husband and wife are incompatible in temperament, ten impartial guardians of the law and ten of the women who oversee marriages shall examine the situation. If they can reconcile the couple, the reconciliation shall be formalized. But if the couple's passions are too turbulent for reconciliation, the panel shall try to find them new partners. Since people in such situations tend not to have gentle temperaments, the aim should be to pair them with deeper and calmer natures.

"Couples who separate with no children or few children should choose new partners with a view to having children. Those who already have enough children should remarry for companionship in old age, so that each partner has someone to care for them.

"If a woman dies and leaves children behind, the law will advise -- but not compel -- the husband to raise the children without bringing a stepmother into the household."
D: Elegant: A father found incapacitated by the court loses all control of his affairs and lives as a child for the rest of his days. Incompatible couples are first counseled by a panel of guardians and marriage-overseeing women; if irreconcilable, they are paired with calmer new partners. Childless separating couples should remarry for procreation; those with enough children remarry for companionship in old age. The law advises widowers against introducing stepmothers.
D: Summary: Incapacitated fathers lose all autonomy. Marital incompatibility is handled by a panel that attempts reconciliation first, then facilitates remarriage with temperamentally suitable new partners. The purposes of remarriage differ based on whether the couple needs children or companionship. Widowers are advised against stepmothers.
D: Distilled: Incapacitated fathers become legal wards, irreconcilable couples are re-paired with calmer temperaments, and remarriage after loss aims at either procreation or companionship depending on the family's needs.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger covers incapacity, divorce, and remarriage. -- The provision reducing an incapacitated father to the status of a child in his own household is a form of legal guardianship (what Roman law called cura furiosi) -- severe but protective. -- The divorce provision is remarkable for several reasons: it involves both male guardians of the law and female marriage supervisors, giving women institutional authority in family matters. -- The principle of pairing difficult temperaments with 'deeper and softer natures' shows psychological insight about complementary temperaments in marriage. -- The distinction between remarriage for procreation and remarriage for companionship recognizes that marriage serves different functions at different life stages -- a nuanced view. -- The gentle recommendation against stepmothers acknowledges the well-known difficulties of blended families in antiquity, reflected in Greek mythology (e.g., Phaedra and Hippolytus). -- Compare Laws VI (772d-774a) on marriage regulations. -- Athenian divorce was relatively straightforward: either party could initiate it, though the details differed for men and women.

D: Original: But if he have no children, then he shall be compelled to marry until he has begotten a sufficient number of sons to his family and to the state. And if a man dies leaving a sufficient number of children, the mother of his children shall remain with them and bring, them up. But if she appears to be too young to live virtuously without a husband, let her relations communicate with the women who superintend marriage, and let both together do what they think best in these matters; if there is a lack of children, let the choice be made with a view to having them; two children, one of either sex, shall be deemed sufficient in the eye of the law. When a child is admitted to be the offspring of certain parents and is acknowledged by them, but there is need of a decision as to which parent the child is to follow-in case a female slave have intercourse with a male slave, or with a freeman or freedman, the offspring shall always belong to the master of the female slave. Again, if a free woman have intercourse with a male slave, the offspring shall belong to the master of the slave; but if a child be born either of a slave by her master, or of his mistress by a slave-and this be provence offspring of the woman and its father shall be sent away by the women who superintend marriage into another country, and the guardians of the law shall send away the offspring of the man and its mother.
D: Narrative:
"But if he have no children, then he shall be compelled to marry until he has begotten a sufficient number of sons to his family and to the state. And if a man dies leaving a sufficient number of children, the mother of his children shall remain with them and bring them up. But if she appears to be too young to live virtuously without a husband, let her relations communicate with the women who superintend marriage, and let both together do what they think best in these matters; if there is a lack of children, let the choice be made with a view to having them; two children, one of either sex, shall be deemed sufficient in the eye of the law.

"When a child is admitted to be the offspring of certain parents and is acknowledged by them, but there is need of a decision as to which parent the child is to follow -- in case a female slave have intercourse with a male slave, or with a freeman or freedman, the offspring shall always belong to the master of the female slave. Again, if a free woman have intercourse with a male slave, the offspring shall belong to the master of the slave; but if a child be born either of a slave by her master, or of his mistress by a slave -- and this be proven -- the offspring of the woman and its father shall be sent away by the women who superintend marriage into another country, and the guardians of the law shall send away the offspring of the man and its mother."
D: Refashioned:
"A childless widower must be compelled to remarry until he has fathered enough sons for both his family and the state. If a man dies leaving sufficient children, their mother shall remain with them and raise them. But if she appears too young to live virtuously without a husband, her relatives shall consult with the women who oversee marriages, and together they shall determine what is best. If more children are needed, the remarriage should aim at producing them. The law considers two children sufficient -- one of each sex.

"When the parentage of a child is acknowledged but a decision is needed about which parent the child follows: if a female slave has a child by a male slave, a freeman, or a freedman, the offspring always belongs to the master of the female slave. If a free woman has a child by a male slave, the offspring belongs to the slave's master. But if a child is born of a slave woman by her own master, or of a mistress by her slave -- and this is proven -- the women who oversee marriages shall send the woman's child and its father out of the country, and the guardians of the law shall send the man's child and its mother away."
D: Elegant: Childless widowers must remarry until producing sufficient heirs; two children, one of each sex, satisfies the law. A young widow may remarry at the joint recommendation of her relatives and the marriage supervisors. Children of slaves belong to the master of the mother. But children born of sexual relations between masters and their own slaves -- in either direction -- must be sent out of the country along with the non-citizen parent.
D: Summary: Childless widowers must remarry to produce heirs, and young widows may be encouraged to do the same. Children of slaves follow the mother's master. However, children born of master-slave relationships within the same household must be expatriated with their non-citizen parent.
D: Distilled: Childless widowers must remarry, children of slaves belong to the mother's master, and offspring of master-slave unions within the same household are exiled.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses remarriage obligations and the status of children born to slaves. -- The compulsion to remarry for childless widowers reflects the Laws' overriding concern with maintaining the 5,040 household units and their population. -- The two-child standard (one of each sex) appears in several Greek discussions of ideal family size. -- The rule that children follow the status of the mother's master (partus sequitur ventrem in later Roman law) was common in ancient slave-holding societies. -- The exile of children born from master-slave sexual relations within the same household is a unique and harsh provision, designed to prevent the moral corruption of the household and the blurring of status boundaries. -- The involvement of 'women who superintend marriage' in these decisions continues the Laws' pattern of granting women institutional authority in family matters. -- Compare Aristotle, Politics I.5-6, on the natural-slave theory, and the Athenian practice whereby children of citizens and slaves had varying legal statuses depending on the specific circumstances.

D: Original:
Neither God, nor a man who has understanding, will ever advise any one to neglect his parents. To a discourse concerning the honour and dishonour of parents, a prelude such as the following, about the service of the Gods, will be a suitable introduction:-There are ancient customs about the Gods which are universal, and they are of two kinds: some of the Gods we see with our eyes and we honour them, of others we honour the images, raising statues of them which we adore; and though they are lifeless, yet we imagine that the living Gods have a good will and gratitude to us on this account. Now, if a man has a father or mother, or their fathers or mothers treasured up in his house stricken in years, let him consider that no statue can be more potent to grant his requests than they are, who are sitting at his hearth if only he knows how to show true service to them.

And what do you call the true mode of service?

I will tell you, O my friend, for such things are worth listening to.

Proceed.
D: Narrative:
"Neither God, nor a man who has understanding, will ever advise any one to neglect his parents. To a discourse concerning the honour and dishonour of parents, a prelude such as the following, about the service of the Gods, will be a suitable introduction: There are ancient customs about the Gods which are universal, and they are of two kinds: some of the Gods we see with our eyes and we honour them, of others we honour the images, raising statues of them which we adore; and though they are lifeless, yet we imagine that the living Gods have a good will and gratitude to us on this account.

"Now, if a man has a father or mother, or their fathers or mothers treasured up in his house stricken in years, let him consider that no statue can be more potent to grant his requests than they are, who are sitting at his hearth, if only he knows how to show true service to them."

Cleinias: "And what do you call the true mode of service?"

"I will tell you, O my friend, for such things are worth listening to."

Cleinias: "Proceed."
D: Refashioned:
"Neither God nor any person of understanding would ever advise anyone to neglect their parents. As an introduction to the topic of honoring and dishonoring parents, a prelude about the service of the gods is appropriate.

"There are ancient and universal customs regarding the gods, and they come in two kinds. Some of the gods we see directly and honor them. Others we honor through images -- we raise statues to them and worship these; though the statues are lifeless, we believe the living gods look upon this with good will and gratitude.

"Now consider: if a man has a father or mother, or their parents, living in his house and stricken with age, he should realize that no statue can be more powerful in granting his requests than these living elders sitting at his own hearth -- provided he knows how to serve them properly."

"And what do you call the true mode of service?" asked Cleinias.

"I will tell you, my friend, for such things are worth hearing."

"Go on," said Cleinias.
D: Elegant: No god or sensible person would advise neglecting parents. We honor the gods in two ways: by worshipping them directly or through lifeless statues. But aged parents sitting at one's own hearth are more powerful than any statue -- living shrines whose prayers and blessings can be won through proper service.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger introduces the topic of filial duty by comparing aged parents to divine images: no statue is more potent than a living parent sitting at the family hearth, provided one knows how to serve them properly.
D: Distilled: Aged parents at the hearth are more powerful intercessors than any divine statue, if only one knows how to honor them properly.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias responding. -- The comparison of aged parents to divine statues is one of the most striking images in the Laws, elevating filial piety to the level of religious worship. -- The two forms of divine worship (direct observation of celestial gods and image-worship of others) reflects actual Greek religious practice: the visible gods include the sun, moon, and stars (cf. Laws X, 886d-887c), while image-worship involved temple statues and household shrines. -- The concept of the hearth (hestia) as a sacred center of the household was fundamental to Greek religion; Hestia was the goddess of the hearth. -- This passage anticipates the Confucian emphasis on filial piety (xiao) as the foundation of social virtue, though developed independently. -- Compare Republic I (331a-b), where Cephalus speaks of the comfort his wealth gives him regarding debts to both gods and men. -- The Athenian Stranger's promise that 'such things are worth listening to' signals the importance of the passage that follows.

D: Original:
Oedipus, as tradition says, when dishonoured by his sons, invoked on them curses which every one declares to have been heard and ratified by the Gods, and Amyntor in his wrath invoked curses on his son Phoenix, and Theseus upon Hippolytus, and innumerable others have also called down wrath upon their children, whence it is clear that the Gods listen to the imprecations of parents; for the curses of parents are, as they ought to be, mighty against their children as no others are. And shall we suppose that the prayers of a father or mother who is specially dishonoured by his or her children, are heard by the Gods in accordance with nature; and that if a parent is honoured by them, and in the gladness of his heart earnestly entreats the Gods in his prayers to do them good, he is not equally heard, and that they do not minister to his request? If not, they would be very unjust ministers of good, and that we affirm to be contrary to their nature.

Certainly.
D: Narrative:
"Oedipus, as tradition says, when dishonoured by his sons, invoked on them curses which every one declares to have been heard and ratified by the Gods, and Amyntor in his wrath invoked curses on his son Phoenix, and Theseus upon Hippolytus, and innumerable others have also called down wrath upon their children, whence it is clear that the Gods listen to the imprecations of parents; for the curses of parents are, as they ought to be, mighty against their children as no others are.

"And shall we suppose that the prayers of a father or mother who is specially dishonoured by his or her children, are heard by the Gods in accordance with nature; and that if a parent is honoured by them, and in the gladness of his heart earnestly entreats the Gods in his prayers to do them good, he is not equally heard, and that they do not minister to his request? If not, they would be very unjust ministers of good, and that we affirm to be contrary to their nature."

Cleinias: "Certainly."
D: Refashioned:
"Oedipus, as tradition tells us, cursed his sons when they dishonored him, and everyone agrees the gods heard and fulfilled those curses. Amyntor in his rage cursed his son Phoenix, and Theseus cursed Hippolytus, and countless others have called down divine wrath upon their children. From all this it is clear that the gods listen to the prayers of parents -- for parental curses are, as they rightly should be, more powerful against children than any other kind.

"If the gods hear the curses of dishonored parents in accordance with nature, must we not also suppose that when a parent is honored by their children and joyfully prays to the gods for their welfare, those prayers are equally heard? If the gods responded to curses but ignored blessings, they would be unjust dispensers of good -- and that, we maintain, is contrary to their nature."

"Certainly," said Cleinias.
D: Elegant: Mythology proves that gods hear parental curses: Oedipus cursed his sons, Amyntor cursed Phoenix, Theseus cursed Hippolytus. If the gods answer curses against dishonorable children, they must equally answer blessings for honorable ones -- otherwise they would be unjust, which is contrary to their nature.
D: Summary: The examples of Oedipus, Amyntor, and Theseus demonstrate that the gods hear parental curses. By the same logic, the gods must also hear parental blessings for dutiful children, since failing to do so would make them unjust.
D: Distilled: If the gods hear parental curses against disobedient children, justice requires they equally hear parental blessings for dutiful ones.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias affirming. -- Three mythological examples illustrate parental curses: Oedipus cursed his sons Eteocles and Polynices (Aeschylus, Seven Against Thebes; Sophocles, Oedipus at Colonus); Amyntor cursed Phoenix for sleeping with his concubine (Homer, Iliad IX.447-461); Theseus cursed Hippolytus after Phaedra's false accusation (Euripides, Hippolytus). -- The argument from divine justice -- that gods cannot selectively hear curses while ignoring blessings -- is a compact theological argument drawing on the principle established in Laws X that the gods are just by nature. -- Compare Republic II (364b-365a), where Adeimantus describes popular beliefs about gods being swayed by prayers and sacrifices. Here in the Laws, Plato uses the same popular beliefs constructively rather than critically. -- The emotional power of parental blessings and curses was deeply felt in Greek culture, where the dying words of parents were considered especially potent.

D: Original:
May we not think, as I was saying just now, that we can possess no image which is more honoured by the Gods, than that of a father or grandfather, or of a mother stricken in years? whom when a man honours, the heart of the God rejoices, and he is ready to answer their prayers. And, truly, the figure of an ancestor is a wonderful thing, far higher than that of a lifeless image. For the living, when they are honoured by us, join in our prayers, and when they are dishonoured, they utter imprecations against us; but lifeless objects do neither. And therefore, if a man makes a right use of his father and grandfather and other aged relations, he will have images which above all others will win him the favour of the Gods.

Excellent.
D: Narrative:
"May we not think, as I was saying just now, that we can possess no image which is more honoured by the Gods, than that of a father or grandfather, or of a mother stricken in years? Whom when a man honours, the heart of the God rejoices, and he is ready to answer their prayers.

"And, truly, the figure of an ancestor is a wonderful thing, far higher than that of a lifeless image. For the living, when they are honoured by us, join in our prayers, and when they are dishonoured, they utter imprecations against us; but lifeless objects do neither.

"And therefore, if a man makes a right use of his father and grandfather and other aged relations, he will have images which above all others will win him the favour of the Gods."

Cleinias: "Excellent."
D: Refashioned:
"As I was saying, we can possess no image more honored by the gods than a father, a grandfather, or a mother in her old age. When a man honors them, the heart of the god rejoices and he is ready to answer their prayers.

"A living ancestor is truly a wonderful thing -- far superior to any lifeless statue. The living, when we honor them, join their prayers to ours; when we dishonor them, they utter curses against us. Lifeless objects do neither.

"Therefore, if a man treats his father, grandfather, and other aged relations properly, he will possess the most powerful intercessors imaginable -- living images that, above all others, will win him the favor of the gods."

"Excellent," said Cleinias.
D: Elegant: No divine image is more honored by the gods than a living parent or grandparent. Living ancestors surpass lifeless statues because they actively respond: when honored, they join their prayers to ours; when dishonored, they curse us. Proper treatment of aged relatives provides the most powerful intercessors with the gods.
D: Summary: Living parents and grandparents are superior to any divine statue because they actively pray for those who honor them and curse those who dishonor them. Proper care of aged relatives is the most effective form of divine intercession.
D: Distilled: Living parents are superior to lifeless statues as divine intercessors because they actively pray for or against their children depending on how they are treated.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias affirming. -- The argument reaches its conclusion: the living parent is a more efficacious religious object than any carved image because the parent actively participates in the prayer relationship. -- This is a remarkable theological-ethical argument that elevates domestic care of the elderly into the highest form of religious practice. -- The phrase 'the heart of the God rejoices' is unusual in Plato, who typically avoids attributing emotions to the divine. Here in the Laws, he employs popular religious language strategically. -- The comparison between living and lifeless images has philosophical implications for the theory of representation: a living exemplar is always superior to a lifeless copy, connecting to the Republic's critique of mimesis (Book X). -- The practical implication is powerful: the most effective form of prayer is not temple worship but filial devotion. -- Compare the Roman concept of the paterfamilias and the lares (household gods representing ancestors) as parallel cultural expressions of ancestral reverence.

D: Original: Every man of any understanding fears and respects the prayers of parents, knowing well that many times and to many persons they have been accomplished. Now these things being thus ordered by nature, good men think it a blessing from heaven if their parents live to old age and reach the utmost limit of human life, or if taken away before their time they are deeply regretted by them; but to bad men parents are always a cause of terror. Wherefore let every man honour with every sort of lawful honour his own parents, agreeably to what has now been said. But if this prelude be an unmeaning sound in the cars of any one, let the law follow, which may be rightly imposed in these terms:-If any one in this city be not sufficiently careful of his parents, and do not regard and gratify in every respect their wishes more than those of his sons and of his other offspring or of himself-let him who experiences this sort of treatment either come himself, or send some one to inform the three eldest guardians of the law, and three of the women who have the care of marriages; and let them look to the matter and punish youthful evil-doers with stripes and bonds if they are under thirty years of age, that is to say, if they be men, or if they be women, let them undergo the same punishment up to forty years of age.
D: Narrative:
"Every man of any understanding fears and respects the prayers of parents, knowing well that many times and to many persons they have been accomplished. Now these things being thus ordered by nature, good men think it a blessing from heaven if their parents live to old age and reach the utmost limit of human life, or if taken away before their time they are deeply regretted by them; but to bad men parents are always a cause of terror.

"Wherefore let every man honour with every sort of lawful honour his own parents, agreeably to what has now been said. But if this prelude be an unmeaning sound in the ears of any one, let the law follow, which may be rightly imposed in these terms: If any one in this city be not sufficiently careful of his parents, and do not regard and gratify in every respect their wishes more than those of his sons and of his other offspring or of himself -- let him who experiences this sort of treatment either come himself, or send some one to inform the three eldest guardians of the law, and three of the women who have the care of marriages; and let them look to the matter and punish youthful evil-doers with stripes and bonds if they are under thirty years of age, that is to say, if they be men, or if they be women, let them undergo the same punishment up to forty years of age."
D: Refashioned:
"Every person of understanding fears and respects the prayers of parents, knowing full well that such prayers have been fulfilled many times for many people. Given how nature has ordered these things, good people consider it a divine blessing when their parents reach extreme old age, and they mourn deeply if their parents die prematurely. But for bad people, parents are always a source of dread.

"Therefore let every person honor their parents with every lawful form of respect, in accordance with what has been said. But if this prelude falls on deaf ears, let the law follow, and it may rightly be stated as follows: if anyone in this city fails to care sufficiently for his parents, and does not prioritize their wishes above those of his children, his other descendants, or even himself -- the neglected parent may either appear in person or send someone to report the situation to the three eldest guardians of the law and three of the women who oversee marriages. They shall investigate and punish young offenders: men under thirty and women under forty shall be subject to flogging and imprisonment."
D: Elegant: Sensible people fear parental prayers, knowing they are often fulfilled. Good people consider long-lived parents a divine blessing; bad people dread them. The law requires that parents' wishes be prioritized above those of children or oneself. Neglected parents may report to the three eldest guardians of the law and three marriage-overseeing women. Offenders under thirty (men) or forty (women) face flogging and imprisonment.
D: Summary: The prelude concludes by noting that wise people fear and respect parental prayers. The law requires parents' wishes to be prioritized above all others; neglected parents may report their mistreatment, and young offenders face corporal punishment.
D: Distilled: Parents' wishes must be honored above all others, and those who neglect their parents face flogging and imprisonment upon complaint to the guardians.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger transitions from prelude to enforceable law. -- The requirement to honor parents' wishes above those of one's own children or oneself is extraordinarily demanding, reversing the typical modern hierarchy of obligations. -- The different age thresholds for men (under 30) and women (under 40) receiving corporal punishment may reflect Greek assumptions about the different rates at which men and women achieve full moral responsibility. -- The joint involvement of male guardians of the law and female marriage overseers continues the pattern of mixed-gender institutional oversight in family matters. -- The phrase 'if this prelude be an unmeaning sound' candidly acknowledges that moral persuasion sometimes fails, necessitating coercive law -- a key structural principle of the Laws' legislative method. -- Compare Exodus 20:12 ('Honor thy father and thy mother'), the Confucian Classic of Filial Piety (Xiaojing), and Solon's laws, which reportedly allowed prosecution for failure to support elderly parents. -- Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics VIII.14 (1163b15-25), discusses the degree to which children owe their parents.

D: Original: But if, when they are still more advanced in years, they continue the same neglect of their parents, and do any hurt to any of them, let them be brought before a court in which every single one of the eldest citizens shall be the judges, and if the offender be convicted, let the court determine what he ought to pay or suffer, and any penalty may be imposed on him which a man can pay or suffer. If the person who has been wronged be unable to inform the magistrates, let any freeman who hears of his case inform, and if he do not, he shall be deemed base, and shall be liable to have a suit for damage brought against him by any one who likes. And if a slave inform, he shall receive freedom; and if he be the slave of the injurer or injured party, he shall be set free by the magistrates, or if he belong to any other citizen, the public shall pay a price on his behalf to the owner; and let the magistrates take heed that no one wrongs him out of revenge, because he has given information.
D: Narrative:
"But if, when they are still more advanced in years, they continue the same neglect of their parents, and do any hurt to any of them, let them be brought before a court in which every single one of the eldest citizens shall be the judges, and if the offender be convicted, let the court determine what he ought to pay or suffer, and any penalty may be imposed on him which a man can pay or suffer.

"If the person who has been wronged be unable to inform the magistrates, let any freeman who hears of his case inform, and if he do not, he shall be deemed base, and shall be liable to have a suit for damage brought against him by any one who likes.

"And if a slave inform, he shall receive freedom; and if he be the slave of the injurer or injured party, he shall be set free by the magistrates, or if he belong to any other citizen, the public shall pay a price on his behalf to the owner; and let the magistrates take heed that no one wrongs him out of revenge, because he has given information."
D: Refashioned:
"If people who are past the age threshold for corporal punishment continue to neglect or harm their parents, they shall be brought before a court composed of the eldest citizens as judges. If convicted, the court may impose any penalty that a person can pay or endure -- there is no limit.

"If the wronged parent is unable to report to the magistrates, any freeman who learns of the situation must report it. If he fails to do so, he shall be considered base and shall be vulnerable to a damages suit brought by anyone who chooses.

"If a slave provides the information, he shall be freed. If the slave belongs to either the offender or the victim, the magistrates shall free him. If the slave belongs to a third party, the state shall pay the owner fair compensation. The magistrates must ensure that no one retaliates against the informant for having provided information."
D: Elegant: Older offenders who harm their parents face a court of the eldest citizens with unlimited sentencing power. If the wronged parent cannot report, any freeman who learns of the abuse must inform the authorities or face a damages suit for moral cowardice. Slave informants are freed at public expense, and the magistrates must protect them from retaliation.
D: Summary: Older offenders who neglect or harm parents face a court of the eldest citizens with unlimited penalty power. Bystanders who fail to report parental abuse are legally liable, and slave informants receive their freedom with protection from retaliation.
D: Distilled: Parental abuse is judged by the eldest citizens with unlimited sentencing power, bystander reporting is mandatory, and slave informants are rewarded with freedom.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the laws on filial obligations. -- The court composed entirely of the eldest citizens is a special tribunal reserved for the gravest offenses -- its composition ensures that the judges have the most personal understanding of aging and parental vulnerability. -- The unlimited sentencing authority ('any penalty which a man can pay or suffer') is the most expansive judicial discretion in the entire Laws, reflecting the extreme gravity of parental abuse. -- The mandatory reporting obligation for freemen, backed by damages liability, creates a system of collective moral responsibility -- every citizen is a guardian of filial duty. -- The slave-informant provision parallels the treasure-trove laws in 11.2, offering freedom as incentive and suggesting a consistent policy of rewarding slaves who serve the public interest. -- The anti-retaliation protection for informants is a sophisticated procedural safeguard. -- Compare Solon's laws requiring adult children to maintain their elderly parents, with penalties for non-compliance (Plutarch, Life of Solon 22). -- The provision that slave informants receive freedom mirrors Athenian legal practice in certain types of public actions.

D: Original: Cases in which one man injures another by poisons, and which prove fatal, have been already discussed; but about other cases in which a person intentionally and of malice harms another with meats, or drinks, or ointments, nothing has as yet been determined. For there are two kinds of poisons used among men, which cannot clearly be distinguished. There is the kind just now explicitly mentioned, which injures bodies by the use of other bodies according to a natural law; there is also another kind which persuades the more daring class that they can do injury by sorceries, and incantations, and magic knots, as they are termed, and makes others believe that they above all persons are injured by the powers of the magician. Now it is not easy to know the nature of all these things; nor if a man do know can he readily persuade others to believe him. And when men are disturbed in their minds at the sight of waxen images fixed either at their doors, or in a place where three ways meet, or on the sepulchres of parents, there is no use in trying to persuade them that they should despise all such things because they have no certain knowledge about them.
D: Narrative:
"Cases in which one man injures another by poisons, and which prove fatal, have been already discussed; but about other cases in which a person intentionally and of malice harms another with meats, or drinks, or ointments, nothing has as yet been determined.

"For there are two kinds of poisons used among men, which cannot clearly be distinguished. There is the kind just now explicitly mentioned, which injures bodies by the use of other bodies according to a natural law; there is also another kind which persuades the more daring class that they can do injury by sorceries, and incantations, and magic knots, as they are termed, and makes others believe that they above all persons are injured by the powers of the magician.

"Now it is not easy to know the nature of all these things; nor if a man do know can he readily persuade others to believe him. And when men are disturbed in their minds at the sight of waxen images fixed either at their doors, or in a place where three ways meet, or on the sepulchres of parents, there is no use in trying to persuade them that they should despise all such things because they have no certain knowledge about them."
D: Refashioned:
"We have already addressed cases where one person fatally poisons another. But we have not yet dealt with cases where a person intentionally and maliciously harms another through food, drink, or ointments in ways that are not fatal.

"There are two kinds of harmful substances used among people, and they are difficult to distinguish clearly. The first is the kind already mentioned: substances that injure bodies through other bodies according to natural physical laws. The second kind is different: it involves sorcery, incantations, and what are called 'magic knots,' which convince the more audacious that they can harm others through supernatural means, and make potential victims believe they are especially vulnerable to a magician's power.

"The true nature of these things is not easy to know, and even someone who does understand cannot easily persuade others. When people see wax figures placed at their doors, at crossroads, or on their parents' graves, and are disturbed by them, it is useless to try persuading them to dismiss such things on the grounds that nobody really knows whether they have any power."
D: Elegant: Two kinds of poisoning must be distinguished: physical poisoning through natural substances, and supposed sorcery through incantations, magic knots, and wax images. The nature of the second kind is genuinely difficult to determine, and people who are frightened by magical practices at their doors, crossroads, or ancestral tombs cannot simply be told to ignore what they do not understand.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger distinguishes two types of harm: physical poisoning by natural substances and supposed harm through sorcery and magic. He acknowledges that the nature of sorcery is genuinely uncertain and that people's fears of magical practices cannot simply be dismissed.
D: Distilled: Physical poisoning and sorcery must be addressed by law separately, since the nature of magical harm is genuinely uncertain and popular fear of it cannot simply be dismissed.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger introduces the laws on poisoning and sorcery. -- The distinction between natural and supernatural poisoning reflects actual Greek cultural anxiety about the boundary between medicine (pharmakon as remedy) and witchcraft (pharmakon as poison/spell). The word pharmakon encompassed both meanings. -- Wax images (keroplaston) placed at doors, crossroads, and tombs were a real practice in Greek curse magic (katadesmoi/defixiones). Archaeological evidence of curse tablets and binding spells is abundant throughout the Greek world. -- Crossroads (triodoi) were sacred to Hecate and associated with magical practices. -- Plato's remarkably balanced treatment -- neither dismissing sorcery as nonsense nor affirming it as real -- reflects a philosophical sophistication about the limits of knowledge. He acknowledges that fear is a genuine social problem regardless of whether the feared object is real. -- Compare Laws X (909b-910d), where the Athenian Stranger discusses impiety and unauthorized religious practices. -- The Hippocratic treatise On the Sacred Disease takes a more rationalist stance, arguing that epilepsy is not caused by divine or magical forces.

D: Original: But we must have a law in two parts, concerning poisoning, in whichever of the two ways the attempt is made, and we must entreat, and exhort, and advise men not to have recourse to such practices, by which they scare the multitude out of their wits, as if they were children, compelling the legislator and the judge to heal the fears which the sorcerer arouses, and to tell them in the first place, that he who attempts to poison or enchant others knows not what he is doing, either as regards the body (unless he has a knowledge of medicine), or as regards his enchantments (unless he happens to be a prophet or diviner). Let the law, then, run as follows about poisoning or witchcraft:-He who employs poison to do any injury, not fatal, to a man himself, or to his servants, or any injury, whether fatal or not, to his cattle or his bees, if he be a physician, and be convicted of poisoning, shall be punished with death; or if he be a private person, the court shall determine what he is to pay or suffer. But he who seems to be the sort of man injures others by magic knots, or enchantments, or incantations, or any of the like practices, if he be a prophet or diviner, let him die; and if, not being a prophet, he be convicted of witchcraft, as in the previous case, let the court fix what he ought to pay or suffer.
D: Narrative:
"But we must have a law in two parts, concerning poisoning, in whichever of the two ways the attempt is made, and we must entreat, and exhort, and advise men not to have recourse to such practices, by which they scare the multitude out of their wits, as if they were children, compelling the legislator and the judge to heal the fears which the sorcerer arouses, and to tell them in the first place, that he who attempts to poison or enchant others knows not what he is doing, either as regards the body (unless he has a knowledge of medicine), or as regards his enchantments (unless he happens to be a prophet or diviner).

"Let the law, then, run as follows about poisoning or witchcraft: He who employs poison to do any injury, not fatal, to a man himself, or to his servants, or any injury, whether fatal or not, to his cattle or his bees, if he be a physician, and be convicted of poisoning, shall be punished with death; or if he be a private person, the court shall determine what he is to pay or suffer. But he who seems to be the sort of man who injures others by magic knots, or enchantments, or incantations, or any of the like practices, if he be a prophet or diviner, let him die; and if, not being a prophet, he be convicted of witchcraft, as in the previous case, let the court fix what he ought to pay or suffer."
D: Refashioned:
"We need a two-part law covering poisoning in both its forms. We must also entreat, exhort, and advise people not to resort to such practices, which terrify the public as if they were children, forcing the legislator and judge to address the fears that sorcerers create. People should understand that those who attempt to poison or enchant others generally do not know what they are doing -- not regarding the body, unless they have medical training, and not regarding enchantments, unless they happen to be a genuine prophet or diviner.

"The law on poisoning and witchcraft is as follows. Anyone who uses poison to cause non-fatal injury to a person, his servants, or any injury, whether fatal or not, to his livestock or bees: if the offender is a physician convicted of poisoning, he shall be punished with death. If the offender is a private person, the court shall determine the appropriate penalty. As for anyone who appears to harm others through magic knots, enchantments, incantations, or similar practices: if the offender is a prophet or diviner, he shall die. If he is not a prophet but is convicted of witchcraft, the court shall determine the penalty."
D: Elegant: A two-part law addresses both physical poisoning and sorcery. Physicians convicted of poisoning face death; laypeople face court-determined penalties. Prophets or diviners convicted of harmful sorcery face death; non-prophets convicted of witchcraft face court-determined penalties. The severity scales with the offender's expertise: those who should know better are punished more harshly.
D: Summary: The law on poisoning and sorcery imposes death on physicians convicted of poisoning and on prophets convicted of harmful sorcery, as experts who should know better. Non-experts in either category face penalties determined by the court.
D: Distilled: Expert poisoners and expert sorcerers face death, while non-experts in either category face court-determined penalties proportional to the harm caused.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger states the law on poisoning and sorcery. -- The key principle is that expertise increases culpability: physicians who poison and prophets who curse are treated more severely than laypeople, because they abuse specialized knowledge. This parallels the consumer-protection laws in 11.6-7, where skilled sellers face harsher penalties than ordinary ones. -- The distinction between prophets/diviners and ordinary people practicing sorcery creates a two-tier system: professional practitioners of the sacred arts bear greater responsibility. -- The death penalty for physician-poisoners reflects the special trust placed in medical professionals -- a concern that echoes the Hippocratic Oath's injunction to 'do no harm.' -- The pragmatic note that most would-be sorcerers 'know not what they are doing' suggests Plato views much sorcery as ineffective -- but harmful to social order nonetheless. -- Compare Republic III (389b-d) on the analogy between doctors, rulers, and the legitimate use of deception.

D: Original: When a man does another any injury by theft or violence, for the greater injury let him pay greater damages to the injured man, and less for the smaller injury; but in all cases, whatever the injury may have been, as much as will compensate the loss. And besides the compensation of the wrong, let a man pay a further penalty for the chastisement of his offence: he who has done the wrong instigated by the folly of another, through the lightheartedness of youth or the like, shall pay a lighter penalty; but he who has injured another through his own folly, when overcome by pleasure or pain, in cowardly fear, or lust, or envy, or implacable anger, shall endure a heavier punishment. Not that he is punished because he did wrong, for that which is done can never be undone, but in order that in future times, he, and those who see him corrected, may utterly hate injustice, or at any rate abate much of their evil-doing. Having an eye to all these things, the law, like a good archer, should aim at the right measure of punishment, and in all cases at the deserved punishment. In the attainment of this the judge shall be a fellow-worker with the legislator, whenever the law leaves to him to determine what the offender shall suffer or pay; and the legislator, like a painter, shall give a rough sketch of the cases in which the law is to be applied. This is what we must do, Megillus and Cleinias, in the best and fairest manner that we can, saying what the punishments are to be of all actions of theft and violence, and giving laws of such a kind as the Gods and sons of Gods would have us give.
D: Narrative:
"When a man does another any injury by theft or violence, for the greater injury let him pay greater damages to the injured man, and less for the smaller injury; but in all cases, whatever the injury may have been, as much as will compensate the loss. And besides the compensation of the wrong, let a man pay a further penalty for the chastisement of his offence: he who has done the wrong instigated by the folly of another, through the lightheartedness of youth or the like, shall pay a lighter penalty; but he who has injured another through his own folly, when overcome by pleasure or pain, in cowardly fear, or lust, or envy, or implacable anger, shall endure a heavier punishment.

"Not that he is punished because he did wrong, for that which is done can never be undone, but in order that in future times, he, and those who see him corrected, may utterly hate injustice, or at any rate abate much of their evil-doing.

"Having an eye to all these things, the law, like a good archer, should aim at the right measure of punishment, and in all cases at the deserved punishment. In the attainment of this the judge shall be a fellow-worker with the legislator, whenever the law leaves to him to determine what the offender shall suffer or pay; and the legislator, like a painter, shall give a rough sketch of the cases in which the law is to be applied.

"This is what we must do, Megillus and Cleinias, in the best and fairest manner that we can, saying what the punishments are to be of all actions of theft and violence, and giving laws of such a kind as the Gods and sons of Gods would have us give."
D: Refashioned:
"When someone injures another through theft or violence, the damages should be proportional: greater damages for greater injuries, lesser for smaller ones, but in every case enough to fully compensate the loss. Beyond compensation, an additional penalty shall be imposed as corrective punishment. Those who acted under the influence of another's folly, or through youthful recklessness and the like, shall receive a lighter penalty. But those who acted from their own weakness -- overcome by pleasure or pain, cowardly fear, lust, envy, or implacable anger -- shall endure heavier punishment.

"The purpose of punishment is not retribution for the past, since what is done can never be undone. Rather, it is to ensure that in the future, both the offender and those who witness his correction come to hate injustice completely, or at the very least significantly reduce their wrongdoing.

"The law, like a skilled archer, should aim precisely at the right measure of punishment -- always the deserved penalty. In achieving this, the judge shall be the legislator's collaborator whenever the law leaves it to the judge to determine the specific penalty. The legislator, like a painter, provides the rough sketch; the judge fills in the details.

"This is what we must do, Megillus and Cleinias, as well and fairly as we can: specifying punishments for all acts of theft and violence, and crafting laws worthy of the gods and the sons of gods."
D: Elegant: Damages for theft and violence must be proportional, fully compensating the loss plus an additional corrective penalty. Those influenced by others or by youth receive lighter penalties; those driven by their own lust, envy, fear, or anger receive heavier ones.
