Punishment is not retribution for the past but education for the future -- aimed at making offenders and witnesses hate injustice. The law, like a good archer, aims at the right measure; the legislator sketches, the judge fills in the details.
D: Summary: Penalties for theft and violence must compensate the victim and correct the offender, scaled to the offender's moral culpability. Punishment is forward-looking -- not retribution but education for offender and community alike. The legislator provides the framework; the judge applies it to specifics.
D: Distilled: Punishment for theft and violence serves not retribution but future deterrence, with the law aiming like a good archer at the proportional penalty each offender deserves.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses Megillus and Cleinias, stating the general theory of punishment. -- The forward-looking theory of punishment ('not because he did wrong, for that which is done can never be undone') is one of the Laws' most philosophically important statements, anticipating modern consequentialist and rehabilitative theories of criminal justice. -- The distinction between acting from one's own vice (lust, envy, anger) versus external influence or youth creates a graduated culpability scale anticipating modern distinctions between principal and accessory liability, and between adult and juvenile offenders. -- The archer metaphor for proportional punishment connects to the Laws' broader theme of measure and proportion as principles of good order. -- The painter-legislator analogy appears also in Republic VI (484c-d), where the philosopher-ruler is compared to a painter working from the model of the Forms. -- The invocation of 'Gods and sons of Gods' as the ultimate standard for legislation reflects the Laws' theocratic framework. -- Compare Protagoras 324a-c, where Socrates articulates a similar forward-looking theory of punishment.

D: Original: If a man is mad he shall not be at large in the city, but his relations shall keep him at home in any way which they can; or if not, let them pay a penalty-he who is of the highest class shall pay a penalty of one hundred drachmae, whether he be a slave or a freeman whom he neglects; and he of the second class shall pay four-fifths of a mina; and he of the third class three-fifths; and he of the fourth class two-fifths. Now there are many sorts of madness, some arising out of disease, which we have already mentioned; and there are other kinds, which originate in an evil and passionate temperament, and are increased by bad education; out of a slight quarrel this class of madmen will often raise a storm of abuse against one another, and nothing of that sort ought to be allowed to occur in a well-ordered state. Let this, then, be the law about abuse, which shall relate to all cases:-No one shall speak evil of another; and when a man disputes with another he shall teach and learn of the disputant and the company, but he shall abstain from evilspeaking; for out of the imprecations which men utter against one another, and the feminine habit of casting aspersions on one another, and using foul names, out of words light as air, in very deed the greatest enmities and hatreds spring up.
D: Narrative:
"If a man is mad he shall not be at large in the city, but his relations shall keep him at home in any way which they can; or if not, let them pay a penalty -- he who is of the highest class shall pay a penalty of one hundred drachmas, whether he be a slave or a freeman whom he neglects; and he of the second class shall pay four-fifths of a mina; and he of the third class three-fifths; and he of the fourth class two-fifths.

"Now there are many sorts of madness, some arising out of disease, which we have already mentioned; and there are other kinds, which originate in an evil and passionate temperament, and are increased by bad education; out of a slight quarrel this class of madmen will often raise a storm of abuse against one another, and nothing of that sort ought to be allowed to occur in a well-ordered state.

"Let this, then, be the law about abuse, which shall relate to all cases: No one shall speak evil of another; and when a man disputes with another he shall teach and learn of the disputant and the company, but he shall abstain from evil-speaking; for out of the imprecations which men utter against one another, and the feminine habit of casting aspersions on one another, and using foul names, out of words light as air, in very deed the greatest enmities and hatreds spring up."
D: Refashioned:
"A person who is mad shall not roam freely in the city. His relatives must keep him confined at home by whatever means they can. If they fail to do so, they face penalties scaled to their property class: the highest class pays one hundred drachmas, the second class eighty drachmas, the third class sixty, and the fourth class forty -- regardless of whether the person they neglect is a slave or a free person.

"There are many forms of madness. Some arise from disease, which we have already discussed. But other kinds originate in an evil and passionate temperament, made worse by poor upbringing. This second type will escalate a trivial disagreement into a storm of verbal abuse -- and nothing of the sort should be tolerated in a well-ordered state.

"Here, then, is the law on verbal abuse, applying to all cases: no one shall speak ill of another. When people disagree, they should seek to teach and learn from the other party and from those present, but they must refrain from abusive language. For from the curses people hurl at each other, from the habit of casting aspersions and using foul names -- from words as light as air -- the greatest and most serious enmities and hatreds arise."
D: Elegant: The insane must be confined at home by their relatives, with penalties scaled by property class for failure. Madness comes in two forms: from disease and from passionate temperament worsened by bad education. The second type fuels abusive speech, which the law bans entirely. Disputes must aim at mutual instruction, not insult, for from words light as air the greatest hatreds arise.
D: Summary: Relatives must confine the insane at home or face scaled fines. The law then pivots to verbal abuse, which it treats as a form of madness: all evil-speaking is banned, because disputes must aim at mutual learning, not insult, since even light words can generate profound enmity.
D: Distilled: Madness must be confined and abusive speech banned, because verbal insults -- though light as air -- generate the greatest enmities.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses both literal madness and the 'madness' of abusive speech. -- The scaled penalties by property class ensure proportional economic impact, a principle used throughout the Laws. One mina equaled 100 drachmas. -- The remarkable transition from clinical madness to the 'madness' of passionate speech suggests that Plato sees verbal abuse as a form of social insanity. -- The rule that disputants should 'teach and learn' rather than abuse each other reflects the Socratic method: proper disagreement is a form of collaborative inquiry. -- The phrase 'words light as air' generating 'the greatest enmities' anticipates modern understanding of how verbal aggression escalates into physical violence. -- The 'feminine habit' of casting aspersions reflects misogynistic Greek cultural assumptions, but the law itself applies equally to men and women. -- Compare Republic V (452a-e) on the importance of rational discourse over mockery. -- Aristotle's Rhetoric II.2-3 provides a detailed analysis of anger and its provocations that complements this passage.

D: Original: For the speaker gratifies his anger, which is an ungracious element of his nature; and nursing up his wrath by the entertainment of evil thoughts, and exacerbating that part of his soul which was formerly civilized by education, he lives in a state of savageness and moroseness, and pays a bitter penalty for his anger. And in such cases almost all men take to saying something ridiculous about their opponent, and there is no man who is in the habit of laughing at another who does not miss virtue and earnestness altogether, or lose the better half of greatness. Wherefore let no one utter any taunting word at a temple, or at the public sacrifices, or at games, or in the agora, or in a court of justice, or in any public assembly. And let the magistrate who presides on these occasions chastise an offender, and he shall be blameless; but if he fails in doing so, he shall not claim the prize of virtue; for he is one who heeds not the laws, and does not do what the legislator commands.
D: Narrative:
"For the speaker gratifies his anger, which is an ungracious element of his nature; and nursing up his wrath by the entertainment of evil thoughts, and exacerbating that part of his soul which was formerly civilized by education, he lives in a state of savageness and moroseness, and pays a bitter penalty for his anger.

"And in such cases almost all men take to saying something ridiculous about their opponent, and there is no man who is in the habit of laughing at another who does not miss virtue and earnestness altogether, or lose the better half of greatness.

"Wherefore let no one utter any taunting word at a temple, or at the public sacrifices, or at games, or in the agora, or in a court of justice, or in any public assembly. And let the magistrate who presides on these occasions chastise an offender, and he shall be blameless; but if he fails in doing so, he shall not claim the prize of virtue; for he is one who heeds not the laws, and does not do what the legislator commands."
D: Refashioned:
"The speaker who indulges in abuse is gratifying his anger -- an ugly part of his nature. By nursing his rage through dwelling on malicious thoughts, he inflames the very part of his soul that education had previously civilized. He sinks into a condition of savagery and bitterness, paying a heavy price for his indulgence.

"In such situations, nearly everyone resorts to mocking their opponent. But no person who habitually laughs at others can achieve true virtue and seriousness -- or at the very least, such a person loses the better half of greatness.

"Therefore, no one shall utter taunting words at a temple, during public sacrifices, at athletic games, in the marketplace, in a court of law, or in any public assembly. The presiding magistrate must punish any offender and shall face no blame for doing so. But a magistrate who fails to enforce this rule forfeits his claim to virtue, for he has disregarded the laws and refused to carry out the legislator's command."
D: Elegant: Verbal abuse gratifies anger but destroys the soul: it reverses education's civilizing work and reduces a person to savagery. Habitual mockery destroys virtue and seriousness. Taunting is therefore banned in all public spaces -- temples, sacrifices, games, courts, and assemblies. Presiding magistrates must punish offenders or forfeit their own claim to virtue.
D: Summary: Verbal abuse degrades the speaker's soul by reversing education's civilizing influence and fostering savagery. Habitual mockery is incompatible with virtue. Taunting is banned in all public spaces, and presiding magistrates must enforce the ban or be considered negligent.
D: Distilled: Verbal abuse destroys the abuser's soul by reversing the civilizing work of education, and is therefore banned in all public spaces with mandatory enforcement by magistrates.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger provides the psychological rationale for banning verbal abuse. -- The concept that anger 're-savages' the soul, undoing what education civilized, is a powerful psychological insight connecting to the tripartite soul theory of Republic IV: the spirited element (thumos), when not governed by reason, regresses to a pre-civilized state. -- The observation that habitual mockery undermines virtue and 'the better half of greatness' reflects Plato's longstanding critique of comedy and ridicule; compare Republic X (606c), where Socrates argues that laughing at comedy weakens our moral resistance to base behavior. -- The comprehensive list of public spaces (temple, sacrifice, games, agora, court, assembly) covers every dimension of Greek public life, leaving no venue for verbal abuse. -- The duty imposed on presiding magistrates, with the penalty of losing their claim to virtue, makes enforcement a test of the magistrate's own character. -- Compare Laws VII (816d-817e) on the regulation of dramatic performances, and Republic III (388e-389a) on the dangers of uncontrolled laughter.

D: Original: And if in any other place any one indulges in these sort of revilings, whether he has begun the quarrel or is only retaliating, let any elder who is present support the law, and control with blows those who indulge in passion, which is another great evil; and if he do not, let him be liable to pay the appointed penalty. And we say now, that he who deals in reproaches against others cannot reproach them without attempting to ridicule them; and this, when done in a moment of anger, is what we make matter of reproach against him. But then, do we admit into our state the comic writers who are so fond of making mankind ridiculous, if they attempt in a good-natured manner to turn the laugh against our citizens? or do we draw the distinction of jest and earnest, and allow a man to make use of ridicule in jest and without anger about any thing or person; though as we were saying, not if he be angry have a set purpose? We forbid earnest-that is unalterably fixed; but we have still to say who are to be sanctioned or not to be sanctioned by the law in the employment of innocent humour. A comic poet, or maker of iambic or satirical lyric verse, shall not be permitted to ridicule any of the citizens, either by word or likeness, either in anger or without anger. And if any one is disobedient, the judges shall either at once expel him from the country, or he shall pay a fine of three minae, which shall be dedicated to the God who presides over the contests. Those only who have received permission shall be allowed to write verses at one another, but they shall be without anger and in jest; in anger and in serious earnest they shall not be allowed. The decision of this matter shall be left to the superintendent of the general education of the young, and whatever he may license, the writer shall be allowed to produce, and whatever he rejects let not the poet himself exhibit, or ever teach anybody else, slave or freeman, under the penalty of being dishonoured, and held disobedient to the laws.
D: Narrative:
"And if in any other place any one indulges in these sort of revilings, whether he has begun the quarrel or is only retaliating, let any elder who is present support the law, and control with blows those who indulge in passion, which is another great evil; and if he do not, let him be liable to pay the appointed penalty.

"And we say now, that he who deals in reproaches against others cannot reproach them without attempting to ridicule them; and this, when done in a moment of anger, is what we make matter of reproach against him. But then, do we admit into our state the comic writers who are so fond of making mankind ridiculous, if they attempt in a good-natured manner to turn the laugh against our citizens? Or do we draw the distinction of jest and earnest, and allow a man to make use of ridicule in jest and without anger about any thing or person; though as we were saying, not if he be angry and have a set purpose?

"We forbid earnest -- that is unalterably fixed; but we have still to say who are to be sanctioned or not to be sanctioned by the law in the employment of innocent humour. A comic poet, or maker of iambic or satirical lyric verse, shall not be permitted to ridicule any of the citizens, either by word or likeness, either in anger or without anger. And if any one is disobedient, the judges shall either at once expel him from the country, or he shall pay a fine of three minas, which shall be dedicated to the God who presides over the contests.

"Those only who have received permission shall be allowed to write verses at one another, but they shall be without anger and in jest; in anger and in serious earnest they shall not be allowed. The decision of this matter shall be left to the superintendent of the general education of the young, and whatever he may license, the writer shall be allowed to produce, and whatever he rejects let not the poet himself exhibit, or ever teach anybody else, slave or freeman, under the penalty of being dishonoured, and held disobedient to the laws."
D: Refashioned:
"If anyone indulges in abusive speech anywhere else -- whether as the instigator or in retaliation -- any elder present must uphold the law and physically restrain those carried away by passion, which is itself a grave evil. An elder who fails to act shall be liable for the prescribed penalty.

"We maintain that a person who reproaches others inevitably tries to ridicule them. When done in anger, this is precisely what we condemn. But what about comic writers who good-naturedly try to make citizens laugh at themselves? Do we admit them into the state? Can we distinguish jest from earnest, allowing harmless ridicule made without anger while prohibiting malicious mockery?

"Our position is firm: angry, serious ridicule is absolutely forbidden. As for innocent humor, the rules are as follows. No comic poet, iambic writer, or satirical lyricist may ridicule any citizen, either in words or in caricature, whether in anger or in jest. Violators face immediate expulsion from the country or a fine of three minas dedicated to the god of the contests.

"Only those who have received explicit permission may compose verses about one another, and these must be without anger and purely in jest -- never in anger or serious earnest. The superintendent of youth education shall decide what is permissible. Whatever he approves may be performed; whatever he rejects, the poet may neither exhibit nor teach to anyone, slave or free, on pain of dishonor and conviction for disobedience to the laws."
D: Elegant: If anyone indulges in abusive speech anywhere outside official public spaces, any elder present must restrain them with blows or face the appointed penalty. Reproach inevitably involves ridicule, which in anger is condemned. But what of comic writers who good-naturedly mock citizens? The state draws a clear line: angry, serious ridicule is absolutely forbidden. No comic poet, iambic writer, or satirical lyricist may ridicule any citizen, by word or likeness, in anger or otherwise. Violators face immediate expulsion or a fine of three minas dedicated to the god of the contests. Only those who receive explicit permission from the superintendent of youth education may compose humorous verse about fellow citizens, and this must be purely in jest, never in anger or earnest. Whatever the superintendent rejects, the poet may not exhibit or teach to anyone, slave or free, on pain of dishonor and disobedience to the laws.
D: Summary: Elders must enforce the abuse ban by physical restraint. Comic poets and satirists are prohibited from ridiculing citizens in any form. Only verse explicitly licensed by the superintendent of youth education may be composed about fellow citizens, and it must be purely jesting, never angry or serious.
D: Distilled: Comic poets may not ridicule citizens without explicit permission from the superintendent of education, and all angry or serious mockery is absolutely forbidden.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger extends the abuse ban to literary satire. -- This is one of the most discussed passages in the Laws for its implications regarding censorship and freedom of expression. It extends the Republic's famous censorship of poetry (Books II-III, X) into the domain of comedy and satire. -- The three-mina fine is substantial -- roughly equivalent to 300 days' wages for a skilled laborer. -- The distinction between jest and earnest is philosophically interesting but practically dissolved by the law's prohibition of citizen-directed ridicule even in jest. -- The superintendent of youth education (who appears in Laws VII as well) serves as a state censor -- a role Plato considers essential for maintaining civic virtue. -- Aristophanes, the great Athenian comic poet, regularly ridiculed prominent citizens; his mockery of Socrates in the Clouds is the most famous example and may have contributed to the prejudice that led to Socrates' trial. -- The passage may reflect Plato's belief that such mockery was genuinely harmful to individuals and to civic life. -- Compare Republic III (394-398), where poetic mimesis is restricted to representations of virtuous characters.

D: Original: Now he is not to be pitied who is hungry, or who suffers any bodily pain, but he who is temperate, or has some other virtue, or part of a virtue, and at the same time suffers from misfortune; it would be an extraordinary thing if such an one, whether slave or freeman, were utterly forsaken and fell into the extremes of poverty in any tolerably well-ordered city or government. Wherefore the legislator may safely make a law applicable to such cases in the following terms:-Let there be no beggars in our state; and if anybody begs, seeking to pick up a livelihood by unavailing prayers, let the wardens of the agora turn him out of the agora, and the wardens of the city out of the city, and the wardens of the country send him out of any other parts of the land across the border, in order that the land may be cleared of this sort of animal.
D: Narrative:
"Now he is not to be pitied who is hungry, or who suffers any bodily pain, but he who is temperate, or has some other virtue, or part of a virtue, and at the same time suffers from misfortune; it would be an extraordinary thing if such an one, whether slave or freeman, were utterly forsaken and fell into the extremes of poverty in any tolerably well-ordered city or government.

"Wherefore the legislator may safely make a law applicable to such cases in the following terms: Let there be no beggars in our state; and if anybody begs, seeking to pick up a livelihood by unavailing prayers, let the wardens of the agora turn him out of the agora, and the wardens of the city out of the city, and the wardens of the country send him out of any other parts of the land across the border, in order that the land may be cleared of this sort of animal."
D: Refashioned:
"The person truly deserving of pity is not the one who is hungry or in physical pain, but the one who possesses temperance or some other virtue -- or even part of a virtue -- yet suffers misfortune despite it. In any tolerably well-ordered city, it would be extraordinary if such a virtuous person, whether slave or free, were completely abandoned and fell into extreme poverty.

"The legislator can therefore safely enact the following law: there shall be no beggars in our state. If anyone attempts to beg, seeking a livelihood through futile appeals, the wardens of the agora shall expel him from the marketplace, the wardens of the city from the city, and the wardens of the country from the countryside and across the border. The land shall be cleared of this sort of creature."
D: Elegant: True pity belongs to the virtuous person struck by misfortune, not to anyone who merely suffers hunger or pain. In a well-ordered state, virtuous people should never fall into destitution. Begging is therefore banned: beggars shall be expelled from agora, city, and countryside alike, driven beyond the borders.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger argues that a well-ordered state should never allow virtuous people to fall into destitution, making begging unnecessary. Begging is therefore banned outright, with beggars to be expelled from all parts of the state by the appropriate wardens.
D: Distilled: In a well-ordered state, no virtuous person should fall into destitution, so begging is banned and beggars are expelled across the border.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses poverty and begging. -- This passage reveals a troubling logic: the ban on begging assumes that a well-ordered state provides for all virtuous citizens, so anyone who begs must be either lacking virtue or gaming the system. -- The harsh language ('cleared of this sort of animal') is striking and reflects Greek attitudes toward those considered social parasites. -- The assumption that the state can eliminate poverty through good governance is idealistic; the Laws elsewhere acknowledges that the property system cannot prevent all inequality. -- Compare Republic II (369b-374a), where the ideal city is designed to meet all citizens' needs through the division of labor, theoretically eliminating poverty. -- The distinction between deserving and undeserving poor has a long history in Western political thought, from this passage through the Elizabethan Poor Laws to modern welfare debates. -- The three-warden enforcement system (agora, city, country) parallels the enforcement of marketplace regulations in 11.9-10.

D: Original: If a slave of either sex injure anything, which is not his or her own, through inexperience, or some improper practice, and the person who suffers damage be not himself in part to blame, the master of the slave who has done the harm shall either make full satisfaction, or give up the the slave who has done has done the injury. But if master argue that the charge has arisen by collusion between the injured party and the injurer, with the view of obtaining the slave, let him sue the person, who says that he has been injured, for malpractices. And if he gain a conviction, let him receive double the value which the court fixes as the price of the slave; and if he lose his suit, let him make amends for the injury, and give up the slave. And if a beast of burden, or horse, or dog, or any other animal, injure the property of a neighbour, the owner shall in like manner pay for the injury.
D: Narrative:
"If a slave of either sex injure anything, which is not his or her own, through inexperience, or some improper practice, and the person who suffers damage be not himself in part to blame, the master of the slave who has done the harm shall either make full satisfaction, or give up the slave who has done the injury.

"But if the master argue that the charge has arisen by collusion between the injured party and the injurer, with the view of obtaining the slave, let him sue the person who says that he has been injured, for malpractices. And if he gain a conviction, let him receive double the value which the court fixes as the price of the slave; and if he lose his suit, let him make amends for the injury, and give up the slave.

"And if a beast of burden, or horse, or dog, or any other animal, injure the property of a neighbour, the owner shall in like manner pay for the injury."
D: Refashioned:
"If a slave of either sex damages someone else's property through inexperience or carelessness, and the injured party is not partly at fault, the slave's master must either make full compensation or surrender the slave who caused the damage.

"However, if the master claims the charge is based on collusion between the injured party and the slave, aimed at fraudulently obtaining the slave, the master may sue the supposed victim for fraud. If the master wins, he shall receive double the court-assessed value of the slave. If he loses, he must compensate for the damage and surrender the slave.

"The same principle applies to animals: if a beast of burden, horse, dog, or any other animal damages a neighbor's property, the owner shall pay for the injury in the same manner."
D: Elegant: When a slave damages another's property without the victim's contributory fault, the master must either pay full compensation or surrender the slave. If the master suspects the claim is a fraudulent scheme to acquire the slave, he may countersue; winning earns double the slave's value, losing means paying damages and surrendering the slave. The same principle applies to damage caused by animals.
D: Summary: Masters are liable for damage caused by their slaves through either compensation or surrender of the slave. A defense of collusion is available, with double damages for a successful countersuit. The same liability framework applies to damage caused by animals.
D: Distilled: Masters bear strict liability for damage caused by their slaves or animals, with an option to surrender the offending slave or pay full compensation.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses liability for slaves and animals. -- The choice between compensation and surrender of the slave (noxal surrender) is one of the most ancient principles of property law, found in the Twelve Tables of Roman law (451-450 BCE) and in Near Eastern legal codes before that. -- The contributory-fault defense ('the person who suffers damage be not himself in part to blame') is a remarkably early expression of comparative negligence. -- The collusion defense protects masters against fraudulent schemes to acquire their slaves through staged accidents -- apparently a recognized problem in the ancient world. -- The extension of the principle to animals (horses, dogs, beasts of burden) establishes a unified framework of owner liability for property under one's control. -- Compare Exodus 21:28-36, which contains similar provisions for liability when an ox gores a person or another animal. -- Roman law developed noxal surrender (noxae deditio) into a sophisticated legal institution, allowing masters to avoid personal liability by surrendering the offending slave.

D: Original: If any man refuses to be a witness, he who wants him shall summon him, and he who is summoned shall come to the trial; and if he knows and is willing to bear witness, let him bear witness, but if he says he does not know let him swear by the three divinities Zeus, and Apollo, and Themis, that he does not, and have no more to do with the cause. And he who is summoned to give witness and does not answer to his summoner, shall be liable for the harm which ensues according to law. And if a person calls up as a witness any one who is acting as a judge, let him give his witness, but he shall not afterwards vote in the cause. A free woman may give her witness and plead, if she be more than forty years of age, and may bring an action if she have no husband; but if her husband be alive she shall only be allowed to bear witness. A slave of either sex and a child shall be allowed to give evidence and to plead, but only in cases of murder; and they must produce sufficient sureties that they will certainly remain until the trial, in case they should be charged with false witness. And either of the parties in a cause may bring an accusation of perjury against witnesses, touching their evidence in whole or in part, if he asserts that such evidence has been given; but the accusation must be brought previous to the final decision of the cause.
D: Narrative:
"If any man refuses to be a witness, he who wants him shall summon him, and he who is summoned shall come to the trial; and if he knows and is willing to bear witness, let him bear witness, but if he says he does not know let him swear by the three divinities Zeus, and Apollo, and Themis, that he does not, and have no more to do with the cause.

"And he who is summoned to give witness and does not answer to his summoner, shall be liable for the harm which ensues according to law. And if a person calls up as a witness any one who is acting as a judge, let him give his witness, but he shall not afterwards vote in the cause.

"A free woman may give her witness and plead, if she be more than forty years of age, and may bring an action if she have no husband; but if her husband be alive she shall only be allowed to bear witness. A slave of either sex and a child shall be allowed to give evidence and to plead, but only in cases of murder; and they must produce sufficient sureties that they will certainly remain until the trial, in case they should be charged with false witness.

"And either of the parties in a cause may bring an accusation of perjury against witnesses, touching their evidence in whole or in part, if he asserts that such evidence has been given; but the accusation must be brought previous to the final decision of the cause."
D: Refashioned:
"If someone refuses to serve as a witness, the party who needs his testimony shall issue a formal summons. The summoned person must appear at trial. If he knows the relevant facts and is willing to testify, he shall testify. But if he claims to have no knowledge, he must swear by the three deities -- Zeus, Apollo, and Themis -- that he does not, and then he shall be released from any further involvement in the case.

"Anyone summoned as a witness who fails to appear shall be legally liable for whatever harm results. If a sitting judge is called as a witness, he shall give his testimony but may not subsequently vote in the case.

"A free woman over forty years of age may testify and plead in court. She may also bring legal actions, but only if she has no living husband. If her husband is alive, she may only serve as a witness. Slaves of either sex and children may give evidence and plead, but only in murder cases, and they must provide sufficient sureties guaranteeing they will remain available through the trial in case they are charged with giving false testimony.

"Either party in a case may accuse witnesses of perjury, whether regarding all or part of their testimony. However, the perjury accusation must be filed before the final judgment in the case is rendered."
D: Elegant: Summoned witnesses must appear at trial. Those who claim ignorance must swear by Zeus, Apollo, and Themis. Absent witnesses are liable for resulting harm. Judges called as witnesses must testify but cannot vote. Free women over forty may testify and, if unmarried, bring legal actions. Slaves and children may testify only in murder cases, with sureties required. Perjury accusations must be filed before final judgment.
D: Summary: Witnesses are compelled to appear and testify, with a divine oath available for those claiming ignorance. Judges may testify but not vote. Women over forty gain limited legal standing, and slaves and children may testify only in murder cases. Perjury charges must precede the final verdict.
D: Distilled: The law compels witness attendance, provides for oath-based refusal, grants limited legal standing to women over forty and to slaves in murder cases, and allows pre-verdict perjury challenges.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses witness law. -- The oath by Zeus, Apollo, and Themis invokes the three deities most associated with truth and justice: Zeus as supreme authority, Apollo as god of prophecy and truth, and Themis as goddess of divine law and customary right. -- The recusal of judge-witnesses from voting is an early conflict-of-interest provision. -- The age threshold of forty for women's legal standing is notable: it grants older women significant legal capacity (testimony, pleading, bringing actions) while restricting married women to testimony only. This is more liberal than Athenian practice, where women had very limited legal standing and usually needed a male representative (kyrios). -- The restriction of slave and child testimony to murder cases reflects both the gravity of such cases and the general suspicion of testimony from low-status persons. -- The pre-verdict perjury requirement ensures that false witness claims are resolved before they can affect the outcome. -- Compare Demosthenes, Against Stephanus, for extended discussion of witness law in classical Athens.

D: Original: The magistrates shall preserve the accusations of false witness, and have them kept under the seal of both parties, and produce them on the day when the trial for false witness takes place. If a man be twice convicted of false witness, he shall not be required, and if thrice, he shall not be allowed to bear witness; and if he dare to witness after he has been convicted three times, let any one who pleases inform against him to the magistrates, and let the magistrates hand him over to the court, and if he be convicted he shall be punished with death. And in any case in which the evidence is rightly found to be false, and yet to have given the victory to him who wins the suit, and more than half the witnesses are condemned, the decision which was gained by these means shall be a discussion and a decision as to whether the suit was determined by that false evidence or and in whichever way the decision may be given, the previous suit shall be determined accordingly.
D: Narrative:
"The magistrates shall preserve the accusations of false witness, and have them kept under the seal of both parties, and produce them on the day when the trial for false witness takes place. If a man be twice convicted of false witness, he shall not be required, and if thrice, he shall not be allowed to bear witness; and if he dare to witness after he has been convicted three times, let any one who pleases inform against him to the magistrates, and let the magistrates hand him over to the court, and if he be convicted he shall be punished with death.

"And in any case in which the evidence is rightly found to be false, and yet to have given the victory to him who wins the suit, and more than half the witnesses are condemned, the decision which was gained by these means shall be reconsidered, and there shall be a discussion and a decision as to whether the suit was determined by that false evidence or not; and in whichever way the decision may be given, the previous suit shall be determined accordingly."
D: Refashioned:
"The magistrates shall preserve all accusations of false witness under the seal of both parties and present them on the day the perjury trial takes place. If a person is convicted of false witness twice, he shall no longer be required to testify. If convicted three times, he shall be forbidden from testifying at all. If he dares to give testimony after three convictions, anyone may report him to the magistrates, who shall hand him over to the court. If convicted again, he shall be punished with death.

"Furthermore, if evidence is correctly determined to be false and that false evidence gave the winning party his victory, and more than half the witnesses in the case are convicted of perjury, the original verdict shall be reopened. A new proceeding shall determine whether the false testimony actually decided the outcome. The original case shall then be resolved according to that finding."
D: Elegant: False witness accusations are preserved under seal for trial. Two perjury convictions exempt a person from compulsory testimony; three convictions ban testimony entirely, with death for defiance. When false testimony is found to have decided a case and more than half the witnesses are convicted, the original verdict is reopened and re-decided based on whether the perjury actually determined the outcome.
D: Summary: The law creates an escalating system for perjurers: two convictions exempt from compulsory testimony, three convictions ban testimony entirely, and further violation brings death. When perjured testimony is found to have determined a verdict, the original case is reopened.
D: Distilled: Perjurers face escalating sanctions from exemption to ban to death, and verdicts obtained through proven false testimony are subject to reopening.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the laws on testimony and perjury. -- The escalating sanctions (exemption, ban, death) create a progressive system that permanently removes habitual perjurers from the judicial process. -- The sealed preservation of perjury accusations is a procedural safeguard ensuring that evidence is preserved for the separate perjury trial. -- The provision for reopening verdicts obtained through perjured testimony is a remarkable early form of what modern law calls 'fraud on the court' -- the principle that a judgment obtained through fraud is not truly final. -- The requirement that more than half the witnesses be convicted before reopening shows a high threshold for disturbing settled judgments, balancing finality against justice. -- Athenian law took perjury seriously: the graphe pseudomarturion (public action for false witness) was a common legal proceeding, and Demosthenes' speeches Against Stephanus are devoted to false witness claims. -- The death penalty for testifying after three perjury convictions reflects the view that chronic dishonesty in judicial proceedings is a fundamental threat to the legal order.

D: Original: There are many noble things in human life, but to most of them attach evils which are fated to corrupt and spoil them. Is not justice noble, which has been the civilizer of humanity? How then can the advocate of justice be other than noble? And yet upon this profession which is presented to us under the fair name of art has come an evil reputation. In the first place; we are told that by ingenious pleas and the help of an advocate the law enables a man to win a particular cause, whether just or unjust; and the power of speech which is thereby imparted, are at the service of him sho is willing to pay for them. Now in our state this so-called art, whether really an art or only an experience and practice destitute of any art, ought if possible never to come into existence, or if existing among us should litten to the request of the legislator and go away into another land, and not speak contrary to justice. If the offenders obey we say no more; but those who disobey, the voice of the law is as follows:-If anyone thinks that he will pervert the power of justice in the minds of the judges, and unseasonably litigate or advocate, let any one who likes indict him for malpractices of law and dishonest advocacy, and let him be judged in the court of select judges; and if he be convicted, let the court determine whether he may be supposed to act from a love of money or from contentiousness. And if he is supposed to act from contentiousness, the court shall fix a time during which he shall not be allowed to institute or plead a cause; and if he is supposed to act as be does from love of money, in case he be a stranger, he shall leave the country, and never return under penalty of death; but if he be a citizen, he shall die, because he is a lover of money, in whatever manner gained; and equally, if he be judged to have acted more than once from contentiousness, he shall die.
D: Narrative:
"There are many noble things in human life, but to most of them attach evils which are fated to corrupt and spoil them. Is not justice noble, which has been the civilizer of humanity? How then can the advocate of justice be other than noble? And yet upon this profession which is presented to us under the fair name of art has come an evil reputation.

"In the first place, we are told that by ingenious pleas and the help of an advocate the law enables a man to win a particular cause, whether just or unjust; and the power of speech which is thereby imparted, are at the service of him who is willing to pay for them. Now in our state this so-called art, whether really an art or only an experience and practice destitute of any art, ought if possible never to come into existence, or if existing among us should listen to the request of the legislator and go away into another land, and not speak contrary to justice.

"If the offenders obey we say no more; but those who disobey, the voice of the law is as follows: If anyone thinks that he will pervert the power of justice in the minds of the judges, and unseasonably litigate or advocate, let any one who likes indict him for malpractices of law and dishonest advocacy, and let him be judged in the court of select judges; and if he be convicted, let the court determine whether he may be supposed to act from a love of money or from contentiousness.

"And if he is supposed to act from contentiousness, the court shall fix a time during which he shall not be allowed to institute or plead a cause; and if he is supposed to act as he does from love of money, in case he be a stranger, he shall leave the country, and never return under penalty of death; but if he be a citizen, he shall die, because he is a lover of money, in whatever manner gained; and equally, if he be judged to have acted more than once from contentiousness, he shall die."
D: Refashioned:
"There are many noble things in human life, but most of them attract evils that corrupt and ruin them. Is not justice noble -- the very thing that has civilized humanity? How then can the advocate of justice be anything but noble? Yet this profession, which presents itself under the fair name of an art, has acquired a terrible reputation.

"We are told that through clever arguments and the help of advocates, the law enables people to win cases whether their cause is just or unjust, and that this power of persuasive speech is available to whoever can pay for it. In our state, this so-called art -- whether it truly deserves to be called an art or is merely a knack acquired through practice -- should ideally never come into existence. If it does exist among us, it should heed the legislator's request and take itself to another country rather than speak against justice.

"If practitioners comply, nothing more need be said. But for those who disobey, the law speaks as follows: anyone who attempts to pervert the judges' understanding of justice, or who litigates or advocates vexatiously, may be indicted by any citizen for legal malpractice and dishonest advocacy before the court of select judges. If convicted, the court shall determine whether the offender acted from love of money or from mere contentiousness.

"If the motive is contentiousness, the court shall bar him from initiating or pleading cases for a fixed period. If the motive is love of money: a foreigner shall be permanently expelled and face death if he returns; a citizen shall be put to death, because he is a lover of money in whatever form. And if anyone is found to have acted from contentiousness more than once, he too shall die."
D: Elegant: Justice has civilized humanity, yet advocacy -- which should serve justice -- has been corrupted into a tool for winning cases regardless of merit, available to the highest bidder. Ideally, this corrupt practice should not exist; failing that, it should be expelled. Those who pervert justice through vexatious litigation or dishonest advocacy face indictment. The contentious are temporarily barred from the courts. Money-motivated advocates face death (citizens) or permanent banishment under penalty of death (foreigners). Repeat contentiousness also brings death.
D: Summary: Book 11 concludes with a fierce attack on corrupt advocacy. While justice itself is noble, the legal profession has been corrupted by serving anyone who can pay. The law bans vexatious litigation and dishonest advocacy, with penalties ranging from temporary court bans to death depending on whether the motive is contentiousness or greed.
D: Distilled: Corrupt legal advocacy -- which perverts justice by serving the highest bidder -- is punishable by temporary court bans for the contentious and death for the money-motivated.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger concludes Book 11 with a critique of legal advocacy. -- The observation that noble things attract corrupting evils is a recurring Platonic theme; compare Republic VI (490d-495b) on how philosophy attracts charlatans who corrupt it. -- The question whether advocacy is a true 'art' (techne) or merely an 'experience' (empeiria) echoes the Gorgias (462b-466a), where Socrates distinguishes genuine arts from flattering knacks, classifying rhetoric as the latter. -- The invitation for corrupt advocacy to 'go away into another land' is darkly humorous -- Plato offers the profession a chance to leave voluntarily before imposing legal sanctions. -- The death penalty for money-motivated citizens is the harshest penalty in Book 11, reflecting Plato's view that selling justice for profit is among the gravest possible offenses against the state. -- The distinction between contentiousness (philoneikia) and love of money (philochrematia) as motives for corrupt advocacy shows psychological sophistication in sentencing. -- Compare Apology 31c-32a, where Socrates explains why he avoided public advocacy. -- The passage is also relevant to the Sophist, where Plato distinguishes genuine from false practitioners of intellectual arts.

D: Original: If a herald or an ambassador carry a false message from our city to any other, or bring back a false message from the city to which he is sent, or be proved to have brought back, whether from friends or enemies, in his capacity of herald or ambassador, what they have never said, let him be indicted for having violated, contrary to the law, the commands and duties imposed upon him by Hermes and Zeus, and let there be a penalty fixed, which he shall suffer or pay if he be convicted.
D: Narrative: "If a herald or an ambassador carry a false message from our city to any other, or bring back a false message from the city to which he is sent, or be proved to have brought back, whether from friends or enemies, in his capacity of herald or ambassador, what they have never said, let him be indicted for having violated, contrary to the law, the commands and duties imposed upon him by Hermes and Zeus, and let there be a penalty fixed, which he shall suffer or pay if he be convicted."
D: Refashioned: If any diplomatic envoy or official messenger deliberately transmits a false communication from our city to another, or brings back a fabricated report from the city to which he was sent, or is proven to have conveyed -- whether from allies or adversaries -- statements they never actually made, then he must be prosecuted for violating the sacred duties of his office, duties consecrated by the gods of communication and authority. A specific penalty shall be established, which he must suffer or pay upon conviction.
D: Elegant: Any diplomat or envoy who falsifies messages -- whether outgoing or incoming, whether dealing with allies or enemies -- must be prosecuted for violating his sacred office. A fixed penalty shall apply upon conviction.
D: Summary: Heralds and ambassadors who carry false messages, fabricate reports, or misrepresent what foreign parties have said must be prosecuted and penalized. The office of diplomatic communication is held sacred.
D: Distilled: Diplomats who falsify messages betray a sacred trust and must face prosecution.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on diplomatic integrity. The invocation of Hermes (god of messengers) and Zeus (god of oaths and authority) frames diplomacy as a sacred duty, not merely a political function. This reflects Plato's consistent view that political offices carry divine sanction -- compare Republic 389b-d, where lying is restricted to rulers acting for the city's benefit. The Laws takes a stricter position: even state representatives cannot distort truth in their official capacity.

D: Original: Theft is a mean, and robbery a shameless thing; and none of the sons of Zeus delight in fraud and violence, or ever practised, either. Wherefore let no one be deluded by poets or mythologers into a mistaken belief of such such things, nor let him suppose, when he thieves or is guilty of violence, that he is doing nothing base, but only what the Gods themselves do. For such tales are untrue and improbable; and he who steals or robs contrary to the law, is never either a God or the son of a God; of this the legislator ought to be better informed than all the, poets put together. Happy is he and may he be forever happy, who is persuaded and listens to our words; but he who disobeys shall have to contend against the following law:-If a man steal anything belonging to the public, whether that which he steals be much or little, he shall have the same punishment. For he who steals a little steals with the same wish as he who steals much, but with less power, and he who takes up a greater amount; not having deposited it, is wholly unjust. Wherefore the law is not disposed to inflict a less penalty on the one than on the other because his theft, is less, but on the ground that the thief may possibly be in one case still curable, and may in another case be incurable. If any one convict in a court of law a stranger or a slave of a theft of public property, let the court determine what punishment he shall suffer, or what penalty he shall pay, bearing in mind that he is probably not incurable. But the citizen who has been brought up as our citizens will have been, if he be found guilty of robbing his country by fraud or violence, whether he be caught in the act or not, shall be punished with death; for he is incurable.
D: Narrative:
"Theft is a mean, and robbery a shameless thing; and none of the sons of Zeus delight in fraud and violence, or ever practised, either. Wherefore let no one be deluded by poets or mythologers into a mistaken belief of such things, nor let him suppose, when he thieves or is guilty of violence, that he is doing nothing base, but only what the Gods themselves do. For such tales are untrue and improbable; and he who steals or robs contrary to the law, is never either a God or the son of a God; of this the legislator ought to be better informed than all the poets put together."

"Happy is he and may he be forever happy, who is persuaded and listens to our words; but he who disobeys shall have to contend against the following law: If a man steal anything belonging to the public, whether that which he steals be much or little, he shall have the same punishment. For he who steals a little steals with the same wish as he who steals much, but with less power, and he who takes up a greater amount, not having deposited it, is wholly unjust. Wherefore the law is not disposed to inflict a less penalty on the one than on the other because his theft is less, but on the ground that the thief may possibly be in one case still curable, and may in another case be incurable. If any one convict in a court of law a stranger or a slave of a theft of public property, let the court determine what punishment he shall suffer, or what penalty he shall pay, bearing in mind that he is probably not incurable. But the citizen who has been brought up as our citizens will have been, if he be found guilty of robbing his country by fraud or violence, whether he be caught in the act or not, shall be punished with death; for he is incurable."
D: Refashioned:
Theft is contemptible, and robbery is shameless. None of the gods take pleasure in fraud and violence, nor have they ever practiced such things. Therefore, let no one be misled by poets or storytellers into believing otherwise, nor imagine that in stealing or committing violence he is merely doing what the gods themselves do. Such stories are false and absurd. Anyone who steals or robs in violation of the law is neither a god nor descended from gods -- and the legislator should understand this better than all the poets combined.

Blessed is the person who accepts and follows these words. But whoever disobeys must face this law: if someone steals public property, whether the amount is large or small, the punishment shall be the same. The petty thief steals with the same corrupt intention as the grand thief -- he simply has less opportunity. And whoever appropriates a larger sum that was not rightfully his is wholly unjust. The law does not impose a lighter sentence because the theft was smaller. The only relevant distinction is whether the thief can still be reformed or is beyond redemption.

If a foreigner or slave is convicted of stealing public property, the court shall determine an appropriate punishment, keeping in mind that such a person is probably still capable of reform. But a citizen -- someone raised with all the advantages of our education -- who is found guilty of defrauding or robbing the state, whether caught in the act or not, shall be put to death. Such a person is beyond cure.
D: Elegant:
Theft is contemptible, robbery shameless -- and no god endorses either. Let no one cite mythology to justify crime. The legislator knows better than all poets combined.

The law: all theft of public property receives equal punishment regardless of amount, because the criminal intent is identical -- only the opportunity differs. The sole distinction that matters is whether the thief can be reformed. Foreigners and slaves convicted of public theft may receive varied sentences, since reform remains possible. But a citizen raised under our laws who steals from the state -- by fraud or force, caught or uncaught -- shall die. He is incurable.
D: Summary: Plato attacks the mythological justification for theft, insisting that no god endorses fraud or violence. Public theft receives equal punishment regardless of amount, since the criminal intent is the same. The critical distinction is reformability: foreigners and slaves may be rehabilitated, but a properly educated citizen who steals from the state is deemed incurable and must be executed.
D: Distilled: All public theft is equally criminal in intent; the only question is whether the thief can be reformed, and a citizen who robs the state cannot be.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger continues legislating. The attack on poets justifying theft through divine myths echoes Republic 377d-378e, where Plato censors stories of gods behaving immorally. The principle that criminal intent matters more than the amount stolen is remarkably modern. The harsh distinction between citizens and non-citizens reflects Plato's view that education creates greater moral obligation -- a citizen who steals despite proper upbringing reveals irredeemable corruption. Compare Republic 409e-410a on the incurable soul.

D: Original: Now for expeditions of war much consideration and many laws are required; the great principle of all is that no one of either sex should be without a commander; nor should the mind of any one be accustomed to do anything, either in jest or earnest, of his own motion, but in war and in peace he should look to and follow his leader, even in the least things being under his guidance; for example, he should stand or move, or exercise, or wash, or take his meals, or get up in the night to keep guard and deliver messages when he is bidden; and in the hour of danger he should not pursue and not retreat except by order of his superior; and in a word, not teach the soul or accustom her to know or understand how to do anything apart from others. Of all soldiers the life should be always and in all things as far as possible in common and together; there neither is nor ever will be a higher, or better, or more scientific principle than this for the attainment of salvation and victory in war. And we ought in time of peace from youth upwards to practise this habit of commanding others, and of being commanded by others; anarchy should have no place in the life of man or of the beasts who are subject to man. I may add that all dances ought to be performed with view to military excellence; and agility and ease should be cultivated for the same object, and also endurance of the want of meats and drinks, and of winter cold and summer heat, and of hard couches; and, above all, care should be taken not to destroy the peculiar qualities of the head and the feet by surrounding them with extraneous coverings, and so hindering their natural growth of hair and soles.
D: Narrative:
"Now for expeditions of war much consideration and many laws are required; the great principle of all is that no one of either sex should be without a commander; nor should the mind of any one be accustomed to do anything, either in jest or earnest, of his own motion, but in war and in peace he should look to and follow his leader, even in the least things being under his guidance; for example, he should stand or move, or exercise, or wash, or take his meals, or get up in the night to keep guard and deliver messages when he is bidden; and in the hour of danger he should not pursue and not retreat except by order of his superior; and in a word, not teach the soul or accustom her to know or understand how to do anything apart from others."

"Of all soldiers the life should be always and in all things as far as possible in common and together; there neither is nor ever will be a higher, or better, or more scientific principle than this for the attainment of salvation and victory in war. And we ought in time of peace from youth upwards to practise this habit of commanding others, and of being commanded by others; anarchy should have no place in the life of man or of the beasts who are subject to man. I may add that all dances ought to be performed with a view to military excellence; and agility and ease should be cultivated for the same object, and also endurance of the want of meats and drinks, and of winter cold and summer heat, and of hard couches; and, above all, care should be taken not to destroy the peculiar qualities of the head and the feet by surrounding them with extraneous coverings, and so hindering their natural growth of hair and soles."
D: Refashioned:
Military campaigns require extensive deliberation and many laws. The overriding principle is this: no person, male or female, should ever be without a commander. No one's mind should grow accustomed to acting independently, whether in serious matters or trivial ones. In war and in peace alike, every person should look to a leader and follow orders, even in the smallest things -- when to stand, when to move, when to exercise, wash, eat, or rise in the night for guard duty and message-carrying. In battle, no one should pursue or retreat except on a superior's command. In short, the soul should never learn or practice doing anything independently of the group.

For soldiers above all, life should be lived in common as much as possible. There is no higher, better, or more scientifically sound principle for achieving survival and victory in war than collective discipline. This habit of commanding and being commanded should be practiced from youth onward during peacetime as well. Anarchy has no place in human life, nor even in the management of animals.

Furthermore, all dances should be designed to develop military fitness. Agility and grace should be cultivated for the same purpose, along with the ability to endure hunger, thirst, cold, heat, and uncomfortable sleeping conditions. Above all, the head and feet should not be weakened by unnecessary coverings that prevent the natural toughening of hair and soles.
D: Elegant:
The supreme military principle: no one should ever be without a commander. The mind must never grow accustomed to independent action. In war and peace, follow orders in everything -- movement, meals, guard duty, pursuit, retreat. The soul should not learn to act apart from the group.

Collective living is the highest principle for military success. Practice both commanding and obeying from youth. Anarchy belongs nowhere in human life. All physical training -- dance, endurance of hunger, cold, heat, discomfort -- should serve military readiness. Avoid softening the body with unnecessary protections.
D: Summary: The supreme principle of military organization is that no one should ever act without a commander's direction. Soldiers should live communally, and the habit of commanding and obeying should be practiced from youth in peacetime. All physical training, including dance, should serve military fitness and hardiness.
D: Distilled: The foundation of military excellence is absolute discipline under command, collective living, and physical hardening practiced from youth.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on military organization. The extreme emphasis on collective discipline and the abolition of independent action echoes Republic 416d-417b (communal life of guardians) but goes further -- extending military discipline to all of peacetime life. The insistence that no one should act independently, even in jest, represents Plato's most authoritarian statement on social organization. The detail about bare heads and feet reflects Spartan training practices that Plato admired. Compare Sparta's agoge system and the militarized education in Republic 537a-b.

D: Original: For these are the extremities, and of all the parts of the body, whether they are preserved or not is of the greatest consequence; the one is the servant of the whole body, and the other the master, in whom all the ruling senses are by nature set. Let the young man imagine that he hears in what has preceded the praises of the military life; the law shall be as follows:-He shall serve in war who is on the roll or appointed to some special service, and if any one is absent from cowardice, and without the leave of the generals; he shall be indicted before the military commanders for failure of service when the army comes home; and the soldiers shall be his judges; the heavy armed, and the cavalry, and the other arms of the service shall form separate courts; and they shall bring the heavy-armed before the heavy-armed, and the horsemen before the horsemen, and the others in like manner before their peers; and he who is found guilty shall never be allowed to compete for any prize of valour, or indict another for not serving on an expedition, or be an accuser at all in any military matters. Moreover, the court shall further determine what punishment he shall suffer, or what penalty he shall pay. When the suits for failure of service are completed, the leaders of the several kinds of troops shall again hold an assembly, and they shall adjudge the prizes of valour; and he who likes shall give judgment in his own branch of the service, saying nothing about any former expedition, nor producing any proof or witnesses to confirm his statement, but speaking only of the present occasion. The crown of victory shall be an olive wreath which the victor shall offer up the temple of any war-god whom he likes, adding an inscription for a testimony to last during life, that such an one has received the first, the second, or prize. If any one goes on an expedition, and returns home before the appointed time, when the generals. have not withdrawn the army, be shall be indicted for desertion before the same persons who took cognisance of failure of service, and if he be found guilty, the same punishment shall be inflicted on him.
D: Narrative:
"For these are the extremities, and of all the parts of the body, whether they are preserved or not is of the greatest consequence; the one is the servant of the whole body, and the other the master, in whom all the ruling senses are by nature set. Let the young man imagine that he hears in what has preceded the praises of the military life; the law shall be as follows:"

"He shall serve in war who is on the roll or appointed to some special service, and if any one is absent from cowardice, and without the leave of the generals, he shall be indicted before the military commanders for failure of service when the army comes home; and the soldiers shall be his judges; the heavy armed, and the cavalry, and the other arms of the service shall form separate courts; and they shall bring the heavy-armed before the heavy-armed, and the horsemen before the horsemen, and the others in like manner before their peers; and he who is found guilty shall never be allowed to compete for any prize of valour, or indict another for not serving on an expedition, or be an accuser at all in any military matters."

"Moreover, the court shall further determine what punishment he shall suffer, or what penalty he shall pay. When the suits for failure of service are completed, the leaders of the several kinds of troops shall again hold an assembly, and they shall adjudge the prizes of valour; and he who likes shall give judgment in his own branch of the service, saying nothing about any former expedition, nor producing any proof or witnesses to confirm his statement, but speaking only of the present occasion. The crown of victory shall be an olive wreath which the victor shall offer up at the temple of any war-god whom he likes, adding an inscription for a testimony to last during life, that such an one has received the first, the second, or third prize. If any one goes on an expedition, and returns home before the appointed time, when the generals have not withdrawn the army, he shall be indicted for desertion before the same persons who took cognisance of failure of service, and if he be found guilty, the same punishment shall be inflicted on him."
D: Refashioned:
The feet and head are the body's extremities, and their condition matters most of all. The feet serve the whole body, while the head is its master, housing all the governing senses. Let the young man take what has been said as praise of the military life. Now the law:

Every person on the military rolls or assigned to special duty must serve when called. Anyone who is absent out of cowardice, without leave from the generals, shall be prosecuted for failure of service when the army returns home. His fellow soldiers shall judge him -- infantry judged by infantry, cavalry by cavalry, each branch trying its own. Anyone found guilty shall be permanently barred from competing for military honors, from accusing others of failing to serve, and from bringing any charges in military matters.

The court shall also determine additional punishment or fines. After these trials are concluded, the commanders of each branch shall convene assemblies to award prizes for valor. Each soldier may speak only about the current campaign in his own branch of service -- no references to past expeditions, no outside proof or witnesses. The prize shall be an olive wreath, which the winner shall dedicate at the temple of whichever war-god he chooses, with an inscription recording whether he won first, second, or third place -- a testimony lasting his lifetime.

Anyone who leaves a campaign before the appointed time, without the army having been formally withdrawn by the generals, shall be prosecuted for desertion before the same courts that handle failure of service, and shall receive the same punishment if convicted.
D: Elegant:
The head and feet matter most -- one houses the senses that rule, the other serves the whole body. Now the military law:

Every enrolled person must serve. Cowardly absence without leave means prosecution by one's own branch of service upon return. The guilty are permanently barred from military honors and from bringing military accusations. Additional penalties are set by the court.

After these trials, valor prizes are awarded by branch. Soldiers speak only of the current campaign -- no past references, no outside witnesses. Winners receive olive wreaths dedicated at a war-god's temple with a lasting inscription. Desertion during an active campaign carries the same penalties as failure to serve.
D: Summary: Military service is compulsory for all enrolled citizens, with cowardly absence judged by one's peers in the same branch of service. The guilty lose all military standing. Valor prizes are awarded strictly for the current campaign, and desertion carries the same penalties as failure to serve.
D: Distilled: Cowardly absence from military duty is judged by one's peers and results in permanent disgrace, while valor is rewarded with olive wreaths and temple inscriptions.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger establishes military courts organized by branch of service -- a peer-judgment system. The principle that only fellow soldiers can judge military conduct reflects a practical wisdom about expertise. The prohibition against using past campaigns as evidence for valor awards emphasizes present merit over accumulated reputation. The permanent stripping of military standing from cowards -- including the right to accuse others -- creates a comprehensive civic death in military affairs. Compare Republic 468a-e on honors for military valor.

D: Original: Now every man who is engaged in any suit ought to be very careful of bringing false witness against any one, either intentionally or unintentionally, if he can help; for justice is truly said to be an honourable maiden, and falsehood is naturally repugnant to honour and justice. A witness ought to be very careful not to sift against justice, as for example in what relates to the throwing away of arms-he must distinguish the throwing them away when necessary, and not make that a reproach, or bring in action against some innocent person on that account. To make the distinction maybe difficult; but still the law must attempt to define the different kinds in some way. Let me endeavour to explain my meaning by an ancient tale:-If Patroclus had been brought to the tent still alive but without his arms (and this has happened to innumerable persons), the original arms, which the poet says were presented to Peleus by the Gods as a nuptial gift when he married. Thetis, remaining in the hands of Hector, then the base spirits of that day might have reproached the son of Menoetius with having cast away his arms. Again, there is the case of those who have been thrown down precipices and lost their arms; and of those who at sea, and in stormy places, have been suddenly overwhelmed by floods of water; and there are numberless things of this kind which one might adduce by way of extenuation, and with the view of justifying a misfortune which is easily misrepresented. We must, therefore, endeavour to divide to the best of our power the greater and more serious evil from the lesser. And a distinction may be drawn in the use of terms of reproach. A man does not always deserve to be called the thrower away of his shield; he may be only the loser of his arms.
D: Narrative:
"Now every man who is engaged in any suit ought to be very careful of bringing false witness against any one, either intentionally or unintentionally, if he can help; for justice is truly said to be an honourable maiden, and falsehood is naturally repugnant to honour and justice. A witness ought to be very careful not to sin against justice, as for example in what relates to the throwing away of arms -- he must distinguish the throwing them away when necessary, and not make that a reproach, or bring an action against some innocent person on that account."

"To make the distinction may be difficult; but still the law must attempt to define the different kinds in some way. Let me endeavour to explain my meaning by an ancient tale: If Patroclus had been brought to the tent still alive but without his arms (and this has happened to innumerable persons), the original arms, which the poet says were presented to Peleus by the Gods as a nuptial gift when he married Thetis, remaining in the hands of Hector, then the base spirits of that day might have reproached the son of Menoetius with having cast away his arms."

"Again, there is the case of those who have been thrown down precipices and lost their arms; and of those who at sea, and in stormy places, have been suddenly overwhelmed by floods of water; and there are numberless things of this kind which one might adduce by way of extenuation, and with the view of justifying a misfortune which is easily misrepresented. We must, therefore, endeavour to divide to the best of our power the greater and more serious evil from the lesser. And a distinction may be drawn in the use of terms of reproach. A man does not always deserve to be called the thrower away of his shield; he may be only the loser of his arms."
D: Refashioned:
Anyone involved in a legal proceeding must be extremely careful not to bear false witness -- intentionally or unintentionally. Justice is rightly called an honorable maiden, and falsehood is naturally offensive to both honor and justice. A witness must be particularly cautious not to sin against justice in matters like the loss of arms in battle. He must distinguish between losing arms through necessity and deliberately abandoning them, and must not bring accusations against innocent people who lost their weapons through no fault of their own.

Making this distinction may be difficult, but the law must try to define the different cases. Consider an ancient example: if Patroclus had been carried back to his tent alive but stripped of his armor -- as has happened to countless soldiers -- with the divine arms that the gods gave to Peleus at his wedding to Thetis now in Hector's hands, the petty-minded men of that era might have accused him of throwing away his weapons.

Or consider those who have fallen from cliffs and lost their arms, or those overwhelmed by sudden floods at sea or in storms. There are countless such circumstances that could explain the loss of weapons -- misfortunes easily misrepresented as cowardice. We must therefore do our best to distinguish the serious moral failure from the lesser misfortune. The very language of reproach should reflect this distinction: a man does not always deserve to be called one who "threw away" his shield. He may simply be one who "lost" his arms.
D: Elegant:
No one should bear false witness in legal proceedings. Justice demands careful distinction, especially in military matters like the loss of arms. Losing weapons through necessity is entirely different from abandoning them through cowardice.

Consider Patroclus: had he survived but lost his divine armor to Hector, petty minds would have called him a coward. Consider soldiers swept off cliffs or overwhelmed by floods. Misfortune is easily misrepresented as moral failure.

The law must distinguish the serious evil from the lesser one. Even our language should reflect this: there is a difference between "throwing away" a shield and "losing" one's arms.
D: Summary: Witnesses must be careful not to bring false accusations, especially in military matters. The law must distinguish between soldiers who lose their arms through misfortune and those who abandon them through cowardice. Even the language of reproach should reflect this difference -- losing one's shield is not the same as throwing it away.
D: Distilled: Justice requires distinguishing between losing one's arms through misfortune and abandoning them through cowardice, and even our language of blame should reflect this difference.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger uses Homer's Iliad to illustrate a legal principle. The Patroclus example (Iliad 16) is striking -- Plato, usually hostile to Homer, here uses epic narrative constructively to show how circumstances can be misread. The linguistic distinction between 'throwing away' (rhipsaspis) and 'losing' arms was a real concern in Greek military law; Archilochus famously boasted of throwing away his shield. This passage shows Plato's sensitivity to how language shapes moral judgment -- compare Cratylus on the power of names, and Statesman 291a-b on distinguishing similar-seeming acts.

D: Original: For there is a great or rather absolute difference between him who is deprived of his arms by a sufficient force, and him who voluntarily lets his shield go.
