The Athenian Stranger explained how: First, the official observer must be at least fifty years old and a man of distinguished reputation, particularly in military matters, so that he can serve as a worthy representative of the guardians of the law when visiting other cities. After sixty, he shall no longer serve in this capacity. He may conduct his observations for as many years within his ten-year term as he sees fit, and upon returning home he shall join the assembly that reviews the laws.

This assembly shall be a mixed body of older and younger men, required to meet daily between dawn and sunrise. Its membership shall include: first, those priests who have earned the rewards of virtue; second, the ten eldest guardians of the law; and the superintendent of education, both the current holder and all former holders of that office. Each senior member shall select a younger companion of his choosing, between thirty and forty years of age.

This assembly shall conduct ongoing deliberations about the laws of their own city and about any particularly good laws they learn of from elsewhere. They shall also pursue whatever branches of knowledge appear useful and would illuminate their review of the laws -- or whose absence would leave legal questions obscure and uncertain. Whatever knowledge the elders deem valuable, the younger members shall study with full dedication. And if any young man brought into the assembly proves unworthy, the entire body shall hold his sponsor accountable.
D: Elegant:
The observer must be at least fifty, of military distinction, and shall serve until sixty. On returning, he joins the law-review assembly.

This assembly meets daily at dawn. Members: priests rewarded for virtue, the ten eldest law-guardians, the superintendent of education (current and former), and younger companions (ages thirty to forty) chosen by each elder.

They discuss their own laws, noteworthy foreign laws, and any knowledge that illuminates legal questions. Elders direct the younger members' studies. If any young member proves unworthy, his sponsor bears the blame.
D: Summary: Official observers aged fifty to sixty study foreign institutions and report to a dawn assembly that reviews the laws. This assembly combines elder priests, senior law-guardians, and education superintendents with younger companions aged thirty to forty. They study their own laws, foreign laws, and relevant knowledge, with elders directing younger members' education.
D: Distilled: The law-review assembly combines experienced elders with selected younger companions who meet daily at dawn to study both domestic and foreign laws and the knowledge needed to understand them.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger describes what will become the Nocturnal Council (nukterinos sullogos) -- so called because it meets before dawn. This is the Laws' equivalent of the Republic's philosopher-kings, but institutionalized as a deliberative body rather than individual rulers. The mentor-companion structure (elder choosing younger member) mirrors the Academy's own educational model. The emphasis on continuous study of law and knowledge connects to the Republic's requirement that rulers study dialectic (Republic 532a-535a). The daily meeting schedule -- dawn to sunrise -- is both practical (before the business day) and symbolic (light emerging from darkness, knowledge from inquiry).

D: Original: The rest of the city shall watch over those among the young men who distinguish themselves, having an eye upon them, and especially honouring them if they succeed, but dishonouring them above the rest if they turn out to be inferior. This is the assembly to which he who has visited the institutions of other men, on his return home shall straightway go, and if he have discovered any one who has anything to say about the enactment of laws or education or nurture, or if he have himself made any observations, let him communicate his discoveries to the whole assembly. And if he be seen to have come home neither better nor worse, let him be praised at any rate for his enthusiasm; and if he be much better, let him be praised so much the more; and not only while he lives but after his death let the assembly honour him with fitting honours. But if on his return home he appear to have been corrupted, pretending to be wise when he is not, let him hold no communication with any one, whether young or old; and if he will hearken to the rulers, then he shall be permitted to live as a private individual; but if he will not, let him die, if he be convicted in a court of law of interfering about education and the laws, And if he deserve to be indicted, and none of the magistrates indict him, let that be counted as a disgrace to them when the rewards of virtue are decided.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "The rest of the city shall watch over those among the young men who distinguish themselves, having an eye upon them, and especially honouring them if they succeed, but dishonouring them above the rest if they turn out to be inferior. This is the assembly to which he who has visited the institutions of other men, on his return home shall straightway go, and if he have discovered any one who has anything to say about the enactment of laws or education or nurture, or if he have himself made any observations, let him communicate his discoveries to the whole assembly."

"And if he be seen to have come home neither better nor worse, let him be praised at any rate for his enthusiasm; and if he be much better, let him be praised so much the more; and not only while he lives but after his death let the assembly honour him with fitting honours. But if on his return home he appear to have been corrupted, pretending to be wise when he is not, let him hold no communication with any one, whether young or old; and if he will hearken to the rulers, then he shall be permitted to live as a private individual; but if he will not, let him die, if he be convicted in a court of law of interfering about education and the laws. And if he deserve to be indicted, and none of the magistrates indict him, let that be counted as a disgrace to them when the rewards of virtue are decided."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: The rest of the city shall keep watch over those young men who distinguish themselves in the assembly, honoring them especially if they succeed, and holding them in particular contempt if they prove inferior.

This is the assembly to which any citizen-observer must report immediately upon returning home. If he has discovered anyone abroad with insights about lawmaking, education, or child-rearing, or if he has made observations of his own, he must share his findings with the entire body.

If a returning observer appears neither better nor worse for his travels, he should at least be praised for his dedication. If he has clearly improved, he should receive greater praise -- both during his life and after his death, with fitting honors from the assembly. But if he appears to have been corrupted abroad, pretending to wisdom he does not possess, he must be forbidden from communicating with anyone, young or old. If he accepts the rulers' authority and lives quietly as a private citizen, he may be allowed to live. But if he refuses and is convicted of meddling in matters of education and law, he shall die. And if magistrates fail to indict someone who deserves prosecution, that failure shall count against them when the prizes of virtue are awarded.
D: Elegant:
The city watches the assembly's young members -- honoring success, shaming failure. Returning observers must report their findings immediately.

Those who return unchanged receive praise for effort. Those who return improved receive greater praise, both in life and death. But those who return corrupted -- pretending wisdom they lack -- must be silenced entirely. If they accept private life, they may live. If they persist in meddling with education and laws, they die. Magistrates who fail to bring such prosecutions are themselves disgraced.
D: Summary: Returning citizen-observers must report their findings to the assembly. Those unchanged are praised for dedication; those improved receive higher honors. But those corrupted abroad who pretend to false wisdom must be silenced and, if they refuse to withdraw from public life, executed. Magistrates who fail to prosecute such cases are themselves disgraced.
D: Distilled: Foreign travel should make observers better or at worst leave them unchanged; those who return corrupted and refuse to withdraw from public life face death.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The severe treatment of those 'corrupted' abroad reflects Plato's deep anxiety about sophistic influence -- the pretense of wisdom being more dangerous than simple ignorance (compare Apology 21d-22e on Socrates' discovery that those who think themselves wise are worst off). The accountability chain extending to magistrates who fail to prosecute creates systemic responsibility. The principle that even dedication without results deserves praise shows a more generous side of Plato's thinking about human effort. Compare Republic 539b-d on the danger of young minds exposed to dialectic prematurely -- here the danger is foreign philosophical influence on insufficiently prepared minds.

D: Original: Let such be the character of the person who goes abroad, and let him go abroad under these conditions. In the next place, the stranger who comes from abroad should be received in a friendly spirit. Now there are four kinds of strangers, of whom we must make some mention-the first is he who comes and stays throughout the summer; this class are like birds of passage, taking wing in pursuit of commerce, and flying over the sea to other cities, while the season lasts; he shall be received in market-places and harbours and public buildings, near the city but outside, by those magistrates who are appointed to superintend these matters; and they shall take care that a stranger, whoever he be, duly receives justice; but he shall not be allowed to make any innovation. They shall hold the intercourse with him which is necessary, and this shall be as little as possible. The second kind is just a spectator who comes to see with his eyes and hear with his ears the festivals of the Muses; such ought to have entertainment provided them at the temples by hospitable persons, and the priests and ministers of the temples should see and attend to them. But they should not remain more than a reasonable time; let them see and hear that for the sake of which they came, and then go away, neither having suffered nor done any harm. The priests shall be their judges, if any of them receive or do any wrong up to the sum of fifty drachmae, but if any greater charge be brought, in such cases the suit shall come before the wardens of the agora.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Let such be the character of the person who goes abroad, and let him go abroad under these conditions. In the next place, the stranger who comes from abroad should be received in a friendly spirit. Now there are four kinds of strangers, of whom we must make some mention -- the first is he who comes and stays throughout the summer; this class are like birds of passage, taking wing in pursuit of commerce, and flying over the sea to other cities, while the season lasts; he shall be received in market-places and harbours and public buildings, near the city but outside, by those magistrates who are appointed to superintend these matters; and they shall take care that a stranger, whoever he be, duly receives justice; but he shall not be allowed to make any innovation. They shall hold the intercourse with him which is necessary, and this shall be as little as possible."

"The second kind is just a spectator who comes to see with his eyes and hear with his ears the festivals of the Muses; such ought to have entertainment provided them at the temples by hospitable persons, and the priests and ministers of the temples should see and attend to them. But they should not remain more than a reasonable time; let them see and hear that for the sake of which they came, and then go away, neither having suffered nor done any harm. The priests shall be their judges, if any of them receive or do any wrong up to the sum of fifty drachmae, but if any greater charge be brought, in such cases the suit shall come before the wardens of the agora."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: So much for the character and conditions of citizens traveling abroad. Now regarding the foreigners who come to us -- they should be received in a friendly spirit. There are four kinds of foreign visitors, each requiring different treatment.

The first kind are commercial travelers who come for the season, like migratory birds taking flight in pursuit of trade, flying over the sea from city to city while the weather permits. These shall be received in marketplaces, harbors, and public buildings located near but outside the city proper, under the supervision of the magistrates appointed for this purpose. These officials shall ensure that every foreign trader receives justice, but no trader shall be permitted to introduce any innovations. Official contact with them shall be limited to what is strictly necessary, and kept to a minimum.

The second kind are cultural visitors -- spectators who come to see and hear the festivals of the Muses. These should be offered hospitality at the temples, provided by generous hosts, and the priests and temple officials should attend to their needs. They should not stay beyond a reasonable time. Let them experience what they came for and then depart, having neither suffered nor caused any harm. For disputes involving such visitors up to fifty drachmae, the priests shall serve as judges. For larger claims, the case goes before the wardens of the marketplace.
D: Elegant:
Four kinds of foreign visitors require different treatment. First: seasonal traders, like migratory birds pursuing commerce. They are received outside the city in commercial areas, guaranteed justice, but forbidden from introducing innovations. Contact is kept to a minimum.

Second: cultural visitors attending festivals. They receive temple hospitality from generous hosts, with priests attending to them. They stay only as long as needed, departing without harm given or received. Minor disputes (up to fifty drachmae) are judged by priests; larger ones by marketplace wardens.
D: Summary: Foreign visitors fall into four categories receiving different treatment. Commercial traders are housed outside the city and kept at arm's length. Cultural visitors attending festivals receive temple hospitality but should not overstay. Each category has appropriate judicial authorities for resolving disputes.
D: Distilled: Foreign visitors are categorized by purpose -- commercial traders kept at the city's edge, cultural visitors hosted at temples -- with contact carefully calibrated to minimize disruption.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The fourfold classification of strangers is one of the Laws' most systematic policy frameworks. The image of traders as 'birds of passage' is vivid and slightly dismissive -- commerce is tolerated but not embraced, consistent with Laws 704d-705a on the corrupting influence of maritime trade. The physical segregation of traders outside the city walls reflects the principle that commercial activity should not penetrate the civic core. The more generous reception of cultural visitors shows Plato's hierarchy: cultural exchange ranks above commercial. Compare Republic 371c-372a on the necessity of trade and its moral risks.

D: Original: The third kind of stranger is he who comes on some public business from another land, and is to be received with public honours. He is to be received only by the generals and commanders of horse and foot, and the host by whom he is entertained, in conjunction with the Prytanes, shall have the sole charge of what concerns him. There is a fourth dass of persons answering to our spectators, who come from another land to look at ours. In the first place, such visits will be rare, and the visitor should be at least fifty years of age; he may possibly be wanting to see something that is rich and rare in other states, or himself to show something in like manner to another city. Let such an one, then, go unbidden to the doors of the wise and rich, being one of them himself: let him go, for example, to the house of the superintendent of education, confident that he is a fitting guest of such a host, or let him go to the house of some of those who have gained the prize of virtue and hold discourse with them, both learning from them, and also teaching them; and when he has seen and heard all, he shall depart, as a friend taking leave of friends, and be honoured by them with gifts and suitable tributes of respect. These are the customs, according to which our city should receive all strangers of either sex who come from other countries, and should send forth her own citizens, showing respect to Zeus, the God of hospitality, not forbidding strangers at meals and sacrifices, as is the manner which prevails among the children of the Nile, nor driving them away by savage proclamations.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "The third kind of stranger is he who comes on some public business from another land, and is to be received with public honours. He is to be received only by the generals and commanders of horse and foot, and the host by whom he is entertained, in conjunction with the Prytanes, shall have the sole charge of what concerns him."

"There is a fourth class of persons answering to our spectators, who come from another land to look at ours. In the first place, such visits will be rare, and the visitor should be at least fifty years of age; he may possibly be wanting to see something that is rich and rare in other states, or himself to show something in like manner to another city. Let such an one, then, go unbidden to the doors of the wise and rich, being one of them himself: let him go, for example, to the house of the superintendent of education, confident that he is a fitting guest of such a host, or let him go to the house of some of those who have gained the prize of virtue and hold discourse with them, both learning from them, and also teaching them; and when he has seen and heard all, he shall depart, as a friend taking leave of friends, and be honoured by them with gifts and suitable tributes of respect."

"These are the customs, according to which our city should receive all strangers of either sex who come from other countries, and should send forth her own citizens, showing respect to Zeus, the God of hospitality, not forbidding strangers at meals and sacrifices, as is the manner which prevails among the children of the Nile, nor driving them away by savage proclamations."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: The third kind of foreign visitor comes on official state business and must be received with public honors. Only generals and commanders of cavalry and infantry shall receive him, and his host -- in coordination with the Prytanes -- shall have sole responsibility for his care.

The fourth kind corresponds to our own citizen-observers: distinguished visitors from abroad who come to examine our institutions. Such visits will be rare, and the visitor should be at least fifty years old. He may wish to observe something remarkable in our state, or to share something valuable from his own. Such a person -- being himself wise and accomplished -- should go directly and without formal invitation to the doors of the wise and distinguished. He might visit the superintendent of education, confident of being a worthy guest, or seek out holders of the virtue prize to engage in mutual discourse -- both learning from them and teaching them. When he has seen and heard everything, he shall depart as a friend leaving friends, honored with gifts and fitting marks of respect.

These are the customs by which our city should receive all foreign visitors, male and female, and send forth its own citizens -- showing respect to Zeus, the god of hospitality. We shall not exclude strangers from meals and sacrifices, as the Egyptians do, nor drive them away with savage proclamations.
D: Elegant:
Third: official state visitors, received with public honors by military commanders and hosted under the Prytanes' oversight.

Fourth: distinguished philosophical visitors -- rare, at least fifty years old -- who come to study our institutions or share their own wisdom. These may go directly to the homes of the education superintendent or virtue-prize holders, engaging in mutual learning and teaching. They depart as friends, honored with gifts.

In all cases, the city respects Zeus the god of hospitality -- neither excluding strangers from common meals like the Egyptians, nor banishing them with cruel edicts.
D: Summary: Official state visitors are received with military honors. Philosophical visitors -- rare, distinguished, over fifty -- may freely approach the city's wisest citizens for mutual intellectual exchange. In all cases, the city honors Zeus the god of hospitality, avoiding both Egyptian exclusionism and Spartan xenophobia.
D: Distilled: The highest category of foreign visitor is the philosopher-observer, who is welcomed into the homes of the wise for mutual learning as an honored friend.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the fourfold visitor classification. The fourth category -- philosophical visitors -- receives the warmest welcome, revealing Plato's value hierarchy: philosophical exchange ranks above commerce, tourism, and even diplomacy. The 'unbidden' access to the wise echoes the Academy's open-door ethos and Socrates' practice of engaging anyone in dialogue. The reference to 'children of the Nile' (Egyptians) and their exclusionary practices provides a negative model, while the invocation of Zeus Xenios (god of hospitality) frames the entire policy in religious terms. Compare Statesman 311b-c on the mixing of different character types as essential to good governance.

D: Original: When a man becomes surety, let him give the security in a distinct form, acknowledging the whole transaction in a written document, and in the presence of not less than three witnesses if the sum be under a thousand drachmae, and of not less than five witnesses if the sum be above a thousand drachmae. The agent of a dishonest or untrustworthy seller shall himself be responsible; both the agent and the principal shall be equally liable. If a person wishes to find anything in the house of another, he shall enter naked, or wearing only a short tunic and without a girdle, having first taken an oath by the customary Gods that he expects to find it there; he shall then make his search, and the other shall throw open his house and allow him to search things both sealed and unsealed.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "When a man becomes surety, let him give the security in a distinct form, acknowledging the whole transaction in a written document, and in the presence of not less than three witnesses if the sum be under a thousand drachmae, and of not less than five witnesses if the sum be above a thousand drachmae. The agent of a dishonest or untrustworthy seller shall himself be responsible; both the agent and the principal shall be equally liable."

"If a person wishes to find anything in the house of another, he shall enter naked, or wearing only a short tunic and without a girdle, having first taken an oath by the customary Gods that he expects to find it there; he shall then make his search, and the other shall throw open his house and allow him to search things both sealed and unsealed."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: When someone acts as surety, he must formalize the guarantee in a clear written document, with at least three witnesses for amounts under one thousand drachmae and at least five witnesses for amounts above that sum. An agent who represents a dishonest or unreliable seller shall himself be held responsible -- both the agent and the seller are equally liable.

If a person wishes to search another's house for missing property, he must enter either naked or wearing only a short tunic without a belt, having first sworn an oath to the customary gods that he believes the item will be found there. He may then conduct his search, and the homeowner must open the entire house for inspection, including both sealed and unsealed items.
D: Elegant:
Surety agreements must be documented in writing with three witnesses for smaller sums and five for larger. Agents of dishonest sellers share equal liability with their principals.

To search another's home for missing property, one must enter wearing minimal clothing and no belt, after swearing an oath that the item is expected to be found there. The homeowner must allow a complete search of all property, sealed and unsealed.
D: Summary: Financial surety requires written documentation with appropriate witnesses, and agents share liability with dishonest principals. Searches of private homes require the searcher to enter in minimal clothing (to prevent planting evidence) after swearing an oath, with the homeowner obligated to allow full access.
D: Distilled: Commercial guarantees require written documentation and shared liability, while property searches require the searcher to prove his good faith by entering virtually unclothed.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on commercial law and search procedures. The requirement to search while naked or nearly so is a practical anti-fraud measure -- preventing the searcher from smuggling in items to plant as 'evidence.' This detail reveals the Laws' intensely practical orientation compared to the Republic's more abstract approach. The shared liability of agents and principals addresses a real problem in ancient commerce where agents might serve as shields for dishonest sellers. The graduated witness requirements based on transaction size show a proportional approach to legal formality. Compare Laws 849a-850c on marketplace regulations.

D: Original: And if a person will not allow the searcher to make his search, he who is prevented shall go to law with him, estimating the value of the goods after which he is searching, and if the other be convicted he shall pay twice the value of the article. If the master be absent from home, the dwellers in the house shall let him search the unsealed property, and on the sealed property the searcher shall set another seal, and shall appoint any one whom he likes to guard them during five days; and if the master of the house be absent during a longer time, he shall take with him the wardens of the city, and so make his search, opening the sealed property as well as the unsealed, and then, together with the members of the family and the wardens of the city, he shall seal them up again as they were before. There shall be a limit of time in the case of disputed things, and he who has had possession of them during a certain time shall no longer be liable to be disturbed. As to houses and lands there can be no dispute in this state of ours; but if a man has any other possessions which he has used and openly shown in the city and in the agora and in the temples, and no one has put in a claim to them, and some one says that he was looking for them during this time, and the possessor is proved to have made no concealment, if they have continued for a year, the one having the goods and the other looking for them, the claim of the seeker shall not be allowed after the expiration of the year; or if he does not use or show the lost property in the market or in the city, but only in the country, and no one offers himself as the owner during five years, at the expiration of the five years the claim shall be barred for ever after; or if he uses them in the city but within the house, then the appointed time of claiming the goods shall be three years, or ten years if he has them in the country in private.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "And if a person will not allow the searcher to make his search, he who is prevented shall go to law with him, estimating the value of the goods after which he is searching, and if the other be convicted he shall pay twice the value of the article. If the master be absent from home, the dwellers in the house shall let him search the unsealed property, and on the sealed property the searcher shall set another seal, and shall appoint any one whom he likes to guard them during five days; and if the master of the house be absent during a longer time, he shall take with him the wardens of the city, and so make his search, opening the sealed property as well as the unsealed, and then, together with the members of the family and the wardens of the city, he shall seal them up again as they were before."

"There shall be a limit of time in the case of disputed things, and he who has had possession of them during a certain time shall no longer be liable to be disturbed. As to houses and lands there can be no dispute in this state of ours; but if a man has any other possessions which he has used and openly shown in the city and in the agora and in the temples, and no one has put in a claim to them, and some one says that he was looking for them during this time, and the possessor is proved to have made no concealment, if they have continued for a year, the one having the goods and the other looking for them, the claim of the seeker shall not be allowed after the expiration of the year; or if he does not use or show the lost property in the market or in the city, but only in the country, and no one offers himself as the owner during five years, at the expiration of the five years the claim shall be barred for ever after; or if he uses them in the city but within the house, then the appointed time of claiming the goods shall be three years, or ten years if he has them in the country in private."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: If someone refuses to allow a search, the person denied may bring a lawsuit, estimating the value of the property sought. If the one who refused the search is convicted, he must pay double the article's value.

If the homeowner is absent, the household members must permit a search of unsealed property. Sealed items shall receive an additional seal from the searcher, who may appoint a guard of his choosing for five days. If the homeowner's absence extends beyond that, the searcher shall bring the city wardens and conduct a full search of both sealed and unsealed property, afterward resealing everything in its original condition together with the family members and wardens.

Statutes of limitation shall apply to disputed property. Houses and land cannot be disputed in our state, since ownership is fixed. For other possessions: if someone has openly used and displayed goods in the city, marketplace, and temples, without concealment, and no one has claimed them for a full year, the claim is forever barred. If the possessor uses them only in the countryside without anyone claiming ownership for five years, the claim is similarly barred. If they are used within the city but kept inside the house, the limitation period is three years. If kept privately in the country, the period is ten years.
D: Elegant:
Refusing a search brings a lawsuit; conviction means paying double. When the homeowner is absent, unsealed property is searched immediately; sealed property is guarded for five days, then searched with city wardens if the absence continues.

Statutes of limitation: property openly displayed in public -- one year. Property used only in the countryside -- five years. Property kept within a city house -- three years. Property kept privately in the country -- ten years. After these periods, all claims are barred. Real estate cannot be disputed at all, since land ownership is fixed.
D: Summary: Refusing a property search results in double damages. Absent homeowners' sealed property is initially guarded but can eventually be searched with official oversight. Statutes of limitation protect possessors of disputed goods after one to ten years depending on how publicly the goods were used, while real estate ownership is permanently fixed.
D: Distilled: The more publicly goods are displayed, the shorter the window for ownership claims; concealment extends the statute of limitations, reflecting the principle that openness deserves legal protection.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The graduated statute of limitations based on the visibility of possession is a sophisticated legal principle -- public display creates a presumption of legitimate ownership more quickly than private use. The fixed ownership of land and houses reflects Magnesia's foundational principle of equal and inalienable land allotments (Laws 740b-741e). The procedural detail about resealing property shows concern for protecting both the searcher's rights and the homeowner's property -- a balance between investigative power and personal security that modern legal systems still struggle with. Compare the Fourth Amendment's protections against unreasonable searches.

D: Original:
And if he has them in another land, there shall be no limit of time or prescription, but whenever the owner finds them he may claim them.

If any one prevents another by force from being present at a trial, whether a principal party or his witnesses; if the person prevented be a slave, whether his own or belonging to another, the suit shall be incomplete and invalid; but if he who is prevented be a freeman, besides the suit being incomplete, the other who has prevented him shall be imprisoned for a year, and shall be prosecuted for kidnapping by any one who pleases. And if any one hinders by force a rival competitor in gymnastic or music, or any other sort of contest, from being present at the contest, let him who has a mind inform the presiding judges, and they shall liberate him who is desirous of competing; and if they are not able, and he who hinders the other from competing wins the prize, then they shall give the prize of victory to him who is prevented, and inscribe him as the conqueror in any temples which he pleases; and he who hinders the other shall not be permitted to make any offering or inscription having reference to that contest, and in any case he shall be liable for damages, whether he be defeated or whether he conquer.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "And if he has them in another land, there shall be no limit of time or prescription, but whenever the owner finds them he may claim them."

"If any one prevents another by force from being present at a trial, whether a principal party or his witnesses; if the person prevented be a slave, whether his own or belonging to another, the suit shall be incomplete and invalid; but if he who is prevented be a freeman, besides the suit being incomplete, the other who has prevented him shall be imprisoned for a year, and shall be prosecuted for kidnapping by any one who pleases."

"And if any one hinders by force a rival competitor in gymnastic or music, or any other sort of contest, from being present at the contest, let him who has a mind inform the presiding judges, and they shall liberate him who is desirous of competing; and if they are not able, and he who hinders the other from competing wins the prize, then they shall give the prize of victory to him who is prevented, and inscribe him as the conqueror in any temples which he pleases; and he who hinders the other shall not be permitted to make any offering or inscription having reference to that contest, and in any case he shall be liable for damages, whether he be defeated or whether he conquer."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: If the goods are in another land entirely, there shall be no statute of limitations -- the owner may claim them whenever he discovers them.

If anyone uses force to prevent another person from attending a trial, whether the person prevented is a principal party or a witness: if the person prevented is a slave (whether the obstructor's own slave or someone else's), the trial shall be declared incomplete and invalid. If the person prevented is a free citizen, the trial is likewise incomplete, but additionally the obstructor shall be imprisoned for one year and may be prosecuted for kidnapping by any citizen who wishes to bring the charge.

If anyone uses force to prevent a rival competitor from participating in an athletic, musical, or any other kind of contest, anyone may report this to the presiding judges, who shall free the person being hindered. If the judges cannot free him, and the obstructor wins the prize, the victory shall be awarded instead to the person who was prevented from competing, and his name shall be inscribed as the winner in whatever temples he chooses. The obstructor shall be forbidden from making any offering or inscription related to that contest, and shall be liable for damages whether he won or lost.
D: Elegant:
Property held in foreign lands has no statute of limitations -- the owner may claim it whenever found.

Forcibly preventing someone from attending a trial invalidates the proceedings. If the prevented person is free, the obstructor also faces a year's imprisonment and kidnapping charges.

Forcibly preventing a competitor from a contest: judges must intervene. If they cannot, and the obstructor wins, the victory is awarded to the prevented competitor. The obstructor is banned from contest-related honors and liable for damages regardless of outcome.
D: Summary: Property held abroad has no statute of limitations. Forcibly preventing trial attendance invalidates proceedings and brings imprisonment and kidnapping charges. Forcibly blocking a competitor from contests results in the victory being awarded to the prevented party, with the obstructor banned from all related honors.
D: Distilled: Force used to obstruct justice or fair competition is punished by invalidating the results and transferring the honors to the obstructed party.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on obstruction of justice and fair competition. The principle that forcible prevention of trial attendance invalidates the entire proceeding is a strong protection of due process. The competition rules are equally striking -- victory won through obstruction is not merely nullified but transferred to the victim, creating a powerful disincentive. The distinction between slave and free witnesses reflects the unfortunate reality of ancient Greek legal status, where slave testimony was already given less weight and sometimes required torture to be admissible. Compare Laws 937a-b on the general principle of fair dealing in all contests.

D: Original: If any one knowingly receives anything which has been stolen, he shall undergo the same punishment as the thief, and if a man receives an exile he shall be punished with death. Every man should regard the friend and enemy of the state as his own friend and enemy; and if any one makes peace or war with another on his own account, and without the authority of the state, he, like the receiver of the exile, shall undergo the penalty of death. And if any fraction of the City declare war or peace against any, the generals shall indict the authors of this proceeding, and if they are convicted death shall be the penalty. Those who serve their country ought to serve without receiving gifts, and there ought to be no excusing or approving the saying, "Men should receive gifts as the reward of good, but not of evil deeds"; for to know which we are doing, and to stand fast by our knowledge, is no easy matter. The safest course is to obey the law which says, "Do no service for a bribe," and let him who disobeys, if he be convicted, simply die. With a view to taxation, for various reasons, every man ought to have had his property valued: and the tribesmen should likewise bring a register of the yearly produce to the wardens of the country, that in this way there may be two valuations; and the public officers may use annuary whichever on consideration they deem the best, whether they prefer to take a certain portion of the whole value, or of the annual revenue, after subtracting what is paid to the common tables.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "If any one knowingly receives anything which has been stolen, he shall undergo the same punishment as the thief, and if a man receives an exile he shall be punished with death. Every man should regard the friend and enemy of the state as his own friend and enemy; and if any one makes peace or war with another on his own account, and without the authority of the state, he, like the receiver of the exile, shall undergo the penalty of death."

"And if any fraction of the City declare war or peace against any, the generals shall indict the authors of this proceeding, and if they are convicted death shall be the penalty. Those who serve their country ought to serve without receiving gifts, and there ought to be no excusing or approving the saying, 'Men should receive gifts as the reward of good, but not of evil deeds'; for to know which we are doing, and to stand fast by our knowledge, is no easy matter. The safest course is to obey the law which says, 'Do no service for a bribe,' and let him who disobeys, if he be convicted, simply die."

"With a view to taxation, for various reasons, every man ought to have had his property valued: and the tribesmen should likewise bring a register of the yearly produce to the wardens of the country, that in this way there may be two valuations; and the public officers may use annually whichever on consideration they deem the best, whether they prefer to take a certain portion of the whole value, or of the annual revenue, after subtracting what is paid to the common tables."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Anyone who knowingly receives stolen property shall be punished the same as the thief. Anyone who shelters an exile shall be punished with death.

Every citizen must treat the state's friends as his own friends and the state's enemies as his own enemies. If anyone makes peace or war on his own authority, without the state's sanction, he shall suffer death -- the same penalty as harboring an exile. If any faction of the city declares war or peace independently, the generals shall prosecute the ringleaders, and conviction means death.

Those who serve their country should serve without accepting gifts. The popular saying -- "Accept gifts for good deeds, but not for bad ones" -- should not be tolerated or approved. The difficulty is this: it is never easy to know with certainty whether one's actions are truly good or bad, or to remain firm in that knowledge. The safest course is simply to obey the law that says: "Perform no public service for a bribe." Anyone convicted of violating this shall die.

Regarding taxation: every citizen should have his total property valued, and each tribe should also file a register of annual produce with the country wardens. This creates two valuations, allowing public officials to choose annually whichever basis they find more appropriate -- whether to tax a portion of total property value or of yearly revenue, after deducting contributions to the common meals.
D: Elegant:
Receiving stolen goods brings the thief's punishment. Harboring exiles means death. Every citizen must treat the state's friends and enemies as his own -- unauthorized peace or war, whether by individuals or factions, is punishable by death.

Public servants must accept no gifts. The saying 'accept gifts for good deeds only' is dangerous because we rarely know with certainty whether our actions are good or bad. Safer to follow the absolute rule: no bribes, on pain of death.

For taxation: dual valuations -- total property value and annual revenue -- allow officials to choose the most appropriate tax basis each year, after deducting common-meal contributions.
D: Summary: Receiving stolen goods carries the thief's penalty; harboring exiles or conducting unauthorized foreign policy means death. Public servants must accept no gifts whatsoever, since the distinction between good and bad service is unreliable. Taxation is based on dual property valuations to give officials flexibility.
D: Distilled: Because we cannot reliably distinguish good service from bad, the only safe rule is absolute: no public servant may accept any gift, on pain of death.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger covers several topics rapidly. The ban on private diplomacy reflects Plato's insistence on state sovereignty over foreign affairs -- any unauthorized peace or war-making is treason. The argument against selective gift-giving is philosophically subtle: rather than trusting moral judgment to distinguish acceptable from unacceptable gifts, Plato imposes an absolute ban because human self-knowledge is unreliable. This connects to the Socratic theme that genuine self-knowledge is rare (Apology 21d). The dual taxation system shows practical flexibility unusual in Platonic political theory. Compare Republic 416d-e on guardians owning no gold or silver.

D: Original: Touching offerings to the Gods, a moderate man should observe moderation in what he offers. Now the land and the hearth of the house of all men is sacred to all Gods; wherefore let no man dedicate them a second time to the Gods. Gold and silver, whether possessed by private persons or in temples, are in other cities provocative of envy, and ivory, the product of a dead body, is not a proper offering; brass and iron, again, are instruments of war; but of wood let a man bring what offerings he likes, provided it be a single block, and in like manner of stone, to the public temples; of woven work let him not offer more than one woman can execute in a month. White is a colour suitable to the Gods, especially in woven works, but dyes should only be used for the adornments of war. The most divine of gifts are birds and images, and they should be such as one painter can execute in a single day. And let all other offerings follow a similar rule.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Touching offerings to the Gods, a moderate man should observe moderation in what he offers. Now the land and the hearth of the house of all men is sacred to all Gods; wherefore let no man dedicate them a second time to the Gods. Gold and silver, whether possessed by private persons or in temples, are in other cities provocative of envy, and ivory, the product of a dead body, is not a proper offering; brass and iron, again, are instruments of war; but of wood let a man bring what offerings he likes, provided it be a single block, and in like manner of stone, to the public temples; of woven work let him not offer more than one woman can execute in a month."

"White is a colour suitable to the Gods, especially in woven works, but dyes should only be used for the adornments of war. The most divine of gifts are birds and images, and they should be such as one painter can execute in a single day. And let all other offerings follow a similar rule."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Regarding offerings to the gods, a moderate person should practice moderation in what he gives. The land and hearth of every household are already sacred to all the gods, so no one should dedicate them a second time.

Gold and silver -- whether in private hands or in temples -- provoke envy in other cities and are inappropriate offerings. Ivory, being the product of a dead creature, is not a proper gift. Bronze and iron are instruments of war and equally unsuitable. But offerings of wood may be freely given, provided each is a single block; likewise stone offerings to the public temples. Woven offerings should not exceed what one woman can produce in a month.

White is the color appropriate for the gods, especially in woven works; dyes should be reserved for military decorations. The most divine gifts are birds and painted images, and these should be limited to what a single painter can complete in one day. Let all other offerings follow this same principle of moderation.
D: Elegant: Offerings to the gods should be moderate. Land and hearths are already sacred -- do not dedicate them again. Gold and silver provoke envy; ivory comes from death; bronze and iron serve war. Acceptable: single blocks of wood or stone, woven works limited to one month's labor by one woman, paintings limited to one day's work by one painter. White for the gods; dyes for war only. Birds and images are the most divine gifts.
D: Summary: Temple offerings must be moderate and appropriate: no gold, silver, ivory, or metals of war. Acceptable offerings include single blocks of wood or stone, modest woven works in white, and small paintings or birds. The principle is that offerings should reflect individual moderation, not competitive display.
D: Distilled: Divine offerings should reflect moderation and purity -- no precious metals, no products of death, nothing that could provoke envy or competitive display.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on religious offerings. This passage embodies Plato's deep suspicion of material wealth in religious contexts -- a theme from Euthyphro 14c-15a (piety is not a commercial transaction with the gods) and Republic 416e (guardians must avoid gold). The prohibition on ivory as a 'product of a dead body' adds a purity concern unique to the Laws. The strict limits on offering size -- one day's painting, one month's weaving, single blocks of material -- prevent competitive display and ensure that religious devotion does not become a venue for wealth competition. White as the divine color connects to broader Greek associations of purity with the divine.

D: Original: Now that the whole city has been divided into parts of which the nature and number have been described, and laws have been given about all the most important contracts as far as this was possible, the next thing will be to have justice done. The first of the courts shall consist of elected judges, who shall be chosen by the plaintiff and the defendant in common: these shall be called arbiters rather than judges. And in the second court there shall be judges of the villages and tribes corresponding to the twelvefold division of the land, and before these the litigants shall go to contend for greater damages, if the suit be not decided before the first judges; the defendant, if he be defeated the second time, shall pay a fifth more than the damages mentioned in the indictment; and if he find fault with his judges and would try a third time, let him carry the suit before the select judges, and if he be again defeated, let him pay the whole of the damages and half as much again. And the plaintiff, if when defeated before the first judges he persist in going on to the second, shall if he wins receive in addition to the damages a fifth part more, and if defeated he shall pay a like sum; but if he is not satisfied with the previous decision, and will insist on proceeding to a third court, then if he win he shall receive from the defendant the amount of the damages and, as I said before, half as much again, and the plaintiff, if he lose, shall pay half of the damages claimed, Now the assignment by lot of judges to courts and the completion of the number of them, and the appointment of servants to the different magistrates, and the times at which the several causes should be heard, and the votings and delays, and all the things that necessarily concern suits, and the order of causes, and the time in which answers have to be put in and parties are to appear-of these and other things akin to these we have indeed already spoken, but there is no harm in repeating what is right twice or thrice:-All lesser and easier matters which the elder legislator has omitted may be supplied by the younger one.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Now that the whole city has been divided into parts of which the nature and number have been described, and laws have been given about all the most important contracts as far as this was possible, the next thing will be to have justice done. The first of the courts shall consist of elected judges, who shall be chosen by the plaintiff and the defendant in common: these shall be called arbiters rather than judges."

"And in the second court there shall be judges of the villages and tribes corresponding to the twelvefold division of the land, and before these the litigants shall go to contend for greater damages, if the suit be not decided before the first judges; the defendant, if he be defeated the second time, shall pay a fifth more than the damages mentioned in the indictment; and if he find fault with his judges and would try a third time, let him carry the suit before the select judges, and if he be again defeated, let him pay the whole of the damages and half as much again."

"And the plaintiff, if when defeated before the first judges he persist in going on to the second, shall if he wins receive in addition to the damages a fifth part more, and if defeated he shall pay a like sum; but if he is not satisfied with the previous decision, and will insist on proceeding to a third court, then if he win he shall receive from the defendant the amount of the damages and, as I said before, half as much again, and the plaintiff, if he lose, shall pay half of the damages claimed."

"Now the assignment by lot of judges to courts and the completion of the number of them, and the appointment of servants to the different magistrates, and the times at which the several causes should be heard, and the votings and delays, and all the things that necessarily concern suits, and the order of causes, and the time in which answers have to be put in and parties are to appear -- of these and other things akin to these we have indeed already spoken, but there is no harm in repeating what is right twice or thrice: All lesser and easier matters which the elder legislator has omitted may be supplied by the younger one."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Now that the city has been divided into its constituent parts and laws have been established for all the most important transactions, the next requirement is a system of justice.

The first court shall consist of judges elected jointly by the plaintiff and defendant -- these are properly called arbiters rather than judges. The second court shall consist of judges from the villages and tribes, organized according to the twelvefold division of the land. Cases not resolved at the first level proceed here for larger claims. A defendant defeated a second time must pay the original damages plus one-fifth more. If he still objects and takes the case to a third level -- the select judges -- and loses again, he pays the full damages plus half again as much.

For the plaintiff: if he loses at the first level but persists to the second court and wins, he receives the damages plus one-fifth. If he loses again, he pays a matching amount. If he still insists on proceeding to the third court and wins, he receives the damages plus half again as much. If he loses at this final stage, he pays half the damages he originally claimed.

As for the procedural details -- the assignment of judges by lot, their numbers, the appointment of court staff, scheduling of hearings, voting procedures, continuances, case order, deadlines for filing answers and appearing -- all of this has already been discussed, but there is no harm in repeating what is correct two or three times. Any lesser details the senior legislator has overlooked, the younger one may supply.
D: Elegant:
The court system has three tiers. First: arbiters chosen jointly by both parties. Second: village and tribal judges for unresolved or larger cases. Third: select judges as the final court.

Escalation carries increasing risk. Defendants who lose at each higher level pay progressively more -- damages plus one-fifth at the second tier, damages plus half at the third. Plaintiffs face similar stakes: winning at higher levels brings larger awards, but losing brings proportional penalties.

Procedural details -- judge selection, scheduling, voting -- have been covered already. What is correct bears repeating. What the senior legislator omits, the younger may supply.
D: Summary: The judicial system has three tiers: mutual arbiters, tribal judges, and select judges. Each level of appeal carries increasing financial risk for both parties -- defendants pay progressively higher penalties for repeated losses, while plaintiffs also face escalating costs for persistent litigation. This structure discourages frivolous appeals while preserving access to justice.
D: Distilled: A three-tier court system with escalating financial stakes at each appeal level discourages frivolous litigation while ensuring that genuine disputes receive thorough review.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. This tiered judicial system with escalating penalties is remarkably modern in its approach to discouraging frivolous appeals while preserving the right to seek further review. The first tier -- jointly chosen arbiters -- reflects a preference for consensual dispute resolution. The escalating stakes create a natural equilibrium: only parties genuinely convinced of their case will risk higher courts. The pragmatic note that 'what is right may be repeated twice or thrice' and that younger legislators may fill gaps shows the Laws' realistic, incremental approach to institution-building -- a marked contrast to the Republic's top-down design. Compare Athenian arbitration procedures and the dikasteria system.

D: Original: Private courts will be sufficiently regulated in this way, and the public and state courts, and those which the magistrates must use in the administration of their several offices, exist in many other states. Many very respectable institutions of this sort have been framed by good men, and from them the guardians of the law may by reflection derive what is necessary, for the order of our new state, considering and correcting them, and bringing them to the test of experience, until every detail appears to be satisfactorily determined; and then putting the final seal upon them, and making them irreversible, they shall use them for ever afterwards. As to what relates to the silence of judges and the abstinence from words of evil omen and the reverse, and the different notions of the just and good and honourable which exist in our: own as compared with other states, they have been partly mentioned already, and another part of them will be mentioned hereafter as we draw near the end. To all these matters he who would be an equal judge, shall justly look, and he shall possess writings about them that he may learn them.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Private courts will be sufficiently regulated in this way, and the public and state courts, and those which the magistrates must use in the administration of their several offices, exist in many other states. Many very respectable institutions of this sort have been framed by good men, and from them the guardians of the law may by reflection derive what is necessary for the order of our new state, considering and correcting them, and bringing them to the test of experience, until every detail appears to be satisfactorily determined; and then putting the final seal upon them, and making them irreversible, they shall use them for ever afterwards."

"As to what relates to the silence of judges and the abstinence from words of evil omen and the reverse, and the different notions of the just and good and honourable which exist in our own as compared with other states, they have been partly mentioned already, and another part of them will be mentioned hereafter as we draw near the end. To all these matters he who would be an equal judge, shall justly look, and he shall possess writings about them that he may learn them."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Private courts will be adequately regulated by these provisions. As for public and state courts, and those that magistrates require for administering their various offices -- many respectable institutions of this kind already exist in other states, designed by capable lawmakers. The guardians of the law may study these, adapt what is useful for our new state, correct what needs improvement, and test everything through practical experience until every detail is satisfactorily determined. Once finalized, they shall set the permanent seal upon these institutions and use them unchanged thereafter.

Regarding judicial conduct -- the silence of judges, the avoidance of ill-omened words, and the varying conceptions of justice, goodness, and honor that distinguish our state from others -- these matters have been partly discussed already and will be addressed further as we approach the end of our discussion. Any person who aspires to be a fair and equal judge must attend carefully to all of these matters, and must possess written texts about them for study.
D: Elegant:
Private courts are now regulated. For public and state courts, the guardians of the law should study existing institutions from other states, adapt and correct them through practical testing, and once satisfied, fix them permanently.

Judicial conduct -- silence, avoidance of ill-omened words, and the distinctive conceptions of justice in our state versus others -- has been partly discussed and will be completed near the end. Any fair judge must study written texts on all these matters.
D: Summary: Private courts are sufficiently regulated. Public courts should be modeled on the best existing institutions from other states, adapted through experience, and then permanently established. Fair judges must study written texts on judicial conduct and the state's distinctive conception of justice.
D: Distilled: Good institutions need not be invented from scratch; they should be borrowed from the best existing models, tested through experience, and then made permanent.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. This passage reveals a striking pragmatism -- rather than designing public courts from first principles, Plato recommends studying existing institutions and adapting them. This empirical, comparative approach distinguishes the Laws sharply from the Republic's more deductive method. The emphasis on written texts for judges is significant: Plato, who in the Phaedrus (275a-278b) expressed deep reservations about writing, here recognizes its necessity for institutional continuity. The phrase 'putting the final seal upon them, and making them irreversible' shows the tension between adaptability and permanence that runs throughout the Laws -- institutions must be tested but eventually fixed.

D: Original: For of all kinds of knowledge the knowledge of good laws has the greatest power of improving the learner; otherwise there would be no meaning the divine and admirable law possessing a name akin to mind (nous, nomos). And of all other words, such as the praises and censures of individuals which occur in poetry and also in prose, whether written down or uttered in daily conversation, whether men dispute about them in the spirit of contention or weakly assent to them, as is often the case-of all these the one sure test is the writings of the legislator, which the righteous judge ought to have in his mind as the antidote of all other words, and thus make himself and the city stand upright, procuring for the good the continuance and increase of justice, and for the bad, on the other hand, a conversion from ignorance and intemperance, and in general from all unrighteousness, as far as their evil minds can be healed, but to those whose web of life is in reality finished, giving death, which is the only remedy for souls in their condition, as I may say truly again and again. And such judges and chiefs of judges will be worthy of receiving praise from the whole city.
D: Narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "For of all kinds of knowledge the knowledge of good laws has the greatest power of improving the learner; otherwise there would be no meaning in the divine and admirable law possessing a name akin to mind (nous, nomos). And of all other words, such as the praises and censures of individuals which occur in poetry and also in prose, whether written down or uttered in daily conversation, whether men dispute about them in the spirit of contention or weakly assent to them, as is often the case -- of all these the one sure test is the writings of the legislator, which the righteous judge ought to have in his mind as the antidote of all other words, and thus make himself and the city stand upright, procuring for the good the continuance and increase of justice, and for the bad, on the other hand, a conversion from ignorance and intemperance, and in general from all unrighteousness, as far as their evil minds can be healed, but to those whose web of life is in reality finished, giving death, which is the only remedy for souls in their condition, as I may say truly again and again. And such judges and chiefs of judges will be worthy of receiving praise from the whole city."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Of all forms of knowledge, the knowledge of good laws has the greatest power to improve the person who studies them. This is no accident -- there is a deep meaning in the fact that the word for law (nomos) is related to the word for mind (nous).

All other forms of discourse -- the praise and blame of individuals found in poetry and prose, whether written or spoken in daily conversation, whether hotly debated or weakly accepted -- all of these find their true standard in the writings of the legislator. The righteous judge should carry these writings in his mind as an antidote to all other words. In this way he makes both himself and the city stand upright: for the good, securing the continuation and growth of justice; for the bad, working a conversion from ignorance, excess, and all forms of injustice, insofar as their corrupted minds can still be healed. But for those whose life's thread is truly exhausted -- the incurable -- death is the only remedy for souls in their condition, as I have said and will say again.

Judges and chief judges who fulfill this role will be worthy of the entire city's praise.
D: Elegant:
The knowledge of good laws is the most improving of all knowledge -- fittingly, since the word for law (nomos) is kin to the word for mind (nous). The legislator's writings are the one sure standard against which all other words -- poetry, prose, conversation, debate -- must be tested.

The righteous judge carries this standard as an antidote, making himself and the city upright: sustaining justice for the good, converting the bad from ignorance insofar as they can be healed, and giving death to the incurable as their only remedy. Such judges deserve the city's highest praise.
D: Summary: The knowledge of good laws is supremely educative, reflecting the etymological kinship between law (nomos) and mind (nous). The legislator's writings serve as the ultimate standard for evaluating all discourse. Judges who apply this standard serve the good by sustaining justice, the reformable by converting them from vice, and the incurable by granting death as their only remedy.
D: Distilled: Law is akin to mind itself, and the legislator's writings are the one sure standard by which all other words and deeds must be measured.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The etymology connecting nomos (law/custom) and nous (mind/intellect) is one of Plato's most famous wordplays, suggesting that true law is inherently rational -- not merely conventional but expressive of intelligence itself. Compare Cratylus 411b-c on the relationship between names and natures. The image of the legislator's writings as an 'antidote' (alexipharmakon) to other words casts law as medicine for the soul -- consistent with the therapeutic penology developed throughout the Laws. The threefold judicial function -- sustaining the good, reforming the bad, removing the incurable -- provides a complete moral typology of the citizens. Compare Republic 409e-410a on incurable souls.

D: Original: When the suits of the year are completed the following laws shall regulate their execution:-In the first place, the judge shall assign to the party who wins the suit the whole property of him who loses, with the exception of mere necessaries, and the assignment shall be made through the herald immediately after each decision in the hearing of the judges; and when the month arrives following the month in which the courts are sitting (unless the gainer of the suit has been previously satisfied), the court shall follow up the case, and hand over to the winner the goods of the loser; but if they find that he has not the means of paying, and the sum deficient is not less than a drachma, the insolvent person shall not have any right of going to law with any other man until he have satisfied the debt of the winning party; but other persons shall still have the right of bringing suits against him. And if any one after he is condemned refuses to acknowledge the authority which condemned him, let the magistrates who are thus deprived of their authority bring him before the court of the guardians of the law, and if he be cast, let him be punished with death, as a subverter of the whole state and of the laws.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "When the suits of the year are completed the following laws shall regulate their execution: In the first place, the judge shall assign to the party who wins the suit the whole property of him who loses, with the exception of mere necessaries, and the assignment shall be made through the herald immediately after each decision in the hearing of the judges; and when the month arrives following the month in which the courts are sitting (unless the gainer of the suit has been previously satisfied), the court shall follow up the case, and hand over to the winner the goods of the loser; but if they find that he has not the means of paying, and the sum deficient is not less than a drachma, the insolvent person shall not have any right of going to law with any other man until he have satisfied the debt of the winning party; but other persons shall still have the right of bringing suits against him."

"And if any one after he is condemned refuses to acknowledge the authority which condemned him, let the magistrates who are thus deprived of their authority bring him before the court of the guardians of the law, and if he be cast, let him be punished with death, as a subverter of the whole state and of the laws."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: After the year's lawsuits are concluded, the following rules shall govern their enforcement.

First, the judge shall award the winning party all property belonging to the losing party, except for bare necessities. This assignment shall be publicly announced by the herald immediately after each verdict, in the judges' presence. If the winner has not been satisfied by the month following the court session, the court shall pursue the case and transfer the loser's property to the winner. If the loser lacks the means to pay and the shortfall is at least one drachma, the insolvent party shall lose the right to bring any lawsuit against anyone else until the debt is fully satisfied. Others, however, may still bring suits against him.

If any person, after being condemned, refuses to recognize the authority of the court that convicted him, the magistrates whose authority has been defied shall bring him before the court of the guardians of the law. If he is found guilty there, he shall be executed as a subverter of the entire state and its laws.
D: Elegant:
Enforcement of judgments: the winner receives the loser's property (except bare necessities), announced immediately by herald. If payment is not made within a month, the court transfers property directly. Insolvent debtors lose the right to bring lawsuits until the debt is paid, though others may still sue them.

Anyone who refuses to recognize a court's authority after conviction shall be brought before the guardians of the law and, if found guilty, executed as a subverter of the state.
D: Summary: Court judgments are enforced swiftly: winners receive the loser's property except necessities, with court-ordered transfer if payment is delayed. Insolvent debtors lose litigation rights until debts are paid. Refusal to acknowledge a court's authority after conviction is treated as treason against the state, punishable by death.
D: Distilled: Defying a court's authority after conviction is an attack on the state itself and warrants death, because the legal system's authority is the foundation of all social order.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The swift enforcement mechanism -- public announcement, one-month grace period, then forced transfer -- shows a practical concern with judgment-proof defendants that plagues legal systems to this day. The loss of litigation rights for insolvent debtors creates pressure to pay while still allowing others to hold them accountable. The extreme penalty for contempt of court (death) reflects Plato's view that legal authority is constitutive of the state itself -- to reject it is not merely disobedience but dissolution of the political order. Compare Crito 50a-54d, where Socrates argues that citizens owe obedience to the laws that formed them.

D: Original: Thus a man is born and brought up, and after this manner he begets and brings up his own children, and has his share of dealings with other men, and suffers if he has done wrong to any one, and receives satisfaction if he has been wronged, and so at length in due time he grows old under the protection of the laws, and his end comes in the order of nature. Concerning the dead of either sex, the religious ceremonies which may fittingly be performed, whether appertaining to the Gods of the underworld or of this, shall be decided by the interpreters with absolute authority. Their sepulchres are not to be in places which are fit for cultivation, and there shall be no monuments in such spots, either large or small, but they shall occupy that part of the country which is naturally adapted for receiving and concealing the bodies of the dead with as little hurt as possible to the living. No man, living or dead, shall deprive the living of the sustenance which the earth, their foster-parent, is naturally inclined to provide for them. And let not the mound be piled higher than would be the work of five men completed in five days; nor shall the stone which is placed over the spot be larger than would be sufficient to receive the praises of the dead included in four heroic lines. Nor shall the laying out of the dead in the house continue for a longer time than is sufficient to distinguish between him who is in a trance only and him who is really dead, and speaking generally, the third day after death will be a fair time for carrying out the body to the sepulchre.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Thus a man is born and brought up, and after this manner he begets and brings up his own children, and has his share of dealings with other men, and suffers if he has done wrong to any one, and receives satisfaction if he has been wronged, and so at length in due time he grows old under the protection of the laws, and his end comes in the order of nature."

"Concerning the dead of either sex, the religious ceremonies which may fittingly be performed, whether appertaining to the Gods of the underworld or of this world, shall be decided by the interpreters with absolute authority. Their sepulchres are not to be in places which are fit for cultivation, and there shall be no monuments in such spots, either large or small, but they shall occupy that part of the country which is naturally adapted for receiving and concealing the bodies of the dead with as little hurt as possible to the living. No man, living or dead, shall deprive the living of the sustenance which the earth, their foster-parent, is naturally inclined to provide for them."

"And let not the mound be piled higher than would be the work of five men completed in five days; nor shall the stone which is placed over the spot be larger than would be sufficient to receive the praises of the dead included in four heroic lines. Nor shall the laying out of the dead in the house continue for a longer time than is sufficient to distinguish between him who is in a trance only and him who is really dead, and speaking generally, the third day after death will be a fair time for carrying out the body to the sepulchre."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: And so a person is born and raised, and in the same manner raises children of his own, engages in dealings with others, suffers consequences for wrongs he has committed, and receives justice for wrongs done to him. In due course he grows old under the protection of the laws, and his end arrives in the natural order of things.

Regarding the dead of both sexes: the religious ceremonies that should be performed -- whether directed to the gods of the underworld or of this world -- shall be determined by the religious interpreters with absolute authority. Burial sites must not occupy land suitable for farming, and no monuments of any size shall be placed on arable ground. Graves shall occupy only land naturally suited to receiving and concealing the dead, causing as little harm as possible to the living. No person, living or dead, shall deprive the living of the sustenance that the earth, our collective foster-parent, naturally provides.

The burial mound shall not be larger than what five men could build in five days. The gravestone shall be no bigger than what is needed to hold the deceased's epitaph -- no more than four lines of verse. The body shall not be laid out in the house any longer than necessary to confirm that the person is truly dead and not merely unconscious. Generally, the third day after death is the appropriate time for carrying the body to the tomb.
D: Elegant:
A person is born, raised, raises children, deals justly with others under the law's protection, and dies in natural course.

For the dead: religious interpreters have absolute authority over funeral rites. Graves must not occupy farmland -- the dead shall not deprive the living of sustenance. Mounds shall be no larger than five men can build in five days. Gravestones shall hold only four lines of verse. Bodies shall remain in the house only long enough to confirm death -- generally three days.
D: Summary: The law covers the entire arc of human life from birth to death. Burial regulations prioritize the living: graves must not occupy farmland, mounds and gravestones are strictly limited in size, and bodies must be buried by the third day. Religious interpreters have final authority over funeral rites.
D: Distilled: The dead must not burden the living -- graves shall not consume farmland, monuments shall be modest, and burial shall be swift.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The sweeping summary of a life lived under law ('born, raised, raises children, grows old, dies') is one of the Laws' most poignant passages, placing individual life within the framework of legal order. The burial regulations are strikingly practical and egalitarian: no one's grave may consume productive land, mounds are limited to prevent competitive display, and the four-line epitaph cap prevents the wealthy from dominating even in death. The three-day waiting period to confirm death (versus mere trance) shows medical awareness.
