Compare Republic 427b on leaving religious matters to the Delphic oracle, and Laws 958d-960b for the complete burial code.

D: Original: Now we must believe the legislator when he tells us that the soul is in all respects superior to the body, and that even in life what makes each one us to be what we are is only the soul; and that the body follows us about in the likeness of each of us, and therefore, when we are dead, the bodies of the dead are quite rightly said to be our shades or images; for the true and immortal being of each one of us which is called the soul goes on her way to other Gods, before them to give an account-which is an inspiring hope to the good, but very terrible to the bad, as the laws of our fathers tell us; and they also say that not much can be done in the way of helping a man after he is dead.
D: Narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "Now we must believe the legislator when he tells us that the soul is in all respects superior to the body, and that even in life what makes each one of us to be what we are is only the soul; and that the body follows us about in the likeness of each of us, and therefore, when we are dead, the bodies of the dead are quite rightly said to be our shades or images; for the true and immortal being of each one of us which is called the soul goes on her way to other Gods, before them to give an account -- which is an inspiring hope to the good, but very terrible to the bad, as the laws of our fathers tell us; and they also say that not much can be done in the way of helping a man after he is dead."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: We must accept the legislator's teaching that the soul is in every way superior to the body. Even during life, what makes each of us who we are is the soul alone. The body merely follows us about, bearing our outward likeness. When we die, therefore, it is entirely correct to call the bodies of the dead our "shades" or "images" -- because the true and immortal essence of each person, the soul, departs on its journey to the other gods to give an account of its life.

This is an inspiring hope for the good, but a terrible prospect for the wicked, as our ancestral traditions tell us. Those traditions also teach that very little can be done to help a person after death.
D: Elegant: The legislator teaches that the soul is entirely superior to the body. Even in life, the soul alone makes us who we are -- the body is merely our likeness. In death, bodies rightly become 'shades' or 'images,' while the true immortal self -- the soul -- departs to give an account before the gods. This is hope for the good and terror for the wicked. Little can be done to help anyone after death.
D: Summary: The soul is superior to the body in every respect and constitutes our true identity. After death, the body becomes a mere image while the immortal soul journeys to give account before the gods -- an inspiring hope for the virtuous and a dread for the wicked. Very little can be done for someone after death.
D: Distilled: The soul is our true identity; the body is merely its likeness, and after death the soul alone faces divine judgment.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. This passage presents the Laws' eschatology, which is more restrained than the elaborate afterlife myths in the Republic (Er myth, 614b-621d), Gorgias (523a-527a), and Phaedo (107c-115a). The reversal of 'shades' -- usually the soul's ghostly afterimage in Homer -- to mean the body left behind is philosophically bold: Plato inverts the Homeric worldview entirely. The claim that 'not much can be done' after death motivates the practical conclusion that follows: spend resources on the living, not on elaborate funerals. This connects to the Phaedo's argument that philosophy is 'practice for death' (67e) -- preparation must happen in life.

D: Original: But the living-he should be helped by all his kindred, that while in life he may be the holiest and justest of men, and after death may have no great sins to be punished in the world below. If this be true, a man ought not to waste his substance under the idea that all this lifeless mass of flesh which is in process of burial is connected with him; he should consider that the son, or brother, or the beloved one, whoever he may be, whom he thinks he is laying in the earth, has gone away to complete and fulfil his own destiny, and that his duty is rightly to order the present, and to spend moderately on the lifeless altar of the Gods below. But the legislator does not intend moderation to be take, in the sense of meanness. Let the law, then, be as follows:-The expenditure on the entire funeral of him who is of the highest class shall not exceed five minae; and for him who is of the second class, three minae, and for him who is of the third class, two minae, and for him, who is of the fourth class, one mina, will be a fair limit of expense.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "But the living -- he should be helped by all his kindred, that while in life he may be the holiest and justest of men, and after death may have no great sins to be punished in the world below. If this be true, a man ought not to waste his substance under the idea that all this lifeless mass of flesh which is in process of burial is connected with him; he should consider that the son, or brother, or the beloved one, whoever he may be, whom he thinks he is laying in the earth, has gone away to complete and fulfil his own destiny, and that his duty is rightly to order the present, and to spend moderately on the lifeless altar of the Gods below."

"But the legislator does not intend moderation to be taken in the sense of meanness. Let the law, then, be as follows: The expenditure on the entire funeral of him who is of the highest class shall not exceed five minae; and for him who is of the second class, three minae, and for him who is of the third class, two minae, and for him who is of the fourth class, one mina, will be a fair limit of expense."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: It is the living who should be helped -- by all their relatives -- so that during life they may become the holiest and most just of people, and after death may face no great sins requiring punishment in the world below.

If this is true, then no one should squander resources on the belief that the lifeless mass of flesh being buried is still somehow connected to the person they loved. They should understand that the son, brother, or beloved they think they are laying in the earth has already departed to complete and fulfill his own destiny. The duty of the living is to attend to the present and to spend moderately on what amounts to a lifeless altar offered to the gods below.

However, the legislator does not mean that moderation should be taken as stinginess. The law shall therefore be as follows: funeral expenses shall not exceed five minae for those of the highest property class, three minae for the second class, two minae for the third, and one mina for the fourth.
D: Elegant:
Help the living, not the dead. Relatives should ensure that a person lives justly, so that after death he faces no great punishment. Do not waste resources on a corpse under the illusion that the lifeless flesh is still connected to the person -- he has departed to fulfill his destiny. Spend moderately on funeral rites.

But moderation does not mean stinginess. Funeral expenses are capped by property class: five minae for the first, three for the second, two for the third, one mina for the fourth.
D: Summary: Resources should be devoted to helping the living become just rather than lavished on funeral expenses for the dead. The person buried has already departed; the body is merely lifeless flesh. Funeral costs are capped according to property class, with moderation distinguished from meanness.
D: Distilled: Help the living become just rather than lavishing resources on the dead, for the departed soul has moved on and the body is merely its abandoned image.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The practical application of the soul-body distinction from 12.34: if the soul is the true person and has already departed, extravagant funerals are literally misdirected. The phrase 'lifeless altar of the Gods below' is remarkably frank -- the corpse is merely a ritual object, not the person. The graduated funeral expense caps (by property class) prevent competitive display in death, consistent with the Laws' pervasive concern with equality and moderation. Compare Phaedo 115c-e, where Socrates tells Crito that the body being buried is not Socrates. The phrase about the departed fulfilling 'his own destiny' echoes the myth of Er (Republic 617e) where souls choose their next lives.

D: Original:
The guardians of the law ought to take especial care of the different ages of life, whether childhood, or manhood, or any other age. And at the end of all, let there be some one guardian of the law presiding, who shall be chosen by the friends of the deceased to superintend, and let it be glory to him to manage with fairness and moderation what relates to the dead, and a discredit to him if they are not well managed. Let the laying out and other ceremonies be in accordance with custom, but to the statesman who adopts custom as his law we must give way in certain particulars. It would be monstrous for example that he should command any man to weep or abstain from weeping over the dead; but he may forbid cries of lamentation, and not allow the voice of the mourner to be heard outside the house; also, he may forbid the bringing of the dead body into the open streets, or the processions of mourners in the streets, and may require that before daybreak they should be outside the city. Let these, then, be our laws relating to such matters, and let him who obeys be free from penalty; but he who disobeys even a single guardian of the law shall be punished by them all with a fitting penalty. Other modes of burial, or again the denial of burial, which is to be refused in the case of robbers of temples and parricides and the like, have been devised and are embodied in the preceding laws, so that now our work of legislation is pretty nearly at an end; but in all cases the end does not consist in doing something or acquiring something or establishing something-the end will be attained and finally accomplished, when we have provided for the perfect and lasting continuance of our institutions until then our creation is incomplete.

That is very good Stranger; but I wish you would tell me more clearly what you mean.

O Cleinias, many things of old time were well said and sung; and the saying about the Fates was one of them.

What is it?
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "The guardians of the law ought to take especial care of the different ages of life, whether childhood, or manhood, or any other age. And at the end of all, let there be some one guardian of the law presiding, who shall be chosen by the friends of the deceased to superintend, and let it be glory to him to manage with fairness and moderation what relates to the dead, and a discredit to him if they are not well managed."

"Let the laying out and other ceremonies be in accordance with custom, but to the statesman who adopts custom as his law we must give way in certain particulars. It would be monstrous for example that he should command any man to weep or abstain from weeping over the dead; but he may forbid cries of lamentation, and not allow the voice of the mourner to be heard outside the house; also, he may forbid the bringing of the dead body into the open streets, or the processions of mourners in the streets, and may require that before daybreak they should be outside the city."

"Let these, then, be our laws relating to such matters, and let him who obeys be free from penalty; but he who disobeys even a single guardian of the law shall be punished by them all with a fitting penalty. Other modes of burial, or again the denial of burial, which is to be refused in the case of robbers of temples and parricides and the like, have been devised and are embodied in the preceding laws, so that now our work of legislation is pretty nearly at an end; but in all cases the end does not consist in doing something or acquiring something or establishing something -- the end will be attained and finally accomplished, when we have provided for the perfect and lasting continuance of our institutions; until then our creation is incomplete."

Cleinias: "That is very good, Stranger; but I wish you would tell me more clearly what you mean."

The Athenian Stranger: "O Cleinias, many things of old time were well said and sung; and the saying about the Fates was one of them."

Cleinias: "What is it?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: The guardians of the law should take special care of citizens at every stage of life -- childhood, adulthood, and every age in between. At the very end of life, a particular guardian shall be chosen by the friends of the deceased to oversee funeral matters. It shall be to his credit if he manages everything with fairness and moderation, and to his discredit if he does not.

The laying out and other ceremonies should follow custom, but we must allow the statesman who codifies custom into law some latitude in certain particulars. It would be monstrous, for instance, to command anyone to weep or to refrain from weeping. But the law may forbid loud lamentation that carries beyond the house. It may prohibit displaying the dead body in the open streets or conducting mourning processions through them. And it may require that funeral parties exit the city before daybreak.

These shall be our funeral laws. Those who comply face no penalty; anyone who disobeys even a single guardian shall be punished by all of them with a fitting penalty. Special cases -- such as the denial of burial to temple-robbers, parricides, and similar criminals -- have already been addressed in earlier laws.

And so our work of legislation is very nearly complete. But in all endeavors, the true end does not consist in merely doing, acquiring, or establishing something. The end is truly achieved only when we have provided for the perfect and lasting continuance of our institutions. Until then, our creation remains incomplete.

Cleinias replied: "That is well said, Stranger, but I wish you would explain more clearly what you mean."

The Athenian Stranger answered: "Cleinias, many things were well said and sung in ancient times, and the saying about the Fates was one of them."

Cleinias asked: "What saying is that?"
D: Elegant:
Guardians oversee every life stage. At death, a designated guardian manages funeral proceedings -- earning credit for fairness and moderation, discredit for mismanagement.

Funeral customs may be codified into law with some latitude: no commanding tears, but lamentation may be confined to the house, bodies kept from the streets, and processions required to leave the city before dawn. Denial of burial for temple-robbers and parricides follows earlier laws.

The legislation is nearly complete -- but merely establishing laws is not the true end. The real achievement comes only when we provide for their perfect and lasting continuance. Until then, the work is incomplete.

Cleinias asked for clarification. The Athenian Stranger replied that the ancient saying about the Fates holds the answer.
D: Summary: Funeral procedures are regulated with sensitivity -- weeping cannot be commanded, but loud lamentation, street processions, and public display of corpses are restricted. With legislation nearly complete, the Athenian Stranger raises the crucial remaining question: how to ensure the laws' permanent continuance. He introduces this through the myth of the three Fates.
D: Distilled: Establishing laws is not the end; the true achievement is ensuring their perfect and lasting continuance, which remains the final unsolved problem.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This is the pivotal transition in Book 12 -- from completing the legal code to the deeper question of institutional permanence. The distinction between 'doing/acquiring/establishing' and 'providing for lasting continuance' frames the entire remaining discussion of the Nocturnal Council. The sensitivity about funeral emotions -- you cannot legislate tears -- shows Plato's awareness of law's limits in the emotional sphere, even as he restricts external expressions. The reference to the Fates (Moirai) prepares the metaphor of the state's institutional fabric as something 'woven' that must be made permanent. Compare Republic 546a-547a on the eventual decline of even the best state.

D: Original: The saying that Lachesis or the giver of the lots is the first of them, and that Clotho or the spinster is the second of them, and that Atropos or the unchanging one is the third of them; and that she is the preserver of the things which we have spoken, and which have been compared in a figure to things woven by fire, they both (i.e., Atropos and the fire) producing the quality of unchangeableness. I am speaking of the things which in a state and government give not only health and salvation to the body, but law, or rather preservation of the law, in the soul; and, if I am not mistaken, this seems to be still wanting in our laws: we have still to see how we can implant in them this irreversible nature.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "The saying that Lachesis or the giver of the lots is the first of them, and that Clotho or the spinster is the second of them, and that Atropos or the unchanging one is the third of them; and that she is the preserver of the things which we have spoken, and which have been compared in a figure to things woven by fire, they both (i.e., Atropos and the fire) producing the quality of unchangeableness."

"I am speaking of the things which in a state and government give not only health and salvation to the body, but law, or rather preservation of the law, in the soul; and, if I am not mistaken, this seems to be still wanting in our laws: we have still to see how we can implant in them this irreversible nature."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger explained: The saying holds that Lachesis, the giver of lots, is the first of the three Fates; Clotho, the spinner, is the second; and Atropos, the unchanging one, is the third. Atropos is the preserver of the things we have been discussing, which may be compared figuratively to things woven by fire -- both Atropos and fire producing the quality of irreversibility.

I am speaking of those elements in a state and government that provide not merely health and physical preservation, but law -- or rather, the preservation of law -- within the soul. Unless I am mistaken, this quality of permanence is precisely what our laws still lack. We have yet to discover how to implant in them this irreversible nature.
D: Elegant:
The three Fates: Lachesis assigns the lots, Clotho spins the thread, and Atropos -- the unchanging one -- preserves what has been woven, giving it the irreversibility of things sealed by fire.

The state needs its own Atropos: something that provides not just physical preservation but the preservation of law in the soul. This quality of permanence is exactly what our legislation still lacks.
D: Summary: Using the myth of the three Fates, the Athenian Stranger identifies what the legal code still needs: the quality of Atropos, the unchanging one, who preserves what has been woven and makes it irreversible. The state requires something that implants the laws permanently in the soul, not merely on paper.
D: Distilled: Every legal system needs its Atropos -- a force that makes laws irreversible by implanting them not just in the code but in the soul.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The three Fates (Moirai) from Greek mythology -- Lachesis (allotter), Clotho (spinner), Atropos (unturnable) -- become a political metaphor. Plato's use of them also appears in Republic 617c (the Allegory of Er), where the three Fates spin the cosmic spindle. Here the focus shifts from cosmic to political: the state needs its own principle of irreversibility. The comparison to 'things woven by fire' suggests a tempering process -- laws must be hardened through heat, not merely assembled. This metaphor prepares the argument that the Nocturnal Council will serve as the state's Atropos, preserving its laws through philosophical understanding rather than mere enforcement.

D: Original:
It will be no small matter if we can only discover how such a nature can be implanted in anything.

But it certainly can be; so much I clearly see.

Then let us not think of desisting until we have imparted this quality to our laws; for it is ridiculous, after a great deal of labour has been spent, to place a thing at last on an insecure foundation.

I approve of your suggestion, and am quite of the same mind with you.

Very good: And now what, according to you, is to be the salvation of our government and of our laws, and how is it to be effected?
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "It will be no small matter if we can only discover how such a nature can be implanted in anything."

The Athenian Stranger: "But it certainly can be; so much I clearly see."

Cleinias: "Then let us not think of desisting until we have imparted this quality to our laws; for it is ridiculous, after a great deal of labour has been spent, to place a thing at last on an insecure foundation."

Megillus: "I approve of your suggestion, and am quite of the same mind with you."

Cleinias: "Very good. And now what, according to you, is to be the salvation of our government and of our laws, and how is it to be effected?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias observed: "It would be no small achievement if we could discover how to implant such permanence in anything."

The Athenian Stranger replied: "But it certainly can be done -- that much I see clearly."

Cleinias urged: "Then let us not stop until we have imparted this quality to our laws. It would be absurd, after all this labor, to place our creation on an insecure foundation."

Megillus agreed: "I approve entirely. I am of the same mind."

Cleinias pressed: "Very good. Then what, in your view, will be the salvation of our government and our laws, and how can it be achieved?"
D: Elegant: Cleinias: discovering how to implant permanence would be a great achievement. The Stranger: it can certainly be done. Cleinias: then we must not stop until we succeed -- it would be absurd to build so much on an insecure foundation. Megillus agrees. Cleinias asks: what will save our government, and how?
D: Summary: The three interlocutors agree that they must not stop until they discover how to give their laws the quality of permanence. After all the labor of constructing the legal code, it would be absurd to leave it on an insecure foundation. Cleinias asks what will provide salvation for their government.
D: Distilled: After building an entire legal code, the three agree it would be absurd to leave it without a mechanism for permanent preservation.
Notes: Cleinias, the Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. This brief exchange is dramatically significant -- it is one of the rare moments when all three interlocutors actively participate, and it builds urgency for the Nocturnal Council proposal. Cleinias' architectural metaphor ('insecure foundation') echoes Republic 497c-d on the philosopher-king as the foundation of the just city. Megillus' agreement, though brief, is important because as a Spartan he represents practical political experience endorsing the need for institutional permanence. The question 'how is it to be effected?' drives the remainder of Book 12.

D: Original: Were we not saying that there must be in our city a council which was to be of this sort:-The ten oldest guardians of the law, and all those who have obtained prizes of virtue, were to meet in the same assembly, and the council was also to include those who had visited foreign countries in the hope of hearing something that might be of use in the preservation of the laws, and who, having come safely home, and having been tested in these same matters, had proved themselves to be worthy to take part in the assembly;-each of the members was to select some young man of not less than thirty years of age, he himself judging in the, first instance whether the young man was worthy by nature and education, and then suggesting him to the others, and if he seemed to them also to be worthy they were to adopt him; but if not, the decision at which they arrived was to be kept a secret from the citizens at large; and, more especially, from the rejected candidate. The meeting of the council was to be held early in the morning, when everybody was most at leisure from all other business, whether public or private-was not something of this sort said by us before?
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Were we not saying that there must be in our city a council which was to be of this sort: The ten oldest guardians of the law, and all those who have obtained prizes of virtue, were to meet in the same assembly, and the council was also to include those who had visited foreign countries in the hope of hearing something that might be of use in the preservation of the laws, and who, having come safely home, and having been tested in these same matters, had proved themselves to be worthy to take part in the assembly; each of the members was to select some young man of not less than thirty years of age, he himself judging in the first instance whether the young man was worthy by nature and education, and then suggesting him to the others, and if he seemed to them also to be worthy they were to adopt him; but if not, the decision at which they arrived was to be kept a secret from the citizens at large, and more especially from the rejected candidate."

"The meeting of the council was to be held early in the morning, when everybody was most at leisure from all other business, whether public or private -- was not something of this sort said by us before?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Did we not say that our city must have a council of this character? The ten eldest guardians of the law and all those who have won prizes for virtue were to assemble together. The council was also to include those citizens who had traveled abroad seeking knowledge useful for preserving the laws, and who, upon returning safely and being tested in these matters, proved themselves worthy of membership.

Each member was to select a younger companion of at least thirty years of age, judging first by his own assessment whether the young man was worthy by nature and education, and then presenting him to the others. If they too judged him worthy, he was accepted. If not, the rejection was to be kept secret from the general public and especially from the rejected candidate himself.

The council was to meet early in the morning, when everyone is most free from public and private business. Did we not establish something along these lines earlier?
D: Elegant: The council's composition was already outlined: the ten eldest law-guardians, all virtue-prize winners, and tested citizen-observers who traveled abroad for useful knowledge. Each member selects a younger companion (at least thirty), first judging his worth personally, then presenting him to the group. Rejections are kept secret -- especially from the rejected candidate. The council meets at dawn, when all are free from other obligations.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger recapitulates the council's already-established composition: the eldest law-guardians, virtue-prize holders, and tested foreign observers, each sponsoring a younger companion. The group meets at dawn and keeps its rejection decisions secret, particularly from rejected candidates.
D: Distilled: The council that will preserve the laws combines the city's most virtuous elders with tested younger members, meeting at dawn in a body whose internal deliberations remain confidential.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger recapitulates the Nocturnal Council's structure from earlier discussion (12.20). The secrecy around rejections serves a dual purpose: protecting rejected candidates from public shame and maintaining the council's mystique. The composition -- combining proven virtue, foreign experience, and youth -- mirrors the Academy's own structure of experienced philosophers mentoring younger students. This is Plato's mature institutional solution to the Republic's philosopher-king problem: not a single wise ruler, but a self-perpetuating body of the wisest citizens. Compare Republic 502c-d on the difficulty of establishing philosopher-rulers, and Statesman 292d-293a on the rarity of true political knowledge.

D: Original:
True.

Then, returning to the council, I would say further, that if we let it down to be the anchor of the state, our city, having everything which is suitable to her, will preserve all that we wish to preserve.

What do you mean?

Now is the time for me to speak the truth in all earnestness.

Well said, and I hope that you will fulfil your intention.

Know, Cleinias, that everything, in all that it does, has a natural saviour, as of an animal the soul and the head are the chief saviours.

Once more, what do you mean?

The well-being of those two is obviously the preservation of every living thing.

How is that?

The soul, besides other things, contains mind, and the head, besides other things, contains sight and hearing; and the mind, mingling with the noblest of the senses, and becoming one with them, may be truly called the salvation of all.

Yes, Quite so.

Yes, indeed; but with what is that intellect concerned which, mingling with the senses, is the salvation of ships in storms as well as in fair weather? In a ship, when the pilot and the sailors unite their perceptions with the piloting mind, do they not save both themselves and their craft?

Very true.

We do not want many illustrations about such matters:-What aim would the general of an army, or what aim would a physician propose to himself, if he were seeking to attain salvation?

Very good.

Does not the general aim at victory and superiority in war, and do not the physician and his assistants aim at producing health in the body?

Certainly.

And a physician who is ignorant about the body, that is to say, who knows not that which we just now called health, or a general who knows not victory, or any others who are ignorant of the particulars of the arts which we mentioned, cannot be said to have understanding about any of these matters.

They cannot.

And what would you say of the state? If a person proves to be ignorant of the aim to which the statesman should look, ought he, in the first place, to be called a ruler at all; further, will he ever be able to preserve that of which he does not even know the aim?

Impossible.
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "True."

The Athenian Stranger: "Then, returning to the council, I would say further, that if we let it down to be the anchor of the state, our city, having everything which is suitable to her, will preserve all that we wish to preserve."

Cleinias: "What do you mean?"

The Athenian Stranger: "Now is the time for me to speak the truth in all earnestness."

Cleinias: "Well said, and I hope that you will fulfil your intention."

The Athenian Stranger: "Know, Cleinias, that everything, in all that it does, has a natural saviour, as of an animal the soul and the head are the chief saviours."

Cleinias: "Once more, what do you mean?"

The Athenian Stranger: "The well-being of those two is obviously the preservation of every living thing."

Cleinias: "How is that?"

The Athenian Stranger: "The soul, besides other things, contains mind, and the head, besides other things, contains sight and hearing; and the mind, mingling with the noblest of the senses, and becoming one with them, may be truly called the salvation of all."

Cleinias: "Yes, quite so."

The Athenian Stranger: "Yes, indeed; but with what is that intellect concerned which, mingling with the senses, is the salvation of ships in storms as well as in fair weather? In a ship, when the pilot and the sailors unite their perceptions with the piloting mind, do they not save both themselves and their craft?"

Cleinias: "Very true."

The Athenian Stranger: "We do not want many illustrations about such matters. What aim would the general of an army, or what aim would a physician propose to himself, if he were seeking to attain salvation?"

Cleinias: "Very good."

The Athenian Stranger: "Does not the general aim at victory and superiority in war, and do not the physician and his assistants aim at producing health in the body?"

Cleinias: "Certainly."

The Athenian Stranger: "And a physician who is ignorant about the body, that is to say, who knows not that which we just now called health, or a general who knows not victory, or any others who are ignorant of the particulars of the arts which we mentioned, cannot be said to have understanding about any of these matters."

Cleinias: "They cannot."

The Athenian Stranger: "And what would you say of the state? If a person proves to be ignorant of the aim to which the statesman should look, ought he, in the first place, to be called a ruler at all; further, will he ever be able to preserve that of which he does not even know the aim?"

Cleinias: "Impossible."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias confirmed this was correct.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "Returning to the council -- if we establish it as the anchor of the state, then our city, possessing everything suitable to it, will preserve all that we wish to preserve."

Cleinias asked what he meant.

The Athenian Stranger replied: "Now I must speak the truth with full seriousness. Know this, Cleinias: everything that exists has a natural preserver. In an animal, the soul and the head are the chief preservers."

Cleinias asked him to explain further.

The Athenian Stranger elaborated: "The well-being of those two -- soul and head -- is obviously what preserves every living thing. The soul contains mind, among other things, and the head contains sight and hearing, among other things. When mind mingles with the noblest senses and becomes one with them, this union may truly be called the salvation of all."

Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger pressed on: "Consider what kind of intellect this is -- the kind that, when united with the senses, saves ships in both storms and fair weather. In a ship, when the pilot and sailors unite their perceptions with the pilot's guiding intelligence, do they not save both themselves and their vessel?"

Cleinias agreed this was true.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "We need not multiply examples. Simply consider: what aim does a general pursue for the army's preservation? What aim does a physician pursue? The general aims at victory and military superiority. The physician and his assistants aim at producing health in the body. A physician ignorant of health, a general ignorant of victory, or anyone ignorant of the specific aim of his art -- none of these can be said to understand their field."

Cleinias confirmed this.

The Athenian Stranger concluded: "And what of the state? If someone is ignorant of the aim toward which the statesman should direct everything, should he even be called a ruler? And will he ever be able to preserve something whose very purpose he does not understand?"

Cleinias replied: "Impossible."
D: Elegant:
The council must serve as the state's anchor. Every living thing has a natural preserver: in an animal, the soul and head. The soul contains mind, the head contains sight and hearing. Mind united with the noblest senses produces salvation.

Examples: a pilot's intelligence united with sailors' perceptions saves the ship. A general aims at victory, a physician at health. Without knowing their aim, none can succeed. The same applies to the state: a ruler ignorant of the statesman's true aim cannot preserve what he does not understand.
D: Summary: Through a series of analogies -- the soul and head preserving the body, the pilot saving the ship, the general pursuing victory, the physician pursuing health -- the Athenian Stranger establishes that every domain requires knowledge of its essential aim. The state is no different: a ruler who does not know the statesman's true aim cannot preserve the state.
D: Distilled: Just as a physician must know health and a general must know victory, a statesman must know the true aim of governance, or he cannot preserve the state.
Notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. This passage employs Plato's characteristic craft analogy (techne analogy) -- comparing statesmanship to medicine, navigation, and generalship -- which runs throughout the dialogues from early works like the Gorgias (464b-465e) to the Statesman (293a-e). The crucial move is asking what the statesman's aim is, paralleling the Republic's argument that rulers must know the Form of the Good (Republic 504e-505a). The metaphor of the council as the state's 'anchor' is nautical, connecting to the ship of state imagery used throughout Greek political philosophy. The soul-head analogy will be developed further as the Nocturnal Council becomes the state's 'head' containing its 'mind.'

D: Original: And therefore, if our settlement of the country is to be perfect, we ought to have some institution, which, as I was saying, will tell what is the aim of the state, and will inform us how we are to attain this, and what law or what man will advise us to that end. Any state which has no such institution is likely to be devoid of mind and sense, and in all her actions will proceed by mere chance.
D: Narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "And therefore, if our settlement of the country is to be perfect, we ought to have some institution, which, as I was saying, will tell what is the aim of the state, and will inform us how we are to attain this, and what law or what man will advise us to that end. Any state which has no such institution is likely to be devoid of mind and sense, and in all her actions will proceed by mere chance."
D: Refashioned: The Athenian Stranger continued: Therefore, if our settlement is to be truly complete, we need an institution that does three things: it identifies the aim of the state, it explains how to achieve that aim, and it determines what laws or what persons can guide us toward it. Any state that lacks such an institution is essentially mindless and senseless, and everything it does will be governed by mere chance.
D: Elegant: A complete state needs an institution that identifies its aim, explains how to achieve it, and determines what laws and leaders can guide it there. Without this, the state is mindless -- all its actions governed by chance.
D: Summary: Every state needs a central institution that can articulate the state's purpose, explain how to achieve it, and identify the laws and leaders needed. Without such an institution, the state acts mindlessly and by chance.
D: Distilled: A state without an institution that knows and articulates its purpose is mindless and acts by mere chance.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. This passage states the core argument for the Nocturnal Council with crystalline clarity. The three functions -- identifying the aim, explaining the method, and selecting the guides -- correspond to theoretical, practical, and evaluative wisdom. The description of a state without such guidance as 'devoid of mind and sense' makes explicit the body-politic analogy: the Nocturnal Council is literally the state's brain. Compare Republic 428b-429a, where the wisdom of the whole city resides in its smallest part (the guardians). The phrase 'proceed by mere chance' (tuche) opposes the Platonic ideal that governance should be guided by knowledge (episteme), not fortune.

D: Original:
Very true.

In which, then, of the parts or institutions of the state is any such guardian power to be found? Can we say?

I am not quite certain, Stranger; but I have a suspicion that you are referring to the assembly which you just now said was to meet at night.

You understand me perfectly, Cleinias; and we must assume, as the argument iniplies, that this council possesses all virtue; and the beginning of virtue is not to make mistakes by guessing many things, but to look steadily at one thing, and on this to fix all our aims.

Quite true.
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "Very true."

The Athenian Stranger: "In which, then, of the parts or institutions of the state is any such guardian power to be found? Can we say?"

Cleinias: "I am not quite certain, Stranger; but I have a suspicion that you are referring to the assembly which you just now said was to meet at night."

The Athenian Stranger: "You understand me perfectly, Cleinias; and we must assume, as the argument implies, that this council possesses all virtue; and the beginning of virtue is not to make mistakes by guessing many things, but to look steadily at one thing, and on this to fix all our aims."

Cleinias: "Quite true."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger asked: "In which part or institution of the state, then, is this guardian power to be found? Can we identify it?"

Cleinias replied: "I am not entirely certain, Stranger, but I suspect you are referring to the assembly you just described as meeting before dawn."

The Athenian Stranger confirmed: "You understand me perfectly, Cleinias. And as our argument requires, we must assume that this council possesses all virtue. The beginning of virtue is not to stumble by guessing at many different aims, but to look steadily at one single thing and direct everything toward it."

Cleinias agreed: "Quite true."
D: Elegant: Which institution will serve as the state's guardian intelligence? Cleinias correctly identifies the pre-dawn assembly. The Stranger confirms: this council must possess all virtue. The foundation of virtue is not guessing at many aims but fixing steadily on one -- and directing everything toward it.
D: Summary: Cleinias correctly identifies the Nocturnal Council as the institution that will serve as the state's guardian intelligence. The Athenian Stranger confirms this and states its essential principle: virtue begins with looking steadily at one thing rather than guessing at many, and directing all aims toward it.
D: Distilled: The Nocturnal Council is the state's guardian intelligence, and its virtue lies in fixing steadily on a single aim rather than scattering among many.
Notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. Cleinias' identification of the 'assembly which meets at night' gives the Nocturnal Council (nukterinos sullogos) its name -- though it actually meets before dawn. The principle that virtue means 'looking steadily at one thing' rather than 'guessing many things' is the practical application of the Republic's Form of the Good (505a-509b): the philosopher-ruler's power derives from seeing the single unifying principle behind all particular goods. The contrast between 'guessing' (stochazesthai) and 'looking steadily' (blepein) distinguishes empirical groping from genuine knowledge. This is Plato's last word on the unity of virtue -- a question that occupied him from the Protagoras through the Republic.

D: Original: Then now we shall see why there is nothing wonderful in states going astray-the reason is that their legislators have such different aims; nor is there anything wonderful in some laying down as their rule of justice, that certain individuals should bear rule in the state, whether they be good or bad, and others that the citizens should be rich, not caring whether they are the slaves of other men or not. The tendency of others, again, is towards freedom; and some legislate with a view to two things at once-they want to be at the same time free and the lords of other states; but the wisest men, as they deem themselves to be, look to all these and similar aims, and there is no one of them which they exclusively honour, and to which they would have all things look.
D: Narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "Then now we shall see why there is nothing wonderful in states going astray -- the reason is that their legislators have such different aims; nor is there anything wonderful in some laying down as their rule of justice, that certain individuals should bear rule in the state, whether they be good or bad, and others that the citizens should be rich, not caring whether they are the slaves of other men or not. The tendency of others, again, is towards freedom; and some legislate with a view to two things at once -- they want to be at the same time free and the lords of other states; but the wisest men, as they deem themselves to be, look to all these and similar aims, and there is no one of them which they exclusively honour, and to which they would have all things look."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Now we can see why it is no surprise that states go astray -- the reason is that their legislators pursue such different aims. It is equally unsurprising that some establish as their principle of justice that certain individuals should rule, regardless of whether they are good or bad. Others make wealth their aim, indifferent to whether citizens are enslaved to other nations or not. Still others aim primarily at freedom. Some try to pursue two goals simultaneously -- wanting their citizens to be both free and rulers over other states.

And those who consider themselves the wisest of all look to all these aims and more, without settling exclusively on any single one to which everything else should be directed.
D: Elegant: States go astray because their legislators pursue conflicting aims: some prioritize who rules (regardless of merit), others prioritize wealth (regardless of servitude), others freedom, and some want both freedom and empire simultaneously. The supposedly wisest look to all these aims at once, without fixing exclusively on any single one.
D: Summary: States fail because their legislators lack a unified aim. Some prioritize political power, others wealth, others freedom, and some try to combine freedom with imperial domination. Even those considered wisest scatter their attention across multiple aims without settling on one.
D: Distilled: States go astray because their legislators scatter their aims across power, wealth, freedom, and empire, rather than fixing on the single aim of virtue.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. This passage provides a typology of failed political aims that maps onto the degenerate regimes in Republic Book 8: timocracy (rule by the strong), oligarchy (wealth), democracy (freedom), and tyranny (power). The critique of those who pursue 'two things at once' -- freedom and empire -- pointedly describes Athens and perhaps Sparta. The key philosophical move is arguing that even eclecticism (pursuing all these aims without hierarchy) is a failure, because the diversity of aims guarantees internal contradiction. Only virtue as a unified aim can provide coherent direction. Compare Republic 544c-545b on the progression of degenerate constitutions.

D: Original:
Then, Stranger, our former assertion will hold, for we were saying that laws generally should look to one thing only; and this, as we admitted, was rightly said to be virtue.

Yes.

And we said that virtue was of four kinds?

Quite true.

And that mind was the leader of the four, and that to her the three other virtues and all other things ought to have regard?
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "Then, Stranger, our former assertion will hold, for we were saying that laws generally should look to one thing only; and this, as we admitted, was rightly said to be virtue."

The Athenian Stranger: "Yes."

Cleinias: "And we said that virtue was of four kinds?"

The Athenian Stranger: "Quite true."

Cleinias: "And that mind was the leader of the four, and that to her the three other virtues and all other things ought to have regard?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias said: "Then our earlier position stands, Stranger. We were saying that all laws should be directed toward one thing only, and we correctly identified that one thing as virtue."

The Athenian Stranger confirmed: "Yes."

Cleinias continued: "And we said that virtue has four kinds?"

The Athenian Stranger agreed: "Quite true."

Cleinias concluded: "And that mind is the leader of the four, and that the three other virtues -- along with everything else -- should be directed toward it?"
D: Elegant: Cleinias recapitulates: laws should aim at one thing -- virtue. Virtue has four kinds. Mind leads the four, and the other three virtues along with everything else should be directed toward it.
D: Summary: Cleinias confirms the established position: all laws should aim at virtue, virtue has four kinds (courage, temperance, justice, and wisdom), and mind or wisdom leads the other three. All other things should be subordinated to it.
D: Distilled: All laws should aim at virtue, and among virtue's four kinds, mind is the leader to which the other three must defer.
Notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. This recapitulation references the foundational argument of Laws Book 1 (631b-632d), where the four virtues are established with wisdom (phronesis/nous) as their leader. The four cardinal virtues -- wisdom, courage, temperance, justice -- were a standard framework in Platonic thought, developed fully in Republic Book 4 (427e-435b). The hierarchical ordering with mind at the top is crucial for what follows: the Nocturnal Council, as the state's embodiment of mind, must therefore lead all other institutions. Cleinias' active recollection of these principles shows the dialogue's pedagogical progress -- the student can now reconstruct the argument himself.

D: Original: You follow me capitally, Cleinias, and I would ask you to follow me to the end, for we have already said that the mind of the pilot, the mind of the physician and of the general look to that one thing to which they ought to look; and now we may turn to mind political, of which, as of a human creature, we will ask a question:-O wonderful being, and to what are you looking? The physician is able to tell his single aim in life, but you, the superior, as you declare yourself to be, of all intelligent beings, when you are asked are not able to tell. Can you, Megillus, and you, Cleinias, say distinctly what is the aim of mind political, in return for the many explanations of things which I have given you?
D: Narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "You follow me capitally, Cleinias, and I would ask you to follow me to the end, for we have already said that the mind of the pilot, the mind of the physician and of the general look to that one thing to which they ought to look; and now we may turn to mind political, of which, as of a human creature, we will ask a question: O wonderful being, and to what are you looking? The physician is able to tell his single aim in life, but you, the superior, as you declare yourself to be, of all intelligent beings, when you are asked are not able to tell. Can you, Megillus, and you, Cleinias, say distinctly what is the aim of mind political, in return for the many explanations of things which I have given you?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: "You follow me admirably, Cleinias, and I ask you to stay with me to the end. We have established that the pilot's mind, the physician's mind, and the general's mind each look toward the one thing they ought to look toward. Now let us turn to the political mind and address it directly, as we would a human being:

'O remarkable being -- toward what are you looking? The physician can state his single aim clearly. But you -- who declare yourself superior to all other intelligent beings -- when asked, you cannot state yours.'

Can you, Megillus, and you, Cleinias, after all the explanations I have provided, tell me distinctly: what is the aim of political intelligence?"
D: Elegant:
The pilot's mind aims at safe passage, the physician's at health, the general's at victory. Now address the political mind directly: 'What are you looking toward? The physician can state his aim clearly -- but you, who claim superiority over all intelligent beings, cannot.'

Can Megillus and Cleinias, after all these explanations, state the aim of political intelligence?
D: Summary: After establishing that each profession's mind has a clear aim, the Athenian Stranger directly challenges the political mind to state its own. He asks Megillus and Cleinias whether, after extensive discussion, they can identify the specific aim of political intelligence.
D: Distilled: The physician knows he aims at health, the general at victory -- but what is the single aim of political intelligence? Most statesmen cannot say.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The dramatic device of addressing the 'political mind' as though it were a person (prosopopoeia) creates a powerful rhetorical effect -- political wisdom is personified and then found wanting. The question 'to what are you looking?' demands the kind of clear, single-aim answer that Republic 505a-b poses about the Good: what is it that makes everything else worthwhile? The comparison to medicine, navigation, and generalship is a version of the craft analogy that runs from Plato's earliest dialogues (Ion, Hippias Minor) through the Statesman. The challenge to both Cleinias and Megillus personalizes the question -- these practical statesmen represent Cretan and Spartan political wisdom, which Plato respects but finds ultimately inadequate.

D: Original:
We cannot, Stranger.

Well, but ought we not to desire to see it, and to see where it is to be found?

For example, where?

For example, we were saying that there are four kinds of virtue, and as there are four of them, each of them must be one.

Certainly.

And further, all four of them we call one; for we say that courage is virtue, and that prudence is virtue, and the same of the two others, as if they were in reality not many but one, that is, virtue.

Quite so.

There is no difficulty in seeing in what way the two differ from one another, and have received two names, and so of the rest. But there is more difficulty in explaining why we call these two and the rest of them by the single name of virtue.

How do you mean?

I have no difficulty in explaining what I mean. Let us distribute the subject questions and answers.

Once more, what do you mean?
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "We cannot, Stranger."

The Athenian Stranger: "Well, but ought we not to desire to see it, and to see where it is to be found?"

Cleinias: "For example, where?"

The Athenian Stranger: "For example, we were saying that there are four kinds of virtue, and as there are four of them, each of them must be one."

Cleinias: "Certainly."

The Athenian Stranger: "And further, all four of them we call one; for we say that courage is virtue, and that prudence is virtue, and the same of the two others, as if they were in reality not many but one, that is, virtue."

Cleinias: "Quite so."

The Athenian Stranger: "There is no difficulty in seeing in what way the two differ from one another, and have received two names, and so of the rest. But there is more difficulty in explaining why we call these two and the rest of them by the single name of virtue."

Cleinias: "How do you mean?"

The Athenian Stranger: "I have no difficulty in explaining what I mean. Let us distribute the subject between questions and answers."

Cleinias: "Once more, what do you mean?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias admitted: "We cannot, Stranger."

The Athenian Stranger replied: "But should we not desire to see it and to discover where it can be found?"

Cleinias asked: "Where, for example?"

The Athenian Stranger began: "We said there are four kinds of virtue, and since there are four, each must be distinct -- each one."

Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "Yet we also call all four by a single name. We say courage is virtue, prudence is virtue, and the same for the other two, as though they were not many things but one -- virtue."

Cleinias confirmed this.

The Athenian Stranger elaborated: "It is easy enough to see how any two of them differ from each other and have received different names. But it is far more difficult to explain why we call them all by the single name of virtue."

Cleinias asked what he meant.

The Athenian Stranger proposed: "What I mean is not hard to explain. Let us proceed through questions and answers."
D: Elegant: Cleinias admits they cannot identify the political aim. The Stranger presses: we should desire to see it. The path leads through the problem of virtue's unity. There are four virtues, each distinct -- yet we call them all 'virtue,' as though they were one. It is easy to explain how they differ; it is far harder to explain what makes them all deserve the same name.
D: Summary: Unable to state the aim of political intelligence, the interlocutors approach the question through the problem of virtue's unity and plurality. Each of the four virtues is distinct and has its own name, yet all are called by the single name 'virtue.' Explaining this unity is the harder philosophical task.
D: Distilled: The key to political wisdom lies in understanding what makes the four distinct virtues genuinely one -- the problem of the unity of virtue.
Notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The problem of the unity of virtue is one of Plato's most persistent philosophical concerns, addressed in the Protagoras (329c-333b), Meno (73e-74a), Laches (190d-199e), and Republic Book 4. Here, near the end of his last dialogue, Plato returns to it with renewed urgency: the Nocturnal Council must understand not just each virtue individually but what makes them all 'one.' This is equivalent to understanding the Form of the Good in the Republic -- the single principle that unifies all values. The dialectical method of questions and answers recalls the Socratic method at its purest.

D: Original:
Ask me what is that one thing which call virtue, and then again speak of as two, one part being courage and the other wisdom. I will tell you how that occurs:-One of them has to do with fear; in this the beasts also participate, and quite young children-I mean courage; for a courageous temper is a gift of nature and not of reason. But without reason there never has been, or is, or will be a wise and understanding soul; it is of a different nature.

That is true.
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Ask me what is that one thing which we call virtue, and then again speak of as two, one part being courage and the other wisdom. I will tell you how that occurs: One of them has to do with fear; in this the beasts also participate, and quite young children -- I mean courage; for a courageous temper is a gift of nature and not of reason. But without reason there never has been, or is, or will be a wise and understanding soul; it is of a different nature."

Cleinias: "That is true."
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger proposed: "Ask me what that one thing is which we call virtue and yet also divide into two, with courage as one part and wisdom as the other. Here is how the division occurs: one of them -- courage -- has to do with fear. Even animals and very young children share in it, because a courageous temperament is a gift of nature, not of reason. But wisdom is entirely different: without reason, there has never been, is not now, and never will be a wise and understanding soul. Wisdom is of a fundamentally different nature from courage."

Cleinias agreed: "That is true."
D: Elegant: The division: courage deals with fear and is shared by animals and children -- it is a gift of nature, not reason. Wisdom requires reason absolutely -- no soul has ever been or will ever be wise without it. They are fundamentally different in nature, yet both are called virtue.
D: Summary: Courage and wisdom differ fundamentally in nature. Courage relates to fear and is a natural endowment shared by animals and children. Wisdom requires reason and has never existed without it. Yet both are called virtue, which raises the question of what unites them.
D: Distilled: Courage is a natural gift even animals possess; wisdom requires reason absolutely -- yet both are called virtue, demanding an explanation of their unity.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The distinction between natural courage (shared with animals) and rational wisdom anticipates Aristotle's distinction between natural virtue and virtue proper (Nicomachean Ethics 1144b). The claim that wisdom is 'of a different nature' from courage directly engages the problem raised in the Protagoras: is all virtue really knowledge? Here Plato acknowledges that courage has a non-rational component, which is more nuanced than the Socratic identification of virtue with knowledge (Protagoras 360d). The phrase 'never has been, is, or will be' gives wisdom a quasi-eternal status -- it cannot arise from nature alone but requires the cultivation of reason. This has direct implications for the Nocturnal Council's educational program.

D: Original:
I have now told you in what way the two are different, and do you in return tell me in what way they are one and the same. Suppose that I ask you in what way the four are one, and when you have answered me, you will have a right to ask of me in return in what way they are four; and then let us proceed to enquire whether in the case of things which have a name and also a definition to them, true knowledge consists in knowing the name only and not the definition. Can he who is good for anything be ignorant of all this without discredit where great and glorious truths are concerned?

I suppose not.

And is there anything greater to the legislator and the guardian of the law, and to him who thinks that he excels all other men in virtue, and has won the palm of excellence, that these very qualities of which we are now speaking-courage, temperance, wisdom, justice?

How can there be anything greater?
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "I have now told you in what way the two are different, and do you in return tell me in what way they are one and the same. Suppose that I ask you in what way the four are one, and when you have answered me, you will have a right to ask of me in return in what way they are four; and then let us proceed to enquire whether in the case of things which have a name and also a definition to them, true knowledge consists in knowing the name only and not the definition. Can he who is good for anything be ignorant of all this without discredit where great and glorious truths are concerned?"

Cleinias: "I suppose not."

The Athenian Stranger: "And is there anything greater to the legislator and the guardian of the law, and to him who thinks that he excels all other men in virtue, and has won the palm of excellence, than these very qualities of which we are now speaking -- courage, temperance, wisdom, justice?"

Cleinias: "How can there be anything greater?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: "I have explained how courage and wisdom differ. Now you must tell me how they are one and the same. And suppose I then ask how the four virtues are one -- once you answer that, you may ask me in return how the one is four.

But then let us consider this further question: for things that have both a name and a definition, does true knowledge consist in knowing only the name, without the definition? Can anyone of real merit be ignorant of such things without disgrace, especially when great and noble truths are at stake?"

Cleinias replied: "I suppose not."

The Athenian Stranger pressed: "And is there anything of greater importance to the legislator, the guardian of the law, and the person who believes he excels all others in virtue and has won the highest distinction -- anything greater than these very qualities we are discussing: courage, temperance, wisdom, and justice?"

Cleinias answered: "How could there be anything greater?"
D: Elegant:
The two virtues differ -- now explain how they are one. Then how the four are one, and how the one is four. True knowledge requires not just knowing the name of something but also its definition. Can anyone of real worth be ignorant of these things without disgrace?

For the legislator and law-guardian -- the person who claims to excel all others in virtue -- nothing is more important than understanding courage, temperance, wisdom, and justice.
D: Summary: Understanding the unity and diversity of virtue requires knowing not just the names but the definitions. True knowledge grasps both how the four virtues differ and how they are one. For legislators and guardians of the law, nothing is more important than this understanding.
D: Distilled: True knowledge of virtue requires grasping not just its name but its definition -- understanding both how the four virtues differ and what makes them one.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The distinction between knowing a name and knowing a definition echoes the Seventh Letter (342a-343c), where Plato distinguishes levels of knowledge: name, definition, image, knowledge, and the thing itself. The insistence that guardians must know virtue's definition, not merely its name, establishes the philosophical curriculum for the Nocturnal Council. This is the practical application of the Republic's demand that rulers know the Form of the Good (504e-505b). The reciprocal questioning structure -- 'how are the four one? how is the one four?' -- is classic dialectical method, requiring understanding from both directions. Compare Meno 71b on the impossibility of knowing a thing's properties without knowing what it is.

D: Original: And ought not the interpreters, the teachers the lawgivers, the guardians of the other citizens, to excel the rest of mankind, and perfectly to show him who desires to learn and know or whose evil actions require to be punished and reproved, what is the nature of virtue and vice? Or shall some poet who has found his way into the city, or some chance person who pretends to be an instructor of youth, show himself to be better than him who has won the prize for every virtue? And can we wonder that when the guardians are not adequate in speech or action, and have no adequate knowledge of virtue, the city being unguarded should experience the common fate of cities in our day?
D: Narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "And ought not the interpreters, the teachers, the lawgivers, the guardians of the other citizens, to excel the rest of mankind, and perfectly to show him who desires to learn and know or whose evil actions require to be punished and reproved, what is the nature of virtue and vice? Or shall some poet who has found his way into the city, or some chance person who pretends to be an instructor of youth, show himself to be better than him who has won the prize for every virtue? And can we wonder that when the guardians are not adequate in speech or action, and have no adequate knowledge of virtue, the city being unguarded should experience the common fate of cities in our day?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Should not the interpreters of the law, the teachers, the lawgivers, and the guardians of the citizenry surpass all other people? Should they not be able to demonstrate perfectly -- to anyone who wishes to learn, and to anyone whose wrongdoing requires punishment and correction -- the true nature of virtue and vice?

Or shall some poet who wanders into the city, or some random individual who claims to be an instructor of youth, prove himself superior to the person who has won the prize for every form of virtue?

And when the guardians prove inadequate in both speech and action, lacking adequate knowledge of virtue -- when the city is effectively unguarded -- can we be surprised that it suffers the same fate as every other city in our time?
D: Elegant:
The state's interpreters, teachers, lawgivers, and guardians must surpass everyone in demonstrating the nature of virtue and vice -- to those who wish to learn and to those who need correction. Otherwise, some wandering poet or self-proclaimed youth instructor will outshine the supposedly virtuous.

When guardians lack adequate knowledge of virtue in both speech and action, the city is effectively unguarded -- and its collapse is no surprise.
D: Summary: The state's guardians must have superior knowledge of virtue and vice, able to teach the willing and correct the wayward. If they cannot, random poets and self-proclaimed teachers will fill the void. When guardians lack adequate virtue in speech and action, the city is unguarded and inevitably falls.
D: Distilled: When a city's guardians cannot demonstrate virtue in speech and action, the city is effectively unguarded and will inevitably fall.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The contempt for poets and 'chance persons who pretend to be instructors' echoes the Republic's expulsion of poets (595a-607b) and the Protagoras' critique of the sophists. The argument that guardians must excel in both speech and action -- logos and ergon -- reflects the Platonic insistence that theoretical knowledge must be united with practical virtue, a theme central to the Statesman (258e-260b). The phrase 'the common fate of cities in our day' is a somber reference to the political instability of fourth-century Greece, with constant coups, civil wars, and external invasions. This pragmatic concern drives Plato's insistence on the Nocturnal Council's philosophical competence.

D: Original:
Wonder! no.

Well, then, must we do as we said? Or can we give our guardians a more precise knowledge of virtue in speech and action than the many have? or is there any way in which our city can be made to resemble the head and senses of rational beings because possessing such a guardian power?

What, Stranger, is the drift of your comparison?
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "Wonder! No."

The Athenian Stranger: "Well, then, must we do as we said? Or can we give our guardians a more precise knowledge of virtue in speech and action than the many have? Or is there any way in which our city can be made to resemble the head and senses of rational beings because possessing such a guardian power?"

Cleinias: "What, Stranger, is the drift of your comparison?"
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias exclaimed: "No, no surprise at all!"

The Athenian Stranger asked: "Then must we proceed as we proposed? Can we equip our guardians with a more precise knowledge of virtue -- in both speech and action -- than the general public possesses? Is there some way to make our city resemble the head and senses of a rational being, by giving it such a guardian power?"

Cleinias asked: "What exactly is the point of your comparison, Stranger?"
D: Elegant: Can we equip our guardians with knowledge of virtue more precise than what ordinary people have? Can the city be made to resemble the head and senses of a rational being through such guardian power? Cleinias asks what the comparison means.
D: Summary: The Athenian Stranger asks whether the guardians can be given precise knowledge of virtue exceeding ordinary understanding, making the city like a rational being with a functioning head and senses. Cleinias asks him to clarify the analogy.
D: Distilled: The question is whether the city's guardians can possess such precise knowledge of virtue that the city itself becomes like a rational being with a functioning mind.
Notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The comparison of the city to a rational being with a 'head and senses' develops the body-politic metaphor introduced at 12.40. The Nocturnal Council is being explicitly identified as the city's head -- its rational faculty. This mirrors the Republic's tripartite soul analogy (435a-441c), where the city's three classes correspond to reason, spirit, and appetite. But the Laws' version is more institutional: instead of a philosophical class, there is a specific deliberative body. The question 'can we give our guardians more precise knowledge than the many have?' is a late echo of the Republic's fundamental question about whether philosopher-rulers can know the Good better than ordinary people.

D: Original: Do we not see that the city is the trunk, and are not the younger guardians, who are chosen for their natural gifts, placed in the head of the state, having their souls all full of eyes, with which they look about the whole city? They keep watch and hand over their perceptions to the memory, and inform the elders of all that happens in the city; and those whom we compared to the mind, because they have many wise thoughts-that is to say, the old men-take counsel and making use of the younger men as their ministers, and advising with them-in this way both together truly preserve the whole state:-Shall this or some other be the order of our state? Are all our citizens to be equal in acquirements, or shall there be special persons among them who have received a more careful training and education?
D: Narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Do we not see that the city is the trunk, and are not the younger guardians, who are chosen for their natural gifts, placed in the head of the state, having their souls all full of eyes, with which they look about the whole city? They keep watch and hand over their perceptions to the memory, and inform the elders of all that happens in the city; and those whom we compared to the mind, because they have many wise thoughts -- that is to say, the old men -- take counsel and, making use of the younger men as their ministers, and advising with them -- in this way both together truly preserve the whole state."

"Shall this or some other be the order of our state? Are all our citizens to be equal in acquirements, or shall there be special persons among them who have received a more careful training and education?"
D: Refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger explained: Can we not see that the city is like a body's trunk, and the younger guardians -- selected for their natural abilities -- are placed in the head of the state? Their souls are full of eyes with which they survey the entire city. They keep watch, commit their observations to memory, and report everything that happens to the elders. The elders, whom we compared to the mind because of their accumulated wisdom, take counsel using the younger members as their assistants, deliberating alongside them. In this way, both together truly preserve the whole state.

Shall this be the arrangement of our state, or some other? Shall all citizens be equal in their knowledge and training, or shall there be select individuals who receive more rigorous education?
D: Elegant:
The city is the trunk; the younger guardians, with their keen perceptions, are its eyes in the head, surveying everything and reporting to the elders. The elders, as the mind, take counsel and deliberate using the younger members as assistants. Together they preserve the state.

The question: shall all citizens be equal in education, or shall some receive more rigorous training?
D: Summary: The body-politic metaphor is completed: the city is the trunk, younger guardians are the eyes in the head, and the elders are the mind that processes their reports. Together they preserve the state. This raises the critical question of whether all citizens should be educated equally or whether some should receive specialized, more rigorous training.
D: Distilled: The state needs a head -- young guardians as its eyes, elder councilors as its mind -- and this requires that some citizens receive training far beyond what the ordinary citizen receives.
Notes: The Athenian Stranger. The organic metaphor of the state is now fully developed: trunk (citizen body), eyes (younger guardians), mind (elder councilors). This tripartite structure maps onto the Republic's three classes (producers/auxiliaries/rulers) but with the important difference that the Laws' system is institutional rather than class-based. The question about educational equality versus specialization is loaded -- Plato will argue for specialization, as he did in Republic 412b-414b (selecting guardians for special education). The image of souls 'full of eyes' recalls Argus Panoptes, the all-seeing mythological guardian, and anticipates the panopticon concept in political theory.

D: Original:
That they should be equal, my; good, sir, is impossible.

Then we ought to proceed to some more exact training than any which has preceded.

Certainly.

And must not that of which we are in need be the one to which we were just now alluding?

Very true.

Did we not say that the workman or guardian, if he be perfect in every respect, ought not only to be able to see the many aims, but he should press onward to the one? this he should know, and knowing, order all things with a view to it.

True.

And can any one have a more exact way of considering or contemplating. anything, than the being able to look at one idea gathered from many different things?

Perhaps not.

Not "Perhaps not," but "Certainly not," my good sir, is the right answer. There never has been a truer method than this discovered by any man.

I bow to your authority, Stranger; let us proceed in the way which you propose.
D: Narrative:
Cleinias: "That they should be equal, my good sir, is impossible."

The Athenian Stranger: "Then we ought to proceed to some more exact training than any which has preceded."

Cleinias: "Certainly."

The Athenian Stranger: "And must not that of which we are in need be the one to which we were just now alluding?"

Cleinias: "Very true."

The Athenian Stranger: "Did we not say that the workman or guardian, if he be perfect in every respect, ought not only to be able to see the many aims, but he should press onward to the one? This he should know, and knowing, order all things with a view to it."

Cleinias: "True."

The Athenian Stranger: "And can any one have a more exact way of considering or contemplating anything, than the being able to look at one idea gathered from many different things?"

Cleinias: "Perhaps not."

The Athenian Stranger: "Not 'Perhaps not,' but 'Certainly not,' my good sir, is the right answer. There never has been a truer method than this discovered by any man."

Cleinias: "I bow to your authority, Stranger; let us proceed in the way which you propose."
D: Refashioned:
Cleinias replied: "That all citizens should be equal in training is impossible."

The Athenian Stranger agreed: "Then we must proceed to a more rigorous education than anything we have yet described."

Cleinias confirmed this was necessary.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "And the training we need is the one we just alluded to. We said that a perfect guardian must not only be able to see the many particular aims but must press beyond them to the one. He must know that single principle and order everything with a view to it."

Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger asked: "And is there any more exact way of examining anything than the ability to see one idea gathered from many different things?"

Cleinias answered hesitantly: "Perhaps not."

The Athenian Stranger corrected him: "Not 'perhaps not' but 'certainly not,' my friend. No truer method has ever been discovered by any human being."

Cleinias yielded: "I defer to your authority, Stranger. Let us proceed as you propose."
D: Elegant:
Equal education for all is impossible. The guardians need more rigorous training: specifically, the ability to see beyond the many particular aims to the one unifying principle, and to order everything accordingly.

The truest method of understanding anything: seeing one idea gathered from many different things. No better method has ever been discovered. The Stranger corrects Cleinias' hesitant 'perhaps not' to an emphatic 'certainly not.'
